# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle 1

### Sūtra 1

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should contemplate form as impermanent. Contemplating in this way is right contemplation. Right contemplation gives rise to revulsion. Revulsion gives rise to the joy of liberation. The joy of liberation gives rise to the realization of liberation. Realizing liberation, one knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'

"Likewise, contemplate feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as impermanent. Contemplating in this way is right contemplation. Right contemplation gives rise to revulsion. Revulsion gives rise to the joy of liberation. The joy of liberation gives rise to the realization of liberation. Realizing liberation, one knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 2

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should contemplate form as suffering. Contemplating in this way is right contemplation. Right contemplation gives rise to revulsion. Revulsion gives rise to the joy of liberation. The joy of liberation gives rise to the realization of liberation. Realizing liberation, one knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'

"Likewise, contemplate feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as suffering. Contemplating in this way is right contemplation. Right contemplation gives rise to revulsion. Revulsion gives rise to the joy of liberation. The joy of liberation gives rise to the realization of liberation. Realizing liberation, one knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 3

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should contemplate form as empty. Contemplating in this way is right contemplation. Right contemplation gives rise to revulsion. Revulsion gives rise to the joy of liberation. The joy of liberation gives rise to the realization of liberation. Realizing liberation, one knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'

"Likewise, contemplate feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as empty. Contemplating in this way is right contemplation. Right contemplation gives rise to revulsion. Revulsion gives rise to the joy of liberation. The joy of liberation gives rise to the realization of liberation. Realizing liberation, one knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 4

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should contemplate form as non-self. Contemplating in this way is right contemplation. Right contemplation gives rise to revulsion. Revulsion gives rise to the joy of liberation. The joy of liberation gives rise to the realization of liberation. Realizing liberation, one knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'

"Likewise, contemplate feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as non-self. Contemplating in this way is right contemplation. Right contemplation gives rise to revulsion. Revulsion gives rise to the joy of liberation. The joy of liberation gives rise to the realization of liberation. Realizing liberation, one knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 5

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "With regard to form, you should know form, the arising of form, and the cessation of form. With regard to feeling, you should know feeling, the arising of feeling, and the cessation of feeling. With regard to perception, you should know perception, the arising of perception, and the cessation of perception. With regard to formations, you should know formations, the arising of formations, and the cessation of formations. With regard to consciousness, you should know consciousness, the arising of consciousness, and the cessation of consciousness.

"What is form? The four great elements and the form derived from the four great elements are called form. The arising of form means that contact with delight arises. The cessation of form means that contact with delight ceases.

"What is feeling? There are six kinds of feeling. Feelings born of eye-contact, feelings born of ear-contact, feelings born of nose-contact, feelings born of tongue-contact, feelings born of body-contact, and feelings born of mind-contact are called feeling. The arising of feeling means that contact with delight arises. The cessation of feeling means that contact with delight ceases.

"What is perception? There are six kinds of perception. Perception of forms, perception of sounds, perception of smells, perception of tastes, perception of tangibles, and perception of mental objects are called perception. The arising of perception means that contact with delight arises. The cessation of perception means that contact with delight ceases.

"What are formations? There are six kinds of formations. Volitions regarding forms, volitions regarding sounds, volitions regarding smells, volitions regarding tastes, volitions regarding tangibles, and volitions regarding mental objects are called formations. The arising of formations means that contact with delight arises. The cessation of formations means that contact with delight ceases.

"What is consciousness? There are six kinds of consciousness body. Eye-consciousness, ear-consciousness, nose-consciousness, tongue-consciousness, body-consciousness, and mind-consciousness are called consciousness. The arising of consciousness means that contact with delight arises. The cessation of consciousness means that contact with delight ceases.

"A bhikṣu who knows form, the arising of form, and the cessation of form in this way, and who, with regard to form, is a knower, an understander, and a dispassionate contemplator, is called a bhikṣu who has right contemplation with regard to form. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: one who knows consciousness, the arising of consciousness, and the cessation of consciousness, and who, with regard to consciousness, is a knower, an understander, and a dispassionate contemplator, is called a bhikṣu who has right contemplation with regard to consciousness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 6

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "With regard to form, one who does not know, does not understand, does not leave behind, and is not dispassionate toward form, is unable to end suffering. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, one who does not know, does not understand, does not leave behind, and is not dispassionate toward consciousness, is unable to end suffering.

"Bhikṣus, with regard to form, one who is able to know, to understand, to leave behind, and to be dispassionate toward form, is able to end suffering. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, one who is able to know, to understand, to leave behind, and to be dispassionate toward consciousness, is able to end suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 7

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "With regard to form, one who does not know, does not understand, does not leave behind, and is not dispassionate toward form, one's mind is not liberated and one is unable to transcend fear. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, one who does not know, does not understand, does not leave behind, and is not dispassionate toward consciousness, one's mind is not liberated and one is unable to transcend fear.

"Bhikṣus, with regard to form, one who is able to know, to understand, to leave behind, and to be dispassionate toward form, one's mind is liberated and one is able to transcend fear. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, one who is able to know, to understand, to leave behind, and to be dispassionate toward consciousness, one's mind is liberated and one is able to transcend fear."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 8

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "With regard to form, if one takes delight in it, one takes delight in suffering. One who takes delight in suffering is not liberated from suffering. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one takes delight in them, one takes delight in suffering. One who takes delight in suffering is not liberated from suffering.

"Bhikṣus, with regard to form, if one does not take delight in it, one does not take delight in suffering. One who does not take delight in suffering is liberated from suffering. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one does not take delight in them, one does not take delight in suffering. One who does not take delight in suffering is liberated from suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 9

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "With regard to form, if one is bound by it, one is not liberated from suffering. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one is bound by them, one is not liberated from suffering.

"Bhikṣus, with regard to form, if one is not bound by it, one is liberated from suffering. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one is not bound by them, one is liberated from suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 10

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "With regard to form, if one takes delight in it, that is taking delight in the aggregation of suffering. One who takes delight in the aggregation of suffering is not liberated from suffering. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one takes delight in them, that is taking delight in the aggregation of suffering. One who takes delight in the aggregation of suffering is not liberated from suffering.

"Bhikṣus, with regard to form, if one does not take delight in it, that is not taking delight in the aggregation of suffering. One who does not take delight in the aggregation of suffering is liberated from suffering. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one does not take delight in them, that is not taking delight in the aggregation of suffering. One who does not take delight in the aggregation of suffering is liberated from suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 11

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "With regard to form, if one is bound by it, that is being bound by the aggregation of suffering. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one is bound by them, that is being bound by the aggregation of suffering.

"Bhikṣus, with regard to form, if one is not bound by it, that is not being bound by the aggregation of suffering. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one is not bound by them, that is not being bound by the aggregation of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 12

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Bhikṣus, a well-learned noble disciple who contemplates in this way becomes revulsed toward form, revulsed toward feeling, revulsed toward perception, revulsed toward formations, and revulsed toward consciousness. Being revulsed, one becomes dispassionate. Being dispassionate, one is liberated. Being liberated, the knowledge arises: 'I am liberated.' One knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 13

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"Bhikṣus, if it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Bhikṣus, a well-learned noble disciple who contemplates in this way becomes revulsed toward form. Being revulsed, one becomes dispassionate. Being dispassionate, one is liberated. Being liberated, the knowledge arises: 'I am liberated.' One knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'

"Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Bhikṣus, a well-learned noble disciple who contemplates in this way becomes revulsed toward feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Being revulsed, one becomes dispassionate. Being dispassionate, one is liberated. Being liberated, the knowledge arises: 'I am liberated.' One knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 14

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"Bhikṣus, if it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"A noble disciple who contemplates in this way, with right wisdom well seeing as it truly is, becomes free from craving and attachment to form, free from craving and attachment to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Being free from craving and attachment, one is liberated. Being liberated, the knowledge arises: 'I am liberated.' One knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 15

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"Bhikṣus, if it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"A noble disciple who contemplates in this way, with right wisdom well seeing as it truly is, becomes free from craving and attachment to form, free from craving and attachment to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. I say that such a person is able to end suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 16

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"Bhikṣus, if it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"A noble disciple who contemplates in this way, with right wisdom well seeing as it truly is, becomes free from craving and attachment to form, free from craving and attachment to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Being free from craving and attachment, one is liberated. Being liberated, the knowledge arises: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 17

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"A well-learned noble disciple who contemplates in this way becomes revulsed toward form, revulsed toward feeling, revulsed toward perception, revulsed toward formations, and revulsed toward consciousness. Being revulsed, one does not take delight. Not taking delight, one is liberated. Not taking delight and being liberated, one knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 19

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"A noble disciple who contemplates in this way becomes revulsed. Being revulsed, one becomes dispassionate. Being dispassionate, one is liberated. Being liberated, the knowledge arises: 'I am liberated.' One knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 20

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"A well-learned noble disciple who contemplates in this way becomes revulsed toward form, revulsed toward feeling, revulsed toward perception, revulsed toward formations, and revulsed toward consciousness. Being revulsed, one becomes dispassionate. Being dispassionate, one is liberated. Being liberated, one knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.' I say that such a person has transcended all suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 21

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"A well-learned noble disciple who contemplates in this way becomes revulsed toward form, revulsed toward feeling, revulsed toward perception, revulsed toward formations, and revulsed toward consciousness. Being revulsed, one does not take delight. Not taking delight, one is liberated. Being liberated, one knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.' I say that such a person has transcended all suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"A well-learned noble disciple who contemplates in this way becomes revulsed toward form, revulsed toward feeling, revulsed toward perception, revulsed toward formations, and revulsed toward consciousness. Being revulsed, one does not take delight. Not taking delight, one is liberated, and the knowledge arises: 'I am liberated.' One knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.' I say that such a person has transcended all suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 23

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"A noble disciple who contemplates in this way, with right wisdom well seeing as it truly is, becomes free from craving and attachment to form, free from craving and attachment to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. I say that such a person has transcended all suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 24

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"A noble disciple who contemplates in this way, with right wisdom well seeing as it truly is, becomes free from craving and attachment to form, free from craving and attachment to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Being free from craving and attachment, one is liberated. Being liberated, the knowledge arises: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.' I say that such a person has transcended all suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 25

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple regard it thus: 'This is mine, this is I, this is my self'?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, is not different from self, and does not exist within self, nor does self exist within it. This should be contemplated with right wisdom as it truly is.

"A noble disciple who contemplates in this way, with right wisdom well seeing as it truly is, the mind becomes free from craving and attachment to form, the mind becomes free from craving and attachment to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. I say that such a person has transcended all suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

> Impermanent and suffering, empty, and non-self;
> Aggregation, taste, harm, and leaving behind; and two in each;
> Fourteen in the dialogue section;
> These have been transmitted from mouth to mouth.

### Sūtra 26

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "With regard to form, you should know the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. With regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, you should know the gratification, the danger, and the escape.

"What is the gratification of form? Conditioned by form, pleasure and joy arise. This is the gratification of form. What is the danger of form? Form is impermanent, suffering, and subject to change. This is the danger of form. What is the escape from form? With regard to form, being able to regulate and abandon desire and craving, to transcend desire and craving. This is the escape from form.

"What is the gratification of feeling? Conditioned by feeling, pleasure and joy arise. This is the gratification of feeling. What is the danger of feeling? Feeling is impermanent, suffering, and subject to change. This is the danger of feeling. What is the escape from feeling? With regard to feeling, being able to regulate and abandon desire and craving, to transcend desire and craving. This is the escape from feeling.

"What is the gratification of perception? Conditioned by perception, pleasure and joy arise. This is the gratification of perception. What is the danger of perception? Perception is impermanent, suffering, and subject to change. This is the danger of perception. What is the escape from perception? With regard to perception, being able to regulate and abandon desire and craving, to transcend desire and craving. This is the escape from perception.

"What is the gratification of formations? Conditioned by formations, pleasure and joy arise. This is the gratification of formations. What is the danger of formations? Formations are impermanent, suffering, and subject to change. This is the danger of formations. What is the escape from formations? With regard to formations, being able to regulate and abandon desire and craving, to transcend desire and craving. This is the escape from formations.

"What is the gratification of consciousness? Conditioned by consciousness, pleasure and joy arise. This is the gratification of consciousness. What is the danger of consciousness? Consciousness is impermanent, suffering, and subject to change. This is the danger of consciousness. What is the escape from consciousness? With regard to consciousness, being able to regulate and abandon desire and craving, to transcend desire and craving. This is the escape from consciousness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 27

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "With regard to form, if one does not know the gratification of form as it truly is, does not know the danger of form as it truly is, and does not know the escape from form as it truly is, then one is unable to transcend form. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one does not know the gratification as it truly is, does not know the danger as it truly is, and does not know the escape as it truly is, then one is unable to transcend consciousness."

"Bhikṣus, with regard to form, if one knows the gratification of form as it truly is, knows the danger of form as it truly is, and knows the escape from form as it truly is, then one is able to transcend form. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one knows the gratification as it truly is, knows the danger as it truly is, and knows the escape as it truly is, then one is able to transcend consciousness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 28

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "With regard to form, if one does not know the gratification of form as it truly is, does not know the danger of form as it truly is, and does not know the escape from form as it truly is, then one is unable to leave behind form, to be dispassionate toward form, and to be liberated from form. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one does not know the gratification as it truly is, does not know the danger as it truly is, and does not know the escape as it truly is, then one is unable to leave behind consciousness, to be dispassionate toward consciousness, and to be liberated from consciousness."

"Bhikṣus, with regard to form, if one knows the gratification of form as it truly is, knows the danger of form as it truly is, and knows the escape from form as it truly is, then one is able to leave behind form, to be dispassionate toward form, and to be liberated from form. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one knows the gratification as it truly is, knows the danger as it truly is, and knows the escape as it truly is, then one is able to leave behind consciousness, to be dispassionate toward consciousness, and to be liberated from consciousness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 29

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "With regard to form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form, if one does not know them as they truly are, then one is unable to end suffering, to transcend suffering. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the gratification, the danger, and the escape, if one does not know them as they truly are, then one is unable to end suffering, to transcend suffering.

"Bhikṣus, with regard to form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form, if one knows them as they truly are, then one is able to end suffering, to transcend suffering. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the gratification, the danger, and the escape, if one knows them as they truly are, then one is able to end suffering, to transcend suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 30

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "With regard to form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form, if one does not know them as they truly are, then one's mind is not liberated from form and one is unable to transcend fear. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the gratification, the danger, and the escape, if one does not know them as they truly are, then one's mind is not liberated from consciousness and one is unable to transcend fear.

"Bhikṣus, with regard to form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form, if one knows them as they truly are, then one's mind is liberated from form and one is able to transcend fear. Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the gratification, the danger, and the escape, if one knows them as they truly are, then one's mind is liberated from consciousness and one is able to transcend fear."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 31

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I formerly, before the attainment of complete, perfect enlightenment, while still a bodhisattva, contemplated thus: 'What is the gratification of form, what is the danger of form, and what is the escape from form? What is the gratification of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, what is the danger, and what is the escape?'

"I contemplated thus: 'Conditioned by form, the pleasure and joy that arises; this is the gratification of form. That form is impermanent, suffering, and subject to change; this is the danger of form. With regard to form, being able to regulate and abandon desire and craving, to transcend desire and craving; this is the escape from form.'

"'Conditioned by feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the pleasure and joy that arises; this is the gratification of consciousness. That consciousness is impermanent, suffering, and subject to change; this is the danger of consciousness. With regard to consciousness, being able to regulate and abandon desire and craving, to transcend desire and craving; this is the escape from consciousness.'

"Bhikṣus, I, by truly knowing the gratification of form as the gratification, the danger as the danger, and the escape as the escape in this way, attained the realization of unexcelled, complete, perfect enlightenment.

"Likewise, I, by truly knowing the gratification of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as the gratification, the danger as the danger, and the escape as the escape, attained the realization of unexcelled, complete, perfect enlightenment."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 32

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa, with regard to form, does not know the gratification of form as it truly is, does not know the danger of form as it truly is, and does not know the escape from form as it truly is, then such a person is unable, with regard to form, to leave behind, to become dispassionate, and to be truly liberated. Being unable to be truly liberated, then such a śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa cannot be said to have the correct result, to abide in the correct result, to abide in the Dharma above.

"Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one does not know the gratification as it truly is, does not know the danger as it truly is, and does not know the escape as it truly is, then such a person is unable, with regard to consciousness, to leave behind, to become dispassionate, and to be truly liberated. Being unable to be truly liberated, then such a śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa cannot be said to have the correct result, to abide in the correct result, to abide in the Dharma above.

"Bhikṣus, if a śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa, with regard to form, knows the gratification of form as it truly is, knows the danger of form as it truly is, and knows the escape from form as it truly is, then such a person is able, with regard to form, to leave behind, to become dispassionate, and to be truly liberated. Being truly liberated, then such a śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa can be said to have the correct result, to abide in the correct result, to abide in the Dharma above.

"Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one knows the gratification as it truly is, knows the danger as it truly is, and knows the escape as it truly is, then such a person is able, with regard to consciousness, to leave behind, to become dispassionate, and to be truly liberated. Being truly liberated, then such a śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa can be said to have the correct result, to abide in the correct result, to abide in the Dharma above."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

### Sūtra 33

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa, with regard to form, does not know the gratification of form as it truly is, does not know the danger of form as it truly is, and does not know the escape from form as it truly is, then I do not regard such a person among the śramaṇas as a true śramaṇa, or among the brāhmaṇas as a true brāhmaṇa. Such a person is unable, in the present life, to know and abide in and realize by oneself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'

"Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one does not know the gratification as it truly is, does not know the danger as it truly is, and does not know the escape as it truly is, then I do not regard such a person among the śramaṇas as a true śramaṇa, or among the brāhmaṇas as a true brāhmaṇa. Such a person is unable, in the present life, to know and abide in and realize by oneself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'

"Bhikṣus, if a śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa, with regard to form, knows the gratification of form as it truly is, knows the danger of form as it truly is, and knows the escape from form as it truly is, then I regard such a person among the śramaṇas as a true śramaṇa, among the brāhmaṇas as a true brāhmaṇa. Such a person is able, in the present life, to know and abide in and realize by oneself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'

"Likewise, with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if one knows the gratification as it truly is, knows the danger as it truly is, and knows the escape as it truly is, then I regard such a person among the śramaṇas as a true śramaṇa, among the brāhmaṇas as a true brāhmaṇa. Such a person is able, in the present life, to know and abide in and realize by oneself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, were delighted and put it into practice.

> Impermanent and suffering, empty, and non-self;
> Knowing, not knowing, being able, not being able;
> Delight, binding, aggregation likewise;
> Fourteen in the dialogue, and also eight more;
> Two gratification sections, together with self-realization,
> Fear, śramaṇa, and the brāhmaṇa sūtra:
> All together there are thirty-two sūtras,
> The first compilation is completed.

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle One

### (1)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should contemplate form as impermanent. Contemplating in this way is right contemplation. One who rightly contemplates gives rise to disenchantment. From disenchantment, delight and craving are exhausted. When delight and craving are exhausted, this is called liberation of mind.

"Likewise, contemplate feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as impermanent. Contemplating in this way is right contemplation. One who rightly contemplates gives rise to disenchantment. From disenchantment, delight and craving are exhausted. When delight and craving are exhausted, this is called liberation of mind.

"In this way, bhikṣus, one whose mind is liberated, if wishing to verify it oneself, can verify it: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'

"As with contemplating impermanence, so too with suffering, emptiness, and non-self."

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (2)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Regarding form, you should rightly reflect, contemplating form as impermanent and knowing it as it truly is. Why is that? Bhikṣus, one who rightly reflects on form, contemplating form as impermanent and knowing it as it truly is, cuts off desire and craving for form. When desire and craving are cut off, this is called liberation of mind.

"Likewise, regarding feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, you should rightly reflect, contemplating consciousness as impermanent and knowing it as it truly is. Why is that? When one rightly reflects on consciousness, contemplating consciousness as impermanent, then desire and craving for consciousness are cut off. When desire and craving are cut off, this is called liberation of mind.

"One whose mind is thus liberated, if wishing to verify it oneself, can verify it: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.' As with rightly reflecting on impermanence, so too with suffering, emptiness, and non-self."

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (3)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Regarding form, if one does not know, does not understand clearly, does not cut off, and does not become free from desire, then one cannot cut off suffering. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: if one does not know, does not understand clearly, does not cut off, and does not become free from desire, then one cannot cut off suffering.

"Bhikṣus, regarding form, if one knows, if one understands clearly, if one cuts off, if one becomes free from desire, then one can cut off suffering. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: if one knows, if one understands clearly, if one cuts off, if one becomes free from desire, then one is capable of cutting off suffering."

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (4)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Regarding form, if one does not know, does not understand clearly, does not cut off, does not become free from desire, and the mind is not liberated, then one cannot transcend the terror of birth, old age, sickness, and death. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: if one does not know, does not understand clearly, does not cut off, does not become free from desire and craving, and the mind is not liberated, then one cannot transcend the terror of birth, old age, sickness, and death.

"Bhikṣus, regarding form, if one knows, if one understands clearly, if one cuts off, if one becomes free from desire, then one can transcend the terror of birth, old age, sickness, and death. Bhikṣus, if one knows, if one understands clearly, if one becomes free from desire and craving, and the mind is liberated, then one can transcend the terror of birth, old age, sickness, and death. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: if one knows, if one understands clearly, if one cuts off, if one becomes free from desire and craving, and the mind is liberated, then one can transcend the terror of birth, old age, sickness, and death."

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (5)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "One who delights in and is attracted to form delights in and is attracted to suffering. One who delights in and is attracted to suffering cannot gain liberation from suffering, cannot understand clearly, and cannot become free from desire. Likewise, one who delights in and is attracted to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness delights in and is attracted to suffering. One who delights in and is attracted to suffering cannot gain liberation from suffering.

"Bhikṣus, one who does not delight in or become attracted to form does not delight in suffering. One who does not delight in suffering gains liberation from suffering. Likewise, one who does not delight in or become attracted to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness does not delight in suffering. One who does not delight in suffering gains liberation from suffering.

"Bhikṣus, regarding form, if one does not know, does not understand clearly, does not become free from desire and craving, and the mind is not liberated; when the craving mind is not liberated, then one cannot cut off suffering. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: if one does not know, does not understand clearly, does not become free from desire and craving, and the mind is not liberated, then one cannot cut off suffering.

"Regarding form, if one knows, if one understands clearly, if one becomes free from desire and craving, and the mind attains liberation, then one can cut off suffering. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: if one knows, if one understands clearly, if one becomes free from desire and craving, and the mind attains liberation, then one can cut off suffering."

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (6)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Regarding form, if one does not know, does not understand clearly, does not become free from desire and craving, and the mind is not liberated, then one cannot transcend the terror of birth, old age, sickness, and death. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: if one does not know, does not understand clearly, does not become free from desire and craving, and the mind is not liberated, then one cannot transcend the terror of birth, old age, sickness, and death.

"Bhikṣus, regarding form, if one knows, if one understands clearly, if one becomes free from desire and craving, and the mind is liberated, then one can transcend the terror of birth, old age, sickness, and death. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: if one knows, if one understands clearly, if one becomes free from desire and craving, and the mind is liberated, then one can transcend the terror of birth, old age, sickness, and death."

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (7)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "One who delights in and is attracted to form delights in and is attracted to suffering. One who delights in and is attracted to suffering cannot gain liberation from suffering. Likewise, one who delights in and is attracted to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness delights in and is attracted to suffering. One who delights in and is attracted to suffering cannot gain liberation from suffering.

"Bhikṣus, one who does not delight in or become attracted to form does not delight in suffering. One who does not delight in suffering gains liberation from suffering. Likewise, one who does not delight in or become attracted to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness does not delight in suffering. One who does not delight in suffering gains liberation from suffering."

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

> Impermanence, suffering, emptiness, non-self, and right reflection;
> The four kinds of not knowing, and delight in form.

### (8)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Past and future form is impermanent, how much more so present form! Noble disciples, contemplating thus, do not look back on past form, do not long for future form, and regarding present form become disenchanted, free from desire, and rightly directed toward cessation. Likewise, past and future feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent, how much more so present consciousness! Noble disciples, contemplating thus, do not look back on past consciousness, do not delight in future consciousness, and regarding present consciousness become disenchanted, free from desire, and rightly directed toward cessation. As with impermanence, so too with suffering, emptiness, and non-self."

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (9)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Form is impermanent. What is impermanent is suffering. What is suffering is non-self. What is non-self is also not mine. Contemplating thus is called true and right contemplation. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent. What is impermanent is suffering. What is suffering is non-self. What is non-self is also not mine. Contemplating thus is called true contemplation.

"Noble disciples, contemplating thus become disenchanted with form, disenchanted with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Being disenchanted, they find no delight. Finding no delight, they attain liberation. In liberation, true knowledge arises: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'"

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (10)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Form is impermanent. What is impermanent is suffering. What is suffering is non-self. What is non-self is not mine. Contemplating thus is called true contemplation. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent. What is impermanent is suffering. What is suffering is non-self. What is non-self is not mine. Contemplating thus is called true contemplation.

"Noble disciples, contemplating thus are liberated from form, liberated from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. I say that such ones are liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, suffering, and distress."

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (11)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Form is impermanent. Whatever causes and conditions give rise to forms, they too are impermanent. How could forms born from impermanent causes and impermanent conditions be permanent? Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent. Whatever causes and conditions give rise to consciousness, they too are impermanent. How could consciousness born from impermanent causes and impermanent conditions be permanent? In this way, bhikṣus, form is impermanent; feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent. What is impermanent is suffering. What is suffering is non-self. What is non-self is not mine.

"Noble disciples, contemplating thus become disenchanted with form, disenchanted with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Being disenchanted, they find no delight. Finding no delight, they are liberated. In liberation, the knowledge and vision arises: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'"

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (12)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Form is impermanent. Whatever causes and conditions give rise to forms, they too are impermanent. How could forms born from impermanent causes and impermanent conditions be permanent? Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent. Whatever causes and conditions give rise to consciousness, they too are impermanent. How could consciousness born from impermanent causes and impermanent conditions be permanent?

"In this way, bhikṣus, form is impermanent; feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent. What is impermanent is suffering. What is suffering is non-self. What is non-self is not mine. Contemplating thus is called true contemplation. Noble disciples, contemplating thus are liberated from form, liberated from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. I say that such ones are liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, suffering, and distress."

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (13)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If beings do not taste the gratification in form, then they do not become defiled by form. It is because beings taste the gratification in form that they become attached. Likewise, if beings do not taste the gratification in feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, those beings do not become defiled by consciousness. It is because beings taste the gratification in feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness that those beings become attached to consciousness.

"Bhikṣus, if form were not a danger to beings, those beings would have no reason to become disenchanted with form. Because form is a danger to beings, those beings become disenchanted with form. Likewise, if feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness were not a danger, those beings would have no reason to become disenchanted with consciousness. Because feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are a danger to beings, those beings become disenchanted with consciousness.

"Bhikṣus, if there were no escape from form for beings, those beings would not be able to escape from form. Because there is an escape from form for beings, those beings escape from form. Likewise, if there were no escape from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness for beings, those beings would not be able to escape from consciousness. Because there is an escape from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness for beings, those beings escape from consciousness.

"Bhikṣus, if I did not truly know regarding these five aggregates of clinging that gratification is gratification, danger is danger, and escape is escape, then among the devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, and the assemblies of devas and humans, I would not have been freed, would not have gone beyond, would not have become separated, and would have forever dwelt in delusion, nor would I have been able to realize by myself anuttara-samyak-saṃbodhi.

"Bhikṣus, because I truly know regarding these five aggregates of clinging that gratification is gratification, danger is danger, and escape is escape, among the devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, and the assemblies of devas and humans, I have realized by myself that I am freed, have gone beyond, have become separated, have been liberated from fetters, forever no longer dwell in delusion, and have been able to realize by myself anuttara-samyak-saṃbodhi."

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (14)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Formerly I sought and investigated the gratification of form. When I awakened in accordance with the gratification of form, then I saw with wisdom the gratification of form as it truly is. Likewise, I sought and investigated the gratification of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. When I awakened in accordance with the gratification of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, then I saw with wisdom the gratification of consciousness as it truly is.

"Bhikṣus, I sought and investigated the danger of form. When I awakened in accordance with the danger of form, then I saw with wisdom the danger of form as it truly is. Likewise, I sought and investigated the danger of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. When I awakened in accordance with the danger of consciousness, then I saw with wisdom the danger of consciousness as it truly is.

"Bhikṣus, I sought and investigated the escape from form. When I awakened in accordance with the escape from form, then I saw with wisdom the escape from form as it truly is. Likewise, I sought and investigated the escape from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. When I awakened in accordance with the escape from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, then I saw with wisdom the escape from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as it truly is.

"Bhikṣus, if I did not truly know regarding the five aggregates of clinging that gratification is gratification, danger is danger, and escape is escape, then among the devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, and the assemblies of devas and humans, I would not have been freed, would not have become separated, would not have gone beyond, would have forever dwelt in delusion, and would not have been able to realize by myself anuttara-samyak-saṃbodhi.
