"Bhikṣus, because I truly know regarding the five aggregates of clinging that gratification is gratification, danger is danger, and escape is escape, among the devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, and the assemblies of devas and humans, I have been freed, have become separated, have gone beyond, forever no longer dwell in delusion, and have been able to realize by myself anuttara-samyak-saṃbodhi."

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

> The four teachings on the past, disenchantment and liberation;
> The two teachings on causes and conditions, and the two on gratification.

### (15)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, then stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "Excellent, World-Honored One! Please now briefly teach me the essentials of the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I will dwell alone in a quiet place and practice with diligence. Having practiced with diligence, I will further reflect: 'This is the reason a good man leaves the household life, shaves off his hair and beard, puts on the Dharma robes, and with faith leaves the household life for homelessness, in order to ultimately realize the unsurpassed holy life and to directly witness in the present life: "My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence."'"

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed that bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! Bhikṣu, well have you spoken these words, saying: 'Please briefly teach me the essentials of the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I will dwell alone in a quiet place and practice with diligence, up to knowing by myself that I will not receive further existence.' Did you speak thus?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Listen carefully! Listen carefully! Reflect well upon it, and I will explain it for you. Bhikṣu, if one is driven by the underlying tendencies, then one dies by the underlying tendencies. If one dies by the underlying tendencies, one is bound by clinging. Bhikṣu, if one is not driven by the underlying tendencies, then one does not die by the underlying tendencies. If one does not die by the underlying tendencies, then one is liberated from clinging."

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "I understand, World-Honored One! I understand, Well-Gone One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "How do you broadly understand the meaning of what I have briefly taught?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, form drives one by the underlying tendencies, and form causes one to die by the underlying tendencies. Driven by the underlying tendencies and dying by the underlying tendencies, one is bound by clinging. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: driven by the underlying tendencies and dying by the underlying tendencies; driven by the underlying tendencies and dying by the underlying tendencies, one is bound by clinging.

"World-Honored One, if regarding form one is not driven by the underlying tendencies and does not die by the underlying tendencies; not driven by the underlying tendencies and not dying by the underlying tendencies, one is liberated from clinging. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: not driven by the underlying tendencies and not dying by the underlying tendencies; not driven by the underlying tendencies and not dying by the underlying tendencies, one is liberated from clinging. Thus, World-Honored One, I broadly understand the meaning of the briefly taught Dharma."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! Bhikṣu, you have broadly understood the meaning of my briefly taught Dharma. Why is that? Regarding form, being driven by the underlying tendencies and dying by the underlying tendencies; driven by the underlying tendencies and dying by the underlying tendencies, one is bound by clinging. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: driven by the underlying tendencies and dying by the underlying tendencies; driven by the underlying tendencies and dying by the underlying tendencies, one is bound by clinging.

"Bhikṣu, regarding form, not being driven by the underlying tendencies and not dying by the underlying tendencies; not driven by the underlying tendencies and not dying by the underlying tendencies, one is liberated from clinging. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: not driven by the underlying tendencies and not dying by the underlying tendencies; not driven by the underlying tendencies and not dying by the underlying tendencies, one is liberated from clinging."

At that time, the bhikṣu, having heard what the Buddha said, was greatly delighted, paid respects to the Buddha, and withdrew. Dwelling alone in a quiet place, he practiced diligently, abiding in non-negligence. Having practiced diligently and abided in non-negligence, he reflected: "This is the reason a good man leaves the household life, shaves off his hair and beard, puts on the Dharma robes, and with faith leaves the household life for homelessness, up to knowing by himself that he will not receive further existence."

At that time, that bhikṣu became an arhat, and his mind was liberated.

### (16)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu came to where the Buddha was. The questions were as above. The difference was: "Being driven by the underlying tendencies and dying by the underlying tendencies, one increases the numbers of rebirth. If one is not driven by the underlying tendencies and does not die by the underlying tendencies, then one does not increase the numbers of rebirth."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "How do you broadly understand the meaning of what I have briefly taught?"

At that time, the bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if regarding form one is driven by the underlying tendencies and dies by the underlying tendencies; driven by the underlying tendencies and dying by the underlying tendencies, one increases the numbers of rebirth. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: driven by the underlying tendencies and dying by the underlying tendencies; driven by the underlying tendencies and dying by the underlying tendencies, one increases the numbers of rebirth.

"World-Honored One, if regarding form one is not driven by the underlying tendencies and does not die by the underlying tendencies; not driven by the underlying tendencies and not dying by the underlying tendencies, one does not increase the numbers of rebirth. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: not driven by the underlying tendencies and not dying by the underlying tendencies; not driven by the underlying tendencies and not dying by the underlying tendencies, one does not increase the numbers of rebirth. Thus, World-Honored One, I broadly understand the meaning of the briefly taught Dharma." In this way, he ultimately attained arhatship, and his mind was liberated.

### (17)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

A certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, bared his right shoulder, and with palms joined addressed the Buddha: "Excellent, World-Honored One! Please briefly teach me the essentials of the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I will dwell alone in a quiet place, concentrate my mind in reflection, and abide in non-negligence: 'This is the reason a good man leaves the household life, shaves off his hair and beard, puts on the Dharma robes, and with faith leaves the household life for homelessness and trains in the path, in order to ultimately realize the unsurpassed holy life and to directly witness in the present body: "My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence."'"

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed that bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! You have spoken thus: 'World-Honored One, please briefly teach me the essentials of the Dharma. I will broadly understand the meaning of the briefly taught Dharma, and will dwell alone in a quiet place, concentrate my mind in reflection, and abide in non-negligence, up to knowing by myself that I will not receive further existence.' Did you speak thus?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Listen carefully! Listen carefully! Reflect well upon it, and I will explain it for you. Bhikṣu, regarding dharmas that are not what you should hold to, you should swiftly cut them off. Cutting off those dharmas will bring benefit in meaning and long-lasting peace and happiness."

At that time, the bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "I understand, World-Honored One! I understand, Well-Gone One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "How do you broadly understand the meaning of what I have briefly taught?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, form is not what I should hold to; it should be swiftly cut off. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are not what I should hold to; they should be swiftly cut off. This will bring benefit in meaning and long-lasting peace and happiness. Therefore, World-Honored One, I broadly understand the meaning of the World-Honored One's briefly taught Dharma."

The Buddha said: "Excellent! Excellent! Bhikṣu, you have broadly understood the meaning of my briefly taught Dharma. Why is that? Form is not what you should hold to; it should be swiftly cut off. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are not what you should hold to; they should be swiftly cut off. Having cut them off, it will bring benefit in meaning and long-lasting peace and happiness."

At that time, that bhikṣu, having heard what the Buddha said, was greatly delighted, paid respects to the Buddha, and withdrew. Dwelling alone in a quiet place, he practiced diligently, abiding in non-negligence. Having practiced diligently and abided in non-negligence, he reflected: "This is the reason a good man leaves the household life, shaves off his hair and beard, puts on the Dharma robes, and with right faith leaves the household life for homelessness, up to knowing by himself that he will not receive further existence."

At that time, that bhikṣu became an arhat, and his mind was liberated.

### (18)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, bared his right shoulder, made prostrations to the Buddha, then stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "Excellent, World-Honored One! Please briefly teach me the essentials of the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I will dwell alone in a quiet place, concentrate my mind in reflection, and abide in non-negligence, up to knowing by myself that I will not receive further existence."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! You have spoken thus: 'World-Honored One, please briefly teach me the essentials of the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I will dwell alone in a quiet place, concentrate my mind in reflection, and abide in non-negligence, up to knowing by myself that I will not receive further existence.' Is that so?"

At that time, the bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Listen carefully! Listen carefully! Reflect well upon it, and I will explain it for you. Whatever is not what you should hold to, and also not what any other person should hold to, these dharmas should be swiftly cut off. Having cut off those dharmas, it will bring benefit in meaning and long-lasting peace and happiness."

At that time, the bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "I understand, World-Honored One! I understand, Well-Gone One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "How do you broadly understand the meaning of what I have briefly taught?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, form is not self, is not what I should hold to, and is also not what any other person should hold to. These dharmas should be swiftly cut off. Having cut off those dharmas, it will bring benefit in meaning and long-lasting peace and happiness. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are not self, are not what I should hold to, and are also not what any other person should hold to. They should be swiftly cut off. Having cut off those dharmas, it will bring benefit in meaning and long-lasting peace and happiness. Therefore, I broadly understand the meaning of the Tathāgata's briefly taught Dharma."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! You have broadly understood the meaning of my briefly taught Dharma. Why is that? Bhikṣu, form is not self, is not what you should hold to, and is also not what any other person should hold to. These dharmas should be swiftly cut off. Having cut off those dharmas, it will bring benefit in meaning and long-lasting peace and happiness. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are not self, are not what you should hold to, and are also not what any other person should hold to. These dharmas should be swiftly cut off. Having cut off those dharmas, it will bring benefit in meaning and long-lasting peace and happiness."

At that time, that bhikṣu, having heard what the Buddha said, was greatly delighted, paid respects to the Buddha, and withdrew. Dwelling alone in a quiet place, he practiced diligently, abiding in non-negligence, up to knowing by himself that he will not receive further existence.

At that time, that bhikṣu's mind was liberated, and he became an arhat.

### (19)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, made prostrations to the Buddha, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, please briefly teach me the essentials of the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I will dwell alone in a quiet place, concentrate my mind in reflection, and abide in non-negligence. Having abided in non-negligence, I will reflect: 'This is the reason a good man with right faith leaves the household life for homelessness, up to knowing by himself that he will not receive further existence.'"

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed that bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! You have now spoken thus: 'Excellent, World-Honored One! Please briefly teach me the essentials of the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I will dwell alone in a quiet place, concentrate my mind in reflection, and abide in non-negligence, up to knowing by myself that I will not receive further existence.' Is that so?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Listen carefully! Listen carefully! Reflect well upon it, and I will explain it for you. Bhikṣu, dharmas that are bound by fetters should be swiftly cut off. Having cut off those dharmas, it will bring benefit in meaning and long-lasting peace and happiness."

At that time, the bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "I understand, World-Honored One! I understand, Well-Gone One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "How do you broadly understand the meaning of what I have briefly taught?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, form is a dharma bound by fetters. These dharmas bound by fetters should be swiftly cut off. Having cut off those dharmas, it will bring benefit in meaning and long-lasting peace and happiness. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are dharmas bound by fetters. These dharmas bound by fetters should be swiftly cut off. Having cut off those dharmas, it will bring benefit in meaning and long-lasting peace and happiness. Therefore, I broadly understand the meaning of the World-Honored One's briefly taught Dharma."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! You have broadly understood the meaning of my briefly taught Dharma. Why is that? Form is a dharma bound by fetters. This dharma should be swiftly cut off. Having cut off that dharma, it will bring benefit in meaning and long-lasting peace and happiness. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are dharmas bound by fetters. These dharmas should be swiftly cut off. Having cut off those dharmas, it will bring benefit in meaning and long-lasting peace and happiness."

At that time, that bhikṣu, having heard what the Buddha said, was greatly delighted, paid respects to the Buddha, and withdrew. Dwelling alone in a quiet place, he concentrated his mind in reflection, abiding in non-negligence, up to his mind being liberated, and he became an arhat.

### (20)

The sūtra on defilement is also stated in the same way.

### (21)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, made prostrations to the Buddha, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, please briefly teach me the essentials of the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I will dwell alone in a quiet place, concentrate my mind in reflection, and abide in non-negligence. Having abided in non-negligence, I will reflect: 'This is the reason a good man with right faith leaves the household life for homelessness, up to knowing by himself that he will not receive further existence.'"

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed that bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! You have now spoken thus: 'Excellent, World-Honored One! Please briefly teach me the essentials of the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I will dwell alone in a quiet place, concentrate my mind in reflection, and abide in non-negligence, up to knowing by myself that I will not receive further existence.' Is that so?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Listen carefully! Listen carefully! Reflect well upon it, and I will explain it for you. Bhikṣu, when there is agitation, one is bound by Māra. If one does not become agitated, then one is liberated from Pāpīyas."

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "I understand, World-Honored One! I understand, Well-Gone One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "How do you broadly understand the meaning of what I have briefly taught?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, when form becomes agitated, one is bound by Māra. If it does not become agitated, one is liberated from Pāpīyas. Likewise, when feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness become agitated, one is bound by Māra. If they do not become agitated, one is liberated from Pāpīyas. Therefore, I broadly understand the meaning of the World-Honored One's briefly taught Dharma."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! You have broadly understood the meaning of my briefly taught Dharma. Why is that? When form becomes agitated, one is bound by Māra. If it does not become agitated, one is liberated from Pāpīyas. Likewise, when feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness become agitated, one is bound by Māra. If they do not become agitated, one is liberated from Pāpīyas." Up to knowing by himself that he will not receive further existence, his mind was liberated, and he became an arhat.

### (22)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Kalpa who came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, then stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "As the World-Honored One has said, a bhikṣu whose mind attains good liberation: World-Honored One, how does a bhikṣu's mind attain good liberation?"

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed Kalpa: "Excellent! Excellent! You are able to ask the Tathāgata about good liberation of mind. Excellent, Kalpa! Listen carefully! Listen carefully! Reflect well upon it, and I will explain it for you. Kalpa, you should contemplate and know that regarding all forms whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of them are entirely impermanent. Having rightly contemplated impermanence, craving for form is removed. When craving for form is removed, the mind is well liberated. Likewise, contemplate feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near; all of them are entirely impermanent. Having rightly contemplated impermanence, craving for consciousness is removed. When craving for consciousness is removed, I say the mind is well liberated. Kalpa, in this way, a bhikṣu whose mind is well liberated is what the Tathāgata calls a mind well liberated. Why is that? Because craving and desire are cut off. When craving and desire are cut off, the Tathāgata calls it a mind well liberated."

At that time, Bhikṣu Kalpa, having heard what the Buddha said, was greatly delighted, paid respects to the Buddha, and withdrew.

At that time, Bhikṣu Kalpa, having received the Buddha's teaching, dwelt alone in a quiet place, concentrated his mind in reflection, and abided in non-negligence, up to knowing by himself that he will not receive further existence. His mind was well liberated, and he became an arhat.

### (23)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Venerable Rāhula went to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, then stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, how should one know, how should one see, so that regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, there will be no view of self, no view of what belongs to self, and no bondage by the tendency of conceit?"

The Buddha told Rāhula: "Excellent! Excellent! You are able to ask the Tathāgata: 'How should one know, how should one see, so that regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, there will be no view of self, no view of what belongs to self, and no bondage by the tendency of conceit?' Is that so?"

Rāhula addressed the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told Rāhula: "Excellent! Listen carefully! Listen carefully! Reflect well upon it, and I will explain it for you. Rāhula, you should contemplate that regarding all forms whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of them are not self, not other than self, and neither exists within the other. This is what is called contemplating with the wisdom of equanimity. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near; all of them are not self, not other than self, and neither exists within the other. This is what is called contemplating as it truly is with the wisdom of equanimity.

"In this way, Rāhula, when a bhikṣu knows thus and sees thus; knowing thus and seeing thus, regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, there is no view of self, no view of what belongs to self, and no bondage by the tendency of conceit.

"Rāhula, if a bhikṣu is thus regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, having no view of self, no view of what belongs to self, and no bondage by the tendency of conceit, then this bhikṣu is called one who has cut off craving and desire, turned away from all fetters, rightly and without interval realized the complete end of suffering."

At that time, Rāhula, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (24)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed Rāhula: "Bhikṣu, how should one know, how should one see, so that regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, there is no view of self, no view of what belongs to self, and no bondage by the tendency of conceit?"

Rāhula addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the lord of the Dharma, is the guide, is the shelter. Excellent, World-Honored One! Please expound this meaning for the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus, having heard it from the Buddha, will accept and uphold and practice in accordance."

The Buddha told Rāhula: "Listen carefully! Listen carefully! Reflect well upon it, and I will explain it for you."

Rāhula addressed the Buddha: "Yes, I accept the teaching."

The Buddha told Rāhula: "You should contemplate that regarding all forms whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of them are not self, not other than self, and neither exists within the other. This is what is called contemplating as it truly is with the wisdom of equanimity. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near; all of them are not self, not other than self, and neither exists within the other. This is what is called contemplating as it truly is with the wisdom of equanimity.

"Bhikṣu, knowing thus and seeing thus regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, there is no view of self, no view of what belongs to self, and no bondage by the tendency of conceit.

"Rāhula, if a bhikṣu is thus regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, having no view of self, no view of what belongs to self, and no bondage by the tendency of conceit, then he transcends doubt, is distant from all signs, and is in tranquil liberation. This is called a bhikṣu who has cut off craving and desire, turned away from all fetters, rightly and without interval realized the complete end of suffering."

At that time, Rāhula, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

> Underlying tendencies, increasing the numbers; not mine, not others';
> Fetters, agitation;
> The question of Kalpa, and also the two sūtras of Rāhula's questions.

### (25)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu came to where the Buddha was, made prostrations to the Buddha, then stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "As the World-Honored One has spoken of 'one of much learning,' what does it mean to be 'one of much learning'?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! Do you now ask me about the meaning of 'much learning'?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "Yes, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Listen carefully, reflect well, and I will explain it for you. Bhikṣu, you should know: if one has heard that form is the dharma of giving rise to disenchantment, freedom from desire, complete cessation, and peace, this is called 'much learning.' Likewise, having heard that feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are the dharmas of giving rise to disenchantment, freedom from desire, complete cessation, and peace, this is called a bhikṣu of much learning. This is what the Tathāgata calls 'much learning.'"

At that time, that bhikṣu, having heard what the Buddha said, leaped with joy, made prostrations, and departed.

### (26)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, then stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "As the World-Honored One has spoken of 'a teacher of the Dharma,' what does it mean to be called 'a teacher of the Dharma'?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! Do you now wish to know the meaning of 'teacher of the Dharma' as spoken by the Tathāgata?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "Yes, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Listen carefully, reflect well, and I will explain it for you."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "If regarding form one teaches the dharma of giving rise to disenchantment, freedom from desire, complete cessation, and peace, that person is called a teacher of the Dharma. If regarding feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness one teaches the dharma of giving rise to disenchantment, freedom from desire, complete cessation, and peace, that person is called a teacher of the Dharma. This is what the Tathāgata calls 'a teacher of the Dharma.'"

At that time, that bhikṣu, having heard what the Buddha said, leaped with joy, made prostrations, and departed.

### (27)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head in prostration, then stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "As the World-Honored One has spoken of 'practicing the Dharma in accordance with the Dharma,' what does it mean to practice the Dharma in accordance with the Dharma?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! Do you now wish to know about practicing the Dharma in accordance with the Dharma?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "Yes, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Listen carefully, reflect well, and I will explain it for you. Bhikṣu, regarding form, one is directed toward disenchantment, freedom from desire, and complete cessation. This is called practicing the Dharma in accordance with the Dharma. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: regarding consciousness, one is directed toward disenchantment, freedom from desire, and complete cessation. This is called practicing the Dharma in accordance with the Dharma."

At that time, that bhikṣu, having heard what the Buddha said, leaped with joy, made prostrations, and departed.

### (28)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, then stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, as the World-Honored One has spoken of attaining 'nirvāṇa visible in the present life,' how does a bhikṣu attain nirvāṇa visible in the present life?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! Do you now wish to know about nirvāṇa visible in the present life?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "Yes, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Listen carefully, reflect well, and I will explain it for you."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Regarding form, one gives rise to disenchantment, freedom from desire, and complete cessation, and does not give rise to the outflows; the mind is rightly liberated. This is called a bhikṣu who has attained nirvāṇa visible in the present life. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: regarding consciousness, one gives rise to disenchantment, freedom from desire, and complete cessation, and does not give rise to the outflows; the mind is rightly liberated. This is called a bhikṣu who has attained nirvāṇa visible in the present life."

At that time, that bhikṣu, having heard what the Buddha said, leaped with joy, made prostrations, and departed.

### (29)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu named Samiddhi came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, then stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "As the World-Honored One has spoken of 'a teacher who expounds the Dharma,' what does it mean to be called 'a teacher who expounds the Dharma'?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Do you now wish to know the meaning of 'a teacher who expounds the Dharma'?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "Yes, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Listen carefully, reflect well, and I will explain it for you. If a bhikṣu regarding form teaches disenchantment, freedom from desire, and complete cessation, that person is called a teacher who expounds the Dharma. Likewise, regarding feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if regarding consciousness one teaches disenchantment, freedom from desire, and complete cessation, that person is called a teacher who expounds the Dharma."

At that time, that bhikṣu, having heard what the Buddha said, leaped with joy, made prostrations, and departed.

> Much learning, good teaching of the Dharma, practicing the Dharma in accordance, and nirvāṇa;
> Samiddhi asked: "What does it mean to be a teacher who expounds the Dharma?"

### (30)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra was on Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain.

At that time, there was a householder's son named Śroṇa, who wandered about daily and arrived at Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain. He went to the Venerable Śāriputra, inquired after his health, then sat down to one side, and said to Śāriputra: "If śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, regarding impermanent form, form that is subject to change and is not secure, say 'I am superior,' 'I am equal,' or 'I am inferior,' why do these śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas conceive such notions and not see the truth? If śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, regarding impermanent, subject to change, and insecure feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, say 'I am superior,' 'I am equal,' or 'I am inferior,' why do these śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas conceive such notions and not see the truth?

"If śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, regarding impermanent form, form that is subject to change and is not secure, say 'I am superior,' 'I am equal,' or 'I am inferior,' what are they reckoning based on that they do not see the truth? Regarding impermanent, subject to change, and insecure feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, if they say 'I am superior,' 'I am equal,' or 'I am inferior,' what are they reckoning based on that they do not see the truth?"

"Śroṇa, what do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent."

"Śroṇa, if it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

He answered: "It is suffering."

"Śroṇa, if it is impermanent and suffering, being a dharma subject to change, what do you think? Would a noble disciple see within it that form is self, other than self, or that they exist within each other?"

He answered: "No."

"Śroṇa, what do you think? Are feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent."

"If impermanent, is it suffering?"

He answered: "It is suffering."

"Śroṇa, if consciousness is impermanent and suffering, being a dharma subject to change, what do you think? Would a noble disciple see within it that consciousness is self, other than self, or that they exist within each other?"

He answered: "No."

"Śroṇa, you should know that regarding form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all that form is not self, not other than self, and neither exists within the other. This is called knowing as it truly is. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all that consciousness is not self, not other than self, and neither exists within the other. This is called knowing as it truly is. Śroṇa, thus regarding form, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, one gives rise to disenchantment, freedom from desire, and liberation. In liberation, the knowledge and vision arises: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'"

When Śāriputra had finished speaking this sūtra, the householder's son Śroṇa became free from dust and stain and attained the purification of the Dharma eye. At that time, the householder's son Śroṇa, having seen the Dharma, having attained the Dharma, not depending on others, and having attained fearlessness in the true Dharma, rose from his seat, bared his right shoulder, knelt on one knee, joined his palms, and addressed Śāriputra: "I have now crossed over. From this day forward, I take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, and take refuge in the Sangha, as a lay disciple. From this day forward, for the rest of my life, I purely take refuge in the Three Jewels."

At that time, the householder's son Śroṇa, having heard what Śāriputra said, was joyful and delighted, made prostrations, and departed.

### (31)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra was on Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain.

At that time, there was a householder's son named Śroṇa, who wandered about daily and arrived at Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain. He went to where Śāriputra was, bowed his head at Śāriputra's feet, then sat down to one side.

At that time, Śāriputra said: "Śroṇa, if śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas do not know form as it truly is, do not know the arising of form as it truly is, do not know the cessation of form as it truly is, and do not know the path leading to the cessation of form as it truly is, then, Śroṇa, you should know that these śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas are not capable of cutting off form. Likewise, if śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas do not know feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as they truly are, do not know the arising of consciousness as it truly is, do not know the cessation of consciousness as it truly is, and do not know the path leading to the cessation of consciousness as it truly is, then they are not capable of cutting off consciousness.

"Śroṇa, if śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas know form as it truly is, know the arising of form as it truly is, know the cessation of form as it truly is, and know the path leading to the cessation of form as it truly is, then, Śroṇa, you should know that these śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas are capable of cutting off form. Likewise, Śroṇa, if śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas know feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as they truly are, know the arising of consciousness as it truly is, know the cessation of consciousness as it truly is, and know the path leading to the cessation of consciousness as it truly is, then, Śroṇa, you should know that these śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas are capable of cutting off consciousness.

"Śroṇa, what do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent."

He also asked: "If impermanent, is it suffering?"

He answered: "It is suffering."

Śāriputra said: "If form is impermanent and suffering, being a dharma subject to change, would a noble disciple see within it that form is self, other than self, or that they exist within each other?"

He answered: "No."

"Śroṇa, likewise, are feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent."

He also asked: "If impermanent, is it suffering?"

He answered: "It is suffering."

He also asked: "If impermanent and suffering, being a dharma subject to change, would a noble disciple see within it that consciousness is self, other than self, or that they exist within each other?"

He answered: "No."

"Śroṇa, you should know that regarding form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, regarding all form it is not self, not other than self, and neither exists within the other. This is called knowing as it truly is. Śroṇa, a noble disciple gives rise to disenchantment with form, freedom from desire, and liberation; liberation from birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, suffering, and distress.

"Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all that consciousness is not self, not other than self, and neither exists within the other. This is called knowing as it truly is. Śroṇa, a noble disciple gives rise to disenchantment with consciousness, freedom from desire, and liberation; liberation from birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, suffering, and distress."

At that time, Śroṇa, having heard what Śāriputra said, was joyful and delighted, made prostrations, and departed.

### (32)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra was on Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain.

At that time, there was a householder's son named Śroṇa, who wandered about daily and arrived at Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain. He went to where Śāriputra was, bowed his head at Śāriputra's feet, then sat down to one side.

At that time, Śāriputra told Śroṇa: "If śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas do not know form as it truly is, do not know the arising of form as it truly is, do not know the cessation of form as it truly is, do not know the gratification of form as it truly is, do not know the danger of form as it truly is, and do not know the escape from form as it truly is, then they are not capable of transcending form. If śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas do not know feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as they truly are, do not know the arising of consciousness as it truly is, do not know the cessation of consciousness as it truly is, do not know the gratification of consciousness as it truly is, do not know the danger of consciousness as it truly is, and do not know the escape from consciousness as it truly is, then these śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas are not capable of transcending consciousness.

"If śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas know form, the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form as they truly are, then these śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas are capable of transcending form. If śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas know feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, and the escape from consciousness as they

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.
