# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Two

---

### (33)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Form is not self. If form were self, then form would not give rise to illness and suffering, and one could command form to be thus or not be thus. Because form is not self, form gives rise to illness and suffering, and one cannot command form to be thus or not be thus. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise. Bhikṣus, what do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "Impermanent, World-Honored One!"

"Bhikṣus, if it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "It is suffering, World-Honored One!"

"If it is impermanent and suffering, being a dharma subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple see therein a self, something other than self, or the two as mutually inhering?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One!"

"Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise. Therefore, bhikṣus, all forms whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of these are not self, not other than self, and the two do not mutually inhere. One should observe thus. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise.

"Bhikṣus, a well-learned noble disciple observes as it really is that these five aggregates of clinging are not self and not belonging to self. Having observed as it really is, regarding all things in the world, there is nothing to grasp. Because there is nothing to grasp, there is nothing to cling to. Because there is nothing to cling to, one personally realizes nirvāṇa: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (34)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Vārāṇasī, at the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the group of five bhikṣus: "Form does not possess a self. If form possessed a self, then within form there would be no arising of illness and suffering, and one could not command form to be thus or not be thus. Because form is not self, within form there is illness and suffering, and one can command form to be thus or not be thus. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise. Bhikṣus, what do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "Impermanent, World-Honored One!"

"Bhikṣus, if it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "It is suffering, World-Honored One!"

"Bhikṣus, if it is impermanent and suffering, being a dharma subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple see therein a self, something other than self, or the two as mutually inhering?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One!"

"Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise. Therefore, bhikṣus, all forms whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of these are not self and not belonging to self. One should observe as it really is. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise.

"Bhikṣus, a well-learned noble disciple sees these five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self. Observing thus, regarding all things in the world, there is nothing to grasp. Because there is nothing to grasp, there is nothing to cling to. Because there is nothing to cling to, one personally realizes nirvāṇa: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the group of five bhikṣus did not give rise to any outflows, and their minds were liberated.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (35)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Bamboo Grove Monastery in Cetī.

At that time, there were three upright men who had recently gone forth, namely the Venerable Anuruddha, the Venerable Nandiya, and the Venerable Kimbila.

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing what was in their minds, gave them this instruction: "Bhikṣus! This mind, this intention, this consciousness: you should contemplate this, do not contemplate that. Abandon this desire, abandon this form. Realize with the body and dwell in full attainment. Bhikṣus, is there any form that is permanent, unchanging, and abiding correctly?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Good! Good! Form is impermanent, a dharma subject to change. One should be disenchanted with it, be free from desire for it; it should cease, become tranquil, and disappear. Thus, form from the very beginning has always been impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change. Knowing thus, all the outflows, harms, burning afflictions, and sorrows that arise conditioned by form are all completely extinguished. Being extinguished, there is nothing to cling to. With nothing to cling to, one dwells in ease. Dwelling in ease, one attains parinirvāṇa. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise."

When the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the three upright men did not give rise to any outflows, and their minds were liberated.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (36)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Mathurā, beside the Bhadri River, in the Canopy Āmra Tree Garden.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Dwell as your own island, dwell with yourself as refuge. Dwell with the Dharma as your island, dwell with the Dharma as your refuge. Do not take another island, do not take another refuge. Bhikṣus, you should rightly investigate, dwelling with yourself as island and yourself as refuge, with the Dharma as island and the Dharma as refuge, not taking another island and not taking another refuge. From what cause do sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering arise? Due to what do they exist? Due to what cause? What is clung to? How does one observe oneself such that sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering that have not yet arisen come to arise, and those already arisen grow and increase?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. May you please explain! The bhikṣus, having heard, will practice as instructed."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully and reflect well. I shall explain it to you. Bhikṣus, there is form; conditioned by form, clinging to form, one observes in oneself that sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering that have not yet arisen come to arise, and those already arisen grow and expand further. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise. Bhikṣus, is there any form that is permanent, enduring, unchanging, and abiding correctly?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Good! Good! Bhikṣus, form is impermanent. If a good man knows that form is impermanent, suffering, and subject to change, and is free from desire for it, it ceases, becomes tranquil, and disappears. From the very beginning, all form has been impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change. Knowing this, if sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering arise conditioned by form, they are cut off. Those being cut off, there is nothing to cling to. Not clinging, one dwells in peaceful ease. Dwelling in peaceful ease, this is called nirvāṇa. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise."

When the Buddha spoke this sūtra, sixteen bhikṣus did not give rise to any outflows, and their minds were liberated.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

> Bamboo Grove, Vaiśālī, purity, right investigation,
> Impermanence, suffering, non-self: five, three, and sixteen.

---

### (37)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I do not dispute with the world; it is the world that disputes with me. Why is that? Bhikṣus, one who speaks in accordance with the Dharma does not dispute with the world. What the wise in the world say exists, I too say exists. How is it that what the wise in the world say exists, I too say exists? Bhikṣus, form is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change; the wise in the world say this exists, and I too say it exists. Likewise feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are impermanent, suffering, and dharmas subject to change; the wise in the world say this exists, and I too say it exists. What the wise in the world say does not exist, I too say does not exist. That is, that form is permanent, enduring, unchanging, and abiding correctly; the wise in the world say this does not exist, and I too say it does not exist. That feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are permanent, enduring, unchanging, and abiding correctly; the wise in the world say this does not exist, and I too say it does not exist. This is what is meant by what the wise in the world say does not exist, I too say does not exist. Bhikṣus, there are worldly dharmas of the world. I too know them myself, realize them myself, and for people I analyze, expound, and make them clear. Those in the world who are blind and without eyes do not know and do not see. That is not my fault.

"Bhikṣus, what are the worldly dharmas of the world that I myself know, myself realize, and for people expound, analyze, and make clear, yet the blind and those without eyes do not know and do not see? Bhikṣus, form is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change; this is called a worldly dharma of the world. Likewise feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are impermanent, suffering; these are worldly dharmas of the world. Bhikṣus, these are the worldly dharmas of the world. I myself know and myself realize them, and for people I analyze, expound, and make them clear, yet the blind and those without eyes do not know and do not see. What can I do about those who are blind and without eyes, who do not know and do not see?"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (38)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "People of the world engage in lowly occupations, seeking wealth in various ways to make a living, and they obtain great riches. The people of the world all know this. Just as the people of the world know it, I too speak likewise. Why is that? So that I will not be different from the people of the world.

"Bhikṣus, it is like a single vessel: in one place people call it a ghaṇṭī, in another they call it a pātra, in another they call it a pippala, in another they call it a cāru, in another they call it a viṣṭa, in another they call it a bhājana, in another they call it a sarāva. Just as they know it, I too speak likewise. Why is that? So that I will not be different from the people of the world. Likewise, bhikṣus, there are worldly dharmas of the world. I myself know and myself realize them, and for people I analyze, expound, and make them clear. I know and see and then speak. Yet the blind and those without eyes in the world do not know and do not see. What can I do about the blind and those without eyes in the world who do not know and do not see?

"Bhikṣus, what are the worldly dharmas of the world that I myself know and myself realize, and so forth, up to those who do not know and do not see? Form is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change; this is a worldly dharma of the world. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are impermanent, suffering, and dharmas subject to change; these are worldly dharmas of the world. Bhikṣus, these are called worldly dharmas of the world. I myself know and myself see, and so forth, up to the blind and those without eyes who do not know and do not see. What can I do about it?"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (39)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five kinds of seeds. What are the five? They are: root seeds, stem seeds, node seeds, self-dropping seeds, and fruit seeds. If these five kinds of seeds are unbroken, unspoiled, unrotted, undamaged by wind, fresh and ripe and solid, and if there is the earth element but no water element, those seeds will not grow and increase. If those seeds are fresh, ripe, and solid, unbroken, unspoiled, and undamaged by wind, and there is the water element but no earth element, those seeds also will not grow and increase. If those seeds are fresh, ripe, and solid, unbroken, unspoiled, unrotted, and undamaged by wind, and there are both the earth element and the water element, then those seeds will grow and increase.

"Bhikṣus, those five kinds of seeds are an analogy for consciousness together with the aggregates of clinging. The earth element is an analogy for the four stations of consciousness. The water element is an analogy for delight and craving, by which consciousness clings to and abides in the four supports through grasping. What are the four? Consciousness abides in form, clinging to form, moistened by delight and craving, and grows and increases. Consciousness abides in feeling, perception, and volitional formations, clinging to feeling, perception, and volitional formations, moistened by craving and delight, and grows and increases. Bhikṣus, consciousness therein comes, goes, stays, passes away, or grows and increases.

"Bhikṣus, if apart from form, feeling, perception, and volitional formations, consciousness had any coming, going, staying, or arising, that would be mere words. If questioned about it, one would not know, and confusion would only increase, because it is not within the domain of experience. When one is free from craving toward the realm of form, and having been freed from craving, the mental bondage of attachment to form is severed; when the mental bondage of attachment to form is severed, the support is severed; when the support is severed, consciousness has no place to abide, and it no longer grows and increases. Likewise when one is free from craving toward the realms of feeling, perception, and volitional formations, and having been freed from craving, the mental bondage of attachment to formations is severed; when the mental bondage of attachment to formations is severed, the support is severed; when the support is severed, that consciousness has nowhere to abide, and it no longer grows and increases. Not growing, it does not create formations. Not creating formations, it abides. Abiding, it is content. Being content, it is liberated. Being liberated, regarding all things in the world, there is nothing to grasp and nothing to cling to. With nothing to grasp and nothing to cling to, one personally realizes nirvāṇa: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.' I say that this consciousness does not go east, west, south, north, to the four intermediate directions, upward, or downward. It goes nowhere at all. It only sees the Dharma, desiring to enter nirvāṇa: tranquil cessation, coolness, purity, and truth."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (40)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "One who is attached is not liberated; one who is not attached is liberated. How is one attached and not liberated? Bhikṣus, consciousness abides by clinging to the four aggregates of grasping. What are the four? Consciousness abides attached to form; consciousness abides attached to feeling, perception, and volitional formations, and so forth, up to 'because it is not within the domain of experience.' This is called being attached and therefore not liberated. How is one not attached and therefore liberated? When one is free from craving toward the realm of form, and free from craving toward feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness, and so forth, up to 'purity and truth,' this is being not attached and therefore liberated."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (41)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five aggregates of clinging: the form aggregate of clinging, and the feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging. Regarding these five aggregates of clinging, I know five things as they really are. I know form as it really is; I know the arising of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form as they really are. Likewise, I know feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness as they really are; I know the arising of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, and the escape from consciousness as they really are.

"How does one know form as it really is? All form whatsoever, being entirely the four great elements and form derived from the four great elements, is called form. Thus one knows form as it really is. How does one know the arising of form as it really is? Delight and craving toward form is called the arising of form. Thus one knows the arising of form as it really is. How does one know the gratification of form as it really is? The pleasure and joy that arise conditioned by form is called the gratification of form. Thus one knows the gratification of form as it really is. How does one know the danger of form as it really is? That form is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change is called the danger of form. Thus one knows the danger of form as it really is. How does one know the escape from form as it really is? The taming of desire and craving toward form, the cutting off of desire and craving, the transcending of desire and craving, is called the escape from form. Thus one knows the escape from form as it really is.

"How does one know feeling as it really is? There are six classes of feeling: feeling born of eye-contact, feeling born of ear-contact, feeling born of nose-contact, feeling born of tongue-contact, feeling born of body-contact, and feeling born of mind-contact. This is called feeling. Thus one knows feeling as it really is. How does one know the arising of feeling as it really is? The arising of contact is the arising of feeling. Thus one knows the arising of feeling as it really is. How does one know the gratification of feeling as it really is? The pleasure and joy that arise conditioned by the six feelings is called the gratification of feeling. Thus one knows the gratification of feeling as it really is. How does one know the danger of feeling as it really is? That feeling is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change is called the danger of feeling. Thus one knows the danger of feeling as it really is. How does one know the escape from feeling as it really is? The taming of desire and craving toward feeling, the cutting off of desire and craving, the transcending of desire and craving, is called the escape from feeling. Thus one knows the escape from feeling as it really is.

"How does one know perception as it really is? There are six classes of perception. What are the six? Perception born of eye-contact, perception born of ear-contact, perception born of nose-contact, perception born of tongue-contact, perception born of body-contact, and perception born of mind-contact. This is called perception. Thus one knows perception as it really is. How does one know the arising of perception as it really is? The arising of contact is the arising of perception. Thus one knows the arising of perception as it really is. How does one know the gratification of perception as it really is? The pleasure and joy that arise conditioned by perception is called the gratification of perception. Thus one knows the gratification of perception as it really is. How does one know the danger of perception as it really is? That perception is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change is called the danger of perception. Thus one knows the danger of perception as it really is. How does one know the escape from perception as it really is? The taming of desire and craving toward perception, the cutting off of desire and craving, the transcending of desire and craving, is called the escape from perception. Thus one knows the escape from perception as it really is.

"How does one know volitional formations as it really is? There are six classes of volition: volition born of eye-contact, volition born of ear-contact, volition born of nose-contact, volition born of tongue-contact, volition born of body-contact, and volition born of mind-contact. This is called volitional formations. Thus one knows volitional formations as it really is. How does one know the arising of volitional formations as it really is? The arising of contact is the arising of volitional formations. Thus one knows the arising of volitional formations as it really is. How does one know the gratification of volitional formations as it really is? The pleasure and joy that arise conditioned by volitional formations is called the gratification of volitional formations. Thus one knows the gratification of volitional formations as it really is. How does one know the danger of volitional formations as it really is? That volitional formations are impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change is called the danger of volitional formations. Thus one knows the danger of volitional formations as it really is. How does one know the escape from volitional formations as it really is? The taming of desire and craving toward volitional formations, the cutting off of desire and craving, the transcending of desire and craving, is called the escape from volitional formations. Thus one knows the escape from volitional formations as it really is.

"How does one know consciousness as it really is? There are six classes of consciousness: eye-consciousness, ear-consciousness, nose-consciousness, tongue-consciousness, body-consciousness, and mind-consciousness. This is called consciousness. Thus one knows consciousness as it really is. How does one know the arising of consciousness as it really is? The arising of name-and-form is the arising of consciousness. Thus one knows the arising of consciousness as it really is. How does one know the gratification of consciousness as it really is? The pleasure and joy that arise conditioned by consciousness is called the gratification of consciousness. Thus one knows the gratification of consciousness as it really is. How does one know the danger of consciousness as it really is? That consciousness is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change is called the danger of consciousness. Thus one knows the danger of consciousness as it really is. How does one know the escape from consciousness as it really is? The taming of desire and craving toward consciousness, the cutting off of desire and craving, the transcending of desire and craving, is called the escape from consciousness. Thus one knows the escape from consciousness as it really is.

"Bhikṣus, if śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas know form in this way and see it in this way, and knowing thus and seeing thus, they turn toward freedom from desire, this is called the right direction. If one is on the right path, I say that person enters. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise. If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas know form as it really is and see it as it really is, they become disenchanted with form, free from desire, and do not give rise to outflows; the mind is liberated. If the mind is liberated, it becomes unified. Being unified, the holy life is established. With the holy life established, one is free from the control of others. This is called the end of suffering. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (42)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There is being skilled in seven matters and three kinds of investigating meaning. One who fully masters this Dharma attains the ending of outflows, attains the outflow-free, the liberation of mind and the liberation by wisdom, knowing and realizing for oneself in this present life, dwelling in full attainment: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.' How is a bhikṣu skilled in seven matters? A bhikṣu knows as it really is: form, the arising of form, the cessation of form, the path leading to the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Likewise for feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness: one knows as it really is the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, the path leading to the cessation of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, and the escape from consciousness.

"How does one know form as it really is? All form whatsoever, being entirely the four great elements and form derived from the four great elements, is called form. Thus one knows form as it really is. How does one know the arising of form as it really is? Delight and craving is called the arising of form. Thus one knows the arising of form as it really is. How does one know the cessation of form as it really is? The cessation of delight and craving is called the cessation of form. Thus one knows the cessation of form as it really is. How does one know the path leading to the cessation of form as it really is? The Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration. This is called the path leading to the cessation of form. Thus one knows the path leading to the cessation of form as it really is. How does one know the gratification of form as it really is? The pleasure and joy that arise conditioned by form is called the gratification of form. Thus one knows the gratification of form as it really is. How does one know the danger of form as it really is? That form is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change is called the danger of form. Thus one knows the danger of form as it really is. How does one know the escape from form as it really is? The taming of desire and craving toward form, the cutting off of desire and craving, the transcending of desire and craving, is called the escape from form. Thus one knows the escape from form as it really is.

"How does one know feeling as it really is? There are six feelings: feeling born of eye-contact, feeling born of ear-contact, feeling born of nose-contact, feeling born of tongue-contact, feeling born of body-contact, and feeling born of mind-contact. This is called feeling. Thus one knows feeling as it really is. How does one know the arising of feeling as it really is? The arising of contact is the arising of feeling. Thus one knows the arising of feeling as it really is. How does one know the cessation of feeling as it really is? The cessation of contact is the cessation of feeling. Thus one knows the cessation of feeling as it really is. How does one know the path leading to the cessation of feeling as it really is? The Noble Eightfold Path: right view and so forth up to right concentration. This is called the path leading to the cessation of feeling. Thus one knows the path leading to the cessation of feeling as it really is. How does one know the gratification of feeling as it really is? The pleasure and joy that arise conditioned by feeling is called the gratification of feeling. Thus one knows the gratification of feeling as it really is. How does one know the danger of feeling as it really is? That feeling is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change is called the danger of feeling. Thus one knows the danger of feeling as it really is. How does one know the escape from feeling as it really is? The taming of desire and craving toward feeling, the cutting off of desire and craving, the transcending of desire and craving, is called the escape from feeling. Thus one knows the escape from feeling as it really is.

"How does one know perception as it really is? There are six perceptions: perception born of eye-contact, perception born of ear-contact, perception born of nose-contact, perception born of tongue-contact, perception born of body-contact, and perception born of mind-contact. This is called perception. Thus one knows perception as it really is. How does one know the arising of perception as it really is? The arising of contact is the arising of perception. Thus one knows the arising of perception as it really is. How does one know the cessation of perception as it really is? The cessation of contact is the cessation of perception. Thus one knows the cessation of perception as it really is. How does one know the path leading to the cessation of perception as it really is? The Noble Eightfold Path: right view and so forth up to right concentration. This is called the path leading to the cessation of perception. Thus one knows the path leading to the cessation of perception as it really is. How does one know the gratification of perception as it really is? The pleasure and joy that arise conditioned by perception is called the gratification of perception. Thus one knows the gratification of perception as it really is. How does one know the danger of perception as it really is? That perception is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change is called the danger of perception. Thus one knows the danger of perception as it really is. How does one know the escape from perception as it really is? The taming of desire and craving toward perception, the cutting off of desire and craving, the transcending of desire and craving, is called the escape from perception. Thus one knows the escape from perception as it really is.

"How does one know volitional formations as it really is? There are six classes of volition: volition born of eye-contact, volition born of ear-contact, volition born of nose-contact, volition born of tongue-contact, volition born of body-contact, and volition born of mind-contact. This is called volitional formations. Thus one knows volitional formations as it really is. How does one know the arising of volitional formations as it really is? The arising of contact is the arising of volitional formations. Thus one knows the arising of volitional formations as it really is. How does one know the cessation of volitional formations as it really is? The cessation of contact is the cessation of volitional formations. Thus one knows the cessation of volitional formations as it really is. How does one know the path leading to the cessation of volitional formations as it really is? The Noble Eightfold Path: right view and so forth up to right concentration. This is called the path leading to the cessation of volitional formations. Thus one knows the path leading to the cessation of volitional formations as it really is. How does one know the gratification of volitional formations as it really is? The pleasure and joy that arise conditioned by volitional formations is called the gratification of volitional formations. Thus one knows the gratification of volitional formations as it really is. How does one know the danger of volitional formations as it really is? That volitional formations are impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change is called the danger of volitional formations. Thus one knows the danger of volitional formations as it really is. How does one know the escape from volitional formations as it really is? The taming of desire and craving toward volitional formations, the cutting off of desire and craving, the transcending of desire and craving, is called the escape from volitional formations. Thus one knows the escape from volitional formations as it really is.

"How does one know consciousness as it really is? There are six classes of consciousness: eye-consciousness, ear-consciousness, nose-consciousness, tongue-consciousness, body-consciousness, and mind-consciousness. This is called consciousness. Thus one knows consciousness as it really is. How does one know the arising of consciousness as it really is? The arising of name-and-form is the arising of consciousness. Thus one knows the arising of consciousness as it really is. How does one know the cessation of consciousness as it really is? The cessation of name-and-form is the cessation of consciousness. Thus one knows the cessation of consciousness as it really is. How does one know the path leading to the cessation of consciousness as it really is? The Noble Eightfold Path: right view and so forth up to right concentration. This is called the path leading to the cessation of consciousness. Thus one knows the path leading to the cessation of consciousness as it really is. How does one know the gratification of consciousness as it really is? The pleasure and joy that arise conditioned by consciousness is called the gratification of consciousness. Thus one knows the gratification of consciousness as it really is. How does one know the danger of consciousness as it really is? That consciousness is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change is called the danger of consciousness. Thus one knows the danger of consciousness as it really is. How does one know the escape from consciousness as it really is? The taming of desire and craving toward consciousness, the cutting off of desire and craving, the transcending of desire and craving, is called the escape from consciousness. Thus one knows the escape from consciousness as it really is. Bhikṣus, this is called being skilled in seven matters.

"What are the three kinds of investigating meaning? Bhikṣus, if in an empty place, under a tree, or in an open area, one investigates the aggregates, elements, and sense-bases, and with right effort contemplates their meaning, this is called a bhikṣu's three kinds of investigating meaning. This is called a bhikṣu being skilled in seven matters and the three kinds of investigating meaning. One who fully masters this Dharma attains the ending of outflows, attains the outflow-free, the liberation of mind and the liberation by wisdom, knowing and realizing for oneself in this present life, dwelling in full attainment: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (43)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Through grasping, attachment arises; without grasping, there is no attachment. Listen carefully and reflect well. I shall explain it to you."

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "Yes, we receive the teaching!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "How does attachment arise through grasping? A foolish, unlearned ordinary person sees form as self, as other than self, or the two as mutually inhering. He sees form as self or as belonging to self, and grasps it. Having grasped, when that form changes or alters, his mind turns along with it. When the mind turns along with it, grasping and attachment arise, and the mind is seized and held. Because the mind is seized and held, fear, obstruction, and mental confusion arise, due to grasping and attachment. A foolish, unlearned ordinary person sees feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness as self, as other than self, or the two as mutually inhering. He sees consciousness as self or as belonging to self, and grasps it. Having grasped, when that consciousness changes or alters, his mind turns along with it. When the mind turns along with it, grasping and attachment arise, and the mind is seized and held. Being seized and held, fear, obstruction, and mental confusion arise, due to grasping and attachment. This is called attachment through grasping.

"What is called not grasping and not being attached? A well-learned noble disciple does not see form as self, as other than self, or the two as mutually inhering. Regarding form, he does not see self or belonging to self, and does not grasp. Not seeing self or belonging to self and not grasping form, when that form changes or alters, his mind does not turn along with it. Because the mind does not turn along with it, grasping and attachment do not arise, and the mind is not seized and held. Because the mind is not seized and held, fear, obstruction, and mental confusion do not arise, because of not grasping and being attached. Likewise with feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness: he does not see self, other than self, or the two as mutually inhering. He does not see self or belonging to self, and does not grasp. When that consciousness changes or alters, his mind does not turn along with it. Because the mind does not turn along with it, grasping and attachment do not arise, and the mind is not seized and held. Because the mind is not seized and held, there is no fear, no obstruction, no mental confusion, because of not grasping and being attached. This is called not grasping and not being attached. This is the explanation of grasping and attachment, and not grasping and not being attached."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (44)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If something arises, then there is bondage; if it does not arise, then there is no bondage. Listen carefully and reflect well. I shall explain it to you.

"How is it that if something arises, then there is bondage? A foolish, unlearned ordinary person, because he does not know as it really is the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form, delights in form, praises form, grasps and clings to form, regards form as self or belonging to self, and grasps it. Having grasped, when that form changes or alters, his mind follows the change. Because his mind follows the change, the mind is seized and held in grasping attachment. Because the mind is seized and held, fear, obstruction, anxiety, and brooding arise, due to the arising of bondage. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise. This is called arising gives rise to bondage.

"How is it that without arising there is no bondage? A well-learned noble disciple knows as it really is the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Knowing as it really is, he does not delight in it, does not praise it, does not grasp and cling to it, does not bind it as self or belonging to self, and does not grasp. Because he does not grasp, when that form changes or alters, his mind does not follow the change. Because his mind does not follow the change, the mind is not seized and held in grasping attachment. Because the mind is not seized and held, there is no fear, no obstruction, no anxiety, no brooding, because nothing arises and there is no attachment. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise. This is called not arising and no bondage."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (45)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The form aggregate of clinging, and the feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging. If any śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas see a self, they all see it within these five aggregates of clinging. Śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas see form as self, form as other than self, self as being in form, or form as being in self. They see feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness as self, consciousness as other than self, self as being in consciousness, or consciousness as being in self. A foolish, unlearned ordinary person, because of ignorance, sees form as self, other than self, or the two as mutually inhering, and says: 'This is my true self, which cannot be abandoned.' Because he does not abandon it, the sense faculties increase. When the sense faculties increase, contacts multiply. Through what the six sense-entry points contact, the foolish, unlearned ordinary person gives rise to feelings of pleasure and pain, arising from contact at the sense-entry points. What are the six? The eye sense-entry point, and the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind sense-entry points.

"Thus, bhikṣus, there is the mind element, the dharma element, and the ignorance element. A foolish, unlearned ordinary person, due to contact born of ignorance, gives rise to the perception of existence, the perception of non-existence, the perception of both existence and non-existence, the perception of 'I am superior,' the perception of 'I am equal,' the perception of 'I am inferior,' the perception of 'I know, I see.' Such knowing and such seeing as perceptions all arise through the six sense-entry points. A well-learned noble disciple, at these six sense-entry points, abandons ignorance and gives rise to wisdom. He does not give rise to the perception of existence, the perception of non-existence, the perception of both existence and non-existence, the perception of being superior, the perception of being equal, the perception of being inferior, or the perception of 'I know, I see.' Having known thus and seen thus, the contact born of ignorance that previously arose ceases, and the perception born of subsequent contact with wisdom arises."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (46)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The form, feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging. If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas, through knowledge of past lives, themselves recall various past lives, whether already recalled, to be recalled, or now recalling, all of these are recalled within these five aggregates of clinging, whether already recalled, to be recalled, or now recalled: 'In the past I experienced such form, such feeling, such perception, such volitional formations, such consciousness.' What can be obstructed and divided is called the form aggregate of clinging. What is obstructed? Hands, stones, sticks, knives, cold, heat, thirst, hunger, mosquitoes, gadflies, poisonous insects, wind, and rain contact: this is called contact-obstruction. Therefore, obstruction characterizes the form aggregate of clinging. Furthermore, this form aggregate of clinging is impermanent, suffering, and subject to change. The characteristic of sensing is the feeling aggregate of clinging. What is sensed? Sensing suffering, sensing pleasure, sensing neither-suffering-nor-pleasure. Therefore, the characteristic of sensing is called the feeling aggregate of clinging. Furthermore, this feeling aggregate of clinging is impermanent, suffering, and subject to change. The various perceptions are the perception aggregate of clinging. What is perceived? Perceiving little, perceiving much, perceiving the immeasurable, or perceiving nothingness as 'there is nothing at all.' Therefore it is called the perception aggregate of clinging. Furthermore, this perception aggregate of clinging is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change. The characteristic of constructing is the volitional formations aggregate of clinging. What is constructed? Constructing regarding form, constructing regarding feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness. Therefore, the characteristic of constructing is called the volitional formations aggregate of clinging. Furthermore, this volitional formations aggregate of clinging is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change. The characteristic of discriminating and knowing is the consciousness aggregate of clinging. What is cognized? Cognizing form, cognizing sound, smell, taste, touch, and dharmas. Therefore the characteristic of discriminating and knowing is called the consciousness aggregate of clinging. Furthermore, this consciousness aggregate of clinging is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change.

"Bhikṣus, a well-learned noble disciple trains regarding this form aggregate of clinging in this way: 'I am now being consumed by present form. In past lives I was also consumed by that form, just as I am now in the present.' He further reflects: 'I am now being consumed by present form. If I again delight in and cling to future form, I will also be consumed by that form in the future, just as I am now in the present.' Having known thus, he does not look back at past form, does not delight in or cling to future form, and toward present form he develops disenchantment, freedom from desire, the cessation of affliction, and turns toward cessation. A well-learned noble disciple trains regarding the feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging: 'I am now in the present being consumed by present consciousness. In past lives I was also consumed by consciousness, just as I am now in the present. I am now already being consumed by present consciousness. If I again delight in and cling to future consciousness, I will also be consumed by that consciousness in the future, just as I am now in the present.' Having known thus, he does not look back at past consciousness, does not delight in future consciousness, and toward present consciousness he develops disenchantment, freedom from desire, the cessation of affliction, and turns toward cessation. He ceases and does not increase, retreats and does not advance, ceases and does not arise, relinquishes and does not grasp.

"In regard to what does one cease and not increase? Form ceases and does not increase; feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness cease and do not increase. In regard to what does one retreat and not advance? Form retreats and does not advance; feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness retreat and do not advance. In regard to what does one cease and not arise? Form ceases and does not arise; feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness cease and do not arise. In regard to what does one relinquish and not grasp? Form is relinquished and not grasped; feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are relinquished and not grasped.

"Ceasing and not increasing, one dwells in tranquil cessation. Retreating and not advancing, one dwells in tranquil retreat. Ceasing and not arising, one dwells in tranquil cessation. Relinquishing and not grasping, one does not give rise to bondage. Not being bound, one personally realizes nirvāṇa: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha spoke this sūtra, many bhikṣus did not give rise to any outflows, and their minds were liberated.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

> Self, lowly, seeds, attachment, five turnings, seven,
> Two bondages and perception, three-times aggregates, worldly consumption.

---

### (47)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "A faithful good man should make this resolve: 'I should follow the Dharma. I should dwell cultivating much disenchantment toward form, and dwell cultivating much disenchantment toward feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness.' A faithful good man then dwells cultivating much disenchantment toward form, and dwells cultivating much disenchantment toward feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness. Therefore, toward form he attains disenchantment; toward feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness he attains disenchantment. Being disenchanted, he is freed from desire, and is liberated. In liberation, the knowledge arises: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (48)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "A faithful good man, having rightly gone forth in faith from the household life into homelessness, should reflect: 'I should follow the Dharma. Toward form I should dwell cultivating much disenchantment; toward feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness I should dwell cultivating much disenchantment.' A faithful good man, having rightly gone forth in faith from the household life into homelessness, dwells cultivating much disenchantment toward form, and dwells cultivating much disenchantment toward feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness. Having cultivated thus, toward form he attains separation; toward feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness he attains separation. I say that such a person is completely separated from all birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (49)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the Venerable Ānanda, saying: "If a faithful householder or householder's son comes and asks you: 'With regard to what dharmas does one know their arising and ceasing?' How would you answer?"

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if a householder or householder's son comes and asks me, I would answer: 'Know that form is a dharma that arises and ceases; know that feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are dharmas that arise and cease.' World-Honored One, if a householder or householder's son asks thus, I would answer thus."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Good! Good! You should answer thus. Why is that? Form is a dharma that arises and ceases; feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are dharmas that arise and cease. To know that form is a dharma that arises and ceases is called knowing form. To know that feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are dharmas that arise and cease is called knowing consciousness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (50)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the Venerable Ānanda, saying: "If wanderers of other sects come and ask you: 'Ānanda, for what purpose does the World-Honored One teach people to cultivate the holy life?' If asked thus, how would you answer?"

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if wanderers of other sects come and ask me: 'Ānanda, for what purpose does the World-Honored One teach people to cultivate the holy life?' I would answer: 'For the purpose of cultivating disenchantment toward form, freedom from desire, complete cessation, liberation, and non-arising, the World-Honored One teaches people to cultivate the holy life. For the purpose of cultivating disenchantment toward feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness, freedom from desire, complete cessation, liberation, and non-arising, he teaches people to cultivate the holy life.' World-Honored One, if wanderers of other sects ask thus, I would answer thus."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Good! Good! You should answer thus. Why is that? I truly do teach people to cultivate the holy life for the purpose of cultivating disenchantment toward form, freedom from desire, complete cessation, liberation, and non-arising. For the purpose of cultivating disenchantment toward feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness, freedom from desire, complete cessation, liberation, and non-arising, I teach people to cultivate the holy life."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (51)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now explain to you the dharma of what is destructible and what is indestructible. Listen carefully and reflect well. I shall explain it to you. Bhikṣus, form is a destructible dharma; the nirvāṇa that is the cessation of that form is an indestructible dharma. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are destructible dharmas; the nirvāṇa that is the cessation of that consciousness is an indestructible dharma."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (52)

The Uḍḍīka Sūtra is as explained in the section on the fourth dharma of the Ekottarāgama.

---

### (53)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people in the country of Kosala, and was dwelling in the Śiṃśapā Grove, north of the village of Sālā.

At that time, the village headman, a great brāhmaṇa of prominent clan, heard that the śramaṇa of the Śākya clan, from the great Śākya lineage, had shaved off his hair and beard, put on the kāṣāya robe, and in right faith had left the household life to study the Way, and had attained unsurpassed, perfect, complete awakening. He was now traveling among the people in the country of Kosala and had arrived north of the Sālā village, dwelling in the Śiṃśapā Grove. Furthermore, that śramaṇa Gautama was of such fine appearance and renown, with true merit and virtue, praised by devas and humans, his fame heard in all eight directions. He was the Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha, one endowed with knowledge and conduct, Well-Gone One, Knower of the World, Unsurpassed One, Trainer of Men, Teacher of Devas and Humans, Buddha, World-Honored One. Among all the worlds, among devas, māras, Brahmās, śramaṇas, and brāhmaṇas, with great wisdom he could himself verify and know: "My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that I will not receive further existence." He taught the Dharma for the world, good in the beginning, good in the middle, good in the end, good in meaning, good in flavor, pure and complete, with the holy life purified and clear, expounding the wondrous Dharma. "How excellent it would be to see him! How excellent it would be to go to him! How excellent it would be to pay him reverence!" Having thought thus, he immediately prepared his carriage, brought along a large retinue of attendants, holding a golden vase, staff, and parasol, and went to where the Buddha was, respectfully to serve him. Arriving at the entrance of the grove, he descended from his carriage and proceeded on foot to the World-Honored One's presence, exchanged courteous greetings, and sat to one side. He addressed the World-Honored One: "Śramaṇa Gautama, what do you discuss and what do you teach?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "I discuss causes and teach about causes."

He further asked the Buddha: "How do you discuss causes? How do you teach about causes?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "There are causes and conditions for the arising of the world; there are causes and conditions by which the world arises. There are causes and conditions for the cessation of the world; there are causes and conditions by which the world ceases."

The brāhmaṇa addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, how is it that there are causes and conditions for the arising of the world, and causes and conditions by which the world arises?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "A foolish, unlearned ordinary person does not know as it really is the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Not knowing as it really is, he delights in form, praises form, and dwells with a mind attached and stained. Because he delights in form, he grasps. Conditioned by grasping, there is existence. Conditioned by existence, there is birth. Conditioned by birth, there is old age, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering. This is the great mass of suffering that arises. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise. Brāhmaṇa, this is what is meant by there being causes and conditions for the arising of the world, and causes and conditions by which the world arises."

The brāhmaṇa addressed the Buddha: "How is it that there are causes and conditions for the cessation of the world, and causes and conditions by which the world ceases?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "A well-learned noble disciple knows as it really is the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Having known as it really is, he does not delight in form, does not praise form, is not stained by or attached to form, and does not dwell in it. Because he does not delight in or dwell in form, craving for form ceases. When craving ceases, grasping ceases. When grasping ceases, existence ceases. When existence ceases, birth ceases. When birth ceases, old age, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering cease. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise. Brāhmaṇa, this is what is meant by there being causes and conditions for the cessation of the world, and this is what is meant by there being causes and conditions by which the world ceases. Brāhmaṇa, this is what is meant by discussing causes and teaching about causes."

The brāhmaṇa addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, you discuss causes in this way and teach about causes in this way. The world has many affairs; I now beg to take my leave."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "You may go when you see fit."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the brāhmaṇas, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully rejoiced, bowed at his feet, and departed.

---

### (54)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Vārāṇasī, at the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park.

At that time, a brāhmaṇa from the village of Vikatamukhā came to where the Buddha was, greeted him respectfully, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, I have a young disciple who knows astronomy and genealogies. For the great assembly he reads omens of fortune and misfortune. What he says will be, will be. What he says will not be, will not be. What he says will succeed, will succeed. What he says will fail, will fail. Gautama, what do you think of this?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "Set aside your young disciple who knows astronomy and genealogies. I now question you; answer as you see fit. Brāhmaṇa, what do you think? Was form originally without seed?"

He answered: "So it is, World-Honored One."

"Were feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness originally without seed?"

He answered: "So it is, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "You say your young disciple knows astronomy and genealogies, and for the great assembly makes such declarations: 'What will be, will be. What will not be, will not be.' Is his knowledge and vision not unreliable?"

The brāhmaṇa addressed the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "What do you think? Is there any form that can endure permanently for a hundred years? Or is it the case that things arise differently and perish differently? Can feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness endure permanently for a hundred years? Or do they arise differently and perish differently?"

He answered: "So it is, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "What do you think? When your young disciple who knows astronomy and genealogies tells the great assembly that what succeeds will not be destroyed, is his knowledge and vision not different from the truth?"

He answered: "So it is, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "What do you think? Between this teaching and that teaching, between this explanation and that explanation, which is superior?"

The brāhmaṇa addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! This accords with the Dharma; as the Buddha has spoken, it is revealed and made clear. It is like one who can rescue a person who has fallen into water, who can save a person from imprisonment, who can show the way to one who is lost, who can bring a lamp into the darkness. The World-Honored One today has well expounded the supreme Dharma; likewise it is revealed and made clear."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the brāhmaṇa of Vikatamukhā, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully rejoiced, rose from his seat, bowed at the Buddha's feet, and departed.

---

### (55)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was in the country of Vārāṇasī, at the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now explain the aggregates and the aggregates of clinging. What are the aggregates? All form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of this collectively is called the form aggregate. All feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness whatsoever are likewise. All of these collectively are called the feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness aggregates. These are called the aggregates. What are the aggregates of clinging? If form is with outflows and subject to grasping, and that form, whether past, future, or present, gives rise to craving, aversion, delusion, and various other defiling mental states, feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise. These are called the aggregates of clinging."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully put it into practice.

---

### (56)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Vārāṇasī, at the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now explain dharmas with outflows and dharmas without outflows. If form has outflows and is subject to grasping, that form can give rise to craving and aversion. Likewise feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness, having outflows and being subject to grasping, that consciousness can give rise to craving and aversion

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099, Fascicle 3

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.
