## (59)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging. Observe these five aggregates of clinging as phenomena of arising and ceasing. That is to say: this is form, this is the arising of form, this is the cessation of form; this is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, this is the arising of consciousness, this is the cessation of consciousness. What is the arising of form? What is the cessation of form? What is the arising of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness? What is the cessation of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness? The arising of craving and delight is the arising of form; the cessation of craving and delight is the cessation of form. The arising of contact is the arising of feeling, perception, and formations; the cessation of contact is the cessation of feeling, perception, and formations. The arising of name-and-form is the arising of consciousness; the cessation of name-and-form is the cessation of consciousness. Bhikṣus! Such is the arising and cessation of form; this is the arising and cessation of form. Such is the arising and cessation of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness; this is the arising and cessation of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (60)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging. It is good, bhikṣus, to not delight in form, not praise form, not grasp at form, not be attached to form. It is good, bhikṣus, to not delight in feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, not praise consciousness, not grasp at consciousness, not be attached to consciousness. Why is this? If a bhikṣu does not delight in form, does not praise form, does not grasp at form, does not attach to form, then with regard to form, delight ceases, and the mind attains liberation. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: not delighting in consciousness, not praising consciousness, not grasping at consciousness, not attaching to consciousness, then with regard to consciousness, delight ceases, and the mind attains liberation. If a bhikṣu does not delight in form, the mind attains liberation. Likewise, if feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are not delighted in, the mind attains liberation. Without ceasing, without arising, one dwells in equanimous relinquishment, with right mindfulness and right knowledge.

"That bhikṣu who knows thus and sees thus, with regard to the past, sees it all completely exhausted without remainder. Having seen the past completely exhausted without remainder, with regard to the future, also sees it completely exhausted without remainder. Having seen the future completely exhausted without remainder, with regard to both past and future together, sees them completely exhausted without remainder, without any attachment. One who is without attachment has nothing whatsoever to grasp at in the world. One who has nothing to grasp at also has nothing to seek. One who has nothing to seek realizes nirvāṇa for oneself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know by myself that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (61)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging.

"What is the form aggregate of clinging? All form, that is, the four great elements and form derived from the four great elements: this is called the form aggregate of clinging. Furthermore, that form is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change. If that form aggregate of clinging is permanently cut off without remainder, ultimately relinquished, exhausted, freed from desire, stilled, and extinguished, and no further form aggregate of clinging continues, arises, or emerges, this is called sublime, this is called peaceful, this is called the relinquishment of all residual remainders, the exhaustion of craving, freedom from desire, cessation, nirvāṇa.

"What is the feeling aggregate of clinging? It refers to the six bodies of feeling. What are the six? Feeling born from eye-contact, feeling born from ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact: this is called the feeling aggregate of clinging. Furthermore, that feeling aggregate of clinging is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change, and so on up to cessation, nirvāṇa.

"What is the perception aggregate of clinging? It refers to the six bodies of perception. What are the six? Perception born from eye-contact, and so on up to perception born from mind-contact: this is called the perception aggregate of clinging. Furthermore, that perception aggregate of clinging is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change, and so on up to cessation, nirvāṇa.

"What is the formations aggregate of clinging? It refers to the six bodies of volition. What are the six? Volition born from eye-contact, and so on up to volition born from mind-contact: this is called the formations aggregate of clinging. Furthermore, that formations aggregate of clinging is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change, and so on up to cessation, nirvāṇa.

"What is the consciousness aggregate of clinging? It refers to the six bodies of consciousness. What are the six? The eye-consciousness body, and so on up to the mind-consciousness body: this is called the consciousness aggregate of clinging. Furthermore, that consciousness aggregate of clinging is impermanent, suffering, and a dharma subject to change, and so on up to cessation, nirvāṇa.

"Bhikṣus! If with regard to these dharmas one contemplates, examines, discriminates, and accepts them through wisdom, this is called a faith-follower. One transcends and departs from the state of birth, crosses beyond the ground of ordinary beings, and though one has not yet attained the fruit of stream-entry, if one does not die in the interim, one will certainly attain the fruit of stream-entry.

"Bhikṣus! If with regard to these dharmas one contemplates, examines, and accepts them through superior wisdom, this is called a dharma-follower. One transcends and departs from the state of birth, crosses beyond the ground of ordinary beings, and though one has not yet attained the fruit of stream-entry, if one does not die in the interim, one will certainly attain the fruit of stream-entry.

"Bhikṣus! When with regard to these dharmas one sees with true right wisdom, the three fetters are completely cut off and known: namely, the view of self-identity, attachment to precepts and rituals, and doubt. Bhikṣus! This is called the fruit of stream-entry. One does not fall into evil destinies and is certain to proceed correctly toward the three awakenings. After seven births among devas and humans, one then ultimately reaches the end of suffering.

"Bhikṣus! When with regard to these dharmas one sees with true right wisdom and the mind's outflows do not arise, this is called an arhat. All outflows are exhausted, what was to be done has been done, the heavy burden is laid down, one's own benefit is attained, all fetters of existence are ended, and through right knowledge the mind attains liberation."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (62)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five aggregates of clinging, namely the form aggregate of clinging, the feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging. The foolish, unlearned ordinary person, without wisdom, without clear knowledge, with regard to the five aggregates of clinging, gives rise to views of self and is bound by attachment, causing the mind to be bound and giving rise to desire. Bhikṣus! The well-learned noble disciple, having wisdom and clear knowledge, with regard to these five aggregates of clinging, does not view them as self and is not bound by attachment, so that the mind is fettered and desire arises.

"How does the foolish, unlearned ordinary person, without wisdom, without clear knowledge, view the five aggregates of clinging as self and become bound by attachment, causing the mind to be fettered and desire to arise? Bhikṣus! The foolish, unlearned ordinary person, without wisdom, without clear knowledge, views form as self, as other than self, or as mutually residing. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: views them as self, as other than self, or as mutually residing. Thus the foolish, unlearned ordinary person, without wisdom, without clear knowledge, with regard to the five aggregates of clinging, speaks of self and becomes bound by attachment, causing the mind to be fettered and desire to arise.

"Bhikṣus! How does the noble disciple, having wisdom and clear knowledge, not speak of self and become bound by attachment, so that the mind is fettered and desire arises? The noble disciple does not view form as self, as other than self, or as mutually residing. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: does not view them as self, as other than self, or as mutually residing. Thus the well-learned noble disciple, having wisdom and clear knowledge, with regard to the five aggregates of clinging, does not view them as self and is not bound by attachment, so that the mind is fettered and desire arises. Whatever form there is, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether good or bad, whether far or near, all of it one rightly observes as entirely impermanent. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether good or bad, whether far or near, all of it one rightly observes as entirely impermanent."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (63)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five aggregates of clinging, namely the form aggregate of clinging, the feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging. Bhikṣus! If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas conceive of a self, they all conceive of a self within these five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The various śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas view form as self, as other than self, or as mutually residing; likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, they view them as self, as other than self, or as mutually residing. Thus the foolish, unlearned ordinary person conceives of a self, with ignorance, discriminating and observing in this way, and does not depart from 'mine.' Not departing from 'mine,' one enters into the sense faculties. Having entered into the sense faculties, one gives rise to contact. Contacted through the six sense-entry points, the foolish, unlearned ordinary person gives rise to pleasure and pain; from this arise these and others. That is to say, the six contact bodies. What are the six? The eye-contact entry point, the ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact entry points. Bhikṣus! There is the mind element, the dharma element, and the ignorance element. Touched by the contact of ignorance, the foolish, unlearned ordinary person speaks of existence, speaks of non-existence, speaks of both existence and non-existence, speaks of neither existence nor non-existence, speaks of 'I am supreme,' speaks of 'I am equal,' saying 'I know, I see.'

"Furthermore, bhikṣus! The well-learned noble disciple, dwelling in the six contact entry points, is able to become disenchanted with ignorance and give rise to clear knowledge. With regard to ignorance, becoming free from desire, one gives rise to clear knowledge. One does not assert existence, does not assert non-existence, does not assert both existence and non-existence, does not assert neither existence nor non-existence, does not assert 'I am supreme,' does not assert 'I am inferior,' does not assert 'I am equal,' saying 'I know, I see.' Having known and seen thus, the previous contact of ignorance that arose ceases, and the subsequent contact of clear knowledge arises."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (64)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in the Eastern Park, the Hall of Mṛgāra's Mother.

At that time, the World-Honored One, in the late afternoon, emerged from meditation and came out of the lecture hall. In the shade of the hall, before the great assembly, he had a seat spread out and sat down. At that time, the World-Honored One praised with an udāna verse:

> "In dharmas there is no self,
> And also no 'mine.'
> Since 'I' shall not exist in the future,
> How could 'mine' arise?
> The bhikṣu who is liberated from this
> Then cuts the lower fetters."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, bared his right shoulder, placed his right knee on the ground, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One! How is it that 'in dharmas there is no self, and also no mine; since I shall not exist in the future, how could mine arise? The bhikṣu who is liberated from this then cuts the lower fetters'?"

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣu: "The foolish, unlearned ordinary person conceives form as self, as other than self, or as mutually residing; conceives feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as self, as other than self, or as mutually residing. The well-learned noble disciple does not view form as self, as other than self, or as mutually residing; does not view feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as self, as other than self, or as mutually residing; and is neither one who knows nor one who sees in that way. This form is impermanent; feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent. Form is suffering; feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are suffering. Form is non-self; feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are non-self. This form shall not exist in the future; feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness shall not exist in the future. This form is subject to destruction; feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are subject to destruction. Therefore, it is not self, not mine; self and mine shall not exist in the future. One who is liberated in this way then cuts the five lower fetters."

At that time, that bhikṣu addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One! Having cut the five lower fetters, how does one, with outflows exhausted, attain the outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation through wisdom, knowing and witnessing for oneself in the present life, fully abiding in it: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know by myself that I will not receive further existence'?"

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣu: "The foolish ordinary person, the unlearned being, in the place of non-fear gives rise to fear. The foolish ordinary person, the unlearned being, fears:

> 'No self, no mine,
> Both shall not arise in the future.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

> Arising, ceasing, and displeasure,
> Together with three kinds of discrimination,
> Grasping and contemplation:
> These are called the udāna.

---

## (65)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should always cultivate skillful means in meditation, inwardly stilling the mind. Why is this? Bhikṣus, when you always cultivate skillful means in meditation, inwardly stilling the mind, you can observe in accordance with reality. How does one observe in accordance with reality? This is form, this is the arising of form, this is the cessation of form; this is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, this is the arising of consciousness, this is the cessation of consciousness.

"What is the arising of form, and the arising of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness? The foolish, unlearned ordinary person, with regard to pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-pleasant-nor-painful feeling, does not observe in accordance with reality; does not observe in accordance with reality the arising of feeling, the cessation of feeling, the gratification of feeling, the danger of feeling, and the escape from feeling. Because of not observing in accordance with reality, one delights in and attaches to feeling, and grasps. Conditioned by grasping there is becoming; conditioned by becoming there is birth; conditioned by birth there are old age, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, and distress. Thus the entire great mass of suffering arises from its conditions. This is called the arising of form; this is called the arising of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness.

"What is the cessation of form, and the cessation of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness? The well-learned noble disciple, when experiencing pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-pleasant-nor-painful feeling, observes in accordance with reality; observes in accordance with reality the arising of feeling, the cessation of feeling, the gratification of feeling, the danger of feeling, and the escape from feeling. Because of observing in accordance with reality, delight and attachment to feeling ceases. When attachment ceases, grasping ceases. When grasping ceases, becoming ceases. When becoming ceases, birth ceases. When birth ceases, old age, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, and distress all cease. Thus the entire great mass of suffering is completely extinguished. This is called the cessation of form, and the cessation of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness.

"Therefore, bhikṣus! You should always cultivate skillful means in meditation, inwardly stilling the mind. Bhikṣus! Abiding in meditation, inwardly stilling the mind, being diligent in skillful means, observe in accordance with reality."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with observation, so too with discrimination, various kinds of discrimination, knowing, thorough knowing, various kinds of knowing, drawing near, drawing near and cultivating, entering, touching, and realizing: two sūtras should likewise be spoken in full.

---

## (66)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should always cultivate skillful means in meditation, inwardly stilling the mind. Why is this? Having cultivated skillful means in meditation, inwardly stilling the mind, one observes in accordance with reality. How does one observe in accordance with reality? One observes in accordance with reality: this is form, this is the arising of form, this is the cessation of form; this is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, this is the arising of consciousness, this is the cessation of consciousness.

"What is the arising of form? What is the arising of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness? Bhikṣus! The foolish, unlearned ordinary person, because of not observing in accordance with reality the arising of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form, delights in that form, praises and loves it, and in the future, form is again produced." Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness should likewise be spoken of in full. "When that form arises, and feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness arise, one is not liberated from form, not liberated from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. I say that such a one is not liberated from birth, old age, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, and distress, the entire great mass of suffering. This is called the arising of form, and the arising of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness.

"What is the cessation of form, and the cessation of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness? The well-learned noble disciple observes in accordance with reality the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form, knowing them as they truly are. Knowing them as they truly are, one does not delight in form, does not praise form, does not delight in and attach to form, and does not give rise to future form." Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness should likewise be spoken of in full. "When form does not arise, and feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness do not arise, one attains liberation from form, attains liberation from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. I say that such a one is liberated from birth, old age, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, and distress, the entire great mass of suffering. This is called the cessation of form, and the cessation of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness.

"Therefore, bhikṣus! You should always cultivate skillful means in meditation, inwardly stilling the mind, being diligent in skillful means, observing in accordance with reality."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with observation, so too up to realizing: twelve sūtras should likewise be spoken in full.

---

## (67)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should always cultivate skillful means in meditation, inwardly stilling the mind. Why is this? Bhikṣus, having cultivated skillful means in meditation, inwardly stilling the mind, one observes in accordance with reality. How does one observe in accordance with reality? One knows in accordance with reality: this is form, this is the arising of form, this is the cessation of form; this is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, this is the arising of consciousness, this is the cessation of consciousness.

"What is the arising of form, and the arising of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness? The foolish, unlearned ordinary person does not know in accordance with reality the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Not knowing in accordance with reality, one delights in and attaches to that form, and praises form. Because of delighting in and attaching to form, and praising form, one grasps. Conditioned by grasping there is becoming; conditioned by becoming there is birth; conditioned by birth there are old age, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, and distress. Thus the entire great mass of suffering arises. This is called the arising of form, and the arising of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness.

"What is the cessation of form, and the cessation of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness? The well-learned noble disciple knows in accordance with reality the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Knowing in accordance with reality, one does not delight in or attach to form, does not praise form. Because of not delighting in, attaching to, or praising form, craving and delight cease. When craving and delight cease, grasping ceases. When grasping ceases, becoming ceases. When becoming ceases, birth ceases. When birth ceases, old age, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, and distress cease. Thus the entire great mass of suffering ceases. How does the well-learned noble disciple know in accordance with reality feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness? One knows in accordance with reality the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, and the escape from consciousness. Knowing them, one does not delight in or attach to that consciousness, does not praise consciousness. Because of not delighting in, attaching to, or praising consciousness, craving and delight cease. When craving and delight cease, grasping ceases. When grasping ceases, becoming ceases. When becoming ceases, birth ceases. When birth ceases, old age, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, and distress cease. Thus the entire great mass of suffering ceases, all completely extinguished. Bhikṣus! This is called the cessation of form, and the cessation of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness.

"Bhikṣus! You should always cultivate skillful means in meditation, inwardly stilling the mind."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with observation, so too up to realizing: twelve sūtras should likewise be spoken in full.

---

## (68)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should always cultivate skillful means in meditation, inwardly stilling the mind, observing in accordance with reality. How does one observe in accordance with reality? One knows in accordance with reality: this is form, this is the arising of form, this is the cessation of form; this is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, this is the arising of consciousness, this is the cessation of consciousness.

"What is the arising of form, and the arising of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness? Conditioned by the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The conjunction of the three produces contact. Conditioned by contact, feeling arises. Conditioned by feeling, craving arises, and so on up to the arising of the entire great mass of suffering. This is called the arising of form. Likewise, conditioned by the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind; conditioned by the mind and dharmas, mind-consciousness arises. The conjunction of the three produces contact. Conditioned by contact, feeling arises. Conditioned by feeling, craving arises. Thus and so on up to the arising of the entire great mass of suffering. This is called the arising of form, and the arising of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness.

"What is the cessation of form, and the cessation of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness? Conditioned by the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The conjunction of the three produces contact. When contact ceases, feeling ceases, and so on up to the cessation of the entire great mass of suffering. Likewise with the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind: conditioned by the mind and dharmas, mind-consciousness arises. The conjunction of the three produces contact. When contact ceases, feeling ceases; when feeling ceases, and so on up to the cessation of the entire great mass of suffering. This is called the cessation of form, and the cessation of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness.

"Therefore, bhikṣus! You should always cultivate skillful means in meditation, inwardly stilling the mind."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with observation, so too up to realizing: twelve sūtras should likewise be spoken in full.

> Feeling, arising, and delight,
> Also spoken are the six sense-entry points;
> Each one in twelve kinds:
> Sūtras on meditation and samādhi.

---

## (69)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach the path leading to the accumulation of the existing body, and the path leading to the cessation of the accumulation of the existing body. What is the path leading to the accumulation of the existing body? The foolish, unlearned ordinary person does not know in accordance with reality the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Not knowing in accordance with reality, one delights in form, praises form, attaches to form, and abides in form. Delighting in form, praising form, attaching to form, and abiding in form, craving and delight arise and one grasps. Conditioned by grasping there is becoming; conditioned by becoming there is birth; conditioned by birth there are old age, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, and distress. Thus the entire great mass of suffering arises. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness should likewise be spoken of in full. This is called the path leading to the accumulation of the existing body. Bhikṣus! The path leading to the accumulation of the existing body, you should know, is the path leading to the accumulation of suffering.

"What is the path leading to the cessation of the accumulation of the existing body? The well-learned noble disciple knows in accordance with reality form, the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Knowing in accordance with reality, one does not delight in form, does not praise form, does not attach to form, does not abide in form. Not delighting, not praising, not attaching, not abiding, that craving and delight for form ceases. When craving and delight cease, grasping ceases. When grasping ceases, becoming ceases. When becoming ceases, birth ceases. When birth ceases, old age, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, and distress, the entire great mass of suffering, all cease. As with form, so too with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. This is called the path leading to the cessation of the existing body. The path leading to the cessation of the existing body is the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Therefore, I speak of the path leading to the cessation of the existing body."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with "I shall teach," so too with "there is" and "you should know," it is likewise spoken.

---

## (70)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach the boundary of the suffering of the existing body, the boundary of the accumulation of the existing body, and the boundary of the cessation of the existing body. Listen carefully, reflect well upon it, and I shall explain it for you. What is the boundary of the suffering of the existing body? It refers to the five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging. This is called the boundary of the suffering of the existing body. What is the boundary of the accumulation of the existing body? It refers to craving that leads to future existence, accompanied by delight and passion, finding pleasure here and there. This is called the boundary of the accumulation of the existing body. What is the boundary of the cessation of the existing body? It is precisely this craving that leads to future existence, accompanied by delight and passion, finding pleasure here and there, being cut off without remainder, expelled, exhausted, freed from desire, ceased, stilled, and extinguished. This is called the boundary of the cessation of the existing body. Therefore, I speak of the boundary of the suffering of the existing body, the boundary of the accumulation of the existing body, and the boundary of the cessation of the existing body."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with "I shall teach," so too with "there is" and "you should know," it is likewise spoken.

---

## (71)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach the existing body, the accumulation of the existing body, the cessation of the existing body, and the path leading to the cessation of the existing body. Listen carefully, reflect well, and I shall explain it for you. What is the existing body? It refers to the five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging. This is called the existing body. What is the accumulation of the existing body? It is craving that leads to future existence, accompanied by delight and passion, finding pleasure in this and that with attachment. This is called the accumulation of the existing body. What is the cessation of the existing body? It is craving that leads to future existence, accompanied by delight and passion, finding pleasure in this and that, being cut off without remainder, expelled, exhausted, freed from desire, and ceased. This is called the cessation of the existing body.

"What is the path leading to the cessation of the existing body? It refers to the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration. This is called the path leading to the cessation of the existing body. This is the teaching of the existing body, the accumulation of the existing body, the cessation of the existing body, and the path leading to the cessation of the existing body."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

Another version says with this difference: "You should know the existing body; you should know to abandon the accumulation of the existing body; you should know to realize the cessation of the existing body; you should know to cultivate and practice the path leading to the cessation of the existing body."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with "I shall teach," so too with "there is" and "you should know," it is likewise spoken. Yet another version with this difference: "The bhikṣu who knows the existing body, who abandons the accumulation of the existing body, who realizes the cessation of the existing body, and who cultivates and practices the path leading to the cessation of the existing body, this bhikṣu is called one who has cut the bonds of craving, equalized all fetters, cultivated uninterrupted insight, and reached the ultimate end of suffering."

Yet another version with this difference: "This bhikṣu is called one who has reached the ultimate boundary, who has ultimately departed from defilement, who has ultimately completed the holy life, a purely clean supreme being."

Yet another version with this difference: "This bhikṣu is called an arhat who has exhausted all outflows. What was to be done has been done. The heavy burden has been laid down. One's own benefit has been attained. All fetters of existence have been ended. Through right knowledge, the mind has attained liberation."

Yet another version with this difference: "This bhikṣu is called one who has broken through the barriers, crossed the moat, transcended the boundaries, and freed oneself from the sentinels, raising the banner of the holy Dharma."

Yet another version with this difference: "How does one break through the barriers? It means cutting off the five lower fetters. How does one cross the moat? It means crossing the deep moat of ignorance. How does one transcend the boundaries? It means ultimately ending beginningless saṃsāra. How does one free oneself from the sentinels? It means the exhaustion of craving for existence. How does one raise the banner of the holy Dharma? It means the exhaustion of the conceit 'I am.'"

Yet another version with this difference: "This bhikṣu is called one who has cut off the five branches, perfected the six branches, guards the one, relies on the four kinds, has abandoned all doctrinal positions, is free from all seeking, has purified all thoughts, whose bodily activities are stilled, whose mind is well liberated, whose wisdom is well liberated, one who stands solely in the holy life, a supreme being."

> The path has three kinds, and realization also three kinds.
> The existing body is spoken of in four kinds; the arhat in six kinds.

---

## (72)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach what is to be known, knowledge, and the knower. Listen carefully, reflect well, and I shall explain it for you. What is that which is to be known? It refers to the five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging. This is called that which is to be known.

"What is knowledge? It is taming desire, cutting off desire, transcending desire. This is called knowledge.

"Who is the knower? The arhat. The arhat is one who neither exists after death in another world, nor does not exist after death in another world, nor both exists and does not exist after death in another world, nor neither exists nor does not exist after death in another world. Spoken of broadly and without limit, all such designations are permanently extinguished.

"This is the teaching of what is to be known, knowledge, and the knower."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (73)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach the heavy burden, the taking up of the burden, the laying down of the burden, and the burden-bearer. Listen carefully, reflect well, and I shall explain it for you. What is the heavy burden? It refers to the five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging.

"What is the taking up of the burden? It is craving that leads to future existence, accompanied by delight and passion, finding pleasure here and there.

"What is the laying down of the burden? It is when craving that leads to future existence, accompanied by delight and passion, finding pleasure here and there, has been permanently cut off without remainder, has ceased, been expelled, exhausted, freed from desire, ceased, extinguished.

"Who is the burden-bearer? It is the person. The person is one with such a name, such a birth, such a clan, such food, such experience of pleasure and pain, such longevity, such duration of life, such a limit of lifespan. This is the teaching of the heavy burden, the taking up of the burden, the laying down of the burden, and the burden-bearer."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse:

> "Having laid down the heavy burden,
> One should not take it up again.
> The heavy task is great suffering;
> Laying down the task is great happiness.
> When all craving is cut off,
> Then all formations are exhausted.
> Fully understanding the domain of the remainder,
> One does not return again to existence."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (74)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging. The foolish, unlearned ordinary person does not know in accordance with reality form, the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Not knowing in accordance with reality, one delights in, praises, is bound by, and abides in form; one is bound by the bondage of form, bound by the inner bondage. Not knowing the root, not knowing the boundary, not knowing the way out: this is called the foolish, unlearned ordinary person. Bound, one is born; bound, one dies; bound, one goes from this world to the next world. There also, bound, one is born; bound, one dies. This is called the foolish, unlearned ordinary person. One follows Māra's control, enters Māra's net, is transformed according to Māra's will, bound by Māra's bonds, and led by Māra. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise.

"The well-learned noble disciple knows in accordance with reality form, the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Knowing in accordance with reality, one does not delight in form with craving, does not praise it, is not bound by it, does not abide in it; one is not bound by the bondage of form, not bound by the inner bondage. One knows the root, knows the fording place, knows the way out. This is called the well-learned noble disciple. One is not born bound, does not die bound, does not go from this world to the next world bound. One does not follow Māra's control, does not enter Māra's grasp, does not follow Māra's deeds, is not bound by Māra's bonds, is freed from Māra's bonds, is free from being led by Māra. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (75)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The form aggregate of clinging. Bhikṣus, with regard to form, becoming disenchanted, becoming free from desire, ceasing, not arising, being liberated: this is called the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Awakened One. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: becoming disenchanted, becoming free from desire, ceasing, not arising, being liberated: this is called the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Awakened One. A bhikṣu also, with regard to form, becoming disenchanted, becoming free from desire, ceasing: this is called an arhat, liberated through wisdom. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: becoming disenchanted, becoming free from desire, ceasing: this is called an arhat, liberated through wisdom. Bhikṣus! Between the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Awakened One, and the arhat liberated through wisdom, what is the difference?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The Tathāgata is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. We wish that the World-Honored One would explain this meaning broadly for the bhikṣus. Having heard it, the bhikṣus will receive and practice accordingly."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully, reflect well, and I shall explain it for you. The Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Awakened One, having never before heard the Dharma, was able to awaken to the Dharma by himself, penetrate unsurpassed bodhi, and in the future open the way for and awaken śrāvakas, teaching the Dharma for them, namely the four foundations of mindfulness, the four right efforts, the four bases of spiritual power, the five faculties, the five powers, the seven factors of awakening, and the Eightfold Path. Bhikṣus! This is called the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Awakened One, who attains what was not attained, benefits where there was no benefit, knows the path, distinguishes the path, teaches the path, penetrates the path, and further is able to accomplish the instruction and admonition of śrāvakas. One who teaches the correct and harmonious delight in wholesome dharmas: this is called the difference between the Tathāgata and the arhat."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (76)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging. Bhikṣus! You should examine form. Having examined form, do you see in it self, other than self, or mutual indwelling?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "Good! Good! Form is non-self. What is non-self is impermanent. What is impermanent is suffering. If it is suffering, then all of it is not self, not other than self, not mutually indwelling. One should observe it in this way. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. The well-learned noble disciple, observing these five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self, having observed thus, has nothing whatsoever to grasp at in the world. Having nothing to grasp at, one has nothing to attach to. Having nothing to attach to, one realizes nirvāṇa for oneself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know by myself that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (77)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should cut off desire and passion for form. When desire and passion are cut off, then form is cut off. When form is cut off, one attains the knowledge of cutting off. Having attained the knowledge of cutting off, then the root is cut off, like cutting off the crown of a palmyra tree, so that in the future it will never again grow. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: when desire and passion are cut off, and so on up to never again growing in the future."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (78)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If form arises, persists, and comes forth, then suffering arises here, illness persists here, and old age and death come forth here. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. Bhikṣus! If form ceases, subsides, and disappears, then suffering ceases here, illness subsides here, and old age and death disappear here. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (79)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Even past and future form is impermanent, how much more so present form! The well-learned noble disciple, having observed thus, does not look back at past form, does not look forward to future form, and with regard to present form, becomes disenchanted, free from desire, ceasing, and stilled. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise.

"Bhikṣus! If there were no past form, the well-learned noble disciple would have no need to not look back at past form. Because there is past form, the well-learned noble disciple does not look back at past form. If there were no future form, the well-learned noble disciple would have no need to not look forward to future form. Because there is future form, the well-learned noble disciple does not look forward to future form. If there were no present form, the well-learned noble disciple would not generate disenchantment, freedom from desire, and the inclination toward complete cessation with regard to present form. Because there is present form, the well-learned noble disciple generates disenchantment, freedom from desire, and the inclination toward complete cessation with regard to present form. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with impermanence, so too with suffering, emptiness, and non-self: three sūtras are likewise spoken.

---

## (80)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach the seal of the holy Dharma and the purification of view. Listen carefully, reflect well. If a bhikṣu makes this claim: 'Without having attained anything in the emptiness samādhi, I give rise to the signless, the state of nothingness, and the knowledge and vision of freedom from conceit,' one should not make such a claim. Why is this? If one has not attained emptiness and yet claims to have attained the signless, the state of nothingness, and the knowledge and vision of freedom from conceit, there is no such possibility! If a bhikṣu makes this claim: 'I have attained emptiness, and am able to give rise to the signless, the state of nothingness, and the knowledge and vision of freedom from conceit,' this is well spoken. Why is this? If having attained emptiness, one is able to give rise to the signless, the state of nothingness, and the knowledge and vision of freedom from conceit, this is indeed possible! What is the seal of the holy Dharma and the purification of view?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The Buddha is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. We wish that you would explain it! Having heard the teaching, the bhikṣus will practice as taught."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu, sitting at the foot of a tree in a secluded place, skillfully contemplates form as impermanent, wearing away, a dharma free from desire; likewise contemplates feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as impermanent, wearing away, dharmas free from desire. Contemplating those aggregates as impermanent, wearing away, not solid, dharmas subject to change, the mind becomes joyful, purified, and liberated. This is called emptiness. One who contemplates in this way is still not able to be free from conceit or to have purified knowledge and vision.

"Furthermore, there is right contemplation in samādhi: one contemplates the cessation of the characteristics of form, sound, smell, taste, touch, and dharmas. This is called the signless. One who contemplates in this way is still not yet free from conceit or having purified knowledge and vision.

"Furthermore, there is right contemplation in samādhi: one contemplates the cessation of the characteristics of desire, the cessation of the characteristics of aversion and delusion. This is called the state of nothingness. One who contemplates in this way is still not yet free from conceit or having purified knowledge and vision.

"Furthermore, there is right contemplation in samādhi: one contemplates from where self and mine arise.

"Furthermore, there is right contemplation in samādhi: one contemplates that self and mine arise from seeing, hearing, smelling, tasting, touching, and cognizing.

"Furthermore, one makes this examination: 'If consciousness arises due to conditions and causes, are those conditions and causes of consciousness permanent or impermanent?'

"Furthermore, one reflects: 'If consciousness arises due to conditions and causes, those conditions and causes are all impermanent.' Furthermore, those conditions and causes being all impermanent, how could the consciousness born from them be permanent?

"That which is impermanent is conditioned, fabricated, dependently arisen; it is a dharma of affliction, a dharma of cessation, a dharma of freedom from desire, a dharma of cutting off and knowing. This is called the seal of the holy Dharma, the purification of knowledge and vision. This is the teaching for bhikṣus of the seal of the holy Dharma and the purification of knowledge and vision." Thus it is broadly spoken.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (81)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pool, in the Double-Storied Lecture Hall.

At that time, there was a Licchavi named Mahānāma who would go daily to visit the Buddha. At that time, the Licchavi had this thought: "If I go to the World-Honored One too early, the World-Honored One and the bhikṣus known to me will all be in meditation. I should now go to the Seven Āmra Trees, to the Ājīvika ascetics' place." So he went to the dwelling of Pūraṇa Kāśyapa.

At that time, Pūraṇa Kāśyapa, the leader of the heterodox community, was surrounded by five hundred ascetics, front and back, making loud noise, playing, and discussing worldly matters. Pūraṇa Kāśyapa saw the Licchavi Mahānāma coming from afar and told his followers to be still and quiet: "Be silent! This is the Licchavi Mahānāma, a disciple of the śramaṇa Gautama. He is a lay disciple of the śramaṇa Gautama, the most eminent among the laity of Vaiśālī, one who always delights in quietude and praises quietude. The assembly he visits should be a quiet assembly. Therefore, you should be quiet."

Then Mahānāma went to that assembly, to where Pūraṇa was. He exchanged greetings and words of comfort with Pūraṇa, and then sat down to one side. Then Mahānāma said to Pūraṇa: "I have heard that Pūraṇa teaches his disciples the Dharma thus: 'Without cause, without conditions, beings become defiled; without cause, without conditions, beings become purified.' Does such a teaching exist in the world? Is it truly held by you, or are these slanderous words from outsiders, things composed by worldly people? Is it Dharma or non-Dharma? Are there people in the world who jointly debate, question, and censure you about this or not?"

Pūraṇa Kāśyapa said: "Such a teaching truly exists; it is not a false rumor in the world. I established this teaching; it is a teaching in accordance with Dharma. What I teach is all in accord with Dharma. There is no one in the world who comes to debate and censure me about it. Why is this? Mahānāma! I see it thus and teach it thus: 'Without cause, without conditions, beings become defiled; without cause, without conditions, beings become purified.'"

Then Mahānāma, hearing what Pūraṇa said, felt displeased in his mind. He rebuked him, rose from his seat, and departed toward the place of the World-Honored One. He paid homage at the Buddha's feet, then sat down to one side. He then recounted in full to the Buddha the discussion he had had with Pūraṇa.

The Buddha addressed the Licchavi Mahānāma: "That Pūraṇa speaks from his own fancy; it is not worth recording. This Pūraṇa is foolish, undiscerning, unskilful, and speaks without valid basis, saying: 'Without cause, without conditions, beings become defiled; without cause, without conditions, beings become purified.' Why is this? There are causes and conditions for beings becoming defiled; there are causes and conditions for beings becoming purified.

"Mahānāma! What are the causes and conditions for beings becoming defiled? What are the causes and conditions for beings becoming purified? Mahānāma! If form were exclusively suffering, without pleasure, without accompanying pleasure, without being nourished by pleasure, and apart from pleasure, then beings should not, on account of this, give rise to delighted attachment. Mahānāma! Because form is not exclusively suffering, but is pleasurable, accompanied by pleasure, nourished by pleasure, and not apart from pleasure, therefore beings become defiled with attachment to form. Being defiled with attachment, they are bound. Being bound, they have affliction. Mahānāma! If feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness were exclusively suffering, without pleasure, without accompanying pleasure, without being nourished by pleasure, and apart from pleasure, then beings should not, on account of this, give rise to delighted attachment. Mahānāma! Because consciousness is not exclusively suffering, but is pleasurable, accompanied by pleasure, nourished by pleasure, and not apart from pleasure, therefore beings become defiled with attachment to consciousness. Being defiled with attachment, they are bound. Being bound, they have affliction. Mahānāma! This is called having causes and conditions for beings becoming defiled.

"Mahānāma! What are the causes and conditions for beings becoming purified? Mahānāma! If form were exclusively pleasurable, without suffering, without accompanying suffering, without being nourished by sorrow and suffering, and apart from suffering, then beings should not, on account of form, give rise to disenchantment. Mahānāma! Because form is not exclusively pleasurable, but is suffering, accompanied by suffering, nourished by sorrow and suffering, and not apart from suffering, therefore beings become disenchanted with form. Being disenchanted, they find no delight. Finding no delight, they are liberated. Mahānāma! If feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness were exclusively pleasurable, without suffering, without accompanying suffering, without being nourished by sorrow and suffering, and apart from suffering, then beings should not, on account of consciousness, give rise to disenchantment. Mahānāma! Because feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are not exclusively pleasurable, but are suffering, accompanied by suffering, nourished by sorrow and suffering, and not apart from suffering, therefore beings become disenchanted with consciousness. Being disenchanted, they find no delight. Finding no delight, they are liberated. Mahānāma! This is called having causes and conditions for beings becoming purified."

Then Mahānāma, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was delighted. He paid homage to the Buddha and departed.

> Knowing Dharma and the heavy burden;
> Going to visit, observing, desire and passion;
> Arising, and the concise teaching;
> The Dharma seal, and Pūraṇa.

---

## (82)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Ceti, in the Bamboo Grove Monastery.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In what does the well-learned noble disciple see impermanence and suffering?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. We wish that you would explain it! Having heard it, the bhikṣus will practice as taught."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully, reflect well, and I shall explain it for you. The well-learned noble disciple sees impermanence and suffering in form, and sees impermanence and suffering in feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Bhikṣus! Is form permanent or impermanent?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "Impermanent, World-Honored One!"

"Bhikṣus! Is the impermanent suffering?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "It is suffering, World-Honored One!"

"Bhikṣus! If it is impermanent and suffering, a dharma subject to change, would the well-learned noble disciple see in it a self, something other than self, or mutual indwelling?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One!"

"Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. Therefore, bhikṣus! All form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether good or bad, whether far or near, all of it is not self, not other than self, not mutually indwelling. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. The well-learned noble disciple, observing thus, becomes disenchanted with form, disenchanted with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Being disenchanted, one finds no delight. Finding no delight, one is liberated. Being liberated: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know by myself that I will not receive further existence.'"

At that time, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (83)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pool, in the Double-Storied Lecture Hall.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In what does the well-learned noble disciple see not self, not other than self, and not mutually indwelling? How does one, with such equal and right observation, know and see in accordance with reality?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. We wish that you would explain it! Having heard it, the bhikṣus will practice as taught."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully, reflect well, and I shall explain it for you. The well-learned noble disciple sees in form not self, not other than self, and not mutually indwelling. This is called true right observation. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "Is form permanent or impermanent?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "Impermanent, World-Honored One!"

He further addressed the bhikṣus: "If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "It is suffering, World-Honored One!"

"Bhikṣus! If it is impermanent and suffering, a dharma subject to change, would the well-learned noble disciple see in it a self, something other than self, or mutual indwelling?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One!"

"Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. Therefore, bhikṣus! All form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether good or bad, whether far or near, all of it is not self, not other than self, not mutually indwelling. This is called true right observation. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. The well-learned noble disciple, observing thus, attains liberation from form, attains liberation from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. I say that such a one is liberated from birth, old age, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, and distress, the entire great mass of suffering."

When the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (84)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Form is impermanent. What is impermanent is suffering. What is suffering is non-self. What is non-self: all of it is not self, not other than self, not mutually indwelling. Knowing in accordance with reality is called right observation. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. The well-learned noble disciple observes these five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self. Having observed thus, there is nothing whatsoever to grasp at in the world. Having nothing to grasp at, one has nothing to attach to. Having nothing to attach to, one realizes nirvāṇa for oneself

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Sanskrit into Chinese during the Liu Song dynasty by Guṇabhadra.

Without the Chinese source text, I am unable to provide a translation. If you could supply the full Chinese text for the fascicle you would like translated, I will translate it in its entirety.

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099, Fascicle 4

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## (88)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a young brahmin named Uttara came to visit the Buddha. Having exchanged greetings and pleasantries face to face with the World-Honored One, he withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, I always beg for alms in accordance with the Dharma, and use what I obtain to support my father and mother, enabling them to have happiness and be free from suffering. World-Honored One, by acting in this way, do I gain much merit?"

The Buddha told Uttara: "You truly gain much merit. Why is this? If someone begs for alms in accordance with the Dharma and supports their father and mother, causing them to be at ease and happy, and removing their suffering and affliction, they truly gain great merit." At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse:

> "As you toward your father and mother,
> Respectfully cultivate offerings and support,
> In this present life your fame will spread,
> And at life's end you will be born in heaven."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the young man Uttara rejoiced and expressed his approval, made obeisance, and departed.

---

## (89)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a young brahmin named Upaka came to visit the Buddha. Having exchanged greetings and pleasantries face to face with the World-Honored One, he withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, the brahmins always praise the great sacrificial assembly. Does the śramaṇa Gautama also praise the great sacrificial assembly?"

The Buddha told Upaka: "I do not praise them unconditionally. There are some great sacrificial assemblies that may be praised, and there are some great sacrificial assemblies that may not be praised."

Upaka addressed the Buddha: "What kind of great sacrificial assembly may be praised? What kind of great sacrificial assembly may not be praised?"

The Buddha told Upaka: "If at a great sacrificial assembly, herds of young bulls, water-buffalo bulls, water-buffalo cows, and various lambs and small creatures are all bound up and slaughtered, and servants and laborers are coerced and oppressed with suffering, whipped and threatened, weeping and crying out, unhappy and joyless, made to toil in all kinds of misery, such a great sacrificial assembly I do not praise, because it creates great calamity. But if a great assembly does not bind herds of cattle, and so on down to not causing any beings to toil in suffering, such a great sacrificial assembly I do praise, because it does not create great calamity." At that time, the World-Honored One spoke verses:

> "Horse sacrifices and other great assemblies,
> That create various great calamities,
> Such sacrificial assemblies
> The Great Sage does not praise.
>
> Binding up various living beings,
> Killing even the tiniest insects,
> This is not a proper assembly;
> The Great Sage does not approve of it.
>
> If one does not harm living beings
> Or create various calamities,
> Such is called a proper assembly;
> The Great Sage praises it with approval.
>
> Generous giving and cultivating offerings,
> Making a sacrificial assembly in accord with the Dharma,
> The giver with a pure heart,
> And those of pure conduct as excellent fields of merit.
>
> Such a great assembly
> Is an assembly of arhats.
> Such an assembly gains great fruit;
> All the devas rejoice.
>
> Respectfully making invitation oneself,
> Giving with one's own hands,
> Both giver and recipient are purified;
> Such giving gains great fruit.
>
> The wise one giving thus,
> With faith should attain liberation.
> In a world without blame and with happiness,
> The wise one goes to be reborn there."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the brahmin Upaka, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, made obeisance, and departed.

---

## (90)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. Explained in full as above. The difference is that he spoke different verses:

> "Without harmful deeds, without calamities,
> The sacrificial assembly is then pure.
> Practicing in accord with the Dharma,
> Sheltering and protecting those of pure conduct.
>
> Its fragrance returns to the world,
> Surpassing all that is common and lowly.
> The Buddha, regarding what is good in the sacrifice,
> Praises such a sacrificial assembly.
>
> Generous giving and cultivating offerings,
> The sacrifice according to what is fitting,
> With pure faith giving equally,
> Those of pure conduct are excellent fields of merit.
>
> One who makes such offerings
> Is giving to the field of arhats.
> Such vast and great giving
> Is praised by all the devas.
>
> Respectfully making invitation oneself,
> Making offerings with one's own hands,
> Because one equally embraces self and others,
> The sacrificial assembly gains great fruit.
>
> The wise one giving thus,
> With pure faith the mind is liberated.
> In a world without blame,
> The wise one goes to be reborn there."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the brahmin Upaka, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced, made obeisance, and departed.

---

## (91)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a young brahmin named Ujjaka came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, how many dharmas should a layperson living at home practice to attain well-being in this present life and happiness in this present life?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "There are four dharmas by which a layperson living at home attains well-being in the present life and happiness in the present life. What are the four? They are: accomplishment of industriousness, accomplishment of guarding, accomplishment of good friendship, and accomplishment of right livelihood.

"What is accomplishment of industriousness? It means a good man engages in various crafts, trades, and occupations to support his livelihood, such as farming, commerce, serving the king, or clerical work including writing, arithmetic, and drawing. In whichever craft, trade, or occupation he is engaged, he diligently applies himself. This is called accomplishment of industriousness.

"What is accomplishment of guarding? It means a good man, with regard to whatever money and grain he has obtained through his industriousness, produced by his own hands, and obtained in accordance with the Dharma, he is able to guard them well, not allowing them to be seized by kings, robbed by thieves, swept away by floods, consumed by fire, or lost through other calamities; not letting them be lost through poor guarding, taken by those who have no affection for him, or destroyed by various disasters. This is called a good man's good guarding.

"What is accomplishment of good friendship? If a good man has friends who are not dissolute, not heedless, not deceitful, and not dangerous, such friends are able to give good counsel: they prevent sorrows and sufferings that have not yet arisen from arising, and cause sorrows and sufferings that have already arisen to be dispelled. They cause joys and happiness that have not yet arisen to arise quickly, and protect joys and happiness that have already arisen so they are not lost. This is called a good man's accomplishment of good friendship.

"What is accomplishment of right livelihood? It means a good man measures his income and expenditure of money and wealth, managing them in a balanced way, not allowing much income and little expenditure, nor much expenditure and little income. Just as one who holds a balance scale adds when it is light and removes when it is heavy, knowing when it is level and then setting it down, so too a good man measures his wealth, keeping income and expenditure equal, not allowing income to greatly exceed expenditure or expenditure to greatly exceed income. If a good man has no money or wealth yet spends lavishly, people will all call him an udumbara fruit, without seed, foolish and greedy, heedless of the future. Or if a good man has abundant wealth but cannot use it for food and needs, people around will say he is a fool, like a dog that starves to death. Therefore, a good man who can measure his wealth, keeping income and expenditure equal, is called one accomplished in right livelihood. Thus, brahmin, when these four dharmas are accomplished, there is well-being in the present life and happiness in the present life."

The brahmin addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, how many dharmas does a layperson living at home have that can bring well-being in the next life and happiness in the next life?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "A layperson living at home has four dharmas that can bring well-being in the next life and happiness in the next life. What are the four? They are: accomplishment of faith, accomplishment of morality, accomplishment of generosity, and accomplishment of wisdom.

"What is accomplishment of faith? It means a good man has obtained a mind of faith and reverence toward the Tathāgata, establishing the root of faith, which cannot be destroyed by any devas, Māra, Brahmā, or other worldly beings of the same class. This is called a good man's accomplishment of faith.

"What is accomplishment of morality? It means a good man does not kill living beings, does not steal, does not engage in sexual misconduct, does not speak falsehood, and does not drink intoxicants. This is called accomplishment of morality.

"What is accomplishment of generosity? It means a good man, free from the stain of miserliness in his heart, while living as a householder, practices the giving of liberation, always giving with his own hands, delighting in cultivating relinquishment, and giving with an impartial mind. This is called a good man's accomplishment of generosity.

"What is accomplishment of wisdom? It means a good man truly knows the noble truth of suffering, and truly knows the noble truths of its arising, its cessation, and the path. This is called a good man's accomplishment of wisdom. If a good man living at home practices these four dharmas, he can attain well-being in the next life and happiness in the next life."

At that time, the World-Honored One further spoke verses:

> "Through industriousness one establishes various enterprises,
> Accumulates and is able to guard them.
> A good man with good friends
> Sustains himself with right livelihood.
>
> Pure faith and morality accomplished,
> Generous giving, free from the stain of miserliness,
> Purifying the swift path,
> One attains well-being and happiness in the next life.
>
> If one dwelling as a householder
> Accomplishes these eight dharmas,
> As truthfully declared by the Honored One,
> As known by the Perfectly Enlightened One,
>
> In the present life one attains peace and security,
> In the present life one dwells in joy and happiness,
> And in the next life one dwells in joy and happiness."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, Ujjaka, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, made obeisance, and departed.

---

## (92)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kosala, and arrived at Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, in Śrāvastī there was an arrogant brahmin who dwelled there. His father's and mother's lineages were both pure, without any blemish that anyone could point to, pure through seven generations of ancestors. He was a teacher of brahmins, accomplished in discourse, fully versed in all the treatises and records, understanding all the terms, able to distinguish the superior and inferior of the Dharma, clear in all its aspects, explaining each phrase and sentence, and handsome in appearance. He was proud of his birth, proud of his clan, proud of his beauty, proud of his intelligence, and proud of his wealth. He showed no respect to his father and mother, or to his elders and teachers. He heard that the śramaṇa Gautama was traveling among the people of Kosala and had arrived at Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. Having heard this, he had this thought: "I shall go to visit the śramaṇa Gautama. If he has something to say, I will debate with him; if he has nothing to say, I will return in silence."

Then the arrogant brahmin mounted his white horse-drawn chariot, with young brahmins leading and following as attendants, holding a golden-handled parasol and carrying a golden pitcher, and went to see the World-Honored One. Arriving at the garden gate, he dismounted and proceeded on foot.

At that time, the World-Honored One, surrounded by a great assembly, was teaching the Dharma, and did not turn to look at the arrogant brahmin.

Then the arrogant brahmin had this thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama does not pay me any attention. I should just go back."

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing the arrogant brahmin's thought, spoke a verse:

> "Having come here in arrogance,
> It is not good to further increase pride.
> Since you came for the sake of meaning,
> You should instead increase that meaning."

Then the arrogant brahmin had this thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." He wished to pay his respects with a bow.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the arrogant brahmin: "Stop! Stop! There is no need to bow. That your mind is purified is already sufficient."
