Then the great assembly all raised their voices and proclaimed: "How extraordinary! The World-Honored One is of great virtue and great power. This arrogant brahmin, relying on his pride of birth, pride of clan, pride of beauty, pride of intelligence, and pride of wealth, shows no respect to his father and mother, or to his elders and teachers. Yet now, before the śramaṇa Gautama, he is humble and lowly, wishing to bow at his feet!"

Then the arrogant brahmin, before the great assembly, called for silence and spoke this verse:

> "How does one not give rise to pride?
> How does one give rise to respect?
> How does one give good counsel?
> How does one make good offerings?"

At that time, the World-Honored One answered in verse:

> "Toward father, mother, and elder brothers,
> Preceptors and various teachers,
> And all who are worthy of reverence,
> One should not give rise to pride.
>
> One should respectfully honor them,
> Humbly make inquiry and greeting,
> Wholeheartedly attend and serve them,
> And also provide various offerings.
>
> Those free from greed, hatred, and delusion,
> Arhats whose defilements are exhausted,
> With right knowledge, well liberated,
> Before whom all pride is subdued:
>
> To these noble and worthy ones,
> With palms joined, one should bow one's head in homage."

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the arrogant brahmin the Dharma in various ways, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him. As is the manner of the Buddha, the World-Honored One taught the Dharma in sequence: teaching about giving, upholding precepts, and the merits of rebirth in heaven; about the flavor, danger, and affliction of sensual desire; and about purity, renunciation, detachment, and the various factors of purity, explaining them extensively. Just as a clean white cloth, free of any dark stains, readily receives dye, so too the arrogant brahmin, right there on his seat, comprehended the Four Noble Truths of suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the path, and attained the realization without interval.

Then the arrogant brahmin, having seen the Dharma, obtained the Dharma, known the Dharma, entered the Dharma, crossed beyond all doubts, not depending on another for crossing, gained fearlessness in the true Dharma. He immediately rose from his seat, arranged his garments, made obeisance to the Buddha, and with palms joined addressed the Buddha: "May I now be permitted to go forth and receive full ordination in the true Dharma?"

The Buddha told the arrogant brahmin: "You may now go forth and receive full ordination in the true Dharma."

He then went forth and, dwelling alone in right contemplation, reflected: "It is for this reason that a good man shaves off his hair and beard, dons the kāṣāya robe, and with right faith leaves the household to study the Way." He attained arhatship, and his mind was well liberated.

---

## (93)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kosala, and arrived at Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a brahmin named Dīrghanakha who was preparing a great sacrificial assembly. He had seven hundred bulls lined up and tied to posts, along with bulls, cows, water buffaloes, and various lambs and small creatures, all bound. He prepared various foods and drinks, distributing them widely. Various non-Buddhist practitioners from many countries all came and gathered at the place of the sacrificial assembly.

Then the brahmin Dīrghanakha heard that the śramaṇa Gautama was traveling among the people of Kosala and had arrived at Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. He had this thought: "I am now preparing a great sacrificial assembly, with seven hundred bulls lined up and tied to posts, and so on down to the smallest creatures, all bound, for the sake of this great sacrificial assembly, and various practitioners from many countries have come to the assembly place. I should now go to the śramaṇa Gautama and ask about the dharma of the sacrificial assembly, so that in preparing my great sacrificial assembly there will be no deficiency in any of the required portions." Having thought this, he mounted his white horse-drawn chariot, with young brahmins leading and following as attendants, holding a golden-handled parasol, carrying a golden water pitcher, departed from Śrāvastī city, and went to the World-Honored One, respectfully attending upon him. Arriving at the gate of the monastery, he dismounted and proceeded on foot. Coming before the Buddha, he exchanged greetings and pleasantries face to face, then withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, I now wish to perform a great sacrificial assembly. I have seven hundred bulls lined up and tied to posts, and so on down to the smallest creatures, all bound, for the sake of this great sacrificial assembly. Various practitioners from many countries have all come to the assembly place. I have also heard that Gautama has been traveling among the people of Kosala and has arrived at Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. I have therefore come specifically to ask Gautama about the dharma of the great sacrificial assembly and the portions of the various items, so that in preparing my great sacrificial assembly there will be no deficiency in any of the required portions."

The Buddha told the brahmin: "There are some great sacrificial assembly hosts who, in performing giving and making merit, actually generate sin, being cut and carved by three swords and knives, and receive unwholesome results. What are the three? They are the sword of the mind, the sword of the mouth, and the sword of the body.

"What is the sword of the mind that produces painful results? A certain assembly host prepares a great assembly and thinks thus: 'For my great sacrificial assembly, I shall slaughter such-and-such a number of young strong bulls, such-and-such a number of water-buffalo bulls and cows, such-and-such a number of lambs and various small creatures.' This is called the sword of the mind that produces painful results. Although such an assembly host intends various kinds of giving and various kinds of offerings, he actually generates sin.

"What is the sword of the mouth that produces painful results? A certain assembly host prepares a great assembly and gives this command: 'I am now performing a great sacrificial assembly. You shall slaughter such-and-such a number of young strong bulls, and so on down to such-and-such a number of tiny creatures.' This is called the sword of the mouth that produces painful results. Although the great assembly host performs such giving and offerings, he actually generates sin.

"What is the sword of the body that produces painful results? A certain great assembly host prepares a great assembly and with his own hands injures and slaughters such-and-such a number of bulls, and so on down to killing various small creatures. This is called the sword of the body that produces painful results. Although the great assembly host intends various kinds of giving and various kinds of offerings, he actually generates sin.

"However, brahmin, one should diligently tend to three fires, respecting them at the proper times, making obeisance, attending upon them, and providing them with comfort and happiness. What are the three? The first is the root fire, the second is the household fire, and the third is the field of merit fire.

"What is the root fire, which one should respectfully attend upon, make offerings to, and provide with comfort and happiness at the proper times? It means a good man obtains wealth through industriousness, through the toil of hands and feet, obtained in accordance with the Dharma, and uses it to support his father and mother, causing them to be at ease and happy. This is called the root fire. Why is it called the root? Because the good man was born from them, namely his father and mother, therefore they are called the root. A good man, out of reverence for this root, at the proper times respectfully attends upon, makes offerings, and provides comfort and happiness.

"What is the household fire, which a good man nurtures at the proper times and provides with comfort and happiness? It means a good man obtains wealth through industriousness, through the toil of hands and feet, obtained in accordance with the Dharma, and uses it to provide for his wife and children, kinsmen and relatives, servants and hired laborers, giving to them at the proper times, respectfully providing comfort and security. This is called the household fire. Why is it called the household? Because the good man dwells in a household; in happiness they share the happiness, in suffering they share the suffering, and in all activities they accord with one another. Therefore it is called the household. Therefore a good man provides for them at the proper times, giving comfort and happiness.

"What is called the field fire, which a good man at the proper times respectfully honors, reveres, makes offerings to, and provides with comfort and happiness? It means a good man obtains wealth through industriousness, through the toil of hands and feet, obtained in accordance with the Dharma, and uses it to attend upon and make offerings to various śramaṇas and brahmins who are well able to tame greed, hatred, and delusion. Such śramaṇas and brahmins establish fields of merit, to be honored and advanced toward, as shares of happiness and rewards of happiness, for future rebirth in heaven. This is called the field fire. Why is it called a field? Because it is a field of merit for the world, worthy of offerings. Therefore it is called a field. A good man at the proper times respectfully attends upon, makes offerings, and provides comfort and happiness." At that time, the World-Honored One further spoke verses:

> "The root, the household,
> And the worthy field of merit fire:
> These fires, when offerings are increased,
> Bring sufficiency, peace, and happiness.
>
> In a world without blame and with happiness,
> The wise one goes to be reborn there.
> With wealth obtained in accordance with the Dharma,
> One supports those who should be supported.
>
> Because one supports those who should be supported,
> One is reborn in heaven and gains good renown.

"However, brahmin, the good man should first extinguish and put out the three fires that were previously tended. What are the three? They are the fire of greed, the fire of hatred, and the fire of delusion. Why is this? If the fire of greed is not cut off and not extinguished, one harms oneself, harms others, and harms both self and others; one incurs blame in the present life, incurs blame in future lives, and incurs blame in both present and future lives; and because of it one's mind generates the suffering of mental states. The fire of hatred and the fire of delusion are likewise. Brahmin, if a good man tends a fire of stacked firewood, he toils at the proper times, lights it at the proper times, and extinguishes it at the proper times, yet because of the fire he undergoes suffering."

At that time, the brahmin Dīrghanakha sat in silence.

At that time, there was a brahmin's son named Uttara sitting in the assembly.

After a moment of silent reflection, the brahmin Dīrghanakha told Uttara: "Can you go to the sacrificial assembly place and release the bulls tied to the posts and all the living beings that are bound? Release them all! And tell them: 'The brahmin Dīrghanakha says to you: Go freely as you please. In the mountains, marshes, and open wilderness, eat uncut grass, drink from pure flowing water, and enjoy happiness in the winds from the four directions.'"

Uttara replied: "I shall follow the great teacher's instruction!" He immediately went to the sacrificial assembly place and released all the living beings, telling them: "The brahmin Dīrghanakha says to you: Go freely as you please, to the mountains, marshes, and open wilderness; drink water, eat grass, and enjoy the breezes from the four directions."

At that time, the World-Honored One knew of Uttara's action. Knowing this, he taught the Dharma in various ways for the brahmin Dīrghanakha, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him. As is the custom, the World-Honored One taught the Dharma in proper sequence: teaching about morality, about giving, about the merits of rebirth in heaven, about the flavor and danger of sensual desire, about renunciation, purity, and the purification of afflictions, revealing and displaying them. Just as a fresh, clean white cloth readily receives dye, so too the brahmin Dīrghanakha, right there on his seat, saw the Four Noble Truths and attained the realization without interval.

Then the brahmin Dīrghanakha, having seen the Dharma, obtained the Dharma, known the Dharma, entered the Dharma, crossed beyond all doubts, not depending on another for crossing, gained fearlessness in the true Dharma. He immediately rose from his seat, arranged his garments, bared his right shoulder, and with palms joined addressed the Buddha: "I have crossed over, World-Honored One! From this day forward, for the rest of my life, I take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, and take refuge in the Sangha of bhikṣus. I am a lay disciple. Please acknowledge me. I only wish that the World-Honored One and the great assembly accept a meal from me." At that time, the World-Honored One silently consented.

Then the brahmin Dīrghanakha, knowing the Buddha had accepted the invitation, made obeisance to the Buddha, circumambulated him three times to the right, and departed. The brahmin Dīrghanakha returned to the sacrificial assembly place and prepared various pure, fine, and excellent offerings. He arranged seats and sent a messenger to invite the Buddha, saying: "The time has come; may the Sage know it is time."

At that time, the World-Honored One put on his robes and took his bowl, and surrounded by the great assembly, went to the brahmin Dīrghanakha's assembly place and took his seat before the great assembly.

Then the brahmin Dīrghanakha, knowing the World-Honored One was settled in his seat, personally served various foods and drinks with his own hands. After the meal was finished, the washing, rinsing, and cleaning of the bowl were completed. He then separately spread a low seat and, before the great assembly, sat upright to listen to the Dharma.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the Dharma in various ways for the brahmin Dīrghanakha, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him, and then rose from his seat and departed.

---

## (94)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a young brahmin named Saṅkara came to visit the Buddha. Having exchanged greetings and pleasantries face to face with the World-Honored One, he withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, how can an unwholesome man be recognized?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "He is like the moon."

The brahmin further asked: "How can a wholesome man be recognized?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "He is like the moon."

The brahmin addressed the Buddha: "How is an unwholesome man like the moon?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "Just as the moon in its dark fortnight loses its brightness, loses its color, and loses what it is conjoined with, waning day and night, until it is no longer visible. So too, there are people who, toward the Tathāgata, attain a mind of faith for the household, receive and uphold pure precepts, study extensively and learn much, diminish themselves in generosity, and have right view that is true and real. Having attained pure faith toward the Tathāgata, upholding precepts, generosity, much learning, and right view that is true and straight, they then regress and lose it. Precepts, learning, generosity, and right view are all forgotten, waning day and night, until in an instant everything is forgotten.

"Furthermore, brahmin, if a good man does not associate with good friends, does not frequently hear the Dharma, does not engage in right reflection, and engages in evil conduct of body, evil conduct of speech, and evil conduct of mind, then because of these evil causes and conditions, when the body breaks up and life ends, he falls into the evil destinies and hell. Thus, brahmin, an unwholesome man is likened to the moon."

The brahmin addressed the Buddha: "How is a wholesome man likened to the moon?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "Just as the bright moon in its bright fortnight increases in brightness and radiance day and night, until it reaches the full moon, completely round and pure. So too, a good man, toward the Tathāgata's Dharma and Vinaya, attains a mind of pure faith, and so on up to right view that is true, pure, and increasingly bright. Precepts increase, generosity increases, learning increases, wisdom increases, increasing day and night. Furthermore, at other times he draws near to good friends, hears the true Dharma explained, engages in right internal reflection, practices good conduct of body, practices good conduct of speech, and practices good conduct of mind. Because of these causes and conditions, when the body breaks up and life ends, he is born by transformation in the heavens. Brahmin, therefore a wholesome man is likened to the moon."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse:

> "Just as the stainless moon
> Travels through the open sky,
> Among all the lesser stars,
> Its light is the most brilliant.
>
> Pure faith is likewise:
> With precepts, learning, and generosity free from miserliness,
> In this miserly world,
> Such generosity is exceptionally radiant."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the brahmin Saṅkara, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

## (95)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a brahmin named Śrutaśravas came to visit the Buddha. Having exchanged greetings and pleasantries face to face with the World-Honored One, he withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, I have heard that Gautama says: 'One should give only to me and not to others. Giving to me yields great fruit, but giving to others does not yield great fruit. One should give to my disciples and not to the disciples of others. Giving to my disciples yields great fruit, but giving to the disciples of others does not yield great fruit.' Gautama, do those who say this speak truthfully? Or are they slandering Gautama? Do they speak in accordance with what was said, in accordance with the Dharma? Do they speak according to the proper sequence of the Dharma, so that no one else can come with the same Dharma to reprove them?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "Those who say such things are slandering me! They do not speak in accordance with what was said, nor in accordance with the Dharma, nor according to the proper sequence of the Dharma, and they invite reproof from others using the same Dharma. Why is this? I do not say: 'One should give only to me and not to others. Giving to me yields great fruit, but giving to others does not yield great fruit. One should give to my disciples. Giving to my disciples yields great fruit, but giving to the disciples of others does not yield great fruit.' However, brahmin, were I to say such a thing, I would create two kinds of obstruction: obstructing the giver's giving, and obstructing the recipient's benefit. Brahmin, even if a man pours his leftover washing water onto clean ground, enabling the beings in that place to receive benefit and happiness, I say that even this enters the gate of merit, how much more so giving to people? However, brahmin, I further say that giving to those who uphold the precepts yields results that are not the same as giving to those who violate the precepts."

The brahmin Śrutaśravas addressed the Buddha, saying: "So it is, Gautama! I also say the same: giving to those who uphold the precepts yields great fruit, not the same as giving to those who violate the precepts."

At that time, the World-Honored One further spoke verses:

> "Whether black or white,
> Whether red or of other colors,
> Mottled, golden-colored,
> Pure yellow, or dove-colored,
> Among such cows,
> Those that bear beautiful calves,
> Strong, with full power,
> Well-trained, moving swiftly,
> As long as they can bear heavy loads,
> One does not ask about their color of birth.
>
> People are also like this:
> Each born in their respective station,
> Kṣatriya, brahmin,
> Vaiśya, śūdra,
> Caṇḍāla, or the lowly born,
> Their births are all different.
> But so long as one upholds pure precepts,
> Free from the heavy burden of afflictions,
> Purely cultivating the holy life,
> An arhat whose defilements are exhausted,
> A Sugata in the world,
> Giving to such a one yields great fruit.
>
> The foolish, lacking wisdom,
> Who have never heard the true Dharma,
> Giving to them does not yield great fruit,
> Because they do not associate with good friends.
> But if one associates with good friends,
> The Tathāgata and his śrāvakas,
> With pure faith in the Sugata,
> One's roots grow firm and strong.
>
> The good destinies where one may dwell,
> And birth in families of great lineage,
> And the ultimate attainment of parinirvāṇa:
> This is what the Great Sage says."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the brahmin Śrutaśravas, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, made obeisance, and departed.

---

## (96)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, in the early morning, the World-Honored One put on his robes and took his bowl to enter Śrāvastī city to beg for alms. At that time, there was an old brahmin, aged and with faculties matured, leaning on a staff and carrying a bowl, going from house to house begging for alms.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the brahmin: "Why is it that you, now aged with matured faculties, lean on a staff and carry a bowl, going from house to house begging for alms?" The brahmin addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, all the wealth and property in my household I gave to my son, and I arranged a wife for him. After that, I was cast out from the home. Therefore I lean on a staff and carry a bowl, going from house to house begging for alms."

The Buddha told the brahmin: "Can you receive and memorize one verse from me, and then return to the assembly of your household and recite it to your son?"

The brahmin addressed the Buddha: "I can receive it, Gautama."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "When a son was born, the heart rejoiced,
> For the son one gathered wealth,
> And also arranged a wife for him,
> Then oneself was cast out from home.
>
> This rustic bumpkin of a son
> Has gone against and betrayed his father.
> With a human form but a rākṣasa's heart,
> He has abandoned the honored elder.
>
> Like an old horse no longer of use,
> He seizes away my grain and food.
> The son is young while the father is old,
> Going from house to house begging for food.
>
> My crooked staff is the most excellent,
> Far more kind than a son.
> For me it fends off dangerous bulls,
> Keeps me safe on perilous ground,
> Can drive away ferocious dogs,
> Supports me walking in dark places,
> Helps me avoid deep pits, empty wells,
> And thickets of grass, wood, and thorns.
> Relying on the staff's majestic power,
> I stand firm and do not fall."

At that time, the brahmin, having received this verse from the World-Honored One, returned to the great assembly of brahmins and recited it for his son. He first addressed the great assembly: "Listen to what I have to say." Then he recited the verse as fully stated above. His son, ashamed and frightened, immediately embraced his father and took him back into the home. He bathed his body, washed him, dressed him in fine blue garments, and established him as the head of the household.

Then the brahmin had this thought: "I have now regained my distinguished clan status. This is the grace of the śramaṇa Gautama. As stated in my scriptures: 'One who serves as a teacher should be honored as a teacher; one who serves as a preceptor should be honored as a preceptor.' All that I have obtained is through the power of the śramaṇa Gautama. He is my teacher. I should now offer the most excellent and finest garment to Gautama."

Then the brahmin, carrying the finest garment, went to the World-Honored One's place. Having exchanged greetings and pleasantries face to face, he withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, my household has now been restored. This is through Gautama's power. As recorded in my scriptures: 'One who serves as a teacher should be honored as a teacher; one who serves as a preceptor should be honored as a preceptor.' Today Gautama is my teacher. Please accept this garment, out of compassion!"

The World-Honored One accepted it, out of compassion.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the Dharma in various ways for the brahmin, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him.

The brahmin, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, made obeisance, and departed.

---

## (97)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, in the early morning, the World-Honored One put on his robes and took his bowl to enter Śrāvastī city to beg for alms.

At that time, there was an old brahmin, aged and with faculties matured, carrying a staff and holding a bowl, going from house to house begging for alms. That brahmin saw the World-Honored One from a distance and had this thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama carries a staff and holds a bowl, going from house to house begging for alms. I also carry a staff and hold a bowl, going from house to house begging for alms. Both Gautama and I are bhikṣus."

At that time, the World-Honored One answered in verse:

> "What is called a bhikṣu
> Is not merely one who begs for alms.
> If one takes up the ways of a householder,
> How can one be called a bhikṣu?
>
> One who, regarding both merit and fault,
> Has set them both aside and cultivates the right practice,
> Whose mind is without any fear:
> This is what is called a bhikṣu."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, that brahmin, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, made obeisance, and departed.

---

## (98)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kosala and arrived at a village called Ekanāḷa, dwelling in the Ekanāḷa forest.

At that time, the World-Honored One put on his robes and took his bowl to enter Ekanāḷa village to beg for alms. He had this thought: "It is still too early today. I should now pass by the place where the plowing brahmin Kasi Bhāradvāja is preparing food."

At that time, the plowing brahmin Kasi Bhāradvāja was plowing with five hundred plows and preparing food. Then the plowing brahmin Kasi Bhāradvāja saw the World-Honored One from afar and said: "Gautama, I now plow the fields and sow seeds in order to provide food. The śramaṇa Gautama should also plow the fields and sow seeds in order to provide food."

The Buddha told the brahmin: "I also plow the fields and sow seeds in order to provide food."

The brahmin addressed the Buddha: "I do not at all see the śramaṇa Gautama with any plow, yoke, harness, reins, plowshare, or goad. Yet now Gautama says: 'I also plow the fields and sow seeds in order to provide food.'"

At that time, the plowing brahmin Kasi Bhāradvāja spoke a verse:

> "You claim to be a plower,
> Yet I do not see you plowing.
> Explain plowing to me,
> So that I may know the method of plowing."

At that time, the World-Honored One answered in verse:

> "Faith is the seed,
> Austerity is the timely rain,
> Wisdom is the yoke,
> And a sense of shame is the shaft.
>
> Right mindfulness guards and protects;
> This is the skilled charioteer.
> Guarding the deeds of body and speech,
> Knowing moderation in food is the inner storehouse.
>
> Truthfulness is the true vehicle,
> Joyful abiding is the resting place.
> Diligent effort plows the fallow field,
> Proceeding safely and swiftly.
>
> Going straight without turning back,
> One arrives at the place free from sorrow.
> One who plows in this way
> Attains the fruit of the deathless nectar.
> One who plows in this way
> Does not return to any state of existence."

At that time, the plowing brahmin Kasi Bhāradvāja addressed the Buddha, saying: "Excellent plowing, Gautama! Supreme plowing, Gautama!" Thereupon the plowing brahmin Kasi Bhāradvāja, having heard the World-Honored One's verse, felt his faith greatly increase, and he offered a full bowl of fragrant and delicious food to the World-Honored One. The World-Honored One did not accept it, because it was obtained through speaking a verse. He then spoke a verse:

> "Not because of speaking the Dharma
> Should one accept that food and eat."

This was explained extensively, as previously stated in full for the fire-worshipping brahmin.

At that time, the plowing brahmin Kasi Bhāradvāja addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, where should I now place this food?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "I do not see among all devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brahmins, gods, or worldly beings anyone who could eat this food and have it nourish their body. Brahmin, you should take this food and place it in water without insects, or on ground with little living grass."

Then the brahmin took the food and placed it in water without insects. The water immediately smoked, surged, and boiled, hissing and sizzling. Just as a heated iron ball cast into cold water hisses and sizzles, so too when that food was placed in the water without insects, it smoked, surged, and boiled, hissing and sizzling.

Then the brahmin had this thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is truly extraordinary! Of great virtue and great power, to cause food to undergo such a miraculous transformation."

At that time, that brahmin, seeing the miraculous sign of the food, felt his faith further increase. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, may I now be permitted to go forth in the true Dharma and receive full ordination?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "You may now go forth in the true Dharma, receive full ordination, and attain the portion of a bhikṣu." Having gone forth, he dwelt alone in quiet contemplation, reflecting: "It is for this reason that a son of good family shaves off his hair and beard, dons the kāṣāya robe, and with right faith leaves the household to study the Way." And so on, until he attained arhatship, and his mind was well liberated.

---

## (99)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha.

At that time, there was a venerable one named Suddhideva, who was traveling among the people of Videha and arrived at the Āmra Garden in the city of Mithilā. The venerable Suddhideva, in the early morning, put on his robes and took his bowl to enter Mithilā city to beg for alms. Begging in sequential order, he came to his own former family home. At that time, Suddhideva's mother, who was old, was in the central hall holding food and making offerings to the sacrificial fire, seeking rebirth in the Brahma heaven. She did not notice that the venerable Suddhideva was standing outside the door.

At that time, Vaiśravaṇa, the Heavenly King, had extremely deep faith and reverence toward the venerable Suddhideva. At that time, Vaiśravaṇa, the Heavenly King, with yakṣas leading and following his retinue, was traveling through the air. He saw the venerable Suddhideva standing outside the door. He also saw his mother holding food in her hands, in the central hall making offerings to the sacrificial fire, not seeing her son standing outside the door. Seeing this, he descended from the sky, came before Suddhideva's mother, and spoke this verse:

> "This brahmin woman,
> Brahma heaven is extremely far away.
> Seeking to be born there,
> You make offerings to this sacrificial fire.
>
> This is not the path to Brahma heaven.
> Why do you sacrifice here in vain?
> You, brahmin woman,
> Suddhideva stands at the door.
>
> His defilements are utterly exhausted;
> He is a deva among devas.
> Possessing nothing at all,
> Alone, not relying on another,
>
> He has entered this house to beg for food.
> He is the one to whom offerings should be made.
> Suddhideva has well cultivated his person;
> He is an excellent field of merit for humans and devas.
>
> Far removed from all evils,
> Not stained by any defilement,
> His virtue is equal to Brahmā's,
> Though his form abides in the human world.
>
> Not attached to any dharma,
> Like a thoroughly matured dragon,
> The bhikṣu abides in right mindfulness,
> His mind well liberated.
>
> To him one should offer the first food;
> He is the supreme field of merit.
> With a mind of right faith,
> One should swiftly give at the right time.
>
> One should establish a refuge in advance,
> To bring happiness in the future.
> Behold this sage
> Who has already crossed the ocean of suffering.
>
> Therefore, with a mind of faith,
> One should swiftly give at the right time.
> One should establish a refuge in advance,
> To bring happiness in the future."
>
> Vaiśravaṇa, the Heavenly King,
> Awakened and inspired her to give.

At that time, the venerable Suddhideva taught the Dharma in various ways for his mother, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting her. Then he went on his way again.

---

## (100)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain brahmin came to visit the Buddha. Having exchanged greetings and pleasantries face to face, he withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, what is called 'Buddha'? What does 'Buddha' mean? Is it a name given by one's father and mother? Or is it a name given by brahmins?" Then the brahmin spoke this verse:

> "The name 'Buddha' in this world
> Is a supreme name of one who crosses over.
> Was it father and mother who established
> This name of 'Buddha'?"

At that time, the World-Honored One answered in verse:

> "The Buddha sees the past,
> And likewise sees the future,
> And also sees the present world:
> The arising and ceasing of all conditioned processes.
>
> What bright wisdom has fully understood,
> What should be cultivated has been cultivated,
> What should be abandoned has all been abandoned:
> Therefore he is called 'Buddha.'
>
> Through countless kalpas of searching and choosing,
> Only suffering without momentary happiness,
> All that is born is utterly destroyed.
> Distanced from them, at rest, with dust and defilement stilled,
>
> The roots of all the barbs of latent tendencies uprooted,
> Perfectly and equally awakened: therefore called 'Buddha.'"

After the Buddha spoke this verse, that brahmin, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

## (101)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kosala. Between the village of Kāṭi and the village of Dākula, he sat beneath a tree and entered a midday meditative attainment.

At that time, a brahmin of the Doṇa clan was walking along that road, following behind the Buddha. He saw the Buddha's footprints, marked with the thousand-spoked wheel design, the impressions clearly visible, with even spokes and round rims, replete with all excellent marks. Seeing them, he had this thought: "I have never seen footprints like this among humans. I should now follow these tracks to find this person." He then followed the footprints until he reached the Buddha. He came and saw the World-Honored One seated beneath a tree, having entered a midday meditative attainment. His majestic countenance was unmatched in the world, his faculties were clear and still, his mind was tranquil and settled, supremely tamed, accomplished in right contemplation, his radiant form imposing and magnificent, like a mountain of gold. Seeing this, he said: "Are you a deva?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "I am not a deva."

"Are you a nāga, a yakṣa, a gandharva, an asura, a garuḍa, a kiṃnara, a mahoraga, a human, or a non-human?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "I am not a nāga, nor any of the others down to human or non-human."

The brahmin addressed the Buddha: "If you say you are not a deva, not a nāga, and so on down to not human and not non-human, then what are you?"

At that time, the World-Honored One answered in verse:

> "Devas, nāgas, gandharvas,
> Kiṃnaras, yakṣas,
> Unwholesome asuras,
> And the various mahoragas,
>
> Humans and non-humans:
> All arise from afflictions.
> Such afflictions and outflows
> I have entirely abandoned.
>
> They have been shattered and ground to dust.
> Like a puṇḍarīka lotus
> That, though born in water,
> Has never been wetted by the water,
>
> Though I am born in the world,
> I am not attached to the world.
> Through countless kalpas of constant choosing,
> Only suffering without momentary happiness,
>
> All conditioned formations
> Being subject to arising and ceasing,
> Free from defilement, unshaken,
> Having uprooted all sword-like barbs,
>
> The matter of birth and death ultimately ended:
> Therefore I am called 'Buddha.'"

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the brahmin of the Doṇa clan, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, and departed along the road.

---

## (102)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, in the early morning, the World-Honored One put on his robes and took his bowl to enter Rājagṛha city to beg for alms. Begging in sequential order, he came to the house of the brahmin Bhāradvāja who worshipped fire.

At that time, the brahmin was holding a wooden ladle, with various foods and drinks laid out, equipped with fire-worship implements, standing by the door. He saw the Buddha coming from afar. Seeing him, he addressed the Buddha with these words: "Stop! Stop! Outcaste! Do not come near my door!"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "Do you know what an outcaste is, and what the dharma of an outcaste is?"

The brahmin said: "I do not know what an outcaste is, nor do I know the dharma of an outcaste. Does the śramaṇa Gautama know what an outcaste is, and the dharma of an outcaste?"

The Buddha said: "I well know what an outcaste is, and the dharma of an outcaste."

At that time, the brahmin immediately set aside his fire-worship implements, quickly spread a seat, and invited the Buddha to sit. He said: "Gautama, please explain to me what an outcaste is, and the dharma of an outcaste."

The Buddha then took the seat and spoke in verse:

> "One whose angry mind harbors resentment,
> Who conceals and hides his faults and misdeeds,
> Who violates the precepts and holds wrong views,
> Who is false and not truthful:
> Know that such a person is an outcaste.
>
> One who is cruel, greedy, and stingy,
> Of evil desires, miserly, deceitful, and false,
> Without a sense of shame or conscience:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> Whether born once or born twice,
> He kills and harms all beings,
> Without any mind of compassion:
> This one is an outcaste.
>
> If one kills, binds, beats, and strikes
> In villages and cities,
> Oppressing without justice through harsh punishment:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> Whether dwelling at home or on the road,
> Being the leader of a group,
> Harshly oppressing those beneath him,
> Intimidating and coercing them,
> Taking profit to serve himself:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> In villages and empty places,
> Whether things have an owner or not,
> Seizing and taking them for oneself:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> One who abandons and slights his own wife,
> Yet does not visit brothels,
> But violates another's beloved:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> Among inner and outer relatives,
> And intimate good friends,
> One who violates what they hold dear:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> One who speaks falsely and deceives others,
> Fraudulently obtaining goods without evidence,
> And when others ask for repayment, does not return it:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> Whether for oneself or for others,
> Lending and borrowing or giving wealth,
> Or going along with another's words,
> Bearing false witness for another:
> Such a false speaker,
> Know that this one is an outcaste.
>
> One who commits evil, unwholesome deeds
> Where no one knows,
> Concealing and hiding his evil:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> If someone asks him about meaning
> And he answers with what is not meaning,
> Misleading and deceiving people with distortions:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> One who in truth has nothing at all,
> Yet denigrates the wise,
> Out of foolishness and desire for gain:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> One who is haughty and praises himself,
> While destroying and demeaning others:
> This is the most base and lowly pride;
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> One who creates misdeeds himself,
> Then shifts the blame to falsely accuse others,
> Speaking falsehood to slander the pure:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> One who previously received benefits from another,
> And when that person comes to visit him,
> Has no mind to repay with respect:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> When śramaṇas and brahmins
> Come in accordance with the Dharma to beg,
> He scolds them and gives nothing:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> If one's father and mother are aged,
> Their youthful vigor already gone,
> And one does not diligently attend and nourish them:
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> Toward father, mother, and various elders,
> Brothers and close relatives,
> One who is truly not an arhat
> Yet displays the virtue of an arhat:
> He is a great thief of the world;
> Know that such a one is an outcaste.
>
> One born into an upper caste,
> Who studies the brahminical scriptures,
> Yet in the course of his life
> Practices various evil deeds:
> His high birth does not prevent
> Being reproved and falling into evil destinies.
> In the present life he receives reproof,
> And in the next life falls into evil destinies.
>
> One born into a caṇḍāla family,
> Known in the world as Sudāyi,
> Whose fame spreads throughout the world,
> What caṇḍālas have never achieved:
> Honored and served by
> Brahmins and kṣatriyas of great families.
>
> Riding upon the pure path to heaven,
> Dwelling in equanimity and uprightness,
> Birth does not prevent
> One from being born in the Brahma heaven.
> In the present life one gains good renown,
> In the next life one is born in good destinies.
>
> Twice-born ones, you should know,
> As I have revealed and shown:
> It is not because of one's birth
> That one is called an outcaste.
> It is not because of one's birth
> That one is called a brahmin.
> It is by one's deeds that one is an outcaste;
> It is by one's deeds that one is a brahmin."

The brahmin addressed the Buddha, saying:

> "So it is, O Great Striver!
> So it is, O Great Sage!
> It is not because of one's birth
> That one is called an outcaste.
> It is not because of one's birth
> That one is called a brahmin.
> By deeds one is an outcaste;
> By deeds one is a brahmin."

At that time, the fire-worshipping brahmin Bhāradvāja gained faith. He offered a full bowl of fine food to the World-Honored One. The World-Honored One did not accept it, because it was obtained through speaking a verse. The verse is as stated above.

At that time, the fire-worshipping brahmin Bhāradvāja, seeing the miraculous sign of the food, felt his faith further increase. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, may I now be permitted to go forth in the true Dharma and Vinaya and receive full ordination?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "You may now go forth in the true Dharma and Vinaya, and receive full ordination." He then went forth. Dwelling alone in quiet contemplation, as previously stated, and so on, until he attained arhatship, and his mind was well liberated.

At that time, the brahmin Bhāradvāja, having attained arhatship, his mind well liberated, experienced joy and happiness, and spoke this verse:

> "Seeking purity through a wrong path,
> Making offerings and worshipping fire,
> Not recognizing the path to purity,
> Like one blind from birth.
>
> Now I have attained peace and happiness,
> Having gone forth and received full ordination,
> Attaining the three knowledges.
> What the Buddha taught has been accomplished.
>
> Previously a brahmin in name only;
> Now I am truly a brahmin.
> Bathed and washed, free from dust and defilement,
> I have crossed to the far shore beyond the devas."

# 雜阿含經

## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Four

This sūtra, in this fascicle: the Guo and Song editions are entirely identical in text and meaning, both containing nineteen sūtras totaling twenty-five sheets. The Dan edition contains fifteen sūtras; if written out according to the Song canon format, it would amount to twenty-seven sheets. Furthermore, its text and meaning are entirely different from the Guo and Song editions, and it is unknown which should be preferred or rejected. Upon examining the Guo and Song editions' sūtras, they are found further on in the Liu collection, being Fascicle Forty-two of this scripture. The Song canon erroneously re-carved that fascicle here as the initial fourth fascicle, and the Guo edition followed suit in this error. Therefore, that version is rejected, and the Dan edition's sūtras are adopted here.

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099, Fascicle 5

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

---

## (103)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, there were many senior bhikṣus dwelling in the Ghositārāma garden in the country of Kauśāmbī.

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Khema dwelling in the Badarīka garden in the country of Kauśāmbī, who had become severely ill.

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Dāsaka who was his attendant in illness. Dāsaka then went to the senior bhikṣus, bowed at the feet of the senior bhikṣus, and stood to one side.

The senior bhikṣus told Bhikṣu Dāsaka: "Go to Bhikṣu Khema and tell him: 'The senior bhikṣus ask you: Is your body somewhat better and at ease? Are your painful afflictions not increasing in severity?'"

Then Bhikṣu Dāsaka, receiving the instruction of the senior bhikṣus, went to Bhikṣu Khema and told him: "The senior bhikṣus inquire after you: Are your painful afflictions gradually improving? Are the many sufferings not increasing?"

Bhikṣu Khema told Bhikṣu Dāsaka: "My illness is not improving, my body is not at ease, and the various sufferings are increasing without relief. It is like many strong men seizing a weak person, binding a rope around his head, and twisting tightly with both hands, causing extreme great pain. My pain now surpasses even that. It is like a butcher, who with a sharp knife cuts open the belly of a living ox and removes its internal organs; how could that ox endure such belly pain! My belly pain now is worse than that ox's. It is like two strong men seizing a weak man, suspending him over a fire, and burning both his feet. The heat in my two feet now surpasses even that."

Then Bhikṣu Dāsaka returned to the senior bhikṣus and fully reported to them the description of Bhikṣu Khema's illness as he had stated it.

Then the senior bhikṣus again dispatched Bhikṣu Dāsaka to go to Bhikṣu Khema and tell him: "The World-Honored One has spoken of the five aggregates of clinging. What are the five? The form aggregate of clinging, the feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness aggregates of clinging. Can you, Khema, at least examine these five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self?"

Then Bhikṣu Dāsaka, having received the instruction of the senior bhikṣus, went and told Bhikṣu Khema: "The senior bhikṣus say to you: The World-Honored One taught the five aggregates of clinging. Can you at least examine them as not self and not belonging to self?"

Bhikṣu Khema told Dāsaka: "I am able to examine those five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self."

Bhikṣu Dāsaka returned and reported to the senior bhikṣus: "Bhikṣu Khema says: 'I am able to examine the five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self.'"

The senior bhikṣus again dispatched Bhikṣu Dāsaka to tell Bhikṣu Khema: "You are able to examine the five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self; is this like an arahant whose outflows are exhausted?"

Then Bhikṣu Dāsaka, receiving the instruction of the senior bhikṣus, went to Bhikṣu Khema and told him: "Bhikṣu, if one can thus examine the five aggregates of clinging, is this like an arahant whose outflows are exhausted?"

Bhikṣu Khema told Bhikṣu Dāsaka: "I examine the five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self, but I am not an arahant whose outflows are exhausted."

Then Bhikṣu Dāsaka returned to the senior bhikṣus and reported: "Bhikṣu Khema says: 'I examine the five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self, yet I am not an arahant whose outflows are exhausted.'"

Then the senior bhikṣus told Bhikṣu Dāsaka: "Go back and tell Bhikṣu Khema: 'You say: "I examine the five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self, yet I am not an arahant whose outflows are exhausted." This is contradictory, the former and latter statements being inconsistent.'"

Bhikṣu Dāsaka, receiving the instruction of the senior bhikṣus, went and told Bhikṣu Khema: "You say: 'I examine the five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self, yet I am not an arahant whose outflows are exhausted.' This is contradictory, the former and latter statements being inconsistent."

Bhikṣu Khema told Bhikṣu Dāsaka: "I examine the five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self, yet the reason I am not an arahant is that regarding the five aggregates of clinging, the conceit 'I am,' the desire 'I am,' and the underlying tendency 'I am' have not yet been cut off, not yet fully known, not yet abandoned, not yet expelled."

Bhikṣu Dāsaka returned to the senior bhikṣus and reported: "Bhikṣu Khema says: 'I examine the five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self, yet the reason I am not an arahant whose outflows are exhausted is that regarding the five aggregates of clinging, the conceit "I am," the desire "I am," and the underlying tendency "I am" have not yet been cut off, not yet fully known, not yet abandoned, not yet expelled.'"

The senior bhikṣus again dispatched Bhikṣu Dāsaka to tell Bhikṣu Khema: "You say there is a self. Where is this self? Is form the self? Or is the self other than form? Are feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness the self? Or is the self other than consciousness?"

Bhikṣu Khema told Bhikṣu Dāsaka: "I do not say that form is the self or that the self is other than form; nor that feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are the self, or that the self is other than consciousness. Yet regarding the five aggregates of clinging, the conceit 'I am,' the desire 'I am,' and the underlying tendency 'I am' have not yet been cut off, not yet fully known, not yet abandoned, not yet expelled."

Bhikṣu Khema told Bhikṣu Dāsaka: "Why trouble you to run back and forth? Bring me a staff, and I will support myself with it and go to those senior bhikṣus. Please give me the staff." Bhikṣu Khema then supported himself with the staff and went to the senior bhikṣus.

At that time, the senior bhikṣus saw Bhikṣu Khema coming from afar, supporting himself with a staff. They prepared a seat for him, set out a footrest, went out to welcome him, took his robe and bowl, and invited him to be seated. They exchanged mutual greetings. After the greetings, they said to Bhikṣu Khema: "You speak of the conceit 'I am.' Where do you see this self? Is form the self? Is the self other than form? Are feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness the self? Is the self other than consciousness?"

Bhikṣu Khema replied: "Form is not the self, and the self is not other than form. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are not the self, and the self is not other than consciousness. Yet regarding the five aggregates of clinging, the conceit 'I am,' the desire 'I am,' and the underlying tendency 'I am' have not yet been cut off, not yet fully known, not yet abandoned, not yet expelled. It is like the fragrance of an utpala lotus, a padma lotus, a kumuda lotus, or a puṇḍarīka lotus: is the fragrance identical with the root? Or is the fragrance other than the root? Is it the fragrance of the stalk, leaves, pistil, or pollen? Or is the fragrance other than the pollen? Would you say they are equivalent?"

The senior bhikṣus answered: "No, Bhikṣu Khema! The root of an utpala, padma, kumuda, or puṇḍarīka lotus is not identical with the fragrance, nor is the fragrance other than the root. Also, the stalk, leaves, pistil, and pollen are not the fragrance, nor is the fragrance other than the pollen."

Bhikṣu Khema asked again: "Then what is that fragrance?"

The senior bhikṣus answered: "It is the fragrance of the flower."

Bhikṣu Khema then said: "I am likewise. Form is not the self, and the self is not apart from form. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are not the self, and the self is not apart from consciousness. Yet I examine the five aggregates of clinging and see them as not self and not belonging to self, and still, regarding the conceit 'I am,' the desire 'I am,' and the underlying tendency 'I am,' these have not yet been cut off, not yet fully known, not yet abandoned, not yet expelled. Venerable seniors, listen as I speak a simile, for the wise understand through simile. It is like the garment of a wet nurse, which is given to a washerman. Using various lye and soap-water, he washes and scrubs away the dirt and grime, yet a residual scent remains. One must use various mixed fragrances to fumigate it until the scent is completely eliminated. In the same way, a well-instructed noble disciple, although correctly examining the five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self, still has the conceit 'I am,' the desire 'I am,' and the underlying tendency 'I am' not yet cut off, not yet fully known, not yet abandoned, not yet expelled. Afterwards, by further cultivating and contemplating the arising and passing away of the five aggregates of clinging, observing: 'This is form, this is the arising of form, this is the cessation of form; this is feeling, perception, volitional formations, consciousness; this is the arising of consciousness, this is the cessation of consciousness,' when one has thus contemplated the arising and passing away of the five aggregates of clinging, the conceit 'I am,' the desire 'I am,' and the underlying tendency 'I am' are all entirely removed. This is called true right contemplation."

When Bhikṣu Khema spoke this Dharma, those senior bhikṣus became free from dust and stains and attained the purity of the Dharma eye. Bhikṣu Khema, without giving rise to any outflows, had his mind liberated. Through the benefit of Dharma joy, his bodily illness was entirely cured.

At that time, the senior bhikṣus said to Bhikṣu Khema: "When we heard what you first spoke, we already understood and were delighted, how much more so upon hearing it repeated! The reason we questioned you was to draw out your subtle and marvelous eloquence, not to trouble you. You are well able to broadly expound the Dharma of the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One."

At that time, the senior bhikṣus, having heard what Bhikṣu Khema said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (104)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Yamaka who had arisen an evil wrong view, saying thus: "As I understand the Dharma taught by the Buddha, an arahant whose outflows are exhausted, upon the breakup of the body at the end of life, ceases to exist entirely."

At that time, many bhikṣus heard what he said and went to his place and said to Bhikṣu Yamaka: "Did you truly say this: 'As I understand the Dharma taught by the Buddha, an arahant whose outflows are exhausted, upon the breakup of the body at the end of life, ceases to exist entirely'?"

He answered: "Indeed so, venerables!"

Then the bhikṣus said to Yamaka: "Do not slander the World-Honored One! To slander the World-Honored One is unwholesome. The World-Honored One did not say this. You should entirely abandon this evil wrong view."

When the bhikṣus said these words, Bhikṣu Yamaka still clung to his evil wrong view, saying: "Venerables! Only this is true; anything else is false." He said this three times.

Then the bhikṣus, unable to reform Bhikṣu Yamaka, left him and went to the Venerable Śāriputra and said: "Venerable one! You should know that Bhikṣu Yamaka has arisen an evil wrong view, saying: 'As I understand and know the Dharma taught by the Buddha, an arahant whose outflows are exhausted, upon the breakup of the body at the end of life, ceases to exist entirely.' We, having heard what he said, went and asked Bhikṣu Yamaka: 'Did you truly hold such a view?' He answered us: 'Venerables, indeed so; anything else is a foolish statement.' We then said: 'Do not slander the World-Honored One! The World-Honored One did not say this. You should abandon this evil wrong view.' We admonished him two or three times, yet he still would not abandon his evil wrong view. Therefore we have now come to the venerable one. We wish that the venerable one would cause Bhikṣu Yamaka to abandon his evil wrong view, out of compassion for him!"

Śāriputra said: "Very well, I shall cause him to abandon his evil wrong view."

Then the many bhikṣus, hearing Śāriputra's words, were delighted and rejoiced, and returned to their own places.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra, in the early morning, put on his robes and took his bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, he left the city, returned to the monastery, put away his robes and bowl, and went to Bhikṣu Yamaka's place. Bhikṣu Yamaka, seeing the Venerable Śāriputra coming from afar, prepared a seat, washed his feet, set out a footrest, went out to welcome him, took his robes and bowl, and invited him to be seated.

After the Venerable Śāriputra was seated and had washed his feet, he said to Bhikṣu Yamaka: "Did you truly say this: 'As I understand and know the Dharma taught by the World-Honored One, an arahant whose outflows are exhausted, upon the breakup of the body at the end of life, ceases to exist entirely'?"

Bhikṣu Yamaka said to Śāriputra: "Indeed so, Venerable Śāriputra!"

Śāriputra said: "I will now question you; answer as you see fit. What do you think, Yamaka? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent, Venerable Śāriputra!"

He asked again: "If impermanent, is it suffering?"

He answered: "It is suffering."

He asked again: "If impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-instructed noble disciple regard it as self, as other than self, or as mutually inhering?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Śāriputra!"

"Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise." He asked again: "What do you think, Yamaka? Is form the Tathāgata?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Śāriputra!"

"Are feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness the Tathāgata?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Śāriputra!"

He asked again: "What do you think, Yamaka? Is there a Tathāgata apart from form? Is there a Tathāgata apart from feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Śāriputra!"

He asked again: "Is there a Tathāgata within form? Is there a Tathāgata within feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Śāriputra!"

He asked again: "Is there form within the Tathāgata? Are there feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness within the Tathāgata?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Śāriputra!"

He asked again: "Is there a Tathāgata apart from form, feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Śāriputra!"

"Thus, Yamaka! The Tathāgata, seen in the present Dharma as real and as he abides, cannot be apprehended, cannot be designated. How then can you say: 'As I understand and know what the World-Honored One has taught, an arahant whose outflows are exhausted, upon the breakup of the body at the end of life, ceases to exist entirely'? Was that a timely statement?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Śāriputra!"

He asked again: "Yamaka! Previously you said: 'As I understand and know what the World-Honored One has taught, an arahant whose outflows are exhausted, upon the breakup of the body at the end of life, ceases to exist entirely.' Why do you now say otherwise?"

Bhikṣu Yamaka said: "Venerable Śāriputra! Previously, due to my lack of understanding and ignorance, I made such an evil wrong view statement. Having heard the Venerable Śāriputra's teaching, my lack of understanding and ignorance have all been entirely cut off."

He asked again: "Yamaka! If someone were to ask you: 'Bhikṣu, regarding the evil wrong view you formerly held, what knowledge and vision have you now attained to have entirely abandoned it?' How would you answer?"

Yamaka answered: "Venerable Śāriputra! If someone came and asked, I would answer thus: 'The form of an arahant whose outflows are exhausted is impermanent; what is impermanent is suffering; what is suffering is quiescent, cool, and forever extinguished. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise.' If someone came and asked, I would answer thus."

Śāriputra said: "Excellent! Excellent, Bhikṣu Yamaka! You should answer thus. Why? Because the form of an arahant whose outflows are exhausted is impermanent; what is impermanent is suffering; what is impermanent and suffering is subject to arising and ceasing. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise."

When the Venerable Śāriputra spoke this Dharma, Bhikṣu Yamaka became free from dust and stains and attained the purity of the Dharma eye.

The Venerable Śāriputra said to Bhikṣu Yamaka: "I shall now speak a simile, for the wise understand through simile. It is like an elder or elder's son, greatly wealthy with much property, who widely employs servants to carefully guard his possessions. At that time, an enemy, a malicious person, comes deceitfully pretending to be friendly, acting as a servant, always watching for an opportunity, sleeping late and rising early, attending to the left and right, being diligent and respectful in his duties, being humble in his speech, causing the master to be pleased, regarding him as a close friend, regarding him as a son, trusting him completely without doubt, and not guarding against him. Afterwards, with a sharp knife in hand, he takes the master's life. Bhikṣu Yamaka! What do you think? Was that evil enemy, though appearing as the elder's friend, not from the very beginning employing stratagems, with murderous intent always watching for an opportunity, right up until the end? Yet that elder could not perceive it, and thus was harmed."

He answered: "Indeed so, Venerable One!"

Śāriputra said to Bhikṣu Yamaka: "What do you think? If that elder had originally known that person was a deceitful friend intending harm, and had guarded himself well, would he not have been harmed?"

He answered: "That is so, Venerable Śāriputra!"

"In the same way, Bhikṣu Yamaka! Foolish, unlearned ordinary people regard the five aggregates of clinging with the perception of permanence, the perception of security, the perception of freedom from illness, the perception of self, and the perception of belonging to self. They cling to, maintain, and cherish these five aggregates of clinging, and in the end are harmed by these five aggregates of clinging, their enemies. It is like that elder who was harmed by the deceitful enemy-friend without perceiving it. Yamaka! A well-instructed noble disciple examines these five aggregates of clinging as a disease, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer, as impermanent, as suffering, as empty, as not self, as not belonging to self. Regarding these five aggregates of clinging, he does not attach, does not grasp. Because he does not grasp, he does not attach. Because he does not attach, he realizes nirvāṇa for himself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no more future existence.'"

When the Venerable Śāriputra spoke this Dharma, Bhikṣu Yamaka, without giving rise to any outflows, had his mind liberated. After the Venerable Śāriputra taught the Dharma for Bhikṣu Yamaka, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him, he rose from his seat and departed.

---

## (105)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandakanivāpa in the Veṇuvana.

At that time, there was a wanderer named Sabhiya Kaccāna (Xianní), who came to the Buddha, respectfully greeted him, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One! On a previous day, various śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, cārakas, and renunciants gathered at the Rare Hall. Such a discussion arose: Pūraṇa Kassapa, being a great teacher with five hundred disciples surrounding him, among whom were some of the highest intelligence and some of dull faculties; when they died, he never declared where any of them were reborn. Furthermore, Makkhali Gosāla was a great teacher with five hundred disciples surrounding him. Among his disciples, there were some of intelligence and some of dull faculties; when they died, he never declared where any of them were reborn. Likewise, Sañjaya Belaṭṭhiputta, Ajita Kesakambalī, Pakudha Kaccāyana, and Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta, each with five hundred disciples surrounding them, were also as stated above. The śramaṇa Gautama was also mentioned in that discussion. The śramaṇa Gautama, being a great teacher, when his disciples died, would declare: 'So-and-so was reborn in such-and-such a place; so-and-so was reborn in such-and-such a place.' Previously, doubt arose in me: 'How has the śramaṇa Gautama attained such a Dharma?'"

The Buddha told Sabhiya: "Do not give rise to doubt. It is because of confusion that doubt arises. Sabhiya! You should know there are three kinds of teachers. What are the three? There is one teacher who sees the present world as truly having a self, and teaches as he knows, but is unable to know what happens after death. This is called the first kind of teacher appearing in the world. Furthermore, Sabhiya, there is one teacher who sees the present world as truly having a self, and also sees that after death there is still a self, and teaches as he knows. Furthermore, Sabhiya, there is one teacher who does not see the present world as truly having a self, and also does not see that after death there is truly a self.

"Sabhiya! That first teacher, who sees the present world as truly having a self and teaches as he knows, is called the annihilationist view. That second teacher, who sees both the present world and the future world as truly having a self and teaches as he knows, this is the eternalist view. That third teacher, who does not see the present world as truly having a self, and who after death also does not see a self, this is the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, who teaches the cutting off of craving for present phenomena, the abandonment of desire, cessation, and nirvāṇa."

Sabhiya addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Having heard what the World-Honored One has said, my doubt has increased even further."

The Buddha told Sabhiya: "It is right that your doubt should increase. Why? Because this is a very profound matter, difficult to see and difficult to know, requiring the most profound illumination, subtle and supreme, understood only by the wise. Beings of ordinary kind are unable to discern it. Why? Because beings through the long night hold different views, different convictions, different pursues, and different desires."

Sabhiya addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! My mind has gained pure faith in the World-Honored One. I only wish the World-Honored One would teach me the Dharma, so that right here on this seat, my wisdom eye may be purified."

The Buddha told Sabhiya: "I shall now teach you as you wish to hear."

The Buddha told Sabhiya: "Is form permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent."

The World-Honored One asked again: "Sabhiya! If impermanent, is it suffering?"

He answered: "It is suffering."

The World-Honored One asked Sabhiya again: "If impermanent and suffering, being subject to change, would a well-instructed noble disciple regard it as self, as other than self, or as mutually inhering?"

He answered: "No, World-Honored One!"

"Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise." He asked again: "What do you think, Sabhiya? Is form the Tathāgata?"

He answered: "No, World-Honored One!"

"Are feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness the Tathāgata?"

He answered: "No, World-Honored One!"

He asked again: "Sabhiya! Is there a Tathāgata apart from form? Is there a Tathāgata apart from feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness?"

He answered: "No, World-Honored One!"

He asked again: "Sabhiya! Is there a Tathāgata within form? Is there a Tathāgata within feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness?"

He answered: "No, World-Honored One!"

He asked again: "Sabhiya! Is there form within the Tathāgata? Are there feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness within the Tathāgata?"

He answered: "No, World-Honored One!"

He asked again: "Sabhiya! Is there a Tathāgata apart from form, feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness?"

He answered: "No, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told Sabhiya: "Those disciples of mine who, having heard what I teach, do not fully understand the meaning and give rise to conceit without penetration; because they lack penetration, conceit is not cut off. Because conceit is not cut off, after abandoning these aggregates, they continue in conjunction with aggregates and are reborn. Therefore, Sabhiya, I declare of these disciples that upon the breakup of the body at the end of life, they are reborn in such-and-such a place. Why? Because they still have residual conceit.

"Sabhiya! Those disciples of mine who, regarding what I teach, are able to understand the meaning, they attain penetration regarding conceits. Because they attain penetration, conceits are cut off. Because conceits are cut off, upon the breakup of the body at the end of life, there is no further continuity. Sabhiya! Regarding such disciples, I do not declare that after abandoning these aggregates they are reborn in such-and-such a place. Why? Because there is no cause or condition by which to make such a declaration. If one wishes me to make a declaration, I should declare: 'They have cut off all craving and desire, have forever departed from the bonds of existence, have been rightly liberated in mind, and have reached the ultimate end of suffering.' From the past until now in the present, I constantly teach the root of conceit, the accumulation of conceit, the arising of conceit, and the emergence of conceit. If one attains penetration regarding conceit through contemplation, the many sufferings do not arise."

When the Buddha taught this Dharma, the wanderer Sabhiya became free from dust and stains and attained the purity of the Dharma eye.

At that time, the wanderer Sabhiya, having seen the Dharma, attained the Dharma, severed all doubts, and without reliance on another and without being led by another, in the true Dharma his mind attained fearlessness. He rose from his seat, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! May I be allowed to go forth and practice the holy life in the true Dharma?"

The Buddha told Sabhiya: "You may go forth in the true Dharma, receive full ordination, and obtain the status of a bhikṣu."

At that time, after Sabhiya had gone forth, alone in a quiet place, practicing diligently without negligence, he dwelt contemplating thus: "This is the reason why sons of good family shave their heads and faces, with right faith leave the household life, go forth to study the Way, practice the holy life, and see the Dharma, knowing for themselves and realizing: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no more future existence.'" He attained arahantship.

Having heard what the Buddha taught, he rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (106)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandakanivāpa in the Veṇuvana. At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Anuradha dwelling on Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain.

At that time, many wanderers of other sects went to Anuradha, exchanged greetings with one another. After exchanging greetings, they stood to one side and addressed Anuradha: "We wish to ask a question. Do you have the leisure to explain?"

Anuradha told the wanderers: "Ask as you wish. If I know, I shall answer."

The wanderers asked again: "What do you say, venerable one? Does the Tathāgata exist after death?"

Anuradha said: "As the World-Honored One has taught, this is undeclared."

They asked again: "Does the Tathāgata not exist after death?"

Anuradha said: "As the World-Honored One has taught, this too is undeclared."

They asked again: "Does the Tathāgata both exist and not exist after death? Does the Tathāgata neither exist nor not exist after death?"

Anuradha said: "As the World-Honored One has taught, these too are undeclared."

The wanderers asked Anuradha again: "What is this, venerable one? 'Does the Tathāgata exist after death?' You say: 'Undeclared.' 'Does the Tathāgata not exist after death?' You say: 'Undeclared.' 'Does the Tathāgata both exist and not exist after death?' 'Does the Tathāgata neither exist nor not exist after death?' You say: 'Undeclared.' What is this, venerable one? Does the śramaṇa Gautama not know and not see?"

Anuradha said: "The World-Honored One is not one who does not know, not one who does not see."

At that time, the wanderers, displeased with what Anuradha said, reviled him and rose from their seats and departed.

Then Anuradha, knowing the wanderers had departed, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, stood to one side, and reported in full to the Buddha the questions asked by the wanderers, saying: "World-Honored One! When they asked thus, I answered thus. Did I answer in accordance with the Dharma? Have I not slandered the World-Honored One? Was it in accordance with the Dharma or contrary to the Dharma? Will it not cause others to come with challenging questions and cause me to fall into a position of censure?"

The Buddha told Anuradha: "I will now question you; answer as you see fit. Anuradha! Is form permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent."

"Are feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One!"

As was broadly taught in the Yamaka Sūtra, up to: "Is consciousness the Tathāgata?"

He answered: "No."

The Buddha told Anuradha: "One who speaks thus speaks in accordance with the teaching, does not slander the Tathāgata, does not transgress the proper order. As the Tathāgata teaches, in the proper sequence of Dharma, there is no one who can come with challenging questions and censure. Why? Because I truly know form as it really is; the arising of form, the cessation of form, and the path leading to the cessation of form, I truly know as they really are. Anuradha! If one abandons what the Tathāgata has made known and says 'He does not know, does not see,' this is not a proper statement."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, Anuradha, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (107)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Bharga country, at Śeṣavat Mountain, in the Deer Park deep forest.

At that time, there was the householder Nakulapitā, one hundred and twenty years old, aged and with faculties fully ripened, frail, weak, and afflicted with painful illness. Yet he wished to pay his respects to the World-Honored One and the bhikṣus whom he had long revered and valued. He came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit at one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I am old and declining, frail, weak, and afflicted with painful illness. By my own effort and exertion, I have come to pay respects to the World-Honored One and the bhikṣus whom I have long revered and valued. I only wish the World-Honored One would teach me the Dharma, so that through the long night I may have peace and happiness!"

At that time, the World-Honored One told the householder Nakulapitā: "Excellent, householder! It is true that you are old, with ripened faculties, frail, weak, and afflicted with painful illness, yet you are able by your own effort to come and pay respects to the Tathāgata as well as the other revered and valued bhikṣus. Householder, you should know: with regard to this body afflicted by painful illness, you should always train yourself to maintain a mind that is not afflicted by painful illness."

At that time, the World-Honored One instructed, taught, illuminated, and delighted the householder Nakulapitā, and then remained silent.

The householder Nakulapitā, having heard what the Buddha taught, was delighted and rejoiced, bowed to the Buddha, and departed.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra was sitting under a tree not far from the World-Honored One. The householder Nakulapitā went to the Venerable Śāriputra, bowed at his feet, withdrew to sit at one side. Then the Venerable Śāriputra asked the householder: "Your faculties are now harmonious and joyful, your complexion is bright and clear. Did you hear a profound Dharma from the World-Honored One?"

The householder Nakulapitā said to Śāriputra: "Today the World-Honored One taught me the Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting me, sprinkling my body and mind with the ambrosia Dharma. Therefore, my faculties are now harmonious and joyful, and my complexion is bright and clear."

The Venerable Śāriputra asked the householder: "What Dharma did the World-Honored One teach you, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting, moistening you with ambrosia?"

The householder Nakulapitā said to Śāriputra: "Earlier I went to the World-Honored One and addressed him: 'I am old and declining, frail, weak, and afflicted with painful illness.
