By my own effort I have come to pay respects to the World-Honored One and the revered and valued bhikṣus.' The Buddha told me: 'Excellent, householder! It is true that you are old, frail, weak, and afflicted with painful illness, yet you are able by your own effort to come to me and see the bhikṣus you have long revered. You should now, with regard to this body afflicted by painful illness, always train yourself to maintain a mind that is not afflicted by painful illness.'

"The World-Honored One taught me such Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting, moistening me with ambrosia."

The Venerable Śāriputra asked the householder: "Why did you not further ask the World-Honored One: 'What is it to have a body afflicted by painful illness and a mind also afflicted by painful illness? What is it to have a body afflicted by painful illness but a mind not afflicted by painful illness?'"

The householder answered: "It is precisely for this meaning that I have come to the venerable one. I only wish you would briefly explain the essential Dharma for me."

The Venerable Śāriputra said to the householder: "Excellent, householder! Listen carefully now, and I shall explain it to you. A foolish, unlearned ordinary person does not truly know the arising of form, the cessation of form, the danger in form, the gratification of form, or the escape from form. Because he does not truly know these, he delights in form, saying that form is self, is belonging to self, and he grasps and clings to it. If that form deteriorates or changes, his mind and consciousness follow it in turning, and afflicting suffering arises. When afflicting suffering has arisen, there is fear, obstruction, concern, grief, and entangled longing. Regarding feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness, it is likewise. This is called having both body and mind afflicted by painful illness.

"What is it to have a body afflicted by painful illness but a mind not afflicted by painful illness? A well-instructed noble disciple truly knows the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger in form, and the escape from form. Having truly known these, he does not give rise to delight, does not see form as self or belonging to self. If that form changes or becomes different, his mind does not follow it in turning, and afflicting suffering does not arise. When afflicting suffering does not arise, he does not experience fear, obstruction, concern, or entangled longing. Regarding feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness, it is likewise. This is called having a body afflicted by painful illness but a mind not afflicted by painful illness."

When the Venerable Śāriputra spoke this Dharma, the householder Nakulapitā attained the purity of the Dharma eye. At that time, the householder Nakulapitā, having seen the Dharma, attained the Dharma, known the Dharma, entered the Dharma, crossed beyond all doubts, not relying on another, in the true Dharma his mind attained fearlessness. He rose from his seat, straightened his robes, respectfully joined his palms, and addressed the Venerable Śāriputra: "I have transcended, I have crossed over. I now take refuge in the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha. Take me as a lay devotee; know me thus. From today until the end of my life, I take refuge in the Three Jewels."

At that time, the householder Nakulapitā, having heard what the Venerable Śāriputra taught, was delighted and rejoiced, made his salutation, and departed.

---

## (108)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Śākyan land at the Devadaha settlement.

At that time, many bhikṣus from the West wished to return to the West for the rains retreat. They went to the World-Honored One, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit at one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught them the Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting them. After various instruction, teaching, illumination, and delight, the bhikṣus from the West rose from their seats, joined their palms, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! We, the many bhikṣus from the West, wish to return to the West for the rains retreat. We now request permission to take our leave."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus from the West: "Have you taken leave of Śāriputra?"

They answered: "We have not taken our leave."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus from the West: "Śāriputra practices the holy life with purity. You should pay your respects and take leave of him. He is able to benefit you with the meaning and to bring you long-lasting peace and happiness."

Then the bhikṣus from the West took their leave and were about to go. At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra was sitting under a sturdy tree not far from the Buddha. The bhikṣus from the West went to the Venerable Śāriputra, bowed at his feet, withdrew to sit at one side, and addressed the Venerable Śāriputra: "We wish to return to the West for the rains retreat, so we have come to take our leave."

Śāriputra said: "Have you taken leave of the World-Honored One?"

They answered: "We have already taken our leave."

Śāriputra said: "When you return to the West, in various places and various countries, various assemblies will certainly question you. You, having heard the well-spoken Dharma from the World-Honored One, should well receive it, well retain it, well contemplate it, and well penetrate it, so that you are fully able to expound it completely for them. Will you not misrepresent the Buddha? Will you not cause those assemblies to challenge, question, and censure, putting you in a position of defeat?"

Those bhikṣus said to Śāriputra: "It is to hear the Dharma that we have come to the venerable one. We only wish the venerable one would fully explain it for us, out of compassion!"

The Venerable Śāriputra told the bhikṣus: "The people of Jambudvīpa are intelligent and sharp-witted. Whether kṣatriyas, brāhmaṇas, householders, or śramaṇas, they will certainly ask you: 'What does your great teacher teach? With what instruction does he instruct you?' You should answer: 'The great teacher teaches only the taming of desire and passion, and he instructs us with this teaching.'

"They will ask again: 'In what dharmas does he tame desire and passion?' You should then answer: 'The great teacher teaches only the taming of desire and passion regarding the form aggregate, and the taming of desire and passion regarding the feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness aggregates. Our great teacher thus teaches the Dharma.'

"They will ask again: 'What fault is there in desire and passion that your great teacher teaches the taming of desire and passion regarding form, and the taming of desire and passion regarding feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness?' You should then answer: 'If, regarding form, desire is not cut off, passion is not cut off, love is not cut off, longing is not cut off, thirst is not cut off, then when that form changes or becomes different, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering arise. Regarding feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness, it is likewise. Seeing that desire and passion have such faults, therefore regarding form one tames desire and passion; regarding feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness one tames desire and passion.'

"They will ask again: 'What benefit is seen in cutting off desire and passion that your great teacher teaches the taming of desire and passion regarding form, and the taming of desire and passion regarding feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness?' You should then answer: 'If, regarding form, desire is cut off, passion is cut off, longing is cut off, love is cut off, thirst is cut off, then when that form changes or becomes different, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering do not arise. Regarding feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness, it is likewise.'

"Venerables! If by accepting unwholesome dharmas as causes and conditions, one were to obtain pleasant abiding in the present Dharma, without suffering, without obstruction, without vexation, without burning, and upon the breakup of the body at the end of life were to be reborn in a good destination, then the World-Honored One would never say: 'You should cut off unwholesome dharmas,' nor would he instruct people to cultivate the holy life within the Buddha's Dharma to reach the end of suffering. But because accepting unwholesome dharmas as causes and conditions results in present suffering, obstruction, burning, and vexation, and upon the breakup of the body at the end of life one falls into evil destinies, therefore the World-Honored One says: 'You should cut off unwholesome dharmas; within the Buddha's Dharma, cultivate the holy life, equally ending suffering, ultimately reaching the end of suffering.'

"If by accepting wholesome dharmas as causes and conditions, one were to experience suffering in the present Dharma, obstruction, burning, and vexation, and upon the breakup of the body at the end of life one were to fall into evil destinies, then the World-Honored One would never say to accept and uphold wholesome dharmas, nor to cultivate the holy life within the Buddha's Dharma to equally end suffering and ultimately reach the end of suffering. But because accepting and upholding wholesome dharmas results in pleasant abiding in the present Dharma, without suffering, without obstruction, without vexation, without burning, and upon the breakup of the body at the end of life one is reborn in a good destination, therefore the World-Honored One praises and instructs people to accept wholesome dharmas, to cultivate the holy life within the Buddha's Dharma, equally ending suffering, ultimately reaching the end of suffering."

When the Venerable Śāriputra spoke this Dharma, the bhikṣus from the West, without giving rise to any outflows, had their minds liberated. When the Venerable Śāriputra spoke this Dharma, the bhikṣus were delighted and rejoiced, made their salutation, and departed.

---

## (109)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Suppose there is a pool of water, fifty yojanas on each side and equally deep, filled to the brim with water. Then suppose a man takes a hair, a blade of grass, or a fingernail and dips it into that water. Bhikṣus! What do you think? Is the water on that man's dip more, or is the pool water more?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The water the man dipped with a hair, a blade of grass, or a fingernail is scant, too scant to even speak of. The pool water is vastly more, a hundred thousand ten-thousand times, beyond any comparison."

"Just so, bhikṣus! The mass of suffering cut off by one who has seen the truth is like that pool water, and in the future it will never arise again."

At that time, after the World-Honored One had spoken this Dharma, he entered his room to sit in meditation. Then the Venerable Śāriputra, sitting among the assembly, after the World-Honored One had entered his room, told the bhikṣus: "Never before heard! Today the World-Honored One has well spoken the simile of the pool. Why? A noble disciple who has fully seen the truth and attained the fruit of uninterrupted penetration: whatever ordinary wrong views, the root of personality-view, the accumulation of personality-view, the arising of personality-view, the emergence of personality-view, namely anxiety, concealment, celebration, cherishing, claiming a self, claiming a being, claiming a wonder, and haughty pride, all such wrong things are entirely eliminated, cut off at the root, like a tāla tree cut down, never to arise again in the future.

"Bhikṣus! What are the various wrong things that a noble disciple who has seen the truth cuts off, never to arise again in the future? A foolish, unlearned ordinary person sees form as self, as other than self, as self within form, or as form within self. He sees feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness as self, as other than self, as self within consciousness, or as consciousness within self.

"How does one see form as self? One enters the totality of earth as the base of complete absorption, and having contemplated, thinks: 'Earth is self, self is earth; self and earth are solely one, not two, not different, not separate.' Likewise with water, fire, wind, blue, yellow, red, white, entering each totality as the base of complete absorption, and having contemplated, thinks: 'This element is self, self is this element; solely one, not two, not different, not separate.' Thus, regarding each totality base, one by one one conceives a self. This is called 'form is self.' How does one see the self as other than form? If one sees feeling as self, then having seen feeling as self, one sees form as belonging to self. Or one sees perception, volitional formations, or consciousness as self, and sees form as belonging to self.

"How does one see self within form? If one sees feeling as self, one sees form as within self. Or one sees perception, volitional formations, or consciousness as self, and sees form as within self. How does one see self within form? If one sees feeling as self, abiding within form, entering into form, pervading all four limbs. Or one sees perception, volitional formations, or consciousness as self, abiding within form, pervading all four limbs. This is called 'self within form.'

"How does one see feeling as self? Regarding the six bodies of feeling: feeling born of eye-contact, feeling born of ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact, one sees each of these six feeling-bodies as self. Self is feeling. This is called 'feeling is self.' How does one see self as other than feeling? If one sees form as self, then feeling is belonging to self. Or if one sees perception, volitional formations, or consciousness as self, then feeling is belonging to self. This is called 'self other than feeling.'

"How does one see self within feeling? If one sees form as self, with feeling within it. Or if one sees perception, volitional formations, or consciousness as self, with feeling within it. How does one see self within feeling? If one sees form as self, abiding within feeling, pervading all four limbs. Or if one sees perception, volitional formations, or consciousness as self, abiding within feeling, pervading all four limbs. This is called 'self within feeling.'

"How does one see perception as self? Regarding the six bodies of perception: perception born of eye-contact, perception born of ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact, one sees each of these six perception-bodies as self. This is called 'perception is self.' How does one see self as other than perception? If one sees form as self, then perception is belonging to self. Or if one sees feeling, volitional formations, or consciousness as self, then perception is belonging to self. This is called 'self other than perception.'

"How does one see self within perception? If one sees form as self, with perception abiding within it. Or if one sees feeling, volitional formations, or consciousness as self, with perception abiding within it. How does one see self within perception? If one sees form as self, abiding within perception, pervading all four limbs. This is called 'self within perception.'

"How does one see volitional formations as self? Regarding the six bodies of volition: volition born of eye-contact, volition born of ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact, one sees each of these six volition-bodies as self. This is called 'volitional formations are self.' How does one see self as other than volitional formations? If one sees form as self, then volitional formations are belonging to self. Or if one sees feeling, perception, or consciousness as self, then volitional formations are belonging to self. This is called 'self other than volitional formations.'

"How does one see self within volitional formations? If one sees form as self, with volitional formations abiding within it. Or if one sees feeling, perception, or consciousness as self, with volitional formations abiding within it. This is called 'self within volitional formations.' How does one see self within volitional formations? If one sees form as self, abiding within volitional formations, pervading all four limbs. Or if one sees feeling, perception, or consciousness as self, abiding within volitional formations, pervading all four limbs. This is called 'self within volitional formations.'

"How does one see consciousness as self? Regarding the six bodies of consciousness: eye-consciousness, ear-consciousness, nose-consciousness, tongue-consciousness, body-consciousness, and mind-consciousness. One sees each of these six consciousness-bodies as self. This is called 'consciousness is self.' How does one see self as other than consciousness? If one sees form as self, then consciousness is belonging to self. Or if one sees feeling, perception, or volitional formations as self, then consciousness is belonging to self. This is called 'self other than consciousness.'

"How does one see self within consciousness? If one sees form as self, with consciousness abiding within it. Or if one sees feeling, perception, or volitional formations as self, with consciousness abiding within it. This is called 'self within consciousness.' How does one see self within consciousness? If one sees form as self, abiding within consciousness, pervading all four limbs. Or if one sees feeling, perception, or volitional formations as self, abiding within consciousness, pervading all four limbs. This is called 'self within consciousness.'

"Thus, a noble disciple who sees the Four Noble Truths and attains the fruit of uninterrupted penetration cuts off all wrong views, never to arise again in the future. All form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, is gathered together and contemplated thus: 'All is impermanent, all is suffering, all is empty, all is not self. One should not delight in, grasp, or cherish it. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are likewise; one should not delight in, grasp, or cherish them.' Contemplating thus, the mind is well established and abides, not deluded regarding dharmas. One further contemplates with energy, abandoning all slackness. The mind attains joy and happiness, body and mind become tranquil, abiding in quiescent equanimity, complete with the factors of the path, cultivating them to fulfillment, forever departing from all evil. It is not that there is no melting away, not that there is no quenching. What is extinguished does not arise; what is diminished does not increase; what is cut off is not born. Without grasping, without attachment, one realizes nirvāṇa for oneself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no more future existence.'"

When Śāriputra spoke this Dharma, sixty bhikṣus, without accepting any outflows, had their minds liberated. After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (110)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pool. In the country of Vaiśālī, there was a Nirgrantha (Jain) who was intelligent, wise, and skilled in various treatises, having the pride of his cleverness. He had widely compiled various treatises, with subtle wisdom penetrating the fine points. He taught the Dharma to assemblies, surpassing other debaters. He constantly thought: "Among all the śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, none can match me, even including the Tathāgata, with whom I can debate. Those debate masters, upon hearing my name, sweat from their foreheads and armpits, and water flows from their pores. The wind of my argumentation can flatten grass and break trees, shatter metal and stone, and subdue dragons and elephants. How much less can any human debate masters stand against me!"

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Aśvajit, who in the early morning put on his robes and took his bowl, with dignified and orderly deportment, eyes cast downward, and walking evenly, entered the city to beg for food. At that time, Saccaka the Nirgrantha, having some small errand, was going to the villages. Coming out from the city gate, he saw from afar Bhikṣu Aśvajit and went to him, asking: "How does the śramaṇa Gautama teach the Dharma to his disciples? With what Dharma does he instruct his disciples, causing them to cultivate and practice?"

Aśvajit said: "Aggivessana! The World-Honored One teaches the Dharma thus and instructs his disciples to follow and train accordingly, saying: 'Bhikṣus! Regarding form, you should contemplate it as not self. Regarding feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness, you should contemplate them as not self. These five aggregates of clinging should be diligently and skillfully contemplated as a disease, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer, as impermanent, as suffering, as empty, as not self.'"

When Saccaka the Nirgrantha heard these words, his mind was displeased, and he said: "Aśvajit! You must have misheard. The śramaṇa Gautama would never say such a thing. If the śramaṇa Gautama does say such a thing, it is a wrong view. I shall go to him, challenge him with difficult questions, and make him stop."

At that time, Saccaka the Nirgrantha went to the village where the Licchavī princes were assembled. He said to the Licchavīs: "Today I encountered the śramaṇa Gautama's foremost disciple, named Aśvajit, and briefly debated with him. If what he says is so, I shall go to the śramaṇa Gautama and debate with him, turning him back and forth, making him follow my will at every turn.

"It is like a man cutting and pulling out balbaja grass, grasping its stalk, and shaking it in the air, casting off all the tangled rubbish. I too shall go to the śramaṇa Gautama, debate and challenge him, seizing his essential points, turning him back and forth as I wish, removing his wrong statements.

"It is like a liquor merchant who squeezes his liquor sack, pressing out the clear and pure, removing the dregs and residue. I too shall go to the śramaṇa Gautama, debate and challenge him, turning him back and forth, extracting his pure essence, removing all wrong statements.

"It is like a mat-maker who puts dirty things in his mats, and when he wishes to sell them at market, washes them with water, removing all the foul smells. I too shall go to the śramaṇa Gautama, debate and challenge him, turning him back and forth, seizing the essential framework, removing all foul statements.

"It is like a royal elephant trainer who leads a great intoxicated elephant into deep water, washing its body, four limbs, ears, and nose, bathing it thoroughly, removing all coarse dirt. I too shall go to the śramaṇa Gautama, debate and challenge him, turning him back and forth, at will and freely, seizing the essential points, removing all foul statements. You Licchavīs should also come together to observe the outcome."

Among them, some Licchavīs said: "There is no way that Saccaka the Nirgrantha can debate with the śramaṇa Gautama."

Others said: "Saccaka the Nirgrantha is intelligent and sharp-witted; he can debate with him."

Then five hundred Licchavīs went together with Saccaka the Nirgrantha to the Buddha for the sake of debate.

At that time, the World-Honored One was sitting under a tree in the great forest, abiding in a divine abiding. At that time, many bhikṣus had come out of their rooms and were walking in meditation in the forest. They saw Saccaka the Nirgrantha coming from afar, gradually approaching the bhikṣus. He asked the bhikṣus: "Where is the śramaṇa Gautama dwelling?"

The bhikṣus answered: "In the great forest, under a tree, abiding in a divine abiding."

Saccaka the Nirgrantha then went to the Buddha, respectfully greeted him, and sat to one side. The Licchavī elders also went to the Buddha; some greeted him with respect, some with palms joined in salutation. After their greetings, they stood to one side.

Then Saccaka the Nirgrantha addressed the Buddha: "I have heard that Gautama teaches the Dharma thus and instructs his disciples thus: to instruct disciples to examine form as not self, feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness as not self; to diligently and skillfully examine these five aggregates of clinging as a disease, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer, as impermanent, as suffering, as empty, as not self. Does Gautama have such a teaching? Or are those who transmit this slandering Gautama? Are they speaking as it was said, or not as it was said? Are they speaking in accordance with the Dharma? Are they speaking the Dharma in proper sequence? Is there no one who holds a different view who can come with challenging questions and put you in a position of defeat?"

The Buddha told Saccaka the Nirgrantha: "As you have heard, they spoke as it was said, spoke in accordance with the Dharma, spoke the Dharma in proper sequence. It is not a slander, and there is no challenging question that would put one in a position of defeat. Why? Because I truly teach my disciples in this way. I truly always instruct my disciples, causing them to follow the teaching and contemplate form as not self, feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness as not self, and to contemplate these five aggregates of clinging as a disease, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer, as impermanent, as suffering, as empty, as not self."

Saccaka the Nirgrantha addressed the Buddha: "Gautama! I shall now speak a simile."

The Buddha told Saccaka the Nirgrantha: "Speak as you see fit."

"It is like all the activities in the world that depend upon the ground. In the same way, form is the person, the self, and good and evil arise from it. Feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are the person, the self, and good and evil arise from them. Also, it is like the realm of humans, the realm of spirits, medicinal herbs, and trees, all depending on the ground for their growth. Likewise, form is the person, the self; feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are the person, the self."

The Buddha told Aggivessana: "You say that form is the person, the self; that feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are the person, the self?"

He answered: "That is so, Gautama! Form is the person, the self; feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness are the person, the self. All these assembled people also say this."

The Buddha told Aggivessana: "Set forth your own thesis. Why bring in the multitude?"

Saccaka the Nirgrantha addressed the Buddha: "Form is truly the person, the self."

The Buddha told Aggivessana: "I will now question you; answer as you see fit. Suppose a king, within his own country, regarding those who have committed offenses: could he execute, imprison, banish, or flog them, sever their hands and feet? And regarding those who have meritorious service: could he bestow elephants, horses, carriages, cities, or treasures? Could he do so or not?"

He

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Six

---

### (111)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula.

At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha. In the afternoon, having arisen from meditation, he went to the Buddha, paid homage at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "As the World-Honored One has spoken of 'the flood of existence,' what is called the flood of existence? And what is called the cessation of the flood of existence?"

The Buddha told Rādha: "Excellent, your question! I shall explain it for you. What is called the flood of existence is this: a foolish, unlearned ordinary person does not know as it truly is the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Because he does not know as it truly is, he delights in form, praises form, grasps and clings to form, and is defiled by attachment to it. Conditioned by delight in form, there is clinging; conditioned by clinging, there is existence; conditioned by existence, there is birth; conditioned by birth, aging, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, affliction, and suffering increase. Thus does this entire great mass of suffering arise. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. This is called the flood of existence.

"The well-learned noble disciple knows as it truly is the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Because he knows as it truly is, he does not give rise to delight in that form, does not praise it, does not grasp and cling to it, and is not defiled by attachment to it. Because he does not delight in, praise, grasp and cling to, or become defiled by attachment to it, craving for form ceases. When craving ceases, clinging ceases. When clinging ceases, existence ceases. When existence ceases, birth ceases. When birth ceases, aging, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, suffering, and affliction cease. Thus does this entire great mass of suffering cease. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. This is called what the Tathāgata has taught as the flood of existence and the cessation of the flood of existence."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (112)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula.

At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha. In the afternoon, having arisen from meditation, he went to the Buddha, paid homage at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One! As the World-Honored One has spoken of the complete knowledge of the cutting off of form, and the complete knowledge of the cutting off of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, World-Honored One, what is the complete knowledge of the cutting off of form, and the complete knowledge of the cutting off of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness?"

The Buddha told Rādha: "Excellent, your question! I shall explain it for you. When with regard to form, sorrow, lamentation, suffering, and affliction are exhausted, when there is separation from desire, cessation, subsiding, and disappearance, this is called the complete knowledge of the cutting off of form. When with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, sorrow, lamentation, affliction, and suffering are exhausted, when there is separation from desire, cessation, subsiding, and disappearance, this is called the complete knowledge of the cutting off of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (113)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. His attendant bhikṣu was named Rādha.

At that time, a number of non-Buddhist renunciants went to the Venerable Rādha. Having exchanged greetings with one another, they withdrew and sat to one side, and asked the Venerable Rādha: "Why have you gone forth under the śramaṇa Gautama to cultivate the holy life?"

The Venerable Rādha answered: "I have gone forth under the World-Honored One to cultivate the holy life in order to cut off suffering."

They asked again: "What suffering do you seek to cut off by going forth under the śramaṇa Gautama to cultivate the holy life?"

Rādha answered: "In order to cut off the suffering of form, I have gone forth under the World-Honored One to cultivate the holy life. In order to cut off the suffering of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, I have gone forth under the World-Honored One to cultivate the holy life."

At that time, the non-Buddhist renunciants, hearing what the Venerable Rādha said, were displeased in their minds, rose from their seats, reviled him, and departed.

At that time, the Venerable Rādha, knowing that the non-Buddhist renunciants had left, had this thought: "In what I just said, have I perhaps misrepresented the World-Honored One? Did I speak as he has spoken? Did I speak in accordance with the Dharma, and in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma? Would I not fall into a position of defeat if challenged and questioned by others?"

At that time, the Venerable Rādha, in the afternoon, having arisen from meditation, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and fully reported the above matter to the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, in what I said earlier, did I commit any fault? Have I perhaps misrepresented the World-Honored One? Would I not be challenged and questioned by others and fall into a position of defeat? Did I speak as he has spoken? Did I speak in accordance with the Dharma, and in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma?"

The Buddha told Rādha: "You spoke truthfully. You did not misrepresent the Tathāgata. You spoke as he has spoken, in accordance with the Dharma, and in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma. Why is that? Rādha, form is suffering. In order to cut off that suffering, one goes forth to cultivate the holy life. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are suffering. In order to cut off that suffering, one goes forth to cultivate the holy life."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (114)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. His attendant bhikṣu was named Rādha.

At that time, a number of non-Buddhist renunciants went to the Venerable Rādha. Having exchanged greetings with one another, they withdrew and sat to one side, and asked Rādha: "Why have you gone forth under the śramaṇa Gautama to cultivate the holy life?"

Rādha answered: "I have gone forth under the World-Honored One to cultivate the holy life in order to fully know suffering."

At that time, the non-Buddhist renunciants, hearing what Rādha said, were displeased in their minds, rose from their seats, reviled him, and departed.

At that time, Rādha, in the afternoon, having arisen from meditation, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. He fully reported the above matter to the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, in what I said earlier, have I perhaps misrepresented the World-Honored One? Would others not challenge and question me and cause me to fall into a position of defeat? Did I not speak as he has spoken, not speak in accordance with the Dharma, not speak in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma?"

The Buddha told Rādha: "You spoke truthfully. You did not misrepresent the Tathāgata. You would not be challenged and questioned by others and fall into a position of defeat. You spoke as he has spoken, in accordance with the Dharma, and in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma. Why is that? Form is suffering. In order to fully know that suffering, one goes forth under the Tathāgata to cultivate the holy life. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are suffering. In order to fully know that suffering, one goes forth under the Tathāgata to cultivate the holy life."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (115)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha.

At that time, a number of non-Buddhist renunciants went to the Venerable Rādha. Having exchanged greetings with one another, they withdrew and sat to one side, and asked Rādha: "Why have you gone forth under the śramaṇa Gautama to cultivate the holy life?"

Rādha answered: "In order that sorrow, lamentation, affliction, and suffering regarding form be exhausted, that there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance, I have gone forth under the Tathāgata to cultivate the holy life. In order that sorrow, lamentation, affliction, and suffering regarding feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness be exhausted, that there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance, I have gone forth under the Tathāgata to cultivate the holy life."

At that time, the many non-Buddhist renunciants, having heard this, were displeased in their minds, rose from their seats, reviled him, and departed.

At that time, Rādha, in the afternoon, having arisen from meditation, went to the Buddha, bowed his head in homage at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side. He fully reported the above matter to the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, have I perhaps slandered the World-Honored One? Would others not come to challenge and question me and cause me to fall into a position of defeat? Did I not speak as he has spoken, not speak in accordance with the Dharma, not speak in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma?"

The Buddha told Rādha: "You spoke truthfully. You did not slander the Tathāgata. You would not be challenged and questioned by others and fall into a position of defeat. You spoke as he has spoken, in accordance with the Dharma, and in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma. Why is that? Rādha, sorrow, lamentation, affliction, and suffering regarding form: in order to cut off these, one goes forth under the Tathāgata to cultivate the holy life. Sorrow, lamentation, affliction, and suffering regarding feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: in order to cut off these, one goes forth under the Tathāgata to cultivate the holy life."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (116)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha.

At that time, a number of non-Buddhist renunciants went to Rādha. Having exchanged greetings with one another, they withdrew and sat to one side, and asked Rādha: "Why have you gone forth under the śramaṇa Gautama to cultivate the holy life?"

Rādha answered: "With regard to form, the view of self, of what belongs to self, and the bondage of the tendency of conceit: if these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance; with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the view of self, of what belongs to self, and the bondage of the tendency of conceit: if these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance; for this reason I have gone forth under the World-Honored One to cultivate the holy life."

The non-Buddhist renunciants, hearing these words, were displeased in their minds, rose from their seats, reviled him, and departed.

The bhikṣu Rādha, in the afternoon, having arisen from meditation, went to the Buddha, bowed his head in homage at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side. He fully reported the above matter to the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, in what I said, have I perhaps misrepresented the World-Honored One? Would others not challenge and question me and cause me to fall into a position of defeat? Did I not speak as he has spoken, not speak in accordance with the Dharma, not speak in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma?"

The Buddha told Rādha: "You spoke truthfully. You did not slander the Tathāgata. You would not be challenged and questioned by others and fall into a position of defeat. You spoke as he has spoken, in accordance with the Dharma, and in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma. Why is that? With regard to form, the view of self, of what belongs to self, and the bondage of the tendency of conceit: if these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance. With regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the view of self, of what belongs to self, and the bondage of the tendency of conceit: if these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance; for this reason one goes forth under the Tathāgata to cultivate the holy life."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (117)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha.

At that time, a number of non-Buddhist renunciants went to Rādha. Having exchanged greetings with one another, they withdrew and sat to one side, and asked Rādha: "Why have you gone forth under the śramaṇa Gautama to cultivate the holy life?"

Rādha answered: "Form has outflows, obstructions, burning afflictions, sorrow, and lamentation. If these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness have outflows, obstructions, burning afflictions, sorrow, and lamentation. If these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance; for this reason I have gone forth under the Tathāgata to cultivate the holy life."

At that time, the many non-Buddhist renunciants, having heard this, were displeased in their minds, rose from their seats, reviled him, and departed.

At that time, Rādha, in the afternoon, having arisen from meditation, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. He fully reported the above matter to the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, in what I said, have I perhaps slandered the World-Honored One? Would others not challenge and question me and cause me to fall into a position of defeat? Did I not speak as he has spoken, not speak in accordance with the Dharma, not speak in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma?"

The Buddha told Rādha: "You spoke truthfully. You did not slander the Tathāgata. Why is that? Form has outflows, has obstructions, burning afflictions, sorrow, and lamentation. If these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness have outflows, obstructions, burning afflictions, sorrow, and lamentation. If these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance; for this reason one goes forth under the Tathāgata to cultivate the holy life."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (118)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha.

At that time, non-Buddhist renunciants went to Rādha. Having exchanged greetings with one another, they withdrew and sat to one side, and asked Rādha: "Why have you gone forth under the śramaṇa Gautama to cultivate the holy life?"

Rādha answered: "With regard to form, greed, hatred, and delusion: if these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance; with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, greed, hatred, and delusion: if these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance; for this reason I have gone forth under the Tathāgata to cultivate the holy life."

The non-Buddhist renunciants, hearing these words, were displeased in their minds, rose from their seats, reproached him, and departed.

The bhikṣu Rādha, in the afternoon, having arisen from meditation, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. He fully reported the above matter to the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, in what I said, have I perhaps slandered the World-Honored One? Would others not challenge and question me and cause me to fall into a position of defeat? Did I not speak as he has spoken, not speak in accordance with the Dharma, not speak in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma?"

The Buddha told Rādha: "You spoke truthfully. You did not slander the Tathāgata. You would not be challenged and questioned by others and fall into a position of defeat. You spoke as he has spoken, in accordance with the Dharma, and in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma. Why is that? With regard to form, greed, hatred, and delusion: if these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance; with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, greed, hatred, and delusion: if these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance; for this reason one goes forth under the Tathāgata to cultivate the holy life."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (119)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha.

At that time, a number of non-Buddhist renunciants went to Rādha. Having exchanged greetings with one another, they withdrew and sat to one side, and asked Rādha: "Why have you gone forth under the śramaṇa Gautama to cultivate the holy life?"

Rādha answered: "With regard to form, desire, craving, and delight: if these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance; with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, desire, craving, and delight: if these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance; for this reason I have gone forth under the Tathāgata to cultivate the holy life."

At that time, the non-Buddhist renunciants, hearing these words, were displeased in their minds, rose from their seats, reviled him, and departed.

The bhikṣu Rādha, in the afternoon, having arisen from meditation, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. He fully reported the above to the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, in what I said, have I not slandered the Tathāgata? Would others not challenge and question me and cause me to fall into a position of defeat? Did I not speak as he has spoken, not speak in accordance with the Dharma, not speak in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma?"

The Buddha told Rādha: "You spoke truthfully. You did not slander the Tathāgata. You would not be challenged and questioned by others and fall into a position of defeat. You spoke as he has spoken, in accordance with the Dharma, and in accordance with the proper sequence of the Dharma. Why is that? With regard to form, desire, craving, and delight: if these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance; with regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, desire, craving, and delight: if these be exhausted, if there be separation from desire, cessation, quiescence, and disappearance; for this reason one goes forth under the Tathāgata to cultivate the holy life."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (120)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha.

At that time, the World-Honored One told Rādha: "All form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of that should be contemplated as entirely the work of Māra. All feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of that should be contemplated as entirely the work of Māra."

The Buddha told Rādha: "Is form permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

He asked again: "If impermanent, is it suffering?"

He answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise." He asked again: "Rādha, if something is impermanent and suffering, being a thing subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple see therein form as self, as other than self, or as mutually inherent?"

He answered: "No, World-Honored One." "Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise."

The Buddha told Rādha: "If a well-learned noble disciple does not see these five aggregates of clinging as self or as belonging to self, then with regard to all the world, he takes nothing. Because he takes nothing, he has no attachment. Because he has no attachment, he himself realizes nirvāṇa: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (121)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣu Rādha: "All form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of that is entirely subject to death. All feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of that is entirely subject to death."

The Buddha told Rādha: "Is form permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

He asked again: "If impermanent, is it suffering?"

He answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"Are feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

He asked again: "If impermanent, is it suffering?"

He answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

He asked again: "If something is impermanent and suffering, being a thing subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple see therein self, other than self, or mutual inherence?"

He answered: "No, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Rādha: "If a well-learned noble disciple truly contemplates these five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self, then with regard to all the world, he takes nothing. Because he takes nothing, he has no attachment. Because he has no attachment, he himself realizes nirvāṇa: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (122)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula.

At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha, who addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, what is called a 'sentient being'? What is meant by 'sentient being'?"

The Buddha told Rādha: "That which is dyed by and entangled in form is called a sentient being. That which is dyed by and entangled in feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness is called a sentient being."

The Buddha told Rādha: "I say that regarding the domain of form, one should scatter, destroy, and eliminate it. Regarding the domain of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, one should scatter, destroy, and eliminate it. One should cut off craving and desire. When craving is exhausted, then suffering is exhausted. When suffering is exhausted, I declare that one has reached the end of suffering. It is like the little boys and girls in a village who play and pile up dirt to make city walls, houses, and dwellings, and whose minds delight in and are attached to them. While their craving is not yet exhausted, their desire is not yet exhausted, their longing is not yet exhausted, their thirst is not yet exhausted, their minds constantly delight in, take pleasure in, and guard them, saying: 'My city walls! My houses!' But when toward those mounds of dirt, craving is exhausted, desire is exhausted, longing is exhausted, thirst is exhausted, then with hands they scatter them and with feet they kick them apart, causing them to be dispersed. In the same way, Rādha, when form is scattered, destroyed, and eliminated, and craving is exhausted, when craving is exhausted, then suffering is exhausted. When suffering is exhausted, I declare that one has reached the end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (123)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula.

At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha, who went to the Buddha, bowed his head in homage at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "Excellent, World-Honored One! Please teach me the Dharma in brief. Having heard the Dharma, I shall dwell alone in a quiet place, with concentrated mind in contemplation, dwelling without negligence, reflecting: 'This is why a son of good family shaves off his beard and hair, puts on the dyed robes, and with right faith, leaves the household life to go forth and train in the Way, increasing his diligence, cultivating the holy life, seeing the Dharma for himself, knowing and realizing: "My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence."'"

At that time, the World-Honored One told Rādha: "Excellent, Rādha! You are able to ask such a question before the Buddha. Listen carefully, reflect well, and I shall explain it for you. Rādha, you should know that there is the person, the arising of the person, the cessation of the person, and the path leading to the cessation of the person. What is the person? It refers to the five aggregates of clinging: the aggregate of clinging to form, the aggregates of clinging to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. What is the arising of the person? It is craving for future existence, accompanied by greed and delight, taking pleasure here and there. This is called the arising of the person. What is the cessation of the person? It is when that craving for future existence, accompanied by delight and greed, that takes pleasure here and there, is without remainder abandoned, relinquished, expelled, exhausted, separated from desire, quiescent, and disappeared. This is called the cessation of the person. What is the path leading to the cessation of the person? It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration. This is called the path leading to the cessation of the person. The person should be known. The arising of the person should be cut off. The cessation of the person should be realized. The path leading to the cessation of the person should be cultivated. Rādha, if a well-learned noble disciple, regarding the person, either knows or cuts off, regarding the arising of the person, either knows or cuts off, regarding the cessation of the person, either knows or realizes, and regarding the path leading to the cessation of the person, either knows or cultivates, then, Rādha, he is called one who has cut off craving, separated from craving, broken the fetters, thoroughly ended conceit without remainder, and reached the ultimate end of suffering."

The bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly. He rose from his seat, paid his respects, and departed. After the World-Honored One had thus instructed him, the bhikṣu Rādha, dwelling alone in a quiet place, with concentrated mind in contemplation, reflected: "This is why a good man shaves off his beard and hair, puts on the dyed robes, and with right faith, leaves the household life to go forth and train in the Way, increasing his diligence, cultivating the holy life, seeing the Dharma for himself, knowing and realizing: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'" He became an arhat, with his mind well liberated.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (124)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣu Rādha: "Bhikṣus, all form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of that should be contemplated as entirely Māra. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of that should be contemplated as entirely Māra. Rādha, what do you think: is form permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If impermanent, is it suffering?"

He answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"If something is impermanent and suffering, being a thing subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple see therein a self?"

He answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. Therefore, Rādha, a well-learned noble disciple becomes disenchanted with form, becomes disenchanted with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Disenchanted, he finds no delight. Finding no delight, he is liberated. With the knowledge of liberation, he knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (125)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣu Rādha: "All form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of that is entirely the work of Māra. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise."

The Buddha told Rādha: "What do you think: is form permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

He asked again: "If impermanent, is it suffering?"

He answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

He asked again: "Are feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

He asked again: "If impermanent, is it suffering?"

He answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Rādha: "If something is impermanent and suffering, being a thing subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple see therein self, other than self, or mutual inherence?"

He answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Therefore, Rādha, a well-learned noble disciple becomes disenchanted with form, and disenchanted, he finds no delight. He becomes disenchanted with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, and disenchanted, he finds no delight. Finding no delight, he is liberated. With the knowledge of liberation, he knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

The third sūtra is likewise. The difference is that the Buddha told Rādha: "A well-learned noble disciple, with regard to these five aggregates of clinging, contemplates each aggregate as not self and not belonging to self. Having so contemplated, with regard to all the world, he takes nothing. Because he takes nothing, he has no attachment. Because he has no attachment, he himself realizes nirvāṇa: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (126)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha.

At that time, the World-Honored One told Rādha: "All form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, should be contemplated as entirely subject to death. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise."

The remainder is as previously stated.

---

### (127)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha.

At that time, the World-Honored One told Rādha: "All form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of that should be contemplated as entirely subject to destruction. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. When a well-learned noble disciple contemplates thus, he becomes disenchanted with form, becomes disenchanted with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Disenchanted, he finds no delight. Finding no delight, he is liberated. With the knowledge of liberation, he himself knows: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As I have contemplated subject to destruction, so too one contemplates subject to cessation, contemplates subject to abandonment, contemplates as impermanent, contemplates as suffering, contemplates as empty, contemplates as not-self, contemplates as impermanent, suffering, empty, and not-self, contemplates as subject to illness, contemplates as a boil, contemplates as a thorn, contemplates as killing, contemplates as the root of killing, contemplates as subject to illness, a boil, a thorn, killing, and the root of killing. All such sūtras are as stated above.

---

### (128)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha.

At that time, the World-Honored One told Rādha: "All form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of that should be contemplated as entirely subject to destruction. Having so contemplated, desire and lust for form are cut off. When desire and lust for form are cut off, I declare that the mind is well liberated. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

In this manner, the fourteen sūtras are also as stated above.

---

### (129)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling on Mount Makula. At that time, there was an attendant bhikṣu named Rādha.

At that time, the World-Honored One told Rādha: "All form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of that should be contemplated as entirely subject to destruction. Having contemplated it as subject to destruction, desire and lust for form are cut off. I declare that the mind is well liberated. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Rādha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (130)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If one wishes to cut off the five aggregates of clinging, one should seek a great teacher. Which five? The aggregate of clinging to form, and the aggregates of clinging to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. If one wishes to cut off these five aggregates of clinging, one should seek a great teacher."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

Just as "should cut off," so too "should know, should expel, should still, should abandon" are likewise. Just as "seek a great teacher," so too "one who is a superior teacher, one who is a progressive teacher, one who admonishes, one who is a superior admonisher, one who is a progressive admonisher, one who penetrates, one who broadly penetrates, one who completely penetrates, one who guides, one who broadly guides, one who ultimately guides, one who explains, one who broadly explains, one who explains in proper sequence, one who is upright, one who is a companion, one who is a true good friend, one who is intimate, one who is compassionate, one who is sympathetic, one who values meaning, one who gives comfort, one who values happiness, one who values contact, one who values consolation, one who has desire, one who has diligence, one who has skillful means, one who is industrious, one who is courageous, one who is firm, one who is strong, one who is capable, one who is focused, one whose mind does not retreat, one who firmly upholds, one who constantly practices, one who is not negligent, one who is harmonious, one who reflects, one who is mindful, one who is aware, one who knows, one who has insight, one who has wisdom, one who receives, one who contemplates, one who cultivates the holy life, one who practices the foundations of mindfulness, one who practices right effort, one who practices the bases of spiritual power, one who practices the faculties, one who practices the powers, one who practices the factors of awakening, one who practices the factors of the path, one who practices calm, one who practices insight, one who practices mindfulness of the body, one who practices right recollection" are likewise.

---

### (131)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If any śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas engage with form, they are subject to Māra's control, enter into Māra's hands, are subject to Māra's will, are bound by Māra's bonds, and do not escape Māra's fetters. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas do not engage with form, such śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas are not subject to Māra's control, do not enter into Māra's hands, are not subject to Māra's will, are freed from Māra's bonds, and are released from Māra's fetters. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

In this manner, those who associate with, those who attach to, those who relish, those who are firmly attached to, those who dwell on, those who are driven by latent tendencies, those who pursue, those who discriminate, those who do not relinquish, and those who do not expel: for all such śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, they are subject to Māra's control, as stated above.

---

### (132)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If any śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas do not associate with form, they are not subject to Māra's control, do not enter into Māra's hands, are not subject to Māra's will, are not bound by Māra's bonds, and are released from Māra's fetters. Those who do not engage with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

Up through "expelling form" is likewise.

---

### (133)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Because of what existing, because of what arising, because of what binding and attachment, because of what view of self, are sentient beings covered by ignorance, fettered by craving at their head, driven along the long road, revolving in the cycle of birth and death, flowing and turning in birth and death, not knowing the beginning?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. Excellent, World-Honored One! Please, out of compassion, explain this meaning in full. The bhikṣus, having heard, shall receive and practice it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully, reflect well, and I shall explain it for you. Bhikṣus, because form exists, because of events concerning form, because of binding and attachment to form, because of the view of self in form, sentient beings are covered by ignorance, fettered by craving at their head, driven along the long road, revolving in the cycle of birth and death, flowing and turning in birth and death. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. Bhikṣus, is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

He asked again: "If impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"Thus, bhikṣus, if something is impermanent, it is suffering. Because this suffering exists, because of events concerning it, because of binding and attachment, because of the view of self, sentient beings are covered by ignorance, fettered by craving at their head, driven along the long road, revolving in the cycle of birth and death, flowing and turning in birth and death. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. Therefore, bhikṣus, all form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of that is not self, not other than self, and they do not mutually inhere. This is called right wisdom. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise.

"In this way, what is seen, heard, sensed, and cognized, what is sought and obtained, what is recollected, what is contemplated accordingly: all of that is not self, not other than self, and they do not mutually inhere. This is called right wisdom.

"If there is a view that says, 'There is a self, there is a world, there is this world; it is permanent, everlasting, and not subject to change,' all of that is not self, not other than self, and they do not mutually inhere. This is called right wisdom.

"If further there is a view that says, 'This is not my self, this is not what belongs to my self; there will not be a future self, there will not be what belongs to a future self,' all of that is not self, not other than self, and they do not mutually inhere. This is called right wisdom.

"If a well-learned noble disciple contemplates these six bases of views as not self and not belonging to self, by so contemplating, doubt regarding the Buddha is cut off, doubt regarding the Dharma and the Sangha is cut off. This is called, bhikṣus, a well-learned noble disciple who is no longer capable of performing bodily, verbal, or mental actions that would lead to the three evil destinies. Even if he is negligent, such a noble disciple is certain to progress toward the three awakenings, and after seven more existences among gods and humans, will reach the end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (134)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "A well-learned noble disciple contemplates these six bases of views as not self and not belonging to self. By so contemplating, doubt regarding suffering is cut off, doubt regarding its arising, cessation, and the path is cut off. This is called, bhikṣus, a well-learned noble disciple who is no longer capable of performing bodily, verbal, or mental actions that would lead to the three evil destinies." This is explained in full as above, up to "reaching the end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (135)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus. He spoke extensively as above. The difference is: "If a well-learned noble disciple contemplates these six bases of views as not self and not belonging to self. By so contemplating, doubt regarding the Buddha is cut off, doubt regarding the Dharma, the Sangha, suffering, its arising, cessation, and the path is cut off." This is explained in full as above, up to "reaching the end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (136)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Regarding what existing, because of what arising, because of what binding and attachment, because of what view of self, bhikṣus, are those sentient beings covered by ignorance, fettered by craving at their head, driven along the long road, revolving in the cycle of birth and death, flowing and turning in birth and death, not knowing the beginning?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. Excellent, World-Honored One! Please, out of compassion, explain this meaning in full. The bhikṣus, having heard, shall receive and practice it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully, reflect well, and I shall explain it for you. Bhikṣus, because form exists, because of events concerning form, because of binding and attachment to form, because of the view of self in form, sentient beings are covered by ignorance, fettered by craving at their head, driven along the long road, revolving in the cycle of birth and death, flowing and turning in birth and death. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. Bhikṣus, is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

He asked again: "If impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"Thus, bhikṣus, if something is impermanent, it is suffering. Because this suffering exists, because of events concerning it, because of binding and attachment, because of the view of self, those sentient beings are covered by ignorance, fettered by craving at their head, driven along the long road, revolving in the cycle of birth and death, flowing and turning in birth and death. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise.

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of that is not self, not other than self, and they do not mutually inhere. To see thus is called right wisdom. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise.

"In this way, what is seen, heard, sensed, and cognized, what is sought and obtained, what is recollected, what is contemplated accordingly: all of that is not self, not other than self, and they do not mutually inhere. This is called right wisdom.

"If there is a view that says, 'There is a self, there is this world, there is another world; it is permanent, everlasting, and not subject to change,' all of that is not self, not other than self, and they do not mutually inhere. This is called right wisdom.

"If further there is a view that says, 'This is not my self, this is not what belongs to my self; there will not be a future self, there will not be what belongs to a future self,' all of that is not self, not other than self, and they do not mutually inhere. This is called right wisdom.

"If a well-learned noble disciple contemplates these six bases of views as not self and not belonging to self, by so contemplating, doubt regarding the Buddha is cut off, doubt regarding the Dharma and the Sangha is cut off. This is called, bhikṣus, one who is no longer capable of performing bodily, verbal, or mental actions that would lead to the three evil destinies. Even if negligent, all noble disciples are certain to progress toward the three awakenings, and after seven more existences among gods and humans, will reach the final end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (137)

The second sūtra is likewise. The difference is: doubt regarding suffering, its arising, cessation, and the path is cut off.

---

### (138)

The third sūtra is likewise. The difference is: doubt regarding the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha is cut off, and doubt regarding suffering, its arising, cessation, and the path is cut off.
