# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Seven

---

### (139)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to what, attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sorrow, grief, vexation, and suffering that have not yet arisen come to arise, and sorrow, grief, vexation, and suffering that have already arisen further increase and expand?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. We only wish that you would explain extensively. Having heard, the bhikṣus will receive and practice accordingly."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Because form exists, because of arising from form, being bound to and attached to form, seeing a self in form, sorrow, grief, vexation, and suffering that have not yet arisen come to arise, and sorrow, grief, vexation, and suffering that have already arisen further increase and expand. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. Bhikṣus, what do you think? Is form permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One!"

He asked again: "If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One!"

"Thus, bhikṣus, if what is impermanent is suffering, and because this suffering exists, this matter arises, one is bound to it, attached to it, and sees a self in it; sorrow, grief, vexation, and suffering that have not yet arisen come to arise, and sorrow, grief, vexation, and suffering that have already arisen further increase and expand. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise.

"Therefore, bhikṣus, all forms whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of these are not self, not other than self, and do not mutually abide within one another. This is called right wisdom. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise.

"Furthermore, whatever is seen, heard, sensed, or cognized, whatever arises, is sought, recollected, followed upon with thought, or followed upon with contemplation, all of that is not self, not other than self, and does not mutually abide within one another. This is called right wisdom.

"If one holds the view that there is a self, there is a world, there is this world, there is another world, permanent, eternal, unchanging, all of that is not self, not other than self, and does not mutually abide within one another. This is called right wisdom.

"If furthermore one holds the view that 'this is not my self in this world, this is not what belongs to my self in this world, there will not be my self in the future, there will not be what belongs to my self in the future,' all of that is not self, not other than self, and does not mutually abide within one another. This is called right wisdom.

"If a well-learned noble disciple, with regard to these six bases of views, contemplates them as not self and not belonging to self, and contemplates in this way, then doubt regarding the Buddha is abandoned, doubt regarding the Dharma and the Sangha is abandoned. This is called, bhikṣus, the well-learned noble disciple who is no longer capable of creating bodily, verbal, or mental actions that lead to the three evil destinies. Even if such a one is negligent, the noble disciple is certain to progress toward the three awakenings, and after seven more existences among gods and humans, passing back and forth, will make an end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (140, 141)

The next sūtra is likewise. The difference is: doubt regarding suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the path is abandoned.

The next sūtra is likewise. The difference is: doubt regarding the Buddha, the Dharma, the Sangha, suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the path is abandoned.

---

### (142)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do the fetters of 'I,' 'mine,' and the conceit 'I am' that have not yet arisen come to arise, and the fetters of 'I,' 'mine,' and the conceit 'I am' that have already arisen further increase and expand?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken as above, up to:

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (143, 144)

The second and third sūtras are also as above.

---

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

### (153)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'This self and that are one and the same, not two, not different, not subject to destruction'?"

### (154)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'There is no giving, no offering, no sacrifice; there are no fruits or results of good and evil actions; there is no this world and no other world; there is no mother, no father, no sentient beings spontaneously born; there are no recluses or brāhmaṇas in the world who are arahants, rightly gone, rightly directed, who in this world or another world realize the Dharma through their own direct knowledge and abide in the complete realization, declaring: "Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that there is no further existence"'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

### (155)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'There is no power, no effort, no power of effort, no human endeavor, no human exertion, no human endeavor and exertion, no self-making, no other-making, no self-and-other-making. All people, all sentient beings, all spirits are without means, without power, without strength, without effort, without capacity. Fixed by destiny, by association, by transformation, they experience pleasure and pain in the six destinies'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

### (156)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'Sentient beings live in this world, and after death are annihilated and cease to exist. A person composed of the four great elements, when the body's life ends, earth returns to earth, water returns to water, fire returns to fire, wind returns to wind, and the sense faculties pass into space. The bier and bed make a fifth, and four people carry the dead person to the charnel ground. Until it is burned, one can still recognize it; once burned, the bones are the color of a pigeon. The conceited know giving; the clever know receiving. If anyone says there is something after death, all of that is false, deceptive, and a lie. Whether foolish or wise, after death in another world, both are annihilated and cease to exist'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'The defilements of sentient beings are without cause and without condition'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'Sentient beings are without knowledge and without vision, without cause and without condition'?"

### (160)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements?"

At that time, the bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

### (161)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'There are seven bodies that are not made, not produced by making, not transformed, not produced by transformation; they do not kill, are unmoving, and are firm. What are the seven? They are the earth body, the water body, the fire body, the wind body, pleasure, pain, and life. These seven bodies are not made, not produced by making, not transformed, not produced by transformation; they do not kill, are unmoving, firm, do not turn, do not change, and do not oppress one another. Whether merit or evil, whether merit and evil, whether suffering or pleasure, whether suffering and pleasure, whether the heads of warriors or the heads of men, they also do not oppress the world. If a knife were to pass back and forth through the gaps between the life, the body, and the seven bodies, it would not harm life. In this there is no killing, no killer, no binding, no binder, no thinking, no thinker, no teaching, no teacher'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

### (162)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'Acting, causing to act, cutting, causing to cut, cooking, causing to cook, killing, causing to kill, harming sentient beings, stealing others' property, engaging in sexual misconduct, knowingly speaking falsehoods, drinking alcohol, breaking through walls, breaking locks, plundering, blocking roads, raiding villages, raiding cities, raiding people; with extremely sharp wheel-blades of lead, slashing and hacking, making great heaps of flesh, training in this way: "This is not a cause of evil, nor does it attract evil. Going south of the Ganges to kill and harm, and coming north of the Ganges to perform great assemblies, this is not a cause of merit or evil, nor does it attract merit or evil. Generous giving, self-control, guarding, acting for the benefit of others, sharing benefits: in all of this, there is no making of merit"'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

### (163)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'In this there are fourteen hundred thousand gates of birth, sixty thousand and six hundred and five kinds of action, three kinds of action, two kinds of action, one kind of action, half an action, sixty-two paths, sixty-two inner eons, one hundred and twenty hells, one hundred and thirty sense faculties, thirty-six realms of desire, forty-nine thousand nāga families, forty-nine thousand garuḍa families, forty-nine thousand heterodox ascetic families, forty-nine thousand heterodox renunciants, seven eons with perception, seven eons without perception, seven asuras, seven piśācas, seven heavens, seven humans, seven hundred seas, seven dreams, seven hundred dreams, seven precipices, seven hundred precipices, seven awakenings, seven hundred awakenings, six births, ten stages of progress, eight great noble grounds. Throughout these eighty-four thousand great eons, whether foolish or wise, passing back and forth through them, one ultimately reaches the end of suffering.' There is no recluse or brāhmaṇa who says: 'I constantly maintain moral precepts, undertake various austerities, and cultivate the holy life, so that unripened karma becomes ripened, and already-ripened karma is discarded; progress and regression cannot be known.' This suffering and pleasure is permanent, and the measure of birth and death is fixed. It is like a ball of thread thrown into the air that gradually descends and comes to rest upon the ground of its own accord. Likewise, the measure of birth and death throughout eighty-four thousand great eons is also like this."

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

### (164)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'The wind does not blow, fire does not burn, water does not flow, arrows are not shot, pregnant women do not give birth, milk cannot be milked, the sun and moon neither rise nor set, and whether it is bright or dark cannot be known'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

### (165)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'This Great Brahmā is sovereign, creates naturally, and is the father of all sentient beings'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

### (166)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'Form is the self, all else is a mere name; the formless is the self, all else is a mere name; both form and formless is the self, all else is a mere name; neither form nor formless is the self, all else is a mere name; the self is finite, all else is a mere name; the self is infinite, all else is a mere name; the self is both finite and infinite, all else is a mere name; the self is neither finite nor infinite, all else is a mere name. One perception, various perceptions, many perceptions, immeasurable perceptions; the self is exclusively happy, exclusively suffering, both suffering and happy, or neither suffering nor happy, all else is a mere name'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," extensively spoken in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

### (167)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'Form is the self, all else is a delusion; neither form nor not-form is the self, all else is a delusion; the self is finite, all else is a delusion; the self is infinite, all else is a delusion; the self is neither finite nor infinite, all else is a delusion. The self has one perception, various perceptions, few perceptions, immeasurable perceptions; the self is exclusively happy, exclusively suffering, both suffering and happy, or neither suffering nor happy'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

### (168)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'The world is eternal; the world is not eternal; the world is both eternal and not eternal; the world is neither eternal nor not eternal; the world is finite; the world is infinite; the world is both finite and infinite; the world is neither finite nor infinite; the life-force is the same as the body; the life-force is different from the body; the Tathāgata exists after death; the Tathāgata does not exist after death; the Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death; the Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

### (169)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'The self of the world is eternal; the self of the world is not eternal; the self of the world is both eternal and not eternal; the self of the world is neither eternal nor not eternal; the self is suffering that is eternal; the self is suffering that is not eternal; the self is suffering that is both eternal and not eternal; the self is suffering that is neither eternal nor not eternal; the self of the world is self-made; the self of the world is other-made; the self of the world is both self-made and other-made; the self of the world is neither self-made nor other-made, arising without cause; the suffering of the self of the world is self-made; the suffering of the self of the world is other-made; the suffering of the self of the world is both self-made and other-made; the suffering of the self of the world is neither self-made nor other-made, arising without cause'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

### (170)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'If one is without the five kinds of sensual pleasure and entertainment, this is the visible-life nirvāṇa; if one, separated from evil and unwholesome dharmas, with initial thought and sustained thought, with joy and happiness born of seclusion, enters the first dhyāna, up to the fourth dhyāna, this is the ultimate nirvāṇa'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

### (171)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Due to the existence of what, arising from what, bound to and attached to what, seeing a self in what, do sentient beings produce such views and such statements as: 'If the coarse material form of the four great elements is cut off and destroyed, ceasing to exist, this is called the true annihilation of the self; if furthermore the self in the desire realm is cut off and destroyed, ceasing to exist after death, this is called the true annihilation of the self; if furthermore the self in the form realm is cut off and destroyed after death, ceasing to exist, this is called the true annihilation of the self; if one attains the base of infinite space, the base of infinite consciousness, the base of nothingness, or the base of neither-perception-nor-non-perception, and the self after death is cut off and destroyed, ceasing to exist, this is called the true annihilation of the self'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma," and so forth extensively spoken, in sequence as in the above three sūtras.

---

### (172)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Whatever dharma is impermanent should be abandoned. Having abandoned that dharma, it will be for one's benefit and welfare, for long-lasting peace and happiness. What dharma is impermanent? Form is impermanent; feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (173)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Whatever past impermanent dharma should be abandoned. Having abandoned that dharma, it will be for one's benefit and welfare, for long-lasting peace and happiness. What is the past impermanent dharma? Past form is an impermanent dharma, past desire is an impermanent dharma; that dharma should be abandoned. Having abandoned that dharma, it will be for one's benefit and welfare, for long-lasting peace and happiness. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

Likewise for the future, the present, the past and present, the future and present, the past and future, and the past, future, and present.

---

### (174)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In order to abandon impermanent dharmas, one should seek a great teacher. What is the impermanent dharma? Form is an impermanent dharma; in order to abandon that dharma, one should seek a great teacher. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

Likewise for the past, the future, the present, and the past, future, and present, one should seek a great teacher; eight kinds of sūtras are thus. Various teachings, following along, establishing, broadly establishing, universally establishing, guiding, broadly guiding, ultimately guiding, explaining, broadly explaining, explaining in accordance, a second companion, a true spiritual friend, same intention, compassion, sympathy, valuing benefit, valuing comfort, delight, valuing contact, valuing ease, desire, effort, means, broad means, capable means, firmness, strength, courageous body and mind, courageous and hard to overcome, gathering in constant learning, non-negligence in cultivation, reflection, mindfulness, awakening, knowing, clarity, wisdom, discernment, deliberation, holy life, psychic powers, foundations of mindfulness, right efforts, faculties, powers, factors of awakening, path factors, calm, insight, mindfulness of the body, right recollection: for each one, eight sūtras, also as stated above. As with the meaning of abandoning, likewise the meaning of knowing, the meaning of exhausting, the meaning of removing, the meaning of ceasing, and the meaning of relinquishing are also thus.

---

### (175)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose a person's head or clothing were on fire; how should one rescue it?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! One should arouse supreme desire and earnestly apply skillful means to quickly extinguish it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Even if one's head or clothing is burning, one may briefly forget about it. But the raging fire of impermanence should be entirely eliminated, cut off, and extinguished. In order to cut off the fire of impermanence, one should diligently seek a great teacher. What impermanent thing should one cut off by diligently seeking a great teacher? It is to cut off the impermanence of form by diligently seeking a great teacher, and to cut off the impermanence of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness by diligently seeking a great teacher."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with cutting off impermanence, likewise for past impermanence, future impermanence, present impermanence, past and future impermanence, past and present impermanence, future and present impermanence, and past, future, and present impermanence: thus are the eight kinds of sūtras with the simile of the head ablaze, as extensively spoken above. As with seeking a great teacher, likewise seeking various teachings, following teachings, as extensively spoken above. As with the meaning of abandoning, likewise the meaning of knowing, the meaning of exhausting, the meaning of removing, the meaning of ceasing, the meaning of relinquishing, the meaning of extinguishing, and the meaning of disappearing are also thus.

---

### (176)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In order to cut off impermanence, one should cultivate the contemplation of the body internally, abiding in the observation of the body. What dharma is impermanent? Form is impermanent; in order to cut off that, one should cultivate the contemplation of the body internally, abiding in the observation of the body. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent; in order to cut off those, one should cultivate the contemplation of the body internally, abiding in the observation of the body."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with impermanence, likewise for past form being impermanent, future form, present form, past and future form, past and present form, future and present form, and past, future, and present form being impermanent; in order to cut off those, one should cultivate the observation of the body, abiding in the contemplation of the body. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise.

As with the eight kinds of cultivating the contemplation of the body internally, abiding in the observation of the body, likewise for the external contemplation of the body, the internal and external contemplation of the body, the internal contemplation of feelings in feelings, the external contemplation of feelings in feelings, the internal and external contemplation of feelings in feelings, the internal contemplation of mind in mind, the external contemplation of mind in mind, the internal and external contemplation of mind in mind, the internal contemplation of dharmas in dharmas, the external contemplation of dharmas in dharmas, and the internal and external contemplation of dharmas in dharmas, abiding: for each one, eight sūtras, also as stated above.

As with the meaning of cutting off impermanence through cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness, likewise for the meaning of knowing, the meaning of exhausting, the meaning of removing, the meaning of ceasing, the meaning of relinquishing, the meaning of extinguishing, and the meaning of disappearing, through cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness, also as stated above.

---

### (177)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose a person's head or clothing were on fire; how should one rescue it?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! One should arouse supreme desire and earnestly apply skillful means to quickly extinguish it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Even if one's head or clothing is burning, one may briefly forget about it. But the raging fire of impermanence should be entirely cut off. In order to cut off the fire of impermanence, one should cultivate the contemplation of the body internally, abiding in the observation of the body. How does one cut off the fire of impermanence by cultivating the contemplation of the body internally, abiding in the observation of the body? Form is impermanent; in order to cut off that, one cultivates the contemplation of the body internally, abiding in the observation of the body. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent; in order to cut off those, one cultivates the contemplation of the body internally, abiding in the observation of the body," extensively spoken up to:

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with impermanence, likewise for past impermanence, future impermanence, present impermanence, past and future impermanence, past and present impermanence, future and present impermanence, and past, future, and present impermanence. As with the internal contemplation of the body, abiding in the observation of the body: eight sūtras; likewise for the external contemplation of the body: eight sūtras; and the internal and external contemplation of the body: eight sūtras, as stated above.

As with the twenty-four sūtras on the body foundation of mindfulness, likewise for the feeling foundation of mindfulness, the mind foundation of mindfulness, and the dharma foundation of mindfulness: twenty-four sūtras each, as stated above.

As with the ninety-six sūtras on cutting off impermanence, likewise for knowing, removing, exhausting, ceasing, relinquishing, extinguishing, and disappearing: ninety-six sūtras for each one, also as stated above.

---

### (178)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose a person's head or clothing were on fire; how should one rescue it?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Arouse supreme desire and earnestly apply skillful means to quickly extinguish it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Even if one's head or clothing is burning, one may briefly forget about it. But the raging fire of impermanence should be entirely cut off. In order to cut off the fire of impermanence, evil and unwholesome dharmas that have already arisen should be abandoned; one should arouse desire, exert effort, collect the mind, and cause it to increase. What impermanent dharma should one cut off, so that one abandons already-arisen evil and unwholesome dharmas by arousing desire, applying means, collecting the mind, and making progress? It is because form is impermanent, because feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent and should be cut off, that one abandons already-arisen evil and unwholesome dharmas by arousing desire, applying means, collecting the mind, and making progress," extensively spoken up to:

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the sūtra on impermanence, likewise for past impermanence, future impermanence, present impermanence, past and future impermanence, past and present impermanence, future and present impermanence, and past, future, and present impermanence: eight sūtras, also as stated above.

As with abandoning already-arisen evil and unwholesome dharmas, likewise for preventing unarisen evil and unwholesome dharmas from arising, causing unarisen wholesome dharmas to arise, and causing already-arisen wholesome dharmas to increase and expand, by arousing desire, applying means, collecting the mind, and making progress: eight sūtras each, also as stated above.

As with the thirty-two sūtras on cutting off impermanence, likewise for knowing, removing, exhausting, ceasing, relinquishing, extinguishing, and disappearing: thirty-two sūtras for each one, extensively spoken as above.

---

### (179)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose a person's head or clothing were on fire; how should one rescue it?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! One should arouse supreme desire and earnestly apply skillful means to quickly extinguish it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Even if one's head or clothing is burning, one may briefly forget about it. But the raging fire of impermanence should be entirely cut off. In order to cut off the fire of impermanence, one should cultivate the base of psychic power endowed with concentration of desire and the factor of striving. What impermanent dharma should one cut off? One should cut off the impermanence of form, and cut off the impermanence of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, by cultivating the base of psychic power endowed with concentration of desire and the factor of striving," as the sūtra extensively states, up to:

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with impermanence, likewise for past impermanence, future impermanence, present impermanence, past and future impermanence, past and present impermanence, future and present impermanence, and past, future, and present impermanence: eight sūtras, also as stated above.

As with cultivating concentration of desire, likewise concentration of effort, concentration of intention, and concentration of investigation are also thus. As with the thirty-two sūtras on cutting off, likewise for knowing, removing, exhausting, ceasing, relinquishing, extinguishing, and disappearing: thirty-two sūtras for each one, also as stated above.

### (180)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose a person's head or clothing were on fire; how should one rescue it?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! One should arouse supreme desire and earnestly apply skillful means to quickly extinguish it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Even if one's head or clothing is burning, one may briefly forget about it. But the raging fire of impermanence should be entirely cut off. In order to cut off the fire of impermanence, one should cultivate the faculty of faith. What impermanent dharma should one cut off? One should cut off the impermanence of form, and cut off the impermanence of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, by cultivating the faculty of faith," and so forth extensively spoken, up to:

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with impermanence, likewise for past impermanence, future impermanence, present impermanence, past and future impermanence, past and present impermanence, future and present impermanence, and past, future, and present impermanence, also as stated above.

As with the eight sūtras on the faculty of faith, likewise for cultivating the faculty of effort, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom: eight sūtras each, also as stated above.

As with the forty sūtras on cutting off, likewise for knowing, removing, exhausting, ceasing, relinquishing, extinguishing, and disappearing: forty sūtras each, also as stated above.

### (181)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose a person's head or clothing were on fire; how should one rescue it?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! One should arouse supreme desire and earnestly apply skillful means to quickly extinguish it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Even if one's head or clothing is burning, one may briefly forget about it. But the raging fire of impermanence should be entirely cut off. In order to cut off the fire of impermanence, one should cultivate the power of faith. What impermanent thing should one cut off by cultivating the power of faith? It is to cut off the impermanence of form by cultivating the power of faith, and to cut off the impermanence of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness by cultivating the power of faith," and so forth extensively spoken, up to:

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the forty sūtras on cutting off, likewise for knowing, removing, exhausting, ceasing, relinquishing, extinguishing, and disappearing: forty sūtras for each one, also as stated above.

### (182)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose a person's head or clothing were on fire; how should one rescue it?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! One should arouse supreme desire and earnestly apply skillful means to quickly extinguish it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Even if one's head or clothing is burning, one may briefly forget about it. But the raging fire of impermanence should be entirely cut off. In order to cut off the fire of impermanence, one should cultivate the awakening factor of mindfulness. What impermanent dharma should one cut off by cultivating the awakening factor of mindfulness? It is to cut off the impermanence of form by cultivating the awakening factor of mindfulness, and to cut off the impermanence of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness by cultivating the awakening factor of mindfulness," and so forth extensively spoken, up to:

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with impermanence, likewise for past impermanence, future impermanence, present impermanence, past and future impermanence, past and present impermanence, future and present impermanence, and past, future, and present impermanence: eight sūtras, as stated above.

As with the eight sūtras on the awakening factor of mindfulness, likewise the awakening factor of investigation of dharmas, the awakening factor of effort, the awakening factor of joy, the awakening factor of tranquility, the awakening factor of equanimity, and the awakening factor of concentration: eight sūtras for each one, also as stated above.

As with the fifty-six sūtras on cutting off, likewise for knowing, removing, exhausting, ceasing, relinquishing, extinguishing, and disappearing: fifty-six sūtras for each one, as stated above.

### (183)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose a person's head or clothing were on fire; how should one rescue it?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! One should arouse supreme desire and earnestly apply skillful means to quickly extinguish it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Even if one's head or clothing is burning, one may briefly forget about it. But the raging fire of impermanence should be entirely cut off. In order to cut off the fire of impermanence, one should cultivate right view. What impermanent dharma should one cut off by cultivating right view? One should cut off the impermanence of form by cultivating right view, and cut off the impermanence of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness by cultivating right view," and so forth extensively spoken, up to:

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with impermanence, likewise for past impermanence, future impermanence, present impermanence, past and future impermanence, past and present impermanence, future and present impermanence, and past, future, and present impermanence, also as stated above.

As with the eight sūtras on right view, likewise right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration: eight sūtras for each one, also as stated above.

As with the sixty-four sūtras on cutting off, likewise for knowing, removing, exhausting, ceasing, relinquishing, extinguishing, and disappearing: sixty-four sūtras for each one, also as stated above.

### (184)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose a person's head or clothing were on fire; how should one rescue it?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! One should arouse supreme desire and earnestly apply skillful means to quickly extinguish it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Even if one's head or clothing is burning, one may briefly forget about it. But the raging fire of impermanence should be entirely cut off without remainder. In order to cut off the fire of impermanence, one should cultivate the path of suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the way. What impermanent dharma should one cut off by cultivating the path of suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the way? It is to cut off the impermanence of form by cultivating the path of suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the way; and to cut off the impermanence of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness by cultivating the path of suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the way," and so forth extensively spoken, up to:

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with impermanence, likewise for past impermanence, future impermanence, present impermanence, past and future impermanence, past and present impermanence, future and present impermanence, and past, future, and present impermanence, also as stated above.

As with the eight sūtras on the path of suffering, arising, cessation, and the way, likewise the path of suffering and cessation, the path of happiness and non-cessation, and the path of happiness and cessation: eight sūtras for each one, also as stated above.

As with the thirty-two sūtras on cutting off, likewise for knowing, removing, exhausting, ceasing, relinquishing, extinguishing, and disappearing: thirty-two sūtras for each one, also as stated above.

---

### (185)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose a person's head or clothing were on fire; how should one rescue it?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! One should arouse supreme desire and earnestly apply skillful means to quickly extinguish it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Even if one's head or clothing is burning, one may briefly forget about it. But the raging fire of impermanence should be entirely cut off without remainder. In order to cut off the fire of impermanence, one should cultivate the Dharma phrase of non-greed. What impermanent dharma should one cut off by cultivating the Dharma phrase of non-greed? One should cut off the impermanence of form by cultivating the Dharma phrase of non-greed, and cut off the impermanence of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness by cultivating the Dharma phrase of non-greed," and so forth extensively spoken, up to:

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with impermanence, likewise for past impermanence, future impermanence, present impermanence, past and future impermanence, past and present impermanence, future and present impermanence, and past, future, and present impermanence, also as stated above.

As with the eight sūtras on cultivating the Dharma phrase of non-greed, likewise non-hatred, non-delusion, and other phrases, right phrases, Dharma phrases: eight sūtras for each one, as stated above.

As with the twenty-four sūtras on cutting off, likewise for knowing, removing, exhausting, ceasing, relinquishing, extinguishing, and disappearing: twenty-four sūtras for each one, also as stated above.

---

### (186)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose a person's head or clothing were on fire; how should one rescue it?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! One should arouse supreme desire and earnestly apply skillful means to quickly extinguish it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Even if one's head or clothing is burning, one may briefly forget about it. But the raging fire of impermanence should be entirely cut off. In order to cut off the fire of impermanence, one should cultivate calm. What impermanent dharma should one cut off by cultivating calm? It is to cut off the impermanence of form by cultivating calm, and to cut off the impermanence of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness by cultivating calm," and so forth extensively spoken, up to:

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with impermanence, likewise for past impermanence, future impermanence, present impermanence, past and future impermanence, past and present impermanence, future and present impermanence, and past, future, and present impermanence, also as stated above.

As with the eight sūtras on cultivating calm, likewise the eight sūtras on cultivating insight, also as stated above.

As with the sixteen sūtras on cutting off, likewise for knowing, removing, exhausting, ceasing, relinquishing, extinguishing, and disappearing: sixteen sūtras for each one, also as stated above.

"All forms whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of them are not self, not other than self, and do not mutually abide within one another; knowing this as it truly is. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. When a well-learned noble disciple contemplates rightly in this way, one generates disenchantment toward form; generates disenchantment toward feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Being disenchanted, one does not delight. Not delighting, one is liberated. Being liberated, there is the knowledge of liberation: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that there is no further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

"As with impermanence, likewise shaking, revolving, weakness and decay, breaking apart, being swept away swiftly, rotting and decaying, being precarious and faltering, being inconstant, being insecure, being subject to change, being vexation and suffering, being a calamity, being Māra's evil, being Māra's power, being Māra's instrument; like foam, like a bubble, like a plantain trunk, like an illusion; being paltry and inferior, being craved and coveted, being the mark of killing, being a sword, being envy and jealousy, being mutual harm, being diminishment, being decline and depletion, being bondage, being a beating, being a malignant sore, being an abscess, being a sharp thorn, being an affliction, being a punishment; being the aggregates, being coverings, being a danger, being a place of sorrow, grief, being a bad friend; being suffering, being empty, being not-self, being not belonging to self; being an enemy's chains; being not beneficial, being not comforting; being heat and vexation, being without shade, being without refuge, being without shelter, being without reliance, being without protection; being subject to birth, being subject to old age, being subject to sickness, being subject to death, being subject to sorrow and grief, being subject to vexation and suffering; being without power, being weak and inferior, being undesirable, being enticing, being nourishing, being involving suffering, being involving killing, being involving vexation, being involving heat, being involving characteristics, being involving blowing, being involving grasping; being deeply perilous, being rough and difficult, being improper, being violent, being involving greed, being involving hatred, being involving delusion, being non-abiding, being burning, being obstructing, being a disaster, being subject to arising, being subject to cessation; being a heap of bones, being a lump of flesh, being a held torch, being a fire pit; like a poisonous snake, like a dream, like something borrowed, like the fruit of a tree, like a butcher of cattle, like a killer of people, like dew touched upon, like being submerged in water, like a swift current, like a thread being woven, like a wheel passing through water, like a bouncing stick, like a poisonous bottle, like a poisonous body, like a poisonous flower, like a poisonous fruit, being the agitation of affliction. Thus, bhikṣus, up to cutting off the impermanence of past, future, and present, up to extinction and disappearing, one should cultivate calm and insight.

"What past, future, and present dharma of impermanence, up to extinction and disappearing, should one cut off by cultivating calm and insight? It is to cut off form's past, future, and present impermanence, up to extinction and disappearing, by cultivating calm and insight. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise.

"Therefore, all forms whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of them are not self, not other than self, and do not mutually abide within one another; knowing this as it truly is. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise.

"When a well-learned noble disciple contemplates in this way, one generates disenchantment toward form; generates disenchantment toward feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Being disenchanted, one does not delight. Not delighting, one is liberated. Being liberated, there is the knowledge of liberation: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that there is no further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (187)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "When one is accomplished in a single dharma, one is no longer capable of knowing that form is impermanent, of knowing that feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent. What is the single dharma one is accomplished in? It is greed. When one is accomplished in the single dharma of greed, one is not capable of knowing that form is impermanent, of knowing that feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent. What is the single dharma one is accomplished in? It is non-greed. When one is accomplished in the dharma of non-greed, one is capable of knowing that form is impermanent, capable of knowing that feeling, perception, formations,
