# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Eight

---

### (188)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should rightly observe that the eye is impermanent. One who observes in this way is said to have right view. Because of right observation, disenchantment arises. Because disenchantment arises, one becomes free from delight and free from craving. Because one is free from delight and craving, I say the mind is rightly liberated. Likewise with the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind: being free from delight and free from craving, because one is free from delight and craving, bhikṣus, I say the mind is rightly liberated. One whose mind is rightly liberated is able to declare of oneself: 'My birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with impermanence, so too with suffering, emptiness, and non-self: it is likewise spoken.

---

### (189)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Regarding the eye, you should rightly contemplate and observe its impermanence. Why is this? Because by rightly contemplating and observing the eye as impermanent, desire and craving for the eye are cut off. Because desire and craving are cut off, I say the mind is rightly liberated. Regarding the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind: because of rightly contemplating and observing them, desire and craving are cut off. When desire and craving are cut off, I say the mind is rightly liberated. Thus, bhikṣus, one whose mind is rightly liberated is able to declare of oneself: 'My birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (190)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If regarding the eye one does not recognize, does not know, does not cut off, and does not become free from desire, one is not capable of rightly making a complete end of suffering. Regarding the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind, it is likewise. Bhikṣus, if regarding the eye one recognizes, knows, cuts off, and becomes free from desire, one is capable of rightly making a complete end of suffering. Regarding the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind, if one recognizes, knows, cuts off, and becomes free from desire, one is capable of rightly making a complete end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (191)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If regarding the eye one does not recognize, does not know, does not cut off, and does not become free from desire, one is not capable of crossing beyond the suffering of birth, old age, sickness, and death. Regarding the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind, if one does not recognize, does not know, does not cut off, and does not become free from desire, one is not capable of crossing beyond the suffering of birth, old age, sickness, and death. Bhikṣus, if regarding the eye one recognizes, knows, cuts off, and becomes free from desire, one is capable of crossing beyond the suffering of birth, old age, sickness, and death. Regarding the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind, if one recognizes, knows, cuts off, and becomes free from desire, one is capable of crossing beyond the suffering of birth, old age, sickness, and death."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (192)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If regarding the eye one has not become free from desire and the mind is not liberated, one is not capable of rightly making a complete end of suffering. Regarding the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind, if one has not become free from desire and the mind is not liberated, one is not capable of rightly making a complete end of suffering. Bhikṣus, if regarding the eye one becomes free from desire and the mind is liberated, that person is capable of rightly making a complete end of suffering. Regarding the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind, if one becomes free from desire and the mind is liberated, one is capable of rightly making a complete end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (193)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If regarding the eye and forms one has not become free from desire and the mind is not liberated, one is not capable of crossing beyond the suffering of birth, old age, sickness, and death. Regarding the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind, if one has not become free from desire and the mind is not liberated, one is not capable of crossing beyond the suffering of birth, old age, sickness, and death. Bhikṣus, if regarding the eye and forms one becomes free from desire and the mind is liberated, one is capable of crossing beyond the suffering of birth, old age, sickness, and death. Regarding the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind, if one becomes free from desire and the mind is liberated, one is capable of crossing beyond the suffering of birth, old age, sickness, and death."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (194)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If one produces delight in the eye, then one produces delight in suffering. If one produces delight in suffering, I say that person is not liberated from suffering. If one produces delight in the ear, nose, tongue, body, or mind, then one produces delight in suffering. If one produces delight in suffering, I say that person is not liberated from suffering.

"Bhikṣus, if one does not produce delight in the eye, then one does not produce delight in suffering. If one does not produce delight in suffering, I say that person is liberated from suffering. If one does not produce delight in the ear, nose, tongue, body, or mind, then one does not produce delight in suffering. If one does not produce delight in suffering, I say that person is liberated from suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (195)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "All is impermanent. What is this 'all that is impermanent'? The eye is impermanent. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether painful feelings, pleasant feelings, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant feelings, these too are impermanent. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. A well-learned noble disciple who observes thus develops disenchantment with the eye, with forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether painful feelings, pleasant feelings, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant feelings; regarding these one develops disenchantment. Regarding the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind; sounds, odors, tastes, tangibles, and mental objects; mind-consciousness, mind-contact, and the feelings born from mind-contact as condition, whether painful feelings, pleasant feelings, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant feelings, regarding these too one develops disenchantment. Being disenchanted, one does not delight. Not delighting, one is liberated. In liberation, the knowledge and vision arises: 'My birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the sūtra on impermanence, so too with suffering, emptiness, and non-self: it is likewise spoken.

---

### (196)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "All is impermanent. What is this 'all that is impermanent'? The eye is impermanent. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, these too are impermanent. Likewise, the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind; mental objects, mind-consciousness, mind-contact, and the feelings born from mind-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, these too are impermanent. A well-learned noble disciple who observes thus is liberated with regard to the eye. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, from these too one is liberated. Likewise, the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind; mental objects, mind-consciousness, mind-contact, and the feelings born from mind-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, from these too one is liberated. I say that person is liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As spoken regarding all being impermanent, so too with all being suffering, all being empty, all being non-self, all being phenomena of vain activities, all being phenomena of destruction, all being phenomena of arising, all being phenomena of aging, all being phenomena of sickness, all being phenomena of death, all being phenomena of sorrow and grief, all being phenomena of affliction, all being phenomena of origination, all being phenomena of cessation, all being phenomena to be fully known, all being phenomena to be comprehended, all being phenomena to be cut off, all being phenomena to be awakened to, all being phenomena to be directly realized, all being Māra, all being Māra's power, all being Māra's implements, all being burning, all being blazing, all being on fire: all are extensively spoken as in the above two sūtras.

---

### (197)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Gajāśīrṣa shrine, together with a thousand bhikṣus, all of whom were formerly matted-hair brāhmaṇas.

At that time, the World-Honored One gave the thousand bhikṣus three kinds of teaching demonstrations. What are the three? The demonstration of miraculous powers, the demonstration of reading others' minds, and the demonstration of instruction.

The demonstration of miraculous powers: The World-Honored One, in accordance with what was appropriate, displayed entering dhyāna and meditative absorption. Rising into the sky, he went to the east and performed the four deportments: walking, standing, sitting, and lying down. He entered the fire samādhi and emitted various kinds of fire-light: blue, yellow, red, white, crimson, and crystal-colored. He manifested water and fire together. Sometimes fire came forth from the lower part of his body while water came forth from the upper part, and sometimes fire came forth from the upper part while water came forth from the lower part. In all four directions around, it was likewise. At that time, the World-Honored One, having performed various miraculous transformations, sat down in the assembly. This is called the demonstration of miraculous powers.

The demonstration of reading others' minds: Knowing that mind, knowing that intention, knowing that consciousness, knowing what that person should think, what that person should not think, what that person should relinquish, and what that person should realize and abide in through direct experience. This is called the demonstration of reading others' minds.

The demonstration of instruction: As the World-Honored One said: "Bhikṣus, all is burning. What is this 'all that is burning'? The eye is burning. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, these too are burning. Likewise, the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are burning. Mental objects, mind-consciousness, mind-contact, and the feelings born from mind-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, these too are burning. With what are they burning? Burning with the fire of craving, burning with the fire of hatred, burning with the fire of delusion, burning with the fire of birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering."

At that time, the thousand bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, did not give rise to further outflows, and their minds attained liberation. When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (198)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

At that time, the Venerable Rāhula went to where the Buddha was, paid homage at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and addressed the Buddha saying: "World-Honored One, how should one know and how should one see, so that regarding this body of inner consciousness and all external signs, the attachment bound by the conceits of 'I,' 'mine,' and 'I am' does not arise?"

At that time, the World-Honored One said to Rāhula: "Excellent, Rāhula! You are able to ask the Tathāgata about such a profound meaning."

The Buddha told Rāhula: "Regarding the eye, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether superior or inferior, whether far or near, all of that is neither self, nor other than self, nor existing within the other, as known in accordance with reality. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise.

"Rāhula, knowing in this way and seeing in this way, regarding this body of inner consciousness and all external signs, the attachment bound by the conceits of 'I,' 'mine,' and 'I am' does not arise. Rāhula, when the attachment bound by the conceits of 'I,' 'mine,' and 'I am' does not arise, Rāhula, this is called cutting off craving, overturning the fetters, rightly and completely penetrating, and reaching the final end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Rāhula, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the internal sense bases, so too with the external sense bases: forms, sounds, odors, tastes, tangibles, and mental objects; eye-consciousness, ear-consciousness, nose-consciousness, tongue-consciousness, body-consciousness, and mind-consciousness; eye-contact, ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact; feelings born from eye-contact, feelings born from ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact; perceptions born from eye-contact, perceptions born from ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact; volitions born from eye-contact, volitions born from ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact; craving born from eye-contact, craving born from ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact: it is also spoken as above.

---

### (199)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One said to Rāhula: "How should one know and how should one see, so that regarding this body of consciousness and all external signs, there is no attachment bound by the conceits of 'I,' 'mine,' and 'I am'?"

Rāhula addressed the Buddha saying: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. Excellent, World-Honored One! May you extensively explain this meaning for the bhikṣus. The bhikṣus, having heard it, will receive and practice accordingly."

The Buddha told Rāhula: "Excellent! Listen carefully. I shall explain it for you. Regarding all eyes, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether superior or inferior, whether far or near, all of that is neither self, nor other than self, nor existing within the other, as rightly observed in accordance with reality. Rāhula, the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise.

"Rāhula, knowing in this way and seeing in this way, regarding this body of inner consciousness and all external signs, the attachment bound by the conceits of 'I,' 'mine,' and 'I am' does not arise. Rāhula, such a bhikṣu has transcended duality, is free from all signs, and is liberated in quiescence. Rāhula, such a bhikṣu has cut off all craving and desire, overturned all fetters, and reached the final end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Rāhula, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the internal sense bases, so too with the external sense bases, up to and including the feelings born from mind-contact as condition: it is likewise extensively spoken.

---

### (200)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Rāhula went to where the Buddha was, paid homage at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and addressed the Buddha saying: "Excellent, World-Honored One! Please teach me the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I will go to a solitary, quiet place and with focused concentration contemplate, dwelling in heedfulness. In a solitary, quiet place, with focused concentration contemplating, dwelling in heedfulness, I shall thus reflect: 'This is why a clansman shaves off his hair and beard, puts on the faith of homelessness, leaves the household to study the Way, cultivates and upholds the holy life, and sees the Dharma, knowing for himself and realizing: "My birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence."'"

At that time, the World-Honored One examined Rāhula's mind and saw that his wisdom for liberation was not yet mature and he was not yet capable of receiving the higher teaching. He asked Rāhula: "Have you already taught others the five aggregates of clinging?"

Rāhula addressed the Buddha: "Not yet, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Rāhula: "You should teach others the five aggregates of clinging."

At that time, Rāhula, having received the Buddha's instruction, at another time taught others the five aggregates of clinging. Having finished teaching, he returned to where the Buddha was, paid homage at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and addressed the Buddha saying: "World-Honored One, I have already taught others the five aggregates of clinging. May the World-Honored One please teach me the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I will go to a solitary, quiet place and with focused concentration contemplate, dwelling in heedfulness, up to knowing for myself that I will not receive further existence."

At that time, the World-Honored One again examined Rāhula's mind and saw that his wisdom for liberation was not yet mature and he was not yet capable of receiving the higher teaching. He asked Rāhula: "Have you already taught others the six sense bases?"

Rāhula addressed the Buddha: "Not yet, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Rāhula: "You should teach others the six sense bases."

At that time, Rāhula at another time taught others the six sense bases. Having finished teaching the six sense bases, he came to where the Buddha was, paid homage at his feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and addressed the Buddha saying: "World-Honored One, I have already taught others the six sense bases. May the World-Honored One please teach me the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I will go to a solitary, quiet place and with focused concentration contemplate, dwelling in heedfulness, up to knowing for myself that I will not receive further existence."

At that time, the World-Honored One examined Rāhula's mind and saw that his wisdom for liberation was not yet mature and he was not yet capable of receiving the higher teaching. He asked Rāhula: "Have you already taught others the nidāna teaching?"

Rāhula addressed the Buddha: "Not yet, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Rāhula: "You should teach others the nidāna teaching."

At that time, Rāhula at another time extensively taught others the nidāna teaching. Having finished, he came to where the Buddha was, paid homage at his feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and addressed the Buddha saying: "World-Honored One, please teach me the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I will go to a solitary, quiet place and with focused concentration contemplate, dwelling in heedfulness, up to knowing for myself that I will not receive further existence."

At that time, the World-Honored One again examined Rāhula's mind and saw that his wisdom for liberation was not yet mature, extensively spoken as above, up to telling Rāhula: "You should, regarding all the teachings spoken above, go to a solitary, quiet place, and with focused concentration contemplate and examine their meaning."

At that time, Rāhula, having received the Buddha's instruction, regarding all the Dharma he had heard and all the Dharma he had taught, contemplated, weighed, and examined their meaning. He reflected thus: "All these dharmas entirely tend toward nirvāṇa, flow into nirvāṇa, and ultimately abide in nirvāṇa." Then Rāhula went to where the Buddha was, paid homage at his feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and addressed the Buddha saying: "World-Honored One, regarding all the Dharma I have heard and all the Dharma I have taught, I have gone to a solitary, quiet place and contemplated, weighed, and examined their meaning. I have come to know that all these dharmas tend toward nirvāṇa, flow into nirvāṇa, and ultimately abide in nirvāṇa."

At that time, the World-Honored One examined Rāhula's mind and saw that his wisdom for liberation was mature and he was capable of receiving the higher teaching. He told Rāhula: "Rāhula, all is impermanent. What dharmas are impermanent? The eye is impermanent. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact..." extensively spoken as above regarding impermanence.

At that time, Rāhula, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was delighted. He paid homage to the Buddha and withdrew.

At that time, Rāhula, having received the Buddha's instruction, went to a solitary, quiet place and with focused concentration contemplated, dwelling in heedfulness: "This is why a clansman shaves off his hair and beard, dons the kāṣāya robe, puts on the faith of homelessness, leaves the household to study the Way, purely cultivates the holy life, up to seeing the Dharma, knowing for himself and realizing: 'My birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'" He became an arhat, his mind well liberated.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Rāhula, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (201)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu came to where the Buddha was, paid homage at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and addressed the Buddha saying: "World-Honored One, how should one know and how should one see to progressively and swiftly attain the complete ending of the outflows?"

At that time, the World-Honored One told that bhikṣu: "You should rightly observe impermanence. What dharmas are impermanent? The eye is impermanent. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, should be observed as impermanent. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind should be observed as impermanent. Mental objects, mind-consciousness, mind-contact, and the feelings born from mind-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, these too are impermanent. Bhikṣu, knowing in this way and seeing in this way, one progressively brings the outflows to a complete end."

At that time, that bhikṣu, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced, paid homage, and departed.

Likewise for the sūtras spoken to bhikṣus. Where there are differences, they are: "How should one know, how should one see, to progressively bring a complete end to all fetters, to cut off all bonds, to cut off all latent tendencies, to cut off all higher afflictions, to cut off all knots, to cut off all floods, to cut off all yokes, to cut off all clingings, to cut off all contacts, to cut off all hindrances, to cut off all entanglements, to cut off all defilements, to cut off all cravings, to cut off all intentions, to cut off wrong views and give rise to right views, to cut off ignorance and give rise to clear knowledge? Bhikṣu, thus observing the eye as impermanent, up to knowing in this way and seeing in this way, progressively ignorance is cut off and clear knowledge arises."

At that time, that bhikṣu, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced. Having rejoiced, he paid homage and departed.

---

### (202)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu went to where the Buddha was, paid homage at the Buddha's feet, and addressed the Buddha saying: "World-Honored One, how should one know and how should one see, so that progressively the view of self is cut off and the view of non-self arises?"

The Buddha told that bhikṣu: "Regarding the eye, rightly observe it as non-self. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, these too should be rightly observed as non-self. Likewise, up to the feelings born from mind-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, these too should be rightly observed as non-self. Bhikṣu, knowing in this way and seeing in this way, progressively the view of self is cut off and the view of non-self arises."

At that time, that bhikṣu, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced. Having rejoiced, he paid homage and departed.

---

### (203)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, in the physician Jīvaka Kumārabhṛta's Mango Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If there is a bhikṣu who is able to cut off one dharma, then he attains right knowledge and is able to declare of himself: 'My birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha saying: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma." May you please expound it. The bhikṣus, having heard, will receive and practice accordingly.

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully and reflect well. I shall explain it for you. Bhikṣus, what is this one dharma that, when cut off, leads up to not receiving further existence? It is ignorance. Being free from desire for ignorance, clear knowledge arises, and one attains right knowledge and is able to declare of oneself: 'My birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'"

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, bared his right shoulder, paid homage to the Buddha, placed his right knee on the ground, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha saying: "World-Honored One, how should one know and how should one see so that ignorance is abandoned and clear knowledge arises?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "You should rightly observe the eye as impermanent. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, these too should be rightly observed as impermanent. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. Bhikṣu, knowing in this way and seeing in this way, ignorance is abandoned and clear knowledge arises."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (204)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, in the physician Jīvaka Kumārabhṛta's Mango Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the Venerable Ānanda: "Regarding the eye, one should know it as it really is and see it as it really is. The eye, forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, these too should be known as they really are and seen as they really are. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. Having known as it really is and seen as it really is, one develops disenchantment with the eye. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, regarding these too one develops disenchantment. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. Being disenchanted, one does not delight. Not delighting, one is liberated. In liberation, the knowledge and vision arises: 'My birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (205)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, in the physician Jīvaka Kumārabhṛta's Mango Grove.

At that time, after the World-Honored One had spoken all the udāna verses, he addressed the Venerable Ānanda: "The eye is impermanent, suffering, subject to change, and of a nature to become otherwise. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, these too are impermanent, suffering, subject to change, and of a nature to become otherwise. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. A well-learned noble disciple who observes thus attains liberation with regard to the eye. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, from these too one is liberated. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind; mental objects, mind-consciousness, mind-contact, and the feelings born from mind-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, from these one is liberated. I say that person is liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (206)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the city of Vaiśālī, in the physician Jīvaka Kumārabhṛta's Mango Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should strive with diligent skill in meditative contemplation, inwardly stilling the mind. Why is this? Bhikṣus, when one strives with diligent skill in meditative contemplation and inwardly stills the mind, things are known as they really are and clearly manifest. Regarding what are things known as they really are and clearly manifest? Regarding the eye, things are known as they really are and clearly manifest. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, these too are known as they really are and clearly manifest. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. These dharmas are impermanent and conditioned; thus too they are known as they really are and clearly manifest."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (207)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, in the physician Jīvaka Kumārabhṛta's Mango Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should cultivate the immeasurable samādhis, diligently maintaining mindfulness. Why is this? When one has cultivated the immeasurable samādhis and diligently maintained mindfulness, then things are known as they really are and clearly manifest. Regarding what are things known as they really are and clearly manifest? Regarding the eye, things are known as they really are and clearly manifest," extensively spoken as above, up to "these dharmas are impermanent and conditioned; thus they are known as they really are and clearly manifest."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (208)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, in the physician Jīvaka Kumārabhṛta's Mango Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "The eye of the past and the eye of the future are impermanent, how much more so the eye of the present! A well-learned noble disciple who observes thus does not look back to the eye of the past, does not yearn for the eye of the future, and regarding the present eye develops disenchantment, does not delight, becomes free from desire, and turns toward cessation. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with impermanence, so too with suffering, emptiness, and non-self: it is likewise spoken.

As with the four sūtras on the internal sense bases, so too with the four sūtras on the external sense bases of forms, sounds, odors, tastes, tangibles, and mental objects, and the four sūtras on the internal and external sense bases combined: it is likewise spoken.

---

### (209)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, in the physician Jīvaka Kumārabhṛta's Mango Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six contact bases. What are the six? The eye-contact base, the ear-contact base, the nose-contact base, the tongue-contact base, the body-contact base, and the mind-contact base. If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas do not know as it really is the origination, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape regarding these six contact bases, you should know that these śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas are far from my Dharma and Vinaya, as far as the sky is from the earth."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, paid homage to the Buddha, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha saying: "I fully and truly know the origination, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape regarding these six contact bases."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "I will now question you; answer me as you see fit. Bhikṣu, do you see the eye-contact base as self, as other than self, or as existing within the other?"

He answered: "No, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! Regarding this eye-contact base, it is neither self, nor other than self, nor existing within the other. One who knows and sees in accordance with reality does not give rise to outflows, the mind is not defiled, and the mind attains liberation. This is called the first contact base having been cut off and fully known, its root severed, like cutting off the head of a tāla tree, so that in future dharmas it will never arise again, namely, eye-consciousness and forms. Do you see the ear-contact base, nose-contact base, tongue-contact base, body-contact base, or mind-contact base as self, as other than self, or as existing within the other?"

He answered: "No, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! Regarding the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind contact bases, they are neither self, nor other than self, nor existing within the other. One who thus knows and sees in accordance with reality does not give rise to outflows, the mind is not defiled, and the mind attains liberation. This is called the bhikṣu's six contact bases having been cut off and fully known, their roots severed, like cutting off the head of a tāla tree, so that in future lives they will never arise again, namely, mind-consciousness and mental objects."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (210)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, in the physician Jīvaka Kumārabhṛta's Mango Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Do not delight, do not suffer. Why is this? There exists a hell of the six contact bases. Those beings born in that hell, with the eye they see only unlovely forms and do not see lovely forms, they see unpleasant forms and do not see pleasant forms, they see unwholesome forms and do not see wholesome forms. Because of these conditions, they unilaterally experience grief and suffering. Regarding the ear and sounds, the nose and odors, the tongue and tastes, the body and tangibles, the mind and mental objects, they encounter only the unlovely and not the lovely, the unpleasant and not the pleasant, the unwholesome and not the wholesome. Because of these conditions, they enduringly experience grief and suffering.

"Bhikṣus, there exists a heaven of the six contact bases. Those beings born in that place, with the eye they see lovely forms and not unlovely forms, they see pleasant forms and not unpleasant forms, they see wholesome forms and not unwholesome forms. Because of these conditions, they unilaterally and enduringly experience joy and happiness. Regarding the ear and sounds, the nose and odors, the tongue and tastes, the body and tangibles, the mind and mental objects, they encounter the lovely and not the unlovely, the pleasant and not the unpleasant, the wholesome and not the unwholesome."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (211)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, in the physician Jīvaka Kumārabhṛta's Mango Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In the past, before I had attained perfect awakening, while alone in a quiet place, in meditative reflection I contemplated: 'Where does my mind mostly tend and observe?' I observed that my mind mostly pursued the merit of the five objects of past desire, and less so the merit of the five objects of present desire, and even less the merit of the five objects of future desire. Having observed that my mind mostly pursued the merit of the five objects of past desire, I generated the utmost diligent effort to guard myself, so that I would no longer allow my mind to follow the merit of the five objects of past desire.

"Because of this diligent self-guarding, I gradually drew near to anuttarā-samyak-saṃbodhi. You bhikṣus likewise mostly pursue the merit of the five objects of past desire, with present and future also being less. You too should now, because your minds mostly pursue the merit of the five objects of past desire, increase your self-guarding, and before long you will bring all the outflows to a complete end: the outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation of wisdom, knowing for yourselves in this present life and realizing: 'My birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'

"Why is this? From the eye seeing forms as condition, inner feelings arise, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant. From the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind and their objects as condition, inner feelings arise, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding those sense bases, you should be aware: when the eye ceases, the perception of forms is abandoned. When the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind cease, the perception of their objects is abandoned."

The Buddha said: "You should be aware of the six sense bases." Having spoken, he entered his chamber to sit in meditation. At that time, after the World-Honored One had departed, many bhikṣus engaged in discussion: "The World-Honored One briefly stated the essential Dharma for us without explaining it extensively, and then entered his chamber to sit in meditation. The World-Honored One said: 'You should be aware of the six sense bases. When the eye ceases, the perception of forms is abandoned; when the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind cease, the perception of their objects is abandoned.' We still do not understand the World-Honored One's brief teaching today. Among this assembly, who has the power of wisdom to be able to extensively expound the meaning of the World-Honored One's brief teaching for us?"

They further reflected: "Only the Venerable Ānanda, who constantly attends the World-Honored One, is constantly praised by the Great Teacher as being intelligent and of holy conduct. Only the Venerable Ānanda is capable of expounding the meaning of the World-Honored One's brief teaching for us. Let us all today go together to where the Venerable Ānanda is, ask him the essential meaning, and whatever the Venerable Ānanda says, we will all uphold it."

At that time, the many bhikṣus went to where the Venerable Ānanda was. Having exchanged greetings, they sat to one side. They addressed the Venerable Ānanda saying: "Venerable one, you should know that the World-Honored One briefly stated the essential Dharma for us." As stated above, they fully questioned Ānanda, asking him to extensively explain the meaning for them.

The Venerable Ānanda said to the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully and reflect well. I shall extensively explain the meaning of the World-Honored One's brief teaching for you. What the World-Honored One briefly stated is precisely the teaching of the cessation of the six sense bases with a remainder. Therefore he said: 'When the eye base ceases, the perception of forms is abandoned; when the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind bases cease, the perception of their objects is abandoned.' The World-Honored One, having briefly stated this Dharma, entered his chamber to sit in meditation. I have now explained the meaning for you."

After the Venerable Ānanda had explained this meaning, the bhikṣus, having heard what he said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (212)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I do not teach the practice of heedfulness to all bhikṣus, nor do I not teach the practice of heedfulness to all bhikṣus.

"To what kind of bhikṣus do I not teach the practice of heedfulness? If bhikṣus have attained arhatship, have exhausted all outflows, have put down the heavy burden, have realized their own benefit, have completely ended all fetters of existence, and their minds are rightly liberated, to such bhikṣus I do not teach the practice of heedfulness. Why is this? Those bhikṣus have already accomplished heedfulness, and they are no longer able to engage in heedless conduct. Now I see those venerable ones as having attained the fruit of heedfulness, and therefore I do not teach them the practice of heedfulness.

"To what kind of bhikṣus do I teach the practice of heedfulness? To those bhikṣus who are in the stage of training, who have not yet attained the higher peace and security of mind, who are abiding in the direction of nirvāṇa, to such bhikṣus I teach the practice of heedfulness. Why is this? Because those bhikṣus are training in the faculties, and their minds delight in following the requisites of livelihood. By drawing near to good friends, before long they will bring all the outflows to a complete end: the outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation of wisdom, knowing for themselves in this present life and realizing: 'My birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.' Why is this? Regarding the forms that are lovable, delightful, and attractive to eye-consciousness, those bhikṣus, having seen them, do not delight, do not praise, do not become defiled, and do not become attached. Because they do not delight, do not praise, do not become defiled, and do not become attached, they advance with focused effort. Body and mind come to rest, the mind is settled in the utmost peace, unforgetting, constantly concentrated, single-minded. The immeasurable Dharma-joy they attain is only the attainment of the foremost samādhi and right attainment, which will never decay or follow along with eye and forms. Regarding the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind-consciousness and their objects, it is likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (213)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach you about the two dharmas. Listen carefully and reflect well. What are the two? The eye and forms are a pair. The ear and sounds, the nose and odors, the tongue and tastes, the body and tangibles, the mind and mental objects are each a pair. These are called the two dharmas.

"If there are śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas who say: 'These are not the two dharmas. Regarding the two dharmas spoken by the śramaṇa Gautama, these are not the two,' and such persons by their own thinking speak of two dharmas, those are merely words. When questioned, they do not know, and their doubts increase, because it is not within their domain of experience.

"Why is this? Conditioned by the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The conjunction of the three is contact. From contact as condition, feeling arises, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant. If regarding this feeling one does not know as it really is the origination of feeling, the cessation of feeling, the gratification of feeling, the danger of feeling, and the escape from feeling, then one plants the bodily contact of craving, plants the bodily contact of aversion, plants the bodily contact of clinging to precepts and practices, and plants the bodily contact of the view of self. One also plants, increases, and grows various unwholesome dharmas. Thus the entire great mass of pure suffering arises from origination. Likewise, the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind; from the condition of mental objects, mind-consciousness arises. The conjunction of the three is contact," extensively spoken as above.

"Furthermore, conditioned by the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The conjunction of the three is contact. From contact as condition, feeling arises, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant. If regarding these feelings one knows as it really is the origination, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape, then one does not plant the bodily contact of craving, does not plant the bodily contact of aversion, does not plant the bodily contact of clinging to precepts and practices, and does not plant the bodily contact of the view of self, and does not plant various unwholesome dharmas. Thus these various unwholesome dharmas cease, and the entire great mass of pure suffering ceases. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (214)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are two conditions that give rise to consciousness. What are the two? The eye and forms, the ear and sounds, the nose and odors, the tongue and tastes, the body and tangibles, the mind and mental objects," extensively spoken as above, up to "because it is not within their domain of experience. Why is this? From the conditions of the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. These are impermanent, conditioned, and arisen from mental conditions. Forms, the eye, and consciousness are impermanent, conditioned, and arisen from mental conditions. When these three dharmas come together, there is contact. Having contacted, there is feeling. Having felt, there is intention. Having intended, there is perception. These various dharmas are impermanent, conditioned, and arisen from mental conditions, namely, contact, perception, and intention. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (215)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Pūrṇa went to where the Buddha was, paid homage at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and addressed the Buddha saying: "World-Honored One, you speak of 'the directly visible Dharma,' 'the quenching of burning,' 'not dependent on time,' 'the rightly directed,' 'that which is to be seen here,' and 'to be personally known.' World-Honored One, how is it the directly visible Dharma, up to personally known?"

The Buddha told Pūrṇa: "Excellent, Pūrṇa! You are able to ask this question. Pūrṇa, listen carefully and reflect well. I shall explain it for you. Pūrṇa, when a bhikṣu, having seen forms with the eye, is aware of forms and is aware of craving for forms, and knows as it really is: 'Within me there is eye-consciousness and craving for forms; within me there is eye-consciousness and craving for forms,' Pūrṇa, if having seen forms with the eye, the bhikṣu is aware of forms and is aware of craving for forms, and knows as it really is: 'Within me there is eye-consciousness and craving for forms,' this is called the directly visible Dharma.

"How is it the quenching of burning? How is it not dependent on time? How is it rightly directed? How is it that which is to be seen here? How is it to be personally known? Pūrṇa, when a bhikṣu, having seen forms with the eye, is aware of forms, and does not give rise to the awareness of craving for forms, and knows as it really is: 'I have no inner eye-consciousness and craving for forms; I do not give rise to the awareness of craving for forms.' Pūrṇa, if a bhikṣu, having seen forms with the eye, is aware of forms and does not give rise to the awareness of craving for forms, and knows as it really is that he does not give rise to the awareness of craving for forms, this is called the quenching of burning, not dependent on time, rightly directed, that which is to be seen here, and to be personally known. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Pūrṇa, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (216)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What is called 'the great ocean' is what foolish worldlings say; it is not what the noble ones say. It is merely a great amount of water. What is it that the noble ones call the ocean? When, having cognized forms with eye-consciousness, there is fond attachment and deep clinging, and craving and delight in bodily, verbal, and mental actions, this is called the ocean. All the asura hosts of the world, and even devas and humans, all become immersed in it with craving and delight and sink within it, like a dog's innards, like a tangled mass of grass. This world and the next are twisted, knotted, and chained; it is likewise. When ear-consciousness cognizes sounds, nose-consciousness cognizes odors, tongue-consciousness cognizes tastes, body-consciousness cognizes tangibles, this world and the next are twisted, knotted, and chained; it is likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with bodily, verbal, and mental actions, so too with craving, aversion, and delusion, and old age, sickness, and death: it is likewise spoken. As with the three sūtras on the five faculties and the three sūtras on the six faculties: it is likewise spoken.

---

### (217)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What is called 'the ocean' is what the foolish worldlings say; it is not what the noble ones say. The ocean is merely a great amount of water. The eye is the great ocean of a person, and forms are its surging waves. If one can endure the surging waves of forms, one crosses the great ocean of the eye, having overcome the surging waves, whirlpools, waters, evil creatures, and rākṣasī she-demons. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are the great ocean of a person, and sounds, odors, tastes, tangibles, and mental objects are the surging waves. If one can endure the surging waves of those objects, one crosses the ocean of the mind, having overcome the surging waves, whirlpools, evil creatures, and rākṣasī she-demons."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke these verses:

> "The great ocean with its massive surging waves,
> Its evil creatures and fearsome rākṣasīs,
> Difficult to cross, yet one who crosses it,
> Having left behind origination, without remainder,
> Is able to cut off all suffering,
> And will not receive further existence.
> Forever entering parinirvāṇa,
> Never again returning to heedlessness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (218)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach you the path of the origination of suffering and the path of the cessation of suffering. Listen carefully and reflect well. I shall explain it for you.

"What is the path of the origination of suffering? Conditioned by the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The conjunction of the three is contact. From contact as condition, feeling arises. From feeling as condition, craving arises. From craving as condition, clinging arises. From clinging as condition, becoming arises. From becoming as condition, birth arises. From birth as condition, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering arise and accumulate. Thus the entire great mass of pure suffering accumulates. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. This is called the path of the origination of suffering.

"What is the path of the cessation of suffering? Conditioned by the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The conjunction of the three is contact. When contact ceases, feeling ceases. When feeling ceases, craving ceases. When craving ceases, clinging ceases. When clinging ceases, becoming ceases. When becoming ceases, birth ceases. When birth ceases, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering cease. Thus the entire great mass of pure suffering ceases. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise spoken. This is called the path of the cessation of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (219)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now explain the path leading to nirvāṇa. What is the path leading to nirvāṇa? It means observing the eye as impermanent. Forms, eye-consciousness, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether inwardly felt as painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, these too are impermanent. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. This is called the path leading to nirvāṇa."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (220)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There is a path that resembles the path leading to nirvāṇa. What is the path that resembles the path leading to nirvāṇa? It means observing the eye as non-self. Forms, eye-consciousness, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether inwardly felt as painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, these too are observed as impermanent. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. This is called the path that resembles the path leading to nirvāṇa."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (221)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There is a path leading to all forms of clinging. What is the path leading to all forms of clinging? Conditioned by the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The conjunction of the three is contact. From contact as condition, feeling arises. From feeling as condition, craving arises. From craving as condition, clinging arises. Because of clinging and what is clung to, the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. Because of clinging and what is clung to, this is called the path leading to all forms of clinging.

"What is the path of cutting off all forms of clinging? Conditioned by the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises. The conjunction of the three is contact. When contact ceases, feeling ceases. When feeling ceases, craving ceases. When craving ceases, clinging ceases. Knowing thus, the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (222)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should know the Dharma of knowing all things and the Dharma of comprehending all things. Listen carefully and reflect well. I shall explain it for you.

"What is the Dharma of knowing all things and comprehending all things? Bhikṣus, the eye is a dharma to be known and comprehended. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether inwardly felt as painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, all of these are dharmas to be known and comprehended. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (223)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I do not say that any single dharma, if not known and not comprehended, can lead to reaching the final end of suffering. What do I mean by not saying that any single dharma, if not known and not comprehended, can lead to reaching the final end of suffering? I do not say that regarding the eye, if one does not know and does not comprehend it, one can reach the final end of suffering. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether inwardly felt as painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, likewise I do not say that without knowing and comprehending them one can reach the final end of suffering. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (224)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "All dharmas of desire should be abandoned. What are the dharmas of desire that should be abandoned? The eye is a dharma of desire that should be abandoned. Forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as condition, whether inwardly felt as painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, all of these are dharmas of desire that should be abandoned. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (225)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's
