# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099, Fascicle 9

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

---

## (230)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Samiddhi, who went to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One! What is called 'the world'? What does 'the world' mean?"

The Buddha told Samiddhi: "It means the eye, forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as conditions, internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind; dharmas, mind-consciousness, mind-contact, and the feelings born from mind-contact as conditions, internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant: this is called the world. Why is this? When the six sense bases gather, then contact gathers, and so on up to the gathering of the entire great mass of suffering.

"Samiddhi! If there were no eye, no forms, no eye-consciousness, no eye-contact, no feelings born from eye-contact as conditions, internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant; no ear, nose, tongue, body, or mind; no dharmas, no mind-consciousness, no mind-contact, no feelings born from mind-contact as conditions, internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant, then there would be no world, nor would a world be designated. Why is this? Because when the six sense bases cease, contact ceases, and so on up to the cessation of the entire great mass of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with "the world," so also with "sentient beings" and "Māra," they are likewise explained.

---

## (231)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Samiddhi, who went to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One! What is called 'the world'? What does 'the world' mean?"

The Buddha told Samiddhi: "That which is perilous, fragile, and subject to destruction is called the world. What is perilous, fragile, and subject to destruction? Samiddhi! The eye is a dharma that is perilous, fragile, and subject to destruction. As for forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as conditions, internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant, all of these are also perilous, fragile, and subject to destruction. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. What is spoken of as perilous, fragile, and subject to destruction is called the world."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Samiddhi, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (232)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Samiddhi, who went to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One! What is called 'the world is empty'? What does 'the world is empty' mean?"

The Buddha told Samiddhi: "The eye is empty: empty of a permanent, enduring, unchanging dharma; empty of self and what belongs to self. Why is this? Such is its nature. As for forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as conditions, whether painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant, they are also empty: empty of a permanent, enduring, unchanging dharma; empty of self and what belongs to self. Why is this? Such is their nature. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. This is called the empty world."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Samiddhi, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (233)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now explain the world, the origination of the world, the cessation of the world, and the path leading to the cessation of the world. Listen carefully and reflect well upon it.

"What is the world? It means the six internal sense bases. Which six? The eye as an internal sense base, the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind as internal sense bases.

"What is the origination of the world? It is craving for future existence, accompanied by delight and passion, finding delight here and there.

"What is the cessation of the world? It is the remainderless cessation of that craving for future existence, accompanied by delight and passion, finding delight here and there: its abandonment, its relinquishment, its exhaustion, its fading away, its cessation, its stilling, its disappearance.

"What is the path leading to the cessation of the world? It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (234)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I do not say that anyone can reach the end of the world by traveling, yet I also do not say that without reaching the end of the world one can make an end of suffering." Having spoken thus, he entered his room to sit in meditation.

At that time, after the World-Honored One had departed, many bhikṣus discussed together, saying: "The World-Honored One just now briefly spoke the Dharma, saying: 'I do not say that anyone can reach the end of the world by traveling, yet I also do not say that without reaching the end of the world one can make an end of suffering.' Having spoken thus, he entered his room to sit in meditation. We have not yet understood the meaning of the World-Honored One's brief teaching. Among the venerables here, who is able to expound the meaning of the World-Honored One's brief teaching in detail for us?"

They further said: "Only the Venerable Ānanda, wise and learned, who always attends the World-Honored One at his side, and whom the World-Honored One has praised for his learning and holy conduct, is able to expound the meaning of the World-Honored One's brief teaching in detail for us. We should now go to the Venerable Ānanda and request him to explain it." Then the many bhikṣus went to the Venerable Ānanda. After exchanging greetings, they sat to one side and fully recounted the above matter, questioning Ānanda at length.

At that time, Ānanda addressed the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully and reflect well upon it. I shall now explain. The world, the name of the world, the perception of the world, the verbal expressions of the world, and the spoken designations of the world: all these fall within the category of 'the world.' Venerables, the eye is the world, the name of the world, the perception of the world, the verbal expressions of the world, and the spoken designations of the world; all of these fall within the category of 'the world.' The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. A learned noble disciple who knows as it really is the origination, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape regarding the six sense bases: this is called a noble disciple who has reached the end of the world, who knows the world, who has fulfilled what the world requires, and who has crossed beyond the world."

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda further spoke in verse:

> "It is not by traveling on foot
> that one can reach the end of the world.
> Without reaching the end of the world,
> one cannot be freed from all suffering.
> Therefore the Sage, the Honored One,
> is called the knower of the world.
> He has reached the end of the world;
> all holy conduct has been established.
> The end of the world can only be
> truly understood through right wisdom.
> With awakened wisdom penetrating the world,
> he is said to have crossed to the other shore.

"Thus, Venerables, the World-Honored One earlier briefly spoke the Dharma and entered his room to sit in meditation. I have now analyzed and explained it in detail for you."

When the Venerable Ānanda had spoken this Dharma, the many bhikṣus, having heard what he said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (235)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If there is a teacher and a resident disciple, then dwelling alone is suffering. If there is no teacher and no resident disciple, then dwelling alone is pleasant.

"How is it that having a teacher and a resident disciple makes dwelling alone suffering? Dependent on the eye and forms, unwholesome thoughts arise, accompanied by desire, aversion, and delusion. If a bhikṣu practices these dharmas, he is called 'one who has a teacher.' If he abides in these, he is called 'a resident disciple.' The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. Thus, having a teacher and a resident disciple, one always suffers in dwelling alone.

"How is it that having no teacher and no resident disciple makes dwelling alone pleasant? Dependent on the eye and forms, unwholesome thoughts arise, accompanied by desire, aversion, and delusion, yet the bhikṣu does not practice them: this is called 'having no teacher.' Not abiding in these: this is called 'having no resident disciple.' This is called having no teacher and no resident disciple, always dwelling alone in pleasure. If a bhikṣu has no teacher and no resident disciple, I say that he has attained the merit of the holy life. Why is this? A bhikṣu without a teacher and without a resident disciple, in my established holy life, is able to rightly end suffering and reach the complete end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (236)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra, in the early morning, put on his robes and took up his bowl, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After begging for food, he returned to the monastery, put away his robes and bowl, washed his feet, took his sitting cloth, entered the forest, and sat in meditation during the day. Then Śāriputra arose from meditation and went to the World-Honored One. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side.

At that time, the Buddha addressed Śāriputra: "Where have you come from?"

Śāriputra answered: "World-Honored One, I have come from daytime meditation in the forest."

The Buddha addressed Śāriputra: "What kind of meditation did you enter and abide in?"

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I entered the emptiness samādhi and abided in meditation in the forest."

The Buddha addressed Śāriputra: "Excellent! Excellent! Śāriputra, you have entered the meditation of an elder and sat in meditation. If bhikṣus wish to enter the meditation of an elder, they should train as follows:

'When entering a city, when going on alms round, or when leaving the city, one should reflect thus: "When my eye now sees forms, do desire, affection, love, or attachment arise or not?"'

"Śāriputra, when a bhikṣu observes in this way, if eye-consciousness has love, affection, or attachment toward forms, that bhikṣu, in order to cut off unwholesome states, should make vigorous effort, being able to bind his attention and train. Just as when a person's head or clothing catches fire, in order to fully extinguish it, one should make supreme effort and diligently cause it to be extinguished. That bhikṣu should likewise make supreme vigorous effort, binding his attention and training.

"If, when a bhikṣu observes, whether on the road, or while walking for alms in a village, or while leaving the village, and in the intervals between, his eye-consciousness has no love, affection, or attachment toward forms, that bhikṣu should aspire, with this joyful wholesome root, to be diligent day and night, binding his attention and training. This is called a bhikṣu who, in walking, standing, sitting, and lying down, has purified his alms-going. Therefore this sūtra is called 'The Pure Alms-Going Dwelling.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Śāriputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (237)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pond, in the Pavilion Hall.

At that time, there was a householder named Ugra, who went to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One! Why is it that some bhikṣus attain parinirvāṇa in this very life, while other bhikṣus do not attain parinirvāṇa in this very life?"

The Buddha told the householder: "If a bhikṣu's eye-consciousness has love, affection, and attachment toward forms, because of that love, affection, and attachment, he always depends on consciousness. Because he is bound by that, because he grasps at that, he does not attain parinirvāṇa in this very life. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind-consciousness regarding dharmas are likewise.

"If a bhikṣu's eye-consciousness does not love, delight in, or become attached to forms, and because he does not love, delight in, or become attached, he does not depend on consciousness, he does not contact, does not attach, and does not grasp; therefore such bhikṣus attain parinirvāṇa in this very life. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind-consciousness regarding dharmas are likewise.

"Therefore, householder, there are bhikṣus who attain parinirvāṇa in this very life, and there are those who do not attain parinirvāṇa in this very life."

As with the sūtra questioned by the householder, so also the sūtra questioned by Ānanda and the sūtra spoken by the Buddha himself to the bhikṣus are likewise explained as above.

---

## (238)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pond, in the Pavilion Hall.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu went to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One! What is the cause and condition for eye-consciousness to arise? What is the cause and condition for ear-consciousness, nose-consciousness, tongue-consciousness, body-consciousness, and mind-consciousness to arise?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "The eye in conjunction with forms gives rise to eye-consciousness. Why is this? Because whatever eye-consciousness arises, it all arises due to the eye and forms as conditions. The ear and sounds as conditions, the nose and odors as conditions, the tongue and tastes as conditions, the body and tangibles as conditions, the mind and dharmas as conditions give rise to mind-consciousness. Why is this? Because whatever mind-consciousness there is, it all arises due to the mind and dharmas as conditions. This is what is meant, bhikṣu, by eye-consciousness arising from conditions, up to mind-consciousness arising from conditions."

At that time, that bhikṣu, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and approved. He paid respects and departed.

---

## (239)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pond, in the Pavilion Hall.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now explain the dharmas that are bound by fetters and the fetter dharma. What are the dharmas bound by fetters? The eye and forms, the ear and sounds, the nose and odors, the tongue and tastes, the body and tangibles, the mind and dharmas: these are called the dharmas bound by fetters. What is the fetter dharma? It is desire and passion: this is called the fetter dharma."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (240)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pond, in the Pavilion Hall.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now explain the dharmas that are grasped and the grasping dharma. What are the dharmas that are grasped? The eye and forms, the ear and sounds, the nose and odors, the tongue and tastes, the body and tangibles, the mind and dharmas: these are called the dharmas that are grasped. What is the grasping dharma? It is desire and passion: this is called the grasping dharma."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (241)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pond, in the Pavilion Hall.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Foolish, unlearned ordinary people! Bhikṣus! It would be better to have one's eyes burned with hot copper needles, making them blaze, than for eye-consciousness to grasp at the signs of forms and grasp at their attractive features. Why is this? Because by grasping at the signs of forms and grasping at their attractive features, one falls into evil destinies, sinking like an iron ball.

"Foolish, unlearned ordinary people! It would be better to have one's ears pierced with burning iron awls than for ear-consciousness to grasp at the signs of sounds and grasp at their attractive features. Why is this? Because when ear-consciousness grasps at the signs of sounds and grasps at their attractive features, at the breaking up of the body and the end of life, one falls into evil destinies, sinking like an iron ball.

"Foolish, unlearned ordinary people! It would be better to have one's nose cut off with a sharp knife than for nose-consciousness to grasp at the signs of odors and grasp at their attractive features. Why is this? Because by grasping at the signs of odors and grasping at their attractive features, at the breaking up of the body and the end of life, one falls into evil destinies, sinking like an iron ball.

"Foolish, unlearned ordinary people! It would be better to have one's tongue cut off with a sharp knife than for tongue-consciousness to grasp at the signs of tastes and grasp at their attractive features. Why is this? Because by grasping at the signs of tastes and grasping at their attractive features, at the breaking up of the body and the end of life, one falls into evil destinies, sinking like an iron ball.

"Foolish, unlearned ordinary people! It would be better to have one's body pierced with a hard iron spear than for body-consciousness to grasp at the signs of tangibles and grasp at their attractive features. Why is this? Because by grasping at the signs of tangibles and grasping at their attractive features, at the breaking up of the body and the end of life, one falls into evil destinies, sinking like an iron ball.

"Bhikṣus! To sleep is the life of a fool, a foolish existence, without benefit and without merit. Yet, bhikṣus, it would be better to sleep than to give rise to perceptions regarding those forms. If one gives rise to perceptions, one will surely produce entanglements and disputes, which can cause many people to engage in what is not beneficial and cannot bring benefit and happiness to gods and humans.

"A learned noble disciple should train thus: 'I would rather have my eyes pierced through with a blazing iron spear than for my eye-consciousness to grasp at the signs of forms and fall into the three evil destinies, suffering for a long night. From this day forward, I shall reflect rightly: "Contemplating the eye as impermanent, conditioned, a dharma produced by mental conditions. As for forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as conditions, internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant, they too are impermanent, conditioned, dharmas produced by mental conditions."' For the ear, nose, tongue, and body sense bases, one should train thus: 'I would rather have my body pierced through with an iron spear than for my body-consciousness to grasp at the signs of tangibles and grasp at their attractive features, and thereby fall into the three evil destinies. From this day forward, I shall reflect rightly: "Contemplating the body as impermanent, conditioned, a dharma produced by mental conditions. As for tangibles, body-consciousness, body-contact, and the feelings born from body-contact as conditions, internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant, they too are impermanent, conditioned, dharmas produced by mental conditions."'

"A learned noble disciple should train thus: 'To sleep is the life of a fool, a foolish existence, without fruit, without benefit, and without merit. I should neither sleep nor give rise to perceptions. If perceptions arise, they produce entanglements and disputes, causing many people to lack righteous benefit and fail to attain happiness.'

"When a learned noble disciple contemplates in this way, he generates disenchantment toward the eye. As for forms, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and the feelings born from eye-contact as conditions, internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant, he also generates disenchantment toward them. Being disenchanted, he does not delight in them. Not delighting in them, he is liberated. Being liberated, he knows and sees: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.' The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (242)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pond, in the Pavilion Hall.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If regarding the eye one does not know, does not recognize, does not sever, and does not become free from desire, one is unable to rightly make an end of suffering. If regarding the eye one knows, recognizes, severs, and becomes free from desire, one is able to rightly make an end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the four sūtras on the eye, so also up to the twenty-four sūtras on the mind are explained as above.

---

## (243)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pond, in the Pavilion Hall.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas relish the eye, they should know that such śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas cannot gain freedom from Māra's hand, are bound by Māra's bonds, and enter into Māra's bondage. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas do not relish the eye, they should know that such śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas are not subject to Māra, are free from Māra's hand, and do not enter into Māra's bondage."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with "relishing," so also with "delighting in," "praising," "becoming attached to," "firmly abiding in," "loving and enjoying," and "hating," they are likewise explained.

As with the seven sūtras on the internal sense bases, the seven sūtras on the external sense bases are likewise explained.

---

## (244)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pond, in the Pavilion Hall.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six hooks of Māra. What are the six? When the eye relishes and is attached to forms, this is a hook of Māra. When the ear relishes and is attached to sounds, this is a hook of Māra. When the nose relishes and is attached to odors, this is a hook of Māra. When the tongue relishes and is attached to tastes, this is a hook of Māra. When the body relishes and is attached to tangibles, this is a hook of Māra. When the mind relishes and is attached to dharmas, this is a hook of Māra. If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas have eyes that relish and are attached to forms, they should know that such śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas have Māra's hook hooked in their throats and are not free from Māra."

The impure and pure explanations are expounded in detail as above.

---

## (245)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Kuru, at the Kalmāṣadamya village of tamed dappled cattle.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now expound the Dharma for you, good at the beginning, good in the middle, and good at the end, good in meaning and good in flavor, complete and pure, purified and clear, the holy life: namely, the Sūtra of Four Categories of Dharma. Listen carefully and reflect well upon it. I shall explain it for you.

"What is the Sūtra of Four Categories of Dharma? There are forms cognized by the eye that are lovable, agreeable, pleasing, and entrancing. When a bhikṣu sees them, he delights in, praises, enjoys, and firmly abides in them. And there are forms cognized by the eye that are unlovable, disagreeable, unpleasing, and unpleasant, causing distress. When a bhikṣu sees them, he becomes angry and disdainful. Such a bhikṣu is not free from Māra, up to not being liberated from Māra's bondage. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise.

"There are forms cognized by the eye that are lovable, agreeable, pleasing, and entrancing. When a bhikṣu sees them, he knows delight but does not praise, enjoy, or firmly abide in them. And there are forms cognized by the eye that are unlovable, disagreeable, unpleasing, and unpleasant. When a bhikṣu sees them, he does not become angry or disdainful. Such a bhikṣu is not subject to Māra, is free, up to being liberated from Māra's bondage. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. This is called the bhikṣu's Sūtra of Four Categories of Dharma."

---

## (246)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

At that time, the World-Honored One, in the early morning, put on his robes and took up his bowl, and entered the city of Rājagṛha to beg for food.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama, in the early morning, has put on his robes and taken up his bowl and entered the city of Rājagṛha to beg for food. I shall now go and confuse his mind."

Then Māra Pāpīyas transformed himself into the likeness of an ox-cart driver, holding a staff, searching for cattle. He wore tattered clothes, with disheveled hair, hands and feet cracked and rough, holding a cattle prod. He came before the World-Honored One and asked: "Gautama! Have you seen my cattle?"

The World-Honored One had this thought: "This is the evil Māra, who has come to confuse me." He then told Māra: "Evil Māra! Where are there any cattle? What use have you for cattle?"

Māra had this thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama knows that I am Māra." He then addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama! The eye-contact sense base is my vehicle. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind-contact sense bases are my vehicles."

He further asked: "Gautama! Where do you intend to go?"

The Buddha told the evil Māra: "You have the eye-contact sense base, the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind-contact sense bases. But if there were no eye-contact sense base, no ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind-contact sense bases, a place where you cannot reach, I go to that place."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas then spoke in verse:

> "If there is always a self,
> then all is mine.
> Everything belongs to me;
> Gautama, where will you go?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "If someone says there is a self,
> that so-called self is not real.
> Therefore know, Pāpīyas,
> that you have already fallen into defeat."

Māra again spoke in verse:

> "If you claim to know the path,
> secure and leading to nirvāṇa,
> then go alone by yourself;
> why bother teaching others?"

The World-Honored One again replied in verse:

> "If there are those who seek freedom from Māra,
> asking about the path to the other shore,
> for them I teach with equanimity,
> the truth, without any remainder.
> When they practice without negligence,
> they are forever free from Māra's dominion."

Māra again spoke in verse:

> "There is a stone that looks like meat.
> A hungry crow comes wanting to eat.
> It imagines it soft and delicious,
> wanting to fill its empty hunger.
> In the end it gets no flavor,
> and breaks its beak as it flies into the void.
> I am now like that crow,
> and Gautama is like that stone.
> Finding nothing, I depart in shame,
> like the crow soaring through the void."
> His heart filled with sorrow and vexation,
> he then vanished and was not seen.

---

## (247)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas allow the eye to habituate toward forms, they follow Māra's dominion, up to not being liberated from Māra's bondage. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise.

"If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas do not allow the eye to habituate toward forms, they do not follow Māra but are free, up to being liberated from Māra's bondage. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with "habituating," so also with "becoming attached to," "relishing," "being neighborly to," "accepting and holding on to with attachment," "my seeking and desire," and "being thick and unseparated," they are likewise explained as above.

---

## (248)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Pāṭaliputra, at the Kukkuṭārāma (Cock's Grove).

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda went to visit the Venerable Mahācunda. After exchanging greetings, he sat to one side.

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda said to the Venerable Cunda: "I wish to ask a question. Do you have the leisure to answer me?"

The Venerable Cunda said to the Venerable Ānanda: "Ask as you wish. If I know, I shall answer."

The Venerable Ānanda asked the Venerable Cunda: "As the World-Honored One, the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Enlightened One, knowing and seeing, has explained the four great elements and derivative form, revealing and disclosing that these four great material elements are not self, has the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Enlightened One, knowing and seeing, also said that consciousness is not self?"

The Venerable Cunda said to the Venerable Ānanda: "The Venerable One is foremost in learning. I have come from afar to the Venerable One's presence precisely to ask about this Dharma. Today, Venerable One, please explain this meaning."

The Venerable Ānanda said to Cunda: "I shall now ask you, and you may answer as you see fit. Venerable Cunda, is there an eye, are there forms, and is there eye-consciousness?"

He answered: "Yes."

The Venerable Ānanda further asked: "Is it that dependent on the eye and forms, eye-consciousness arises?"

He answered: "It is so."

The Venerable Ānanda further asked: "If the eye and forms give rise to eye-consciousness, are those causes and conditions permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent."

The Venerable Ānanda further asked: "When those causes and conditions that give rise to eye-consciousness undergo impermanent change, does that consciousness remain?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Ānanda."

The Venerable Ānanda further asked: "What do you think? Regarding those dharmas that can be known as arising and ceasing, would a learned noble disciple see therein a self, something other than self, or one existing within the other?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Ānanda."

"The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind with dharmas: what do you think? Is there a mind, are there dharmas, and is there mind-consciousness?"

He answered: "Yes, Venerable Ānanda."

He further asked: "Is it that dependent on the mind and dharmas, mind-consciousness arises?"

He answered: "It is so, Venerable Ānanda."

He further asked: "If the mind dependent on dharmas gives rise to mind-consciousness, are those causes and conditions permanent or impermanent?"

He answered: "Impermanent, Venerable Ānanda."

He further asked: "When those causes and conditions that give rise to mind-consciousness undergo impermanent change, does that mind-consciousness remain?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Ānanda."

He further asked: "What do you think? Regarding those dharmas that can be known as arising and ceasing, would a learned noble disciple see therein a self, something other than self, or one existing within the other?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Ānanda."

The Venerable Ānanda said to Cunda: "Therefore, Venerable One, the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Enlightened One, knowing and seeing, has also said that consciousness is impermanent. Just as a man takes an axe and enters the mountains. He sees a plantain tree and thinks it might serve as timber. He cuts the root, strips the leaves, chops the branches, and peels the bark, seeking something solid. He peels it down to nothing, and finds no solid core at all. In the same way, a learned noble disciple rightly contemplates eye-consciousness, ear-consciousness, nose-consciousness, tongue-consciousness, body-consciousness, and mind-consciousness. When rightly contemplating, there is nothing to be grasped. Because there is nothing to be grasped, there is nothing to be attached to. Because there is nothing to be attached to, one realizes nirvāṇa for oneself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the two worthy ones had spoken this Dharma, they rejoiced in turn in each other's words, and each returned to his own place.

---

## (249)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda went to visit the Venerable Śāriputra and said to the Venerable Śāriputra: "I wish to ask a question. Do you have the leisure to explain it?"

Śāriputra said: "Ask as you wish. If I know, I shall answer."

The Venerable Ānanda asked the Venerable Śāriputra: "When the six contact sense bases are exhausted, freed from desire, ceased, stilled, and disappeared, is there something remaining?"

The Venerable Śāriputra said to Ānanda: "Do not ask such a question: 'When the six contact sense bases are exhausted, freed from desire, ceased, stilled, and disappeared, is there something remaining?'"

Ānanda further asked the Venerable Śāriputra: "When the six contact sense bases are exhausted, freed from desire, ceased, stilled, and disappeared, is there nothing remaining?"

The Venerable Śāriputra answered Ānanda: "One should also not ask such a question: 'When the six contact sense bases are exhausted, freed from desire, ceased, stilled, and disappeared, is there nothing remaining?'"

Ānanda further asked the Venerable Śāriputra: "When the six contact sense bases are exhausted, freed from desire, ceased, stilled, and disappeared, is there both something remaining and nothing remaining, or neither something remaining nor nothing remaining?"

The Venerable Śāriputra answered Ānanda: "This too should not be asked: 'When the six contact sense bases are exhausted, freed from desire, ceased, stilled, and disappeared, is there both something remaining and nothing remaining, or neither something remaining nor nothing remaining?'"

The Venerable Ānanda further asked Śāriputra: "As the Venerable One has said: 'When the six contact sense bases are exhausted, freed from desire, ceased, stilled, and disappeared, one should not say "there is something remaining," should not say "there is nothing remaining," should not say "there is both something remaining and nothing remaining," and should not say "there is neither something remaining nor nothing remaining."' What is the meaning of this?"

The Venerable Śāriputra said to the Venerable Ānanda: "When the six contact sense bases are exhausted, freed from desire, ceased, stilled, and disappeared: 'Is there something remaining?' This is mere verbal proliferation. 'Is there nothing remaining?' This is mere verbal proliferation. 'Is there both something remaining and nothing remaining?' This is mere verbal proliferation. 'Is there neither something remaining nor nothing remaining?' This is mere verbal proliferation. If one says that when the six contact sense bases are exhausted, freed from desire, ceased, stilled, and disappeared, one is freed from all verbal proliferation and attains parinirvāṇa: this is what the Buddha has taught."

Then the two worthy ones rejoiced in turn in each other's words, and each returned to his own place.

---

## (250)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra and the Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila were both on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

The Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila, in the late afternoon, arose from meditation and went to visit the Venerable Śāriputra. After exchanging greetings, he withdrew and sat to one side, and said to Śāriputra: "I wish to ask a question. Do you have the leisure to answer me?"

The Venerable Śāriputra said to Mahākoṣṭhila: "Ask as you wish. If I know, I shall answer."

The Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila asked the Venerable Śāriputra: "Tell me, Venerable Śāriputra, does the eye bind forms, or do forms bind the eye? Do sounds bind the ear, or does the ear bind sounds? Do odors bind the nose, or does the nose bind odors? Do tastes bind the tongue, or does the tongue bind tastes? Do tangibles bind the body, or does the body bind tangibles? Does the mind bind dharmas, or do dharmas bind the mind?"

The Venerable Śāriputra answered the Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila: "It is not the eye that binds forms, nor forms that bind the eye, up to not the mind that binds dharmas, nor dharmas that bind the mind. Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila, in between the two, it is the desire and passion there that is the binding. Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila, suppose there are two oxen, one black and one white, yoked together with a single yoke and harness. If someone were to ask: 'Does the black ox bind the white ox, or does the white ox bind the black ox?' would that be a proper question?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Śāriputra. It is not the black ox that binds the white ox, nor the white ox that binds the black ox. Rather, it is the yoke and the harness between them that is the binding."

"Just so, Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila. It is not the eye that binds forms, nor forms that bind the eye, up to not the mind that binds dharmas, nor dharmas that bind the mind. It is the desire and passion in between that is the binding.

"Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila, if the eye bound forms, or forms bound the eye, up to if the mind bound dharmas, or dharmas bound the mind, the World-Honored One would not teach people to establish the holy life and reach the complete end of suffering. Because it is not the eye that binds forms, nor forms that bind the eye, up to not the mind that binds dharmas, nor dharmas that bind the mind, therefore the World-Honored One teaches people to establish the holy life and reach the complete end of suffering.

"Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila, the World-Honored One sees forms with the eye, whether good or bad, and does not give rise to desire and passion. Other sentient beings, when the eye sees forms whether good or bad, give rise to desire and passion. Therefore the World-Honored One says one should sever desire and passion, and then the mind is liberated. Up to the mind and dharmas, it is likewise."

Then the two worthy ones rejoiced in turn in each other's words, and each returned to his own place.

---

## (251)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra and the Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila were both on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

The Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila, in the late afternoon, arose from meditation and went to visit the Venerable Śāriputra. After exchanging greetings, he withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Venerable Śāriputra: "I wish to ask a question. Do you have the leisure to answer me?"

Śāriputra said: "Ask as you wish. If I know, I shall answer."

The Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila asked the Venerable Śāriputra: "What is called ignorance? What is ignorance?"

The Venerable Śāriputra said: "What is called not-knowing; not-knowing is ignorance. What is not-knowing? Not knowing as it really is that the eye is impermanent: this is called not-knowing. Not knowing as it really is that the eye is subject to arising and ceasing: this is called not-knowing. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. Thus, Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila, regarding these six contact sense bases, not knowing as it really is, not seeing, not having immediate knowledge, being deluded, being in darkness, being in great obscurity: this is called ignorance."

The Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila further asked the Venerable Śāriputra: "What is called knowledge? What is knowledge?"

Śāriputra said: "What is called knowing; knowing is knowledge. What is known? Knowing as it really is that the eye is impermanent, knowing as it really is that the eye is impermanent. Knowing as it really is that the eye is subject to arising and ceasing, knowing as it really is that the eye is subject to arising and ceasing. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila, regarding these six contact sense bases, knowing as it really is, seeing, illumination, awakening, realization, wisdom, and immediate knowledge: this is called knowledge."

Then the two worthy ones, each having heard what was spoken, rejoiced in turn in each other's words, and each returned to his own place.

---

## (252)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Upasena, dwelling at the Cold Forest charnel ground near Rājagṛha, at the Snake's Head Rock, in the place frequented by kaliṅga serpents. At that time, the Venerable Upasena was sitting alone inside, practicing meditation.

At that time, a small venomous snake, about a cubit in length, fell from the rock above onto Upasena's body. Upasena called out to Śāriputra: "Tell the bhikṣus, a venomous snake has fallen on my body. The poison has entered my body. Come quickly and carry my body outside. Do not let it disintegrate inside, like a heap of chaff."

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra was nearby, sitting beneath a tree. Hearing Upasena's words, he immediately went to Upasena's location and said to Upasena: "As I now observe your complexion, your faculties are no different from usual. Yet you say the poison has entered you, that I should carry your body out, lest it crumble like a heap of chaff. What do you mean by this?"

Upasena said to Śāriputra: "If someone were to say: 'My eye is my self or belongs to my self. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind; the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are my self or belong to my self. Forms, sounds, odors, tastes, tangibles, and dharmas; forms, sounds, odors, tastes, tangibles, and dharmas are my self or belong to my self. The earth element; the earth element is my self or belongs to my self. The water, fire, wind, space, and consciousness elements; the water, fire, wind, space, and consciousness elements are my self or belong to my self. The aggregate of form; the aggregate of form is my self or belongs to my self. The aggregates of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness; the aggregates of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are my self or belong to my self,' then his complexion and faculties should undergo change. But it is not so with me. The eye is not my self or what belongs to my self, up to the aggregate of consciousness is not my self or what belongs to my self. Therefore my complexion and faculties undergo no change."

Śāriputra said: "So it is, Upasena. If you have long since, throughout the long night, been free from the bonds of 'I,' 'mine,' and the conceit 'I am,' having cut their root entirely, like cutting off the crown of a tāla tree, so that in future lives they will never arise again, how could your complexion and faculties undergo any change?"

Then Śāriputra supported and carried Upasena's body out of the cave. Upasena's body, poisoned, crumbled apart like a heap of chaff.

At that time, Śāriputra then spoke in verse:

> "Long cultivating the holy practices,
> well developing the Noble Eightfold Path,
> joyfully he relinquished his life,
> as one would cast away a bowl of poison.
> Long cultivating the holy practices,
> well developing the Noble Eightfold Path,
> joyfully he relinquished his life,
> like a person healed of grave illness.
> Long cultivating the holy practices,
> well developing the Noble Eightfold Path,
> like leaving a house engulfed in flames,
> at the time of death, no grief or regret.
> Long cultivating the holy practices,
> well developing the Noble Eightfold Path,
> with wisdom contemplating the world,
> as if it were foul weeds and brush,
> seeking nothing further elsewhere,
> nor will anything further continue."

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra, having performed the funeral rites for Upasena's remains, went to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, the Venerable Upasena was bitten by a small venomous snake, as tiny as an eye-treatment probe, which fell upon his body, and his body immediately crumbled like a heap of chaff."

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "If Upasena had recited these verses, then the poison would not have taken effect, and his body would not have crumbled like a heap of chaff."

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what verses should be recited? What are the words?"

The Buddha then spoke verses for Śāriputra:

> "Always with loving-kindness toward them,
> firmly toward the Rāṣṭrakūṭas,
> with loving-kindness toward Irāvaṇa,
> toward Śiva and Putra,
> toward Kṛṣṇa and the Supreme Horse,
> and also with loving-kindness toward Kakuṭṭha,
> and toward the Black Gautama,
> toward Nanda and Upananda.
> With loving-kindness and compassion toward the footless,
> and toward those with two feet,
> toward those with four feet and those with many feet,
> also generating loving-kindness and compassion.
> With loving-kindness and compassion toward all nāgas,
> those dwelling in water and on land,
> with loving-kindness toward all sentient beings,
> both limited and limitless.
> May all be happy and at ease,
> and freed from the arising of afflictions.
> May all virtuous ones prosper,
> and may no evil arise for anyone.
> Always dwelling at Snake's Head Rock,
> may evils not gather here.
> The vicious, harmful, venomous snakes
> that can destroy the lives of beings:
> by this truthful declaration,
> spoken by the Supreme Teacher without peer,
> I now recite and practice
> the true words of the Great Teacher.
> All evil poisons
> cannot harm my body.
> Greed, aversion, and delusion:
> these are the three poisons of the world.
> Such evil three poisons
> are forever removed: this is called the Buddha-jewel.
> The Dharma-jewel destroys all poisons,
> the Sangha-jewel also leaves none remaining.
> Destroying vicious, harmful poisons,
> gathering in and protecting the virtuous,
> the Buddha destroys all poisons;
> may your snake poison now be destroyed."

Therefore this mantra chapter and phrases are spoken, namely:

*Uḍaṃbhare ḍaṃbhare haṃlukpā bhaḍaṃ lunāḍi sunāḍi ṭibhaḍi munayi samāyi dāndhi nīrakṣi śibharā kubhi ūre ūgure* svāhā

"Śāriputra, if the good man Upasena had at that time spoken these verses and spoken these mantra chapter and phrases, the snake poison would not have been able to enter his body, and his body would not have crumbled like a heap of chaff."

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, Upasena had never before heard these verses, had never before heard these mantra chapter and phrases. The World-Honored One's speaking them today is solely for the sake of those in future ages."

The Venerable Śāriputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced, paid respects, and departed.

---

## (253)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Venerable Udāyin was traveling through the Kosala country, moving among the people, and arrived at the village of Kubandha. He went to the āmra garden of the brāhmaṇī Veṇukatyāyanī and stayed there.

At that time, the brāhmaṇī Veṇukatyāyanī had several young disciples who were traveling about gathering firewood. They came to the āmra garden and saw the Venerable Udāyin sitting beneath a tree, with a handsome appearance, his faculties calm and still, his mind peaceful and composed, having accomplished the foremost taming. Seeing him, they went to his place. After exchanging greetings, they withdrew and sat to one side. Then Udāyin taught the Dharma to the young men in various ways, encouraging and inspiring them, and then became silent. Those young men, having heard what the Venerable Udāyin said, rejoiced and approved, rose from their seats, and departed.

At that time, the young men carried their bundles of firewood and returned to the brāhmaṇī Veṇukatyāyanī's place. They set down their bundles of firewood on the ground, went to the brāhmaṇī Veṇukatyāyanī, and said: "Our teacher, you should know that in the āmra garden there is a śramaṇa named Udāyin, of the Gautama clan, dwelling there, who teaches the Dharma exceedingly well."

The brāhmaṇī Veṇukatyāyanī said to the young men: "You should go and invite the śramaṇa Udāyin of the Gautama clan to come here tomorrow for a meal."

At that time, the young disciples, having received the brāhmaṇī Veṇukatyāyanī's instructions, went to the Venerable Udāyin's place and said to Udāyin: "Venerable One, you should know that our teacher, the brāhmaṇī Veṇukatyāyanī, invites the Venerable Udāyin for a meal tomorrow morning."

At that time, Udāyin silently accepted the invitation.

At that time, the young men, knowing that Udāyin had accepted the invitation, returned to their teacher, the brāhmaṇī Veṇukatyāyanī, and said: "Teacher, we have conveyed your words and invited the Venerable Udāyin. The Venerable Udāyin silently accepted the invitation. Please prepare as you see fit."

Then the Venerable Udāyin, when the night had passed and morning had come, put on his robes and took up his bowl, and went to the residence of the brāhmaṇī Veṇukatyāyanī. At that time, the brāhmaṇī Veṇukatyāyanī, seeing the Venerable Udāyin coming from afar, quickly spread a seat and invited him to sit. She set out various kinds of food and drink, serving him personally with her own hands, abundantly and fully. When he had finished eating, rinsed his mouth, and washed his bowl, he returned to his original seat.

At that time, the brāhmaṇī Veṇukatyāyanī, knowing that the meal was finished, put on fine leather sandals, covered her head with a cloth, and separately set out a high seat, displaying a haughty demeanor, sitting with arrogance and pride. She said to Udāyin: "I wish to ask a question. Do you have the leisure to answer me?"

Udāyin answered: "Sister, this is not the proper time." Having said this, he rose from his seat and departed.

Thus it was. The next day, the disciples again went to the āmra garden to gather firewood and listen to the Dharma. They returned and again informed their teacher. The teacher again sent them to invite him for a meal, as before, three times. Each time, up to the request for Dharma, he answered: "It is not the time," and did not teach the Dharma.

The young disciples again told their teacher: "In the āmra garden, the śramaṇa Udāyin teaches the Dharma exceedingly well."

The teacher answered: "I also know that he teaches the Dharma exceedingly well. I have invited him two or three times, prepared food, and asked about the Dharma, yet he always says 'It is not the time' and leaves without teaching."

The disciples said to their teacher: "Teacher, you wore fine leather sandals, covered your head with a cloth, and sat without respect. How could he teach? The reason is that the Venerable Udāyin, out of respect for the Dharma, departed without teaching."

The teacher answered: "If that is so, then invite him for me once more."

The disciples received her instruction and invited him again with offerings as before. At that time, when the teacher knew the meal was finished, she removed her leather sandals, arranged her clothes properly, sat on a lower seat, and said respectfully: "I wish to ask a question. Do you have the leisure to answer me?"

Udāyin answered: "You may now ask. I shall explain it for you."

She then asked: "There are śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who say that suffering and pleasure are self-made. There are others who say that suffering and pleasure are made by another. There are others who say that suffering and pleasure are both self-made and made by another. And there are those who say that suffering and pleasure are made neither by self nor by another. Venerable One, what do you say?"

The Venerable Udāyin answered: "Sister, the Arhat says that suffering and pleasure arise differently from how those views describe. It is not explained in those ways."

The brāhmaṇī further asked: "What is the meaning?"

Udāyin answered: "The Arhat says: 'From causes and conditions, suffering and pleasure arise.'"

Udāyin further said to the brāhmaṇī: "I shall now ask you, and you may answer as you wish. What do you think? Is there an eye?"

She answered: "Yes."

"Are there forms?"

She answered: "Yes."

"Are there eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and feelings born from eye-contact as conditions, internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant?"

She answered: "It is so, Venerable Udāyin."

Udāyin further asked: "Are there the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind, and feelings born from mind-contact as conditions, internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant?"

She answered: "It is so, Venerable Udāyin."

Udāyin said: "This is what the Arhat says: 'From causes and conditions, suffering and pleasure arise.'"

The brāhmaṇī said: "Venerable Udāyin, does the Arhat thus say that from causes and conditions, suffering and pleasure arise?"

Udāyin answered: "It is so, brāhmaṇī."

The brāhmaṇī further asked: "Śramaṇa, how does the Arhat explain the cessation of suffering, pleasure, and neither-suffering-nor-pleasure that arise from causes and conditions?"

Udāyin answered: "I shall now ask you, and you may answer as you wish. Brāhmaṇī, if the eye were entirely, at all times, ceased without remainder, would there still be feelings born from eye-contact as conditions, internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant?"

She answered: "No, śramaṇa."

"Likewise, the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind: if at all times they were ceased, exhausted, and entirely without remainder, would there still be feelings born from mind-contact as conditions, internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant?"

She answered: "No, śramaṇa."

"Thus, brāhmaṇī, this is what the Arhat says: 'The suffering, pleasure, and neither-suffering-nor-pleasure that arise from causes and conditions cease.'"

When the Venerable Udāyin spoke this Dharma, the brāhmaṇī Veṇukatyāyanī became free from dust and stain, and attained the purity of the Dharma-eye. At that time, the brāhmaṇī Veṇukatyāyanī saw the Dharma, obtained the Dharma, knew the Dharma, entered the Dharma, crossed beyond doubt and perplexity, and without depending on another, entered the Buddha's teaching. Regarding the Dharma, she attained fearlessness. She rose from her seat, arranged her clothes, respectfully joined her palms, and said to the Venerable Udāyin: "Today I have entered into certainty. From this day forth, I take refuge in the Buddha, I take refuge in the Dharma, I take refuge in the Sangha. From this day forth, for the rest of my life, I take refuge in the Three Jewels."

At that time, Udāyin taught the Dharma to the brāhmaṇī: instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening her. He then rose from his seat and departed.

---

## (254)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Venerable Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa was dwelling on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa, constantly and diligently cultivating the factors of awakening.

At that time, the Venerable Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa, alone in quiet meditation, had this thought: "Among the World-Honored One's disciples who are diligent hearers, I am counted among them. Yet today I have not yet exhausted all the outflows. I am of a renowned clan, with abundant wealth and possessions. I could now return to enjoying the five sensual pleasures, broadly performing acts of giving and making merit."

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing the thoughts in Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa's mind, told a certain bhikṣu: "You now go to the place of Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa and tell him: 'The World-Honored One summons you.'"

That bhikṣu, having received the Buddha's instruction, went to the place of Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa and said: "The World-Honored One summons you."

Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa, hearing that bhikṣu invoke the Great Teacher's name, immediately went to the World-Honored One's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and stood to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa: "Did you truly, in quiet meditation alone, have this thought: 'Among the World-Honored One's diligently training hearers, I am counted among them, and yet now I have not attained the exhaustion of outflows and liberation. I am of a renowned clan, and moreover have much wealth. I could return to lay life, enjoy the five sensual pleasures, and broadly perform acts of giving and making merit'?"

At that time, Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa thought: "The World-Honored One already knows my mind." Startled with hair standing on end, he addressed the Buddha: "Indeed so, World-Honored One."

The Buddha addressed Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa: "I shall now ask you, and you may answer as you wish. Śroṇa Koṭiviṃśa, when you were a layperson, were you skilled at playing the vīṇā?"

He answered: "Yes, World-Honored One."

He further asked: "What do you think? When you played the vīṇā,
