# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099, Fascicle 10

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

---

### (256)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, Venerable Śāriputra and Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila were on Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain.

At that time, Venerable Kauṣṭhila, in the late afternoon, arose from meditation and went to the place of Venerable Śāriputra. They exchanged greetings and delighted each other in various ways, then he sat down to one side.

Then Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila said to Śāriputra: "I wish to ask a question. Do you have some leisure to explain it for me?"

Śāriputra said: "Ask whatever you wish, Venerable One. If I know, I shall explain."

Mahākauṣṭhila asked Śāriputra: "What is called ignorance? What is ignorance? Who possesses this ignorance?"

Śāriputra answered: "Ignorance means not knowing. Not knowing is ignorance."

"Not knowing what?"

"Not knowing that form is impermanent; not knowing as it truly is that form is impermanent. Not knowing that form is subject to wearing away; not knowing as it truly is that form is subject to wearing away. Not knowing that form is subject to arising and ceasing; not knowing as it truly is that form is subject to arising and ceasing. Not knowing that feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent; not knowing as it truly is that feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent. Not knowing that consciousness is subject to wearing away; not knowing as it truly is that consciousness is subject to wearing away. Not knowing that consciousness is subject to arising and ceasing; not knowing as it truly is that consciousness is subject to arising and ceasing. Mahākauṣṭhila! Regarding these five aggregates of clinging, not knowing as it truly is, not seeing, having no direct penetration, being deluded, darkened, and uncomprehending: this is called ignorance. One who possesses this is called one who has ignorance."

He further asked: "Śāriputra! What is called wisdom? What is wisdom? Who possesses this wisdom?"

Śāriputra said: "Mahākauṣṭhila! What is called wisdom is knowing. Knowing is what is called wisdom."

He further asked: "Knowing what?"

"Knowing that form is impermanent; knowing as it truly is that form is impermanent. Knowing that form is subject to wearing away; knowing as it truly is that form is subject to wearing away. Knowing that form is subject to arising and ceasing; knowing as it truly is that form is subject to arising and ceasing. Knowing that feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent; knowing as it truly is that feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent. Knowing that consciousness is subject to wearing away; knowing as it truly is that consciousness is subject to wearing away. Knowing that consciousness is subject to arising and ceasing; knowing as it truly is that consciousness is subject to arising and ceasing. Kauṣṭhila! Regarding these five aggregates of clinging, knowing as it truly is, seeing, having clarity, awakening, having discernment, and direct penetration: this is called wisdom. One who possesses this dharma is called one who has wisdom."

These two upright men, each having heard what was said, rejoiced in turn, rose from their seats, and each returned to his own place.

---

### (257)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, Venerable Śāriputra and Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila were on Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain.

At that time, Mahākauṣṭhila, in the late afternoon, arose from meditation and went to the place of Venerable Śāriputra. They exchanged greetings and delighted each other in various ways, then he sat down to one side.

Then Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila said to Śāriputra: "I wish to ask a question. Do you have a little leisure to explain it for me?"

Śāriputra said: "Please ask, Venerable One. If I know, I shall explain."

Mahākauṣṭhila asked Śāriputra: "What is called ignorance? And what again is ignorance? Who possesses this ignorance?"

Śāriputra answered: "Ignorance means not knowing. Not knowing is ignorance."

"Not knowing what?"

"Not knowing form as it truly is; not knowing as it truly is the arising of form, the cessation of form, and the path leading to the cessation of form. Not knowing feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as they truly are; not knowing as it truly is the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, and the path leading to the cessation of consciousness. Mahākauṣṭhila! Regarding these five aggregates of clinging, not knowing as it truly is, not knowing, not seeing, having no direct penetration, being deluded, darkened, and uncomprehending: this is called ignorance. One who possesses this is called one who has ignorance."

He further asked Śāriputra: "What is wisdom? Who possesses this wisdom?"

Śāriputra said: "What is called wisdom is knowing. Knowing is wisdom."

He further asked: "Knowing what?"

Śāriputra said: "Knowing form as it truly is; knowing as it truly is the arising of form, the cessation of form, and the path leading to the cessation of form. Knowing feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as they truly are; knowing as it truly is the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, and the path leading to the cessation of consciousness. Kauṣṭhila! Regarding these five aggregates of clinging, knowing as it truly is, seeing, having clarity, awakening, having discernment, and direct penetration: this is called wisdom. One who possesses this dharma is called one who has wisdom."

These two upright men, each having heard what was said, rejoiced in turn, rose from their seats, and each returned to his own place.

---

### (258)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, Venerable Śāriputra and Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila were on Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain.

At that time, Mahākauṣṭhila, in the late afternoon, arose from meditation and went to the place of Śāriputra. They exchanged greetings and delighted each other, then he sat down to one side.

Then Mahākauṣṭhila said to Śāriputra: "I wish to ask a question. Does the Venerable One have some leisure to give an answer?"

Śāriputra said: "Please ask, Venerable One. If I know, I shall answer."

Then Mahākauṣṭhila said to Śāriputra: "What is called ignorance? What does ignorance mean? Who possesses this ignorance?"

Śāriputra said: "Not knowing is ignorance."

"Not knowing what?"

"Not knowing form as it truly is; not knowing as it truly is the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Not knowing feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as they truly are; not knowing as it truly is the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, and the escape from consciousness. Mahākauṣṭhila! Regarding these five aggregates of clinging, not knowing as it truly is, not seeing as it truly is, having no direct penetration, being in darkness, being deluded: this is called ignorance. One who possesses this dharma is called one who has ignorance."

He further asked: "What about wisdom? What is wisdom? Who possesses this wisdom?"

Śāriputra said: "Knowing is wisdom."

"Knowing what?"

Śāriputra said: "Knowing form as it truly is; knowing as it truly is the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Likewise knowing feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as they truly are; knowing as it truly is the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, and the escape from consciousness. Mahākauṣṭhila! Regarding these five aggregates of clinging, knowing as it truly is, seeing as it truly is, having clarity, awakening, having discernment, and direct penetration: this is called wisdom. One who possesses this is called one who has wisdom."

Then the two upright men, each having heard what was said, rejoiced and departed.

---

### (259)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, Venerable Śāriputra and Mahākauṣṭhila were on Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain.

Mahākauṣṭhila, in the late afternoon, arose from meditation and went to the place of Śāriputra. They exchanged greetings and delighted each other, then he sat down to one side.

Then Mahākauṣṭhila said to Śāriputra: "I wish to ask a question. Does the Venerable One have some leisure to give an answer?"

Śāriputra said: "Please ask, Venerable One. If I know, I shall answer."

Then Mahākauṣṭhila asked Śāriputra: "If a bhikṣu has not yet attained direct penetration and wishes to seek direct penetration, what method should he employ? What dharmas should he contemplate?"

Śāriputra said: "If a bhikṣu has not yet attained direct penetration and wishes to seek direct penetration, he should diligently contemplate: 'The five aggregates of clinging are as a disease, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer; they are impermanent, suffering, empty, and not-self.' Why is this? Because this is the appropriate basis. If a bhikṣu diligently contemplates these five aggregates of clinging, he attains the realization of the fruit of stream-entry."

He further asked: "Śāriputra! Having attained the realization of the fruit of stream-entry, if one wishes to attain the realization of the fruit of once-returning, what dharmas should one contemplate?"

Śāriputra said: "Kauṣṭhila! Having already attained the realization of the fruit of stream-entry, if one wishes to attain the realization of the fruit of once-returning, one should also diligently contemplate: 'These five aggregates of clinging are as a disease, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer; they are impermanent, suffering, empty, and not-self.' Why is this? Because this is the appropriate basis. If a bhikṣu diligently contemplates these five aggregates of clinging, he attains the realization of the fruit of once-returning."

Mahākauṣṭhila further asked Śāriputra: "Having attained the realization of the fruit of once-returning, if one wishes to attain the realization of the fruit of non-returning, what dharmas should one contemplate?"

Śāriputra said: "Kauṣṭhila! Having attained the realization of the fruit of once-returning, if one wishes to attain the realization of the fruit of non-returning, one should further diligently contemplate: 'These five aggregates of clinging are as a disease, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer; they are impermanent, suffering, empty, and not-self.' Why is this? Because this is the appropriate basis. If a bhikṣu diligently contemplates these five aggregates of clinging, he attains the realization of the fruit of non-returning."

Mahākauṣṭhila further asked Śāriputra: "Having attained the realization of the fruit of non-returning, if one wishes to attain the realization of the fruit of arhatship, what dharmas should one contemplate?"

Śāriputra said: "Kauṣṭhila! Having attained the realization of the fruit of non-returning, if one wishes to attain the realization of the fruit of arhatship, one should further diligently contemplate: 'These five aggregates of clinging are as a disease, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer; they are impermanent, suffering, empty, and not-self.' Why is this? Because this is the appropriate basis. If a bhikṣu diligently contemplates the dharma of these five aggregates of clinging, he attains the realization of the fruit of arhatship."

Mahākauṣṭhila further asked Śāriputra: "Having attained the realization of the fruit of arhatship, what dharmas does one further contemplate?"

Śāriputra said: "Mahākauṣṭhila! An arhat also further contemplates: 'These five aggregates of clinging are as a disease, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer; they are impermanent, suffering, empty, and not-self.' Why is this? For the sake of attaining what has not yet been attained, realizing what has not yet been realized, and for abiding in the happiness of seeing the Dharma in this very life."

Then the two upright men, each having heard what was said, rejoiced and departed.

---

### (260)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra went to the place of Venerable Ānanda. They exchanged greetings and then he sat down to one side.

Then Venerable Śāriputra asked Venerable Ānanda: "I wish to ask a question. Does the Venerable One have some leisure to give an answer?"

Ānanda said: "Please ask, Venerable One. If I know, I shall answer."

Śāriputra said: "Ānanda! What is called cessation? What is cessation? Who possesses this cessation?"

Ānanda said: "Śāriputra! The five aggregates of clinging are produced by past actions and past intentions. They are impermanent, subject to cessation. When those dharmas cease, this is called cessation. What are the five? Form as an aggregate of clinging is produced by past actions and past intentions. It is impermanent, subject to cessation. When that dharma ceases, this is called cessation. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are produced by past actions and past intentions. They are impermanent, subject to cessation. When those dharmas cease, this is called cessation."

Śāriputra said: "So it is, so it is. Ānanda! As you have said, these five aggregates of clinging are produced by past actions and past intentions. They are impermanent, subject to cessation. When those dharmas cease, this is called cessation. What are the five? Form as an aggregate of clinging is produced by past actions and past intentions. It is impermanent, subject to cessation. When that dharma ceases, this is called cessation. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are produced by past actions and past intentions. They are impermanent, subject to cessation. When those dharmas cease, this is called cessation. Ānanda! If these five aggregates of clinging were not produced by past actions and past intentions, how could they cease? Ānanda! It is precisely because the five aggregates of clinging are produced by past actions and past intentions, and are impermanent and subject to cessation, that when those dharmas cease, this is called cessation."

Then the two upright men, each having heard what was said, rejoiced and departed.

---

### (261)

Thus have I heard. At one time, Venerable Ānanda was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī at Ghoṣita's Park.

At that time, Venerable Ānanda addressed the bhikṣus: "When the Venerable Pūrṇa Maitrāyaṇīputra was young and had first gone forth, he would always expound the profound Dharma, speaking in this way: 'Ānanda! It is regarding what has arisen that one conceives "this is my self," not regarding what has not arisen. Ānanda! How is it that regarding what has arisen one conceives "this is my self," and not regarding what has not arisen? Form, having arisen, is that regarding which one conceives "this is my self," not what has not arisen. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, having arisen, are that regarding which one conceives "this is my self," not what has not arisen. It is like a man who, taking a clear mirror or a clean water-mirror in hand, sees the reflection of his face. It is because of its arising that he sees it, not because of its non-arising. Therefore, Ānanda! It is because form has arisen that one conceives "this is my self," not because of its non-arising. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, having arisen, are that regarding which one conceives "this is my self," not what has not arisen. What do you think, Ānanda? Is form permanent or impermanent?' I answered: 'Impermanent.'

"He further asked: 'Being impermanent, is it suffering?' I answered: 'It is suffering.' He further asked: 'If it is impermanent and suffering, being a dharma subject to change, would a noble disciple within it conceive of a self, something other than self, or the two as mutually inherent?' I answered: 'No.' 'Likewise, is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?' I answered: 'Impermanent.' 'If impermanent, is it suffering?' I answered: 'It is suffering.' He further asked: 'If it is impermanent and suffering, being a dharma subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple within it conceive of a self, something other than self, or the two as mutually inherent?' I answered: 'No.'

"'Ānanda! Therefore, all form, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, not other than self, and the two are not mutually inherent. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, not other than self, and the two are not mutually inherent. Knowing as it truly is and observing as it truly is, observing in this way, the noble disciple generates disenchantment with form, becomes free from desire, and is liberated: "My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence." Likewise regarding feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, one generates disenchantment, becomes free from desire, and is liberated: "My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence."'

"Bhikṣus, you should know that that Venerable One was of great benefit to me. After hearing the Dharma from that Venerable One, I attained freedom from dust and defilement and obtained the purification of the Dharma eye. From that time on, I have always taught this Dharma to the four assemblies, not to those who have gone forth among other non-Buddhist ascetics or brahmins."

---

### (262)

Thus have I heard. At one time, numerous elder bhikṣus were dwelling in the country of Vārāṇasī at the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park, not long after the Buddha's parinirvāṇa.

At that time, the elder Channa, in the early morning, put on his robes and took his bowl, and entered the city of Vārāṇasī to beg for food. After eating, he returned, put away his robes and bowl, washed his feet, and taking a door-hook, went from grove to grove, from dwelling to dwelling, from walking path to walking path, everywhere requesting the bhikṣus, saying: "Please instruct me, teach me the Dharma, so that I may know the Dharma and see the Dharma. I shall then know in accordance with Dharma and observe in accordance with Dharma."

At that time, the bhikṣus said to Channa: "Form is impermanent; feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent. All formations are impermanent. All dharmas are without self. Nirvāṇa is peaceful extinction."

Channa said to the bhikṣus: "I already know that form is impermanent; that feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent; that all formations are impermanent; that all dharmas are without self; that nirvāṇa is peaceful extinction."

Channa further said: "Yet I do not delight in hearing: 'All formations are empty and tranquil, unobtainable; craving is exhausted, desire is abandoned, there is cessation, nirvāṇa.' In this, how can there be a self that says: 'Knowing thus, seeing thus, this is called seeing the Dharma'?" He spoke likewise a second and a third time.

Channa further said: "Among you, who else has the ability and power to teach me the Dharma, so that I may know the Dharma and see the Dharma?" Then he had this thought: "Venerable Ānanda is now in the country of Kauśāmbī at Ghoṣita's Park. He once attended upon and was close to the World-Honored One, praised by the Buddha, and known by all who practice the holy life. He surely has the ability to teach me the Dharma, so that I may know the Dharma and see the Dharma."

Then Channa, after that night had passed, in the early morning put on his robes and took his bowl, entered the city of Vārāṇasī to beg for food. After eating, he returned and put away his bedding. After putting away his bedding, he took his robes and bowl and proceeded toward the country of Kauśāmbī. Traveling gradually, he arrived in the country of Kauśāmbī. He put away his robes and bowl, washed his feet, and went to the place of Venerable Ānanda. They exchanged greetings and then he sat down to one side.

Then Channa said to Venerable Ānanda: "Once, the elder bhikṣus were dwelling in the country of Vārāṇasī at the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park. At that time, in the early morning I put on my robes and took my bowl and entered the city of Vārāṇasī to beg for food. After eating, I returned, put away my robes and bowl, washed my feet, and taking a door-hook, went from grove to grove, from dwelling to dwelling, from walking path to walking path, everywhere meeting bhikṣus, and I requested them, saying: 'Please instruct me, teach me the Dharma, so that I may know the Dharma and see the Dharma.' At that time, the bhikṣus taught me the Dharma, saying: 'Form is impermanent; feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent. All formations are impermanent. All dharmas are without self. Nirvāṇa is peaceful extinction.' I at that time said to the bhikṣus: 'I already know that form is impermanent; that feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent; that all formations are impermanent; that all dharmas are without self; that nirvāṇa is peaceful extinction. Yet I do not delight in hearing: "All formations are empty and tranquil, unobtainable; craving is exhausted, desire is abandoned, there is cessation, nirvāṇa." In this, how can there be a self that says: "Knowing thus, seeing thus, this is called seeing the Dharma"?' At that time I had this thought: 'Among them, who else has the ability and power to teach me the Dharma, so that I may know the Dharma and see the Dharma?' At that time I further had this thought: 'Venerable Ānanda is now in the country of Kauśāmbī at Ghoṣita's Park. He once attended upon and was close to the World-Honored One, praised by the Buddha, and known by all who practice the holy life. He surely has the ability to teach me the Dharma, so that I may know the Dharma and see the Dharma.' Excellent! May Venerable Ānanda now teach me the Dharma, so that I may know the Dharma and see the Dharma."

At that time, Venerable Ānanda said to Channa: "Excellent! Channa! My heart is greatly delighted. I rejoice that the Venerable One is able, before those who practice the holy life, to be without concealment, to break the thorn of falsehood. Channa! The deluded, foolish ordinary person cannot understand that form is impermanent; that feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are impermanent; that all formations are impermanent; that all dharmas are without self; that nirvāṇa is peaceful extinction. You are now capable of receiving the supreme, wonderful Dharma. Listen carefully now, and I shall explain it to you."

At that time, Channa had this thought: "I am now joyful, having attained a supreme mind of exultation. I am now capable of receiving the supreme, wonderful Dharma."

Then Ānanda said to Channa: "I personally heard from the Buddha when he taught Mahākātyāyana, saying: 'The people of the world, in their delusions, depend on two extremes: either existence or non-existence. The people of the world grasp at objects and the mind becomes attached. Kātyāyana! If one does not accept, does not grasp, does not abide in, and does not conceive of a self, then when this suffering arises, it arises, and when it ceases, it ceases. Kātyāyana! Regarding this, one does not doubt, is not perplexed, and can know by oneself without depending on others. This is called right view as taught by the Tathāgata. Why is this? Kātyāyana! When one rightly observes with true knowledge the arising of the world, there does not arise the view that the world is non-existent. When one rightly observes with true knowledge the cessation of the world, there does not arise the view that the world is existent. Kātyāyana! The Tathāgata, leaving both extremes, teaches the middle path, namely: because this exists, that exists; because this arises, that arises. That is, conditioned by ignorance there are formations, and so on up to the arising of the entire mass of birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, suffering, and distress. When this does not exist, that does not exist; when this ceases, that ceases. That is, when ignorance ceases, formations cease, and so on up to the cessation of the entire mass of birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, suffering, and distress.'"

When Venerable Ānanda spoke this Dharma, the bhikṣu Channa attained freedom from dust and defilement and obtained the purification of the Dharma eye. At that time, the bhikṣu Channa saw the Dharma, obtained the Dharma, knew the Dharma, entered the Dharma, crossed beyond doubt, and without depending on others, attained fearlessness in the teaching of the Great Teacher. He respectfully joined his palms and said to Venerable Ānanda: "It should indeed be just so. Such is the instruction and admonition of a wise companion in the holy life, a good spiritual friend who teaches the Dharma. I have now heard such Dharma from Venerable Ānanda, and regarding all formations being empty, all being tranquil, all being unobtainable, craving being exhausted, desire being abandoned, cessation, nirvāṇa, my mind delights in it and abides rightly in liberation. It no longer turns back. I no longer see a self; I see only the true Dharma."

Then Ānanda said to Channa: "You have now obtained great benefit. In the profound Buddha-Dharma, you have obtained the holy eye of wisdom."

Then the two upright men rejoiced in turn at what had been said, rose from their seats, and each returned to his own place.

> Śūrpāraka, three kinds; ignorance also has three;
> Direct penetration and cessation; Pūrṇa and Channa.

---

### (263)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Kuru country at a village of variegated cowherds.

At that time, the Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "It is through knowing and seeing that I have attained the complete destruction of the āsravas (taints), not through not knowing and not seeing. How is it through knowing and seeing that I have attained the complete destruction of the āsravas, not through not knowing and not seeing? Namely: 'This is form, this is the arising of form, this is the cessation of form. This is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness; this is the arising of consciousness, this is the cessation of consciousness.' If one does not cultivate the method of practice that leads to fulfillment, yet uses the mind to wish: 'May my āsravas be exhausted and my mind be liberated,' one should know that that bhikṣu will never be able to attain the exhaustion of āsravas and liberation. Why is this? Because of not cultivating. Not cultivating what? Namely, not cultivating the foundations of mindfulness, right effort, bases of supernatural power, faculties, powers, factors of awakening, and the path. It is like a hen that has laid many eggs but is unable to properly incubate them at the right time, warming and cooling them, yet wishes that her chicks would use their beaks and claws to peck through the shells and safely emerge. One should know that those chicks do not have the ability to safely use their beaks and claws to emerge from the shells. Why is this? Because the mother hen was unable to properly incubate, warm, and nurture her chicks at the right time.

"Likewise, bhikṣus, if one does not diligently cultivate the practice that leads to fulfillment, yet wishes to attain the exhaustion of āsravas and liberation, that is impossible. Why is this? Because of not cultivating. Not cultivating what? Namely, not cultivating the foundations of mindfulness, right effort, bases of supernatural power, faculties, powers, factors of awakening, and the path.

"If a bhikṣu cultivates the practice that leads to fulfillment, then even without wishing for the exhaustion of āsravas and liberation, that bhikṣu will naturally have his āsravas exhausted and his mind liberated. Why is this? Because of cultivation. What does he cultivate? Namely, he cultivates the foundations of mindfulness, right effort, bases of supernatural power, faculties, powers, factors of awakening, and the path. It is like a hen that properly nurtures her chicks, incubating them at the right time, warming and cooling them appropriately. Even without wishing that the chicks use their own effort to peck through the shells and emerge, those chicks can naturally and safely emerge from the shells. Why is this? Because that brooding hen incubated them at the right time, and the warming and cooling were appropriate.

"Likewise, bhikṣus, if one properly cultivates the method of practice, even without wishing for the exhaustion of āsravas and liberation, that bhikṣu will naturally have his āsravas exhausted and his mind liberated. Why is this? Because of diligent cultivation. What does he cultivate? Namely, he cultivates the foundations of mindfulness, right effort, bases of supernatural power, faculties, powers, factors of awakening, and the path. It is like a skilled craftsman or a craftsman's apprentice who holds an axe handle in his hand. By grasping it constantly, gradually and imperceptibly it wears away, and the marks of his fingers appear. Yet he is not aware of the axe handle gradually wearing away while the marks appear.

"Likewise, bhikṣus, if one diligently cultivates the practice that leads to fulfillment, one does not oneself know and see, 'Today so many āsravas were exhausted, tomorrow so many āsravas will be exhausted.' Yet that bhikṣu knows that the āsravas are exhausted. Why is this? Because of cultivation. What does he cultivate? Namely, he cultivates the foundations of mindfulness, right effort, bases of supernatural power, faculties, powers, factors of awakening, and the path. It is like a great ship that sits on the seashore. After six months of summer, wind blowing and sun beating upon it, the rattan bindings gradually break apart.

"Likewise, bhikṣus, if one diligently cultivates the practice that leads to fulfillment, all fetters, latent tendencies, afflictions, and entanglements are gradually liberated. Why is this? Because of proper cultivation. What does he cultivate? Namely, he cultivates the foundations of mindfulness, right effort, bases of supernatural power, faculties, powers, factors of awakening, and the path."

When this Dharma was spoken, sixty bhikṣus, without giving rise to further āsravas, had their minds liberated.

The Buddha spoke this sūtra. The bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (264)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu, while contemplating in meditation, had this thought: "Is there perhaps any form that is permanent, enduring, unchanging, and abiding as such? Likewise, are feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent, enduring, unchanging, and abiding as such?"

That bhikṣu, in the late afternoon, arose from meditation, went to the place of the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and stood to one side. He said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! While contemplating in meditation, I had this thought: 'Is there perhaps any form that is permanent, enduring, unchanging, and abiding as such? Likewise, are feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent, enduring, unchanging, and abiding as such?' Now I ask the World-Honored One: Is there perhaps any form that is permanent, enduring, unchanging, and abiding as such? Is there perhaps any feeling, perception, formations, or consciousness that is permanent, enduring, unchanging, and abiding as such?"

At that time, the World-Honored One, holding a small lump of earth in his hand, said to that bhikṣu: "Do you see this lump of earth in my hand?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "I see it, World-Honored One."

"Bhikṣu! Even this small amount of earth I cannot find to be a self. If a self could be found, then it would be a permanent, enduring, unchanging, and abiding dharma."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "I myself recall my past lives. Through the long night I cultivated merit and obtained various superior, wonderful, and delightful fruits of reward. Once I cultivated the mind of loving-kindness for seven years, and for seven kalpas of formation and destruction I did not return to this world. When the kalpas were being destroyed, I was born in the Ābhāsvara Heaven. When the kalpas were being formed, I was reborn in the Brahma World, in an empty palace, as a Great Brahmā King, without superior, supreme, ruling over a thousand worlds. After that, I was thirty-six more times Lord Śakra. Furthermore, hundreds of thousands of times I was a wheel-turning sage king, ruling over the four continents, governing with the true Dharma, endowed with the seven treasures, namely: the wheel treasure, the elephant treasure, the horse treasure, the maṇi treasure, the maiden treasure, the treasurer minister treasure, and the general minister treasure. I had a thousand sons, all brave and valiant. Within the four seas, the land was flat and even, without thorns or poisonous things. I did not oppress or coerce, but governed through the Dharma.

"The dharma of the anointed king includes eighty-four thousand dragon elephants, all adorned with various treasures and decorated, covered with nets of jewels, with jeweled banners raised. The elephant king named Poṣadha was the foremost leader. Morning and evening they would assemble before the palace. At that time I thought: 'This great herd of elephants, going back and forth twice daily, tramples and kills countless beings. I wish that forty-two thousand elephants come only once in a hundred years.' Immediately it was as I wished: among the eighty-four thousand elephants, forty-two thousand came only once in a hundred years.

"The dharma of the anointed king further includes eighty-four thousand horses, also equipped with riding gear of pure gold, covered with golden nets, with the horse king Valāha as their foremost leader.

"The dharma of the anointed king includes eighty-four thousand chariots of four kinds: gold chariots, silver chariots, lapis lazuli chariots, and crystal chariots, covered and cushioned with lion, tiger, and leopard skins and multicolored kambala cloth. The chariot named Vaijayanta-nandi-ghoṣa was the foremost leader.

"The dharma of the anointed king rules over eighty-four thousand cities, all secure and prosperous, with thriving populations. The city of Kuśāvatī was the foremost.

"The dharma of the anointed king includes eighty-four thousand palaces of four kinds: gold, silver, lapis lazuli, and crystal and maṇi-lapis lazuli. Yūha was the foremost.

"Bhikṣu! The dharma of the anointed king includes eighty-four thousand jeweled beds of four kinds: gold, silver, lapis lazuli, and crystal, spread with various silk coverings, woolen rugs, fine carpets, and Kaliṅga bedding, with red pillows placed on them.

"Furthermore, bhikṣu! The dharma of the anointed king includes eighty-four thousand garments of four kinds: namely, Kāśi fine cloth, kṣauma cloth, tūkūla cloth, and kauśeya cloth.

"Furthermore, bhikṣu! The dharma of the anointed king includes eighty-four thousand maidens: namely, kṣatriya women and women resembling kṣatriya women, not to speak of the rest.

"Furthermore, bhikṣu! The dharma of the anointed king includes eighty-four thousand kinds of food, fully endowed with all flavors. Bhikṣu! Among the eighty-four thousand maidens, only one was used as an attendant. Among the eighty-four thousand jeweled garments, only one was worn. Among the eighty-four thousand jeweled beds, only one was slept upon. Among the eighty-four thousand palaces, only one was inhabited. Among the eighty-four thousand cities, only one was dwelt in, called Kuśāvatī. Among the eighty-four thousand jeweled chariots, only one was ridden, called Vaijayanta-nandi-ghoṣa, for going out of the city to survey. Among the eighty-four thousand jeweled horses, only one was ridden, called Valāha, with dark indigo mane and tail. Among the eighty-four thousand dragon elephants, only one was ridden, called Poṣadha, for going out of the city to survey.

"Bhikṣu! What kind of karmic reward is it that yields such power and sovereignty? This is the reward of three kinds of karma. What are the three? First, generosity; second, self-restraint; third, cultivation of the path. Bhikṣu, you should know that ordinary people are tainted and habituated to the five sensual pleasures without ever being satisfied, while the noble one, fulfilled in wisdom, always knows contentment. Bhikṣu! All formations of the past are entirely extinguished and changed. Those naturally obtained possessions and fame have all been worn away. Therefore, bhikṣu, permanently put to rest all formations; be disenchanted, abandon desire, and be liberated. Bhikṣu! Is form permanent or impermanent?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If impermanent, is it suffering?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"Bhikṣu! If it is impermanent and suffering, being a dharma subject to change, would a noble disciple within it conceive of a self, something other than self, or the two as mutually inherent?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, are feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"If impermanent, is it suffering?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

"Bhikṣu! If it is impermanent and suffering, being a dharma subject to change, would a noble disciple within it conceive of a self, something other than self, or the two as mutually inherent?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "All form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, not other than self, and the two are not mutually inherent. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, not other than self, and the two are not mutually inherent. Bhikṣu! Regarding form, one should generate disenchantment, disenchantment and freedom from desire, and liberation. Likewise regarding feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, one should generate disenchantment, freedom from desire, and liberation. With the knowledge and vision of liberation: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'"

At that time, that bhikṣu, hearing what the Buddha said, leaped for joy and rejoiced, bowed, and departed. He constantly reflected on the teaching of the lump of earth simile. Alone in a quiet place, he diligently contemplated, dwelling without negligence. Having dwelt without negligence: "The purpose for which a good man shaves off his hair and beard, with right faith leaves the household life to go forth and study the Way, is to reach the ultimate, the unsurpassed holy life, to see the Dharma and know for oneself, realizing directly: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'"

At that time, that venerable one also knew the Dharma for himself, and his mind was liberated. He became an arhat.

---

### (265)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at a place called Ayodhyā beside the Ganges River.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "It is like when the Ganges River has a great surge of water. Foam gathers as the current flows. A clear-sighted man carefully observes and analyzes it. When he carefully observes and analyzes it, there is nothing there; it has no solidity, no substance, no firmness. Why is this? Because in that mass of foam there is nothing solid. Likewise, all form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near: bhikṣus, carefully observe, contemplate, and analyze it. It is without substance, without solidity, without reality, without any firmness. It is like a disease, like a boil, like a thorn, like a killer; it is impermanent, suffering, empty, and not-self. Why is this? Because form has no solidity.

"Bhikṣus! It is like when there is a heavy rain and water bubbles arise and burst, one after another. A clear-sighted man carefully observes, contemplates, and analyzes them. When he carefully observes, contemplates, and analyzes them, there is nothing there; they have no solidity, no substance, no firmness. Why is this? Because those water bubbles have no solidity. Likewise, bhikṣus, all feeling whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near: bhikṣus, carefully observe, contemplate, and analyze it. When one carefully observes, contemplates, and analyzes it, there is nothing there; it has no solidity, no substance, no firmness. It is like a disease, like a boil, like a thorn, like a killer; it is impermanent, suffering, empty, and not-self. Why is this? Because feeling has no solidity.

"Bhikṣus! It is like in late spring and early summer, when there are no clouds and no rain, and the sun is blazing at midday, mirages shimmer and flow. A clear-sighted man carefully observes, contemplates, and analyzes them. When he carefully observes, contemplates, and analyzes them, there is nothing there; they have no solidity, no substance, no firmness. Why is this? Because those mirages have no solidity. Likewise, bhikṣus, all perception whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near: bhikṣus, carefully observe, contemplate, and analyze it. When one carefully observes, contemplates, and analyzes it, there is nothing there; it has no solidity, no substance, no firmness. It is like a disease, like a boil, like a thorn, like a killer; it is impermanent, suffering, empty, and not-self. Why is this? Because perception has no solidity.

"Bhikṣus! It is like a clear-sighted man seeking solid wood, holding a sharp axe, who enters the mountain forest. He sees a great plantain tree, tall, straight, and large. He then cuts its root, lops off its top, and peels away leaf after leaf. There is nothing solid at all. He carefully observes, contemplates, and analyzes it. When he carefully observes, contemplates, and analyzes it, there is nothing there; it has no solidity, no substance, no firmness. Why is this? Because that plantain tree has no solidity. Likewise, bhikṣus, all formations whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near: bhikṣus, carefully observe, contemplate, and analyze them. When one carefully observes, contemplates, and analyzes them, there is nothing there; they have no solidity, no substance, no firmness. They are like a disease, like a boil, like a thorn, like a killer; they are impermanent, suffering, empty, and not-self. Why is this? Because those formations have no solidity.

"Bhikṣus! It is like a magician or a magician's apprentice who, at a crossroads, conjures up an elephant army, a horse army, a chariot army, and an infantry army. A wise, clear-sighted man carefully observes, contemplates, and analyzes them. When he carefully observes, contemplates, and analyzes them, there is nothing there; they have no solidity, no substance, no firmness. Why is this? Because that magical conjuring has no solidity. Likewise, bhikṣus, all consciousness whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near: bhikṣus, carefully observe, contemplate, and analyze it. When one carefully observes, contemplates, and analyzes it, there is nothing there; it has no solidity, no substance, no firmness. It is like a disease, like a boil, like a thorn, like a killer; it is impermanent, suffering, empty, and not-self. Why is this? Because consciousness has no solidity."

At that time, the World-Honored One, wishing to further declare this meaning, spoke in verse:

> "Observe form as a mass of foam,
> Feeling as a bubble on water,
> Perception as a springtime mirage,
> Formations as a plantain tree,
> And consciousness as a magical illusion:
> So has the Kinsman of the Sun, the Honored One, declared.
> Thoroughly and carefully contemplate,
> With right mindfulness, skillfully observe:
> There is nothing real, nothing solid,
> No self and nothing belonging to a self.
> Regarding this body of suffering aggregates,
> The great sage has analytically taught:
> When separated from three things,
> The body becomes an abandoned thing.
> Life-warmth and consciousness:
> When these depart, the remaining body
> Is forever discarded among mounds and graves,
> Like wood, without consciousness or perception.
> This body is always thus,
> A deceptive lure for the foolish.
> Like a killer, like a poisonous thorn,
> There is nothing solid in it.
> Bhikṣus, diligently cultivate,
> Observe and examine this body of aggregates.
> Day and night, always with focused effort,
> Abide in right wisdom with mindfulness bound.
> Let conditioned formations be put to rest forever,
> And permanently attain the place of coolness."

At that time, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (266)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "In the beginningless cycle of birth and death, covered by ignorance, bound by the fetter of craving, beings have been wandering through the long night of saṃsāra, not knowing the beginning of suffering. There are times when, for a very long period, no rain falls, and all the hundred grains and plants on the earth completely dry up. Bhikṣus! If beings are covered by ignorance and bound by the fetter of craving, they cycle through birth and death in saṃsāra. As long as the fetter of craving is not cut, one cannot reach the end of suffering. Bhikṣus! There are times when, through the long night, no rain falls, and the waters of the great ocean all completely dry up. Bhikṣus! If beings are covered by ignorance and bound by the fetter of craving, they cycle through birth and death in saṃsāra. As long as the fetter of craving is not cut, one cannot reach the end of suffering. Bhikṣus! There are times when, through the long night, Mount Sumeru the King of Mountains entirely collapses. If beings are covered by ignorance and bound by the fetter of craving, they cycle through birth and death in saṃsāra through the long night. As long as the fetter of craving is not cut, one cannot reach the end of suffering. Bhikṣus! There are times when, through the long night, this great earth entirely breaks apart. Yet beings who are covered by ignorance and bound by the fetter of craving cycle through birth and death in saṃsāra through the long night. As long as the fetter of craving is not cut, one cannot reach the end of suffering.

"Bhikṣus! It is like a dog tied to a post. As long as the tether is not broken, it goes round and round the post through the long night, revolving and turning. Likewise, bhikṣus! The foolish, ordinary being who does not know as it truly is form, the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form, revolves through the long night, turning in accordance with form. Likewise, one who does not know as it truly is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, and the escape from consciousness, revolves through the long night, turning in accordance with consciousness. Bhikṣus! One turns in accordance with form, turns in accordance with feeling, turns in accordance with perception, turns in accordance with formations, turns in accordance with consciousness. Because one turns in accordance with form, one is not freed from form. Because one turns in accordance with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, one is not freed from consciousness. Because one is not freed, one is not freed from birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, suffering, and distress. A well-learned noble disciple who knows as it truly is form, the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form, does not turn in accordance with form. One who knows as it truly is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, and the escape from consciousness, does not turn in accordance with consciousness. Not turning in accordance, one is freed from form, freed from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. I declare that such a one is freed from birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, suffering, and distress."

The Buddha spoke this sūtra. At that time, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (267)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Beings, in the beginningless cycle of birth and death, covered by ignorance, bound by the fetter of craving, have been wandering through the long night of saṃsāra through birth and death, not knowing the limit of suffering. Bhikṣus! It is like a dog tied by a rope to a post. Because the tether is not broken, it turns around the post. Whether standing or lying down, it does not leave the post. Likewise, the foolish, ordinary being, regarding form, does not abandon desire, does not abandon craving, does not abandon affection, does not abandon thirst. One revolves around form, turns in accordance with form. Whether standing or lying down, one does not leave form. Likewise with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness: one turns in accordance with feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Whether standing or lying down, one does not leave consciousness.

"Bhikṣus! You should skillfully contemplate and observe the mind. Why is this? Through the long night, the mind has been defiled by desire, defiled by anger, defiled by delusion. Bhikṣus! When the mind is afflicted, beings are afflicted. When the mind is pure, beings are pure. Bhikṣus! I do not see any single form of as many varieties as a multicolored bird, and yet the mind surpasses even this. Why is this? Because the minds of those animals are of various kinds, and therefore their colors are of various kinds.

"Therefore, bhikṣus, you should skillfully contemplate and observe the mind. Bhikṣus! Through the long night, the mind has been defiled by desire, defiled by anger, defiled by delusion. When the mind is afflicted, beings are afflicted. When the mind is pure, beings are pure. Bhikṣus, you should know: have you seen the cāraṇa bird with its various mixed colors?"

They answered: "We have seen it, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Just as the cāraṇa bird has various mixed colors, I say that its mind being of various kinds is also just like this. Why is this? Because the cāraṇa bird's mind is of various kinds, therefore its colors are of various kinds. Therefore, you should skillfully observe, contemplate, and examine the mind. Through the long night, it has been defiled by various desires, anger, and delusion. When the mind is afflicted, beings are afflicted. When the mind is pure, beings are pure.

"It is like a painter or a painter's apprentice who skillfully prepares a clean surface and, having various colors, paints all kinds of images as he wishes.

"Likewise, bhikṣus, the foolish, ordinary being who does not know as it truly is form, the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form: because of not knowing as it truly is, one delights in and is attached to form. Because one delights in and is attached to form, one further produces forms in the future. Likewise, the foolish, ordinary being does not know as it truly is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, and the escape from consciousness. Because of not knowing as it truly is, one delights in and is attached to consciousness. Because one delights in and is attached to consciousness, one further produces consciousness in the future. Because one will produce form, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness in the future, one is not liberated from form, not liberated from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. I declare that such a one is not liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, suffering, and distress.

"A well-learned noble disciple who knows as it truly is form, the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form: because of knowing as it truly is, one does not delight in or become attached to form. Because one does not delight in or become attached, one does not produce forms in the future. One who knows as it truly is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, and the escape from consciousness: because of knowing as it truly is, one does not become defiled by or attached to consciousness. Because one does not delight in or become attached, one does not produce consciousness in the future. Because one does not delight in or become attached to form, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, one is liberated from form, liberated from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. I declare that such ones are liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, suffering, and distress."

The Buddha spoke this sūtra. At that time, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (268)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "It is like river water rushing down from mountain ravines. The water is deep and swift, its current fierce and turbulent, carrying much along. On both banks of the river, various grasses and trees grow. The great water bends them down, pressing them along the water's edge. When people try to ford across, many are carried away by the water, swept along and drowned by the current. When by chance they approach the bank, they reach out and grab hold of grasses and trees, but the grasses and trees break, and again they are carried away by the water.

"Likewise, bhikṣus, if foolish, ordinary beings do not know as it truly is form, the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form: because of not knowing as it truly is, one delights in and is attached to form, saying 'Form is my self,' and that form then breaks away. Likewise, one who does not know as it truly is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, and the escape from consciousness: because of not knowing as it truly is, one delights in and is attached to consciousness, saying 'Consciousness is my self,' and consciousness then breaks away.

"A well-learned noble disciple who knows as it truly is form, the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form: because of knowing as it truly is, one does not delight in or become attached to form. One who knows as it truly is feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, and the escape from consciousness: because of knowing as it truly is, one does not delight in or become attached to consciousness. Because one does not delight in or become attached, one knows for oneself and attains parinirvāṇa: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'"

The Buddha spoke this sūtra. At that time, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (269)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Whatever is not properly yours, you should completely abandon it. Having abandoned those dharmas, you will have peace and happiness through the long night. Bhikṣus! What dharmas are not properly yours, that you should quickly abandon? Thus form, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are not properly yours. You should completely abandon them. Having abandoned those dharmas, you will have peace and happiness through the long night. It is like the trees in Jeta's Grove: if someone were to cut off their branches and carry them away, you would not be troubled. Why is this? Because those trees are not self and not belonging to self.

"Likewise, bhikṣus, whatever is not properly yours, you should completely abandon it. Having abandoned it, you will have peace and happiness through the long night. What is not properly yours? Form is not properly yours; you should completely abandon it. Having abandoned it, you will have peace and happiness through the long night. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are not properly yours; you should quickly abandon them. Having abandoned those dharmas, you will have peace and happiness through the long night. Bhikṣus! Is form permanent or impermanent?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"Bhikṣu! If impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "If it is impermanent and suffering, being a dharma subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple see within it a self, something other than self, or the two as mutually inherent?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, are feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness permanent or impermanent?"

They answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

"Bhikṣu! If impermanent, is it suffering?"

They answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "If it is impermanent and suffering, being a dharma subject to change, would a well-learned noble disciple see within it a self, something other than self, or the two as mutually inherent?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Bhikṣus! Therefore, all form whatsoever, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, not other than self, and the two are not mutually inherent. Likewise, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or subtle, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, all of it is not self, not other than self, and the two are not mutually inherent. The noble disciple who observes these five aggregates of clinging as not self and not belonging to self, observing in this way, regarding all things in the world, has no attachment. Being without attachment, one naturally attains nirvāṇa: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'"

The Buddha spoke this sūtra. At that time, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (270)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "The perception of impermanence, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, is able to cut off all desire for sensual pleasure, desire for form, desire for the formless, restlessness, conceit, and ignorance. It is like a farmer who, at the end of summer and beginning of autumn, deeply plows his land, uprooting the roots and cutting the weeds. Likewise, bhikṣus, the perception of impermanence, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, is able to cut off all desire for sensual pleasure, desire for form, desire for the formless, restlessness, conceit, and ignorance.

"It is like, bhikṣus, when a man cuts grass, grasping it at the top with his hand, lifting and shaking it. The withered and dried parts all fall off, and he keeps the long ones. Likewise, bhikṣus, the perception of impermanence, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, is able to cut off all desire for sensual pleasure, desire for form, desire for the formless, restlessness, conceit, and ignorance.

"It is like āmra fruits attached to a tree: when a fierce wind shakes the branches, the fruits all fall off. Likewise, the perception of impermanence, when

# 雜阿含經

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.
