# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Twelve

---

### (283)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If, with regard to dharmas bound by fetters, one follows along generating relish, cherishing and recollecting, with the mind becoming bound, then craving arises. With craving as condition, clinging arises; with clinging as condition, becoming arises; with becoming as condition, birth arises; with birth as condition, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering arise. Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering gather together. It is like a person planting a tree. At first it is small and tender. One carefully nurtures and protects it, heaps manure and soil around it, waters it at the proper times, and adjusts for cold and warmth appropriately. By these causes and conditions, that tree afterward grows and becomes large. In the same way, bhikṣus, if one relishes and nourishes the dharmas bound by fetters, then affectionate attachment arises. With craving as condition, clinging arises; with clinging as condition, becoming arises; with becoming as condition, birth arises; with birth as condition, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering arise. Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering gather together.

"If, with regard to dharmas bound by fetters, one follows along with the contemplation of impermanence, dwells in the contemplation of arising and ceasing, the contemplation of desirelessness, the contemplation of cessation, the contemplation of relinquishment, and does not give rise to cherishing and recollecting, and the mind does not become bound and attached, then craving ceases. With the cessation of craving, clinging ceases; with the cessation of clinging, becoming ceases; with the cessation of becoming, birth ceases; with the cessation of birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering cease. Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering cease. It is like planting a tree. At first it is small and tender. One does not nurture and protect it, does not keep it secure, does not heap manure and soil around it, does not water it at the proper times, and the cold and warmth are not suitable, so it does not grow. If moreover one cuts its roots, severs its branches, chops it into sections, breaks it apart piece by piece, lets the wind scatter it and the sun parch it, and burns it with fire, burning it until it becomes ash, or lets the fierce wind blow it away, or casts it into flowing water. Bhikṣus, what do you think? Is it not the case that, with that tree having had its roots severed, and so on up to being burned by fire, causing it to be ground to nothing, it becomes in future worlds a dharma that will not arise again?"

They answered: "So it is, World-Honored One."

"In the same way, bhikṣus, with regard to dharmas bound by fetters, if one follows along with the contemplation of impermanence, dwells in the contemplation of arising and ceasing, the contemplation of desirelessness, the contemplation of cessation, the contemplation of relinquishment, and does not give rise to cherishing and recollecting, and the mind does not become bound and attached, then craving ceases. With the cessation of craving, clinging ceases; with the cessation of clinging, becoming ceases; with the cessation of becoming, birth ceases; with the cessation of birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering cease. Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering cease."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (284)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If, with regard to dharmas that are clung to, one follows along generating relish, cherishing and recollecting, with the mind becoming bound, then one's consciousness races about, pursuing name-and-form. With name-and-form as condition, the six sense bases arise; with the six sense bases as condition, contact arises; with contact as condition, feeling arises; with feeling as condition, craving arises; with craving as condition, clinging arises; with clinging as condition, becoming arises; with becoming as condition, birth arises; with birth as condition, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering arise. Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering gather together. It is like a great tree whose roots, trunk, branches, limbs, leaves, flowers, and fruits have deep and firm roots below, heaped with manure and soil, and watered. That tree stands firm and does not decay for ages. In the same way, bhikṣus, with regard to dharmas that are clung to, if one follows along generating relish, cherishing and recollecting, with the mind becoming bound, then one's consciousness races about, pursuing name-and-form. With name-and-form as condition, the six sense bases arise; with the six sense bases as condition, contact arises; with contact as condition, feeling arises; with feeling as condition, craving arises; with craving as condition, clinging arises; with clinging as condition, becoming arises; with becoming as condition, birth arises; with birth as condition, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering arise. Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering gather together.

"If, with regard to dharmas that are clung to, one follows along with the contemplation of impermanence, dwells in the contemplation of arising and ceasing, the contemplation of desirelessness, the contemplation of cessation, the contemplation of revulsion, and the mind does not cherish and recollect, and there is no binding or attachment, then consciousness does not race about pursuing name-and-form, and name-and-form ceases. With the cessation of name-and-form, the six sense bases cease; with the cessation of the six sense bases, contact ceases; with the cessation of contact, feeling ceases; with the cessation of feeling, craving ceases; with the cessation of craving, clinging ceases; with the cessation of clinging, becoming ceases; with the cessation of becoming, birth ceases; with the cessation of birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering cease. Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering cease. It is like planting a tree. One does not nurture and protect it at the proper times, does not keep it secure, does not heap manure and soil around it, does not water it at the proper times, and the cold and warmth are not suitable, so it does not grow. If moreover one cuts its roots, severs its branches, chops it into sections, breaks it apart piece by piece, lets the wind scatter it and the sun parch it, and burns it with fire, burning it until it becomes ash, or lets the fierce wind blow it away, or casts it into flowing water. Bhikṣus, what do you think? Is it not the case that, with that tree having had its roots severed, and so on up to being burned by fire, causing it to be ground to nothing, it becomes in future worlds a dharma that will not arise again?"

They answered: "So it is, World-Honored One."

"In the same way, bhikṣus, with regard to dharmas that are clung to, if one follows along with the contemplation of impermanence, dwells in the contemplation of arising and ceasing, the contemplation of desirelessness, the contemplation of cessation, the contemplation of relinquishment, and does not give rise to cherishing and recollecting, and the mind does not become bound and attached, then consciousness does not race about pursuing name-and-form, and name-and-form ceases. With the cessation of name-and-form, the six sense bases cease; with the cessation of the six sense bases, contact ceases; with the cessation of contact, feeling ceases; with the cessation of feeling, craving ceases; with the cessation of craving, clinging ceases; with the cessation of clinging, becoming ceases; with the cessation of becoming, birth ceases; with the cessation of birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering cease. Thus does the entire great mass of suffering cease."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (285)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I recall that in a past life, before I had attained perfect awakening, dwelling alone in a quiet place, with concentrated and diligent meditation, this thought arose: 'The world is hard to penetrate, namely, birth, old age, sickness, death, transmigration, and taking rebirth. Yet sentient beings do not truly know birth, old age, death, and what they depend upon.'

"I then had this thought: 'When what exists does birth exist? What is the condition for birth to exist?' Then, through right contemplation, direct knowledge arose: 'When becoming exists, birth exists; with becoming as condition, birth exists.'

"I further contemplated: 'When what exists does becoming exist? What is the condition for becoming to exist?' Then, through right contemplation, true direct knowledge arose: 'When clinging exists, becoming exists; with clinging as condition, becoming exists.'

"I further had this thought: 'What is the condition for clinging? When what exists does clinging exist? What is the condition for clinging to exist?' Then, through right contemplation, true direct knowledge arose: 'When one relishes dharmas that are clung to, cherishes and recollects them, and the mind becomes bound, craving and desire increase. When that craving exists, clinging exists. With craving as condition, clinging arises; with clinging as condition, becoming arises; with becoming as condition, birth arises; with birth as condition, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering arise.' Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering gather together.

"Bhikṣus, what do you think? Just as a lamp burns by relying on oil and a wick, if one repeatedly adds oil and trims the wick, would that lamp be able to burn for a long time?"

They answered: "So it is, World-Honored One."

"In the same way, bhikṣus, if one relishes, cherishes, recollects, and becomes bound by craving with regard to form, increasing craving as a condition, then clinging arises; with clinging as condition, becoming arises; with becoming as condition, birth arises; with birth as condition, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering arise. Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering gather together.

"I then further had this thought: 'When what does not exist is there no old age, sickness, and death? With the cessation of what do old age, sickness, and death cease?' Then, through right contemplation, true direct knowledge arose: 'When there is no birth, there is no old age, sickness, and death. With the cessation of birth, old age, sickness, and death cease.'

"I further had this thought: 'When what does not exist is there no birth? With the cessation of what does birth cease?' Then, through right contemplation, true direct knowledge arose: 'When there is no becoming, there is no birth. With the cessation of becoming, birth ceases.'

"I further contemplated: 'When what does not exist is there no becoming? With the cessation of what does becoming cease?' Then, through right contemplation, true direct knowledge and observation arose: 'When there is no clinging, there is no becoming. With the cessation of clinging, becoming ceases.'

"I further had this thought: 'When what does not exist is there no clinging? With the cessation of what does clinging cease?' Then, through right contemplation, true direct knowledge and observation arose: 'When the dharmas that are clung to are seen as impermanent, arising and ceasing, free from desire, ceasing, extinguished, and relinquished, and the mind does not cherish and recollect, and the mind does not become bound and attached, then craving ceases. With the cessation of that craving, clinging ceases; with the cessation of clinging, becoming ceases; with the cessation of becoming, birth ceases; with the cessation of birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering cease.' Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering cease.

"Bhikṣus, what do you think? Suppose a lamp burns with oil and a wick. If one does not add oil or tend the wick, would not that lamp's flame in the future fail to arise, and be exhausted and extinguished?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One."

"In the same way, bhikṣus, when one contemplates the dharmas that are clung to as impermanent, arising and ceasing, free from desire, ceasing, extinguished, and relinquished, and the mind does not cherish and recollect, and the mind does not become bound and attached, then craving ceases. With the cessation of craving, clinging ceases, and so on up to the cessation of the entire great mass of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (286)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I recall that in a past life, before I had attained perfect awakening, dwelling alone in a quiet place, with concentrated and diligent meditation," as extensively spoken above. The difference is: "Suppose one loads ten bundles of firewood, twenty bundles, thirty bundles, forty bundles, fifty bundles, a hundred bundles, a thousand bundles, a hundred thousand bundles, piles them up and sets them ablaze, making a great mass of fire. If someone further adds dry grass and firewood, bhikṣus, what do you think? Would that fire continue to blaze throughout the long night?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One."

"In the same way, bhikṣus, if one relishes, cherishes, recollects, and has the mind bound and attached to the dharmas that are clung to, increasing craving as a condition, then clinging arises; with clinging as condition, becoming arises, and so on up to the gathering together of the entire great mass of suffering. Bhikṣus, if that blazing mass of fire has no firewood or grass added to it, bhikṣus, what do you think? Would that fire then go out?"

They answered: "So it is, World-Honored One."

"In the same way, bhikṣus, when one contemplates the dharmas that are clung to as impermanent, arising and ceasing, free from desire, ceasing, extinguished, and relinquished, and the mind does not cherish, recollect, or become bound and attached, then craving ceases. With the cessation of craving, clinging ceases," as extensively spoken, and so on up to "the cessation of the entire great mass of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (287)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I recall that in a past life, before I had attained perfect awakening, dwelling alone in a quiet place, with concentrated and diligent meditation, this thought arose: 'When what exists does old age and death exist? What is the condition for old age and death to exist?' Then, through right contemplation, true direct knowledge arose: 'When birth exists, old age and death exist; with birth as condition, old age and death exist.' In the same way for becoming, clinging, craving, feeling, contact, the six sense bases, and name-and-form. 'When what exists does name-and-form exist? What is the condition for name-and-form to exist?' Then, through right contemplation, true direct knowledge arose: 'When consciousness exists, name-and-form exists; with consciousness as condition, name-and-form exists.' When I contemplated thus, it turned back at consciousness and could not go beyond it. That is to say, with consciousness as condition, name-and-form arises; with name-and-form as condition, the six sense bases arise; with the six sense bases as condition, contact arises; with contact as condition, feeling arises; with feeling as condition, craving arises; with craving as condition, clinging arises; with clinging as condition, becoming arises; with becoming as condition, birth arises; with birth as condition, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering arise. Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering gather together.

"I then had this thought: 'When what does not exist is there no old age and death? With the cessation of what do old age and death cease?' Then, through right contemplation, true direct knowledge arose: 'When there is no birth, there is no old age and death. With the cessation of birth, old age and death cease.' In the same way for birth, becoming, clinging, craving, feeling, contact, the six sense bases, name-and-form, consciousness, and formations," as extensively spoken.

"I further had this contemplation: 'When what does not exist are there no formations? With the cessation of what do formations cease?' Then, through right contemplation, true direct knowledge arose: 'When there is no ignorance, there are no formations. With the cessation of ignorance, formations cease. With the cessation of formations, consciousness ceases. With the cessation of consciousness, name-and-form ceases. With the cessation of name-and-form, the six sense bases cease. With the cessation of the six sense bases, contact ceases. With the cessation of contact, feeling ceases. With the cessation of feeling, craving ceases. With the cessation of craving, clinging ceases. With the cessation of clinging, becoming ceases. With the cessation of becoming, birth ceases. With the cessation of birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering cease.' Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering cease.

"I then had this thought: 'I have found the ancient path of the ancient sages, the ancient trail of the ancient sages, the ancient tracks of the ancient sages. The ancient sages departed by following this path, and now I follow it.' It is like a person wandering in the wilderness, pushing through the overgrowth to find a road, who suddenly discovers an ancient path where people of old once traveled. He then follows it along, gradually advancing, and sees an ancient city, an ancient royal palace, with parks, ponds, bathing pools, forests, and pure clearings. He then thinks: 'I should now go and inform the king.' He goes and tells the king: 'Great King, you should know that while wandering in the wilderness, pushing through the overgrowth to find a road, I suddenly discovered an ancient path where people of old once traveled. I followed it along. Following it, I saw an ancient city, an ancient royal palace, with parks, ponds, bathing pools, streams, forests, and pure clearings. Great King, you could go and dwell there.' The king then goes there, takes up residence, and the place becomes prosperous, peaceful, and secure, with the people flourishing.

"Now I am likewise. I have found the ancient path of the ancient sages, the ancient trail of the ancient sages, the ancient tracks of the ancient sages. The ancient sages departed by that path, and I have followed it. That is to say, the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration. Following that path, I saw old age, sickness, and death; the arising of old age, sickness, and death; the cessation of old age, sickness, and death; and the path leading to the cessation of old age, sickness, and death. I saw birth, becoming, clinging, craving, feeling, contact, the six sense bases, name-and-form, consciousness, formations; the arising of formations; the cessation of formations; and the path leading to the cessation of formations. I, with regard to this Dharma, through my own knowledge and my own realization, attained perfect and complete awakening. I then taught it for bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, upāsikās, as well as other non-Buddhist śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, householders, and renunciants. Those four assemblies, hearing the Dharma, were rightly directed, with faith and delight, and came to know the Dharma well. The holy life increased and spread widely, greatly benefiting and illuminating many, being revealed and made manifest."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (288)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra and Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila were on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra, having risen from meditation in the afternoon, went to visit Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila. Having exchanged greetings and kind inquiries, he sat to one side and said to Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila: "I wish to ask a question. Do you have the leisure to answer?"

Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila said to Venerable Śāriputra: "Venerable one, please ask. If I know, I shall answer."

Venerable Śāriputra asked Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila: "How is it? Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila, is there old age?"

He answered: "There is."

Venerable Śāriputra further asked: "Is there death?"

He answered: "There is."

He further asked: "How is it? Is old age and death self-made? Is it made by another? Is it made by both self and other? Or is it made neither by self nor by other, arising without a cause?"

He answered: "Venerable Śāriputra, old age and death is not self-made, not made by another, not made by both self and other, and also not made neither by self nor by other, arising without a cause. Rather, it is because of birth as condition that there is old age and death."

"In the same way for birth, becoming, clinging, craving, feeling, contact, the six sense bases, and name-and-form: is it self-made, made by another, made by both self and other, or made neither by self nor by other, arising without a cause?"

He answered: "Venerable Śāriputra, name-and-form is not self-made, not made by another, not made by both self and other, and not made neither by self nor by other, arising without a cause. Rather, name-and-form arises with consciousness as condition."

He further asked: "Is that consciousness self-made, made by another, made by both self and other, or made neither by self nor by other, arising without a cause?"

He answered: "Venerable Śāriputra, that consciousness is not self-made, not made by another, not made by both self and other, and not made neither by self nor by other, arising without a cause. Rather, that consciousness arises with name-and-form as condition."

Venerable Śāriputra further asked: "Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila, you previously said that name-and-form is not self-made, not made by another, not made by both self and other, and not made neither by self nor by other, arising without a cause, but rather that name-and-form arises with consciousness as condition. And now you further say that consciousness arises with name-and-form as condition. What is the meaning of this?"

Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila answered: "I shall now give an analogy, for the wise can understand through analogy. It is like three reeds standing on open ground, mutually supporting each other, and thereby able to stand upright. If you remove one, the other two also cannot stand. If you remove two, the remaining one also cannot stand. They mutually support each other and thereby are able to stand upright. Consciousness conditioning name-and-form is also like this. They mutually support each other and thereby grow."

Venerable Śāriputra said: "Excellent! Excellent! Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila! Among the World-Honored One's voice-hearers, those with penetrating wisdom, well-tamed and fearless, who have seen the Dharma of the deathless nectar and who have personally realized the Dharma of the deathless nectar with the fullness of their being, this refers to Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila, who is able to engage in such profoundly meaningful dialectics, answering all manner of difficult questions. Like a priceless jewel that the world places upon its head, I now place Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila upon my head in the same way. Today, in your presence, I have well obtained a good benefit. Other practitioners of the holy life who frequently visit you also obtain good benefit, because that venerable one teaches the Dharma well. Today, because of what Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila has spoken, I shall praise, extol, and rejoice with thirty kinds of commendation."

Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila said: "One who teaches revulsion toward old age and death, dispassion, and complete cessation is called a Dharma teacher. One who teaches revulsion toward birth, becoming, clinging, craving, feeling, contact, the six sense bases, name-and-form, and consciousness, dispassion, and complete cessation is called a Dharma teacher. If a bhikṣu is directed toward revulsion, dispassion, and complete cessation with regard to old age and death, this is called a Dharma teacher. And so on up to one directed toward revulsion, dispassion, and complete cessation with regard to consciousness, this is called a Dharma teacher. If a bhikṣu, through revulsion toward old age and death, dispassion, and complete cessation, gives rise to no more outflows and the mind is well liberated, this is called a Dharma teacher. And so on up to one who, through revulsion toward consciousness, dispassion, and complete cessation, gives rise to no more outflows and the mind is well liberated, this is called a Dharma teacher."

Venerable Mahākauṣṭhila said to Venerable Śāriputra: "Excellent! Excellent! Among the World-Honored One's voice-hearers, those with penetrating wisdom, well-tamed and fearless, who have seen the Dharma of the deathless nectar and who have personally realized the Dharma of the deathless nectar with the fullness of their being, this refers to Venerable Śāriputra, who is able to ask such various kinds of profoundly wise and correct questions. Like a priceless jewel in the world that everyone places upon their head, you are likewise universally placed upon the head by all practitioners of the holy life, who revere and serve you. Today I have well obtained a good benefit, having been able to discuss the sublime meaning together with the venerable one."

At that time, the two excellent ones, having rejoiced in each other, each returned to their dwelling.

---

### (289)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Foolish, unlearned worldlings may develop revulsion, dispassion, and turn away from the body composed of the four great elements, but not from consciousness. Why is this? They see that the body composed of the four great elements has increase and decrease, has taking up and discarding. But with regard to mind, mentation, and consciousness, the foolish, unlearned worldlings are unable to develop revulsion, dispassion, and liberation. Why is this? Because for a long time they have clung to this, cherished it, and identified it as self, saying: 'This is what I obtain, this is what I grasp, saying: this is my self, this is what belongs to me, it is inherent in me.' Therefore, the foolish, unlearned worldlings are unable to develop revulsion, dispassion, and turn away from it. It would be better for the foolish, unlearned worldlings to identify the body composed of the four great elements as self rather than to identify consciousness as self. Why is this? The body composed of the four great elements may be seen to last ten years, twenty, thirty, and up to a hundred years, or somewhat more if well cared for. But mind, mentation, and consciousness change and transform moment by moment, day and night, ceaselessly, arising differently and perishing differently. It is like a monkey roaming through the forest trees, seizing branches from place to place moment by moment, releasing one and grasping another. Mind, mentation, and consciousness are also like this, arising differently and perishing differently.

"The well-learned noble disciple carefully contemplates and examines dependent origination, namely: 'A pleasant contact conditions the arising of a pleasant feeling. When one is aware of a pleasant feeling, one truly knows: I am aware of a pleasant feeling. When that pleasant contact ceases, the feeling born from the condition of that pleasant contact also ceases, stops, cools, is stilled, and disappears.' Just as with pleasant feeling, so too with painful contact, joyful contact, sorrowful contact, and equanimous contact. 'An equanimous contact conditions the arising of an equanimous feeling. When one is aware of an equanimous feeling, one truly knows: I am aware of an equanimous feeling. When that equanimous contact ceases, the equanimous feeling born from the condition of that equanimous contact also ceases, stops, cools, is stilled, and disappears.' One then contemplates thus: 'This feeling is born from contact, delighted by contact, bound by contact. Because of delight in this or that contact, there is delight in this or that feeling. When delight in this or that contact ceases, delight in this or that feeling also ceases, stops, cools, is stilled, and disappears.' Thus, the well-learned noble disciple develops revulsion toward form, develops revulsion toward feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness. Through revulsion, one finds no delight. Through finding no delight, one is liberated. With liberation comes the knowledge and vision: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (290)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Foolish, unlearned worldlings may develop revulsion, dispassion, and turn away from the body composed of the four great elements and form, but not from consciousness. Why is this? The body composed of the four great elements and form is seen to have increase and decrease, to have taking up and discarding. But with regard to mind, mentation, and consciousness, the foolish, unlearned worldlings are unable to develop revulsion, dispassion, and turn away from consciousness. For a long time they have clung to this, cherished it, and identified it as self, saying: 'This is what I obtain, this is what I grasp, saying: this is my self, this is what belongs to me, it is inherent in me.' Therefore, the foolish, unlearned worldlings are unable to develop revulsion, dispassion, and turn away from it.

"It would be better for the foolish, unlearned worldlings to identify the body composed of the four great elements and form as self rather than to identify consciousness as self. Why is this? The body composed of the four great elements and form may be seen to last ten years, twenty, thirty, and up to a hundred years, or somewhat more if well cared for. But mind, mentation, and consciousness, moment by moment, day and night, ceaselessly, undergo various transformations, arising differently and perishing differently. It is like a monkey roaming through the forest trees, seizing branches from place to place moment by moment, releasing one and grasping another. Mind, mentation, and consciousness are also like this, undergoing various changes, arising differently and perishing differently.

"The well-learned noble disciple contemplates and examines dependent origination, namely: 'A pleasant contact conditions the arising of a pleasant feeling. When one is aware of a pleasant feeling, one truly knows: I am aware of a pleasant feeling. When that pleasant contact ceases, the pleasant feeling born from the condition of that pleasant contact also ceases, stops, cools, is stilled, and disappears.' Just as with pleasant feeling, so too with painful contact, joyful contact, sorrowful contact, and equanimous contact. 'An equanimous contact conditions the arising of an equanimous feeling. When one is aware of an equanimous feeling, one truly knows: I am aware of an equanimous feeling. When that equanimous contact ceases, the equanimous feeling born from the condition of that equanimous contact also ceases, stops, cools, is stilled, and disappears.' It is like two sticks being rubbed together: from their combination, fire arises. If the two sticks are separated, the fire also ceases. In the same way, feelings arise through the gathering of contact. They are born from the gathering of contact. When this or that gathering of contact arises, this or that feeling also arises. When that gathering of this or that contact ceases, the gathering of this or that feeling also ceases, stops, cools, is stilled, and disappears. The well-learned noble disciple who contemplates thus is liberated from form, is liberated from feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, is liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering. I declare that such a one is liberated from suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (291)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I have taught the Dharma of internal contact. Have you all grasped it?"

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, adjusted his robes, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, joined his palms, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, the Dharma of internal contact that you have spoken, I have grasped it." That bhikṣu then declared his understanding before the Buddha in such and such a manner. But as he spoke in such and such a manner, the World-Honored One was not pleased.

At that time, Venerable Ānanda was behind the Buddha, holding a fan and fanning the Buddha. The Buddha said to Ānanda: "The Dharma of internal contact as taught in the noble Dharma and Vinaya is different from what this bhikṣu has stated."

Ānanda said to the Buddha: "Now is the right time. May the World-Honored One please teach the bhikṣus the Dharma of internal contact according to the noble Dharma and Vinaya. Having heard it, the bhikṣus will accept and practice it."

The Buddha said to Ānanda: "Good! Listen carefully, and I shall explain it to you. These bhikṣus should grasp the Dharma of internal contact by contemplating thus: 'Whatever various sufferings arise for sentient beings, what is the cause of this suffering, what is the origin, what is the source, what is the occasion?' When one grasps it thus, one should know: this suffering has upādāna (attachment) as its cause, upādāna as its origin, upādāna as its source, upādāna as its occasion.

"Furthermore, bhikṣus, with the Dharma of internal contact, one should ask: what is the cause, origin, source, and occasion of upādāna? When one grasps it thus, one should further know: upādāna has craving as its cause, craving as its origin, craving as its source, craving as its occasion.

"Furthermore, bhikṣus, grasping the Dharma of internal contact, one should further know: what is the cause, origin, source, and occasion of craving? When one grasps it thus, one should know: whatever pleasing and attractive forms there are in the world, craving arises there, is born there, binds and is bound there, settles and abides there.

"If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas, with regard to pleasing and attractive forms in the world, give rise to the perception of permanence, the perception of constancy, the perception of security, the perception of freedom from illness, the perception of self, the perception of what belongs to self, then craving increases. When craving increases, upādāna increases. When upādāna increases, suffering increases. When suffering increases, one is not liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering. I say that such a one is not liberated from suffering. It is like a clear, cool pool of water by the roadside, with perfect fragrance and taste. Someone has put poison in it. In the heat of spring, travelers oppressed by wind, heat, and thirst, compete to come and drink. Someone says to them: 'Gentlemen! This is a clear, cool pool with perfect color, fragrance, and taste. However, there is poison in it. Do not drink from it. If you drink, it may cause your death or suffering close to death.' But those thirsty ones do not believe and drink. Although they enjoy the pleasant taste, in a short time some die or suffer close to death. In the same way, if śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas see the pleasing and attractive forms of the world and give rise to the view of permanence, the view of constancy, the view of security, the view of freedom from illness, the view of self, the view of what belongs to self, and so on up to being unable to attain liberation from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering.

"If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas, with regard to the pleasing and attractive forms in the world, contemplate them as disease, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer, as impermanent, as suffering, as empty, as non-self, then craving is abandoned. When craving is abandoned, upādāna is abandoned. When upādāna is abandoned, suffering is abandoned. When suffering is abandoned, one is separated from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering. It is like a clear, cool pool of water by the roadside, with perfect fragrance and taste. Someone has put poison in it. In the heat of spring, travelers oppressed by wind, heat, and thirst, compete to come and drink. Someone says to them: 'This water has poison! Do not drink from it. If you drink, it may cause your death or suffering close to death.' They then think: 'This water has poison. If I drink it, it may cause my death or suffering close to death. I shall endure my thirst and eat dry grain and rice rather than drink this water.' In the same way, śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who, with regard to the pleasing forms of the world, contemplate them as disease, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer, as impermanent, as suffering, as empty, as non-self, and so on up to attaining liberation from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering. Therefore, Ānanda, with regard to this Dharma, as it is seen, as it is heard, as it is sensed, as it is known, with regard to the past and the future as well, one should contemplate in this same way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (292)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "How does one contemplate and examine to completely end suffering and reach the ultimate end of suffering? At that time, one contemplates all the various sufferings of sentient beings, with their manifold distinctions. What is the cause of these sufferings, what is the origin, what is the source, what is the occasion? One contemplates that clinging is the cause, clinging is the origin, clinging is the source, clinging is the occasion. If that clinging ceases without remainder, all sufferings cease. One truly knows the path leading to the cessation of those sufferings one has been carrying, and practices the sequential Dharma leading thereto. This is called a bhikṣu directed toward completely ending suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering, namely, the cessation of clinging.

"Furthermore, a bhikṣu contemplates and examines to completely end suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering. At that time, one contemplates: what is the cause, origin, source, and occasion of that clinging? One contemplates that clinging has craving as its cause, craving as its origin, craving as its source, craving as its occasion. When that craving ceases completely without remainder, clinging also ceases accordingly. One truly knows the path leading to the cessation of the suffering one has been carrying, namely the cessation of clinging, and practices the sequential Dharma leading thereto. This is called a bhikṣu directed toward completely ending suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering, namely, the cessation of craving.

"Furthermore, a bhikṣu contemplates and examines to completely end suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering. One then contemplates: what is the cause, origin, source, and occasion of that craving? One knows that craving has feeling as its cause, feeling as its origin, feeling as its source, feeling as its occasion. When that feeling ceases completely without remainder, craving ceases. One truly knows the path leading to the cessation of craving, and practices the sequential Dharma leading thereto. This is called a bhikṣu directed toward completely ending suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering, namely, the cessation of feeling.

"Furthermore, a bhikṣu contemplates and examines to completely end suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering. At that time, one contemplates: what is the cause, origin, source, and occasion of that feeling? One knows that feeling has contact as its cause, contact as its origin, contact as its source, contact as its condition. When that contact ceases completely without remainder, feeling ceases. One truly knows the path leading to the cessation of contact, and practices the sequential Dharma leading thereto. This is called a bhikṣu directed toward completely ending suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering.

"Furthermore, a bhikṣu contemplates and examines to completely end suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering. At that time, one contemplates: what is the cause, origin, source, and occasion of that contact? One should know that contact has the six sense bases as its cause, the six sense bases as its origin, the six sense bases as its source, the six sense bases as its occasion. When the six sense bases cease without remainder, contact ceases. One truly knows the path leading to the cessation of the six sense bases, and practices the sequential Dharma leading thereto. This is called a bhikṣu directed toward completely ending suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering.

"Furthermore, a bhikṣu contemplates and examines to completely end suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering. At that time, one contemplates: what is the cause, origin, source, and occasion of the six sense bases? One knows that the six sense bases have name-and-form as their cause, name-and-form as their origin, name-and-form as their source, name-and-form as their occasion. When name-and-form ceases completely without remainder, the six sense bases cease. One truly knows the path leading to the cessation of name-and-form, and practices the sequential Dharma leading thereto. This is called a bhikṣu directed toward completely ending suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering, namely, the cessation of name-and-form.

"Furthermore, a bhikṣu contemplates to completely end suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering. At that time, one contemplates: what is the cause, origin, source, and occasion of name-and-form? One knows that name-and-form has consciousness as its cause, consciousness as its origin, consciousness as its source, consciousness as its occasion. When consciousness ceases without remainder, name-and-form ceases. One truly knows the path leading to the cessation of consciousness, and practices the sequential Dharma leading thereto. This is called a bhikṣu directed toward completely ending suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering, namely, the cessation of consciousness.

"Furthermore, a bhikṣu contemplates and examines to completely end suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering. At that time, one contemplates: what is the cause, origin, source, and occasion of that consciousness? One knows that consciousness has formations as its cause, formations as its origin, formations as its source, formations as its occasion. When one performs meritorious formations, wholesome consciousness arises. When one performs demeritorious and unwholesome formations, unwholesome consciousness arises. When one performs formations of nothingness, consciousness of nothingness arises. This is how consciousness has formations as its cause, formations as its origin, formations as its source, formations as its occasion. When formations cease without remainder, consciousness ceases. One truly knows the path leading to the cessation of formations, and practices the sequential Dharma leading thereto. This is called a bhikṣu directed toward completely ending suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering, namely, the cessation of formations.

"Furthermore, a bhikṣu contemplates and examines to completely end suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering. At that time, one contemplates: what is the cause, origin, source, and occasion of those formations? One knows that formations have ignorance as their cause, ignorance as their origin, ignorance as their source, ignorance as their occasion. Meritorious formations are conditioned by ignorance, demeritorious formations are also conditioned by ignorance, and formations that are neither meritorious nor demeritorious are also conditioned by ignorance.

"Therefore, one should know: formations have ignorance as their cause, ignorance as their origin, ignorance as their source, ignorance as their occasion. When that ignorance ceases completely without remainder, formations cease. One truly knows the path leading to the cessation of ignorance, and practices the sequential Dharma leading thereto. This is called a bhikṣu directed toward completely ending suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering, namely, the cessation of ignorance."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? If one abandons delight in ignorance and gives rise to clear knowing, would one again, conditioned by that ignorance, perform meritorious formations, demeritorious formations, or formations of nothingness?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One. Why is this? The well-learned noble disciple abandons delight in ignorance and gives rise to clear knowing. When ignorance ceases, formations cease. When formations cease, consciousness ceases. And so on, up to the cessation of birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering. Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering cease."

The Buddha said: "Excellent! Excellent, bhikṣus! I also say this, and you also understand this. When, in connection with this or that dharma, this or that dharma arises; when, in connection with this or that dharma arising, this or that dharma comes to be; when this or that dharma ceases, this or that dharma ceases, stops, cools, is stilled, and disappears. When the well-learned noble disciple becomes dispassionate toward ignorance and gives rise to clear knowing, and there is bodily feeling up to the extent of the body, one truly knows the bodily feeling at the time of experiencing it. When there is feeling up to the extent of the lifespan, one truly knows the feeling at the time of experiencing it. When the body breaks apart and the lifespan is about to end, all feelings and all that is experienced completely cease without remainder. It is like a strong man who takes a newly fired earthen pot and, while it is still hot, places it on the ground. In a short time it breaks apart and its heat is entirely extinguished. In the same way, bhikṣus, when one becomes dispassionate toward ignorance and gives rise to clear knowing, one truly knows the bodily feeling up to the extent of the body, and truly knows the feeling up to the extent of the lifespan. When the body breaks apart and life ends, all feelings and all that is experienced completely cease without remainder."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (293)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed a certain bhikṣu: "I have already crossed over doubt, left behind hesitation, pulled out the thorn of wrong views, and no longer turn back. Because the mind has no attachment, from what position would I teach the Dharma for that bhikṣu? I teach the Dharma for that bhikṣu by teaching the noble, world-transcending Dharma of emptiness, corresponding to dependent origination, in accord with the sequential Dharma. That is to say, because this exists, that exists; because this arises, that arises. Namely, with ignorance as condition, formations arise; with formations as condition, consciousness arises; with consciousness as condition, name-and-form arises; with name-and-form as condition, the six sense bases arise; with the six sense bases as condition, contact arises; with contact as condition, feeling arises; with feeling as condition, craving arises; with craving as condition, clinging arises; with clinging as condition, becoming arises; with becoming as condition, birth arises; with birth as condition, old age, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering arise. Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering gather together, and so on up to the cessation of the entire great mass of suffering.

"Having taught the Dharma in this way, if that bhikṣu still has doubt and hesitation, having previously not obtained the perception of having obtained, not having achieved the perception of having achieved, not having realized the perception of having realized, and now, after hearing the Dharma, gives rise to sorrow, suffering, regret, confusion, and obstruction in the mind, why is this? This is a profoundly deep matter, namely dependent origination. And doubly more profoundly deep and hard to see is the relinquishment of all clinging, the exhaustion of craving, desirelessness, cessation, nirvāṇa. These two dharmas, the conditioned and the unconditioned: the conditioned has arising, abiding, changing, and ceasing; the unconditioned has no arising, no abiding, no changing, and no ceasing. This is called, bhikṣus, the suffering of all formations, their peaceful cessation, nirvāṇa. Because of the gathering of causes, suffering gathers. Because of the cessation of causes, suffering ceases. Having cut off all paths and extinguished continuity, when continuity is extinguished, this is called the end of suffering. Bhikṣus, what is it that ceases? It is the remaining suffering. When that ceases, stops, cools, is stilled, and disappears, this is called the complete relinquishment of all clinging, the exhaustion of craving, desirelessness, cessation, nirvāṇa."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (294)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Foolish, unlearned worldlings, covered by ignorance and bound by the fetters of craving, obtain this body of consciousness. Internally there is this body of consciousness; externally there is name-and-form. From these two as conditions, contact arises. Through the contact of these six sense bases, the foolish, unlearned worldlings experience feelings of pleasure and pain, and from these various kinds arise. What are the six? The eye-contact sense base, the ear-contact sense base, the nose-contact sense base, the tongue-contact sense base, the body-contact sense base, and the mind-contact sense base. If the wise person, covered by ignorance and bound by the fetters of craving, obtains this body of consciousness, in the same way, internally there is the body of consciousness, externally there is name-and-form, and from these two as conditions, the six contact sense bases arise. Touched by the six kinds of contact, the wise person also experiences feelings of pleasure and pain, and from these various kinds arise. What are the six? The eye-contact sense base, the ear-contact sense base, the nose-contact sense base, the tongue-contact sense base, the body-contact sense base, and the mind-contact sense base. What then is the difference between the foolish person and the wise person regarding those who cultivate the holy life under me?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. Excellent, World-Honored One! May you please expound this. Having heard it, the bhikṣus will accept and practice it."

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully and reflect well. I shall explain it to you. Bhikṣus, the foolish, unlearned worldling, covered by ignorance and bound by the fetters of craving, obtains this body of consciousness. That person's ignorance is not cut off and the fetters of craving are not exhausted. When the body breaks apart and life ends, one again receives a body. Because one again receives a body, one is not liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering. Why is this? Because this foolish worldling has not previously cultivated the holy life directed toward the complete ending of suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering. Therefore, when the body breaks apart and life ends, one again receives a body. Because one again receives a body, one is not liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering.

"If the wise person, covered by ignorance and bound by the fetters of craving, obtains this body of consciousness, that person's ignorance is cut off and the fetters of craving are exhausted. Because ignorance is cut off and the fetters of craving are exhausted, when the body breaks apart and life ends, one does not again receive a body. Because one does not again receive a body, one is liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering. Why is this? Because that person previously cultivated the holy life, rightly directed toward the complete ending of suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering. Therefore, when that person's body breaks apart and life ends, one does not again receive a body. Because one does not again receive a body, one is liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering. This is the difference between the worldling and the wise person regarding those who cultivate the holy life under me, with their various distinctions."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (295)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "This body does not belong to you, nor does it belong to anyone else. It is said that the six contact sense bases, through formerly cultivated intentional actions, have received this body. What are the six? The eye-contact sense base, the ear-contact sense base, the nose-contact sense base, the tongue-contact sense base, the body-contact sense base, and the mind-contact sense base. The well-learned noble disciple carefully and correctly contemplates and examines dependent origination: 'There are these six consciousness-bodies, six contact-bodies, six feeling-bodies, six perception-bodies, and six volition-bodies. Because this exists, there will be birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering in the future. Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering gather together. This is called: with cause and with condition, there is the arising of the world.' 'Because this does not exist, the six consciousness-bodies do not exist; the six contact-bodies, six feeling-bodies, six perception-bodies, and six volition-bodies do not exist. Because this does not exist, there will be no birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering in the future. Thus, thus does the entire great mass of suffering cease.' If the well-learned noble disciple truly and correctly knows the arising of the world and the cessation of the world, sees it well, perceives it well, and enters it well, this is called a noble disciple who has attained this good Dharma, obtained this good Dharma, knows this good Dharma, entered this good Dharma, who perceives and sees the arising and ceasing of the world, and has accomplished the noble transcendence that is real, peaceful, the complete ending of suffering, reaching the ultimate end of suffering. Why is this? Because the well-learned noble disciple truly knows the arising and cessation of the world, sees it well, perceives it well, and enters it well."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (296)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now explain the dharma of conditionality and conditionally arisen dharmas.

"What is the dharma of conditionality? This means: because this exists, that exists. That is, with ignorance as condition, formations arise; with formations as condition, consciousness arises, and so on up to the gathering together of the entire great mass of suffering.

"What are conditionally arisen dharmas? They are ignorance, formations, and so on. Whether a Tathāgata appears in the world or does not appear in the world, this Dharma abides constantly; the Dharma abides in the realm of Dharma. The Tathāgata himself awakens to this Dharma and attains perfect and complete awakening, and then for people expounds, teaches, reveals, and makes manifest: 'With ignorance as condition, there are formations,' and so on up to 'with birth as condition, there is old age and death.' Whether a Tathāgata appears in the world or does not appear in the world, this Dharma abides constantly; the Dharma abides in the realm of Dharma. The Tathāgata himself awakens to it and attains perfect and complete awakening, and then for people expounds, teaches, reveals, and makes manifest: 'Because of birth as condition, there is old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering.' These various dharmas are dharma-abiding, dharma-emptiness, dharma-suchness, dharma-thusness, not departing from suchness, not different from suchness, examined and verified as true and real, not inverted. Accordingly following dependent origination, these are called conditionally arisen dharmas. They are ignorance, formations, consciousness, name-and-form, the six sense bases, contact, feeling, craving, clinging, becoming, birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and suffering. These are called conditionally arisen dharmas.

"The well-learned noble disciple, with regard to this dharma of conditionality and these conditionally arisen dharmas, truly knows and rightly sees. One does not seek the past, saying: 'Did I exist in the past, or did I not exist? What kind of being was I in the past? What was I like in the past?' One does not seek the future: 'Will I exist in the future, or will I not exist? What kind of being will I be? What will I be like?' One does not have internal doubt: 'What is this? How did this come to be? Who was it before? Who will it ultimately become? Where did this being come from? Having departed from here, where will it go?' If there are śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas who give rise to ordinary worldly views and are bound by them, namely, bound by the view of self, bound by the view of beings, bound by the view of a lifespan, and bound by superstitious and auspicious views, at that time all these are cut off and fully known. Their roots are severed, like cutting off the top of a tāla tree, so that in the future they become dharmas that will not arise again. This is called the well-learned noble disciple who, with regard to the dharma of conditionality and conditionally arisen dharmas, truly and correctly knows, rightly sees, rightly perceives, rightly cultivates, and rightly enters."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (297)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Kuru country, in the village of Kalmāṣadamya.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall teach you a Dharma that is good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, with good meaning and good expression, pure and unadulterated, the holy life made perfectly clear. It is called the Sūtra on the Great Emptiness. Listen carefully and reflect well. I shall explain it to you. What is the Sūtra on the Great Emptiness? It is this: because this exists, that exists; because this arises, that arises. Namely, with ignorance as condition, formations arise; with formations as condition, consciousness arises, and so on up to the gathering together of the entire great mass of suffering.

"Regarding 'with birth as condition, there is old age and death': if someone were to ask, 'Whose old age and death is it? To whom does old age and death belong?', one would answer: 'Old age and death is myself. Old age and death belongs to me now. Old age and death is me.' Saying: 'The life-force is the same as the body,' or saying: 'The life-force is different from the body.' This is one and the same meaning, but spoken of in various ways. If one holds the view that 'the life-force is the same as the body,' the practitioner of the holy life would have no such view. If one holds the view that 'the life-force is different from the body,' the practitioner of the holy life would have no such view. Without following either of these two extremes, the mind is rightly directed to the Middle Way. This is the noble, world-transcending, true and non-inverted right view, namely: with birth as condition, there is old age and death. In the same way for birth, becoming, clinging, craving, feeling, contact, the six sense bases, name-and-form, consciousness, and formations: with ignorance as condition, there are formations.

"If someone were to further ask: 'Whose are these formations? To whom do formations belong?', one would answer: 'The formations are myself. The formations are mine.' One says thus: 'The life-force is the same as the body,' or says: 'The life-force is different from the body.' If one holds the view that the life-force is the same as the body, the practitioner of the holy life would have no such view. If one says the life-force is different from the body, the practitioner of the holy life would also have no such view. Leaving aside these two extremes, one is rightly directed to the Middle Way. This is the noble, world-transcending, true and non-inverted right view and knowledge, namely: with ignorance as condition, there are formations.

"Bhikṣus, if through dispassion toward ignorance, clear knowing arises, then as for 'whose old age and death is it? To whom does old age and death belong?', old age and death is then cut off. One knows that its root is severed, like cutting off the top of a tāla tree, so that in the future it becomes a dharma that will not arise again. If a bhikṣu, through dispassion toward ignorance, gives rise to clear knowing, then as for 'whose birth is it? To whom does birth belong?', and so on up to 'whose are these formations? To whom do formations belong?', formations are then cut off. One knows that their root is severed, like cutting off the top of a tāla tree, so that in the future they become dharmas that will not arise again. If a bhikṣu, through dispassion toward ignorance, gives rise to clear knowing, then with the cessation of that ignorance, formations cease, and so on up to the cessation of the entire great mass of suffering. This is called the Sūtra on the Great Emptiness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (298)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Kuru country, in the village of Kalmāṣadamya.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now explain the Dharma of dependent origination, both as a doctrinal explanation and a meaning explanation. Listen carefully and reflect well. I shall explain it to you.

"What is the doctrinal explanation of the Dharma of dependent origination? It is this: because this exists, that exists; because this arises, that arises. Namely, with ignorance as condition, formations arise, and so on up to the gathering together of the entire great mass of suffering. This is called the doctrinal explanation of the Dharma of dependent origination.

"What is the meaning explanation
