### (304)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the village of Adjusting the Ox in the country of Kuru.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach you the Dharma, which is good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, good in meaning and good in flavor, pure and complete, the holy life made clear. Listen carefully and reflect well. There are the six sets of six dharmas. What are the six sets of six? They are: the six internal sense bases, the six external sense bases, the six consciousness-groups, the six contact-groups, the six feeling-groups, and the six craving-groups.

"What are the six internal sense bases? They are: the eye sense base, the ear sense base, the nose sense base, the tongue sense base, the body sense base, and the mind sense base. What are the six external sense bases? The form sense base, the sound sense base, the smell sense base, the taste sense base, the touch sense base, and the dharma sense base. What are the six consciousness-groups? The eye-consciousness group, the ear-consciousness group, the nose-consciousness group, the tongue-consciousness group, the body-consciousness group, and the mind-consciousness group. What are the six contact-groups? Eye-contact, ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact. What are the six feeling-groups? Feeling born of eye-contact, feeling born of ear-contact, feeling born of nose-contact, feeling born of tongue-contact, feeling born of body-contact, and feeling born of mind-contact. What are the six craving-groups? Craving born of eye-contact, craving born of ear-contact, craving born of nose-contact, craving born of tongue-contact, craving born of body-contact, and craving born of mind-contact.

"If someone says 'the eye is the self,' this is not so. Why? Because the eye is subject to arising and perishing. If the eye were the self, then the self would undergo birth and death. Therefore, to say the eye is the self is not so. Likewise, if form, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, or feeling born of eye-contact were the self, this is not so. Why? Because feeling born of eye-contact is a dharma of arising and perishing. If feeling born of eye-contact were the self, then the self would undergo birth and death. Therefore, to say feeling born of eye-contact is the self is not so. Therefore, feeling born of eye-contact is not self. Likewise, feeling born of ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact is not self. Why? Because feeling born of mind-contact is a dharma of arising and perishing. If it were the self, then the self would undergo birth and death. Therefore, to say feeling born of mind-contact is the self is not so. Therefore, feeling born of mind-contact is not self.

"Thus, bhikṣus, one should know as it really is: the function of the eye, the function of wisdom, the function of cessation, the opening of spiritual penetrations, the direct path toward nirvāṇa. How does one know and see as it really is the function of the eye, up to the direct path toward nirvāṇa? Thus, bhikṣus: the eye is not self; form, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and feeling born through the condition of eye-contact, whether internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, one also observes that these are not self. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way. This is called knowing and seeing as it really is the function of the eye, up to the direct path toward nirvāṇa. This is called the Sūtra of the Six Sets of Six."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (305)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the village of Adjusting the Ox in the country of Kuru.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach you the Dharma, which is good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, good in meaning and good in flavor, pure and complete, the holy life made clear, namely the Sūtra of the Sixfold Analysis of the Six Sense Bases. Listen carefully and reflect well, and I shall explain it to you.

"What is the Sūtra of the Sixfold Analysis of the Six Sense Bases? If, regarding the eye sense base, one does not know and see as it really is; regarding form, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and feeling born through the condition of eye-contact, whether internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, one does not know and see as it really is; because one does not know and see as it really is, one becomes attached to the eye. Regarding form, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and feeling born through the condition of eye-contact, whether internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, one generates attachment to all of them. Likewise for the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind: regarding dharmas, mind-consciousness, mind-contact, and feeling born through the condition of mind-contact, whether internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, one does not know and see as it really is; because one does not know and see as it really is, one generates attachment. With such attachment, accompanied by delusion, looking back with longing, the mind is bound and fettered. One nourishes the five clinging-aggregates; and future existence with craving, desire, and delight all increase. Body and mind become weary and distressed; body and mind are ruined and burnt; body and mind blaze; body and mind are in turmoil; the body gives rise to painful feelings. Because the body gives rise to painful feelings, in future lives, birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, affliction, and suffering all increase. This is called the gathering of the entire great mass of pure suffering.

"Bhikṣus, if regarding the eye one knows and sees as it really is; regarding form, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and feeling born through the condition of eye-contact, whether internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, one knows and sees as it really is; having seen thus, one is not attached to the eye. Regarding form, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and feeling born through the condition of eye-contact, whether internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, one is not attached. Likewise for the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind: regarding dharmas, one knows and sees as it really is; regarding dharmas, mind-consciousness, mind-contact, and feeling born through the condition of mind-contact, whether internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, one knows and sees as it really is. Because one knows and sees as it really is, one is not attached to the mind. Regarding dharmas, mind-consciousness, mind-contact, and feeling born through the condition of mind-contact, whether internally experienced as painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, one is not attached. Because one is not attached, there is no mingling, no delusion, no looking back with longing, no bondage. The five clinging-aggregates diminish. Future existence with craving, desire, and delight, and all those various attachments, are entirely extinguished. The body is not weary with suffering, the mind is not weary with suffering. The body does not burn, the mind does not burn. The body does not blaze, the mind does not blaze. The body experiences happiness, the mind experiences happiness. Because body and mind experience happiness, in future lives, birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, affliction, and suffering are entirely extinguished. Thus the entire great mass of pure suffering is extinguished.

"One who knows thus and sees thus is called one who cultivates right view to fulfillment: right intention, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration, and the previously mentioned right speech, right action, and right livelihood are cultivated to purity and fulfillment. This is called cultivating the Noble Eightfold Path to purity and fulfillment. When the Noble Eightfold Path is cultivated to fulfillment, the four foundations of mindfulness are cultivated to fulfillment; the four right efforts, the four bases of spiritual power, the five faculties, the five powers, and the seven factors of awakening are cultivated to fulfillment.

"Whatever dharmas should be known and fully understood, they are all known and fully understood. Whatever dharmas should be known and abandoned, they are all known and abandoned. Whatever dharmas should be known and realized, they are all realized. Whatever dharmas should be known and cultivated, they are all cultivated. What dharmas should be known and fully understood, that are all known and fully understood? Namely, name-and-form. What dharmas should be known and abandoned? Namely, ignorance and craving for existence. What dharmas should be known and realized? Namely, knowledge and liberation. What dharmas should be known and cultivated? Namely, calm-abiding and insight. If a bhikṣu, regarding these dharmas that should be known and fully understood, has fully known and fully understood them; regarding dharmas that should be known and abandoned, has fully known and abandoned them; regarding dharmas that should be known and realized, has fully known and realized them; regarding dharmas that should be known and cultivated, has fully known and cultivated them, then this bhikṣu is called one who has cut the bonds of craving, with correct and uninterrupted penetration, having reached the final end of suffering. Bhikṣus, this is called the Sūtra of the Sixfold Analysis of the Six Sense Bases."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (306)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu, alone in a quiet place, diligently contemplating, had this thought: "How should a bhikṣu know, how should he see, in order to see the Dharma?" Having reflected thus, he rose from meditation, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, alone in a quiet place, diligently contemplating, I had this thought: 'How should a bhikṣu know, how should he see, in order to see the Dharma?'"

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed that bhikṣu: "Listen carefully and reflect well, and I shall explain it to you. There are two dharmas. What are the two? The eye and form are two." This was explained extensively, up to "it is not within their domain. Why? Because the eye and form as conditions give rise to eye-consciousness; the meeting of these three is contact; together with contact there arise feeling, perception, and volition. These four formless aggregates, together with the eye and form, these dharmas are called 'a person.' Regarding these dharmas, one constructs the notion of a person, a being, a nara, a mānuja, a māṇava, a man, a pudgala, a jīva, a jantu.

"Furthermore, one speaks thus: 'My eye sees form, my ear hears sound, my nose smells odor, my tongue tastes flavor, my body feels touch, my mind cognizes dharmas.' And one designates and speaks thus: 'This venerable one has such a name, was born thus, has such a family name, eats such food, experiences such pleasure and pain, has such a lifespan, dwells for such a long time, has such a span of life.' Bhikṣus, these are merely perceptions, merely designations, merely verbal expressions. All these dharmas are impermanent, conditioned, produced by the conditions of intention and aspiration. Whatever is impermanent, conditioned, and produced by the conditions of intention and aspiration, that is suffering. Furthermore, that suffering arises, that suffering persists, that suffering perishes, that suffering arises again and again; all is suffering. If that suffering is completely cut off without remainder, expelled, exhausted, the desire for it is abandoned, it is extinguished, stilled, and disappears, and no further suffering continues or arises, then that is peace, that is sublime. Namely, relinquishing all remainder, the complete exhaustion of all craving, the absence of desire, cessation, nirvāṇa. The ear, nose, tongue, body: body-contact as condition gives rise to body-consciousness; the meeting of these three is contact; together with contact there arise feeling, perception, and volition. These four are the formless aggregates, and the body-faculty is the form aggregate. This is called 'a person.'" As explained above, up to "cessation, nirvāṇa. Conditioned by the mind and dharmas, mind-consciousness arises; the meeting of these three is contact; together with contact there arise feeling, perception, and volition. These four formless aggregates, together with the four great elements upon which the person depends, these dharmas are called 'a person.'" As explained extensively above, up to "cessation, nirvāṇa. If there is someone whose mind, regarding all these dharmas, enters, abides in liberation, and does not turn back, and regarding whatever attachments arise there, there is no sense of 'I,' then, bhikṣus, knowing thus and seeing thus is called seeing the Dharma."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (307)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu, alone in a quiet place, diligently contemplating, had this thought: "How should one know, how should one see, to be called seeing the Dharma?" Having reflected, he rose from meditation, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, alone in a quiet place, diligently contemplating, I had this thought: 'How should a bhikṣu know, how should he see, to be called seeing the Dharma?' I now ask the World-Honored One; may you please explain."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣu: "Listen carefully and reflect well, and I shall explain it to you. There are two dharmas. The eye and form as conditions give rise to eye-consciousness." As explained extensively above. "Venerable One, as spoken in verse:

> "Eye and form, these two conditions,
> Give rise to mind and mental factors.
> Consciousness, contact, and what arises together,
> Feeling, perception, and so forth, have their causes.
> They are not self, not belonging to self,
> Not a pudgala,
> Not a mānuja,
> Not a māṇava.
> These are arising and perishing,
> Dharmas of the suffering-aggregate, subject to change.
> Regarding these, one constructs notions,
> Designating them as 'beings,'
> 'Nara,' 'mānuja,'
> And also 'māṇava.'
> And the many other notions
> All arise dependent on the suffering-aggregate.
> Actions, craving, and ignorance
> Cause the accumulation of aggregates in future lives.
> Other śramaṇas and followers of other paths,
> Who speak differently of these two dharmas,
> Have only verbal assertions;
> Hearing them only increases confusion and delusion.
> When craving ceases without remainder,
> Ignorance sinks and is permanently extinguished.
> When craving is exhausted, all suffering ceases;
> This is what the unsurpassed Buddha-eye declares."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (308)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "The devas and worldly people are attached to form, delighting in it and dwelling in it. When that form becomes impermanent, changes, and is destroyed, those devas and people then experience great suffering. Regarding sound, smell, taste, touch, and dharmas, they are attached, delighting in them and dwelling in them. When those dharmas change, become impermanent, and are destroyed, those devas and people dwell in great suffering.

"The Tathāgata, regarding form, the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form, knows as it really is. Having known as it really is, regarding form he no longer dwells attached to it, delighting in it. When that form changes, becomes impermanent, and is destroyed, then he dwells in happiness. Regarding sound, smell, taste, touch, and dharmas, their arising, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape, he knows as it really is. Having known as it really is, he no longer dwells attached to them, delighting in them. When those forms change, become impermanent, and are destroyed, then he dwells in happiness. Why? The eye and form as conditions give rise to eye-consciousness; the meeting of these three is contact. Contact conditions feeling, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant. Regarding this feeling, its arising, its cessation, its danger, and its escape, he knows as it really is. The obstruction produced by the condition of form, when that obstruction is exhausted, is called the unsurpassed peace of nirvāṇa. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind: dharmas as conditions give rise to mind-consciousness; the meeting of these three is contact. Contact conditions feeling, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant. He knows as it really is the arising of that feeling, the cessation of feeling, the gratification of feeling, the danger of feeling, and the escape from feeling. Having known as it really is, the obstruction produced by the condition of those dharmas, when that obstruction is exhausted, is called the unsurpassed peace of nirvāṇa."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse:

> "Regarding form, sound, smell, taste,
> Touch, and dharmas, the six objects,
> Exclusively generating delight,
> With craving and deep attachment:
> The devas and worldly people
> Consider only these as happiness.
> When they change and are destroyed,
> They then experience great suffering.
> Only the noble saints
> See their cessation as happiness.
> What the world takes as happiness,
> They observe is entirely the enemy.
> What the noble saints see as suffering,
> The world takes as happiness.
> What the world takes as suffering,
> For the saints is happiness.
> This Dharma, so deep and hard to understand,
> Generates doubt and confusion in the world.
> Engulfed in great darkness,
> Blind, seeing nothing.
> Only those with wisdom
> Open the darkness and reveal the great light.
> Such profound sentences
> Who but the noble can understand?
> Those who no longer take up a body,
> Deeply penetrate and clearly comprehend the truths."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (309)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Campā, beside the Gaggarā Lotus Pond.

At that time, the Venerable Mṛgaśīrṣa came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, as the World-Honored One has spoken, there is 'dwelling with a second' and there is 'dwelling alone.' What is dwelling with a second? What is dwelling alone?"

The Buddha addressed Mṛgaśīrṣa: "Excellent! Excellent! Mṛgaśīrṣa, you are able to ask the Tathāgata about such a meaning."

The Buddha addressed Mṛgaśīrṣa: "If the eye perceives forms that are lovable, delightful, charming, and agreeable, which nourish desire, and that bhikṣu, having seen them, takes delight in them, praises them, and dwells attached to them; having delighted, praised, and dwelt attached to them, his mind turns to exhilaration; having become exhilarated, there is deep pleasure; having deep pleasure, there is craving; having craved, there is obstruction. Exhilaration, deep pleasure, craving, and obstruction: this is called dwelling with a second. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way. Mṛgaśīrṣa, a bhikṣu of such a kind, even if dwelling alone in a secluded empty place, is still called one who dwells with a second. Why? Because craving and delight have not been cut off and have not ceased. For one whose craving and desire have not been cut off and not been known, the Buddhas, the Tathāgatas, say he dwells with a second.

"If there is a bhikṣu who, regarding forms that are lovable, delightful, charming, agreeable, and which nourish desire, that bhikṣu, having seen them, does not take delight in them, does not praise them, does not dwell attached to them; not having delighted, praised, or dwelt attached to them, he does not become exhilarated; because he is not exhilarated, he has no deep pleasure; because he has no deep pleasure, he has no craving; because he has no craving, he has no obstruction. Without exhilaration, deep pleasure, craving, and obstruction: this is called dwelling alone. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way. Mṛgaśīrṣa, a bhikṣu of such a kind, even if dwelling in a high pavilion or multi-storied hall, is still one who dwells alone. Why? Because craving has been exhausted and fully known. For one whose craving has been exhausted and fully known, the Buddhas, the Tathāgatas, say he dwells alone."

At that time, the Venerable Mṛgaśīrṣa, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and delighted, bowed, and departed.

---

### (310)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Campā, beside the Gaggarā Lotus Pond.

At that time, the Venerable Mṛgaśīrṣa came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "Excellent! World-Honored One, please teach me the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I shall dwell alone in a quiet place, diligently contemplating, dwelling without negligence, until I know for myself that I will not receive further existence."

The Buddha addressed Mṛgaśīrṣa: "Excellent! Excellent! Mṛgaśīrṣa, you are able to ask the Tathāgata about such a meaning. Listen carefully and reflect well, and I shall explain it to you."

The Buddha addressed Mṛgaśīrṣa: "If the eye sees forms that are lovable, delightful, agreeable, charming, and which nourish desire, and having seen them, one delights in them, praises them, and dwells attached to them; having delighted, praised, and dwelt attached to them, then exhilaration gathers. When exhilaration gathers, suffering gathers. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way. Mṛgaśīrṣa, if there is a bhikṣu whose eye sees forms that are lovable, delightful, charming, agreeable, and which nourish desire, and having seen them, he does not delight in them, does not praise them, does not dwell attached to them; because he does not delight, does not praise, and does not dwell attached, exhilaration does not gather. Because exhilaration does not gather, suffering ceases. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind regarding dharmas are also spoken of in the same way."

At that time, the Venerable Mṛgaśīrṣa, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and delighted, bowed, and departed.

At that time, the Venerable Mṛgaśīrṣa, having heard the Buddha's teaching and instruction, dwelt alone in a quiet place, diligently contemplating, dwelling without negligence, until he attained arhatship, and his mind was well liberated.

---

### (311)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Pūrṇa came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "Excellent! World-Honored One, please teach me the Dharma. I shall sit alone in a quiet place, diligently contemplating, dwelling without negligence, until I know for myself that I will not receive further existence."

The Buddha addressed Pūrṇa: "Excellent! Excellent! You are able to ask the Tathāgata about such a meaning. Listen carefully and reflect well, and I shall explain it to you. If there is a bhikṣu whose eye sees forms that are lovable, delightful, charming, agreeable, and which nourish desire, and having seen them, he delights in them, praises them, and dwells attached to them; having delighted, praised, and dwelt attached, he becomes exhilarated. Having become exhilarated, there is deep pleasure. Having deep pleasure, there is craving. Having craved, there is obstruction. Because of exhilaration, deep pleasure, craving, and obstruction, he is far from nirvāṇa. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way.

"Pūrṇa, if a bhikṣu's eye sees forms that are lovable, delightful, charming, agreeable, and which nourish desire, and having seen them, he does not delight in them, does not praise them, does not dwell attached to them; because he does not delight, does not praise, and does not dwell attached, he does not become exhilarated. Because he is not exhilarated, he has no deep pleasure. Because he has no deep pleasure, he has no craving. Because he has no craving, he has no obstruction. Because he is without exhilaration, deep pleasure, craving, and obstruction, he gradually approaches nirvāṇa. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way."

The Buddha addressed Pūrṇa: "I have given you a brief teaching. Where do you wish to dwell?"

Pūrṇa addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, having received the World-Honored One's brief instruction, I wish to travel among the people of Sunāparanta in the western region."

The Buddha addressed Pūrṇa: "The people of Sunāparanta in the west are fierce, reckless, violent, and fond of abusive speech. Pūrṇa, if you hear their fierce, reckless, violent, abusive, and insulting words, what will you do?"

Pūrṇa addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, if the people of western Sunāparanta are fierce, abusive, violent, and insult me to my face, I shall think: 'The people of western Sunāparanta are wise and good. Although they are fierce, violent, and abuse and insult me to my face, they still do not strike or throw stones at me with their hands.'"

The Buddha addressed Pūrṇa: "The people of western Sunāparanta are merely fierce, reckless, violent, and abusive; regarding you, you may escape that. But what if they were to strike you and throw stones at you with their hands?"

Pūrṇa addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, if the people of western Sunāparanta were to strike me with hands or throw stones at me, I would think: 'The people of Sunāparanta are wise and good. Although they strike me with hands and throw stones at me, they do not use swords or clubs.'"

The Buddha addressed Pūrṇa: "What if those people were to strike you with swords or clubs?"

Pūrṇa addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, if those people were to strike me with swords or clubs, I would think: 'The people of Sunāparanta are wise and good. Although they strike me with swords and clubs, they do not kill me.'"

The Buddha addressed Pūrṇa: "Suppose those people were to kill you; what then?"

Pūrṇa addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, if the people of western Sunāparanta were to kill me, I would think: 'There are disciples of the World-Honored One who, being weary and disgusted with the body, kill themselves with a knife, take poison, hang themselves with a rope, or throw themselves into a deep pit. The people of western Sunāparanta are wise and good; regarding my decaying body, with but little effort they bring about my liberation.'"

The Buddha said: "Excellent! Pūrṇa! You have well learned patience. You are now capable of dwelling among the people of Sunāparanta. Go now and liberate those who have not been liberated, bring peace to those who have not found peace, and lead those who have not attained nirvāṇa to attain nirvāṇa."

At that time, Pūrṇa, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and delighted, bowed, and departed.

At that time, the Venerable Pūrṇa, after the night had passed, in the early morning, put on his robes and took his bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, he came out, entrusted his bedding, took his robes and bowl, and departed. He traveled among the people of western Sunāparanta. Having arrived, during the summer retreat, he taught the Dharma to five hundred lay followers, established five hundred monasteries, and rope-beds, bedding, and all offerings and requisites were fully provided. When the three months had passed, he perfected the three knowledges, and right there he entered nirvāṇa without remainder.

---

### (312)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Māluṅkyāputta came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "Excellent! World-Honored One, please teach me the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I shall dwell alone in a quiet place, diligently contemplating, dwelling without negligence, until I do not receive further existence."

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed Māluṅkyāputta, saying: "Even young people who are bright and sharp-witted, who have recently gone forth in my Dharma and Vinaya, are without negligence regarding my Dharma and Vinaya. How much more so you, who are now elderly and whose faculties are mature, and yet wish to hear my brief instruction!"

Māluṅkyāputta addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, although I am elderly and my faculties are mature, I still wish to hear the World-Honored One's brief instruction. May the World-Honored One please give me a brief instruction. Having heard the Dharma, I shall dwell alone in a quiet place, diligently contemplating, until I know for myself that I will not receive further existence." A second and third time he made the same request.

The Buddha addressed Māluṅkyāputta: "Stop now!" Three times he refused and would not teach.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed Māluṅkyāputta: "I shall now question you; answer as you wish."

The Buddha addressed Māluṅkyāputta: "If the eye has never before seen a form, would you then wish to see it, and regarding that form give rise to desire, craving, longing, or attachment?"

He replied: "No, World-Honored One."

"Ear and sound, nose and smell, tongue and taste, body and touch, mind and dharmas are also spoken of in the same way."

The Buddha addressed Māluṅkyāputta: "Excellent! Excellent! Māluṅkyāputta! In the seen, let there be just the seen. In the heard, let there be just the heard. In the sensed, let there be just the sensed. In the cognized, let there be just the cognized." And he spoke this verse:

> "If you are not in that,
> And that is also not in this,
> And neither between the two,
> Then this is the end of suffering."

Māluṅkyāputta addressed the Buddha, saying: "I have understood, World-Honored One! I have understood, Sugata!"

The Buddha addressed Māluṅkyāputta: "How do you, in the brief teaching I have given, understand its meaning in full?"

At that time, Māluṅkyāputta spoke verses and addressed the Buddha:

> "If the eye has seen a form,
> And one loses right mindfulness,
> Then regarding the form that was seen,
> One grasps at the sign of craving and longing.
> One who grasps the sign of craving and delight,
> Their mind is constantly attached.
> Giving rise to various cravings,
> Countless forms aggregate and arise.
> Thoughts of covetousness, ill-will, and harm
> Cause the mind to deteriorate.
> They nourish a mass of suffering,
> And are forever far from nirvāṇa.
> Seeing form without grasping signs,
> The mind follows right mindfulness.
> Not stained by unwholesome craving,
> Nor generating attachment.
> Not giving rise to various cravings,
> Countless forms do not aggregate and arise.
> Thoughts of covetousness, ill-will, and harm
> Cannot damage their mind.
> Gradually lessening the mass of suffering,
> Step by step approaching nirvāṇa.
> This the Sun-lineage Honored One has declared:
> Abandoning craving, one attains parinirvāṇa.
> If the ear hears various sounds,
> And the mind loses right mindfulness,
> And one grasps at the signs of sounds,
> Seizing and not letting go;
> If the nose smells, the tongue tastes flavors,
> The body touches, and the mind contemplates dharmas,
> And one loses right mindfulness,
> Grasping signs is also the same.
> Their mind generates craving and delight,
> And dwells firmly attached.
> Giving rise to various cravings,
> Countless dharmas aggregate and arise.
> Thoughts of covetousness, ill-will, and harm
> Deteriorate and damage their mind.
> They nourish a mass of suffering,
> And are forever far from nirvāṇa.
> Not stained regarding any dharma,
> Abiding in right wisdom and right mindfulness,
> Their mind is not defiled,
> Nor do they delight in attachment.
> Not giving rise to various cravings,
> Countless dharmas do not aggregate and arise.
> Thoughts of covetousness, ill-will, and harm
> Do not deteriorate and diminish their mind.
> Suffering is gradually diminished and destroyed,
> Approaching parinirvāṇa step by step.
> The exhaustion of craving is parinirvāṇa;
> This is what the World-Honored One has declared."

This is the meaning broadly understood from the World-Honored One's brief teaching.

The Buddha addressed Māluṅkyāputta: "You have truly, in my brief teaching, broadly understood its meaning. Why? Because it is as you have spoken in verse:

> 'If the eye sees various forms,
> And one loses right mindfulness,
> Then regarding the form that was seen,
> One grasps at the sign of craving and longing.'"

As explained extensively above.

At that time, the Venerable Māluṅkyāputta, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and delighted, bowed, and departed.

At that time, the Venerable Māluṅkyāputta, having broadly understood the meaning from the World-Honored One's brief teaching, dwelt alone in a quiet place, diligently contemplating, dwelling without negligence, until he became an arhat, and his mind attained liberation.

---

### (313)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Is there a sūtra teaching, bhikṣus, which one inclines toward, and yet apart from faith, apart from desire, apart from learning, apart from reasoning through practice, apart from investigation and reflective acceptance of views, one correctly knows and declares: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, and I know for myself that I will not receive further existence'?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha, saying: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. Excellent, World-Honored One! May you please explain it extensively. The bhikṣus, having heard, shall receive and practice it."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully and reflect well, and I shall explain it to you. A bhikṣu, having seen form with the eye, is aware of form but not aware of craving for form. 'Previously, my eye-consciousness had craving regarding form, but now my eye-consciousness has no craving regarding form,' thus he knows as it really is. If a bhikṣu, having seen form with the eye, is aware of form but does not give rise to craving for form, and is aware that 'previously, my eye-consciousness had craving,' and declares that 'now my eye-consciousness has no craving regarding form,' and knows this as it really is, then, bhikṣus, what do you think: does that person, regarding this, have faith, desire, learning, reasoning through practice, or investigation and reflective acceptance of views?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

"Does he return to this Dharma, and correctly know through what is known and seen as it really is?"

They answered: "Just so, World-Honored One."

The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind regarding dharmas are also spoken of in the same way.

"Bhikṣus, this is called having a sūtra teaching. A bhikṣu who inclines toward this sūtra teaching, apart from faith, apart from desire, apart from learning, apart from reasoning through practice, apart from investigation and reflective acceptance of views, correctly knows and declares: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, and I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (314)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should cut off desire. When desire for the eye is cut off, the eye is then cut off and fully known, its root cut, like cutting off the top of a palmyra tree, so that in future lives it will never arise again. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (315)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If the eye arises, persists, and emerges, then suffering arises, disease persists, and aging-and-death emerge. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way. If the eye ceases, is stilled, and disappears, then the arising of suffering ceases, disease is stilled, and death disappears. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (316)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "The eye is impermanent. If the eye were permanent, it would not be subject to oppressive suffering, and one could say regarding the eye, 'Let it be thus; let it not be thus.' Because the eye is impermanent, the eye is subject to oppressive suffering arising, and therefore one cannot regarding the eye say, 'Let it be thus; let it not be thus.' The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (317)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "The eye is suffering. If the eye were happiness, it would not be subject to oppressive suffering, and one could regarding the eye say, 'Let it be thus; let it not be thus.' Because the eye is suffering, it is subject to oppressive suffering, and one cannot regarding the eye say, 'Let it be thus; let it not be thus.' The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (318)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "The eye is not self. If the eye were self, it would not be subject to oppressive suffering, and one could regarding the eye say, 'Let it be thus; let it not be thus.' Because the eye is not self, it is subject to oppressive suffering, and one cannot regarding the eye say, 'Let it be thus; let it not be thus.' The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

Just as the three sūtras on the six internal sense bases, the three sūtras on the six external sense bases are also spoken of in the same way.

---

### (319)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi went to visit the Buddha. After exchanging greetings, he withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gotama, what is called 'everything'? How is 'everything' defined?"

The Buddha addressed the brahmin: "'Everything' refers to the twelve sense bases: eye and form, ear and sound, nose and smell, tongue and taste, body and touch, mind and dharmas. This is called 'everything.' If someone were to say, 'This is not everything; the everything that the śramaṇa Gotama speaks of, I now reject, and I shall establish a different everything,' such a person would have only words. When questioned, they would not know, and their doubts would only increase. Why? Because it is not within their domain."

At that time, the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced, delighted, and practiced accordingly.

---

### (320)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi went to visit the Buddha. After exchanging greetings, he withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gotama, what is called 'everything exists'? How is 'everything exists' defined?"

The Buddha addressed the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi: "I shall now question you; answer as you wish. Brahmin, what do you think: does the eye exist or not?"

He answered: "It exists, śramaṇa Gotama."

"Does form exist or not?"

He answered: "It exists, śramaṇa Gotama."

"Brahmin, does form exist, does the eye exist, does eye-consciousness exist, does eye-contact exist, does feeling born through the condition of eye-contact, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, exist or not?"

He answered: "It exists, śramaṇa Gotama."

"The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way."

This was explained extensively, up to "it is not within their domain."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and delighted, rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (321)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi went to visit the Buddha. After exchanging greetings, he withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "Śramaṇa Gotama, what is called 'all dharmas'? How are 'all dharmas' defined?"

The Buddha addressed the brahmin: "The eye and form, eye-consciousness, eye-contact, and feeling born through the condition of eye-contact, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind; dharmas, mind-consciousness, mind-contact, and feeling born through the condition of mind-contact, whether painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant: these are called all dharmas. If someone were to say, 'This is not all dharmas; the all dharmas that the śramaṇa Gotama speaks of, I now reject, and I shall establish a different all dharmas,' such a person would have only verbal assertions. When questioned, they would not know, and their confusion and delusion would only increase. Why? Because it is not within their domain."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and delighted, rose from his seat and departed.

Just as the three sūtras asked by the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi, the three sūtras asked by a certain bhikṣu, the three sūtras asked by the Venerable Ānanda, and the three sūtras in which the World-Honored One speaks as the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, and the foundation of the Dharma are also spoken of as above.

---

### (322)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu went to the Buddha, bowed at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, as the World-Honored One has said, the eye is an internal sense base. The World-Honored One spoke briefly and did not explain in detail. How is the eye an internal sense base?"

The Buddha addressed that bhikṣu: "The eye as an internal sense base is derived matter made from the four great elements; it is invisible and resistant. The ear, nose, tongue, and body as internal sense bases are also spoken of in the same way."

He further addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, as the World-Honored One has said, the mind is an internal sense base. You did not explain in detail. How is the mind an internal sense base?" The Buddha addressed the bhikṣu: "The mind as internal sense base: the mind, mentation, and consciousness are not form, are invisible, and are without resistance. This is called the mind as internal sense base."

He further asked: "As the World-Honored One has said, form is an external sense base. The World-Honored One spoke briefly and did not explain in detail. How, World-Honored One, is form an external sense base?"

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣu: "Form as an external sense base: if form is made from the four great elements, it is visible and resistant. This is called form as an external sense base."

He further addressed the Buddha, saying: "The World-Honored One said sound is an external sense base but did not explain in detail. How is sound an external sense base?"

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣu: "If sound is made from the four great elements, it is invisible and resistant. Just as sound, smell and taste are also the same."

He further asked: "The World-Honored One said touch is an external sense base but did not explain in detail. How is touch an external sense base?"

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣu: "Touch as an external sense base: it is the four great elements and form derived from the four great elements; it is invisible and resistant. This is called touch as an external sense base."

He further asked: "The World-Honored One said dharmas are an external sense base but did not explain in detail. How are dharmas an external sense base?"

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣu: "Dharmas as an external sense base: that which is not included in the eleven sense bases, is invisible, and is without resistance. This is called dharmas as an external sense base."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (323)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six internal sense bases: the eye as internal sense base, and the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind as internal sense bases."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (324)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six external sense bases. What are the six? Form is an external sense base; sound, smell, taste, touch, and dharmas are external sense bases. These are called the six external sense bases."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (325)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six consciousness-groups. What are the six? The eye-consciousness group, the ear-consciousness group, the nose-consciousness group, the tongue-consciousness group, the body-consciousness group, and the mind-consciousness group. These are called the six consciousness-groups."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (326)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six contact-groups. What are the six contact-groups? The eye-contact group, the ear-contact group, the nose-contact group, the tongue-contact group, the body-contact group, and the mind-contact group. These are called the six contact-groups."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (327)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six feeling-groups. What are the six? Feeling born of eye-contact, feeling born of ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact. These are called the six feeling-groups."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (328)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six perception-groups. What are the six? Perception born of eye-contact, perception born of ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact. These are called the six perception-groups."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (329)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six volition-groups. What are the six? Volition born of eye-contact, volition born of ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact. These are called the six volition-groups."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (330)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six craving-groups. What are the six craving-groups? Craving born of eye-contact, craving born of ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, and mind-contact. These are called the six craving-groups."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (331)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six longings. What are the six? Longing for form, longing for sound, longing for smell, longing for taste, longing for touch, and longing for dharmas. These are called the six longings."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (332)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six coverings. What are the six? Form with outflows, which is subject to clinging, covers the mind. Sound, smell, taste, touch, and dharmas with outflows, which are subject to clinging, cover the mind. These are called the six coverings."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (333)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "The eye of the past and future is impermanent; how much more so the present eye. A learned noble disciple who observes thus does not look back on the past eye, does not delight in the future eye, and regarding the present eye generates revulsion, dispassion, and moves toward cessation. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way."

Just as with impermanence, so also with suffering, emptiness, and not-self, it is spoken of in the same way. Just as with the four sūtras on the internal sense bases, the four sūtras on the external sense bases are also spoken of in the same way.

---

### (334)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the village of Adjusting the Ox in the country of Kuru.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach you the Dharma, which is good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, good in meaning and good in flavor, pure and complete, the holy life made clear. Listen carefully and reflect well. It is the sūtra on dharmas that have causes, conditions, and bonds.

"What is the sūtra on dharmas that have causes, conditions, and bonds? The eye has a cause, a condition, and a bond. What is the cause of the eye, the condition of the eye, and the bond of the eye? The eye has action as cause, action as condition, and action as bond. Action has a cause, a condition, and a bond. What is the cause of action, the condition of action, and the bond of action? Action has craving as cause, craving as condition, and craving as bond. Craving has a cause, a condition, and a bond. What is the cause of craving, the condition of craving, and the bond of craving? Craving has ignorance as cause, ignorance as condition, and ignorance as bond. Ignorance has a cause, a condition, and a bond. What is the cause of ignorance, the condition of ignorance, and the bond of ignorance? Ignorance has unwise attention as cause, unwise attention as condition, and unwise attention as bond. Unwise attention has a cause, a condition, and a bond. What is the cause of unwise attention, the condition of unwise attention, and the bond of unwise attention? Conditioned by the eye and form, unwise attention arises, and delusion is produced.

"Conditioned by the eye and form, unwise attention arises, and delusion is produced. That delusion is ignorance. The deluded seeking of desire is called craving. What craving produces is called action. Thus, bhikṣus, unwise attention is the cause of ignorance; ignorance is the cause of craving; craving is the cause of action; action is the cause of the eye." The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way.

"This is called the sūtra on dharmas that have causes, conditions, and bonds."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (335)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the village of Adjusting the Ox in the country of Kuru.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach you the Dharma, which is good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, good in meaning and good in flavor, pure and complete, the holy life made clear, namely the Sūtra on Emptiness in the Ultimate Sense. Listen carefully and reflect well, and I shall explain it to you.

"What is the Sūtra on Emptiness in the Ultimate Sense? Bhikṣus, when the eye arises, it comes from nowhere; when it ceases, it goes nowhere. Thus the eye, not being real, arises, and having arisen, is completely extinguished. There is the fruition of action, but no agent. This aggregate ceases, and a different aggregate continues, apart from conventional designations. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are also spoken of in the same way, apart from conventional designations. What are conventional designations? They mean: because this exists, that exists; because this arises, that arises. As with ignorance conditioning formations, formations conditioning consciousness," explained extensively, up to "the arising of the entire great mass of pure suffering. Furthermore, because this does not exist, that does not exist; because this ceases, that ceases. With the cessation of ignorance, formations cease; with the cessation of formations, consciousness ceases," explained extensively in this way, up to "the cessation of the entire great mass of pure suffering. Bhikṣus, this is called the Sūtra on Emptiness in the Ultimate Sense."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (336)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six courses of delight. What are the six? Thus, bhikṣus, if the eye sees a form and there is delight, one courses in that object of form. The ear and sound, nose and smell, tongue and taste, body and touch, mind-consciousness and dharmas: if there is delight, one courses in that object of dharmas. Bhikṣus, these are called the six courses of delight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (337)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six courses of grief. What are the six? Bhikṣus, if the eye sees a form and there is grief, one courses in that object of form. The ear and sound, nose and smell, tongue and taste, body and touch, mind-consciousness and dharmas: if there is grief, one courses in that object of dharmas. Bhikṣus, these are called the six courses of grief."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (338)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six courses of equanimity. What are the six? Bhikṣus, if the eye sees a form and there is equanimity, one courses in that object of form. The ear and sound, nose and smell, tongue and taste, body and touch, mind-consciousness and dharmas: if there is equanimity, one courses in that object of dharmas. These are called the bhikṣu's six courses of equanimity."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (339)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six constant practices. What are the six? If a bhikṣu sees form with the eye, without suffering and without pleasure, he abides with equanimity, right mindfulness, and right knowledge. The ear and sound, nose and smell, tongue and taste, body and touch, mind-consciousness and dharmas: without suffering and without pleasure, he abides with equanimity, right mindfulness, and right knowledge. These are called the bhikṣu's six constant practices."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (340)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are six constant practices. What are the six? If a bhikṣu sees form with the eye, without suffering and without pleasure, he abides with equanimity, right mindfulness, and right knowledge. The ear and sound, nose and smell, tongue and taste, body and touch, mind-consciousness and dharmas: without suffering and without pleasure, he abides with equanimity, right mindfulness, and right knowledge. If a bhikṣu has accomplished these six constant practices
