# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Fourteen

---

### (343)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, the Venerable Pūmika Bhikṣu was staying on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

Then, many non-Buddhist renunciants came to the Venerable Pūmika, and they exchanged greetings and courtesies. After exchanging greetings and courtesies, they withdrew and sat to one side. They said to the Venerable Pūmika: "We wish to ask something. Would you have the leisure to answer us or not?"

The Venerable Pūmika said to the non-Buddhist renunciants: "Ask as you wish, and I shall explain for you."

Then the non-Buddhist renunciants asked the Venerable Pūmika: "Is suffering and pleasure self-made?"

The Venerable Pūmika answered: "Regarding those non-Buddhist renunciants who say suffering and pleasure are self-made, the World-Honored One has said: 'This is indeterminate.'"

They further asked: "Is suffering and pleasure made by another?"

He answered: "Regarding suffering and pleasure being made by another, the World-Honored One has said: 'This is indeterminate.'"

They further asked: "Is suffering and pleasure made both by self and by another?"

He answered: "Regarding suffering and pleasure being made both by self and by another, the World-Honored One has said: 'This is indeterminate.'"

They further asked: "Is suffering and pleasure made neither by self nor by another, arising without cause?"

He answered: "Regarding suffering and pleasure being made neither by self nor by another, arising without cause, the World-Honored One has said: 'This is indeterminate.'"

The non-Buddhist renunciants further asked: "How is it, Venerable Pūmika, that when asked 'Is suffering and pleasure self-made?' you say 'indeterminate'; when asked 'Is suffering and pleasure made by another?' you say 'indeterminate'; when asked 'Is suffering and pleasure made both by self and by another?' you say 'indeterminate'; when asked 'Is suffering and pleasure made neither by self nor by another, arising without cause?' you say 'indeterminate'? Then how does the śramaṇa Gautama say suffering and pleasure arise?"

The Venerable Pūmika answered: "Non-Buddhist renunciants, the World-Honored One says that suffering and pleasure arise from conditions."

Then the non-Buddhist renunciants, having heard what the Venerable Pūmika said, were displeased in mind, reproached him, and departed.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra was seated under a tree not far from the Venerable Pūmika.

Then the Venerable Pūmika, knowing that the non-Buddhist renunciants had departed, went to the Venerable Śāriputra. Having arrived, he exchanged greetings and courtesies with Śāriputra. After the courtesies, he fully reported to the Venerable Śāriputra the questions asked by those non-Buddhist renunciants: "Did my answer avoid misrepresenting the World-Honored One? Did I speak as he speaks, speak in accordance with the Dharma, practice the Dharma in speaking of the Dharma? Would others who argue based on different grounds not come to cross-question and reproach me?"

The Venerable Śāriputra said: "Venerable Pūmika, what you said is truly as the Buddha has said. You did not misrepresent the Tathāgata. You spoke as he speaks, spoke in accordance with the Dharma, practiced the Dharma in speaking of the Dharma. Others who argue on different grounds would not come to cross-question and reproach you. Why is that? Because the World-Honored One has said that suffering and pleasure arise from conditions. Venerable Pūmika, those śramaṇas and brahmins who ask whether suffering and pleasure are self-made: that too arises from conditions. To say it does not arise from conditions is impossible. Those who say suffering and pleasure are made by another, made by both self and another, or made neither by self nor by another arising without cause: those too arise from conditions. To say they do not arise from conditions is impossible. Venerable Pūmika, those śramaṇas and brahmins who say suffering and pleasure are self-made: that too arises dependent on contact. To say it does not arise from contact is impossible. Those who say suffering and pleasure are made by another, made by both self and another, or made neither by self nor by another arising without cause: those too arise dependent on contact. To say they do not arise dependent on contact is impossible."

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda was seated under a tree not far from Śāriputra and heard the discussion between the Venerable Śāriputra and the Venerable Pūmika. Having heard it, he rose from his seat and went to the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and stood to one side. He reported in full to the World-Honored One the entire discussion between the Venerable Pūmika and the Venerable Śāriputra.

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Excellent! Excellent, Ānanda! The Venerable Śāriputra, when anyone comes to ask, is able to answer in a timely manner. Excellent, Śāriputra! Because he possesses timely wisdom, when anyone comes to ask, he is able to answer in a timely manner. If my disciples are asked timely questions, they should give timely answers, just as Śāriputra has spoken.

"Ānanda, formerly when I was dwelling in the Seer's Dwelling on the mountain at Rājagṛha, non-Buddhist renunciants came to me with just such meaning, just such phrases, just such import, and asked me. I answered those people with just such meaning, just such phrases, just such import, just as the Venerable Śāriputra has spoken.

"Ānanda, if śramaṇas or brahmins say suffering and pleasure are self-made, I would go to them and ask: 'Do you truly make this statement that suffering and pleasure are self-made?' They would answer me: 'Yes.' I would then ask them: 'Can you firmly hold to this position and say it is the truth, and that all others are fools? I do not accept this. Why? Because I say the arising of suffering and pleasure is different from this.' If they were to ask me: 'How does Gautama say the arising of suffering and pleasure is different?' I would answer: 'From conditions arising, suffering and pleasure are produced.' Likewise, for those who say suffering and pleasure are made by another, made by both self and another, or made neither by self nor by another arising without cause, I would also go to them and speak as above."

Ānanda said to the Buddha: "As for the meaning the World-Honored One has spoken, I have already understood. Because there is birth, there is old age and death, not dependent on anything else; because there is birth, there is old age and death. And so on down to: because there is ignorance, there are formations, not dependent on anything else; because there is ignorance, there are formations. When ignorance ceases, formations cease, and so on down to: when birth ceases, then old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, affliction, and suffering cease. Thus the entire mass of great suffering ceases."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and complied, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (344)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra and the Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila were staying on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

Then, in the afternoon, the Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila arose from meditation and went to the Venerable Śāriputra. They exchanged greetings and courtesies. After exchanging greetings and courtesies, he withdrew and sat to one side. He said to the Venerable Śāriputra: "I wish to ask something. Would you have the leisure to answer or not?"

The Venerable Śāriputra said to the Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila: "Please ask, venerable one. If I know, I shall answer."

The Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila said to the Venerable Śāriputra: "When a well-learned noble disciple in this Dharma and Vinaya has accomplished what quality, is he called one endowed with right view, one who has accomplished direct view, one who has accomplished unshakable confidence in the Buddha, one who has entered the true Dharma, one who has attained this true Dharma, one who has realized this true Dharma?"

The Venerable Śāriputra said to the Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila: "A well-learned noble disciple truly knows unwholesome dharmas, truly knows unwholesome roots, truly knows wholesome dharmas, and truly knows wholesome roots.

"How does one truly know unwholesome dharmas? Unwholesome bodily actions, verbal actions, and mental actions are called unwholesome dharmas. Thus one truly knows unwholesome dharmas. How does one truly know unwholesome roots? The three unwholesome roots are the unwholesome root of greed, the unwholesome root of hatred, and the unwholesome root of delusion. These are called the unwholesome roots. Thus one truly knows the unwholesome roots.

"How does one truly know wholesome dharmas? Wholesome bodily actions, verbal actions, and mental actions are called wholesome dharmas. Thus one truly knows wholesome dharmas. How does one truly know wholesome roots? The three wholesome roots are non-greed, non-hatred, and non-delusion. These are called the three wholesome roots. Thus one truly knows wholesome roots.

"Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila, because a well-learned noble disciple thus truly knows unwholesome dharmas, truly knows unwholesome roots, truly knows wholesome dharmas, and truly knows wholesome roots, in this Dharma and Vinaya he is endowed with right view, has accomplished direct view, has accomplished unshakable confidence in the Buddha, has entered the true Dharma, has attained this true Dharma, and has realized this true Dharma."

The Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila said to the Venerable Śāriputra: "Is this the only way, or are there others?"

The Venerable Śāriputra said: "There are. If a well-learned noble disciple truly knows food, and truly knows the arising of food, the cessation of food, and the path leading to the cessation of food.

"How does one truly know food? There are four kinds of food. What are the four? First, coarse material food; second, subtle contact food; third, mental volition food; fourth, consciousness food. These are called food. Thus one truly knows food. How does one truly know the arising of food? Craving for future existence, accompanied by delight and passion, finding pleasure here and there: this is called the arising of food. Thus one truly knows the arising of food. How does one truly know the cessation of food? If that craving for future existence, accompanied by delight and passion, finding pleasure here and there, is completely abandoned, relinquished, expelled, exhausted, made to fade away, ceased, stilled, and disappeared: this is called the cessation of food. Thus one truly knows the cessation of food. How does one truly know the path leading to the cessation of food? It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration. This is called the path leading to the cessation of food. Thus one truly knows the path leading to the cessation of food.

"If a well-learned noble disciple truly knows food, truly knows the arising of food, truly knows the cessation of food, and truly knows the path leading to the cessation of food, for this reason the well-learned noble disciple is endowed with right view in the true Dharma and Vinaya, has accomplished direct view, has accomplished unshakable confidence in the Buddha, has entered the true Dharma, has attained this true Dharma, and has realized this true Dharma."

The Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila further asked the Venerable Śāriputra: "Is this the only way, or are there others?"

The Venerable Śāriputra said: "Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila, there are further others. A well-learned noble disciple truly knows affliction, truly knows the arising of affliction, truly knows the cessation of affliction, and truly knows the path leading to the cessation of affliction.

"How does one truly know affliction? There are three afflictions: the affliction of desire, the affliction of existence, and the affliction of ignorance. These are called afflictions. Thus one truly knows affliction. How does one truly know the arising of affliction? The arising of ignorance is the arising of affliction. This is called truly knowing the arising of affliction. How does one truly know the cessation of affliction? The cessation of ignorance is the cessation of affliction. Thus one truly knows the cessation of affliction. How does one truly know the path leading to the cessation of affliction? It is the Noble Eightfold Path, as previously explained. Thus one truly knows the path leading to the cessation of affliction.

"Because a well-learned noble disciple truly knows affliction, truly knows the arising of affliction, truly knows the cessation of affliction, and truly knows the path leading to the cessation of affliction, the well-learned noble disciple is endowed with right view in this Dharma and Vinaya, and so on down to has realized this true Dharma."

The Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila asked the Venerable Śāriputra: "Is this the only way, or are there others?"

The Venerable Śāriputra said to the Venerable Mahākoṣṭhila: "There are further others. A well-learned noble disciple truly knows suffering, truly knows the arising of suffering, truly knows the cessation of suffering, and truly knows the path leading to the cessation of suffering.

"How does one truly know suffering? The suffering of birth, the suffering of old age, the suffering of sickness, the suffering of death, the suffering of separation from what one loves, the suffering of encountering what one detests, the suffering of not obtaining what one desires, and in brief, the suffering of the five aggregates subject to clinging: this is called suffering. Thus one truly knows suffering. How does one truly know the arising of suffering? Craving for future existence, accompanied by delight and passion, finding pleasure here and there: this is called the arising of suffering. Thus one truly knows the arising of suffering. How does one truly know the cessation of suffering? If that craving for future existence, accompanied by delight and passion, that attachment here and there, is completely abandoned, and so on down to stilled and disappeared: this is called the cessation of suffering. Thus one truly knows the cessation of suffering. How does one truly know the path leading to the cessation of suffering? It is the Noble Eightfold Path, as explained above. This is called the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Thus one truly knows the path leading to the cessation of suffering.

"A well-learned noble disciple who thus truly knows suffering, truly knows the arising of suffering, the cessation of suffering, and the path leading to the cessation of suffering: such a noble disciple is endowed with right view in my Dharma and Vinaya, has accomplished direct view, has accomplished unshakable confidence in the Buddha, has entered the right path, has attained this true Dharma, and has realized this true Dharma."

He further asked the Venerable Śāriputra: "Is this the only way, or are there others?"

The Venerable Śāriputra answered: "There are further others. A well-learned noble disciple truly knows old age and death, truly knows the arising of old age and death, truly knows the cessation of old age and death, and truly knows the path leading to the cessation of old age and death, as explained in the previous detailed sūtra. How does one truly know the arising of old age and death? The arising of birth is the arising of old age and death. The cessation of birth is the cessation of old age and death. The path leading to the cessation of old age and death is the Noble Eightfold Path, as previously explained. A well-learned noble disciple who thus truly knows old age and death, and so on down to truly knows the path leading to the cessation of old age and death: such a noble disciple is endowed with right view in my Dharma and Vinaya, has accomplished direct view, has accomplished unshakable confidence in the Buddha, has entered the true Dharma, has attained this true Dharma, and has realized this true Dharma. Likewise for birth, existence, clinging, craving, feeling, contact, the six sense bases, name-and-form, consciousness, and formations. A noble disciple truly knows formations, truly knows the arising of formations, the cessation of formations, and the path leading to the cessation of formations.

"How does one truly know formations? There are three kinds of formations: bodily formations, verbal formations, and mental formations. Thus one truly knows formations. How does one truly know the arising of formations? The arising of ignorance is the arising of formations. Thus one truly knows the arising of formations. How does one truly know the cessation of formations? The cessation of ignorance is the cessation of formations. Thus one truly knows the cessation of formations. How does one truly know the path leading to the cessation of formations? It is the Noble Eightfold Path, as previously explained. Mahākoṣṭhila, this is called a noble disciple who truly knows formations, truly knows the arising of formations, the cessation of formations, and the path leading to the cessation of formations. In my Dharma and Vinaya he is endowed with right view, has accomplished direct view, has accomplished unshakable confidence in the Buddha, has entered the true Dharma, has attained this true Dharma, and has realized this true Dharma."

Mahākoṣṭhila further asked the Venerable Śāriputra: "Is this the only way, or are there others?"

Śāriputra answered: "Mahākoṣṭhila, what more would you pursue? You will never be able to exhaust all these arguments and reach their end. If a noble disciple has severed ignorance and given rise to clear knowledge, what more need he seek?"

Then the two worthy ones, having completed their discussion, each returned to their own place.

---

### (345)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One said to the Venerable Śāriputra: "As I have spoken, the question of Ajita in the Pārāyaṇa:

> 'One who has attained the enumeration of dharmas,
> And the various kinds of training,
> Equipped with deportment and practice:
> Please explain and distinguish this for me.'

"Śāriputra, what is the training? What is the enumeration of dharmas?"

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra remained silent and did not answer. A second and third time, he likewise remained silent.

The Buddha said: "The real, Śāriputra!"

Śāriputra said to the Buddha: "The real, World-Honored One! World-Honored One, when a bhikṣu realizes the real, he develops revulsion, dispassion, and tends toward complete cessation. Food has arisen from its cause of arising. That bhikṣu, on account of food, develops revulsion, dispassion, and tends toward complete cessation. The cessation of that food is the real cessation. Having realized this, that bhikṣu develops revulsion, dispassion, and tends toward complete cessation. This is called the training."

"Again, the real, Śāriputra!"

Śāriputra said to the Buddha: "The real, World-Honored One! World-Honored One, when a bhikṣu realizes the real, he develops revulsion, dispassion, and complete cessation, and the taints do not arise; his mind is well liberated. That which arises from the cause of the arising of food, if the real is itself cessation, having realized this, the bhikṣu develops revulsion, dispassion, and complete cessation regarding cessation. The taints do not arise, and his mind is well liberated. This is the enumeration of dharmas."

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "So it is, so it is, as you have said. When a bhikṣu develops revulsion, dispassion, and complete cessation regarding the real, this is called the enumeration of dharmas." Having said this, the World-Honored One immediately rose and entered his chamber to sit in meditation.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra, knowing the World-Honored One had departed, shortly after said to the bhikṣus: "Venerables, I was unable to discern the World-Honored One's initial question, and therefore I remained silent in contemplation. When the World-Honored One shortly afterward posed the joy-inspiring question, I immediately understood such meaning. Even if the World-Honored One were to ask about this meaning with different phrases and different nuances for one day and one night, or even up to seven nights, I would also be able, for up to seven nights, to explain it with different phrases and different nuances."

Then a certain bhikṣu went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and stood to one side. He said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, the Venerable Śāriputra has made a remarkable and unprecedented declaration. In the great assembly, he made a definitive lion's roar, saying: 'I was completely unable to discern the World-Honored One's initial question. Even after being asked three times, I remained silent without answering. When the World-Honored One shortly afterward posed the joy-inspiring question, I immediately understood. Even if the World-Honored One were to ask about this meaning with different phrases and different nuances for one day and one night, or even up to seven nights, I would also be able, for up to seven nights, to explain it with different phrases and different nuances.'"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "That bhikṣu Śāriputra is truly able, regarding the meaning that I ask with different phrases and different nuances for one day and one night, or even up to seven nights, to explain it for up to seven nights with different phrases and different nuances. Why is that? Because the bhikṣu Śāriputra has well penetrated the realm of Dharma."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (346)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are three things in the world that are unlovely, unpleasant, and undesirable. What are the three? They are old age, sickness, and death. If there were no such three unlovely, unpleasant, and undesirable things in the world, then the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One would not appear in the world, and the world would not know that there is a Tathāgata, a Worthy One, a Perfectly Enlightened One who knows and sees and teaches the true Dharma and Vinaya. Because the world has these three things of old age, sickness, and death, which are unlovely, unpleasant, and undesirable, therefore the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One appears in the world, and the world knows that there is a Tathāgata, a Worthy One, a Perfectly Enlightened One who knows and sees and teaches the true Dharma and Vinaya.

"Because three things are not cut off, one is unable to be free from old age, sickness, and death. What are the three? They are greed, hatred, and delusion. Again, because three things are not cut off, one is unable to be free from greed, hatred, and delusion. What are the three? They are the view of a self in the body, clinging to precepts and observances, and doubt. Again, because three things are not cut off, one is unable to be free from the view of a self in the body, clinging to precepts and observances, and doubt. What are the three? They are improper attention, frequenting wrong paths, and a lazy mind. Again, because three things are not cut off, one is unable to be free from improper attention, frequenting wrong paths, and a lazy mind. What are the three? They are loss of mindfulness, lack of clear comprehension, and a confused mind. Again, because three things are not cut off, one is unable to be free from loss of mindfulness, lack of clear comprehension, and a confused mind. What are the three? They are restlessness, non-restraint, and not training in the precepts. Again, because three things are not cut off, one is unable to be free from restlessness, non-restraint, and not training in the precepts. What are the three? They are lack of faith, being difficult to teach, and laziness. Again, because three things are not cut off, one is unable to be free from lack of faith, being difficult to teach, and laziness. What are the three? They are not wishing to see noble ones, not wishing to hear the Dharma, and constantly seeking others' faults. Again, because three things are not cut off, one is unable to be free from not wishing to see noble ones, not wishing to hear the Dharma, and constantly seeking others' faults. What are the three? They are disrespect, harsh speech, and associating with bad friends. Again, because three things are not cut off, one is unable to be free from disrespect, harsh speech, and associating with bad friends. What are the three? They are shamelessness, lack of moral dread, and heedlessness. Because these three things are not cut off, one is unable to be free from disrespect, harsh speech, and associating with bad friends.

"Why is that? Because of shamelessness and lack of moral dread, one is heedless. Because of heedlessness, one is disrespectful. Because of disrespect, one associates with bad friends. Because of associating with bad friends, one does not wish to see noble ones, does not wish to hear the Dharma, and constantly seeks others' faults. Because of seeking others' faults, one lacks faith, is difficult to teach, speaks harshly, and is lazy. Because of laziness, one is restless, lacks restraint, and does not train in the precepts. Because of not training in the precepts, one loses mindfulness, lacks clear comprehension, and has a confused mind. Because of a confused mind, one has improper attention, frequents wrong paths, and has a lazy mind. Because of a lazy mind, one has the view of a self in the body, clings to precepts and observances, and has doubt. Because of doubt, one does not separate from greed, hatred, and delusion. Because one does not separate from greed, hatred, and delusion, one is unable to be free from old age, sickness, and death.

"By cutting off three things, one is able to be free from old age, sickness, and death. What three? They are greed, hatred, and delusion. When these three things are cut off, one is able to be free from old age, sickness, and death. Again, by cutting off three things, one is able to be free from greed, hatred, and delusion. What three? They are the view of a self in the body, clinging to precepts and observances, and doubt. When these three things are cut off, one is able to be free from greed, hatred, and delusion. Again, by cutting off three things, one is able to be free from the view of a self in the body, clinging to precepts and observances, and doubt. What three? They are improper attention, frequenting wrong paths, and arising of a lazy mind. When these three things are cut off, one is able to be free from the view of a self in the body, clinging to precepts and observances, and doubt. Again, by cutting off three things, one is able to be free from improper attention, frequenting wrong paths, and a lazy mind. What three? They are loss of mindfulness, lack of clear comprehension, and a confused mind. When these three things are cut off, one is able to be free from improper attention, frequenting wrong paths, and a lazy mind. Again, by cutting off three things, one is able to be free from loss of mindfulness, lack of clear comprehension, and a confused mind. What three? They are restlessness, non-restraint, and breaking the precepts. When these three things are cut off, one is able to be free from loss of mindfulness, lack of clear comprehension, and a confused mind. Again, by cutting off three things, one is able to be free from restlessness, non-restraint, and breaking the precepts. What three? They are lack of faith, being difficult to teach, and laziness. When these three things are cut off, one is able to be free from restlessness, non-restraint, and breaking the precepts. Again, by cutting off three things, one is able to be free from lack of faith, being difficult to teach, and laziness. What three? They are not wishing to see noble ones, not delighting in hearing the Dharma, and enjoying seeking others' faults. When these three things are cut off, one is able to be free from lack of faith, being difficult to teach, and laziness. Again, by cutting off three things, one is able to be free from not wishing to see noble ones, not wishing to hear the Dharma, and enjoying seeking others' faults. What three? They are disrespect, harsh speech, and associating with bad friends. When these three things are cut off, one is free from not wishing to see noble ones, not wishing to hear the Dharma, and enjoying seeking others' faults. Again, by cutting off three things, one is able to be free from disrespect, harsh speech, and associating with bad friends. What three? They are shamelessness, lack of moral dread, and heedlessness.

"Why is that? Because of a sense of shame and moral dread, one is not heedless. Because of not being heedless, one is respectful, speaks gently, and is a good friend. Because of being a good friend, one delights in seeing noble ones, delights in hearing the true Dharma, and does not seek others' faults. Because of not seeking others' faults, one gives rise to faith, speaks gently, and is diligent. Because of diligence, one is not restless, dwells in restraint, and trains in the precepts. Because of training in the precepts, one does not lose mindfulness, has clear comprehension, and dwells with an unconfused mind. Because of an unconfused mind, one has proper attention, frequents the right path, and does not have a lazy mind. Because of not having a lazy mind, one does not cling to the view of a self in the body, does not cling to precepts and observances, and overcomes doubt. Because of not doubting, one does not give rise to greed, hatred, and delusion. Separated from greed, hatred, and delusion, one is able to be free from old age, sickness, and death."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (347)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. Kings, great ministers, brahmins, elders, householders, and other people of the world all reverenced, honored, and made offerings to the Buddha and the Sangha of disciples, who greatly received gains of robes, food, bedding, and medicine. They did not at all reverence, honor, or make offerings of robes, food, bedding, and medicine to the various heterodox non-Buddhist paths.

At that time, many non-Buddhists gathered at the Lecture Hall That Had Never Been, and made this discussion: "In the past, we were always honored and served by the king, great ministers, elders, householders, and all others, who reverenced and made offerings of robes, food, bedding, and medicine. Now all this has been completely cut off, and they only reverently make offerings of robes, food, bedding, and medicine to the śramaṇa Gautama and his great assembly of disciples. Now, among us, who has the wisdom and great ability to secretly go among the followers of the śramaṇa Gautama and go forth as a renunciant, learn their Dharma, and come back to explain it widely? We shall then use what he has heard of their Dharma to influence the kings, great ministers, elders, and householders, causing them to have faith and delight, so that we can again receive offerings as before."

Then someone said: "There is a young man named Susīma, who is clever and sharp-witted, and is capable of secretly going among the followers of the śramaṇa Gautama to go forth as a renunciant. Having heard their Dharma, he can come back and proclaim it."

Then the non-Buddhists went to Susīma and said: "Today our great assembly has gathered at the Lecture Hall That Had Never Been and made this discussion: 'In the past, we were honored and served by the kings, great ministers, elders, householders, and people of the world, who reverenced and made offerings of robes, food, bedding, and medicine. Now all this has been completely cut off, and the kings, great ministers, elders, householders, and people of the world all honor and serve the śramaṇa Gautama and his great assembly of disciples. Among us, who is clever and sharp-witted, and capable of secretly going among the followers of the śramaṇa Gautama to go forth and study the path, learn their Dharma, and come back to proclaim it, so as to influence the kings, great ministers, elders, and householders, that our assembly may again receive reverence, honor, and offerings?' Among us, some said: 'Only Susīma is clever and sharp-witted, capable of secretly going among the followers of Gautama's Dharma to go forth and study the path, to hear their teachings, receive and retain them all, and come back to proclaim them.' Therefore we have come specifically to request you. Please, kind sir, go."

At that time, Susīma silently accepted the request and went to the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove in Rājagṛha.

At that time, many bhikṣus had come out of their dwellings and were walking in meditation in the open air. Then Susīma went to the many bhikṣus and said: "Venerables, may I now go forth and receive full ordination in the true Dharma and cultivate the holy life?"

Then the many bhikṣus brought Susīma to the World-Honored One, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew, and stood to one side. They said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, this non-Buddhist Susīma wishes to seek to go forth in the true Dharma, to receive full ordination, and to cultivate the holy life."

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing the thoughts in the mind of the non-Buddhist Susīma, told the bhikṣus: "You should ordain this non-Buddhist Susīma and allow him to go forth." Then the bhikṣus proceeded to ordain Susīma.

After he had gone forth, after half a month had passed, a bhikṣu said to Susīma: "Susīma, you should know that we have ended birth and death, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, and we know for ourselves that we will not receive further existence."

At that time, Susīma said to the bhikṣu: "Venerable, how is this? Having studied separation from desires and unwholesome dharmas, with applied thought and sustained thought, with joy and pleasure born of seclusion, did you attain and dwell in the first jhāna, and without the arising of taints, was the mind well liberated?"

The bhikṣu answered: "No, Susīma."

He further asked: "How is this? Having left behind applied thought and sustained thought, with inner tranquility and unification of mind, without applied thought and without sustained thought, with joy and pleasure born of concentration, did you attain and dwell in the second jhāna, and without the arising of taints, was the mind well liberated?"

The bhikṣu answered: "No, Susīma."

He further asked: "How is this? Venerable, having abandoned joy, dwelling with equanimity, with right mindfulness and right awareness, experiencing bodily pleasure, which the noble ones describe as dwelling with equanimity, did you attain and dwell in the third jhāna, and without the arising of taints, was the mind well liberated?"

He answered: "No, Susīma."

He further asked: "How is this? Venerable, having abandoned suffering and extinguished pleasure, with grief and joy having previously ceased, neither suffering nor pleasure, purified by equanimity and mindfulness, did you attain and dwell in the fourth jhāna, and without the arising of taints, was the mind well liberated?"

He answered: "No, Susīma."

He further asked: "Or, having attained the peaceful liberations that transcend form, in the formless attainments, having realized with the body and dwelt in them, without the arising of taints, was the mind well liberated?"

He answered: "No, Susīma."

Susīma further asked: "How is this? What the venerables have said is inconsistent, contradicting what was said before and after. How can you declare realization without having attained meditative absorption?"

The bhikṣu answered: "I am one liberated by wisdom."

Having said this, the many bhikṣus each rose from their seats and departed.

At that time, Susīma, knowing the many bhikṣus had left, thought: "What these venerables have said is inconsistent, contradicting what was said before and after. They say they have not attained meditative attainment, yet they declare that they know and have realized for themselves." Having thought this, he went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and stood to one side. He said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, those many bhikṣus declared in my presence: 'We have ended birth and death, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, and we know for ourselves that we will not receive further existence.' I then asked those venerables: 'Did you attain separation from desires and unwholesome dharmas, and so on down to realizing with the body and dwelling in the formless attainments, without the arising of taints, with the mind well liberated?' They answered me: 'No, Susīma.' I then said: 'What you say is inconsistent, contradicting what was said before and after. You say you have not entered meditative attainment, yet you declare that you know and have realized for yourselves.' They answered me: 'We have attained liberation by wisdom.' Having said this, they each rose from their seats and departed. Now I ask the World-Honored One: 'How is it that what they said is inconsistent, contradicting what was said before and after, not having attained meditative attainment, yet declaring: "We know and have realized for ourselves"?'"

The Buddha told Susīma: "They first knew the knowledge of the abiding of dharmas, and afterward knew nirvāṇa. Those good men, alone in a quiet place, with focused thought and diligent contemplation, dwelling without heedlessness, separated from the view of self, without the arising of taints, their minds were well liberated."

Susīma said to the Buddha: "I now do not understand 'first knowing the knowledge of the abiding of dharmas, and afterward knowing nirvāṇa. Those good men, alone in a quiet place, with focused thought and diligent contemplation, dwelling without heedlessness, separated from the view of self, without the arising of taints, their minds well liberated.'"

The Buddha told Susīma: "Whether or not you understand, you should first know the knowledge of the abiding of dharmas, and afterward know nirvāṇa. Those good men, alone in a quiet place, with focused thought and diligent contemplation, dwelling without heedlessness, separated from the view of self, their minds well liberated."

Susīma said to the Buddha: "I only wish the World-Honored One would teach me the Dharma, so that I may attain the knowledge of the abiding of dharmas, so that I may see the knowledge of the abiding of dharmas."

The Buddha told Susīma: "I will now question you; answer as you see fit. Susīma, what do you think? Is it because there is birth that there is old age and death? Apart from birth, is there old age and death?"

Susīma answered: "So it is, World-Honored One."

"Because there is birth, there is old age and death; apart from birth, there is no old age and death. Likewise for birth, existence, clinging, craving, feeling, contact, the six sense bases, name-and-form, consciousness, formations, and ignorance: is it because there is ignorance that there are formations? Apart from ignorance, are there formations?"

Susīma said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One. Because there is ignorance, there are formations. Apart from ignorance, there are no formations."

The Buddha told Susīma: "Is it because there is no birth that there is no old age and death? Is it not apart from the cessation of birth that old age and death cease?"

Susīma said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One. Because there is no birth, there is no old age and death. It is not apart from the cessation of birth that old age and death cease."

"Likewise, and so on down to: is it because there is no ignorance that there are no formations? Is it not apart from the cessation of ignorance that formations cease?"

Susīma said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One. Because there is no ignorance, there are no formations. It is not apart from the cessation of ignorance that formations cease."

The Buddha told Susīma: "One who knows and sees in this way: would he have separated from desires and unwholesome dharmas, and so on down to having realized with the body and dwelt in the formless attainments, or not?"

Susīma said to the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Susīma: "This is called first knowing the knowledge of the abiding of dharmas, and afterward knowing nirvāṇa. Those good men, alone in a quiet place, with focused thought and diligent contemplation, dwelling without heedlessness, separated from the view of self, without the arising of taints, their minds were well liberated."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the Venerable Susīma became free from dust and stain, and attained the purity of the Dharma-eye.

At that time, Susīma, having seen the Dharma, attained the Dharma, realized the Dharma, and crossed beyond doubt, not relying on the faith of another, not relying on another for deliverance, attained fearlessness in the true Dharma. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I now repent. I secretly went forth as a renunciant by stealth in the true Dharma. For this reason I repent."

The Buddha told Susīma: "How did you secretly go forth as a renunciant by stealth in the true Dharma?"

Susīma said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, many non-Buddhist renunciants came to me and said: 'Susīma, you should know that we were formerly honored and served by the kings, great ministers, elders, householders, and other people of the world, who reverenced and made offerings. Now all this has been cut off, and they all make offerings to the śramaṇa Gautama and his great assembly of disciples. You should now go secretly to go forth among the śramaṇa Gautama and his disciples, receive their Dharma, and having obtained their Dharma, return and proclaim it to us. We shall then use what has been heard of their Dharma to teach the world and cause them to reverence and make offerings as at first.' Therefore, World-Honored One, I went forth by stealth in the true Dharma and Vinaya. Today I repent. I only wish the World-Honored One would accept my repentance, out of compassion."

The Buddha told Susīma: "I accept your repentance. You should declare fully: 'Formerly I was foolish, unskillful, and without wisdom. I went forth by stealth in the true Dharma and Vinaya. Today I repent. I see my fault, I know my fault. In the future I shall accomplish the observances, with merit increasing and never diminishing.' Why is that? When a person who has committed a fault sees it himself, knows it himself, and repents, in the future he will accomplish the observances, with merit increasing and never diminishing."

The Buddha told Susīma: "I shall now give a parable, for the wise can understand through parables. Suppose a king has border guards who capture a thief, bind him, and send him to the king, reporting: 'Great King, this person has committed robbery. May the king impose the punishment.' The king says: 'Take the criminal away, bind his two hands behind his back, proclaim his offense in a harsh voice throughout the entire kingdom, then take him outside the city to the place of execution. Pierce his entire body, all four limbs, with a hundred spears.' The executioner receives the king's command, takes the criminal, binds his two hands behind his back, proclaims his offense in a harsh voice throughout the city, and takes him outside the city to the place of execution, where he pierces his entire body, all four limbs, with a hundred spears. At midday, the king asks: 'Is the criminal still alive?' The minister reports: 'He is alive.' The king further commands the minister: 'Pierce him again with a hundred spears.' By the late afternoon, he is pierced again with a hundred spears, and still he does not die."

The Buddha told Susīma: "When the king punished the criminal with three hundred spear-piercings, was there any part of the criminal's body left intact, even the size of a palm?"

Susīma said to the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One."

He further asked Susīma: "At that time, due to the three hundred spear-piercings, did the criminal experience suffering, extreme and intense suffering?"

Susīma said to the Buddha: "Extreme suffering, World-Honored One! If even one spear-piercing causes unbearable pain, how much more so three hundred spear-piercings, how could one endure that?"

The Buddha told Susīma: "Even this is bearable. But if one goes forth by stealth in the true Dharma and Vinaya, stealthily receives and upholds the Dharma, and proclaims it to others, the suffering one must endure is many times greater than that."

When the Buddha spoke this Dharma, the non-Buddhist Susīma exhausted all taints and his mind was liberated. After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the Venerable Susīma, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (348)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "The Tathāgata has accomplished the ten powers, has attained the four fearlessnesses, knows the abode of former Buddhas, is able to turn the Brahma wheel, and in the great assembly makes the lion's roar, saying: 'Because this exists, that exists; because this arises, that arises; conditioned by ignorance, there are formations.'" He explained extensively, and so on down to "'the entire mass of great suffering arises, and the entire mass of great suffering ceases.' Bhikṣus, this is the true teaching revealed, which cuts off the stream of birth and death, and so on down to, for those people it is all well revealed. This true teaching being thus revealed, cutting off the stream of birth and death, it is sufficient for a good man of right faith to go forth from home, to practice diligently with skillful means, to dwell without heedlessness, to persevere with vigor in the true Dharma and Vinaya, until skin, sinews, and bones stand exposed and flesh and blood dry up. If he has not yet attained what should be attained, he does not abandon his earnest and diligent effort, remaining firm and capable. Why is that? One who dwells in heedlessness suffers, and can give rise to various unwholesome dharmas and bonds to future existence, blazing and increasing, and in the future world, birth, old age, sickness, and death increase, retreating from the great purpose. But one who with diligence delights in dwelling alone does not give rise to various unwholesome dharmas and bonds to future existence, blazing and painful retribution, and does not in the future world increase birth, old age, sickness, and death. The great purpose is fulfilled, and one achieves the foremost arena of teaching. That is, receiving the teaching face to face from the great teacher, with the teaching of the Dharma, quiescent nirvāṇa, directed toward bodhi, the Well-Gone One, the Perfectly Enlightened One. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should contemplate your own benefit, the benefit of others, and the mutual benefit of self and others, and diligently practice and study. 'I have now gone forth. I am not foolish, not deluded. There is fruit and there is happiness. All those who provide offerings of robes, food, bedding, and medicine will receive great fruit, great merit, and great benefit.' You should study in this way."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (349)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Welcome, bhikṣus! It is good to go forth, good to gain one's own benefit. In the vast expanse of time, one occasionally obtains birth in a noble land, with faculties intact, not foolish, not deluded, not needing sign language, and able to understand the meaning of what is well spoken and ill spoken. I now in this world have become a Buddha, a Tathāgata, a Worthy One, a Perfectly Enlightened One, Perfect in Knowledge and Conduct, a Well-Gone One, a Knower of the World, an Unsurpassed One, a Trainer of Persons, a Teacher of Gods and Humans, a Buddha, a World-Honored One. I teach the Dharma of quiescence, nirvāṇa, directed toward bodhi, the Well-Gone One, the Perfectly Enlightened One. That is, because this exists, that exists; because this arises, that arises; conditioned by ignorance, there are formations; conditioned by formations, there is consciousness; and so on down to the entire mass of great suffering arises. When ignorance ceases, formations cease, and so on down to the entire mass of great suffering ceases.

"Bhikṣus, the difficult-to-obtain place has been obtained: birth in a noble land, with faculties intact, and so on down to the entire mass of great suffering arises, and the entire mass of great suffering ceases. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should study in this way: benefit for self, benefit for others, and mutual benefit for self and others. Having thus gone forth, being not foolish, not deluded, there is fruit and there is happiness, with happy fruition and reward. Those who provide offerings of robes, food, bedding, and medicine will receive great fruit, great merit, and great benefit. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should study in this way."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (350)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "A well-learned noble disciple does not think: 'What existing causes this to exist? What arising causes this to arise? What not existing causes this not to exist? What ceasing causes this to cease?' Rather, that well-learned noble disciple knows: 'Because this exists, that exists; because this arises, that arises; conditioned by ignorance, there are formations,' and so on down to 'the entire mass of great suffering arises. Because ignorance ceases, formations cease,' and so on down to 'the entire mass of great suffering ceases.'"

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (351)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Venerable Nala, the Venerable Musīla, the Venerable Savittha, and the Venerable Ānanda were dwelling near the Elephant Ear Pool in Śrāvastī.

At that time, the Venerable Nala said to the Venerable Musīla: "Apart from faith, apart from preference, apart from what has been heard, apart from reasoned reflection on appearances, apart from acceptance after pondering views, is there self-realized knowledge and vision that arises, namely: 'Because there is birth, there is old age and death; apart from birth, there is no old age and death'?" The Venerable Musīla said: "Apart from faith, apart from preference, apart from what has been heard, apart from reasoned reflection on appearances, apart from acceptance after pondering views, there is such self-realized knowledge and vision that arises, namely: 'Because there is birth, there is old age and death; apart from birth, there is no old age and death.' Thus it is said."

"Venerable Musīla, apart from faith, and so on down to apart from acceptance after pondering, is there self-realized knowledge and vision that arises, namely: 'There is cessation, quiescence, nirvāṇa'?"

The Venerable Musīla answered: "Apart from faith, and so on down to apart from acceptance after pondering, there is self-realized knowledge and vision that arises, namely: 'There is cessation, quiescence, nirvāṇa.'"

He further asked: "Venerable Musīla, if you say 'cessation is quiescence, nirvāṇa,' then are you now an arahant with all taints exhausted?"

The Venerable Musīla remained silent and did not answer. He was asked a second and third time, and likewise remained silent.

At that time, the Venerable Savittha said to the Venerable Musīla: "Please desist for now. I shall answer the Venerable Nala on your behalf."

The Venerable Musīla said: "I shall desist for now. You answer on my behalf."

Then the Venerable Savittha said to the Venerable Nala: "Apart from faith, and so on down to apart from acceptance after pondering, there is self-realized knowledge and vision that arises, namely: 'There is cessation, and cessation is quiescence, nirvāṇa.'"

Then the Venerable Nala asked the Venerable Savittha: "Apart from faith, and so on down to apart from acceptance after pondering, there is self-realized knowledge and vision that arises, namely: 'There is cessation, and cessation is quiescence, nirvāṇa.' Are you then an arahant with all taints exhausted?"

The Venerable Savittha said: "I say that cessation is quiescence, nirvāṇa, yet I am not an arahant with taints exhausted."

The Venerable Nala said: "What you say is inconsistent, contradicting what was said before and after. As the venerable says, 'Cessation is quiescence, nirvāṇa,' yet you further say you are not an arahant with taints exhausted?"

The Venerable Savittha said to the Venerable Nala: "I shall now give a parable, for the wise can understand through parables. Suppose beside a road in the wilderness there is a well, with no rope and no bucket to draw its water. At that time, a traveler comes, oppressed by heat and thirst. He searches around the well but finds no rope and no bucket. He carefully observes the water in the well and truly knows and sees it, yet does not touch it with his body. Likewise, I say that cessation is quiescence, nirvāṇa, yet I myself have not attained the state of an arahant with taints exhausted."

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda said to the Venerable Nala: "As for what the Venerable Savittha has said, what do you say?"

The Venerable Nala said to the Venerable Ānanda: "What the Venerable Savittha has said is good and true. What more could I say?"

Then those worthy ones, having each made their statements, rose from their seats and departed.

---

### (352)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If any śramaṇas or brahmins do not truly know dharmas, do not truly know the arising of dharmas, the cessation of dharmas, and the path leading to the cessation of dharmas, they are not śramaṇas among śramaṇas, not brahmins among brahmins. They also do not attain the meaning of a śramaṇa or the meaning of a brahmin, and do not in this life realize and bear witness with direct knowledge: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'

"How does one not truly know dharmas? How does one not truly know the arising of dharmas? How does one not truly know the cessation of dharmas? How does one not truly know the path leading to the cessation of dharmas? Not truly knowing the dharma of old age and death, not truly knowing the arising of old age and death, the cessation of old age and death, and the path leading to the cessation of old age and death. Likewise for birth, existence, clinging, craving, feeling, contact: not truly knowing the six sense bases, not truly knowing the arising of the six sense bases, the cessation of the six sense bases, and the path leading to the cessation of the six sense bases. Thus one does not truly know dharmas, does not truly know the arising of dharmas, the cessation of dharmas, and the path leading to the cessation of dharmas.

"If any śramaṇas or brahmins truly know dharmas, truly know the arising of dharmas, the cessation of dharmas, and the path leading to the cessation of dharmas, they should be known as śramaṇas among śramaṇas, brahmins among brahmins. They attain the meaning of a śramaṇa and the meaning of a brahmin, and in this life realize and bear witness with direct knowledge: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'

"What dharmas does one truly know? What arising of dharmas, cessation of dharmas, and path leading to the cessation of dharmas does one truly know? Truly knowing the dharma of old age and death, truly knowing the arising of old age and death, the cessation of old age and death, and the path leading to the cessation of old age and death. Likewise for birth, existence, clinging, craving, feeling, contact: truly knowing the six sense bases, truly knowing the arising of the six sense bases, the cessation of the six sense bases, and the path leading to the cessation of the six sense bases. Thus one truly knows dharmas, truly knows the arising of dharmas, the cessation of dharmas, and the path leading to the cessation of dharmas."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (353)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If śramaṇas or brahmins do not truly know dharmas, do not truly know the arising of dharmas, the cessation of dharmas, and the path leading to the cessation of dharmas, they should be known as śramaṇas who are not among śramaṇas, brahmins who are not among brahmins. They also do not attain the meaning of a śramaṇa or the meaning of a brahmin, and do not in this life realize and bear witness with direct knowledge: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'

"What dharmas do they not truly know? What arising of dharmas, cessation of dharmas, and path leading to the cessation of dharmas do they not truly know? Not truly knowing the dharma of the six sense bases, not truly knowing the arising of the six sense bases, the cessation of the six sense bases, and the path leading to the cessation of the six sense bases. If they claim to truly know contact, there is no such possibility. If they claim to truly know the arising of contact, the cessation of contact, and the path leading to the cessation of contact, there is no such possibility. Likewise for feeling, craving, clinging, existence, birth, old age and death: if they claim to truly know them, there is no such possibility.

"If śramaṇas or brahmins truly know the six sense bases, truly know the arising of the six sense bases, the cessation of the six sense bases, and the path leading to the cessation of the six sense bases, then it is possible for them to truly know contact. Likewise for feeling, craving, clinging, existence, birth, old age and death: it is possible for them to truly know these."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (354)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "If any śramaṇas or brahmins do not truly know the six sense bases, and wish to transcend contact, there is no such possibility. If they wish to transcend the arising of contact, the cessation of contact, and the path leading to the cessation of contact, there is no such possibility. Likewise, if they wish to transcend feeling, craving, clinging, existence, birth, old age and death, there is no such possibility. If they wish to transcend the arising of old age and death, the cessation of old age and death, and the path leading to the cessation of old age and death, there is no such possibility. If śramaṇas or brahmins truly know the six sense bases, truly know the arising of the six sense bases, the cessation of the six sense bases, and the path leading to the cessation of the six sense bases, then to transcend contact, there is such possibility. Likewise, to transcend feeling, craving, clinging, existence, birth, old age and death, there is such possibility, and so on down to transcending the path leading to the cessation of old age and death, there is such possibility."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with old age and death, and so on down to the six sense bases: three sūtras.

Likewise for old age and death, and so on down to formations: three sūtras are also spoken in the same way.

---

### (355)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "You should realize old age and death, realize the arising of old age and death, the cessation of old age and death, and the path leading to the cessation of old age and death. Likewise, and so on down to you should realize formations, the arising of formations, the cessation of formations, and the path leading to the cessation of formations.

"How should one realize old age and death? Realize that because there is birth, there is old age and death. Thus one realizes old age and death. How does one realize the arising of old age and death? The arising of birth is the arising of old age and death. Thus one realizes the arising of old age and death. How does one realize the cessation of old age and death? The cessation of birth is the cessation of old age and death. Thus one realizes the cessation of old age and death. How does one realize the path leading to the cessation of old age and death? The Noble Eightfold Path is the path leading to the cessation of old age and death. Thus one realizes the path leading to the cessation of old age and death. And so on down to: how does one realize formations? There are three kinds of formations: bodily formations, verbal formations, and mental formations. Thus one realizes formations. How does one realize the arising of formations? The arising of ignorance is the arising of formations. Thus one realizes the arising of formations. How does one realize the cessation of formations? The cessation of ignorance is the cessation of formations. Thus one realizes the cessation of formations. How does one realize the path leading to the cessation of formations? The Noble Eightfold Path is the path leading to the cessation of formations. Thus one realizes the path leading to the cessation of formations."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (356)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are forty-four kinds of knowledge. Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain them for you. What are the forty-four kinds of knowledge? They are: knowledge of old age and death, knowledge of the arising
