# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Fifteen

---

### (365)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I speak of parinirvāṇa in the present life. How does the Tathāgata explain parinirvāṇa in the present life?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. Excellent, World-Honored One! May you please explain parinirvāṇa in the present life. Having heard it, the bhikṣus will receive and practice it. How does a bhikṣu attain parinirvāṇa in the present life?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it for you. If a bhikṣu, with regard to old age, sickness, and death, develops revulsion, dispassion, and complete cessation, and without giving rise to the outflows, his mind is well liberated, this is called a bhikṣu who attains parinirvāṇa in the present life."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (366)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "When the Buddha Vipaśyin had not yet attained perfect awakening, while alone in a quiet place, with focused and diligent meditation, he had this thought: 'The entire world enters into birth and death; it is self-arising, self-ripening, self-perishing, and self-sinking. Yet those beings do not truly know the supramundane path that transcends old age and death.' He then investigated: 'What is the condition for this old age and death?' Thus, through right contemplation and investigation, he gained true knowledge through uninterrupted realization: 'Because there is birth, there is this old age and death; conditioned by birth, there is old age and death.' He further contemplated rightly: 'What is the condition for this birth?' Again through right contemplation, through uninterrupted realization he knew: 'Conditioned by existence, there is birth.' He further contemplated rightly: 'What is the condition for this existence?' Again through right contemplation, through true uninterrupted realization he knew: 'Because there is clinging, there is existence.' He further contemplated rightly: 'What is the condition for this clinging?' Again through right contemplation, through true uninterrupted realization he investigated: 'Clinging arises from relishing and pondering phenomena, increased and nourished by contact and craving.' One should know: conditioned by craving there is clinging, conditioned by clinging there is existence, conditioned by existence there is birth, conditioned by birth there is old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering. Thus arises this entire great mass of suffering. Just as a lamp burns dependent on oil and wick, and when one continually adds oil and trims the wick, that lamp constantly shines bright and blazes without ceasing." As previously explained in full with the praise simile and the city simile.

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

As with the Buddha Vipaśyin, so also with the Buddha Śikhin, the Buddha Viśvabhū, the Buddha Krakucchanda, the Buddha Kanakamuni, and the Buddha Kāśyapa; all spoke likewise.

---

### (367)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should diligently and skillfully cultivate and practice dhyāna contemplation, inwardly stilling the mind. Why is this? When a bhikṣu practices dhyāna contemplation, inwardly stilling the mind, diligently and skillfully, then things are manifested as they truly are. How are they manifested as they truly are? Old age and death are manifested as they truly are; the arising of old age and death, the cessation of old age and death, and the path leading to the cessation of old age and death are manifested as they truly are. Birth, existence, clinging, craving, feeling, contact, the six sense bases, name-and-form, consciousness, and volitional formations are manifested as they truly are. The arising of volitional formations, the cessation of volitional formations, and the path leading to the cessation of volitional formations are manifested as they truly are. That all these phenomena are impermanent, conditioned, and subject to outflows is manifested as it truly is."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (368)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should cultivate immeasurable samāpatti, with focused and mindful attention. Having cultivated immeasurable samāpatti with focused and mindful attention, things are manifested as they truly are. How are they manifested as they truly are? Old age and death are manifested as they truly are, and so on up to volitional formations are manifested as they truly are. That all these phenomena are impermanent, conditioned, and subject to outflows; thus they are manifested as they truly are."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (369)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In the past, when the Buddha Vipaśyin had not yet attained perfect awakening, he was at the place of awakening and was about to attain Buddhahood before long. He went to the Bodhi tree, spread grass for a seat, sat cross-legged, sat upright with right mindfulness, and sat for seven days, observing the twelve links of dependent arising in forward and reverse order, namely: 'Because this exists, that exists; because this arises, that arises. Conditioned by ignorance, there are volitional formations,' and so on up to 'conditioned by birth, there is old age and death,' and the arising of the entire great mass of suffering, and the cessation of the entire great mass of suffering.

"After the Buddha Vipaśyin had sat upright for seven days, he arose from samādhi and spoke these verses:

> 'When these phenomena arise,
> The brahmin diligently contemplates in meditation,
> Forever leaving behind all doubts and perplexities,
> Knowing the Dharma arising from causes and conditions.
>
> If one knows suffering arising from causes,
> And knows the complete cessation of all feelings,
> And knows the exhaustion of conditioned phenomena,
> Then one knows the exhaustion of all outflows.
>
> When these phenomena arise,
> The brahmin diligently contemplates in meditation,
> Forever leaving behind all doubts and perplexities,
> Knowing that suffering arises from causes.
>
> When these phenomena arise,
> The brahmin diligently contemplates in meditation,
> Forever leaving behind all doubts and perplexities,
> Knowing the complete cessation of all feelings.
>
> When these phenomena arise,
> The brahmin diligently contemplates in meditation,
> Forever leaving behind all doubts and perplexities,
> Knowing the exhaustion of conditioned phenomena.
>
> When these phenomena arise,
> The brahmin diligently contemplates in meditation,
> Forever leaving behind all doubts and perplexities,
> Knowing the exhaustion of all outflows.
>
> When these phenomena arise,
> The brahmin diligently contemplates in meditation,
> Universally illuminating all worlds,
> Like the sun abiding in empty space.
>
> Destroying the armies of Māra,
> Awakening to the fetters, attaining liberation.'"

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

As with the Buddha Vipaśyin, so also with the Buddha Śikhin, the Buddha Viśvabhū, the Buddha Krakucchanda, the Buddha Kanakamuni, and the Buddha Kāśyapa; all spoke likewise.

---

### (370)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Uruvelā, by the Nairañjanā River, at the great place of awakening. Shortly before attaining perfect awakening, he went to the Bodhi tree, spread grass for a seat, sat cross-legged, with body upright and right mindfulness, as previously explained in full.

---

### (371)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of nutriment that benefit sentient beings, enabling them to abide in the world, sustain and nourish themselves. What are the four? The first is coarse physical food, the second is subtle contact food, the third is mental volition food, and the fourth is consciousness food.

"What are the cause, the origin, the arising, and the condition of these four foods? These foods have craving as their cause, craving as their origin, craving as their arising, and craving as their condition. What is the cause, origin, arising, and condition of this craving? Craving has feeling as its cause, feeling as its origin, feeling as its arising, and feeling as its condition. What is the cause, origin, arising, and condition of this feeling? Feeling has contact as its cause, contact as its origin, contact as its arising, and contact as its condition. What is the cause, origin, arising, and condition of this contact? Contact has the six sense bases as its cause, the six sense bases as its origin, the six sense bases as its arising, and the six sense bases as its condition. The arising of the six sense bases is the arising of contact; the arising of contact is the arising of feeling; the arising of feeling is the arising of craving; the arising of craving is the arising of the foods. Because of the arising of the foods, in the future there is the arising of birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering. Thus arises this entire great mass of suffering.

"Likewise, when the six sense bases cease, contact ceases; when contact ceases, feeling ceases; when feeling ceases, craving ceases; when craving ceases, the foods cease. Because the foods cease, in the future birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering cease. Thus ceases this entire great mass of suffering."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (372)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of nutriment that benefit sentient beings, enabling them to abide in the world, sustain and nourish themselves. What are the four? First, coarse physical food; second, subtle contact food; third, mental volition food; fourth, consciousness food."

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Phagguna, who was sitting behind the Buddha fanning him. He said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, who eats this consciousness?"

The Buddha told Phagguna: "I do not say there is one who eats consciousness. If I said there is one who eats consciousness, then you should ask such a question. I say consciousness is food. You should ask: 'What is the condition for consciousness food?' I would then answer: 'It is capable of attracting future existence, causing the continuation of birth. Because there is existence, there are the six sense bases; conditioned by the six sense bases, there is contact.'"

Phagguna further asked: "Who makes contact?"

The Buddha told Phagguna: "I do not say there is one who makes contact. If I said there is one who makes contact, then you should ask: 'Who makes contact?' You should ask thus: 'What is the condition for the arising of contact?' I should answer thus: 'Conditioned by the six sense bases, there is contact; conditioned by contact, there is feeling.'"

He further asked: "Who feels?"

The Buddha told Phagguna: "I do not say there is one who feels. If I said there is one who feels, you should ask: 'Who feels?' You should ask: 'What is the condition for feeling?' I should answer thus: 'Conditioned by contact, there is feeling; conditioned by feeling, there is craving.'"

He further asked: "World-Honored One, who craves?"

The Buddha told Phagguna: "I do not say there is one who craves. If I said there is one who craves, you should ask: 'Who craves?' You should ask: 'What is the condition for craving?' I should answer thus: 'Conditioned by feeling, there is craving; conditioned by craving, there is clinging.'"

He further asked: "World-Honored One, who clings?"

The Buddha told Phagguna: "I do not say there is one who clings. If I said there is one who clings, you should ask: 'Who clings?' You should ask: 'What is the condition for clinging?' I should answer: 'Conditioned by craving, there is clinging; conditioned by clinging, there is existence.'"

He further asked: "World-Honored One, who has existence?"

The Buddha told Phagguna: "I do not say there is one who has existence. If I said there is one who has existence, you should ask: 'Who has existence?' You should now ask: 'What is the condition for existence?' I should answer: 'Conditioned by clinging, there is existence. That which is capable of attracting future existence and the arising of contact through birth is called existence. There are the six sense bases; conditioned by the six sense bases, there is contact; conditioned by contact, there is feeling; conditioned by feeling, there is craving; conditioned by craving, there is clinging; conditioned by clinging, there is existence; conditioned by existence, there is birth; conditioned by birth, there is old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering. Thus arises this entire great mass of suffering. When the six sense bases cease, contact ceases; when contact ceases, feeling ceases; when feeling ceases, craving ceases; when craving ceases, clinging ceases; when clinging ceases, existence ceases; when existence ceases, birth ceases; when birth ceases, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering cease. Thus ceases this entire great mass of suffering.'"

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (373)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of nutriment that benefit sentient beings, enabling them to abide in the world, sustain and nourish themselves. What are the four? The first is coarse physical food, the second is subtle contact food, the third is mental volition food, and the fourth is consciousness food.

"How should a bhikṣu contemplate physical food? Suppose there were a husband and wife who had only one child, whom they loved and cherished. They wished to cross a wilderness, a dangerous and difficult road. Their provisions were exhausted, and they were extremely hungry and desperate, with no means of survival. They discussed together: 'We have only this one child whom we dearly love. If we eat his flesh, we can cross this danger. Let us not allow all three of us to die here.' Having decided thus, they killed their child. Suppressing their grief and shedding tears, they forced themselves to eat his flesh, and they crossed the wilderness. How is it, bhikṣus? Would that husband and wife take pleasure in eating their child's flesh, savoring it with greedy enjoyment, or not?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One."

He further asked: "Bhikṣus, did they force themselves to eat that flesh in order to cross the wilderness danger, or not?"

They answered: "So it was, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Whenever one eats physical food, one should contemplate it in this way. Contemplating it thus, physical food is fully understood and abandoned. When physical food is fully understood and abandoned, then craving and attachment to the five strands of sensual pleasure are severed. When craving and attachment to the five strands of sensual pleasure are severed, I do not see that a well-learned noble disciple would have even a single fetter regarding the five strands of sensual pleasure that remains unbroken. If even one fetter remains, one would return to be born in this world.

"How should a bhikṣu contemplate contact food? Suppose there were a cow that had been flayed alive. Wherever it went, insects would bite and consume it, sand and dust would press upon it, grass, wood, and thorns would prick it. If it rested on the ground, ground insects would eat it. If it went into water, water insects would eat it. If it went into the air, flying insects would eat it. Whether lying down or rising up, this body would constantly suffer bitter pain. Likewise, bhikṣus, regarding contact food, one should contemplate it in this way. Contemplating it thus, contact food is fully understood and abandoned. When contact food is fully understood and abandoned, the three feelings are severed. When the three feelings are severed, for the well-learned noble disciple there is nothing further to be done above this, for what was to be done has been done.

"How should a bhikṣu contemplate mental volition food? Suppose near a village or city, a fire broke out with neither smoke nor flame. At that time, a man who was wise and intelligent, who turned away from suffering and sought happiness, who disliked death and loved life, had this thought: 'There is a great fire, without smoke or without flame. One who walks about should avoid it, lest one fall into it and surely die.' Having reflected thus, he constantly generated the thought and wish to leave it far behind and go away. Contemplating mental volition food should also be like this. Contemplating it thus, mental volition food is fully understood and abandoned. When mental volition food is fully understood and abandoned, the three kinds of craving are severed. When the three kinds of craving are severed, for the well-learned noble disciple there is nothing further to be done above this, for what was to be done has been done.

"Bhikṣus, how should one contemplate consciousness food? Suppose a king had patrol guards who captured a thief and bound him, sending him before the king," as previously explained in full in the Susīma Sūtra. "Due to those causes and conditions, he experienced the suffering sensation of three hundred spears, day and night in painful agony. Contemplating consciousness food should also be like this. Contemplating it thus, consciousness food is fully understood and abandoned. When consciousness food is fully understood and abandoned, name-and-form is fully understood and abandoned. When name-and-form is fully understood and abandoned, for the well-learned noble disciple there is nothing further to be done above this, for what was to be done has been done."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (374)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of nutriment that benefit sentient beings, enabling them to abide in the world, sustain and nourish themselves. What are the four? First, physical food; second, contact food; third, mental volition food; fourth, consciousness food.

"If a bhikṣu has delight and craving for these four foods, then consciousness becomes established and increases. Because consciousness becomes established and increases, it enters into name-and-form. Because it enters into name-and-form, volitional formations increase. Because volitional formations increase, future existence increases. Because future existence increases, birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering arise. Thus arises this entire great mass of suffering.

"If regarding the four foods there is no craving and no delight, because there is no craving and no delight, consciousness does not become established and does not increase. Because consciousness does not become established and does not increase, it does not enter into name-and-form. Because it does not enter into name-and-form, volitional formations do not increase. Because volitional formations do not increase, future existence does not arise and does not grow. Because future existence does not arise and does not grow, in the future, birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering do not arise. Thus ceases this entire great mass of suffering."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (375)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of nutriment that benefit sentient beings, enabling them to abide in the world, sustain and nourish themselves. What are the four? First, physical food; second, contact food; third, mental volition food; fourth, consciousness food. Bhikṣus, if regarding these four foods there is craving and delight, then there is grief and sorrow, and there is defilement. If regarding the four foods there is no craving and no delight, then there is no grief and sorrow, and also no defilement."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (376)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of nutriment that benefit sentient beings, enabling them to abide in the world, sustain and nourish themselves. What are the four? First, physical food; second, contact food; third, mental volition food; fourth, consciousness food.

"Bhikṣus, if regarding these four foods there is craving and delight, consciousness becomes established and increases," and so on up to "this entire great mass of suffering arises. Just as a multi-storied palace, extending long and wide to the north and west, with windows on east and west, when the sun rises in the east, its light shines on the western wall. Likewise, bhikṣus, if regarding these four foods there is craving and delight," as previously explained in full, up to "this entire great mass of suffering arises.

"If regarding the four foods there is no craving and no delight," as previously explained in full, up to "this entire great mass of suffering ceases. Suppose, bhikṣus, a multi-storied palace, extending long and wide to the north and west, with windows on east and west. When the sun rises in the east, where should the light shine?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "It should shine on the western wall."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "If there were no western wall, where should it shine?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "It should shine into empty space, with nothing to attach to."

"Likewise, bhikṣus, if regarding these four foods there is no craving and no delight, consciousness has nowhere to become established," and so on up to "thus ceases this entire great mass of suffering."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (377)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of nutriment that benefit sentient beings, enabling them to abide in the world, sustain and nourish themselves. What are the four? First, physical food; second, contact food; third, mental volition food; fourth, consciousness food. Bhikṣus, if regarding these four foods there is craving and delight, consciousness becomes established and increases, and so on up to this entire great mass of suffering arises. Suppose, bhikṣus, a multi-storied palace, extending long and wide to the north and west, with windows on east and west. When the sun rises in the east, where should the light shine?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "It should shine on the western wall."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Likewise, if regarding the four foods there is craving and delight, consciousness becomes established and increases, and so on up to thus arises this entire great mass of suffering. If regarding the four foods there is no craving and no delight, and consciousness does not become established and does not increase, and so on up to thus ceases this entire great mass of suffering. Suppose, bhikṣus, a painter or a painter's apprentice gathered various pigments and wished to paint upon empty space. Could they paint upon it or not?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "They could not, World-Honored One. Why? Because empty space is not material form, is without resistance, and is invisible."

"Likewise, bhikṣus, if regarding these four foods there is no craving and no delight, and consciousness does not become established and does not increase, and so on up to thus ceases this entire great mass of suffering."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (378)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of nutriment that benefit sentient beings, enabling them to abide in the world, sustain and nourish themselves. What are the four? First, physical food; second, contact food; third, mental volition food; fourth, consciousness food. Bhikṣus, if regarding these four foods there is craving and delight, consciousness becomes established and increases, and so on up to this entire great mass of suffering arises. Suppose, bhikṣus, a painter or a painter's apprentice gathered various pigments and wished to paint upon a surface of material form, making various images. Bhikṣus, what do you think? Can that painter or painter's apprentice paint upon material form or not?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One. They can paint upon material form."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "If regarding these four foods there is craving and delight, consciousness becomes established and increases, and so on up to thus arises this entire great mass of suffering. Bhikṣus, if regarding the four foods there is no craving and no delight, consciousness does not become established and does not increase, and so on up to thus ceases this entire great mass of suffering. Bhikṣus, suppose a painter or a painter's apprentice gathered various pigments and wished, apart from material form, to paint somewhere and make various images. Could they paint or not?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "They could not, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, bhikṣus, if regarding the four foods there is no craving and no delight, consciousness does not become established and does not increase, and so on up to thus ceases this entire great mass of suffering."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (379)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the five bhikṣus: "This noble truth of suffering, a Dharma previously unheard, when rightly contemplated, gives rise to vision, knowledge, clarity, and awakening. This noble truth of the origin of suffering, this noble truth of the cessation of suffering, this noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, a Dharma previously unheard, when rightly contemplated, gives rise to vision, knowledge, clarity, and awakening.

"Furthermore, the noble truth of suffering should be known and fully comprehended. This Dharma previously unheard, when rightly contemplated, gives rise to vision, knowledge, clarity, and awakening. The noble truth of the origin of suffering, having been known, should be abandoned. This Dharma previously unheard, when rightly contemplated, gives rise to vision, knowledge, clarity, and awakening.

"Furthermore, regarding the cessation of the origin of suffering, this noble truth of the cessation of suffering, having been known, should be directly realized. This Dharma previously unheard, when rightly contemplated, gives rise to vision, knowledge, clarity, and awakening. Furthermore, this noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, having been known, should be cultivated. This Dharma previously unheard, when rightly contemplated, gives rise to vision, knowledge, clarity, and awakening.

"Furthermore, bhikṣus, this noble truth of suffering has been known and fully comprehended; this Dharma previously unheard, when rightly contemplated, gives rise to vision, knowledge, clarity, and awakening. Furthermore, this noble truth of the origin of suffering has been known and has been abandoned; this Dharma previously unheard, when rightly contemplated, gives rise to vision, knowledge, clarity, and awakening.

"Furthermore, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering has been known and has been directly realized; this Dharma previously unheard, when rightly contemplated, gives rise to vision, knowledge, clarity, and awakening. Furthermore, the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering has been known and has been cultivated; this Dharma previously unheard, when rightly contemplated, gives rise to vision, knowledge, clarity, and awakening.

"Bhikṣus, if regarding these Four Noble Truths in three turnings and twelve aspects I had not given rise to vision, knowledge, clarity, and awakening, I would never have been able, among the assemblies of devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, and those who hear the Dharma, to declare liberation, to declare release, to declare freedom, nor could I have personally realized anuttara-samyak-saṃbodhi. Because I have, regarding the Four Noble Truths in three turnings and twelve aspects, given rise to vision, knowledge, clarity, and awakening, therefore among the assemblies of devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, and those who hear the Dharma, I have attained release, attained freedom, and have personally realized and accomplished anuttara-samyak-saṃbodhi."

At that time, when the World-Honored One spoke this Dharma, the Venerable Kauṇḍinya and eighty thousand devas became free from dust and stains and attained the purity of the Dharma-eye.

At that time, the World-Honored One asked the Venerable Kauṇḍinya: "Have you understood the Dharma?"

Kauṇḍinya said to the Buddha: "I have understood, World-Honored One."

He again asked the Venerable Kauṇḍinya: "Have you understood the Dharma?"

Kauṇḍinya said to the Buddha: "I have understood, Sugata."

Because the Venerable Kauṇḍinya had understood the Dharma, he was therefore named Ājñāta-Kauṇḍinya.

After the Venerable Ājñāta-Kauṇḍinya had understood the Dharma, the earth deities raised their voices and proclaimed: "Venerable ones! The World-Honored One, at the Deer Park in the Abode of Sages in the land of Vārāṇasī, has turned the Dharma-wheel of three turnings and twelve aspects, which had never been turned by any śramaṇa, brāhmaṇa, deva, Māra, or Brahmā. This is of great benefit, of great comfort, out of compassion for the world, for the welfare and benefit, the peace and happiness of devas and humans. It increases the hosts of devas and diminishes the hosts of asuras."

After the earth deities had proclaimed this, the deities of empty space, the Four Great Kings' Heaven, the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, the Yāma Heaven, the Tuṣita Heaven, the Nirmāṇarati Heaven, and the Paranirmita-vaśavartin Heaven successively proclaimed it, and in a brief moment it was heard in the Brahmā Heaven. The Brahmā deities raised their voices and proclaimed: "Venerable ones! The World-Honored One, at the Deer Park in the Abode of Sages in the land of Vārāṇasī, has turned the Dharma-wheel of three turnings and twelve aspects, which had never been turned by any śramaṇa, brāhmaṇa, deva, Māra, or Brahmā, or anyone in the world who has heard the Dharma. This is of great benefit, of great comfort, for the welfare and benefit of devas and worldly beings. It increases the hosts of devas and diminishes the hosts of asuras." Because the World-Honored One turned the Dharma-wheel at the Deer Park in the Abode of Sages in the land of Vārāṇasī, this sūtra is therefore named the Sūtra of Turning the Dharma-Wheel.

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (380)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (381)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. If a bhikṣu has not yet attained uninterrupted realization regarding these Four Noble Truths, he should cultivate uninterrupted realization, arouse supreme desire, apply skillful effort, and practice with right mindfulness and right knowing."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (382)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Regarding the noble truth of suffering, a bhikṣu should know and comprehend it. Regarding the noble truth of the origin of suffering, he should know and abandon it. Regarding the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, he should know and realize it. Regarding the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, he should know and cultivate it."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (383)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. When a bhikṣu has already known and comprehended the noble truth of suffering, has already known and abandoned the noble truth of the origin of suffering, has already known and realized the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and has already known and cultivated the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, such a bhikṣu then severs craving and desire, turns away from the fetters, and through the uninterrupted realization of conceit, reaches the final end of suffering."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (384)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. When a bhikṣu has already known and comprehended the noble truth of suffering, has already known and abandoned the noble truth of the origin of suffering, has already known and realized the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and has already known and cultivated the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, such a bhikṣu is called an arhat, whose outflows are exhausted, whose task is accomplished, who has laid down the heavy burden, who has attained his own benefit, who has completely ended the fetters of existence, and who is well liberated through right knowledge."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (385)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. When a bhikṣu has already known and comprehended the noble truth of suffering, has already known and abandoned the noble truth of the origin of suffering, has already known and realized the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and has already known and cultivated the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, such a bhikṣu has reached the ultimate limit, the ultimate boundary, the boundary free of defilement. His holy life is completed; he is pure and unblemished. He is called a supreme person."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (386)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. When a bhikṣu has already known and comprehended the noble truth of suffering, has already known and abandoned the noble truth of the origin of suffering, has already known and realized the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and has already known and cultivated the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, such a bhikṣu is without barriers, has leveled the city moat, has crossed over dangers and difficulties, has been freed from bonds and fetters, and is called a noble one who has erected the noble banner."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (387)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. When a bhikṣu has already known and comprehended the noble truth of suffering, has already known and abandoned the noble truth of the origin of suffering, has already known and realized the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and has already known and cultivated the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, such a bhikṣu is without barriers, has leveled the city moat, has crossed over dangers and difficulties, and is called a noble one who has erected the noble banner. Bhikṣus, how is one without barriers? The five lower fetters have been severed and fully known; this is called being free of barriers. How has one leveled the city moat? Ignorance is called the deep moat; having severed and fully known it, this is called having leveled the city moat. How has one crossed over dangers and difficulties? The beginningless cycle of birth and death is ended, reaching the final limit of suffering; this is called having crossed over dangers and difficulties. How has one been freed from bonds and fetters? Craving has been severed and fully known. How has one erected the noble banner? The conceit of 'I am' has been severed and fully known; this is called having erected the noble banner."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (388)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. When a bhikṣu has already known and comprehended the noble truth of suffering, has already known and abandoned the noble truth of the origin of suffering, has already known and realized the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and has already known and cultivated the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, this bhikṣu is called one who has severed the five branches, who has accomplished the six factors, who guards the one, who relies upon the four, who has abandoned the various truths claimed by others, who has left the four crossroads, who has realized the various awakened perceptions, whose bodily actions are stilled, whose mind is well liberated, and whose wisdom is well liberated. Pure and unblemished, he is called a supreme person."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (389)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "When four qualities are accomplished, one is called a Great King of Physicians, possessing the requisites and the attributes of a king. What are the four? First, skillfully knowing disease; second, skillfully knowing the source of disease; third, skillfully knowing the treatment for disease; fourth, skillfully knowing that after a disease is treated, it will not recur in the future. How does a good physician skillfully know disease? A good physician skillfully knows the various kinds of diseases, such and such types of disease. This is called a good physician who skillfully knows disease. How does a good physician skillfully know the source of disease? A good physician skillfully knows that this disease arises from wind, arises from bile, arises from phlegm, arises from cold, arises from present circumstances, or arises from seasonal changes. This is called a good physician who skillfully knows the source of disease. How does a good physician skillfully know the treatment for disease? A good physician skillfully knows the various diseases and whether they should be treated with ointments, with emetics, with purgatives, with nasal medicines, with fumigation, or with sweating treatments. These various kinds of treatments are called a good physician who skillfully knows treatment. How does a good physician skillfully know that after treatment the disease will not recur in the future? A good physician skillfully treats various diseases so that they are completely eliminated and will never recur in the future. This is called a good physician who skillfully knows that after treatment the disease will not recur.

"The Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Awakened One, as the Great King of Physicians, accomplishes four virtues and heals the diseases of sentient beings in the same way. What are the four? The Tathāgata knows: 'This is the noble truth of suffering,' and knows it as it truly is. 'This is the noble truth of the origin of suffering,' and knows it as it truly is. 'This is the noble truth of the cessation of suffering,' and knows it as it truly is. 'This is the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering,' and knows it as it truly is. Bhikṣus, those worldly good physicians do not truly know the root treatment for birth, and do not truly know the root treatment for old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering. The Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Awakened One, as the Great King of Physicians, truly knows the root treatment for birth, and truly knows the root treatment for old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering. Therefore, the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Awakened One is called the Great King of Physicians."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (390)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas do not truly know this noble truth of suffering, do not truly know this noble truth of the origin of suffering, do not truly know this noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and do not truly know this noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, they are not śramaṇas among śramaṇas, not brāhmaṇas among brāhmaṇas. They also do not realize through direct knowledge in this very life the meaning of being a śramaṇa or a brāhmaṇa, declaring: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, and I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'

"If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas truly know this noble truth of suffering, truly know this noble truth of the origin of suffering, truly know this noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and truly know this noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, one should know that they are śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, śramaṇas among śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas among brāhmaṇas. They realize through direct knowledge in this very life the meaning of being a śramaṇa or a brāhmaṇa, declaring: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, and I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.' Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, you should attain uninterrupted realization, arouse supreme desire, be diligent and capable, and skillfully cultivate and practice. What are the four? They are the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (391)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages, as explained in full above. The difference is: "Those who do not truly know the Four Noble Truths, one should know that such śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas are not counted among śramaṇas, not counted among brāhmaṇas. Those who truly know the Four Noble Truths are counted among śramaṇas and counted among brāhmaṇas." And so on.

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (392)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas do not truly know the noble truth of suffering, do not truly know the noble truth of the origin of suffering, do not truly know the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and do not truly know the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, one should know that such śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas are not freed from suffering. If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas truly know the noble truth of suffering, truly know the noble truth of the origin of suffering, truly know the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and truly know the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, one should know that such śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas are liberated from suffering."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

Just as with not being liberated from suffering and being liberated from suffering, likewise: not being liberated from the evil destinies and being liberated from the evil destinies; being able to abandon the precepts and regress, and not abandoning the precepts and regressing; being able to declare for oneself the attainment of superhuman dharmas and realization, and not being able to declare for oneself the attainment of superhuman dharmas and realization; being able to seek good fields of merit outside of this, and not being able to seek good fields of merit outside of this;

being able to seek a teacher outside of this, and not being able to seek a teacher outside of this; not being able to transcend suffering, and being able to transcend suffering; not being able to be freed from suffering, and being able to be freed from suffering.

Thus the above sūtras are spoken again, all followed by these verses:

> "If one does not know suffering,
> And the causes of that suffering,
> And the Dharma of all sufferings
> Being utterly ceased without remainder;
>
> If one does not know the path
> That can quell all suffering,
> With mind liberated from suffering,
> And wisdom liberated as well;
>
> One cannot transcend the mass of suffering,
> Nor reach the ultimate release from suffering.
>
> If one truly knows suffering,
> And also knows the causes of suffering,
> And knows that all sufferings
> Are utterly extinguished without remainder;
>
> And if one also truly knows
> The path that quells suffering,
> Then mental liberation is fulfilled,
> And wisdom liberation as well.
>
> One can transcend the mass of suffering,
> And ultimately attain liberation."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (393)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "When a good son, having right faith, leaves the household life to study the path, he should in all cases come to know the Dharma of the Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are knowing the noble truth of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the origin of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and knowing the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Therefore, bhikṣus, those who have not yet attained uninterrupted realization regarding the Four Noble Truths should diligently and skillfully cultivate uninterrupted realization. Regarding this passage, all the sūtras on the Four Noble Truths should be spoken in full."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

In this way, knowing, seeing, and uninterrupted realization should all be spoken. "Furthermore, when the three fetters are exhausted, one attains the fruit of stream-entry. In all cases one should know the Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are knowing the noble truth of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the origin of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and knowing the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering." Thus should one know, thus should one see, and uninterrupted realization likewise.

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

"When the three fetters are exhausted and greed, hatred, and delusion are attenuated, one attains the fruit of once-returning. All of this is because of truly knowing the Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are knowing the noble truth of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the origin of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and knowing the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering." Thus should one know, thus should one see, and thus uninterrupted realization; this is also spoken in this way.

"When the five lower fetters are exhausted, one attains parinirvāṇa as a non-returner who does not return to this world. All of this is through knowing the Four Noble Truths. What are the four? Knowing the noble truth of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the origin of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and knowing the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering." Thus knowing, thus seeing, and thus uninterrupted realization; this is also spoken in this way.

"When all outflows are exhausted, with the outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation through wisdom, one sees the Dharma and knows for oneself, declaring: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, and I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.' All of this is through knowing the Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are knowing the noble truth of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the origin of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and knowing the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering." Thus knowing, thus seeing, and thus uninterrupted realization; this is also spoken in this way.

"When one attains the path of a pratyekabuddha through realization, all of this is through knowing the Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are knowing the noble truth of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the origin of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and knowing the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering." Thus knowing, thus seeing, and thus uninterrupted realization; this is also spoken in this way.

"When one attains unsurpassed perfect awakening, all of this is through knowing the Four Noble Truths. What are the four? They are knowing the noble truth of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the origin of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and knowing the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering." Thus knowing, thus seeing, and thus uninterrupted realization; this is also spoken in this way.

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (394)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Just as when the sun rises, the sign of dawn appears first, likewise, for the complete ending of suffering, there is a preceding sign. What is it? Knowing the Four Noble Truths. What are the four? Knowing the noble truth of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the origin of suffering, knowing the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and knowing the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

In this way, knowing, seeing, and uninterrupted realization are also spoken in this way.

---

### (395)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If the sun and moon did not appear in the world, all the stars also would not appear in the world. Day and night, half-months, months, seasons, years, moments, and instants would all not be manifest. The world would always be in darkness, without illumination. There would only be the long night, with nothing but great darkness and suffering manifest in the world. If the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Awakened One does not appear in the world, the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering are not made manifest in the world. The world is blind and dark, without illumination. Thus during the long night, nothing but great darkness appears in the world.

"If the sun and moon appear in the world, the stars also appear. Day and night, half-months, months, seasons, years, moments, and instants all become manifest in the world. The long night is illuminated and brightness appears in the world. Likewise, when the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Awakened One appears in the world, the noble truth of suffering is made manifest in the world, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering are made manifest in the world. No more is there darkness; the long night is illuminated, and pure, unified wisdom appears in the world."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (396)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Just as when the sun rises and travels through space, it destroys all darkness and luminously shines forth, likewise, when a noble disciple's every phenomenon that is subject to arising has all ceased, free from dust and stains, the Dharma-eye arises. Together with uninterrupted realization, the three fetters are cut off: namely, the view of self-identity, attachment to precepts and practices, and doubt. When these three fetters are exhausted, one is called a stream-enterer, one who is not subject to falling into the evil destinies, who is certain of right awakening, and who will be reborn at most seven times among devas and humans before making an end of suffering. That noble disciple, even though grief and suffering may arise in between, listen: that noble disciple, free from sensual desires and unwholesome states, with applied thought and sustained thought, with rapture and happiness born of seclusion, dwells having attained the first dhyāna. I do not see that such a noble disciple has even a single dharma unbroken that could cause him to return to birth in this world. This is the great purpose of the noble disciple's attaining the Dharma-eye. Therefore, bhikṣus, those who have not yet attained uninterrupted realization regarding the Four Noble Truths should diligently and skillfully arouse supreme desire and diligently cultivate and practice."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (397)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "One should speak thus: 'If regarding the noble truth of suffering I have not yet attained uninterrupted realization, and regarding the noble truth of the origin of suffering and the noble truth of the cessation of suffering I have not yet attained uninterrupted realization, yet I claim that I shall attain uninterrupted realization of the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering,' this claim is not fitting. Why? Because there is no such possibility.

"If one has not yet attained uninterrupted realization regarding the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, and the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, yet desires uninterrupted realization of the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, there is no such possibility. It is like a person saying, 'I wish to take khadira leaves and bind them together to make a vessel, to carry water in it.' There is no such possibility. Why? Because there is no such possibility. Likewise, saying 'I have not yet attained uninterrupted realization regarding the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, and the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, yet I wish to attain uninterrupted realization of the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering,' there is no such possibility.

"If, however, someone says, 'After I have attained uninterrupted realization of the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, and the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, I shall further attain uninterrupted realization of the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering,' this is well spoken. Why? Because there is such a possibility.

"If one has attained uninterrupted realization regarding the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, and the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and then desires uninterrupted realization of the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, there is such a possibility. It is like someone saying, 'I shall take pure tāmala leaves or māluvā leaves and bind them together to carry water.' This is well spoken. Why? Because there is such a possibility. Likewise, saying 'After I have attained uninterrupted realization of the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, and the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, I wish to attain uninterrupted realization of the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering,' this is well spoken. Why? Because there is such a possibility. If one has attained uninterrupted realization regarding the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, and the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and then desires uninterrupted realization of the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, there is such a possibility."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (398)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Vārāṇasī, in the Deer Park at the Abode of Sages.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Just as a small cotton ball, or a small ball of kapok fiber, is placed at a crossroads, and when winds blow from the four directions, it follows the wind and goes in one direction. Likewise, if śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas do not truly know the noble truth of suffering, do not truly know the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, or the noble truth of the path leading to the
