# 雜阿含經
## Connected Discourses (Saṃyuktāgama)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Sixteen

### Miscellaneous Causes Section, Part Three, Chapter Four

---

### (407)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, a great number of bhikṣus gathered in the dining hall, thinking worldly thoughts and contemplating them.

At that time, the World-Honored One knew what the bhikṣus were thinking in their minds. He went to the dining hall, spread his seat and sat down, and told the bhikṣus: "You bhikṣus, be careful not to engage in worldly thoughts. Why is this? Worldly thoughts are not beneficial in meaning, not beneficial in the Dharma, not beneficial for the holy life, not conducive to wisdom, not conducive to awakening, and do not lead toward nirvāṇa. You should rightly contemplate: 'This is the noble truth of suffering, this is the noble truth of the origin of suffering, this is the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, this is the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering.' Why is this? Such contemplation is beneficial in meaning, beneficial in the Dharma, beneficial for the holy life, conducive to right wisdom, right awakening, and rightly directed toward nirvāṇa.

"In a past age, there was a man who left Rājagṛha and sat properly beside the Kukuṭṭhīra Pool, thinking worldly thoughts. While he was thinking, he saw four kinds of armies: elephant armies, horse armies, chariot armies, and infantry, innumerable and measureless, all entering into the hole of a single lotus root. Having seen this, he thought: 'I have gone mad and lost my senses. What does not exist in the world, I have now seen.' At that time, not far from the pool, there was a great assembly gathered in one place. The man went to the great assembly and said: 'Good people, I have now gone mad! I have now lost my senses! What does not exist in the world, I have now seen,' and he described it in full as above. At that time, the great assembly all said the man had gone mad and lost his senses, for what does not exist in the world, he had seen."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "However, that man was not mad and had not lost his senses. What he saw was real. Why is this? At that time, not far from the Kukuṭṭhīra Pool, the devas and asuras had raised four kinds of armies and were battling in the sky. At that time, the devas were victorious and the asura armies were defeated, and they retreated into a single lotus-root hole in that pool. Therefore, bhikṣus, be careful not to engage in worldly thoughts. Why is this? Worldly thoughts are not beneficial in meaning, not beneficial in the Dharma, not beneficial for the holy life, not conducive to wisdom, not conducive to awakening, and do not rightly lead toward nirvāṇa. You should contemplate the Four Noble Truths. What are the four? The noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (408)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a great number of bhikṣus gathered in the dining hall and engaged in such discussions: some said the world is eternal, some said the world is not eternal, the world is both eternal and not eternal, the world is neither eternal nor not eternal; the world is finite, the world is infinite, the world is both finite and infinite, the world is neither finite nor infinite; the life-force is the same as the body, the life-force is different from the body; the Tathāgata exists after death, the Tathāgata does not exist after death, the Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death, the Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death.

At that time, the World-Honored One, seated in meditation in a certain place, with his divine ear heard the sound of the bhikṣus' discussions in the dining hall. Having heard, he went to the dining hall, spread his seat before the great assembly and sat down, and told the bhikṣus: "You bhikṣus, having gathered in great number, what have you been discussing?"

At that time, the bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, we, a great number of bhikṣus, gathered in this dining hall and engaged in such discussions: some said it is eternal, some said it is not eternal," and so forth as described above in full.

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "You should not engage in such discussions. Why is this? Such discussions are not beneficial in meaning, not beneficial in the Dharma, not beneficial for the holy life, not conducive to wisdom, not conducive to right awakening, and do not rightly lead toward nirvāṇa. You bhikṣus should discuss thus: 'This is the noble truth of suffering, this is the noble truth of the origin of suffering, this is the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, this is the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering.' Why is this? Such discussions are beneficial in meaning, beneficial in the Dharma, beneficial for the holy life, conducive to right wisdom, right awakening, and rightly directed toward nirvāṇa. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (409)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, a great number of bhikṣus gathered in the dining hall. Some were occupied with thoughts of desire, some with thoughts of ill-will, some with thoughts of harmfulness.

At that time, the World-Honored One knew what the bhikṣus were thinking in their minds. He went to the dining hall, spread his sitting cloth before the assembly and sat down, and told the bhikṣus: "Do not give rise to thoughts of desire, do not give rise to thoughts of ill-will, do not give rise to thoughts of harmfulness. Why is this? These thoughts are not beneficial in meaning, not beneficial in the Dharma, not beneficial for the holy life, not conducive to wisdom, not conducive to right awakening, and do not lead toward nirvāṇa. You should give rise to contemplation of the noble truth of suffering, contemplation of the noble truth of the origin of suffering, contemplation of the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and contemplation of the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Why is this? Contemplation of these Four Noble Truths is beneficial in meaning, beneficial in the Dharma, beneficial for the holy life, conducive to right wisdom, right awakening, and directed toward nirvāṇa. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, you should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and with right wisdom and right mindfulness, energetically cultivate and train."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (410)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, and so forth as described above in full. The difference is: giving rise to thoughts about relatives, thoughts about countries and peoples, and thoughts about immortality, and so forth until: hearing what the Buddha had said, they rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (411)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, a great number of bhikṣus gathered in the dining hall and engaged in such discussions: some discussed affairs of kings, affairs of thieves, affairs of battles and wars, affairs of money and wealth, affairs of clothing and bedding, affairs of food and drink, affairs of men and women, affairs of worldly gossip, affairs of occupations, and affairs about things in the oceans.

At that time, the World-Honored One, while in meditative concentration, with his divine ear heard the sound of the bhikṣus' discussions. He immediately rose from his seat, went to the dining hall, spread his sitting cloth before the assembly and sat down, and told the bhikṣus: "You bhikṣus, having gathered in great number, what have you been discussing?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, we gathered here and some discussed affairs of kings," and so forth as described above in full.

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "You should not engage in such discussions, discussing affairs of kings, and so forth, which do not lead toward nirvāṇa. If you are to discuss, you should discuss: 'This is the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering.' Why is this? These Four Noble Truths are beneficial in meaning, beneficial in the Dharma, beneficial for the holy life, conducive to right wisdom, right awakening, and rightly directed toward nirvāṇa."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (412)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a great number of bhikṣus gathered in the dining hall and spoke thus: "I understand the Dharma and Vinaya; you do not. What I say is accomplished; what you say accords with reason. What you say is not accomplished and does not accord with reason. What should have been said first, you said last; what should have been said last, you said first." And they argued together, saying: "My arguments are superior; you are no match. If you can answer, then answer."

At that time, the World-Honored One, while in meditative concentration, with his divine ear heard the sound of the bhikṣus' arguments, and so forth as described above in full, up to: "Regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (413)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, a great number of bhikṣus gathered in the dining hall and engaged in such discussions: "Between King Prasenajit and King Bimbisāra, which one has greater power? Which one has greater wealth?"

At that time, the World-Honored One, while in meditative concentration, with his divine ear heard the sound of the bhikṣus' discussions. He immediately rose from his seat, went to the dining hall, spread his sitting cloth before the assembly and sat down, and asked the bhikṣus: "What have you been discussing?"

At that time, the bhikṣus reported the above matter in full to the World-Honored One.

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "What use is it for you to discuss which kings have greater power or greater wealth? You bhikṣus should not engage in such discussions. Why is this? This is not beneficial in meaning, not beneficial in the Dharma, not beneficial for the holy life, not conducive to wisdom, not conducive to right awakening, and does not lead toward nirvāṇa. You should discuss: 'This is the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering.' Why is this? These Four Noble Truths are beneficial in meaning, beneficial in the Dharma, beneficial for the holy life, conducive to right wisdom, right awakening, and rightly directed toward nirvāṇa. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (414)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, a great number of bhikṣus gathered in the dining hall and engaged in such discussions: "In your former lives, what kinds of work did you do? What were your crafts? How did you make a living?"

At that time, the World-Honored One, while in meditative concentration, with his divine ear heard the sound of the bhikṣus' discussions. He immediately rose from his seat, went to the dining hall, spread his sitting cloth before the assembly and sat down, and asked the bhikṣus: "What have you been discussing?"

At that time, the bhikṣus reported the above in full to the World-Honored One.

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "You bhikṣus should not engage in such talk about 'what was done in former lives.' Why is this? This is not beneficial in meaning, not beneficial in the Dharma, not beneficial for the holy life, not conducive to wisdom, not conducive to right awakening, and does not lead toward nirvāṇa. You bhikṣus should discuss together: 'This is the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering.' Why is this? This is beneficial in meaning, beneficial in the Dharma, beneficial for the holy life, conducive to right wisdom, right awakening, and rightly directed toward nirvāṇa. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (415)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, a great number of bhikṣus gathered in the dining hall and engaged in such discussions: "A certain donor provides coarse and rough food. Having eaten it, it has no flavor and no strength. It would be better for us to abandon that coarse food and go on alms round instead. Why is this? When bhikṣus go on alms round, they obtain good food, they see pleasant sights, they hear pleasant sounds, they become known by many people, and they also obtain robes, bedding, and medicines."

At that time, the World-Honored One, while in meditative concentration, with his divine ear heard the sound of the bhikṣus' discussions. He immediately went to the dining hall, and so forth as described above in full, up to: "rightly directed toward nirvāṇa."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (416)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Do you uphold the Four Noble Truths that I have taught?"

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, paid homage to the Buddha, joined his palms and said to the Buddha: "Indeed, the Four Noble Truths taught by the World-Honored One, I fully uphold them."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "How do you uphold the Four Noble Truths?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "The World-Honored One has said: 'This is the noble truth of suffering,' and I uphold it. 'This is the noble truth of the origin of suffering, this is the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, this is the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering.' Thus the World-Honored One has taught the Four Noble Truths, and I uphold them."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Good! Good! I taught the noble truth of suffering, and you truly uphold it. I taught the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, and you truly uphold them."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (417)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Do you uphold the Four Noble Truths that I have taught?"

At that time, a bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, bared his right shoulder, paid homage to the Buddha, joined his palms and said to the Buddha: "Indeed, the Four Noble Truths taught by the World-Honored One, I fully uphold them."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "How do you uphold the Four Noble Truths that I have taught?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "The World-Honored One has taught the noble truth of suffering, and I fully uphold it. It is in accordance with suchness, not apart from suchness, not different from suchness; it is true, real, certain, and not inverted. It is what the noble ones have realized as truth. This is called the noble truth of suffering. The World-Honored One has taught the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, which are in accordance with suchness, not apart from suchness, not different from suchness; they are true, real, certain, and not inverted. They are what the noble ones have realized as truth. These are the Four Noble Truths taught by the World-Honored One, and I fully uphold them."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Good! Good! You truly uphold the Four Noble Truths that I have taught: in accordance with suchness, not apart from suchness, not different from suchness; true, real, certain, and not inverted. This is called a bhikṣu who truly upholds my Four Noble Truths."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (418)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Do you uphold the Four Noble Truths that I have taught?"

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, paid homage to the Buddha, joined his palms and said to the Buddha: "Indeed, the Four Noble Truths taught by the World-Honored One, I fully uphold them. What are the four? The World-Honored One has taught the noble truth of suffering, and I fully uphold it. The noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, I fully uphold them."

The Buddha told that bhikṣu: "Good! Good! The Four Noble Truths as I have taught them, you fully uphold them. Bhikṣus, if any śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa were to say: 'The noble truth of suffering as taught by the śramaṇa Gautama, I shall abandon and establish another noble truth of suffering,' that would be mere words. If questioned, they would not know, and their doubts would only increase, because it is not within their range of experience. The noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering: 'I shall now abandon these and establish other Four Noble Truths,' they would have mere words. If questioned, they would not know, and their doubts would only increase, because it is not within their range of experience. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (419)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu has doubt about the Buddha, then he has doubt about the noble truth of suffering, doubt about the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. If he has doubt about the Dharma or the Sangha, then he has doubt about the noble truth of suffering, doubt about the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. If he has no doubt about the Buddha, then he has no doubt about the noble truth of suffering, no doubt about the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. If he has no doubt about the Dharma or the Sangha, then he has no doubt about the noble truth of suffering, no doubt about the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (420)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If any śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa has doubt about the noble truth of suffering, then he has doubt about the Buddha, and has doubt about the Dharma and the Sangha. If he has doubt about the origin, cessation, and path, then he has doubt about the Buddha, and has doubt about the Dharma and the Sangha. If he has no doubt about the noble truth of suffering, then he has no doubt about the Buddha, and no doubt about the Dharma and the Sangha. If he has no doubt about the noble truths of the origin, cessation, and path, then he has no doubt about the Buddha, and no doubt about the Dharma and the Sangha."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (421)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Let us go together to the deep, precipitous cliff."

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "So be it, World-Honored One!"

At that time, the World-Honored One, together with the great assembly, went to the deep, precipitous cliff, spread his seat and sat down. Having looked around at the deep, precipitous cliff, he told the bhikṣus: "This cliff is extremely deep and precipitous."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, paid homage to the Buddha, joined his palms and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, this is extremely deep and precipitous. But is there something even more deeply precipitous than this, even more fearsome?"

The Buddha, knowing his intention, told him: "Indeed so, bhikṣu. This is extremely deep and precipitous, but there is something even more deeply precipitous and fearsome than this, namely: those śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who do not know the noble truth of suffering as it really is, who do not know the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering as they really are. They delight in and are attached to the formations that are the root of birth. They delight in and are attached to the formations that are the root of old age, illness, death, grief, sorrow, pain, and distress. Having performed such actions, the actions of old age, illness, death, grief, sorrow, pain, and distress further increase, and they fall into the deep precipice of birth; they fall into the deep precipice of old age, illness, death, grief, sorrow, pain, and distress. Thus, bhikṣus, this is the great deep precipice, more precipitous than this one. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (422)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There is a great hot hell. If beings are born there, they are entirely and thoroughly blazing."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, paid homage to the Buddha, joined his palms and said to the Buddha: "As the World-Honored One has said, this is great heat. World-Honored One, is this the only great heat, or is there another great heat surpassing this, even more fearsome, with nothing beyond it?"

"Indeed so, bhikṣu. This is great heat, but there is yet another great heat surpassing this, even more fearsome, with nothing beyond it. What is this other great heat, even more fearsome, surpassing this? It is those śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who do not know the noble truth of suffering as it really is, who do not know the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering as they really are. Thus, and so forth, up to birth, old age, illness, death, grief, sorrow, pain, and distress, blazing with great heat. This is called, bhikṣus, the great heat of burning, even more fearsome, with nothing surpassing it. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (423)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There is a great dark hell. Those beings who are born there cannot see their own bodily parts."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, paid homage to the Buddha, joined his palms and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, this is great darkness. Is this the only great darkness, or is there yet another great darkness, even more fearsome, surpassing this?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Indeed so. There is yet another great darkness, even more fearsome, surpassing this, namely: those śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who do not know the Four Noble Truths as they really are, and so forth, up to falling into the great darkness of birth, old age, illness, death, grief, sorrow, pain, and distress. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (424)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "As the sun travels and illuminates all the worlds, up to a thousand suns and a thousand moons illuminating a thousand worlds, a thousand Mount Sumerus, a thousand Pūrvavidehas, a thousand Jambudvīpas, a thousand Aparagodānīyas, a thousand Uttarakurus, a thousand realms of the Four Great Kings, a thousand Trāyastriṃśa Heavens, a thousand Yāma Heavens, a thousand Tuṣita Heavens, a thousand Nirmāṇarati Heavens, a thousand Paranirmitavaśavartin Heavens, and a thousand Brahmā Heavens: this is called a minor thousandfold world. In the interstices between these thousand worlds, there is darkness where the light of the sun and moon, though possessing great power and virtue, does not illuminate. Beings born in those places cannot see their own bodily parts."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, paid homage to the Buddha, joined his palms and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, as the World-Honored One has described, this is great darkness. But is there yet another great darkness surpassing this?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "There is a great darkness surpassing this, namely: those śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who do not know the noble truth of suffering as it really is, and so forth, up to falling into the great darkness of birth, old age, illness, death, grief, sorrow, pain, and distress. This is called, bhikṣus, the great darkness that surpasses the darkness in the interstices between worlds. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (425)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "When the minor thousandfold world is multiplied to a thousand, this is called a middle thousandfold world. In the interstices of this middle thousandfold world, there is darkness," as described above, and so forth, up to: "Regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (426)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "When the middle thousandfold world is multiplied to a thousand, this is called the great trichiliocosm. In the interstices of this world, there are places of darkness. Though the sun and moon travel and illuminate the world universally, they do not illuminate those places. And so forth, up to falling into the great darkness of birth, old age, illness, death, grief, sorrow, pain, and distress. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (427)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach the Four Noble Truths. Listen carefully! Listen carefully! Reflect well upon it. What are the four? They are the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. These are called the Four Noble Truths."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As "I shall teach," so also "there are," and "you should know," are likewise as stated above.

---

### (428)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "You should diligently practice dhyāna contemplation, rightly apply skillful means, and inwardly still your minds. Why is this? When a bhikṣu practices dhyāna contemplation and inwardly stills his mind, having accomplished this, things are manifested as they really are. How are they manifested as they really are? The noble truth of suffering is manifested as it really is; the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering are manifested as they really are."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (429)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "You should cultivate immeasurable samādhi, with a focused mind and right mindfulness. Why is this? When one has cultivated immeasurable samādhi with a focused mind and right mindfulness, things are manifested as they really are. How are they manifested as they really are? The noble truth of suffering is manifested as it really is; the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering are manifested as they really are."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (430)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "It is like a person who throws a stick into the air: it immediately falls back down, landing either on its base, on its middle, or on its top. In the same way, śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who do not know the noble truth of suffering as it really is, who do not know the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering as they really are: you should know that such śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas will fall either into the hells, into the animal realm, or into the hungry ghost realm. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (431)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "It is like a person who throws a stick into the air: it will certainly fall back down, either landing on clean ground or landing on unclean ground. In the same way, śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who do not know the noble truth of suffering as it really is, who do not know the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering as they really are: because they do not know as it really is, they are either reborn in good destinies or reborn in evil destinies. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (432)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "It is like a wheel with five sections joined together in succession, which a strong man sets spinning rapidly. In the same way, śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who do not know the noble truth of suffering as it really is, who do not know the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering as they really are: they revolve through the five destinies, spinning rapidly. They fall into the hells, or fall into the animal realm, or fall into the hungry ghost realm, or into the human or heavenly realms, and then fall back into the evil destinies, revolving through the long night. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (433)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "What the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, supremely teaches as Dharma is the Four Noble Truths: revealing, establishing, setting up, distinguishing, explaining, manifesting, and making clear. What are the four? They are the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (434)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "What constitutes being wise? Is it knowing the noble truth of suffering as it really is, knowing the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering as they really are, or not knowing them?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "As we understand what the World-Honored One has said, one who knows the Four Noble Truths as they really are, this constitutes being wise."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Good! Good! One who knows the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering as they really are, that one is truly wise. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (435)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the householder Sudatta went to where the Buddha was, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, are these Four Noble Truths penetrated gradually and sequentially, or are they penetrated all at once?"

The Buddha told the householder: "These Four Noble Truths are penetrated gradually and sequentially, not all at once."

The Buddha told the householder: "If someone were to say that without having achieved penetrative insight into the noble truth of suffering, they could achieve penetrative insight into the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, such a statement would not be valid. Why is this? If one has not achieved penetrative insight into the noble truth of suffering and yet wishes to achieve penetrative insight into the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, that is impossible.

"It is like a person who takes two thin tree leaves, joins them together to make a vessel, and tries to carry water in it: that is impossible. In the same way, without having achieved penetrative insight into the noble truth of suffering, to wish to achieve penetrative insight into the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering is impossible.

"It is like a person who takes lotus leaves and joins them together to make a vessel, and carries water while traveling: that is possible. In the same way, householder, having achieved penetrative insight into the noble truth of suffering, to then wish to achieve penetrative insight into the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering: that is possible. Therefore, householder, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (436)

As with the question of the householder Sudatta, a certain bhikṣu also asked, and it was explained in the same way. Only the simile differs: "It is like four stairways for ascending to a palace hall. If someone were to say that without climbing the first stairway, they could climb the second, third, and fourth stairways to ascend to the palace hall, that would be impossible. Why is this? One must first ascend the first stairway, and then sequentially climb the second, third, and fourth stairways to ascend to the palace hall. In the same way, bhikṣu, without having achieved penetrative insight into the noble truth of suffering, to wish to achieve penetrative insight into the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering is impossible.

"It is like, bhikṣu, if someone were to say: 'By four stairways one ascends to a palace hall. One must first ascend the first stairway, and then sequentially climb the second, third, and fourth stairways to ascend to the palace hall.' This statement should be made. Why is this? Because one must first ascend the first stairway, and then sequentially climb the second, third, and fourth stairways to ascend to the palace hall, that is possible. In the same way, bhikṣu, if one says that having achieved penetrative insight into the noble truth of suffering, one then sequentially achieves penetrative insight into the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, this statement should be made. Why is this? Having achieved penetrative insight into the noble truth of suffering, to then sequentially achieve penetrative insight into the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering is possible."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (437)

As with the question of the certain bhikṣu, what Ānanda asked was also explained in the same way. Only the simile differs.

The Buddha told Ānanda: "It is like a ladder with four rungs for ascending to a palace hall. If someone were to say that without climbing the first rung, they could climb the second, third, and fourth rungs to ascend to the palace hall, that would be impossible. In the same way, Ānanda, without having achieved penetrative insight into the noble truth of suffering, to wish to achieve penetrative insight into the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, this statement should not be made. Why is this? Without having achieved penetrative insight into the noble truth of suffering, to achieve penetrative insight into the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering is impossible.

"It is like, Ānanda, ascending to a palace hall by a ladder with four rungs. If someone were to say that one must first climb the first rung, and then sequentially climb the second, third, and fourth rungs to ascend to the palace hall, this statement should be made. Why is this? Because one must first climb the first rung, and then sequentially climb the second, third, and fourth rungs to ascend to the palace hall, that is possible. In the same way, Ānanda, having achieved penetrative insight into the noble truth of suffering, to then sequentially achieve penetrative insight into the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering: that is possible."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (438)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Suppose all the grass and trees on the great earth were taken and made into skewers, and used to pierce all the aquatic creatures in the great ocean. Could all of them be pierced?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "They could not, World-Honored One. Why is this? The creatures in the great ocean are of various forms and types. Some are too fine and small to be pierced, and some are extremely large and cannot be pierced."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Indeed so, indeed so. The realm of sentient beings is innumerable and immeasurable. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (439)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One held some soil and stones in his hand and asked the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is the soil and stones in my hand more, or is the soil and stones on the great Snowy Mountains more?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "The soil and stones in the World-Honored One's hand are exceedingly few. The soil and stones of the Snowy Mountains are extremely abundant, immeasurable, hundreds of billions, beyond calculation or comparison."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Those beings who know the noble truth of suffering as it really is, who know the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering as they really are, are like the soil and stones I hold in my hand. Those beings who do not know the noble truth of suffering as it really is, who do not know the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering as they really are, are like the soil and stones of the Snowy Mountains, immeasurable in number. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (440)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Suppose there is a lake, fifty yojanas in depth and breadth, filled to the brim with water. If a man were to dip a hair, a strand of fur, or a fingertip into that lake, up to two or three times: what do you think, bhikṣus? Would the water taken by that man be more, or would the water in the lake be more?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "The water taken by that man on a hair, strand of fur, or fingertip, two or three times, would be exceedingly little. The great water of that lake is immeasurable in quantity, beyond calculation or comparison."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "The great lake water is extremely abundant and immeasurable. In the same way, a learned noble disciple who possesses the vision of truth, who has attained the fruit of the noble path, who has cut off the roots of suffering, like cutting off the head of a tāla tree, so that in future lives it becomes a dharma of non-arising: the suffering remaining is like the water on the hair, fur, or fingertip of that man. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the great lake water simile, so also with Sāla-daṭṭhaka, the Ganges, the Yamunā, the Sarāyu, the Irāvatī, the Mahī, and the four great oceans: the similes are also as stated above.

---

### (441)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One took in his hand a lump of earth the size of a myrobalan fruit and told the bhikṣus: "What do you think, bhikṣus? Is this lump of earth in my hand more, or is the earth and stone of the great Snowy Mountains more?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "The lump of earth in the World-Honored One's hand is exceedingly little. The earth and stone of the king of Snowy Mountains is extremely abundant, hundreds of billions of nayutas, beyond calculation or comparison."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Like the lump of earth I hold, so are the beings who know the noble truth of suffering as it really is, who know the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering as they really are: they are just like this. Like the earth and stone of the great king of Snowy Mountains, so are the beings who do not know the noble truth of suffering as it really is, who do not know the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering as they really are: they are also just like this. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means, arouse supreme aspiration, and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the king of Snowy Mountains, so also with Mount Nimindara, Mount Vinataka, Mount Aśvakarṇa, Mount Sudarśana, Mount Khadiraka, Mount Iṣādhara, Mount Yugaṃdhara, Mount Sumeru the king of mountains, and the earth and stone of the great land: they are also just like this.

As with the myrobalan fruit, so also with an āmalaka fruit, a bhadra fruit, a karaka fruit, a bean, and even a garlic seed as similes: they are also just like this.

---

### (442)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One scooped up some earth on his fingernail and told the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is the earth on my fingernail more, or is the earth of this great land more?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "The earth on the World-Honored One's fingernail is exceedingly little. The earth of this great land is extremely abundant and immeasurable, beyond calculation or comparison."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Like the earth on a fingernail, so are those beings whose forms are visible: they are also just like this. Those whose forms are subtle and invisible are like the earth of the great land. Therefore, bhikṣus, regarding the Four Noble Truths, those who have not yet attained penetrative insight should diligently apply skillful means and train in penetrative insight."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with land, so also with water: it is the same.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are beings on the human path: they are also just like this.

Like the earth of the great land, so are those who are non-human: it is the same.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are those born in the central lands: it is the same.

Like the earth of the great land, so are those born in the borderlands: it is the same.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are those who possess the noble eye of wisdom: they are also just like this.

Like the earth of the great land, so are those who do not possess the noble eye of wisdom: it is the same.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are beings who know this Dharma and Vinaya: they are also just like this.

Like the earth of the great land, so are beings who do not know the Dharma and Vinaya: it is the same. As with "know," so also "fully know," "universally know," "right thought," "right awakening," "right understanding," and "penetrative insight into the Dharma" are also the same.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are beings who know they have a father and mother: it is the same.

Like the earth of the great land, so are beings who do not know they have a father and mother: it is the same.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are those who know there are śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, who know the elders of the household, who do what should be done, who perform meritorious deeds, who in this world and the next fear wrongdoing, practice generosity, receive the upoṣadha, and maintain the precepts: it is the same.

Like the earth of the great land, so are those who do not know there are śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, who do not know the elders of the household, who do not do what should be done, who do not perform meritorious deeds, who in this world and the next do not fear wrongdoing, do not practice generosity, do not receive the upoṣadha, and do not maintain the precepts: it is also stated in the same way.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are beings who do not kill, do not steal, do not engage in sexual misconduct, do not speak falsely, do not engage in divisive speech, do not speak harshly, and do not engage in idle talk: it is the same.

Like the earth of the great land, so are beings who do not maintain these precepts: it is the same. Likewise, being free from greed, ill-will, and wrong views, and not being free from greed, ill-will, and wrong views, are also stated in the same way.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are those who do not kill, do not steal, do not engage in sexual misconduct, do not speak falsely, and do not drink intoxicants.

Like the earth of the great land, so are those who do not maintain the five precepts: it is the same.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are beings who maintain the eight precepts: it is also the same.

Like the earth of the great land, so are beings who do not maintain the eight precepts: it is the same.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are beings who maintain the ten wholesome actions: it is also the same.

Like the earth of the great land, so are beings who do not maintain the ten wholesome actions: it is also the same.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are beings who, having died in the hells, are reborn among humans: it is also the same.

Like the earth of the great land, so are beings who, having died in the hells, are reborn again in the hells: it is also the same. As with the hells, so also with the animal realm and the hungry ghost realm: it is the same.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are beings who, having died in the hells, are reborn in the heavens: it is also the same.

Like the earth of the great land, so are beings who, having died in the hells, are reborn again in the hells: it is also the same. As with the hells, so also with the animal realm and the hungry ghost realm: it is the same.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are beings who, having passed away from the human realm, are reborn again in the human realm: it is also the same.

Like the earth of the great land, so are those beings who, having passed away from the human realm, are reborn in the hells: it is also the same. As with the hells, so also with the animal realm and the hungry ghost realm: it is the same.

Like the earth on a fingernail, so are those beings who, having died in the heavens, are reborn again in the heavens: it is also the same.

Like the earth of the great land, so are those beings who, having passed away from the heavens, are reborn in the hells: it is also the same. As with the hells, so also with the animal realm and the hungry ghost realm: it is also the same.

---

### (443)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Before I had heard the Dharma, I attained right contemplation of the noble truth of suffering, and right view had arisen. Right contemplation of the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering: right view had arisen."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As "has already arisen," so also "now arises" and "will arise."

As "arising," so also "developed," "practiced," "closely cultivated," "extensively cultivated," "directly experienced," and "realized" are also the same.

---

### (444)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Suppose there is an eye-medicine pill, one yojana in depth and breadth. If a man were to take this medicine pill and place it world by world, he could quickly exhaust it, yet the boundaries of those worlds could not be found. You should know that the realms are innumerable. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should skillfully train in the realms, skillfully train in the various kinds of realms. You should train in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (445)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Beings always abide together with their element and are conjoined with their element. How do beings always abide together with their element? When beings generate unwholesome mental states, they abide together with the unwholesome element. When they generate wholesome mental states, they abide together with the wholesome element. When they generate superior mental states, they abide together with the superior element. When they generate inferior mental states, they abide together with the inferior element. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train in this way: skillfully distinguishing the various kinds of elements."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (446)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus as described above in full. The difference is that he then spoke this verse:

> "Because of constant association, there is constant arising;
> when separated, arising is cut off.
> Like a person grasping a small piece of wood
> who enters the great ocean,
> both the person and the wood sink together;
> so it is with indolence.
> One should abandon indolence
> and feeble effort.
> The noble ones are not indolent;
> they dwell securely in seclusion.
> With diligent, energetic dhyāna,
> they cross beyond the stream of birth and death.
> As lacquer finds its base,
> as fire blazes with the wind,
> as conch shell and milk are the same color,
> beings abide together with their element.
> Similar ones join and harmonize together,
> and thus they increase."

---

### (447)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Beings always abide together with their element and are conjoined with their element. How do they abide together with their element? When beings generate unwholesome mental states, they abide together with the unwholesome element. When they generate wholesome mental states, they abide together with the wholesome element. When they generate inferior mental states, they abide together with the inferior element. When they generate superior mental states, they abide together with the superior element.

"At that time, the Venerable Kauṇḍinya was walking in meditation nearby with many bhikṣus. They were all senior monks, learned and of great virtue, who had gone forth long ago and had fully cultivated the holy life.

"Furthermore, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa was walking in meditation nearby with many bhikṣus. They were all of few desires, content, practitioners of the dhūta ascetic practices, and did not store up surplus.

"The Venerable Śāriputra was walking in meditation nearby with many bhikṣus. They were all of great wisdom and eloquence.

"At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana was walking in meditation nearby with many bhikṣus. They were all endowed with great supernatural powers.

"At that time, Aniruddha was walking in meditation nearby with many bhikṣus. They were all endowed with the clear and penetrating divine eye.

"At that time, the Venerable Śroṇa-koṭiviṃśa was walking in meditation nearby with many bhikṣus. They were all courageous and energetic, devoted to diligent practice.

"At that time, the Venerable Dravya was walking in meditation nearby with many bhikṣus. They were all able to prepare offerings for the great assembly.

"At that time, the Venerable Upāli was walking in meditation nearby with many bhikṣus. They were all thoroughly versed in the Vinaya practice.

"At that time, the Venerable Pūrṇa was walking in meditation nearby with many bhikṣus. They were all eloquent and skilled in teaching the Dharma.

"At that time, the Venerable Kātyāyana was walking in meditation nearby with many bhikṣus. They

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Seventeen

### Section on Miscellaneous Causes, Chapter Three, Part Five

---

### (456)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There is the light element, the pure element, the element of the base of infinite space, the element of the base of infinite consciousness, the element of the base of nothingness, the element of the base of neither perception nor non-perception, and the element of cessation."

Then a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, bowed his head to the Buddha's feet, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! That light element, pure element, element of the base of infinite space, element of the base of infinite consciousness, element of the base of nothingness, element of the base of neither perception nor non-perception, and element of cessation: by what conditions can these various elements be known?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "The light element can be known by reference to darkness. The pure element can be known by reference to impurity. The element of the base of infinite space can be known by reference to form. The element of the base of infinite consciousness can be known by reference to what is internal. The element of the base of nothingness can be known by reference to what exists. The element of the base of neither perception nor non-perception can be known by reference to the foremost existence. The element of cessation can be known by reference to the existing body."

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! That light element, up to the element of cessation: by what meditative attainment are they obtained?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "The light element, the pure element, the element of the base of infinite space, the element of the base of infinite consciousness, and the element of the base of nothingness: these various elements are obtained through meditative attainment of their own respective practices. The element of the base of neither perception nor non-perception is obtained through meditative attainment in the foremost existence. The element of cessation is obtained through meditative attainment of the cessation of the existing body."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (457)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at the Eastern Park, in the Hall of Mṛgāra's Mother.

At that time, in the afternoon, the World-Honored One emerged from meditation and in the shade of the hall spread a seat. Sitting before the great assembly, he spoke an udāna verse and addressed the bhikṣus: "Conditioned by elements, speech arises, not without elements. Conditioned by elements, views arise, not without elements. Conditioned by elements, perceptions arise, not without elements. Conditioned by inferior elements, I say there arise inferior speech, inferior views, inferior perceptions, inferior thoughts, inferior desires, inferior aspirations, inferior persons, inferior actions, inferior designations, inferior establishments, inferior divisions, inferior manifestations, and inferior rebirths. Likewise for the middling. Likewise for the superior elements: conditioned by superior elements, I say there arise superior speech, superior views, superior perceptions, superior thoughts, superior aspirations, superior persons, superior actions, superior designations, superior establishments, superior divisions, superior manifestations, and superior rebirths."

At that time, the bhikṣu Vakkali was standing behind the Buddha, fanning the Buddha. He addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! If someone gives rise to a non-samyak-saṃbuddha view regarding the Samyak-saṃbuddha, does that view also arise conditioned by an element?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Regarding the Samyak-saṃbuddha, if one gives rise to a non-samyak-saṃbuddha view, that also arises conditioned by an element, not without an element. Why is this? The element of the ordinary person is the element of ignorance. As I previously said, conditioned by inferior elements there arise inferior speech, inferior views, up to inferior rebirths. Conditioned by middling and superior elements, there arise superior speech, superior views, up to superior rebirths."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (458)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are causes for the arising of sensual thought, not without causes. There are causes for the arising of thoughts of ill-will and thoughts of harm, not without causes.

"How do causes give rise to sensual thought? It is by being conditioned by the sensual element. Conditioned by the sensual element, sensual thought arises, sensual desire, sensual intention, sensual fever, and sensual seeking. When the foolish, ignorant ordinary person gives rise to sensual seeking, this being gives rise to wrongness in three areas, namely body, speech, and mind. Due to such wrong conditions, one dwells in suffering in the present life, with suffering, with obstruction, with affliction, with fever, and at the breaking up of the body after death, one is reborn in a bad destination. This is called the cause and condition giving rise to sensual thought.

"How do causes and conditions give rise to thoughts of ill-will and thoughts of harm? It is by the element of harm. Conditioned by the element of harm, thoughts of harm arise, desire for harm, intention of harm, fever of harm, and seeking of harm. When the foolish, ignorant ordinary person gives rise to seeking of harm, this being gives rise to wrongness in three areas, namely body, speech, and mind. Having given rise to wrongness in these three areas as conditions, one dwells in suffering in the present life, with suffering, with obstruction, with affliction, with fever, and at the breaking up of the body after death, one is reborn in a bad destination. This is called the cause and condition giving rise to thoughts of harm.

"Bhikṣus! If śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas abide settled in such arising, giving rise to dangerous thoughts, not seeking to abandon them, not being aware of them, not expelling them, then in the present life they dwell in suffering, with suffering, with obstruction, with affliction, with fever, and at the breaking up of the body after death, they are reborn in a bad destination. It is like a city or village not far from a wilderness where a great fire suddenly breaks out, and there is no one with the power to extinguish it. One should know that all the creatures in that wilderness will be harmed by the fire. Likewise, those śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who abide settled in such arising, giving rise to dangerous thoughts, at the breaking up of the body after death are reborn in a bad destination.

"Bhikṣus! There are causes for the arising of thoughts of renunciation, not without causes. How are there causes for the arising of thoughts of renunciation? It is the element of renunciation. Conditioned by the element of renunciation, thoughts of renunciation arise, desire for renunciation, intention of renunciation, fever of renunciation, and seeking of renunciation. When that wise person engages in seeking renunciation, the being gives rise to rightness in three areas, namely body, speech, and mind. Having given rise to rightness through such conditions, one dwells in happiness in the present life, without suffering, without obstruction, without affliction, without fever, and at the breaking up of the body after death, one is reborn in a good destination. This is called the cause and condition giving rise to thoughts of renunciation.

"How do causes and conditions give rise to thoughts of non-ill-will and non-harm? It is the element of non-harm. Conditioned by the element of non-harm, thoughts of non-harm arise, desire for non-harm, intention of non-harm, fever of non-harm, and seeking of non-harm. When that wise person engages in seeking non-harm, the being is right in three areas, namely body, speech, and mind. Having given rise to such right conditions, one dwells in happiness in the present life, without suffering, without obstruction, without affliction, without fever, and at the breaking up of the body after death, one is reborn in a good destination. This is called the cause and condition giving rise to thoughts of non-harm.

"If śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas abide settled in such arising, giving rise to thoughts of non-harm, not abandoning them, not being aware of them, not expelling them, they dwell in happiness in the present life, without suffering, without obstruction, without affliction, without fever, and at the breaking up of the body after death, they are reborn in a good destination. It is like a city or village beside which there is a wilderness where a great fire suddenly breaks out, and there is a person capable of extinguishing the fire with hands and feet. One should know that all those beings dependent on grass and trees will not be harmed. Likewise, those śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who abide settled in such arising, giving rise to right thoughts, not abandoning them, not being aware of them, not expelling them, dwell in happiness in the present life, without suffering, without obstruction, without affliction, without fever, and at the breaking up of the body after death, are reborn in a good destination."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (459)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a brāhmaṇa came to visit the Buddha. Having exchanged courteous greetings face to face with the World-Honored One, he stood to one side and addressed the Buddha: "Beings are neither self-made nor made by another?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "One who holds such a theory, I do not meet with. You have come here yourself, and yet you say they are neither self-made nor made by another?"

The brāhmaṇa said: "How is it, Gautama? Are beings self-made or made by another?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "I will now ask you; answer as you wish. Brāhmaṇa, what do you think? Is there an element of effort in beings that causes beings to know how to make effort?"

The brāhmaṇa said: "Gautama, there is an element of effort in beings that causes beings to know how to make effort."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "If there is an element of effort that causes beings to know there is effort, then this means beings are self-made, and this means they are made by another. Brāhmaṇa, what do you think? Is there in beings an element of stability, an element of firmness, an element of emergence, and an element of action, causing those beings to know there is action?"

The brāhmaṇa addressed the Buddha: "There is in beings an element of stability, an element of firmness, an element of emergence, and an element of action, causing beings to know there is action."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "If there is an element of stability, an element of firmness, an element of emergence, and an element of action, causing beings to know there is action, then this means beings are self-made, and this means they are made by another."

The brāhmaṇa addressed the Buddha: "There is self-making by beings, and there is making by another. Gautama, I have many worldly affairs; I must now take my leave."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "You have many worldly affairs; you may go when you see fit."

Then that brāhmaṇa, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and approved, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (460)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, the householder Ghoṣira went to visit Venerable Ānanda, bowed at Venerable Ānanda's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed Venerable Ānanda: "It is said, 'various elements.' How are there various elements?"

Venerable Ānanda told the householder Ghoṣira: "The eye element is different, the form element is different, and there is a place of joy. From these two conditions consciousness arises, and the meeting of the three gives rise to contact. Furthermore, joy-contact gives rise as a condition to pleasant feeling. Likewise for the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind with their respective objects, it should be stated in the same way.

"Furthermore, householder, there is a different eye element, a different form element, and a place of sorrow. From these two conditions consciousness arises, and the meeting of the three gives rise to painful contact. That painful contact gives rise as a condition to painful feeling. Likewise for the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind with their respective objects, it should be stated in the same way.

"Furthermore, householder, there is a different eye element, a different form element, and a place of equanimity. From these two conditions consciousness arises, and the meeting of the three gives rise to neither-painful-nor-pleasant contact. Neither-painful-nor-pleasant contact gives rise as a condition to neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. Likewise for the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind with their respective objects, it should be stated in the same way."

At that time, the householder Ghoṣira, hearing what Venerable Ānanda had said, rejoiced and approved, bowed at his feet, and departed.

---

### (461)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, the householder Ghoṣira went to visit Venerable Ānanda, bowed his head at his feet, and sat to one side. He addressed Venerable Ānanda: "It is said, 'various elements.' How are there various elements?"

Venerable Ānanda told the householder Ghoṣira: "There are three elements. What are the three? They are the desire element, the form element, and the formless element."

At that time, Venerable Ānanda then spoke a verse:

> "Clearly understanding the desire element,
> and likewise the form element,
> abandoning all that has remainder,
> one attains the remainderless cessation and stillness.
> Regarding the elements conjoined with the body,
> having forever exhausted them without remainder and realized this,
> the Samyak-saṃbuddha has spoken
> the sorrowless, stainless verse."

After Venerable Ānanda had spoken this sūtra, the householder Ghoṣira rejoiced and approved, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (462)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, the householder Ghoṣira went to visit Venerable Ānanda, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed Venerable Ānanda: "It is said, 'various elements.' How are they called various elements?"

Venerable Ānanda told the householder Ghoṣira: "There are three elements: the form element, the formless element, and the cessation element. These are called the three elements." He then spoke a verse:

> "Those beings in the form element,
> and those abiding in the formless element,
> who do not know the cessation element,
> return again to undergo further existences.
> If one cuts off the form element,
> and does not abide in the formless element,
> with the mind liberated in the cessation element,
> one forever leaves birth and death."

After Venerable Ānanda had spoken this sūtra, the householder Ghoṣira rejoiced and approved, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (463)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, the householder Ghoṣira went to visit Venerable Ānanda, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed Venerable Ānanda: "It is said, 'various elements.' How are there various elements?"

Venerable Ānanda answered the householder Ghoṣira: "There are three kinds of emergence elements. What are the three? Emergence from the desire element to the form element, emergence from the form element to the formless element, and the cessation element in which all formations and all perceptions cease.
