These are called the three emergence elements." He then spoke a verse:

> "Knowing emergence from the desire element,
> transcending the form element,
> all formations being stilled,
> one diligently cultivates right effort.
> Having cut off all craving,
> all formations being completely extinguished,
> knowing all that has remainder,
> one does not return again to existence."

After Venerable Ānanda had spoken this sūtra, the householder Ghoṣira rejoiced and approved, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (464)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, Venerable Ānanda went to visit a certain senior elder bhikṣu. Having gone, he respectfully greeted and inquired after him. After inquiring, he withdrew and sat to one side. He asked the senior elder bhikṣu: "If a bhikṣu is in an empty place, under a tree, or in a quiet room, engaged in contemplation, with what dharmas should he apply himself with focused concentration?"

The senior elder answered: "Venerable Ānanda! One who contemplates in an empty place, under a tree, or in a quiet room should apply himself with focused concentration on two dharmas, namely calm (śamatha) and insight (vipaśyanā)."

Venerable Ānanda further asked the senior elder: "If one cultivates calm and cultivates it extensively, what is accomplished? If one cultivates insight and cultivates it extensively, what is accomplished?"

The senior elder answered: "Venerable Ānanda! Cultivating calm ultimately accomplishes insight. Cultivating insight also accomplishes calm. When a noble disciple cultivates both calm and insight together, he attains the various elements of liberation."

Ānanda further asked the senior elder: "What are the various elements of liberation?"

The senior elder answered: "Venerable Ānanda! The element of cutting off, the element of dispassion, and the element of cessation: these are called the various elements of liberation."

Venerable Ānanda further asked the senior elder: "What is the element of cutting off, up to the element of cessation?"

The senior elder answered: "Venerable Ānanda! Cutting off all formations is called the element of cutting off. Cutting off and removing craving and desire is the element of dispassion. The cessation of all formations is called the element of cessation."

At that time, Venerable Ānanda, hearing what the senior elder had said, rejoiced and approved. He then went to visit five hundred bhikṣus, respectfully greeted and inquired after them, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the five hundred bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu is in an empty place, under a tree, or in a quiet room, engaged in contemplation, with what dharmas should he apply himself with focused concentration?"

At that time, the five hundred bhikṣus answered Venerable Ānanda: "One should apply oneself with focused concentration on two dharmas, up to the element of cessation, as the senior elder has stated."

Then Venerable Ānanda, hearing what the five hundred bhikṣus had said, rejoiced and approved. He went to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! If a bhikṣu is in an empty place, under a tree, or in a quiet room, engaged in contemplation, with what dharmas should he apply himself with focused concentration?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "If a bhikṣu is in an empty place, under a tree, or in a quiet room, engaged in contemplation, he should apply himself with focused concentration on two dharmas, up to the element of cessation, as the five hundred bhikṣus have stated."

Then Venerable Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "How wonderful, World-Honored One! The great teacher and his disciples all share the same Dharma, the same phrases, the same meaning, and the same flavor. I went to visit the senior elder and asked about this meaning, and he answered me with this meaning, these phrases, and this flavor, just as the World-Honored One has now spoken. I then went to visit the five hundred bhikṣus, and they also asked and answered with this meaning, these phrases, and this flavor, just as the World-Honored One has now spoken. Therefore, one should know that the teacher and disciples all share the same Dharma, the same meaning, the same phrases, and the same flavor."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Do you know what kind of bhikṣu that senior elder is?"

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "I do not know, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "That senior elder is an arhat whose defilements are exhausted, who has laid down the heavy burden, whose mind is well liberated through right knowledge. Those five hundred bhikṣus are all likewise."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Venerable Ānanda, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (465)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, Venerable Rāhula went to visit the World-Honored One, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! How should one know and how should one see, so that regarding this consciousness-body of mine and all external signs, there is no view of self, no view of what belongs to self, and no bondage of the tendency to conceit?"

The Buddha told Rāhula: "Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it to you. Rāhula! If a bhikṣu, regarding all earth elements, whether past, future, or present, whether internal or external, whether coarse or fine, whether beautiful or ugly, whether far or near, knows all of it as it truly is: 'This is not mine, this is not different from me, they do not exist within each other.' Likewise for the water element, fire element, wind element, space element, and consciousness element.

"Rāhula! When a bhikṣu knows thus and sees thus, regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, there is no view of self, no view of what belongs to self, and no bondage of the tendency to conceit. Rāhula! If a bhikṣu, regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, has no view of self, no view of what belongs to self, and no bondage of the tendency to conceit, this is called cutting off craving, turning away from the fetters, rightly penetrating conceit without interval, and reaching the final end of suffering."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Venerable Rāhula, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (466)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, Venerable Rāhula went to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! How should one know and how should one see, so that regarding this consciousness-body of mine and all external signs, there is no view of self, no view of what belongs to self, and no bondage of the tendency to conceit?"

The Buddha told Rāhula: "There are three feelings: painful feeling, pleasant feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. What is the cause, what is the accumulation, what is the arising, and what is the production of these three feelings? These three feelings have contact as their cause, contact as their accumulation, contact as their arising, and contact as their production. Each respective contact is the cause of each respective feeling arising. If each respective contact ceases, each respective feeling also ceases, stops, cools, and disappears. Knowing thus and seeing thus, regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, one attains the absence of any view of self, view of what belongs to self, or bondage of the tendency to conceit."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Venerable Rāhula, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (467)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, Venerable Rāhula went to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! How should one know and how should one see, so that regarding this consciousness-body of mine and all external signs, there is no view of self, no view of what belongs to self, and no bondage of the tendency to conceit?"

The Buddha told Rāhula: "There are three feelings: painful feeling, pleasant feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. Contemplating pleasant feeling, one should generate the perception of suffering. Contemplating painful feeling, one should generate the perception of a sword-thrust. Contemplating neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, one should generate the perception of impermanence. If that bhikṣu contemplates pleasant feeling with the perception of suffering, contemplates painful feeling with the perception of a sword-thrust, and contemplates neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling with the perception of impermanence and cessation, this is called right view."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke a verse:

> "Contemplate pleasure with the perception of suffering,
> painful feeling as a sword-thrust,
> regarding neither-painful-nor-pleasant,
> cultivate the perception of impermanence and cessation.
> This is indeed a bhikṣu
> who has accomplished right view;
> the path of tranquil happiness,
> abiding at the final boundary,
> forever free from all afflictions,
> having crushed the host of Māra."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Venerable Rāhula, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (468)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, Venerable Rāhula went to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! How should one know and how should one see, so that regarding this consciousness-body of mine and all external signs, there is no view of self, no view of what belongs to self, and no bondage of the tendency to conceit?"

The Buddha told Rāhula: "There are three feelings: painful feeling, pleasant feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. Contemplating pleasant feeling, in order to sever the tendency of craving associated with pleasant feeling, one cultivates the holy life under me. In order to sever the tendency of aversion associated with painful feeling, one cultivates the holy life under me. In order to sever the tendency of ignorance associated with neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, one cultivates the holy life under me.

"Rāhula! If a bhikṣu has already severed and already known the tendency of craving associated with pleasant feeling, has already severed and already known the tendency of aversion associated with painful feeling, and has already severed and already known the tendency of ignorance associated with neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, this bhikṣu is called one who has cut off craving, turned away from the fetters, rightly penetrated conceit without interval, and reached the final end of suffering."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke a verse:

> "When experiencing pleasant feeling,
> one does not know the pleasant feeling;
> driven by the tendency of craving,
> one does not see the path of escape.
> When experiencing painful feeling,
> one does not know the painful feeling;
> driven by the tendency of aversion,
> one does not see the path of escape.
> Neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling,
> which the Perfectly Awakened One has taught,
> if one does not skillfully observe it,
> one never crosses to the other shore.
> A bhikṣu diligently striving,
> with right knowledge, unshaken,
> regarding all these feelings,
> the wise one is able to fully know.
> Having fully known all feelings,
> in the present life one exhausts all defilements.
> When the wise one's life ends,
> one does not fall among the counted.
> The counted being already severed,
> one forever abides in parinirvāṇa."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Venerable Rāhula, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (469)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "The ocean is deep and perilous: this is what the foolish worldlings say about what is deep and perilous, not what is spoken of as deep and perilous in the noble Dharma and Vinaya. What worldlings speak of is merely the accumulation of a great mass of water. If from the body there arise various feelings, and the multitude of sufferings press upon one, causing affliction or death, this is called the truly deep and perilous place of the great ocean. The foolish, ignorant ordinary person, when from the body there arise various feelings of suffering that press upon one, causing affliction or death, gives rise to worry and grief, cries and wails, beats the breast and wails aloud, and the mind becomes disordered and mad, sinking and drowning for a long time with no place of rest. But the learned noble disciple, when from the body there arise various feelings of suffering that press upon one, causing affliction or death, does not give rise to worry and grief, crying and wailing, beating the breast and wailing aloud, or mental disorder and madness; he does not sink in birth and death, and finds a place of rest."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke a verse:

> "When from the body arise various painful feelings,
> pressing even unto death,
> with worry and grief one cannot bear in patience,
> crying and wailing, the mind becomes disordered and mad.
> The mind itself creates obstructions,
> inviting and accumulating increased suffering,
> forever sinking in the ocean of birth and death,
> not knowing a place of rest.
> One who can abandon the body's various feelings,
> the suffering and affliction born from the body,
> pressing even unto death,
> does not give rise to thoughts of worry and grief,
> does not cry or wail aloud,
> is able to endure the multitude of sufferings;
> the mind does not create obstructions,
> inviting and accumulating increased suffering.
> One does not sink in birth and death,
> and forever attains the place of peace and safety."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (470)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "The foolish, ignorant ordinary person experiences painful feeling, pleasant feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. The learned noble disciple also experiences painful feeling, pleasant feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. Bhikṣus! What difference is there between the ordinary person and the noble one?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. Excellent, World-Honored One! May you please explain this fully. The bhikṣus, hearing it, will accept and practice it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "The foolish, ignorant ordinary person, when bodily contact gives rise to various feelings of suffering that press upon one, even to the point of death, worries and grieves, cries and wails, beats the breast and wails aloud."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it to you. Bhikṣus! The foolish, ignorant ordinary person, when bodily contact gives rise to various feelings, with the increase of suffering pressing upon one, even to the point of death, worries and grieves, beats the breast and wails, cries and wails aloud, and the mind becomes disordered and mad. At that time, two feelings increase: bodily feeling and mental feeling.

"It is like a man who is struck by two poisoned arrows at once and experiences extreme suffering. The foolish, ignorant ordinary person is likewise: two feelings increase, bodily feeling and mental feeling, and extreme suffering is experienced. Why? Because the foolish, ignorant ordinary person does not understand. Regarding the five sensual pleasures, when pleasant feeling through contact is experienced, one experiences the pleasure of the five desires. Experiencing the pleasure of the five desires, one is driven by the tendency of craving. When painful feeling through contact arises, aversion is generated. Generating aversion, one is driven by the tendency of aversion. Regarding these two feelings, their accumulation, their cessation, their gratification, their danger, and the escape from them, one does not know them as they truly are. Not knowing them as they truly are, one experiences neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling and is driven by the tendency of ignorance. Being bound by pleasant feeling, one is never freed. Being bound by painful feeling, one is never freed. Being bound by neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, one is never freed. What is one bound by? One is bound by craving, aversion, and ignorance; one is bound by birth, aging, sickness, death, worry, grief, affliction, and suffering.

"The learned noble disciple, when bodily contact gives rise to painful feeling and great suffering presses upon one, even to the point of death, does not give rise to worry, grief, beating the breast, wailing, crying and wailing aloud, or mental disorder and madness. At that time, only one feeling arises, namely bodily feeling, not mental feeling.

"It is like a man who is struck by one poisoned arrow and is not struck by a second poisoned arrow. At that time, only one feeling arises, namely bodily feeling, not mental feeling. Experiencing pleasant feeling through contact, one is not stained by sensual pleasure. Not being stained by sensual pleasure, regarding that pleasant feeling, the tendency of craving does not drive one. Experiencing painful feeling through contact, one does not generate aversion. Not generating aversion, the tendency of aversion does not drive one. Regarding these two tendencies, their accumulation, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape, one knows them as they truly are. Knowing them as they truly are, regarding neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, the tendency of ignorance does not drive one. Regarding that pleasant feeling, one is liberated and unbound. Regarding painful feeling and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, one is liberated and unbound. From what is one unbound? One is unbound from craving, aversion, and ignorance; unbound from birth, aging, sickness, death, worry, grief, affliction, and suffering."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke a verse:

> "The learned one, regarding suffering and pleasure,
> is not without experiencing and knowing them.
> Compared with the ordinary person,
> there is indeed a great difference for the learned one.
> With pleasant feeling, one is not heedless;
> with painful contact, one does not increase in worry.
> Both suffering and pleasure are relinquished,
> neither following along nor opposing.
> A bhikṣu diligently striving,
> with right knowledge, unshaken,
> regarding all these feelings,
> the wise one is able to fully know.
> Having fully known all feelings,
> in the present life one exhausts all defilements.
> At the death of the body, one does not fall among the counted;
> one forever abides in parinirvāṇa."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (471)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Just as in the sky, wild winds suddenly arise, coming from the four directions: dusty winds, dustless winds, vaiśvānara winds, vairambha winds, thin winds, thick winds, up to winds of the wind-wheel arising. Likewise, the winds of feeling arising in the body are the same: various feelings arise, including pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling; pleasant bodily feeling, painful bodily feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant bodily feeling; pleasant mental feeling, painful mental feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant mental feeling; pleasant feeling with nutriment, painful feeling with nutriment, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling with nutriment; pleasant feeling without nutriment, painful feeling without nutriment, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling without nutriment; pleasant feeling of attachment, painful feeling of attachment, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling of attachment; pleasant feeling of renunciation, painful feeling of renunciation, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling of renunciation."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke a verse:

> "Just as in the empty sky,
> various wild winds arise:
> east, west, south, and north winds,
> and from the four intermediate directions likewise,
> some dusty and some dustless,
> up to the wind-wheel arising.
> Just so, within this body,
> the arising of various feelings is likewise:
> whether pleasant or painful feeling,
> and neither-painful-nor-pleasant,
> with nutriment and without nutriment,
> attached and not attached.
> A bhikṣu diligently striving,
> with right knowledge, unshaken,
> regarding all these feelings,
> the wise one is able to fully know.
> Having fully known all feelings,
> in the present life one exhausts all defilements.
> At the death of the body, one does not fall among the counted;
> one forever abides in parinirvāṇa."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (472)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Just as in a guesthouse, various people dwell: kṣatriyas, brāhmaṇas, elders, householders, wild men, hunters, those who keep the precepts, those who violate the precepts, those who are householders and those who have gone forth, all dwelling therein. This body is likewise: various feelings arise, including pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling; pleasant bodily feeling, painful bodily feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant bodily feeling; pleasant mental feeling, painful mental feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant mental feeling; pleasant feeling with nutriment, painful feeling with nutriment, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling with nutriment; pleasant feeling without nutriment, painful feeling without nutriment, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling without nutriment; pleasant feeling of attachment, painful feeling of attachment, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling of attachment; pleasant feeling of renunciation, painful feeling of renunciation, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling of renunciation."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke a verse:

> "Just as in a guesthouse,
> various people dwell and stay:
> kṣatriyas, brāhmaṇas,
> elders, householders, and the like,
> caṇḍālas, wild men,
> those keeping and violating precepts,
> householders and those gone forth,
> such various kinds.
> This body is likewise:
> various feelings arise.
> Whether pleasant or painful feeling,
> and neither-painful-nor-pleasant,
> with nutriment and without nutriment,
> attached and not attached.
> A bhikṣu diligently striving,
> with right knowledge, unshaken,
> regarding all these feelings,
> the wise one is able to fully know.
> Having fully known all feelings,
> in the present life one exhausts all defilements.
> At the death of the body, one does not fall among the counted;
> one forever abides in parinirvāṇa."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (473)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu, alone in a quiet place, was engaged in meditative contemplation and thought: "The World-Honored One speaks of three feelings: pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. Yet he also says that whatever is felt is all suffering. What is the meaning of this?" Having had this thought, the bhikṣu arose from meditation, went to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! While in a quiet place engaged in meditative contemplation, I thought: 'The World-Honored One speaks of three feelings: pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. Yet he also says that whatever is felt is all suffering. What is the meaning of this?'"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Because all formations are impermanent, and because all formations are subject to change, I say that whatever is felt is all suffering."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke a verse:

> "Knowing that all formations are impermanent,
> and all are subject to change,
> therefore I say all feelings are suffering;
> this is what the Perfectly Awakened One knows.
> A bhikṣu diligently striving,
> with right knowledge, unshaken,
> regarding all feelings,
> the wise one is able to fully know.
> Having fully known all feelings,
> in the present life one exhausts all defilements.
> At the death of the body, one does not fall among the counted;
> one forever abides in parinirvāṇa."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (474)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, Venerable Ānanda, alone in a quiet place, was engaged in meditative contemplation and thought: "The World-Honored One speaks of three feelings: pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. Yet he also says that whatever is felt is all suffering. What is the meaning of this?" Having had this thought, he arose from meditation, went to visit the World-Honored One, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! While alone in a quiet place engaged in meditative contemplation, I thought: 'As the World-Honored One has said, there are three feelings: pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. Yet he also says that all feelings are entirely suffering. What is the meaning of this?'"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Because all formations are impermanent and because all formations are subject to change, I say that whatever is felt is all suffering. Furthermore, Ānanda, because I speak of the gradual stilling of formations, and because I speak of the gradual cessation of formations, I say that all feelings are entirely suffering."

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "How is it, World-Honored One, that you speak of feelings being gradually stilled?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "When one enters the meditative attainment of the first dhyāna, speech is stilled. When one enters the meditative attainment of the second dhyāna, initial and sustained thought are stilled. When one enters the meditative attainment of the third dhyāna, joy of mind is stilled. When one enters the meditative attainment of the fourth dhyāna, in-breath and out-breath are stilled. When one enters the meditative attainment of the base of infinite space, the perception of form is stilled. When one enters the meditative attainment of the base of infinite consciousness, the perception of the base of infinite space is stilled. When one enters the meditative attainment of the base of nothingness, the perception of the base of infinite consciousness is stilled. When one enters the meditative attainment of the base of neither perception nor non-perception, the perception of the base of nothingness is stilled. When one enters the meditative attainment of the cessation of perception and feeling, perception and feeling are stilled. This is called the gradual stilling of formations."

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! How is it that formations are gradually brought to cessation?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "When one enters the meditative attainment of the first dhyāna, speech ceases. When one enters the meditative attainment of the second dhyāna, initial and sustained thought cease. When one enters the meditative attainment of the third dhyāna, joy of mind ceases. When one enters the meditative attainment of the fourth dhyāna, in-breath and out-breath cease. When one enters the meditative attainment of the base of infinite space, the perception of form ceases. When one enters the meditative attainment of the base of infinite consciousness, the perception of the base of infinite space ceases. When one enters the meditative attainment of the base of nothingness, the perception of the base of infinite consciousness ceases. When one enters the meditative attainment of the base of neither perception nor non-perception, the perception of the base of nothingness ceases. When one enters the meditative attainment of the cessation of perception and feeling, perception and feeling cease. This is called the gradual cessation of formations."

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! This is called the gradual cessation of formations."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "There is also a superior cessation, a marvelous cessation, a supreme cessation, an unsurpassed cessation. Such cessation is not surpassed by any other cessation."

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "What is this superior cessation, marvelous cessation, supreme cessation, unsurpassed cessation that is not surpassed by any other cessation?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "When the mind does not delight in and is liberated from the craving for desire, when the mind does not delight in and is liberated from aversion and ignorance, this is called the superior cessation, the marvelous cessation, the supreme cessation, the unsurpassed cessation that is not surpassed by any other cessation."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Venerable Ānanda, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (475)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Before the Tathāgata Vipaśyin had attained Buddhahood, while alone in a quiet place, engaged in meditative contemplation and reflection, he made this observation, examining feelings: 'What are feelings? What is the accumulation of feelings? What is the cessation of feelings? What is the path leading to the accumulation of feelings? What is the path leading to the cessation of feelings? What is the gratification of feelings? What is the danger in feelings? What is the escape from feelings?' Observing thus, there are three feelings: pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. The accumulation of contact is the accumulation of feelings. The cessation of contact is the cessation of feelings. If regarding feelings one delights in them, praises them, is stained by attachment to them, and firmly abides in them, this is called the path leading to the accumulation of feelings. If regarding feelings one does not delight in them, does not praise them, is not stained by attachment to them, and does not firmly abide in them, this is called the path leading to the cessation of feelings. If conditioned by feelings there arises pleasure and joy, this is called the gratification of feelings. If feelings are impermanent and subject to change, this is called the danger in feelings. If regarding feelings one cuts off desire and craving, transcends desire and craving, this is called the escape from feelings."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

As for the Buddha Vipaśyin, likewise for the Buddha Śikhin, the Buddha Viśvabhū, the Buddha Krakucchanda, the Buddha Kanakamuni, the Buddha Kāśyapa, and myself, the Buddha Śākyamuni: before attaining Buddhahood, the contemplation and examination of feelings was also the same.

---

### (476)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu, alone in a quiet place, was engaged in meditative contemplation. He thus examined feelings: "What are feelings? What is the accumulation of feelings? What is the cessation of feelings? What is the path leading to the accumulation of feelings? What is the path leading to the cessation of feelings? What is the gratification of feelings? What is the danger in feelings? What is the escape from feelings?"

Then that bhikṣu, having arisen from meditation, went to visit the World-Honored One, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! While alone in a quiet place engaged in meditative contemplation, I examined feelings: 'What are feelings? What is the accumulation of feelings? What is the cessation of feelings? What is the path leading to the accumulation of feelings? What is the path leading to the cessation of feelings? What is the gratification of feelings? What is the danger in feelings? What is the escape from feelings?'"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "There are three feelings: pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. The accumulation of contact is the accumulation of feelings. The cessation of contact is the cessation of feelings. If regarding feelings one delights in them, praises them, is stained by attachment to them, and firmly abides in them, this is called the path leading to the accumulation of feelings. If regarding feelings one does not delight in them, does not praise them, is not stained by attachment to them, and does not firmly abide in them, this is called the path leading to the cessation of feelings. If conditioned by feelings there arises pleasure and joy, this is called the gratification of feelings. If feelings are impermanent and subject to change, this is called the danger in feelings. If regarding feelings one cuts off desire and craving, transcends desire and craving, this is called the escape from feelings."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (477)

As with the sūtra asked by the certain bhikṣu, the sūtra asked by Venerable Ānanda is likewise.

---

### (478)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What are feelings? What is the accumulation of feelings? What is the cessation of feelings? What is the path leading to the accumulation of feelings? What is the path leading to the cessation of feelings?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. Excellent, World-Honored One! May you please explain this fully. The bhikṣus, hearing it, will accept and practice it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it to you." The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "There are three feelings: pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. The accumulation of contact is the accumulation of feelings. The cessation of contact is the cessation of feelings. If regarding feelings one delights in them, praises them, is stained by attachment to them, and firmly abides in them, this is called the path leading to the accumulation of feelings. If regarding feelings one does not delight in them, does not praise them, is not stained by attachment to them, and does not firmly abide in them, this is called the path leading to the cessation of feelings. If conditioned by feelings there arises pleasure and joy, this is called the gratification of feelings. If feelings are impermanent and subject to change, this is called the danger in feelings. If regarding feelings one cuts off desire and craving, transcends desire and craving, this is called the escape from feelings."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (479)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If I had not known feelings as they truly are, had not known as they truly are the accumulation of feelings, the cessation of feelings, the path leading to the accumulation of feelings, the path leading to the cessation of feelings, the gratification of feelings, the danger in feelings, and the escape from feelings, then among the gods, the world, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, gods, and humans, I would not have attained liberation, emergence, freedom from all inversions, nor would I have attained anuttarā-samyak-saṃbodhi. Because I have known as they truly are feelings, the accumulation of feelings, the cessation of feelings, the path leading to the accumulation of feelings, the path leading to the cessation of feelings, the gratification of feelings, the danger in feelings, and the escape from feelings, among the gods, the world, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, gods, and humans, I am liberated, have emerged, am freed from all inversions, and have attained anuttarā-samyak-saṃbodhi."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (480)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas do not know feelings as they truly are, do not know as they truly are the accumulation of feelings, the cessation of feelings, the path leading to the accumulation of feelings, the path leading to the cessation of feelings, the gratification of feelings, the danger in feelings, and the escape from feelings, they are not true śramaṇas, not true brāhmaṇas, not in accord with śramaṇas, not in accord with brāhmaṇas, not fulfilling the purpose of a śramaṇa, not fulfilling the purpose of a brāhmaṇa, and cannot in the present life know for themselves and realize: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, and I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'

"If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas know feelings as they truly are, know as they truly are the accumulation of feelings, the cessation of feelings, the path leading to the accumulation of feelings, the path leading to the cessation of feelings, the gratification of feelings, the danger in feelings, and the escape from feelings, they are śramaṇas among śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas among brāhmaṇas, in accord with śramaṇas, in accord with brāhmaṇas, fulfilling the purpose of a śramaṇa, fulfilling the purpose of a brāhmaṇa, and in the present life know for themselves and realize: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, and I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'"

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

As with "śramaṇa" and "non-śramaṇa," likewise "counted among śramaṇas" and "not counted among śramaṇas" is also the same.

---

### (481)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Iṣaṇaṅgala, in the Iṣaṇaṅgala Forest.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I wish to sit in meditation here for half a month. Bhikṣus, you should not go wandering about, except for begging alms and the upoṣadha." He then sat in meditation and did not go wandering about, except for begging alms and the upoṣadha.

At that time, when the half month had passed, the World-Honored One spread out his seat and sat before the assembly. He told the bhikṣus: "With just a small portion of the meditative dharmas that I contemplated when I first attained Buddhahood, in this half month I have contemplated and reflected thus: 'Regarding all sentient beings, feelings that arise all have causes and conditions, not without causes and conditions. What are the causes and conditions? Desire is a cause and condition, intention is a cause and condition, and contact is a cause and condition.'

"Bhikṣus! When desire is not stilled, intention is not stilled, and contact is not stilled, because of those causes and conditions, beings give rise to feelings; because the causes and conditions are not stilled, beings give rise to feelings. When desire is stilled, but intention is not stilled and contact is not stilled, because of those causes and conditions, beings give rise to feelings; because the causes and conditions are not stilled, beings give rise to feelings. When desire is stilled and intention is stilled, but contact is not stilled, because of those causes and conditions, beings give rise to feelings; because the causes and conditions are not stilled, beings give rise to feelings. When desire is stilled, intention is stilled, and contact is stilled, because of those causes and conditions, beings give rise to feelings; because those causes and conditions are stilled, beings give rise to feelings.

"Because of the cause and condition of wrong view, beings give rise to feelings; because the cause and condition of wrong view is not stilled, beings give rise to feelings. Because of the causes and conditions of wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, wrong concentration, wrong liberation, and wrong knowledge, beings give rise to feelings; because the cause and condition of wrong knowledge is not stilled, beings give rise to feelings.

"Because of the cause and condition of right view, beings give rise to feelings; because the cause and condition of right view is stilled, beings give rise to feelings. Because of the causes and conditions of right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration, right liberation, and right knowledge, beings give rise to feelings; because the cause and condition of right knowledge is stilled, beings give rise to feelings.

"When that which one has not yet attained is attained, what one has not yet acquired is acquired, and what one has not yet realized is realized, because of those causes and conditions, beings give rise to feelings; because those causes and conditions are stilled, beings give rise to feelings.

"This is called: because of unstilled causes and conditions, beings give rise to feelings; and because of stilled causes and conditions, beings give rise to feelings.

"If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas do not know as they truly are these conditions, the accumulation of conditions, the cessation of conditions, the path leading to the accumulation of conditions, and the path leading to the cessation of conditions, they are not śramaṇas among śramaṇas, not brāhmaṇas among brāhmaṇas, not in accord with śramaṇas, not in accord with brāhmaṇas, not fulfilling the purpose of a śramaṇa, not fulfilling the purpose of a brāhmaṇa, and cannot in the present life know for themselves and realize: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, and I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'

"If śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas know as they truly are these conditions, the accumulation of conditions, the cessation of conditions, the path leading to the accumulation of conditions, and the path leading to the cessation of conditions, one should know they are śramaṇas among śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas among brāhmaṇas, in accord with śramaṇas, in accord with brāhmaṇas, fulfilling the purpose of a śramaṇa, fulfilling the purpose of a brāhmaṇa, and in the present life know for themselves and realize: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, and I know for myself that I will not receive further existence.'"

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (482)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. It was the time of the summer retreat.

At that time, the householder Anāthapiṇḍada came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The Buddha taught him the Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening him, speaking various dharmas. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened him, the householder rose from his seat, arranged his robes, made obeisance to the Buddha, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha: "May the World-Honored One, together with the great assembly, accept my invitation for three months of robes, food and drink, and medicine for the sick."

At that time, the World-Honored One silently accepted.

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍada, knowing the Buddha had silently accepted the invitation, rose from his seat and departed, returning to his own home. After the three months had passed, he came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The Buddha told the householder Anāthapiṇḍada: "Excellent, householder! For three months you have provided robes, food and drink, and medicine for the sick. You have adorned and purified the higher path, and in future lives you shall obtain the fruit of happiness. However, you should not merely be content to silently enjoy this dharma. You should strive diligently, and from time to time practice the joy and happiness of seclusion, accomplishing realization through the body."

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍada, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and approved, rose from his seat, and departed.

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra was sitting among the assembly. Knowing that the householder Anāthapiṇḍada had departed, he addressed the Buddha: "How wonderful, World-Honored One! You have well taught the Dharma for the householder Anāthapiṇḍada, and well encouraged the householder Anāthapiṇḍada, saying: 'You have for three months fully provided for the Tathāgata and the great assembly, and have purified the higher path. In future lives you shall receive the fruit of happiness. You should not merely be content to silently enjoy this merit. You should from time to time practice the joy and happiness of seclusion, accomplishing realization through the body.' World-Honored One! If a noble disciple practices the joy and happiness of seclusion and accomplishes realization through the body, he will distance himself from five things and cultivate the fulfillment of five things. How does one distance oneself from five things? One cuts off joy nourished by desire, cuts off sorrow nourished by desire, cuts off equanimity nourished by desire, cuts off joy nourished by unwholesomeness, and cuts off sorrow nourished by unwholesomeness. These are called the five things one distances oneself from. How does one cultivate the fulfillment of five things? Namely: sympathetic joy, gladness, tranquility, happiness, and one-pointed mind."

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "So it is, so it is. If a noble disciple practices and learns the joy and happiness of seclusion, accomplishing realization through the body, he distances himself from five things and cultivates the fulfillment of five things."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (483)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There is mindfulness with nutriment, there is mindfulness without nutriment, and there is mindfulness without nutriment and without nutriment. There is joy with nutriment, joy without nutriment,

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Seventeen (35 of 101)

---

### (455)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Conditioned by various elements, various contacts arise. Conditioned by various contacts, various perceptions arise. Conditioned by various perceptions, various desires arise. Conditioned by various desires, various intentions arise. Conditioned by various intentions, various fevers arise. Conditioned by various fevers, various seekings arise.

"What are the various elements? They are the eighteen elements: the eye element, up to the dharma element.

"How is it that conditioned by various elements, various contacts arise? How is it that, up to, conditioned by various fevers, various seekings arise? Conditioned by the eye element, eye-contact arises; it is not that conditioned by eye-contact, the eye element arises; rather, it is only conditioned by the eye element that eye-contact arises. Conditioned by eye-contact, eye-perception arises; it is not that conditioned by eye-perception, eye-contact arises; rather, it is only conditioned by eye-contact that eye-perception arises. Conditioned by eye-perception, eye-desire arises; it is not that conditioned by eye-desire, eye-perception arises; rather, it is only conditioned by eye-perception that eye-desire arises. Conditioned by eye-desire, eye-intention arises; it is not that conditioned by eye-intention, eye-desire arises; rather, it is only conditioned by eye-desire that eye-intention arises. Conditioned by eye-intention, eye-fever arises; it is not that conditioned by eye-fever, eye-intention arises; rather, it is only conditioned by eye-intention that eye-fever arises. Conditioned by eye-fever, eye-seeking arises; it is not that conditioned by eye-seeking, eye-fever arises; rather, it is only conditioned by eye-fever that eye-seeking arises. In the same way, for the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind elements: conditioned by the mind element, mind-contact arises, up to, conditioned by mind-fever, mind-seeking arises; this should also be explained in full in the same way. This is called, bhikṣus, conditioned by various elements, various contacts arise, up to, conditioned by various fevers, various seekings arise. It is not that conditioned by various seekings, various fevers arise, up to, it is not that conditioned by various contacts, various elements arise. Rather, it is only conditioned by various elements that various contacts arise, up to, conditioned by various fevers that various seekings arise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the internal six sense bases, so too should it be spoken regarding the external six sense bases.

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Eighteen

### Recitations Spoken by Disciples, Fourth Section

---

### (490)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Magadha at the village of Nālā.

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra was also in the country of Magadha at the village of Nālā.

Then a non-Buddhist renunciant named Jambukhādaka, who was an old friend of Śāriputra, came to visit Śāriputra. After exchanging greetings and mutual courtesies, he withdrew and sat to one side. He asked Śāriputra: "In the noble Dharma and Vinaya, what is difficult?"

Śāriputra told Jambukhādaka: "Only going forth is difficult."

"How is going forth difficult?"

He answered: "Finding delight in it is difficult."

"How is finding delight difficult?"

He answered: "Constantly cultivating wholesome dharmas while finding delight is difficult."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, leads to the constant increase of wholesome dharmas?"

He answered: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration."

Jambukhādaka said: "Śāriputra, this is indeed a good path, this is indeed a good way. When cultivated and much cultivated, wholesome dharmas are constantly cultivated and increased. Śāriputra, if one who has gone forth constantly cultivates this path, before long one will swiftly exhaust all the āsravas."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

In this manner, there is a sequence of forty sūtras based on the questions of Jambukhādaka.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is meant by one who well expounds the Dharma? What is meant by one rightly directed in the world? What is meant by one well gone in the world?"

Śāriputra said: "If one expounds the Dharma that tames desire and lust, tames anger, and tames delusion, this is called a Dharma-teacher of the world. If one is directed toward taming desire and lust, directed toward taming anger, and directed toward taming delusion, this is called being rightly directed. If desire and lust are already exhausted, completely cut off and fully known, and anger and delusion are already exhausted, completely cut off and fully known, this is called being well cut off."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, can give rise to being well cut off?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "That which is called nirvāṇa, what is nirvāṇa?"

Śāriputra said: "Nirvāṇa is the permanent ending of desire and lust, the permanent ending of anger, the permanent ending of delusion, the permanent ending of all afflictions. This is called nirvāṇa."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, leads to attaining nirvāṇa?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "For what reason have you gone forth to cultivate the holy life under the śramaṇa Gautama?"

Śāriputra said: "For the sake of cutting off desire and lust, for cutting off anger, for cutting off delusion, I have gone forth to cultivate the holy life under the śramaṇa Gautama."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, leads to cutting off desire and lust, anger, and delusion?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called the exhaustion of the āsravas? What is the exhaustion of the āsravas?"

Śāriputra said: "The āsravas are three āsravas, namely the āsrava of sensual desire, the āsrava of existence, and the āsrava of ignorance. When these three āsravas are exhausted without remainder, this is called the exhaustion of the āsravas."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, leads to the exhaustion of the āsravas?"

Śāriputra answered: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called an arhat? What does it mean to be an arhat?"

Śāriputra said: "When desire and lust are cut off without remainder, and anger and delusion are cut off without remainder, this is called an arhat."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, leads to becoming an arhat?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called an arhat? What does it mean to be an arhat?"

Śāriputra said: "When desire and lust are permanently ended without remainder, and anger and delusion are permanently ended without remainder, this is called being an arhat."

He further asked: "Is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, leads to becoming an arhat?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called ignorance? What is ignorance?"

Śāriputra said: "What is called ignorance is not knowing with regard to the past, not knowing with regard to the future, not knowing with regard to the past, future, and present. Not knowing of the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha jewels. Not knowing of suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. Not knowing of wholesome, unwholesome, and indeterminate. Not knowing internally, not knowing externally. Whatever not knowing and dark obstruction regarding this or that matter, this is called ignorance."

Jambukhādaka said to Śāriputra: "This is a great heap of darkness!" He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, cuts off ignorance?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka again asked Venerable Śāriputra: "What are called the āsravas? What are the āsravas?" As previously stated.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called existence? What is existence?"

Śāriputra said: "Existence refers to three existences: desire existence, form existence, and formless existence."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, cuts off these existences?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called the existing body (satkāya)? What is the existing body?"

Śāriputra said: "The existing body refers to the five aggregates of clinging. What are the five aggregates of clinging? They are the aggregate of clinging to form, the aggregates of clinging to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, that cuts off this existing body?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called suffering? What is suffering?"

Śāriputra said: "Suffering refers to the suffering of birth, the suffering of old age, the suffering of illness, the suffering of death, the suffering of being separated from those one loves, the suffering of being together with those one resents, the suffering of not obtaining what one seeks. In brief, the suffering of the five aggregates of clinging. This is called suffering."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, that cuts off this suffering?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What are called the floods? What are the floods?"

Śāriputra said: "The floods are the flood of sensual desire, the flood of existence, the flood of views, and the flood of ignorance."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, cuts off these floods?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What are called the yokes? What are the yokes?" The yokes are explained as the floods.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What are called the clingings? What are the clingings?"

Śāriputra said: "The clingings are four clingings, namely clinging to sensual desire, clinging to self, clinging to views, and clinging to rules and rituals."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, cuts off these clingings?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What are called the bonds? What are the bonds?"

Śāriputra said: "The bonds are four bonds, namely the bond of desire and lust, the bond of anger, the bond of clinging to rules and rituals, and the bond of the view of self."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, cuts off these bonds?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What are called the fetters? What are the fetters?"

Śāriputra said: "The fetters are nine fetters, namely the fetter of craving, the fetter of aversion, the fetter of conceit, the fetter of ignorance, the fetter of views, the fetter of clinging to others' views, the fetter of doubt, the fetter of jealousy, and the fetter of miserliness."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, cuts off these fetters?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What are called the latent tendencies? What are the latent tendencies?"

Śāriputra said: "The latent tendencies are seven latent tendencies, namely the latent tendency of desire and lust, the latent tendency of anger, the latent tendency of craving for existence, the latent tendency of conceit, the latent tendency of ignorance, the latent tendency of views, and the latent tendency of doubt."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, cuts off these latent tendencies?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What are called desires? What are desires?"

Śāriputra said: "Desires refer to forms cognized by the eye that are lovable, pleasant, and agreeable, and to which one is attached. Sounds cognized by the ear, odors cognized by the nose, tastes cognized by the tongue, and tangibles cognized by the body that are lovable, pleasant, and agreeable, and to which one is attached. Jambukhādaka, these qualities are not themselves desire; rather, it is the mental conception and deliberation regarding them that constitutes desire." At that time, Śāriputra then spoke this verse:

> "It is not those attractive objects,
> The various forms in the world, that cause desire;
> Only the mental conception regarding them
> Is truly the desire of a person.
> Those various forms
> Always remain in the world;
> One who tames the mind of craving desire
> Is truly the wise one."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, cuts off these desires?"

Śāriputra answered: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What are called the nutriments? What are the nutriments?"

Śāriputra said: "The nutriments are five nutriments, namely the nutriment of desire and lust, the nutriment of anger, the nutriment of sloth and torpor, the nutriment of restlessness and remorse, and the nutriment of doubt."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, cuts off these five nutriments?"

Śāriputra answered: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called refreshment? What is refreshment?"

Śāriputra said: "Refreshment refers to the cutting off of the three fetters."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, cuts off the three fetters?"

Śāriputra answered: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called having attained refreshment? What does it mean to have attained refreshment?"

Śāriputra said: "Having attained refreshment means that the three fetters are already exhausted and already known."

He further asked: "Is there a path, is there a way, that cuts off these fetters?"

Śāriputra answered: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called attaining supreme refreshment? What is attaining supreme refreshment?"

Śāriputra said: "Attaining supreme refreshment means that desire and lust are permanently ended, anger and delusion are permanently ended. This is called attaining supreme refreshment."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, leads to attaining supreme refreshment?"

Śāriputra answered: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called the state of supreme refreshment? What is the state of supreme refreshment?"

Śāriputra said: "The state of supreme refreshment means that desire and lust have been cut off and fully known, permanently ended without remainder; anger and delusion have been cut off and fully known, permanently ended without remainder. This is called the state of supreme refreshment."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, leads to the state of supreme refreshment?"

Śāriputra answered: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called coolness? What is coolness?"

Śāriputra said: "Coolness is the ending of the five lower fetters, namely the view of the existing body, clinging to rules and rituals, doubt, desire and lust, and anger."

He further asked: "Is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, cuts off these five lower fetters and attains coolness?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called having attained coolness? What does it mean to have attained coolness?"

Śāriputra said: "When the five lower fetters are already ended and already known, this is called having attained coolness."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, which when cultivated and much cultivated, leads to attaining coolness?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called supreme coolness? What is supreme coolness?"

Śāriputra said: "Supreme coolness means that desire and lust are permanently ended without remainder, anger and delusion are permanently ended without remainder, all afflictions are permanently ended without remainder. This is called supreme coolness."

He further asked: "Is there a path, is there a way, that leads to this supreme coolness?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called having attained supreme coolness? What does it mean to have attained supreme coolness?"

Śāriputra said: "Having attained supreme coolness means that desire and lust are permanently ended without remainder, already cut off and already known; anger and delusion are permanently ended without remainder, already cut off and already known. This is called having attained supreme coolness."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, that leads to this supreme coolness?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What is called craving? What is craving?"

Śāriputra said: "There are three cravings, namely craving for sensual desire, craving for form, and craving for the formless."

He further asked: "Is there a path, is there a way, that cuts off these three cravings?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What are called courses of action? What are courses of action?"

Śāriputra said: "Courses of action are the ten unwholesome courses of action, namely killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, false speech, divisive speech, harsh speech, idle chatter, covetousness, anger, and wrong views."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, that cuts off these ten courses of action?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

Jambukhādaka asked Śāriputra: "What are called defilements? What are defilements?"

Śāriputra said: "Defilements are three defilements: the defilement of desire and lust, the defilement of anger, and the defilement of delusion."

He further asked: "Śāriputra, is there a path, is there a way, that cuts off these three defilements?"

Śāriputra said: "There is. It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view up to right concentration."

Then the two worthy men, having concluded their discussion, each rose from their seats and departed.

As with defilements, so too with stains, grease, thorns, attachments, and bonds, the same applies.

---

### (491)

As with the sūtras of Jambukhādaka's questions, so too the questions of the renunciant are likewise.

---

### (492)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, Venerable Śāriputra was also dwelling there.

Then Venerable Śāriputra addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu attains the immeasurable samādhi, realizes it with the body and dwells having fully accomplished it, yet his mind does not delight in the cessation of the existing body, which is nirvāṇa, and instead looks back with longing at the existing body, it is like a man with glue on his hands who grasps a tree branch: his hands stick to the tree and he cannot get free. Why is this? Because of the glue on his hands. Bhikṣus, if one attains the immeasurable samāpatti, realizes it with the body, yet the mind does not delight in the cessation of the existing body, which is nirvāṇa, and instead looks back with longing at the existing body, one will ultimately not be freed and will not attain the teaching that accords with the Dharma in this present life. Even until the end of life, one will attain nothing, and will return to be born again in this world, ultimately unable to break through the darkness of delusion. It is like a pond beside a village, extremely deep in mud, that has long been without rain so that the water dries up and the ground cracks. In the same way, a bhikṣu who does not attain the insight that accords with the Dharma teaching, even until the end of life, attains nothing, and when born again will still fall back into this world.

"If a bhikṣu attains the immeasurable samādhi, realizes it with the body and dwells having fully accomplished it, and his mind gives rise to faith and delight regarding the cessation of the existing body, which is nirvāṇa, and does not look back with longing at the existing body, it is like a man with clean, dry hands who grasps a tree branch: his hands do not stick to the tree. Why is this? Because his hands are clean. In the same way, a bhikṣu who attains the immeasurable samādhi, realizes it with the body and dwells having fully accomplished it, and whose mind gives rise to faith and delight regarding the cessation of that which has consciousness, which is nirvāṇa, and does not look back with longing at the existing body, will in this present life accord with the Dharma teaching, and until the end of life will not return to be born again in this world. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should diligently strive to break through and destroy ignorance. It is like a pond beside a village where water flows in from all four directions and rain frequently falls, so that water constantly enters the pond, the water overflows in abundance, the foul water flows out, and the pond becomes clean and pure. In the same way, all can in this present life accord with the Dharma teaching, and until the end of life will not return to be born again in this world. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should diligently strive to break through and destroy ignorance."

When Venerable Śāriputra had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what he had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (493)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu dwelling in the wilderness, or on open ground, in a forest, or under a tree, should train as follows, inwardly observing and contemplating: 'Do I perceive within my mind any thought of desire?' If one does not perceive it, one should then turn to objects of experience, or to a sign of purity. If craving desire arises, it goes against seclusion. It is like a man who, using strength to row a boat against the current going upstream, becomes slightly tired and weary, and the boat then drifts back and goes downstream. In the same way, a bhikṣu who contemplates the sign of purity and then gives rise to craving desire goes against seclusion. This bhikṣu, while training, cultivates an inferior method; he does not attain complete purity, and is therefore carried away again by craving desire, does not attain the power of the Dharma, his mind is not tranquil, his mind is not unified, and he gives rise to craving desire following the sign of purity, flowing along swiftly, going against seclusion. One should know that this bhikṣu cannot dare to declare of himself that he is freed from desire with regard to the five qualities of sensual pleasure.

"If a bhikṣu, on open ground, in a forest, or under a tree, contemplates thus: 'Has my inner mind become free from desire?' This bhikṣu should then turn to objects of experience, or take up the sign of purity. If he perceives that his mind is directed toward seclusion, flowing toward it swiftly. It is like a feather that enters fire and curls up, unable to be spread out again. In the same way, a bhikṣu who takes up the sign of purity and immediately flows toward seclusion, streaming swiftly. Bhikṣus, one should know in this way: in one's diligent practice, the mind is not lax, one attains the tranquility of the Dharma, calm, cessation, ease, and joy, purely unified in mind. That is to say, 'Having contemplated, regarding the sign of purity I flow toward seclusion, following the path in accordance.' One is then able and worthy to declare of oneself that one is freed from desire with regard to the five qualities of sensual pleasure."

When Venerable Śāriputra had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what he had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (494)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. Venerable Śāriputra was on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra, in the early morning, put on his robes and took his bowl, left Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa, and entered Rājagṛha to beg for food. On the roadside he saw a large withered tree. He then spread his sitting cloth beneath the tree, composed his body, and sat upright. He addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu cultivates dhyāna contemplation and attains the power of supernatural abilities, with the mind self-mastered, and wishes to make this withered tree become earth, it immediately becomes earth. Why is this? Because within this withered tree there is the earth element. Therefore, a bhikṣu who attains the power of supernatural abilities, when the mind resolves upon earth, it immediately becomes earth, without difference.

"If a bhikṣu attains the power of supernatural abilities, self-mastered and at will, and wishes to make this tree become water, fire, wind, gold, silver, or other substances, all are accomplished without difference. Why is this? Because within this withered tree there is the water element. Therefore, bhikṣus, one who through dhyāna contemplation attains the power of supernatural abilities, self-mastered and at will, who wishes to make the withered tree become gold, it immediately becomes gold without difference, and likewise for various other substances, all accomplished without difference. Why is this? Because that withered tree has various elements within it. Therefore, bhikṣus, one who through dhyāna contemplation attains the power of supernatural abilities, self-mastered and at will, can make various substances, all accomplished without difference. Bhikṣus, you should know that the domain of supernatural abilities attained through a bhikṣu's dhyāna contemplation is inconceivable.
