Therefore, bhikṣus, you should diligently practice dhyāna contemplation and learn the supernatural abilities."

When Śāriputra had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what he had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (495)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, Venerable Śāriputra was on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra addressed the bhikṣus: "One who violates the precepts, because the precepts are broken, that upon which one relies deteriorates, and the mind does not dwell in joy. When it does not dwell in joy, one loses delight, ease, joy, tranquil samādhi, knowledge and vision as they truly are, disenchantment, dispassion, and liberation. Having lost these, one is forever unable to attain nirvāṇa without remainder. It is like a tree whose roots have decayed: its branches, leaves, flowers, and fruits all fail to develop. A bhikṣu who violates the precepts is likewise. Merit deteriorates, the mind does not dwell in joy, and without faith and delight, one loses delight, ease, joy, tranquil samādhi, knowledge and vision as they truly are, disenchantment, dispassion, and liberation. Having lost liberation, one is forever unable to attain nirvāṇa without remainder.

"A bhikṣu who upholds the precepts has the foundation complete, that upon which one relies is complete, and the mind attains faith and delight. Having attained faith and delight, one attains joy, ease, joy, tranquil samādhi, knowledge and vision as they truly are, disenchantment, dispassion, and liberation. Having attained liberation, one can swiftly attain nirvāṇa without remainder. It is like a tree whose roots are not decayed: its branches, leaves, flowers, and fruits all develop successfully. A bhikṣu who upholds the precepts is likewise. The foundation is complete, that upon which one relies is accomplished, the mind attains faith and delight. Having attained faith and delight, one attains joy, ease, joy, tranquil samādhi, knowledge and vision as they truly are, disenchantment, dispassion, and liberation, and swiftly attains nirvāṇa without remainder."

When Venerable Śāriputra had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what he had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (496)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Śāriputra addressed the bhikṣus: "If among bhikṣus disputes arise and mutual accusations are made, with an offending bhikṣu and an accusing bhikṣu, if they do not rely on right thinking to examine themselves, one should know that those bhikṣus will for a long time be stubborn, their disputes will increase, they will oppose each other, resentment will deepen, and regarding the offenses that have arisen, they will be unable to use the proper Dharma and Vinaya to bring them to rest. If bhikṣus have such arising disputes, whether the offending bhikṣu or the accusing bhikṣu, if both rely on right thinking to examine and reproach themselves, one should know that those bhikṣus will not for a long time be stubborn, will not oppose each other, resentment will not increase, and regarding the offenses that have arisen, they can use the Dharma and Vinaya to bring them to rest.

"How does a bhikṣu examine himself with right thinking? A bhikṣu should think thus: 'It was wrong and improper of me to commit an offense where another could see me. If I had not committed this offense, the other would not have seen it. Because he saw my offense and was displeased and disapproving, he therefore accused me. Other bhikṣus who hear of it will also be displeased and disapproving. Therefore, for a long time disputes and stubbornness will increase, accusations will be exchanged, and regarding the offenses that have arisen, they cannot be brought to rest using the proper Dharma and Vinaya. I now know this myself and accept it as one accepts paying a tax.' This is called a bhikṣu who can examine himself regarding the offense that has arisen.

"How does an accusing bhikṣu examine himself? The accusing bhikṣu should think thus: 'That elder bhikṣu committed an improper offense that I saw. If he had not committed this improper offense, I would not have seen it. Having seen his offense and being displeased, I accused him. Other bhikṣus who see it will also be displeased and accuse him. For a long time disputes will increase without ceasing, and they cannot use the proper Dharma and Vinaya to bring the arisen offenses to rest. From today, I should let it go, accepting it as one accepts paying a tax.' Thus the accusing bhikṣu can well rely on right thinking to examine himself inwardly.

"Therefore, bhikṣus, both the offender and the accuser should rely on right thinking and examine themselves, not allowing stubbornness to grow over a long time. Bhikṣus, may you be free from disputes, and may arisen disputes be brought to rest using the Dharma and Vinaya."

When Venerable Śāriputra had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus heard it, rejoiced, and practiced accordingly.

---

### (497)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if an accusing bhikṣu wishes to accuse another of an offense, in how many qualities should he establish his mind in order to accuse another of an offense?"

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "If a bhikṣu establishes his mind in five qualities, he may accuse another of an offense. What are the five? It should be truthful and not untruthful; timely and not untimely; beneficial in meaning and not without benefit in meaning; gentle and not harsh; with a mind of loving-kindness and not with anger. Śāriputra, an accusing bhikṣu who possesses these five qualities may accuse another of an offense."

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, in how many qualities should the accused bhikṣu establish his own mind?"

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "The accused bhikṣu should establish his mind with five qualities. He should think: 'May the other's accusation be truthful and not untruthful; may it be timely and not untimely; may it be beneficial in meaning and not without benefit in meaning; may it be gentle and not harsh; may it be with a mind of loving-kindness and not with anger.' Śāriputra, the accused bhikṣu should possess these five qualities to settle his own mind."

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I have seen those who accuse others of offenses doing so untruthfully and not truthfully, at the wrong time and not the right time, without benefit in meaning and not for benefit in meaning, harshly and not gently, with anger and not with loving-kindness. World-Honored One, with how many qualities should an untruthful accusing bhikṣu be benefited to cause him to change and repent?"

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "An untruthful accusing bhikṣu should be benefited with five qualities to cause him to change and repent. One should say to him: 'Elder, your accusation is untruthful and not truthful; you should change and repent. It was untimely and not timely, without benefit in meaning and not for benefit in meaning, harsh and not gentle, with anger and not with loving-kindness; you should change and repent.' Śāriputra, an untruthful accusing bhikṣu should be benefited with these five qualities to cause him to change and repent, and also so that future bhikṣus will not make untruthful accusations against others."

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, in how many qualities should the bhikṣu who has been untruthfully accused be established so that he does not become distressed and regretful?"

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "The bhikṣu who has been untruthfully accused should establish himself with five qualities so as not to become distressed and regretful. He should think thus: 'That bhikṣu accused me untruthfully and not truthfully; it was untimely and not timely; without benefit in meaning and not for benefit in meaning; harsh and not gentle; with anger and not with loving-kindness. I truly will not become distressed and regretful.' The bhikṣu who has been untruthfully accused should establish his mind with these five qualities, not becoming distressed and regretful."

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, suppose a bhikṣu accuses truthfully and not untruthfully, timely and not untimely, with benefit in meaning and not without benefit, gently and not harshly, with loving-kindness and not with anger. In how many qualities should such a truthful accusing bhikṣu be benefited so that he does not change and become regretful?"

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "A truthful accusing bhikṣu should be benefited with five qualities so that he does not become regretful. One should say to him: 'Elder, your accusation is truthful and not untruthful; timely and not untimely; beneficial in meaning and not without benefit; gentle and not harsh; with loving-kindness and not with anger.' Śāriputra, a truthful accusing bhikṣu should be benefited with these five qualities so that he does not become regretful, and also so that future truthful accusing bhikṣus will not become regretful."

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, in how many qualities should the bhikṣu who has been truthfully accused be benefited so that he does not become regretful?"

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "The accused bhikṣu should be benefited with five qualities so that he does not become regretful. One should say to him: 'That bhikṣu truthfully accused you and not untruthfully; do not become regretful. It was timely and not untimely; beneficial in meaning and not without benefit; gentle and not harsh; with loving-kindness and not with anger; do not become regretful.'"

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I have seen bhikṣus who have been truthfully accused and who become angry. World-Honored One, in how many qualities should such a truthfully accused and angry bhikṣu be helped to awaken from his anger and resentment?"

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "A truthfully accused and angry bhikṣu should be helped to awaken with five qualities. One should say to him: 'Elder, that bhikṣu truthfully accused you; it was not untruthful. Do not be angry.' And so on, up to: 'With loving-kindness and not with anger; do not be angry.' Śāriputra, a truthfully accused and angry bhikṣu should be helped to awaken from his resentment with these five qualities."

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, whether one truthfully or untruthfully accuses me of an offense, regarding both persons, I should settle my own mind. If the accusation is truthful, I should acknowledge it myself. If it is untruthful, I should explain to myself, saying: 'This is not true; I myself know that I have no such quality.' World-Honored One, I should be thus. As the World-Honored One taught in the Simile of the Saw Sūtra, instructing the śramaṇas: 'If bandits come, seize you, and saw your body apart, if you give rise to evil thoughts or evil words toward those bandits, you create obstacles for yourselves. Therefore, bhikṣus, if someone saws your body apart, you should not give rise to evil mind, change, or evil words, creating obstacles for yourselves. Toward those persons, you should give rise to a mind of loving-kindness, without enmity and without hatred, and dwelling pervading the four directions with a mind of loving-kindness, fully accomplished in right mental composure. This is what you should learn.' Therefore, World-Honored One, I should be thus. As the World-Honored One taught, regarding the suffering of having the body sawn apart, I should patiently endure it. How much more so for small suffering and small slander, which I should patiently endure! The benefit of the śramaṇa, the aspiration of the śramaṇa: to wish to abandon unwholesome dharmas, to wish to cultivate wholesome dharmas. Regarding these unwholesome dharmas that should be abandoned and wholesome dharmas that should be cultivated, with diligent effort, well guarding oneself, mindfully contemplating, practicing without negligence: this is what should be learned."

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if I should accuse another bhikṣu of an offense, truthfully and not untruthfully, timely and not untimely, with benefit in meaning and not without benefit, gently and not harshly, with loving-kindness and not with anger, yet that accused bhikṣu harbors anger."

The Buddha asked Śāriputra: "What sort of bhikṣu, upon hearing his offense accused, gives rise to anger?"

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if that bhikṣu is deceptive, cunning, fraudulent, faithless, without shame, without moral dread, lazy, with lost mindfulness, without concentration, with evil wisdom, slack, opposed to seclusion, disrespectful of the precepts and Vinaya, heedless of the śramaṇa life, not diligent in practice, not self-examining, gone forth for the sake of livelihood and not seeking nirvāṇa, such a person, upon hearing my accusation, gives rise to anger."

The Buddha asked Śāriputra: "What sort of bhikṣu, upon hearing your accusation, does not become angry?"

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if there is a bhikṣu who is not deceptive, not cunning, not fraudulent, faithful, possessed of shame and moral dread, diligent, with right mindfulness, right concentration, and wisdom, not slack, not abandoning seclusion, deeply respectful of the precepts and Vinaya, mindful of the śramaṇa life, revering nirvāṇa, gone forth for the sake of the Dharma and not for the sake of livelihood. Such a bhikṣu, upon hearing my accusation, joyfully receives it upon his head, as if drinking sweet ambrosia. It is like a kṣatriya or brāhmaṇa woman who has bathed and cleansed herself, and upon receiving a fine and lovely garland, delights in placing it upon her head to adorn herself. In the same way, a bhikṣu who is not deceptive, not cunning, not fraudulent, of right faith, possessed of shame and moral dread, diligent, with right mindfulness, right concentration, and wisdom, not slack, with a mind intent on seclusion, deeply respectful of the precepts and Vinaya, mindful of the śramaṇa life, diligently practicing self-examination, gone forth for the sake of the Dharma, with the aspiration to seek nirvāṇa. Such a bhikṣu, upon hearing my accusation, joyfully receives it upon his head, as if drinking sweet ambrosia."

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "If that bhikṣu is deceptive, cunning, fraudulent, faithless, without shame, without moral dread, lazy, with lost mindfulness, without concentration, with evil wisdom, slack, opposed to seclusion, disrespectful of the precepts and Vinaya, heedless of the śramaṇa life, not seeking nirvāṇa, gone forth for the sake of livelihood, such a bhikṣu should not be instructed or spoken with. Why is this? Because such bhikṣus destroy the holy life. If that bhikṣu is not deceptive, not cunning, not fraudulent, faithful, possessed of shame and moral dread, diligent, with right mindfulness, right concentration, and wisdom, not slack, with a mind intent on seclusion, deeply respectful of the precepts and Vinaya, mindful of the śramaṇa life, with the aspiration to revere nirvāṇa, gone forth for the sake of the Dharma, such a bhikṣu should be instructed. Why is this? Because such a bhikṣu is able to cultivate the holy life and able to establish himself."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Venerable Śāriputra, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (498)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Nālandā, in the Mango Grove of the Cloth-Seller.

At that time, Śāriputra went to the World-Honored One, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I have deep faith in the World-Honored One. Among all śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas of the past, the future, and the present, none possesses wisdom equal to the World-Honored One's bodhi, much less surpassing it."

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "Excellent! Excellent! Śāriputra, excellent is what you have said, the foremost of statements. You are able to make a lion's roar among the assembly, saying that you have deep faith in the World-Honored One, saying that among all śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas of the past, future, and present, none possesses wisdom equal to the Buddha's bodhi, much less surpassing it." The Buddha asked Śāriputra: "Can you truly know the superior morality of the samyak-saṃbuddhas of the past?"

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "I do not know."

The World-Honored One further asked: "Śāriputra, do you know such dharma, such wisdom, such clarity, such liberation, such abiding?"

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "I do not know, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "Do you know the superior morality of the samyak-saṃbuddhas of the future, such dharma, such wisdom, such clarity, such liberation, such abiding?"

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "I do not know, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "Do you know the superior morality of the presently existing Buddha, such dharma, such wisdom, such clarity, such liberation, such abiding?"

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "I do not know, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "If you do not know the dharmas within the minds of the Buddhas, World-Honored Ones, of the past, future, and present, how can you make such a proclamation? Making a lion's roar in the great assembly, saying: 'I have deep faith in the World-Honored One. Among all śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas of the past and future, none possesses wisdom equal to the World-Honored One's bodhi, much less surpassing it'?"

Śāriputra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I cannot know the mental range of the Buddhas, World-Honored Ones, of the past, future, and present. However, I can know the Dharma range of the Buddhas, World-Honored Ones. I have heard the World-Honored One teach the Dharma, progressively deeper, progressively more excellent, progressively higher, progressively more subtle. As I hear the World-Honored One teach the Dharma, knowing one dharma I then cut off one dharma, knowing one dharma I then realize one dharma, knowing one dharma I then cultivate one dharma, reaching the ultimate in the Dharma, and attaining pure faith in the great Teacher, the mind becoming pure.

"The World-Honored One is a Samyak-Saṃbuddha. World-Honored One, it is like a king who has a border city, with walls all around that are straight, strong, firm, and dense, with only one gate and no second gate, and a gatekeeper is stationed there. The people enter and exit all through this gate. Whether entering or exiting, though the gatekeeper may not know the number of people, he knows that the people only pass through this gate, and there is no other place. In the same way, I know that the Buddhas of the past, the Tathāgatas, the Worthy Ones, the Perfectly Enlightened Ones, all cut off the five hindrances that afflict the mind, weaken the power of wisdom, belong to the category of obstacles, and do not lead toward nirvāṇa; they abided in the four foundations of mindfulness, cultivated the seven factors of awakening, and attained anuttarā-samyak-saṃbodhi. The Buddhas, World-Honored Ones, of the future will also cut off the five hindrances that afflict the mind, weaken the power of wisdom, belong to the category of obstacles, and do not lead toward nirvāṇa; they will abide in the four foundations of mindfulness, cultivate the seven factors of awakening, and attain anuttarā-samyak-saṃbodhi. The presently existing Buddhas, World-Honored Ones, the Tathāgatas, the Worthy Ones, the Perfectly Enlightened Ones, also cut off the five hindrances that afflict the mind, weaken the power of wisdom, belong to the category of obstacles, and do not lead toward nirvāṇa; they abide in the four foundations of mindfulness, cultivate the seven factors of awakening, and attain anuttarā-samyak-saṃbodhi."

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "So it is, so it is. Śāriputra, the Buddhas of the past, future, and present all cut off the five hindrances that afflict the mind, weaken the power of wisdom, belong to the category of obstacles, and do not lead toward nirvāṇa; they abide in the four foundations of mindfulness, cultivate the seven factors of awakening, and attain anuttarā-samyak-saṃbodhi."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Venerable Śāriputra, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (499)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, Venerable Śāriputra was on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Candraputta, who was a disciple of Devadatta. He went to Venerable Śāriputra. After exchanging greetings and courtesies, he withdrew and stood to one side. Having withdrawn and stood to one side, Venerable Śāriputra asked the bhikṣu Candraputta: "Does the bhikṣu Devadatta teach the Dharma for the bhikṣus?"

The bhikṣu Candraputta answered: "He teaches the Dharma."

Venerable Śāriputra asked the bhikṣu Candraputta: "How does Devadatta teach the Dharma?"

The bhikṣu Candraputta said to Venerable Śāriputra: "That Devadatta teaches the Dharma thus, saying: 'When a bhikṣu cultivates the mind through mental dharmas, that bhikṣu can declare of himself: "I have become free from desire, liberated from the five qualities of sensual pleasure."'"

Śāriputra said to the bhikṣu Candraputta: "Why does your Devadatta not teach the Dharma saying: 'When a bhikṣu well cultivates the mind through mental dharmas, with a mind free from desire, a mind free from anger, a mind free from delusion, attaining the state of non-greed, the state of non-anger, the state of non-delusion, with the dharma of not returning to desire-existence, form-existence, or formless-existence, that bhikṣu can declare of himself: "My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence"'?"

The bhikṣu Candraputta said: "He cannot do so, Venerable Śāriputra."

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra said to the bhikṣu Candraputta: "If a bhikṣu well cultivates the mind through mental dharmas, with a mind free from desire and lust, free from anger, free from delusion, attaining the state of non-greed, the state of non-anger, the state of non-delusion, that bhikṣu can declare of himself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'

"It is like a great stone mountain near a village, unbroken, undamaged, without holes, thick and dense. Even if wind comes from the east, it cannot move it, nor can it pass through to the west. Likewise with wind from the south, west, north, or the four intermediate directions: it cannot shake it, nor can it pass through. In the same way, when a bhikṣu well cultivates the mind through mental dharmas, with a mind free from desire and lust, free from anger, free from delusion, attaining the state of non-greed, the state of non-anger, the state of non-delusion, that bhikṣu can declare of himself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'

"It is like a pillar of indra copper, iron, and copper deeply driven into the ground, compacted until firm and solid: the winds from the four directions cannot shake it. In the same way, when a bhikṣu well cultivates the mind through mental dharmas, with a mind free from desire and lust, free from anger, free from delusion, attaining the state of non-greed, the state of non-anger, the state of non-delusion, that bhikṣu can declare of himself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'

"It is like a stone pillar sixteen cubits long with eight cubits driven into the ground: the winds from the four directions cannot shake it. In the same way, when a bhikṣu well cultivates the mind through mental dharmas, completely free from a mind of desire and lust, free from a mind of anger, free from a mind of delusion, attaining the state of non-greed, the state of non-anger, the state of non-delusion, he can declare of himself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'

"It is like fire that burns what has not yet been burned; once burned, it does not burn again. In the same way, when a bhikṣu cultivates the mind through mental dharmas, with a mind free from desire and lust, free from anger, free from delusion, attaining the state of non-greed, the state of non-anger, the state of non-delusion, he can declare of himself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know that I will not receive further existence.'"

When Śāriputra had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what he had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (500)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, Venerable Śāriputra was also dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, Venerable Śāriputra, in the early morning, put on his robes and took his bowl, entered Rājagṛha to beg for food. Having begged for food, he sat beneath a tree to eat.

At that time, a female non-Buddhist renunciant named Śucimukhī came out of Rājagṛha on some minor errand. She saw Venerable Śāriputra sitting beneath a tree eating. Having seen him, she asked: "Śramaṇa, are you eating?"

Venerable Śāriputra answered: "I am eating."

She further asked: "How so? Śramaṇa, do you eat with a downward-facing mouth?"

He answered: "No, sister."

She further asked: "Do you eat with an upward-facing mouth?"

He answered: "No, sister."

She further asked: "How so? Do you eat with a sideward-facing mouth?"

He answered: "No, sister."

She further asked: "Do you eat with a mouth facing the four intermediate directions?"

He answered: "No, sister."

She further asked: "I asked the śramaṇa if he is eating, and you answered 'I am eating.' I asked 'with an upward-facing mouth?' and you answered 'no.' 'With a downward-facing mouth?' and you answered 'no.' 'With a sideward-facing mouth?' and you answered 'no.' 'With a mouth facing the four intermediate directions?' and you answered 'no.' What is the meaning of what you have said?"

Venerable Śāriputra said: "Sister, there are śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who are skilled in divination, skilled in reading omens, and who seek food through wrong livelihood. Such śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas eat with a downward-facing mouth. If there are śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who look upward to read the stars and celestial signs, seeking food through wrong livelihood, such śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas eat with an upward-facing mouth. If there are śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who serve as messengers for others, seeking food through wrong livelihood, such śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas eat with a sideward-facing mouth. If there are śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who practice various medical arts and treat various diseases, seeking food through wrong livelihood, such śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas eat with a mouth facing the four intermediate directions. Sister, I do not fall into any of these four kinds of wrong livelihood to seek food. Rather, sister, I only seek food through the Dharma and sustain myself. Therefore I say I do not eat through any of these four kinds."

At that time, the female non-Buddhist renunciant Śucimukhī, having heard what Venerable Śāriputra said, rejoiced and went on her way.

Then the female non-Buddhist renunciant Śucimukhī, in the lanes and crossroads of Rājagṛha, praised and proclaimed: "The śramaṇas of the Śākya clan sustain themselves through pure livelihood, through the most pure livelihood! Anyone who wishes to make offerings should make offerings to the śramaṇas of the Śākya clan. Anyone who wishes to make merit should make merit at the place of the śramaṇas of the Śākya clan."

At that time, there were other non-Buddhist renunciants who heard the female non-Buddhist renunciant Śucimukhī praising the śramaṇas of the Śākya clan. Out of jealousy and envy, they killed the female non-Buddhist renunciant Śucimukhī. After her death, she was reborn in the Tuṣita heaven, because of the faith she had generated toward Venerable Śāriputra.

---

### (501)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana was in Rājagṛha on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

At that time, Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana addressed the bhikṣus: "Once, the World-Honored One was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove, and I was dwelling on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa. Alone in a quiet place, I had this thought: 'What is the noble silence?' I further thought: 'If a bhikṣu stills thought and examination, with inner purity and unified mind, without thought and without examination, with the joy and bliss born of samādhi, dwells having fully accomplished the second dhyāna, this is called the noble silence.' I further thought: 'I too should now practice the noble silence, stilling thought and examination, with inner purity and unified mind, without thought and without examination, with the joy and bliss born of samādhi, dwelling fully accomplished, abiding extensively. Having abided extensively, thoughts with thought and examination arose again.' At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing my thoughts, from the Bamboo Grove monastery disappeared and appeared before me on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa, and said to me: 'Maudgalyāyana, you should practice the noble silence. Do not be negligent.' Having heard the World-Honored One's instruction, I again stilled thought and examination, with inner purity and unified mind, without thought and without examination, with the joy and bliss born of samādhi, and dwelt having fully accomplished the second dhyāna. In this way three times, the Buddha also three times instructed me: 'You should practice the noble silence. Do not be negligent.' I then again stilled thought and examination, with inner purity and unified mind, without thought and without examination, with the joy and bliss born of samādhi, and dwelt having fully accomplished the second dhyāna.

"If one is to rightly say who is the Buddha's son, born from the Buddha's mouth, born from transformation through the Dharma, having received the Buddha's share of the Dharma, then it is I myself. Why is this? I am the Buddha's son, born from the Buddha's mouth, born from transformation through the Dharma, having received the Buddha's share of the Dharma. With little effort, I attain dhyāna, liberation, samādhi, and samāpatti. Just as the eldest son of a wheel-turning sage-king, though not yet anointed, has already received the king's principles, and without laborious effort can
