# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Nineteen

---

### (504)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana was on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

At that time, Śakra, Lord of the Devas, residing in his supreme and wonderful hall-observatory, came by night to the place of the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, bowed his head in homage at his feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. At that time, the radiance of Śakra, Lord of the Devas, illuminated Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa all around with great brilliance. Then, after Śakra, Lord of the Devas, had seated himself, he spoke this verse:

> "One who can subdue the stain of miserliness,
> A person of great virtue who gives according to the occasion,
> This one is called the wise among givers,
> And in future lives will see the supremely excellent."

Then Mahāmaudgalyāyana asked Śakra, saying: "Kauśika! What does it mean to subdue the stain of miserliness and see the supremely excellent, such that you speak thus:

> "'One who can subdue the stain of miserliness,
> A person of great virtue who gives according to the occasion,
> This one is called the wise among givers,
> And in future lives will see the supremely excellent'?"

Then the Lord of the Devas, Śakra, replied: "Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana! Surpassing the great brahmin clans, surpassing the great kṣatriya clans, surpassing the great elder clans, surpassing the Four Great Kings' Heaven, surpassing the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, they bow their heads and pay reverence. Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana! I am one whom the great brahmin clans, the great kṣatriya clans, the great elder clans, the Four Great Kings' Heaven, and the Heaven of the Thirty-Three respectfully venerate with homage. Seeing this fruit of reward, I therefore spoke this verse.

"Furthermore, Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana! Extending to the range where the sun travels, shining upon all directions, reaching a thousand worlds: a thousand moons, a thousand suns, a thousand Mount Sumerus, a thousand Pūrvavidehas, a thousand Uttarakurus, a thousand Aparagodānīyas, a thousand Jambudvīpas, a thousand Four Great Kings' Heavens, a thousand Trāyastriṃśa Heavens, Yāma Heavens, Tuṣita Heavens, Nirmāṇarati Heavens, Paranirmitavaśavartin Heavens, and a thousand Brahmā Heavens: this is called a small thousandfold world. Within this small thousandfold world, there is no hall-observatory equal to the Vaijayanta hall-observatory. The Vaijayanta has one hundred and one towers, each tower has seven stories, each story has seven chambers, each chamber has seven celestial queens, and each queen has seven attendant maidens. Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana! In the entire small thousandfold world, there is no hall-observatory as magnificent and beautiful as the Vaijayanta. I see that it is through subduing miserliness that this wonderful reward exists, and therefore I spoke that verse."

Mahāmaudgalyāyana said to Śakra: "Excellent! Excellent! Kauśika! You are able to see this supreme and wonderful fruit of reward and therefore spoke that verse."

Then Śakra, Lord of the Devas, having heard what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, and suddenly vanished.

---

### (505)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha. At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana was in Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, alone in a quiet place, was meditating and contemplating. He had this thought: "In the past, at one time, Śakra, Lord of the Devas, in the stone cave at the Vediyaka Mountain, asked the World-Honored One about the meaning of liberation through the ending of craving. The World-Honored One explained it, and upon hearing it, Śakra rejoiced. It seemed as though he wished to ask further about the meaning. I should now go and ask him about the intention behind his rejoicing." Having had this thought, just as a strong man might flex and extend his arm, he vanished from Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa and appeared in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, standing not far from the Puṇḍarīka Pool.

At that time, the Lord of the Devas, Śakra, together with five hundred celestial maidens, was sporting and bathing in the pool. There were celestial women whose voices were wondrously beautiful. Then Śakra, seeing the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana from afar, said to the celestial women: "Stop singing! Stop singing!" Thereupon the celestial women immediately fell silent. The Lord of the Devas, Śakra, then went to the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, bowed his head in homage at his feet, and withdrew to stand to one side.

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana asked Śakra: "Previously, at the Vediyaka Mountain, you asked the World-Honored One about the meaning of liberation through the ending of craving. Upon hearing it, you rejoiced. What was your intention? Did you rejoice because of what you heard explained? Or did you rejoice because you wished to ask something further?"

The Lord of the Devas, Śakra, said to the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "We of the Thirty-Three Heaven are much attached to the pleasures of heedlessness. Sometimes I recall past events, and sometimes I do not. The World-Honored One is now in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. If the Venerable wishes to know what I previously asked about at the Vediyaka Mountain, you may now go and ask the World-Honored One. As the World-Honored One explains, you should receive and uphold it. However, here I have a fine hall-observatory, newly completed not long ago. You may enter and view it."

Then the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana silently accepted the invitation and entered the hall-observatory together with the Lord of the Devas, Śakra. Those celestial women, seeing Śakra approaching from afar, all played celestial music; some sang and some danced. The celestial women, adorned with bodily ornaments and jeweled adornments, produced wondrous sounds harmonizing with the five types of music, like skillfully performed music with no discordant sounds. When the celestial women saw the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, they all felt ashamed and retreated to hide within their chambers.

Then the Lord of the Devas, Śakra, said to the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "Behold this hall-observatory! The ground is level and smooth, and the walls, pillars, beams, multi-storied pavilions, windows, nets, curtains, and screens are all magnificently adorned."

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said to Śakra: "Kauśika! It is through previously cultivating wholesome dharmas and the causes and conditions of merit that this wonderful fruit has been achieved."

In this manner, Śakra three times praised it himself and asked the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, and the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana also answered three times in the same way.

Then the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana had this thought: "This Śakra is now extremely heedless, attached to the pleasures of the sense-realm, praising this hall-observatory. I should cause his mind to generate disenchantment." He then entered samādhi, and by his supernormal power, with one toe he nudged the hall-observatory and caused the entire structure to shake and tremble. Then the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana vanished and was no longer seen.

The multitude of celestial women, seeing the hall-observatory shaking, swaying, quaking, and trembling, were terrified and frightened. They ran about east and west, and said to Śakra: "Is this Kauśika's great teacher who has such great meritorious power?"

Then the Lord of the Devas, Śakra, said to the celestial women: "This is not my teacher. This is a disciple of the Great Teacher, Mahāmaudgalyāyana, one of pure brahmacarya practice, of great virtue and great power."

The celestial women said: "How wonderful, Kauśika! That you have such a fellow practitioner of brahmacarya, of great virtue and great power. How much greater must the virtue and power of the Great Teacher himself be?"

---

### (506)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, upon the Pāṇḍukambala stone, not far from the Pārijāta and Kovidāra fragrant trees, spending the rains retreat there, teaching the Dharma for his mother and the devas of the Thirty-Three. At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana was spending the rains retreat at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, the park of Anāthapiṇḍada.

At that time, the fourfold assembly came to the place of the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, bowed their heads in homage at his feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and said to the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "Do you know where the World-Honored One is spending his rains retreat?"

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana replied: "I have heard that the World-Honored One is in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, upon the Pāṇḍukambala stone, not far from the Pārijāta and Kovidāra fragrant trees, spending the rains retreat, teaching the Dharma for his mother and the devas of the Thirty-Three."

At that time, the fourfold assembly, having heard what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said, rejoiced and expressed their approval. Each rose from their seat, paid homage, and departed.

After the fourfold assembly had completed the three-month rains retreat, they again came to the place of the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, bowed their heads in homage at his feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. Then the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, for the fourfold assembly, taught the Dharma in various ways: instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening them. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened them, he sat in silence.

Then the fourfold assembly rose from their seats, bowed their heads in homage, and said to the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana! You should know that we have not seen the World-Honored One for a long time. The assembly yearns greatly and thirsts to see the World-Honored One. Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, if you would not mind the trouble, we wish that for our sake you would go to the Heaven of the Thirty-Three and on our behalf convey greetings to the World-Honored One, asking: 'Are you free from illness, free from affliction? Is your daily life easy and comfortable? Do you dwell in ease and happiness?' Also, please tell the World-Honored One: 'The fourfold assembly of Jambudvīpa wishes to see the World-Honored One, but we lack the supernormal power to ascend to the Heaven of the Thirty-Three to pay reverence to the World-Honored One. The devas of the Thirty-Three themselves have the supernormal power to come down to the human world. We humbly wish that the World-Honored One would return to Jambudvīpa, out of compassion.'"

Then the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana silently consented.

When the fourfold assembly knew that the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana had silently consented, each rose from their seat, paid homage, and departed.

Then the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, knowing the fourfold assembly had left, entered samādhi. In accordance with his concentration attainment, just as a strong man might flex and extend his arm, he vanished from Śrāvastī and appeared in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, upon the Pāṇḍukambala stone, not far from the Pārijāta and Kovidāra fragrant trees. At that time, the World-Honored One, surrounded by an immeasurable retinue of the devas of the Thirty-Three, was teaching the Dharma.

Then the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, seeing the World-Honored One from afar, was elated with joy and had this thought: "Today, the World-Honored One, surrounded by a great assembly of devas, teaching the Dharma, is no different from an assembly in Jambudvīpa."

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing the thought in the mind of the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, said to the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "Mahāmaudgalyāyana! It is not by one's own power. When I wish to teach the Dharma to the devas, they come and gather. When I wish them to go, they depart. They come according to my intention and go according to my intention."

Then the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and said to the World-Honored One: "Among the various great assemblies of devas gathered here like clouds, are there among these deva assemblies those who, having previously heard the Dharma taught by the Buddha, the World-Honored One, attained unbreakable faith, and upon the breaking up of the body at the end of life, were reborn here?"

The Buddha told the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "Just so, just so. Among the various devas gathered here like clouds, there are those who in past lives heard the Dharma and attained unbreakable faith in the Buddha, unbreakable faith in the Dharma, unbreakable faith in the Sangha, and fulfilled the noble precepts, and upon the breaking up of the body at the end of life, were reborn here."

At that time, the Lord of the Devas, Śakra, seeing the World-Honored One and the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana praising and explaining, after they had finished speaking with the deva assembly, said to the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "Just so, just so, Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana! Among the various assemblies here, all attained in past lives, having once heard the true Dharma, unbreakable faith in the Buddha, unbreakable faith in the Dharma, unbreakable faith in the Sangha, and fulfilled the noble precepts, and upon the breaking up of the body at the end of life, were reborn here."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu, seeing the World-Honored One, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, and the Lord of the Devas, Śakra, speaking words of mutual agreement and approval, said to the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "Just so, just so, Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana! Among the various devas who have come to this assembly, all in past lives once heard the true Dharma, attained unbreakable faith in the Buddha, unbreakable faith in the Dharma, unbreakable faith in the Sangha, and fulfilled the noble precepts, and upon the breaking up of the body at the end of life, were reborn here."

At that time, a certain deva rose from his seat, arranged his garments, bared his right shoulder, joined his palms, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I too have accomplished unbreakable faith in the Buddha, and therefore was reborn here."

Another deva said: "I attained unbreakable faith in the Dharma."

Others said they attained unbreakable faith in the Sangha, and others said they had fulfilled the noble precepts, and therefore were reborn here. Thus, innumerable thousands of devas, each before the World-Honored One, personally declared that they had attained the fruit of stream-entry, and then all vanished from the Buddha's presence.

At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, knowing the deva assembly had departed, shortly after rose from his seat, arranged his garments, bared his right shoulder, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The fourfold assembly of Jambudvīpa bows their heads in reverence at the World-Honored One's feet and conveys greetings, asking: 'Are you free from illness, free from affliction? Is your daily life easy and comfortable? Do you dwell in ease and happiness?' The fourfold assembly yearns and wishes to see the World-Honored One. They also say to the World-Honored One: 'We in the human world lack the supernormal power to ascend to the Heaven of the Thirty-Three to pay reverence to the World-Honored One. However, those devas possess great meritorious power and are fully able to come down to Jambudvīpa. We humbly wish that the World-Honored One would return to Jambudvīpa, out of compassion for the fourfold assembly.'"

The Buddha told Maudgalyāyana: "You may return there and tell the people of Jambudvīpa: 'After seven days, the World-Honored One will descend from the Heaven of the Thirty-Three and return to Jambudvīpa, at the city of Saṃkāśya, outside the outer gate, beneath the Udumbara tree.'"

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, having received the World-Honored One's instruction, entered samādhi. Just as a strong man might flex and extend his arm, he vanished from the Heaven of the Thirty-Three and arrived in Jambudvīpa, and announced to the fourfold assembly: "All of you should know: after seven days, the World-Honored One will descend from the Heaven of the Thirty-Three and return to Jambudvīpa, at the city of Saṃkāśya, outside the outer gate, beneath the Udumbara tree."

As promised, on the seventh day, the World-Honored One descended from the Heaven of the Thirty-Three to Jambudvīpa, at the city of Saṃkāśya, beneath the Udumbara tree. Devas, nāgas, ghosts and spirits, and even Brahmā, all came down following him. At that very time, this assembly was named "The Place of the Descent from Heaven."

---

### (507)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, forty deva sons came to the place of the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, bowed in homage, and withdrew to sit to one side.

Then the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said to the deva sons: "Excellent, deva sons! You have accomplished unbreakable faith in the Buddha, unbreakable faith in the Dharma and the Sangha."

Then the forty deva sons rose from their seats, arranged their garments, bared their right shoulders, joined their palms, and said to the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "We attained unbreakable faith in the Buddha, unbreakable faith in the Dharma and the Sangha, and fulfilled the noble precepts, and therefore were reborn in heaven." One deva said: "I attained unbreakable faith in the Buddha." Another said: "I attained unbreakable faith in the Dharma." Another said: "I attained unbreakable faith in the Sangha." Another said: "I fulfilled the noble precepts, and upon the breaking up of the body at the end of life, was reborn in heaven."

Then the forty deva sons, before the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, each personally declared that they had attained the fruit of stream-entry, and then vanished and were no longer seen. As with the forty deva sons, so likewise four hundred, eight hundred, and ten thousand deva sons also spoke in the same way.

---

### (508)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana and the Venerable Lakṣaṇa Bhikṣu were together on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

The Venerable Lakṣaṇa went early in the morning to the place of the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana and said to the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "Let us go out from Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa and enter Rājagṛha to beg for food." Then the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana silently consented, and they went out together from Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa and entered Rājagṛha to beg for food. When they reached a certain place, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana had something on his mind and smiled joyfully.

The Venerable Lakṣaṇa, seeing his smile, asked the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "When the Buddha or the Buddha's disciples smile joyfully, it is not without cause and condition. Venerable one, for what cause and condition do you smile joyfully today?"

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said: "The question is untimely. Let us first enter Rājagṛha to beg for food. When we return, before the World-Honored One, you should ask about this matter. That will be the proper time to ask, and I will explain it to you."

Then the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana and the Venerable Lakṣaṇa entered Rājagṛha to beg for food and returned, washed their feet, put away their robes and bowls, and together went to the Buddha's place, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The Venerable Lakṣaṇa asked the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "This morning, when you and I went out together from Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa to beg for food, you smiled joyfully at a certain place. I asked you about the cause and condition of your smile, and you answered me saying the question was untimely. Now I ask you again: for what cause and condition did you smile joyfully?"

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said to the Venerable Lakṣaṇa: "On the road I saw a being whose body was as large as a tower, crying and wailing, weeping with grief and pain, traveling through the air. Seeing this, I had this thought: 'Such a being has received such a body and suffers such grief and great pain.' Therefore I smiled."

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Excellent! Excellent! Among my śrāvakas, there are those who dwell in the true eye, true knowledge, true meaning, and true Dharma, with definitive penetration, and see such beings. I too have seen this being, but the reason I did not speak of it is that I feared people would not believe. Why is that? If there are those who do not believe what the Tathāgata says, those foolish people will suffer through the long night."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "In past lives, that large-bodied being was in this city of Rājagṛha a butcher's son who slaughtered cattle. Because of the karmic cause of slaughtering cattle, for hundreds of thousands of years he fell into the hells. Having emerged from the hells, due to the residual karma of slaughtering cattle, he received such a body and constantly suffers such grief, affliction, and pain. Thus, bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (509)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana and the Venerable Lakṣaṇa were on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

The Venerable Lakṣaṇa went early in the morning to the place of the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana and said to the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "Let us go out from Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa and enter Rājagṛha to beg for food." The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana silently consented, and they went out together from Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa and entered Rājagṛha to beg for food.

When they reached a certain place, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana had something on his mind and smiled joyfully. The Venerable Lakṣaṇa, seeing the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana smile, asked: "Venerable one! When the Buddha or the Buddha's śrāvaka disciples smile joyfully, it is not without cause and condition. Venerable one, for what cause and condition do you smile joyfully today?"

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said: "The question is untimely. Let us first beg for food. When we return, before the World-Honored One, you should ask about this matter. That will be the proper time to ask."

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana and the Venerable Lakṣaṇa together entered the city to beg for food. Having eaten, they returned, washed their feet, put away their robes and bowls, and together went to the Buddha's place, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The Venerable Lakṣaṇa asked the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "This morning when you and I together entered Rājagṛha to beg for food, you smiled joyfully at a certain place. I asked you about the cause and condition of your smile, and you answered me saying the question was untimely. Now I ask you: for what cause and condition did you smile joyfully?"

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said to the Venerable Lakṣaṇa: "On the road I saw a being, sinews and bones connected, the entire body impure, foul and loathsome, with crows, kites, eagles, vultures, jackals, and hungry dogs following and seizing it to eat, or probing from the ribs into the internal organs to extract and devour them, in extreme pain, crying and wailing. Seeing this, I immediately thought: 'Such a being has received such a body and suffers such unbeneficial suffering.'"

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Excellent, bhikṣus! Among my śrāvakas, there are those who dwell in the true eye, true knowledge, true meaning, and true Dharma, with definitive penetration, and see such beings. I too have seen this being, but the reason I did not speak of it is that I feared they would not believe. Why is that? If there are those who do not believe what the Tathāgata says, those foolish people will suffer through the long night, receiving unbeneficial suffering. Bhikṣus, this being, in past lives, in this city of Rājagṛha, was an apprentice butcher of cattle. Because of the sin of slaughtering cattle, for hundreds of thousands of years he fell into the hells and suffered immeasurable pain. Due to the residual karma of that evil conduct of slaughtering cattle, he now has received this body and continues to suffer such unbeneficial pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (510)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana and the Venerable Lakṣaṇa were on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

The Venerable Lakṣaṇa went early in the morning to the place of the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana and said to the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "Let us go out from Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa and enter Rājagṛha to beg for food." The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana silently consented, and they went out together from Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa and entered Rājagṛha to beg for food.

When they reached a certain place, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana had something on his mind and smiled joyfully. The Venerable Lakṣaṇa, seeing the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana smile, asked: "Venerable one! When the Buddha or the Buddha's śrāvaka disciples smile joyfully, it is not without cause and condition. Venerable one, for what cause and condition do you smile joyfully today?"

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said: "The question is untimely. Let us first beg for food. When we return, before the World-Honored One, you should ask about this matter. That will be the proper time to ask."

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana and the Venerable Lakṣaṇa together entered the city to beg for food. Having finished, they returned, washed their feet, put away their robes and bowls, and together went to the Buddha's place, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The Venerable Lakṣaṇa asked the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "This morning when we together entered Rājagṛha to beg for food, you smiled joyfully at a certain place. I asked you about the cause and condition of your smile, and you answered me saying the question was untimely. Now I ask you: for what cause and condition did you smile joyfully?"

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said to Lakṣaṇa: "On the road I saw a large-bodied being, entirely without skin, a sheer mass of flesh, traveling through the air, with crows, kites, eagles, vultures, jackals, and hungry dogs following and seizing it to eat, or probing from the ribs into the internal organs to extract and devour them, in agonizing pain, crying and wailing. I then thought: 'Such a being has received such a body, suffering such unbeneficial pain.'"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Excellent, bhikṣus! Among my śrāvakas, there are those who dwell in the true eye, true knowledge, true meaning, and true Dharma, with definitive penetration, and see such beings. I too have seen this being, but the reason I did not speak of it is that I feared they would not believe. Why is that? If there are those who do not believe what the Tathāgata says, those foolish people will suffer through the long night, receiving unbeneficial suffering. Bhikṣus, this being, in past lives, in this city of Rājagṛha, was a sheep butcher. Because of this sin, for hundreds of thousands of years he fell into the hells and suffered immeasurable pain. Now, having obtained this body, due to residual karma he continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (511)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, and so forth, until the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana on the road saw a large-bodied being, entirely without skin, in form like dried meat, traveling through the air. And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this city of Rājagṛha, was an apprentice sheep butcher. Because of the sin of slaughtering sheep, for hundreds of thousands of years he fell into the hells and suffered immeasurable pain. Now, having obtained this body, he continues to suffer the consequences of this sin. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (512)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, and so forth, until on the road he saw a large-bodied being, entirely without skin, in form like a mass of flesh, traveling through the air. And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this city of Rājagṛha, caused the abortion of her own fetus. Because of this sin, she fell into the hells for hundreds of thousands of years and suffered immeasurable pain. Due to the residual karma, she now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (513)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, and so forth, until the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana on the road saw a large-bodied being, whose entire body was covered with hair, and the hairs were like large needles, and the needles were all ablaze with fire, burning back into his body, with pain penetrating to the marrow of his bones. And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this city of Rājagṛha, was an elephant trainer. Because of this sin, for hundreds of thousands of years he fell into the hells and suffered immeasurable pain. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

Just as with the elephant trainer, so too with a horse trainer, a cattle trainer, one who delights in slander, and all those who torment others in various ways: it is likewise.

---

### (514)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, and so forth, until the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana on the road saw a large-bodied being, whose entire body was covered with hair, the hairs sharp as knives, the hairs ablaze with fire, cutting back into his body, with pain penetrating to the marrow of his bones. And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this city of Rājagṛha, delighted in fighting and warfare, wounding people with swords and blades. For hundreds of thousands of years he fell into the hells and suffered immeasurable pain. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (515)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, and so forth, until the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana on the road saw a large-bodied being, whose entire body was covered with hair, the hairs resembling arrows, all ablaze with fire, burning back into his body, with pain penetrating to the marrow of his bones. And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this city of Rājagṛha, had been a hunter, shooting birds and beasts. Because of this sin, for hundreds of thousands of years he fell into the hells and suffered immeasurable pain. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (516)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, and so forth, until: "On the road I saw a large-bodied being, whose entire body was covered with hair, the hairs like lances and spears, the hairs all ablaze with fire, burning back into his body, with pain penetrating to the marrow of his bones." And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this city of Rājagṛha, was a pig butcher, who slaughtered pigs by spearing them. Because of this sin, for hundreds of thousands of years he fell into the hells and suffered immeasurable pain. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (517)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, and so forth, until: "On the road I saw a large-bodied headless being, with eyes growing on both sides and a mouth growing on its chest. Blood constantly flowed from its body, and various insects gnawed and ate at it, with pain penetrating to the marrow of its bones." And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this city of Rājagṛha, delighted in cutting off people's heads. Because of this sin, for hundreds of thousands of years he fell into the hells and suffered immeasurable pain. Now, having obtained this body, he continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with cutting off people's heads, so too with holding heads: it is likewise.

---

### (518)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha. And so forth, until: "On the road I saw a being whose scrotum and testicles were as large as a water jar. When sitting, he would squat upon them; when walking, he would carry them on his shoulders." And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in Rājagṛha, was a coppersmith who made counterfeit vessels to cheat people. Because of this sin, he had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the coppersmith, so too with those who cheat people with measures and scales, village headmen, and market inspectors: it is likewise.

---

### (519)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha. And so forth, until: on the road he saw a being who had wrapped his own body in a copper and iron net, constantly blazing with fire, burning back into his body, with pain penetrating to the marrow of his bones, traveling through the air. The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this city of Rājagṛha, was a fisherman. Because of this sin, he had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has received this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the fisherman, so too with a bird catcher and a rabbit trapper: it is likewise.

---

### (520)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha. And so forth, until: on the road he saw a being who had an iron millstone on the crown of his head, full of blazing fire, turning and grinding his head, traveling through the air, suffering immeasurable pain. And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this city of Rājagṛha, was a woman fortune-teller, who turned divination boards to perform divination, deceiving and deluding people in order to obtain wealth. Because of this sin, she had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, she now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (521)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha. And so forth, until: on the road he saw a being whose body was spinning alone, like a whirlwind, traveling through the air. And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this city of Rājagṛha, was a male fortune-teller, who deceived and deluded many people in order to obtain wealth. Because of this sin, he had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (522)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha. And so forth, until: on the road he saw a being, hunched over and moving furtively, as if in terror, whose entire body and clothing were all ablaze with fire, burning back into his body, traveling through the air. The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this city of Rājagṛha, was fond of committing adultery. Because of this sin, he had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (523)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park. At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana and the Venerable Lakṣaṇa Bhikṣu, early in the morning, together entered the city of Vārāṇasī to beg for food. On the road, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana was contemplating and reflecting, and smiled joyfully.

Then the Venerable Lakṣaṇa said to the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "When the World-Honored One or disciples of the World-Honored One smile joyfully, there must be a cause and condition. For what cause and condition does the Venerable smile joyfully today?"

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said to the Venerable Lakṣaṇa: "This question is untimely. Let us first beg for food. When we return, before the World-Honored One, you should ask about this matter."

Then they both entered the city to beg for food. Having returned, they washed their feet, put away their robes and bowls, and together went to the World-Honored One, bowed their heads in homage at his feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. Then the Venerable Lakṣaṇa asked the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "This morning on the road, for what cause and condition did you smile joyfully?"

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said to the Venerable Lakṣaṇa: "On the road I saw a large-bodied being, the entire body festering and decayed, foul and impure, traveling through the air, with crows, kites, eagles, vultures, jackals, and hungry dogs following and seizing it to eat, crying and wailing. I thought: 'That a being could receive such a body and suffer such pain, how very painful!'"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "I too have seen this being, but the reason I did not speak of it is that I feared they would not believe. Why is that? If there are those who do not believe what the Tathāgata says, those foolish people will suffer through the long night. This being, in past lives, in this city of Vārāṇasī, was a woman who made a living by selling her body. At that time, there was a bhikṣu who had gone forth under the Buddha Kāśyapa. That woman, with an impure mind, invited that bhikṣu. The bhikṣu, with a straightforward mind, accepted the invitation and did not understand her intention. The woman became angry and splashed the bhikṣu's body with unclean water. Because of this sin, she had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, she now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (524)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park. And so forth, until: "On the road I saw a large-bodied being, the entire body ablaze with fire, traveling through the air, crying and wailing, suffering various pains." And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this city of Vārāṇasī, was the chief queen of the sovereign king. While sleeping with the king, she arose with angry thoughts and poured burning lamp oil on the king's body. Because of this sin, she had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, she now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (525)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park. And so forth, until the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said: "On the road I saw a being, the entire body covered and smeared with excrement, also eating excrement, traveling through the air, foul and suffering, crying and wailing." And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this city of Vārāṇasī, was a brahmin, the teacher of the sovereign king. With a jealous and hateful mind, he invited the Sangha of śrāvakas of the Buddha Kāśyapa and placed excrement beneath the food in the rice, to test and vex the Sangha. Because of this sin, he had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (526)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, the park of Anāthapiṇḍada. And so forth, until the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said: "On the road I saw a large-bodied being, who had a great copper cauldron on his head, blazing with fire, filled with molten copper flowing down over his body, traveling through the air, crying and wailing." And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this Śrāvastī, had gone forth under the Buddha Kāśyapa and served as the monastery steward bhikṣu. A donor sent oil that should have been distributed to the bhikṣus. At that time, there were many visiting bhikṣus. The steward bhikṣu did not distribute the oil in time, waiting until the visiting bhikṣus had departed before distributing it. Because of this sin, he had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (527)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, the park of Anāthapiṇḍada. And so forth, until the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said: "On the road I saw a large-bodied being, who had blazing hot iron balls entering and exiting his body, traveling through the air, in agonizing pain, crying and wailing." And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this Śrāvastī, had gone forth under the Dharma of the Buddha Kāśyapa and served as a śrāmaṇera. He was assigned to guard the Sangha's fruit garden. He stole seven fruits and offered them to his preceptor. Because of this sin, he had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (528)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī. And so forth, until the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said: "On the road I saw a large-bodied being, whose tongue was broad and long. There appeared sharp adzes, blazing with fire, which adze-planed his tongue, traveling through the air, crying and wailing." And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this Śrāvastī, had gone forth under the Dharma of the Buddha Kāśyapa and served as a śrāmaṇera. He used an axe to break up rock-sugar to provide for the Sangha. What stuck to the axe blade, he stole and ate. Because of this sin, he entered the hells and suffered immeasurable pain. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (529)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī. And so forth, until the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said: "On the road I saw a large-bodied being, who had twin iron wheels beneath both armpits, blazing and spinning, burning back into his body, traveling through the air, crying and wailing." And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this Śrāvastī, had gone forth under the Dharma of the Buddha Kāśyapa and served as a śrāmaṇera. He was carrying rock-sugar cakes to provide for the Sangha, and he stole two cakes and hid them under his arms. Because of this sin, he had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (530)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī. And so forth, until the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said: "On the road I saw a large-bodied being, wrapped in blazing iron sheets around his body, whose clothing, bedding, and resting place were all of hot iron, blazing with fire, and who ate hot iron balls, traveling through the air, crying and wailing." And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this Śrāvastī, had gone forth under the Dharma of the Buddha Kāśyapa and served as a bhikṣu. He solicited robes and food for the Sangha, but after providing for the Sangha, he freely used the remainder for himself. Because of this sin, he had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with a bhikṣu, so too with a bhikṣuṇī, a śikṣamāṇā, a śrāmaṇera, a śrāmaṇerī, an upāsaka, and an upāsikā: it is likewise.

### (531)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī. And so forth, until the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said: "On the road I saw a large-bodied being, with a blazing iron cart yoked to his neck. They pulled out the sinews of his neck and connected them with the sinews of all four legs to harness his neck. He walked upon burning hot iron ground, traveling through the air, crying and wailing." And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this Śrāvastī, made a living by driving an ox-cart. Because of this sin, he suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (532)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī. And so forth, until the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said: "On the road I saw a large-bodied being, whose tongue was long and broad, with blazing iron nails nailing his tongue, traveling through the air, crying and wailing." And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this Śrāvastī, had gone forth under the Dharma of the Buddha Kāśyapa and served as a bhikṣu who was the monastery steward. He rebuked the bhikṣus saying: 'Venerable ones! You should all leave this place. It is meager and cannot provide for you. Each of you should go as you please, seeking a prosperous and pleasant place, abundant in robes and food, with robes, food, bedding, and medicines for illness readily available without shortage.' The bhikṣus who had been residing there all abandoned the place and left. Visiting bhikṣus, hearing of this, also did not come. Because of this sin, he had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (533)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī. And so forth, until the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said: "On the road I saw a large-bodied being, in the appearance of a bhikṣu, entirely clad in iron sheets as clothing. His whole body was ablaze with fire, and he also had an iron bowl filled with hot iron balls which he ate." And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this Śrāvastī, had gone forth under the Dharma of the Buddha Kāśyapa and served as a bhikṣu who was the monastery steward. He used abusive speech to insult and defame the bhikṣus, saying such things as: 'This one is a wicked bald-head, this one has evil and unruly ways, this one has evil robes.' Because of his abusive speech, those who had been residing there departed, and those who had not yet come did not come. Because of this sin, he had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (534)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī. And so forth, until the Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This being, in past lives, in this Śrāvastī, had gone forth under the Dharma of the Buddha Kāśyapa and served as a bhikṣu. He was fond of stirring up disputes and quarrels, causing discord in the Sangha, creating all manner of verbal strife, making the Sangha lose harmony. The bhikṣus who had been residing there, feeling disgusted, abandoned the place and left. Those who had not yet come did not come. Because of this sin, he had already suffered immeasurable pain in the hells. Due to residual karma from the hells, he now has obtained this body and continues to suffer this pain. Bhikṣus, what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana saw is true and not different. You should receive and uphold this."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (535)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, the park of Anāthapiṇḍada. At that time, the Venerable Anuruddha was staying at the Pine Forest Monastery. At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana was staying in the Vajji village, at the Śaśamāra Hill, in a fearsome dense forest inhabited by wild animals.

At that time, the Venerable Anuruddha, alone in a quiet place, was meditating and contemplating. He had this thought: "There is a one-vehicle path that purifies beings, removes sorrow, grief, affliction, and suffering, and leads to the attainment of the true Dharma: namely, the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body as body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas as dharmas. Those who are distant from the four foundations of mindfulness are distant from the noble Dharma. Those who are distant from the noble Dharma are distant from the noble path. Those who are distant from the noble path are distant from the ambrosia Dharma. Those who are distant from the ambrosia Dharma are unable to be liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, affliction, and suffering. Those who have faith and delight in the four foundations of mindfulness have faith and delight in the noble Dharma. Those who have faith and delight in the noble Dharma have faith and delight in the noble path. Those who have faith and delight in the noble path have faith and delight in the ambrosia Dharma. Those who have faith and delight in the ambrosia Dharma attain liberation from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, affliction, and suffering."

At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, knowing the thoughts in the mind of the Venerable Anuruddha, just as a strong man might flex and extend his arm, by supernormal power vanished from the Vajji village, from the Śaśamāra Hill, from the fearsome dense forest inhabited by wild animals, and appeared before the Venerable Anuruddha at the Pine Forest Monastery in Śrāvastī. He said to Anuruddha: "You were alone in a quiet place, meditating and contemplating, and you had this thought: 'There is a one-vehicle path that causes beings to be purified, to be liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, affliction, and suffering, and to attain the true Dharma: namely, the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body as body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas as dharmas. Those who do not de
