# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Twenty

---

### (537)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana and Venerable Anuruddha were staying in Śrāvastī beside the Hasta-constructed Bathing Pool.

Venerable Śāriputra went to Venerable Anuruddha's place, and after they had exchanged greetings and courtesies, he sat to one side. Venerable Śāriputra said to Venerable Anuruddha: "How wonderful, Anuruddha! You possess great virtue and spiritual power. Through the cultivation and repeated cultivation of what merit have you been able to attain this?"

Venerable Anuruddha said to Venerable Śāriputra: "Through the cultivation and repeated cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness I have achieved this great virtue and spiritual power. What are the four foundations of mindfulness? Internally contemplating the body as body, with diligent effort, right mindfulness and right awareness, subduing worldly desire and sorrow. Likewise, externally contemplating the body, internally and externally contemplating the body; internally contemplating feelings, externally contemplating feelings, internally and externally contemplating feelings; internally contemplating the mind, externally contemplating the mind, internally and externally contemplating the mind; internally contemplating dharmas, externally contemplating dharmas, internally and externally contemplating dharmas as a foundation of mindfulness, with diligent effort, right mindfulness and right awareness, thus subduing worldly desire and sorrow. Venerable Śāriputra! This is called cultivating and repeatedly cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness, achieving this great virtue and spiritual power.

"Venerable Śāriputra! Because I have well cultivated the four foundations of mindfulness, with just slight effort I can survey and observe the entire minor thousandfold world, just as a clear-sighted man standing atop a high tower can observe various objects on the level ground below. With just slight effort, I observe the minor thousandfold world in the same way. Thus I have cultivated and repeatedly cultivated the four foundations of mindfulness and achieved this great virtue and spiritual power."

When the two noble ones had finished their discussion, each rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (538)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. Venerable Śāriputra, Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, Venerable Ānanda, and Venerable Anuruddha were staying in Śrāvastī.

At that time, Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana went to Venerable Anuruddha's place, and after they had exchanged greetings and courtesies, he sat to one side. Then Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana asked Venerable Anuruddha: "Through the cultivation and repeated cultivation of what merit have you achieved this great virtue and spiritual power?"

Venerable Anuruddha said to Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "Through the cultivation and repeated cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness I have achieved this great virtue and spiritual power. What are the four? Internally contemplating the body as body, binding the mind and abiding therein, with diligent effort, right mindfulness and right awareness, removing worldly desire and sorrow; externally contemplating the body, internally and externally contemplating the body; internally contemplating feelings, externally contemplating feelings, internally and externally contemplating feelings; internally contemplating the mind, externally contemplating the mind, internally and externally contemplating the mind; internally contemplating dharmas, externally contemplating dharmas, internally and externally contemplating dharmas, binding the mind and abiding therein, with diligent effort, removing worldly desire and sorrow. This is called cultivating and repeatedly cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness. Having achieved this great virtue and spiritual power, with just slight effort I can survey and observe a thousand Mount Sumerus, just as a clear-sighted man ascending to a high mountain peak can look down upon a thousand tāla tree groves below. Thus, through the cultivation and repeated cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness, I have achieved this great virtue and spiritual power, and with just slight effort I can see a thousand Mount Sumerus. Thus, Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, through the cultivation and repeated cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness I have achieved this great virtue and spiritual power."

When the two noble ones had finished their discussion, each rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (539)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. Venerable Śāriputra, Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, Venerable Ānanda, and Venerable Anuruddha were staying in Śrāvastī beside the Hasta-constructed Bathing Pool.

At that time, Venerable Ānanda went to Venerable Anuruddha's place, and after they had exchanged greetings and courtesies, he sat to one side. Venerable Ānanda asked Venerable Anuruddha: "Through the cultivation and repeated cultivation of what merit have you achieved such great virtue, great power, and great spiritual penetration?"

Venerable Anuruddha said to Venerable Ānanda: "Through the cultivation and repeated cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness I have achieved this great virtue and great power. What are the four? Internally contemplating the body as body, a foundation of mindfulness, binding the mind and abiding therein, with diligent effort, right mindfulness and right awareness, removing worldly desire and sorrow. Likewise, externally contemplating the body, internally and externally contemplating the body; internally contemplating feelings, externally contemplating feelings, internally and externally contemplating feelings; internally contemplating the mind, externally contemplating the mind, internally and externally contemplating the mind; internally contemplating dharmas, externally contemplating dharmas, internally and externally contemplating dharmas as a foundation of mindfulness, binding the mind and abiding therein, with diligent effort, removing worldly desire and sorrow. Thus, Venerable Ānanda, through the cultivation and repeated cultivation of these four foundations of mindfulness, with just slight effort, with the purified divine eye surpassing the eyes of gods and humans, I see sentient beings at the time of death and at the time of birth: those of good appearance and bad appearance, those of superior station and inferior station, those in good destinies and bad destinies, receiving birth according to their karma. I see all of this as it truly is. Those sentient beings who engage in bodily misconduct, verbal and mental misconduct, who slander the noble ones, and through the causes and conditions of wrong views, at the breaking up of the body after death, are reborn in the hells. Those sentient beings who engage in bodily good conduct, verbal and mental good conduct, who do not slander the noble ones, and who have accomplished right views, through these causes and conditions, at the breaking up of the body after death, are reborn in the heavens.

"Just as a clear-sighted man standing at a four-way crossroads can see all the people coming and going, sitting and lying down, so too do I, through the cultivation and repeated cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness, achieve this great virtue, great power, and spiritual penetration, seeing sentient beings at the time of death and at the time of birth, in good destinies and bad destinies. Those sentient beings who engage in bodily misconduct, verbal and mental misconduct, who slander the noble ones, through the causes and conditions of wrong views, are reborn in the hells. Those sentient beings who engage in bodily good conduct, verbal and mental good conduct, who do not slander the noble ones, through the causes and conditions of right views, at the breaking up of the body after death, are reborn in the heavens. Thus, Venerable Ānanda, through the cultivation and repeated cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness I have achieved this great virtue, great power, and spiritual penetration."

When the two noble ones had finished their discussion, each rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (540)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Anuruddha was in Śrāvastī at the Pine Forest Hermitage, afflicted by bodily illness and suffering.

Then many bhikṣus went to Venerable Anuruddha's place, and after inquiring about his health and offering comfort, they stood to one side and said to Venerable Anuruddha: "Venerable Anuruddha, is your ailment increasing or decreasing? Can you bear it? Is the illness gradually decreasing rather than increasing?"

Venerable Anuruddha said: "My illness is not comfortable; it is difficult to bear. The bodily pains keep increasing without any decrease." He then spoke the three kinds of similes, as told above in the Kṣama Sūtra.

"Nevertheless, although my body has been afflicted by these pains, I shall bear them with patience, maintaining right mindfulness and right awareness."

The bhikṣus asked Venerable Anuruddha: "In what does the mind abide such that you can patiently endure such great suffering, maintaining right mindfulness and right awareness?"

Venerable Anuruddha said to the bhikṣus: "Abiding in the four foundations of mindfulness, I am able to patiently endure these bodily pains that have arisen, maintaining right mindfulness and right awareness. What are the four foundations of mindfulness? Namely, internally contemplating the body as body, a foundation of mindfulness, and so forth up to contemplating dharmas as dharmas, a foundation of mindfulness. This is called abiding in the four foundations of mindfulness, whereby one can patiently endure bodily pains, maintaining right mindfulness and right awareness."

When the noble ones had finished their discussion, they rejoiced and expressed their approval, and each rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (541)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Anuruddha was in Śrāvastī at the Pine Forest Hermitage, having recently recovered from illness.

Then many bhikṣus went to Venerable Anuruddha's place, and after inquiring about his health and offering comfort, they sat to one side and asked Venerable Anuruddha: "Are you dwelling in comfort and ease?"

Anuruddha said: "I am dwelling in comfort and ease. My bodily pains have gradually subsided."

The bhikṣus asked Venerable Anuruddha: "Abiding in what dwelling have your bodily pains become comfortable?"

Venerable Anuruddha said: "Abiding in the four foundations of mindfulness, my bodily pains have gradually become comfortable. What are the four? Namely, internally contemplating the body as body, a foundation of mindfulness, and so forth up to contemplating dharmas as dharmas, a foundation of mindfulness. These are called the four foundations of mindfulness. Because I abide in these four foundations of mindfulness, my bodily pains have gradually subsided."

When the noble ones had finished their discussion, they rejoiced and expressed their approval, and each rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (542)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Anuruddha was in Śrāvastī at the Pine Forest Hermitage.

Then many bhikṣus went to Venerable Anuruddha's place, and after they had exchanged greetings and courtesies, they sat to one side and asked Venerable Anuruddha: "If a bhikṣu is at the stage of learning, abiding with the mind directed upward seeking the peace of nirvāṇa, how should a noble disciple cultivate and repeatedly cultivate so that in this Dharma and Vinaya he may bring all the outflows to an end, and attain the outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, in this very life knowing and realizing for himself: 'My births are exhausted, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further existence'?"

Venerable Anuruddha said to the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu is at the stage of learning, abiding with the mind directed upward seeking the peace of nirvāṇa, how should a noble disciple cultivate and repeatedly cultivate so that in this Dharma and Vinaya he may bring all the outflows to an end, and attain the outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, in this very life knowing and realizing for himself: 'My births are exhausted, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further existence'? He should abide in the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? Namely, internally contemplating the body as body, a foundation of mindfulness, and so forth up to contemplating dharmas as dharmas, a foundation of mindfulness. When these four foundations of mindfulness are cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, in this Dharma and Vinaya one brings all the outflows to an end, attaining the outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, in this very life knowing and realizing for oneself: 'My births are exhausted, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further existence.'"

Then the bhikṣus, having heard what Venerable Anuruddha said, rejoiced and expressed their approval, and each rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (543)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Anuruddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at the Pine Forest Hermitage.

Then many bhikṣus went to Venerable Anuruddha's place, and after they had exchanged greetings and courtesies with Venerable Anuruddha, they sat to one side. They said to Venerable Anuruddha: "If an arahant bhikṣu has exhausted all the outflows, has done what was to be done, has laid down the heavy burden, has severed the bonds of existence, and is well liberated in mind through right knowledge, does he also cultivate the four foundations of mindfulness?"

Venerable Anuruddha said to the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu has exhausted all the outflows, has done what was to be done, has laid down the heavy burden, has severed the bonds of existence, and is well liberated in mind through right knowledge, he also cultivates the four foundations of mindfulness. Why? So that what has not been attained may be attained, what has not been realized may be realized, and for the sake of pleasant abiding in the present life. Why? Because I too have separated from all outflows, have attained arahantship, have done what was to be done, am well liberated in mind, and I also cultivate the four foundations of mindfulness. Therefore, what has not been attained is attained, what has not been reached is reached, what has not been realized is realized, and so forth up to pleasant abiding in the present life."

When the noble ones had finished their discussion, they rejoiced and expressed their approval, and each rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (544)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Anuruddha was in Śrāvastī at the Pine Forest Hermitage.

Then many non-Buddhist renunciants went to Venerable Anuruddha's place, and after they had exchanged greetings and courtesies, they sat to one side. They said to Venerable Anuruddha: "Venerable one, for what reason did you go forth in the Dharma of the śramaṇa Gautama?"

Venerable Anuruddha said: "For the sake of cultivation."

They asked again: "What do you cultivate?"

He answered: "I cultivate the faculties, cultivate the powers, cultivate the factors of awakening, cultivate the foundations of mindfulness. Which of these do you wish to hear about?"

They asked again: "The faculties, powers, and factors of awakening: we do not even know their names, much less could we ask about their meaning. However, we wish to hear about the foundations of mindfulness."

Venerable Anuruddha said: "Listen carefully and ponder well; I shall explain it to you. A bhikṣu internally contemplates the body as body, a foundation of mindfulness, and so forth up to contemplating dharmas as dharmas, a foundation of mindfulness."

Then the many non-Buddhist renunciants, having heard what Venerable Anuruddha said, rejoiced and expressed their approval, and each rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (545)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Anuruddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at the Pine Forest Hermitage.

Then Venerable Anuruddha said to the bhikṣus: "Just as a great tree that grows leaning downward follows the gradient and flows along, and if its root is cut, the tree will certainly fall in the direction it leans, so too, a bhikṣu who cultivates the four foundations of mindfulness, through the long night, leans, flows, and inclines toward seclusion; leans, flows, and inclines toward renunciation; leans, flows, and inclines toward nirvāṇa."

After Venerable Anuruddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what he said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (546)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Mahākātyāyana was in the Bālānā village, beside the Black-Mud Pool.

Then a brahmin who carried a water-pitcher staff came to Mahākātyāyana's place, and after they had exchanged greetings and courtesies, he sat to one side. He asked Mahākātyāyana: "What is the cause and condition by which kings quarrel with kings, and brahmins and householders quarrel with brahmins and householders?"

Mahākātyāyana answered the brahmin: "It is because of the binding of craving desire that kings quarrel with kings, and brahmins and householders quarrel with brahmins and householders."

The brahmin asked again: "What is the cause and condition by which renunciants quarrel with renunciants?"

Mahākātyāyana answered: "It is because of the binding of view-desire that renunciants quarrel with renunciants."

The brahmin asked again: "Mahākātyāyana, is there anyone who can be free from the binding of craving desire and also free from the binding of view-desire?"

Venerable Mahākātyāyana answered: "Brahmin, there is. My great teacher, the Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One, Perfect in Knowledge and Conduct, Well-Gone One, Knower of the World, Unsurpassed One, Tamer of Those to Be Tamed, Teacher of Gods and Humans, the Buddha, the World-Honored One, is able to be free from the binding of craving desire and the binding of view-desire."

The brahmin asked again: "Where does the Buddha, the World-Honored One, now dwell?"

He answered: "The Buddha, the World-Honored One, now dwells among the people of Vārāṇasī, in the Kośala country, in the city of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park."

At that time, the brahmin rose from his seat, arranged his garments, bared his right shoulder, placed his right knee on the ground, and with palms joined, facing the direction where the Buddha dwelled, praised him with these words:

"Homage, homage to the Buddha, the World-Honored One, the Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One, who is able to be free from the bindings of craving desire, who is able to be entirely free from the bonds of craving desire and from view-desire, purified at the root."

Then the brahmin who carried the water-pitcher staff, having heard what Venerable Mahākātyāyana said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (547)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. Venerable Mahākātyāyana was at Vārāṇā beside the Black-Mud Pool, gathered with many bhikṣus in the dining hall, dealing with a matter concerning robes.

At that time, there was a staff-bearing brahmin, advanced in age with mature faculties, who came to the dining hall and stood to one side leaning on his staff. After remaining silent for a moment, he said to the bhikṣus: "Elders, why is it that when you see an aged and venerable man, you do not converse with him, greet him, show respect, or offer him a seat?"

At that time, Venerable Mahākātyāyana was also seated in the assembly. Venerable Mahākātyāyana said to the brahmin: "In our Dharma, when one who is truly old and venerable comes, we do converse with them, greet them, show respectful obeisance, and offer them a seat."

The brahmin said: "I see no one in this assembly older than myself, yet none show me respectful obeisance or offer me a seat. How can you say: 'In our Dharma, when we see one who is truly old and venerable, we show respectful obeisance and offer them a seat'?"

Mahākātyāyana said: "Brahmin, if someone is aged, eighty or ninety years old, with white hair and lost teeth, yet accomplishes the qualities of youth, then this person is not truly a venerable elder. Though one may be young, only twenty-five, with fair skin and black hair, in the prime of strength and beauty, yet if that person accomplishes the qualities of the aged, they are counted among the venerable elders."

The brahmin asked: "What do you mean that one who is eighty or ninety, with white hair and lost teeth, may yet accomplish the qualities of youth, while one who is twenty-five, with fair skin and black hair, in the prime of strength and beauty, may be counted among the venerable elders?"

Venerable Mahākātyāyana said to the brahmin: "There are five qualities of sensual desire. That is, forms cognized by the eye that are loved, enjoyed, and remembered; sounds cognized by the ear, scents cognized by the nose, tastes cognized by the tongue, tactile objects cognized by the body that are loved, enjoyed, and remembered. If one has not separated from craving for these five qualities of sensual desire, has not separated from desire, has not separated from love, has not separated from longing, has not separated from defilement, then, brahmin, even though one is eighty or ninety, with white hair and lost teeth, that person accomplishes the qualities of youth. Even if one is young, only twenty-five, with fair skin and black hair, in the prime of strength and beauty, if one has separated from craving for the five qualities of sensual desire, separated from desire, separated from love, separated from longing, separated from defilement, then, even though young, only twenty-five, with fair skin and black hair, in the prime of strength and beauty, that person accomplishes the qualities of an elder and is counted among the venerable elders."

Then the brahmin said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "As the venerable one has explained this meaning, I examine myself: though old in years, I am young. You, though young in years, have accomplished the qualities of the aged. The world has many affairs, so I shall take my leave and go."

Venerable Mahākātyāyana said: "Brahmin, you may go when you see fit."

Then the brahmin, having heard what Venerable Mahākātyāyana said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, and returned to his own place.

---

### (548)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. Venerable Mahākātyāyana was in the Dense Forest.

At that time, the king of Mathurā, a prince of the western regions, went to Venerable Mahākātyāyana's place, paid homage at Mahākātyāyana's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and asked Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "The brahmins claim: 'We are supreme; others are inferior. We are white; others are dark. Brahmins are pure; non-brahmins are not. Brahmins are sons of Brahmā, born from his mouth, created by Brahmā, belonging to Brahmā.' Venerable Mahākātyāyana, what is the meaning of this?"

Venerable Mahākātyāyana said to the king of Mathurā: "Great King, this is merely worldly convention. It is worldly convention that says: 'Brahmins are supreme; others are inferior. Brahmins are white; others are dark. Brahmins are pure; non-brahmins are not. Brahmins are born from Brahmā, born from his mouth, created by Brahmā, belonging to Brahmā.' Great King, you should know that it is karma that is true and real; it is upon karma that one depends."

The king said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "This is only stated briefly; I do not understand. Would you please explain in detail?"

Venerable Mahākātyāyana said: "Now I shall ask you; answer as you see fit." He then asked: "Great King, suppose you are a brahmin king in your own country, and you summon all the brahmins, kṣatriyas, householders, and elders, these four classes of people, and with wealth and power make them serve and attend you, rising before you and retiring after you, and carrying out all commands as you wish. Would they comply?"

He answered: "They would comply."

He asked again: "Great King, if a kṣatriya were king, or a householder were king, or an elder were king, in their own country, and they summoned all four classes and with wealth and power made them serve and attend them, rising before them and retiring after them, and carrying out all commands as they wished, would they comply?"

He answered: "They would comply."

He asked again: "Great King, if all four classes are thus equal, what difference is there? You should know, Great King, the four classes are all equal; there is no distinction of superiority or inferiority."

The king of Mathurā said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "Indeed, Venerable One, the four classes are equal; there is no variety of superiority or inferiority."

"Therefore, Great King, you should know that the four classes have only worldly convention as distinction, but in terms of karma, there is truly no distinction. Furthermore, Great King, if there is a brahmin in this country who steals, what should be done?"

The king said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "If a brahmin steals, he may be whipped, bound, exiled from the country, fined in gold, or have his hands, feet, ears, or nose cut off; if the crime is grave, he is executed. Whatever he stole makes him a thief. Yet he is still called a brahmin, though he is called a thief."

He asked again: "Great King, if a kṣatriya, householder, or elder steals, what should be done?"

The king said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "They too are whipped, bound, exiled from the country, fined in gold, and also may have their hands, feet, ears, or nose cut off; if the crime is grave, they are executed."

"Thus, Great King, are not the four classes all equal? Is there any variety of distinction?"

The king said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "In this matter, there is truly no variety of superiority or inferiority."

Venerable Mahākātyāyana further said to the king: "You should know, Great King, the four classes: worldly convention says 'Brahmins are supreme; others are inferior. Brahmins are white; others are dark. Brahmins are pure; non-brahmins are not.' But should one depend on the truth of karma, or on karma?"

He asked again: "Great King, if a brahmin kills living beings, steals, commits sexual misconduct, speaks falsely, speaks harshly, speaks divisively, speaks frivolously, is covetous, hateful, and holds wrong views, having performed these ten unwholesome courses of action, will he be reborn in a bad destiny or a good destiny? What have you heard from the arahants?"

The king said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "If a brahmin performs the ten unwholesome courses of action, he will fall into a bad destiny. From the arahants, I have heard this. The same is said for kṣatriyas, householders, and elders."

He asked again: "Great King, if a brahmin practices the ten wholesome courses of action, abstaining from killing, and so forth up to right views, where will he be reborn? In a good destiny or a bad destiny? What have you heard from the arahants?"

The king said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "If a brahmin practices the ten wholesome courses of action, he will be reborn in a good destiny. From the arahants, I have heard this. The same is said for kṣatriyas, householders, and elders."

He asked again: "How about it, Great King? Are the four classes equal, or is there any variety of superiority or inferiority?"

The king said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "In this matter, they are equal; there is no variety of superiority or inferiority."

"Therefore, Great King, you should know that the four classes are all equal; there is no variety of superiority or inferiority. It is only worldly convention that says: 'Brahmins are supreme. Brahmins are white; others are dark. Brahmins are pure; non-brahmins are not. Brahmins are born from Brahmā, born from his mouth, created by Brahmā, belonging to Brahmā.' You should know that karma is true and real; one depends upon karma."

The king said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "Indeed, as you have said. It is all merely worldly convention that says: 'Brahmins are supreme; others are inferior. Brahmins are white; others are dark. Brahmins are pure; non-brahmins are not. Brahmins are born from Brahmā, born from his mouth, created by Brahmā, belonging to Brahmā.' It is all karma that is true and real; one depends upon karma."

Then the king of Mathurā, having heard what Venerable Mahākātyāyana said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (549)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Mahākātyāyana was dwelling in the Āvaṇṭi country, at the Kurāraghara Hermitage. Venerable Mahākātyāyana, in the early morning, put on his robes and took his bowl and entered Kurāraghara for alms, going from house to house, until he came to the house of the laywoman Kālikā.

At that time, the laywoman saw Venerable Mahākātyāyana, and she at once prepared a seat and invited him to sit. She paid homage at Venerable Mahākātyāyana's feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "As the World-Honored One said in answer to the question of the maiden Saṅgītā, as the World-Honored One spoke the verse answering the maiden Saṅgītā's question:

> "True meaning abides within the heart, tranquil and undisturbed;
> Having conquered the valiant and the lovely, pleasing forms,
> With single mind in solitary contemplation, partaking of the sublime joy of dhyāna;
> This then is being far removed from worldly companions.
> As for worldly companions, none draw near to me."

"Venerable Mahākātyāyana, what is the meaning of this verse spoken by the World-Honored One?"

Venerable Mahākātyāyana said to the laywoman: "Sister, there are some śramaṇas and brahmins who say: 'The earth kasiṇa absorption is the unsurpassed attainment, and this is the fruit they seek.' Sister, if those śramaṇas and brahmins who enter the earth kasiṇa absorption attain purity and brilliant whiteness, they then see its origin, see its peril, see its cessation, and see the path leading to its cessation. Because they see its origin, see its peril, see its cessation, and see the path leading to its cessation, they attain true meaning abiding within the heart, tranquil and undisturbed. Sister, likewise with the water kasiṇa, fire kasiṇa, wind kasiṇa, blue kasiṇa, yellow kasiṇa, red kasiṇa, white kasiṇa, space kasiṇa, and consciousness kasiṇa, those who regard these as unsurpassed and seek these as their fruit:

"Sister, if there are śramaṇas and brahmins who enter even the consciousness kasiṇa absorption and attain purity and brilliant whiteness, they see its origin, see its peril, see its cessation, and see the path leading to its cessation. Because they see its origin, see its peril, see its cessation, and see the path leading to its cessation, this is then true meaning abiding within the heart, tranquil and undisturbed, well seen and well entered. Therefore the World-Honored One spoke the verse answering the maiden Saṅgītā's question:

> "True meaning abides within the heart, tranquil and undisturbed;
> Having conquered the valiant and the lovely, pleasing forms,
> With single mind in solitary contemplation, partaking of the sublime joy of dhyāna;
> This then is being far removed from worldly companions.
> As for worldly companions, none draw near to me."

"Thus, sister, I understand the World-Honored One spoke this verse with this meaning."

The laywoman said: "Excellent, Venerable One! You have explained the true meaning. Would the Venerable One please accept my offering of food?"

At that time, Venerable Mahākātyāyana silently accepted the invitation.

Then the laywoman Kālikā, knowing that Venerable Mahākātyāyana had accepted the invitation, prepared various kinds of pure and delicious food and drink, and with respect and reverence, personally served the food.

When the laywoman knew that Venerable Mahākātyāyana had finished eating, had washed his bowl and rinsed his mouth, she spread out a low seat before Venerable Mahākātyāyana and respectfully listened to the Dharma.

Venerable Mahākātyāyana taught the laywoman Kālikā with various kinds of Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting her. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and delighted her, he rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (550)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Mahākātyāyana was in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Venerable Mahākātyāyana said to the bhikṣus: "The Buddha, World-Honored One, the Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One, knowing and seeing, has taught six dharmas for going beyond suffering and ascending to the supreme place, teaching the one path for purifying beings, removing afflictions and suffering, so that grief and sorrow are entirely extinguished, and the true Dharma is attained. What are the six?

"The noble disciple recollects the Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One, and the pure Dharma practiced by him. The Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One, Perfect in Knowledge and Conduct, Well-Gone One, Knower of the World, Unsurpassed One, Tamer of Those to Be Tamed, Teacher of Gods and Humans, the Buddha, World-Honored One. When the noble disciple recollects the Dharma practiced by the Tathāgata, the Arhat, he separates from thoughts of craving, separates from thoughts of ill-will, and separates from thoughts of harm. Thus the noble disciple goes beyond the defiled mind. What is the defiled mind? It is the five qualities of sensual desire. Separating from craving, ill-will, and delusion with regard to these five qualities of sensual desire, abiding in right mindfulness and right awareness, riding upon the straight path, cultivating recollection of the Buddha, heading directly toward nirvāṇa. This is called what the Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One knew and saw, and taught as the first going beyond suffering, ascending to the supreme place, the one path for purifying beings, removing suffering and affliction, extinguishing grief and sorrow, and attaining the true Dharma.

"Furthermore, the noble disciple recollects the true Dharma. He recollects the World-Honored One's Dharma and Vinaya of the present life, which is free from burning afflictions, not bound by time, directly accessible, pertaining to the present life, to be personally realized through self-awakening. At that time, when the noble disciple recollects this true Dharma, he does not give rise to thoughts of craving, thoughts of ill-will, or thoughts of harm. Thus the noble disciple goes beyond the defiled mind. What is the defiled mind? It is the five qualities of sensual desire. Separating from craving, ill-will, and delusion with regard to these five qualities of sensual desire, abiding in right mindfulness and right awareness, riding upon the straight path, cultivating recollection of the Dharma, heading directly toward nirvāṇa. This is called what the Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One knew and saw, and taught as the second going beyond suffering, ascending to the supreme place, the one path for purifying beings, removing suffering and affliction, extinguishing grief and sorrow, and attaining the true Dharma.

"Furthermore, the noble disciple recollects the Sangha. The Sangha practices the good way, the right way, the direct way, the equal way, cultivating the practice in accordance. That is, those who practice toward stream-entry and those who have attained the fruit of stream-entry, those who practice toward once-returning and those who have attained once-returning, those who practice toward non-returning and those who have attained non-returning, those who practice toward arahantship and those who have attained arahantship. Thus there are four pairs and eight types of persons. This is called the Sangha of the World-Honored One's disciples, who are endowed with morality, endowed with concentration, endowed with wisdom, endowed with liberation, endowed with the knowledge and vision of liberation. They are a field of offerings, reverence, and homage, an unsurpassed field of merit in the world. When the noble disciple thus recollects the Sangha, at that time the noble disciple does not give rise to thoughts of craving, thoughts of ill-will, or thoughts of harm. Thus the noble disciple goes beyond the defiled mind. What is the defiled mind? It is the five qualities of sensual desire. Separating from craving, ill-will, and delusion with regard to these five qualities of sensual desire, abiding in right mindfulness and right awareness, riding upon the straight path, cultivating recollection of the Sangha, heading directly toward nirvāṇa. This is called what the Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One knew and saw, and taught as the third going beyond suffering, ascending to the supreme place, the one path for purifying beings, removing suffering and affliction, extinguishing grief and sorrow, and attaining the true Dharma.

"Furthermore, the noble disciple recollects the virtue of morality. He recollects unbroken morality, uncut morality, pure and thick morality, uninterrupted morality, non-stolen morality, perfectly accomplished morality, praiseworthy morality, morality of the holy life that is not hateful. When the noble disciple recollects this morality, reflecting upon the morality accomplished within himself, at that time he does not give rise to thoughts of craving, thoughts of ill-will, or thoughts of harm. Thus the noble disciple goes beyond the defiled mind. What is the defiled mind? It is the five qualities of sensual desire. Separating from craving, ill-will, and delusion with regard to these five qualities of sensual desire, abiding in right mindfulness and right awareness, riding upon the straight path, cultivating recollection of morality, heading directly toward nirvāṇa. This is called what the Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One knew and saw, and taught as the fourth going beyond suffering, ascending to the supreme place, the one path for purifying beings, removing suffering and affliction, extinguishing grief and sorrow, and attaining the true Dharma.

"Furthermore, the noble disciple recollects the Dharma of giving. His mind feels delight: 'I have now left behind the stain of miserliness. Though living as a householder, I give with a liberated mind: constant giving, relinquishing giving, joyful giving, complete giving, impartial giving.' When the noble disciple recollects his own Dharma of giving, he does not give rise to thoughts of craving, thoughts of ill-will, or thoughts of harm. Thus the noble disciple goes beyond the defiled mind. In what is the mind defiled? It is the five qualities of sensual desire. Separating from craving, ill-will, and delusion with regard to these five qualities of sensual desire, abiding in right mindfulness and right awareness, riding upon the straight path, cultivating recollection of giving, heading directly toward nirvāṇa. This is called what the Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One knew and saw, and taught as the fifth going beyond suffering, ascending to the supreme place, the one path for purifying beings, removing suffering and affliction, extinguishing grief and sorrow, and attaining the true Dharma.

"Furthermore, the noble disciple recollects the virtue of the devas. He recollects the Four Great Kings' Heaven, the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, the Yāma Heaven, the Tuṣita Heaven, the Nirmāṇarati Heaven, and the Paranirmitavaśavartin Heaven. Those with pure faith and morality, upon ending this life, are reborn among those devas. I too have such faith, morality, generosity, learning, and wisdom, and upon ending this life, I shall be reborn among those devas. Thus, when the noble disciple recollects the virtues of the devas, he does not give rise to thoughts of craving, thoughts of ill-will, or thoughts of harm. Thus the noble disciple goes beyond the defiled mind. In what is the mind defiled? It is the five qualities of sensual desire. Separating from craving, ill-will, and delusion with regard to these five qualities of sensual desire, abiding in right mindfulness and right awareness, riding upon the straight path, cultivating recollection of the devas, heading directly toward nirvāṇa. This is called what the Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One knew and saw, and taught as the sixth going beyond suffering, ascending to the supreme place, the one path for purifying beings, removing suffering and affliction, extinguishing grief and sorrow, and attaining the true Dharma."

After Venerable Mahākātyāyana had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what he said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (551)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Mahākātyāyana was dwelling in the Śākyan Hārī village hermitage.

Then the elder of Hārī village went to Venerable Mahākātyāyana's place, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "As the World-Honored One in the Arthavargīya answered the question of Māgandiya with this verse:

> "Cut off all the streams, and also block their sources;
> Associating closely with the village, the sage does not commend.
> Empty of the five desires, they are never again filled;
> The contentions and disputes of the world are ultimately done with forever."

"Venerable Mahākātyāyana, what is the meaning of this verse?"

Venerable Mahākātyāyana answered the elder: "The stream of the eye means that eye-consciousness gives rise to craving; dependent on the eye-element, the craving desire flows out. Therefore it is called a stream. The streams of the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind mean that mind-consciousness gives rise to craving; dependent on the mind-element, the craving consciousness flows out. Therefore it is called a stream."

The elder further asked Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "What is called not flowing?"

Venerable Kātyāyana said to the elder: "Eye-consciousness and the forms cognized by eye-consciousness give rise to dependent love and delight. If that is exhausted, free from desire, ceased, stilled, and extinguished, this is called not flowing. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind; mind-consciousness and the dharmas cognized by mind-consciousness give rise to dependent craving desire. If that is exhausted, free from desire, ceased, stilled, and extinguished, this is called not flowing."

He asked again: "What does it mean?"

Venerable Mahākātyāyana answered: "Dependent on the eye and on form, eye-consciousness arises. The conjunction of these three gives rise to contact. Dependent on contact, feeling arises: pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. Dependent on this, there is defiled attachment and flowing. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind; mind-consciousness and the dharmas of mind-consciousness: the conjunction of these three gives rise to contact. Dependent on contact, feeling arises: pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling. Dependent on this feeling, love and delight flow forth. This is called the source of the stream. What does 'also block their sources' mean? When the eye-element grasps the mental dharmas of the sense-realm, with the binding tendency of attachment, if that is exhausted, free from desire, ceased, stilled, and extinguished, this is called blocking the source of the stream. When the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind grasp the mental dharmas of the sense-realm, with the binding tendency of attachment, if that is exhausted, free from desire, ceased, stilled, and extinguished, this is called 'also blocking their sources.'"

He asked again: "What does 'associating closely and commending' mean?"

Venerable Mahākātyāyana answered: "When householders and renunciants associate closely with each other, sharing the same joys, the same sorrows, the same pleasures, the same pains, and whatever they do, they do together: this is called 'associating closely and commending.'"

He asked again: "What does 'not commending' mean?"

"When householders and renunciants do not associate closely with each other, do not share the same joys, do not share the same sorrows, do not share the same pains, do not share the same pleasures, and whatever they do, they do not please each other: this is called 'not commending.'"

"What does 'not empty of desires' mean?"

"The five qualities of sensual desire: forms cognized by the eye that are loved, enjoyed, and remembered, nourishing and sustaining; craving and desire, deeply defiled and attached. Sounds of the ear, scents of the nose, tastes of the tongue, tactile objects of the body that are loved, enjoyed, and remembered, nourishing and sustaining; craving and desire, deeply defiled and attached. If, regarding these five desires, one has not separated from craving, not separated from desire, not separated from love, not separated from longing, not separated from thirst: this is called 'not empty of desires.'"

"What does 'empty of desires' mean?"

"If, regarding these five qualities of sensual desire, one has separated from craving, separated from desire, separated from love, separated from longing, separated from thirst: this is called 'empty of desires.' If one speaks of the binding tendency of attachment, this is called the mind's dharmas again becoming full. That arahant bhikṣu whose outflows are entirely exhausted, who has cut them off at the root like cutting off the crown of a tāla tree so that in the future they will never again arise: how could he then again engage in disputes with others? Therefore the World-Honored One in the Arthavargīya answered Māgandiya's question with this verse:

> "If one cuts off all the streams, and also blocks their sources;
> Associating closely with the village, the sage does not commend.
> Empty of desires, they are never again filled;
> No longer engaging with the world in speech, contention, and dispute."

"This is the explanation of the meaning of the verse spoken by the Tathāgata."

Then the elder of Hārī village, having heard what Venerable Mahākātyāyana said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (552)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Mahākātyāyana was dwelling in the Śākyan Hārī village hermitage.

Then the chief elder of Hārī village went to Venerable Mahākātyāyana's place, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "As the World-Honored One said in the Vediyaka Mountain cave of Sakka, lord of the devas: 'Kauśika, if śramaṇas or brahmins have attained the unsurpassed liberation through the exhaustion of craving, their minds are rightly and well liberated, reaching the ultimate limit, the ultimate freedom from defilement, the ultimate holy life, and the final purity.' How is it that in this Dharma and Vinaya one reaches the ultimate limit, the ultimate freedom from defilement, the ultimate holy life, and the final purity?"

Venerable Mahākātyāyana said to the elder: "The eye, eye-consciousness, and the forms cognized by eye-consciousness give rise to dependent delight. If that is exhausted, free from desire, ceased, stilled, and extinguished, then in this Dharma and Vinaya one reaches the ultimate limit, the ultimate freedom from defilement, the ultimate holy life, and the final purity. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind; mind-consciousness and the dharmas cognized by mind-consciousness give rise to dependent delight. If that is exhausted, ceased, stilled, and extinguished, then a bhikṣu in this Dharma and Vinaya reaches the ultimate freedom from defilement, the ultimate holy life, and the final purity."

Then the chief elder of Hārī village, having heard what Venerable Mahākātyāyana said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (553)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Mahākātyāyana was in the Śākyan Hārī village.

The chief elder of the village went to Venerable Mahākātyāyana's place, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and asked Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "As the World-Honored One said in the Vediyaka Mountain stone cave to Sakka, lord of the devas: 'Kauśika, if śramaṇas or brahmins have attained the unsurpassed liberation through the exhaustion of craving, their minds are well liberated, reaching the ultimate limit, the ultimate freedom from defilement, the ultimate holy life, and the final purity.' How is it that in this Dharma and Vinaya one reaches the ultimate limit, the ultimate freedom from defilement, the ultimate holy life, and the final purity?"

Venerable Kātyāyana said to the elder: "If a bhikṣu's eye-element grasps the mental dharmas of the sense-realm, with the binding tendency of attachment, and if that is exhausted, free from desire, stilled, and extinguished, then in this Dharma and Vinaya one reaches the ultimate limit, the ultimate freedom from defilement, the ultimate holy life, and the final purity. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind; the mind-element grasps the mental dharmas of the sense-realm, with the binding tendency of attachment. If that is exhausted, separated, ceased, stilled, and extinguished, then in this Dharma and Vinaya one reaches the ultimate limit, the ultimate freedom from defilement, the ultimate holy life, and the final purity."

Then the chief elder of Hārī village, having heard what Venerable Mahākātyāyana said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (554)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Mahākātyāyana was dwelling in the Śākyan Hārī village.

At that time, the chief elder of Hārī village was afflicted by bodily illness and suffering. Venerable Mahākātyāyana heard that the chief elder of Hārī village was afflicted by bodily illness and suffering. Having heard this, in the early morning he put on his robes and took his bowl and entered Hārī village for alms, going from house to house until he reached the house of the chief elder of Hārī village. The chief elder of Hārī village, seeing Venerable Mahākātyāyana from afar, tried to rise from his seat.

Venerable Mahākātyāyana, seeing the elder trying to rise, at once told him: "Elder, do not rise! Fortunately there is another seat; I can sit on another seat." He said to the elder: "How is it, Elder? Can you endure the illness? Are the bodily pains gradually improving? They are not increasing, are they?"

The elder answered: "Venerable One, my illness is hard to endure. The bodily pains keep increasing without any decrease."

He then spoke the three kinds of similes, as in the earlier Kṣama Bhikṣu Sūtra.

Venerable Mahākātyāyana said to the elder: "Therefore, you should cultivate unwavering confidence in the Buddha, unwavering confidence in the Dharma, unwavering confidence in the Sangha, and the accomplishment of noble morality. You should train in this way."

The elder answered: "As for the four kinds of unwavering confidence taught by the Buddha, I have accomplished them all. I have now accomplished unwavering confidence in the Buddha, unwavering confidence in the Dharma, unwavering confidence in the Sangha, and the accomplishment of noble morality."

Venerable Mahākātyāyana said to the elder: "You should rely on these four kinds of unwavering confidence and cultivate the six recollections. Elder, you should recollect the virtues of the Buddha: this Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One, Perfect in Knowledge and Conduct, Well-Gone One, Knower of the World, Unsurpassed One, Tamer of Those to Be Tamed, Teacher of Gods and Humans, the Buddha, World-Honored One. Recollect the virtues of the Dharma: the World-Honored One's true Dharma and Vinaya of the present life, free from burning afflictions, not bound by time, directly accessible, to be personally realized through self-awakening. Recollect the virtues of the Sangha: those who practice the good way, the right way, the direct way, the equal way, cultivating the practice in accordance. That is, those who practice toward stream-entry and those who have attained the fruit of stream-entry, those who practice toward once-returning and those who have attained once-returning, those who practice toward non-returning and those who have attained non-returning, those who practice toward arahantship and those who have attained arahantship. Thus there are four pairs and eight types of persons. This is called the Sangha of the World-Honored One's disciples, endowed with morality, concentration, wisdom, liberation, and the knowledge and vision of liberation; a place of offerings, reverence, and veneration; a field worthy of being the world's unsurpassed field of merit. Recollect the virtues of morality: personally maintaining right morality, not defiled, not broken, not cut, not damaged, non-stolen morality, perfectly accomplished morality, praiseworthy morality, morality of the holy life, not hateful morality. Recollect the virtues of giving: personally reflecting on one's own giving, the mind feeling delight, having abandoned the defilement of miserliness. Though dwelling as a householder, giving with a liberated mind: constant giving, joyful giving, complete giving, impartial giving. Recollect the virtues of the devas: recollecting the Four Great Kings' Heaven, the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, the Yāma Heaven, the Tuṣita Heaven, the Nirmāṇarati Heaven, and the Paranirmitavaśavartin Heaven. Those with pure faith and morality, upon ending this life, are reborn among those devas. I too have such pure faith, morality, generosity, learning, and wisdom, and upon ending this life, I shall be reborn among those devas. Elder, in this way, relying on the four kinds of unwavering confidence, you should increase the six recollections."

The elder said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana: "The World-Honored One taught relying on the four kinds of unwavering confidence and increasing the six recollections, and I have accomplished them all. I shall cultivate the recollection of the virtues of the Buddha, recollection of the Dharma, recollection of the Sangha, recollection of morality, recollection of giving, and recollection of the devas."

Venerable Mahākātyāyana said to the elder: "Excellent, Elder! You are able to declare of yourself that you have attained the fruit of non-returning."

At that time, the elder said to Venerable Mahākātyāyana that he wished to offer him a meal.

Venerable Mahākātyāyana silently accepted the invitation.

The chief elder of Hārī village, knowing that Venerable Mahākātyāyana had accepted the invitation, prepared various kinds of pure and delicious food and personally served it. After the meal was finished, the bowl was washed and the mouth rinsed. He then taught the elder various kinds of Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and delighted him, he rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (555)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Mahākātyāyana was dwelling in the Śākyan Hārī village.

At that time, there was an elder of the Eight Cities named Dāśī who was afflicted by bodily illness and suffering. Venerable Mahākātyāyana heard that the elder Dāśī was afflicted by bodily illness. In the early morning he put on his robes and took his bowl and entered the Eight Cities for alms, and in due course he went to the house of the elder Dāśī. As in the Hārī Elder Sūtra, it is explained in full.

---

### (556)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the city of Sāketa, in the Añjana Grove.

At that time, many bhikṣuṇīs went to the Buddha's place, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew and stood to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the many bhikṣuṇīs various kinds of Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting them. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and delighted them, he remained silent.

Then the bhikṣuṇīs said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if the signless concentration of mind is neither surging nor sinking, liberated and abiding, abiding and liberated, what fruit and what merit does this signless concentration of mind have, as the World-Honored One teaches?"

The Buddha said to the bhikṣuṇīs: "If the signless concentration of mind is neither surging nor sinking, liberated and abiding, abiding and liberated, this signless concentration of mind has the fruit of knowledge and the merit of knowledge."

Then the bhikṣuṇīs, having heard what the World-Honored One said, rejoiced and expressed their approval, paid their respects, and departed.

Then the many bhikṣuṇīs went to Venerable Ānanda's place, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to Venerable Ānanda: "If the signless concentration of mind is neither surging nor sinking, liberated and abiding, abiding and liberated, what fruit and what merit does this samādhi have?"

Venerable Ānanda said to the bhikṣuṇīs: "Sisters, if the signless concentration of mind is neither surging nor sinking, liberated and abiding, abiding and liberated, the World-Honored One says it has the fruit of knowledge and the merit of knowledge."

The bhikṣuṇīs said: "How wonderful! Venerable Ānanda! The great teacher and the disciple are the same in phrase, the same in flavor, the same in meaning, that is, the supreme meaning. Now these bhikṣuṇīs went to the World-Honored One and asked this very question with this phrase, this flavor, and this meaning, and the World-Honored One also explained it to us with this very phrase, this flavor, and this meaning, just as Venerable Ānanda has said, without any difference. Therefore this is remarkable: the great teacher and the disciple are the same in phrase, the same in flavor, and the same in meaning."

Then the bhikṣuṇīs, having heard what Venerable Ānanda said, rejoiced and expressed their approval, paid their respects, and departed.

---

### (557)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Kauśāmbī, in Ghositārāma. At that time, Venerable Ānanda was also dwelling there.

Then the bhikṣuṇī Jatilā went to Venerable Ānanda's place, bowed her head at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and asked Venerable Ānanda: "If the signless concentration of mind is neither surging nor sinking, liberated and abiding, abiding and liberated, Venerable Ānanda, what fruit and what merit does the World-Honored One say this has?"

Venerable Ānanda said to the bhikṣuṇī Jatilā: "If the signless concentration of mind is neither surging nor sinking, liberated and abiding, abiding and liberated, the World-Honored One says it has the fruit of knowledge and the merit of knowledge."

The bhikṣuṇī Jatilā said: "How wonderful! Venerable Ānanda! The great teacher and the disciple are the same in phrase, the same in flavor, and the same in meaning. Venerable Ānanda, formerly at one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the city of Sāketa, in the Añjana Grove. At that time, many bhikṣuṇīs went to the Buddha's place and asked this very question. At that time, the World-Honored One explained it to the bhikṣuṇīs with this phrase, this flavor, and this meaning. Therefore, you should know this is remarkable: the great teacher and the disciple speak with the same phrase, the same flavor, and the same meaning, that is, the supreme meaning."

Then the bhikṣuṇī Jatilā, having heard what Venerable Ānanda said, rejoiced and expressed her approval, paid her respects, and departed.

As with the bhikṣuṇī Jatilā, so too with the bhikṣuṇī Kā
