# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Twenty-One

---

### (559)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Pāṭaliputra. Venerable Ānanda and Venerable Kāma were also at the Kukkuṭārāma (Cock Grove Monastery) in Pāṭaliputra.

At that time, Venerable Kāma went to Venerable Ānanda's place. After exchanging greetings and courtesies, he sat to one side and said to Venerable Ānanda: "How wonderful, Venerable Ānanda! There are eyes and forms, ears and sounds, a nose and odors, a tongue and tastes, a body and tangibles, a mind and mental objects, yet there are bhikṣus who, possessing these faculties and objects, are able to not perceive them. How is this, Venerable Ānanda? Does that bhikṣu not perceive them while having perception, or does he not perceive them because he is without perception?"

Venerable Ānanda said to Bhikṣu Kāma: "One who has perception also does not perceive them; how much more so one who is without perception."

He further asked Venerable Ānanda: "What is it to have perception yet not perceive existing things?"

Venerable Ānanda said to Bhikṣu Kāma: "If a bhikṣu, separated from desires and unwholesome states, with initial thought and sustained thought, with joy and happiness born of seclusion, dwells having attained the first dhyāna, such a bhikṣu with perception has existing things yet does not perceive them. Likewise with the second, third, and fourth dhyānas, and dwelling having attained the sphere of boundless space, the sphere of boundless consciousness, and the sphere of nothingness. Such a bhikṣu with perception has existing things yet does not perceive them. How does one without perception have existing things yet not perceive them? When a bhikṣu does not attend to any perceptions whatsoever and dwells having realized with the body the signless concentration of mind, this is called a bhikṣu who, without perception, has existing things yet does not perceive them."

Venerable Bhikṣu Kāma further asked Venerable Ānanda: "If a bhikṣu, in the signless concentration of mind, neither surges up nor sinks down, is liberated and then abides, abides and then is liberated, what does the World-Honored One say is the fruit of this, the benefit of this?"

Venerable Ānanda said to Bhikṣu Kāma: "If a bhikṣu, in the signless concentration of mind, neither surges up nor sinks down, is liberated and then abides, abides and then is liberated, the World-Honored One says this is the fruit of knowledge, the benefit of knowledge."

At that time, the two noble ones, having concluded their discussion, rejoiced and expressed approval, and each rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (560)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī in Ghośitārāma. At that time, Venerable Ānanda was also dwelling there.

At that time, Venerable Ānanda addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī before me personally declares their attainment, I shall properly congratulate and encourage them, or guide them by means of four paths. What are the four? If a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī, sitting, thus establishes the mind, well-establishes the mind, confines the mind, tames the mind, with calm and insight unified, with equanimous reception, distinguishing and measuring phenomena, and having cultivated and repeatedly cultivated this, attains the severance of the latent tendencies. If any bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī before me personally declares this, I then properly congratulate and encourage them, or guide them. This is called the teaching of the first path.

"Furthermore, if a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī, sitting upright in contemplation, selects and investigates phenomena, deliberates, establishes the mind, well-establishes it, confines it, tames it, with calm and insight unified, with equanimous reception. Thus rightly directed and dwelling much in this way, one attains separation from the latent tendencies. If any bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī before me personally declares this, I shall properly congratulate and encourage them, or guide them. This is called the teaching of the second path.

"Furthermore, if a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī is seized by restlessness and agitation, and with a tamed mind sits, sits upright, establishes the mind, well-establishes the mind, confines the mind, tames it, with calm and insight unified, with equanimous reception and transformation. Thus rightly directed and dwelling much in this way, one severs the latent tendencies. If any bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī before me personally declares this, I then properly congratulate and encourage them, or guide them. This is called the teaching of the third path.

"Furthermore, if a bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī, with calm and insight harmonized and practiced together, thus rightly directed and dwelling much in this way, severs the latent tendencies. If any bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī before me personally declares this, I then properly congratulate, encourage, and instruct them, or guide them. This is called the teaching of the fourth path."

At that time, the bhikṣus heard what Venerable Ānanda said, rejoiced, and put it into practice.

---

### (561)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī in Ghośitārāma. Venerable Ānanda was also dwelling there.

At that time, a certain brahmin went to Venerable Ānanda's place. After exchanging greetings and courtesies, he sat to one side and asked Venerable Ānanda: "Why do you practice the holy life under the śramaṇa Gautama?"

Venerable Ānanda said to the brahmin: "For the sake of abandoning."

He further asked: "Venerable one, what do you abandon?"

He answered: "We abandon craving."

He further asked: "Venerable Ānanda, depending on what do you attain the abandoning of craving?"

He answered: "Brahmin, depending on desire, one abandons craving."

He further asked: "Venerable Ānanda, is this not then without end?"

He answered: "Brahmin, it is not without end. There is indeed an end; it is not without end."

He further asked: "Venerable Ānanda, how does it have an end and is not without end?"

He answered: "Brahmin, let me ask you now; answer as you wish. Brahmin, what do you think? Did you previously have the desire to come to this monastery?"

The brahmin answered: "Yes, Ānanda."

"Just so, brahmin. Having arrived at the monastery, did that desire then cease?"

He answered: "Yes, Venerable Ānanda. Through effort, exertion, and deliberation, I came to the monastery."

He further asked: "Having arrived at the monastery, did that effort, exertion, and deliberation then cease?"

He answered: "Yes, Venerable Ānanda."

He further said to the brahmin: "Just so, brahmin. The Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, knowing and seeing, has taught the four bases of spiritual power as the one vehicle path to purify beings, eliminate suffering, and sever grief and sorrow. What are the four? The basis of spiritual power accomplished through concentration of desire with effort of abandoning, the basis of spiritual power accomplished through concentration of vigor, concentration of mind, and concentration of investigation with effort of abandoning. Thus, a noble disciple cultivates the basis of spiritual power accomplished through concentration of desire with effort of abandoning, dependent on seclusion, dependent on dispassion, dependent on cessation, dependent on relinquishment, directed toward release, until craving is severed. When craving is severed, that desire also ceases. One cultivates the basis of spiritual power accomplished through concentration of vigor, concentration of mind, and concentration of investigation with effort of abandoning, dependent on seclusion, dependent on dispassion, dependent on cessation, dependent on relinquishment, directed toward release, until craving is exhausted. When craving is exhausted, investigation then ceases. Brahmin, what do you think? Is this not an end?"

The brahmin said: "Venerable Ānanda, this is an end, not without end."

At that time, the brahmin, hearing what Venerable Ānanda said, rejoiced and expressed approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (562)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī in Ghośitārāma. Venerable Ānanda was also dwelling there.

At that time, the householder Ghośita went to Venerable Ānanda's place, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed Venerable Ānanda: "What is meant by 'one who teaches the Dharma in the world'? What is meant by 'one well-directed in the world'? What is meant by 'one who has well-arrived in the world'?"

Venerable Ānanda said to the householder Ghośita: "Let me now ask you; answer as you wish. Householder, what do you think? If there is one who teaches the Dharma that tames greed, tames anger, and tames delusion, can that person be called 'one who teaches the Dharma in the world' or not?"

The householder answered: "Venerable Ānanda, if there is one who teaches the Dharma that can tame greed, anger, and delusion, that person is indeed called 'one who teaches the Dharma in the world.'"

He further asked: "Householder, what do you think? If one in the world is directed toward taming greed, taming anger, and taming delusion, is this called 'well-directed in the world'? If one in the world has already tamed greed, anger, and delusion, is this called 'well-arrived,' or not?"

The householder answered: "Venerable Ānanda, if one has tamed greed, severed it without remainder, and severed anger and delusion without remainder, this is called 'well-arrived.'"

Venerable Ānanda replied: "Householder, I put questions to you tentatively, and you answered truthfully. The meaning is just so; you should receive and uphold it."

Householder Ghośita, hearing what Venerable Ānanda said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed, and departed.

---

### (563)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Vaiśālī at the Markaṭahrada-tīra (Monkey Pond Side) in the Kūṭāgāraśālā (Double-Storied Hall). Venerable Ānanda was also dwelling there.

At that time, the Licchavi Abhaya, a disciple of the Nirgrantha, and the Licchavi Paṇḍitakumāra, a disciple of the Ājīvika, both went to Venerable Ānanda's place. After exchanging greetings and courtesies, they sat to one side. At that time, the Licchavi Abhaya said to Venerable Ānanda: "My teacher Nirgrantha Jñātaputra teaches the method of extinguishing the burning, purification, and transcendence, and instructs his disciples with this path: 'Through the practice of austerities, one can entirely disgorge the karma of past lives. By not performing bodily actions, one cuts off the bridge, and in future lives there will be no further defilements. When all karma is exhausted, all suffering is exhausted. When suffering is exhausted, one reaches the ultimate end of suffering.' Venerable Ānanda, what do you think of this?"

Venerable Ānanda said to the Licchavi: "The Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, knowing and seeing, has taught three kinds of paths for extinguishing the burning, purification, and transcendence, as the one vehicle path to purify beings, remove grief and sorrow, overcome suffering, and attain the true Dharma. What are the three? A noble disciple dwells established in pure moral conduct, undertakes the Prātimokṣa, is complete in deportment, generates fearful apprehension regarding offenses and transgressions, and receives and upholds such complete pure moral conduct. Past karma is gradually disgorged, and one attains in the present life the extinguishing of the burning, without waiting for a future time. One is able to attain the true Dharma, penetrating it, seeing it directly, observing it, and realizing it through one's own wisdom. Licchavi householder, this is called the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, teaching from what he knows and sees the extinguishing of the burning, purification, and transcendence, as the one vehicle path to purify beings, eliminate suffering, overcome grief and sorrow, and attain the true Dharma.

"Furthermore, Licchavi, with such pure moral conduct complete, one separates from desires and unwholesome states, up to dwelling having attained the fourth dhyāna. This is called the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, teaching the extinguishing of the burning, up to attaining the true Dharma. Furthermore, there is the right attainment of samādhi, whereby one knows as it truly is this noble truth of suffering, this noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Endowed with such wisdom and mind, one no longer creates further karma, and past karma is gradually severed. One attains in the present the true Dharma, free from burning, without waiting for a future time, penetrating it, seeing it directly, and generating self-realized wisdom. Licchavi, this is called the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, teaching from what he knows and sees the third path of extinguishing the burning, purification, and transcendence, as the one vehicle path to purify beings, remove suffering, eliminate grief and sorrow, and attain the true Dharma."

At that time, the Nirgrantha disciple, the Licchavi Abhaya, remained silent.

Then the Ājīvika disciple, the Licchavi Paṇḍitakumāra, again said to the Licchavi Abhaya: "How strange! Abhaya, why do you remain silent? Do you not rejoice upon hearing the well-spoken Dharma taught by the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, from what he knows and sees?"

The Licchavi Abhaya replied: "I was contemplating the meaning; that is why I remained silent. Who, having heard the Dharma taught by the śramaṇa Gautama, would not rejoice? If anyone, having heard the Dharma taught by the śramaṇa Gautama, does not rejoice, that person is a fool who will long endure, through the long night, what is harmful and unbeneficial suffering."

At that time, the Nirgrantha disciple, the Licchavi Abhaya, and the Ājīvika disciple, Paṇḍitakumāra, having heard the Dharma spoken by the Buddha and expounded by Venerable Ānanda, rejoiced and expressed approval, rose from their seats, and departed.

---

### (564)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. Venerable Ānanda was also dwelling there.

At that time, there was a certain bhikṣuṇī who had developed an impure attachment toward Venerable Ānanda. She sent a messenger to tell Venerable Ānanda: "My body is afflicted with illness and suffering. I pray that the Venerable one will have compassion and come to see me."

Venerable Ānanda, in the early morning, put on his robes and took up his bowl, and went to that bhikṣuṇī's dwelling.

That bhikṣuṇī, seeing Venerable Ānanda approaching from afar, lay on her bed with her body exposed. Venerable Ānanda, seeing from afar that bhikṣuṇī's body, immediately restrained and guarded his sense faculties, turned his body around, and stood with his back toward her. That bhikṣuṇī, seeing Venerable Ānanda restrain his sense faculties, turn his body, and stand with his back toward her, immediately felt ashamed and embarrassed. She rose, put on her robes, spread out a seat, and came out to welcome Venerable Ānanda. She invited him to sit down, bowed at his feet, withdrew, and stood to one side.

At that time, Venerable Ānanda spoke the Dharma to her, saying: "Sister, this body of ours is nourished by impure food, nourished by pride, nourished by craving, and nourished by lust. Sister, that which depends on impure food, one should sever impure food. That which depends on pride, one should sever pride. That which depends on craving, one should sever craving. Sister, how does one depend on impure food and sever impure food? A noble disciple, with regard to food, eats by reflecting and measuring, without thoughts of attachment and pleasure, without thoughts of pride, without thoughts of embellishment, without thoughts of adornment, but for the sake of sustaining the body, for the sake of maintaining life, for the sake of curing the illness of hunger and thirst, for the sake of supporting the holy life, so that former feelings may cease and new feelings may not arise, cultivating and nourishing strength, happiness, and blameless ease. One should dwell thus. It is like a merchant who applies oil and grease to his cart, without thoughts of attachment, without thoughts of pride, without thoughts of embellishment, without thoughts of adornment, but for the sake of transporting goods. It is like one with a wound who applies oil to it, without thoughts of attachment and pleasure, without thoughts of pride, without thoughts of embellishment, without thoughts of adornment, but for the sake of healing the wound. Just so, a noble disciple eats by reflecting and measuring, without thoughts of attachment, without thoughts of pride, without thoughts of embellishment, without thoughts of adornment, but for the sake of maintaining life, for the sake of curing hunger and thirst, for the sake of supporting the holy life, so that former feelings may depart and new feelings may not arise, abiding in strength, happiness, and blameless ease of security. Sister, this is called depending on food to sever food.

"Depending on pride to sever pride: how does one depend on pride to sever pride? A disciple hears that a certain venerable one, a certain venerable one's disciple, has exhausted all defilements, and through the defilement-free liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, in the present life knows and bears witness for himself: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, and I know for myself that there will be no further becoming.' Having heard this, one thinks: 'That noble disciple has exhausted all defilements, up to knowing for himself that there will be no further becoming. Why then should I not exhaust all defilements? Why should I not know for myself that there will be no further becoming?' At that time, one is then able to sever all defilements, up to knowing for oneself that there will be no further becoming. Sister, this is called depending on pride to sever pride.

"Sister, how does one depend on craving to sever craving? A noble disciple hears that a certain venerable one, a certain venerable one's disciple, has exhausted all defilements, up to knowing for himself that there will be no further becoming. 'Why should we not exhaust all defilements, up to knowing for ourselves that there will be no further becoming?' At that time, one is able to sever all defilements, up to knowing for oneself that there will be no further becoming. Sister, this is called depending on craving to sever craving. Sister, for one who has no further action, the bridge of lust is cut off and severed."

When Venerable Ānanda spoke this Dharma, that bhikṣuṇī, far removed from dust and defilement, attained the purity of the Dharma-eye. That bhikṣuṇī saw the Dharma, obtained the Dharma, awakened to the Dharma, entered the Dharma, crossed beyond doubt, and not depending on others, attained fearlessness with regard to the true Dharma and Vinaya. She bowed at Venerable Ānanda's feet and said to Venerable Ānanda:

"I now openly confess my transgression. Foolish and unskillful, I had done such an unbecoming thing. Now, before Venerable Ānanda, I see my fault, know my fault, and openly confess and repent. Have compassion on me!"

Venerable Ānanda said to the bhikṣuṇī: "You now truly see your offense, truly know your offense; foolish and unskillful, you yourself know you committed an unbecoming offense. Since you now yourself know, yourself see, and repent, in the future you will attain the fulfillment of the precepts. I now accept your repentance, out of compassion, so that your wholesome qualities may grow and never diminish. Why is this? If one who sees one's own offense, knows one's own offense, and is able to repent, that person in the future will attain the fulfillment of the precepts, and wholesome qualities will grow and never diminish."

Venerable Ānanda then taught that bhikṣuṇī various aspects of the Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening her. Having done so, he rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (565)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was wandering among the people of Kiṃsuka, together with Venerable Ānanda, and arrived at the Bhaddasālā Grove, north of the village of Bhadda.

At that time, the young men of the village of Bhadda heard that Venerable Ānanda was wandering among the people of Kiṃsuka and was staying in the Bhaddasālā Grove, north of the village of Bhadda. Having heard this, they called to one another and gathered together, and went to Venerable Ānanda's place. They bowed at Venerable Ānanda's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side.

At that time, Venerable Ānanda addressed the young men: "Sons of noble lineage! The Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, taught four kinds of purity: purity of moral conduct, purity of mind, purity of view, and purity of liberation.

"What is purity of moral conduct? A noble disciple abides established in the precepts, undertakes the Prātimokṣa, with growing moral conduct and complete deportment, generating fearful apprehension regarding even the slightest offenses, and receives and upholds the training rules. If the aggregate of moral conduct is not yet fulfilled, one brings it to fulfillment; if already fulfilled, one maintains it in accordance. One desires to make effort, striving to transcend, being energetic and courageous, capable of sustaining all bodily and mental states, always able to maintain them. This is called the severance through purity of moral conduct.

"Sons of noble lineage, what is called the severance through purity of mind? A noble disciple, separated from desires and unwholesome states, up to dwelling having attained the fourth dhyāna. If the aggregate of concentration is not yet fulfilled, one brings it to fulfillment; if already fulfilled, one maintains it in accordance. One desires to make effort, up to constantly maintaining it. This is called the severance through purity of mind.

"Sons of noble lineage, what is called the severance through purity of view? A noble disciple hears the Great Teacher teach the Dharma. As it is thus taught, one thus enters into true and direct observation. Through such entering, one obtains joy, obtains sympathetic joy, and gains faith in the Buddha.

"Furthermore, a noble disciple, not hearing the Great Teacher teach the Dharma, yet hearing from other wise and esteemed practitioners of the holy life. As those esteemed practitioners of the holy life thus teach, one thus enters into true observation. Through such observation, one obtains joy and sympathetic joy with regard to that Dharma, and faith in the true Dharma.

"Furthermore, a noble disciple, neither hearing the Great Teacher teach the Dharma, nor hearing from wise and esteemed practitioners of the holy life, but reciting and reviewing what was previously heard and received. As one thus recites and reviews what was previously heard and received, one thus enters into that Dharma, up to gaining faith in the true Dharma.

"Furthermore, a noble disciple, neither hearing the Great Teacher teach the Dharma, nor hearing from wise and esteemed practitioners of the holy life, nor being able to recite and review what was previously received, but broadly explaining to others the Dharma previously heard. As one thus broadly explains to others the Dharma previously heard, one thus enters into the Dharma, observing with right wisdom, up to gaining faith in the true Dharma.

"Furthermore, a noble disciple, neither hearing the Great Teacher teach the Dharma, nor hearing from wise and esteemed practitioners of the holy life, nor being able to recite and review what was previously received, nor broadly explaining to others the Dharma previously heard, but in solitude, in a quiet place, contemplating and examining the Dharma previously heard. As one thus contemplates and examines, one thus enters into the true Dharma, up to gaining faith in the true Dharma. Thus, hearing from others and right internal contemplation: this is called causing right view that has not yet arisen to arise, and causing right view that has already arisen to increase. This is called bringing the aggregate of moral conduct that is not yet fulfilled to fulfillment, and maintaining what is already fulfilled in accordance. One desires to make effort, striving, up to constantly maintaining. This is called the severance through purity of view.

"Sons of noble lineage, what is the severance through purity of liberation? A noble disciple, with a mind of greed, attains liberation free from desire; with a mind of aversion and delusion, attains liberation free from desire. Thus, if liberation is not yet fulfilled, one brings it to fulfillment; if already fulfilled, one maintains it in accordance. One desires to make effort, up to constantly maintaining. This is called the severance through purity of liberation."

Sons of noble lineage! When Venerable Ānanda taught this Dharma, the young men of the village of Bhadda, hearing what Venerable Ānanda said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed, and departed.

---

### (566)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Āmra village in the Āmra grove, together with many senior bhikṣus.

At that time, the householder Citta went to the senior bhikṣus, bowed at their feet, withdrew, and sat to one side.

At that time, the senior bhikṣus taught the householder Citta various aspects of the Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening him. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened him with various teachings, they fell silent. Then the householder Citta bowed at the feet of the senior bhikṣus and went to the quarters of Bhikṣu Nāgadatta. He bowed at Bhikṣu Nāgadatta's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side.

At that time, Bhikṣu Nāgadatta asked the householder Citta about the spoken verse:

> "Branches green, covered with white,
> A one-spoked chariot that rolls along,
> Free from bonds, it comes observing,
> The stream is cut, no longer bound."

"Householder, what is the meaning of this verse?"

The householder Citta said: "Venerable Nāgadatta, did the World-Honored One speak this verse?"

He answered: "Yes."

The householder Citta said to Venerable Nāgadatta: "Venerable one, allow me a moment of silence to contemplate this meaning."

After a moment of silent contemplation, he said to Venerable Nāgadatta: "'Green' refers to moral conduct. 'Covered with white' refers to liberation. 'One spoke' refers to mindfulness of the body. 'Rolling' refers to rolling forth. 'Chariot' refers to calm and insight. 'Free from bonds' refers to the three kinds of bonds, namely greed, aversion, and delusion. Those arahants whose defilements are ended, extinguished, fully known, and whose roots are severed, like a palmyra tree whose crown is cut off and will not grow again, are subject to the principle of non-arising in future lives.

"'Observing' refers to seeing. 'Coming' refers to a person. 'The stream is cut' refers to the stream of craving in saṃsāra. Those arahant bhikṣus whose defilements are ended, fully known, whose roots are severed, like a palmyra tree whose crown is cut off and will not grow again, are subject to the principle of non-arising in future lives.

"'No longer bound' refers to the three bonds: the bond of sensual desire, the bond of aversion, and the bond of delusion. Those arahant bhikṣus whose defilements are ended, severed, fully known, whose roots are severed, like a palmyra tree whose crown is cut off and will not grow again, are subject to the principle of non-arising in future lives. Therefore, Venerable Nāgadatta, the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "Branches green, covered with white,
> A one-spoked chariot that rolls along,
> Free from bonds, it comes observing,
> The stream is cut, no longer bound.

"This verse spoken by the World-Honored One, I have now explained."

Venerable Nāgadatta asked the householder Citta: "Had you heard this meaning before?"

He answered: "I had not heard it."

Venerable Nāgadatta said: "Householder, you have obtained great benefit! In this most profound Buddha-Dharma, you have entered with the noble eye of wisdom."

At that time, the householder Citta, hearing what Venerable Nāgadatta said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed, and departed.

---

### (567)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Āmra village in the Āmra grove monastery, together with many senior bhikṣus.

At that time, the householder Citta went to the senior bhikṣus, bowed at their feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. At that time, the senior bhikṣus taught the householder Citta various aspects of the Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening him. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened him, they fell silent.

At that time, the householder Citta went to Venerable Bhikṣu Nāgadatta, bowed at his feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. Venerable Nāgadatta addressed the householder Citta: "There is the immeasurable mind samādhi, the signless mind samādhi, the nothingness mind samādhi, and the emptiness mind samādhi. How is it, householder? Are these Dharmas of different meaning with different names, or are they of one meaning with different names?"

The householder Citta asked Venerable Nāgadatta: "Were these samādhis taught by the World-Honored One, or are they the Venerable one's own ideas?"

Venerable Nāgadatta answered: "These were taught by the World-Honored One."

The householder Citta said to Venerable Nāgadatta: "Allow me to briefly contemplate this meaning, and then I shall answer."

After briefly contemplating, he said to Venerable Nāgadatta: "There are Dharmas of different meaning, different phrasing, and different flavor, and there are Dharmas of one meaning with different flavor."

He further asked: "Householder, what Dharmas are of different meaning, different phrasing, and different flavor?"

The householder answered: "The immeasurable samādhi is when a noble disciple, with a mind of loving-kindness, without enmity, without hatred, without anger, with a broad and expansive mind, cultivating immeasurably and pervading universally, fills one direction. Likewise the second direction, the third direction, the fourth direction, above and below, throughout the entire world, with a mind of loving-kindness, without enmity, without hatred, without anger, with a broad and expansive mind, cultivating immeasurably, filling all directions, pervading and dwelling throughout the entire world. This is called the immeasurable samādhi. What is the signless samādhi? When a noble disciple does not attend to any signs whatsoever, and realizes with the body the signless mind samādhi, this is called the signless mind samādhi. What is the nothingness mind samādhi? When a noble disciple, having transcended the sphere of boundless consciousness entirely, enters 'there is nothing,' and abides with a mind of nothingness, this is called the nothingness mind samādhi. What is the emptiness samādhi? When a noble disciple contemplates the world as empty, truly observing that the world is empty, ever-abiding and unchanging, not self, not belonging to self, this is called the emptiness mind samādhi. These are Dharmas of different meaning, different phrasing, and different flavor."

He further asked: "Householder, what Dharmas are of one meaning with different flavor?"

He answered: "Venerable one, greed is measurable. If there is no contention, it is supremely immeasurable. Greed has a sign; anger and delusion have signs. No contention is signless. Greed is 'something'; anger and delusion are 'something.' No contention is nothingness. Furthermore, no contention is empty: empty of greed, empty of anger, empty of delusion, ever-abiding and unchanging, empty of self, empty of what belongs to self. This is called the Dharma of one meaning with different flavor."

Venerable Nāgadatta asked: "How is it, householder? Had you heard this meaning before?"

He answered: "Venerable one, I had not heard it."

He further said to the householder: "You have obtained great benefit! In the most profound Buddha-Dharma, you have entered with the noble eye of wisdom in the present."

The householder Citta, hearing what Venerable Nāgadatta said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed, and departed.

---

### (568)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Āmra village in the Āmra grove, together with senior bhikṣus.

At that time, the householder Citta went to the senior bhikṣus, paid respects to the senior monks, then went to Venerable Bhikṣu Kāma's quarters, bowed at Venerable Kāma's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed Venerable Bhikṣu Kāma: "What are called 'formations'? How are formations defined?"

Bhikṣu Kāma said: "Formations are the three formations: bodily formations, verbal formations, and mental formations."

He further asked: "What are bodily formations? What are verbal formations? What are mental formations?"

He answered: "Householder, exhalation and inhalation are called bodily formations. Initial thought and sustained thought are called verbal formations. Perception and intention are called mental formations."

He further asked: "Why are exhalation and inhalation called bodily formations? Why are initial thought and sustained thought called verbal formations? Why are perception and intention called mental formations?"

He answered: "Householder, exhalation and inhalation are bodily phenomena; they depend on the body, belong to the body, and operate in dependence on the body. Therefore exhalation and inhalation are called bodily formations. Because there is initial thought and sustained thought, there is then verbal speech. Therefore initial thought and sustained thought are verbal formations. Perception and intention are mental formations; they depend on the mind, belong to the mind, and operate in dependence on the mind. Therefore perception and intention are mental formations."

He further said: "Venerable one, because initial thought and sustained thought give rise to verbal speech, initial thought and sustained thought are called verbal formations. Perception and intention are mental concomitants; they depend on the mind, belong to the mind, and the perceptual process operates. Therefore perception and intention are called mental formations."

He further asked: "Venerable one, how many dharmas are there such that:

> "When a person relinquishes the body,
> That body, a corpse, lies upon the ground,
> Discarded among the charnel grounds,
> Mindless, like wood or stone?"

He answered: "Householder,

> "Life, warmth, and consciousness,
> When one relinquishes the body, are all relinquished together.
> That body, discarded among the charnel grounds,
> Is mindless, like wood or stone."

He further asked: "Venerable one, is there a difference between one who has died and one who has entered the cessation attainment?"

He answered: "When one relinquishes life-force and warmth, all sense faculties are destroyed, and body and life are separated, this is called death. In the cessation attainment, bodily, verbal, and mental formations cease, yet one does not relinquish the life-force, does not separate from warmth, and the sense faculties are not destroyed; body and life remain connected. This is the distinguishing characteristic between death and entering the cessation attainment."

He further asked: "Venerable one, how does one enter the cessation attainment?"

He answered: "Householder, one who enters the cessation attainment does not say: 'I am entering the cessation attainment,' or 'I shall enter the cessation attainment.' Rather, one has previously made the gradual calming preparation, and in accordance with that prior preparation, one enters the attainment."

He further asked: "Venerable one, when entering the cessation attainment, which dharma ceases first: bodily formations, verbal formations, or mental formations?"

He answered: "Householder, for one entering the cessation attainment, verbal formations cease first, then bodily formations, then mental formations."

He further asked: "Venerable one, how does one emerge from the cessation attainment?"

He answered: "Householder, one who emerges from the cessation attainment also does not think: 'I am now emerging from the attainment,' or 'I shall emerge from the attainment.' Rather, one has previously made the preparation of mind, and in accordance with that prior mind, one arises."

He further asked: "Venerable one, when arising from the cessation attainment, which dharma arises first: bodily formations, verbal formations, or mental formations?"

He answered: "Householder, when arising from the cessation attainment, mental formations arise first, then bodily formations, then verbal formations."

He further asked: "Venerable one, one who enters the cessation attainment: toward what does one tend, flow, and pour?"

He answered: "Householder, one who enters the cessation attainment tends toward seclusion, flows toward seclusion, pours toward seclusion; tends toward renunciation, flows toward renunciation, pours toward renunciation; tends toward nirvāṇa, flows toward nirvāṇa, pours toward nirvāṇa."

He further asked: "Venerable one, when abiding in the cessation attainment, how many contacts does one contact?"

He answered: "Householder, one contacts the contact of the imperturbable, contacts the signless, and contacts nothingness."

He further asked: "Venerable one, when entering the cessation attainment, how many dharmas does one perform?"

He answered: "Householder, you should have asked this question first. Why do you ask it now? Nevertheless, I shall explain it for you. A bhikṣu who enters the cessation attainment performs two dharmas: calm and insight."

At that time, the householder Citta, hearing what Venerable Kāma said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed, and departed.

---

### (569)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Āmra village in the Āmra grove, together with many senior bhikṣus.

At that time, the householder Citta went to the senior bhikṣus, bowed at their feet, and sat to one side. The senior bhikṣus taught the householder Citta various aspects of the Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening him. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened him, they fell silent.

At that time, the householder Citta rose from his seat, bared his right shoulder, placed his right knee on the ground, joined his ten fingers in a gesture of respect, and invited the senior monks, saying: "May all the Venerable ones accept a modest meal from me." At that time, the senior monks silently accepted the invitation.

At that time, the householder, knowing the senior monks had silently accepted the invitation, bowed at their feet and departed. He returned to his home, prepared various foods and drinks, set up seats, and early in the morning sent a messenger to announce: "The time has come."

At that time, the senior monks put on their robes, took up their bowls, went to the householder's residence, and took their seats. The householder bowed at the feet of the senior monks, sat to one side, and addressed the senior monks: "What is meant by 'various elements'? What are the various elements?"

At that time, the senior monks remained silent. This happened three times.

At that time, Venerable Isidatta, who held the lowest seat among them, addressed the senior bhikṣus: "Venerable ones, I wish to answer the householder's question."

The senior monks answered: "You may."

The householder Citta then asked: "Venerable one, what is meant by 'various elements'? What are the various elements?"

Isidatta answered: "Householder, the eye element is distinct, the form element is distinct, the eye-consciousness element is distinct. The ear element is distinct, the sound element is distinct, the ear-consciousness element is distinct. The nose element is distinct, the odor element is distinct, the nose-consciousness element is distinct. The tongue element is distinct, the taste element is distinct, the tongue-consciousness element is distinct. The body element is distinct, the tangible element is distinct, the body-consciousness element is distinct. The mind element is distinct, the dharma element is distinct, the mind-consciousness element is distinct. Thus, householder, these are called the various elements."

At that time, the householder Citta served various pure and fine foods and drinks as offerings. After the Sangha had finished eating, rinsed their mouths, and washed their bowls, the householder Citta set out a low seat and sat before the senior monks to hear the Dharma.

At that time, the senior monks taught the householder various aspects of the Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening him. Having done so, they rose from their seats and departed.

At that time, the senior monks said to Isidatta along the road: "Excellent! Excellent, Bhikṣu Isidatta! You truly have skillful eloquence and know how to speak appropriately. At other times, you should always respond thus."

At that time, the senior monks, hearing what Isidatta said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (570)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Āmra village in the Āmra grove, together with many senior bhikṣus.

At that time, the householder Citta went to the senior monks, bowed at their feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the senior monks: "In the various views held in the world, some speak of a self, some speak of a being, some speak of a life-span, some speak of good and bad fortune in the world. How is it, Venerable ones? What is the basis of these various views? What is their origin? What gives rise to them? What causes their occurrence?"

At that time, the senior monks remained silent and did not answer. This happened three times; three times they remained silent.

At that time, there was a junior bhikṣu named Isidatta who addressed the senior monks: "I wish to answer the householder's question."

The senior monks said: "If you are able to answer well, then answer."

At that time, the householder asked Isidatta: "Venerable one, all the views held in the world: what is their basis? What is their origin? What gives rise to them? What causes their occurrence?"

Venerable Isidatta answered: "Householder, all the views held in the world, whether speaking of a self, a being, a life-span, or good and bad fortune in the world, all these views are entirely based on the view of a self-identity, originate from the view of a self-identity, arise from the view of a self-identity, and occur through the view of a self-identity."

He further asked: "Venerable one, what is the view of a self-identity?"

He answered: "Householder, an ignorant, unlearned ordinary person sees form as self, form as other than self, self as in form, or form as in self. He sees feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness as self, consciousness as other than self, self as in consciousness, or consciousness as in self. Householder, this is called the view of a self-identity."

He further asked: "Venerable one, how does one become free from this view of a self-identity?"

He answered: "Householder, a learned noble disciple does not see form as self, does not see form as other than self, does not see self as in form or form as in self. He does not see feeling, perception, formations, or consciousness as self, does not see consciousness as other than self, does not see self as in consciousness or consciousness as in self. This is called being free from the view of a self-identity."

He further asked: "Venerable one, what was your father's name? Where were you born?"

He answered: "Householder, I was born in a householder family in the eastern region."

The householder Citta said to Venerable Isidatta: "My father and the Venerable one's father were originally good friends."

Isidatta answered: "Just so, householder."

The householder Citta said to Isidatta: "Venerable one, if you could stay in this Āmra grove, I would supply you for the rest of my life with robes, food and drink, and medicine in case of illness." Venerable Isidatta silently accepted the invitation.

At that time, Venerable Isidatta, having accepted the householder Citta's invitation, was hindered by the offerings and for a long time did not go to see the World-Honored One.

At that time, the senior bhikṣus taught the householder Citta various aspects of the Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening him. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened him, the householder Citta rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed, and departed.

---

### (571)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Āmra village in the Āmra grove, together with many senior bhikṣus.

At that time, the householder Citta went to the senior monks, bowed at their feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the senior bhikṣus: "May all the Venerable ones accept my invitation for a meal at the cattle-herding place."

At that time, the senior monks silently accepted the invitation.

The householder Citta, knowing the senior monks had silently accepted the invitation, returned home. Through the night he prepared various foods and drinks, and early in the morning set up seats and sent a messenger to announce to the senior monks: "The time has come."

The senior monks put on their robes, took up their bowls, went to the householder Citta's residence at the cattle-herding place, and took their seats.

At that time, the householder Citta personally served various foods and drinks. After eating, they washed their bowls and rinsed their mouths. The householder Citta set out a low seat and sat before the senior monks to hear the Dharma.

At that time, the senior monks taught the householder various aspects of the Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening him. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened him, they rose from their seats and departed. The householder Citta also followed behind them.

The senior monks, having eaten their fill of ghee, curds, and honey, in the hot season after spring, became fatigued and exhausted on the road.

At that time, there was a junior bhikṣu named Mahaka who addressed the senior monks: "Today is very hot. I wish to conjure up clouds, rain, and a cool breeze. Would that be acceptable?"

The senior monks answered: "If you are able, that would be excellent."

At that time, Mahaka then entered samādhi, and in accordance with his right attainment, clouds arose at once, a fine rain fell gently, and a cool breeze came softly from all four directions. Upon arriving at the gate of the monastery, Venerable Mahaka said to the senior monks: "Shall I stop now?"

They answered: "You may stop."

At that time, Venerable Mahaka then stopped his supernatural power and returned to his own quarters.

At that time, the householder Citta thought: "Even the most junior bhikṣu has such great supernatural powers; how much more so the middle-ranked and the senior monks!" He then bowed at the feet of the senior bhikṣus and followed Bhikṣu Mahaka to his dwelling quarters. He bowed at Venerable Mahaka's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side, saying: "Venerable one, I wish to see the Venerable one display supernatural transformation surpassing ordinary human capacities."

Venerable Mahaka said: "Householder, do not be frightened."

Thus he requested three times, and three times was not granted permission. The householder still repeatedly requested to see the Venerable one's supernatural transformation.

Venerable Mahaka said to the householder: "Go outside first, gather dry grass and wood into a pile, and cover it with a white cotton cloth."

The householder Citta did as instructed, went outside, piled up firewood into a heap, and came to tell Venerable Mahaka: "The woodpile is ready, covered with a white cloth."

At that time, Venerable Mahaka entered the fire-light samādhi, and from the keyhole of the door, flames of fire light emerged and burned the pile of firewood completely, with only the white cloth remaining unburned. He said to the householder: "Do you see now?"

He answered: "I have seen it. Venerable one, it is truly marvelous!"

Venerable Mahaka said to the householder: "You should know that all this has heedfulness as its basis, originates from heedfulness, arises from heedfulness, and occurs through heedfulness. Through heedfulness one attains anuttarā-samyak-saṃbodhi. Therefore, householder, this and other merits are all based on heedfulness, originate from heedfulness, arise from heedfulness, and occur through heedfulness. Through heedfulness one attains anuttarā-samyak-saṃbodhi, as well as the other factors of the path."

The householder Citta said to Venerable Mahaka: "May you always dwell in this grove. I shall for the rest of my life provide robes, bedding, food and drink, and medicine in case of illness."

Venerable Mahaka, having reasons for traveling, did not accept the invitation.

The householder Citta, having heard the Dharma teaching, rejoiced and expressed approval, then rose from his seat, bowed, and departed.

Venerable Mahaka, not wishing for the obstruction and offense of being bound by offerings, then rose from his seat and departed, and never returned.

---

### (572)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Āmra grove, together with many senior bhikṣus.

At that time, many senior bhikṣus gathered in the dining hall and engaged in this discussion: "Venerable ones, what do you think? Is it the eye that binds forms, or forms that bind the eye? Likewise with the ear and sounds, the nose and odors, the tongue and tastes, the body and tangibles, the mind and mental objects: is it the mind that binds mental objects, or mental objects that bind the mind?"

At that time, the householder Citta, having some business to attend to, happened to pass by the monastery. Seeing the senior bhikṣus gathered in the dining hall, he went forward, bowed at the feet of the senior monks, and after bowing asked: "Venerable ones, gathered in the dining hall, what Dharma were you discussing?"

The senior monks answered: "Householder, today we gathered in this dining hall and engaged in this discussion: 'Is it the eye that binds forms, or forms that bind the eye? Likewise with the ear and sounds, the nose and odors, the tongue and tastes, the body and tangibles, the mind and mental objects: is it the mind that binds mental objects, or mental objects that bind the mind?'"

The householder asked: "What was the Venerable ones' conclusion on this matter?"

The senior monks said: "What does the householder think?"

The householder answered the senior monks: "In my understanding, it is not the eye that binds forms, nor forms that bind the eye, and so on up to not the mind that binds mental objects, nor mental objects that bind the mind. Rather, in between, where there is desire and lust, it is by that that the binding occurs. It is like two oxen, one black and one white, yoked together by a yoke and harness. If someone were to ask: 'Does the black ox bind the white ox, or does the white ox bind the black ox?' would that be a proper question?"

They answered: "Householder, that would not be a proper question. Why? Because it is not the black ox that binds the white ox, nor the white ox that binds the black ox. Rather, the yoke and harness are the binding."

"Just so, Venerable ones, it is not the eye that binds forms, nor forms that bind the eye, and so on up to not the mind that binds mental objects, nor mental objects that bind the mind. Rather, in between, desire and lust are the binding."

At that time, the householder Citta, hearing what the senior monks said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed, and departed.

---

### (573)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Āmra grove. At that time, there was an Ājīvika ascetic who was an old friend of the householder Citta's family. He came to the householder Citta's place. After exchanging greetings and courtesies, he stood to one side.

The householder Citta asked the Ājīvika ascetic: "How long have you been a renunciant?"

He answered: "Householder, I have been a renunciant for over twenty years."

The householder Citta asked: "In over twenty years since your going forth, have you attained any superhuman state, any ultimate knowledge and vision, any peaceful abiding?"

He answered: "Householder, although I have been a renunciant for over twenty years, I have not attained any superhuman state, any ultimate knowledge and vision, or any peaceful abiding. I have only nakedness, plucking out of hair, begging for food, wandering among people, and sleeping in the dust and ashes."

The householder Citta said: "This is not a praiseworthy Dharma and discipline. This is wrong knowledge, not a path leading out, not an awakening worthy of the name, not a place deserving praise, not something to be relied upon. In vain have you been a renunciant for over twenty years, going naked, plucking out hair, begging for food, wandering among people, and sleeping in ashes and dust."

The Ājīvika asked the householder Citta: "How long have you been a disciple of the śramaṇa Gautama?"

The householder Citta answered: "I have been a disciple of the World-Honored One for over twenty years."

He further asked the householder Citta: "Having been a disciple of the śramaṇa Gautama for over twenty years, have you attained any superhuman state, any superior and ultimate knowledge and vision?"

The householder Citta answered: "You should know this now: the householder Citta will certainly never again pass through a womb to be reborn, will never again add to the charnel grounds, will never again receive a body of blood and flesh. As the World-Honored One has taught, the five lower fetters: I do not see a single one of them that has not been severed. If a single fetter were not severed, one would return again to be born in this world."

When this was said, the Ājīvika wept and shed tears, wiping his face with his garment. He said to the householder Citta: "What should I do now?"

The householder Citta answered: "If you are able to go forth in the true Dharma and Vinaya, I shall provide you with robes, a bowl, and requisites for your person."

The Ājīvika thought for a moment, then said to the householder Citta: "I now agree. Show me what I should do."

At that time, the householder Citta brought the Ājīvika to the senior monks, bowed at their feet, sat to one side, and addressed the senior bhikṣus: "Venerable ones, this Ājīvika is an old friend of my family. He now wishes to go forth and become a bhikṣu. May the Venerable ones ordain him and let him go forth. I shall provide robes, a bowl, and all requisites."

The senior monks then allowed him to go forth, shaved off his hair and beard, and he put on the kāṣāya robe. Having gone forth, he contemplated: "For this purpose have good men shaved off their hair and beard, put on the kāṣāya robe, gone forth from household life, and progressed in the training of the path, purely cultivating the holy life." He attained arahantship.

---

### (574)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Āmra village in the Āmra grove, together with senior bhikṣus.

At that time, Nirgrantha Jñātaputra, with five hundred followers, came to the Āmra grove, wishing to entice the householder Citta to become his disciple. The householder Citta heard that Nirgrantha Jñātaputra had come to the Āmra grove with five hundred followers, wishing to entice him to become a disciple. Having heard this, he went to his place. After exchanging greetings, each sat to one side.

At that time, Nirgrantha Jñātaputra said to the householder Citta: "Do you believe that the śramaṇa Gautama attains a samādhi without initial thought and without sustained thought?"

The householder Citta answered: "I did not come here based on faith."

The Ājīvika said: "Householder, you are not deceitful, not fraudulent; you are straightforward, born of straightforwardness. Householder, if one could stop initial thought and sustained thought, one could also tie up the wind with a rope. If one could stop initial thought and sustained thought, one could also cut off the flow of the Ganges with a handful of dirt. In walking, standing, sitting, and lying down, knowledge and vision are constantly arising for me."

The householder Citta asked Nirgrantha Jñātaputra: "Which comes first, faith or knowledge? Between faith and knowledge, which is prior? Which is superior?"

Nirgrantha Jñātaputra answered: "Faith should come first, and then knowledge follows. Comparing faith and knowledge, knowledge is superior."

The householder Citta said to Nirgrantha Jñātaputra: "I have already attained the cessation of initial thought and sustained thought, with inner purity and mental unification, without initial thought and without sustained thought, and dwell having attained the second dhyāna with the joy and happiness born of samādhi. I dwell in this samādhi during the day, and I dwell in this samādhi at night; throughout the entire night I constantly abide in this samādhi. Having such knowledge, what need have I for faith in the World-Honored One?"

Nirgrantha Jñātaputra said: "You are deceitful, fraudulent, crooked, and born of crookedness!"

The householder Citta said: "You first said I was not deceitful, not fraudulent, straightforward, and born of straightforwardness. Now how do you say I am deceitful, fraudulent, crooked, and born of crookedness? If your first statement was true, then your latter statement is false. If your latter statement is true, then your first statement is false. You first said: 'In walking, standing, sitting, and lying down, knowledge and vision are constantly arising for me.' Yet regarding what comes before and after, you don't even know about small matters. How could you know about superhuman states, knowing and seeing, or peaceful abiding?" The householder further asked Nirgrantha Jñātaputra: "There exists one who, with one question, one explanation, and one reasoned discourse, up to ten questions, ten explanations, and ten reasoned discourses, can answer. Do you have this ability or not? If you do not have even one question, one explanation, one reasoned discourse, up to ten questions, ten explanations, ten reasoned discourses, how could you entice me? And yet you came to this Āmra grove wishing to deceive me?"

Thereupon Nirgrantha Jñātaputra closed his mouth, shook his head, crossed his arms, and departed, never looking back.

---

### (575)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Āmra village in the Āmra grove, together with many senior bhikṣus.

At that time, the householder Citta was suffering from illness, and his relatives were gathered around him. Many devas came to the householder and said to the householder Citta: "Householder, you should make a vow to become a wheel-turning king."

The householder Citta said to the devas: "Even a wheel-turning king is impermanent, suffering, empty, and without self."

At that time, the householder's relatives said to the householder: "You should focus your mind! You should focus your mind!"

The householder Citta said to his relatives: "Why do you tell me to focus my mind, focus my mind?"

His relatives said: "You said: 'Impermanent, suffering, empty, without self.' That is why we tell you to focus your mind, focus your mind."

The householder said to his relatives: "Devas and celestial beings have come to my place and said to me: 'You should make a vow to become a wheel-turning sage king; according to your vow, you will attain the result.' I answered them: 'Even a wheel-turning king is also impermanent, suffering, empty, and without self.'"

His relatives said to the householder Citta: "What merit does a wheel-turning king have, that those devas urged you to aspire to it?"

The householder answered: "A wheel-turning king rules and transforms through the true Dharma. Therefore the devas, seeing such merit and benefit, came to urge me to make the aspiration."

His relatives said: "What is your mind inclined toward now?"

The householder answered: "Relatives, my mind is set only on this: I shall never again pass through a womb to be reborn, I shall never again add to the charnel grounds, I shall never again receive a body of blood and flesh. As the World-Honored One has taught, the five lower fetters: I do not see any that have not been severed by me. If a single fetter were not severed, I would return to be born in this world."

Thereupon the householder rose from his bed, sat cross-legged, established right mindfulness before him, and spoke this verse:

> "Food and possessions long accumulated,
> Broadly crossing over many difficulties,
> Giving to the supreme fields of merit,
> I planted these five kinds of power.
> With such purpose and aspiration,
> A layperson dwelling in a household,
> I have fully obtained these benefits,
> Already freed from many difficulties.
> What the world has heard and learned,
> Keeping far from many difficulties,
> To know that happiness born of life is gradually hard to obtain,
> Following in accord with the Perfectly Enlightened One.
> Making offerings to those who uphold the precepts,
> Who have well cultivated the holy life,
> The arahants whose defilements are ended,
> And the śrāvakas, the munis.
> Thus transcending all views,
> In the supreme places above,
> Constantly practicing the giving of a noble person,
> In the end one obtains great fruit.
> Practicing many kinds of giving,
> Giving to excellent fields of merit,
> At the end of life in this world,
> One is reborn in the heavens.
> With the five desires fully satisfied,
> Immeasurable joy and delight of mind,
> One obtains these marvelous rewards
> Because of having no stinginess.
> In whatever place one is reborn,
> One is never without delight."

After the householder Citta spoke this verse, he immediately passed away and was reborn in the Avṛha heaven.

At that time, the deva Citta had this thought: "I should not remain here. I should go to Jambudvīpa to pay respects to the senior bhikṣus." In the time it takes a strong man to flex and extend his arm, by his divine power he arrived at the Āmra grove and released his heavenly radiance, illuminating the entire Āmra grove.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu had risen during the

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099, Fascicle 22

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

---

## (576)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse to the Buddha:

> "Without dwelling in Nandana Grove,
> One can never obtain true happiness;
> Within the palace of the Trāyastriṃśa Heaven,
> One gains the fame of Lord of the Gods."

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "Foolish child, what do you know
> Of what the arahants have said?
> All conditioned things are impermanent;
> This is the dharma of arising and ceasing.
> What arises will also cease;
> The bliss of their complete cessation is true happiness."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (577)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse:

> "Having cut all hooks and chains,
> The sage is without a home.
> That a śramaṇa should engage in teaching others,
> I do not call that 'well done.'"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "All classes of living beings
> Are mutually bound and entangled.
> Those who possess wisdom,
> Who among them could not feel compassion?
> Out of compassion, the Sugata
> Constantly instructs living beings.
> For one who has compassion for beings,
> This is what the Dharma requires."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, and instantly vanished.

---

## (578)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse:

> "One who constantly cultivates a heart of shame and conscience,
> Such a person appears from time to time.
> He can keep far from all evil,
> Like a fine horse that responds to the sight of the whip."

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "One who constantly cultivates a heart of shame and conscience,
> Such a person is truly rare.
> He can keep far from all evil,
> Like a fine horse that responds to the sight of the whip."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (579)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse, questioning the Buddha:

> "Not drawing near to the true Dharma,
> Delighting and attached to wrong views,
> Asleep and unaware,
> After long kalpas, can the mind awaken?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "Exclusively cultivating the true Dharma,
> Keeping far from unwholesome deeds,
> This is the arahant whose outflows are exhausted;
> In a perilous and evil world, he remains equanimous."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (580)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse:

> "Through the Dharma, well tamed and subdued,
> Not following various views,
> Though one may fall into sleep,
> One is able to awaken at the proper time."

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "If through the Dharma one is tamed and subdued,
> Not following other divergent views,
> When ignorance is fully exhausted,
> One can cross beyond worldly attachments."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (581)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse, questioning the Buddha:

> "If an arahant bhikṣu,
> Whose own task has been completed,
> Whose outflows are entirely exhausted,
> Who bears this final body,
> Does he declare 'I exist,'
> And does he speak of 'mine' or not?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "If an arahant bhikṣu,
> Whose own task has been completed,
> Whose outflows are entirely exhausted,
> Who bears this final body,
> Even if he says 'I exist,'
> And speaks of 'mine,' there is no fault."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "If an arahant bhikṣu,
> Whose own task has been completed,
> Whose outflows are entirely exhausted,
> Who bears this very last body,
> Does his mind rely on the conceit of 'I,'
> When he says 'I exist'
> And speaks of 'mine'?
> Does such speaking occur?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "He has already left behind the conceit of 'I';
> No more does the conceit of 'I' arise in his mind.
> Having transcended 'I' and 'mine,'
> I declare him one whose outflows are exhausted.
> Regarding 'I' and 'mine,'
> His mind is forever unattached.
> Well understanding worldly conventions of language,
> He speaks using merely conventional designations."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (582)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse to the Buddha:

> "If an arahant bhikṣu,
> Whose outflows are exhausted, bearing his final body,
> Does he perhaps say 'I exist,'
> And speak of 'mine' or not?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "If an arahant bhikṣu,
> Whose outflows are exhausted, bearing his final body,
> He also says 'I exist,'
> And also speaks of 'mine.'"

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "If an arahant bhikṣu,
> Whose own task has been completed,
> Whose outflows are entirely exhausted,
> Who bears only this very last body,
> What does he mean when he says 'I exist'?
> What does he mean by 'mine'?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "If an arahant bhikṣu,
> Whose own task has been completed,
> Whose outflows are entirely exhausted,
> Who bears only this very last body,
> He says 'my outflows are exhausted,'
> And he is not attached to 'mine.'
> Well understanding worldly conventions of language,
> He speaks using merely conventional designations."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (583)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Rāhu, the asura king, obstructed the moon deity. Then all the moon deities were greatly frightened and came to the Buddha's abode, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and stood to one side. They spoke a verse praising the Buddha:

> "Now we pay homage to the Supreme Awakened One,
> Who is able to free all from obstructions.
> We now encounter suffering and distress;
> Therefore we come to take refuge.
> We, the moon deities,
> Take refuge in the Sugata.
> The Buddha has compassion for the world;
> May he release the asura."

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "Destroying all darkness,
> Light illuminates the open sky.
> Now Vairocana
> Shines forth with pure radiance.
> Rāhu, release the sky!
> Swiftly let go of the hare-marked one!"

Rāhu the asura then released the moon and departed. His entire body broke out in sweat, and he trembled with fear, unable to calm himself. His spirit was confused and his mind bewildered, like a person gravely ill.

At that time, there was an asura named Vemacitrin. Seeing Rāhu the asura hastily release the moon and depart, he spoke a verse:

> "Rāhu the asura,
> Why did you release the moon so swiftly?
> Your whole body is drenched in sweat,
> Like a person gravely ill."

Rāhu the asura replied in verse:

> "Gautama spoke a dhāraṇī verse.
> Had I not swiftly released the moon,
> My head would have split into seven pieces,
> And I would have suffered agonies near death."

Vemacitrin the asura then spoke a verse:

> "The arising of a Buddha is unprecedented;
> He brings peace and safety to the world.
> He speaks a dhāraṇī verse that causes
> Rāhu to release the moon."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the moon deities, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, paid their respects, and departed.

---

## (584)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse, questioning the Buddha:

> "Is there a clan origin or not?
> Is there a continuing birth clan?
> Are there mutual connections or not?
> How is one freed from bondage?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "I have no clan origin,
> Nor any continuing birth clan.
> Mutual connections are forever severed;
> I am liberated from all bondage."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "What is called 'clan origin'?
> What is the 'continuing birth clan'?
> What are 'mutual connections'?
> What is called 'firm bondage'?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "The mother is the world's clan origin;
> The wife is called the continuing birth clan;
> Children are the mutual connections;
> Craving and desire are the firm bondage.
> I have no such clan origin,
> Nor any continuing birth clan.
> Mutual connections also are no more;
> This is called freedom from firm bondage."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Excellent, to be without clan origin!
> Excellent also, to be without a birth clan!
> Excellent, to be without connections!
> Excellent, to be freed from bondage!
> Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All enmities he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (585)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling among the Śākyans at the Ullatanā stūpa.

At that time, the World-Honored One had recently shaved his beard and hair. During the last watch of the night, he sat cross-legged, with body upright and mind correct, mindfulness established before him, with his robe covering his head.

At that time, near the Ullatanā stūpa there was a deity dwelling. Releasing radiance from his body, which illuminated the entire monastery, he said to the Buddha: "Śramaṇa, are you grieving?"

The Buddha said to the deity: "What have I lost?"

The deity asked again: "Śramaṇa, are you joyful?"

The Buddha said to the deity: "What have I gained?"

The deity asked again: "Śramaṇa, are you neither grieving nor joyful?"

The Buddha said to the deity: "Just so, just so."

At that time, the deity spoke a verse:

> "Is it that you are free from afflictions?
> Is it that you are without joy?
> Why do you dwell alone?
> Not destroyed by displeasure?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "I am without affliction, liberated;
> Also without joy.
> Displeasure cannot destroy me;
> Therefore I dwell alone."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "How are you without affliction?
> How are you without joy?
> How do you dwell alone,
> Not destroyed by displeasure?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "Affliction gives rise to joy;
> Joy also gives rise to affliction.
> Without affliction and without joy,
> This, deity, you should uphold and protect."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Excellent, to be without affliction!
> Excellent, to be without joy!
> Excellent, to dwell alone,
> Not destroyed by displeasure!
> Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (586)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse:

> "As though a sharp sword threatens,
> And as though one's head is on fire,
> One should sever the fire of craving and desire,
> With right mindfulness, seeking to be far removed."

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "As though a sharp sword threatens,
> And as though one's head is on fire,
> One should sever attachment to future bodies,
> With right mindfulness, seeking to be far removed."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (587)
