Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse:

> "Surrounded by hosts of celestial maidens,
> Like a company of piśācī,
> Within this thicket of delusion,
> How can one find a way out?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "The straight and level path of equanimity,
> The direction free from fear,
> Riding the chariot of tranquil silence,
> With Dharma-intention as its dense covering,
> Shame and conscience as its long reins,
> Right mindfulness as its halter and bridle,
> Wisdom as the skillful charioteer,
> Right view as the guide leading the way;
> Such a wondrous vehicle
> Is ridden by both men and women,
> Emerging from the thicket of birth and death,
> Reaching the place of peace and happiness."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (588)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse, questioning the Buddha:

> "Having four turnings and nine gates,
> Filled with craving and desire it dwells.
> Deeply mired in dark mud,
> How can the great elephant escape?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "Cut the long reins of delight in craving,
> And the evils of desire and the like.
> Uproot the very root of craving and desire,
> Heading directly toward that place."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (589)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse, questioning the Buddha:

> "In the land of Rāṣṭrapāla,
> There are many merchant traders,
> Of great wealth and abundant riches,
> Each competing to seek more wealth.
> Their contrivances for profit
> Are like a blazing fire.
> With such competitive hearts,
> Craving and desire constantly racing,
> How should one sever craving
> And put to rest worldly striving?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "Leaving the household to go forth as homeless,
> Abandoning wife, children, and wealth,
> With desire, aversion, and delusion freed from craving,
> The arahant exhausts all outflows.
> With right knowledge, the mind is liberated;
> When craving ceases, striving is put to rest."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (590)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In the past, there were merchants of the land of Kosala, with five hundred carts, traveling together on a trading expedition. They entered a wilderness. In the wilderness, five hundred bandits followed behind them, watching for an opportunity to rob them. At that time, in the wilderness there was a deity dwelling by the roadside.

"Then that deity had this thought: 'I should go to those Kosalan merchants and ask them about the meaning of the Dharma. If the merchants are pleased by my question and explain the meaning at the right time, I will use skillful means to keep them safe and free them from the danger of bandits. If they are not pleased by my question, I will abandon them, as other deities would.'

"Then that deity, having thought this, released radiance from his body, which illuminated the entire camp of the merchants, and spoke a verse:

> "'Who, among the awakened, is asleep?
> Who, among the sleeping, is awake?
> Who understands the meaning of this?
> Who can explain it to me?'

"At that time, among the merchants there was an upāsaka who had faith in the Buddha, faith in the Dharma, and faith in the Bhikṣu Sangha; who was single-minded toward the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha; who had taken refuge in the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha; who was free from doubt regarding the Buddha, free from doubt regarding the Dharma and Sangha, and free from doubt regarding suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path. Having seen the Four Noble Truths, he had attained the first fruit of uninterrupted realization. He was among the merchants, traveling together with them as a companion. That upāsaka, during the last watch of the night, sat upright in meditation, with mindfulness established before him, contemplating the twelve links of dependent origination in forward and reverse order. That is to say: because this exists, that exists; because this arises, that arises. Namely, conditioned by ignorance, there are formations; conditioned by formations, there is consciousness; conditioned by consciousness, there is name-and-form; conditioned by name-and-form, there are the six sense bases; conditioned by the six sense bases, there is contact; conditioned by contact, there is feeling; conditioned by feeling, there is craving; conditioned by craving, there is clinging; conditioned by clinging, there is becoming; conditioned by becoming, there is birth; conditioned by birth, there are aging, death, grief, sorrow, distress, and suffering. Thus does the entire mass of suffering arise. Likewise, when ignorance ceases, formations cease; when formations cease, consciousness ceases; when consciousness ceases, name-and-form ceases; when name-and-form ceases, the six sense bases cease; when the six sense bases cease, contact ceases; when contact ceases, feeling ceases; when feeling ceases, craving ceases; when craving ceases, clinging ceases; when clinging ceases, becoming ceases; when becoming ceases, birth ceases; when birth ceases, aging, death, grief, sorrow, distress, and suffering cease. Thus does the entire mass of suffering cease.

"Then that upāsaka, having contemplated thus, spoke a verse:

> "'I, among the awakened, am asleep;
> I, among the sleeping, am awake.
> I understand the meaning of this
> And can explain it to others.'

"Then that deity asked the upāsaka: 'How are you awake among the sleeping? How are you asleep among the awakened? How can you understand this? How can you explain it?'

"Then the upāsaka replied in verse:

> "'Craving, aversion,
> And delusion, freed from desire,
> The arahant whose outflows are exhausted,
> With right knowledge and mind liberated;
> They are the truly awakened,
> And I, compared to them, am asleep.
> Those who do not know suffering born of causes,
> Nor the origin and accumulation of suffering's conditions,
> Nor the complete, remainderless cessation
> Of all this suffering,
> Nor the true path
> Leading equally to the place where suffering ceases;
> Such as these are ever asleep,
> And compared to them, I am awake.
> Thus am I awake among the sleeping;
> Thus am I asleep among the awakened.
> Thus do I well understand the meaning;
> Thus can I explain it.'

"Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "'Excellent, to be awake among the sleeping!
> Excellent, to be awakened amid slumber!
> Excellent, to understand the meaning!
> Excellent, to be able to explain it!
> After a long time, at last I see
> A fellow disciple who has come.
> Because of the power of your merit,
> All these merchant travelers
> Shall be freed from the danger of bandits
> And travel safely along the road.'

"Thus, bhikṣus, those Kosalan merchants in the wilderness all safely emerged from the wilderness."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (591)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In the past, an upāsaka on a coastal island went to the home of another upāsaka where there was a gathering. There he vigorously denounced sensual desires, saying: 'These desires are false and unreal, deceptive dharmas, like illusions, deceiving children.' But upon returning to his own home, he indulged freely in the five sensual desires. At that upāsaka's home there was a deity dwelling. At that time, the deity had this thought: 'This upāsaka is improper and unbecoming. At the assembly in the other upāsaka's home, he vigorously denounced desires, saying: "Such desires are false and unreal, deceptive dharmas, like deceiving children." Yet upon returning to his own home, he freely indulges in the five sensual desires. I should now rouse him to awareness.' Then the deity spoke a verse:

> "'In the great assembly,
> You denounce desires as impermanent,
> Yet you yourself sink in craving and desire,
> Like a cow mired deep in mud.
> I observe in that assembly
> The various upāsakas:
> Those of much learning who clearly understand the Dharma,
> Who uphold the pure precepts.
> You see those who delight in the Dharma
> And declare that desires are impermanent,
> Yet how can you yourself indulge in desires,
> Not severing craving and attachment?
> Why do you delight in the world,
> Keeping wife, children, and retinue?'

"At that time, that deity thus and thus awakened that upāsaka. Thus and thus that upāsaka, having been awakened, shaved off his beard and hair, donned the kāṣāya robe, and with right faith left the household life to go forth and train in the Way. Diligently cultivating and practicing, he exhausted all outflows and attained arahantship."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (592)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Cool Grove, among the charnel grounds.

At that time, the householder Anāthapiṇḍada had some minor business that brought him to Rājagṛha, and he lodged at a certain householder's home. During the night, he saw the householder instruct his wife, children, servants, and workers, saying: "All of you, arise! Chop firewood, light fires, cook rice, prepare cakes, season the various dishes, and adorn the hall."

The householder Anāthapiṇḍada, seeing this, had this thought: "What is this householder preparing for? Is he marrying off a daughter or taking a bride? Is he inviting guests, the king, or great ministers?" Having thought this, he immediately asked the householder: "What are you preparing for? Are you marrying off a daughter or taking a bride? Are you inviting guests, the king, or great ministers?"

Then that householder answered the householder Anāthapiṇḍada, saying: "I am not marrying off a daughter or taking a bride, nor am I inviting the king or great ministers. I only wish to invite the Buddha and the Bhikṣu Sangha and offer them provisions."

At that time, the householder Anāthapiṇḍada, having heard the name "Buddha," which he had never heard before, was filled with great joy. All the pores of his body thrilled with delight. He asked the householder: "What is meant by 'Buddha'?"

The householder answered: "There is a śramaṇa named Gautama, a son of the Śākya clan. Among the Śākyas, he shaved off his beard and hair, donned the kāṣāya robe, and with right faith left the household life to go forth and train in the Way. He attained anuttarā samyaksaṃbodhi. He is called 'the Buddha.'"

The householder Anāthapiṇḍada said: "What is meant by 'Sangha'?"

That householder said: "Whether those of the brāhmaṇa class who shave off their beard and hair, don the kāṣāya robe, and with faith leave the household to follow the Buddha in going forth; or those of the kṣatriya class, the vaiśya class, or the śūdra class, good sons who shave off their beard and hair, don the kāṣāya robe, and with right faith leave the household, following the Buddha in going forth; this is called 'the Sangha.' Today I am inviting the Buddha and the present Sangha to offer them various provisions."

The householder Anāthapiṇḍada asked that householder: "May I now go to see the World-Honored One?"

That householder answered: "You should stay here for now. I have invited the World-Honored One to come to my home, and you can see him here."

At that time, the householder Anāthapiṇḍada, that very night, wholeheartedly contemplated the Buddha, and thereby fell asleep. Before dawn, he suddenly perceived a bright light and thought it was already daybreak. Wishing to leave his lodging, he walked toward the city gate. Arriving at the base of the city gate, it was only the second watch of the night, and the city gate had not yet been opened. By the king's standing rule, they waited for distant messengers coming and going; at the end of the first watch, the city gate would be closed, and at the end of the middle watch, it would be opened again, so that travelers could come and go early.

At that time, the householder Anāthapiṇḍada saw the city gate open and thought: "Surely the night has passed, it is dawn, and the gate is open." Relying on the bright light, he exited through the city gate. Once he had passed through, the bright light immediately vanished, and darkness returned. The householder Anāthapiṇḍada was immediately frightened, and his hair stood on end. He thought: "Could it be that some person, non-human, or treacherous person is trying to frighten me?" He then wished to turn back.

At that time, by the city gate there was a deity dwelling. That deity released radiance from his body, illuminating the way from the city gate all the way to the Cool Grove and the charnel grounds. He told the householder Anāthapiṇḍada: "Go forward! You will obtain supreme benefit. Do not turn back!"

Then that deity spoke a verse:

> "A hundred fine horses,
> A hundred pounds of gold,
> Mule-drawn carriages and horse-drawn carriages,
> A hundred of each,
> Laden with all manner of rarities
> And precious treasures,
> The meritorious reward obtained
> From planting good roots in past lives;
> If a person, with a heart of reverence,
> Takes a single step toward the Buddha,
> Even one-sixteenth of that merit
> Surpasses the former reward.

"Therefore, householder, you should go forward. Do not turn back!" Then he spoke another verse:

> "A great dragon-elephant from the Snowy Mountains,
> Adorned with pure gold,
> Enormous in body with great tusks;
> To give such an elephant to another
> Does not equal the merit of heading toward the Buddha,
> Not even one-sixteenth.

"Therefore, householder, you should hasten forward and obtain the great benefit, not turn back!" He spoke yet another verse:

> "Maidens of the land of Kamboja,
> Numbering a hundred,
> Adorned with all manner of wondrous jewels
> And necklaces and ornaments;
> To take all these and give them away
> Does not equal the merit of walking toward the Buddha,
> A single step's merit,
> Not even one-sixteenth.

"Therefore, householder, you should hasten forward and obtain the supreme benefit, not turn back!"

Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍada asked the deity: "Worthy one, who are you?"

The deity answered: "I am Māṭhurasiṃha-dāmānava. Previously I was a good friend of the householder. Through giving rise to faith and reverence toward the Venerable Śāriputra and Mahāmaudgalyāyana, by merit of that, I have now been reborn as a deity and am in charge of this city gate. Therefore I tell the householder: 'You should only go forward. Do not turn back. Going forward brings benefit, not turning back.'"

At that time, the householder Anāthapiṇḍada thought: "The arising of a Buddha in the world is no small matter. To hear the true Dharma is also no small matter. Therefore this deity urges me to go forward to see the World-Honored One." Then the householder Anāthapiṇḍada followed the light and proceeded directly to the Cool Grove and the charnel grounds.

At that time, the World-Honored One had come out of his room and was walking meditation in the open. The householder Anāthapiṇḍada saw the Buddha from afar and immediately went before him. Using the customary lay etiquette, he respectfully paid his respects and inquired: "How is it, World-Honored One? Did you sleep peacefully?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "The brāhmaṇa who has attained nirvāṇa
> Is always at peace and happiness.
> He is not stained by craving and desire;
> His liberation is complete, without remainder.
> Having severed all hopes and longings,
> He has subdued the burning of the heart.
> The mind has attained tranquil cessation;
> In cessation, he sleeps in peace."

At that time, the World-Honored One led the householder Anāthapiṇḍada into his room, where they sat down. With body upright and mindfulness established, the World-Honored One then taught him the Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening him. The World-Honored One said: "All dharmas are impermanent. One should practice the meritorious deed of giving, the meritorious deed of keeping precepts, and the meritorious deed of heavenly rebirth." He spoke of the gratification of desires, the danger in desires, the escape from desires, and the merit of renunciation.

The householder Anāthapiṇḍada, having heard the Dharma, seen the Dharma, attained the Dharma, entered the Dharma, and understood the Dharma, crossed beyond all doubts. Not relying on the faith of another, not being guided by another, he entered the true Dharma and Vinaya, and his mind attained fearlessness. He then rose from his seat, straightened his robes, paid homage to the Buddha, placed his right knee on the ground, joined his palms, and said to the Buddha: "I have crossed over, World-Honored One! I have crossed over, Sugata! From this day forward, for the rest of my life, I take refuge in the Buddha, I take refuge in the Dharma, I take refuge in the Bhikṣu Sangha, as an upāsaka. Please bear witness for me."

At that time, the World-Honored One asked the householder Anāthapiṇḍada: "What is your name?"

The householder said to the Buddha: "My name is Sudatta. Because I always provide for the orphaned, the poor, the suffering, and the distressed, people call me Anāthapiṇḍada."

The World-Honored One asked again: "Where do you live?"

The householder said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I dwell among the people of Kosala, in a city called Śrāvastī. I beseech the World-Honored One to come to Śrāvastī, and for the rest of my life I will offer robes, food and drink, lodging, bedding, and medicines for the sick."

The Buddha asked the householder: "Is there a monastery in Śrāvastī?"

The householder said to the Buddha: "There is none, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the householder: "You may establish a monastery there, so that bhikṣus may come and go and lodge there."

The householder said to the Buddha: "If only the World-Honored One would come to Śrāvastī, I will build a monastery and monks' quarters, so that bhikṣus may come and go and stay there." At that time, the World-Honored One silently accepted the invitation.

When the householder knew that the Buddha, the World-Honored One, had silently accepted his invitation, he rose from his seat, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and departed.

---

## (593)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the householder Anāthapiṇḍada, having fallen ill and died, was reborn in Tuṣita Heaven as a Tuṣita deity. He had this thought: "I should not remain here for long without seeing the World-Honored One." Having thought this, as quickly as a strong man might bend and extend his arm, he vanished from Tuṣita Heaven and appeared before the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit to one side. The deity Anāthapiṇḍada released radiance from his body, which illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then the deity Anāthapiṇḍada spoke a verse:

> "In this Jeta Grove,
> Where the Sangha of sages dwells,
> Where kings also abide,
> My joyful heart increases.
> Deep faith, pure precepts, and wholesome deeds,
> Wisdom as the supreme life;
> By these are beings purified,
> Not by clan, lineage, or wealth.
> The greatly wise Śāriputra,
> Of right mindfulness and constant tranquility,
> Dwelling in seclusion and cultivating renunciation;
> He is the foremost companion for those beginning their practice."

Having spoken this verse, he instantly vanished.

At that time, the World-Honored One, when that night had passed, entered the assembly of monks, spread his sitting cloth, sat before the assembly, and told the bhikṣus: "During this night, a certain deity of supremely wondrous appearance came to my abode, bowed his head at my feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and spoke a verse:

> "In this Jeta Grove,
> Where the Sangha of sages dwells,
> Where kings also abide,
> My joyful heart increases.
> Deep faith, pure precepts, and wholesome deeds,
> Wisdom as the supreme life;
> By these are beings purified,
> Not by clan, lineage, or wealth.
> The greatly wise Śāriputra,
> Of right mindfulness and constant tranquility,
> Dwelling in seclusion and cultivating renunciation;
> He is the foremost companion for those beginning their practice."

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, as I understand what the World-Honored One has said, it was the householder Anāthapiṇḍada who was reborn in that heaven and came to see the World-Honored One, for the householder Anāthapiṇḍada held the Venerable Śāriputra in the highest reverence and respect."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "So it is, so it is. Ānanda, the householder Anāthapiṇḍada was reborn in that heaven and came to see me."

At that time, the World-Honored One, on account of the Venerable Śāriputra, spoke a verse:

> "All the wisdom in the world,
> Except only that of the Tathāgata,
> Compared to Śāriputra's wisdom,
> Does not equal one-sixteenth.
> And Śāriputra's wisdom,
> With all devas and humans combined as equals,
> Compared to the Tathāgata's wisdom,
> Does not equal one-sixteenth."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (594)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Wilderness Monastery.

At that time, the householder of the Wilderness, having fallen ill and died, was reborn in the Avṛha Heaven. Having been reborn there, he immediately had this thought: "I should not remain here for long without seeing the World-Honored One." Having thought this, as quickly as a strong man might bend and extend his arm, he vanished from the Avṛha Heaven and appeared before the Buddha.

At that time, that deity's celestial body was so delicate that it collapsed upon the ground and could not stand by itself, just as butter or oil poured on the ground cannot hold its shape. So too, that deity's celestial body was so fine and soft that he could not support himself to stand.

At that time, the World-Honored One told that deity: "You should transform and create a coarser body and stand upon the ground."

Then that deity immediately transformed his form, creating a coarser body, and stood upon the ground. Then the deity went before the Buddha, bowed at his feet, and withdrew to sit to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the deity Hasta: "Hasta, deity, when you were formerly in the human realm in a human body, the sūtras and Dharma that you received and studied, do you still remember them now, or have you entirely forgotten?"

The deity Hasta said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what I formerly received and upheld I have not forgotten at all. Furthermore, when I was in the human realm, there was Dharma I heard but did not fully grasp; now I also remember it, just as the World-Honored One has well taught. The World-Honored One said: 'If a person is in a place of ease and happiness, he can retain the Dharma; this is not so in a place of suffering.' This statement is true. Just as the World-Honored One in Jambudvīpa, surrounded by the four assemblies of various kinds, teaches them the Dharma, and those four assemblies, having heard the Buddha's teaching, all practice accordingly; I likewise, in the Avṛha Heaven, teach the Dharma for the assembly of devas, and all those devas receive and practice it."

The Buddha told the deity Hasta: "When you were in the human realm, with regard to how many dharmas were you unsatiated, such that upon the dissolution of your body at death, you were reborn in the Avṛha Heaven?"

The deity Hasta said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I was unsatiated with regard to three dharmas, and upon the dissolution of my body at death, I was reborn in the Avṛha Heaven. What three? I was unsatiated in seeing the Buddha, and upon the dissolution of my body at death, I was reborn in the Avṛha Heaven. I was unsatiated with the Buddha's Dharma, and I was reborn in the Avṛha Heaven. I was unsatiated in making offerings to the Sangha, and upon the dissolution of my body at death, I was reborn in the Avṛha Heaven." Then the deity Hasta spoke a verse:

> "Seeing the Buddha, never satiated;
> Hearing the Dharma, also never satiated;
> Making offerings to the Sangha,
> I never knew satisfaction.
> Receiving and upholding the Dharma of the Noble Ones,
> Taming the stain of miserliness and attachment,
> Unsatiated with these three dharmas,
> Therefore I was born in the Avṛha Heaven."

At that time, the deity Hasta, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, and instantly vanished.

---

## (595)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a deity from the Avṛha Heaven, of supremely wondrous appearance, came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse:

> "Born in that Avṛha Heaven,
> Seven bhikṣus have been liberated.
> Their craving and aversion fully exhausted,
> They have transcended the world and crossed beyond attachment.
> Who has crossed the floods,
> The difficult-to-cross army of Māra, the Lord of Death?
> Who has severed the bonds of Māra?
> Who has forever transcended the yoke of affliction?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "The Venerable Upaka,
> And Palagaṇḍa,
> Pukkusāti, Bhaddhiya, Kuṇḍadhāna,
> Also Bāhudantī,
> And Piṅgiya;
> All such as these have crossed the floods,
> Severed the bonds of Māra,
> Crossed what is difficult to cross.
> They have severed the bonds of Māra,
> Transcended the yoke of the devas.
> They spoke the profound and wondrous Dharma,
> Awakening those who are difficult to know.
> You who skillfully question on deep meaning,
> Who are you now?"

Then that deity spoke a verse to the Buddha:

> "I am an anāgāmin,
> Born in that Avṛha Heaven.
> Therefore I can know these:
> The seven bhikṣus who are liberated,
> Their craving and aversion fully exhausted,
> Having forever transcended worldly attachment."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke another verse:

> "Eye, ear, nose, tongue, body,
> And the sixth, the mind sense base;
> When name-and-form there
> Are completely, without remainder, extinguished;
> Those who know these dharmas
> Are the seven liberated bhikṣus.
> Their craving for existence is fully exhausted;
> They have forever transcended worldly attachment."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "In the village of Vebhaliṅga,
> I once dwelt there.
> My name was Nandabāla,
> A maker of pottery.
> As a disciple of Kāśyapa Buddha,
> I upheld the upāsaka precepts,
> Supported my parents,
> And, free from desire, cultivated the brahma-conduct.
> Life after life he was my friend,
> And I was also his companion.
> Such great men as these,
> In past lives were in harmony together.
> Well cultivated in body and mind,
> They bear this, their final body."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke another verse:

> "So it is, worthy one,
> Just as you have said.
> In the village of Vebhaliṅga,
> The one named Nandabāla,
> A disciple of Kāśyapa Buddha,
> Who upheld the upāsaka precepts,
> Supported his parents,
> And, free from desire, cultivated the brahma-conduct;
> Formerly he was your companion,
> And you also were his good friend.
> Such upright men as these,
> In past lives were in harmony together.
> Well cultivated in body and mind,
> They bear this, their final body."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, that deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, and instantly vanished.

---

## (596)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse, questioning the Buddha:

> "This world is full of terrors;
> Beings are constantly troubled and afflicted.
> What has already arisen is also suffering;
> What has not yet arisen will also become suffering.
> Is there a place free from fear?
> May the One of Wisdom-Eye please explain."

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "There is no escape other than austerity,
> No other than subduing the sense faculties,
> No other than relinquishing everything,
> By which one may see liberation."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (597)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse, questioning the Buddha:

> "How do beings
> Receive bodies of wondrous form?
> How do they cultivate skillful means
> To attain the vehicle of the path to escape?
> In what dharma do beings abide?
> What do they cultivate and practice?
> What kinds of beings
> Are honored by the devas?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "Keeping precepts, possessing wisdom,
> Personally cultivating right concentration,
> With an upright mind and mindfulness established,
> Blazingly diligent, all sorrows are extinguished.
> Attaining the wisdom of equanimity,
> Their minds are well liberated.
> Through such causes and conditions,
> They receive bodies of wondrous form,
> Achieve the vehicle of the path to escape,
> And the mind abides therein in training.
> Those so endowed with virtue
> Are honored by the devas."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (598)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse:

> "Sinking and drowning in sleep,
> Yawning and without delight,
> Overeating with a confused mind,
> Laziness and lack of diligence;
> These ten cover beings,
> And the Noble Path does not appear."

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "The mind sinking in sleep,
> Yawning and without delight,
> Overeating with a confused mind,
> Laziness and lack of diligence;
> One who diligently cultivates and practices
> Can open and reveal the Noble Path."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (599)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse:

> "Outer tangles are not true tangles;
> Inner tangles entangle beings.
> Now I ask Gautama:
> Who, with regard to tangles, is free from tangles?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "The wise one establishes the precepts,
> And inwardly cultivates wisdom.
> A bhikṣu who diligently practices
> Can, regarding tangles, untangle the tangles."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (600)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse:

> "Difficult to cross and difficult to endure,
> The śramaṇa, through ignorance,
> Raises many hardships and difficulties.
> Heavy, dull, one sinks and drowns.
> The mind follows thoughts at will;
> Again and again one sinks and drowns.
> How should the śramaṇa practice
> To well guard and protect his mind?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "Like the turtle's skillful method,
> Hiding its six limbs within its shell,
> So the bhikṣu, practicing meditation,
> Well gathers in all thoughts and intentions.
> His mind depends on nothing;
> Others cannot frighten him.
> This is called being hidden and secure;
> No one can slander such a one."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (601)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse, questioning the Buddha:

> "The Sallā, the small streams:
> Where do they turn back and flow in reverse?
> On the path of birth and death,
> Where does the turning cease?
> The various pleasures and pains of the world,
> By what means are they extinguished without remainder?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "Eye, ear, nose, tongue, body,
> And the mind sense base;
> When name-and-form is extinguished without remainder,
> The Sallā, the small streams, turn back.
> The path of birth and death no longer turns;
> Pleasures and pains are extinguished without remainder."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (602)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse, questioning the Buddha:

> "O deer-footed one of the Iṣi,
> Most honored among the sages,
> Eating little and not craving tastes,
> Delighting in meditation in the mountain forests;
> I now reverently bow
> And ask Gautama:
> How does one escape from suffering?
> How does one attain liberation from suffering?
> I now ask about liberation:
> Where is it extinguished?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "The five qualities of sensual desire in the world,
> And the mind-dharma said to be the sixth;
> Being without desire toward those desires,
> One is liberated from all suffering.
> Thus does one escape from suffering;
> Thus is one liberated from suffering.
> As for the liberation you ask about,
> It is there that it is extinguished."

Then that deity spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond;
> Forever he has transcended worldly attachments."

At that time, the deity, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

## (603)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain deity, of supremely wondrous appearance, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's abode, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance from his body illuminated the entirety of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that deity spoke a verse, questioning the Buddha:

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

---

It appears that the text content for Fascicle 22 (卷第二十二) was not included in your message. The body of the fascicle between the two separator lines is empty, and only the header for Fascicle 23 appears at the very end.

Could you please provide the full Chinese text of the fascicle you would like translated? I am ready to translate every passage in full once the source text is supplied.

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Twenty-Three

---

### (604)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One, in the early morning, put on his robe and took up his bowl, and together with the assembly of bhikṣus entered the city to beg for food, as the verse says:

> "His body's hue like a golden mountain,
> Dignified and supremely subtle,
> Walking with the gait of a king of geese,
> His face like the pure full moon,
> The World-Honored One went together with the assembly."

At that time, the World-Honored One's foot stepped upon the threshold of the city gate, and the earth quaked in six ways, as the verse says:

> "The great ocean and the great earth,
> City walls and all the mountains,
> Where the Muni's foot trod,
> Shook and swayed like a boat upon the waves."

The Buddha manifested such spiritual power. At that time, the people raised their voices and proclaimed: "Extraordinary and unprecedented dharma! Such manifestation of spiritual power, like that of the Buddha, the World-Honored One, entering the city and displaying such various unprecedented dharmas." As the verse says:

> "The low ground became level,
> High ground turned into low,
> By the Buddha's awesome spiritual power,
> Thorns, tiles, and rubble
> All were no longer seen.
> The deaf, blind, mute, and dumb
> Instantly could see, hear, and speak.
> The musical instruments of the city
> Produced wondrous sounds without being struck."

At that time, the World-Honored One's radiant appearance shone universally, like the blaze of a thousand suns, as the verse says:

> "The World-Honored One's bodily radiance
> Illuminated throughout the city.
> The people bathed in the Buddha's light
> Felt cool as if anointed with sandalwood."

At that time, the World-Honored One walked along through the town. At that time, there were two boys, one of a higher caste and one of a secondary caste, playing together in the sand. One was named Jaya, and the other was named Vijaya. They saw the World-Honored One coming from afar, his body adorned with the thirty-two marks of a great man. At that time, the boy Jaya thought in his mind: "I shall offer barley meal." So with his hands he scooped up fine sand and placed it in the World-Honored One's bowl.

At that time, Vijaya joined his palms and rejoiced accordingly, as the verse says:

> "Seeing the greatly compassionate World-Honored One,
> With a fathom of light surrounding his body,
> With a bold countenance beholding the World-Honored One,
> His heart gave rise to great reverence and faith.
> Scooping sand, he offered it as a gift,
> And thereby transcended the bounds of birth and old age."

At that time, the boy made a vow, saying: "By the merit and good roots of this charitable giving, may I become a king ruling one realm under a single canopy, and in this very life may I be able to make offerings to the Buddhas." As the verse says:

> "The Muni knew his heart
> And the wish of his intent.
> Receiving the fruit increases good roots,
> And by the power of the field of merit,
> With great compassion in his heart,
> He received the offering of sand."

At that time, because of these good roots, Jaya would become a king ruling over Jambudvīpa, and would even attain supreme perfect enlightenment. Therefore, the World-Honored One gave a subtle smile.

At that time, Ānanda saw the World-Honored One give a subtle smile. He immediately joined his palms, facing the Buddha, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One! Buddhas, World-Honored Ones, Arhats, Samyaksaṃbuddhas do not give a subtle smile without cause or condition. For what cause and condition does the Buddha, the World-Honored One, now give a subtle smile?" As the verse says:

> "The World-Honored One is free from jest,
> The unsurpassed honored one in the world.
> With teeth white as shell and jade,
> The Supreme One now gives a smile.
> Heroic, diligent, and energetic,
> Without a teacher, self-awakened,
> With wondrous words pleasing to hear,
> An unsurpassed gentle voice,
> He gives a prediction for that boy.
> With Brahmā-like voice, distant and clear,
> The unsurpassed honored one among bipeds
> Predicts the fruit of that sand offering."

At that time, the World-Honored One told Ānanda: "So it is, so it is, as you have said. Buddhas do not give a smile without cause or condition. The reason I now smile has its cause and condition. Ānanda, you should know: one hundred years after my passing into nirvāṇa, this boy, in the city of Pāṭaliputra, will rule over one quarter of the world as a wheel-turning king, of the Maurya clan, named Aśoka. He will govern with the right Dharma, and will furthermore widely distribute my relics, and shall build eighty-four thousand Dharma-king stūpas, bringing peace and happiness to immeasurable sentient beings.

"As the verse says:

> "After my passing into nirvāṇa,
> This person shall become a king,
> Of the Maurya clan, named Aśoka,
> Like King Mūrdhagata,
> In this Jambudvīpa,
> He alone shall rule, honored by the world.

"Ānanda! Take the sand that was offered in this bowl and spread it on the Tathāgata's walking path, and then walk upon that place."

Ānanda received the instruction and took the sand from the bowl and spread it on the walking path.

"Ānanda, you should know: in the city of Pāṭaliputra, there will be a king named Candragupta. That king will have a son named Bindusāra, who will rule that country. He in turn will have a son named Susīma.

"At that time, in the country of Campā, there will be a brahmin girl, extremely beautiful, pleasing to behold, treasured by the nation. When the physiognomists see the girl's features, they will predict of her: 'She shall become a queen, and will give birth to two sons: one will rule one realm, and one will go forth to study the Way and will accomplish the marks of a sage.'

"At that time, when that brahmin heard what the physiognomists said, he was immeasurably joyful. He took his daughter to the city of Pāṭaliputra, adorned her with various ornaments, and wished to marry her to Prince Susīma. The physiognomists said: 'She should be married to King Bindusāra. That girl will give birth to a son of great merit, and the son will succeed to the royal lineage.' The brahmin then gave his daughter in marriage to this king. When the king saw the girl, beautiful and virtuous, he made her his consort. The former queen and the attendant women, seeing this new consort arrive, had this thought: 'This woman is extremely beautiful, treasured by the nation. If the king enjoys pleasures with her, he will abandon us and will not even look at us.'

"The women then had her learn the trade of a barber. After she had learned it all, she groomed the king's beard and hair. While she was grooming him, the king was greatly pleased and asked the woman: 'What do you desire?' The woman addressed the king: 'I only wish that the king's heart would love and cherish me!' She said this three times.

"At that time, the king said: 'I am a kṣatriya king who has received the royal consecration. You are a barber. How could I love and cherish you?' The woman addressed the king: 'I am not of a low caste by birth. I am in fact the daughter of a high-clan brahmin. The physiognomists told my father: "This daughter should be married to the king." That is why I have come here!' The king said: 'If that is so, who made you learn such a lowly trade?' The woman addressed the king: 'It was the former queen and the attendant women who made me learn this.'

"The king then commanded: 'From now on, do not practice this lowly trade again.' The king then established her as the first queen. The king constantly enjoyed pleasures with her, and she became pregnant. When her months were fulfilled, she gave birth to a son. The birth was peaceful, and the mother was without worry or distress. After seven days, the child was given the name Aśoka (Without Sorrow). She also gave birth to another son, named Vitaśoka (Free from Sorrow). As for Aśoka, his body was rough and coarse, and the father king did not greatly enjoy holding him; he was not dear to the king's heart. Furthermore, the king wished to test his two sons. He summoned Piṅgalavatsajīva, a brahmin, and said to the brahmin: 'Venerable teacher, observe my sons. After my passing, who shall become king?' The brahmin said: 'Take all the princes out to the Golden Palace park outside the city. There I shall observe their characteristics.' And so they went out to that park.

"At that time, Aśoka's mother said to Aśoka: 'I hear the king has gone to the Golden Palace park to observe the princes and determine who shall be king after his passing. Why do you not go?' Aśoka addressed her: 'The king does not think fondly of me, and he also does not enjoy seeing me.' His mother said again: 'Just go there.' Aśoka again addressed her: 'Since mother commands me to go, I shall go. I wish that mother would send me food and drink.' The mother said: 'Very well. You should go out of the city.'

"At that time, as he was going out the gate, he met a great minister named Anuruddha. This minister asked Aśoka: 'Where is the prince going?' Aśoka replied: 'I have heard that the great king has gone out to the Golden Palace park to observe the princes and determine who shall be king after his passing. I am going there now.'

"The king had previously instructed the great minister: 'If Aśoka comes, have him ride an old, dull elephant, and have elderly people as his attendants.' At that time, Aśoka rode this old elephant and went to the park, and among all the princes, he sat on the ground.

"At that time, the various princes each had food and drink brought to them. Aśoka's mother placed curd-rice in an earthen vessel and sent it to Aśoka. Thus all the princes ate their food and drink. At that time, the father king asked the teacher: 'Among these, who has the marks of a king and will succeed to my throne?' At that time, the physiognomist examined the princes and saw that Aśoka possessed all the marks of a king and would succeed to the throne. But he also thought: 'This Aśoka is not favored by the king. If I say he shall become king, the king will surely be worried and displeased.' So he said: 'I shall now give a general prediction.' The king replied: 'As the teacher instructs.' The teacher said: 'Among these, whoever has the best conveyance, that person shall become king.' At that time, the princes, hearing what was said, each thought: 'I have a fine conveyance.' At that time, Aśoka said: 'I ride an old, venerable elephant. I shall become king.'

"At that time, the king again told the teacher: 'Please observe further and give a prediction.' The teacher replied: 'Among these, whoever has the foremost seat, that person shall become king.' The princes each said to one another: 'I sit on the foremost seat.' Aśoka said: 'I now sit on the ground. This is my supreme seat. I shall become king.'

"Again the king told the teacher: 'Please observe once more.' The teacher again replied: 'Among these, whoever has the finest vessel and finest food, that person shall be king.' And so Aśoka thought: 'I have the supreme conveyance, the supreme seat, and the supreme food.' At that time, the king finished observing the princes' characteristics and returned to the palace.

"At that time, Aśoka's mother asked Aśoka: 'Who shall become king? Whom did the brahmin predict?' Aśoka addressed her: 'The supreme conveyance, the supreme seat, the supreme vessel, and the supreme food shall become the prince who is king. I myself see that I shall become king: an old elephant as my conveyance, the earth as my seat, a plain vessel holding food, and non-glutinous rice mixed with curd for my meal.'

"At that time, the brahmin, knowing Aśoka would become king, frequently paid respects to his mother. His mother also generously gave gifts to the brahmin and then asked: 'After the great king passes away, who shall become king?' The teacher replied: 'This cannot be spoken of.' She asked like this up to three times. The teacher said: 'I shall tell you, but you must not let anyone know. The son you bore, named Aśoka, is that very person.' The queen said: 'Hearing these words, I am overjoyed and ecstatic. If the king were to hear this, he would not have faith and respect for the teacher. Teacher, you should now return to your home. If my son becomes king, the teacher shall receive all good fortune and be supported for life.' 

"At that time, when King Bindusāra's border country of Takṣaśīlā revolted, the king told Aśoka: 'You shall lead the four divisions of the army and go pacify that country.' When the prince departed, he was given no weapons or armor at all.

"At that time, his followers addressed the prince: 'Now we go to subjugate that country, but we have no military equipment. How can we succeed?' Aśoka said: 'If I am destined to become king by the fruit of my good roots, then weapons and armor will come of their own accord.' As he uttered these words, the earth opened with a sound, and weapons and armor emerged from the ground. He then led the four divisions of the army and went to subjugate that country.

"At that time, the people of those countries, hearing that Aśoka was coming, leveled and repaired the roads, adorned the cities, held auspicious water-pitchers, and with various offerings welcomed the prince, saying: 'We do not rebel against the great king or Prince Aśoka. Rather, the ministers have treated us unjustly, and therefore we have gone against the royal authority.' They then made various offerings to the prince and invited him to enter the city. After pacifying this country, he was further sent to subjugate the country of Khasā.

"At that time, two great strongmen cleared and leveled the roads for the king, pushing aside mountain boulders. Furthermore, the devas proclaimed a decree to this country: 'Aśoka shall be king of this realm. You must not harbor thoughts of opposition.' The king of that country then submitted. In this manner, he pacified the entire realm, all the way to the ocean's edge.

"At that time, Prince Susīma had gone out for an excursion and happened to encounter a great minister. The minister did not observe proper etiquette, and the prince had his servants strike and beat the minister's body. The minister thought: 'This prince has not yet obtained the throne, and his temperament is already like this. If he becomes king, it will be unbearable. Furthermore, I have heard that Aśoka has won the realm. He could destroy five hundred great ministers. We should together establish Aśoka as king, to rule this realm.'

"Furthermore, when the country of Takṣaśīlā revolted again, the ministers deliberated together: 'Let us send Prince Susīma.' The king also approved, and so the prince went to that country but was unable to subdue it.

"At that time, the father king fell gravely ill. The king told his ministers: 'I now wish to establish Susīma as king and send Aśoka to that country.' At that time, the ministers, wishing to make Aśoka king, smeared Aśoka's body, face, hands, and feet with a yellow substance. The ministers then addressed the king: 'Prince Aśoka is now gravely ill.' The ministers then adorned Aśoka and brought him before the king, saying: 'For now, let us establish this prince as king. We shall later gradually establish Susīma as king.' At that time, when the king heard these words, he was greatly displeased, worried and unhappy, and remained silent without reply.

"At that time, Aśoka thought in his mind and said aloud: 'If I am truly destined to obtain the royal throne, may the devas come of their own accord and anoint my head with water and bind my head with a white silk ribbon.' With these words, the devas immediately anointed Aśoka's head with water and bound his head with a white silk ribbon. When the king saw these signs, he was greatly distressed, and immediately passed away.

"After King Aśoka performed the proper funeral rites for his father king, he established Anuruddha as his great minister.

"At that time, Prince Susīma heard that his father had passed away and that Aśoka had now been established as king. His heart could not bear it, and he assembled his troops and came to attack Aśoka. King Aśoka placed two strongmen at two of the four gates, stationed his great minister at the third gate, and himself guarded the eastern gate.

"At that time, the great minister Anuruddha constructed a mechanical wooden elephant and also made an image of King Aśoka. The image was placed riding the elephant, positioned outside the eastern gate. He also created a pit of smokeless fire and covered it over with material. When Susīma arrived, the great minister Anuruddha said to Prince Susīma: 'If you wish to become king, Aśoka is at the eastern gate. You may go attack him. If you can defeat this king, you will naturally become king.' At that time, the prince headed straight for the eastern gate and fell into the fire pit, and died immediately.

"At that time, there was a great strongman named Bhadrayudha. Hearing that Susīma had perished, he became weary of the world. Leading a countless number of his retinue, he went forth in the Buddha's Dharma to study the Way. With diligent effort, he attained the exhaustion of the defilements and accomplished the path of an Arhat.

"King Aśoka governed with the right Dharma. At that time, the ministers, thinking that they had together established Aśoka as king, treated the king with contempt and did not observe the proper etiquette between ruler and minister. The king also knew himself that the ministers looked down upon him.

"At that time, the king told the ministers: 'You should cut down the flowering and fruit trees and plant thorny brambles in their place.' The ministers replied: 'We have never seen or heard of anyone removing flowering and fruit trees to plant thorny trees. We have only seen thorny trees being removed and fruit trees being planted.' Even when the king gave the command three times, they still would not comply.

"At that time, the king, furious at the great ministers, took a sharp sword and killed five hundred great ministers. On another occasion, the king, with his attendant women and retinue, went to an outer park for an excursion. He saw an Aśoka tree with flowers in full bloom. Seeing it, the king thought: 'This flowering tree shares my name.' His heart was filled with joy. The king's physical form was ugly, his skin rough and coarse. The attendant women did not love the king in their hearts. Because they hated the king, they tore and broke the Aśoka tree's flowers with their hands. When the king awoke from his nap, he saw the Aśoka tree's flowers scattered on the ground. His heart filled with anger; he bound the attendant women and burned them to death with fire. Because the king performed such cruel deeds, he was called 'Aśoka the Cruel.'

"At that time, the great minister Anuruddha addressed the king: 'The king should not act in this way. How can you kill people, ministers, and attendant women with your own hand? The king should now appoint an executioner. When there are those who deserve to be killed, hand them over to that person.' The king then issued an edict to appoint an executioner. There was a mountain named Giri. Within it lived a weaver's family. The weaver had a son, also named Giri (Girika). He was fierce and cruel, striking, beating, and binding small boys and girls, and catching creatures of water and land. He even defied his own parents. Therefore the people called him 'the cruel son of Giri.'

"At that time, the king's messengers told him: 'Can you execute criminals for the king?' He replied: 'I could purge all those with crimes in the entire Jambudvīpa, how much more just this one region?'

"At that time, the messengers returned and reported to the king: 'We have found the cruel man.' The king said: 'Go find him and bring him here!' The messengers summoned him, and he replied: 'Wait a moment. Let me first bid farewell to my parents.' He described the above matters in full. His parents said: 'Son, you should not engage in such deeds.' They admonished him three times, but he gave rise to a merciless heart and killed his parents, and only then did he go.

"The messengers asked: 'Why did you take so long and not come quickly?' At that time, the cruel one described the above matters in full. The messengers reported this matter in full to the king. The king then commanded him: 'All my criminals whose offenses warrant death, you shall deal with them.' He addressed the king: 'Build me a building.' The king then built him a house with rooms, extremely splendid in appearance, with only one entrance, and the entrance was also extremely well-adorned. Inside, he arranged instruments of punishment arranged in rows, resembling a hell. That prison was extremely fine in appearance. At that time, the cruel man addressed the king: 'I now request a boon from the king: whoever enters this place shall never come out again.' The king replied: 'As you request and beg, so shall it be granted to you.'

"At that time, the executioner went to the monastery and listened to the bhikṣus speak about matters of hell. At that time, a bhikṣu was expounding the sūtra on the hells: 'Beings born in the hells: the hell-wardens seize those sinners, open their mouths with hot iron tongs, and place hot iron balls in their mouths. Then they pour molten copper into their mouths. Then iron axes hack and cut their bodies. Then cangues, fetters, chains, and shackles bind their bodies. Then there are fire carts and furnace coals. Then iron cauldrons. Then the ash river. Then knife mountains and sword trees.' All this was as described in full in the Sūtra of the Five Heavenly Messengers. The executioner, having heard the bhikṣu describe all these matters, opened up his facility and created instruments of punishment exactly as described. According to this method, he punished the criminals.

"On another occasion, a merchant chief took his wife and went to the great sea. While at sea, the wife gave birth to a son, who was named Samudra (Sea). They remained at sea for over ten years, gathering many precious treasures, and returned to their homeland. On the way, they encountered five hundred bandits, who killed the merchant chief and seized his treasures.

"At that time, the merchant chief's son, seeing his father wounded and dead and the treasures lost, became weary of worldly suffering and went forth in the Tathāgata's Dharma to study the Way. He returned to his native land, traveling through various countries, and came in turn to the city of Pāṭaliputra. After spending the night there, in the early morning he put on his robe, took his bowl, and entered the city begging for food in sequence. By mistake, he entered the executioner's building.

"At that time, the bhikṣu saw from afar what was inside the building: fire carts, furnace coals, and other instruments tormenting various beings, just as in the hells. He was immediately seized with fear, his hair standing on end, and he tried to go out through the gate. At that time, the cruel one came and seized the bhikṣu, saying: 'Anyone who enters this place cannot leave. You shall die here.' When the bhikṣu heard what he said, grief and anguish arose in his heart, and tears filled his eyes. The cruel master asked: 'Why do you cry like a small child?'

"At that time, the bhikṣu answered with a verse:

> "'I do not fear death,
> My aspiration seeks liberation.
> What I sought has not borne fruit,
> Therefore I weep.
> A human body is extremely hard to obtain,
> Going forth is likewise so.
> Having met the Lion of the Śākya clan,
> From now on I shall not see him again.'

"At that time, the cruel master told the bhikṣu: 'You are certain to die today. What need is there for worry and distress?' The bhikṣu again pleaded with sorrowful words: 'Grant me a little more time to live, perhaps up to one month.' The cruel one would not allow it. As the number of days gradually decreased to seven days, the cruel one finally agreed.

"At that time, this bhikṣu, knowing death was near, practiced with heroic diligence, sitting in meditation and stilling his mind, but in the end he could not attain the Way. When the seventh day arrived, a woman from within the king's palace committed an offense punishable by death and was sent to the cruel executioner to be punished. The executioner placed this woman in a mortar and pounded her with a pestle until she was ground to pieces.

"At that time, the bhikṣu, seeing this, developed extreme revulsion toward this body: 'Alas! How painful! Before long, I too shall be like this.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'Alas! Great compassionate Teacher,
> Who proclaimed the true and wondrous Dharma:
> This body is like a mass of foam,
> In meaning it has no substance.
> That beautiful woman's form just now,
> Where has it gone?
> Birth and death are utterly to be abandoned,
> Yet the foolish cling to them.
> Binding the mind, focusing on that object,
> Now I shall cast off chains and fetters,
> And cross the ocean of the three realms of existence,
> Ultimately never to be born again.
> With such diligent effort,
> Concentrating on cultivating the Buddha's Dharma,
> Cutting off all fetters,
> I have attained the state of Arhat.'

"At that time, the cruel executioner told this bhikṣu: 'Your time limit has expired.' The bhikṣu asked: 'I do not understand what you are saying.' The cruel one replied: 'The seven days agreed upon before are now complete.' The bhikṣu answered with a verse:

> "'My mind has attained liberation,
> The great darkness of ignorance,
> All obstructive coverings of existence are severed,
> The thieves of afflictions are slain.
> The sun of wisdom has now arisen,
> Illuminating mind, thought, and consciousness,
> Clearly seeing birth and death.
> Now is the time for compassion toward others,
> Practicing in accord with the holy Dharma.
> This body of mine,
> Do with it as you will.
> I have no further attachment or regret.'

"At that time, the cruel master seized the bhikṣu and placed him in a cauldron of iron filled with oil, and stoked the fire with firewood. But the fire would not ignite. Even if it did ignite, it would not heat up. The cruel master, seeing the fire would not burn, beat and struck his servants and lit the fire himself. The fire then blazed fiercely. After a long time, he opened the lid of the iron cauldron and saw the bhikṣu sitting upon a lotus flower inside the iron cauldron. A feeling of wonder arose in him, and he immediately reported to the king. The king prepared his royal chariot and, with a countless retinue, came to see the bhikṣu.

"At that time, when the moment for taming the bhikṣu had arrived, the bhikṣu rose into the sky, like a king of geese, and displayed various transformations, as the verse says.

> "The king saw this bhikṣu,
> His body ascending into the sky.
> His heart filled with great joy,
> He joined his palms and gazed upon the sage:

> "'I now have something to say,
> Something I cannot understand in my mind.
> Your physical form differs not from an ordinary person,
> Yet your spiritual powers are unprecedented.
> Please explain and tell me,
> What Dharma have you cultivated,
> That has made you so pure?
> Expound it widely for me,
> So that I may obtain the supreme and wondrous Dharma.
> Once I have understood the nature of dharmas,
> I shall become your disciple,
> Ultimately without any regret.'

"At that time, the bhikṣu had this thought: 'If I now tame this king, much can be accomplished. I can uphold the Buddha's Dharma, widely distribute the Tathāgata's relics, bring peace and happiness to immeasurable sentient beings, and throughout this Jambudvīpa, cause all to have faith in the Three Jewels.' For this reason, he displayed his virtues. He then spoke a verse to the king:

> "'I am a disciple of the Buddha,
> Who has attained the exhaustion of all defilements.
> I am furthermore a son of the Buddha,
> Not attached to any existence.
> I have now been tamed,
> By the unsurpassed Honored One among bipeds.
> With a stilled mind I have attained tranquility.
> From the great terror of birth and death,
> I have now completely escaped.
> Having separated from the bonds of the three realms of existence,
> In the Tathāgata's holy Dharma,
> I have obtained such benefits.'

"At that time, King Aśoka, hearing what the bhikṣu said, gave rise to great reverence and faith in the Buddha. He also addressed the bhikṣu: 'Before the Buddha passed into nirvāṇa, what predictions did he make?' The bhikṣu replied: 'The Buddha predicted concerning the great king: "One hundred years after my passing into nirvāṇa, in the city of Pāṭaliputra, there will be three hundred million households. In that country, there will be a king named Aśoka, who shall rule this Jambudvīpa as a wheel-turning king, governing with the right Dharma. He will furthermore widely distribute my relics, and in Jambudvīpa shall build eighty-four thousand stūpas." The Buddha thus predicted concerning the great king. However, the great king has now created this great hell and killed immeasurable people. The king should now have compassion for all sentient beings, grant them fearlessness, and give them peace and security. What the Buddha predicted for the great king, the king should practice in accordance with the Dharma.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'Practice with a heart of compassion,
> Do not torment the various beings.
> Cultivate and practice the Buddha's Dharma,
> And widely distribute the Buddha's relics.'

"At that time, King Aśoka gave rise to extreme reverence and faith in the Buddha. He joined his palms, made obeisance to the bhikṣu, and said: 'I have committed a great offense. I now confess before the bhikṣu. What I have done is deeply wrong. As a disciple of the Buddha, please accept my confession. With a forgiving heart, do not reproach me further. I, a foolish person, now take refuge once more.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'I now take refuge in the Buddha,
> The unsurpassed, supreme, and wondrous Dharma,
> And the honored assembly of bhikṣus.
> I now take refuge with my entire life.
> I shall now be heroic and diligent,
> Receiving and upholding the World-Honored One's command.
> Throughout this Jambudvīpa,
> I shall universally establish stūpas for the Buddhas,
> With various kinds of offerings,
> Hanging silk banners and pennants,
> Adorning the World-Honored One's stūpas,
> Wondrously beautiful, rare in the world.'

"At that time, after the bhikṣu had converted King Aśoka, he rose into the sky and disappeared.

"At that time, as the king was leaving the hell-prison, the cruel master addressed the king: 'The king may not leave.' The king said: 'Do you wish to kill me?' He replied: 'Yes.' The king said: 'Who entered this place first?' He answered: 'I did.' The king said: 'If so, you should die first.' The king then commanded his men to take this cruel master and place him inside a building coated with pitch, and burn it with fire. He further commanded that this hell-prison be demolished, and he granted fearlessness to all beings.

"At that time, the king wished to construct reliquary stūpas. Leading the four divisions of the army, he went to the city of Rājagṛha, took the relics from King Ajātaśatru's stūpa of the Buddha, and restored and repaired the stūpa so that it was no different from before. In this manner, he took relics from seven Buddha-stūpas, and came to the village of Rāmagrāma. At that time, the Nāga kings took the king into the Nāga palace. The king requested relics from the Nāgas for worship, and the Nāgas gave them to him. The king then departed from there, as the verse says:

> "'In the village of Rāmagrāma,
> Whatever Buddha-stūpas there were,
> The Nāga kings served them,
> Guarding and making offerings.
> The king requested a share from the Nāgas,
> The Nāgas gave open-heartedly.
> He then took these relics
> And proceeded gradually to other regions.'

"At that time, the king made eighty-four thousand caskets of gold, silver, lapis lazuli, and crystal, to contain the Buddha's relics. He also made eighty-four thousand vases of the four treasures to hold these caskets. He also made immeasurable hundreds of thousands of banners, pennants, and parasols. He commanded various spirits and deities, each to carry relics and offering implements, and instructed the spirits and deities, saying: 'Throughout Jambudvīpa, all the way to the ocean's edge, in every city or village with one hundred million households, establish a reliquary stūpa for the World-Honored One.'

"At that time, there was a country named Takṣaśīlā with thirty-six hundred million households. The people of that country said to the spirits and deities: 'Give us thirty-six caskets of relics, and we shall erect Buddha-stūpas.' The king devised a plan: for countries with fewer people, he would distribute shares to them, letting them meet the household quota, and then erect stūpas.

"At that time, in the city of Pāṭaliputra, there was an elder named Yaśas. The king went to visit him and addressed the elder: 'I wish, in a single day, to erect eighty-four thousand Buddha-stūpas throughout this Jambudvīpa. This is my wish.' As the verse of praise says:

> "'The great king named Aśoka,
> From the previous eight stūpas,
> Took relics from each,
> And throughout this Jambudvīpa,
> Established Buddha-stūpas,
> Eighty-four thousand,
> Of extensive and wondrous excellence,
> All to be completed in a single day.'

"At that time, the elder addressed the king: 'Excellent, great king! On the fifteenth day from now, at the time of the lunar eclipse, cause all of Jambudvīpa to raise the Buddha-stūpas.' And so, in a single day, eighty-four thousand stūpas were erected. The people of the world rejoiced immeasurably and together gave him the title 'Dharma King Aśoka.' As the verse of praise says:

> "'The king of the holy Maurya lineage,
> Who brings peace and happiness to the people of the world,
> Throughout this Jambudvīpa,
> Established supremely wondrous stūpas.
> Originally called the Cruel King,
> He now accomplished supremely wondrous deeds.
> Together they titled him the Dharma King,
> A name transmitted to posterity.'

"After the king had built the eighty-four thousand stūpas, he was overjoyed and ecstatic. Leading his ministers, he went to the Kukkuṭārāma (Cock-Pheasant Monastery) and addressed the Elder Yaśas: 'Are there yet other bhikṣus whom the Buddha predicted would carry out the Buddha's work? I wish to go to visit them and make offerings and pay respects.'

"The elder replied: 'When the Buddha was on the verge of entering parinirvāṇa, he subdued the Nāga King Āpalāla, the potter, and the Nāga Caṇḍa-Gopālaka.' He then went to the country of Mathurā and told Ānanda: 'After my parinirvāṇa, a hundred generations hence, there will be a householder named Gupta, whose son will be named Upagupta. He will go forth to study the Way. As a teacher of the Buddha's teaching without distinguishing marks, he will be foremost among all. He shall carry out the Buddha's work.' The Buddha told Ānanda: 'Do you see that mountain in the distance?' Ānanda addressed the Buddha: 'I see it, World-Honored One!' The Buddha told Ānanda: 'That mountain is called Urumuṇḍa. It is a forest hermitage called Naṭabhaṭikā, conducive to tranquility.' And the verse of praise says:

> "'The bhikṣu Upagupta,
> Foremost among teachers,
> Whose fame resounds in the four directions,
> Predicted by the Supreme One.
> After my passing into nirvāṇa,
> He shall carry out the Buddha's work,
> Liberating sentient beings,
> Whose number is without limit.'

"At that time, the king asked the elder: 'Has the Venerable Upagupta already appeared in the world?' The elder replied: 'He has already appeared in the world. He has gone forth to study the Way, conquered the afflictions, and is an Arhat. Together with an immeasurable retinue of eighteen thousand bhikṣus, he dwells on Mount Urumuṇḍa in the forest hermitage, having compassion for sentient beings. As the Buddha taught, he expounds the pure and wondrous Dharma, liberating immeasurable devas and humans, causing them to enter the city of ambrosia.' When the king heard this, he was overjoyed and ecstatic. He immediately ordered his ministers to swiftly prepare the royal chariot, and with a countless retinue, went to visit that place to pay respects and make offerings to Upagupta.

"At that time, a minister addressed the king: 'Since the sage is within the king's domain, it would be proper to send a message inviting him, and he will come of his own accord.' The king answered the minister: 'It is not fitting to send a message to his place. I should go in person. It is not fitting for him to come.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'Have you obtained a diamond tongue,
> That it cannot be broken?
> You advise me not to go there,
> To be near a country-dwelling person.'

"The king then dispatched a message to the venerable one's dwelling, saying: 'On such-and-such a day, I shall come to your honored place.' At that time, the venerable one reflected: 'If the king comes, with his immeasurable retinue, they will suffer great hardships, trampling and killing insects, and troubling the villagers.' Having thought thus, he replied to the messenger: 'I shall go to the king's place myself.' When the king heard the venerable one would come himself, he was overjoyed and ecstatic. From Mathurā to Pāṭaliputra, along the route, he opened and arranged boat crossings, and on the boats hung various banners and parasols.

"At that time, the Venerable Upagupta, out of compassion for the king, led his assembly of eighteen thousand Arhats, following the waterway directly to the king's capital. At that time, the people of the country reported to the king: 'The Venerable Upagupta has come, leading eighteen thousand bhikṣus.'

"When the king heard this, he was greatly overjoyed and ecstatic. He immediately removed a necklace worth ten million and bestowed it. The king, together with his great ministers and retinue, went out at once to the venerable one's place. He arranged food offerings, prostrated with all five limbs touching the ground, made obeisance before him, knelt with palms joined, and said: 'I now rule this Jambudvīpa, having received the royal throne, and I do not take joy in this. But seeing the venerable one today, my joy is boundless. That a disciple of the Tathāgata can be like this is like seeing the Buddha himself.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'You have attained tranquil cessation and crossed beyond the world.
> You now carry out the Buddha's work.
> The darkness of ignorance in the world is extinguished,
> Like the sun shining in the Buddha's world.
> As a guide for the world,
