> Foremost in teaching the Dharma,
> A refuge for sentient beings.
> My joy today is great.'

"At that time, the king ordered his messengers to proclaim throughout the realm: 'The Venerable Upagupta Bhikṣu has now come to this country.' Thus they proclaimed:

> "'Those who desire wealth and honor,
> Freedom from the suffering of poverty,
> Constant enjoyment of heavenly bliss,
> And liberation in nirvāṇa,
> Should go to Upagupta,
> Pay respects and make offerings now.
> Those who have never seen the Buddhas
> May now behold Upagupta.'

"At that time, the king adorned the entire realm, leveled and repaired the roads, hung silk banners and parasols, burned incense, scattered flowers, and provided various music. All the people of the country came out to welcome the Venerable Upagupta, making offerings and paying respects.

"At that time, the Venerable Upagupta addressed the king: 'Great king! You should govern with the right Dharma, have compassion for sentient beings. The Three Jewels are difficult to encounter. Among the Three Jewels, you should constantly make offerings, pay respects, practice mindfulness, offer praise, and widely teach people. Why is this? The Tathāgata, Worthy of Offerings, Perfectly Enlightened One, who knows and sees people, always gave this prediction: "My right Dharma is entrusted to kings and to my Bhikṣu Sangha."' And he spoke this verse:

> "'The World Hero, Honored One among humans,
> The right, supreme, and wondrous great Dharma,
> Is entrusted to the great king
> And to my Bhikṣu Sangha.'

"At that time, the king addressed Upagupta: 'I have already established the right Dharma.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'I have already built the stūpas,
> Adorned the various realms,
> With various kinds of offerings,
> Banners, pennants, and various treasures.
> I have widely distributed the Buddha's relics
> Throughout Jambudvīpa.
> I have generated such merit,
> And my wishes are all fulfilled.
> My own body, wife, and children,
> Treasures, and this land,
> I have now entirely given in offering,
> Making offerings to the stūpas of the holy ones.'

"At that time, the Venerable Upagupta praised the king: 'Excellent! Excellent! The great king should practice such Dharma.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'Giving up body, wealth, and life,
> Life after life, there shall be no worry.
> Receiving blessings without end,
> One will surely attain unsurpassed awakening.'

"At that time, the king invited the Venerable Upagupta to enter the city. He arranged various seats and invited the venerable one to take his seat. The Sangha was directed to go to the Kukkuṭārāma (Cock-Pheasant Monastery). He addressed the venerable one: 'The venerable one's countenance is handsome and his body soft, while my form is ugly and my skin rough and coarse.'

"The venerable one spoke this verse:

> "'When I practiced giving,
> With a pure mind and fine goods,
> It does not compare with the king's giving,
> Offering sand to the Buddha.'

"At that time, the king replied with a verse:

> "'When I was a boy,
> I offered sand,
> And now I have obtained such fruit.
> How much more so for other wondrous gifts!'

"The venerable one again spoke a verse of praise:

> "'How excellent, good great king!
> Who offered sand,
> In the unsurpassed field of merit,
> The fruit planted is inexhaustible.'

"At that time, King Aśoka told his great ministers: 'I offered sand to the Buddha, and the fruit I received is like this. How could I not pay reverence to the World-Honored One?' The king further addressed Upagupta: 'Venerable one! Show me the places where the Buddha taught the Dharma and where he traveled, so that I may go to make offerings and prostrate, and for the sake of future sentient beings, to plant good roots.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'Show me where the Buddha taught the Dharma,
> The various countries and dwelling places.
> I shall make offerings and pay respects,
> For the sake of future sentient beings.'

"The venerable one said: 'Excellent! Excellent! The great king has made a wondrous aspiration. I shall show the king these places, for the sake of future sentient beings.'

"At that time, the king led the four divisions of the army, carrying various offerings of incense, flowers, banners, and pennants, as well as various music, and departed together with the venerable one. The venerable one said: 'Lumbinī Grove. This is where the Tathāgata was born.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'At the place where the Tathāgata was first born,
> At birth he walked seven steps,
> Surveyed the four directions,
> Raised his hand and pointed to the sky:
> "This is my final birth.
> I shall attain the unsurpassed Way.
> Among devas and humans,
> I am the unsurpassed honored one."'

"At that time, the king prostrated with all five limbs touching the ground, made offerings and obeisance, and immediately erected a Buddha-stūpa. The venerable one addressed the king: 'Does the great king wish to see the place where the devas saw the Buddha take seven steps at birth?' The king said: 'I wish to see it and would be delighted.'

"At that time, the venerable one raised his hand, pointed to the tree branch that Queen Māyā had grasped, and called to the tree spirit: 'Tree spirit! Appear now and let the king see, to generate great joy.' Immediately upon hearing the call, the spirit appeared, standing beside the venerable one, and said: 'What instruction shall I carry out?' The venerable one told the king: 'This spirit witnessed the Buddha's birth.'

"The king addressed the spirit with a verse:

> "'You saw the splendidly adorned body,
> At birth, like a blue lotus flower,
> Walking seven steps on foot,
> Speaking words from his mouth.'

"The spirit answered with a verse:

> "'I saw the body endowed with marks of beauty,
> At birth, the Honored One among bipeds,
> Lifting his feet, walking seven steps,
> Speaking words from his mouth:
> "Among all devas and humans,
> I am the unsurpassed honored one."'

"At that time, the king asked the spirit: 'What auspicious signs were there when the Buddha was born?' The spirit answered: 'I cannot fully describe the wondrous and supreme events. I shall now briefly describe a small portion:

> "'His radiance could illuminate all,
> His body was complete with marks of beauty,
> Causing people to joyfully behold him,
> Shaking heaven and earth.'

"At that time, the king, hearing what the spirit said, was overjoyed. He bestowed a hundred thousand measures of precious treasures and departed. He then brought the king into the city and said: 'In this place, the Bodhisattva manifested the thirty-two marks and eighty minor characteristics, adorning his body of purple-gold color.' At that time, the king made obeisance at this place and made various offerings.

"He then brought the king to the temple of the devas and told the king: 'When the prince was born, he was brought to bow before that deity. At that time, all the deities bowed to the Bodhisattva. At that time, the people gave the Bodhisattva his name: "Deva above devas" (Devātideva).' At that time, the king again made various offerings.

"He then showed a place and told the king: 'At this place, the father king showed the Bodhisattva to the brahmins, who examined his marks and virtues.' The king again made various offerings.

"He also showed: 'This is the place of the Bodhisattva's school. This is where he learned to ride elephants. This is where he learned to ride horses, ride chariots, and use bows and crossbows. These are the places where he learned all skills and arts. This is the place where the Bodhisattva groomed himself. This is the place where the Bodhisattva enjoyed pleasures with sixty thousand consorts. This is the place where the Bodhisattva saw the old, the sick, and the dead. This is the place where the Bodhisattva sat beneath the Jambu tree, practiced meditation and attained freedom from desire, and the tree's shadow did not leave his body, and the father king bowed before him. This is the place where the Bodhisattva, accompanied by a hundred thousand devas, departed from the city. This is the place where the Bodhisattva removed his necklace and gave it to Chandaka, sending the horse back to the kingdom.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'At this place, the Bodhisattva
> Removed his necklace and crown,
> Gave them to Chandaka,
> And sent the horse back to the kingdom.
> Traveling alone without companions,
> He entered the mountain to study the Way.'

"'Also, at this place, the Bodhisattva obtained the kāṣāya robe from a hunter in exchange. Having donned this robe, he went forth from home. This is the place where the ṛṣis made their invitations. This is the place where King Bimbisāra offered the Bodhisattva half his kingdom. This is the place where he inquired of the sage Ārāḍa Kālāma. This is the place where the Bodhisattva practiced austerities for six years.' As the verse says:

> "'Practicing austerities for six years,
> Enduring extreme suffering,
> Knowing this was not the true path,
> He abandoned the practices he had followed.'

"'This is the place where two women offered the Bodhisattva milk-porridge.' As the verse says:

> "'The Great Sage in this place
> Received two women's milk-porridge.
> Rising from this place, he departed
> And proceeded to the Bodhi tree.'

"'This is the place where the Nāga Kāla praised the Bodhisattva.' As the verse says:

> "'At this place, the Nāga Kāla
> Praised the Bodhisattvas:
> "Follow the path of the ancients,
> And realize the unsurpassed and wondrous fruit."'

"At that time, the king addressed the venerable one and spoke this verse:

> "'I now wish to see the Nāga,
> That Nāga who saw the Buddha,
> From this place proceeding to Bodhi,
> And realizing the supreme and wondrous fruit.'

"At that time, the venerable one pointed with his hand to the Nāga palace and said: 'Nāga King Kāla! You who have seen the Buddha, now manifest your body.' At that time, upon hearing the call, the Nāga King immediately emerged, stood before the venerable one, and with palms joined, said: 'What instruction is there?' At that time, the venerable one told the king: 'This Nāga King saw the Buddha and praised the Tathāgata.'

"At that time, the king joined his palms toward the Nāga and spoke this verse:

> "'You saw the diamond body,
> My teacher without peer,
> His face like the pure full moon.
> Tell me of his virtues,
> The merits of the ten powers,
> When he proceeded to the seat of enlightenment.'

"At that time, the Nāga King answered with a verse:

> "'I shall now describe
> When his feet trod upon the earth:
> The great earth quaked in six ways,
> His radiance surpassed the sun,
> Illuminating the three-thousandfold world,
> As he proceeded to the Bodhi tree.'

"At that time, the king made various offerings at all such places and erected stūpas and shrines.

"At that time, the venerable one brought the king to beneath the Bodhi tree and told the king: 'Under this tree, the Bodhisattva Mahāsattva, with the power of the samādhi of loving-kindness and compassion, vanquished Māra's armies and attained anuttarā-samyak-saṃbodhi.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'The Muni, Bull among Honored Ones,
> Under this Bodhi tree,
> Vanquished the hosts of evil Māra
> And attained the supreme fruit of Bodhi,
> Uniquely honored among devas and humans,
> None able to equal him.'

"At that time, the king offered immeasurable precious treasures and various offerings, and erected a great stūpa and shrine.

"'This is the place where the four heavenly kings each held a bowl and offered it to the Buddha, merging them into one bowl. This is the place where the Tathāgata received food from the merchant brothers Trapuṣa and Bhallika. This is the place where, when the Tathāgata was going to the country of Vārāṇasī, the non-Buddhist ascetic Ājīvika questioned the Buddha. This is the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park (Mṛgadāva), where the Tathāgata turned the Dharma wheel for the five bhikṣus, with three turnings and twelve aspects.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'At this place, the Deer Park,
> The Tathāgata turned the Dharma wheel,
> Three turnings and twelve aspects,
> Five persons attained the path's fruits.'

"At that time, the king made various offerings at this place and erected stūpas and shrines.

"'This is the place where the Tathāgata converted the sages Uruvilvā Kāśyapa and others to the Way. This is the place where the Tathāgata taught the Dharma to King Bimbisāra, and the king attained the vision of truth, and immeasurable people and devas attained the Way. This is the place where the Tathāgata taught the Dharma to Indra, lord of the devas, and Indra together with eighty thousand devas attained the Way. This is the place where the Tathāgata displayed great spiritual powers, with various transformations. This is the place where the Tathāgata ascended to heaven, taught the Dharma for his mother, and leading a multitude of devas, descended back to the human realm.'

"The king again made various offerings and erected stūpas and shrines.

"At that time, the venerable one told King Aśoka, upon arriving at the country of Kuśinagara: 'This is the place where the Tathāgata, having fully completed the Buddha's work, entered parinirvāṇa in the nirvāṇa without remainder.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'Having liberated devas and humans,
> Asuras, Nāgas, and yakṣas,
> Having established the inexhaustible Dharma,
> The Buddha's work having been completed,
> Having attained quiescence within existence,
> The Greatly Compassionate One entered nirvāṇa,
> Like a fire extinguished when fuel is spent,
> Ultimately attaining the eternal abode.'

"At that time, when the king heard these words, he was grief-stricken and dazed, and fell to the ground. His ministers washed his chest and face with water. After a long while, he revived, weeping and shedding tears. In this manner, he made various offerings and erected a great stūpa and shrine.

"At that time, the king further addressed the venerable one: 'My heart's wish is to see the great disciples of the Buddha whom the Buddha predicted. I wish to make offerings to their relics. Please show them to me.' At that time, the venerable one addressed the king: 'Excellent! Excellent! The great king can give rise to such a wondrous aspiration.'

"At that time, the venerable one brought the king to the country of Śrāvastī, entered the Jetavana Monastery, and pointed to a stūpa: 'This is the stūpa of the Venerable Śāriputra. The king should make offerings.' The king asked: 'What are his merits?' The venerable one said: 'He was the second Dharma King, who turned the Dharma wheel in accord with the Buddha.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'The wisdom of all sentient beings,
> Compared to Śāriputra's,
> Is but one-sixteenth,
> Excluding the Tathāgata's wisdom.
> When the Tathāgata turned the Dharma wheel,
> He was able to turn it in accord.
> He possesses immeasurable virtues;
> Who could fully proclaim them?'

"At that time, the king was greatly overjoyed. He offered a hundred thousand measures of precious treasures in offering to the stūpa, and spoke this verse:

> "'I bow to Śāriputra,
> Who is liberated from all fears,
> Whose fame is universal in the world,
> Whose wisdom has no equal.'

"Next, the venerable one showed the stūpa of Mahāmaudgalyāyana: 'The king should make offerings to this stūpa.'

"The king asked: 'What are his merits?' The venerable one replied: 'He was foremost in supernatural powers. With a touch of his toe upon the ground, the earth would tremble, reaching even to the heavenly palaces. He subdued the Nāga Kings Nanda and Upananda.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'With a touch of his toe, the earth trembled,
> Reaching to the palace of Indra.
> In supernatural powers, none were his equal;
> Who could fully proclaim them?
> The two Nāga Kings were fierce and violent,
> And all who saw them were terrified.
> But by the power of his supernatural abilities,
> He subdued them and stilled their wrath.'

"At that time, the king offered a hundred thousand measures of precious treasures in offering to this stūpa, and spoke this verse of praise:

> "'Foremost in supernatural powers,
> Free from old age, sickness, and death,
> Possessing such merits,
> I now bow to Maudgalyāyana.'

"Next, the venerable one showed the stūpa of Mahākāśyapa and told the king: 'This is the stūpa of Mahākāśyapa. You should make offerings.' The king asked: 'What are his merits?' He answered: 'He was of few desires, content, and foremost in the ascetic practices (dhūta). The Tathāgata offered him half of his seat and his saṃghāṭī robe. He had compassion for sentient beings and established the right Dharma.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'Foremost as a field of merit,
> Compassionate toward the poor and destitute,
> Wearing the Buddha's saṃghāṭī robe,
> Able to establish the right Dharma,
> Possessing such virtues;
> Who could fully proclaim them?'

"At that time, the king offered a hundred thousand measures of precious treasures in offering to this stūpa, and spoke this verse of praise:

> "'Always delighting in tranquility,
> Dwelling among groves and thickets,
> Rich in few desires and contentment,
> I now bow to Mahākāśyapa.'

"Next, the venerable one showed the stūpa of the Venerable Bakkula: 'This is the stūpa of Bakkula. You should make offerings.' The king asked: 'What are his merits?' The venerable one replied: 'He was foremost in being free from illness. He never even spoke a single sentence of Dharma to anyone, remaining utterly silent.' The king said: 'I shall offer one coin in offering.' The ministers addressed the king: 'The merits are equal; why do you offer only one coin to this one?' The king told them: 'Listen to what I say:

> "'Although he removed the darkness of ignorance
> And his wisdom could discern clearly,
> Though he bore the name Bakkula,
> What benefit was he to the world?'

"At that time, that one coin returned to the king. When the great ministers saw this wondrous event, they unanimously praised him: 'Alas! Venerable one! Of few desires and content, not even needing a single coin.'

"Then the venerable one showed the stūpa of Ānanda and told the king: 'This is the stūpa of Ānanda. You should make offerings.' The king asked: 'What are his merits?' He answered: 'This person was the Buddha's attendant, foremost in learning, who compiled the Buddha's sūtras.' And he spoke this verse:

> "'Holding the Muni's bowl,
> With mindful and decisive attention,
> A great ocean of learning,
> With eloquence and a gentle voice,
> Able to delight the assemblies of devas and humans.
> Well knowing the minds of the three Buddhas,
> Understanding all completely,
> A treasure chest of merit.
> Praised by the Supreme One,
> Conquering the strife of afflictions;
> With such merits as these,
> One should cultivate offerings.'

"The king then offered ten billion measures of precious treasures in offering to the stūpa.

"At that time, the ministers addressed the king: 'Why is the offering to this one entirely superior to the previous ones?' The king said: 'Listen to the reason in my heart:

> "'The Tathāgata's physical body,
> The Dharma body, pure in nature,
> He was able to uphold it all.
> Therefore the offering is superior.
> The Dharma lamp always remains in the world,
> Extinguishing the darkness of ignorance.
> All of this came from him.
> Therefore the offering is superior.
> Like the water of the great ocean
> Cannot be contained in a hoof-print,
> So too the ocean of the Buddha's wisdom
> Cannot be held by other people.
> Only the Venerable Ānanda,
> Hearing once, could retain it all,
> Never forgetting at any time.
> Therefore the offering is superior.'

"At that time, the king, having made such various offerings, joined his palms before the venerable one and said:

> "'I have now received this body,
> And shall not fail this body.
> Cultivating immeasurable merits,
> I am now the lord among humans.
> I now take what is solid and real,
> Building stūpas and shrines,
> Adorning the world,
> As stars adorn the moon.
> Serving the Buddha's disciples' Dharma,
> Performing all proper rites,
> I have now accomplished all of this.
> I bow at the venerable one's feet.
> By the venerable one's gracious power,
> I have now seen supremely wondrous things.
> I have quickly obtained great good benefit,
> Henceforth distinguishing the Dharma.'

"At that time, the king's supreme and various offerings always especially favored the Bodhi tree at the seat of enlightenment. Under this tree, the Tathāgata attained anuttarā-samyak-saṃbodhi. With rare and precious treasures of the world as offerings, he made offerings to the Bodhi tree.

"At that time, the king's queen was named Tiṣyarakṣitā. The queen had this thought: 'The king deeply loves and cherishes me, and I also cherish the king. But now the king abandons me and goes, taking precious treasures to the Bodhi tree. I shall now devise a stratagem to kill this Bodhi tree. When the tree is withered and dead and its leaves have fallen, the king will no longer go there, and then he may enjoy pleasures with me constantly.' She then summoned a sorcerer and told the sorcerer: 'Can you kill the Bodhi tree?' He replied: 'I can. Give me a thousand pieces of gold.'

"At that time, the queen gave him a thousand pieces of gold. The sorcerer went to the Bodhi tree, used spells to curse the tree, and tied threads around

# 雜阿含經
