# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Twenty-Four

### Fifth Recitation, Path Chapter, Part One

### (605)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? They are: the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (606)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? They are: the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas. Thus, bhikṣus, you should train in cultivating and fulfilling these four foundations of mindfulness, with energetic effort, right mindfulness and right knowing."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (607)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There is a single path for the purification of beings, enabling them to overcome sorrow and grief, to extinguish vexation and suffering, and to attain the true Dharma. It is called the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (608)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu abandons the four foundations of mindfulness, then he abandons the true noble Dharma. One who abandons the true noble Dharma abandons the noble path. One who abandons the noble path abandons the deathless Dharma. One who abandons the deathless Dharma does not gain liberation from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, vexation, and suffering. I declare that such a person does not attain liberation from suffering.

"If a bhikṣu does not abandon the four foundations of mindfulness, then he does not abandon the noble true Dharma. One who does not abandon the noble true Dharma does not abandon the noble path. One who does not abandon the noble path does not abandon the deathless Dharma. One who does not abandon the deathless Dharma gains liberation from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, vexation, and suffering. I declare that such a person is liberated from all suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (609)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach the arising and passing away of the four foundations of mindfulness. Listen carefully and reflect well. What is the arising and passing away of the four foundations of mindfulness? When food arises, the body arises; when food ceases, the body passes away. Thus, one abides contemplating the arising of the body, one abides contemplating the passing away of the body, one abides contemplating the arising and passing away of the body. Then one abides without dependence, and in regard to all things in the world, one clings to nothing whatsoever.

"Likewise, when contact arises, feelings arise; when contact ceases, feelings pass away. Thus, one abides contemplating feelings according to the arising of dharmas, one abides contemplating feelings according to the ceasing of dharmas, one abides contemplating feelings according to the arising and ceasing of dharmas. Then one abides without dependence, and in regard to all things in the world, one clings to nothing whatsoever.

"When name-and-form arises, mind arises; when name-and-form ceases, mind passes away. One abides contemplating mind according to the arising of dharmas, one abides contemplating mind according to the ceasing of dharmas, one abides contemplating mind according to the arising and ceasing of dharmas. Then one abides without dependence, and in regard to all things in the world, one clings to nothing whatsoever.

"When attention arises, dharmas arise; when attention ceases, dharmas pass away. One abides contemplating dharmas according to the arising of dharmas, one abides contemplating dharmas according to the ceasing of dharmas, one abides contemplating dharmas according to the arising and ceasing of dharmas. Then one abides without dependence, and in regard to all things in the world, one clings to nothing whatsoever. This is called the arising and passing away of the four foundations of mindfulness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (610)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall teach the cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness. Listen carefully and reflect well. How does one cultivate the four foundations of mindfulness? One abides in the internal contemplation of the body in the body as a foundation of mindfulness, with energetic effort, right knowledge and right mindfulness, taming worldly sorrow and grief. One abides in the external body, and the internal-and-external body contemplation, with energetic effort, right mindfulness and right knowing, taming worldly sorrow and grief. Likewise for feelings, mind, and dharmas: one abides in the internal dharmas, external dharmas, and internal-and-external dharmas contemplation as a foundation of mindfulness, with energetic effort, right mindfulness and right knowing, taming worldly sorrow and grief. This is called a bhikṣu cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

Regarding cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness in the past and the future, it is also taught in the same way.

### (611)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There is the aggregate of wholesome dharmas and the aggregate of unwholesome dharmas. What is the aggregate of wholesome dharmas? It is called the four foundations of mindfulness; this is the correct statement. Why is this? Because the purely complete and pure aggregate is the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas. What is the unwholesome aggregate? The unwholesome aggregate is called the five hindrances; this is the correct statement. Why is this? Because the purely heedless and completely unwholesome aggregate is the five hindrances. What are the five? They are: the hindrance of sensual desire, the hindrance of ill-will, the hindrance of sloth-and-torpor, the hindrance of restlessness-and-remorse, and the hindrance of doubt."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (612)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose a person grasps four kinds of powerful bows and, with great force and effort, shoots through the shadow of a palmyra tree, passing through swiftly without obstruction. Likewise, if four such disciples of the Tathāgata, with supreme effort, sharp faculties and wisdom, were to live a full hundred years, and during those hundred years the Tathāgata were to continuously teach and instruct them in the Dharma, except only for the time of eating, relieving themselves, and sleeping, and they were constantly teaching and constantly listening during the intervals; with sharp and keen wisdom they would receive and retain to the very bottom all that the Tathāgata spoke, without any obstruction, and without needing to ask the Tathāgata a second time. Yet the Tathāgata's teaching of the Dharma would be without end. Even if they listened to the Dharma for their entire lifespan, dying at one hundred years, the Tathāgata's Dharma teaching still could not be exhausted. You should know that what the Tathāgata teaches is immeasurable and boundless; the names, phrases, and flavors are also immeasurable, without end. This refers to the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of the body, the foundations of mindfulness of feelings, mind, and dharmas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

For all sūtras on the four foundations of mindfulness, this summary phrase is used: "Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train in cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness, arousing supreme aspiration, with energetic effort, right mindfulness and right knowledge."

### (613)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There is the unwholesome aggregate and the wholesome aggregate. What is the unwholesome aggregate? It is the three unwholesome roots; this is the correct statement. Why is this? Because the purely unwholesome accumulation is the three unwholesome roots. What are the three? The unwholesome root of greed, the unwholesome root of hatred, and the unwholesome root of delusion. What is the wholesome aggregate? It is the four foundations of mindfulness. Why is this? Because the purely wholesome and fully complete is the four foundations of mindfulness; this is the correct statement. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of the body, the foundations of mindfulness of feelings, mind, and dharmas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

As with the three unwholesome roots, so too with the three kinds of misconduct: bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct; the three perceptions: the perception of sensual desire, the perception of ill-will, and the perception of harmfulness; the three kinds of thought: the thought of sensual desire, the thought of ill-will, and the thought of harmfulness; the three elements: the element of sensual desire, the element of ill-will, and the element of harmfulness.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (614)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit at one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, as you have spoken of a 'great person,' what constitutes a great person and what constitutes one who is not a great person?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Excellent! Excellent! Bhikṣu, you are able to ask the Tathāgata about the meaning of a great person. Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it for you. If a bhikṣu abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, and having abided in contemplating the body in the body, his mind does not become free from desire, does not attain liberation, and does not exhaust all the outflows, I do not declare that one to be a great person. Why is this? Because his mind is not liberated. If a bhikṣu abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas, and his mind does not become free from desire, does not attain liberation, and does not exhaust all the outflows, I do not declare that one to be a great person. Why is this? Because his mind is not liberated.

"If a bhikṣu abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, and his mind becomes free from desire, his mind attains liberation, and he exhausts all the outflows, I declare that one to be a great person. Why is this? Because his mind is liberated. If one abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas, and having abided in contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas, his mind is freed from craving, his mind attains liberation, and he exhausts all the outflows, I declare that one to be a great person. Why is this? Because his mind is liberated. This is what is called, bhikṣu, a great person and one who is not a great person."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully rejoiced, bowed at his feet, and departed.

### (615)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda, early in the morning, put on his robes and took his bowl to enter the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. On the road, he thought: "I shall first go to the bhikṣuṇī monastery." He then went to the bhikṣuṇī monastery.

The bhikṣuṇīs, seeing the Venerable Ānanda coming from afar, quickly spread out a seat and invited him to sit down.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇīs bowed at the Venerable Ānanda's feet, withdrew to sit at one side, and addressed the Venerable Ānanda: "We bhikṣuṇīs have cultivated the four foundations of mindfulness, keeping our minds anchored, and we ourselves know the advance and decline, the rising and falling."

The Venerable Ānanda told the bhikṣuṇīs: "Excellent! Excellent! Sisters, you should train just as you have described. Whoever cultivates the four foundations of mindfulness with the mind well-anchored should know the advance and decline, the rising and falling, in this way."

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda taught the bhikṣuṇīs the Dharma in various ways. Having taught the Dharma in various ways, he rose from his seat and departed.

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda, having begged for food in the city of Śrāvastī and returned, put away his robes and bowl, washed his feet, went to the World-Honored One, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit at one side, and fully reported to the World-Honored One what the bhikṣuṇīs had said.

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Excellent! Excellent! One should train in this way, cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness with the mind well-anchored, knowing the advance and decline, the rising and falling. Why is this? The mind first seeks externally, and then one controls it, making it seek its own mind. One knows as it really is whether the mind is scattered and not liberated. If a bhikṣu abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, and having abided in contemplating the body in the body, the body becomes drowsy and torpid, and the mind becomes sluggish and lax in regard to dharmas, that bhikṣu should arouse pure faith and take up a pure sign. Having aroused a mind of pure faith and recollected the pure sign, his mind then becomes joyful. When his mind is joyful, delight arises. When his mind is delighted, the body becomes tranquil. When the body is tranquil, one experiences bodily happiness. When one experiences bodily happiness, the mind becomes concentrated. When the mind is concentrated, the noble disciple should train thus: 'With regard to this meaning, I shall gather in and bring to rest the externally scattered mind, not giving rise to discursive thought or examination already contemplated. Without discursive thought or examination, I abide in equanimous mindfulness and happiness. Having abided in happiness, I know as it really is.' The same is said for the foundations of mindfulness of feelings, mind, and dharmas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (616)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should grasp the sign of your own mind; do not let it scatter outward. Why is this? If that bhikṣu is foolish, undiscerning, and unskillful, not grasping the sign of his own mind but grasping external signs, he will afterward decline and generate his own obstructions. It is like a cook who is foolish, undiscerning, and unskillful in blending the various flavors when serving his lord: sour, salty, bitter, and bland, none to his lord's taste. He cannot skillfully ascertain what his lord prefers, whether sour, salty, bitter, or bland, and the harmonious blend of various flavors. He cannot closely attend to his lord, observing what is needed, listening for what is desired, and skillfully discerning his wishes, but instead uses his own ideas to blend the various flavors to serve his lord. If they are not to his lord's taste, his lord is not pleased. Not being pleased, the cook does not receive rank or reward, nor is he cherished. A foolish bhikṣu is likewise: undiscerning and unskillful, abiding in contemplation of the body in the body, he cannot cut off the higher afflictions, cannot gather in his mind, and also does not attain inner mental tranquility. He does not attain the superior right mindfulness and right knowing. He also does not attain the four kinds of elevated mental states, the pleasant abiding in the present life, or the nirvāṇa of peace and security not previously attained. This is called a bhikṣu who is foolish, undiscerning, and unskillful, unable to skillfully grasp the sign of the inner mind, but grasping external signs, thereby generating his own obstructions.

"If there is a bhikṣu who is wise, talented, discerning, and skillful in expedient means, who first grasps the inner mind and afterward grasps external signs, that one will never decline or generate his own obstructions. It is like a cook who is wise, intelligent, discerning, and skillful in expedient means when serving his lord. He can blend the various flavors: sour, salty, bitter, and bland. He skillfully ascertains what his lord prefers and blends the various flavors to suit his lord's wishes. He listens for what flavors his lord desires and repeatedly offers them. When his lord is pleased, the cook will surely receive rank and reward, and will be cherished and valued even more. In this way, a wise cook skillfully ascertains his lord's preferences. A bhikṣu is likewise: abiding in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, he cuts off the higher afflictions, skillfully gathers in his mind, attains inner mental stillness, right mindfulness and right knowing, attains the four elevated mental states, the pleasant abiding in the present life, and attains the nirvāṇa of peace and security not previously attained. This is called a bhikṣu who is wise, talented, discerning, and skillful in expedient means, who grasps the sign of the inner mind, maintains external signs, and never declines or generates his own obstructions. It is the same for the contemplation of feelings, mind, and dharmas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (617)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In the past, there was a bird called a lark, which was seized by a hawk. The hawk flew up high into the sky. The lark cried out from the sky, saying: 'Without being aware of it, I suddenly encountered this calamity. Because I left my parents' territory and wandered in another place, I encountered this disaster. How is it that today I am overpowered by another, without freedom?' The hawk said to the lark: 'Where is your territory where you can be free?' The lark replied: 'In the plowed furrows of the fields, I have my own territory, enough to be free from all dangers. That is the territory of my parents.'

"The hawk, with arrogance toward the lark, said: 'I shall release you. Go back to the plowed furrows. Can you escape or not?' Thereupon, the lark, freed from the hawk's talons, returned to the plowed furrows and settled beneath a large clod of earth, in a safe resting place. Then, from atop the clod, it wanted to fight with the hawk.

"The hawk was greatly enraged: 'That small bird dares to fight with me!' Its anger was extremely intense, and it dove straight down in a fierce swoop. Thereupon, the lark went under the clod, and the hawk, with the force of its dive, struck its breast against the hard clod, shattering its body and dying instantly.

"At that time, the lark, crouching deep under the clod, looked up and spoke this verse:

> "The hawk came with great force,
> But the lark relied on its own domain.
> Riding on fierce and intense anger,
> It brought disaster upon itself, its body shattered.
> I am fully accomplished and adept,
> Relying upon my own territory.
> Having vanquished my enemy, my heart rejoices;
> I observe and delight in my own strength.
> Even if you had the fierce and foolish
> Strength of a hundred thousand dragon-elephants,
> It would not equal my wisdom,
> Not even one-sixteenth of it.
> Behold how my wisdom is supreme,
> Crushing and destroying the hawk."

"Thus, bhikṣus, just as that hawk, foolishly abandoning the territory of its own parents and wandering in another place, met with such disaster, so too should you bhikṣus, in your own territory and the places where you walk, properly guard and maintain yourselves, staying away from others' territory. You should train in this.

"Bhikṣus, 'others' territory and others' domain' refers to the realm of the five desires: when the eye sees forms that are agreeable, pleasant, and delightful, and the mind of desire becomes attached; when ear-consciousness perceives sounds, nose-consciousness perceives odors, tongue-consciousness perceives tastes, and body-consciousness perceives tangibles that are agreeable, pleasant, and delightful, and the mind of desire becomes attached. This is called others' territory and others' domain for a bhikṣu. Bhikṣus, 'one's own territory, the parents' domain' refers to the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train to wander in your own territory, the parents' domain, and to stay far from others' territory and others' domain."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (618)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Through much cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness, one attains four fruits, four kinds of merit and benefit. What are the four? They are: the fruit of stream-entry, the fruit of once-returning, the fruit of non-returning, and the fruit of arhatship."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (619)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the Kosalans and came to a grove called Śiṃśapā, north of the village of Sīcaka.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In the past, there was an acrobat who performed with a balancing pole. He placed the pole on his shoulder and said to his apprentice: 'You, upon the pole, should look after me from above, and I will also look after you. Mutually protecting each other, we shall perform, play, and gain much profit.' At that time, the acrobatic apprentice said to the master: 'It is not as you say. Rather, each of us should protect ourselves. Performing and playing in this way, we shall gain much profit, and our bodies will come down safely and without harm.' The acrobat master replied: 'It is as you say; each should protect oneself. However, the meaning of this is also as I have said. When one protects oneself, one is also protecting others. When one protects others, one is also protecting oneself. When one's mind draws near to oneself, cultivating, attending to, and realizing, this is called protecting oneself while protecting others. How does one protect others while protecting oneself? By not frightening others, not opposing others, not harming others, with a compassionate mind caring for them. This is called protecting others while protecting oneself.' Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'Protecting oneself' is cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness; 'protecting others' is also cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (620)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandakanivāpa Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In the great Snowy Mountains, in places of cold ice and dangerous terrain, there are no monkeys, let alone humans. In some mountains, monkeys dwell but there are no humans. In some mountains, both humans and beasts dwell together. In the places where monkeys travel, hunters smear sticky glue on the grass. Clever monkeys avoid it from afar, but foolish monkeys cannot avoid it. One touches it slightly with a hand, and that hand becomes stuck. It then tries to free itself with both hands, and both hands become stuck. It tries to free itself with its feet, and its feet also become stuck. It bites the grass with its mouth, and its mouth also becomes stuck. With all five places stuck, it lies curled up on the ground. When the hunter arrives, he runs a stick through it, hoists it onto his shoulder, and carries it away.

"Bhikṣus, you should know that the foolish monkey abandoned its own territory, the dwelling of its parents, and wandered in another's territory, thereby meeting with such suffering and distress. Likewise, bhikṣus, a foolish ordinary person who lives near a village, in the early morning puts on robes and takes a bowl to enter the village to beg for food. If he does not properly guard his body and does not guard the sense-gates, when the eye sees forms, attachment arises; when the ear hears sounds, the nose smells odors, the tongue tastes flavors, and the body contacts tangibles, attachment arises in each case. A foolish bhikṣu, with inner faculties and outer objects bound by the five bonds, follows the will of Māra. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: abide depending on your own territory, the parents' domain, and do not follow others' territory and others' domain. What is a bhikṣu's own territory, the parents' domain? It is the four foundations of mindfulness: the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (621)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, the Venerable Ānanda, together with many bhikṣus, came to the World-Honored One, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew to sit at one side.

The Venerable Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, how should these young bhikṣus be instructed? How should the Dharma be taught to them?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "These young bhikṣus should be instructed to cultivate the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? Abiding in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, with energetic effort, practicing without heedlessness, with right knowledge and right mindfulness, calming the mind, up to knowing the body. Abiding in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas, with energetic effort, practicing without heedlessness, with right mindfulness and right knowledge, calming the mind, up to knowing dharmas. Why is this? If a bhikṣu is a learner, one who has not yet attained the higher goal, aspiring toward the peace and security of nirvāṇa, at that time he abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, with energetic effort, practicing without heedlessness, with right mindfulness and right knowledge, calming the mind. He abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas, with energetic effort, practicing without heedlessness, with right mindfulness and right knowledge, calming the mind, up to attaining detachment regarding dharmas. If an arhat whose outflows are already exhausted, whose task is done, who has laid down the heavy burden, who has exhausted all bonds of existence, who has right knowledge and is well-liberated, at that time he also cultivates the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, with energetic effort, practicing without heedlessness, with right mindfulness and right knowledge, calming the mind. He abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas, up to attaining detachment regarding dharmas."

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda joyfully rejoiced, made obeisance, and departed.

### (622)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the Vajjians and came to the city of Vaiśālī, staying in the Āmrapālī Garden.

At that time, the woman Āmrapālī heard that the World-Honored One was traveling among the Vajjians and staying in the Āmrapālī Garden. She adorned herself and mounted her carriage, departed from the city of Vaiśālī, and went to the World-Honored One to pay respect and make offerings. Arriving at the gate of the Āmrapālī Garden, she descended from her carriage and proceeded on foot. From afar she saw the World-Honored One, surrounded by a great assembly, teaching the Dharma.

The World-Honored One saw the woman Āmrapālī approaching from afar and addressed the bhikṣus: "You bhikṣus, exert yourselves and abide with gathered minds, with right mindfulness and right knowledge. Now the woman Āmrapālī is coming; therefore I admonish you. How does a bhikṣu exert himself and abide with a gathered mind? If a bhikṣu has already arisen evil unwholesome dharmas, he should cut them off, generating desire and effort, exerting energy and gathering the mind. For evil unwholesome dharmas that have not yet arisen, he should prevent them from arising. For wholesome dharmas that have not yet arisen, he should cause them to arise. For wholesome dharmas that have already arisen, he should cause them to abide, not to be forgotten, and to be cultivated to fullness, generating desire and effort, exerting energy and gathering the mind. This is called a bhikṣu exerting himself and abiding with a gathered mind.

"How is a bhikṣu called one who abides in right knowledge? If a bhikṣu always abides in right knowledge in the deportment of going and coming, in looking around and gazing, in bending and stretching, in bowing down and looking up, in holding robes and bowl, in walking, standing, sitting, lying down, sleeping, waking, speaking, and being silent, he always abides in right knowledge. This is right knowledge.

"How is a bhikṣu one of right mindfulness? If a bhikṣu abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the internal body in the body, with energetic effort, right knowledge and right mindfulness, taming worldly craving and sorrow. Likewise, he abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas, with energetic effort, right knowledge and right mindfulness, taming worldly craving and sorrow. This is called a bhikṣu of right mindfulness. Therefore, you should exert yourselves and gather your minds, with right knowledge and right mindfulness. Now the woman Āmrapālī is coming; therefore I admonish you."

At that time, the woman Āmrapālī came to the World-Honored One, bowed her head at his feet, and withdrew to stand at one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the woman Āmrapālī the Dharma in various ways, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening her. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened her, he sat silently.

At that time, the woman Āmrapālī straightened her robes, made obeisance to the Buddha, joined her palms, and addressed the Buddha: "I humbly request that the World-Honored One, together with the great assembly, accept my invitation for the midday meal tomorrow."

At that time, the World-Honored One silently accepted the invitation. When the woman Āmrapālī knew that the World-Honored One had silently accepted the invitation, she bowed her head at his feet, returned to her home, and prepared various kinds of food. She arranged seats, and at dawn sent a messenger to inform the Buddha: "The time has come."

At that time, the World-Honored One, together with the great assembly, went to the woman Āmrapālī's residence and took his seat.

At that time, the woman Āmrapālī personally served various kinds of food and drink. When the meal was finished, they rinsed their mouths, and the bowls were washed and put away. At that time, the woman Āmrapālī took a small seat and sat before the Buddha to hear the Buddha teach the Dharma.

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke to the woman Āmrapālī a verse of rejoicing:

> "The generous one is beloved by people,
> Followed by many.
> Their fame and renown increase daily,
> Heard far and near.
> In gatherings, they are always harmonious and gracious,
> Free from miserliness and without fear.
> Therefore the wise give,
> Cutting off miserliness forever and without remainder.
> They are reborn above in Trāyastriṃśa Heaven,
> Enjoying happiness through the long night.
> Throughout their lifespan they constantly cultivate virtue,
> Delighting in the Nandana Garden,
> With a hundred kinds of heavenly pleasures,
> The five desires gladdening their hearts.
> Those who, in this human world,
> Hear the Dharma spoken by the Buddha,
> As disciples of the Well-Gone One,
> Delight in being reborn there through transformation."

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the woman Āmrapālī the Dharma in various ways, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening her. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened her, he rose from his seat and departed.

### (623)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "When the world speaks of a beauty, a world-renowned beauty, can she cause many people to gather and watch?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "Indeed so, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "If a world-renowned beauty, a world-renowned beauty, were also able to perform various songs, dances, and music, would she cause even more people to gather and watch?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "Indeed so, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "If there were a world-renowned beauty, a world-renowned beauty, in one place performing various songs, dances, music, play, and laughter, and a great crowd had gathered in one place, and there were a man who was neither foolish nor deluded, who loves happiness and shuns suffering, who clings to life and fears death, and someone said to him: 'Sir, you must carry a bowl brimming with oil past the world-renowned beauty and the great crowd. A man capable of killing, with a drawn sword, will follow behind you. If you spill a single drop of oil, he will immediately cut off your head.' What do you think, bhikṣus? Could that man carrying the oil bowl fail to be mindful of the oil bowl and fail to be mindful of the killer, and instead watch the dancing woman and the great crowd?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One. Why is this? World-Honored One, that man sees behind him the one with the drawn sword, and he constantly thinks: 'If I let a single drop of oil fall, the one with the drawn sword will cut off my head.' With a single-pointed mind, focusing his attention on the oil bowl, he walks slowly past the world-renowned beauty and the great crowd, not daring to look aside."

"Likewise, bhikṣus, if any śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa keeps the body upright and composes himself, with single-pointed mindfulness, not looking at sights and sounds, skillfully gathering in all mental dharmas, and abiding in the foundation of mindfulness of the body, then he is my disciple, one who follows my teaching. How does a bhikṣu keep the body upright and compose himself, with single-pointed mindfulness, not looking at sights and sounds, gathering in all mental dharmas, and abiding in the foundation of mindfulness of the body? Thus a bhikṣu contemplates the body in the body, with energetic effort, right knowledge and right mindfulness, taming worldly craving and sorrow. Abiding in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas is likewise. This is called a bhikṣu who keeps the body upright and composes himself, with single-pointed mindfulness, not looking at sights and sounds, skillfully gathering in mental dharmas, and abiding in the four foundations of mindfulness."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke this verse:

> "With concentrated mind and right mindfulness,
> Protect and hold the oil bowl.
> With one's own mind guarded by oneself,
> A place never before reached,
> Most difficult to traverse, excellent and subtle:
> What the Buddhas have spoken,
> Their words are teaching's sharp sword.
> One should concentrate the mind,
> With focused attention to protect and hold it.
> This is not something that ordinary people,
> Heedless in their ways,
> Can enter into: such is the teaching of non-heedlessness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (624)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Uttiya came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit at one side, and addressed the Buddha: "Excellent, World-Honored One! Please teach me the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I shall go alone to a quiet place, apply myself with focused thought, dwelling without heedlessness, and contemplate: 'Why is it that a good man shaves off his beard and hair, with right faith, leaving the household life, to go forth and train in the path?'" As above, explained in full, up to: "'Not receiving future existence.'"

The Buddha told Uttiya: "So it is, so it is, as you have said. However, regarding the Dharma I teach, if you do not please my mind, the task you undertake will not be accomplished. Though you follow after me, you will gain no benefit, and instead you will generate obstructions."

Uttiya addressed the Buddha: "Regarding what the World-Honored One has said, I am able to please the World-Honored One's mind, accomplish my own task, and not generate obstructions. I humbly request the World-Honored One to teach me the Dharma. I shall go alone to a quiet place, apply myself with focused thought, and dwell without heedlessness." As above, explained in full, up to: "Not receiving future existence." Thus he asked a second time and a third time.

At that time, the World-Honored One told Uttiya: "You should first purify the preliminary practices, and then cultivate the holy life."

Uttiya addressed the Buddha: "How should I now purify the preliminary practices and cultivate the holy life?"

The Buddha told Uttiya: "You should first purify your moral conduct, straighten your views, and perfect the three kinds of actions. Then cultivate the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? Abiding in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the internal body in the body, with focused effort, right knowledge and right mindfulness, taming worldly craving and sorrow. Likewise, the external body, the internal-and-external body contemplated in the body as a foundation of mindfulness, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas." This is also explained in full as above.

At that time, Uttiya, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully rejoiced, rose from his seat, and departed.

At that time, Uttiya, having heard the Buddha's instruction, went alone to a quiet place, applied himself with focused thought, and dwelt without heedlessness, contemplating: "Why is it that a good man shaves off his beard and hair, puts on the kāṣāya robes, with right faith, leaves the household life, goes forth and trains in the path, up to not receiving future existence."

As Uttiya asked, so too a certain other bhikṣu asked, and it is also taught as above.

### (625)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain bhikṣu named Bāhika, who came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit at one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, excellent World-Honored One! Please teach me the Dharma." This is explained in full as in the previous sūtra of Uttiya. The difference is: "Thus, Bhikṣu Bāhika, when the preliminary practices are purified and one abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, one transcends Māra. When one abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas, one transcends Māra."

At that time, Bhikṣu Bāhika, having heard the Buddha's teaching and admonition, joyfully rejoiced, made obeisance, and departed. Going alone to a quiet place, he applied himself with focused thought, dwelling without heedlessness, up to not receiving future existence.

### (626)

The second sūtra is also taught as above. The difference is: "Thus, bhikṣu, one transcends birth and death."

### (627)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Anuruddha came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit at one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if there is a bhikṣu who is a learner, who has not yet attained the higher goal of the peace and security of nirvāṇa, and who is striving toward it with effort, how should this noble disciple cultivate and extensively cultivate the true Dharma and Vinaya so as to exhaust all outflows, up to knowing for himself: 'I will not receive future existence'?"

The Buddha told Anuruddha: "If a noble disciple is a learner, who has not yet attained the higher goal of the peace and security of nirvāṇa, and who is striving toward it with effort, at that time he should abide in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the internal body in the body, with energetic effort, right knowledge and right mindfulness, taming worldly craving and sorrow. Likewise for the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas, with energetic effort, right knowledge and right mindfulness, taming worldly craving and sorrow. When the noble disciple has cultivated this extensively, he will exhaust all outflows, up to knowing for himself: 'I will not receive future existence.'"

At that time, the Venerable Anuruddha, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully rejoiced, made obeisance, and departed.

### (628)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Pāṭaliputra, in the Kukkuṭārāma (Cock Grove Monastery). At that time, the Venerable Udāyin and the Venerable Ānanda were also dwelling at Pāṭaliputra, in the Kukkuṭārāma.

At that time, the Venerable Udāyin went to the Venerable Ānanda, and after exchanging greetings and words of comfort, he withdrew to sit at one side and addressed the Venerable Ānanda: "The Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, with his knowledge and vision, has taught the noble precepts for the bhikṣus, which are unbroken, unflawed, uncorrupted, unblemished, not taken up as mere rules, well-perfected, well-maintained, praised by the wise, and not despised. Why is it that the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, with his vision, has taught the noble precepts for the bhikṣus that are unbroken, unflawed, up to praised by the wise and not despised?"

The Venerable Ānanda said to Udāyin: "It is for the cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas."

At that time, the two noble ones, having discussed together, each returned to their own place.

### (629)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Pāṭaliputra, in the Kukkuṭārāma. At that time, the Venerable Ānanda and the Venerable Bhadra were also dwelling there.

At that time, the Venerable Bhadra asked the Venerable Ānanda: "Is there a dharma which, when cultivated and extensively cultivated, enables one to attain non-retrogression?"

The Venerable Ānanda said to the Venerable Bhadra: "There is a dharma which, when cultivated and extensively cultivated, enables the practitioner to attain non-retrogression. It is the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas."

At that time, the two noble ones, having discussed together, each returned to their own place.

### (630)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Pāṭaliputra, in the Kukkuṭārāma. At that time, the Venerable Ānanda and the Venerable Bhadra were also dwelling there.

At that time, the Venerable Bhadra asked the Venerable Ānanda: "Is there a dharma which, when cultivated and extensively cultivated, can cause impure beings to become purified, and to increase in radiance?"

The Venerable Ānanda said to the Venerable Bhadra: "There is a dharma which, when cultivated and extensively cultivated, can cause impure beings to become purified and to increase in radiance. It is the four foundations of mindfulness: the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas."

At that time, the two noble ones, having discussed together, each returned to their own place.

### (631)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Pāṭaliputra, in the Kukkuṭārāma. At that time, the Venerable Ānanda and the Venerable Bhadra were also dwelling there.

At that time, the Venerable Bhadra asked the Venerable Ānanda: "Is there a dharma which, when cultivated and extensively cultivated, can enable beings who have not yet crossed to the other shore to cross to the other shore?"

The Venerable Ānanda said to the Venerable Bhadra: "There is a dharma which, when cultivated and extensively cultivated, can enable beings who have not yet crossed to the other shore to cross to the other shore. It is the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas."

At that time, the two noble ones, having discussed together, each returned to their own place.

### (632)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Pāṭaliputra, in the Kukkuṭārāma. At that time, the Venerable Ānanda and the Venerable Bhadra were also dwelling there.

The Venerable Bhadra asked the Venerable Ānanda: "Is there a dharma which, when cultivated and extensively cultivated, enables one to attain arhatship?"

The Venerable Ānanda said to the Venerable Bhadra: "There is a dharma which, when cultivated and extensively cultivated, enables one to attain arhatship. It is the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas."

At that time, the two noble ones, having discussed together, each returned to their own place.

### (633)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Pāṭaliputra, in the Kukkuṭārāma.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "When all dharmas are spoken of, 'all dharmas' refers to the four foundations of mindfulness; this is the correct statement. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (634)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Pāṭaliputra, in the Kukkuṭārāma.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu cultivates and extensively cultivates the four foundations of mindfulness, it is called the noble going-forth. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

As with "going-forth," so too with "the complete ending of suffering, reaching the final limit of suffering, attaining great fruit, attaining great merit and benefit, attaining the deathless Dharma, ultimately reaching the deathless, and realizing the deathless Dharma," as explained in full above.

### (635)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Pāṭaliputra, in the Kukkuṭārāma.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu cultivates and extensively cultivates the four foundations of mindfulness, impure beings will become purified, and those already purified will increase in radiance. What are the four? The foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

As with "purifying beings," so too with "enabling those who have not yet crossed to the other shore to cross, attaining arhatship, attaining pratyekabuddhahood, and attaining anuttarā-samyak-saṃbodhi," it is also taught as above.

### (636)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Pāṭaliputra, in the Kukkuṭārāma.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall teach you the cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness. What is the cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness? If, bhikṣus, a Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Enlightened One, Accomplished in Knowledge and Conduct, Well-Gone One, Knower of the World, Unsurpassed One, Tamer of Men, Teacher of Devas and Humans, Buddha, World-Honored One, arises in the world and proclaims the true Dharma, which is good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, good in meaning and good in flavor, pure, complete, and displaying the holy life. If a son or daughter of good family hears the Dharma from the Buddha and gains a purified mind of faith, and thus trains in this way, seeing the faults of the pleasures and defilements of household life joined together, the bondage of afflictions, and delighting in dwelling in empty solitude, going forth from home to train in the path, not delighting in dwelling as a householder, in the midst of non-household life, desiring single-minded purity, throughout the rest of one's life, purely complete, bright and white in the holy life: 'I shall shave off my beard and hair, put on the kāṣāya robes, with right faith leave the household life, and go forth to train in the path.' Having thought thus, one then relinquishes wealth, possessions, and relatives, shaves off the beard and hair, puts on the kāṣāya robes, with right faith leaves the household life, and goes forth to train in the path. One rectifies one's bodily conduct, guards the mouth from four faults, maintains right livelihood in purity, and practices the noble precepts. One guards the sense-gates and protects the mind with right mindfulness. When the eye sees forms, one does not grasp at signs. If one were to abide with the eye-faculty unrestrained, worldly craving, sorrow, and evil unwholesome dharmas would constantly leak into the mind. But now, regarding the eye, one arouses right restraint. For the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind, one arouses right restraint likewise.

"That one, having accomplished the noble precepts and discipline, skillfully restraining the sense-gates, in coming and going, in turning and looking about, in bending and stretching, in sitting, lying down, sleeping, waking, speaking, and being silent, abides in wise right knowledge. Having accomplished such noble precepts, guarding the sense-gates, with right knowledge and right mindfulness, in peaceful seclusion, in empty places, at the foot of trees, in quiet rooms, sitting alone, with upright body and right mindfulness, with the mind anchored and settled. One cuts off worldly craving and sorrow, free from craving desire, purified from craving desire. One cuts off worldly ill-will, sloth-and-torpor, restlessness-and-remorse, and doubt. Free from ill-will, sloth-and-torpor, restlessness-and-remorse, and doubt; purified from ill-will, sloth-and-torpor, restlessness-and-remorse, and doubt. Having cut off the five hindrances that distress the mind, weaken the power of wisdom, and create obstructions that do not lead to nirvāṇa, therefore, one abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the internal body in the body, with energetic effort, right knowledge and right mindfulness, taming worldly craving and sorrow. Likewise for the external body, the internal-and-external body, and the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas; it is also taught in the same way. This is called a bhikṣu cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (637)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should cultivate the four foundations of mindfulness," as explained in full above. The difference is, up to: "Having thus gone forth, one dwells in a quiet place, takes up the discipline of the Prātimokṣa, with completeness in one's range of conduct, and regarding even the slightest offenses, one generates great fear. One receives and upholds the training rules: abandoning killing, cutting off killing, not delighting in killing, up to all practices as previously described. One's robes and bowl accompany the body, like the two wings of a bird. Having thus accomplished the training in precepts, one cultivates the four foundations of mindfulness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (638)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was in the city of Rājagṛha, in the Kalandakanivāpa Bamboo Grove. At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra was dwelling in the Nālā village in Magadha, ill and approaching nirvāṇa, with the novice Cunda attending and serving him.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra passed into nirvāṇa due to his illness.

At that time, the novice Cunda, having completed the offerings for the Venerable Śāriputra, took the remaining relics, carried his robes and bowl, and came to the city of Rājagṛha. He put away the robes and bowl, washed his feet, and went to the Venerable Ānanda. He bowed at the Venerable Ānanda's feet, then withdrew to stand at one side, and addressed the Venerable Ānanda: "Venerable one, you should know that my preceptor, the Venerable Śāriputra, has entered nirvāṇa. I have brought his relics and robes and bowl."

Thereupon, the Venerable Ānanda, having heard the novice Cunda's words, went to the Buddha and addressed him: "World-Honored One, my entire body feels as if it is falling apart, the four directions have changed their appearance, my capacity for discernment is blocked. The novice Cunda came and told me: 'My preceptor Śāriputra has entered nirvāṇa. He brought the remaining relics and the robes and bowl.'"

The Buddha said: "What do you think, Ānanda? Did Śāriputra enter nirvāṇa taking with him the aggregate of received precepts? Or the aggregate of concentration, the aggregate of wisdom, the aggregate of liberation, or the aggregate of the knowledge and vision of liberation?"

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Did the Dharma that I myself knew and proclaimed upon attaining perfect enlightenment, namely the four foundations of mindfulness, the four right efforts, the four bases of spiritual power, the five faculties, the five powers, the seven factors of awakening, and the Eightfold Path, enter nirvāṇa?"

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One. Although he did not enter nirvāṇa taking with him the aggregate of received precepts, up to the path-factors, nevertheless the Venerable Śāriputra was one who upheld the precepts, was of great learning, of few desires, knowing contentment, always practicing seclusion, energetically diligent, with mindfulness settled in concentration, of one-pointed right absorption, swift in wisdom, deep and keen in wisdom, transcendent in wisdom, analytical in wisdom, of great wisdom, vast wisdom, profound wisdom, unequalled wisdom, accomplished in the treasure of wisdom. He was able to instruct, teach, illuminate, and gladden, and was skilled in praising and teaching the Dharma for the assembly. Therefore, World-Honored One, it is for the sake of the Dharma, and for the sake of those who receive the Dharma, that I am sorrowful and distressed."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Do not be sorrowful and distressed. Why is this? Whether sitting or rising or acting, all conditioned dharmas are subject to decay. How could they not decay? To wish that they not decay: there is no such possibility. I have previously declared that all beloved and cherished things, all the various objects and agreeable matters, are all dharmas subject to separation and cannot be permanently maintained. It is like a great tree, with roots, trunk, branches, leaves, flowers, and fruits flourishing, whose great branch first breaks off; or like a great jeweled mountain, whose great boulder first crumbles. Likewise, among the Tathāgata's great assembly of followers, the great śrāvakas are the first to enter final nirvāṇa. If Śāriputra were still living in that region, then in that region I would have nothing to attend to. Yet that region would not be empty, because Śāriputra was there; I have already said this before. Now, Ānanda, as I have previously said, all beloved and cherished things, all agreeable matters, are dharmas subject to separation. Therefore, you should not be so deeply grieved. Ānanda, you should know that the Tathāgata also will not be long in passing away. Therefore, Ānanda, you should make yourself an island and rely on yourself; make the Dharma an island and rely on the Dharma; make no other island and rely on nothing else."

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, how does one make oneself an island and rely on oneself? How does one make the Dharma an island and rely on the Dharma? How does one make no other island and rely on nothing else?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "If a bhikṣu abides in the foundation of mindfulness of contemplating the body in the body, with energetic effort, right knowledge and right mindfulness, taming worldly craving and sorrow. Likewise for the external body, the internal-and-external body, and the foundations of mindfulness of contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas; it is also taught in the same way. Ānanda, this is called making oneself an island and relying on oneself, making the Dharma an island and relying on the Dharma, making no other island and relying on nothing else."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

### (639)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Mathurā, by the Bhadra River, in the Umbrella-Canopy Āmra Tree Grove. It was not long after the Venerable Śāriputra and Mahāmaudgalyāyana had entered nirvāṇa. At that time, on the fifteenth day of the month, at the time of the upoṣadha observance, the World-Honored One had a seat spread before the great assembly and sat down.

At that time, the World-Honored One surveyed the assembly and then addressed the bhikṣus: "I observe the great assembly, and I see it is now empty, because Śāriputra and Mahā
