# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Twenty-Five

---

### (640)

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the Venerable Ānanda: "In this land of Mathurā, in a future age there will be a merchant's son named Gupta. Gupta will have a son named Upagupta. One hundred years after my passing into nirvāṇa, he shall carry out the work of the Buddha, and among instructing teachers he shall be foremost. Ānanda, do you see in the distance that dark-green forest?"

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "Indeed, I have seen it, World-Honored One!"

"Ānanda, that place is called Mount Urumandā. One hundred years after the Tathāgata's passing, on this mountain there shall be a Naṭabhaṭikā araṇya hermitage. This place, being conducive to tranquil silence, shall be foremost."

At that time, the World-Honored One had this thought: "If I were to entrust the Dharma teaching to humans alone, I fear my teaching would not endure long. If I were to entrust it to heavenly beings alone, I fear my teaching also would not endure long, and the people of the world would then have no one to receive the Dharma from. I should now entrust the true Dharma to both humans and heavenly beings. If devas and humans together uphold and protect the Dharma, then my teaching shall remain unshaken for a thousand years." At that time, the World-Honored One gave rise to this worldly thought.

Then Śakra, Lord of the Devas, and the Four Great Heavenly Kings, knowing the Buddha's thought, came to the Buddha's presence, bowed their heads and touched his feet in reverence, and withdrew to sit to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed Śakra, Lord of the Devas, and the Four Great Heavenly Kings: "Before long the Tathāgata shall enter parinirvāṇa in the remainderless nirvāṇa. After my parinirvāṇa, you all should protect and uphold the true Dharma."

At that time, the World-Honored One further addressed the Heavenly King of the East: "You should protect and uphold the true Dharma in the east." Next he addressed the Heavenly Kings of the South, West, and North: "You should protect and uphold the true Dharma in the north. After a thousand years, when my teaching perishes, unwholesome dharmas shall appear in the world, and the ten virtuous deeds shall all be destroyed. In Jambudvīpa, fierce evil winds shall arise, rain and water shall come untimely, the world shall suffer much famine, rains shall bring calamitous hail, rivers shall dry up, flowers and fruits shall not mature, people shall lose their luster, insect villages and ghost villages shall all be ground to nothing, food and drink shall lose their flavor, precious treasures shall sink and disappear, and the people shall eat coarse, rough grasses and plants.

"At that time, there will be a Śākya king, a Yavana king, a Pāhlava king, and a Tuṣāra king, with many retainers. The Tathāgata's skull-bone relic, the Buddha's tooth, and the Buddha's bowl shall be placed in the east. In the west there will be a king named Pāhlava, with hundreds of thousands of retainers, who shall destroy stūpas and monasteries and slaughter bhikṣus. In the north there will be a king named Yavana, with hundreds of thousands of retainers, who shall destroy stūpas and monasteries and slaughter bhikṣus. In the south there will be a king named Śākya, with hundreds of thousands of retainers, who shall destroy stūpas and monasteries and slaughter bhikṣus. In the east there will be a king named Tuṣāra, with hundreds of thousands of retainers, who shall destroy stūpas and monasteries and slaughter bhikṣus. All four directions shall fall into chaos, and the bhikṣus shall come and gather in the central kingdom.

"At that time, in the kingdom of Kauśāmbī there will be a king named Mahendrasena. That king shall beget a son whose hands appear as if smeared with blood, and whose body is like armor. He shall possess great valor and strength. On the day of his birth, five hundred great ministers shall each beget a son, all resembling the prince, with blood-like hands and armored bodies.

"At that time, in the kingdom of Kauśāmbī, blood shall rain for one day. The king of Kauśāmbī, seeing this evil omen, shall be greatly terrified and shall summon diviners to inquire. The diviners shall address the king: 'The son now born to the king shall rule over Jambudvīpa and shall cause great slaughter of people.' Seven days after the son's birth, he shall be named Durjaya. As the years pass and he grows up, the four evil kings from the four directions shall come to slaughter the people. King Mahendrasena, hearing this, shall be terrified.

"At that time, a heavenly spirit shall proclaim: 'Great King, you should install Durjaya as king. He is well able to subdue those four evil kings.' Then King Mahendrasena shall accept the heavenly spirit's counsel and abdicate the throne in favor of his son. Taking the bright pearl from his topknot, he shall crown his son's head, gather all the great ministers, and anoint him with fragrant water. He shall summon the five hundred sons born on the same day to the great ministers. Clad in armor, they shall march out with the king on campaign. They shall battle with the great hosts of the four evil kings, and victory shall be theirs; they shall slay them all completely. He shall rule Jambudvīpa, governing from the kingdom of Kauśāmbī."

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the Four Great Heavenly Kings: "In the kingdom of Pāṭaliputra, there shall be a brahmin named Agnidatta, learned and accomplished in the Veda scriptures and treatises. That brahmin shall take a wife. At that time, a being in the intermediate existence shall come to be born as his son. When it enters the mother's womb, the mother shall desire to engage in debate with people. The brahmin shall inquire of the diviners, and the diviners shall answer: 'It is because the being in the womb shall master all treatises; therefore, the mother gives rise to such a mind of debate, wishing to argue with people.' Thus when the months are fulfilled, the child shall emerge from the mother's womb. As a youth, he shall master all scriptures and treatises. He shall constantly teach five hundred brahmin youths by means of scriptures and treatises, and shall instruct other people in various other treatises, and teach medical arts to those who study medicine. Thus he shall have a multitude of disciples. Because he has a multitude of disciples, he shall be named 'Disciple.' He shall then seek permission from his parents to leave the household life and study the Way. When his parents give their consent, he shall go forth to leave the household life and study the Way in my Dharma. He shall master the Tripiṭaka, be skilled in expounding the Dharma, possess marvelous eloquence, and through his speech and discourse, shall gather a large retinue."

Furthermore, the World-Honored One addressed the Four Great Heavenly Kings: "In that same kingdom of Pāṭaliputra, there shall be a great merchant-chief named Sudāna. A being in the intermediate existence shall come and enter his wife's womb. When that being enters the mother's womb, it shall cause the mother to become upright and gentle, free from all wrong thoughts, with all her faculties tranquil.

"At that time, the merchant-chief shall inquire of the diviners. The diviners shall answer: 'The being in the womb is exceedingly virtuous; therefore, the mother is like this, with all her faculties tranquil.' When the months are fulfilled, a boy shall be born, named Suratha. As the years pass and he grows, he shall request his parents for permission to leave the household life and study the Way. His parents shall give their consent. He shall leave the household life and study the Way in my Dharma, diligently practicing with vigor and cultivating the path. He shall attain the exhaustion of defilements and realize the fruit of arhatship. However, he shall be of little learning, with few desires, content, and of little renown. He shall dwell in mountain thickets and forests, on a mountain named Gandhamādana.

"At that time, that holy one shall regularly come to teach the Dharma to King Durjaya. That father-king shall pass away. On the day of his passing, Durjaya, seeing his father has died, shall embrace his father's corpse with both arms, weeping and wailing with grief, his heart tormented by sorrow. At that time, the Tripiṭaka master, leading his large retinue, shall come to the king's presence and teach the Dharma to the king. Having heard the Dharma, the king's grief shall cease. He shall give rise to great faith and reverence in the Buddha-Dharma, and shall proclaim aloud: 'From this day forward, I grant to all bhikṣus freedom from fear and pleasurable ease as they wish.' He shall then ask the bhikṣus: 'How many years did the previous four evil kings spend in destroying the Buddha-Dharma?' The bhikṣus shall answer: 'Twelve years.' The king shall declare in his mind and proclaim with the voice of a lion's roar: 'For twelve years I shall provide offerings to the five assemblies, including all manner of requisites.' He shall then carry out this giving. On the day of his giving, the heavens shall shower fragrant and nourishing rain throughout Jambudvīpa, and all seeds of reality shall flourish and grow. People from all directions shall bring offerings and come to the kingdom of Kauśāmbī to make offerings to the Sangha.

"At that time, the bhikṣus shall receive abundant offerings. The bhikṣus, consuming the faithful donations of laypeople, shall not study or recite the scriptures, shall not practice sājjhāya to receive scriptures on behalf of others, shall pass the days in idle talk, spend the nights in sleep, be attached to material gains, delight in adorning themselves, wear fine garments, and be far from the bliss of renunciation, tranquility, the homeless life, and the three kinds of bodhi. Though resembling bhikṣus in form, they shall be devoid of the merits of the śramaṇa. They shall be great thieves within the Dharma, aiding in the final age to destroy the banner of the true Dharma and erect the banner of Māra, extinguishing the torch of the true Dharma and kindling the fire of afflictions, breaking the drum of the true Dharma, ruining the wheel of the true Dharma, drying up the ocean of the true Dharma, destroying the mountain of the true Dharma, breaching the fortress of the true Dharma, uprooting the tree of the true Dharma, ruining dhyāna-concentration and wisdom, severing the ornament of morality, and defiling the true path.

"At that time, the devas, nāgas, ghosts, spirits, yakṣas, gandharvas, and others shall give rise to hostility toward the bhikṣus. They shall revile and criticize the bhikṣus, feel disgust and distance themselves, no longer drawing near. With different mouths but the same voice, they shall cry: 'Alas! Such evil bhikṣus should not exist in the Tathāgata's Dharma.' And they shall speak this verse:

> "'Performing no auspicious deeds but only evil conduct,
> Practicing the dharmas of wrong views,
> These foolish and deluded people
> Smash apart the mountain of the true Dharma.
> Practicing the dharmas of evil conduct,
> Discarding all righteous practices,
> Abandoning all excellent and wondrous dharmas,
> Uprooting the present Buddha-Dharma.
> Without faith, undisciplined,
> Delighting in performing evil deeds,
> Deceitful and fraudulent, deceiving the world,
> Shattering the Dharma of the Muni.
> Mutilating their appearance yet practicing evils,
> Fierce, violent, and acting in a thousand ways,
> Relying on the Dharma to deceive worldly people,
> Wrathful, hateful, arrogant, and conceited.
> Greedy and attached, seeking fame and profit,
> No evil deed unprepared,
> As the Buddha himself declared,
> Such are the signs when the Dharma perishes.
> All this is now fully seen;
> By the wise they are despised and scorned.
> Now that these dharmas have appeared,
> The ocean of the Muni's true Dharma
> Shall before long be dried up.
> The true Dharma now barely remains;
> Evil people come again to destroy it,
> Demolishing our true Dharma.'

"At that time, those devas, nāgas, spirits, and others shall all give rise to hearts of displeasure and shall no longer protect the bhikṣus. With one voice they shall cry: 'After seven more days the Buddha-Dharma shall be utterly extinguished.' Wailing and weeping, they shall say to one another: 'On the day when the bhikṣus recite the precepts, they shall quarrel and fight with one another, and the Tathāgata's true Dharma shall perish therein.' Thus the devas shall weep in grief and sorrow.

"At that time, within the city of Kauśāmbī there shall be five hundred upāsakas who, hearing the words of the devas, shall go together to the assembly of bhikṣus to admonish the bhikṣus about their quarreling. And they shall speak this verse:

> "'Alas, how grievous these bitter years!
> Have pity on all sentient beings born here.
> That Dharma now shall perish,
> The Dharma of the Lion of the Śākya clan.
> The evil wheel destroys the Dharma-wheel;
> Even something as hard as diamond
> Cannot escape being broken.
> The time of peace has already ended;
> The dharma of danger has arisen.
> The bright and wise have already passed;
> Seeing such signs now,
> One should know it shall not be long.
> The Muni's Dharma is cut off and extinguished;
> The world has no more light.
> The stainless, quiescent mouth,
> The sun of the Muni, now sets.
> The people of the world lose their hidden treasure;
> Good and evil are no longer distinguished.
> When good and evil are no longer distinguished,
> Who can attain true awakening?
> The Dharma-lamp still exists in the world;
> While there is time, practice all good deeds.
> The immeasurable fields of merit,
> This Dharma shall now perish.
> Therefore, we and our fellows
> Know that wealth is not enduring;
> While there is time, grasp what is truly solid.'

"On the fifteenth day, at the time for reciting the precepts, the Dharma shall perish. On that day, the five hundred upāsakas shall, in a single day, build five hundred Buddha-stūpas. Thereupon, the upāsakas shall each have other duties and shall no longer come and go among the Sangha.

"At that time, the arhat Suratha, dwelling on Mount Gandhamādana, shall survey Jambudvīpa: 'Where today is there a Sangha assembly reciting the precepts?' He shall see that in the kingdom of Kauśāmbī the Tathāgata's disciples are reciting the precepts for the poṣadha. He shall then go to Kauśāmbī.

"At that time, the Sangha assembly shall number hundreds of thousands of people. Among them there shall be only one arhat, named Suratha. And there shall be one Tripiṭaka master, named Disciple. This shall be the Tathāgata's last great assembly gathering. At that time, the karmadāna shall distribute the śalākā counting-sticks and address the Tripiṭaka elder: 'The Sangha has assembled, numbering hundreds of thousands. Now please recite the Prātimokṣa.' At that time, the elder shall reply: 'The Tathāgata's disciples from all of Jambudvīpa have come and gathered here, numbering hundreds of thousands. In such an assembly, I am the chief. I have mastered the Tripiṭaka, yet even I have not practiced the vinaya, how much less could anyone else have studied it? For whom then should the vinaya be recited?' And he shall speak this verse:

> "'Today is the fifteenth day,
> The night is still, the moon is bright and clear.
> All these bhikṣus
> Have now assembled to hear the precepts recited.
> From all of Jambudvīpa,
> This is the Sangha's final gathering.
> I am the chief among this assembly,
> Yet I have not studied the vinaya,
> How much less the rest of the Sangha,
> That any should have studied it?
> Who could uphold the Muni's Dharma,
> The Lion King of the Śākyas?
> Only one who holds those precepts
> Could be the one to recite them.'

"At that time, that arhat Suratha shall stand before the elder, press his palms together, and address the elder, saying: 'Elder! Please recite the Prātimokṣa. Just as when the Buddha was in the world, the dharmas studied by Śāriputra, Maudgalyāyana, and other great bhikṣus, I have now fully studied them all. Though the Tathāgata has passed into nirvāṇa, and now a thousand years have elapsed, the vinaya rules that he established I have completely fulfilled.' And he shall speak this verse:

> "'Elder, hear my words:
> My name is Suratha.
> With defilements exhausted, an arhat,
> I make a lion's roar within the Sangha.
> A true disciple of the Muni,
> The faithful ghosts and spirits of the Buddha,
> Hearing what that holy one proclaims,
> Shall weep in sorrow, shedding tears.
> Bowing their heads, reflecting on the Dharma's extinction,
> From this day forward,
> There shall be no one to proclaim the Dharma,
> The Vinaya, the Prātimokṣa.
> They shall exist no more in the world;
> The Dharma-bridge is now broken;
> The Dharma-water flows no more;
> The Dharma-ocean has dried up.
> The Dharma-mountain has crumbled;
> The Dharma-assemblies cease from now on;
> The Dharma-banner is seen no more;
> The Dharma-feet walk no more.
> The vinaya precepts are forever submerged;
> The Dharma-lamp shines no more;
> The Dharma-wheel turns no more;
> The gate of ambrosia is closed and sealed.
> The Dharma-teachers are no longer in the world;
> The virtuous ones who preach the wondrous Way,
> Sentient beings no longer recognize the good;
> They are no different from wild beasts.'

"At that time, the Buddha's mother, the great lady Mahāmāyā, shall descend from the heavens and come to the assembly of monks. Wailing and weeping, she shall cry: 'Alas! How bitter! My son, throughout asaṃkhyeya kalpas, cultivated arduous practices, heedless of bodily toil, accumulating merit to become a Buddha. And now, suddenly, all is extinguished.' And she shall speak this verse:

> "'I am the Buddha's own mother;
> My son accumulated arduous practices
> Throughout countless kalpas,
> And ultimately achieved the true Way.
> Weeping and unable to contain myself,
> For now the Dharma is suddenly ground to nothing.
> Alas, O people of wisdom,
> Where are you now?
> Those who upheld the Dharma and forsook quarrels,
> Born from the Buddha's mouth,
> Kings, the unsurpassed honored ones,
> True disciples of the Buddha.
> Practitioners of dhūta-austerities, cultivating wondrous practices,
> Dwelling in forests and thickets,
> Such true sons of the Buddha,
> Where are they now?
> Now in this world
> There are no more persons of majestic virtue;
> In the wilderness and mountain forests,
> The spirits are silent, without a word.
> Giving, morality, compassion for beings,
> Adorning themselves with faith and precepts,
> Patience, uprightness in conduct,
> Observing good and evil.
> All such excellent dharmas
> Are now suddenly and completely finished.'

"At that time, the elder Disciple shall have this thought: 'That bhikṣu Suratha claims: "The precepts and vinaya established by the Tathāgata, I have fully upheld them all."' At that time, the elder shall have a disciple named Aṅgada, who shall give rise to a heart of intolerance, generating extreme anger and resentment. Rising from his seat, he shall revile and insult that holy one: 'You are a junior bhikṣu, foolish and without wisdom, yet you insult my preceptor!' Seizing a sharp knife, he shall slay that holy one. And he shall speak this verse:

> "'My name is Aṅgada,
> Disciple of "Disciple";
> With a sharp sword I slay your body,
> Claiming that I have merit.'

"At that time, there shall be a ghost named Mahādīmukha, who shall have this thought: 'In the world there was only this one arhat, and he has been killed by the disciple of an evil bhikṣu.' Grasping a vajra mace with fire blazing from its tip, he shall strike and shatter that one's head, and that one shall die immediately. And he shall speak this verse:

> "'I am a fierce ghost-spirit,
> Named Mahādīmukha;
> With this vajra mace
> I shatter your head into seven pieces.'

"At that time, the arhat's disciple, seeing the other's disciple slay his teacher, shall be filled with anger and, unable to bear it, shall slay the Tripiṭaka master. At that time, devas and humans shall weep in grief and sorrow: 'Alas! How bitter! The Tathāgata's true Dharma is now completely finished.' Immediately this great earth shall shake in six ways. Countless beings shall wail and weep, overwhelmed with sorrow: 'Alas! Today the true Dharma shall no longer appear in the world.' Having spoken thus, they shall each scatter and depart.

"At that time, the five hundred upāsakas of the kingdom of Kauśāmbī, having heard this, shall go to the monastery, raise their hands to their heads, and cry aloud: 'Alas! The Tathāgata had compassion for the world and saved all living beings, whether great or small. Who shall now teach us the meaning of the Dharma? Now the liberation of humans and devas can no longer be attained. Beings today remain in darkness with no one to guide them. They shall long practice evil deeds and take delight therein, like wild beasts. Not hearing the Muni's wondrous Dharma, when their bodies break and their lives end, they shall fall into the three evil destinies. Like shooting stars, from this day on, the people of the world shall have no more mindful wisdom, tranquility, samādhi, or the wondrous Dharma of the ten powers.'

"At that time, the king of Kauśāmbī, hearing that the bhikṣus had slain the true arhat and the Tripiṭaka Dharma-teacher, shall give rise to a heart of grief and sorrow, and shall sit in lamentation. At that time, the adherents of wrong views shall quarrel and fight, smash stūpas and temples, and harm bhikṣus. From that point on, the Buddha-Dharma shall be utterly and suddenly extinguished."

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed Śakra, Lord of the Devas: "Four Great Heavenly Kings, all devas and humans: after my passing into nirvāṇa, the signs of the Dharma's end shall be as described above. Therefore, you all must now exert diligent effort, be zealous in your vigor, and protect and uphold the true Dharma so that it may long remain in the world."

At that time, the devas and humans, having heard what the Buddha said, each showed sorrowful faces, wiped away their tears with their hands, bowed at the Buddha's feet, and each withdrew and departed.

---

### (641)

#### Sūtra on the Causes and Conditions of King Aśoka's Offering of Half an Āmalaka Fruit

King Aśoka attained great faith and reverence in the Tathāgata's Dharma. At that time, the king asked the bhikṣus: "Who in the Tathāgata's Dharma has practiced the greatest giving?"

The bhikṣus addressed the king: "The elder Anāthapiṇḍada practiced the greatest giving."

The king further asked: "How much treasure did he give?"

The bhikṣus answered: "One hundred million in gold."

Having heard this, the king thought thus: "That elder was able to relinquish one hundred million in gold. I am now a king; why should I also give merely one hundred million in gold? I should give one billion in gold."

At that time, the king erected eighty-four thousand Buddha-stūpas. In each of those stūpas he further donated one hundred thousand in gold. He also held a great quinquennial assembly, at which three hundred thousand bhikṣus attended. He used three hundred koṭis of gold to make offerings to the Sangha. Among that assembly, the first portion were arhats, the second portion were learners, and the third portion were genuine ordinary people. Excluding his private treasury, he gave to the holy Sangha the entirety of Jambudvīpa: his queens, palace women, the crown prince, and great ministers, using four billion in gold to redeem them back. Calculating in this manner, he used ninety-six billion in gold. Eventually, the king fell gravely ill, and the king himself knew that his life was about to end.

At that time, there was a great minister named Rādhagupta. In a former life, the king had been one of the children who together offered earth to the Buddha, and this minister had been his companion. At that time, the great minister Rādhagupta, seeing the king gravely ill with his life about to end, bowed his head and addressed the king with a verse:

> "Your countenance was always fresh and radiant,
> Surrounded by hundreds of thousands of palace women,
> Like lotus flowers
> Around which bees gather.
> Now I behold the holy king's face;
> It has lost all its radiance."

The king then replied with a verse:

> "I have no sorrow now
> Over losing wealth or the royal throne,
> This body, my other kin,
> Or the various treasures.
> What grieves me now
> Is that I can no longer behold the noble saints
> And make offerings to them with the four requisites.
> This alone is what I think of;
> Therefore my countenance has changed,
> And my heart and mind find no peace.

"Furthermore, it has always been my vow to complete one billion in gold for meritorious works. Now my vow cannot be fulfilled, and I am about to depart to the next life." When he calculated all the gold, silver, and precious treasures he had given before and after, he found he was still short by four hundred million, not yet reaching the full amount. The king then prepared various precious treasures and had them sent to the Kukkuṭārāma monastery.

The son of Dharmavivarddhana, named Saṃprati, was crown prince. The ministers addressed the crown prince, saying: "The great king will not last much longer. Now he is sending these precious treasures to the monastery. The treasury's wealth is already exhausted. It is the rule for kings that wealth is of paramount importance. The crown prince should put a stop to this; do not let the great king use it all up." The crown prince then ordered the keeper of the treasury not to release anything more for the great king's use.

At that time, the great king himself knew that when he asked for things, he could no longer obtain them. He sent the gold vessels he ate from to the monastery. The crown prince then forbade the gold vessels and replaced them with silver vessels. The king, after eating, again sent these to the monastery. The silver vessels were also forbidden, and he was given copper vessels. The king likewise sent these to the monastery. The copper vessels were also forbidden, and he was given earthenware vessels.

At that time, the great king held half an āmalaka fruit in his hand. With tears of sorrow, he addressed the great ministers: "Who now is lord of the land?"

The ministers addressed the great king: "The king is lord of the land."

At that time, the king spoke this verse in reply:

> "You all seek to protect my feelings;
> Why speak such empty falsehoods?
> I sit upon the royal throne
> Yet am no longer free.
> Half an āmalaka fruit
> Now rests in my hand;
> This alone is my possession,
> Over which I have freedom.
> Alas, how honorable rank and wealth
> Are fit to be despised and cast away!
> Once I ruled all Jambudvīpa;
> Now in a single morning, poverty has arrived.
> Like the swift current of the Ganges River,
> Once it flows past, it never returns;
> Honor and wealth are likewise:
> Once gone, they never come back."

Furthermore, as the Buddha spoke in verse:

> "All that flourishes must decline;
> Decline is its ultimate end.
> The Tathāgata proclaimed with his divine mouth;
> This truth admits no variation.
> Before, when I gave commands,
> They were carried out swiftly without obstruction.
> Now when I make a request,
> No one any longer follows my command.
> Like wind blocked by a mountain,
> Like water blocked by a bank,
> My commands today
> Are now forever cut off.
> Leading countless hosts,
> Beating drums, blowing conch-shells,
> Always performing music and entertainments,
> Enjoying the five sensual pleasures,
> With hundreds of palace women,
> Day and night taking delight,
> Now all is completely finished,
> Like a tree without flowers or fruit.
> My countenance wastes away,
> My form and strength likewise;
> Like a flower that gradually wilts,
> I too am now just so."

At that time, King Aśoka called his attendant and said: "If you remember the kindness with which I raised you, take this half āmalaka fruit and deliver it to the Kukkuṭārāma monastery. On my behalf, bow at the feet of the bhikṣu Sangha, and say: 'King Aśoka sends greetings to the great and holy assembly. I am King Aśoka, who ruled this Jambudvīpa. Jambudvīpa was mine, but now it is suddenly exhausted. I have no more wealth to give to the Sangha. Over all possessions I have lost freedom. Now only this half āmalaka fruit is mine to give freely. This is my very last act of giving, the perfection of dāna. Out of compassion for me, please accept this offering so that I may gain the merit of making offerings to the Sangha.'" And he spoke this verse:

> "Half an āmalaka fruit
> Is what belongs to me;
> Over it I am free.
> Now I relinquish it to the great assembly.
> My mind relies on the holy ones;
> There is no other who can save me.
> Out of compassion for me,
> Please accept this āmalaka.
> Eat this offering on my behalf;
> Through this, the merit shall be immeasurable.
> Life after life, receiving wondrous bliss,
> Using it without end."

At that time, the attendant, having received the king's command, took this half āmalaka fruit to the Kukkuṭārāma monastery. Before the elder, he prostrated with his five limbs touching the ground, knelt with palms joined, and facing the elder, spoke this verse:

> "He who ruled over Jambudvīpa,
> Under one parasol, with one drum,
> Moving about without obstruction,
> Like the sun illuminating the world,
> His karmic retribution has now arrived;
> He shall not remain long in this world.
> No longer possessing royal majesty,
> Like the sun obscured by clouds.
> He is called King Aśoka,
> Who bows his head at the Sangha's feet.
> He sends this gift of giving,
> Namely half an āmalaka.
> Seeking merit for the life to come,
> Out of compassion for that king,
> May the holy assembly, in pity for him,
> Accept this offering of half a fruit."

At that time, the elder addressed the great assembly: "Who, hearing these words, would not grow weary of the world? Having heard this matter, we cannot fail to give rise to world-weariness and renunciation. As the Buddha's sūtras teach, when seeing the decline of others, one should give rise to renunciation. If any sentient being of understanding hears this matter, how can they not relinquish the world?" And he spoke this verse:

> "Among human kings, the foremost in the world,
> Aśoka of the Maurya clan,
> Sovereign of Jambudvīpa,
> Now is master only of an āmalaka.
> The crown prince and the ministers
> Together seized the great king's giving.
> Sending half an āmalaka
> Subdues those proud of their wealth,
> Causing them to give rise to world-weariness.
> Fools do not recognize giving;
> Through causes and effects one receives wondrous bliss.
> This is shown by the sending of half an āmalaka."

At that time, the elder had this thought: "How can I make it so that this half āmalaka can be divided so all the monks receive a share to eat?" He then instructed that it be ground up and added to a pomegranate broth. When it was distributed, the entire Sangha was able to receive a portion throughout.

At that time, the king again asked a minister beside him: "Who is the king of Jambudvīpa?"

The minister addressed the king: "The great king is."

At that time, the king rose from lying down and sat up. He gazed in the four directions, pressed his palms together in salutation, reflected on the virtues of the Buddhas, and with mind and mouth declared: "I now further give this Jambudvīpa to the Three Jewels, to use as they wish." And he spoke this verse:

> "Now this Jambudvīpa,
> Adorned with many precious treasures,
> I give to the excellent field of merit;
> The reward of fruit shall come naturally.
> By the merit of this giving,
> I do not seek to become Śakra, Lord of the Devas,
> Or Brahmā King, or a human sovereign,
> Or the wondrous pleasures of any world.
> Such fruits and rewards as these,
> I wish none of them.
> By the merit of this giving,
> May I swiftly attain the path of Buddhahood,
> Honored and revered by the world,
> Achieving omniscience,
> Being the good friend of the world,
> The foremost among guiding teachers."

At that time, the king had all these words written on paper, sealed and bound it, and stamped it with his tooth-seal. When this was done, he thereupon passed away.

At that time, the crown prince, the ministers, the palace attendants, the court women, and the people of the kingdom performed various offerings and a funeral procession. According to the rites for a king, his body was cremated.

At that time, the ministers wished to install the crown prince to succeed to the royal throne. Among them was a great minister named Anurāddha, who addressed the ministers: "The crown prince cannot be installed as king. Why is that? When the great King Aśoka was alive, he originally vowed to complete one hundred thousand koṭis of gold in meritorious works. He was still short by four hundred million, not reaching the full amount. For this reason, he gave Jambudvīpa to the Three Jewels, wishing to make up the remainder. Now this great earth belongs to the Three Jewels; how can someone be installed as king?"

At that time, the ministers, having heard this, sent four hundred million in gold to the monastery. They then installed the son of Dharmavivarddhana as king, named Saṃprati. His crown prince in succession was named Bṛhadratha, who succeeded to the throne. Bṛhadratha's crown prince was named Bṛhasena, who succeeded to the throne. Bṛhasena's crown prince was named Puṣyamitra Puṣyadharma, who succeeded to the throne. Puṣyamitra's crown prince was named Puṣyamitra, who succeeded to the throne.

At that time, Puṣyamitra asked the ministers: "What sort of deed should I perform so that my fame and virtue may endure long in the world?"

At that time, the virtuous ministers who had faith and delight in the Three Jewels addressed the king: "The great King Aśoka was of the king's own lineage. When that king was in the world, he erected eighty-four thousand Tathāgata-stūpas and made various offerings. This fame and virtue has been transmitted to the present day. If the king wishes to seek such renown, he should erect eighty-four thousand stūpas and make various offerings."

The king said: "The great King Aśoka possessed great majestic power and could accomplish such things. I cannot do this. Think of something else."

Among them was an evil minister, one without faith, who addressed the king: "There are two kinds of dharmas that are transmitted in the world without perishing: first, doing good; second, doing evil. The great King Aśoka performed virtuous deeds. The king should now carry out evil deeds and destroy the eighty-four thousand stūpas."

At that time, the king heeded the sycophantic minister's words and mustered the four divisions of the army. He went to the monasteries and destroyed the stūpas and temples. The king first went to the Kukkuṭārāma monastery. Before the monastery gate there was a stone lion, which let out a lion's roar. The king, hearing it, was greatly alarmed and terrified. Since it was not a living beast that could roar, he returned to the city. He attempted this three times, seeking to destroy that monastery.

At that time, the king summoned the bhikṣus and asked them: "Is it better for me to destroy the stūpas, or to destroy the monks' quarters?"

The bhikṣus answered: "Neither should be done. But if the king insists on destruction, it is better to destroy the monks' quarters than to destroy the Buddha-stūpas."

At that time, the king slaughtered bhikṣus and destroyed stūpas and monasteries. Proceeding gradually, he reached the kingdom of Śākala. He further issued a proclamation: "Whoever can bring me the head of a śramaṇa disciple of the Śākya clan shall be rewarded with one thousand in gold."

At that time, in that kingdom there was an arhat who magically created many bhikṣu heads and gave them to the common people, ordering them to send these to the king, causing the king's treasury to be completely depleted. When that king heard what the arhat had done, his fury redoubled, and he wished to kill that arhat.

At that time, that arhat entered the absorption of cessation. The king devised countless means to kill that holy one but was utterly unable to do so. By the power of the absorption of cessation, his body could not be harmed. Proceeding gradually, the king arrived at the gate of a Buddha-stūpa.

In that stūpa there was a ghost-spirit dwelling within, guarding the Buddha-stūpa, named Daṃṣṭra. That ghost-spirit had this thought: "I am a disciple of the Buddha and uphold the precept of not killing living beings. I cannot kill this king." He then further thought: "There is a spirit named Kṛmi, who performs evil deeds, is fierce, brave, and strong. He sought my daughter in marriage, but I did not give her. Now, for the sake of protecting the true Dharma, I should give her to him and have him protect the Buddha-Dharma." He then summoned that spirit and said: "I now give my daughter to you in marriage. However, let us establish a solemn pledge: you must subdue this king and prevent him from carrying out evil deeds to destroy the true Dharma."

At that time, the king had a great ghost-spirit named Uḍḍa, replete with majestic power. Because of this, that spirit could do nothing against the king. At that time, the spirit Daṃṣṭra devised a stratagem: "The king's might and power naturally come from this ghost-spirit. I shall now beguile him and befriend him." Thus he befriended that spirit. When they had become close friends, he then led that spirit to the great ocean in the south.

At that time, the spirit Kṛmi pushed and toppled a great mountain, crushing the king along with his four divisions of troops. Not one was left alive. The people cried out: "Excellent! Excellent!" This has been transmitted among the people as "the Excellent Event." That king perished, and the Maurya lineage came to a permanent end.

Therefore, worldly prosperity and pleasure should not be coveted. The great King Aśoka was a person of wisdom who awakened to the impermanence of the world, that body and life are difficult to preserve, and that the wealth of the five claimants is like an illusion. Having awakened to this dharma, he practiced with diligence and vigor, performed meritorious deeds, and even at the end of his life, kept his mind fixed on the Three Jewels, thought after thought without ceasing, without any reluctance or stinginess, with the sole wish to completely attain anuttarā samyaksaṃbodhi.

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.
