It appears that the text content for Fascicle 25 (卷第二十五) was not included in your message. The body of the fascicle between the delimiter lines is empty, and only a brief reference to "Fascicle 026: 雜阿含經" appears at the end.

Could you please provide the full Chinese text of the fascicle you would like translated? I am ready to translate every passage in full once the source text is supplied.

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Twenty-Six

---

### (642)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are three faculties: the faculty of 'I shall come to know the unknown,' the faculty of knowledge, and the faculty of one who has fully known."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke this verse:

> "When awakening to the stage of a learner,
> One progresses following the straight path,
> With vigorous effort and diligent exertion,
> Well guarding one's own mind.
> When one knows for oneself that birth is ended,
> The unobstructed path is already known;
> Having been liberated through knowledge,
> One finally attains the faculty of one who has fully known.
> With unshakable liberation of mind,
> All existence can be exhausted.
> All faculties are fully complete,
> Delighting in the stilling of the faculties,
> Bearing the final body,
> Having conquered the host of Māra, the enemy."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (643)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five faculties. What are the five? They are the faculty of faith, the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (644)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five faculties. What are the five? They are the faculty of faith, the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom. If a bhikṣu truly and skillfully examines these five faculties, and truly and skillfully examines them, he attains the cutting off and knowing of the three fetters, namely: identity view, clinging to rules and observances, and doubt. This is called a stream-enterer, one who is not subject to falling into bad destinies, who is definitely directed toward right awakening, who will undergo at most seven existences among gods and humans, and will reach the end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (645)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "One who truly examines these five faculties does not give rise to the outflows, and the mind attains liberation through freedom from desire. This is called an arahant, one whose outflows are ended, whose task is done, who has laid down the heavy burden, who has attained his own benefit, who has exhausted all fetters of existence, and whose mind is well liberated through right knowledge."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (646)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five faculties. What are the five? They are the faculty of faith, the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom. The faculty of faith: one should know it as the four kinds of unshakable purity. The faculty of vigor: one should know it as the four right efforts. The faculty of mindfulness: one should know it as the four foundations of mindfulness. The faculty of concentration: one should know it as the four dhyānas. The faculty of wisdom: one should know it as the Four Noble Truths."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (647)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five faculties. What are the five? They are the faculty of faith, the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom.

"What is the faculty of faith? If a bhikṣu arises pure faith toward the Tathāgata, firmly rooted and solidly established, such that no other śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, or any others in the world can undermine his mind, this is called the faculty of faith.

"What is the faculty of vigor? Evil and unwholesome states that have already arisen are to be abandoned; for this, one generates desire, makes effort, focuses the mind, and strives. Evil and unwholesome states that have not yet arisen are not to arise; for this, one generates desire, makes effort, focuses the mind, and strives. Wholesome states that have not yet arisen are to be made to arise; for this, one generates desire, makes effort, focuses the mind, and strives. Wholesome states that have already arisen are to be maintained without forgetting, cultivated and broadened; for this, one generates desire, makes effort, focuses the mind, and strives. This is called the faculty of vigor.

"What is the faculty of mindfulness? If a bhikṣu dwells contemplating the body internally in the body, with earnest effort, right mindfulness and right awareness, subduing worldly covetousness and grief; externally in the body, internally and externally in the body, and feelings, mind, and dharmas contemplating dharmas, dwelling likewise, this is called the faculty of mindfulness.

"What is the faculty of concentration? If a bhikṣu, secluded from desires and unwholesome states, with initial thought and sustained thought, with joy and happiness born of seclusion, up to the fourth dhyāna, dwells having fulfilled it, this is called the faculty of concentration.

"What is the faculty of wisdom? If a bhikṣu truly knows the noble truth of suffering as it really is, truly knows the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering as they really are, this is called the faculty of wisdom."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (648)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "If a bhikṣu truly examines these five faculties, he attains the cutting off and knowing of the three fetters. What are the three? They are identity view, clinging to rules and observances, and doubt. This is called a stream-enterer, one who does not fall into bad destinies, who is definitely directed toward the three kinds of bodhi, who will undergo at most seven existences among gods and humans, and will reach the end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (649)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "If a bhikṣu truly examines these five faculties, he is able to exhaust all outflows and attains liberation through freedom from desire. This is called an arahant. His outflows are ended, his task is done, he has laid down the heavy burden, he has attained his own benefit, he has exhausted all fetters of existence, and his mind is liberated through right knowledge."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (650)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "Bhikṣus, if I had not truly known the faculty of faith, the origin of the faculty of faith, the cessation of the faculty of faith, and the path leading to the cessation of the faculty of faith, I could never, among devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, and brāhmaṇas, have emerged and been liberated, with mind free from distortion, nor could I have attained anuttara-samyak-saṃbodhi. As with the faculty of faith, so too with the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom, it is likewise stated.

"Bhikṣus, because I examined the faculty of faith with right knowledge as it truly is, because I examined with right knowledge as it truly is the origin of the faculty of faith, the cessation of the faculty of faith, and the path leading to the cessation of the faculty of faith, among devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, and brāhmaṇas, I emerged and was liberated, with mind free from distortion, and attained anuttara-samyak-saṃbodhi. As with the faculty of faith, so too with the faculties of vigor, mindfulness, concentration, and wisdom, it is likewise stated."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (651)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "Bhikṣus, if I had not truly known the origin of the faculty of faith, the disappearance of the faculty of faith, the gratification of the faculty of faith, the danger of the faculty of faith, and the escape from the faculty of faith, I could not, among devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, and brāhmaṇas, have attained liberation, have emerged and been liberated, with mind free from distortion, and attained anuttara-samyak-saṃbodhi. Likewise with the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom, it is likewise stated.

"Bhikṣus, because I truly know the faculty of faith, the origin of the faculty of faith, the disappearance of the faculty of faith, the gratification of the faculty of faith, the danger of the faculty of faith, and the escape from the faculty of faith, among devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, and brāhmaṇas, I attained liberation, emerged and was liberated, with mind free from distortion, and attained anuttara-samyak-saṃbodhi."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (652)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "If a bhikṣu's five faculties are sharp and fully complete, he attains arahantship. If they are soft and inferior, he attains the state of a non-returner. If softer and more inferior, he attains the state of a once-returner. If softer and more inferior, he attains the state of a stream-enterer. Those who are fully complete accomplish the task of full completion; those who are not fully complete accomplish the task of incomplete attainment. Regarding these five faculties, they are not empty and without fruit. If one is entirely without these five faculties, I declare that person to be counted among the outsiders and ordinary worldlings."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (653)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "If a bhikṣu's five faculties are supremely sharp and fully complete, he attains arahantship with liberation in both ways. If softer and more inferior, he attains body-witness. If softer and more inferior in that, he attains vision-attainment. If softer and more inferior in that, he attains liberation through faith. If softer and more inferior in that, he attains single-seed. If softer and more inferior in that, he attains the state of a once-returner. If softer and more inferior in that, he attains the state of family-to-family. If softer and more inferior in that, he attains seven existences. If softer and more inferior in that, he attains dharma-follower. If softer and more inferior in that, he attains faith-follower. This is called: a bhikṣu, by the perfection of faculties as a condition, knows the perfection of fruits; by the perfection of fruits as a condition, knows the perfection of persons. Thus, the fully complete accomplish the task of full completion, and those with lesser accomplish the task of lesser attainment. Those faculties are not empty and without fruit. If one is without these faculties, I declare that person to be counted among ordinary worldlings."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (654)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five faculties. What are the five? They are the faculty of faith, the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom. These five faculties are all gathered and sustained by the faculty of wisdom. Just as among the many timbers of a great hall, the ridgepole is foremost, and all depend upon the ridgepole, being held together by it, so among the five faculties, wisdom is foremost, holding them together."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (655)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five faculties. What are the five? They are the faculty of faith, the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom. The faculty of faith: one should know it as the four kinds of unshakable purity. The faculty of vigor: one should know it as the four right efforts. The faculty of mindfulness: one should know it as the four foundations of mindfulness. The faculty of concentration: one should know it as the four dhyānas. The faculty of wisdom: one should know it as the Four Noble Truths. All these meritorious qualities have wisdom as foremost, holding them together." And so forth.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (656)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five faculties. What are the five? The faculty of faith, the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom.

"If a noble disciple has accomplished the faculty of wisdom, he is able to cultivate the faculty of faith, relying on seclusion, relying on dispassion, relying on cessation, directed toward relinquishment. This is called the accomplishment of the faculty of faith; the accomplished faculty of faith is itself the faculty of wisdom. As with the faculty of faith, so too with the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom, it is likewise stated. Therefore, among these five faculties, the faculty of wisdom is foremost, holding them together. Just as among the timbers of a great hall, the ridgepole is foremost, and the many timbers depend upon it, being held together by it, so among the five faculties, wisdom is foremost, holding them together."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (657)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five faculties. What are the five? The faculty of faith, the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom. If a noble disciple has accomplished the faculty of faith, he trains in this way. The noble disciple reflects: 'In beginningless saṃsāra, veiled by ignorance and bound by craving, beings have long wandered in the long night of birth and death, coming and going, rushing about, not knowing the beginning. There is a cause for the existence of birth and death; when that cause is permanently exhausted, then there is no birth and death. With the great mass of darkness of ignorance as obstruction, who attains nirvāṇa? Only through the cessation of suffering, the stilling of suffering, the cooling, the disappearing.' As with the faculty of faith, so too with the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom, it is likewise stated. These five faculties have wisdom as foremost, gathered and sustained by wisdom. Just as among the timbers of a great hall, the ridgepole is foremost, gathered and sustained by the ridgepole."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (658)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five faculties: the faculty of faith, the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom. What is the faculty of faith? A noble disciple arises pure faith toward the Tathāgata, firmly rooted and solidly established, which cannot be destroyed by devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, or any worldly dharmas. This is called the faculty of faith. What is the faculty of vigor? It is the four right efforts. What is the faculty of mindfulness? It is the four foundations of mindfulness. What is the faculty of concentration? It is the four dhyānas. What is the faculty of wisdom? It is the Four Noble Truths. All these meritorious qualities have wisdom as foremost. Just as among the timbers of a great hall, the ridgepole is foremost."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (659)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five faculties. What are the five? They are the faculty of faith, the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom. What is the faculty of faith? If a noble disciple obtains pure faith with regard to the Tathāgata's arousal of the mind of bodhi, this is called the faculty of faith. What is the faculty of vigor? The vigorous effort and exertion arisen with regard to the Tathāgata's arousal of the mind of bodhi is called the faculty of vigor. What is the faculty of mindfulness? The mindfulness arisen with regard to the Tathāgata's initial arousal of the mind of bodhi. What is the faculty of concentration? The samādhi arisen with regard to the Tathāgata's initial arousal of the mind of bodhi is called the faculty of concentration. What is the faculty of wisdom? The wisdom arisen with regard to the Tathāgata's initial arousal of the mind of bodhi is called the faculty of wisdom. The rest regarding the simile of the hall is as stated above."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (660)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five faculties. What are the five? They are the faculty of faith, the faculty of vigor, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, and the faculty of wisdom. By cultivating and extensively cultivating these five faculties, all suffering of past, future, and present is cut off."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with cutting off suffering, so too with reaching the end of suffering, exhausting suffering, stilling suffering, disappearing of suffering, crossing the flood of suffering, gaining liberation from bondage, destroying forms of existence, and exhausting all outflows of past, future, and present, it is likewise stated.

---

### (661)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are two kinds of power. What are the two? They are the power of reflection and the power of cultivation. What is the power of reflection? A noble disciple, in an empty forest or under a tree, reflects in this way: 'Bodily misconduct brings evil retribution in the present life and in future lives. If I engage in bodily misconduct, I would reproach myself, others would also reproach me, the great Teacher would also reproach me, and virtuous companions in the holy life would also reproach me, censuring me according to the Dharma. An evil reputation would spread, and at the breaking up of the body after death, I would be born in a bad destination, in hell.' Thus considering present and future retribution, bodily misconduct is abandoned, and good bodily conduct is cultivated. As with bodily misconduct, so too with verbal and mental misconduct, it is likewise stated. This is called the power of reflection. What is the power of cultivation? If a bhikṣu trains in the power of reflection, and a noble disciple accomplishes the power of reflection, he accordingly gains the power of cultivation. Having gained the power of cultivation, the power of cultivation is fulfilled."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (662)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "When a noble disciple trains and accomplishes the power of reflection, greed, hatred, and delusion are diminished or exhausted. Thus the noble disciple, relying upon the power of reflection, established in the power of reflection, accordingly gains the power of cultivation. Having gained the power of cultivation, the power of cultivation is fulfilled."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (663)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "What is the power of cultivation? It is the cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the four foundations of mindfulness, so too with the cultivation of the four right efforts, the four bases of supernormal power, the five faculties, the five powers, the seven factors of awakening, the eightfold noble path, the four paths, the four phrases of Dharma, and calm and insight, it is likewise stated.

---

### (664)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are three kinds of power. What are the three? They are the power of faith, the power of vigor, and the power of wisdom. Furthermore, three powers. What are the three? They are the power of faith, the power of mindfulness, and the power of wisdom. Furthermore, three powers. What are the three? They are the power of faith, the power of concentration, and the power of wisdom."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (665)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are three powers: the power of faith, the power of vigor, and the power of wisdom. Thus, bhikṣus, you should train in this way: 'I shall accomplish the power of faith, the power of vigor, and the power of wisdom.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the power of vigor, so too with the power of mindfulness and the power of concentration, it is likewise stated.

---

### (666)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are three powers: the power of faith, the power of mindfulness, and the power of wisdom. What is the power of faith? A noble disciple enters into pure faith toward the Tathāgata, firmly rooted and solidly established, which cannot be destroyed by devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, or any of the same order. This is called the power of faith. What is the power of vigor? It is the four right efforts. What is the power of wisdom? It is the Four Noble Truths."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

The remaining two sets of three powers are as stated above.

---

### (667)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four powers. What are the four? They are the power of faith, the power of vigor, the power of mindfulness, and the power of wisdom. Furthermore, four powers: the power of faith, the power of mindfulness, the power of concentration, and the power of wisdom. Furthermore, four powers: the power of discernment, the power of vigor, the power of blamelessness, and the power of gathering."

These sūtras are as stated above regarding the three powers. The difference is: "What is the power of discernment? Truly knowing wholesome and unwholesome dharmas, blameworthy and blameless dharmas, dharmas to be associated with and not to be associated with, inferior and superior dharmas, dark and bright dharmas, dharmas with distinctions and without distinctions, dharmas of dependent arising and dharmas not of dependent arising, as they truly are: this is called the power of discernment. What is the power of vigor? It is the four right efforts," as extensively explained before. "What is the power of blamelessness? It is blameless conduct of body, speech, and mind. This is called the power of blamelessness. What is the power of gathering? It is the four means of gathering others: generosity, kind speech, beneficial action, and cooperation."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (668)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "The most excellent form of generosity is the gift of Dharma. The most excellent form of kind speech is teaching the Dharma at the appropriate time to good men and women who delight in hearing it. The most excellent form of beneficial action is enabling those without faith to enter into faith and establishing them in faith, establishing those with morality in pure morality, those who are miserly in generosity, and those of wrong wisdom in right wisdom, enabling them to enter and become established. The most excellent form of cooperation is: an arahant cooperates with another as an arahant, a non-returner with a non-returner, a once-returner with a once-returner, a stream-enterer with a stream-enterer, and one of pure morality cooperates by bestowing pure morality upon the other."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (669)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "Whatever dharmas a community adopts, all of them are the four means of gathering. Some communities adopt generosity, some adopt kind speech, some adopt beneficial action, some adopt cooperation. In the past ages, past communities that had something to adopt also used the four means of gathering. Future communities that will have something to adopt will also use the four means of gathering. Some adopt generosity, some adopt kind speech, some adopt beneficial action, some adopt cooperation."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke this verse:

> "Generosity and kind speech,
> Or those who practice beneficial action,
> And cooperation in various deeds,
> Each according to what is appropriate.
> By these means the world is gathered,
> As a chariot moves by the hub.
> Were there no four means of gathering in the world,
> A mother's kindness and a child's support would be forgotten,
> Nor would there be reverence for a father and such elders,
> Or humble service rendered to them.
> Because there are the four means of gathering,
> And dharmas that follow in accord with them,
> Therefore there are great persons
> Whose virtue extends throughout the world."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (670)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four powers. What are the four? They are the power of discernment, the power of vigor, the power of blamelessness, and the power of gathering," as stated above. "If a bhikṣu accomplishes these four powers, he is freed from the five fears. What are the five? They are the fear of not having a livelihood, the fear of ill repute, the fear of assemblies, the fear of death, and the fear of bad destinies. These are called the five fears."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (671)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "A noble disciple who accomplishes these four powers should train in this way: 'I do not fear not having a livelihood. For what reason should I fear not having a livelihood? If one engages in impure conduct of body, impure conduct of speech, impure conduct of mind, practices wrong desires, is faithless, indolent, not vigorous, loses mindfulness, lacks concentration, is of wrong wisdom, is miserly, and does not gather others, then one should fear not having a livelihood. I possess four powers: the power of discernment, the power of vigor, the power of blamelessness, and the power of gathering. Because I have accomplished these four powers, I need not fear.' As with the fear of not having a livelihood, so too with the fear of ill repute, the fear of assemblies, the fear of death, and the fear of bad destinies, it is likewise stated as above."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (672)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four powers: the power of discernment, the power of vigor, the power of blamelessness, and the power of gathering. What is the power of discernment? It is wisdom, great wisdom, profound wisdom, and wisdom difficult to surpass. This is called the power of discernment. What is the power of vigor? Regarding unwholesome dharmas, unwholesome categories, dark-dark categories, blameworthy categories of the blameworthy, categories not to be associated with that should not be associated with, having separated from these dharmas, regarding whatever remaining wholesome dharmas, wholesome categories, bright-bright categories, blameless categories of the blameless, categories to be associated with that should be associated with, one cultivates and trains in them with supreme vigor, desire, effort, capability, right mindfulness, and right awareness. This is called the power of vigor. The power of blamelessness and the power of gathering are as stated in the above sūtra."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (673)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five powers. What are the five? The power of faith, the power of vigor, the power of mindfulness, the power of concentration, and the power of wisdom."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (674)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "Bhikṣus, you should train in this way: 'I shall diligently strive to accomplish the power of faith, the power of vigor, the power of mindfulness, the power of concentration, and the power of wisdom.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (675)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "The power of faith: one should know it as the four kinds of unshakable purity. The power of vigor: one should know it as the four right efforts. The power of mindfulness: one should know it as the four foundations of mindfulness. The power of concentration: one should know it as the four dhyānas. The power of wisdom: one should know it as the Four Noble Truths."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (676)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train in this way: 'I shall accomplish the power of faith, the power of vigor, the power of mindfulness, the power of concentration, and the power of wisdom.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (677)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five training powers. What are the five? The power of faith is a training power, the power of vigor is a training power, the power of moral shame is a training power, the power of moral dread is a training power, and the power of wisdom is a training power."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (678)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "Bhikṣus, you should train in this way: 'I shall accomplish the power of faith as a training power, accomplish the power of vigor as a training power, accomplish the power of moral shame as a training power, accomplish the power of moral dread as a training power, and accomplish the power of wisdom as a training power.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (679)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "What is the power of faith as a training power? Entering into well-established faith toward the Tathāgata, firmly rooted and solidly established, which cannot be destroyed by devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, or any others of the same order. What is the power of vigor as a training power? It is the four right efforts," as extensively explained before. "What is the power of moral shame as a training power? It is feeling shame, being ashamed of giving rise to evil and unwholesome dharmas, those counted among the defilements, receiving the blazing painful retribution of various existences, and in future lives the suffering of birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, pain, and distress. This is called the power of moral shame as a training power. What is the power of moral dread as a training power? Feeling dread toward what should be dreaded, dreading the arising of evil and unwholesome dharmas counted among the defilements, receiving the blazing painful retribution of various existences, and in future lives the suffering of birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, pain, and distress. This is called the power of moral dread as a training power. What is the power of wisdom as a training power? A noble disciple abides in wisdom, accomplished in wisdom regarding the arising and passing away of worldly phenomena, noble, leading to emergence and disenchantment, definitively making a complete end of suffering. This is called the power of wisdom as a training power."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (680)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train in this way: 'I shall accomplish the power of faith as a training power, the power of vigor, the power of moral shame, the power of moral dread, and the power of wisdom as training powers.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (681)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu changes, regresses, or does not long abide with regard to wholesome dharmas, others should reprove him with five kinds of wholesome principles. What are the five? They say: 'You did not enter into wholesome dharmas through faith. If one relies on faith, one is able to abandon unwholesome dharmas and cultivate wholesome dharmas. You lacked vigor, lacked moral shame, lacked moral dread, and lacked wisdom in entering into wholesome dharmas. If one relies on wisdom, one is able to abandon unwholesome dharmas and cultivate wholesome dharmas.'

"If a bhikṣu does not change, does not regress, and long abides in the true Dharma, others should congratulate and encourage him with five kinds of wholesome principles. What are the five? Right faith enters into wholesome dharmas; if one relies on faith, one abandons unwholesome dharmas and cultivates wholesome dharmas. Vigor, moral shame, moral dread, and wisdom enter into wholesome dharmas; if one relies on wisdom, one abandons unwholesome dharmas and cultivates wholesome dharmas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (682)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu returns the precepts or regresses from the precepts, others should reprove him with five kinds of wholesome principles. What are the five? 'This bhikṣu did not enter into wholesome dharmas through faith. If one relies on faith, one abandons unwholesome dharmas and cultivates wholesome dharmas. He did not enter into wholesome dharmas through vigor, moral shame, moral dread, or wisdom. If one relies on wisdom, one abandons unwholesome dharmas and cultivates wholesome dharmas.' If a bhikṣu, for the duration of his life, purely, completely, and perfectly practices the holy life in purity, others should congratulate and encourage him with five kinds of wholesome principles," as stated above.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (683)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu wishes that evil and unwholesome dharmas not arise, it is only through possessing faith in wholesome dharmas. If faith retreats and disappears and faithlessness permanently abides, then unwholesome dharmas arise. Likewise, wishing that evil and unwholesome dharmas not arise, it is only through possessing vigor, moral shame, moral dread, and wisdom. If vigor, moral shame, moral dread, and the power of wisdom retreat and disappear, and wrong wisdom permanently abides, then evil and unwholesome dharmas arise. If a bhikṣu relies on faith, he abandons unwholesome dharmas and cultivates wholesome dharmas. If he relies on vigor, moral shame, moral dread, and wisdom, he abandons unwholesome dharmas and cultivates wholesome dharmas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (684)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu generates disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, non-arising, and liberation with regard to form, he is called an Arahant, a Perfectly Awakened One. With regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, it is likewise stated. If furthermore a bhikṣu generates disenchantment, dispassion, non-arising, and liberation with regard to form, he is called an arahant liberated through wisdom. With regard to feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, it is likewise stated. Bhikṣus, what are the various kinds of differences between a Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One, and an arahant liberated through wisdom?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. We wish that you would explain this. The bhikṣus, having heard, will accept and practice it."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully and consider it well. I shall explain it to you. The Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One is one who, regarding dharmas not previously heard, was able to awaken by himself, knowing for himself in the present life, attaining the three kinds of bodhi. In the future he is able to teach the true Dharma, awakening the śrāvakas, namely: the four foundations of mindfulness, the four right efforts, the four bases of supernormal power, the five faculties, the five powers, the seven factors of awakening, and the eightfold noble path. This is called the Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One. What had not been attained he was able to attain; the holy life not yet established he was able to establish. He was able to skillfully know the path and skillfully teach the path, being a guide for the multitude. Thereafter the śrāvakas, accomplished in following the Dharma, following the path, delight in practicing the instructions and admonitions of the great Teacher, excelling in the true Dharma. This is called the various kinds of differences between the Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One, and the arahant liberated through wisdom.

"Furthermore, there are five training powers and ten Tathāgata powers. What are the training powers? They are the power of faith, the power of vigor, the power of mindfulness, the power of concentration, and the power of wisdom. What are the ten Tathāgata powers? The Tathāgata truly knows what is possible and what is impossible as it really is. This is called the first power of the Tathāgata. If this power is accomplished, the Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One attains the supreme place of knowledge of previous Buddhas, is able to turn the Brahma-wheel, and is able to roar the lion's roar in the great assembly.

"Furthermore, the Tathāgata truly knows the karmic actions of past, future, and present, their causes, conditions, and retribution as they really are. This is called the second Tathāgata power. The Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One, having accomplished this power, attains the supreme place of knowledge of previous Buddhas, is able to turn the Brahma-wheel, and roars the lion's roar in the great assembly.

"Furthermore, the Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One truly knows the dhyānas, liberations, samādhis, and attainments, the defiled, the evil, the purified, and the cleansed as they really are. This is called the third power of the Tathāgata. If this power is accomplished, the Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One attains the supreme place of knowledge of previous Buddhas, is able to turn the Brahma-wheel, and roars the lion's roar in the great assembly.

"Furthermore, the Tathāgata knows the various differences among the faculties of beings as they really are. This is called the fourth power of the Tathāgata. If this power is accomplished, the Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One attains the supreme place of knowledge of previous Buddhas, is able to turn the Brahma-wheel, and roars the lion's roar in the great assembly.

"Furthermore, the Tathāgata fully knows the various dispositions and inclinations of beings as they really are. This is called the fifth Tathāgata power. If this power is accomplished, the Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One attains the supreme place of knowledge of previous Buddhas, is able to turn the Brahma-wheel, and roars the lion's roar in the great assembly.

"Furthermore, the Tathāgata fully knows the various different elements of beings in the world as they really are. This is called the sixth Tathāgata power. If this power is accomplished, the Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One attains the supreme place of knowledge of previous Buddhas, is able to turn the Brahma-wheel, and roars the lion's roar in the great assembly.

"Furthermore, the Tathāgata truly knows all paths that lead to all destinations as they really are. This is called the seventh Tathāgata power. If this power is accomplished, the Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One attains the supreme place of knowledge of previous Buddhas, is able to turn the Brahma-wheel, and roars the lion's roar in the great assembly.

"Furthermore, the Tathāgata recollects various matters of past lives: 'From one life to hundreds and thousands of lives, from one eon to hundreds and thousands of eons. At that time, in that existence, I was of such a clan, such a family name, such a personal name, with such food, experiencing such suffering and happiness, with such a lifespan, abiding for such a duration, with such a span of life. I died in that place and was born in this place; I was born in that place and died in this place. With such conduct, such causes, such means.' All the past lives experienced are fully known as they really are. This is called the eighth Tathāgata power. If this power is accomplished, the Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One attains the supreme place of knowledge of previous Buddhas, is able to turn the Brahma-wheel, and roars the lion's roar in the great assembly.

"Furthermore, the Tathāgata, with the divine eye that is purified and surpasses the human eye, sees beings as they die and are reborn, with beautiful form or ugly form, in low states or high states, heading toward bad destinies or heading toward good destinies, faring according to their karma, all fully known as it really is: 'These beings, accomplished in evil conduct of body, evil conduct of speech and mind, who revile the noble ones, who hold wrong views and act according to wrong view, because of these causes and conditions, at the breaking up of the body after death, fall into bad destinies and are born in hell. These beings, accomplished in good conduct of body, good conduct of speech and mind, who do not revile the noble ones, who hold right views and act according to right view, because of those causes and conditions, at the breaking up of the body after death, are born in good destinies, in the heavenly realms.' All is fully known as it really is. This is called the ninth Tathāgata power. If this power is accomplished, the Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One attains the supreme place of knowledge of previous Buddhas, is able to turn the Brahma-wheel, and roars the lion's roar in the great assembly.

"Furthermore, the Tathāgata's outflows are fully exhausted; the outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, in the present life knowing for himself and realizing through direct experience: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done is done, I know for myself that there is no further existence.' This is called the tenth Tathāgata power. If this power is accomplished, the Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One attains the supreme place of knowledge of previous Buddhas, is able to turn the Brahma-wheel, and roars the lion's roar in the great assembly.

"These ten powers are accomplished only by the Tathāgata. This is called the various kinds of differences between the Tathāgata and the śrāvakas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (685)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose an infant is born to parents who then entrust the child to a nursemaid. The nursemaid regularly wipes the child, regularly bathes the child, regularly nurses the child, and regularly looks after the child's health. If the nursemaid is not careful, the child might put grass, dirt, or various unclean things into its mouth. The nursemaid should immediately instruct the child to remove them. If the child is able to remove them at the time, that is good. If the child cannot remove them by itself, the nursemaid should hold the child's head with her left hand and with her right hand extract what is stuck. Although the child may suffer at the time, the nursemaid must painfully extract what is stuck, wishing for the long-term well-being and happiness of the child."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "When the infant has grown up and has the ability to distinguish things, would it still put grass, dirt, or unclean things into its mouth?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One. When the infant has grown up and has the ability to distinguish, it would not even touch unclean things with its feet, let alone put them in its mouth."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "When the infant is small, the nursemaid continually looks after and tends to its health. When it has grown up and wisdom is accomplished, the nursemaid lets go and does not diligently tend to it, because having grown up, it does not become heedless on its own. In the same way, bhikṣus, when śrāvakas are beginning to train and their wisdom is not yet sufficient, the Tathāgata teaches and instructs them with the Dharma, tending to them according to the time. When they have trained for a long time and their wisdom is deeply established, the Tathāgata lets go and does not continually and earnestly teach and instruct them, because their wisdom is accomplished and they are not heedless. Therefore, śrāvakas have five training powers, and the Tathāgata accomplishes ten powers of knowledge," as extensively explained above.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (686)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "The Tathāgata has six kinds of power. If these six kinds of power are accomplished, the Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Awakened One attains the supreme place of knowledge of previous Buddhas, is able to turn the Brahma-wheel, and roars the lion's roar in the great assembly. That is, truly knowing what is possible and what is impossible as it really is: this is the first power of the Tathāgata."

"Furthermore, truly knowing past, future, and present mental dispositions and the dharmas they receive as they really are," as extensively explained above, "this is called the second Tathāgata power."

"Furthermore, the Tathāgata truly knows the dhyānas, liberations, samādhis, and attainments as they really are," as extensively explained above, "this is called the third Tathāgata power."

"Furthermore, the Tathāgata truly knows the various past-life events as they really are," as extensively explained above, "this is called the fourth Tathāgata power."

"Furthermore, the Tathāgata's divine eye, purified and surpassing the human eye, sees the beings dying here and being born there," as extensively explained above, "this is called the fifth Tathāgata power."

"Furthermore, the Tathāgata's defilements and outflows are exhausted; the outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom," as extensively explained above, up to "roars the lion's roar in the great assembly. This is called the sixth Tathāgata power."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (687)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "If someone comes and asks me about the Tathāgata's power of knowledge of what is possible and what is impossible, in accord with what the Tathāgata's power of knowledge of what is possible and impossible has known, seen, and realized through perfect awakening, I explain it to them.

"If someone comes and asks about the Tathāgata's power of knowledge regarding one's own experience of karmic retribution, in accord with what the Tathāgata's power of knowledge regarding one's own experience of karmic retribution has known, seen, and realized through perfect awakening, I explain it to them. This is called the second Tathāgata power of knowledge.

"If someone comes and asks about the Tathāgata's power of knowledge of dhyānas, liberations, samādhis, and attainments, in accord with the Tathāgata's dhyānas, liberations, samādhis, and attainments, I explain it to them.

"If someone comes and asks about the power of knowledge of past lives, in accord with what the Tathāgata's past-life knowledge has known, seen, and realized, I explain it to them.

"If someone comes and asks about the Tathāgata's power of knowledge of the divine eye, in accord with what the Tathāgata's divine eye has seen, I explain it to them.

"If someone comes and asks about the Tathāgata's power of knowledge of the exhaustion of outflows, in accord with what the Tathāgata's power of knowledge of the exhaustion of outflows has known, seen, and realized, I explain it to them."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (688)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are seven powers. What are the seven? The power of faith, the power of vigor, the power of moral shame, the power of moral dread, the power of mindfulness, the power of concentration, and the power of wisdom."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke this verse:

> "The power of faith, the power of vigor,
> The power of moral shame and moral dread,
> The power of right mindfulness, concentration, and wisdom:
> These are called the seven powers.
> One who accomplishes the seven powers
> Is able to exhaust all outflows."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (689)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are seven powers," as stated above. The difference is: "Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train in this way: 'I shall accomplish the power of faith.' Likewise the power of vigor, the power of moral shame, the power of moral dread, the power of mindfulness, the power of concentration, and the power of wisdom should also be trained in."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (690)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are seven powers," as stated above. The difference is, at that time, the World-Honored One then spoke this verse:

> "The power of faith, the power of vigor,
> And the powers of moral shame and moral dread,
> The power of mindfulness, concentration, and wisdom:
> These are called the seven powers.
> One who accomplishes the seven powers
> Swiftly severs all outflows."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (691)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are seven powers. What are the seven? The power of faith, the power of vigor, the power of moral shame, the power of moral dread, the power of mindfulness, the power of concentration, and the power of wisdom. What is the power of faith? Arising deep faith toward the Tathāgata, firmly rooted and solidly established, which cannot be destroyed by devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, or any others of the same order. This is called the power of faith. What is the power of vigor? It is the four right efforts," as extensively explained above. "What is the power of moral shame? Feeling shame toward evil and unwholesome dharmas," as stated above. "What is the power of moral dread? Feeling dread toward what should be dreaded, dreading the arising of evil and unwholesome dharmas," as stated above. "What is the power of mindfulness? It is the four foundations of mindfulness," as stated above. "What is the power of concentration? It is the four dhyānas," as stated above. "What is the power of wisdom? It is the Four Noble Truths," as stated above.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (692)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are eight powers. What are the eight? They are: the power of a sovereign king, the power of a minister who adjudicates, the power of a resentful woman, the power of a crying infant, the power of a slandering fool, the power of a discerning wise person, the power of a patient renunciant,
