# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Twenty-Seven

---

### (712)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa, as described above. The difference is: "There are certain śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who hold such views and make such statements: 'Without cause and without condition, beings are without knowledge and without vision; without cause and without condition, beings have knowledge and vision.'"

Thus extensively explained, up to Prince Abhaya hearing what the Buddha had spoken, rejoicing and expressing approval, bowing at the Buddha's feet, and departing.

---

### (713)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a great number of bhikṣus, in the early morning, put on their robes and took their bowls to enter Śrāvastī city to beg for food. Then the many bhikṣus had this thought: "It is still too early today; the time for begging food has not yet come. Let us first visit the hermitage of the non-Buddhist wanderers."

The many bhikṣus then entered the hermitage of the non-Buddhist wanderers. Together with the various non-Buddhist wanderers, they exchanged greetings and courtesies. After exchanging greetings and courtesies, they sat to one side. The non-Buddhist wanderers asked the bhikṣus, saying: "The śramaṇa Gautama teaches his disciples the Dharma: to cut off the five hindrances that cover the mind and weaken the power of wisdom, which are obstructive factors that do not lead to nirvāṇa; to abide in the four foundations of mindfulness; and to cultivate the seven factors of awakening. We also teach our disciples to cut off the five hindrances that cover the mind and weaken the power of wisdom, to well abide in the four foundations of mindfulness, and to cultivate the seven factors of awakening. What difference is there between us and the śramaṇa Gautama? We both teach the Dharma."

At that time, the many bhikṣus, hearing what the non-Buddhist wanderers said, were not pleased in their hearts. They rebuked them, rose from their seats, and departed. They entered Śrāvastī city, begged for food, and having finished, returned to the monastery. They put away their robes and bowls, washed their feet, and went to where the Buddha was. They bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and reported in full to the World-Honored One what the non-Buddhist wanderers had said.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the many bhikṣus: "When those non-Buddhist wanderers make such statements, you should counter-question them, saying: 'Non-Buddhist wanderers! The five hindrances, by category, should number ten. The seven factors of awakening, by category, should number fourteen. What are the ten of the five hindrances and the fourteen of the seven factors of awakening?' If questioned thus, those non-Buddhist wanderers would be startled and scattered. They would speak of various non-Buddhist doctrines, with anger, arrogance, disparagement, resentment, and intolerance arising in their minds; or they would be silent with heads bowed, losing their eloquence and pondering deeply. Why is this? I do not see anyone among devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, or the assemblies of devas and humans who could hear what I have said and joyfully accord with it, except for the Tathāgata and the assembly of his voice-hearers who hear this.

"Bhikṣus! What are the ten of the five hindrances? There is internal sensual desire and there is external sensual desire. That internal sensual desire is a hindrance; it is not wisdom, not equal awakening, and does not lead toward nirvāṇa. That external sensual desire is a hindrance; it is not wisdom, not equal awakening, and does not lead toward nirvāṇa. As for ill-will, there is ill-will and the characteristics of ill-will. Both the ill-will and the characteristics of ill-will are a hindrance; they are not wisdom, not equal awakening, and do not lead toward nirvāṇa. There is sloth and there is torpor. That sloth and that torpor are a hindrance; they are not wisdom, not equal awakening, and do not lead toward nirvāṇa. There is restlessness and there is remorse. That restlessness and that remorse are a hindrance; they are not wisdom, not equal awakening, and do not lead toward nirvāṇa. There is doubt regarding wholesome dharmas and doubt regarding unwholesome dharmas. That doubt regarding wholesome dharmas and doubt regarding unwholesome dharmas are a hindrance; they are not wisdom, not equal awakening, and do not lead toward nirvāṇa. This is called the five hindrances explained as ten.

"What are the seven factors of awakening explained as fourteen? There is mindfulness of the mind established on internal dharmas, and there is mindfulness of the mind established on external dharmas. That internal dharma mindfulness is the mindfulness factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa. That external dharma mindfulness is the mindfulness factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa.

"There is investigation of wholesome dharmas and investigation of unwholesome dharmas. That investigation of wholesome dharmas is the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa. That investigation of unwholesome dharmas is the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa.

"There is energy for abandoning unwholesome dharmas, and there is energy for nurturing wholesome dharmas. That energy for abandoning unwholesome dharmas is the energy factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa. That energy for nurturing wholesome dharmas is the energy factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa.

"There is joy, and there is the basis of joy. That joy is the joy factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa. That basis of joy is also the joy factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa.

"There is tranquility of body, and there is tranquility of mind. That tranquility of body is the tranquility factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa. That tranquility of mind is the tranquility factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa.

"There is concentration, and there is the characteristic of concentration. That concentration is the concentration factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa. That characteristic of concentration is the concentration factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa.

"There is equanimity regarding wholesome dharmas, and there is equanimity regarding unwholesome dharmas. That equanimity regarding wholesome dharmas is the equanimity factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa. That equanimity regarding unwholesome dharmas is the equanimity factor of awakening; it is wisdom, it is equal awakening, and it can lead toward nirvāṇa. This is called the seven factors of awakening explained as fourteen."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the many bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (714)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there were many bhikṣus, as described above. The difference is: "If there are non-Buddhist wanderers who make such statements, you should further question them, saying: 'When the mind is sluggish and hesitant, at that time which factors of awakening should be cultivated? And which are not the right time to cultivate? When the mind is restless and agitated with hesitancy, at that time which factors of awakening should be cultivated? And which are not the right time?' If questioned thus, those non-Buddhist wanderers would be startled and scattered in their minds. They would speak of various other doctrines, with anger, arrogance, disparagement, resentment, and intolerance arising in their minds; or they would be silent with heads bowed, losing their eloquence and pondering deeply. Why is this? I do not see anyone among devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, or the assemblies of devas and humans who could hear what I have said and joyfully approve, except for the Tathāgata and the assembly of his voice-hearers who hear this.

"Bhikṣus! If at that time the mind is sluggish and the mind is hesitant, one should not cultivate the tranquility factor of awakening, the concentration factor of awakening, or the equanimity factor of awakening. Why is this? When a sluggish mind has arisen, when there is sluggish hesitancy, these dharmas would increase that sluggishness. It is like a small fire: if one wishes to make it blaze and adds damp charcoal to it, what do you think, bhikṣus? Would not adding charcoal extinguish the fire?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One!"

"Just so, bhikṣus. When there is sluggish hesitancy, if one cultivates the tranquility factor of awakening, the concentration factor of awakening, or the equanimity factor of awakening, this is not the right time, for it increases indolence. When a restless mind has arisen, when there is restless hesitancy, at that time one should not cultivate the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening, the energy factor of awakening, or the joy factor of awakening. Why is this? When a restless mind has arisen, when there is restless hesitancy, these dharmas can cause it to increase. It is like a blazing fire: if one wishes to extinguish it and adds dry firewood to it, what do you think? Would this not cause the fire to blaze even more fiercely?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Just so. When a restless mind has arisen, when there is restless hesitancy, cultivating the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening, the energy factor of awakening, or the joy factor of awakening increases that restlessness. Bhikṣus! When a sluggish mind has arisen, when there is sluggish hesitancy, at that time one should cultivate the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening, the energy factor of awakening, and the joy factor of awakening. Why is this? When a sluggish mind has arisen, when there is sluggish hesitancy, these dharmas can instruct, teach, illuminate, and gladden. It is like a small fire: if one wishes to make it blaze and adds dry firewood to it, what do you think, bhikṣus? Would this fire not blaze fiercely?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Just so. When a sluggish mind has arisen, when there is sluggish hesitancy, at that time one should cultivate the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening, the energy factor of awakening, and the joy factor of awakening, to instruct, teach, illuminate, and gladden. When a restless mind has arisen, when there is restless hesitancy, one should cultivate the tranquility factor of awakening, the concentration factor of awakening, and the equanimity factor of awakening. Why is this? When a restless mind has arisen, when there is restless hesitancy, these dharmas can cause the mind to settle inwardly, unified and collected. It is like a burning fire: if one wishes to extinguish it and adds damp charcoal, that fire is then extinguished. Just so, bhikṣus, when there is restless hesitancy, cultivating the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening, the energy factor, and the joy factor is not the right time; cultivating the tranquility, concentration, and equanimity factors of awakening is indeed the right time, for these dharmas cause the mind to settle inwardly, unified and collected. As for the mindfulness factor of awakening, it supports all of them."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (715)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are the five hindrances and the seven factors of awakening, each having nutriment and non-nutriment. I will now explain this. Listen carefully and reflect well; I will explain it for you. Just as the body depends on food to be sustained and not on the absence of food, so too the five hindrances depend on nutriment to be sustained and not on the absence of nutriment. What is the nutriment of the sensual desire hindrance? It is the characteristic of contact. Through unwise attention to that, sensual desire that has not yet arisen is caused to arise, and sensual desire that has already arisen is caused to increase and expand. This is called the nutriment of the sensual desire hindrance.

"What is the nutriment of the ill-will hindrance? It is the characteristic of obstruction. Through unwise attention to that, the ill-will hindrance that has not yet arisen is caused to arise, and the ill-will hindrance that has already arisen is caused to increase and expand. This is called the nutriment of the ill-will hindrance.

"What is the nutriment of the sloth-and-torpor hindrance? There are five things. What are the five? Feebleness, lack of enthusiasm, yawning, excessive eating, and laziness. Through unwise attention to these, the sloth-and-torpor hindrance that has not yet arisen is caused to arise, and the sloth-and-torpor hindrance that has already arisen is caused to increase and expand. This is called the nutriment of the sloth-and-torpor hindrance.

"What is the nutriment of the restlessness-and-remorse hindrance? There are four things. What are the four? Namely, thoughts about relatives, thoughts about people, thoughts about devas, and thoughts about pleasures formerly experienced. Recollecting them oneself, or being reminded by others, these thoughts arise. Through unwise attention to these, restlessness-and-remorse that has not yet arisen is caused to arise, and restlessness-and-remorse that has already arisen is caused to increase and expand. This is called the nutriment of the restlessness-and-remorse hindrance.

"What is the nutriment of the doubt hindrance? There are three periods of time. What are the three? The past, the future, and the present. Having uncertainty about the past, uncertainty about the future, and uncertainty about the present, through unwise attention to these, the doubt hindrance that has not yet arisen is caused to arise, and the doubt hindrance that has already arisen is caused to increase and expand. This is called the nutriment of the doubt hindrance.

"Just as the body depends on food to grow and be nourished, and not on the absence of food, so too the seven factors of awakening depend on nutriment to abide, depend on nutriment to grow and be nourished, and not on the absence of nutriment.

"What is the non-nutriment of the mindfulness factor of awakening? Not giving attention to the four foundations of mindfulness; the mindfulness factor of awakening that has not yet arisen does not arise, and the mindfulness factor of awakening that has already arisen declines. This is called the non-nutriment of the mindfulness factor of awakening.

"What is the non-nutriment of the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening? Not giving attention to the investigation of wholesome dharmas and the investigation of unwholesome dharmas; the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening that has not yet arisen does not arise, and the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening that has already arisen declines. This is called the non-nutriment of the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening.

"What is the non-nutriment of the energy factor of awakening? Not giving attention to the four right efforts; the energy factor of awakening that has not yet arisen does not arise, and the energy factor of awakening that has already arisen declines. This is called the non-nutriment of the energy factor of awakening.

"What is the non-nutriment of the joy factor of awakening? There is joy and the basis of joy; not giving attention to these, the joy factor of awakening that has not yet arisen does not arise, and the joy factor of awakening that has already arisen declines. This is called the non-nutriment of the joy factor of awakening.

"What is the non-nutriment of the tranquility factor of awakening? There is tranquility of body and tranquility of mind; not giving attention to these, the tranquility factor of awakening that has not yet arisen does not arise, and the tranquility factor of awakening that has already arisen declines. This is called the non-nutriment of the tranquility factor of awakening.

"What is the non-nutriment of the concentration factor of awakening? There are the four dhyānas; not giving attention to these, the concentration factor of awakening that has not yet arisen does not arise, and the concentration factor of awakening that has already arisen declines. This is called the non-nutriment of the concentration factor of awakening.

"What is the non-nutriment of the equanimity factor of awakening? There are three elements, namely the element of cessation, the element of dispassion, and the element of extinction. Not giving attention to these, the equanimity factor of awakening that has not yet arisen does not arise, and the equanimity factor of awakening that has already arisen declines. This is called the non-nutriment of the equanimity factor of awakening.

"What is the non-nutriment of the sensual desire hindrance? It is contemplation of impurity. By giving attention to that, the sensual desire hindrance that has not yet arisen does not arise, and the sensual desire hindrance that has already arisen is cut off. This is called the non-nutriment of the sensual desire hindrance.

"What is the non-nutriment of the ill-will hindrance? It is giving attention to loving-kindness. The ill-will hindrance that has not yet arisen does not arise, and the ill-will hindrance that has already arisen is extinguished. This is called the non-nutriment of the ill-will hindrance.

"What is the non-nutriment of the sloth-and-torpor hindrance? It is giving attention to luminous awareness. The sloth-and-torpor hindrance that has not yet arisen does not arise, and the sloth-and-torpor hindrance that has already arisen is extinguished. This is called the non-nutriment of the sloth-and-torpor hindrance.

"What is the non-nutriment of the restlessness-and-remorse hindrance? It is giving attention to inner stillness. The restlessness-and-remorse hindrance that has not yet arisen does not arise, and the restlessness-and-remorse hindrance that has already arisen is extinguished. This is called the non-nutriment of the restlessness-and-remorse hindrance.

"What is the non-nutriment of the doubt hindrance? It is giving attention to dependent arising. The doubt hindrance that has not yet arisen does not arise, and the doubt hindrance that has already arisen is extinguished. This is called the non-nutriment of the doubt hindrance.

"Just as the body depends on food to abide and depends on food to be sustained, so too the seven factors of awakening depend on nutriment to abide and depend on nutriment to be sustained.

"What is the nutriment of the mindfulness factor of awakening? By giving attention to the four foundations of mindfulness, the mindfulness factor of awakening that has not yet arisen is caused to arise, and the mindfulness factor of awakening that has already arisen is further developed and caused to increase and expand. This is called the nutriment of the mindfulness factor of awakening.

"What is the nutriment of the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening? There is investigation of wholesome dharmas and investigation of unwholesome dharmas. By giving attention to these, the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening that has not yet arisen is caused to arise, and the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening that has already arisen is further developed and caused to increase and expand. This is called the nutriment of the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening.

"What is the nutriment of the energy factor of awakening? By giving attention to the four right efforts, the energy factor of awakening that has not yet arisen is caused to arise, and the energy factor of awakening that has already arisen is further developed and caused to increase and expand. This is called the nutriment of the energy factor of awakening.

"What is the nutriment of the joy factor of awakening? There is joy and the basis of joy. By giving attention to these, the joy factor of awakening that has not yet arisen is caused to arise, and the joy factor of awakening that has already arisen is further developed and caused to increase and expand. This is called the nutriment of the joy factor of awakening.

"What is the nutriment of the tranquility factor of awakening? There is tranquility of body and tranquility of mind. By giving attention to these, the tranquility factor of awakening that has not yet arisen is caused to arise, and the tranquility factor of awakening that has already arisen is further developed and caused to increase and expand. This is called the nutriment of the tranquility factor of awakening.

"What is the nutriment of the concentration factor of awakening? There are the four dhyānas. By giving attention to these, the concentration factor of awakening that has not yet arisen is caused to arise, and the concentration factor of awakening that has already arisen is further developed and caused to increase and expand. This is called the nutriment of the concentration factor of awakening.

"What is the nutriment of the equanimity factor of awakening? There are three elements. What are the three? The element of cessation, the element of dispassion, and the element of extinction. By giving attention to these, the equanimity factor of awakening that has not yet arisen is caused to arise, and the equanimity factor of awakening that has already arisen is further developed and caused to increase and expand. This is called the nutriment of the equanimity factor of awakening."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (716)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Among internal dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that causes unwholesome dharmas that have not yet arisen to arise, causes unwholesome dharmas that have already arisen to further arise and increase, causes wholesome dharmas that have not yet arisen to not arise, and causes wholesome dharmas that have already arisen to decline, as does unwise attention. Bhikṣus! Unwise attention causes the sensual desire hindrance that has not yet arisen to arise, and that which has already arisen to further arise and increase. It causes the ill-will, sloth-and-torpor, restlessness-and-remorse, and doubt hindrances that have not yet arisen to arise, and those which have already arisen to further arise and increase. It causes the mindfulness factor of awakening that has not yet arisen to not arise, and that which has already arisen to decline. It causes the investigation-of-dharmas, energy, joy, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity factors of awakening that have not yet arisen to not arise, and those which have already arisen to decline.

"I do not see a single dharma that can cause unwholesome dharmas that have not yet arisen to not arise, causes those which have already arisen to be cut off, causes wholesome dharmas that have not yet arisen to arise, and causes those which have already arisen to further arise and increase, as does wise attention. Bhikṣus! Wise attention causes the sensual desire hindrance that has not yet arisen to not arise, and that which has already arisen to be cut off. It causes the ill-will, sloth-and-torpor, restlessness-and-remorse, and doubt hindrances that have not yet arisen to not arise, and those which have already arisen to be cut off. It causes the mindfulness factor of awakening that has not yet arisen to arise, and that which has already arisen to further arise and increase. It causes the investigation-of-dharmas, energy, joy, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity factors of awakening that have not yet arisen to arise, and those which have already arisen to further arise and increase."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (717)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Among external dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that causes unwholesome dharmas that have not yet arisen to arise, causes those which have already arisen to further arise and increase, causes wholesome dharmas that have not yet arisen to not arise, and causes those which have already arisen to decline, like bad friends, bad companions, and bad associates. Bad friends, bad companions, and bad associates cause the sensual desire hindrance that has not yet arisen to arise, and that which has already arisen to further arise and increase. They cause the ill-will, sloth-and-torpor, restlessness-and-remorse, and doubt hindrances that have not yet arisen to arise, and those which have already arisen to further arise and increase. They cause the mindfulness factor of awakening that has not yet arisen to not arise, and that which has already arisen to decline. They cause the investigation-of-dharmas, energy, joy, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity factors of awakening that have not yet arisen to not arise, and those which have already arisen to decline.

"Bhikṣus! I do not see a single dharma that causes unwholesome dharmas that have not yet arisen to not arise, causes those which have already arisen to be cut off, causes wholesome dharmas that have not yet arisen to arise, and causes those which have already arisen to further arise and increase, like good friends, good companions, and good associates. Good friends, good companions, and good associates cause the sensual desire hindrance that has not yet arisen to not arise, and that which has already arisen to be cut off. They cause the ill-will, sloth-and-torpor, restlessness-and-remorse, and doubt hindrances that have not yet arisen to not arise, and those which have already arisen to be cut off. They cause the mindfulness factor of awakening that has not yet arisen to arise, and that which has already arisen to further arise and increase. They cause the investigation-of-dharmas, energy, joy, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity factors of awakening that have not yet arisen to arise, and those which have already arisen to further arise and increase."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (718)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra addressed the bhikṣus: "There are seven factors of awakening. What are the seven? The mindfulness factor of awakening, the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening, the energy factor of awakening, the joy factor of awakening, the tranquility factor of awakening, the concentration factor of awakening, and the equanimity factor of awakening. These seven factors of awakening I have definitively attained, attained without difficulty. I enter their correct attainment at will, whenever I wish. Whether in the morning, at midday, or in the evening, if I wish to enter correct attainment, I do so according to my wish, entering abundantly into correct attainment. It is like a king or great minister who has various garments placed in chests and boxes. Whatever is needed, whether needed at midday or needed in the evening, he takes them at will, freely. Just so, bhikṣus, these seven factors of awakening I have definitively attained, attained without difficulty, entering correct attainment according to my wish. My mindfulness factor of awakening is pure and bright. When it arises, I know it arises; when it ceases, I know it ceases; when it subsides, I know it subsides; when it has arisen, I know it has arisen; when it has ceased, I know it has ceased. Likewise for the investigation-of-dharmas, energy, joy, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity factors of awakening, they are also spoken of in the same way."

When the Venerable Śāriputra had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what he had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (719)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Pāṭaliputra. At that time, the Venerable Upavāṇa and the Venerable Adhimukta were staying at the Kukkuṭārāma monastery in Pāṭaliputra.

At that time, the Venerable Adhimukta, in the late afternoon, rose from meditation and went to the Venerable Upavāṇa. Having exchanged greetings and courtesies, he withdrew to sit to one side and asked the Venerable Upavāṇa: "Venerable one! Can one know the skillful means of the seven factors of awakening, such that one dwells in pleasant correct attainment in this way, and dwells in unpleasant correct attainment in that way?"

Upavāṇa answered, saying: "Venerable Adhimukta! A bhikṣu who skillfully knows the means of cultivating the seven factors of awakening can dwell in pleasant correct attainment in this way, and dwell in unpleasant correct attainment in that way."

He further asked: "How does a bhikṣu skillfully know the means of cultivating the seven factors of awakening?"

Upavāṇa answered, saying: "When a bhikṣu applies the means of cultivating the mindfulness factor of awakening, he first reflects: 'My mind is not well liberated; sloth-and-torpor is not overcome; restlessness-and-remorse is not well subdued; sloth-and-torpor is not overcome. When I contemplate the dharmas of this mindfulness factor, I apply energetic effort but do not attain balance.' Likewise for the investigation-of-dharmas, energy, joy, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity factors of awakening, they are also spoken of in the same way. If, when a bhikṣu applies the means of the mindfulness factor of awakening, he first reflects: 'My mind is well liberated; sloth-and-torpor is properly overcome; restlessness-and-remorse is subdued. When I contemplate the dharmas of this mindfulness factor, without applying much effort I attain balance.' Likewise for the other factors. Thus, Adhimukta, a bhikṣu knows the skillful means of cultivating the seven factors of awakening, and thus dwells in pleasant correct attainment and thus dwells in unpleasant correct attainment."

At that time, the two noble ones, having discussed this meaning, each rose from their seats and departed.

---

### (720)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, the Venerable Anuruddha was also staying at Śrāvastī in the Pine Forest Monastery.

At that time, a great number of bhikṣus went to the Venerable Anuruddha, exchanged greetings and courtesies, and after exchanging greetings and courtesies, withdrew to sit to one side. They said to the Venerable Anuruddha: "Does the Venerable One know the skillful means of cultivating the seven factors of awakening such that pleasant abiding arises?"

The Venerable Anuruddha said to the bhikṣus: "I know how a bhikṣu who skillfully applies the means of cultivating the seven factors of awakening gives rise to pleasant abiding."

The bhikṣus asked the Venerable Anuruddha: "How does one know that a bhikṣu who skillfully applies the means of cultivating the seven factors of awakening gives rise to pleasant abiding?"

The Venerable Anuruddha said to the bhikṣus: "When a bhikṣu applies the means of cultivating the mindfulness factor of awakening, he skillfully reflects: 'My mind is well liberated; sloth-and-torpor is well overcome; restlessness-and-remorse is well subdued.' Having contemplated the dharmas of this mindfulness factor, he applies diligent effort; his mind is not indolent. His body is tranquil and undisturbed. He focuses his mind and causes it to abide. He does not give rise to scattered thoughts. He dwells in one-pointed correct attainment. Likewise for the investigation-of-dharmas, energy, joy, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity factors of awakening, they are also spoken of in the same way. This is called knowing that a bhikṣu who skillfully applies the means of cultivating the seven factors of awakening gives rise to pleasant abiding."

At that time, the many bhikṣus, hearing what the Venerable Anuruddha had said, rejoiced and expressed approval, rose from their seats, and departed.

---

### (721)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "When a wheel-turning sage king appears in the world, seven treasures manifest in the world: the golden wheel treasure, the elephant treasure, the horse treasure, the divine jewel treasure, the jade woman treasure, the treasury minister treasure, and the general minister treasure. Just so, when the Tathāgata appears in the world, the seven treasures of the factors of awakening also manifest. On the fast day, upon a tower, surrounded by great ministers, a golden wheel treasure appears from the east. The wheel has a thousand spokes, with even hub and round rim, its wheel characteristics complete. With this auspicious sign, one knows, 'This must be the wheel-turning sage king.' He then determines, 'I am indeed the wheel-turning king.' Receiving the golden wheel treasure with both hands, placing it in his left hand, he turns it with his right hand and speaks these words: 'If this is the golden wheel treasure of the wheel-turning sage king, let it travel again along the ancient path of the wheel-turning sage kings.' Thereupon the wheel treasure sets forth, with the king's retinue following ahead, and proceeding through the eastern sky, it goes toward the east, traveling the straight and true path of the ancient sage kings. The king follows the wheel treasure, and the four-fold army also follows. Wherever the wheel treasure goes and stops, the king stops there, and the four-fold army also stops. The various small kings of the eastern regions, upon seeing the sage king come, all submit and surrender.

"When the Tathāgata appears in the world, the seven factors of awakening manifest in the world, namely: the mindfulness factor of awakening, the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening, the energy factor of awakening, the joy factor of awakening, the tranquility factor of awakening, the concentration factor of awakening, and the equanimity factor of awakening."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (722)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "When a wheel-turning sage king appears in the world, seven treasures manifest in the world. How does the golden wheel treasure manifest when a wheel-turning sage king appears in the world? At that time, a kṣatriya anointed king, on the fifteenth day of the month, having bathed and purified himself, observing the precepts and fast, upon a tower, surrounded by great ministers, beholds a golden wheel treasure appearing from the east. The wheel has a thousand spokes, with even hub and round rim, its wheel characteristics complete; it is a treasure of genuine celestial gold. There is an ancient tradition that when a kṣatriya anointed great king, on the fifteenth day observes the upoṣadha, having bathed and purified himself, maintaining merit and goodness, the wheel comes: 'Welcome, great king! This is your kingdom. This kingdom is peaceful and secure, the people prosperous and happy. May you remain here, teach and guide the people of this land, and I shall follow you.' The sage king replies: 'Chiefs of the settlements! You should simply govern the people well. If any are disobedient, come and report to me. I shall govern them according to the Dharma. Do not act contrary to the Dharma. Also cause the people of the land to well transform what is contrary to the Dharma. If you do so, you follow my rule.' Thereupon the sage king crosses the eastern sea, traveling along the ancient sage king's road, arriving at the southern sea. Reaching the southern sea, he crosses the southern sea and arrives at the western sea. Traveling along the ancient sage king's road, he crosses the western sea and arrives at the northern sea. The small kings of the southern, western, and northern regions come to welcome and request him, just as extensively described for the eastern direction. Thereupon the golden wheel treasure, followed by the sage king, crosses the northern sea and returns to the king's palace, the hall of correct governance, and abides in the sky above. This is the golden wheel treasure that manifests in the world when a wheel-turning sage king appears in the world.

"How does the white elephant treasure manifest in the world when a wheel-turning sage king appears in the world? There is a kṣatriya anointed great king who has a pure-colored elephant of excellent appearance, with seven limbs touching the ground. When the sage king sees it, his heart is gladdened. 'This treasure elephant has come for me!' He instructs the skilled elephant trainer to quickly train this treasure elephant and, once trained, to bring it to him. The elephant trainer receives the command, and within a single day the elephant is fully trained, complete with all the marks of a trained elephant, just like other elephants that take a year to train. This treasure elephant trained in one day is just the same. Once trained, it is brought to the king. The trainer reports to the great king: 'This elephant is now trained. May the king know the proper time.' At that time, the sage king inspects the elephant and, seeing that its training characteristics are complete, mounts the treasure elephant. In the early morning, he circles the four seas, and by midday returns to the royal palace. This is the elephant treasure that manifests in the world when a wheel-turning sage king appears in the world.

"What is the horse treasure that manifests in the world when a wheel-turning sage king appears in the world? The wheel-turning sage king possesses a horse treasure of pure dark-blue color, with a black head and glossy tail. When the sage king sees the horse, his heart is gladdened. 'This divine horse has come for me!' He entrusts it to the horse trainer, instructing him to quickly train it, and once trained, to bring it to him. The horse trainer receives the command, and within a single day the horse is trained, just like other horses that take a year to train. The horse treasure trained in this way is just the same. Knowing the horse is trained, the trainer brings it to the king and says: 'Great King! This horse is now trained.' At that time, the sage king inspects the treasure horse and, seeing that its training characteristics are complete, in the early morning mounts this treasure horse and circles the four seas, and by midday returns to the royal palace. This is the horse treasure that manifests in the world when a wheel-turning sage king appears in the world.

"What is the maṇi jewel treasure that manifests in the world when a wheel-turning sage king appears in the world? The wheel-turning sage king possesses a jewel of octagonal shape, lustrous and brilliant, without any flaw or blemish. Within the king's palace, it constantly serves as a lamp. The wheel-turning sage king tests the jewel: on a dark and rainy night, leading the four-fold army into a park, he holds the jewel in front as a guide. Its brilliant light illuminates a full yojana on every side. This is the maṇi jewel treasure that manifests in the world when a wheel-turning sage king appears in the world.

"What is the virtuous jade woman treasure that manifests in the world when a wheel-turning sage king appears in the world? The wheel-turning sage king possesses a jade woman who is neither dark nor fair, neither tall nor short, neither coarse nor delicate, neither fat nor thin. Her limbs are well-proportioned. In cold weather her body is warm; in hot weather her body is cool. Her body is soft like Kaliṅga cloth. From all the pores of her body, sandalwood fragrance emanates. From her mouth and nostrils, the fragrance of blue lotus flowers issues forth. She retires after the king and rises before him, observing the king's expression and serving him appropriately, speaking gentle and loving words, with an upright mind and correct thoughts, arousing the king's aspiration for the way. Her mind never transgresses, how much less her body or speech. This is the jade woman treasure of the wheel-turning sage king.

"How does the treasury minister treasure manifest in the world when a wheel-turning sage king appears in the world? The wheel-turning sage king's treasury minister, due to his past practice of generosity, has been born with the divine eye and can see hidden treasures, whether with an owner or without, whether in water or on land, whether far or near; he can see them all. When the wheel-turning sage king requires precious treasures, he instructs and commands him. Whatever the king requires, the minister immediately offers it up. On one occasion, the sage king wished to test that great minister and observe his abilities. He boarded a ship and sailed upon the sea, and told that great minister: 'I require precious treasures.' The minister said to the king: 'Please wait near the shore, and I shall offer them up.' The king told the minister: 'I do not need treasures from the shore. Give them all to me right here.' Thereupon the great minister, from within the water, produced four golden vessels filled with golden treasures, and offered them to the sage king. The king took and used what he needed. Whatever was left over returned to the water. When the sage king appears in the world, such a treasury minister manifests in the world.

"How does the general minister manifest in the world when the sage king appears in the world? There is a general minister who is intelligent, wise, and eloquent. Just like a person of the world who is accomplished in careful deliberation, whatever is suitable for the sage king, he carries out completely: what should be left, what should be stayed, what should go out, what should come in. When the sage king's four-fold army travels, whether on the road or stopping to rest, he ensures they are not fatigued. He fully knows everything that should be done for the sage king, matters of merit for the present life and future lives, and reports them to the sage king. When the wheel-turning sage king appears in the world, there is such a general minister.

"Just so, when the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One appears in the world, the seven factors of awakening manifest in the world. What are the seven? The mindfulness factor of awakening, the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening, the energy factor of awakening, the joy factor of awakening, the tranquility factor of awakening, the concentration factor of awakening, and the equanimity factor of awakening."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (723)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Excellent! Bhikṣus! It is good that members of the Sangha hear the Dharma, and that the junior bhikṣus serve and attend upon the senior elders. Why is this? When junior bhikṣus serve and attend upon senior elder bhikṣus, from time to time they get to hear profound and wondrous Dharma. Having heard the profound Dharma, two correct practices are accomplished: correctness of body and correctness of mind. At that time, one cultivates the mindfulness factor of awakening. Having cultivated the mindfulness factor of awakening, the mindfulness factor of awakening is fulfilled. When the mindfulness factor is fulfilled, one selects among dharmas, distinguishes dharmas, and deliberates upon dharmas. At that time, one applies oneself to cultivate the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening, and so on up to the equanimity factor of awakening being cultivated to fulfillment."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (724)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu upholds the precepts, cultivates virtue, has moral shame and moral dread, and accomplishes the true Dharma, then one who sees this person obtains many fruits and rewards. Likewise one who hears of him, or one who recollects him, or one who follows him into the renunciant life obtains much merit, how much more one who personally attends upon and respectfully serves him. Why is this? When one personally attends upon and serves such a person, from time to time one gets to hear profound and wondrous Dharma. Having heard the profound Dharma, two correct practices are accomplished: correctness of body and correctness of mind. One then applies oneself to cultivate the concentration factor of awakening. Having cultivated it, one cultivates it to fulfillment, and so on up to the equanimity factor of awakening being cultivated to fulfillment."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (725)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If one speaks of an unwholesome accumulation, it is the five hindrances that is the correct statement. Why is this? Because the purely unwholesome accumulation is the five hindrances. What are the five? The sensual desire hindrance, the ill-will hindrance, the sloth-and-torpor hindrance, the restlessness-and-remorse hindrance, and the doubt hindrance. If one speaks of a wholesome accumulation, it is the seven factors of awakening that is the correct statement. Why is this? Because the purely complete and pure accumulation is the seven factors of awakening. What are the seven? The mindfulness factor of awakening, the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening, the energy factor of awakening, the joy factor of awakening, the tranquility factor of awakening, the concentration factor of awakening, and the equanimity factor of awakening."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (726)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha in the Gorge Monastery. At that time, the Venerable Ānanda was also staying there.

The Venerable Ānanda, alone in a quiet place, engaged in meditation and contemplation, had this thought: "Half of the holy life consists of good friends, good companions, and good associates, not bad friends, bad companions, or bad associates."

Then the Venerable Ānanda, rising from meditation, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Alone in a quiet place, engaged in meditation and contemplation, I had this thought: 'Half of the holy life consists of good friends, good companions, and good associates, not bad friends, bad companions, or bad associates.'"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Do not say this: 'Half of the holy life consists of good friends, good companions, and good associates, not bad friends, bad companions, or bad associates.' Why is this? The entirety, the complete fullness and purity of the holy life, clean and bright, consists of good friends, good companions, and good associates, not bad friends, bad companions, or bad associates. Because I am a good friend, there are beings who, relying on me, take up the mindfulness factor of awakening, based on seclusion, based on dispassion, based on cessation, leading to relinquishment. Likewise the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening, the energy, joy, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity factors of awakening, based on seclusion, based on dispassion, based on cessation, leading to relinquishment. Therefore, you should know, Ānanda, the entirety, the complete fullness and purity of the holy life, clean and bright, consists of good friends, good companions, and good associates, not bad friends, not bad companions, not bad associates."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (727)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people in the Malla territory. Between the city of Kuśinagara and the Hiraṇyavatī river, he stopped near a village. He told the Venerable Ānanda: "Fold the World-Honored One's uttarāsaṅga cloth into four and spread it out. My back is ailing, and I wish to lie down for a short rest."

The Venerable Ānanda received the instruction, folded the uttarāsaṅga into four and spread it out, then said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The uttarāsaṅga has been folded into four and spread out. May the World-Honored One choose the time."

At that time, the World-Honored One folded his saṃghāṭī robe thickly as a pillow, lay down on his right side, with one foot upon the other, focusing his mind on the sign of light, with correct mindfulness and clear comprehension, making the resolve to rise. He told the Venerable Ānanda: "Speak on the seven factors of awakening."

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The so-called mindfulness factor of awakening: the World-Honored One himself awakened to attain perfect enlightenment, and taught it as based on seclusion, based on dispassion, based on cessation, leading to relinquishment. The investigation-of-dharmas, energy, joy, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity factors of awakening: the World-Honored One himself awakened to attain perfect enlightenment, and taught them as based on seclusion, based on dispassion, based on cessation, leading to relinquishment."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Did you speak of energy?"

Ānanda said to the Buddha: "I spoke of energy. The World-Honored One speaks of energy. The Well-Gone One speaks of energy."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Indeed, energy! Through cultivating and abundantly cultivating it, one attains anuttarā-samyak-saṃbodhi." Having said this, he sat up straight, with body erect, focusing his mind.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu then spoke a verse:

> "Delighting in hearing the wondrous Dharma,
> enduring illness, he asks another to speak.
> The bhikṣu then teaches the Dharma,
> expounding the seven factors of awakening.
> Excellent, Venerable Ānanda!
> With clear understanding and skillful exposition.
> There is a supreme, bright, and pure Dharma,
> stainless, subtle, and wondrously taught.
> Mindfulness, investigation, energy,
> joy, tranquility, concentration, equanimity as awakening factors:
> these are the seven factors of awakening,
> subtly and well expounded.
> Hearing the seven factors of awakening explained,
> deeply realizing the flavor of true awakening,
> though his body was afflicted with great suffering,
> he endured the illness and sat upright to listen.
> Behold the King of the True Dharma,
> who constantly teaches for the sake of beings!
> He still delights in hearing what is spoken;
> how much more those who have not yet heard?
> The foremost in great wisdom,
> he whom the ten powers honor:
> they too should quickly, quickly
> come to hear the teaching of the true Dharma.
> Those of much learning and penetration,
> versed in the sūtras and abhidharma,
> well versed in the Dharma and Vinaya:
> they should listen, how much more others?
> Hearing the teaching of the true Dharma,
> listening with focused mind and keen wisdom,
> regarding the Dharma the Buddha has taught,
> one attains the joy of freedom from desire.
> With joy, the body becomes tranquil,
> and the mind too becomes happy.
> With a happy mind, one attains correct attainment,
> rightly observing the conditioned processes.
> One who is repulsed by the three destinations,
> freed from desire, the mind is liberated.
> Repulsed by the various realms of existence,
> not accumulating for human or heavenly birth.
> Without remainder, like a lamp extinguished,
> one ultimately attains parinirvāṇa.
> Hearing the Dharma brings much merit:
> this the most supreme one has taught.
> Therefore one should single-mindedly reflect
> and listen to what the Great Teacher teaches."

After this certain bhikṣu had spoken this verse, he rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (728)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are seven factors of awakening. What are the seven? The mindfulness factor of awakening, up to the equanimity factor of awakening."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (729)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "One should cultivate the seven factors of awakening. What are the seven factors of awakening to be cultivated? The mindfulness factor of awakening, up to the equanimity factor of awakening. If a bhikṣu cultivates the mindfulness factor of awakening, based on seclusion, based on dispassion, based on cessation, leading to relinquishment, he likewise cultivates the investigation-of-dharmas, energy, joy, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity factors of awakening, based on seclusion, based on dispassion, based on cessation, leading to relinquishment."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (730)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus as described above. The difference is: "Bhikṣus! In the past, the seven factors of awakening were cultivated in this way; in the future, they should also be cultivated in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (731)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If the mindfulness factor of awakening of a bhikṣu is pure, bright, without nodes, free from all afflictions, and has not yet arisen but does not arise, this is apart from the Buddha's training and instruction. Likewise, up to the equanimity factor of awakening is also spoken of in the same way. Bhikṣus! If the mindfulness factor of awakening is pure, bright, without nodes, free from all afflictions, and has not yet arisen but does arise, this is through the Buddha's training and instruction, and not otherwise. Likewise, up to the equanimity factor of awakening is also spoken of in the same way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (732)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus as described above. The difference is: "Not yet arisen and not arising: this is apart from the Well-Gone One's training and instruction. Not yet arisen and arising: this is through the Well-Gone One's training and instruction, and not otherwise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (733)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and said to the Buddha: "The World-Honored One speaks of 'factors of awakening.' World-Honored One, what are the factors of awakening?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "The so-called factors of awakening refer to the seven factors of the path. Moreover, bhikṣus, the seven factors of awakening arise gradually in sequence, and through cultivation are fulfilled."

The other bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! How do the factors of awakening gradually arise in sequence and through cultivation become fulfilled?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "When a bhikṣu abides in the contemplation of the body as body internally, at the time of abiding in the contemplation of the body as body internally, he collects his mind, focuses his mindfulness, and does not forget. At that time, he applies himself to cultivate the mindfulness factor of awakening. Having applied himself to cultivate the mindfulness factor of awakening, the mindfulness factor of awakening is cultivated to fulfillment. When the mindfulness factor of awakening is fulfilled, he selects among dharmas, distinguishes, and deliberates. At that time, he applies himself to cultivate the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening. Having applied himself, it is cultivated to fulfillment. In this way, up to the equanimity factor of awakening being cultivated to fulfillment. As with the internal contemplation of the body as body, so too with the external body, internal and external body, and the contemplation of feelings, mind, and dharmas as dharmas. At that time, he focuses his mind and does not forget. Up to the equanimity factor of awakening is also spoken of in the same way. Abiding in this way, the factors of awakening gradually arise; having gradually arisen, they are cultivated to fulfillment."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (734)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told that bhikṣu as described above. The difference is: "If a bhikṣu cultivates the seven factors of awakening in this way, he should obtain two kinds of fruit: either in the present life he attains the exhaustion of the outflows and nirvāṇa without remainder, or he attains the fruit of non-returner."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (735)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, as described above. The difference is: "If a bhikṣu cultivates the seven factors of awakening and abundantly cultivates them, he attains four kinds of fruit and four kinds of merit. What are the four? The fruit of stream-entry, the fruit of once-returning, the fruit of non-returning, and the fruit of arhatship."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the other bhikṣu, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (736)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, as described above. The difference is: "If a bhikṣu cultivates the seven factors of awakening and abundantly cultivates them, he should attain seven kinds of fruit and seven kinds of merit. What are the seven? First, the bhikṣu attains realization of knowledge and the happiness of the present Dharma. Second, if at the time of death he does not attain realization of knowledge and the happiness of the present Dharma, then at the time of death, with the complete exhaustion of the five lower fetters, he attains parinirvāṇa in the intermediate state. Third, if he does not attain parinirvāṇa in the intermediate state, he attains parinirvāṇa upon birth. Fourth, if he does not attain parinirvāṇa upon birth, he attains parinirvāṇa without exertion. Fifth, if he does not attain parinirvāṇa without exertion, he attains parinirvāṇa with exertion. Sixth, if he does not attain parinirvāṇa with exertion, he attains parinirvāṇa by streaming upward."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the other bhikṣu, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (737)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "The so-called factors of awakening: what are the factors of awakening?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. May you please explain. The bhikṣus, having heard, will receive and practice it."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus and bhikṣuṇīs: "The seven factors of awakening refer to the seven factors of the path. Bhikṣus! These seven factors of awakening arise gradually; having gradually arisen, they are cultivated to fulfillment."

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "How do the seven factors of awakening arise gradually, and having gradually arisen, become cultivated to fulfillment?"

"When a bhikṣu abides in the contemplation of the body as body, having abided in the contemplation of the body as body, he focuses his mind and does not forget. At that time, he applies himself to cultivate the mindfulness factor of awakening. Having applied himself to cultivate the mindfulness factor of awakening, it is cultivated to fulfillment. Having cultivated the mindfulness factor of awakening, he selects among dharmas. At that time, he applies himself to cultivate the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening. Having applied himself to cultivate the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening, it is cultivated to fulfillment. Likewise the energy, joy, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity factors of awakening are also spoken of in the same way. As with the internal body, so too with the external body, internal and external body, and the contemplation of feelings, mind, and dharmas as dharmas. Focusing his mind and not forgetting, at that time he applies himself to cultivate the mindfulness factor of awakening. Having applied himself to cultivate the mindfulness factor of awakening, it is cultivated to fulfillment. Up to the equanimity factor of awakening is also spoken of in the same way. This is called the bhikṣu's seven factors of awakening gradually arising; having gradually arisen, being cultivated to fulfillment."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (738)

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Sanskrit into Chinese during the Liu Song dynasty by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Twenty-Seven
