# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Twenty-Eight

---

### (748)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Just as the precursor to the rising of the sun is the dawn, the initial light, so too, bhikṣus, the precursor for a bhikṣu to rightly make an end of suffering, to ultimately reach the end of suffering, is right view. One with right view can give rise to right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration. Having given rise to right concentration and right attainment, the noble disciple's mind is rightly liberated from desire, from anger, and from delusion. The mind being thus well liberated, the noble disciple attains right knowledge and vision: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (749)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "When ignorance is the precursor, unwholesome and evil dharmas arise. At that time, shamelessness and lack of moral dread arise in succession. When shamelessness and lack of moral dread have arisen, wrong view arises in succession. When wrong view has arisen, it can give rise to wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration. When clear knowledge is the precursor, wholesome dharmas arise. At that time, moral shame and moral dread arise in succession. When moral shame and moral dread have arisen, they can give rise to right view. When right view has arisen, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration arise in succession. When right concentration has arisen, the noble disciple attains right liberation from desire, from anger, and from delusion. When the noble disciple has thus attained right liberation, one attains right knowledge and vision: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (750)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Bhikṣus! As for all evil and unwholesome dharmas, bhikṣus, they all have ignorance as their root, are gathered by ignorance, born from ignorance, and arise from ignorance. Why is this? Ignorance means not knowing. One does not know as it truly is wholesome and unwholesome dharmas, what has fault and what is faultless, inferior dharmas and superior dharmas, defiled and undefiled, what is to be distinguished and what is not to be distinguished, what is dependently arisen and what is not dependently arisen. Because one does not know as it truly is, wrong view arises. When wrong view has arisen, it can give rise to wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration.

"When wholesome dharmas arise, they all have clear knowledge as their root, are gathered by clear knowledge, born from clear knowledge, and arise from clear knowledge. Clear knowledge is knowing as it truly is wholesome and unwholesome dharmas, what has fault and what is faultless, what should be associated with and what should not be associated with, inferior dharmas and superior dharmas, what is impure and what is pure, what is to be distinguished and what is not to be distinguished, what is dependently arisen and what is not dependently arisen, all known as they truly are. One who knows as it truly is has right view. One with right view can give rise to right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration. When right concentration has arisen, the noble disciple attains right liberation from desire, from anger, and from delusion. When desire, anger, and delusion are liberated, the noble disciple attains right knowledge and vision: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further becoming.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (751)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Whether a householder or a renunciant undertakes wrong endeavors, I do not approve. Why is this? If a householder or renunciant undertakes wrong endeavors, then they will not delight in the true Dharma. What are wrong endeavors? They are wrong view, up to wrong concentration. Whether a householder or a renunciant undertakes right endeavors, I praise them. Why is this? One who undertakes right endeavors delights in the true Dharma and is skilled in the true Dharma. What are right endeavors? They are right view, up to right concentration."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "Whether householders or renunciants,
> who undertake wrong endeavors,
> they shall never delight
> in the unsurpassed true Dharma.
> Whether householders or renunciants,
> who undertake right endeavors,
> their minds shall ever delight
> in the unsurpassed true Dharma."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (752)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the bhikṣu Kāma went to where the Buddha was, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! What is called 'desire'? What is desire?"

The Buddha told Kāma: "Desire refers to the five qualities of sensual pleasure. What are the five? Forms cognized by eye-consciousness that are lovely, agreeable, and memorable, that nurture desire and pleasure. Likewise, sounds cognized by ear-consciousness, smells cognized by nose-consciousness, tastes cognized by tongue-consciousness, and tangible objects cognized by body-consciousness that are lovely, agreeable, and memorable, that nurture desire and pleasure. These are called desire. However, those things themselves are not desire; rather, the craving and attachment toward them is called desire."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "The various colors of the world,
> they themselves are not sensual desire.
> Craving and desirous thought,
> that is a person's desire.
> The various forms ever abide in the world;
> the practitioner cuts off the mind's desire."

The bhikṣu Kāma said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Is there a path and a way to cut off this craving desire?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "There is the Noble Eightfold Path that can cut off craving desire, namely: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Kāma, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (753)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Ariṣṭa who went to where the Buddha was, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! What is called 'ambrosia' (amṛta)? What is meant by ambrosia?"

The Buddha told Ariṣṭa: "Ambrosia is a designation for a realm. However, I presently teach this designation for those who have exhausted the taints."

The bhikṣu Ariṣṭa said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Is there a path and a way that, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, leads to the attainment of the dharma of ambrosia?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "There is, namely the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, up to right concentration."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (754)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra went to where the Buddha was, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! What is called the noble right samādhi with its basis and requisites? What is the noble right samādhi with its basis and requisites?"

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "The seven factors of the right path serve as the basis and requisites for noble right samādhi. What are the seven? They are right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, and right mindfulness. Śāriputra! Having these seven path factors as a foundation, one attains one-pointedness of mind. This is called the noble right samādhi with its basis and requisites."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (755–757)

As in the above three sūtras. Likewise, three sūtras in which the Buddha questioned the bhikṣus are also spoken in this way.

---

### (758)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are fears without mother and child, and there are fears with mother and child. Foolish, unlearned ordinary persons speak of them, yet cannot truly understand the fears without mother and child and the fears with mother and child.

"Bhikṣus! There are three kinds of fears without mother and child that foolish, unlearned ordinary persons speak of. What are the three? Bhikṣus! At times, armed conflict and disorder arise, devastating the country, and people scatter like waves in a current. Children lose their mothers, and mothers lose their children. This is called the first fear without mother and child that foolish, unlearned ordinary persons speak of.

"Furthermore, bhikṣus! At times, a great fire suddenly breaks out, burning cities, towns, and villages. People flee, and mothers and children become separated. This is called the second fear without mother and child that foolish, unlearned ordinary persons speak of.

"Furthermore, bhikṣus! At times, heavy rains in the mountains cause floods to pour out, inundating villages. People flee, and mothers and children become separated. This is called the third fear without mother and child that foolish, unlearned ordinary persons speak of.

"However, these fears are actually fears with mother and child, yet foolish, unlearned ordinary persons call them fears without mother and child. When armed conflict and disorder arise, devastating the country, and people scatter like waves in a current, mothers and children become separated; yet sometimes thereafter mother and child may see each other again. This is called the first fear with mother and child, which foolish, unlearned ordinary persons call a fear without mother and child.

"Furthermore, when a great fire suddenly breaks out, burning cities, towns, and villages, and people flee, mothers and children become separated; yet they may again see each other. This is called the second fear with mother and child, which foolish, unlearned ordinary persons call a fear without mother and child.

"Furthermore, when heavy rains in the mountains cause floods to pour out, inundating villages, and people flee, mothers and children become separated; yet they may soon see each other again. This is called the third fear with mother and child, which foolish, unlearned ordinary persons call a fear without mother and child.

"Bhikṣus! There are three kinds of fears without mother and child, which I myself, having awakened to the three aspects of perfect enlightenment, have declared. What are the three? Bhikṣus! When a child grows old, there is no mother who can say: 'Child! Do not grow old; I shall grow old in your place.' When the mother grows old, there is likewise no child who can say: 'Mother! Do not grow old; I shall grow old in your place.' This is called the first fear without mother and child, which I myself, having awakened to the three aspects of perfect enlightenment, have declared.

"Furthermore, bhikṣus! When a child falls ill, the mother cannot say: 'Child! Do not be sick; I shall be sick in your place.' When the mother falls ill, the child likewise cannot say: 'Mother! Do not be sick; I shall be sick in your place.' This is called the second fear without mother and child, which I myself, having awakened to the three aspects of perfect enlightenment, have declared.

"Furthermore, when a child dies, there is no mother who can say: 'Child! Do not die; I shall die in your place.' When the mother dies, there is no child who can say: 'Mother! Do not die; I shall die in your place.' This is called the third fear without mother and child, which I myself, having awakened to the three aspects of perfect enlightenment, have declared."

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "Is there a path and a way that, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, can cut off the former three kinds of fears with mother and child, and cut off the latter three kinds of fears without mother and child?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "There is a path and a way to cut off those three fears. What is the path? What is the way that, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, cuts off the former three kinds of fears with mother and child and cuts off the latter three kinds of fears without mother and child? It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (759)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are three feelings, impermanent, conditioned, and dependently arisen from the mind. What are the three? They are pleasant feeling, painful feeling, and neither-pleasant-nor-painful feeling."

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Is there a path and a way that, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, cuts off these three feelings?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "There is a path and a way that, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, cuts off these three feelings. What is the path, and what is the way that, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, cuts off these three feelings?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (760)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In the world there are three dharmas that are unpleasant, unlovely, and disagreeable. What are the three? They are old age, sickness, and death. These three dharmas are unpleasant, unlovely, and disagreeable. If in the world there were not these three dharmas that are unpleasant, unlovely, and disagreeable, there would be no Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Enlightened One appearing in the world, nor would the world know of the Tathāgata's teaching the Dharma, instructing, and admonishing. It is because in the world there exist these three dharmas that are unpleasant, unlovely, and disagreeable that a Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Enlightened One appears in the world, and the world comes to know of the Tathāgata's teaching the Dharma, instructing, and admonishing."

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "Is there a path and a way to cut off these three dharmas that are unpleasant, unlovely, and disagreeable?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "There is a path and a way that, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, cuts off these three dharmas that are unpleasant, unlovely, and disagreeable. What is the path? What is the way that, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, cuts off these three dharmas that are unpleasant, unlovely, and disagreeable? It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (761)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall teach about the learner and the non-learner. Listen carefully and reflect well upon it. What is a learner? One who accomplishes the learning of right view, the learning of right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration, is called a learner. What is a non-learner? One who accomplishes the non-learner's right view, the non-learner's right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration, is called a non-learner."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

Just as with learner and non-learner, so too with the true person and the great person, it is also spoken in this way.

---

### (762)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall teach about the noble one whose taints are ended. What is the noble one whose taints are ended? One who accomplishes the non-learner's right view, up to the non-learner's right concentration, is called the noble one whose taints are ended."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (763)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach the Noble Eightfold Path. What are the eight? They are right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (764)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach the cultivation of the Noble Eightfold Path. Listen carefully and reflect well. What is the cultivation of the Noble Eightfold Path? A bhikṣu cultivates right view, based on seclusion, based on dispassion, based on cessation, inclining toward relinquishment. One cultivates right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration, based on seclusion, based on dispassion, based on cessation, inclining toward relinquishment. This is called the cultivation of the Noble Eightfold Path."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (765)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach that bhikṣus in the past have cultivated the Noble Eightfold Path, and in the future will cultivate the Noble Eightfold Path." And so forth.

The bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (766)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu's right view is purified, bright, and clean, without any fault, free from all afflictions, yet has not arisen and does not arise, this is only apart from what the Buddha has tamed. Up to right concentration it is also spoken in this way. If right view is purified, bright, and clean, without any fault, free from all afflictions, and what has not yet arisen can arise, up to right concentration it is also spoken in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

Just as "apart from what the Buddha has taught" is stated, so too "apart from what the Well-Gone One has taught" is also spoken as above.

---

### (767)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "To speak of a heap of unwholesomeness means the five hindrances; this is rightly said. Why is this? A purely unwholesome heap is the five hindrances. What are the five? They are the hindrance of desire, the hindrance of ill-will, the hindrance of sloth and torpor, the hindrance of restlessness and remorse, and the hindrance of doubt. To speak of a heap of wholesome dharmas means the Noble Eightfold Path; this is rightly said. Why is this? A purely complete and pure wholesome heap is the Noble Eightfold Path. What are the eight? They are right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (768)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Mountain Valley Monastery.

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda, being alone in a quiet place, had this thought: "Half of the holy life consists of good friends, good companions, and good associates." And so forth. The Buddha told Ānanda: "The entirety of the complete and pure holy life consists of good friends. Why is this? Because I am a good friend, I cause sentient beings to cultivate right view based on seclusion, based on dispassion, based on cessation, inclining toward relinquishment; up to cultivating right concentration based on seclusion, based on dispassion, based on cessation, inclining toward relinquishment."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (769)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, the Venerable Ānanda, in the early morning, put on his robes, took his bowl, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food.

At that time, the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi was driving a white horse chariot, accompanied by many young men as attendants. The horses were white, the chariot was white, the reins were white, the whip was white, he wore a white cap on his head, had a white parasol, held a white whisk in his hand, wore white garments and white ornaments, his body was anointed with white fragrance, and all his attendants were also in white. He was leaving the city of Śrāvastī, heading to a grove to teach and recite. The people who saw him all said: "A fine vehicle! A fine vehicle! That is, the brahmin vehicle."

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda saw the brahmin with his retinue and all his accoutrements entirely white. Having seen this, he entered the city to beg for food. Returning to the monastery, he put away his robes and bowl, washed his feet, went to where the Buddha was, prostrated at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! This morning I put on my robes, took my bowl, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. I saw the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi driving a white horse chariot, with his retinue and all accoutrements entirely white. The people proclaimed: 'A fine vehicle! A fine vehicle! That is, the brahmin vehicle.' What does the World-Honored One say? In the true Dharma and Vinaya, is this a worldly vehicle or a brahmin vehicle?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "That is a worldly vehicle; it is not the brahmin vehicle of my Dharma and Vinaya. Ānanda! The vehicle of my true Dharma and Vinaya is the divine vehicle, the brahmin vehicle, the great vehicle, which can tame the army of afflictions. Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it for you. Ānanda! What is the vehicle of the true Dharma and Vinaya, the divine vehicle, the brahmin vehicle, the great vehicle, which can tame the army of afflictions? It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, up to right concentration. Ānanda! This is called the vehicle of the true Dharma and Vinaya, the divine vehicle, the Brahmā vehicle, the great vehicle, which can tame the army of afflictions."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "Faith and precepts are the yoke of the Dharma,
> moral shame and dread are the long traces,
> right mindfulness well guarded
> serves as the skilled charioteer.
> Renunciation and samādhi are the shafts,
> wisdom and effort are the wheels.
> Non-attachment and patient endurance are the armor;
> one proceeds in peace, practicing according to Dharma.
> Going straight ahead without retreating,
> one reaches forever the place without sorrow.
> The wise one rides this war chariot
> and vanquishes the foolish enemy."

---

### (770)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should abandon wrong view; you should cut off wrong view. If wrong view could not be cut off, I would never say that one should abandon and cut off wrong view. Because wrong view can be cut off, I say that bhikṣus should abandon wrong view. If one does not abandon wrong view, wrong view will produce what is unprofitable, without benefit, and suffering. Therefore I say one should abandon wrong view. Likewise, wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration are also spoken of in this way.

"Bhikṣus! Having abandoned wrong view, you should cultivate right view. If right view could not be cultivated, I would never say to cultivate right view. Because right view can be cultivated, I say that bhikṣus should cultivate right view. If one does not cultivate right view, it will produce what is unprofitable, without benefit, and suffering. Because not cultivating right view produces what is unprofitable, without benefit, and suffering, therefore I say one should cultivate right view, for it is profitable and beneficial, and one constantly attains peace and happiness. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should cultivate right view. Likewise, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration are also spoken of in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (771)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi came to where the Buddha was, exchanged greetings and cordial words of inquiry with the World-Honored One. After exchanging greetings and cordial words of inquiry, he withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "Gautama! It is said 'not the other shore' and 'the other shore.' Gautama! What is 'not the other shore'? What is 'the other shore'?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "Wrong view is not the other shore. Right view is the other shore. Wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration are not the other shore. Right view is the other shore. Right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration are the other shore."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "Rare are the people
> who can cross to the other shore.
> All beings in the world
> wander back and forth on this shore.
> Those who in this true Dharma and Vinaya
> can skillfully follow and comply,
> these are able to cross
> the shore of birth and death, so hard to cross."

At that time, the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (772–774)

Likewise, a different bhikṣu questioned the Venerable Ānanda, questioned the Buddha, and questioned the bhikṣus. These three sūtras are also as stated above.

---

### (775)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Among internal dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that can cause unarisen evil, unwholesome dharmas to arise, and already arisen ones to multiply and increase, as much as improper attention. Bhikṣus! With improper attention, unarisen wrong view arises, and already arisen wrong view multiplies and increases. Likewise, wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration are also spoken of in this way.

"Bhikṣus! Among internal dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that can cause unarisen evil, unwholesome dharmas not to arise, and already arisen evil, unwholesome dharmas to cease, as much as proper attention. Bhikṣus! With proper attention, unarisen wrong view does not arise, and already arisen wrong view ceases. As with wrong view, wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration are also spoken of in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (776)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Among internal dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that causes unarisen wholesome dharmas not to arise, and already arisen wholesome dharmas to decline, as much as improper attention. Bhikṣus! With improper attention, unarisen right view does not arise, and already arisen right view declines. Likewise, unarisen right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration do not arise, and already arisen ones decline.

"Bhikṣus! Among internal dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that causes unarisen wholesome dharmas to arise, and already arisen wholesome dharmas to multiply and increase, as much as proper attention. Bhikṣus! With proper attention, unarisen right view arises, and already arisen right view multiplies and increases. Likewise, unarisen right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration arise, and already arisen ones multiply and increase."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (777)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Among internal dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that causes unarisen evil, unwholesome dharmas to arise, already arisen evil, unwholesome dharmas to multiply and increase, unarisen wholesome dharmas not to arise, and already arisen ones to decline, as much as improper attention. Bhikṣus! With improper attention, unarisen wrong view arises, and already arisen wrong view multiplies and increases; unarisen right view does not arise, and already arisen right view declines. Likewise, unarisen wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration arise, and already arisen ones multiply and increase; unarisen right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration do not arise, and already arisen ones decline.

"Bhikṣus! Among internal dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that causes unarisen evil, unwholesome dharmas not to arise, already arisen evil, unwholesome dharmas to cease, unarisen wholesome dharmas to arise, and already arisen wholesome dharmas to multiply and increase, as much as proper attention. Bhikṣus! With proper attention, unarisen wrong view does not arise, and already arisen wrong view ceases; unarisen right view arises, and already arisen right view multiplies and increases. Likewise, unarisen wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration do not arise, and already arisen ones cease; unarisen right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration arise, and already arisen ones multiply and increase."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (778)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Among external dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that causes unarisen evil, unwholesome dharmas to arise, and already arisen evil, unwholesome dharmas to multiply and increase, as much as bad friends, bad companions, and bad associates. Bhikṣus! Bad friends, bad companions, and bad associates can cause unarisen wrong view to arise, and already arisen wrong view to multiply and increase. Likewise, unarisen wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration arise, and already arisen ones multiply and increase.

"Bhikṣus! Among external dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that causes unarisen evil, unwholesome dharmas not to arise, and already arisen evil, unwholesome dharmas to cease, as much as good friends, good companions, and good associates. Bhikṣus! Good friends, good companions, and good associates can cause unarisen wrong view not to arise, and already arisen wrong view to cease; unarisen wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration not to arise, and already arisen ones to cease."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (779)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Among external dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that can cause unarisen wholesome dharmas to arise, and already arisen wholesome dharmas to multiply and increase, as much as good friends, good companions, and good associates. Bhikṣus! Good friends, good companions, and good associates can cause unarisen right view to arise, and already arisen right view to multiply and increase. Likewise, unarisen right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration arise, and already arisen ones multiply and increase."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (780)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Among external dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that can cause unarisen evil, unwholesome dharmas to arise, already arisen evil, unwholesome dharmas to multiply and increase, unarisen wholesome dharmas not to arise, and already arisen wholesome dharmas to cease, as much as bad friends, bad companions, and bad associates. Bhikṣus! Bad friends, bad companions, and bad associates can cause unarisen wrong view to arise, and already arisen wrong view to multiply and increase; unarisen right view not to arise, and already arisen right view to decline. Likewise, unarisen wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration arise, and already arisen ones multiply and increase; unarisen right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration do not arise, and already arisen ones decline.

"Bhikṣus! Among external dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that can cause unarisen evil, unwholesome dharmas not to arise, already arisen evil, unwholesome dharmas to cease, unarisen wholesome dharmas to arise, and already arisen wholesome dharmas to multiply and increase, as much as good friends, good companions, and good associates. Bhikṣus! Good friends, good companions, and good associates can cause unarisen wrong view not to arise, already arisen wrong view to cease, unarisen right view to arise, and already arisen right view to multiply and increase. Likewise, unarisen wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration do not arise, and already arisen ones cease; unarisen right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration arise, and already arisen ones multiply and increase."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (781)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Among internal dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that can cause unarisen evil, unwholesome dharmas to arise, already arisen ones to multiply and increase, unarisen wholesome dharmas not to arise, and already arisen ones to decline, as much as improper attention. Bhikṣus! With improper attention, unarisen wrong view arises, already arisen wrong view multiplies and increases, unarisen right view does not arise, and already arisen right view declines.

"Bhikṣus! Among internal dharmas, I do not see a single dharma that can cause unarisen evil, unwholesome dharmas not to arise, already arisen evil, unwholesome dharmas to cease, unarisen wholesome dharmas to arise, and already arisen wholesome dharmas to multiply and increase, as much as proper attention. Bhikṣus! With proper attention, unarisen wrong view does not arise, already arisen wrong view ceases, unarisen right view arises, and already arisen right view multiplies and increases."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As stated for wrong view and right view, so too for wrong intention and right intention, wrong speech and right speech, wrong action and right action, wrong livelihood and right livelihood, wrong effort and right effort, wrong mindfulness and right mindfulness, wrong concentration and right concentration: seven sūtras are as stated above.

As with the eight sūtras on internal dharmas, so too the eight sūtras on external dharmas are also spoken in this way.

---

### (782)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There is what is non-Dharma and what is Dharma. Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it for you. What is non-Dharma and what is Dharma? Wrong view is non-Dharma; right view is Dharma. Up to wrong concentration is non-Dharma; right concentration is Dharma."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with non-Dharma and Dharma, so too with non-Vinaya and Vinaya, non-noble and noble, unwholesome dharmas and wholesome dharmas, dharmas not to be practiced and dharmas to be practiced, dharmas that are not excellent and dharmas that are excellent, dark dharmas and bright dharmas, what is unprofitable and what is profitable, inferior dharmas and superior dharmas, dharmas with fault and dharmas without fault, dharmas to be abandoned and dharmas not to be abandoned: each sūtra is as stated above.

---

### (783)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣita's Park. At that time, the Venerable Ānanda was also residing there.

A certain brahmin came to where the Venerable Ānanda was, exchanged greetings and cordial words of inquiry with the Venerable Ānanda. After exchanging greetings and cordial words of inquiry, he withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Venerable Ānanda: "I wish to ask something. Do you have the leisure to explain it?"

Ānanda replied: "Ask as you wish. If I know, I shall answer."

The brahmin asked: "Venerable Ānanda! Why did you go forth to practice the holy life under the ascetic Gautama?"

Ānanda replied: "Brahmin! For the sake of cutting off."

He asked again: "Cutting off what?"

He replied: "Cutting off desire, cutting off anger, and cutting off delusion."

He asked again: "Ānanda! Is there a path and a way that can cut off desire, anger, and delusion?"

Ānanda replied: "There is, namely the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration."

The brahmin said: "Ānanda! An excellent path! An excellent way! When cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, it can cut off such desire, anger, and delusion."

When the Venerable Ānanda had spoken this Dharma, the brahmin, hearing what he had said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

As with cutting off desire, anger, and delusion, so too with taming desire, anger, and delusion; and attaining nirvāṇa; and disenchantment; and the path not leading to nirvāṇa; and the meaning of being an ascetic; and the meaning of being a brahmin; and liberation; and the cutting off of suffering; and reaching the ultimate end of suffering; and the complete ending of suffering: each sūtra is as stated above.

---

### (784)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There is what is wrong and what is right. Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it for you. What is wrong? It is wrong view, up to wrong concentration. What is right? It is right view, up to right concentration. What is right view? It means saying that there is giving, there is teaching, there are offerings; there are good deeds and evil deeds; there are fruits and results of good and evil deeds; there is this world and the next world; there are father and mother; there are beings who are born; there are arhats who have well arrived and well directed themselves, who in this world and the next world know for themselves, having directly realized and fully abiding: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what had to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further becoming.' What is right intention? It is the intention of renunciation, the intention of non-ill-will, and the intention of non-harming. What is right speech? It is abstaining from false speech, abstaining from divisive speech, abstaining from harsh speech, and abstaining from idle chatter. What is right action? It is abstaining from killing, stealing, and sexual misconduct. What is right livelihood? It is seeking robes, food and drink, bedding, and medicine in accordance with Dharma, not in a way contrary to Dharma. What is right effort? It is desire, diligence, effort, emergence, exertion, capacity, constant practice without regression. What is right mindfulness? It is mindfulness that follows along, mindfulness that is not confused and not false. What is right concentration? It is the mind abiding without distraction, firm, collected, calm, in samādhi, and one-pointed."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (785)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "What is right view? Right view is of two kinds. There is right view that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. There is right view that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering. What is right view that is with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies? If one holds the view that there is giving, there is teaching, up to knowing that in the world there are arhats who will have no further becoming: this is called worldly right view, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. What is right view that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering? When a noble disciple contemplates suffering as suffering, contemplates the arising, cessation, and the path as the path, and in connection with taintless contemplation, there is the selection, discrimination, investigation, cognition, discernment, awakening, and examination of dharmas: this is called right view that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering.

"What is right intention? Right intention is of two kinds. There is right intention that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. There is right intention that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering. What is right intention that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies? Right intention is the thought of renunciation, the thought of non-ill-will, and the thought of non-harming. This is called right intention that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. What is right intention that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering? When a noble disciple contemplates suffering as suffering, contemplates the arising, cessation, and the path as the path, and in connection with taintless contemplation there are mental dharmas of discrimination, self-determination, resolve, reckoning, and establishment of intention: this is called right intention that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering.

"What is right speech? Right speech is of two kinds. There is right speech that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. There is right speech that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering. What is right speech that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies? Right speech is abstaining from false speech, divisive speech, harsh speech, and idle chatter. This is called right speech that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. What is right speech that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering? When a noble disciple contemplates suffering as suffering, contemplates the arising, cessation, and the path as the path, having excluded wrong livelihood, being mindful of the four evil actions of speech and all other evil actions of speech, one separates from them, without taints, distanced, unattached, firmly guarding and maintaining them without transgression, not exceeding the proper time, not overstepping limits and safeguards. This is called right speech that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering.

"What is right action? Right action is of two kinds. There is right action that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. There is right action that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering. What is right action that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies? It is abstaining from killing, stealing, and sexual misconduct. This is called right action that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. What is right action that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering? When a noble disciple contemplates suffering as suffering, contemplates the arising, cessation, and the path as the path, having excluded wrong livelihood, being mindful of the three evil actions of body and all other various evil actions of body, without taints, the mind not delighting in or attaching to them, firmly guarding and maintaining them without transgression, not exceeding the proper time, not overstepping limits and safeguards. This is called right action that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering.

"What is right livelihood? Right livelihood is of two kinds. There is right livelihood that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. There is right livelihood that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering. What is right livelihood that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies? It is seeking robes, food, bedding, and medicine for illness in accordance with Dharma, not in a way contrary to Dharma. This is called right livelihood that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. What is right livelihood that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering? When a noble disciple contemplates suffering as suffering, contemplates the arising, cessation, and the path as the path, with respect to all wrong livelihoods, being without taints, not delighting in or attaching to them, firmly guarding and maintaining them without transgression, not exceeding the proper time, not overstepping limits and safeguards. This is called right livelihood that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering.

"What is right effort? Right effort is of two kinds. There is right effort that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. There is right effort that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering. What is right effort that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies? It is desire, diligence, effort, emergence and firm establishment, capacity for vigorous action, mental dharmas of striving and effort, constancy, and non-cessation. This is called right effort that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. What is right effort that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering? When a noble disciple contemplates suffering as suffering, contemplates the arising, cessation, and the path as the path, and in connection with taintless mindfulness there are mental dharmas of desire, diligence, effort, exertion, emergence, firm establishment, capacity for vigorous action, mental dharmas of striving and effort, constancy, and non-cessation. This is called right effort that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering.

"What is right mindfulness? Right mindfulness is of two kinds. There is right mindfulness that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. There is right mindfulness that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering. What is right mindfulness that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies? Mindfulness, continued mindfulness, repeated mindfulness, recollection, not confused, and not false: this is called right mindfulness that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. What is right mindfulness that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, turning toward the end of suffering? When a noble disciple contemplates suffering as suffering, contemplates the arising, cessation, and the path as the path, and in connection with taintless contemplation there is mindfulness, continued mindfulness, repeated mindfulness, recollection, not confused, and not false: this is called right mindfulness that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, turning toward the end of suffering.

"What is right concentration? Right concentration is of two kinds. There is right concentration that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. There is right concentration that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering. What is right concentration that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies? When the mind abides without distraction, without agitation, collected, calm, in samādhi, and one-pointed: this is called right concentration that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. What is right concentration that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering? When a noble disciple contemplates suffering as suffering, contemplates the arising, cessation, and the path as the path, and in connection with taintless contemplation there are mental dharmas of abiding, non-distraction, non-scattering, collectedness, calm, samādhi, and one-pointedness: this is called right concentration that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (786)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu's mind inclines toward what is wrong, it goes against the Dharma and does not delight in the Dharma. If it inclines toward what is right, the mind delights in the Dharma and does not go against the Dharma. What is wrong? It is wrong view, up to wrong concentration. What is right? It is right view, up to right concentration."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (787)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "One who inclines toward what is wrong goes against the Dharma and does not delight in the Dharma. One who inclines toward what is right delights in the Dharma and does not go against the Dharma. What does it mean that one who inclines toward what is wrong goes against the Dharma and does not delight in the Dharma? A person of wrong view performs bodily actions in accordance with his view, performs verbal actions in accordance with his view; whatever his thoughts, desires, wishes, and deeds, they all follow accordingly, and all obtain undesirable, disagreeable, and unpleasant results. Why is this? Because of evil views, namely wrong view. One with wrong view gives rise to wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration. This is what it means that one who inclines toward what is wrong goes against the Dharma and does not delight in the Dharma.

"What does it mean that one who inclines toward what is right delights in the Dharma and does not go against the Dharma? A person of right view performs bodily actions in accordance with his view, performs verbal actions in accordance with his view; whatever his thoughts, desires, wishes, and deeds, they all follow accordingly, and all obtain desirable, agreeable, and pleasant results. Why is this? Because of good views, namely right view. One with right view can give rise to right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration. This is what it means that one who inclines toward what is right delights in the Dharma and does not go against the Dharma."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (788)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "One who inclines toward what is wrong goes against the Dharma and does not delight in the Dharma. One who inclines toward what is right delights in the Dharma and does not go against the Dharma.

"What does it mean that one who inclines toward what is wrong goes against the Dharma and does not delight in the Dharma? A person of wrong view performs bodily actions in accordance with his view, performs verbal actions in accordance with his view; whatever his thoughts, desires, wishes, and deeds, they all follow accordingly, and all obtain undesirable, disagreeable, and unpleasant results. Why is this? Because of evil views, namely wrong view. One with wrong view gives rise to wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, and wrong concentration. It is like a bitter fruit: when planted in the ground and watered in due season, it absorbs the flavor of earth, the flavor of water, the flavor of fire, and the flavor of wind, and everything it produces is entirely bitter. Why is this? Because the seed is bitter. So too, a person of wrong view performs bodily actions in accordance with his view, performs verbal actions in accordance with his view; whatever his thoughts, desires, wishes, and deeds, they all follow accordingly, and all obtain undesirable, disagreeable, and unpleasant results. Why is this? Because of evil views, namely wrong view. One with wrong view can give rise to wrong intention, up to wrong concentration. This is what it means that one who inclines toward what is wrong goes against the Dharma and does not delight in the Dharma.

"What does it mean that one who inclines toward what is right delights in the Dharma and does not go against the Dharma? A person of right view performs bodily actions in accordance with his view, performs verbal actions in accordance with his view; whatever his thoughts, desires, and deeds, they all follow accordingly, and all obtain desirable, agreeable, and pleasant results. Why is this? Because of good views, namely right view. One with right view can give rise to right intention, up to right concentration. It is like sugar cane, rice, wheat, or grapes: when planted in the ground and watered in due season, they absorb the flavor of earth, the flavor of water, the flavor of fire, and the flavor of wind, and all their flavors are entirely sweet and delicious. Why is this? Because the seed is sweet. So too, a person of right view performs bodily actions in accordance with his view, performs verbal actions in accordance with his view; whatever his thoughts, desires, wishes, and deeds, they all follow accordingly, and all obtain desirable, agreeable, and pleasant results. Why is this? Because of good views, namely right view. One with right view can give rise to right intention, up to right concentration. This is what it means that one who inclines toward what is right delights in the Dharma and does not go against the Dharma."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

The worldly and supramundane are also spoken of in this way. As in the above three sūtras, verses are also spoken:

> "Base dharmas should not be approached,
> heedlessness should not be practiced.
> One should not hold wrong views,
> which increase worldly existence.
> If in the world
> one has superior right view,
> then even through a hundred thousand births,
> one will never fall into evil destinies."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (789)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the brahmin Jāṇussoṇi came to where the Buddha was, prostrated at the Buddha's feet, exchanged greetings and cordial words of inquiry with the World-Honored One. After exchanging greetings, he withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "Gautama! What is called right view? What is right view?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "Right view is of two kinds. There is right view that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. There is right view that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering. What is right view that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies? Right view holds that there is giving, there is teaching, there are offerings, up to knowing for oneself that there will be no further becoming. Brahmin! This is called right view that is worldly, mundane, with taints, with clinging, turning toward good destinies. Brahmin! What is right view that is noble, supramundane, without taints, without clinging, rightly ending suffering, turning toward the end of suffering? When a noble disciple contemplates suffering as suffering, contemplates the arising, cessation, and the path as the path, and in connection with taintless contemplation, there is the selection, discrimination, investigation, discernment, skillful wisdom, and examination of dharmas. This is called

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.
