# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Twenty-Nine

---

### (797)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There is the practice of the śramaṇa and the fruit of the śramaṇa. Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it for you. What is the practice of the śramaṇa? It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, up to right samādhi. What are the fruits of the śramaṇa? They are the fruit of stream-entry, the fruit of once-returning, the fruit of non-returning, and the fruit of arhatship. What is the fruit of stream-entry? It is the destruction of the three fetters. What is the fruit of once-returning? It is the destruction of the three fetters, with greed, hatred, and delusion weakened. What is the fruit of non-returning? It is the complete exhaustion of the five lower fetters. What is the fruit of arhatship? It is the permanent exhaustion of greed, hatred, and delusion, the permanent exhaustion of all afflictions."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (798)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There is the practice of the śramaṇa, the śramaṇa, and the goal of the śramaṇa. Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it for you. What is the practice of the śramaṇa? It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, up to right samādhi. What is the śramaṇa? It is one who has accomplished this practice. What is the goal of the śramaṇa? It is the permanent severing of desire, the permanent severing of hatred and delusion, the permanent severing of all afflictions."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (799)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "There is the fruit of the śramaṇa. What is the fruit of the śramaṇa? It is the fruit of stream-entry, the fruit of once-returning, the fruit of non-returning, and the fruit of arhatship."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (800)

Likewise, the practice of the brāhmaṇa, the brāhmaṇa, the goal of the brāhmaṇa, and the fruit of the brāhmaṇa.

The practice of the holy life, the practitioner of the holy life, the goal of the holy life, and the fruit of the holy life are also as stated above.

---

### (801)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are five dharmas that are of great benefit for cultivating ānāpānasmṛti. What are the five? Abiding in pure morality, in the discipline of the prātimokṣa, complete in deportment and proper conduct, generating fear even toward subtle offenses, receiving and upholding the training rules: this is called the first dharma of great benefit for cultivating ānāpānasmṛti. Furthermore, bhikṣus, having few desires, few affairs, and few duties: this is called the second dharma of great benefit for cultivating ānāpānasmṛti. Furthermore, bhikṣus, knowing moderation in food and drink, obtaining the middle way in amounts, not giving rise to thoughts of craving for food and drink, being diligent in reflection: this is called the third dharma of great benefit for cultivating ānāpānasmṛti. Furthermore, bhikṣus, not indulging in sleep during the first and last watches of the night, being diligent in reflection: this is called the fourth dharma of great benefit for cultivating ānāpānasmṛti. Furthermore, bhikṣus, dwelling in an empty, secluded forest, far from commotion: this is called the fifth dharma of great benefit for cultivating ānāpānasmṛti."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (802)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should cultivate ānāpānasmṛti. If a bhikṣu cultivates and extensively cultivates ānāpānasmṛti, he attains stilling of the body and stilling of the mind, with initial thought and sustained thought, tranquility and purity, and the perception pertaining to the portion of illumination is cultivated to fulfillment."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (803)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Cultivate ānāpānasmṛti. If a bhikṣu cultivates and extensively cultivates ānāpānasmṛti, he attains stilling of body and mind, with initial thought and sustained thought, tranquility and purity, and the perception pertaining to the portion of illumination is cultivated to fulfillment. How is it that cultivating and extensively cultivating ānāpānasmṛti leads to stilling of body and mind, with initial thought and sustained thought, tranquility and purity, and the perception pertaining to the portion of illumination being cultivated to fulfillment? This bhikṣu, if dwelling near a village or town, in the early morning dons his robes and takes his bowl, enters the village to beg for food, well guarding his body, watching over the sense-gates, with mind well-focused and abiding. After begging food, he returns to his dwelling, puts away his robes and bowl, washes his feet, and then either enters the forest, a quiet room, beneath a tree, or an open space, sits upright with body erect, fixes mindfulness before him, cuts off worldly craving and desire, purified and free from desire, severs hatred, drowsiness, restlessness-and-regret, and doubt, crosses beyond all doubts and perplexities, and attains decisiveness regarding wholesome dharmas. He distances himself from the five hindrances, which are afflictions of the mind that weaken the power of wisdom, being obstructive factors that do not tend toward nirvāṇa. He is mindful of the in-breath, with mindfulness well trained. He is mindful of the out-breath, with mindfulness well trained. Whether the breath is long or short, he knows the entire body while breathing in; in knowing the entire body while breathing in, he trains well. He knows the entire body while breathing out; in knowing the entire body while breathing out, he trains well. He knows the calming of all bodily formations while breathing in; in knowing the calming of all bodily formations while breathing in, he trains well. He knows the calming of all bodily formations while breathing out; in knowing the calming of all bodily formations while breathing out, he trains well. He knows joy. He knows happiness. He knows mental formations. He knows the calming of mental formations while breathing in; in knowing the calming of mental formations while breathing in, he trains well. He knows the calming of mental formations while breathing out; in knowing the calming of mental formations while breathing out, he trains well. He knows the mind. He knows the gladdening of the mind. He knows the concentrating of the mind. He knows the liberating of the mind while breathing in; in knowing the liberating of the mind while breathing in, he trains well. He knows the liberating of the mind while breathing out; in knowing the liberating of the mind while breathing out, he trains well. He contemplates impermanence. He contemplates abandonment. He contemplates dispassion. He contemplates cessation while breathing in; in contemplating cessation while breathing in, he trains well. He contemplates cessation while breathing out; in contemplating cessation while breathing out, he trains well. This is called cultivating ānāpānasmṛti: stilling of the body, stilling of the mind, with initial thought and sustained thought, tranquility and purity, and the perception pertaining to the portion of illumination cultivated to fulfillment."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (804)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should cultivate ānāpānasmṛti. When ānāpānasmṛti is cultivated and extensively cultivated, it cuts off all discursive thoughts. How does cultivating and extensively cultivating ānāpānasmṛti cut off all discursive thoughts? If a bhikṣu dwells near a village or town," as extensively explained above, up to "in contemplating cessation while breathing out, he trains well. This is called cultivating and extensively cultivating ānāpānasmṛti to cut off all discursive thoughts."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

Just as with cutting off discursive thoughts, so too with being unshakeable, attaining great fruit and great merit, attaining the ambrosia and ultimate ambrosia, attaining two fruits, four fruits, and seven fruits: each of these sūtras is also as stated above.

---

### (805)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Have you been cultivating the ānāpānasmṛti that I have taught?"

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Ariṣṭa sitting in the assembly. He rose from his seat, arranged his robes, paid respects to the Buddha, knelt on his right knee, placed his palms together, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I have cultivated the ānāpānasmṛti that the World-Honored One has taught."

The Buddha said to the bhikṣu Ariṣṭa: "How have you cultivated the ānāpānasmṛti that I taught?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Regarding past formations, I do not look back with longing. Regarding future formations, I do not give rise to delight. Regarding present formations, I do not give rise to attachment. Regarding internal and external perceptions of obstruction, I skillfully and correctly eliminate them. I have thus cultivated the ānāpānasmṛti taught by the World-Honored One."

The Buddha said to the bhikṣu Ariṣṭa: "You have indeed cultivated the ānāpānasmṛti that I taught; it is not that you have not cultivated it. However, bhikṣu, beyond the ānāpānasmṛti you have cultivated, there is something more excellent and superior. What is the more excellent practice that surpasses the ānāpānasmṛti cultivated by Ariṣṭa? This bhikṣu, dwelling near a town or village," as extensively explained before, up to "in contemplating cessation while breathing out, he trains well. This, bhikṣu Ariṣṭa, is what is more excellent and surpasses the ānāpānasmṛti you have cultivated."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (806)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One, in the early morning, donned his robes and took his bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, he returned to the monastery, put away his robes and bowl, washed his feet, took his sitting mat, and entered the Andha Grove. He sat beneath a tree for daytime meditation.

At that time, the Venerable Kapphiṇa also, in the early morning, donned his robes and took his bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. He returned, put away his robes and bowl, washed his feet, took his sitting mat, and entered the Andha Grove. He sat beneath a tree in meditation, not far from the Buddha, with upright body, unmoving, body and mind straight and upright, dwelling in excellent meditation.

At that time, many bhikṣus, rising from meditation in the afternoon, went to the Buddha, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, and sat to one side.

The Buddha said to the bhikṣus: "Do you see the Venerable Kapphiṇa? Not far from me, sitting upright with body erect, body and mind unmoving, abiding in an excellent abiding."

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! We have frequently seen that venerable one sitting upright with body erect, well-composed in body, not leaning, not moving, with concentrated and excellent mind."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu cultivates samādhi and his body and mind are settled, not leaning, not moving, abiding in an excellent abiding, this bhikṣu attains this samādhi without arduous effort, obtaining it at will."

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "What samādhi is it that a bhikṣu, having attained it, has body and mind unmoving, abiding in an excellent abiding?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu dwells near a village, in the early morning dons his robes and takes his bowl, enters the village to beg for food, then returns to the monastery, puts away his robes and bowl, washes his feet, enters the forest, or a quiet room, or sits in the open, reflecting and fixing mindfulness, up to contemplating cessation while breathing out, training well: this is called the samādhi by which a bhikṣu, sitting upright in meditation, has body and mind unmoving, abiding in an excellent abiding."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (807)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Icchānaṅgala Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I wish to sit in meditation for two months. No bhikṣus should come and go, except for the bhikṣu who brings food and at the time of the upoṣadha." At that time, after the World-Honored One had spoken these words, he sat in meditation for two months. No bhikṣu dared to come and go, except for bringing food and at the time of the upoṣadha.

At that time, when the World-Honored One's two months of meditation had passed, he emerged from meditation and sat before the Sangha of bhikṣus. He addressed the bhikṣus: "If renunciants of other traditions come and ask you, 'In what manner did the śramaṇa Gautama sit in meditation during these two months?' you should answer them: 'The Tathāgata sat in meditation for two months, dwelling in reflection on ānāpānasmṛti.' Why is this? During these two months, I was mindful of ānāpānasmṛti, dwelling extensively in reflection. When breathing in, I was mindful of the in-breath and knew it as it truly is. When breathing out, I was mindful of the out-breath and knew it as it truly is. Whether long or short, being aware of the entire body while breathing in, I was mindful and knew it as it truly is. Being aware of the entire body while breathing out, I was mindful and knew it as it truly is. Calming bodily formations while breathing in, I was mindful and knew it as it truly is, up to contemplating cessation while breathing out, I knew it as it truly is. Having fully known all this, I then had this thought: "This is a coarse mode of dwelling in reflection. I shall now, having stilled this coarse reflection, further cultivate a more subtle mode of dwelling and dwell in it."

"At that time, having stilled the coarse reflection, I then entered into the more subtle reflection and dwelt extensively in it. Then three devas of supremely excellent form came to my presence after nightfall. One deva said: 'The time has come for the śramaṇa Gautama.' Another deva said: 'This is not the time having come; the time is approaching.' The third deva said: 'It is not the time having come, nor is the time approaching. This is a dwelling in meditation; this is the tranquil cessation of an arhat!'"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "If one were to speak correctly of the noble abiding, the divine abiding, the brahmā abiding, the learner's abiding, the non-learner's abiding, the Tathāgata's abiding; that which learners who have not yet attained will attain, have not yet arrived at will arrive at, have not yet realized will realize; and that non-learners dwell in the happiness of the present dharma: it is ānāpānasmṛti. This is the correct statement. Why is this? Ānāpānasmṛti is the noble abiding, the divine abiding, the brahmā abiding, up to the non-learner's dwelling in the happiness of the present dharma."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (808)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Kapilavatthu, in the Nyagrodha Park.

At that time, the Śākyan Mahānāma went to the Venerable Kāmabhū, bowed at the Venerable Kāmabhū's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Venerable Kāmabhū: "How is it, Venerable Kāmabhū? Is the learner's abiding the same as the Tathāgata's abiding, or is the learner's abiding different from the Tathāgata's abiding?"

The Venerable Kāmabhū answered: "Mahānāma, the learner's abiding is different from the Tathāgata's abiding. Mahānāma, the learner's abiding is to dwell mostly with the five hindrances cut off. The Tathāgata's abiding is that regarding the five hindrances, they have been cut off and fully known, their roots destroyed, like cutting off the top of a palmyra tree so that they will never grow again, becoming dharmas that will not arise in the future.

"At one time, the World-Honored One was dwelling in the Icchānaṅgala Grove. At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: 'I wish to sit in meditation for two months here in the Icchānaṅgala Grove. You bhikṣus should not come and go, except for the bhikṣu who brings food and at the time of the upoṣadha.'" He explained in detail as before, up to "the non-learner's dwelling in the happiness of the present dharma. For this reason, Mahānāma, the learner's abiding is different from the Tathāgata's abiding."

The Śākyan Mahānāma, having heard what the Venerable Kāmabhū said, rejoiced, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (809)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Vajji village, beside the Vaggumudā River, in the Sālala Forest.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the bhikṣus the contemplation of impurity and praised the contemplation of impurity, saying: "Bhikṣus, if you cultivate the contemplation of impurity and extensively cultivate it, you will attain great fruit and great merit."

At that time, the bhikṣus, having cultivated the contemplation of impurity, became extremely disgusted with the body. Some killed themselves with knives, some took poison, some hanged themselves with ropes, some threw themselves from cliffs, and some had other bhikṣus kill them.

There was a certain bhikṣu who developed extreme disgust and revulsion toward the impure body. He went to Migalaṇḍika, the son of a brāhmaṇa, and said: "Good sir! If you can kill me, my robes and bowl will belong to you."

Then Migalaṇḍika killed that bhikṣu. Taking his knife to the bank of the Vaggumudā River to wash it, Māra the deva stood in the air and praised Migalaṇḍika: "Excellent! Excellent! Good sir! You have gained immeasurable merit, for you have enabled the śramaṇas, disciples of the Śākyan, who are virtuous and of good conduct, those not yet crossed over to cross over, those not yet liberated to be liberated, those not yet refreshed to gain refreshment, those not yet in nirvāṇa to attain nirvāṇa. All the valuable robes, bowls, and miscellaneous belongings will be yours."

Then Migalaṇḍika, having heard this praise, increased in his evil wrong views and had this thought: "Truly I have performed a great deed of merit, enabling the śramaṇas, disciples of the Śākyan, who are virtuous and of meritorious conduct, those not yet crossed over to cross over, those not yet liberated to be liberated, those not yet refreshed to gain refreshment, those not yet in nirvāṇa to attain nirvāṇa, and all robes, bowls, and miscellaneous belongings are mine." Then, with a sharp knife in hand, he went through the various dwellings, walking paths, individual rooms, and meditation chambers, and seeing the bhikṣus, spoke thus: "Which śramaṇas of virtue and good conduct, not yet crossed over, can I enable to cross over? Not yet liberated, I will liberate. Not yet refreshed, I will grant refreshment. Not yet in nirvāṇa, I will bring to nirvāṇa."

At that time, bhikṣus who were disgusted with their bodies all came out of their rooms and said to Migalaṇḍika: "I have not yet crossed over; you should help me cross over. I have not yet been liberated; you should liberate me. I have not yet been refreshed; you should grant me refreshment. I have not yet attained nirvāṇa; you should bring me to nirvāṇa."

Then Migalaṇḍika killed those bhikṣus with his sharp knife, one after another, until he had killed sixty people.

At that time, the World-Honored One, on the fifteenth day, at the time of the upoṣadha recitation, sat before the assembly of monks and addressed the Venerable Ānanda: "For what cause and what reason have the bhikṣus become progressively fewer, progressively diminished, progressively exhausted?"

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One taught the bhikṣus to cultivate the contemplation of impurity and praised the contemplation of impurity. The bhikṣus, having cultivated the contemplation of impurity, became extremely disgusted with their bodies," explained in detail up to "killing sixty bhikṣus. World-Honored One, it is for this cause and reason that the bhikṣus have become progressively fewer, progressively diminished, progressively exhausted. May the World-Honored One teach another dharma, so that the bhikṣus, having heard it, will diligently cultivate wisdom, delight in receiving the true Dharma, and delight in abiding in the true Dharma."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Therefore, I shall now teach step by step the abiding in the subtle, which leads to sequential awakening, and causes already-arisen and not-yet-arisen unwholesome dharmas to quickly come to rest, just as a great heavenly rain can settle dust that has arisen and has not yet arisen. In the same way, bhikṣu, cultivating the subtle abiding causes all arisen and not-yet-arisen unwholesome dharmas to come to rest. Ānanda, what is the subtle abiding that, when extensively cultivated, leads to sequential awakening and causes already-arisen and not-yet-arisen unwholesome dharmas to come to rest? It is the abiding in ānāpānasmṛti."

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "How does one cultivate the abiding in ānāpānasmṛti so that it leads to sequential awakening and causes already-arisen and not-yet-arisen unwholesome dharmas to come to rest?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "If a bhikṣu dwells near a village," as extensively explained before, up to "contemplating cessation while breathing out, he trains."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (810)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling beside the Vaggumudā River in the Vajji area, in the Sālala Forest.

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda, alone in a quiet place, engaged in meditative reflection, had this thought: "Is there perhaps one dharma which, when cultivated and extensively cultivated, brings four dharmas to fulfillment; and when four dharmas are fulfilled, seven dharmas are fulfilled; and when seven dharmas are fulfilled, two dharmas are fulfilled?"

Then the Venerable Ānanda, emerging from meditation, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, while alone in a quiet place, engaged in meditative reflection, I had this thought: 'Is there perhaps one dharma which, when extensively cultivated, brings four dharmas to fulfillment, up to two dharmas being fulfilled?' I now ask the World-Honored One: is there one dharma which, when extensively cultivated, can bring about the fulfillment, up to two dharmas being fulfilled?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "There is one dharma which, when extensively cultivated, can bring about the fulfillment, up to two dharmas being fulfilled. What is this one dharma? It is ānāpānasmṛti. When extensively cultivated, it can bring the four foundations of mindfulness to fulfillment. When the four foundations of mindfulness are fulfilled, the seven factors of awakening are fulfilled. When the seven factors of awakening are fulfilled, clear knowledge and liberation are fulfilled. How does cultivating ānāpānasmṛti fulfill the four foundations of mindfulness? This bhikṣu dwells near a village, up to contemplating cessation while breathing out, training. Ānanda, when this noble disciple is mindful of the in-breath, he trains as one mindful of the in-breath. When mindful of the out-breath, he trains as one mindful of the out-breath. Whether long or short, being aware of all bodily formations, when mindful of the in-breath, he trains as one mindful of the in-breath. When mindful of the out-breath, he trains as one mindful of the out-breath. When calming bodily formations while breathing in, he trains as one calming bodily formations while breathing in. When calming bodily formations while breathing out, he trains as one calming bodily formations while breathing out. At that time, the noble disciple dwells contemplating the body in the body. As for what is other than the body, he also likewise reflects by comparing it with the body.

"When at times the noble disciple knows joy, knows happiness, knows mental formations, and knows the calming of mental formations, when mindful of the in-breath, he trains as one calming mental formations while breathing in. When calming mental formations while breathing out, he trains as one calming mental formations while breathing out. At that time, the noble disciple dwells contemplating feelings in feelings. As for what is other than feelings, he also reflects on feelings by comparing them with the body.

"When at times the noble disciple knows the mind, knows the gladdening of the mind, knows the concentrating of the mind, and knows the liberating of the mind, when mindful of the in-breath, he trains as one mindful of the in-breath. When liberating the mind while breathing out, he trains as one liberating the mind while breathing out. At that time, the noble disciple dwells contemplating the mind in the mind. As for what is other than the mind, he also reflects by comparing it with the mind.

"When the noble disciple at times contemplates impermanence, abandonment, dispassion, and cessation, and dwells training as one contemplating impermanence, abandonment, dispassion, and cessation, at that time, the noble disciple dwells contemplating dharmas in dharmas. As for what is other than dharmas, he also reflects by comparing them with dharmas. This is called cultivating ānāpānasmṛti to fulfill the four foundations of mindfulness."

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "Thus cultivating ānāpānasmṛti fulfills the four foundations of mindfulness. How does cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness fulfill the seven factors of awakening?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "When a bhikṣu dwells contemplating the body in the body with mindful abiding, and having established mindfulness, abides without forgetting, at that time he cultivates the mindfulness factor of awakening with skillful means. Having cultivated the mindfulness factor of awakening, the mindfulness factor of awakening is fulfilled. When the mindfulness factor of awakening is fulfilled, he investigates, examines, and discerns dharmas. At that time he cultivates the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening with skillful means. Having cultivated the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening, the investigation-of-dharmas factor of awakening is fulfilled. When he has investigated, examined, and discerned dharmas, he attains diligent effort through skillful means. At that time he cultivates the effort factor of awakening with skillful means. Having cultivated the effort factor of awakening, the effort factor of awakening is fulfilled. When he has applied diligent effort through skillful means, the mind becomes joyful. At that time he cultivates the joy factor of awakening with skillful means. Having cultivated the joy factor of awakening, the joy factor of awakening is fulfilled. When joyful, body and mind become tranquil. At that time he cultivates the tranquility factor of awakening with skillful means. Having cultivated the tranquility factor of awakening, the tranquility factor of awakening is fulfilled. When body and mind are happy, he attains samādhi. At that time he cultivates the concentration factor of awakening. Having cultivated the concentration factor of awakening, the concentration factor of awakening is fulfilled. When the concentration factor of awakening is fulfilled, craving and worry are extinguished, and he attains even-minded equanimity. At that time he cultivates the equanimity factor of awakening with skillful means. Having cultivated the equanimity factor of awakening, the equanimity factor of awakening is fulfilled. Contemplating feelings, mind, and dharmas in dharmas as foundations of mindfulness is also explained in the same way. This is called cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness to fulfill the seven factors of awakening."

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "This is called cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness to fulfill the seven factors of awakening. How does cultivating the seven factors of awakening fulfill clear knowledge and liberation?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "If a bhikṣu cultivates the mindfulness factor of awakening, based on seclusion, based on dispassion, based on cessation, tending toward relinquishment; having cultivated the mindfulness factor of awakening, clear knowledge and liberation are fulfilled. Up to cultivating the equanimity factor of awakening, based on seclusion, based on dispassion, based on cessation, tending toward relinquishment; having thus cultivated the equanimity factor of awakening, clear knowledge and liberation are fulfilled. Ānanda, this is called dharmas being of the same kind, dharmas mutually nourishing each other. Thus these thirteen dharmas, with one dharma as the supreme foundation, one dharma as the gateway, progressively advance, being cultivated to fulfillment."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (811–812)

Likewise, what a certain bhikṣu asked, and what the Buddha asked the bhikṣus, in three sūtras, are also as stated above.

---

### (813)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Kimbila settlement, in the Kimbila Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the Venerable Kimbila: "I shall now explain the diligent cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness. Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it for you." At that time, the Venerable Kimbila remained silent. This happened three times.

Then the Venerable Ānanda said to the Venerable Kimbila: "The Great Teacher is now addressing you!" He said this three times.

The Venerable Kimbila said to the Venerable Ānanda: "I already know, Venerable Ānanda! I already know, Venerable Gautama!"

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, now is the time! Now is the time, Well-Gone One! May you please teach the bhikṣus about the diligent cultivation of the four foundations of mindfulness. The bhikṣus, having heard it, will receive and practice accordingly."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it for you. When a bhikṣu is mindful of the in-breath, he trains as one mindful of the in-breath, up to contemplating cessation while breathing out, he trains as one contemplating cessation while breathing out. At that time, when the noble disciple is mindful of the in-breath, he trains as one mindful of the in-breath, up to calming bodily formations while breathing out, he trains as one calming bodily formations while breathing out. At that time, the noble disciple dwells contemplating the body in the body. When the noble disciple has dwelt contemplating the body in the body, he thus knows and skillfully reflects internally."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "Suppose a person riding a chariot came rushing from the east. At that time, would he trample over mounds of earth?"

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "Indeed so, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "In the same way, the noble disciple, when mindful of the in-breath, trains as one mindful of the in-breath, and so forth up to skillfully reflecting internally. When at that time the noble disciple knows joy, up to training in calming mental formations, the noble disciple dwells contemplating feelings in feelings. When the noble disciple has dwelt contemplating feelings in feelings, he thus knows and skillfully reflects internally. Suppose a person riding a chariot came rushing from the south. How about it, Ānanda? Would he trample over mounds of earth?"

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "Indeed so, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "In the same way, the noble disciple dwells contemplating feelings in feelings, knowing and skillfully reflecting internally. When the noble disciple knows the mind, gladdens the mind, concentrates the mind, and knows the liberating of the mind while breathing in, he trains as one knowing the liberating of the mind while breathing in. When liberating the mind while breathing out, he trains as one liberating the mind while breathing out. At that time, the noble disciple dwells contemplating the mind in the mind. When the noble disciple has dwelt contemplating the mind in the mind, he knows and skillfully reflects internally. Suppose a person riding a chariot came from the west. Would he trample over mounds of earth?"

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "Indeed so, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "In the same way, the noble disciple knows the mind, up to training as one liberating the mind while breathing out. At that time, the noble disciple dwells contemplating the mind in the mind, knowing and skillfully reflecting internally. When, regarding body, feelings, and mind, craving and worry are extinguished and relinquished, at that time the noble disciple dwells contemplating dharmas in dharmas. When the noble disciple has dwelt contemplating dharmas in dharmas, he knows and skillfully reflects internally. Ānanda, suppose at a crossroads there is a mound of earth, and a person riding a chariot came rushing from the north. Would he trample over the mound of earth?"

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "Indeed so, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "In the same way, the noble disciple dwells contemplating dharmas in dharmas, knowing and skillfully reflecting internally. Ānanda, this is called a bhikṣu diligently and skillfully cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (814)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should cultivate ānāpānasmṛti. When ānāpānasmṛti is cultivated and extensively cultivated, the body does not become fatigued, the eyes are not afflicted, one dwells happily in accordance with contemplation, one happily knows, and one is not attached to pleasure. How does cultivating ānāpānasmṛti lead to the body not becoming fatigued, the eyes not being afflicted, dwelling happily in accordance with contemplation, happily knowing, and not being attached to pleasure? This bhikṣu dwells near a village, up to contemplating cessation while breathing out, he trains as one contemplating cessation while breathing out. This is called cultivating ānāpānasmṛti so that the body does not become fatigued, the eyes are not afflicted, one dwells happily in accordance with contemplation, one happily knows, and one is not attached to pleasure. One who thus cultivates ānāpānasmṛti attains great fruit and great merit. This bhikṣu, if he wishes to attain separation from desires and unwholesome dharmas, with initial thought and sustained thought, with joy and happiness born of seclusion, and to dwell having fulfilled the first dhyāna, this bhikṣu should cultivate ānāpānasmṛti. Thus cultivating ānāpānasmṛti, he attains great fruit and great merit. This bhikṣu, if he wishes to attain the second, third, and fourth dhyānas, loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity, the base of boundless space, the base of boundless consciousness, the base of nothingness, the base of neither-perception-nor-non-perception; the complete destruction of three fetters to attain the fruit of stream-entry; the complete destruction of three fetters, with greed, hatred, and delusion weakened, to attain the fruit of once-returning; the complete exhaustion of the five lower fetters to attain the fruit of non-returning; to attain immeasurable kinds of supernormal powers, the divine ear, knowledge of others' minds, knowledge of past lives, knowledge of birth and death, and knowledge of the exhaustion of the defilements: such a bhikṣu should cultivate ānāpānasmṛti. Thus cultivating ānāpānasmṛti, he attains great fruit and great merit."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (815)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, for the summer retreat. At that time, many senior elder disciples of the World-Honored One were undertaking the retreat beneath trees and in caves to the left and right of the World-Honored One. At that time, many young bhikṣus went to the Buddha, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, and sat to one side.

The Buddha taught various dharmas for the young bhikṣus, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening them. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened them, he remained silent.

The young bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and approved. They rose from their seats, paid respects, and departed.

The young bhikṣus went to the senior bhikṣus, bowed at the feet of the senior ones, and sat to one side.

At that time, the senior bhikṣus had this thought: "We should take these young bhikṣus under our care. Perhaps one person takes charge of one, or one person takes charge of two, three, or many." Having had this thought, they accordingly took them under their care. Some took charge of one, some of two, three, or many. Some senior ones even took charge of sixty people.

At that time, the World-Honored One, on the fifteenth day, at the time of the upoṣadha, spread his seat before the great assembly and sat down.

At that time, the World-Honored One surveyed the bhikṣus and addressed them: "Excellent! Excellent! I am now pleased that the bhikṣus are conducting proper affairs. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should be diligently energetic."

In this Śrāvastī, when the Kārttika month was complete, the bhikṣus wandering in various places heard that the World-Honored One had completed the summer retreat at Śrāvastī. When the Kārttika month was completed, they finished making their robes, and taking their robes and bowls, wandered among the people toward Śrāvastī. They gradually arrived at Śrāvastī, put away their robes and bowls, washed their feet, and went to the World-Honored One, bowed their heads at his feet, and sat to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught various dharmas for the wandering bhikṣus, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening them. Having done so, he remained silent.

At that time, the wandering bhikṣus, having heard the Buddha's teaching, rejoiced and approved, rose from their seats, paid respects, and departed. They went to the senior bhikṣus, bowed their heads at their feet, and sat to one side.

At that time, the senior ones had this thought: "We should take these wandering bhikṣus under our care. Perhaps one person takes charge of one, or two, three, or even many." They accordingly took them in. Some took charge of one, or two, three, or even up to sixty. The senior bhikṣus received the wandering bhikṣus and instructed and taught them, skillfully knowing the proper sequence.

At that time, the World-Honored One, on the fifteenth day, at the time of the upoṣadha, spread his seat before the great assembly and sat down. He surveyed the assembly of bhikṣus and addressed them: "Excellent! Excellent! Bhikṣus! I rejoice in the proper affairs you are conducting. I am delighted by the proper affairs you are conducting. Bhikṣus, the Buddhas of the past also had assemblies of bhikṣus who conducted proper affairs just like this present assembly. The Buddhas of the future will also have assemblies that conduct proper affairs just like this present assembly. Why is this? In this present assembly, among the senior bhikṣus, there are those who have attained the first dhyāna, the second dhyāna, the third dhyāna, the fourth dhyāna, loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, equanimity, the base of boundless space, the base of boundless consciousness, the base of nothingness, the base of neither-perception-nor-non-perception, and dwell having fulfilled them. There are bhikṣus who, with the complete destruction of three fetters, have attained the fruit of stream-entry, who do not fall into bad destinies, who are determined and rightly directed toward three awakenings, who will be reborn among gods and humans seven times and then make an end of suffering. There are bhikṣus who, with the complete destruction of three fetters, and with greed, hatred, and delusion weakened, have attained the fruit of once-returning. There are bhikṣus who, with the complete exhaustion of the five lower fetters, have attained the fruit of non-returning, who attain parinirvāṇa in that realm and do not return to be reborn in this world. There are bhikṣus who have attained immeasurable supernormal powers and realms, the divine ear, knowledge of others' minds, knowledge of past lives, knowledge of birth and death, and knowledge of the exhaustion of the defilements. There are bhikṣus who cultivate the contemplation of impurity to cut off desire, cultivate the mind of loving-kindness to cut off hatred, cultivate the perception of impermanence to cut off the conceit 'I am,' and cultivate ānāpānasmṛti to cut off discursive thoughts. How, bhikṣus, does cultivating ānāpānasmṛti cut off discursive thoughts? This bhikṣu dwells near a village, up to contemplating cessation while breathing out, he trains as one contemplating cessation while breathing out. This is called cultivating ānāpānasmṛti to cut off discursive thoughts."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (816)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are three trainings. What are the three? They are the higher training in morality, the higher training in mind, and the higher training in wisdom."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "One who is complete in the three trainings,
> That bhikṣu walks the right path.
> In the higher morality, mind, and wisdom,
> In these three dharmas diligently striving,
> Heroic, resolute, a firm fortress,
> Always guarding the sense faculties.
> As by day so by night,
> As by night so by day,
> As before so after,
> As after so before,
> As above so below,
> As below so above.
> Immeasurable samādhis
> Illuminate all directions.
> This is called the path of awakening,
> The foremost gathering of coolness.
> Abandoning the quarrels of ignorance,
> His mind is well liberated.
> I am the awakened one for the world,
> Complete in knowledge and conduct.
> With right mindfulness, dwelling without forgetfulness,
> His mind attains liberation.
> When the body breaks and life ends,
> It is like a lamp extinguished when its fuel is spent."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (817)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are also three trainings. What are the three? They are the higher training in morality, the higher training in mind, and the higher training in wisdom. What is the higher training in morality? If a bhikṣu abides in morality, in the discipline of the prātimokṣa, complete in deportment and proper conduct, seeing even subtle offenses and generating fear, receiving and upholding the training rules. What is the higher training in mind? If a bhikṣu, separated from desires and unwholesome dharmas, up to dwelling having fulfilled the fourth dhyāna. What is the higher training in wisdom? This bhikṣu truly knows the noble truth of suffering, truly knows the noble truths of origination, cessation, and the path. This is called the higher training in wisdom."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse, as stated above.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (818)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are bhikṣus who have the higher training in morality but not the higher training in mind or wisdom. There are those with the higher training in morality and mind but not the higher training in wisdom. When a noble disciple applies skillful means in accordance with the higher training in wisdom and dwells in its accomplishment, the higher training in morality and the higher training in mind are cultivated to fulfillment. When a noble disciple thus applies skillful means in accordance with the higher training in wisdom and dwells in its accomplishment, he lives with the supreme lifespan of wisdom."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (819)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Beyond the more than two hundred and fifty precepts, which are recited in sequence at the half-monthly recitation of the Prātimokṣa Sūtra, for those who wish to train by their own choice, I teach three trainings that can encompass all precepts. What are the three? They are the higher training in morality, the higher training in mind, and the higher training in wisdom."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (820)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "What is the higher training in morality? A bhikṣu who is earnest about morality, with morality supreme, but not earnest about concentration, with concentration not supreme, and not earnest about wisdom, with wisdom not supreme; regarding those various minor and subtle precepts, if he transgresses he immediately repents. Why is that? I do not say he is incapable. If that morality accords with the holy life, benefits the holy life, and enables the holy life to endure long, then such a bhikṣu, with morality firm, morality as his teacher, morality constantly abiding, morality always compliant in arising, receives and upholds the training. With such knowledge and such vision, he destroys three fetters, namely: identity-view, attachment to rules and rituals, and doubt. With the destruction of these three fetters, he attains the fruit of stream-entry, not falling into bad destinies, determined and rightly directed toward three awakenings, traversing seven births among gods and humans, and making an end of suffering. This is called the higher training in morality.

"What is the higher training in mind? This bhikṣu is earnest about morality, with morality supreme, earnest about concentration, with concentration supreme, but not earnest about wisdom, with wisdom not supreme; regarding those various minor and subtle precepts, up to receiving and upholding the training rules. With such knowledge and such vision, he destroys the five lower fetters, namely: identity-view, attachment to rules and rituals, doubt, sensual desire, and ill will. With the destruction of these five lower fetters, he is reborn and attains parinirvāṇa, as a non-returner, not returning to this world. This is called the higher training in mind.

"What is the higher training in wisdom? This bhikṣu is earnest about morality, with morality supreme, earnest about concentration, with concentration supreme, earnest about wisdom, with wisdom supreme. With such knowledge and such vision, his mind is liberated from the defilement of sensual desire, his mind is liberated from the defilement of existence, his mind is liberated from the defilement of ignorance. Being liberated, he has the knowledge of liberation: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further existence.' This is called the higher training in wisdom."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (821)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Beyond the more than two hundred and fifty precepts, which are recited in sequence at the half-monthly recitation of the Prātimokṣa Sūtra, for those good men who wish to train according to their own desire, I teach three trainings. If one trains in these three, they encompass and contain all training rules. What are the three? They are the higher training in morality, the higher training in mind, and the higher training in wisdom.

"What is the higher training in morality? This bhikṣu is earnest about morality, with morality supreme, but not earnest about concentration, with concentration not supreme, and not earnest about wisdom, with wisdom not supreme; regarding those various minor and subtle precepts, up to receiving and upholding the training rules. With such knowledge and such vision, he destroys three fetters, namely: identity-view, attachment to rules and rituals, and doubt; greed, hatred, and delusion are weakened, and he accomplishes the path of one seed. One who has not yet awakened at that stage is called a once-returner. One who has not yet awakened at that stage is called a family-to-family one. One who has not yet awakened at that stage is called a seven-lives one. One who has not yet awakened at that stage is called a dharma-follower. One who has not yet awakened at that stage is called a faith-follower. This is called the higher training in morality.

"What is the higher training in mind? This bhikṣu is earnest about morality, with morality supreme, earnest about concentration, with concentration supreme, but not earnest about wisdom, with wisdom not supreme; regarding those various minor and subtle precepts, up to receiving and upholding the training rules. With such knowledge and such vision, he destroys the five lower fetters, namely: identity-view, attachment to rules and rituals, doubt, sensual desire, and ill will. With the destruction of these five lower fetters, he is able to attain intermediate nirvāṇa. One who has not yet awakened at that stage attains nirvāṇa at birth. One who has not yet awakened at that stage attains nirvāṇa without exertion. One who has not yet awakened at that stage attains nirvāṇa with exertion. One who has not yet awakened at that stage attains nirvāṇa by going upstream. This is called the higher training in mind.

"What is the higher training in wisdom? This bhikṣu is earnest about morality, with morality supreme, earnest about concentration, with concentration supreme, earnest about wisdom, with wisdom supreme. With such knowledge and such vision, his mind is liberated from the defilement of sensual desire, his mind is liberated from the defilement of existence, his mind is liberated from the defilement of ignorance. Being liberated, he has the knowledge of liberation: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further existence.' This is called the higher training in wisdom."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (822)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu abides complete in morality, well maintaining the prātimokṣa, complete in deportment and proper conduct, seeing even subtle offenses and generating fear; when a bhikṣu abides complete in morality, well maintaining the prātimokṣa, complete in deportment and proper conduct, seeing even subtle offenses and generating fear, equally receiving and training in the precepts, this brings the three trainings to cultivated fulfillment. What are the three? The higher training in morality, the higher training in mind, and the higher training in wisdom.

"What is the higher training in morality? This bhikṣu has morality fulfilled, but little concentration and little wisdom; regarding those various minor and subtle precepts, up to receiving and upholding the training rules. With such knowledge and such vision, he destroys three fetters, namely: identity-view, attachment to rules and rituals, and doubt. With the destruction of these three fetters, he attains the fruit of stream-entry, not falling into bad destinies, determined and rightly directed toward three awakenings, traversing seven births among gods and humans, and making an end of suffering.

"What is the higher training in mind? This bhikṣu has concentration fulfilled, samādhi fulfilled, but little wisdom; regarding those various minor and subtle precepts, if he transgresses he immediately repents, up to receiving and upholding the training rules. With such knowledge and such vision, he destroys the five lower fetters, namely: identity-view, attachment to rules and rituals, doubt, sensual desire, and ill will. With the destruction of these five lower fetters, he is reborn and attains parinirvāṇa, as a non-returner, not returning to be reborn in this world. This is called the higher training in mind.

"What is the higher training in wisdom? This bhikṣu has the training in morality fulfilled, concentration fulfilled, and wisdom fulfilled. With such knowledge and such vision, his mind is liberated from the defilement of sensual desire, his mind is liberated from the defilement of existence, his mind is liberated from the defilement of ignorance. Being liberated, he has the knowledge of liberation: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further existence.' This is called the higher training in wisdom."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (823)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu abides complete in morality, well maintaining the prātimokṣa, complete in deportment and proper conduct, seeing even subtle offenses and generating fear, receiving and upholding the training rules, he fulfills the three trainings. What are the three? The higher training in morality, the higher training in mind, and the higher training in wisdom.

"What is the higher training in morality? This bhikṣu has morality fulfilled, but little concentration and little wisdom; regarding those various minor and subtle precepts, up to receiving and upholding the training rules. With such knowledge and such vision, he destroys three fetters, and greed, hatred, and delusion are weakened. He accomplishes the path of one seed. One who has not yet awakened at that stage attains once-returning. One who has not yet awakened at that stage is called a family-to-family one. One who has not yet awakened at that stage attains stream-entry. One who has not yet awakened at that stage attains the dharma-follower stage. One who has not yet awakened at that stage attains the faith-follower stage. This is called the higher training in morality.

"What is the higher training in mind? This bhikṣu has morality fulfilled, concentration fulfilled, but little wisdom; regarding those various minor and subtle precepts, up to receiving and upholding the training rules. With such knowledge and such vision, he destroys the five lower fetters, namely: identity-view, attachment to rules and rituals, doubt, sensual desire, and ill will. With the destruction of these five lower fetters, he attains intermediate nirvāṇa. One who has not yet awakened at that stage attains nirvāṇa at birth. One who has not yet awakened at that stage attains nirvāṇa without exertion. One who has not yet awakened at that stage attains nirvāṇa with exertion. One who has not yet awakened at that stage attains nirvāṇa by going upstream. This is called the higher training in mind.

"What is the higher training in wisdom? This bhikṣu has the training in morality fulfilled, concentration fulfilled, and wisdom fulfilled. With such knowledge and such vision, his mind is liberated from the defilement of sensual desire, his mind is liberated from the defilement of existence, his mind is liberated from the defilement of ignorance. Being liberated, he has the knowledge of liberation: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further existence.' This is called the higher training in wisdom."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (824)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are two trainings. What are the two? They are the higher training in deportment and the higher training in the prātimokṣa."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "When the trainee trains in the precepts,
> He walks the straight path in accordance.
> With dedicated and careful diligent effort,
> He well guards his own person.
> First he attains the knowledge of defilement-exhaustion,
> Then the ultimate knowledge of non-knowing.
> Attaining the liberation of non-knowing,
> Knowledge and vision have entirely crossed over.
> Accomplishing unshakeable liberation,
> All bonds of existence are extinguished.
> His faculties are complete,
> His faculties are tranquil and happy.
> Bearing this final body,
> He conquers and subdues the hosts of Māra."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (825)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Training in the precepts brings much merit and benefit. Wisdom is supreme. Liberation is firm. Mindfulness is paramount. If a bhikṣu's training in the precepts brings merit and benefit, with wisdom as supreme, liberation as firm, and mindfulness as paramount, this brings the three trainings to fulfillment. What are the three? They are the higher training in morality, the higher training in mind, and the higher training in wisdom."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "Training in the precepts brings merit and benefit,
> Concentrated thought in samādhi and dhyāna,
> Wisdom is the most supreme,
> In this present life, the final existence.
> The sage bearing the final body
> Conquers Māra and crosses to the other shore."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (826)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "Bhikṣus, what is training in the precepts that brings merit and benefit? The Great Teacher has established precepts for the śrāvakas, namely: to discipline the Sangha, to fully discipline the Sangha, to inspire faith in those without faith, to increase the faith of those who have faith, to tame wrongdoers, so that those with a sense of shame can dwell happily, for protection against defilements in the present life, for the correct counteraction of future defilements,
