# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Thirty

---

### (830)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Bhaṅgajā Grove at Bhaṅgajā.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the bhikṣus the Dharma related to precepts and praised the establishment of precepts.

At that time, the Venerable Kassapiya, who was staying at the Bhaṅgajā settlement, heard the World-Honored One teaching the Dharma related to precepts and praising those precepts. In his mind he was extremely unable to endure it and was displeased, saying: "This ascetic excessively praises these precepts and excessively lays down these precepts."

At that time, the World-Honored One, having stayed at the Bhaṅgajā settlement as long as he wished, departed toward Śrāvastī, traveling by stages until he reached Śrāvastī, Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then the Venerable Kassapiya, not long after the World-Honored One had departed, felt remorse in his mind: "I have now lost my benefit and gained great misfortune. When the World-Honored One was teaching the Dharma related to precepts and praising the establishment of precepts, I was unable to endure it in my mind toward the World-Honored One and was displeased, with no joy in my heart, and I said: 'This ascetic excessively lays down these precepts and excessively praises these precepts.'"

Then the Venerable Kassapiya, after the night had passed, in the early morning, put on his robe and took his bowl, entered the Bhaṅgajā settlement to beg for food. After eating, he returned to the monastery, entrusted his bedding to another, and taking his own robe and bowl, traveled by stages toward Śrāvastī until he reached the country of Śrāvastī. He put away his robe and bowl, washed his feet, went to see the World-Honored One, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and said to the Buddha: "I repent, World-Honored One! I repent, Sugata! I was foolish, I was deluded, unskillful and undiscerning. When I heard the World-Honored One teaching the bhikṣus the Dharma related to precepts and praising the establishment of precepts, I was unable to endure it toward the World-Honored One and was displeased, with no joy in my heart, and I said: 'This ascetic excessively lays down these precepts and praises these precepts.'"

The Buddha said to Kassapiya: "At what time were you unable to endure it toward me, displeased and without joy, saying: 'This ascetic excessively lays down these precepts and praises these precepts'?"

Kassapiya said to the Buddha: "At that time, when the World-Honored One was at the Bhaṅgajā settlement in the Bhaṅgajā Grove, teaching the bhikṣus the Dharma related to precepts and praising those precepts, I at that time was unable to endure it toward the World-Honored One, was not joyful, had no gladness in my heart, and said: 'This ascetic excessively lays down these precepts and praises these precepts.' World-Honored One! Today I know my own offense and repent, I see my own offense and repent. I only wish that the World-Honored One would accept my repentance, out of compassion!"

The Buddha said to Kassapiya: "You yourself know your remorse, that you were foolish and deluded, unskillful and undiscerning, that when you heard me teaching the bhikṣus the Dharma related to precepts and praising the establishment of precepts, you were unable to endure it toward me and were displeased, with no gladness in your heart, and said: 'This ascetic excessively lays down these precepts and excessively praises these precepts.' Now, Kassapiya, since you yourself know your remorse and see your remorse, for the future I confer upon you the discipline of restraint and the arising of precepts, out of compassion. Kassapiya! For one who repents in this way, wholesome qualities increase and never decline. Why is this? If someone knows their own offense and sees their own offense and repents of it, then in the future, the discipline of restraint and precepts will arise, wholesome qualities will increase, and they will not decline.

"Suppose, Kassapiya, there is an elder who does not wish to train in the precepts, does not value the precepts, and does not praise the establishment of precepts. Such a bhikṣu I do not praise. Why is this? If the Great Teacher praises someone, other people will then associate with that person closely, with respect and intimacy. If other people associate closely with and respect that person, they will adopt the same views and do the same things as that person. If they do the same things as that person, they will for a long night gain no benefit but only suffering. Therefore, from the very beginning I do not praise such an elder, because from the start he does not delight in training in the precepts. As with an elder, so too with those of middle years and those who are young.

"If an elder from the very beginning values training in the precepts and praises the establishment of precepts, such an elder I praise, because from the start he delights in training in the precepts. When the Great Teacher praises someone, other people will also associate with that person closely, with respect and intimacy, and adopt the same views. Because they adopt the same views, in the future, for a long night that person will bring benefit through the meaning of the Dharma. Therefore, such elder bhikṣus should always be praised, because from the start they delight in training in the precepts. So too for those of middle years and those who are young."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (831)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If elder senior bhikṣus from the very beginning do not delight in training in the precepts, do not value the precepts, and when they see other bhikṣus who from the start delight in training in the precepts, value the precepts, and praise the establishment of precepts, they also do not timely praise them, I do not praise such bhikṣus, because from the start they do not delight in training in the precepts. Why is this? If the Great Teacher were to praise them, other people would then associate with them closely, with respect and intimacy, and adopt the same views. Because they adopt the same views, for a long night they would gain no benefit but only suffering. Therefore I do not praise such elders. For those of middle years and those who are young, it is likewise. As for those who delight in training in the precepts, it is as previously stated."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (832)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are three trainings. What are the three? They are the higher training in morality, the higher training in mind, and the higher training in wisdom. What is the higher training in morality? If a bhikṣu abides in the precepts of the prātimokṣa, is complete in deportment and conduct, sees fear in the slightest faults, and undertakes and trains in the precepts, this is called the higher training in morality. What is the higher training in mind? If a bhikṣu, separated from evil and unwholesome states, with initial thought and sustained thought, with joy and happiness born of seclusion, abides having attained the first dhyāna, and so on up to abiding having attained the fourth dhyāna, this is called the higher training in mind. What is the higher training in wisdom? If a bhikṣu truly knows this noble truth of suffering, truly knows this noble truth of the origin of suffering, this noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and this noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering, this is called the higher training in wisdom."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

The remaining sūtras on the three trainings are as previously stated in the section on the foundations of mindfulness.

As with dhyāna, so too with the immeasurables and the formless attainments. As with the Four Noble Truths, so too with the four foundations of mindfulness, the four right efforts, the four bases of supernatural power, the five faculties, the five powers, the seven factors of awakening, the noble Eightfold Path, the four paths, the four statements of Dharma, and the cultivation of calm and insight; they are likewise taught.

---

### (833)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pool, in the Gabled Hall.

At that time, there was a Licchavi elephant trainer named Nanda, who came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One said to the Licchavi Nanda: "If a noble disciple has accomplished the four unshakable purities and wishes to seek long life, he obtains long life; if he seeks good complexion, strength, happiness, eloquence, and sovereignty, he obtains them. What are the four? Namely, accomplishment of unshakable purity toward the Buddha, accomplishment of unshakable purity toward the Dharma, toward the Sangha, and accomplishment of noble precepts. I see that when such a noble disciple, at the end of this life, is reborn in the heavens, he obtains ten things in the heavens. What are the ten? He obtains heavenly lifespan, heavenly complexion, heavenly fame, heavenly happiness, heavenly sovereignty, and heavenly forms, sounds, scents, tastes, and tangibles. If a noble disciple, at the end of life in the heavens, comes to be reborn among humans, I see that he is endowed with ten things. What are the ten? Human lifespan, good complexion, fame, happiness, sovereignty, and forms, sounds, scents, tastes, and tangibles. I declare that such a learned noble disciple does not rely on others' faith, does not rely on others' desires, does not follow what is heard from others, does not adopt others' views, and does not depend on others' thinking. I declare that he has true and right wisdom of knowing and seeing."

At that time, Nanda had an attendant who said to Nanda: "The time for bathing has arrived; you may go now."

Nanda replied: "I have no need now for a bath with worldly water. I am now bathing myself in this supremely wonderful Dharma, namely, the pure faith and delight obtained from the World-Honored One."

At that time, the Licchavi elephant trainer Nanda, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his approval. He rose from his seat, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (834)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pool, in the Gabled Hall.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If a noble disciple has accomplished the four unshakable purities, he will not live in poverty among humans, will not beg in cold destitution, but will naturally be wealthy and self-sufficient. What are the four? Namely, accomplishment of unshakable purity toward the Buddha, accomplishment of unshakable purity toward the Dharma, the Sangha, and the noble precepts. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'I shall accomplish unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (835)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "A wheel-turning king, endowed with the seven treasures and accomplished in the four kinds of supernatural power among humans, rules over the four continents. At the breaking up of the body and the ending of life, he is reborn in the heavens. Although a wheel-turning sage-king is endowed with the seven treasures and accomplished in human supernatural powers, ruling over the four continents, and at the breaking up of the body and the ending of life is reborn in the heavens, he still has not cut off the suffering of the evil destinies of hell, the animal realm, and the realm of hungry ghosts. Why is this? Because the wheel-turning king has not accomplished unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma, unshakable purity toward the Sangha, and has not accomplished the noble precepts.

"A learned noble disciple who wears rag-robes, begs for food from house to house, and sleeps on a bed of grass, yet that learned noble disciple is liberated from the suffering of the evil destinies of hell, the animal realm, and the realm of hungry ghosts. Why is this? Because that learned noble disciple has accomplished unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'We shall accomplish unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (836)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "You should give rise to a mind of sympathy and compassion. If there are people who gladly listen to and gladly receive what you teach, you should teach them the four unshakable purities, causing them to enter into and abide in them. What are the four? Unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma, unshakable purity toward the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts. Why is this? Even if the four great elements of earth, water, fire, and wind undergo change, increase, or decrease, these four unshakable purities have never increased, decreased, or changed. That which does not increase, decrease, or change is this: when a learned noble disciple has accomplished unshakable purity toward the Buddha, that he should fall into hell, the animal realm, or the realm of hungry ghosts, this is impossible. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'I shall accomplish unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts; and I shall also establish others, causing them to accomplish these.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (837)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If one places faith in a person, five kinds of dangers arise. That person may at some time transgress the precepts and violate the discipline, and be expelled by the Sangha. One who reveres that person will think: 'This is my teacher, the one I revere and respect. The Sangha has expelled and rejected him. What reason do I now have to enter the monastery?' Having stopped entering the monastery, one no longer respects the Sangha. Having stopped respecting the Sangha, one does not hear the Dharma. Having stopped hearing the Dharma, one loses wholesome qualities and cannot long abide in the True Dharma. This is called the first danger arising from placing faith in a person.

"Furthermore, one who reveres and trusts a person: that person whom one reveres transgresses the precepts and violates the discipline, and the Sangha carries out a suspension for not acknowledging the offense. One who reveres and trusts that person will think: 'This is my teacher, the one I revere and respect, and now the Sangha has carried out a suspension for not acknowledging. What reason do I now have to enter the monastery?' Having stopped entering the monastery, one no longer respects the Sangha. Having stopped respecting the Sangha, one does not hear the Dharma. Having stopped hearing the Dharma, one loses wholesome qualities and cannot long abide in the True Dharma. This is called the second danger arising from placing faith in a person.

"Furthermore, that person may take his robe and bowl and travel to other regions. One who reveres that person will think: 'The person I revere has taken his robe and bowl and gone traveling among people. What reason do I now have to enter the monastery?' Having stopped entering the monastery, one cannot respectfully attend the Sangha. Having stopped respecting the Sangha, one does not hear the Dharma. Having stopped hearing the Dharma, one loses wholesome qualities and cannot long abide in the True Dharma. This is called the third danger arising from placing faith in a person.

"Furthermore, the person in whom one has faith and whom one reveres may disrobe and return to lay life. One who reveres and trusts that person will think: 'That was my teacher, the one I revered and respected, and he has disrobed and returned to lay life. I now should not enter the monastery.' Having stopped entering the monastery, one no longer respects the Sangha. Having stopped respecting the Sangha, one does not hear the Dharma. Having stopped hearing the Dharma, one loses wholesome qualities and cannot long abide in the True Dharma. This is called the fourth danger arising from placing faith in a person.

"Furthermore, the person in whom one has faith and whom one reveres may die, the body breaking up and life ending. One who reveres and trusts that person will think: 'That was my teacher, the one I revered and respected, and now he has died. What reason do I now have to enter the monastery?' Because one stops entering the monastery, one cannot respectfully attend the Sangha. Having stopped respecting the Sangha, one does not hear the Dharma. Because one does not hear the Dharma, one loses wholesome qualities and cannot long abide in the True Dharma. This is called the fifth danger arising from placing faith in a person.

"Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'I shall accomplish unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (838)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of nutriment that nourish beings and cause the four great elements to increase and be sustained. What are the four? They are: material food, contact food, mental volition food, and consciousness food. Likewise, there is merit-moistening food that serves as food for well-being. What are the four? Namely, unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'I shall accomplish unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (839)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "One who has accomplished unshakable purity toward the Buddha does so in order to hear the Dharma, to be remembered by the Sangha, and to accomplish the noble precepts."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (840)

The next sūtra is also as stated above. The difference is: "If one has accomplished unshakable purity toward the Buddha, toward the Dharma and the Sangha, then, among beings bound by the stain of miserliness, one separates from the stain of miserliness in one's mind and, while dwelling as a householder, abides liberated, practicing generosity of mind, always delighting in giving, always delighting in relinquishment, practicing equitable giving, and accomplishing the noble precepts."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (841)

The next sūtra is also as stated above. The difference is: "Such a noble disciple, with these four kinds of merit-moistening and wholesome-quality-moistening sustenance, if one were to measure and calculate the merit, it cannot be measured as 'so much fruit of merit' or 'so much fruit' or 'so much accumulation of merit-fruit.' However, that person obtains abundant merit; it is a great mass of merit in number. It is like the five rivers flowing together, namely the Ganges, the Yamunā, the Sarāyu, the Airāvatī, and the Mahī. No one can measure their waters by hundreds of pitchers, thousands of pitchers, or hundreds of thousands of millions of pitchers. Yet their waters are vast; it is a great mass of water in number. Likewise, when a noble disciple has accomplished the four kinds of merit-moistening, no one can measure whether the merit is much or little. Yet there is abundant merit; it is a great mass of merit in number. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'I shall accomplish unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts.'"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse:

> "The great sea of all auspiciousness,
> itself pure, able to purify others,
> vast and flowing evenly,
> truly the greatest of all rivers.
> All the rivers and streams,
> relied upon by living beings,
> all flow into the great sea.
> This body is likewise so:
> giving, precepts, and cultivating merit,
> the streams of a hundred blessings all flow to their home."

---

### (842)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "The brāhmaṇas teach a false path, foolish, evil, and deviant, not rightly directed, not wisely and equally awakened toward nirvāṇa. They instruct their disciples in this way: 'On the fifteenth day, bathe the body with a paste of sesame and āmalaka, put on new cotton garments, let long threads hang from your head, smear the ground with cow dung and lie upon it.' They say: 'Good man! Rise early in the morning, remove your garments and set them aside in one place, bare your body, and run eastward. Even if on the road you encounter fierce elephants, vicious horses, mad bulls, rabid dogs, thorns, dense forests, ditches, ravines, or deep water, go straight ahead and do not avoid them. If you meet death by harm, you will certainly be reborn in the Brahmā heaven.' This is the foolish and deviant view of the non-Buddhist practitioners, not wisely and equally awakened toward nirvāṇa. I teach my disciples the level and straight path, not foolish, directed toward wisdom and equal awakening, directed toward nirvāṇa, namely the noble Eightfold Path: right view, and so on up to right samādhi."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (843)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One said to the Venerable Śāriputra: "What is called 'the stream'? What is the stream?"

Śāriputra said to the Buddha: "What the World-Honored One calls the stream is the noble Eightfold Path."

He further asked Śāriputra: "What is called 'the factors for entering the stream'? What are the factors for entering the stream?"

Śāriputra said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, there are four factors for entering the stream. What are the four? They are: associating with good people, hearing the True Dharma, proper internal reflection, and practicing the Dharma in accordance with the Dharma."

He further asked Śāriputra: "How many qualities does one who has entered the stream accomplish?"

Śāriputra said to the Buddha: "There are four qualities that accomplish one who has entered the stream. What are the four? They are: unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma, unshakable purity toward the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts."

The Buddha said to Śāriputra: "As you have said, the stream is the noble Eightfold Path. The factors for entering the stream are four, namely: associating with good people, hearing the True Dharma, proper internal reflection, and practicing the Dharma in accordance with the Dharma. One who has entered the stream accomplishes four qualities, namely: unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma, unshakable purity toward the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Śāriputra, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (844)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra went to visit the Venerable Ānanda. After exchanging greetings and inquiring after his well-being, he withdrew and stood to one side. The Venerable Śāriputra said to the Venerable Ānanda: "I wish to ask something. Do you have leisure to explain it?"

The Venerable Ānanda said to Śāriputra: "Ask as you wish. If I know, I shall answer."

Śāriputra asked the Venerable Ānanda: "How many qualities must be abandoned for the Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha, through his knowledge and vision, to declare of a person that he has attained stream-entry, is not subject to falling into evil destinies, is definitely directed toward right awakening, and after seven more births among gods and humans will make an end of suffering?"

The Venerable Ānanda said to the Venerable Śāriputra: "By abandoning four qualities and accomplishing four qualities, the Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha, through his knowledge and vision, declares of that person that he has attained stream-entry, is not subject to falling into evil destinies, is definitely directed toward the three kinds of bodhi, and after seven more births among gods and humans will make an end of suffering. What are the four? When a noble disciple has been dwelling in lack of faith toward the Buddha, that has already been abandoned and understood, and he has accomplished unshakable purity toward the Buddha. When there has been lack of faith toward the Dharma, toward the Sangha, and bad morality, those have already been abandoned and understood, and he has accomplished unshakable purity toward the Dharma, the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts. Thus, by abandoning four qualities and accomplishing four qualities, the Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha, through his knowledge and vision, declares of that person that he has attained stream-entry, is not subject to falling into evil destinies, is definitely directed toward the three kinds of bodhi, and after seven more births among gods and humans will make an end of suffering."

The Venerable Ānanda said to the Venerable Śāriputra: "So it is! So it is! By abandoning four qualities and accomplishing four qualities, the Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha, through his knowledge and vision, declares of that person that he has attained stream-entry, is definitely directed toward the three kinds of bodhi, and after seven more births among gods and humans will make an end of suffering."

Then the two worthy ones, having discussed together, expressed mutual approval and rejoiced, rose from their seats, and departed.

---

### (845)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu has put to rest the five fears and enmities, is settled without doubt in three matters, and truly knows and sees the noble path, that noble disciple is able to declare of himself: 'The evil destinies of hell, the animal realm, and the realm of hungry ghosts are ended for me. I have attained stream-entry, am not subject to falling into evil destinies, am definitely directed toward the three kinds of bodhi, and after seven more births among gods and humans will make an end of suffering.'

"What are the five fears and enmities that are put to rest? When through the cause and condition of killing living beings, offenses, enmity, and fear arise; when one has departed from killing living beings, the fear arising from the causes and conditions of the offense and enmity of killing is put to rest. When through stealing, sexual misconduct, false speech, and drinking alcohol, offenses, enmity, and fear arise from these causes and conditions; when one has departed from stealing, sexual misconduct, false speech, and drinking alcohol, the offenses, enmity, and fear arising from these causes and conditions are put to rest. These are called the five fears and enmities from offenses that are put to rest.

"What are the three matters that are settled without doubt? It means being settled regarding the Buddha, free from doubt; being settled regarding the Dharma and the Sangha, free from doubt. These are called the three matters settled and free from doubt.

"What is called truly knowing and seeing the noble path? It means truly knowing this noble truth of suffering, truly knowing this noble truth of the origin of suffering, this noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and this noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. This is called truly knowing and seeing the noble path.

"If one has put to rest these five fears and enmities from offenses, is settled and free from doubt in the three matters, and truly knows and sees the noble meaning, that noble disciple is able to declare of himself: 'The evil destinies of hell, the animal realm, and the realm of hungry ghosts are ended for me. I have attained stream-entry, am not subject to falling into evil destinies, am definitely directed toward the three kinds of bodhi, and after seven more births among gods and humans will make an end of suffering.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (846)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "What is called truly knowing and seeing the noble path? It is the noble Eightfold Path: right view, and so on up to right samādhi."

The next sūtra is also taught in the same way. The difference is: "What is called truly knowing and seeing the noble path? It is truly knowing and seeing the twelve links of dependent origination. As it is said: 'Because this exists, that exists; because this arises, that arises. Conditioned by ignorance, there are formations; conditioned by formations, there is consciousness; conditioned by consciousness, there is name-and-form; conditioned by name-and-form, there are the six sense bases; conditioned by the six sense bases, there is contact; conditioned by contact, there is feeling; conditioned by feeling, there is craving; conditioned by craving, there is clinging; conditioned by clinging, there is becoming; conditioned by becoming, there is birth; conditioned by birth, there are old age, sickness, death, sorrow, lamentation, suffering, and distress.' This is called a noble disciple's true knowing and seeing."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (847)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of heavenly paths of the devas. They cause unpurified beings to become purified and those already purified to be further purified. What are the four? When a noble disciple has accomplished unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts. These are called the four kinds of heavenly paths of the devas that cause unpurified beings to become purified and those already purified to be further purified."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (848)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of heavenly paths of the devas. What are the four? When a noble disciple recollects matters concerning the Tathāgata, thus: 'The Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha, endowed with knowledge and conduct, Sugata, knower of the world, unsurpassed, tamer of persons to be trained, teacher of gods and humans, Buddha, World-Honored One.' Upon recollecting matters concerning the Tathāgata, a mind of joyful approval arises. Having joyfully approved, the mind becomes glad. When the mind becomes glad, the body is tranquilized. When the body is tranquilized, one experiences bliss. Having experienced bliss, one enters samādhi. Having entered samādhi, the noble disciple trains thus: 'What are the heavenly paths of the devas?' He further thinks: 'I have heard that freedom from anger is the supreme heavenly path of the devas.' He thinks: 'From this day forward, whether in fear or safety in the world, I shall not give rise to anger. I shall only receive and maintain the pure and complete heavenly path of the devas.' This is called the first heavenly path of the devas, causing unpurified beings to become purified and those already purified to be further purified.

"Furthermore, bhikṣus, a noble disciple recollects matters concerning the Dharma, namely: the Dharma well taught by the Tathāgata, the Dharma and Vinaya, which is visible here and now, free from burning afflictions, not dependent on time, leading to nirvāṇa, to be seen by oneself directly, dependent on one's own realization and understanding. Having thus understood matters concerning the Dharma, a mind of joyful approval arises. Having joyfully approved, the body is tranquilized. When the body is tranquilized, one experiences bliss. Having experienced bliss, one enters samādhi. Having entered samādhi, the noble disciple trains thus: 'What are the heavenly paths of the devas?' He further thinks: 'I have heard that freedom from anger is the supreme heavenly path of the devas. From this day forward, whether in fear or safety in this world, I shall not give rise to anger. I shall receive and maintain the pure and complete heavenly path of the devas.' This is called the second heavenly path of the devas.

"Furthermore, bhikṣus, when one gives rise to right mindfulness concerning matters of the Sangha, namely: the Sangha of the World-Honored One's disciples practices rightly and directly, deserving of respect, reverence, and offerings, the unsurpassed field of merit. Having thus rightly recollected matters concerning the Sangha, a mind of joyful approval arises. Having joyfully approved, gladness arises. Having become glad, the body is tranquilized. When the body is tranquilized, one experiences bliss. Having experienced bliss, one enters samādhi. Having entered samādhi, that noble disciple trains thus: 'What are the heavenly paths of the devas?' He further thinks: 'I have heard that freedom from anger among the devas is the supreme heavenly path of the devas. From this day forward, whether in fear or safety in the world, I shall not give rise to anger. I shall only receive and maintain the pure and complete heavenly path of the devas.' This is called the third heavenly path of the devas.

"Furthermore, bhikṣus, a noble disciple recollects his own precept-related matters, remembering and reflecting: 'My precepts are unbroken, unstained, unmixed, praised by the wise, not disparaged by the wise.' Having thus rightly recollected such precept-related matters, a mind of joyful approval arises. Having joyfully approved, gladness arises. Having become glad, the body is tranquilized. When the body is tranquilized, one experiences bliss. Having experienced bliss, one enters samādhi. Having entered samādhi, the noble disciple thinks: 'What are the heavenly paths of the devas?' He further thinks: 'I have heard that freedom from anger among the devas is supreme. From this day forward, whether in fear or safety in the world, I shall not give rise to anger. I shall receive and maintain the pure and complete heavenly path of the devas.' This is called the fourth heavenly path of the devas, causing unpurified beings to become purified and those already purified to be further purified."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (849)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of heavenly paths of the devas. They cause unpurified beings to become purified and those already purified to increase their purity. What are the four? When a noble disciple recollects matters concerning the Tathāgata, thus: 'The Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha, endowed with knowledge and conduct, Sugata, knower of the world, unsurpassed, tamer of persons to be trained, teacher of gods and humans, Buddha, World-Honored One.' Having thus recollected matters concerning the Tathāgata, one then cuts off evil craving and cuts off unwholesome mental states. Because of recollecting the Tathāgata, a mind of joyful approval arises. Having joyfully approved, gladness arises. Having become glad, the body is tranquilized. When the body is tranquilized, one experiences bliss. Having experienced bliss, one enters samādhi. Having entered samādhi, the noble disciple trains thus: 'What are the heavenly paths of the devas?' He further thinks: 'I have heard that freedom from anger is the supreme heavenly path of the devas. From this day forward, whether in fear or safety in the world, I shall not give rise to anger, but shall only receive and maintain the pure and complete heavenly path of the devas.' Likewise, concerning the Dharma, the Sangha, and the accomplishment of the noble precepts, it is also taught in the same way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (850)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of heavenly paths of the devas. They cause unpurified beings to become purified and those already purified to increase their purity. What are the four? When a noble disciple recollects matters concerning the Tathāgata, thus: 'The Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha, endowed with knowledge and conduct, Sugata, knower of the world, unsurpassed, tamer of persons to be trained, teacher of gods and humans, Buddha, World-Honored One.' Having recollected matters concerning the Tathāgata, the noble disciple's mind separates from the entanglements of craving, the entanglements of anger, and the entanglements of delusion, and the mind becomes upright. By recollecting matters concerning the Tathāgata, this noble disciple gains the stream of Dharma, gains the stream of meaning, gains the benefit of recollecting the Tathāgata, and joyfully approves. Having joyfully approved, gladness arises. Having become glad, the body is tranquilized. When the body is tranquilized, one experiences bliss. Having experienced bliss, one enters samādhi. Having entered samādhi, this noble disciple trains thus: 'What are the heavenly paths of the devas?' He further thinks: 'I have heard that freedom from anger is the supreme heavenly path of the devas. From this day forward, in the world I shall not give rise to anger, but shall purely and completely maintain the heavenly path of the devas.' Likewise, concerning the Dharma, the Sangha, and the accomplishment of the noble precepts, it is also taught in the same way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (851)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach the Dharma Mirror Sūtra. Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall teach it to you. What is the Dharma Mirror Sūtra? When a noble disciple has accomplished unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts, this is called the Dharma Mirror Sūtra."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (852)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, many bhikṣus put on their robes and took their bowls, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. While begging for food, they heard that the bhikṣu Nantu had died, the bhikṣuṇī Nandā had died, the upāsaka Śubha had died, and the upāsikā Śubhā had died. After begging for food, they returned to the monastery, put away their robes and bowls, washed their feet, went to see the Buddha, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew, sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! This morning we entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food and heard that the bhikṣu Nantu, the bhikṣuṇī Nandā, the upāsaka Śubha, and the upāsikā Śubhā have died. World-Honored One, where have those four been reborn upon death?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "That bhikṣu Nantu and bhikṣuṇī Nandā had exhausted all their outflows; attained the outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom; and in this very life knew and realized for themselves: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, and I know for myself that there will be no further existence.' The upāsaka Śubha and the upāsikā Śubhā had exhausted the five lower fetters, attained the state of non-returner, were reborn in the heavens, and will attain parinirvāṇa there without returning to this world."

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach you the Dharma Mirror Sūtra: unshakable purity toward the Buddha, and so on up to accomplishment of the noble precepts. This is called the Dharma Mirror Sūtra."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (853)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, as explained in full above. The difference is: "A certain bhikṣu, a certain bhikṣuṇī, a certain upāsaka, and a certain upāsikā had died," and the rest is also as stated above.

---

### (854)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the settlement of Nādikā, in the Giñjakāvasatha monastery. At that time, many people in the settlement of Nādikā had died.

At that time, many bhikṣus put on their robes, took their bowls, and entered the settlement of Nādikā to beg for food. They heard that in the settlement of Nādikā, the upāsaka Kekaśa had died; Nikaṭa, Khāliṅkara, Katarisabhā, Jāru, Upajāru, Ṛṣṭha, Arṣṭha, Bhadra, Subhadra, Yaśa-Yaśodhara, and Yaśa-Uttara had all died. Having heard this, they returned to the monastery, put away their robes and bowls, washed their feet, went to see the Buddha, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! We, many bhikṣus, this morning entered the settlement of Nādikā to beg for food and heard that the upāsaka Kekaśa and the others had died. World-Honored One, where have they been reborn upon death?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Those such as Kekaśa had exhausted the five lower fetters, attained the state of non-returner, and will attain parinirvāṇa in the heavens without returning to this world."

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, furthermore, over two hundred and fifty upāsakas have died, and furthermore, five hundred upāsakas in this settlement of Nādikā have died, all having exhausted the five lower fetters, attained the state of non-returner, and will attain parinirvāṇa in the heavens without returning to this world. Furthermore, over two hundred and fifty upāsakas have died, all having exhausted the three fetters, with craving, anger, and delusion attenuated, attaining the state of once-returner, and after one more birth will make an end of suffering. In this settlement of Nādikā, furthermore, five hundred upāsakas have died, all having exhausted the three fetters, attained stream-entry, not subject to falling into evil destinies, definitely directed toward the three kinds of bodhi, and after seven more births among gods and humans will make an end of suffering."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "That you ask about this one's death and that one's death is wearisome. This is not what the Tathāgata delights in answering. For what is born must die; what is so remarkable about that? Whether the Tathāgata appears in the world or does not appear, the nature of things constantly abides. The Tathāgata himself knows and attains perfect awakening, reveals, teaches, distinguishes, and elucidates it. That is to say: because this exists, that exists; because this arises, that arises. Conditioned by ignorance, there are formations, and so on up to conditioned by birth, there are old age, sickness, death, sorrow, lamentation, distress, and suffering. Thus is the arising of this entire mass of suffering. When ignorance ceases, formations cease, and so on up to when birth ceases, then old age, sickness, death, sorrow, lamentation, distress, and suffering cease. Thus is the cessation of this entire mass of suffering. I shall now teach you the Dharma Mirror Sūtra. Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall teach it to you. What is the Dharma Mirror Sūtra? When a noble disciple has accomplished unshakable purity toward the Buddha, unshakable purity toward the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (855)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was an upāsaka named Nandika who came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! If a noble disciple does not at any time accomplish these five faculties, is he heedful or heedless?"

The Buddha said to Nandika: "If one does not at any time accomplish these five faculties, I say such a person is counted among ordinary worldlings. If a noble disciple does not accomplish them, as to whether he is heedful or heedless, Nandika, if a noble disciple has accomplished unshakable purity toward the Buddha, yet does not seek to go further, does not in the secluded forest or in the open air, day and night engage in dhyāna meditation, diligently cultivating the practice of excellent liberation and beneficial joyful approval; if he does not joyfully approve, gladness does not arise; if gladness does not arise, the body is not tranquilized; if the body is not tranquilized, painful feeling arises; if painful feeling arises, the mind does not become concentrated; if the mind does not become concentrated, this noble disciple is called heedless. Concerning unshakable purity toward the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts, it is likewise taught. Thus, Nandika, if a noble disciple has accomplished unshakable purity toward the Buddha, and his mind does not give rise to thoughts of complacent satisfaction, but in the secluded forest, under trees, or in the open air, day and night engages in dhyāna meditation, striving diligently, able to give rise to excellent liberation and joyful approval; having joyfully approved, gladness arises; when gladness arises, the body is tranquilized; when the body is tranquilized, one experiences bliss; having experienced bliss, the mind becomes concentrated. If a noble disciple's mind becomes concentrated, he is called not heedless. Concerning unshakable purity toward the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts, it is likewise taught."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the upāsaka Nandika, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, bowed at the Buddha's feet, and departed.

---

### (856)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a Śākyan named Nandika who came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! If a noble disciple does not at any time accomplish the four unshakable purities, is this noble disciple heedful or heedless?"

The Buddha said to the Śākyan Nandika: "If one does not at any time accomplish the four unshakable purities, I say such a person is counted among the outer ordinary worldlings. Śākyan Nandika, as to whether a noble disciple is heedful or heedless, I shall now explain." This was explained in full as above.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Śākyan Nandika, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (857)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. The three-month summer retreat had been completed. Many bhikṣus gathered in the dining hall to sew robes for the Buddha, saying: "Before long, the Tathāgata will finish making his robe, and putting on his robe and taking his bowl, will leave the monastery to travel among the people."

At that time, the Śākyan Nandika heard that many bhikṣus were gathered in the dining hall sewing robes for the Buddha, and that before long the Tathāgata would finish making his robe, put on his robe and take his bowl, and travel among the people. Having heard this, the Śākyan Nandika came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! My four limbs feel weak and unsteady, the four directions seem to shift, and the Dharma I previously heard I have now entirely forgotten. I have heard that many bhikṣus are gathered in the dining hall sewing robes for the World-Honored One, saying: 'Before long the Tathāgata will finish making his robe, put on his robe and take his bowl, and travel among the people.' Therefore my mind now gives rise to great distress. When shall I again see the World-Honored One and the bhikṣus whom I know?"

The Buddha said to the Śākyan Nandika: "Whether you see the Buddha or do not see the Buddha, whether you see the bhikṣus you know or do not see them, you should from time to time cultivate the five places of gladness. What are the five? You should from time to time recollect matters concerning the Tathāgata: 'The Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha, endowed with knowledge and conduct, Sugata, knower of the world, unsurpassed, tamer of persons to be trained, teacher of gods and humans, Buddha, World-Honored One.' Also recollect matters concerning the Dharma, matters concerning the Sangha, your own observance of precepts, and your own practice of generosity. Recollect from time to time: 'I have gained my own benefit. Among beings stained by the taint of miserliness, I should extensively cultivate dwelling free from the taint of miserliness, practicing the generosity of liberation, the generosity of relinquishment, generosity that is always blazing, delighting in relinquishment, practicing equitable giving, and always harboring a mind of generosity.' Thus, Śākyan Nandika, with this five-factored samādhi, whether standing, walking, sitting, or lying down, even when together with your wife and children, you should always bind your mind to this samādhi recollection."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Śākyan Nandika, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (858)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, three months before the summer retreat.

At that time, there was a Śākyan named Nandika who heard that the Buddha was in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, three months before the conclusion of the summer retreat. Having heard this, he thought: "I should go there, and also prepare offerings and attend to various matters of service, to make offerings to the Tathāgata and the bhikṣu Sangha." He then went there. When the three months were completed, many bhikṣus gathered in the dining hall to sew robes for the World-Honored One, saying: "Before long the Tathāgata will finish making his robe, put on his robe and take his bowl, and travel among the people."

At that time, the Śākyan Nandika heard that many bhikṣus were gathered in the dining hall, saying: "Before long the Tathāgata will finish making his robe, put on his robe and take his bowl, and travel among the people." Having heard this, he came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, stood to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! My four limbs feel weak and unsteady, the four directions seem to shift, and the Dharma I previously received I have now entirely forgotten. I have heard that the World-Honored One will travel among the people. When shall I again see the World-Honored One and the bhikṣus whom I know?"

The Buddha said to the Śākyan Nandika: "Whether you see the Tathāgata or do not see him, whether you see the bhikṣus you know or do not see them, you should from time to time cultivate the six recollections. What are the six? You should recollect matters concerning the Tathāgata, the Dharma, the Sangha, your own observance of precepts, your own practice of generosity, and recollect the devas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Śākyan Nandika, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (859)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, three months before the conclusion of the summer retreat, as previously stated. The difference is: at that time there were two brothers, the householder Ṛṣidatta and Purāṇa, who heard that many bhikṣus were gathered in the dining hall sewing robes for the World-Honored One. The rest is explained in full as in the Nandika sūtra above.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the householder Ṛṣidatta and Purāṇa, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed their approval, rose from their seats, paid their respects, and departed.

---

### (860)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. The three-month summer retreat had been completed. Many bhikṣus gathered in the dining hall to sew robes for the World-Honored One.

At that time, there were two brothers, the householder Ṛṣidatta and Purāṇa, who were tending to their fields in the Deer Path Marsh. They heard that many bhikṣus were in the dining hall sewing robes for the World-Honored One, saying: "Before long the Tathāgata will finish making his robe, put on his robe and take his bowl, and travel among the people." Having heard this, they told a man: "You should now go to visit the World-Honored One and see whether he has certainly departed. If so, come quickly and tell us."

The man then received their instructions and went to a certain place. He saw the World-Honored One coming out, and immediately hastened back to report to Ṛṣidatta and Purāṇa: "The World-Honored One has already come, along with the great assembly."

At that time, Ṛṣidatta and Purāṇa went to meet the World-Honored One. The World-Honored One saw Ṛṣidatta and Purāṇa coming along the road from afar, and immediately left the road to one side, spread out his sitting cloth, sat upright with proper posture.

Ṛṣidatta and Purāṇa bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Our four limbs feel weak and unsteady, the four directions seem to shift, and the things we used to remember we have now entirely forgotten. When shall we again see the World-Honored One and the bhikṣus whom we know? The World-Honored One is now going out to Kośala, from Kośala to Kāśī, from Kāśī to Malla, from Malla to Magadha, from Magadha to Aṅga, from Aṅga to Sūma, from Sūma to Puṇḍra, from Puṇḍra to Kaliṅga. Therefore we now feel extreme sorrow and distress. When shall we again see the World-Honored One and the bhikṣus whom we know?"

The Buddha said to Ṛṣidatta and Purāṇa: "Whether you see the Tathāgata or do not see him, whether you see the bhikṣus you know or do not see them, you should from time to time cultivate the six recollections. What are the six? You should recollect matters concerning the Tathāgata," explained in full up to "recollect the devas. However, householders, in the household life there is much bustle and commotion, in the household life there is defilement and attachment. The homeless life of going forth is spacious and open. It is difficult for a layperson to dwell in the homeless state, which is entirely pure, completely full and pure, the holy life being clean and white."

The householder said to the Buddha: "How wonderful, World-Honored One! Well spoken is this Dharma: 'In the household life there is much bustle and commotion, in the household life there is defilement and attachment. The homeless life of going forth is spacious and open. It is difficult for a layperson to dwell in the homeless state, which is entirely pure, completely full and pure, the holy life being clean and white.' I am a great minister of King Prasenajit. When King Prasenajit wished to enter the royal park, he ordered me to ride upon a great elephant, carrying the king's foremost palace ladies, one in front of me and one behind me, with me sitting in between them. When the elephant went downhill, the one in front embraced my neck, and the one behind grasped my back. When the elephant went uphill, the one behind embraced my neck, and the one in front grasped my lapel. Those palace ladies, for the amusement of the king, wore garments of colored silk, applied various fine fragrances, and were adorned with garlands and ornaments. While traveling with them, I constantly guarded myself in three matters: first, controlling the elephant, lest it stray from the proper path; second, guarding my own mind, lest it give rise to attachment; third, guarding my own balance, lest I tumble and fall. World-Honored One, at that time, with regard to the king's palace ladies, there was not a single moment when I did not maintain right thought."

The Buddha said to the householder: "Excellent! Excellent! You are able to well guard your mind."

The householder said to the Buddha: "Whatever possessions I have at home, I always share them for use with the World-Honored One and with the bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, and upāsikās alike, not considering them as my own."

The Buddha said to the householder: "Excellent! Excellent! In the country of Kośala, among those of great wealth, there is no one equal to you, yet you are able to not consider your wealth as your own."

At that time, the World-Honored One taught various Dharma for those householders, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening them. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened them, he rose from his seat and departed.

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.
