# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099, Fascicle 31

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

---

## (861)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Four hundred years in the human realm equals one day and one night in the Tuṣita Heaven. In this way, thirty days make one month, and twelve months make one year. The lifespan in Tuṣita Heaven is four thousand years. Foolish, unlearned ordinary people, upon ending their lives there, are reborn in the hells, among animals, or among hungry ghosts. The well-learned noble disciples, upon ending their lives there, are not reborn in the hells, among animals, or among hungry ghosts."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (862)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Eight hundred years in the human realm equals one day and one night in the Nirmāṇarati Heaven. In this way, thirty days make one month, and twelve months make one year. The lifespan in the Nirmāṇarati Heaven is eight thousand years. Foolish, unlearned ordinary people, upon ending their lives there, are reborn in the hells, among animals, or among hungry ghosts. The well-learned noble disciples, upon ending their lives there, are not reborn in the hells, among animals, or among hungry ghosts."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (863)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "One thousand six hundred years in the human realm equals one day and one night in the Paranirmitavaśavartin Heaven. In this way, thirty days make one month, and twelve months make one year. The lifespan in the Paranirmitavaśavartin Heaven is sixteen thousand years. Foolish, unlearned ordinary people, upon ending their lives there, are reborn in the hells, among animals, or among hungry ghosts. The well-learned noble disciples, upon ending their lives there, are not reborn in the hells, among animals, or among hungry ghosts."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As the Buddha spoke these six sūtras, so too were there six sūtras in which a different bhikṣu asked, and six sūtras in which the Buddha questioned the bhikṣus; they were also spoken in the same way.

---

## (864)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu, with such practice, such form, such characteristics, separated from desires and unwholesome dharmas, with initial thought and sustained thought, with joy and happiness born of separation, dwells having fully attained the first dhyāna. He does not recollect such practice, such form, such characteristics; rather, regarding the dharmas of form, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness in that state, he contemplates them as illness, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer, as impermanent, as suffering, as empty, as not-self. Regarding those dharmas, he generates revulsion, fear, and vigilance. Having generated revulsion, fear, and vigilance, he benefits himself through the gate of the deathless. Such is tranquility, such is supreme excellence, namely: relinquishment, the exhaustion of remaining craving, desirelessness, cessation, nirvāṇa."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (865)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "Having thus known and thus seen, the mind is liberated from the outflow of sensual desire, the mind is liberated from the outflow of existence, the mind is liberated from the outflow of ignorance. In liberation, the knowledge and vision arises: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know there will be no further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (866)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "If one does not attain liberation, then by virtue of the dharma of desire, the dharma of aspiration, and the dharma of delight, one attains parinirvāṇa in the intermediate state. If not thus, then one attains parinirvāṇa upon rebirth. If not thus, then one attains parinirvāṇa with exertion. If not thus, then one attains parinirvāṇa without exertion. If not thus, then one attains parinirvāṇa by going upstream. If not thus, then by virtue of this very dharma of desire, dharma of aspiration, and dharma of delight, one is reborn among the Great Brahmā gods, or reborn among the Brahmā's Ministers gods, or reborn among the Brahmā's Retinue gods."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (867)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu, with such practice, such form, such characteristics, with the stilling of initial thought and sustained thought, with inner purity, with unified mind, without initial thought and without sustained thought, with joy and happiness born of concentration, dwells having fully attained the second dhyāna. If he does not recollect such practice, such form, such characteristics, but regarding the dharmas of form, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, contemplates them as illness, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer, as impermanent, as suffering, as empty, as not-self; regarding these dharmas, the mind generates revulsion, fear, and vigilance. Having generated revulsion and vigilance, he benefits himself through the realm of the deathless dharma. This is tranquility, this is supreme excellence, namely: relinquishment, the exhaustion of all remaining craving, desirelessness, cessation, nirvāṇa."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (868)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "He, having thus known and thus seen, the mind is liberated from the outflow of sensual desire, the mind is liberated from the outflow of existence, the mind is liberated from the outflow of ignorance. In liberation, the knowledge and vision arises: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know there will be no further existence.' If one does not attain liberation, then by virtue of those dharmas, the dharma of desire, the dharma of aspiration, and the dharma of delight, one attains parinirvāṇa in the intermediate state. If not thus, one attains parinirvāṇa upon rebirth. If not thus, one attains parinirvāṇa with exertion. If not thus, one attains parinirvāṇa without exertion. If not thus, one attains parinirvāṇa by going upstream. If not thus, by the dharma of desire, the dharma of aspiration, and the dharma of delight, one is reborn in the Ābhāsvara Heaven. If not thus, one is reborn in the Apramāṇābha Heaven. If not thus, one is reborn in the Parīttābha Heaven."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (869)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu, with such practice, such form, such characteristics, separating from rapture, dwelling in equanimity, with right mindfulness and right knowing, experiencing bodily happiness, that which the noble ones can describe as 'dwelling in happiness with equanimity and mindfulness,' dwells having fully attained the third dhyāna. If not thus, with such practice, such form, such characteristics, regarding the dharmas of feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, one contemplates them as illness, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer, and so forth up to going upstream. If not thus, by virtue of those dharmas, the dharma of desire, the dharma of aspiration, and the dharma of delight, one is reborn in the Śubhakṛtsna Heaven. If not thus, one is reborn in the Apramāṇaśubha Heaven. If not thus, one is reborn in the Parīttaśubha Heaven."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (870)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu, with such practice, such form, such characteristics, with the abandoning of suffering and the ceasing of happiness, with the prior disappearance of sorrow and joy, in neither suffering nor happiness, with equanimity and purity of mindfulness, with unified mind, dwells having fully attained the fourth dhyāna. If he does not thus recollect, but regarding form, feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, contemplates them as illness, as a boil, as a thorn, as a killer, and so forth up to parinirvāṇa by going upstream. If not thus, one is reborn in the Bṛhatphala Heaven. If not thus, one is reborn in the Puṇyaprasava Heaven. If not thus, one is reborn in the Anabhraka Heaven."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the four dhyānas, so too with the four formless attainments; they were also spoken in the same way.

---

## (871)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are wind-cloud devas who have this thought: 'Now I wish to sport with my divine powers.' When they think thus, wind-clouds then arise. As with the wind-cloud devas, so too the flame-lightning devas, the thunder devas, the rain devas, the clear-sky devas, the cold devas, and the heat devas are also spoken of in the same way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As spoken thus, so too when a different bhikṣu asked the Buddha and when the Buddha questioned the bhikṣus, it was also spoken in the same way.

---

## (872)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One, in the darkness of night, with light rain falling and lightning flashing, told Ānanda: "You may cover a lamp with an umbrella-canopy and carry it out."

The Venerable Ānanda received the instruction and covered a lamp with an umbrella-canopy, walking behind the Buddha. When they reached a certain place, the World-Honored One smiled. The Venerable Ānanda addressed the Buddha, saying: "The World-Honored One does not smile without cause. I wonder, what cause and what condition has led the World-Honored One to smile today?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "So it is! So it is! The Tathāgata does not smile without cause. You now carry an umbrella-canopy covering a lamp, walking behind me. I see that Brahmā also likewise carries an umbrella-canopy covering a lamp, walking behind the bhikṣu Kauṇḍinya. Śakra Devānām Indra also carries an umbrella-canopy covering a lamp, walking behind Mahākāśyapa. Dhṛtarāṣṭra, the Heavenly King, also carries an umbrella-canopy covering a lamp, walking behind Śāriputra. Virūḍhaka, the Heavenly King, also carries an umbrella-canopy covering a lamp, walking behind Mahāmaudgalyāyana. Virūpākṣa, the Heavenly King, also carries an umbrella-canopy covering a lamp, walking behind Mahākauṣṭhila. Vaiśravaṇa, the Heavenly King, also carries an umbrella-canopy covering a lamp, walking behind Mahākapphiṇa."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (873)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of well-disciplined assemblies. What are the four? Namely, the disciplined assembly of bhikṣus, the disciplined assembly of bhikṣuṇīs, the disciplined assembly of upāsakas, and the disciplined assembly of upāsikās. These are called the four assemblies."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "If one has eloquence, fearlessness,
> Is learned and penetrates the Dharma,
> Practices the Dharma in proper sequence,
> This is called a good assembly.
> Bhikṣus who maintain pure precepts,
> Bhikṣuṇīs who are learned,
> Upāsakas with pure faith,
> And upāsikās likewise;
> These are called good assemblies.
> Like the sun illuminating itself,
> Such is the good and excellent Sangha.
> This is the best within the Sangha.
> This Dharma makes the Sangha excellent,
> Like the sun illuminating itself."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with "disciplined," so too "eloquent," "gentle," "fearless," "learned," "penetrating the Dharma," "teaching the Dharma," "practicing the Dharma in proper sequence," and "practicing in accordance with the Dharma" are also spoken of in the same way.

---

## (874)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are three kinds of sons. What are the three? There is a son who follows his parents, there is a son who surpasses his parents, and there is a son who is inferior to his parents. What is a son who follows his parents? When the parents do not kill, do not steal, do not commit sexual misconduct, do not speak falsely, and do not drink intoxicants, and the son also follows and learns not to kill, not to steal, not to commit sexual misconduct, not to speak falsely, and not to drink intoxicants, this is called a son who follows his parents. What is a son who surpasses his parents? When the parents do not undertake the precepts of not killing, not stealing, not committing sexual misconduct, not speaking falsely, and not drinking intoxicants, yet the son is able to undertake the precepts of not killing, not stealing, not committing sexual misconduct, not speaking falsely, and not drinking intoxicants, this is called a son who surpasses his parents. What is a son who is inferior to his parents? When the parents undertake the precepts of not killing, not stealing, not committing sexual misconduct, not speaking falsely, and not drinking intoxicants, yet the son is unable to undertake the precepts of not killing, not stealing, not committing sexual misconduct, not speaking falsely, and not drinking intoxicants, this is called a son who is inferior to his parents."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "A son who follows and a son who surpasses,
> These are what the wise father desires.
> A son who is inferior is not what is wished for,
> Because he does not carry on the lineage.
> Being a son of the Dharma among humans,
> One should become an upāsaka.
> Toward the jewels of Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha,
> Diligently cultivate a pure mind.
> When clouds are removed, the moonlight is revealed,
> Bringing radiance to the assembly of kin."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the five precepts, so too the sūtras on faith, precepts, generosity, learning, and wisdom are also spoken of in the same way.

---

## (875)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are four right efforts. What are the four? First, the effort of abandoning; second, the effort of restraint; third, the effort of guarding; fourth, the effort of cultivation."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (876)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are four right efforts. What are the four? First, the effort of abandoning; second, the effort of restraint; third, the effort of guarding; fourth, the effort of cultivation."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "Abandoning and restraint,
> Guarding and cultivation:
> These four right efforts
> Are what the Buddhas have taught."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (877)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are four right efforts. What are the four? First, the effort of abandoning; second, the effort of restraint; third, the effort of guarding; fourth, the effort of cultivation. What is the effort of abandoning? When a bhikṣu abandons already arisen evil, unwholesome dharmas, generating desire, applying means, being diligent and energetic, with mind collected; this is called the effort of abandoning. What is the effort of restraint? When unarisen evil, unwholesome dharmas are prevented from arising, generating desire, applying means, being diligent and energetic, with mind collected; this is called the effort of restraint. What is the effort of guarding? When unarisen wholesome dharmas are caused to arise, generating desire, applying means, being diligent and energetic, with mind collected; this is called the effort of guarding. What is the effort of cultivation? When already arisen wholesome dharmas are increased and cultivated, generating desire, applying means, being diligent and energetic, with mind collected; this is called the effort of cultivation."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (878)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are four right efforts. What are the four? First, the effort of abandoning; second, the effort of restraint; third, the effort of guarding; fourth, the effort of cultivation. What is the effort of abandoning? When a bhikṣu abandons already arisen evil, unwholesome dharmas, generating desire, applying means, being diligent and energetic, with mind collected; this is called the effort of abandoning. What is the effort of restraint? When unarisen evil, unwholesome dharmas are prevented from arising, generating desire, applying means, being diligent and energetic, with mind collected; this is called the effort of restraint. What is the effort of guarding? When unarisen wholesome dharmas are caused to arise, generating desire, applying means, being diligent and energetic, with mind collected; this is called the effort of guarding. What is the effort of cultivation? When already arisen wholesome dharmas are increased and cultivated, generating desire, applying means, being diligent and energetic, with mind collected; this is called the effort of cultivation."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "Abandoning and restraint,
> Guarding and cultivation:
> These four right efforts
> Are what the Buddhas have taught."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (879)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are four right efforts. What are the four? First, the effort of abandoning; second, the effort of restraint; third, the effort of guarding; fourth, the effort of cultivation. What is the effort of abandoning? When a bhikṣu abandons already arisen evil, unwholesome dharmas, generating desire, applying means, being diligent and energetic, with mind collected; prevents unarisen evil, unwholesome dharmas from arising, generating desire, applying means, being diligent and energetic, with mind collected; causes unarisen wholesome dharmas to arise, generating desire, applying means, being diligent and energetic, with mind collected; increases and cultivates already arisen wholesome dharmas, generating desire, applying means, being diligent and energetic, with mind collected; this is called the effort of abandoning. What is the effort of restraint? When a bhikṣu well guards the eye faculty, keeping it hidden, subduing it, progressing forward; likewise with the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind faculties, well guarding them, keeping them hidden, subduing them, progressing forward; this is called the effort of restraint. What is the effort of guarding? When a bhikṣu, regarding each and every characteristic of authentic samādhi, well guards and maintains them, namely the characteristic of the blue-black corpse, the characteristic of the bloated corpse, the characteristic of the festering corpse, the characteristic of the decomposing corpse, and the characteristic of the half-eaten corpse, cultivating and guarding these so they do not deteriorate and decline; this is called the effort of guarding. What is the effort of cultivation? When a bhikṣu cultivates the four foundations of mindfulness and so forth; this is called the effort of cultivation."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "Abandoning, the effort of restraint,
> Guarding, and the effort of cultivation:
> These four kinds of right efforts
> Are what the Perfectly Enlightened One has taught.
> A bhikṣu who applies diligent effort
> Can bring all outflows to an end."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the four foundations of mindfulness, so too the four right efforts, four bases of spiritual power, five faculties, five powers, seven factors of awakening, eightfold noble path, four paths, four phrases of Dharma, and right contemplation and cultivation are also spoken of in the same way.

---

## (880)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Just as when a person makes constructions in the world, all of them depend upon the ground for their establishment; so too, when a bhikṣu cultivates dhyāna practices, all depend upon heedfulness as their root, heedfulness as their accumulation, heedfulness as their origin, and heedfulness as their support. A bhikṣu who is heedful can cultivate the four dhyānas."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (881)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "In this way, a bhikṣu can sever greed, hatred, and delusion."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with severing greed, hatred, and delusion, so too "taming greed, hatred, and delusion," "ultimate end of greed, ultimate end of hatred, ultimate end of delusion," "escape, detachment, nirvāṇa" are also spoken of in the same way.

---

## (882)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Just as all kinds of herbs and trees depend upon the ground for their growth; so too, all kinds of wholesome dharmas depend upon heedfulness as their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among aromatic substances, black agalloch incense is supreme among all fragrances; so too, among all kinds of wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is foremost among them.

"Just as among hard and firm fragrances, red sandalwood is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, all depend upon heedfulness as their root." And so forth, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among water and land flowers, the utpala flower is foremost; so too, all wholesome dharmas depend upon heedfulness as their root." Up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among flowers that grow on land, the mālikā flower is foremost; so too, all wholesome dharmas have heedfulness as their root." Up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as, bhikṣus, among all footprints of animals, the elephant's footprint is the greatest; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is foremost as the root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all animals, the lion is foremost, being the king of beasts; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all buildings, halls, and pavilions, the ridgepole is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root.

"Just as among all Jambudvīpa fruits, only the one that bears the name 'Jambu' is the foremost of fruits; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root.

"Just as among all kubidāra trees, the sarvayajñīra-kubidāra tree is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is the root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all mountains, Mount Sumeru, the king of mountains, is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all gold, Jambudvīpa gold is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all garments, Kāśi fine cotton cloth is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all colors, white is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all birds, the garuḍa bird is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all kings, the wheel-turning sage king is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all heavenly kings, the Four Great Heavenly Kings are foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all the Thirty-Three Gods, Śakra is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among the Yāma Heaven, King Suyāma is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among the Tuṣita Heaven, the Tuṣita Heavenly King is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among the Nirmāṇarati Heaven, the King of the Nirmāṇarati Heaven is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among the Paranirmitavaśavartin Heaven, the Prince of the Paranirmitavaśavartin Heaven is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among the Brahmā gods, Great Brahmā King is foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as all the streams in Jambudvīpa flow toward the great ocean, and the great ocean is the foremost because it receives them all; so too, all wholesome dharmas flow toward heedfulness." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as all raindrops return to the great ocean; so too, all wholesome dharmas flow toward the ocean of heedfulness." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all lakes, Lake Anavatapta is the foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is the foremost." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all rivers in Jambudvīpa, the four great rivers are foremost, namely the Ganges, the Sindhu, the Vakṣu, and the Sītā; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is the foremost." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among the lights of the stars, the moon is the foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is the foremost." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all great-bodied beings, Rāhu the asura king is the foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all those who enjoy the five sensual pleasures, King Māndhātṛ is the foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all supernatural powers in the desire realm, Māra Pāpīyas is the foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all beings, whether footless, two-footed, four-footed, or many-footed, with form or formless, with perception, without perception, or neither with perception nor without perception, the Tathāgata is the foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all dharmas, conditioned and unconditioned, freedom from desire is the foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all assemblies of dharma, the assembly of the Tathāgata is the foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

"Just as among all practices of austerity and all realms, the realm of holy conduct is the foremost; so too, among all wholesome dharmas, heedfulness is their root." As stated above, up to nirvāṇa.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (883)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in samādhi but not skilled in attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in attainment but not skilled in samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in samādhi and also skilled in attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in samādhi nor skilled in attainment.

"Furthermore, there are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in abiding in samādhi but not skilled in abiding in attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in abiding in attainment but not skilled in abiding in samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in abiding in samādhi and also skilled in abiding in attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in abiding in samādhi nor skilled in abiding in attainment.

"Furthermore, there are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in arising from samādhi but not skilled in arising from attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in arising from attainment but not skilled in arising from samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in arising from samādhi and also skilled in arising from attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in arising from samādhi nor skilled in arising from attainment.

"Furthermore, there are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the timing of samādhi but not skilled in the timing of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the timing of attainment but not skilled in the timing of samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the timing of samādhi and also skilled in the timing of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in the timing of samādhi nor skilled in the timing of attainment.

"Furthermore, there are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the domain of samādhi but not skilled in the domain of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the domain of attainment but not skilled in the domain of samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the domain of samādhi and also skilled in the domain of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in the domain of samādhi nor skilled in the domain of attainment.

"Furthermore, there are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the approach to samādhi but not skilled in the approach to attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the approach to attainment but not skilled in the approach to samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the approach to samādhi and also skilled in the approach to attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in the approach to samādhi nor skilled in the approach to attainment.

"Furthermore, there are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in mindfulness of samādhi but not skilled in mindfulness of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in mindfulness of attainment but not skilled in mindfulness of samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in mindfulness of samādhi and also skilled in mindfulness of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in mindfulness of samādhi nor skilled in mindfulness of attainment.

"Furthermore, there are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the mindfulness and non-mindfulness of samādhi but not skilled in the mindfulness and non-mindfulness of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the mindfulness and non-mindfulness of attainment but not skilled in the mindfulness and non-mindfulness of samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the mindfulness and non-mindfulness of samādhi and also skilled in the mindfulness and non-mindfulness of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in the mindfulness and non-mindfulness of samādhi nor skilled in the mindfulness and non-mindfulness of attainment.

"Furthermore, there are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the approach of samādhi but not skilled in the approach of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the approach of attainment but not skilled in the approach of samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the approach of samādhi and also skilled in the approach of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in the approach of samādhi nor skilled in the approach of attainment.

"Furthermore, there are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in dealing with evil in samādhi but not skilled in dealing with evil in attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in dealing with evil in attainment but not skilled in dealing with evil in samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in dealing with evil in samādhi and also skilled in dealing with evil in attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in dealing with evil in samādhi nor skilled in dealing with evil in attainment.

"Furthermore, there are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the means of samādhi but not skilled in the means of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the means of attainment but not skilled in the means of samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the means of samādhi and also skilled in the means of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in the means of samādhi nor skilled in the means of attainment.

"Furthermore, there are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the calming of samādhi but not skilled in the calming of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the calming of attainment but not skilled in the calming of samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the calming of samādhi and also skilled in the calming of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in the calming of samādhi nor skilled in the calming of attainment.

"Furthermore, there are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the uplifting of samādhi but not skilled in the uplifting of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the uplifting of attainment but not skilled in the uplifting of samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the uplifting of samādhi and also skilled in the uplifting of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in the uplifting of samādhi nor skilled in the uplifting of attainment.

"Furthermore, there are four kinds of dhyāna. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the equanimity of samādhi but not skilled in the equanimity of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the equanimity of attainment but not skilled in the equanimity of samādhi. There is dhyāna where one is skilled in the equanimity of samādhi and also skilled in the equanimity of attainment. There is dhyāna where one is neither skilled in the equanimity of samādhi nor skilled in the equanimity of attainment."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (884)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are three kinds of knowledge belonging to those beyond training. What are the three? The direct knowledge of recollecting past lives belonging to those beyond training, the direct knowledge of the births and deaths of beings belonging to those beyond training, and the direct knowledge of the destruction of the outflows belonging to those beyond training."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "Observing and knowing past lives,
> Seeing heavenly and evil destinies of rebirth,
> All outflows of birth and death exhausted:
> These are the knowledges of the muni.
> His mind being liberated
> From all craving and attachment,
> Having penetrated the three throughout the night,
> Therefore they are called the three knowledges."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (885)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are three kinds of knowledge belonging to those beyond training. What are the three? The direct knowledge of recollecting past lives belonging to those beyond training, the direct knowledge of the births and deaths of beings belonging to those beyond training, and the direct knowledge of the destruction of the outflows belonging to those beyond training.

"What is the direct knowledge of recollecting past lives belonging to those beyond training? A noble disciple knows various matters of past lives, from one life to hundreds of thousands of millions of billions of lives, up to the formation and destruction of eons. I and other beings experienced such and such past lives: such names, such births, such clans, such food, such experiences of suffering and happiness, such long lives, such lengths of dwelling, such appointed spans. I and other beings died in such a place and were reborn in another place; dying in another place, they were reborn in this place. There were such practices, such causes, such convictions. All the various matters of past lives are completely known. This is called the knowledge of recollecting past lives.

"What is the knowledge of births and deaths? A noble disciple, with the divine eye purified and surpassing the human eye, sees beings at the time of death and at the time of rebirth: fair appearance and ugly appearance, superior form and inferior form, going toward evil destinies, receiving rebirth according to their karma, truly knowing. Such beings, accomplished in bodily misconduct, accomplished in verbal misconduct, accomplished in mental misconduct, reviling noble ones, holding wrong views and accepting wrong dharmas, by these causes and conditions, at the breaking up of the body after death, are reborn in evil destinies, in the hells. Such beings, accomplished in bodily good conduct, verbal good conduct, and mental good conduct, not reviling noble ones, accomplished in right view, at the breaking up of the body after death, are reborn in good destinies, among heavenly beings and humans. This is called the knowledge of births and deaths.

"What is the knowledge of the destruction of the outflows? A noble disciple truly knows 'This is suffering,' truly knows 'This is the origin of suffering, this is the cessation of suffering, this is the path leading to the cessation of suffering.' Having thus known and thus seen, the mind is liberated from the outflow of sensual desire, the mind is liberated from the outflow of existence, the mind is liberated from the outflow of ignorance. In liberation, the knowledge and vision arises: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know there will be no further existence.' This is called the knowledge of the destruction of the outflows."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "Observing and knowing past lives,
> Seeing heavenly and evil destinies of rebirth,
> All outflows of birth and death exhausted:
> These are the knowledges of the muni.
> Knowing the mind is liberated
> From all craving and attachment,
> Having penetrated the three domains,
> Therefore they are called the three knowledges."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (886)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain brāhmaṇa came to visit the Buddha. He and the World-Honored One exchanged courteous greetings. After the exchange, he withdrew and sat to one side, and said: "These are the three knowledges of a brāhmaṇa. These are the three knowledges of a brāhmaṇa."

At that time, the World-Honored One asked the brāhmaṇa: "What do you call the three knowledges of a brāhmaṇa?"

The brāhmaṇa addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, the brāhmaṇa's father and mother possess the proper characteristics, without any flaws. For seven generations, the lineage of father and mother has been without reproach. Generation after generation, they have always served as teachers and elders, possessing complete eloquence. They recite all the scriptures, know the names of all classes of things, the distinctions among the myriad things, the combinations and divisions of syllables, and the history of ages past and present. These five kinds of records they fully penetrate. Their appearance is handsome. This is what is called, Gautama, the three knowledges of a brāhmaṇa."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "I do not regard names and verbal expressions as the three knowledges. In the dharma of the noble ones, the true and essential three knowledges are spoken of, namely the knowing and seeing of the noble ones, the true three knowledges of the Dharma and Vinaya of the noble ones."

The brāhmaṇa addressed the Buddha: "What are, Gautama, the three knowledges spoken of in the knowing and seeing of the noble ones, the Dharma and Vinaya of the noble ones?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "There are three kinds of knowledge belonging to those beyond training. What are the three? The knowledge of recollecting past lives belonging to those beyond training, the knowledge of births and deaths belonging to those beyond training, and the knowledge of the destruction of the outflows belonging to those beyond training." As extensively explained in the above sūtra.

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "All dharmas are impermanent.
> Through morality, tranquility, and dhyāna,
> One knows all past lives,
> Has seen the heavenly and evil destinies of rebirth,
> And attains the destruction of the outflows from which birth arises:
> These are the supernatural penetrations of the muni.
> Fully knowing the mind is liberated
> From all greed, hatred, and delusion,
> I say these are the three knowledges,
> Not what is spoken of by mere words.

"Brāhmaṇa, these are the three knowledges spoken of in the Dharma and Vinaya of the noble ones."

The brāhmaṇa addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, these are the true three knowledges."

At that time, the brāhmaṇa, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

## (887)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain brāhmaṇa came to visit the Buddha. He and the World-Honored One exchanged courteous greetings. After the exchange, he withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, my name is Faith."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "What is called 'faith' means faith that increases morality, generosity, learning, relinquishment, and wisdom. This is true faith, not merely having a name that means faith."

At that time, the brāhmaṇa, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

## (888)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain brāhmaṇa came to visit the Buddha. He and the World-Honored One exchanged courteous greetings. After the exchange, he withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, my name is Increase."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "What is called 'increase' means increase in faith, increase in morality, learning, relinquishment, and wisdom. This is true increase, not merely having a name that means increase."

At that time, the brāhmaṇa, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

## (889)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain brāhmaṇa came to visit the Buddha. He inquired after his welfare and well-being. After inquiring, he withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, my name is Equal Arising."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "What is true 'equal arising' means the arising of faith, the arising of morality, learning, relinquishment, and wisdom. This is true equal arising, not merely having a name that means equal arising."

At that time, the brāhmaṇa, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

## (890)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "I shall teach you the unconditioned dharma and the path leading to the unconditioned. Listen carefully and reflect well upon it. What is the unconditioned dharma? It is the permanent exhaustion of greed, the permanent exhaustion of hatred and delusion, the permanent exhaustion of all afflictions. This is the unconditioned dharma. What is the path leading to the unconditioned? It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration. This is called the path leading to the unconditioned."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with "unconditioned," so too "hard to see," "unshakable," "unbending," "deathless," "without outflows," "shelter," "island," "crossing over," "refuge," "protection," "non-revolving," "free from blazing," "free from burning," "flowing through," "cool," "subtle and wondrous," "peaceful and secure," "free from illness," "without possessions," and "nirvāṇa" are also spoken of in the same way.

---

## (891)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Suppose there were a lake, fifty yojanas in length and breadth, and equally deep. If a man were to dip a single hair-tip into that lake water, what do you think, bhikṣus? Is the water in the lake more, or is the single drop of water on the man's hair-tip more?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, the drop on the man's hair-tip is extremely little. The lake water is immeasurably thousands of millions of billions of times more; it cannot be compared."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "One who possesses the seeing of the noble truths, who has accomplished right view, a disciple of the World-Honored One who has seen the noble truths, whose right and immediate realization is complete; at that time, what has been severed and known, its root cut off like the crown of a palmyra tree, never to arise again, the suffering that has been severed is exceedingly vast and immeasurable, like the water of the great lake. The suffering that remains is like the drop of water on the hair-tip."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the drop of water on a hair-tip, so too with a drop of water on the tip of a blade of grass, it is also the same.

As with the lake water, so too the Sarovatī pond, the Ganges river, the Yamunā, the Sarāyu, the Irāvatī, the Mahī, and the great ocean are also spoken of in the same way.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (892)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are six internal sense bases. What are the six? The eye as internal sense base, the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind as internal sense bases. One who observes and accepts these six dharmas with patient endurance is called a faith-follower. Such a one has transcended, departed from birth, departed from the ground of the ordinary person, and though not yet having attained the fruit of stream-entry, up until the end of life will certainly attain the fruit of stream-entry. One who observes these dharmas with superior patient endurance is called a dharma-follower. Such a one has transcended, departed from birth, departed from the ground of the ordinary person, and though not yet having attained the fruit of stream-entry, up until the end of life will certainly attain the fruit of stream-entry. One who observes these dharmas with true and right knowledge, when the three fetters are exhausted and known, namely identity-view, attachment to rules and rituals, and doubt, is called a stream-enterer. Such a one does not fall into determinately evil destinies, is destined for the three awakenings, and after seven more existences among heavenly beings and humans, will reach the final end of suffering. One who observes these dharmas with right knowledge, and does not give rise to any outflows, is liberated through the abandoning of desire, and is called an arhat. All outflows have been exhausted, what was to be done has been done, the heavy burden has been laid down, one's own benefit has been obtained, all fetters of existence have been ended, and the mind is well-liberated through right knowledge."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with the six internal sense bases, so too the six external sense bases, the six consciousness bodies, the six contact bodies, the six feeling bodies, the six perception bodies, the six volition bodies, the six craving bodies, the six element bodies, and the five aggregates are also spoken of as above.

---

## (893)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are five kinds of seeds that grow. What are the five? Root seeds, stem seeds, node seeds, cutting seeds, and actual seeds. If these seeds are not broken, not damaged, not rotten, not injured, not pierced, are firm and new, and they obtain the earth element but do not obtain the water element, those seeds cannot grow and increase. If they obtain the water element but do not obtain the earth element, those seeds cannot grow and increase. Only when they obtain both the earth element and the water element can those seeds grow and increase. In the same way, with karma, afflictions, existence, craving, views, conceit, and ignorance, formations arise. If there is karma but no afflictions, craving, views, or ignorance, then formations cease."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with formations, so too consciousness, name-and-form, the six sense bases, contact, feeling, craving, grasping, existence, birth, and aging-and-death are also spoken of in the same way.

---

## (894)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Regarding my knowledge of the world, of the world and the origin of the world, if I did not know them thus, I would never, among the devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, and worldly beings, attain liberation, escape, release, freedom from inverted perception, nor would I be called one who has attained anuttara-samyak-saṃbodhi. Because I truly know the world and the origin of the world, therefore I, among the devas, worldly beings, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, and other beings, have attained liberation, escape, release, the mind freed from inversion, abiding in completeness, having attained anuttara-samyak-saṃbodhi."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

Thus: the world and the origin of the world; the world and the cessation of the world; the origin of the world and the escape from the world; the origin of the world and the cessation of the world; the origin of the world, the cessation of the world, the gratification of the world, the danger of the world, and the escape from the world; the origin of the world, the cessation of the world, and the escape from the world; the origin of the world and the path leading to the cessation of the world; the origin of the world, the cessation of the world, the path leading to the origin of the world, and the path leading to the cessation of the world; the origin of the world, the cessation of the world, the gratification of the world, the danger of the world, and the escape from the world; the origin of the world, the cessation of the world, the path leading to the origin of the world, the path leading to the cessation of the world, the gratification of the world, the danger of the world, and the escape from the world.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (895)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are three kinds of craving. What are the three? Craving for sensual desire, craving for form, and craving for the formless. In order to sever these three cravings, one should seek a great teacher."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with seeking a great teacher, so too seeking a secondary teacher, an instructor, a great guide, a ferryman, a great ferryman, an expounder, a great expounder, a responsive expounder, an ācārya, a companion, a true friend who is a good friend, one who is compassionate, one who is benevolent, one who desires benefit, one who desires peace, one who desires happiness, one who desires contact, one who desires penetration, one who desires, one who is diligent, one who applies means, one who escapes, one who is firm, one who is courageous, one who is capable, one who collects, one who is constant, one who studies, one who is heedful, one who cultivates, one who reflects, one who recollects, one who contemplates, one who deliberates, one who practices the holy life, one who has spiritual powers, one who is wise, one who cognizes, one who has prajñā, one who discriminates, the foundations of mindfulness, right efforts, faculties, powers, factors of awakening, the path, calming and insight, mindfulness of the body, and right reflection; these are also spoken of in the same way.

---

## (896)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There are three outflows. What are the three? The outflow of sensual desire, the outflow of existence, and the outflow of ignorance. In order to sever these three outflows, one should seek a great teacher."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with seeking a great teacher, so too up to seeking right reflection; they are also spoken of in the same way.

---

## (897)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Venerable Rāhula came to the Buddha, prostrated at his feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, how should one know and how should one see so that, regarding this consciousness-body and all external characteristics, there is no recollection of 'I,' and one exhausts all outflows?"

The Buddha told Rāhula: "There are the six internal sense bases. What are the six? The eye sense base, the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind sense bases. Regarding these dharmas, when one observes them with right knowledge, all outflows are exhausted, and the mind is well-liberated through right knowledge. This is called an arhat, whose outflows are all exhausted, who has done what was to be done, who has laid down the heavy burden, who has obtained one's own benefit, who has ended all fetters of existence, and whose mind is liberated
