# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Thirty-Two

---

### (905)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, Venerable Mahākāśyapa and Venerable Śāriputra were staying on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

Then, a number of non-Buddhist wanderers went to visit Venerable Śāriputra. After exchanging greetings and polite inquiries with the venerable one, they withdrew and sat to one side. They said to Venerable Śāriputra: "How is it, Śāriputra? Does the Tathāgata exist after death?"

Śāriputra said: "Wanderers, the World-Honored One has declared this to be indeterminate."

They asked again: "How is it, Śāriputra? Does the Tathāgata not exist after death?"

Śāriputra answered: "Wanderers, the World-Honored One has declared this to be indeterminate."

They asked again: "Śāriputra, does the Tathāgata both exist and not exist after death?"

Śāriputra answered: "The World-Honored One has declared this to be indeterminate."

They asked Śāriputra again: "Does the Tathāgata neither exist nor not exist after death?"

Śāriputra answered: "Wanderers, the World-Honored One has declared this to be indeterminate."

The non-Buddhist wanderers further asked Venerable Śāriputra: "How is it that to every question we ask, whether the Tathāgata exists after death, does not exist after death, both exists and does not exist, or neither exists nor does not exist, you answer in every case: 'The World-Honored One has declared this to be indeterminate'? How can you be a senior monk yet be like a fool, like an idiot, unskilled and inarticulate, like an infant without wisdom of its own?" Having said this, they rose from their seats and departed.

At that time, Venerable Mahākāśyapa and Venerable Śāriputra were not far apart from each other, each sitting under a tree in daytime meditation. After Venerable Śāriputra knew that the non-Buddhist wanderers had departed, he went to Venerable Mahākāśyapa, and after exchanging greetings and polite inquiries, he withdrew and sat to one side. He fully reported to Venerable Mahākāśyapa about the discussion he had just had with the non-Buddhist wanderers: "Venerable Mahākāśyapa, for what cause and what reason does the World-Honored One not declare whether there is existence after death, non-existence after death, both existence and non-existence, or neither existence nor non-existence after death?"

Venerable Mahākāśyapa said to Śāriputra: "If one says the Tathāgata exists after death, that would be to posit form. If one says the Tathāgata does not exist after death, that would be to posit form. If one says the Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death, that would be to posit form. If one says the Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death, that would be to posit form. The Tathāgata's form is already exhausted, and his mind is well-liberated. To say there is existence after death, this is not so. Non-existence after death, both existence and non-existence, neither existence nor non-existence after death, these also are not so. The Tathāgata's form is already exhausted, and his mind is well-liberated: profound, vast, immeasurable, innumerable, quiescent nirvāṇa.

"Śāriputra, if one says the Tathāgata exists after death, that would be to posit feeling, perception, formations, consciousness, activity, deliberation, deception, the conditioned, and craving; and so too for neither existence nor non-existence after death, it is said likewise. The Tathāgata's craving is already exhausted, and his mind is well-liberated. Therefore, to say there is existence afterwards is not so; non-existence afterwards, both existence and non-existence afterwards, neither existence nor non-existence afterwards, these are not so. The Tathāgata's craving is already exhausted, and his mind is well-liberated: profound, vast, immeasurable, innumerable, quiescent nirvāṇa. Śāriputra, it is because of these causes and these conditions that when there are questions to the World-Honored One about whether the Tathāgata exists, does not exist, both exists and does not exist, or neither exists nor does not exist after death, they cannot be declared."

When the two upright ones had finished their discussion, each returned to his original place.

---

### (906)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Venerable Mahākāśyapa was staying in the Eastern Park, the Hall of Mṛgāramātā, in Śrāvastī. In the late afternoon, he rose from meditation and went to where the Buddha was. He bowed his head to the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, for what cause and what reason did the World-Honored One formerly establish few precepts for the voice-hearers, yet many bhikṣus were happy to train in them; whereas now the World-Honored One establishes many precepts for the voice-hearers, yet few bhikṣus are happy to train in them?"

The Buddha said: "It is so, Kāśyapa. When there is the turbidity of lifespan, the turbidity of afflictions, the turbidity of the kalpa, the turbidity of sentient beings, and the turbidity of views, and when the wholesome dharmas of sentient beings decline and diminish, the Great Teacher establishes many prohibitive precepts for the voice-hearers, yet few are happy to train in them. Kāśyapa, just as when a kalpa is about to be destroyed, before genuine jewels have perished, various counterfeit jewels resembling the real ones appear in the world; once the counterfeit jewels appear, the genuine jewels then disappear. In the same way, Kāśyapa, when the Tathāgata's true Dharma is about to perish, a semblance Dharma arises. Once the semblance Dharma appears in the world, the true Dharma then perishes. Just as a ship in the great ocean laden with many treasures sinks suddenly, the Tathāgata's true Dharma is not like this, gradually declining. The Tathāgata's true Dharma is not destroyed by the earth element, nor destroyed by the water, fire, or wind elements. Rather, when evil sentient beings appear in the world who delight in doing evil, desire to do evil, and accomplish evil, saying what is not Dharma is Dharma, what is Dharma is not Dharma, what is not Vinaya is Vinaya, and what is Vinaya is not Vinaya, with the semblance Dharma blazing in phrases and flavors, the Tathāgata's true Dharma thereupon disappears.

"Kāśyapa, there are five causes and conditions that can cause the Tathāgata's true Dharma to sink and disappear. What are the five? If bhikṣus do not respect, do not revere, and do not humbly make offerings to the Great Teacher; having failed to respect, revere, and humbly make offerings to the Great Teacher, they nevertheless continue to dwell relying on him. If they do not respect, do not revere, and do not humbly make offerings to the Dharma, the training, the teachings to be followed, or the fellow practitioners of the holy life whom the Great Teacher has praised, yet still dwell relying on them. These are called, Kāśyapa, the five causes and conditions by which the Tathāgata's true Dharma thereupon sinks and disappears.

"Kāśyapa, there are five causes and conditions that prevent the Tathāgata's Dharma and Vinaya from disappearing, from being forgotten, and from declining. What are the five? If bhikṣus are respectful, reverential, and humbly make offerings to the Great Teacher, and dwell relying on him; if they are respectful, reverential, and humbly make offerings to the Dharma, the training, the teachings to be followed, and the fellow practitioners of the holy life whom the Great Teacher has praised, and dwell relying on them. Kāśyapa, these are called the five causes and conditions by which the Tathāgata's Dharma and Vinaya do not disappear, are not forgotten, and do not decline. Therefore, Kāśyapa, you should train thus: 'Toward the Great Teacher, one should cultivate respect, reverence, humble offerings, and dwell relying on him; toward the Dharma, the training, the teachings to be followed, and the fellow practitioners of the holy life whom the Great Teacher has praised, one should cultivate respect, reverence, humble offerings, and dwell relying on them.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Venerable Mahākāśyapa rejoiced and was delighted, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (907)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, there was a village headman named Cāracūḷa-Nāḷa who came to visit the Buddha. He greeted him with inquiries and polite words, and after the exchange of greetings, he withdrew and sat to one side. He said to the Buddha: "Gotama, I have heard that the elder performers of old, the aged and venerable ones of song, dance, and comedy, used to say this: 'If a performer, in the midst of a great assembly, sings, dances, jokes, and performs various acts, causing that great assembly to be delighted, joyful, and amused, then by this karmic cause, at the breaking up of the body after death, one is reborn in the Heaven of Delight.' Regarding this, what does the Dharma of Gotama say?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "Enough! Do not ask about this matter." In this way, he was asked three times, yet the headman continued to press the question.

The Buddha told the village headman: "I will now ask you; answer as you see fit. In ancient times, the sentient beings of this village were not free from sensual desire, bound by the bond of sensual desire; not free from ill-will, bound by the bond of ill-will; not free from delusion, bound by the bond of delusion. Those performers, in the midst of the great assembly, performed various songs, dances, musical entertainments, and amusements, causing the people of the assembly to be delighted, joyful, and amused. Village headman, when those people were delighted, joyful, and amused, would this not increase the bonds of desire, ill-will, and delusion?"

The village headman said to the Buddha: "It is so, Gotama."

"Village headman, suppose there is a person bound with ropes behind his back. Then another person, with a long-standing malicious mind, wishing this person neither benefit nor peace nor happiness, repeatedly pours water on the ropes that bind him. Would not that person's bonds become ever tighter?"

The village headman said: "It is so, Gotama."

The Buddha said: "Village headman, the sentient beings of ancient times were likewise not free from the bonds of desire, ill-will, and delusion. On account of those amusements, delights, joys, and laughter, their bonds were further increased."

The village headman said: "Truly so, Gotama. Those performers, by causing sentient beings to be delighted, joyful, and amused, further increased the bonds of desire, ill-will, and delusion. Because of this cause and condition, at the breaking up of the body after death, to be reborn in a good destination is impossible!"

The Buddha told the village headman: "If anyone says that performers of old could cause great assemblies to be delighted, joyful, and amused, and that by this karmic cause they are reborn in the Heaven of Delight, that is a wrong view. For one with wrong views, one should be reborn in one of two destinations: either in a hell destiny or an animal destiny."

When these words were spoken, the village headman Cāracūḷa-Nāḷa wept with streaming tears.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the village headman: "This is precisely why I earlier refused to answer your question three times, saying: 'Village headman, enough! Do not ask about this matter.'"

The village headman said to the Buddha: "Gotama, I am not weeping because of what Gotama has said. I am reflecting on how in the past I was deceived by those foolish, undiscerning, unskillful performers who told me that by performing entertainments before a great assembly, one would be reborn in the Heaven of Delight. I now firmly consider: 'How could performers, by singing, dancing, and amusing, be reborn in the Heaven of Delight?' Gotama, from this day forward, I abandon the unwholesome deeds of those performers, and I take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, and take refuge in the Sangha of bhikṣus."

The Buddha said: "Excellent, village headman! This is the true essential."

At that time, the village headman Cāracūḷa-Nāḷa, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was delighted, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and departed joyfully.

---

### (908)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the village headman "Fighter" came to visit the Buddha. He respectfully greeted him and, after the exchange of greetings, withdrew and sat to one side. He said to the Buddha: "Gotama, I have heard the aged and venerable warriors of old say this: 'If a warrior, clad in heavy armor, bearing sharp weapons, as the vanguard of the troops, is skilled in strategy and able to defeat the enemy, then by this karmic reward, he is reborn in the Arrow-Subjugation Heaven.' In the Dharma of Gotama, what is the meaning of this?"

The Buddha told the Fighter: "Enough! Do not ask about this matter."

In this way, he asked twice and three times, and was refused twice and three times, yet he continued to ask.

The Buddha told the village headman: "I will now ask you; answer as you see fit. Village headman, what do you think? If a warrior, clad in armor, serves as the vanguard for the troops, and is skilled in strategy to defeat the enemy, must this person not first give rise to a mind of harm, wishing to seize, bind, shackle, slash, stab, and kill the other?"

The village headman said to the Buddha: "It is so, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the village headman: "For the warrior, there are three kinds of evil wrongness: of body, of speech, and of mind. Because of these three kinds of evil wrongness, at the breaking up of the body after death, to be reborn in the good destiny of the Arrow-Subjugation Heaven is impossible."

The Buddha told the village headman: "If the aged and venerable warriors of old held such a view and made such a statement, that warriors clad in armor, bearing sharp weapons, racing against the enemy at the fore, skilled in strategy to defeat the enemy, would by this cause and condition be reborn in the Arrow-Subjugation Heaven, that is a wrong view. For one with wrong views, one should be reborn in one of two places: either in a hell destiny or an animal destiny."

When these words were spoken, that village headman wept with streaming tears.

The Buddha told the village headman: "It is for this reason that I previously told you two or three times: 'Enough! I will not explain this to you.'"

The village headman said to the Buddha: "I am not weeping because of what Gotama has said. I am reflecting on the aged and venerable warriors of old, who were foolish, unskillful, and undiscerning, and who through the long night deceived others, saying: 'If a warrior, clad in armor, bearing sharp weapons, racing against the enemy at the fore, and so on, will be reborn in the Arrow-Subjugation Heaven.' That is why I weep. I now firmly consider: 'For warriors, because of their unwholesome karmic causes and conditions, at the breaking up of the body after death, to be reborn in the Arrow-Subjugation Heaven is impossible.' Gotama, from this day forward, I abandon those unwholesome deeds and take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, and take refuge in the Sangha of bhikṣus."

The Buddha told the village headman: "This is the true essential."

At that time, the village headman "Fighter," having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was delighted, rose from his seat, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (909)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, there was a village headman who was a horse-trainer who came to visit the Buddha. He respectfully greeted him, then withdrew and sat to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the horse-trainer village headman: "In taming horses, how many methods are there?"

The village headman answered: "Gotama, there are three methods. What are the three? The first is gentleness, the second is harshness, and the third is both gentleness and harshness."

The Buddha told the village headman: "If with these three methods the horse is still untamed, what then?"

The village headman said: "Then it should be killed."

The village headman said to the Buddha: "Gotama, the Unsurpassed Trainer of Persons, how many methods does he use to train persons?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "I also use three methods to train persons. What are the three? The first is gentleness, the second is harshness, and the third is both gentleness and harshness."

The village headman said to the Buddha: "Gotama, if with these three methods of training persons the person is still untamed, what then?"

The Buddha said: "Village headman, if with three methods of training the person is still untamed, then he should be killed. Why? So as not to bring disgrace upon my Dharma."

The horse-trainer village headman said to the Buddha: "In the Dharma of Gotama, killing living beings is impure. In the Dharma of Gotama, there should be no killing. Yet now you say: 'One who is untamed should also be killed'?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "As you say, in the Tathāgata's Dharma, killing living beings is impure, and the Tathāgata should not kill. Village headman, however, when I train persons with three methods, if they remain untamed, I no longer speak with them, no longer instruct them, and no longer admonish them. Village headman, if the Tathāgata, the trainer of persons, no longer speaks with someone, no longer instructs them, and no longer admonishes them, is this not a kind of killing?"

The horse-trainer village headman said to the Buddha: "Gotama, if the trainer of persons no longer speaks with them, no longer instructs them, and no longer admonishes them, that is truly killing indeed. Therefore, from this day forward, I abandon those unwholesome deeds and take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, and take refuge in the Sangha of bhikṣus."

The Buddha told the village headman: "This is the true essential."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the horse-trainer village headman, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was delighted, rose from his seat, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (910)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, there was a village headman called "Fierce" who came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, through failing to cultivate what dharma does one give rise to anger toward others; and having given rise to anger, speak evil words, so that others give one a name meaning 'evil-natured'?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "Through failing to cultivate right view, one gives rise to anger toward others; having given rise to anger, one speaks evil words, and others give one a name meaning 'evil-natured.' Through failing to cultivate right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration, one gives rise to anger toward others; having given rise to anger, one speaks evil words, and others give one a name meaning 'evil-natured.'"

He further asked: "World-Honored One, through cultivating what dharma does one not have anger toward others; and not having anger, speak good words, so that others give one a name meaning 'virtuous and good'?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "Through cultivating right view, one does not have anger toward others; not having anger, one speaks good words, and others give one a name meaning 'virtuous and good.' Through cultivating right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration, one does not have anger toward others; not having anger, one speaks good words, and others give one a name meaning 'virtuous and good.'"

The Fierce village headman said to the Buddha: "How wonderful, World-Honored One! Well spoken are these words. Because I failed to cultivate right view, I gave rise to anger toward others; having given rise to anger, I spoke evil words, and others gave me a name meaning 'evil-natured.' Because I failed to cultivate right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration, I gave rise to anger toward others; having given rise to anger, I spoke evil words, and others gave me a name meaning 'evil-natured.' Therefore, I shall now abandon anger, harshness, and roughness."

The Buddha told the village headman: "This is the true essential."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Fierce village headman rejoiced and was delighted, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (911)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, there was a village headman called Maṇicūḷaka who came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, the other day the king assembled his great ministers and together they discussed, saying: 'Should ascetics of the Śākya-sons, the bhikṣus, accept and keep gold, silver, and precious things for their own use? Is this pure or impure?' Among them, some said: 'Ascetics of the Śākya-sons should accept and keep gold, silver, and precious things.' Others said: 'They should not accept and keep gold, silver, and precious things for their own use.' World-Honored One, those who say that ascetics of the Śākya-sons should accept and keep gold, silver, and precious things for their own use, did they hear this from the Buddha, or did they speak from their own ideas? In making such a statement, do they accord with the Dharma or not? Is it a true statement or a false statement? Will those who say this not fall into a place of reproach?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "This is a false statement, not a true statement, not a Dharma statement, not an accordance statement; it falls into a place of reproach. Why? Because for ascetics of the Śākya-sons to accept and keep gold, silver, and precious things for their own use is impure. If one accepts and keeps gold, silver, and precious things for oneself, this is not the practice of an ascetic, not the practice of a Śākya-son."

The village headman said to the Buddha: "How wonderful, World-Honored One! That for ascetics of the Śākya-sons to accept and keep gold, silver, and precious things is not the practice of an ascetic, not the practice of a Śākya-son; this is a true statement! World-Honored One, one who speaks thus increases what is supreme and excellent. I too make this statement: 'Ascetics of the Śākya-sons should not accept and keep gold, silver, and precious things for their own use.'"

The Buddha told the village headman: "If it were pure for ascetics of the Śākya-sons to accept and keep gold, silver, and treasures for their own use, then all five kinds of sensual pleasure should also be pure!"

The village headman Maṇicūḷaka, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced, paid respects, and departed.

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing that the village headman Maṇicūḷaka had departed, told Venerable Ānanda: "Have all the bhikṣus who dwell depending on the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove summoned and assembled in the dining hall."

At that time, Venerable Ānanda received the Buddha's instruction and made the announcement throughout, summoning the bhikṣus who dwelt depending on the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove to assemble in the dining hall. When the bhikṣus had assembled, he went and informed the World-Honored One: "The bhikṣus have assembled in the dining hall. May the World-Honored One do as he sees fit."

At that time, the World-Honored One went to the dining hall and sat before the great assembly. Having sat down, he told the bhikṣus: "Today, the village headman Maṇicūḷaka came to see me and spoke thus: 'The other day the king assembled his great ministers and there was this discussion: "Should ascetics of the Śākya-sons accept and keep gold, silver, and precious things for their own use? Is this pure or not?" Among them, some said pure, others said not pure. He now asked the World-Honored One whether those who say it is pure heard this from the Buddha or spoke falsely on their own.'" And so forth as above in full. "That village headman Maṇicūḷaka, having heard what I said, rejoiced and was delighted, paid respects, and departed.

"Bhikṣus, when the king and his great ministers were gathered in discussion, that village headman Maṇicūḷaka made a lion's roar before the great assembly, declaring: 'Ascetics of the Śākya-sons should not accept and keep gold, silver, and precious things for their own use.' Bhikṣus, from this day forward, if you need wood, seek wood; if you need grass, seek grass; if you need a cart, seek a cart; if you need a worker, seek a worker. You must never accept or take for yourselves gold, silver, or any kind of precious things!"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (912)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Campā beside the Gaggarā Pond. At that time, a village headman named Rāja-kaṇṇika came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the village headman Rāja-kaṇṇika: "Nowadays, sentient beings rely on two extremes. What are the two? The first is attachment to lowly, rustic, common, ordinary sensual pleasures. The second is self-mortification, which is wrong, not beneficial. Village headman, there are three kinds of indulgence in sensual pleasures that are lowly, rustic, common, and ordinary. There are three kinds of self-mortification that are wrong and not beneficial.

"Village headman, what are the three kinds of lowly, rustic, common, ordinary indulgence in sensual pleasures? There are those who enjoy sensual pleasures, acquiring wealth unlawfully and by theft, not using it to comfort themselves, not providing for father and mother, not supplying brothers, wife and children, servants, relatives, friends, and acquaintances, and also not making timely offerings to ascetics and brahmins, aspiring to a superior place of happy reward and future rebirth in heaven. This is called the first worldly enjoyer of sensual pleasures.

"Furthermore, village headman, there are enjoyers of sensual pleasures who acquire wealth by both lawful and unlawful means and by theft, using it to comfort themselves, providing for father and mother, supplying brothers, wife and children, servants, relatives, friends, and acquaintances, yet not making timely offerings to ascetics and brahmins, aspiring to a superior place of happy reward and future rebirth in heaven. This is called the second enjoyer of sensual pleasures.

"Furthermore, village headman, there are enjoyers of sensual pleasures who seek wealth lawfully, not by theft, using it to comfort themselves, providing for father and mother, supplying brothers, wife and children, servants, relatives, and acquaintances, making timely offerings to ascetics and brahmins, aspiring to a superior place of happy reward and future rebirth in heaven. This is called the third enjoyer of sensual pleasures.

"Village headman, I do not say all enjoyments of sensual pleasures are equal. I say there is one enjoyer of sensual pleasures who is inferior. I say there is one enjoyer of sensual pleasures who is middling. I say there is one enjoyer of sensual pleasures who is superior.

"What is the inferior enjoyer of sensual pleasures? The one who acquires wealth unlawfully and by theft, and so on, who does not aspire to a superior place of happy reward and future rebirth in heaven. This is what I call the inferior enjoyer of sensual pleasures.

"What is the middling enjoyer of sensual pleasures? The one who seeks wealth by both lawful and unlawful means, and so on, who does not aspire to future rebirth in heaven. This is what I call the second, middling enjoyer of sensual pleasures.

"What is the superior enjoyer of sensual pleasures whom I describe? The one who seeks wealth lawfully, and so on, up to future rebirth in heaven. This is what I call the third, superior enjoyer of sensual pleasures.

"What are the three kinds of self-mortification? This painful practice is wrong, not right, not beneficial. There is one who practices self-mortification, living a withered, austere life, who from the very beginning violates precepts and defiles precepts. He cultivates various painful austerities, striving diligently in his dwelling place, yet he is unable in this present life to attain freedom from burning afflictions, the suprahuman Dharma, or the supreme knowledge and vision of comfortable dwelling. Village headman, this is called the first self-mortification, the withered austere life.

"Furthermore, the self-mortification of a withered austere life: one who from the beginning does not violate or defile precepts, yet cultivates various painful austerities; he too is unable thereby in this present life to attain freedom from burning afflictions, the suprahuman Dharma, or the supreme knowledge and vision of comfortable dwelling. This is called the second self-mortification, the withered austere life.

"Furthermore, the self-mortification of a withered austere life: one who from the beginning does not violate or defile precepts, yet cultivates various painful austerities and practices; he too is unable in this present life to be free from burning afflictions, to attain the suprahuman Dharma, or the supreme knowledge and vision of comfortable dwelling. This is called the third self-mortification, the withered austere life.

"Village headman, I do not say all self-mortification of a withered austere life is equal. I say there is a self-mortifier who is an inferior person, there is a self-mortifier who is a middling person, and there is a self-mortifier who is a superior person.

"What is the inferior self-mortifier? If that self-mortifier from the very beginning violates and defiles precepts, and so on, failing to attain the supreme knowledge and vision of comfortable dwelling, this is what I call the inferior self-mortifier.

"What is the middling self-mortifier? If that self-mortifier does not from the beginning violate or defile precepts, and so on, yet fails to attain the supreme knowledge and vision of comfortable dwelling, this is what I call the middling self-mortifier.

"What is the superior self-mortifier? If that self-mortifier of the withered austere life does not from the beginning violate or defile precepts, and so on, yet fails to attain the supreme knowledge and vision of comfortable dwelling, this is what I call the superior self-mortifier.

"Village headman, these are called the three kinds of self-mortification that are painful, wrong, not right, and not beneficial.

"Village headman, there is a path and a way that does not tend toward the three kinds of enjoyment of sensual pleasures with their accommodating practices, lowly, rustic, common, and ordinary; and does not tend toward the three kinds of self-mortification that are painful, wrong, not right, and not beneficial. Village headman, what is this path, what is this way that does not tend toward the three kinds of enjoyment of sensual pleasures or the three kinds of self-mortification? Village headman, because one is obstructed by sensual desire, one may wish to harm oneself, or wish to harm others, or wish to harm both; in this present life and in future lives one incurs these penalties of wrongdoing, and the mind and mental states experience grief and suffering. Because one is obstructed by ill-will and delusion, one may wish to harm oneself, or wish to harm others, or wish to harm both; in this present life and in future lives one incurs these penalties of wrongdoing, and the mind and mental states experience grief and suffering. If one is free from the obstruction of desire, one does not intend to harm oneself, harm others, or harm both; one does not in this present life or future lives incur these penalties of wrongdoing, and the mind and mental states constantly experience joy and happiness. Likewise, free from the obstruction of ill-will and delusion, one does not wish to harm oneself, does not wish to harm others, does not wish to harm both; one does not in this present life or future lives incur these penalties of wrongdoing, and the mind and mental states constantly experience peace and happiness. In this present life, one is far removed from burning afflictions, does not wait for a future time; one draws near to nirvāṇa, and in this very body one realizes and knows for oneself through direct experience. Village headman, this present-life freedom from burning afflictions, not waiting for a future time, drawing near to nirvāṇa, realizing and knowing for oneself through direct experience in this very body: this is the Noble Eightfold Path, namely right view and so on up to right concentration."

When the World-Honored One was teaching this Dharma, the village headman Rāja-kaṇṇika, being far removed from dust and defilement, attained the pure Dharma eye. At that time, the village headman Rāja-kaṇṇika saw the Dharma, attained the Dharma, knew the Dharma, entered deeply into the Dharma, crossed beyond doubt, not depending on another. In the true Dharma and Vinaya he attained fearlessness. He immediately rose from his seat, straightened his robes, joined his palms together, and said to the Buddha: "I have now crossed over. World-Honored One, I take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, and take refuge in the Sangha of bhikṣus. From now until the end of my life, I shall be an upāsaka."

Having heard what the Buddha said, he rejoiced and was delighted, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (913)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was wandering in the land of the Mallas. He arrived at the settlement of Uruvelā, in the Parrot Jambu Grove.

At that time, there was a village headman named Ghaṭṭama who heard: "The ascetic Gotama is wandering in the land of the Mallas and has arrived at the settlement of Uruvelā, in the Parrot Jambu Grove. He teaches about the arising and cessation of suffering in the present life. I should go to that ascetic Gotama. If I go to the ascetic Gotama, he will surely teach me about the arising and cessation of suffering in the present life." He then went to the settlement of Uruvelā, to where the World-Honored One was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I have heard that the World-Honored One constantly teaches people about the arising and cessation of suffering in the present life. Excellent! World-Honored One, please teach me about the arising and cessation of suffering in the present life."

The Buddha told the village headman: "If I were to speak of the arising and cessation of suffering of the past, I would wonder whether you would believe or not believe, whether you would desire it or not desire it, whether you would be mindful of it or not mindful, whether you would find it pleasant or not pleasant. Would you now be in suffering? If I were to speak of the arising and cessation of suffering of the future, I would wonder whether you would believe or not believe, whether you would desire it or not desire it, whether you would be mindful of it or not mindful, whether you would find it pleasant or not pleasant. Would you now be in suffering? I shall now teach you here about the arising and cessation of suffering in the present life. Village headman, whatever suffering arises for sentient beings, all of it has desire as its root; it is born from desire, gathered from desire, arises from desire, caused by desire, conditioned by desire, and thus suffering is born."

The village headman said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, you have spoken the Dharma extremely concisely, without detailed elaboration; I do not understand. Excellent! World-Honored One, please explain in detail so that we may understand."

The Buddha told the village headman: "I will now ask you; answer as you see fit. Village headman, what do you think? If the sentient beings dwelling in this settlement of Uruvelā were bound, beaten, punished, or killed, would your mind give rise to grief, sorrow, distress, and suffering?"

The village headman said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, not necessarily. If among the sentient beings dwelling in this settlement of Uruvelā there are those toward whom I have desire, attachment, love, and affection, those with whom I am close, then if they are bound, beaten, punished, or killed, I would indeed give rise to grief, sorrow, distress, and suffering. But for those sentient beings toward whom I have no desire, attachment, love, affection, or closeness, if they are bound, beaten, punished, or killed, why should I give rise to grief, sorrow, distress, and suffering without cause?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "Therefore you should know: whatever various kinds of suffering arise for sentient beings, all of it has desire as its root; it is born from desire, gathered from desire, arises from desire, caused by desire, conditioned by desire, and thus the many sufferings are born. Village headman, what do you think? Suppose you had never seen or heard of a woman who might become the mother of your children; would you give rise to desire, attachment, love, and affection?"

The village headman said: "No, World-Honored One."

"Village headman, what do you think? If upon seeing or hearing of that woman who might become the mother of your children, would you then give rise to desire, love, and affection?"

The village headman said: "It is so, World-Honored One."

He further asked: "Village headman, what do you think? If that woman who might become the mother of your children were to change through impermanence, would you give rise to grief, sorrow, distress, and suffering?"

The village headman said: "It is so, World-Honored One. If the woman who might become the mother of my children were to change through impermanence, I might nearly die. How much more merely grief, sorrow, distress, and suffering!"

The Buddha told the village headman: "Therefore you should know: whatever suffering arises for sentient beings, all of it has desire and craving as its root; it is born from desire, gathered from desire, arises from desire, caused by desire, conditioned by desire, and thus suffering is born."

The village headman said: "How wonderful, World-Honored One! Well spoken is this simile about the woman who might become the mother of one's children. I have such a woman who dwells in another place. Every day I send a messenger to inquire whether she is well. When the messenger has not yet returned, I am already in grief and suffering. How much more if impermanence were to come, would I not have grief and suffering!"

The Buddha told the village headman: "Therefore I say: whatever grief and suffering sentient beings have, all of it has desire as its root; it is born from desire, gathered from desire, arises from desire, caused by desire, conditioned by desire, and thus grief and suffering are born."

The Buddha told the village headman: "If one has four objects of love and affection that undergo impermanent change, then four griefs and sufferings arise. If three, two, or one object of love and affection undergoes impermanent change, then one grief and suffering arises. Village headman, if there are no objects of love and affection whatsoever, then there is no grief, suffering, or the dust of toil." He then spoke this verse:

> "If in this world one has no love and affection,
> Then there is no grief, suffering, or the dust of toil.
> All grief and suffering are exhausted and extinguished,
> Like a lotus flower unstained by water."

When the World-Honored One was teaching this Dharma, the village headman Ghaṭṭama, being far removed from dust and defilement, attained the pure Dharma eye. He saw the Dharma, attained the Dharma, penetrated deeply into the Dharma, crossed beyond all doubts, not depending on another, not led by another. In the true Dharma and Vinaya he attained fearlessness. He rose from his seat, straightened his robes, joined his palms together, and said to the Buddha: "I have crossed over. World-Honored One, I have gone beyond. World-Honored One, from this day forward, I take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, and take refuge in the Sangha of bhikṣus. For the rest of my life I shall be an upāsaka. Please remember me!"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the village headman Ghaṭṭama, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was delighted, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (914)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was wandering in the land of Magadha, together with one thousand two hundred and fifty bhikṣus, one thousand upāsakas, and five hundred beggars of leftover food, traveling from city to city, from village to village, wandering through the land, and arriving at the Nāḷa settlement, in the Fine Garment Āmra Garden.

At that time, there was a village headman of the Kammāra clan who was a disciple of the Nirgrantha. He went to the Nirgrantha, bowed at the Nirgrantha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side.

At that time, the Nirgrantha said to the Kammāra village headman: "Can you engage the ascetic Gotama in a 'thorn-discourse' such that the ascetic Gotama can neither speak nor remain silent?"

The village headman said: "Venerable sir, what kind of discourse should I set up as a 'thorn-discourse' to make the ascetic Gotama unable to speak or remain silent?"

The Nirgrantha said to the village headman: "Go to where the ascetic Gotama is and ask him thus: 'Gotama, do you always wish and desire that the welfare and prosperity of households be fully increased? Do you hold such a wish and make such a statement?' If he answers 'no,' then you should say: 'What difference is there between the ascetic Gotama and a common fool?' If he says he does hold such a wish and make such a statement, then you should further ask: 'If the ascetic Gotama holds such a wish and makes such a statement, then why is it that in this time of famine, you wander through the land with a great multitude of one thousand two hundred and fifty bhikṣus, one thousand upāsakas, and five hundred beggars of leftover food, going from city to city, from village to village, depleting the resources of the world? Like great hailstones raining down, you only diminish and do not increase. What Gotama says is utterly inconsistent, neither alike nor similar, contradicting what came before and what follows after.' In this way, village headman, this is called the 'thorn-discourse,' which will make that ascetic Gotama unable to speak or remain silent."

At that time, the Kammāra village headman, having received the Nirgrantha's urging and instruction, went to where the Buddha was, respectfully greeted him, and after the exchange of greetings, withdrew to sit to one side. He said to the Buddha: "Gotama, do you always wish and desire that the welfare and prosperity of households be increased?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "The Tathāgata has through the long night wished and desired that the welfare and prosperity of households be increased, and has always made such a statement."

The village headman said: "If that is so, then why does Gotama, in this time of famine, beg for food among the people, leading a great multitude," and so on up to "neither alike nor similar, contradicting what came before and what follows after?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "I recollect ninety-one kalpas back, and I have not seen a single person who, by giving to one bhikṣu, was thereby exhausted or diminished. Village headman, observe how today some households are greatly wealthy, with much money and property, many relatives, and many servants. You should know that such a household has through the long night been generous in giving, truly still and tranquil, and thus has attained this prosperity and welfare. Village headman, there are eight causes and conditions that cause people's prosperity and welfare to be diminished rather than increased. What are the eight? Being oppressed by the king, being robbed by bandits, being burned by fire, being swept away by floods, stored wealth disappearing on its own, debts not being repaid, being destroyed by enemies, and being squandered by unworthy children. These are the eight kinds of difficulties in accumulating wealth. Village headman, I say that impermanence constitutes the ninth. Thus, village headman, you set aside these nine causes and nine conditions and claim that the ascetic Gotama destroys other people's households. If you do not abandon such evil words and evil views, like an iron spear plunging into water, at the breaking up of the body after death, you will be born in hell."

At that time, the Kammāra village headman became filled with fear and dread, his body hair standing on end. He said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I now repent! Like a fool, like an idiot, unskillful and undiscerning, I have been dishonest toward Gotama, speaking untruth and falsehood."

Having heard what the Buddha said, he rejoiced and was delighted, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (915)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Nāḷa settlement, in the Fine Garment Āmra Garden.

At that time, there was a village headman of the Kammāra clan who had previously been a disciple of the Nirgrantha. He went to the Nirgrantha, bowed at the Nirgrantha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side.

At that time, the Nirgrantha said to the village headman: "Can you engage the ascetic Gotama in a 'thorn-discourse' such that the ascetic Gotama can neither speak nor remain silent?"

The village headman said to the Nirgrantha: "Venerable sir, what kind of 'thorn-discourse' would make the ascetic Gotama unable to speak or remain silent?"

The Nirgrantha said to the village headman: "Go to where the ascetic Gotama is and say thus: 'Gotama, do you not always wish to bring comfort and peace to all sentient beings, and praise bringing comfort and peace to all sentient beings?' If he says 'no,' you should say: 'What difference is there between Gotama and a common fool?' If he says that he does always wish to bring comfort and peace to all sentient beings, and praises bringing comfort and peace to all sentient beings, then you should further ask: 'If you wish to bring comfort and peace to all sentient beings, then why is it that you teach the Dharma to some people and not to others?' By asking thus, this is called the 'thorn-discourse,' which will make that ascetic Gotama unable to speak or remain silent."

At that time, the village headman, having received the Nirgrantha's urging, went to where the Buddha was. After respectfully greeting him, he withdrew to sit to one side and said to the Buddha: "Gotama, do you not wish to always bring comfort and peace to all sentient beings, and praise bringing comfort and peace to all sentient beings?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "The Tathāgata has through the long night been compassionate, bringing comfort and peace to all sentient beings, and has always praised bringing comfort and peace to all sentient beings."

The village headman said to the Buddha: "If that is so, then why does the Tathāgata teach the Dharma to some people and not to others?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "I will now ask you; answer as you see fit. Village headman, suppose there is a farmer who has three kinds of fields. One field has rich, fertile, and moist soil. The second field is middling. The third field is poor. What do you think, village headman? That farmer, among his three kinds of fields, which field would he first plow and sow?"

The village headman said: "Gotama, he would first plow and sow the most rich, fertile, and moist one."

"Village headman, which field would he next plow and sow?"

The village headman said: "Gotama, he would next plow and sow the middling field."

The Buddha told the village headman: "Which field would he then next plow and sow?"

The village headman said: "He would next plow and sow the poorest and most barren field."

The Buddha told the village headman: "Why is that?"

The village headman said: "He does not wish to waste his field; he just wants to preserve his seeds."

The Buddha told the village headman: "I too am like this. Like that rich, fertile, and moist field, my bhikṣus and bhikṣuṇīs are just the same. I constantly teach them the true Dharma, good in the beginning, good in the middle, good in the end, with good meaning and good flavor, complete, full, and pure, the holy life clear and white, revealed and displayed. Having heard the Dharma, they rely on my shelter, my island, my covering, my shade, my direction. They constantly observe me with pure eyes and dwell thus, making this reflection: 'The Dharma that the Buddha has taught, I entirely receive and uphold, so that through the long night I may be benefited, peaceful, and happy.'

"Village headman, like that middling field, my lay disciples, upāsakas and upāsikās, are just the same. I also teach them the true Dharma, good in the beginning, good in the middle, good in the end, with good meaning and good flavor, complete, full, and pure, the holy life clear and white, disclosed and revealed. Having heard the Dharma, they rely on my shelter, my island, my covering, my shade, my direction. They constantly observe me with pure eyes and dwell thus, making this reflection: 'The Dharma that the World-Honored One has taught, I entirely receive and uphold, so that through the long night I may be benefited, peaceful, and happy.'

"Village headman, like that farmer's poorest field, so too for the non-Buddhist heterodox Nirgrantha followers, I also teach the Dharma, good in the beginning, good in the middle, good in the end, with good meaning and good flavor, complete, full, and pure, the holy life clear and white, revealed and displayed. However, among them, for those who hear little of the Dharma, I also teach them; for those who hear much of the Dharma, I also teach them. And if among that assembly there are those who, from my well-spoken Dharma, understand even one sentence and know its meaning, they too will through the long night be benefited, peaceful, and happy."

At that time, the village headman said to the Buddha: "How extraordinary, World-Honored One! Well spoken is this simile of the three fields."

The Buddha told the village headman: "Listen, and I will speak to you with another simile. Suppose a man has three water vessels. The first is not cracked, not broken, and does not seep or leak. The second vessel is not cracked or broken but does seep and leak. The third vessel is cracked, broken, and seeps and leaks. What do you think, village headman? That man, among those three vessels, into which one would he always pour pure water?"

The village headman said: "Gotama, he would first pour water into the one that is not cracked, not broken, and does not seep or leak."

The Buddha told the village headman: "Into which vessel would he next pour water?"

The village headman said: "Gotama, he would next pour water into the vessel that is not cracked or broken but does seep and leak."

The Buddha told the village headman: "When that vessel is full, into which vessel would he lastly pour water?"

The village headman said: "Into the vessel that is cracked, broken, and seeps and leaks, he would lastly pour water. Why? Because it is only for a brief moment's minor use."

The Buddha told the village headman: "Like that man's vessel that is not cracked, not broken, and does not seep or leak, my disciples, the bhikṣus and bhikṣuṇīs, are just the same. I constantly teach them the true Dharma, and so on up to through the long night they are benefited, peaceful, and happy. Like the second vessel that is not cracked or broken but does seep and leak, my disciples, the upāsakas and upāsikās, are just the same. I constantly teach them the true Dharma, and so on up to through the long night they are benefited, peaceful, and happy. Like the third vessel that is cracked, broken, and seeps and leaks, the non-Buddhist heterodox Nirgrantha followers are just the same. I also teach them the true Dharma, good in the beginning, good in the middle, good in the end, with good meaning and good flavor, complete, full, and pure, the holy life clear and white, revealed and displayed. I teach them whether many or few. If among them there are those who understand even one sentence of my teaching and know its meaning, they too will attain peace and happiness through the long night."

At that time, the Kammāra village headman, having heard what the Buddha said, was greatly filled with fear and dread, his body hair standing on end. He bowed at the Buddha's feet and repented: "World-Honored One! Like a fool, like an idiot, unskillful and undiscerning, I have not been truthful or honest toward the World-Honored One, but have spoken falsely and deceptively!"

Having heard what the Buddha said, he rejoiced and was delighted, bowed at the Buddha's feet, and departed.

---

### (916)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the Nāḷa settlement, in the Fine Garment Āmra Garden.

At that time, there was a village headman of the Kammāra clan, a disciple of the Nirgrantha, who came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the village headman: "What do you wish to discuss? What does Nirgrantha Jñātaputra teach?"

The village headman said: "That Nirgrantha Jñātaputra says: 'Those who kill living beings all fall into hell, for by doing it repeatedly, they are led there. Likewise, those who steal, commit sexual misconduct, and speak falsely all fall into hell, for by doing it repeatedly, they are led there.'"

The Buddha told the village headman: "If, as Nirgrantha Jñātaputra says, those who kill living beings fall into hell, and by doing it repeatedly are led there, then no sentient being would fall into hell. Village headman, what do you think? What kind of sentient being at all times has a mind to kill? And at what times does one have a mind not to kill? And so on, at what times does one have a mind to speak falsely, and at what times does one have a mind not to speak falsely?"

The village headman said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, a person throughout day and night has only a small amount of time with a mind to kill, and so on, only a small amount of time with a mind to speak falsely; but for the greater part of time, one does not have a mind to kill, and so on, or to speak falsely."

The Buddha told the village headman: "If that is so, is it not the case that no person should fall into hell? Yet the Nirgrantha says: 'Those who kill living beings all fall into hell; by doing it repeatedly, they are led there; and so on up to false speech, likewise.' Village headman, that great teacher appeared in the world, thought and deliberated according to conceptual thinking, dwelt in the realm of conceptual thinking, and from the standpoint of ordinary people devised his own views, deliberating according to his own ideas. He taught his disciples thus, saying: 'Those who kill living beings all fall into hell; by doing it repeatedly, they are led there; and so on up to false speech, likewise.' If his disciples believe his teaching and think: 'My great teacher knows what he knows and sees what he sees, and can teach disciples thus: "Those who kill living beings all fall into hell; by doing it repeatedly, they are led there." I in the past have had a mind to kill, to steal, to commit sexual misconduct, and to speak falsely, and I shall fall into hell.' Having attained such a view, and so on up to not abandoning this view, not becoming disgusted with those deeds, not realizing remorse, in the future they will not abandon killing, and so on up to not abandoning false speech. Their liberation of mind is not fulfilled, and their liberation by wisdom is not fulfilled either. Because liberation of mind is not fulfilled and liberation by wisdom is not fulfilled, this constitutes slandering the noble ones, which is wrong view. Because of the cause and condition of wrong view, at the breaking up of the body after death, they are born in an evil destiny, in hell. Thus, village headman, there are causes and conditions for the afflictions of sentient beings; there are causes and conditions for the karmic afflictions of sentient beings.

"Village headman, when a Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Enlightened One, Perfect in Knowledge and Conduct, Well-Gone One, Knower of the World, Unsurpassed One, Trainer of Persons, Teacher of Gods and Humans, Buddha, World-Honored One appears in the world, he constantly reproves killing and praises non-killing; reproves stealing, sexual misconduct, and false speech, and praises not stealing, not engaging in misconduct, and not speaking falsely. He constantly teaches his voice-hearers with this Dharma, causing them to be mindful, delighted, faithful, and reverential, saying: 'My Great Teacher knows what he knows and sees what he sees; he reproves killing and praises non-killing, and so on up to reproves false speech and praises not speaking falsely. In the past, because of my foolishness and lack of wisdom, I had a mind to kill. Because of this, I now reproach myself with remorse.' Although one cannot undo those past deeds, yet because of this remorse, in the future one can abandon killing, and so on up to abandoning stealing, misconduct, and false speech. One can also fulfill the right liberation of mind and fulfill the liberation by wisdom. When liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom are fulfilled, one does not slander the noble ones and achieves right view. Because of the cause and condition of right view, one is born in a good destiny, in heaven. Thus, village headman, there are causes and conditions for the purification of sentient beings' karmic afflictions.

"Village headman, that learned noble disciple trains thus: 'From time to time, day and night, one observes that one has a mind to kill for only a short time, and for the greater part of time one has a mind not to kill.' Regarding the occasions when one has had a mind to kill, one should reproach oneself with remorse, knowing it is not right, not fitting. Regarding the occasions when one has not had a mind to kill, being without enmity, without hostility, one gives rise to sympathetic joy. Having given rise to sympathetic joy, delight is born. Having given rise to delight, the mind becomes tranquil. When the mind becomes tranquil, the mind experiences happiness. Having experienced happiness, the mind becomes concentrated. When the mind is concentrated, the noble disciple, with a mind imbued with loving-kindness, without enmity, without jealousy, without ill-will, vast and immeasurable, fills one direction and dwells in right attainment; the second direction, the third direction, and so on up to the four directions, the four intermediate directions, above and below, the entire world, the mind imbued with loving-kindness, without enmity, without jealousy, without ill-will, vast and immeasurable, well cultivated, filling all directions, completely dwelling in right attainment."

At that time, the World-Honored One scooped up a little bit of soil on his fingernail and said to the Kammāra village headman: "What do you think, village headman? Is the soil on my fingernail greater, or is the soil of the great earth greater?"

The village headman said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, the soil on the fingernail is extremely little; the soil of the great earth is immeasurable and innumerable."

The Buddha told the village headman: "Just as the soil on the fingernail is very little and the soil of the great earth is immeasurable in quantity, in the same way, when one cultivates and repeatedly cultivates a mind imbued with loving-kindness, whatever limited karma one has is like the soil on the fingernail; it cannot carry one away and cannot cause one to remain in that state. Likewise, against stealing one counters with compassion; against sexual misconduct one counters with sympathetic joy; against false speech one counters with equanimity. They cannot be compared."

When these words were spoken, the Kammāra village headman, being far removed from dust and defilement, attained the pure Dharma eye. The village headman saw the Dharma, attained the Dharma, realized the Dharma, knew the Dharma, entered deeply into the Dharma, left behind all doubts, not depending on another, not following another. In the true Dharma and Vinaya he attained fearlessness. He rose from his seat, straightened his robes, placed his right knee on the ground, joined his palms together, and said to the Buddha: "I have crossed over, World-Honored One. I have gone beyond, World-Honored One. I now take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, and take refuge in the Sangha of bhikṣus. For the rest of my life I shall be an upāsaka. World-Honored One, suppose a man wished to obtain a lamp for light and took horse-tail hair to make a wick, and wished to blow it into flame; he would never obtain light, only exhaust himself in vain, and the lamp would never be lit. I too was like this: wishing to obtain wisdom and insight, I went to the foolish Nirgrantha teachers, foolishly associating with fools, in foolish fellowship, foolishly serving them, toiling in vain without obtaining wisdom and insight. Therefore, I now again take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, and take refuge in the Sangha. From now on, toward those foolish, unskillful, and undiscerning Nirgranthas, I shall have less faith, less respect, less love, and less affection, and shall be far removed from them. Therefore, for the third time, I take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, and take refuge in the Sangha. For the rest of my life I shall be an upāsaka, purifying my own mind."

At that time, the Kammāra village headman, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was delighted, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (917)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In the world there are three kinds of trained horses. What are the three? There is a horse that is complete in speed but not complete in color and not complete in physical form. There is a horse that is complete in color and complete in speed but not complete in physical form. There is a horse that is complete in speed, complete in color, and complete in physical form. In the same way, there are three kinds of trained men. What are the three? There is a man complete in speed but not complete in color and not complete in physical form. There is a man complete in speed and complete in color but not complete in physical form. There is a man complete in speed, complete in color, and complete in physical form.

"Bhikṣus, what is the untrained man who is complete in speed but not complete in color and not complete in physical form? There is a man who truly knows this suffering, truly knows this origin of suffering, this cessation of suffering, and this path leading to the cessation of suffering. Having observed thus, the three fetters are severed: identity view, attachment to precepts and practices, and doubt. With these three fetters severed, one attains stream-entry, never to fall into an evil destiny, certain to proceed rightly toward the three awakenings, with at most seven more rebirths among gods and humans before reaching the final end of suffering. This is called being complete in speed. What is not being complete in color? If asked about
