# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Thirty-Three

---

### (919)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In the world there are three kinds of fine horses. What are the three? There is a horse endowed with swiftness, but not endowed with color, and not endowed with physical form. There is a horse endowed with swiftness and endowed with color, but not endowed with physical form. There is a horse endowed with swiftness, endowed with color, and endowed with physical form. Likewise, in this Dharma and Vinaya there are three kinds of good men. What are the three? There is a good man endowed with swiftness, but not endowed with color, and not endowed with physical form. There is a good man endowed with swiftness and endowed with color, but not endowed with physical form. There is a good man endowed with swiftness, endowed with color, and endowed with physical form.

"What is a good man endowed with swiftness, but not endowed with color, and not endowed with physical form? It means a good man who truly knows this noble truth of suffering, truly knows this noble truth of the origin of suffering, truly knows this noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and truly knows this noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Having thus known and thus seen, his mind is liberated from the outflow of desire, his mind is liberated from the outflow of existence, and his mind is liberated from the outflow of ignorance: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.' This is called being endowed with swiftness. How is he not endowed with color? If someone asks about Abhidharma or Vinaya, he is unable to give a definitive explanation. This is called not being endowed with color. What is not being endowed with physical form? He is not a well-known monk of great virtue, and so forth, to the point of being unable to attract offerings of medicine and requisites. This is called not being endowed with physical form. This is called a good man endowed with swiftness, but not endowed with color, and not endowed with physical form.

"What is a good man endowed with swiftness and endowed with color, but not endowed with physical form? It means a good man who truly knows this noble truth of suffering, and so forth up to not receiving further existence. This is called being endowed with swiftness. How is he endowed with color? If someone asks about Abhidharma or Vinaya, he is able to give a definitive explanation. This is called being endowed with color. What is not being endowed with physical form? He is not a well-known monk of great virtue, and so forth, to the point of being unable to attract offerings of medicine and requisites. This is called a good man endowed with swiftness and endowed with color, but not endowed with physical form.

"What is a good man endowed with swiftness, endowed with color, and endowed with physical form? It means a good man who truly knows this noble truth of suffering, and so forth up to not receiving further existence. This is called being endowed with swiftness. What is being endowed with color? It means a good man who, if someone asks about Abhidharma or Vinaya, is able to give a definitive explanation. This is called being endowed with color. What is being endowed with physical form? It means a good man who is well-known and of great virtue, and so forth, to the point of being able to attract offerings of medicine and requisites. This is called being endowed with physical form. This is called a good man endowed with swiftness, endowed with color, and endowed with physical form."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (920)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In the world there are three kinds of fine horses ridden by kings. What are the three? A fine horse endowed with color, endowed with strength, and endowed with swiftness. Likewise, in the true Dharma and Vinaya there are three kinds of good men who are served, supplied, and revered by the world, being unsurpassed fields of merit. What are the three? A good man endowed with color, endowed with strength, and endowed with swiftness.

"What is being endowed with color? It means a good man who dwells in pure morality, in the discipline of the prātimokṣa, perfect in conduct and deportment, who, seeing even the slightest fault, generates fear, and who upholds the training precepts. This is called being endowed with color.

"What is being endowed with strength? He generates the desire, exerts effort, applies himself, and sustains and increases the abandoning of already-arisen evil and unwholesome dharmas. He generates the desire, exerts effort, applies himself, and sustains and increases the non-arising of unarisen evil and unwholesome dharmas. He generates the desire, exerts effort, applies himself, and sustains and increases the arising of unarisen wholesome dharmas. He generates the desire, exerts effort, applies himself, and sustains and increases the maintenance without loss of already-arisen wholesome dharmas. This is called being endowed with strength.

"What is being endowed with swiftness? He truly knows this noble truth of suffering, and so forth up to attaining arhatship and not receiving further existence. This is called being endowed with swiftness. This is called a good man endowed with color, endowed with strength, and endowed with swiftness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (921)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "When a fine horse in the world possesses four qualities, it should be known as a fine horse ridden by kings. What are the four? Namely: good temperament, swiftness, capability, and gentleness. Likewise, a good man who accomplishes four virtues is honored and esteemed by the world, served and supplied, and is an unsurpassed field of merit. What are the four? A good man who accomplishes the body of a learner-beyond-training's morality, the body of a learner-beyond-training's concentration, the body of a learner-beyond-training's wisdom, and the body of a learner-beyond-training's liberation."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (922)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In the world there are four kinds of fine horses. There is a fine horse that, when harnessed to a level carriage, sees the shadow of the whip and gallops swiftly, skillfully observing the driver's manner, whether slow or fast, left or right, following the driver's intention. This, bhikṣus, is called the first virtue of a fine horse in the world. Furthermore, bhikṣus, there is a fine horse in the world that cannot be startled merely by seeing the shadow of the whip, but when the whip touches its hair and tail, it becomes alert and swift, perceiving the driver's intention, whether slow or fast, left or right. This is called the second fine horse in the world. Furthermore, bhikṣus, there is a fine horse in the world that cannot be startled by seeing the shadow of the whip or by the touching of its skin and hair to follow the person's intention, but when the whip slightly pierces its skin and flesh, it becomes alert and responsive, following the driver's intention, whether slow or fast, left or right. This, bhikṣus, is called the third fine horse. Furthermore, bhikṣus, there is a fine horse in the world that cannot be startled by seeing the shadow of the whip, by the touching of its skin and hair, or by a slight piercing of its skin and flesh, but only when an iron spike is driven into its body, penetrating the skin and injuring the bone, does it then become alert, pulling the cart onto the road, following the driver's intention, whether slow or fast, left or right. This is called the fourth fine horse in the world.

"Likewise, in the true Dharma and Vinaya there are four kinds of good men. What are the four? A good man who hears that in another village a man or woman has fallen ill and is suffering, even to the point of death; hearing this, he generates fear and dread, relies on right reflection, just as that fine horse is tamed merely by seeing the shadow of the whip. This is called the first good man who, in the true Dharma and Vinaya, is able to tame himself. Furthermore, a good man who cannot, upon hearing of another village's men or women suffering from old age, illness, or death, generate fear and dread and rely on right reflection; but upon seeing in another village men or women suffering from old age, illness, or death, he generates fear and dread and relies on right reflection, just as that fine horse is swiftly tamed when the whip touches its hair and tail, following the driver's intention. This is called the second good man who, in the true Dharma and Vinaya, is able to tame himself. Furthermore, a good man who cannot, upon hearing or seeing in another village men or women suffering from old age, illness, or death, generate a mind of fear and dread and rely on right reflection; but upon seeing in his own village or town a close acquaintance or relative suffering from old age, illness, or death, he generates fear and dread and relies on right reflection, just as that fine horse is tamed when the whip pierces its skin and flesh, following the driver's intention. This is called a good man who, in the noble Dharma and Vinaya, tames himself. Furthermore, a good man who cannot, upon hearing or seeing in another village men or women, or his own close relatives, suffering from old age, illness, or death, generate a mind of fear and dread and rely on right reflection; but with regard to his own body's old age, illness, and death, he is able to generate aversion and dread and relies on right reflection, just as that fine horse is tamed only when the spike pierces through muscle and reaches bone, following the driver's intention. This is called the fourth good man who, in the noble Dharma and Vinaya, is able to tame himself."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (923)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a horse trainer named Keśin came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I observe the people of the world to be very lowly, like a flock of sheep. World-Honored One, only I am capable of training horses. With wild and unruly bad horses, I employ methods, and in a short time I make all their flaws manifest. According to their flaws, I use appropriate methods to tame them."

The Buddha asked the horse-trainer village headman: "How many methods do you use to train horses?"

The horse trainer addressed the Buddha: "There are three methods for training bad horses. What are the three? First, gentleness; second, roughness; third, both gentleness and roughness."

The Buddha asked the village headman: "If you use these three methods to train horses and they still cannot be tamed, what do you do then?"

The horse trainer addressed the Buddha: "If they ultimately cannot be tamed, then I kill them. Why? So as not to bring disgrace upon me." The horse trainer addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the unsurpassed tamer of men. How many methods does he use to tame men?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "I also use three methods to tame men. What are the three? First, purely gentle; second, purely rough; third, both gentle and rough."

The Buddha told the village headman: "What is meant by purely gentle? As when it is said: 'This is good conduct of body; this is the reward of good conduct of body. This is good conduct of speech and mind; this is the reward of good conduct of speech and mind. This is called heaven; this is called the human realm; this is called the good destinies of spontaneous birth; this is called nirvāṇa.' This is the gentle teaching.

"What is rough? As when it is said: 'This is bad conduct of body; this is the retribution of bad conduct of body. This is bad conduct of speech and mind; this is the retribution of bad conduct of speech and mind. This is called hell; this is called the animal realm; this is called the hungry ghost realm; this is called the evil destinies; this is called falling into evil destinies.' This is the Tathāgata's rough teaching.

"What is both gentle and rough? It means the Tathāgata sometimes speaks of good conduct of body, sometimes speaks of the reward of good conduct of body; sometimes speaks of good conduct of speech and mind, sometimes speaks of the reward of good conduct of speech and mind. Sometimes he speaks of bad conduct of body, sometimes speaks of the retribution of bad conduct of body; sometimes speaks of bad conduct of speech and mind, sometimes speaks of the retribution of bad conduct of speech and mind. Thus: this is called heaven; thus: this is called the human realm; thus: this is called the good destinies; thus: this is called nirvāṇa. Thus: this is called hell; thus: this is called the animal realm and hungry ghost realm; thus: this is called the evil destinies; thus: this is called falling into evil destinies. This is the Tathāgata's teaching of both gentleness and roughness."

The horse trainer addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if with these three methods of taming beings, there are those who cannot be tamed, what is done then?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "They too are killed. Why? So as not to bring disgrace upon me."

The horse trainer addressed the Buddha: "If killing living beings, in the World-Honored One's Dharma, is impure, and in the World-Honored One's Dharma there is also no killing of living beings, yet now you speak of killing. What is the meaning of this?"

The Buddha told the village headman: "Just so! Just so! In the Tathāgata's Dharma, killing living beings is impure, and in the Tathāgata's Dharma there is no killing of living beings. However, in the Tathāgata's Dharma, those who through three kinds of instruction cannot be tamed are no longer spoken to, no longer taught, no longer admonished. Village headman, what do you think? In the Tathāgata's Dharma, when one is no longer spoken to, no longer taught, no longer admonished, is that not death?"

The horse trainer addressed the Buddha: "Truly so, World-Honored One! When one is no longer spoken to and is forever neither taught nor admonished, that is truly death. World-Honored One, for this reason, from today onward I will abandon all evil and unwholesome actions."

The Buddha told the village headman: "Well spoken indeed!"

At that time, the horse-trainer village headman Keśin, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his delight, bowed at the Buddha's feet, and departed.

---

### (924)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In the world, horses have eight vices. What are the eight? When a bad horse is about to be harnessed to a carriage, it kicks people with its hind legs, kneels on its front legs, and rears its head to bite people. This is called the first vice of a horse in the world. Furthermore, when a bad horse is being harnessed to a carriage, it lowers its head and shakes the yoke. This is called the second vice of a bad horse in the world. Furthermore, when a bad horse in the world is being harnessed to a carriage, it goes off the road, or leans the carriage to one side, causing it to overturn. This is called the third vice. Furthermore, when a bad horse in the world is being harnessed to a carriage, it raises its head and backs up. This is called the fourth vice of a bad horse in the world. Furthermore, when a bad horse in the world is being harnessed to a carriage, upon receiving the slightest touch of the whip, it breaks the reins and snaps the bridle, running wildly in all directions. This is called the fifth vice. Furthermore, when a bad horse in the world is being harnessed to a carriage, it raises its two front legs and stands like a person. This is called the sixth vice. Furthermore, when a bad horse in the world is being harnessed, though the whip is applied to it, it stands firmly without moving. This is called the seventh vice. Furthermore, when a bad horse in the world is being harnessed, it bunches its four legs together and lies on the ground, refusing to get up. This is called the eighth vice.

"Likewise, a bad man in the world has eight kinds of faults in the true Dharma and Vinaya. What are the eight? When a bhikṣu is accused by his fellow practitioners of the holy life based on what is seen, heard, or suspected, he becomes angry and retaliates by reproaching them, saying: 'You are foolish and unable to discern what is good. Others accuse you, yet how can you accuse me?' Just as that bad horse kicks with its hind legs, kneels with its front legs, and breaks the harness and snaps the yoke. This is called the first fault of a man in the true Dharma and Vinaya.

"Furthermore, when a bhikṣu is accused by fellow practitioners of the holy life based on what is seen, heard, or suspected, he retaliates by bringing up the other's faults. Just as a bad horse angrily stiffens its neck and breaks the yoke. This is called the second fault of a man in the true Dharma and Vinaya.

"Furthermore, when a bhikṣu is accused by fellow practitioners of the holy life based on what is seen, heard, or suspected, he does not answer properly but speaks irrelevantly about other matters, displaying anger, arrogance, concealment, resentment, intolerance, and acting without reason. Just as that bad horse goes off the proper road and overturns the carriage. This is called the third fault of a man in the true Dharma and Vinaya.

"Furthermore, when a bhikṣu is accused by fellow practitioners of the holy life based on what is seen, heard, or suspected, and they ask him to recall, he says: 'I do not remember!' He resists and refuses to submit. Just as that bad horse shrinks back and retreats. This is called the fourth fault of a man in the true Dharma and Vinaya.

"Furthermore, when a bhikṣu is accused by fellow practitioners of the holy life based on what is seen, heard, or suspected, he shows contempt, disregarding those persons and disregarding the Sangha, gathers up his robes and bowl, and departs as he pleases. Just as that bad horse, when the whip is applied, runs wildly in all directions. This is called the fifth fault of a man in the true Dharma and Vinaya.

"Furthermore, when a bhikṣu is accused by fellow practitioners of the holy life based on what is seen, heard, or suspected, he seats himself on a high seat and contends with the senior monks over right and wrong. Just as that bad horse stands up on two legs like a person. This is called the sixth fault of a man in the true Dharma and Vinaya.

"Furthermore, when a bhikṣu is accused by fellow practitioners of the holy life based on what is seen, heard, or suspected, he remains silent and does not respond, vexing the assembly. Just as that bad horse, when the whip is applied, stands rigidly without moving. This is called the seventh fault of a man in the true Dharma and Vinaya.

"Furthermore, when a bhikṣu is accused by fellow practitioners of the holy life based on what is seen, heard, or suspected, he then abandons the precepts, producing his own regression and decline. He goes to the monastery gate and says: 'Be silent and enjoy your peace and comfort. I myself will abandon the precepts and fall away.' Just as that bad horse bunches its four legs together and lies on the ground without moving. This is called the eighth fault of a man in the true Dharma and Vinaya. These, bhikṣus, are the eight kinds of faults of a man in the true Dharma and Vinaya."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (925)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "When a fine horse in the world possesses eight virtues, it can travel whatever distance a person wishes. What are the eight? Being born in the land of fine horses: this is called the first virtue of a fine horse. Furthermore, having a gentle temperament, not startling or frightening people: this is called the second virtue of a fine horse. Furthermore, a fine horse does not choose its food or drink: this is called the third virtue of a fine horse. Furthermore, a fine horse is averse to impurity and chooses clean ground on which to lie: this is called the fourth virtue of a fine horse. Furthermore, a fine horse quickly displays its various temperamental habits to the trainer, and the trainer, upon training it, quickly eliminates those habits: this is called the fifth virtue of a fine horse. Furthermore, a fine horse, when settled in harness, does not look at other horses but, regardless of the load's weight, exerts its full strength: this is called the sixth virtue of a fine horse. Furthermore, a fine horse always follows the proper road and does not take a wrong path: this is called the seventh virtue of a fine horse. Furthermore, a fine horse, whether sick or old, strives to pull the carriage without weariness or fatigue: this is called the eighth virtue of a fine horse.

"Likewise, when a man in the true Dharma and Vinaya possesses eight virtues, he should be known as a worthy man. What are the eight? A worthy man who dwells in right morality, in the discipline of the prātimokṣa, perfect in conduct and deportment, who, seeing even the slightest fault, generates fear, and who upholds the training precepts: this is called the first virtue of a man in the true Dharma and Vinaya. Furthermore, a man who by nature is worthy and good, well-tamed and well-settled, not troubling or frightening his fellow practitioners of the holy life: this is called the second virtue of a man. Furthermore, a man who goes on successive alms rounds, and whatever he receives, whether coarse or fine, his mind is equanimous, neither disdaining nor attaching: this is called the third virtue of a man. Furthermore, a man whose mind generates aversion toward evil actions of body, evil actions of speech and mind, evil and unwholesome dharmas and all afflictions, the heavy reception of further existences, the blazing suffering of retribution, and toward the future suffering of birth, old age, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, and distress, increasing his aversion: this is called the fourth virtue of a man. Furthermore, if a man has any faults of a renunciant, being deceitful and dishonest, he quickly informs the Great Teacher and good spiritual friends. When the Great Teacher expounds the Dharma, those faults are immediately eliminated: this is called the fifth virtue of a man. Furthermore, a man who possesses the mind of learning, making this resolve: 'Whether or not others learn, I will learn in all cases': this is called the sixth virtue of a man. Furthermore, a man who practices the Noble Eightfold Path and does not follow wrong paths: this is called the seventh virtue of a man. Furthermore, a man who, for the rest of his life, exerts diligent effort without weariness or fatigue: this is called the eighth virtue of a man. When a man possesses these eight virtues, wherever he practices, he can swiftly advance and progress."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (926)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Nāḷī village at the Deep Valley Monastery.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed Sandha Kātyāyana: "You should cultivate true meditation. Do not practice the meditation of a stubborn horse. Just as a stubborn horse tied to its manger does not think: 'What should I do? What should I not do?' It only thinks of grain and grass. Likewise, a man who has cultivated and habituated himself much in the fetter of sensual desire, with a mind of sensual desire he thinks, but does not truly know the path of escape. His mind constantly races about, and following the fetter of sensual desire, he seeks concentration. Due to much habituation in ill-will, sloth-and-torpor, restlessness-and-remorse, and doubt, he does not truly know the path of escape, and with a mind covered by the hindrance of doubt he thinks and seeks concentration.

"Sandha, just as a thoroughbred horse tied to its manger does not think of water and grass, but only thinks of the tasks of being harnessed and ridden, likewise a man does not think of the fetter of sensual desire, but dwells in escape and truly knows it. He does not seek concentration through the fetter of sensual desire. Nor does he dwell in the fetters of ill-will, sloth-and-torpor, restlessness-and-remorse, or doubt, but dwells much in escape. He truly knows the fetters of ill-will, sloth-and-torpor, restlessness-and-remorse, and doubt, and does not seek concentration through the fetter of doubt.

"Thus, Sandha, a bhikṣu who meditates in this way does not meditate dependent on earth, does not meditate dependent on water, fire, wind, space, consciousness, nothingness, or neither-perception-nor-non-perception. He does not meditate dependent on this world, does not meditate dependent on the other world, not on the sun or moon, not on what is seen, heard, sensed, or cognized, not on what is attained or sought, not following discursive thought, not following examination.

"Sandha, when a bhikṣu meditates in this way, the lords of the gods, Īśvara and Prajāpati, with respect join their palms, bow their heads, make obeisance, and speak this verse:

> "Homage to the great man! Homage to the supreme among men!
> Since we cannot know
> Upon what does he rest in meditation?"

At that time, there was the Venerable Vakkali standing behind the Buddha, holding a fan and fanning the Buddha. The Venerable Vakkali addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, how does a bhikṣu enter meditation without depending on earth, water, fire, wind, and so forth up to discursive thought and examination, and yet cultivate meditative concentration? How is it that when a bhikṣu meditates in this way, the lords of the gods, Īśvara and Prajāpati, join their palms with respect, bow their heads, make obeisance, and speak this verse:

> "Homage to the great man! Homage to the supreme among men!
> Since we cannot know
> Upon what does he rest in meditation?"

The Buddha told Vakkali: "A bhikṣu who, with regard to the perception of earth, is able to subdue the perception of earth; with regard to the perception of water, fire, wind, the perception of the sphere of infinite space, the perception of the sphere of infinite consciousness, the perception of the sphere of nothingness, the perception of the sphere of neither-perception-nor-non-perception, and the perceptions of this world, the other world, sun, moon, what is seen, heard, sensed, cognized, what is attained, what is sought, what is thought, and what is examined: he subdues all those perceptions. Vakkali, a bhikṣu who meditates in this way does not depend on earth, water, fire, wind, and so forth up to not depending on discursive thought and examination to cultivate meditation. Vakkali, when a bhikṣu meditates in this way, the lords of the gods, Īśvara and Prajāpati, with respect join their palms, bow their heads, make obeisance, and speak this verse:

> "Homage to the great man! Homage to the supreme among men!
> Since we cannot know
> Upon what does he rest in meditation?"

When the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣu Sandha Kātyāyana, far from dust and free from defilement, attained the pure Dharma eye. The bhikṣu Vakkali, without giving rise to any outflows, attained liberation of mind.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Vakkali, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (927)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Śākyan country of Kapilavastu, in the Nyagrodha Park.

At that time, there was a Śākyan named Mahānāma who came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is an upāsaka?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "One who dwells at home in purity, cultivating and practicing pure abiding, having fulfilled the male characteristic, who makes this declaration: 'For the rest of my life I take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, take refuge in the Bhikṣu Sangha, as an upāsaka. Bear witness for me!' This is called an upāsaka."

Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is an upāsaka endowed with faith?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "An upāsaka who, with regard to the Tathāgata, has right faith as his foundation, firm and difficult to shake, which cannot be destroyed by any śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, gods, māras, Brahmā, or anyone else in the world. Mahānāma, this is called an upāsaka endowed with faith."

Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is an upāsaka endowed with morality?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "An upāsaka who abstains from killing, from taking what is not given, from sexual misconduct, from false speech, and from drinking intoxicants, taking no delight in doing these. Mahānāma, this is called an upāsaka endowed with morality."

Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is an upāsaka endowed with learning?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "An upāsaka endowed with learning is one who, upon hearing, is able to retain, and upon hearing, accumulates. Whatever the Buddha has taught, good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, good in meaning and good in flavor, complete and pure, manifesting the holy life in its purity: he is able to fully receive and retain it. Mahānāma, this is called an upāsaka endowed with learning."

Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is an upāsaka endowed with generosity?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "An upāsaka endowed with generosity is one who, though his mind is entangled by the stain of miserliness, frees his mind from the stain of miserliness, dwelling in the state of homelessness, cultivating the generosity of liberation, diligent in giving, always giving, delighting in relinquishing possessions, and giving impartially. Mahānāma, this is called an upāsaka endowed with generosity."

Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is an upāsaka endowed with wisdom?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "An upāsaka endowed with wisdom is one who truly knows this suffering, truly knows this origin of suffering, truly knows this cessation of suffering, and truly knows this path leading to the cessation of suffering. Mahānāma, this is called an upāsaka endowed with wisdom."

At that time, the Śākyan Mahānāma, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his delight, rose from his seat, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (928)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Śākyan country of Kapilavastu, in the Nyagrodha Park.

At that time, the Śākyan Mahānāma, together with five hundred upāsakas, came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is an upāsaka?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "An upāsaka is one who dwells at home in purity, and so forth up to, for the rest of his life, takes refuge in the Three Jewels, as an upāsaka: 'Bear witness for me!'"

Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is an upāsaka who is a stream-enterer (srotāpanna)?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "An upāsaka who is a srotāpanna is one in whom the three fetters have been severed and fully known, namely: the view of a self-existing body, attachment to rules and rituals, and doubt. Mahānāma, this is called an upāsaka who is a srotāpanna."

Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is an upāsaka who is a once-returner (sakṛdāgāmin)?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "An upāsaka in whom the three fetters have been severed and fully known, and whose desire, aversion, and delusion have become thin. Mahānāma, this is called an upāsaka who is a sakṛdāgāmin."

Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is an upāsaka who is a non-returner (anāgāmin)?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "An upāsaka who is an anāgāmin is one in whom the five lower fetters have been severed and fully known, namely: the view of a self-existing body, attachment to rules and rituals, doubt, sensual desire, and ill-will. Mahānāma, this is called an upāsaka who is an anāgāmin."

At that time, the Śākyan Mahānāma turned and looked at the five hundred upāsakas and said: "How wonderful! These upāsakas, dwelling at home in purity, have obtained such deep and marvelous meritorious qualities."

At that time, the upāsaka Mahānāma, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his delight, rose from his seat, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (929)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Śākyan country of Kapilavastu, in the Nyagrodha Park.

At that time, the Śākyan Mahānāma came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is an upāsaka?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "An upāsaka is one who dwells at home in purity, and so forth up to, for the rest of his life, takes refuge in the Three Jewels, as an upāsaka: 'Bear witness for me!'"

Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what constitutes the complete fulfillment of all the duties of an upāsaka?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "If an upāsaka has faith but lacks morality, then it is not complete. He should exert diligent effort to perfect his pure morality. If he is complete in faith and morality but does not practice giving, then it is not complete. Because it is not complete, he should exert diligent effort, cultivating generosity to make it fully complete. If faith, morality, and giving are complete, but he is unable to visit śramaṇas at the proper times to hear the true Dharma, then it is not complete. Because it is not complete, he should exert diligent effort, going at the proper times to visit stūpas and monasteries and see śramaṇas. If he does not listen single-mindedly to the true Dharma, it is not complete. If faith, morality, giving, and learning are cultivated to completion, but after hearing he does not retain what he heard, it is not complete. Because it is not complete, he should exert diligent effort, visiting śramaṇas at the proper times, listening to the Dharma with focused attention. Having heard, he should retain it. If he is unable to contemplate the deep meaning of the dharmas, it is not complete. Because it is not complete, he should exert diligent effort. If faith, morality, giving, and learning are complete, and having heard he can retain, but having retained, he contemplates the profound and subtle meaning yet does not practice in accordance, knowing the Dharma and the sequential order of the Dharma, then it is not complete. Because it is not complete, he should exert diligent effort: with faith, morality, giving, and learning, receiving, retaining, and contemplating, fully penetrating the deep meaning, and practicing in accordance, knowing the Dharma and the sequential order of the Dharma. Mahānāma, this is called the complete fulfillment of all duties of an upāsaka."

Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is an upāsaka who is able to comfort himself but not comfort others?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "If an upāsaka is able to establish himself in morality but cannot establish others in right morality; if he himself maintains pure precepts but cannot lead others to maintain precepts fully; if he himself practices giving but cannot establish others in giving; if he himself visits stūpas and monasteries to see śramaṇas but cannot encourage others to visit stūpas and monasteries to see śramaṇas; if he himself listens to the Dharma with concentration but cannot encourage others to delight in hearing the true Dharma; if, having heard the Dharma, he himself retains it but cannot lead others to receive and hold the true Dharma; if he himself can contemplate the profound and subtle meaning but cannot encourage others to contemplate the deep meaning; if he himself knows the deep Dharma and practices in accordance, knowing the Dharma and the sequential order of the Dharma, but cannot encourage others to practice in accordance, knowing the Dharma and the sequential order of the Dharma: Mahānāma, when these eight qualities are accomplished, this is called an upāsaka who can comfort himself but not comfort others."

Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, how many qualities must an upāsaka accomplish to comfort both himself and others?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "If an upāsaka accomplishes sixteen qualities, he is called an upāsaka who comforts both himself and others. What are the sixteen? Mahānāma, if an upāsaka is himself endowed with right faith and also establishes others in it; if he himself maintains pure precepts and also establishes others in pure precepts; if he himself practices giving and teaches others to practice giving; if he himself visits stūpas and monasteries to see śramaṇas and also teaches others to go and see śramaṇas; if he himself listens to the Dharma with concentration and also teaches others to listen; if he himself receives and holds the Dharma and teaches others to receive and hold it; if he himself contemplates the meaning and teaches others to contemplate it; if he himself knows the deep meaning and practices in accordance, knowing the Dharma and the sequential order of the Dharma, and also teaches others to understand the deep meaning and practice in accordance, knowing the Dharma and the sequential order of the Dharma: Mahānāma, when these sixteen qualities are accomplished, this is called an upāsaka who can comfort himself and also comfort others.

"Mahānāma, if an upāsaka accomplishes these sixteen qualities, then all assemblies come to him: the assembly of brāhmaṇas, the assembly of kṣatriyas, the assembly of elders, and the assembly of śramaṇas. Among all these assemblies, his authority and virtue shine brilliantly. Just as the orb of the sun, in the beginning, in the middle, and at the end, shines with radiant light. So too, an upāsaka who accomplishes the sixteen qualities shines with authority and virtue in the beginning, in the middle, and at the end. Thus, Mahānāma, an upāsaka who accomplishes the sixteen qualities is rare in the world."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Śākyan Mahānāma, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his delight, rose from his seat, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (930)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Śākyan country of Kapilavastu, in the Nyagrodha Park.

At that time, the Śākyan Mahānāma came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, this Kapilavastu is peaceful, prosperous, and joyful, with a thriving population. Whenever I go out or come in, I am surrounded by many attendants. Maddened elephants, maddened people, and maddened chariots are always around me. I fear that I may, together with these maddened ones, be born and die together, and that I will forget to be mindful of the Buddha, mindful of the Dharma, and mindful of the Bhikṣu Sangha. I think to myself: at the time of my death, where will I be reborn?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "Do not be afraid, do not be frightened. After your death, you will not be reborn in an evil destiny, and your end will not be bad. It is like a great tree that naturally leans downward, naturally bends downward, and naturally inclines downward. If its base were cut, where would it fall?"

Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "It would fall in the direction in which it naturally leans downward, bends downward, and inclines downward."

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "You too are like this. At the time of death, you will not be reborn in an evil destiny, and your end will not be bad. Why? Because you have long cultivated mindfulness of the Buddha, mindfulness of the Dharma, and mindfulness of the Sangha through the long night. At the time of death, even if this body is burned by fire, or cast away in a charnel ground, blown by the wind and dried by the sun, and over time becomes dust, still your mind and consciousness, through the long night, having been perfumed by right faith, perfumed by morality, generosity, learning, and wisdom, your spiritual consciousness will ascend upward, toward a place of peace and happiness, and in the future be reborn in heaven."

At that time, Mahānāma, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his delight, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (931)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Śākyan country of Kapilavastu, in the Nyagrodha Park.

At that time, the Śākyan Mahānāma came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if a bhikṣu is on the path of training, seeking what has not yet been attained, advancing upward on the path, in the peace of nirvāṇa: World-Honored One, how should he cultivate and extensively cultivate his abiding so that in this Dharma and Vinaya he may exhaust all outflows, and attain the outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, in this very life knowing and realizing for himself: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence'?"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "If a bhikṣu is on the path of training, seeking what has not yet been attained, advancing upward on the path, in the peace of nirvāṇa, at that time he should cultivate the six recollections, and so forth up to advancing and attaining nirvāṇa. It is like a hungry person whose body is emaciated and thin: upon obtaining delicious food, his body becomes plump and healthy. Likewise, a bhikṣu abiding on the path of training, seeking what has not yet been attained, advancing upward on the path, in the peace of nirvāṇa, cultivates the six recollections, and so forth up to quickly attaining the peace of nirvāṇa.

"What are the six recollections? The noble disciple recollects the qualities of the Tathāgata: 'The Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, Perfect in Knowledge and Conduct, the Well-Gone One, the Knower of the World, the Unsurpassed One, the Tamer of Men, the Teacher of Gods and Humans, the Buddha, the World-Honored One.' When the noble disciple recollects thus, the fetters of sensual desire do not arise, the fetters of ill-will and delusion do not arise, and his mind becomes upright. Having attained the meaning of the Tathāgata, having attained the true Dharma of the Tathāgata, he gains a joyful mind toward the Tathāgata's true Dharma and toward the Tathāgata. Having gained a joyful mind, he becomes delighted. Having become delighted, his body becomes tranquil. When his body becomes tranquil, he experiences bliss. Having experienced bliss, his mind becomes concentrated. When his mind is concentrated, that noble disciple, among hostile and dangerous beings, is without any obstruction, and enters the stream of the Dharma, up to nirvāṇa.

"Furthermore, the noble disciple recollects the qualities of the Dharma. The Dharma and Vinaya of the World-Honored One, in this present life, is able to separate one from the burning of birth and death, not waiting for the proper season, penetrating the present Dharma, to be personally known through direct experience. When the noble disciple recollects the Dharma in this way, the fetters of sensual desire, ill-will, and delusion do not arise, and so forth up to, perfumed by the recollection of the Dharma, advancing and progressing toward nirvāṇa.

"Furthermore, the noble disciple recollects the qualities of the Sangha. The World-Honored One's disciples practice well, practice uprightly, practice directly, practice honestly, practice in accordance with the Dharma: those oriented toward the fruit of stream-entry, those who have attained the fruit of stream-entry; those oriented toward the fruit of once-returning, those who have attained the fruit of once-returning; those oriented toward the fruit of non-returning, those who have attained the fruit of non-returning; those oriented toward the fruit of arhatship, those who have attained the fruit of arhatship. These are the four pairs and eight types of noble persons. This is called the Sangha of the World-Honored One's disciples, endowed with pure morality, endowed with concentration, endowed with wisdom, endowed with liberation, and endowed with the knowledge and vision of liberation. They are worthy of receiving offerings, worthy of hospitality, worthy of gifts, worthy of reverential salutation, being an unsurpassed field of merit. When the noble disciple recollects the qualities of the Sangha in this way, the fetters of sensual desire, ill-will, and delusion do not arise, and so forth up to, perfumed by the recollection of the Sangha, advancing and progressing toward nirvāṇa.

"Furthermore, the noble disciple recollects his own pure morality: unbroken morality, undefective morality, unstained morality, unmixed morality, morality not taken for the sake of others, well-guarded morality, morality praised by the wise, morality not despised by the wise. When the noble disciple recollects morality in this way, the fetters of sensual desire, ill-will, and delusion do not arise, and so forth up to, perfumed by the recollection of morality, advancing and progressing toward nirvāṇa.

"Furthermore, the noble disciple recollects his own generosity: 'I have gained great benefit. Among beings stained by miserliness, I have attained freedom from the stain of miserliness. In the state of homelessness I practice the generosity of liberation, always giving with my own hand, delighting in the practice of relinquishment, fully accomplishing equal giving.' When the noble disciple recollects giving in this way, the fetters of sensual desire, ill-will, and delusion do not arise, and so forth up to, perfumed by the recollection of giving, advancing and progressing toward nirvāṇa.

"Furthermore, the noble disciple recollects the qualities of the gods: 'There are the Four Great Kings, the Trāyastriṃśa gods, the Yāma gods, the Tuṣita gods, the Nirmāṇarati gods, and the Paranirmitavaśavartin gods. Those with right faith, upon dying here, are reborn among those gods. I too should practice this right faith. Those who obtained pure morality, generosity, learning, relinquishment, and wisdom, upon dying here, are reborn among those gods. I too should now practice this morality, generosity, learning, and wisdom.' When the noble disciple recollects the qualities of the gods in this way, the fetters of sensual desire, ill-will, and delusion do not arise, and his mind becomes upright, directed toward those gods. That noble disciple, with such an upright mind, attains the benefit of the deep Dharma, attains the benefit of the deep meaning, and attains the benefit and delight of those gods. Having gained delight, he generates joy. Having generated joy, his body becomes tranquil. When his body becomes tranquil, he experiences bliss. Having experienced bliss, his mind becomes concentrated. When his mind is concentrated, that noble disciple, among hostile and dangerous beings, is without any obstruction, and enters the stream of the Dharma. Perfumed by the recollection of the gods, he advances and progresses toward nirvāṇa.

"Mahānāma, if a bhikṣu abides on the path of training, wishing to advance upward toward the peace and bliss of nirvāṇa, by extensively cultivating in this way, he quickly attains nirvāṇa. In the true Dharma and Vinaya he quickly exhausts all outflows, attains the outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, in this very life knowing and realizing for himself: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'"

At that time, the Śākyan Mahānāma, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his delight, rose from his seat, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (932)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Śākyan country of Kapilavastu, in the Nyagrodha Park. At that time, many bhikṣus had gathered in the dining hall to sew robes for the World-Honored One.

At that time, the Śākyan Mahānāma heard that many bhikṣus had gathered in the dining hall to sew robes for the World-Honored One, and that the World-Honored One would soon complete his three-month rains retreat, finish making the robes, take up his robes and bowl, and wander among the people. Having heard this, he went to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, my four limbs are unsteady, I am confused about the four directions, and the Dharma I have heard I have completely forgotten. Having heard that many bhikṣus have gathered in the dining hall to sew robes for the World-Honored One, and that the World-Honored One will soon complete the rains retreat, finish making the robes, take up his robes and bowl, and wander among the people, I therefore now think: 'When will I again be able to see the World-Honored One and the bhikṣus who are my acquaintances?'"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "Whether you see the World-Honored One or do not see the World-Honored One, whether you see the bhikṣus who are your acquaintances or do not see them, you should simply recollect five qualities and diligently cultivate and practice them. Mahānāma, you should take right faith as the basis, not wrong faith; morality, learning, generosity, and wisdom as the foundation, not lack of wisdom. Thus, Mahānāma, relying on these five qualities, cultivate the six places of recollection. What are the six? This, Mahānāma: recollect the Tathāgata, and you should recollect thus: 'The Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, and so forth up to the Buddha, the World-Honored One.' You should recollect the Dharma, the Sangha, morality, generosity, and the qualities of the gods, and so forth up to practicing to attain wisdom. Thus, Mahānāma, when a noble disciple accomplishes these eleven qualities, he is on the path of training, and he will never decay. He is capable of knowledge and vision, capable of decisive determination, abiding at the gate of ambrosia, near to ambrosia, though he may not be able to swiftly obtain the ambrosia of nirvāṇa in every case.

"It is like a hen brooding over her eggs, whether five or ten. She tends to them at the proper times, lovingly protecting and nurturing them. Even if she is occasionally negligent in the middle, she is still able to use her claws and beak to peck the eggs, and the chicks can be born. Why? Because that mother hen initially tended to them at the proper times and lovingly protected them. Likewise, when a noble disciple accomplishes these eleven qualities, abiding on the path of training, he will never decay, and so forth up to being unable to swiftly obtain the ambrosia of nirvāṇa in every case."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Śākyan Mahānāma, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his delight, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (933)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Śākyan country of Kapilavastu, in the Nyagrodha Park. At that time, many bhikṣus had gathered in the dining hall to sew robes for the World-Honored One.

At that time, the Śākyan Mahānāma heard that the bhikṣus had gathered in the dining hall to sew robes for the World-Honored One, and that the World-Honored One would soon complete the rains retreat, finish making the robes, take up his robes and bowl, and wander among the people. Having heard this, he went to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, my four limbs are unsteady, I am confused about the four directions, and the Dharma I previously heard I have now completely forgotten. Having heard that many bhikṣus have gathered in the dining hall to sew robes for the World-Honored One, and so forth up to wandering among the people, I thought: 'When will I again be able to see the World-Honored One and the bhikṣus who are my acquaintances?'"

The Buddha told Mahānāma: "Whether you see the Tathāgata or do not see the Tathāgata, whether you see the bhikṣus or do not see them, you should always diligently cultivate six qualities. What are the six? Right faith as the basis, morality, generosity, learning, emptiness, and wisdom as the foundation, not lack of wisdom. Therefore, Mahānāma, relying on these six qualities, further cultivate the six recollections: recollecting the qualities of the Tathāgata, and so forth up to recollecting the gods. When these twelve kinds of recollection are accomplished, that noble disciple's evil diminishes and does not increase, is extinguished and does not arise, is freed from dust and defilement and does not increase in dust and defilement, relinquishes and does not grasp. Because he does not grasp, he is not attached. Because he does not grasp and is not attached, he himself conditions nirvāṇa: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Śākyan Mahānāma, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed his delight, rose from his seat, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (934)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Śākyan country of Kapilavastu, in the Nyagrodha Park.

At that time, the Śākyan Mahānāma came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, as I understand what the Buddha has taught, it is through right concentration that there is liberation, not without right concentration. How is it, World-Honored One? Does right concentration come first and liberation afterward? Or does liberation come first and right concentration afterward? Or do right concentration and liberation arise simultaneously, neither before nor after?"

At that time, the World-Honored One remained silent. Thus Mahānāma asked a second and a third time, and the Buddha again remained silent each time.

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda was standing behind the Buddha, holding a fan and fanning the Buddha. The Venerable Ānanda thought: "The Śākyan Mahānāma is asking the World-Honored One about this profound meaning, and the World-Honored One has only recently recovered from illness. I should now speak about another matter to redirect the conversation."

"Mahānāma, a learner also has morality, and one beyond learning also has morality. A learner has concentration, and one beyond learning also has concentration. A learner has wisdom, and one beyond learning also has wisdom. A learner has liberation, and one beyond learning also has liberation."

Mahānāma asked the Venerable Ānanda: "What is the morality of a learner? What is the morality of one beyond learning? What is the concentration of a learner? What is the concentration of one beyond learning? What is the wisdom of a learner? What is the wisdom of one beyond learning? What is the liberation of a learner? What is the liberation of one beyond learning?"

The Venerable Ānanda told Mahānāma: "This noble disciple abides in the morality of the prātimokṣa discipline, perfect in conduct and deportment, and upholds the training precepts. Having fully upheld the training precepts, being separated from desire and from evil and unwholesome dharmas, and so forth up to the fourth jhāna, he abides in its fulfillment. Having thus fulfilled concentration, he truly knows this noble truth of suffering, truly knows this noble truth of the origin of suffering, truly knows this noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and truly knows this noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Having thus known and thus seen, the five lower fetters are severed and fully known, namely: the view of a self-existing body, attachment to rules and rituals, doubt, sensual desire, and ill-will. These five lower fetters being severed, he is reborn in that realm and attains parinirvāṇa as an anāgāmin, never to return to this world. At that time, he has accomplished the morality of a learner, the concentration of a learner, the wisdom of a learner, and the liberation of a learner. Subsequently, at another time, he exhausts all outflows, and attains the outflow-free liberation and liberation by wisdom, knowing and realizing for himself: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know I will not receive further existence.' At that time, he has accomplished the morality of one beyond learning, the concentration of one beyond learning, the wisdom of one beyond learning, and the liberation of one beyond learning. Thus, Mahānāma, this is what the World-Honored One has taught regarding the morality of a learner, the concentration of a learner, the wisdom of a learner, and the liberation of a learner; and the morality of one beyond learning, the concentration of one beyond learning, the wisdom of one beyond learning, and the liberation of one beyond learning."

At that time, the Śākyan Mahānāma, hearing what the Venerable Ānanda had said, rejoiced and expressed his delight, rose from his seat, bowed to the Buddha, and departed.

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing that Mahānāma had departed not long before, said to the Venerable Ānanda: "The Śākyans of Kapilavastu are able to discuss profound meaning with bhikṣus?"

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "Indeed so, World-Honored One! The Śākyans of Kapilavastu are able to discuss profound meaning with bhikṣus."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "The Śākyans of Kapilavastu have truly gained great benefit, for they are able to deeply penetrate the noble wisdom-eye of the profound Buddha-Dharma."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (935)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Śākyan country of Kapilavastu, in the Nyagrodha Park.

At that time, a Śākyan named Saddha spoke to the Śākyan Mahānāma: "How many qualities does the World-Honored One say a srotāpanna accomplishes?"

Mahānāma replied: "The World-Honored One says a srotāpanna accomplishes four qualities. What are the four? Unshakable faith in the Buddha, unshakable faith in the Dharma, unshakable faith in the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts. These are the four qualities that accomplish a srotāpanna."

The Śākyan Saddha said to the Śākyan Mahānāma: "Do not say that! Do not say that the World-Honored One teaches four qualities to accomplish a srotāpanna. Rather, it is three qualities that accomplish a srotāpanna. What are the three? Unshakable faith in the Buddha, unshakable faith in the Dharma, and unshakable faith in the Sangha. These three qualities accomplish a srotāpanna." He said this three times. The Śākyan Mahānāma could not make Saddha accept the four qualities, and the Śākyan Saddha could not make Mahānāma accept the three qualities. Together they went to visit the Buddha. They bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side.

The Śākyan Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, the Śākyan Saddha came to my place and asked me: 'How many qualities does the World-Honored One say accomplish a srotāpanna?' I then replied: 'The World-Honored One says four qualities accomplish a srotāpanna. What are the four? Unshakable faith in the Buddha, unshakable faith in the Dharma and the Sangha, and accomplishment of the noble precepts. These four qualities accomplish a srotāpanna.' The Śākyan Saddha then said: 'Śākyan Mahānāma, do not say this, that the World-Honored One teaches four qualities to accomplish a srotāpanna. It is only three qualities that accomplish a srotāpanna. What are the three? Unshakable faith in the Buddha, unshakable faith in the Dharma, and unshakable faith in the Sangha. The World-Honored One says these three qualities accomplish a srotāpanna.' He said this repeatedly. I could not make the Śākyan Saddha accept the four qualities, and the Śākyan Saddha could not make me accept the three qualities. Therefore we have both come to the World-Honored One's presence to ask: how many qualities does a srotāpanna accomplish?"

At that time, the Śākyan Saddha rose from his seat, bowed to the Buddha, placed his palms together, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if such a matter should arise where on one side is the World-Honored One and on the other the Bhikṣu Sangha, I would rather follow the World-Honored One than follow the Bhikṣu Sangha. Or if such a matter should arise where on one side is the World-Honored One and on the other the Bhikṣuṇī Sangha, the upāsakas, the upāsikās, or the gods, māras, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, gods, and worldly people, I would rather follow the World-Honored One! I would not follow the rest."

At that time, the World-Honored One told the Śākyan Mahānāma: "As Mahānāma sees it, when the Śākyan Saddha speaks like this, what would you say?"

Mahānāma addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, when the Śākyan Saddha
