# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Thirty-Four

---

### (940)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering. Bhikṣus, what do you think? If all the grasses and trees of this great earth were cut into counting sticks, each measuring four finger-widths, and used to count the parents upon whom you have depended throughout your long night of transmigrating in birth and death, the counting sticks would be exhausted, yet the number of those parents would still not be exhausted. Bhikṣus, thus in beginningless birth and death, transmigrating through the long night, one does not know the original limit of suffering. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'We should diligently strive to sever all states of existence and not allow them to increase.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (941)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering. How so, bhikṣus? If all the earth and mud of this great earth were entirely formed into pellets the size of *vāra* fruits, and used to count the parents upon whom you have depended through the long night of birth and death, the clay pellets would be exhausted, yet the number of parents upon whom you have depended would still not be exhausted. Bhikṣus, sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering; their number is like this. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should diligently apply skillful means to sever all states of existence and not allow them to increase. You should train thus."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (942)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering. Bhikṣus, when you see sentient beings enjoying safety and various pleasures, you should have this thought: 'Throughout the long night of transmigrating in birth and death, we too have experienced such pleasures, and those destinies are immeasurable.' Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'In beginningless birth and death, transmigrating through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering, we should diligently strive to sever all states of existence and not allow them to increase.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (943)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering. Bhikṣus, when you see sentient beings undergoing various sufferings and afflictions, you should have this thought: 'Throughout the long night of transmigrating in birth and death, I too have experienced such sufferings, and their number is immeasurable. I should diligently apply skillful means to sever all states of existence and not allow them to increase.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (944)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering. Bhikṣus, when you see sentient beings and fear arises in you, with your body-hairs standing on end, you should have this thought: 'In the past, we must have killed living beings, and were those who caused harm, and were evil companions. In beginningless birth and death, transmigrating through the long night, we did not know the original limit of suffering.' Bhikṣus, you should train thus: sever all states of existence and do not allow them to increase."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (945)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering. Bhikṣus, when you see sentient beings whom you feel affection for and delight in, you should have this thought: 'Such sentient beings, in past lives, must have been our parents, brothers, wives, children, relatives, teachers, friends, and acquaintances. Thus through the long night of birth and death, transmigrating, obscured by ignorance, with craving tethered around their necks, they have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering.' Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus, diligently applying skillful means to sever all states of existence and not allow them to increase."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (946)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain brahmin came to visit the Buddha. He respectfully greeted and inquired after the Buddha's health. After greeting him, he withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, how many Buddhas will there be in the future?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "The future Buddhas will be as numerous as the sands of immeasurable Ganges rivers."

At that time, the brahmin had this thought: "In the future there will be samyak-saṃbuddhas as numerous as the sands of immeasurable Ganges rivers. I shall cultivate the holy life under them."

At that time, the brahmin, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed approval, and rising from his seat, departed.

Then, as the brahmin was walking along the road, he reflected: "Today I only asked the ascetic Gautama about future Buddhas, but did not ask about the past." He then turned back along the road and again asked the World-Honored One: "How is it, Gautama? In past ages, how many Buddhas were there?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "The Buddhas of past ages were also as numerous as the sands of immeasurable Ganges rivers."

Then the brahmin had this thought: "In past ages there were Buddhas, World-Honored Ones, as numerous as the sands of immeasurable Ganges rivers, and I never drew close to them. Even if in the future there will be samyak-saṃbuddhas as numerous as the sands of immeasurable Ganges rivers, I would likewise not draw close to them and enjoy their company. I should now cultivate the holy life under the ascetic Gautama." He then joined his palms and addressed the Buddha: "I only wish that you would allow me to go forth and cultivate the holy life in the true Dharma and Vinaya."

The Buddha told the brahmin: "You are permitted to go forth and cultivate the holy life in the true Dharma and Vinaya and to receive the bhikṣu's portion."

At that time, the brahmin then went forth and received full ordination. Having gone forth, alone in a quiet place, he contemplated: "This is why a son of good family, with right faith, leaves the household life and goes forth to study the Way." And so forth, until he attained arhatship.

---

### (947)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, on Mount Vipula.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If one person, through one kalpa of transmigrating in birth and death, had their white bones accumulated without decaying, they would be like Mount Vipula. If a learned noble disciple truly knows this noble truth of suffering as it really is, truly knows this noble truth of the origin of suffering as it really is, truly knows this noble truth of the cessation of suffering as it really is, and truly knows this noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering as it really is, then knowing and seeing thus, they sever the three fetters, namely: identity-view, clinging to rules and observances, and doubt. Having severed these three fetters, they attain the fruit of stream-entry, are no longer subject to falling into the lower realms, are certain and rightly directed toward three kinds of awakening, and after seven more existences among gods and humans, they will reach the end of suffering."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke these verses:

> "One person in one kalpa,
> Accumulating their bodily bones,
> Constantly piled without decaying,
> Would be like Mount Vipula.
> If the noble disciples
> With right wisdom see the true truths:
> This suffering and its cause,
> Separation from suffering, attaining quiescence,
> Cultivating the Eightfold Path,
> Rightly directed toward parinirvāṇa,
> At most after seven existences,
> Passing between gods and humans,
> They exhaust all fetters entirely,
> And reach the final end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (948)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, bared his right shoulder, made obeisance to the Buddha, placed his right knee on the ground, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, how long is a kalpa?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "I am able to explain it to you, but it would be difficult for you to comprehend."

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "Can it be explained by means of a simile?"

The Buddha said: "It can be explained. Bhikṣu, suppose there were an iron city, one yojana on each side, equally so in height and depth, filled with mustard seeds. If a person were to remove one mustard seed every hundred years, all the mustard seeds would be exhausted, yet the kalpa would still not be finished. Thus, bhikṣu, a kalpa is this long. Throughout such long kalpas, hundreds of thousands of millions of billions of great sufferings continue in succession. White bones form hills, pus and blood form streams, through the hells, the animal realm, the hungry ghost realm, and other evil destinies. This is called, bhikṣu, beginningless birth and death, transmigrating through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'Sever all states of existence and do not allow them to increase.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (949)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, made obeisance to the Buddha, placed his right knee on the ground, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, how long is a kalpa?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "I am able to explain it to you, but it would be difficult for you to comprehend."

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "Can it be explained by means of a simile?"

The Buddha said: "It can be explained. Bhikṣu, suppose there were a great stone mountain, without cracks or flaws, one yojana on each side. If a man, using a Kāśī silk cloth, were to brush it once every hundred years, and continued brushing without stopping, the stone mountain would eventually be worn away, yet the kalpa would still not be finished. Bhikṣu, a kalpa is this long. Throughout hundreds of thousands of millions of billions of such kalpas, one undergoes sufferings and afflictions." And so forth, until: "Bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'Sever all states of existence and do not allow them to increase.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (950)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, made obeisance to the Buddha, placed his right knee on the ground, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, how many kalpas have passed?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "I am able to explain them all, but it would be very difficult for you to comprehend."

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "Can it be explained by means of a simile?"

The Buddha said: "It can be explained. Suppose, bhikṣu, there were a man with a lifespan of a hundred years. In the morning he could recall three hundred thousand kalpas, at midday he could recall three hundred thousand kalpas, and in the evening he could recall three hundred thousand kalpas. Thus, day after day, recalling the number of kalpas, at the end of his hundred-year life, he would still be unable to recall the limit of the number of kalpas. Bhikṣu, you should know that the number of past kalpas is immeasurable like this. Throughout immeasurable past kalpas, in the long night one has endured suffering; accumulated bones have formed mountains, marrow and blood have formed streams, and so forth down to the hells, the animal realm, the hungry ghost realm, and other evil destinies. Thus, bhikṣu, in beginningless birth and death, transmigrating through the long night, one does not know the original limit of suffering. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'Sever all states of existence and do not allow them to increase.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (951)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering. There is no place where one has not been born and died. Thus through the long night of beginningless birth and death, one does not know the original limit of suffering. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'Sever all states of existence and do not allow them to increase.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (952)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering. There is no place where one has not had parents, brothers, wives, children, family members, clansmen, teachers, and elders. Thus, bhikṣus, in beginningless birth and death, transmigrating through the long night, one does not know the original limit of suffering. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'Sever all states of existence and do not allow them to increase.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (953)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering. It is like the bubbles that form when heavy rain falls: one arises, another perishes. Just so, sentient beings, obscured by ignorance, with craving tethered around their necks, in beginningless birth and death, those being born and those dying, transmigrate through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'Sever all states of existence and do not allow them to increase.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (954)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering. It is like a universal great rain, a vast deluge, in the east, west, south, and north, with no place where it ceases. Just so, in the east, south, west, and north, in immeasurable lands, kalpas form and kalpas decay, like the great rain raining universally over the world with no place of cessation. Thus in beginningless birth and death, transmigrating through the long night, one does not know the original limit of suffering. It is like tossing a stick into the air: sometimes it falls head-first to the ground, sometimes it falls tail-first, sometimes it falls in the middle. Just so, in beginningless birth and death, transmigrating through the long night, one sometimes falls into the hells, sometimes falls into the animal realm, sometimes falls into the hungry ghost realm. Thus in beginningless birth and death, transmigrating through the long night. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'Sever all states of existence and do not allow them to increase.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (955)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Sentient beings, in beginningless birth and death, have transmigrated through the long night, not knowing the original limit of suffering. It is like, bhikṣus, if a man were to turn a wheel with five spokes, constantly turning without ceasing. Just so, sentient beings turn the wheel of the five destinies: sometimes falling into the hells, the animal realm, or the hungry ghost realm, or into the human and heavenly destinies, constantly turning without ceasing. Thus in beginningless birth and death, transmigrating through the long night, one does not know the original limit of suffering. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'Sever all states of existence and do not allow them to increase.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (956)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, on the side of Mount Vipula.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "All conditioned things are impermanent; all conditioned things are not enduring, not stable, and are subject to change. Bhikṣus, toward all conditioned things you should generate disenchantment, seek happiness, and seek liberation.

"Bhikṣus, in past ages, this Mount Vipula was called 'Long Bamboo Mountain.' There were people who lived surrounding the mountain, in a settlement called Timirapura. The people of Timirapura had a lifespan of forty thousand years. The people of Timirapura, when ascending to the summit of this mountain, took four days for the round trip. At that time, there was a Buddha in the world named Krakucchanda Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyak-Saṃbuddha, Vidyā-caraṇa-saṃpanna, Sugata, Lokavid, Anuttara, Puruṣa-damya-sārathi, Śāstā deva-manuṣyāṇāṃ, Buddha, Bhagavān, who appeared in the world and taught the Dharma. Its beginning, middle, and end were good; good in meaning, good in flavor; pure and complete in its entirety; the holy life clear and pure; he revealed and made manifest. That Long Bamboo Mountain, today even its name has perished. The people of the Timirapura settlement have also passed away. That Buddha Tathāgata has already entered parinirvāṇa. Bhikṣus, you should know that all conditioned things are impermanent, not enduring, not stable, and subject to change. Toward all conditioned things, you should cultivate disenchantment, dispassion, and liberation.

"Bhikṣus, in past ages, this Mount Vipula was called 'Paṅka.' At that time, there were people who lived surrounding the mountain, in a settlement called Abhikapura. At that time, the people had a lifespan of thirty thousand years. The people of Abhika, when ascending to the summit of this mountain, took three days for the round trip. At that time, there was a Buddha in the world named Kanakamuni Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyak-Saṃbuddha, Vidyā-caraṇa-saṃpanna, Sugata, Lokavid, Anuttara, Puruṣa-damya-sārathi, Śāstā deva-manuṣyāṇāṃ, Buddha, Bhagavān, who appeared in the world and expounded the sūtras and Dharma. Its beginning, middle, and end were good; good in meaning, good in flavor; pure and complete in its entirety; the holy life clear and pure; he revealed and made manifest. Bhikṣus, that Paṅka Mountain, its name has long since perished. The people of Abhikapura have also long since passed away. That Buddha, World-Honored One, has also entered parinirvāṇa. Thus, bhikṣus, all conditioned things are impermanent, not enduring, not stable, and subject to change. You bhikṣus should cultivate disenchantment, seek happiness, and seek liberation.

"Bhikṣus, in past ages, this Mount Vipula was called 'Supārśva.' There were people who lived surrounding the mountain, in a settlement called Aśvapura. The people had a lifespan of twenty thousand years. Those people, when ascending to the summit of this mountain, took two days for the round trip. At that time, there was a Buddha named Kāśyapa Tathāgata, Arhat, and so forth, who appeared in the world and expounded the sūtras and Dharma. Its beginning, middle, and end were good; good in meaning, good in flavor; pure and complete in its entirety; the holy life clear and pure; he revealed and made manifest. Bhikṣus, you should know that the name of Supārśva Mountain has long since perished. The people of Aśvapura have also long since passed away. That Buddha, World-Honored One, has also entered parinirvāṇa. Thus, bhikṣus, all conditioned things are impermanent, not enduring, not stable, and subject to change. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should cultivate disenchantment, dispassion, and liberation.

"Bhikṣus, today this mountain is called Vipula. There are people who live surrounding the mountain, in the country called Magadha. These people have a lifespan of one hundred years. If they take good care of themselves, they may reach the full hundred years. The people of Magadha, when ascending to the summit of this mountain, can make the round trip in a short while. I have now attained in this place the state of Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyak-Saṃbuddha, and so forth, Buddha, World-Honored One. I expound the true Dharma, teaching and transforming beings, enabling them to attain quiescent nirvāṇa, the right path, the well-gone, awakening and knowing. Bhikṣus, you should know that the name of this Mount Vipula will also eventually wear away. The people of Magadha will also pass away. The Tathāgata before long will enter parinirvāṇa. Thus, bhikṣus, all conditioned things are impermanent, not enduring, not stable, and subject to change. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should cultivate disenchantment, dispassion, and liberation."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke these verses:

> "In ancient times, Long Bamboo Mountain,
> The village of Timirapura;
> Next, the mountain called Paṅka,
> The settlement of Abhika;
> Supārśva Mountain,
> The settlement called Aśvapura;
> Now Mount Vipula,
> The country called Magadha.
> The famed mountains have all worn away,
> Their people have all passed and perished,
> The Buddhas have entered parinirvāṇa;
> All that exists, nothing does not end.
> All conditioned things are impermanent,
> All are phenomena of arising and ceasing.
> Whatever is born, nothing does not end;
> Only quiescent cessation is bliss."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (957)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a Vātsīputrīya wanderer came to visit the Buddha. He joined his palms and greeted the Buddha, and after greeting him, withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, I wish to ask something. Do you have the leisure to answer me?"

The Buddha told the Vātsīputrīya wanderer: "Ask whatever you wish, and I shall explain it to you."

The Vātsīputrīya wanderer addressed the Buddha: "How is it, Gautama? Is the life-force identical with the body?"

The Buddha told the Vātsīputrīya wanderer: "That the life-force is identical with the body, this is undeclared."

"How is it, Gautama? Is the life-force different from the body?"

The Buddha told the Vātsīputrīya wanderer: "That the life-force is different from the body, this also is undeclared."

The Vātsīputrīya wanderer addressed the Buddha: "How is it, Gautama, that when asked 'Is the life-force identical with the body?' you answer 'Undeclared,' and when asked 'Is the life-force different from the body?' you answer 'Undeclared'? What is so extraordinary about the ascetic Gautama? When a disciple dies, he declares and says: 'Such a one was reborn in such a place; such a one was reborn in such a place.' Those disciples, upon dying here and abandoning this body, mount a mind-made body and are reborn in another place. At that time, is it not that the life-force is different from the body?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "What I speak of is with remainder; I do not speak of without remainder."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, how is it that you speak of with remainder and do not speak of without remainder?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "It is like fire: with remainder it burns, not without remainder."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "I have seen fire burning without remainder as well."

The Buddha told Vaccha: "How have you seen fire burning without remainder?"

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Suppose there is a great blazing mass of fire, and a strong wind comes and blows it, so that the fire flies through the air. Is this not fire without remainder?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "The fire blown by the wind that flies is precisely with remainder, not without remainder."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, how is the fire flying in the air called 'with remainder'?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "The fire flying in the air depends on the wind to remain, depends on the wind to burn. Because it depends on the wind, therefore I say it is with remainder."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "When a sentient being dies here and mounts a mind-made body to be reborn in another place, how is this 'with remainder'?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "When a sentient being dies here and mounts a mind-made body to be reborn in another place, at that time it is because of craving that there is grasping, because of craving that there is abiding. Therefore I say it is with remainder."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Sentient beings, delighting in remainder, are attached to remainder. Only the World-Honored One has attained that without remainder and realized perfect awakening. Ascetic Gautama, I have many affairs in the world. I beg to take my leave and depart."

The Buddha told Vaccha: "You may go when you see fit."

The Vātsīputrīya wanderer, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed approval, and rising from his seat, departed.

---

### (958)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana was also dwelling there.

At that time, a Vātsīputrīya wanderer came to visit the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana. He exchanged greetings and courtesies with the Venerable Maudgalyāyana. After the exchange, he withdrew and sat to one side. He said to the Venerable Maudgalyāyana: "I wish to ask something. Do you have the leisure to answer me?"

Maudgalyāyana replied: "Vaccha, ask as you wish. If I know, I will answer."

Then the Vātsīputrīya wanderer asked the Venerable Maudgalyāyana: "What is the cause, what is the condition, that when other ascetics and brahmins are asked how it is with the Tathāgata after death: whether the Tathāgata exists after death, does not exist after death, both exists and does not exist after death, or neither exists nor does not exist after death, they all answer accordingly, but when the ascetic Gautama is asked whether the Tathāgata exists after death, does not exist after death, both exists and does not exist after death, or neither exists nor does not exist after death, he does not declare?"

Maudgalyāyana said: "Vaccha, other ascetics and brahmins do not truly know form, the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, or the escape from form. Because they do not truly know these, regarding the Tathāgata's existence after death they grasp at it; regarding the Tathāgata's non-existence after death, both existence and non-existence after death, or neither existence nor non-existence after death, they grasp at it. Regarding feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness; regarding the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, or the escape from consciousness, they do not truly know. Because they do not truly know, regarding the Tathāgata's existence after death they grasp at it; regarding non-existence after death, both existence and non-existence after death, or neither existence nor non-existence after death, they grasp at it.

"The Tathāgata truly knows form; truly knows the arising of form, the cessation of form, the gratification of form, the danger of form, and the escape from form. Because he truly knows, regarding the Tathāgata's existence after death, he does not grasp at it; regarding non-existence after death, both existence and non-existence after death, or neither existence nor non-existence after death, he does not grasp at it. Regarding feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, he truly knows; regarding the arising of consciousness, the cessation of consciousness, the gratification of consciousness, the danger of consciousness, and the escape from consciousness, he truly knows. Because he truly knows, regarding the Tathāgata's existence after death, it does not apply; regarding non-existence after death, both existence and non-existence after death, or neither existence nor non-existence after death, it does not apply. It is profound and vast, immeasurable and innumerable, and entirely quiescent.

"Vaccha, it is for these causes and conditions that other ascetics and brahmins, when asked whether the Tathāgata exists after death, does not exist after death, both exists and does not exist after death, or neither exists nor does not exist after death, declare accordingly. It is for these causes and conditions that the Tathāgata, when asked whether the Tathāgata exists after death, does not exist after death, both exists and does not exist after death, or neither exists nor does not exist after death, does not declare."

At that time, the Vātsīputrīya wanderer, hearing what the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana had said, rejoiced and expressed approval, and rising from his seat, departed.

---

### (959)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a Vātsīputrīya wanderer came to visit the Buddha. He joined his palms and greeted the Buddha, and after greeting him, withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, what is the cause, what is the condition, that when other ascetics and brahmins are asked..." as above, explained in full.

At that time, the Vātsīputrīya wanderer exclaimed: "How wonderful! Gautama! The disciple and the great teacher share the same meaning, the same phrasing, the same flavor, even the same ultimate meaning. Gautama, I recently visited Mahāmaudgalyāyana and asked him with this same meaning, this same phrasing, this same flavor, and he also answered me with this same meaning, this same phrasing, this same flavor, just as Gautama has now spoken. Therefore, Gautama, it is truly extraordinary that the great teacher and his disciple share the same meaning, the same phrasing, the same flavor, and the same ultimate meaning."

At that time, the Vātsīputrīya wanderer, having some business, went to the village of Nālika. When his affairs were completed, he visited the Venerable Saṇḍha Kātyāyana. After they exchanged greetings, he withdrew and sat to one side. He asked Saṇḍha Kātyāyana: "What is the cause, what is the condition, that when the ascetic Gautama is asked whether the Tathāgata exists after death, does not exist after death, both exists and does not exist after death, or neither exists nor does not exist after death, he does not declare?"

Saṇḍha Kātyāyana said to the Vātsīputrīya wanderer: "I shall now ask you; answer as you please. What do you think? If the causes, the conditions, and the activities that establish the body, whether form or formless, whether with perception, without perception, or neither with perception nor without perception, if those causes, those conditions, and those activities were to cease without remainder, to be permanently ceased, would the Tathāgata then have anything to declare about it, saying: 'The Tathāgata exists after death, does not exist after death, both exists and does not exist after death, or neither exists nor does not exist after death'?"

The Vātsīputrīya wanderer said to Saṇḍha Kātyāyana: "If those causes, those conditions, and the various established activities, whether form or non-form, whether with perception, without perception, or neither with perception nor without perception, if those causes, those conditions, and those activities were to cease without remainder, how could Gautama declare about the Tathāgata that he exists after death, does not exist after death, both exists and does not exist after death, or neither exists nor does not exist after death?"

Saṇḍha Kātyāyana said to the Vātsīputrīya wanderer: "Therefore the Tathāgata, for these causes and these conditions, when asked whether the Tathāgata exists after death, does not exist after death, both exists and does not exist after death, or neither exists nor does not exist after death, does not declare."

The Vātsīputrīya wanderer asked Saṇḍha Kātyāyana: "How long have you been a disciple of the ascetic Gautama?"

Saṇḍha Kātyāyana replied: "A little more than three years since I went forth in the true Dharma and Vinaya and cultivated the holy life."

The Vātsīputrīya wanderer said: "Saṇḍha Kātyāyana, you have quickly obtained good benefit! In a short time since going forth, you have attained such bodily discipline, such verbal discipline, and also such wisdom and eloquence."

At that time, the Vātsīputrīya wanderer, hearing what Saṇḍha Kātyāyana had said, rejoiced and expressed approval, and rising from his seat, departed.

---

### (960)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a Vātsīputrīya wanderer came to visit the Buddha. He joined his palms and greeted the Buddha, and after greeting him, withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, I wish to ask something. Do you have the leisure to explain it to me?"

The Buddha told the Vātsīputrīya wanderer: "Ask whatever you wish, and I shall explain it to you."

The Vātsīputrīya wanderer addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, what is the cause, what is the condition, that when someone comes and asks whether the Tathāgata exists after death, does not exist after death, both exists and does not exist after death, or neither exists nor does not exist after death, you do not declare?"

The Buddha told the Vātsīputrīya wanderer as Saṇḍha Kātyāyana had explained above in full, up to "neither exists nor does not exist after death."

The Vātsīputrīya wanderer addressed the Buddha: "How wonderful! Gautama! The teacher and his disciple, meaning matches meaning, phrase matches phrase, flavor matches flavor; their principle is entirely consistent, which is the foremost teaching. Gautama, I had a minor errand at the village of Nālikā. When my business was completed, I briefly visited the ascetic Kātyāyana. With this same meaning, this same phrasing, this same flavor, I asked the ascetic Kātyāyana, and he too answered my question with this same meaning, this same phrasing, this same flavor, just as the ascetic Gautama has now spoken. Therefore one should know it is truly extraordinary! The teacher and his disciple share the same meaning, phrasing, and flavor; the same meaning, phrasing, and flavor entirely."

At that time, the Vātsīputrīya wanderer, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed approval, and rising from his seat, departed.

---

### (961)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a Vātsīputrīya wanderer came to visit the Buddha. He joined his palms and greeted the Buddha, and after greeting him, withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "How is it, Gautama? Is there a self?"

At that time, the World-Honored One was silent and did not answer.

He asked thus a second and a third time. At that time, the World-Honored One also did not answer a second and a third time.

At that time, the Vātsīputrīya wanderer had this thought: "I have asked the ascetic Gautama three times, and he has not answered. I should just leave." 

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda was standing behind the Buddha, holding a fan and fanning the Buddha. At that time, Ānanda addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, that Vātsīputrīya wanderer asked three times. Why did the World-Honored One not answer? Would this not increase the Vātsīputrīya wanderer's wrong and evil views, saying that the ascetic cannot answer his question?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "If I had answered that there is a self, it would have increased his prior wrong views. If I had answered that there is no self, he who was previously confused would become even more confused, saying: 'Previously I had a self, but now it is annihilated.' If previously there was a self, that would be the eternalist view. If it is now annihilated, that would be the annihilationist view. The Tathāgata is free from both extremes and teaches the Dharma from the middle, namely: because this exists, that exists; because this arises, that arises. That is to say: conditioned by ignorance, there are formations, and so forth, up to the cessation of birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, affliction, and suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (962)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a Vātsīputrīya wanderer came to visit the Buddha. He exchanged greetings with the World-Honored One, and after greeting him, withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "How is it, Gautama? Does Gautama hold such a view and make such a statement: 'The world is eternal; this is true and the rest is false'?"

The Buddha told the Vātsīputrīya wanderer: "I do not hold such a view or make such a statement: 'The world is eternal; this is true and the rest is false.'"

"How is it, Gautama? Do you hold such a view and make such a statement: 'The world is not eternal; the world is both eternal and not eternal; the world is neither eternal nor not eternal; the world has an end; the world has no end; the world both has and has no end; the world neither has nor has no end; the life-force is identical with the body; the life-force is different from the body; the Tathāgata exists after death; the Tathāgata does not exist after death; the Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death; the Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death'?"

The Buddha told the Vātsīputrīya wanderer: "I do not hold such a view or make such a statement, and so forth, up to: neither exists nor does not exist after death."

At that time, the Vātsīputrīya wanderer addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, regarding these views, what fault do you see in them, that you do not declare any of these views?"

The Buddha told the Vātsīputrīya wanderer: "If one holds the view 'The world is eternal; this is true and the rest is false,' this is an inverted view; this is an speculative view; this is a vacillating view; this is a defiled view; this is a view of bondage. It is suffering, it is obstruction, it is affliction, it is fever. Being bound by the fetter of views, the ignorant and unlearned ordinary person, in future lives, will give rise to birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, lamentation, affliction, and suffering. Vātsīputrīya wanderer, if one holds the view 'The world is not eternal; the world is both eternal and not eternal; the world is neither eternal nor not eternal; the world has an end; the world has no end; the world both has and has no end; the world neither has nor has no end; the life-force is identical with the body; the life-force is different from the body; the Tathāgata exists after death; the Tathāgata does not exist after death; the Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death; the Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death,' this is an inverted view, and so forth, up to: grief, lamentation, affliction, and suffering arise."

The Vātsīputrīya wanderer addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, what view do you hold?"

The Buddha told the Vātsīputrīya wanderer: "The Tathāgata's seeing is already complete. Vātsīputrīya wanderer, however, the Tathāgata sees, namely: he sees this noble truth of suffering, this noble truth of the origin of suffering, this noble truth of the cessation of suffering, this noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Having known and seen thus, regarding all views, all feelings, all births, all views of self and what belongs to self, conceit, attachment, and bondage, he severs and extinguishes them: they are quiescent, cool, and genuine. One who is liberated in this way, bhikṣu, does not apply to 'born,' nor does 'not born' apply."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, why do you say 'born does not apply'?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "I shall now ask you; answer as you please. Vaccha, suppose there were a person who kindled a fire before you. You would see the fire burning, would you not? Then, right before you the fire went out. You would see the fire go out, would you not?"

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Yes, Gautama."

The Buddha told Vaccha: "If someone were to ask you: 'That fire which was just burning, where has it gone? Has it gone to the east, the west, the south, or the north?' If asked thus, how would you answer?"

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, if someone came and asked me thus, I would answer as follows: 'If a fire was burning before me, it burned because of the causes and conditions of fuel and grass. If no more fuel is added, the fire is permanently extinguished and does not arise again. To say it has gone east, south, west, or north, that does not apply.'"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "I too speak in this way. Form has been severed and fully known; feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness have been severed and fully known. Their root is cut off, like the trunk of a tāla tree, never to grow again. In the future they will never arise again. To say it has gone to the east, south, west, or north, that does not apply. It is profound and vast, immeasurable and innumerable, permanently extinguished."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "I shall speak a simile."

The Buddha told Vaccha: "You may speak it now."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, suppose near a city, town, or village, there is fine, clean ground where a strong forest grows. There is one great, sturdy tree that has been growing for several thousand years. As days and nights pass, its branches and leaves have fallen away, its bark and skin have withered and decayed, and only its trunk stands alone. Just so, Gautama, the Tathāgata's Dharma and Vinaya have shed all branches, boughs, and leaves, and only the empty trunk stands firm and alone."

At that time, the Vātsīputrīya wanderer, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed approval, and rising from his seat, departed.

---

### (963)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a Vātsīputrīya wanderer came to visit the Buddha. He exchanged greetings and courtesies with the World-Honored One, and after the exchange, withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, what is it that one does not know, such that one holds such views and makes such statements: 'The world is eternal; this is true and the rest is false; the world is not eternal; the world is both eternal and not eternal; the world is neither eternal nor not eternal; the world has an end; the world has no end; the world both has and has no end; the world neither has nor has no end; the life-force is identical with the body; the life-force is different from the body; the Tathāgata exists after death; the Tathāgata does not exist after death; the Tathāgata both exists and does not exist after death; the Tathāgata neither exists nor does not exist after death'?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "It is because of not knowing form that one holds such views and makes such statements: 'The world is eternal; this is true and the rest is false,' and so forth up to 'neither exists nor does not exist after death.' It is because of not knowing feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness that one holds such views and makes such statements: 'The world is eternal; this is true and the rest is false,' and so forth up to 'neither exists nor does not exist after death.'"

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, through knowing what does one not hold such views and not make such statements: 'The world is eternal; this is true and the rest is false,' and so forth up to 'neither exists nor does not exist after death'?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "Through knowing form, one does not hold such views and does not make such statements: 'The world is eternal; this is true and the rest is false,' and so forth up to 'neither exists nor does not exist after death.' Through knowing feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness, one does not hold such views and does not make such statements: 'The world is eternal; this is true and the rest is false,' and so forth up to 'neither exists nor does not exist after death.' Thus, not knowing versus knowing, not seeing versus seeing, not cognizing versus cognizing, not severing versus severing, not examining versus examining, not investigating versus investigating, not realizing versus realizing."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Vātsīputrīya wanderer, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and expressed approval, and rising from his seat, departed.

---

### (964)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a Vātsīputrīya wanderer came to visit the Buddha. He exchanged courtesies with the World-Honored One, and after the exchange, withdrew and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, I wish to ask something. Do you have the leisure to explain it to me?" At that time, the World-Honored One remained silent.

The Vātsīputrīya wanderer asked a second and third time, and the Buddha also remained silent a second and third time.

At that time, the Vātsīputrīya wanderer addressed the Buddha, saying: "I have always been on friendly terms with Gautama. Now I have something to ask. Why are you silent?"

At that time, the World-Honored One had this thought: "This Vātsīputrīya wanderer has been straightforward through the long night, without deceit or pretense. Whenever he asks questions, it is always because he does not know, not intentionally to cause trouble. I should now receive him through the Abhidharma and Vinaya." Having thought thus, he told the Vātsīputrīya wanderer: "Ask whatever you wish, and I shall explain it to you."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "How is it, Gautama? Are there wholesome dharmas?"

The Buddha answered: "There are."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Please explain wholesome and unwholesome dharmas to me, so that I may understand."

The Buddha told Vaccha: "I shall now briefly explain wholesome and unwholesome dharmas to you. Listen carefully and reflect well. Vaccha, greed is an unwholesome dharma; taming greed is a wholesome dharma. Hatred and delusion are unwholesome dharmas; taming hatred and delusion are wholesome dharmas. Killing living beings is an unwholesome dharma; refraining from killing living beings is a wholesome dharma. Stealing, sexual misconduct, false speech, divisive speech, harsh speech, idle chatter, covetousness, ill will, and wrong views are unwholesome dharmas. Not stealing, and so forth up to right views, are wholesome dharmas. This, Vaccha, is what I have now explained: three kinds of wholesome dharmas and three kinds of unwholesome dharmas. Thus, when a noble disciple truly knows the three kinds of wholesome dharmas and three kinds of unwholesome dharmas, and truly knows the ten kinds of unwholesome dharmas and ten kinds of wholesome dharmas, then greed is completely exhausted without remainder, hatred and delusion are completely exhausted without remainder, and all taints are exhausted. They attain the taintless liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, and in this present life they know for themselves and realize: 'Birth is ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, and I know for myself that there will be no further existence.'"

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Is there even one bhikṣu in this Dharma and Vinaya who has exhausted all taints, attained the taintless liberation of mind, and so forth up to no further existence?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "Not just one, nor two, nor three, but up to five hundred and more. There are many bhikṣus in this Dharma and Vinaya who have exhausted all taints, and so forth up to no further existence."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Setting aside the bhikṣus, is there even one bhikṣuṇī in this Dharma and Vinaya who has exhausted all taints, and so forth up to no further existence?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "Not just one, two, or three bhikṣuṇīs, but up to five hundred and more. There are many bhikṣuṇīs in this Dharma and Vinaya who have exhausted all taints, and so forth up to no further existence."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Setting aside the bhikṣuṇīs, is there even one upāsaka who cultivates the holy life and has crossed over doubt in this Dharma and Vinaya?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "Not just one, two, or three, but up to five hundred and more. There are many upāsakas who cultivate the holy life and, in this Dharma and Vinaya, have severed the five lower fetters, attained the fruit of non-returner, and will not return to be reborn here."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Setting aside the upāsakas, is there even one upāsikā who cultivates the holy life and has crossed over doubt in this Dharma and Vinaya?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "Not just one, two, or three upāsikās, but up to five hundred and more. There are many upāsikās in this Dharma and Vinaya who have severed the five lower fetters, are spontaneously reborn there, attain the fruit of non-returner, and will not return to be reborn here."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Setting aside the bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, and the upāsakas and upāsikās who cultivate the holy life, is there even one upāsaka who enjoys the five sensual pleasures and has crossed over doubt in this Dharma and Vinaya?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "Not just one, two, or three, but up to five hundred and more. There are many upāsakas who are householders with wives and children, adorned with fragrances and flowers, who keep servants, and in this Dharma and Vinaya have severed the three fetters, with greed, hatred, and delusion attenuated, attaining the fruit of once-returner. After one more coming and going, they reach the end of suffering."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Setting aside the upāsakas, is there even one upāsikā who partakes of the five sensual pleasures and has crossed over doubt in this Dharma and Vinaya?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "Not just one, two, or three, but up to five hundred and more. There are many upāsikās who are householders, who raise sons and daughters, partake of the five sensual pleasures, and adorn themselves with flowers and fragrances, and in this Dharma and Vinaya have exhausted the three fetters, attaining the fruit of stream-entry. They are no longer subject to falling into the lower realms, are certain and rightly directed toward three kinds of awakening, and after seven more existences among gods and humans, they reach the end of suffering."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, if the ascetic Gautama had attained perfect awakening, but the bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, and upāsikās who cultivate the holy life, as well as the upāsakas and upāsikās who partake of the five sensual pleasures, had not attained such accomplishments, then it would not be sufficient. But since the ascetic Gautama has attained perfect awakening, and the bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, and upāsikās who cultivate the holy life, as well as the upāsakas and upāsikās who partake of the five sensual pleasures, have accomplished such merits and virtues, it is therefore sufficient. Gautama, I shall now speak a simile."

The Buddha told Vaccha: "Speak as you wish."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Just as when the heavens send a great rain, and the water flows downward, Gautama's Dharma and Vinaya is likewise. Bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, and upāsikās, whether male or female, all flow along, directed toward nirvāṇa, coursing swiftly toward nirvāṇa. How wonderful! The Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, this balanced Dharma and Vinaya! For other wanderers from different traditions who come to Gautama seeking to go forth in the true Dharma and Vinaya and receive full ordination, how long must they wait before being permitted to go forth?"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "If other wanderers wish to come to the true Dharma and Vinaya seeking to go forth and receive full ordination, they must undergo up to four months of probation, dwelling with a preceptor and receiving robes. However, this is merely a rough guideline established for people."

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "If other wanderers who wish to go forth in the true Dharma and Vinaya and receive full ordination are required to receive dependence from a preceptor and, after completing four months, are permitted to go forth, then I am willing to spend four months receiving dependence from a preceptor. If I am then permitted to go forth and receive full ordination in the true Dharma and Vinaya, I shall go forth and receive full ordination in Gautama's teaching and cultivate the holy life."

The Buddha told Vaccha: "Did I not already say it was merely a rough guideline for people?"

Vaccha addressed the Buddha: "Yes, Gautama."

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should ordain this Vaccha wanderer, granting him going forth and full ordination in the true Dharma and Vinaya."

The Vātsīputrīya wanderer then obtained going forth and full ordination in the true Dharma and Vinaya and received the bhikṣu's portion. Within half a month, he learned all that should be known, recognized, seen, attained, realized, and verified; he fully knew, fully recognized, fully saw, fully attained, fully realized, and fully verified the Tathāgata's true Dharma.

The Venerable Vaccha had this thought: "I have now realized all that should be known, recognized, seen, attained, realized, and verified; I have fully known, fully recognized, fully seen, fully attained, fully realized, and fully verified all of it. I should now go see the World-Honored One."

At that time, Vaccha went to the World-Honored One. He bowed his head in obeisance at the Buddha's feet, stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, regarding the learning of all that should be known, recognized, seen, attained, realized, and verified, I have fully known, fully recognized, fully seen, fully attained, fully realized, and fully verified the World-Honored One's true Dharma. I only wish that the World-Honored One would teach me the Dharma. Having heard it, I shall go alone to a quiet place, concentrate my mind in diligent practice, dwelling without negligence, contemplating: 'This is why a son of good family shaves off his hair and beard, dons the kāṣāya robes, goes forth from the household life with right faith to study the Way,' and so forth, up to: 'I know for myself that there will be no further existence.'"

The Buddha told Vaccha: "There are two dharmas which, when cultivated and extensively cultivated, are called śamatha and vipaśyanā. When these two dharmas are cultivated and extensively cultivated, one comes to know the realms, the fruits, to clearly comprehend the realms, to know the various realms, and to clearly comprehend the various realms. Thus, bhikṣu, if you wish to seek separation from desires and unwholesome dharmas, and so forth up to abiding with the complete attainment of the fourth dhyāna; loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity; the base of infinite space, the base of infinite consciousness, the base of nothingness, and the base of neither-perception-nor-non-perception; to exhaust the three fetters and attain the fruit of stream-entry; to exhaust the three fetters, with greed, hatred, and delusion attenuated, to attain the fruit of once-returner; to exhaust the five lower fetters and attain the fruit of non-returner; various kinds of spiritual powers, the divine eye, the divine ear, knowledge of others' minds, knowledge of past lives, knowledge of the passing and rebirth of beings, and knowledge of the exhaustion of taints, all can be attained. Therefore, bhikṣu, you should cultivate these two dharmas, cultivate them extensively. Through cultivating these two dharmas, you will know the various realms, and so forth up to the exhaustion of taints."

At that time, the Venerable Vaccha, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced, made obeisance, and departed. Then, Vaccha went alone to a quiet place, concentrated his mind in diligent practice, dwelling without negligence, and so forth, up to knowing for himself that there would be no further existence.

At that time, a group of many bhikṣus were preparing to go pay their respects and make offerings to the World-Honored One.

At that time, Vaccha asked the many bhikṣus: "Are you preparing to go pay your respects and make offerings to the World-Honored One?"

The bhikṣus answered: "Yes."

At that time, Vaccha said to the bhikṣus: "Venerable ones, please convey my words. Pay respect to the
