# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Thirty-Five

---

### (970)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, in Rājagṛha there was a non-Buddhist renunciant named Śarapu, dwelling beside the Sumāgadhā Pool. Among his own assembly he proclaimed: "I fully know the Dharma of the śramaṇa followers of the Śākyan. I already knew their Dharma and Vinaya beforehand, and then completely abandoned them."

At that time, many bhikṣus, in the early morning, put on their robes and took their bowls to enter Rājagṛha to beg for food. They heard that there was a non-Buddhist named Śarapu, dwelling beside the Sumāgadhā Pool in Rājagṛha, who proclaimed among his own assembly: "I fully know all the Dharma and Vinaya of the śramaṇa followers of the Śākyan. I already knew their Dharma and Vinaya, and then abandoned them." Having heard these words, after finishing their alms round, they returned to the monastery, put away their robes and bowls, washed their feet, went to the Buddha, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, this morning we put on our robes, took our bowls, and entered Rājagṛha to beg for food. We heard that there is a non-Buddhist renunciant named Śarapu, dwelling beside the Sumāgadhā Pool in Rājagṛha, who proclaims among his own assembly: 'I fully know the Dharma of the śramaṇa followers of the Śākyan. Having known their Dharma and Vinaya, I then abandoned them.' It would be excellent, World-Honored One, if you yourself would go to the Sumāgadhā Pool side, out of compassion for him!"

At that time, the World-Honored One silently assented. In the afternoon, he arose from meditation and went to the Sumāgadhā Pool side, to the place of the non-Buddhist renunciant Śarapu.

At that time, the non-Buddhist renunciant Śarapu saw the World-Honored One coming from afar, and immediately prepared a seat, inviting the Buddha to sit.

The Buddha sat down and said to Śarapu: "Did you truly make this statement: 'I fully know all the Dharma and Vinaya of the śramaṇa followers of the Śākyan. Having known their Dharma and Vinaya, I then abandoned them'?" At that time, Śarapu remained silent and did not answer.

The Buddha said to Śarapu: "You should speak now. Why are you silent? If your knowledge is complete, I will rejoice in it. If it is not complete, I will make it complete for you." Śarapu still remained silent. This was said a second and a third time, and he remained silent all three times.

At that time, a brahmacarya disciple of Śarapu said to Śarapu: "Master, you should have gone to the śramaṇa Gautama to speak your knowledge and views. Now the śramaṇa Gautama has come here himself; why do you not speak? The śramaṇa Gautama has also told the master: 'If it is complete, I will rejoice in it. If it is not complete, I will make it complete.' Why are you silent and do not reply?" Even when that brahmacarya disciple of Śarapu urged him, he still remained silent.

At that time, the World-Honored One said to Śarapu: "If anyone were to say, 'The śramaṇa Gautama is not a Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Enlightened One,' and I were to skillfully admonish and question him, then when skillfully admonished and questioned, that person would speak evasively about extraneous matters, or become angry, proud, and obstructive, unable to endure, with no way to manifest a response, or would remain silent, ashamed, head bowed, secretly reflecting within, just as you, Śarapu, are doing now. If anyone were to say, 'The śramaṇa Gautama does not have the true Dharma and Vinaya,' and I were to skillfully admonish and question him, he too would be just as you are today, remaining silent. If anyone were to say, 'The disciples of the śramaṇa Gautama do not practice well,' and I were to skillfully admonish and question him, he too would be, up to and including, just as you are today, remaining silent." At that time, the World-Honored One, having made the lion's roar beside the Sumāgadhā Pool, rose from his seat and departed.

At that time, the brahmacarya disciple of Śarapu said to Śarapu: "It is like a bull whose two horns have been cut off, entering an empty cattle pen, kneeling on the ground and bellowing loudly. The master is just like this, making the lion's roar among those who are not disciples of the śramaṇa Gautama. It is like a woman trying to make a man's voice, but when she opens her mouth, a woman's voice comes out. The master is just like this, making the lion's roar among those who are not disciples of the śramaṇa Gautama. It is like a jackal trying to make a fox's cry, but when it opens its mouth, a jackal's cry comes out. The master is just like this, among those who are not disciples of the śramaṇa Gautama, trying to make the lion's roar."

At that time, the brahmacarya disciple of Śarapu, having rebuked and censured Śarapu to his face, rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (971)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, beside the Sumāgadhā Pool in Rājagṛha there was a non-Buddhist renunciant named Sthavira, dwelling by that pool side. Among his own assembly he said: "I shall speak one verse. If anyone can reply to it, I will practice the holy life under that person."

At that time, many bhikṣus, in the early morning, put on their robes and took their bowls to enter Rājagṛha to beg for food. They heard that there was a non-Buddhist renunciant named Sthavira, dwelling beside the Sumāgadhā Pool, who among his own assembly said: "I shall speak one verse. If anyone can reply to it, I will practice the holy life under that person." After finishing their alms round, they returned to the monastery, put away their robes and bowls, washed their feet, went to the Buddha, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, this morning, together with many bhikṣus, we entered the city to beg for food and heard that there is a non-Buddhist renunciant named Sthavira, dwelling beside the Sumāgadhā Pool, who among his own assembly said: 'I shall speak one verse. If anyone can reply to it, I will practice the holy life under that person.' May the World-Honored One go there himself, out of compassion!"

At that time, the World-Honored One silently assented. That afternoon, he arose from meditation and went to the Sumāgadhā Pool side.

At that time, the non-Buddhist renunciant Sthavira saw the World-Honored One from afar, and immediately prepared a seat, inviting the Buddha to sit. After the World-Honored One sat down, he said to the non-Buddhist renunciant Sthavira: "Did you truly make this statement: 'I shall speak one verse. If anyone can reply to it, I will practice the holy life under that person'? You may now speak your verse; I am able to reply."

At that time, that non-Buddhist piled up rope-beds to make a high seat, climbed up upon it himself, and spoke this verse:

> "A bhikṣu lives by the Dharma,
> Does not frighten living beings,
> With mind tranquil, practicing renunciation,
> Maintaining precepts, following cessation."

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing the mind of that non-Buddhist renunciant Sthavira, spoke this verse:

> "If regarding this verse you speak,
> You can yourself follow and practice it,
> Then I would regard you
> As a worthy person to observe.
> But observing what you now say,
> Your words and actions do not correspond.
> Be tranquil and self-subdued,
> Do not frighten living beings.
> With mind tranquil, practice renunciation,
> Uphold and maintain pure precepts.
> Following subdual and cessation,
> Body, speech, and mind free from evil,
> Well guarding your dwelling place,
> Not allowing heedlessness:
> This is what is called following
> Subdual and cessation."

At that time, the non-Buddhist renunciant Sthavira thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." He then descended from the bed, joined his palms, and said to the Buddha: "May I now obtain ordination and full ordination in the true Dharma and Vinaya, to become a bhikṣu?"

The Buddha said to the non-Buddhist renunciant Sthavira: "You may now obtain ordination and full ordination in the true Dharma and Vinaya, to fulfill the status of a bhikṣu."

Thus the non-Buddhist renunciant Sthavira obtained ordination and, having become a bhikṣu, contemplated: "For the sake of which a good son shaves off his hair and beard, puts on the kāṣāya robes, and with right faith leaves the household life to train in the path." And so forth, until his mind was well liberated, and he attained arhatship.

---

### (972)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, many brāhmaṇa renunciants were dwelling beside the Sumāgadhā Pool, gathered together in one place, engaging in this discussion: "Such is the brāhmaṇa truth. Such is the brāhmaṇa truth."

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing the thoughts of those many brāhmaṇa renunciants, went to the Sumāgadhā Pool side.

At that time, the many brāhmaṇa renunciants saw the Buddha coming from afar, and immediately prepared a seat for the Buddha, inviting him to sit.

The Buddha sat down and addressed the brāhmaṇa renunciants: "What were you all discussing, gathered here beside the Sumāgadhā Pool?"

The brāhmaṇa renunciants said to the Buddha: "Gautama, we many brāhmaṇa renunciants gathered here at this seat were engaging in this discussion: 'Such is the brāhmaṇa truth. Such is the brāhmaṇa truth.'"

The Buddha said to the brāhmaṇa renunciants: "There are three kinds of brāhmaṇa truths, which I myself have awakened to in attaining perfect enlightenment, and which I also teach to others. You brāhmaṇa renunciants say: 'Not harming any living being is the brāhmaṇa truth, and it is not false.' Regarding that, whether one claims 'I am superior,' 'I am equal,' or 'I am inferior,' if one does not cling to that truth, and toward all the world manifests the image of loving-kindness, this is called the first brāhmaṇa truth, which I myself have awakened to in attaining perfect enlightenment, and which I teach to others.

"Furthermore, brāhmaṇas say: 'All that has the nature of arising has the nature of ceasing. This is truth, and it is not false.' And so forth, up to not clinging to that truth, and observing the arising and ceasing throughout all the world. This is called the second brāhmaṇa truth.

"Furthermore, brāhmaṇas say: 'There is no self, no place of self, and no affairs of self; there is utterly nothing. There is no self, no place of self, and no affairs of self; there is utterly nothing. This is truth, and it is not false.' As previously stated, and so forth, up to not clinging to it, and throughout all the world manifesting the image of non-self. This is called the third brāhmaṇa truth, which I myself have awakened to in attaining perfect enlightenment, and which I teach to others." At that time, the many brāhmaṇa renunciants remained silent.

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "Now I have eclipsed those fools and slain those evil ones. In this assembly there is not one who can reflect within himself and create the conditions to practice the holy life in the Dharma of the śramaṇa Gautama." Knowing this, he rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (973)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī at the Ghoṣira Garden. The Venerable Ānanda was also dwelling there.

At that time, there was a non-Buddhist renunciant named Chandaka, who went to the Venerable Ānanda. Having exchanged greetings and courtesies with the Venerable Ānanda, he withdrew and sat to one side. He asked the Venerable Ānanda: "For what reason did you go forth to practice the holy life under the śramaṇa Gautama?"

Ānanda replied: "It is for the purpose of abandoning desire, anger, and delusion that I went forth to practice the holy life under him."

Chandaka asked further: "Can he teach the abandoning of desire, anger, and delusion?"

Ānanda replied: "I too can teach the abandoning of desire, anger, and delusion."

Chandaka asked further: "What faults do you see in desire, anger, and delusion, that you teach the abandoning of desire, anger, and delusion?"

Ānanda replied: "When one is stained and attached to desire, and the mind is covered and obstructed by it, one either harms oneself, or harms others, or harms both. One incurs offenses in the present life, incurs offenses in future lives, and incurs offenses in both present and future lives. One's mind constantly harbors sorrow and painful feelings. When anger covers and obstructs the mind, and when delusion covers and obstructs the mind, one harms oneself, harms others, and harms both oneself and others, and so forth, up to constantly harboring sorrow and painful feelings. Furthermore, desire makes one blind, without eyes, without knowledge, weakens wisdom, creates obstacles, is not clarity, is not equal awakening, and does not turn toward nirvāṇa. Anger and delusion are likewise. Seeing such faults in desire, anger, and delusion, I teach the abandoning of desire, anger, and delusion."

Chandaka asked further: "What benefits do you see in the abandoning of desire, anger, and delusion, that you teach the abandoning of desire, anger, and delusion?"

Ānanda replied: "Having abandoned desire, one does not harm oneself, nor does one harm others, nor does one harm both. Furthermore, one does not incur offenses in the present life, nor offenses in future lives, nor offenses in both present and future lives. One's mind constantly harbors joy and pleasant feelings. Anger and delusion are likewise. In the present life one constantly leaves behind the blazing fire, not depending on time or season, and there is attainment of direct personal realization in the present life. Seeing such virtues and benefits, I teach the abandoning of desire, anger, and delusion."

Chandaka asked further: "Venerable Ānanda, is there a path and a way that, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, can abandon desire, anger, and delusion?"

Ānanda replied: "There is. It is called the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, and so forth, up to right samādhi."

The non-Buddhist Chandaka said to the Venerable Ānanda: "This is an excellent path, an excellent way! When cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, it can abandon desire, anger, and delusion."

At that time, the non-Buddhist Chandaka, having heard what the Venerable Ānanda said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (974)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the Venerable Śāriputra the Dharma in various ways, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening him. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened him, he became silent.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and departed.

At that time, there was a non-Buddhist renunciant named Pūrṇika who was coming along the road. He asked the Venerable Śāriputra: "Where are you coming from?"

Śāriputra replied: "Fire-clan one, I have come from my World-Honored One's presence, having heard the Great Teacher's instructional teaching."

Pūrṇika asked the Venerable Śāriputra: "Are you still not weaned from milk, that you hear instructional teachings from your teacher?"

Śāriputra replied: "Fire-clan one, I am not weaned from the milk of the Great Teacher's instructional teachings."

Pūrṇika said to the Venerable Śāriputra: "I have long since been weaned from milk and abandoned my teacher's instructional teachings."

Śāriputra said: "Your Dharma is a badly proclaimed dharma and vinaya, a wrong realization, not leading to liberation, not the path to right awakening, a ruined dharma, not a praiseworthy dharma, not a dharma to be relied upon. Moreover, that teacher is not a Perfectly Enlightened One. Therefore, you all quickly wean from the milk and depart from the teacher's teachings. It is like a cow that is rough, wild, and unruly, and moreover gives little milk; her calf, when suckling, quickly abandons her and leaves. Likewise, a badly proclaimed dharma and vinaya, a wrong realization, not leading to liberation, not the path to right awakening, a ruined dharma, not praiseworthy, not to be relied upon; moreover, that teacher is not a Perfectly Enlightened One. Therefore one quickly abandons the teacher's instructional teachings. My Dharma is the true Dharma and Vinaya, a good realization, leading to liberation, the path to right awakening, not ruined, praiseworthy, to be relied upon. Moreover, that Great Teacher is a Perfectly Enlightened One. Therefore I long drink its milk, hearing and receiving the Great Teacher's instructional teachings. It is like a cow that is not rough, wild, or unruly, and moreover gives abundant milk; her calf, when suckling, is long satisfied and does not tire. My Dharma is likewise: it is the true Dharma and Vinaya, and so forth, up to long hearing the instructional teachings."

At that time, Pūrṇika said to Śāriputra: "You have all gained good fortune, in the true Dharma and Vinaya, and so forth, up to long hearing the instructional teachings."

At that time, the non-Buddhist renunciant Pūrṇika, having heard what Śāriputra said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, and departed along the road.

---

### (975)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the non-Buddhist renunciant Pūrṇika came to the Buddha. Having exchanged greetings and courtesies with the World-Honored One, he withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "Gautama, on a previous day, many various non-Buddhist renunciant śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas gathered at the Unprecedented Lecture Hall and engaged in this discussion: 'The śramaṇa Gautama's wisdom is like an empty house. He cannot establish arguments in a great assembly: this is fitting, this is not fitting; this agrees, this does not agree. He is like a blind bull that walks crookedly along the edges and does not enter the middle of the field. The śramaṇa Gautama is also like this: no fitting or not fitting, no agreeing or not agreeing.'"

The Buddha said to Pūrṇika: "These discussions of the non-Buddhists about 'fitting and not fitting, agreeing and not agreeing,' in the noble Dharma and Vinaya, are like children's play. It is like a man, eighty or ninety years old, with white hair and fallen teeth, playing children's games, kneading and shaping mud into elephants, horses, and various forms. Everyone says: 'This old man is like a child.' Just so, fire-clan one, these various discussions about fitting and not fitting, agreeing and not agreeing, in the noble Dharma and Vinaya, are like children's play. However, within such matters, there is nothing that constitutes the proper endeavor of a bhikṣu."

Pūrṇika said to the Buddha: "Gautama, in what then does the proper endeavor of a bhikṣu consist?"

The Buddha said to the non-Buddhist: "Purifying the impure: this is called the proper endeavor of a bhikṣu. Taming the untamed: this is called the proper endeavor of a bhikṣu. Causing those who lack right concentration to attain right concentration: this is called the proper endeavor of a bhikṣu. Causing the unliberated to attain liberation: this is called the proper endeavor of a bhikṣu. Causing what is not yet cut off to be cut off; causing what is not yet known to be known; causing what is not yet cultivated to be cultivated; causing what is not yet attained to be attained: this is called the proper endeavor of a bhikṣu.

"How does one purify the impure? It means that those whose precepts are impure are caused to become pure. How does one tame the untamed? It means the eye faculty; the ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind faculties that are untamed are caused to be tamed. This is called taming the untamed. How does one cause those without right concentration to attain right concentration? It means that the mind which lacks right concentration is caused to attain right concentration. How does one cause the unliberated to attain liberation? It means that the mind which is not liberated from desire, anger, and delusion is caused to attain liberation. How does one cause what is not cut off to be cut off? It means ignorance and craving for existence; what is not cut off is caused to be cut off. How does one cause what is not known to be known? It means name-and-form; what is not known is caused to be known. How does one cause what is not cultivated to be cultivated? It means śamatha and vipaśyanā; what is not cultivated is caused to be cultivated. How does one cause what is not attained to be attained? It means parinirvāṇa; what is not attained is caused to be attained. This is called the proper endeavor of a bhikṣu."

Pūrṇika said to the Buddha: "Gautama, this is indeed the proper endeavor of a bhikṣu, this is the firm proper endeavor of a bhikṣu, namely, the complete exhaustion of all outflows."

At that time, the non-Buddhist renunciant Pūrṇika, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (976)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, there was a non-Buddhist renunciant named Śiva, who came to the Buddha. Having exchanged greetings and courtesies with the World-Honored One, he withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "Gautama, what is 'learning'? As for what is called a 'learner,' what does he learn?"

The Buddha said to Śiva: "One learns what is to be learned; therefore one is called a 'learner.'"

Śiva said to the Buddha: "What does one learn?"

The Buddha said to Śiva: "At the appropriate time, one learns the higher morality, the higher mind, and the higher wisdom."

Śiva said to the Buddha: "If an arhat bhikṣu whose outflows are exhausted, who has done what was to be done, who has laid down the heavy burden, who has attained his own benefit, who has completely destroyed the fetters of existence, and who is well liberated through right knowledge, at that time what more does he learn?"

The Buddha said to Śiva: "If an arhat bhikṣu whose outflows are exhausted, and so forth, up to who is well liberated through right knowledge, at that time he realizes that desire is permanently exhausted without remainder; he realizes that anger and delusion are permanently exhausted without remainder. Therefore he no longer creates any evil and constantly practices all that is good. Śiva, this is called learning what is to be learned."

At that time, the non-Buddhist renunciant Śiva, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (977)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the non-Buddhist renunciant Śiva came to the Buddha. Having exchanged greetings and courtesies with the World-Honored One, he withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "Gautama, there are certain śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who hold this view and make this claim: 'Whatever a person knows and feels, all of it is due to past actions as the cause. By cultivating austerities, past karma is exhausted, and no new karma is created. Cutting off the cause and condition, in the future there will be no more outflows. With outflows exhausted, karma is exhausted. With karma exhausted, suffering is exhausted. With suffering exhausted, one reaches the ultimate end of suffering.' What does Gautama say about this?"

The Buddha said to Śiva: "Those śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas truly speak vaguely, without investigation, without analysis, foolishly, unskilfully, and without discernment. Why? Because sometimes suffering arises from wind, and beings are aware of it. Sometimes it arises from phlegm, sometimes from saliva, sometimes from the combined factors, sometimes from self-harm, sometimes from harm by others, sometimes from seasonal changes. As for self-harm: some pull out their hair, some pull out their beards, some constantly stand with raised arms, some squat on the ground, some lie in ashes, some lie on thorns, some lie on pestles, or on boards, or smear the ground with cow dung and lie upon it, some lie in water, some bathe three times a day, some stand on one foot with their body following the sun. Such are the many painful ascetic practices. Śiva, this is called self-harm. As for harm by others: some are harmed by others' hands, stones, knives, clubs, and so forth, with various injuries to the body. This is called harm by others. Śiva, as for harm by seasonal changes: in winter there is great cold, in spring there is great heat, in summer there is both cold and heat. This is called harm from seasonal changes. In the world this is true and not false. Śiva, the world has these truths: harm from wind, up to harm from seasonal changes, and beings truly perceive them. You yourself also have these afflictions: harm from wind, phlegm, saliva, up to harm from seasonal changes. You perceive these, and truly know them as such. Śiva, if those śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas say that everything a person knows and feels is due to past actions as the cause, they abandon worldly truths and follow their own views, making false claims.

"Śiva, there are five causes and five conditions that give rise to mental suffering. What are the five? Due to the fetter of desire, conditioned by the fetter of desire, mental suffering arises. Due to the fetters of anger, drowsiness, restlessness-and-worry, and doubt, conditioned by the fetters of anger, drowsiness, restlessness-and-worry, and doubt, mental suffering arises. Śiva, these are called the five causes and five conditions that give rise to mental suffering. Śiva, there are five causes and five conditions by which mental suffering does not arise. What are the five? Due to the fetter of desire, conditioned by the fetter of desire, mental suffering arises; but being free from the fetter of desire, mental suffering does not arise. This is called the five causes and five conditions by which mental suffering does not arise. In the present life one leaves behind the blazing fire, not depending on time or season, with penetrating direct vision, through one's own self-realization. Śiva, furthermore, there is the present-life leaving behind of the blazing fire, not depending on time or season, with penetrating direct vision, through one's own self-realization, namely the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, and so forth, up to right samādhi."

When this Dharma was spoken, the non-Buddhist renunciant Śiva was freed from dust and stain, and attained the purity of the Dharma-eye. At that time, the non-Buddhist renunciant Śiva, having seen the Dharma, obtained the Dharma, known the Dharma, entered the Dharma, crossed beyond all doubts, not relying on others, having entered the true Dharma and Vinaya and attained fearlessness, immediately rose from his seat, straightened his robes, joined his palms, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, may I now obtain ordination and full ordination in the true Dharma and Vinaya, to attain the status of a bhikṣu?"

The Buddha said to Śiva: "You may now obtain ordination." As stated above, and so forth, up to "his mind was well liberated, and he attained arhatship."

---

### (978)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Nāḷa village at the Pāvārikā Mango Grove. At that time, in the Nāḷa village there was a merchant-chief non-Buddhist renunciant, one hundred and twenty years old, aged with mature faculties, who was respected and honored by the śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, elders, and householders of the Nāḷa village as if he were an arhat.

That merchant-chief non-Buddhist renunciant had a relative who, upon death, was reborn in heaven. That deity, from heaven, saw the merchant-chief non-Buddhist renunciant and thought: "I wish to go and instruct this merchant-chief non-Buddhist renunciant to visit the World-Honored One and practice the holy life. But I fear he will not follow my words. I shall now go to him and pose a mind-riddle for him to ask."

He then descended to the Nāḷa village and went to the merchant-chief non-Buddhist renunciant. He spoke a verse as a question:

> "How is an evil friend
> Who appears as a good friend?
> How is a good friend
> Like one's own body?
> Why does one seek cessation?
> How does one leave behind the blazing fire?"

"If you, O sage, take this mind-riddle and ask it of someone, and there is one who can clearly explain its meaning and answer you, then you should go forth under that one and practice the holy life."

At that time, the merchant-chief non-Buddhist renunciant received the question posed by the deity and took it to Pūraṇa Kāśyapa, asking him this mind-riddle verse. Pūraṇa Kāśyapa could not even understand it himself, let alone answer it. Then the merchant-chief non-Buddhist renunciant went further to Maskarin Gośālīputra, Sañjayin Vairaṭṭīputra, Ajita Keśakambala, Kakuda Kātyāyana, and Nirgrantha Jñātaputra, asking each of them this mind-riddle verse, and none could answer.

At that time, the merchant-chief non-Buddhist renunciant thought: "I have asked this mind-riddle of all the renunciant teachers, and none could answer. Now I should seek ordination elsewhere. But since I still have wealth and possessions, perhaps I should just return home and enjoy the five sensual pleasures." Then he thought again: "I should now go to the śramaṇa Gautama. But those senior teachers, the śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas Pūraṇa Kāśyapa and the others, could not answer, and the śramaṇa Gautama went forth while young; how could he possibly understand? However, I have heard the ancients say: 'Do not look down upon a newly ordained young renunciant. There may be young renunciants of great power and virtue.' I shall go to the śramaṇa Gautama." Having gone, he posed the mind-riddle in his mind, as the verse stated.

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing what was in the merchant-chief's mind, spoke this verse:

> "How is an evil friend
> Who appears as a good friend?
> One whose inner mind is truly ashamed and disgusted,
> Yet whose mouth says, 'I am of one mind with you,'
> Who in undertakings does not joyfully cooperate:
> Thus one knows he is not a good friend.
> His mouth speaks affectionate words,
> But his mind is not truly in accord;
> His actions are not the same.
> The wise should discern this.
> This is called an evil friend
> Who appears as a good friend,
> As if one's own body. How is a good friend
> One with oneself?
> Not that good friend
> Who is heedless and unrestrained,
> Who undermines, harbors doubt,
> And watches for one's weak points.
> One who is secure as a good friend,
> Like a child sleeping in its father's embrace,
> Is not swayed by others' talk.
> One should know the good friend. Why does one seek cessation?
> Where delight arises,
> Coolness and praise abound.
> Cultivating the fruits of merit,
> Coolness and permanent cessation.
> Therefore one seeks cessation. How does one leave behind the blazing fire?
> The taste of tranquility and cessation,
> Knowing the taste of renunciation,
> Renouncing the evil of blazing fire,
> Drinking the taste of Dharma-joy,
> Stilling and leaving behind the fire of desire:
> This is called leaving behind the blazing fire."

At that time, the merchant-chief non-Buddhist renunciant thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama knows my mind." He said to the Buddha: "May I now enter the true Dharma and Vinaya of the śramaṇa Gautama and practice the holy life, going forth and receiving full ordination to fulfill the status of a bhikṣu?"

The Buddha said to the merchant-chief non-Buddhist renunciant: "You may now obtain ordination in the true Dharma and Vinaya, practice the holy life, go forth and receive full ordination, to fulfill the status of a bhikṣu." Having thus gone forth, he contemplated, and so forth, up to his mind being well liberated, and he attained arhatship.

---

### (979)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kuśinagara, in the Śāla grove of the Mallas, between the twin Śāla trees in the strong men's birthplace.

At that time, when the time for the World-Honored One's nirvāṇa had arrived, he told the Venerable Ānanda: "You should spread a rope-bed for the World-Honored One between the twin trees, with the head to the north. Today, in the middle of the night, the Tathāgata will pass into parinirvāṇa in the nirvāṇa without remainder."

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda, following the instruction, spread a rope-bed between the twin trees with the head to the north. When finished, he came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I have spread the rope-bed between the twin trees with the head to the north."

At that time, the World-Honored One went to the twin trees and lay down on the rope-bed with his head to the north, on his right side, with one foot upon the other, mindful and clearly aware, with right mindfulness and right knowledge.

At that time, in the country of Kuśinagara there was a non-Buddhist renunciant named Subhadra, one hundred and twenty years old, aged with mature faculties, who was revered and honored by the people of Kuśinagara as if he were an arhat. That renunciant Subhadra heard that the World-Honored One would pass into parinirvāṇa in the nirvāṇa without remainder in the middle of the night. "However, I have doubts, and I abide in hope. The śramaṇa Gautama has the power to awaken me. I should now go to the śramaṇa Gautama and ask about my doubts." He then left Kuśinagara and went to the World-Honored One.

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda was walking back and forth outside the garden gate. At that time, Subhadra said to Ānanda: "I have heard that the śramaṇa Gautama will pass into parinirvāṇa in the nirvāṇa without remainder tonight in the middle of the night. I have doubts, and I abide in hope. The śramaṇa Gautama has the power to awaken me. If Ānanda would not find it burdensome, please go and tell Gautama that, if he has a little leisure, he might answer my questions."

Ānanda replied: "Do not trouble the World-Honored One. The World-Honored One is weary."

Thus Subhadra asked the Venerable Ānanda three times, and the Venerable Ānanda refused three times.

Subhadra said: "I have heard the ancient, senior renunciant teachers say that only very rarely does a Tathāgata, Worthy One, Perfectly Enlightened One appear in the world, like an udumbara flower. And now tonight, in the middle of the night, the Tathāgata will pass into parinirvāṇa in the realm of nirvāṇa without remainder. I now have doubts about the Dharma, and I abide in faith. The śramaṇa Gautama has the power to awaken me. If Ānanda would not find it burdensome, please tell the śramaṇa Gautama on my behalf."

Ānanda again replied: "Subhadra, do not trouble the World-Honored One. The World-Honored One today is weary."

At that time, the World-Honored One, with his divine ear, heard the exchange between Ānanda and Subhadra, and told the Venerable Ānanda: "Do not obstruct the non-Buddhist renunciant Subhadra. Let him enter and ask his doubts. Why? This is the last discussion with a non-Buddhist renunciant. This is the last one to attain realization as a voice-hearer, to be a 'come-well' bhikṣu, namely Subhadra."

At that time, the World-Honored One opened the way for Subhadra's wholesome roots, and with his joy increasing, Subhadra went to the World-Honored One. Having exchanged greetings and courtesies with the World-Honored One, he withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "Gautama, as for all the worldly bases of knowledge, namely Pūraṇa Kāśyapa and the other six teachers, each claims: 'This is a śramaṇa. This is a śramaṇa.' How is it? Gautama, do they truly each have such a claim?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "At the age of twenty-nine,
> I went forth to cultivate the wholesome path.
> From attaining the Way until now,
> More than fifty years have passed.
> In samādhi, knowledge and conduct complete,
> I have always cultivated pure precepts.
> Apart from this small portion of the path,
> Outside of this there are no śramaṇas."

The Buddha said to Subhadra: "In any dharma and vinaya where the Noble Eightfold Path is not found, there is no first śramaṇa, no second, no third, no fourth śramaṇa. Subhadra, in this Dharma and Vinaya where the Noble Eightfold Path is found, there is the first śramaṇa, the second, third, and fourth śramaṇa. Apart from this, among the non-Buddhists there are no śramaṇas. Their teachers of other paths are empty śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas. Therefore, I now make the lion's roar in this assembly."

When this Dharma was spoken, the non-Buddhist renunciant Subhadra was freed from dust and stain, and attained the purity of the Dharma-eye. At that time, Subhadra, having seen the Dharma, obtained the Dharma, known the Dharma, entered the Dharma, crossed beyond all doubts, not relying on the faith of others, not depending on others for deliverance, attained fearlessness in the true Dharma and Vinaya. He rose from his seat, straightened his robes, placed his right knee on the ground, and said to the Venerable Ānanda: "You have gained good fortune. You have gained the Great Teacher. You are a disciple of the Great Teacher. You have been anointed upon the crown by the Great Teacher's rain. If I may now obtain ordination and full ordination in the true Dharma and Vinaya, to fulfill the status of a bhikṣu, I too should gain such good fortune!"

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, this non-Buddhist renunciant Subhadra now seeks ordination and full ordination in the true Dharma and Vinaya, to fulfill the status of a bhikṣu."

At that time, the World-Honored One said to Subhadra: "Come, bhikṣu, practice the holy life."

The Venerable Subhadra at that very time went forth. That itself was the full ordination, fulfilling the status of a bhikṣu. Having thus contemplated, and so forth, up to his mind being well liberated, he attained arhatship.

At that time, when the Venerable Subhadra attained arhatship, perceiving the bliss of liberation, he thought: "I cannot bear to see the Buddha's parinirvāṇa. I should pass into parinirvāṇa first." At that time, the Venerable Subhadra first passed into parinirvāṇa, and afterward the World-Honored One passed into parinirvāṇa.

---

### (980)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the Vajjians and arrived at the country of Vaiśālī, where he stayed beside the Monkey Pool at the Gabled Hall.

At that time, in the country of Vaiśālī there were many merchants who were preparing to travel to the country of Takṣaśilā. Those many merchants heard that the World-Honored One was traveling among the Vajjians and had arrived at Vaiśālī, staying beside the Monkey Pool at the Gabled Hall. Having heard this, they came to the Buddha, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The Buddha taught the merchants the Dharma in various ways, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening them. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened them, he became silent.

At that time, the merchants rose from their seats, straightened their robes, made obeisance to the Buddha, joined their palms, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, we merchants are preparing and equipping ourselves to travel to the country of Takṣaśilā. May the World-Honored One, together with the great assembly, accept our offering of food tomorrow morning."

At that time, the World-Honored One silently assented.

At that time, the merchants, knowing the World-Honored One had accepted the invitation, rose from their seats, paid respects at the Buddha's feet, and each returned to their own homes. They prepared various pure and fine food and drink, spread out seats, and set out clean water. In the early morning, they sent a messenger to inform the Buddha: "The time has come."

At that time, the World-Honored One, together with the great assembly, put on his robes and took his bowl, went to the merchants' place, and took his seat.

At that time, the merchants served pure and fine food and drink with their own hands. After the meal, when the bowls had been washed, they took a low seat and sat before the great assembly to listen to the Buddha teach the Dharma.

At that time, the World-Honored One said to the merchants: "You will be traveling through the wilderness, where there are terrors and frightening things that make the mind alarmed and the hair stand on end. At such times, you should recollect the Tathāgata's qualities, namely: the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, and so forth, up to the Buddha, the World-Honored One. By such recollection, fear will be dispelled. Also recollect the qualities of the Dharma: the Buddha's true Dharma and Vinaya, in the present life able to leave behind the blazing fire, not depending on time or season, penetrating and directly realizing through one's own self-realization. Also recollect the qualities of the Sangha: the World-Honored One's disciples who are well-directed, rightly directed, and so forth, up to being a field of merit for the world. By such recollection, fear will be dispelled.

"In the past, the devas and asuras fought with each other. At that time, Śakra, Lord of the Devas, told the assembly of devas: 'When you are fighting with the asuras, if fear arises, you should think of my banner, called the Banner of Conquest. By thinking of that banner, fear will be dispelled. If you do not think of my banner, you should think of the banner of Īśāna's son; if you do not think of Īśāna's son's banner, you should think of the banner of Varuṇa's son. By thinking of that banner, fear will be dispelled.' Likewise, merchants, when you are in the wilderness and are frightened, you should recollect the qualities of the Tathāgata, the qualities of the Dharma, and the qualities of the Sangha."

At that time, the World-Honored One, for the Vaiśālī merchants, spoke verses of rejoicing in their offering:

> "Making offerings to the bhikṣu Sangha,
> Food and drink at the proper time,
> With focused mind and careful thought,
> With right knowledge, practicing equanimity.
> Pure offerings and excellent fields of merit:
> You are all fully endowed with these.
> By the merit of this virtue,
> Through the long night you will gain peace and happiness.
> Setting forth your aspirations,
> All benefits will come to fruition.
> Safety for bipeds and quadrupeds,
> Safety in traveling on the roads.
> Safety at night, safety in the day,
> All evil is left far behind.
> Like rich and fertile farmland,
> With pure and excellent seeds,
> Irrigated by timely rain,
> The harvest is beyond measure.
> Pure precepts are excellent fields of merit,
> Fine and excellent food as seeds,
> Right conduct carried out accordingly:
> In the end, wondrous fruits are attained.
> Therefore, those who practice giving,
> Wishing to attain all virtues,
> Should follow the path of wisdom;
> All fruits will naturally be complete.
> To the one perfect in knowledge and conduct,
> With a right mind and full reverence,
> Plant the roots of all goodness,
> And in the end attain great merit.
> Truly knowing the world,
> One is endowed with right view.
> Endowed, one sees the right path;
> Endowed, one advances upward,
> Far from all defilements,
> One attains the path of nirvāṇa,
> Reaching the ultimate end of suffering:
> This is called being complete in all virtues."

At that time, the World-Honored One, having taught the Vaiśālī merchants various Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening them, rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (981)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu who dwells in a secluded place, under a tree, or in an empty dwelling, at times experiences terror and fright, with alarmed mind and hair standing on end, he should recollect the qualities of the Tathāgata, and the qualities of the Dharma and the Sangha," as extensively stated above. "When recollecting the qualities of the Tathāgata, the Dharma, and the Sangha, fear will be dispelled.

"Bhikṣus, in the past, Śakra Devānām Indra and the asuras fought with each other. At that time, the Lord Śakra said to the gods of the Thirty-Three: 'Good sirs, when the devas and asuras are fighting, if fear arises and the mind is alarmed with hair standing on end, you should think of my Banner of Conquest. When thinking of that banner, fear will be dispelled.' Likewise, bhikṣus, if in a secluded place, under a tree, or in an empty dwelling, fear arises with alarmed mind and hair standing on end, you should recollect the Tathāgata: 'The Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, and so forth, up to the Buddha, the World-Honored One.' At that time of recollection, fear will be dispelled. Why? That Lord Śakra harbors desire, anger, and delusion. He is not liberated from birth, aging, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, suffering, and distress. He has fear, dread, and seeks to flee and take refuge. Yet he still tells the gods of the Thirty-Three to think of his Banner of Conquest. How much more so the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, and so forth, up to the Buddha, the World-Honored One, who is free from desire, anger, and delusion, liberated from birth, aging, illness, death, sorrow, lamentation, suffering, and distress, without any fear, dread, fleeing, or taking refuge! How could it be that recollecting the Tathāgata would not dispel all fears?"

The Buddha having spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (982)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Sāketa, in the Añjana Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the Venerable Śāriputra: "I can teach the Dharma in brief or in detail, but those who understand it are rare."

The Venerable Śāriputra said to the Buddha: "May the World-Honored One teach in brief and in detail. There will indeed be those who understand the Dharma."

The Buddha said to Śāriputra: "If there is a being who, with regard to this conscious body and all external signs, is free from the binding tendencies of self-views, possession-of-self-views, and conceit-of-self, and so forth, up to attaining liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, personally knowing and realizing it in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment: with regard to this conscious body and all external signs, being free from self-views, possession-of-self-views, and the binding tendencies of conceit, therefore my mind is liberated and my wisdom is liberated; I personally know and realize this in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment.

"Śāriputra, that bhikṣu, with regard to this conscious body and all external signs, is free from self-views, possession-of-self-views, and the binding tendencies of conceit, and his mind is liberated and his wisdom is liberated; he personally knows and realizes this in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment. With regard to this conscious body and all external signs, being free from self-views, possession-of-self-views, and the binding tendencies of conceit, that mind is liberated and that wisdom is liberated; he personally knows and realizes this in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment.

"Śāriputra, if further a bhikṣu, with regard to this conscious body and all external signs, is free from self-views, possession-of-self-views, and the binding tendencies of conceit, that mind is liberated and that wisdom is liberated; he personally knows and realizes this in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment.

"Śāriputra, if further a bhikṣu, with regard to this conscious body and all external signs, is free from self-views, possession-of-self-views, and the binding tendencies of conceit, that mind is liberated and that wisdom is liberated; he personally knows and realizes this in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment. With regard to this conscious body and all external signs, being free from self-views, possession-of-self-views, and the binding tendencies of conceit, that mind is liberated and that wisdom is liberated; he personally knows and realizes this in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment. Śāriputra, this is called a bhikṣu who has cut off craving, overturned the fetters, and through the complete penetration of conceit, has reached the ultimate end of suffering.

"Śāriputra, regarding this I have an additional explanation, in answer to the question of Puṇṇaka in the Pārāyaṇa:

> 'The world's manifold distinctions:
> Wherever encountered, one is unmoved.
> Tranquil, free from all dust,
> Uprooted, without longing,
> Having crossed the ocean of the three existences,
> Free from the afflictions of aging and death.'"

The Buddha having spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Śāriputra, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (983)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda was in Śrāvastī at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. Alone in a quiet place, he contemplated thus: "There may be one person who thinks: 'With regard to this conscious body and all external signs, being free from self-views, possession-of-self-views, and the binding tendencies of conceit, and attaining liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, I personally know and realize this in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment. With regard to this conscious body and all external signs, being free from self-views, possession-of-self-views, and the binding tendencies of conceit, I shall attain liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, personally knowing and realizing it in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment.'"

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda, in the afternoon, arose from meditation and went to the World-Honored One. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, alone in a quiet place, I contemplated thus: 'If there is one person who says: "With regard to this conscious body and all external signs,"' and so forth, up to 'personally knowing and realizing it, abiding in its fulfillment.'"

The Buddha said to Ānanda: "So it is, so it is! There is one person who thinks: 'With regard to this conscious body and all external signs, being free from self-views, possession-of-self-views, and the binding tendencies of conceit, and attaining liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, I personally know and realize this in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment.' Ānanda, that bhikṣu, with regard to this conscious body and all external signs, is free from self-views, possession-of-self-views, and the binding tendencies of conceit, and his mind is liberated and his wisdom is liberated; he personally knows and realizes this in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment. With regard to this conscious body and all external signs, being free from self-views, possession-of-self-views, and the binding tendencies of conceit, that mind is liberated and that wisdom is liberated; he personally knows and realizes this in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment.

"Ānanda, if further a bhikṣu, with regard to this conscious body and all external signs, and so forth, up to personally knowing and realizing it, abiding in its fulfillment: this is called a bhikṣu who has cut off craving, overturned the fetters, and through the complete penetration of conceit, has reached the ultimate end of suffering.

"Ānanda, regarding this I have an additional explanation, in answer to the question of Udaya in the Pārāyaṇa:

> 'Cutting off the perception of desire,
> Sorrow and suffering are also both abandoned.
> Awakening from drowsiness,
> Eliminating the hindrance of restlessness-and-worry,
> Abandoning desire and anger, purified,
> Directly observing the Dharma in the present,
> I declare wisdom-liberation
> Destroys the darkness of ignorance.'"

The Buddha having spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid respects to the Buddha, and departed.

---

### (984)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "I shall now explain how craving is a net, a glue, a spring, a lotus root. These can serve as obstacles for beings, as coverings, as glue, as guards, as concealment, as closure, as blockage, as darkness, as dog's intestines, as tangled grass, as floss. From this world to the next, from the next world to this, going and returning in transmigration, never a time when it does not revolve.

"Bhikṣus, what craving is a net, is glue, and so forth, up to going and coming in transmigration, never a time when it does not revolve? Namely: 'Because there is a self, there is desire for self, it is thus of self, self exists, self does not exist, self is otherwise; I shall be, I shall not be, I shall have desire, it shall be thus of me, I shall be otherwise; or self, or desire of self, or it is thus of self, or self is otherwise; or so, or desire for so, or it is thus so, or otherwise so. Such are the eighteen courses of craving arising from within.'

"Bhikṣus, saying there is a self, regarding all that one possesses, saying desire of self, it is thus of self, and so forth, up to eighteen courses of craving arising from without. Thus in total there are thirty-six courses of craving.

"These thirty-six courses of craving may arise regarding the past, may arise regarding the future, or may arise regarding the present. Thus in total there are one hundred and eight courses of craving."

"This is called craving that is a net, is glue, is a spring, is a lotus root. It can serve as obstacles for beings, as coverings, as glue, as guards, as concealment, as closure, as blockage, as darkness, as dog's intestines, as tangled grass, as floss. From this world to the next, from the next world to this, going and returning in transmigration, never a time when it does not revolve."

The Buddha having spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (985)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "There is love born from love, hatred born from love; love born from hatred, hatred born from hatred.

"How is love born from love? There is a person who regards a being with delight, love, fondness, and approval. Another person acts toward that being with delight, love, fondness, and approval. This person thinks: 'I regard that being with delight, love, fondness, and approval, and that other person also acts toward that being with delight, love, fondness, and approval. Therefore, I develop love toward that other person.' This is called love born from love.

"How is hatred born from love? There is a person who regards a being with delight, love, fondness, and approval. But another person acts toward that being without delight, without love, without fondness, without approval. This person thinks: 'I regard that being with delight, love, fondness, and approval, yet that other person acts toward that being without delight, without love, without fondness, without approval. Therefore, I develop anger toward that other person.' This is called hatred born from love.

"How is love born from hatred? There is a person who regards a being without delight, without love, without fondness, without approval. Another person also acts toward that being without delight, without love, without fondness, without approval. Therefore, that person develops love toward the other. This is called love born from hatred.

"How is hatred born from hatred? There is a person who regards a being without delight, without love, without fondness, without approval. Yet another person acts toward that being with delight, love, fondness, and approval. This person thinks: 'I regard that being without delight, without love, without fondness, without approval, yet that other person acts toward that being with delight, love, fondness, and approval. Therefore, anger arises toward that other person.' This is called hatred born from hatred.

"If a bhikṣu, separated from desires and unwholesome dharmas, with initial thought and sustained thought, and so forth, up to abiding in the fulfillment of the first dhyāna, the second, third, and fourth dhyāna, then love born from love, hatred born from love, love born from hatred, and hatred born from hatred are already cut off and already known. Their roots are cut, like a palmyra tree whose crown is severed, unable to grow again. In the future, such dharmas will not arise.

"If that bhikṣu has exhausted all outflows, with outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, personally knowing and realizing in the present life: 'My births are exhausted, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I myself know there will be no further becoming.' At that time, he does not exalt himself, does not retaliate, does not raise dust, does not blaze, and does not resent others.

"What is exalting oneself? It means seeing form as self, form as different from self, self within form, or form within self. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. This is called exalting oneself. What is not exalting oneself? It means not seeing form as self, form as different from self, self within form, or form within self. Feeling, perception, formations, and consciousness are likewise. This is called not exalting oneself.

"What is retaliating? It means returning abuse for abuse, returning anger for anger, returning blows for blows, returning provocation for provocation. This is called retaliating. What is not retaliating? It means not returning abuse for abuse, not returning anger for anger, not returning blows for blows, not returning provocation for provocation. This is called not retaliating.

"What is raising dust? It means having self, desire for self, and so forth, up to the eighteen courses of craving. This is called raising dust. What is not raising dust? It means having no self, no desire for self, and so forth, up to the eighteen courses of craving not arising. This is called not raising dust.

"What is blazing? It means having mine, desire for mine, and so forth, up to the external eighteen courses of craving. This is called blazing. What is not blazing? It means having no mine, no desire for mine, and so forth, up to no external eighteen courses of craving. This is called not blazing.

"What is resenting others? It means seeing a real self arising, and the conceit of self, desire for self, and the tendency of self not being cut off and not being known. This is called resenting others. What is not resenting others? It means not seeing a real self. The conceit of self, desire for self, and the tendency of self are already cut off

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.
