# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Thirty-Six

---

### (993)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there were senior bhikṣus who attended the Buddha closely, dwelling in dependence upon him. They were: Venerable Ājñāta-Kauṇḍinya, Venerable Mahākāśyapa, Venerable Śāriputra, Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, Venerable Aniruddha, Venerable Śroṇa-Koṭiviṃśa (Twenty Koṭi Ears), Venerable Drāvya-Mallāputra, Venerable Vānaka-Vāsa, Venerable Yaśas-Śālaka-Vihārin, Venerable Pūrṇa, and Venerable Puṇḍa-Danika. These and other senior bhikṣus attended the Buddha closely, dwelling in dependence upon him.

At that time, Venerable Vaṅgīsa was staying in the Eastern Park, the Hall of Migāra's Mother, in Śrāvatī. Venerable Vaṅgīsa had this thought: "Today the World-Honored One is in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, and the senior bhikṣus attend the Buddha closely, dwelling in dependence upon him. I should now go to the World-Honored One's place and speak verses praising each of the senior bhikṣus."

Having had this thought, he went to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and spoke verses saying:

> "The foremost senior bhikṣu
> Has already severed all greedy desires,
> Transcending all the world's
> Accumulations of every kind.
> Of deep wisdom and few words,
> Courageous and diligent in skillful means,
> His path-virtue pure, bright, and manifest;
> I now bow my head in homage.
>
> Having subdued all Māra's enemies,
> Far removed from gatherings,
> Not bound by the five desires,
> Always dwelling in empty, secluded places,
> Pure, unencumbered, with few desires;
> I now bow my head in homage.
>
> Of the supreme Cārāyaṇa lineage,
> In meditative thought, never heedless,
> His inner mind delighting in right concentration,
> Pure and free from dust and defilement,
> With discerning wisdom revealing profound meaning;
> Therefore I bow my head in homage.
>
> The supernatural wisdom he has attained
> Surpasses all supernatural powers.
> Among the assembly of those with the six supernatural powers,
> He is sovereign and fearless.
> Because his supernatural powers are supreme;
> Therefore I bow my head in homage.
>
> Throughout the great trichiliocosm,
> In the five destinies and various courses of birth,
> Even up to the Brahmā world,
> Distinguishing the superior and inferior among gods and humans,
> With purified divine eye he sees all;
> Therefore I bow my head in homage.
>
> Through the power of vigorous effort,
> He severed the accumulation of all craving,
> Destroyed the net of birth and death,
> His mind always delighting in the true Dharma,
> Free from all thoughts of longing and expectation,
> Having crossed over to the other shore,
> Pure and without dust or defilement;
> Therefore I bow my head in homage.
>
> Forever free from all fears,
> Without reliance, free from possessions,
> Content and having crossed over doubts and confusion,
> Having subdued all Māra's enemies,
> With mindfulness of the body, contemplating purity;
> Therefore I bow my head in homage.
>
> Having none of the world's
> Thickets of thorns and brambles of affliction,
> Fetters, bonds, and latent tendencies forever removed,
> The causes and conditions of the three existences severed,
> Refined and purified, all defilements extinguished,
> Ultimately bright and clearly manifest,
> From the forest, departing the forest;
> Therefore I bow my head in homage.
>
> Without any dwelling or shelter to rely upon,
> Illusion, falsity, delusion, and anger extinguished,
> Having tamed all craving and delight,
> Having emerged from all grounds of views,
> Pure and without blemish or defilement;
> Therefore I bow my head in homage.
>
> His mind turns freely and at will,
> Firm and unmoved,
> With the great power of wisdom and virtue,
> What is hard for Māra to subdue, he can subdue,
> Having severed the bond of ignorance;
> Therefore I bow my head in homage.
>
> The great one, free from darkness,
> The tranquil and silent Muni Lord.
> The true Dharma, free from dust and faults,
> His radiance shining forth of itself,
> Illuminating all the worlds;
> Therefore he is called the Buddha.
>
> The earth gods, the gods of space,
> And the sons of the Thirty-Three Heavens,
> His radiance outshines them all;
> Therefore he is called the Buddha.
>
> He has crossed the boundary of birth-and-death existence,
> Surpassing and transcending the multitudes,
> Gentle and well-tamed,
> The Fully Awakened One, the foremost in awakening.
>
> Severing all fetters and bonds,
> Subduing all heterodox paths,
> Vanquishing all of Māra's enemies,
> Attaining unsurpassed, perfect awakening.
> Free from dust, extinguishing all defilements;
> Therefore I bow my head in homage."

When Venerable Vaṅgīsa had finished praising with verses, the bhikṣus who heard what he said all greatly rejoiced.

---

### (994)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Venerable Vaṅgīsa was staying in the Eastern Park, the Hall of Migāra's Mother, in Śrāvatī, severely afflicted with illness. Venerable Pulinī served as his attendant in illness, providing care and offerings.

At that time, Venerable Vaṅgīsa said to Venerable Pulinī: "Go to the World-Honored One's place and convey my words to the World-Honored One, saying: 'Venerable Vaṅgīsa bows his head at the World-Honored One's feet, and inquires whether the World-Honored One has few illnesses, few troubles, moves about with ease, and abides in comfort and peace.' Further say this: 'Venerable Vaṅgīsa is staying in the Eastern Park, the Hall of Migāra's Mother, severely afflicted with illness, and wishes to see the World-Honored One, but lacks the strength and means to go to the World-Honored One. Excellent, World-Honored One! May you come to the Eastern Park, the Hall of Migāra's Mother, to Venerable Vaṅgīsa's place, out of compassion.'"

Then Venerable Pulinī accepted his words and went to the World-Honored One's place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. He spoke thus: "Venerable Vaṅgīsa is staying in the Eastern Park, the Hall of Migāra's Mother, severely afflicted with illness. He wishes to see the World-Honored One but lacks the strength and means to come and pay his respects. Excellent, World-Honored One! May you go to the Eastern Park, the Hall of Migāra's Mother, to Venerable Vaṅgīsa's place, out of compassion."

At that time, the World-Honored One silently consented.

Then Venerable Pulinī, knowing the Buddha had consented, rose from his seat, bowed at the Buddha's feet, and departed.

The World-Honored One, in the late afternoon, rose from meditation and went to Venerable Vaṅgīsa's place. Venerable Vaṅgīsa saw the World-Honored One from afar and, leaning on his bed, tried to rise.

At that time, the World-Honored One, seeing Venerable Vaṅgīsa leaning on his bed trying to rise, said: "Vaṅgīsa! Do not trouble yourself to move." The World-Honored One then sat down and asked Venerable Vaṅgīsa: "As for the suffering of your illness, is it bearable and endurable? Are the bodily pains increasing or decreasing?"

As was broadly stated in the previous Yamaka Sūtra, up to: "My suffering and illness continue to increase; I do not perceive any decrease."

The Buddha told Vaṅgīsa: "I will now question you; answer as you wish. Have you attained a mind that is undefiled, unattached, unpolluted, liberated, and free from all inversions?"

Vaṅgīsa addressed the Buddha: "My mind is undefiled, unattached, unpolluted, liberated, and free from all inversions."

The Buddha told Vaṅgīsa: "How have you attained a mind that is undefiled, unattached, unpolluted, liberated, and free from all inversions?"

Vaṅgīsa addressed the Buddha: "In the past, regarding eye-consciousness and forms, my mind does not look back with longing. Regarding future forms, I do not delight in imagining them. Regarding present forms, I am not attached. My past, future, and present eye-consciousness regarding forms: the craving, desire, love, delight, and longing for them has been exhausted. I am without desire; it is extinguished, ceased, stilled, departed, liberated. My mind being liberated, therefore it is undefiled, unattached, unpolluted, free from all inversions, and I abide in right concentration. Likewise with ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind-consciousness: regarding past dharmas, my mind does not look back with longing; regarding future dharmas, I do not delight in them; regarding present dharmas, I am not attached. Regarding past, future, and present dharmas, the longing, desire, and craving have been exhausted. I am without desire; it is extinguished, ceased, stilled, departed, liberated. My mind being liberated, therefore it is undefiled, unattached, unpolluted, liberated, and free from all inversions, and I abide in right concentration. I only wish that the World-Honored One today, as a final benefit to me, would allow me to speak verses."

The Buddha told Vaṅgīsa: "You should know this is the proper time."

Venerable Vaṅgīsa rose, sat upright with proper posture, focused his mindfulness before him, and spoke verses saying:

> "I now dwell before the Buddha,
> Bowing my head in reverent homage.
> Regarding all dharmas,
> I have attained complete liberation.
>
> Well understanding the characteristics of all dharmas,
> With deep faith delighting in the true Dharma,
> The World-Honored One, Perfectly Awakened One,
> The World-Honored One is the great teacher.
>
> The World-Honored One vanquished the Māra enemy,
> The World-Honored One, the great Muni,
> Extinguished all latent tendencies,
> Having himself crossed over, he saves living beings.
>
> The World-Honored One in the world
> Has fully awakened to all dharmas.
> In all the world there is none
> Who knows the Dharma surpassing the Buddha.
>
> Among all gods and humans,
> There is also none equal to the Buddha.
> Therefore I today
> Bow my head to the greatly vigorous one.
>
> I bow my head to the foremost among men,
> Who has plucked out the thorns of craving and desire.
> This is now my last time
> To see the World-Honored One.
>
> I bow my head to the Lord of the Solar Lineage;
> This evening I shall enter parinirvāṇa.
> With right knowledge, binding right mindfulness
> To this decaying body,
>
> What arises from residual forces,
> From tonight shall forever cease.
> No longer defiled by the three realms,
> I enter nirvāṇa without remainder.
>
> Painful feelings, pleasant feelings,
> And feelings neither painful nor pleasant,
> Born from the conditions of contact:
> All are now forever severed.
>
> Painful feelings, pleasant feelings,
> And feelings neither painful nor pleasant,
> Born from the conditions of contact:
> All are now fully known.
>
> Whether internal or external,
> Painful, pleasant, and other feelings,
> Toward feelings having no attachment,
> With right knowledge, rightly binding the mind.
>
> Regarding initial, middle, and final
> Aggregates, without obstruction;
> The aggregates having been severed,
> One knows feelings without remainder.
>
> The one who clearly sees reality
> Speaks of ninety-one kalpas.
> In three kalpas there was no emptiness;
> Great sages and honored ones appeared.
>
> The rest were empty, without refuge,
> Only fearful and terrifying kalpas.
> Know that the great sage
> Again appeared in the world,
>
> Comforting gods and humans,
> Opening their eyes, freeing them from the darkness of dust,
> Awakening all sentient beings,
> Causing them to realize all suffering.
>
> Suffering upon suffering, and the arising of suffering,
> The tranquil cessation that transcends suffering,
> The Noble Eightfold Path,
> Peacefully leading to nirvāṇa.
>
> What is difficult to obtain in the world
> Has all been presently obtained:
> Being born in the world, obtaining a human body,
> And the exposition of the true Dharma.
>
> According to one's own wish,
> Free from defilement, seeking purity,
> Devote yourself to cultivating your own welfare;
> Do not let it be empty and fruitless.
>
> If one passes time in vain, sorrow arises,
> Bordering on the sufferings of hell.
> Regarding the true Dharma that has been spoken,
> Those who do not delight in it or wish to receive it
>
> Shall long abide in birth and death,
> With saṃsāra ceaselessly revolving.
> Through the long night they harbor grief and affliction,
> Like merchants who have lost their wealth.
>
> For me now, all blessings are gathered;
> No more birth, old age, or death.
> Saṃsāra has been completely severed;
> I shall not again take rebirth.
>
> The rivers of craving and consciousness
> Are now completely dried up.
> Having uprooted the roots of the aggregates,
> The chain of links no longer continues.
>
> Offerings to the great teacher are complete;
> What was to be done has been done.
> Heavy burdens have all been set down;
> The streams of existence have all been severed.
>
> I no longer delight in taking rebirth,
> Nor is there any death to dread.
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness bound,
> I merely await the time of the end.
>
> Think of the wild dragon-elephant,
> Sixty years a fierce and mighty beast,
> Who one day is freed from yoke and chain,
> And joyfully roams the mountain forests.
>
> Vaṅgīsa is likewise:
> A son born from the great teacher's mouth,
> Weary and departing from the assembly,
> With right mindfulness awaiting the time.
>
> Now I announce to all of you,
> Those who have come and gathered here:
> Hear my final verse,
> Whose meaning is for your benefit.
>
> All that is born returns to cessation;
> All conditioned things are impermanent.
> They are dharmas of swift arising and swift death;
> How can one long rely upon them?
>
> Therefore, strengthen your resolve,
> With vigorous effort and skillful means seek.
> Contemplate the fearfulness of existence,
> And follow the path of the Muni.
>
> Swiftly exhaust this mass of suffering;
> Do not further increase the turning of the wheel."
>
> The son born from the Buddha's mouth,
> Having praised and spoken these verses,
>
> Bade a long farewell to the great assembly.
> Vaṅgīsa entered nirvāṇa.
> Out of compassion and kindness,
> He spoke these supreme verses.
>
> Venerable Vaṅgīsa,
> A son born from the Tathāgata's Dharma,
> With a mind inclined to compassion,
> Spoke these supreme verses,
> And afterward entered parinirvāṇa.
> All should pay reverent homage.

---

### (995)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "A forest-dwelling bhikṣu
> Staying in an empty, secluded place,
> Tranquilly cultivating the holy life,
> Eating only one meal at a single sitting:
> For what reason
> Is his complexion especially bright and fresh?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "He has no sorrow about the past,
> No longing delight for the future.
> In the present, whatever he receives,
> He maintains with right knowledge and mindful awareness.
> Because he is mindful during meals,
> His complexion is always fresh and lustrous.
> Those whose minds race toward the future in imagination,
> Or who pursue sorrow and regret over the past,
> Foolishly burn themselves in their own fire,
> Like hail that cuts down living grass."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (996)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse:

> "If one does not strive to subdue arrogance,
> Does not well tame one's own mind,
> Has never cultivated tranquil silence,
> Nor entered right concentration,
> Dwelling in the forest yet being heedless,
> One cannot cross to the other shore of death."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Having already departed from arrogance,
> The mind constantly enters right concentration.
> With bright wisdom, skillfully discerning,
> Liberated from all bonds,
> Alone dwelling in secluded forests,
> The mind not being heedless,
> Regarding that enemy, the Māra of death,
> One swiftly crosses to the other shore."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (997)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "How can one's merit
> Constantly increase day and night?
> How does one attain rebirth in heaven?
> We pray you will explain."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "By planting gardens and orchards,
> Providing the cool shade of forest trees,
> By building bridges and boats for crossing,
> By constructing halls of merit,
> By digging wells to supply the thirsty,
> By providing guest houses for travelers:
> Such meritorious deeds as these
> Constantly increase day and night.
> If one is complete in Dharma and morality,
> By these conditions one attains rebirth in heaven."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (998)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "Giving what obtains great strength?
> Giving what obtains fine appearance?
> Giving what obtains ease and happiness?
> Giving what obtains clear eyesight?
> What kind of giving is cultivated
> That is called 'all-encompassing giving'?
> I now ask the World-Honored One;
> May you explain and distinguish."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Giving food obtains great strength.
> Giving clothing obtains fine appearance.
> Giving vehicles obtains ease and happiness.
> Giving lamps obtains clear eyesight.
> An open guesthouse to receive visitors:
> This is called all-encompassing giving.
> To instruct others with the Dharma:
> This is the gift of the deathless."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (999)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being named Suvīra, of surpassingly wonderful appearance, who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse:

> "All gods and humans in the world
> All find delight in food.
> Is there anyone in the world
> Whom blessings and happiness constantly follow?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "One who gives with a pure and faithful heart,
> In this world and in the next,
> Wherever that person goes,
> The reward of merit always follows like a shadow.
> Therefore one should abandon miserliness
> And practice immaculate generosity.
> Having given, the heart rejoices;
> In this world and the next one receives the fruit."

At that time, the heavenly being Suvīra addressed the Buddha: "Marvelous, World-Honored One! Well have you spoken this meaning:

> "One who gives with a pure and faithful heart,
> In this world and in the next,
> Wherever that person goes,
> The reward of merit always follows like a shadow.
> Therefore one should abandon miserliness
> And practice immaculate generosity.
> Having given, the heart rejoices;
> In this world and the next one receives the fruit."

The heavenly being Suvīra addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I myself know that in a past life I was a king named Suvīra. At the four city gates I broadly practiced giving as meritorious deeds. Within the city, at the four crossroads, I also there distributed gifts and performed meritorious deeds.

"At that time, the chief queen came and said to me: 'Great king, you perform great meritorious deeds, but I have no means to cultivate meritorious works.' I then told her: 'The giving and meritorious deeds performed outside the eastern city gate shall all belong to you.' Then the princes came and addressed me: 'Great king, you perform many meritorious deeds, and the queen shares in them as well, but we have no means to perform meritorious works. We now wish to rely on the great king to perform some small amount of merit.' I then answered: 'The giving and meritorious deeds performed outside the southern city gate shall all belong to you.' Then the great ministers came and addressed me: 'Today the great king performs many meritorious deeds, and the queen and princes all share in them, but we have no means to perform meritorious works. We wish to rely on the great king to have some small share in the doing.' I then told them: 'The giving and meritorious deeds performed outside the western city gate shall all belong to you.' Then the generals and soldiers came and addressed me: 'Today the great king performs many meritorious deeds, and the queen, crown prince, and great ministers all share in them. Only we lack the means to cultivate meritorious works. We wish to rely on the great king to have some share in the doing.' I then answered: 'The giving and meritorious deeds performed outside the northern city gate shall all belong to you.' The common citizens of the kingdom also came and addressed me: 'Today the great king performs many meritorious deeds, and the queen, princes, great ministers, and generals all share in them. Only we lack the means to cultivate merit. We wish to rely on the great king to have some small share in the doing.' I then answered: 'At the four crossroads within the city, the giving and meritorious deeds performed there shall all belong to you.' At that time, the king's queen, princes, great ministers, generals and soldiers, and common citizens all practiced generosity and performed meritorious deeds. The giving and meritorious deeds I had previously done thereupon ceased.

"At that time, the servants I had assigned to perform meritorious deeds returned to my presence, paid their respects, and addressed me: 'May the great king know that at all the places where merit was being cultivated, the queen, princes, great ministers, generals and soldiers, and common citizens each occupy their respective places, practicing giving and performing merit. The great king's own giving has thereupon ceased.' I then answered them: 'Good men! The taxes and goods from the border regions that should be remitted to me: divide half to be sent to the treasury, and the other half shall remain in those places for generous giving and meritorious deeds.' Having heard the command, they went to the border regions, collected the goods, sent half to the treasury, and kept half there for generous giving and meritorious deeds.

"Through the long night, practicing generosity and performing meritorious deeds in this way, through the long night I constantly obtained lovable, memorable, and pleasing rewards of merit, constantly receiving happiness without end. Because of these meritorious works and the fruits and rewards of merit, all are counted within the great aggregate of merit. It is like the five great rivers merging into one stream: the Ganges, Yamunā, Sarāyu, Irāvatī, and Mahī. When these five rivers merge into one stream, no one can measure the water in hundreds, thousands, millions, or billions of measures. That great river attains to the measure of a great water aggregate. I am likewise: the fruits of my meritorious deeds and the rewards of merit cannot be measured, and all are counted within the great aggregate of merit."

At that time, the heavenly being Suvīra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1000)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "What sort of person can be
> A good companion for traveling afar?
> What sort of person can be
> A good companion at home?
> What sort of person can be
> A good companion in sharing wealth?
> What sort of person can be
> A good companion for the next life?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "A caravan leader
> Is a good companion for traveling.
> A chaste, kind, and virtuous wife
> Is a good companion at home.
> Kinsmen who are close and familiar
> Are good companions in sharing wealth.
> The merits one has cultivated oneself
> Are good companions for the next life."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1001)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse:

> "In darkness, life is carried away;
> Thus people are made short-lived.
> Oppressed by the invasion of old age,
> There is no one who can rescue or protect them.
> Seeing this: old age, illness, and death,
> Causes great fear in people.
> Only by performing meritorious deeds
> Can one happily dwell in the place of highest happiness."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "In darkness, life is carried away;
> Thus people are made short-lived.
> Oppressed by the invasion of old age,
> There is no one who can rescue or protect them.
> Contemplating this surplus of faults,
> Which causes great fear in people,
> One should sever worldly craving and desire,
> And find happiness in nirvāṇa without remainder."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1002)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "How many dharmas should be severed?
> How many dharmas should be abandoned?
> And how many dharmas
> Should be cultivated with superior effort?
> How many aggregates should be transcended
> For a bhikṣu to cross the swift current?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Sever five, abandon five,
> Increasingly cultivate the five faculties,
> Transcend the five associations:
> A bhikṣu crosses the flood and abyss."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1003)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "How many are asleep among the awake?
> How many are awake among the asleep?
> How many take on dust and defilement?
> How many attain purity?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Five are asleep among the awake.
> Five are awake among the asleep.
> Five take on defilement.
> Five attain purity."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1004)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse:

> "A mother and child delight in each other;
> A cattle owner delights in his cattle.
> Beings delight in having attachments;
> None delight in being without attachments."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "A mother and child grieve for each other;
> A cattle owner grieves for his cattle.
> Those with attachments grieve;
> Those without attachments have no grief."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1005)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "What belongs to people?
> What is the foremost companion?
> By what do they sustain life?
> Upon what do beings depend?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Fields and dwellings belong to beings.
> A virtuous wife is the foremost companion.
> By food and drink life is sustained.
> Karma is what beings depend upon."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1006)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse:

> "No love surpasses love for a son.
> No wealth is more precious than cattle.
> No light surpasses the sun.
> No lake surpasses the ocean."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "No love surpasses love for oneself.
> No wealth surpasses grain.
> No light surpasses wisdom.
> No lake surpasses right view."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1007)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet in homage. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse:

> "Among two-footed beings, kṣatriyas are honored.
> Among four-footed, cattle are supreme.
> A young beauty is the finest wife.
> A son of noble birth is the finest son."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Among two-footed beings, the Perfectly Awakened One is honored.
> Among four-footed, a thoroughbred horse is supreme.
> A wife who follows her husband is a worthy wife.
> One whose outflows are exhausted is the finest of sons."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1008)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse:

> "Among beings arising from the earth,
> What is the most excellent?
> Among those falling from the sky,
> What again is the most excellent?
> Wherever one makes supplications,
> What is the foremost?
> Among all forms of speech,
> What is the most excellent discourse?"

At that time, there was a heavenly being who had formerly been a farmer's son and had now been reborn in heaven. Due to his former habits, he immediately spoke a verse answering that heavenly being:

> "The five grains arise from the earth;
> These then are the most excellent.
> Seeds from the sky
> Falling to earth are the most excellent.
> Cattle serve and nourish people;
> These then are the most excellent to rely upon.
> A beloved child who speaks:
> This then is the most excellent among words."

The questioning heavenly being said to the one who answered: "I was not asking you. Why do you speak so much with frivolous haste and reckless talk?" Then he himself spoke a verse asking the World-Honored One:

> "Among beings arising from the earth,
> What is the most excellent?
> Among those falling to the ground from the sky,
> What again is the most excellent?
> Wherever one makes supplications,
> What is the foremost?
> Among all forms of speech,
> What is the most excellent discourse?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Among those springing up from below,
> Those with the three knowledges are the most excellent.
> Among those streaming down from the sky,
> Those with the three knowledges are also foremost.
> The Sangha of noble disciples
> Is the most excellent among those one relies upon as a teacher.
> What the Tathāgata has spoken
> Is the most excellent discourse among all discourses."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "How many dharmas arise in the world?
> How many dharmas are agreeable and harmonious?
> How many dharmas does the world grasp with craving?
> How many dharmas diminish the world?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Six dharmas equally arise in the world.
> Six dharmas are agreeable and harmonious.
> Six dharmas does the world grasp with craving.
> Six dharmas diminish the world."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1009)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "Who carries the world away?
> Who constrains and leads the world?
> What single dharma
> Controls and governs the world?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "The mind carries the world away.
> The mind constrains and leads the world.
> The mind is the single dharma
> That controls and governs the world."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1010)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "Who binds the world?
> Who, taming it, causes liberation?
> By severing what dharma
> Is it said one attains nirvāṇa?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Desire binds the world.
> Taming desire brings liberation.
> By severing craving and desire,
> It is said one attains nirvāṇa."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1011)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "Who covers the world?
> Who envelops and entangles the world?
> Who binds and fetters living beings?
> Where is the world established?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Decline and old age cover the world.
> Death envelops and entangles the world.
> Craving binds and fetters living beings.
> Dharma establishes the world."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1012)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "Who conceals the world?
> Who binds the world?
> Who recollects living beings?
> Who raises the banner of living beings?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Ignorance conceals the world.
> The bond of craving fetters living beings.
> Concealment and recollection attend to living beings.
> Conceit is the banner of living beings."

Then that heavenly being again spoke a verse and asked the Buddha:

> "Who is without concealment?
> Who again is without the bond of craving?
> Who has emerged from concealment?
> Who does not raise the banner of conceit?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "The Tathāgata, Perfectly Awakened One,
> With right knowledge and mind liberated,
> Is not concealed by ignorance,
> Nor bound by the bond of craving.
> He has transcended concealment,
> And demolished the banner of conceit."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1013)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "What is the most excellent wealth
> A person can possess?
> How does one skillfully cultivate
> So as to bring about ease and happiness?
> Among all flavors,
> What is the most excellent flavor?
> Among all beings,
> What is the foremost life?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "A heart of pure faith and joy
> Is called the most excellent wealth of a person.
> Practicing the true Dharma
> Can bring about the fruit of ease and happiness.
> The wondrous teaching of truth
> Is the most excellent among flavors.
> The life of noble wisdom
> Is the foremost among lives."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1014)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "What for a bhikṣu
> Is a second self, a companion?
> What for a bhikṣu
> Is a guide who gives instruction?
> In what place does a bhikṣu
> Let his mind roam and delight?
> Having delighted in that place,
> Can he sever all fetters and bonds?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Faith is a second self, a companion.
> Wisdom is the one who gives instruction.
> Nirvāṇa is the place of joy and delight;
> There a bhikṣu severs fetters and bonds."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1015)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "What ages well?
> What is well established?
> What is a person's treasure?
> What can thieves not steal?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Right morality ages well.
> Pure faith is well established.
> Wisdom is a person's treasure.
> Merit, thieves cannot steal."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1016)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "What dharma gives birth to living beings?
> What runs before them, driving them on?
> How does the cycle of birth and death arise?
> What is it from which there is no liberation?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Craving and desire give birth to living beings.
> The mind runs before them, driving them on.
> Living beings undergo the cycle of birth and death;
> From suffering there is no liberation."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1017)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "What dharma gives birth to living beings?
> What runs before them, driving them on?
> How does the cycle of birth and death arise?
> What dharma can be relied upon?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Craving and desire give birth to living beings.
> The mind runs before them, driving them on.
> Living beings undergo the cycle of birth and death;
> Karma is what can be relied upon."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1018)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "What dharma gives birth to living beings?
> What runs before them, driving them on?
> How does the cycle of birth and death arise?
> What dharma is most to be feared?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Craving and desire give birth to living beings.
> The mind runs before them, driving them on.
> Living beings undergo the cycle of birth and death;
> Karma is what is most to be feared."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1019)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "What is called the wrong path?
> What passes away day and night?
> What defiles the holy life?
> What encumbers the world?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Greed and desire are called the wrong path.
> Life passes away day and night.
> Women are the defilement of the holy life.
> Women encumber the world.
> Ardently cultivating the holy life,
> One has washed away all impure waters."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1020)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "What dharma outshines the world?
> What dharma is unsurpassed?
> What single dharma
> Universally controls and governs all beings?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Name outshines the world.
> Name is unsurpassed in the world.
> There is only the single dharma of name
> That can control and govern the world."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1021)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "What dharma is the cause of verse?
> By what is verse adorned?
> Upon what does verse depend?
> What is the substance of verse?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Desire is the cause of verse.
> Letters adorn the verse.
> Name is what verse depends upon.
> Composition is the substance of verse."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.

---

### (1022)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvatī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a heavenly being of surpassingly wonderful appearance who, in the latter part of the night, came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that heavenly being spoke a verse questioning the Buddha:

> "How does one know a chariot?
> How does one know a fire?
> How does one know a kingdom?
> How does one know a wife?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Seeing a banner and canopy, one knows a chariot.
> Seeing smoke, one knows a fire.
> Seeing a king, one knows a kingdom.
> Seeing a husband, one knows his wife."

Then that heavenly being spoke another verse:

> "Long have I seen this brāhmaṇa
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears are now past;
> He has forever transcended worldly affection."

That heavenly being, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and immediately vanished.
