# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Thirty-Seven

---

### (1023)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, Venerable Phagguna was staying at the Eastern Park, in the Hall of Migāra's Mother, gravely ill and in critical condition.

Venerable Ānanda went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, Venerable Phagguna is staying at the Eastern Park, in the Hall of Migāra's Mother, gravely ill and in critical condition. Bhikṣus with such illness often die from it. It would be excellent, World-Honored One, if you would go to the Eastern Park, to the Hall of Migāra's Mother, to where Venerable Phagguna is, out of compassion!"

At that time, the World-Honored One silently consented. In the afternoon, emerging from meditation, he went to the Eastern Park, the Hall of Migāra's Mother, and arrived at Venerable Phagguna's room. A seat was spread, and he sat down. He gave Venerable Phagguna various teachings on the Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and delighted him, he rose from his seat and departed.

After the World-Honored One had left, Venerable Phagguna soon passed away. At the time of his passing, his faculties were joyful, his countenance was clear and pure, and his skin was bright and white.

Then Venerable Ānanda, having made offerings to the relics of Venerable Phagguna, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, after the World-Honored One came, Venerable Phagguna soon passed away. At the time of his passing, his faculties were joyful, his skin was clear and pure, bright, white, and radiant. May I ask the World-Honored One, in what destination was he reborn? What kind of birth did he receive? What of his future life?"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "If a bhikṣu, before falling ill, has not yet cut off the five lower fetters, and then when he becomes aware of illness arising, his body suffers pain, his mind is unsettled, and his vital force grows weak, and he gets to hear the Great Teacher's instruction, admonition, and various teachings on the Dharma, then having heard this Dharma, he cuts off the five lower fetters. Ānanda, this is the benefit of the Great Teacher's instruction and establishment of Dharma teaching.

"Furthermore, Ānanda, if a bhikṣu, before falling ill, has not yet cut off the five lower fetters, and then illness arises, his body suffers pain, and his vital force grows ever weaker, and he does not receive the Great Teacher's instruction, admonition, and Dharma teaching, but encounters other learned and virtuous ones who practice the holy life, who instruct, admonish, and teach the Dharma, and having heard the Dharma, he cuts off the five lower fetters. Ānanda, this is called the benefit of instruction, admonition, and hearing the Dharma.

"Furthermore, Ānanda, if a bhikṣu, before falling ill, has not cut off the five lower fetters, and so forth until his vital force grows weak, and he does not hear the Great Teacher's instruction, admonition, and Dharma teaching, nor does he hear other learned and virtuous practitioners of the holy life give instruction, admonition, and Dharma teaching, but he takes the Dharma he previously received and, in solitary stillness, reflects upon it, weighs it, and contemplates it, and thereby cuts off the five lower fetters. Ānanda, this is called the benefit obtained from reflecting upon and contemplating the previously heard Dharma.

"Furthermore, Ānanda, if a bhikṣu, before falling ill, has already cut off the five lower fetters but has not attained the supreme liberation through the exhaustion of craving, has not ended the āsravas, and has not attained the liberation of mind through freedom from desire, and then he falls ill, his body suffers pain, and his vital force grows weak, and he gets to hear the Great Teacher's instruction, admonition, and Dharma teaching, and thereby attains the supreme liberation through the exhaustion of craving, ends the āsravas, and attains liberation through freedom from desire. Ānanda, this is called the benefit of the Great Teacher's Dharma teaching.

"Furthermore, Ānanda, if a bhikṣu, before falling ill, has already cut off the five lower fetters but has not attained the supreme liberation through the exhaustion of craving, has not ended the āsravas, and has not attained liberation through freedom from desire, and then he becomes aware of illness arising in his body, suffering extreme pain, and he does not receive the Great Teacher's instruction, admonition, and Dharma teaching, but he receives from other learned and virtuous practitioners of the holy life their instruction, admonition, and Dharma teaching, and thereby attains the supreme liberation through the exhaustion of craving, ends the āsravas, and attains liberation through freedom from desire. Ānanda, this is called the benefit of instruction, admonition, and hearing the Dharma.

"Furthermore, Ānanda, if a bhikṣu, before falling ill, has already cut off the five lower fetters but has not attained the supreme liberation through the exhaustion of craving, has not ended the āsravas, and has not attained liberation through freedom from desire, and then illness arises in his body, producing extreme suffering, and he does not receive the Great Teacher's instruction, admonition, and Dharma teaching, nor does he receive from other learned and virtuous ones their instruction, admonition, and Dharma teaching, but he takes the Dharma he previously heard and, in a solitary, quiet place, reflects upon it, weighs it, and contemplates it, and thereby attains the supreme liberation through the exhaustion of craving, ends the āsravas, and attains liberation through freedom from desire. Ānanda, this is called the benefit obtained from reflecting on the previously heard Dharma.

"How could Bhikṣu Phagguna not have his faculties joyful, his countenance clear and pure, and his skin bright and radiant? Bhikṣu Phagguna, before falling ill, had not yet cut off the five lower fetters. He personally heard from the Great Teacher instruction, admonition, and Dharma teaching, and thereby cut off the five lower fetters." The World-Honored One declared for Venerable Phagguna the prediction of the fruit of non-returner (anāgāmin).

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Venerable Ānanda, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was delighted, bowed, and departed.

---

### (1024)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Venerable Aśvajit was staying at the Eastern Park, in the Hall of Migāra's Mother, afflicted with a grave illness causing extreme suffering. Venerable Pūrṇika was attending to and caring for him, as described previously in the Vakkali Sūtra in full, namely the teaching on the three feelings, up to "it only increases without abating."

The Buddha told Aśvajit: "Do not have regret!"

Aśvajit said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I truly do have regret."

The Buddha told Aśvajit: "You have not broken the precepts, have you?"

Aśvajit said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I have not broken the precepts."

The Buddha told Aśvajit: "If you have not broken the precepts, why do you have regret?"

Aśvajit said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, before I was ill, I had attained the pleasant abiding of bodily tranquility in right attainment and had cultivated it extensively. Now today I can no longer enter that samādhi. I reflect thus: 'Could it be that I have regressed from this samādhi?'"

The Buddha told Aśvajit: "I will now question you; answer as you wish. Aśvajit, do you see form as self, as other than self, or as mutually inhering?"

Aśvajit said to the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One."

He further asked: "Do you see feeling, perception, volitional formations, or consciousness as self, as other than self, or as mutually inhering?"

Aśvajit said to the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Aśvajit: "Since you do not see form as self, as other than self, or as mutually inhering, and do not see feeling, perception, volitional formations, or consciousness as self, as other than self, or as mutually inhering, why do you have regret?"

Aśvajit said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, because of improper attention."

The Buddha told Aśvajit: "If a śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa has firm samādhi and balanced samādhi, and yet cannot enter that samādhi, he should not think: 'I have regressed from samādhi.' Furthermore, if a noble disciple does not see form as self, as other than self, or as mutually inhering, and does not see feeling, perception, volitional formations, or consciousness as self, as other than self, or as mutually inhering, he should simply generate this awareness: 'Desire is forever exhausted without remainder; hatred and delusion are forever exhausted without remainder.' When desire, hatred, and delusion are forever exhausted without remainder, all āsravas are ended, and with the āsrava-free liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, one directly knows and realizes in the present life: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that there is no further existence.'"

When the Buddha spoke this Dharma, Venerable Aśvajit's āsravas did not arise, and his mind attained liberation. He was joyful and elated. Because of his joy and elation, his bodily illness was immediately cured.

Having spoken this sūtra and having caused Venerable Aśvajit to rejoice and be delighted, the Buddha rose from his seat and departed.

The Chama Sūtra is as described in the section on the five aggregates of clinging.

---

### (1025)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, there was a certain bhikṣu who was young and newly ordained, having gone forth in this Dharma and Vinaya not long before. He had few acquaintances, was a solitary traveler, had no one to attend to him, and was staying in a visitors' monks' lodging in a remote village, gravely ill and in critical condition.

Then a number of bhikṣus went to the Buddha, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, there is a bhikṣu who is young and newly ordained, and so forth, who is gravely ill and in critical condition, staying in a visitors' monks' lodging in a remote village. Bhikṣus with such illness often die without surviving. It would be excellent, World-Honored One, if you would go to that place, out of compassion!"

At that time, the World-Honored One silently consented. That very afternoon, emerging from meditation, he went to that dwelling place.

The sick bhikṣu, seeing the World-Honored One from afar, supported himself on his bed and tried to rise.

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Rest and lie down; do not rise. How is it, bhikṣu? Can you endure the pain and suffering?" As previously described in the Chama Sūtra in full, including the three feelings, up to "the illness only increases without abating."

The Buddha told the sick bhikṣu: "I will now question you; answer as you wish. Do you have any regret?"

The sick bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "I truly do have regret, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the sick bhikṣu: "You have not violated the precepts, have you?"

The sick bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I truly have not violated the precepts."

The Buddha told the sick bhikṣu: "If you have not violated the precepts, why do you have regret?"

The sick bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I am young and went forth not long ago. Regarding the superhuman Dharma and the superior, wondrous knowing and seeing, I have not yet attained anything. I think thus: 'At the time of my death, I do not know where I will be reborn.' Therefore I feel regret."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "I will now question you; answer as you wish. How is it, bhikṣu? Is it because there is an eye that there is eye-consciousness?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One."

He further asked: "What do you think, bhikṣu? Is it because there is eye-consciousness that there is eye-contact, and because of the conditions of eye-contact there arises an internal feeling, whether painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One." The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind were also spoken of in the same way.

"How is it, bhikṣu? If there were no eye, would there be no eye-consciousness?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One."

He further asked: "Bhikṣu, if there were no eye-consciousness, would there be no eye-contact? If there were no eye-contact, would there be no internal feeling born from the conditions of eye-contact, whether painful, pleasant, or neither painful nor pleasant?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One." The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind were also spoken of in the same way.

"Therefore, bhikṣu, you should well reflect on such dharmas, and you will attain a good death, and the afterlife will also be good." At that time, the World-Honored One gave various Dharma teachings to the sick bhikṣu, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him, and then rose from his seat and departed.

The sick bhikṣu, after the World-Honored One had left, soon passed away. At the time of his passing, his faculties were joyful, his countenance was clear and pure, and his skin was bright and white.

Then a number of bhikṣus went to the Buddha, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, that young bhikṣu who was gravely ill and in critical condition has now passed away. At the time of his passing, his faculties were joyful, his countenance was clear and pure, and his skin was bright and white. How is it, World-Honored One? Where was this bhikṣu reborn? What kind of birth did he receive? What of his future life?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "That bhikṣu who has passed away was a true treasure. Having heard my teaching of the Dharma, he clearly understood and comprehended. He was fearless regarding the Dharma and attained parinirvāṇa. You should simply make offerings to his relics."

At that time, the World-Honored One declared the foremost prediction for that bhikṣu.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1026)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, as described above. The difference is as follows: "Listen carefully and reflect well; I will explain it for you. If a bhikṣu thinks thus: 'Regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, there is no view of self or of what belongs to self, no conceit-based attachment or underlying tendency, and there is liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, directly knowing and realizing in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment. Regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, there is no view of self or of what belongs to self, no conceit-based attachment or underlying tendency, and there is liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, directly knowing and realizing in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment.' That bhikṣu thinks: 'Regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, there is no view of self or of what belongs to self, no conceit-based attachment or underlying tendency, and there is liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, directly knowing and realizing in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment. Regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, there is no view of self or of what belongs to self, no conceit-based attachment or underlying tendency, and there is liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, directly knowing and realizing in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment.' If that bhikṣu, regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, is free from the view of self or of what belongs to self, free from conceit-based attachment or underlying tendency, and has liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, directly knowing and realizing in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment; regarding this consciousness-body and all external signs, is free from the view of self or of what belongs to self, free from conceit-based attachment or underlying tendency, and has liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, directly knowing and realizing in the present life, abiding in its fulfillment; this is called a bhikṣu who has cut off craving, turned away from all fetters, stopped conceit through thorough penetration, and reached the complete end of suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1027)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, as described above. The difference is as follows, up to the Buddha telling the sick bhikṣu: "You have not yourself violated the precepts, have you?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, it is not for the sake of maintaining pure precepts that I practice the holy life under the World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "For the sake of what Dharma do you practice the holy life under me?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "It is for the sake of abandoning desire that I practice the holy life under the World-Honored One. It is for the sake of abandoning hatred and delusion that I practice the holy life under the World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "So it is, so it is. You are right that it should be for the sake of abandoning desire that you practice the holy life under me, and for the sake of abandoning hatred and delusion that you practice the holy life under me. Bhikṣu, when one is entangled by desire, one cannot attain freedom from desire. When one is entangled by ignorance, wisdom is not purified. Therefore, bhikṣu, through freedom from desire, there is liberation of mind; through freedom from ignorance, there is liberation by wisdom. If a bhikṣu, through freedom from desire, realizes liberation of mind in his own person, and through freedom from ignorance, attains liberation by wisdom, this is called a bhikṣu who has cut off all cravings, turned away from fetters and bonds, stopped conceit through thorough penetration, and reached the complete end of suffering. Therefore, bhikṣu, regarding this Dharma you should reflect well." As described previously in full, up to "receiving the foremost prediction."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and were delighted, bowed, and departed.

---

### (1028)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, a large number of bhikṣus had gathered in the Kārīrī Hall. At that time, many bhikṣus were ill.

Then the World-Honored One, in the afternoon, emerging from meditation, went to the Kārīrī Hall. Before the great assembly, a seat was spread, and he sat down. Having sat, he told the bhikṣus: "You should dwell with right mindfulness and clear comprehension, awaiting the proper time. This is my instruction for you to follow. Bhikṣus, what is right mindfulness? It means a bhikṣu contemplates the body internally as a foundation of mindfulness, with diligent effort, right mindfulness, and clear comprehension, subduing worldly desire and sorrow. He contemplates the body externally, the body both internally and externally; internal feelings, external feelings, feelings both internal and external; internal mind, external mind, mind both internal and external; internal dharmas, external dharmas, dharmas both internal and external as foundations of mindfulness, with diligent effort, right mindfulness, and clear comprehension, subduing worldly desire and sorrow. This is called a bhikṣu's right mindfulness.

"What is clear comprehension? It means a bhikṣu, whether coming or going, abides in clear comprehension; in looking forward and looking around; in bending and stretching; in handling the robe and bowl; in walking, standing, sitting, lying down, sleeping, and waking; and so forth up to fifty or sixty activities, acting with clear comprehension in accordance with speech and silence. Bhikṣus, this is called clear comprehension.

"Thus, bhikṣus, for one who dwells with right mindfulness and clear comprehension, there arises a pleasant feeling with a cause, not without a cause. What is the cause? It is conditioned by the body. One reflects thus: 'This body of mine is impermanent, conditioned, and arisen from mental causes. Pleasant feeling is also impermanent, conditioned, and arisen from mental causes.' One contemplates the body and pleasant feeling as impermanent, contemplates their arising and passing away, contemplates dispassion, contemplates cessation, contemplates relinquishment. Having contemplated the body and pleasant feeling as impermanent, up to relinquishment, whatever tendency of desire regarding the body and pleasant feeling will never again operate.

"Likewise, for one with right mindfulness and clear comprehension, painful feeling arises with a cause, not without a cause. What is the cause? It is conditioned by the body. One reflects thus: 'This body of mine is impermanent, conditioned, and arisen from mental causes. Painful feeling is also impermanent, conditioned, and arisen from mental causes.' One contemplates the body and painful feeling as impermanent, up to relinquishment. Regarding the body and painful feeling, the underlying tendency of aversion will never again operate.

"Likewise, for one with right mindfulness and clear comprehension, neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling arises with a cause, not without a cause. What is the cause? It is conditioned by the body. One reflects thus: 'This body of mine is impermanent, conditioned, and arisen from mental causes. That neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling is also impermanent, conditioned, and arisen from mental causes.' One contemplates that body and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling as impermanent, up to relinquishment. Whatever underlying tendency of ignorance regarding the body and neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling will never again operate. When a learned noble disciple contemplates in this way, he becomes disenchanted with form, disenchanted with feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness. Being disenchanted, he becomes free from desire. Being free from desire, he is liberated. In liberation, the knowledge arises: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that there is no further existence.'"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "When one is affected by pleasant feeling,
> One cannot know the pleasant feeling.
> Led by the underlying tendency of desire,
> One does not see the path of escape.
> When one is affected by painful feeling,
> One cannot know the painful feeling.
> Led by the underlying tendency of aversion,
> One does not see the path of escape.
> Neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling,
> Taught by the Perfectly Enlightened One,
> That too one cannot know,
> And one never crosses to the other shore.
> If a bhikṣu is diligent,
> With clear comprehension, unwavering,
> Regarding all those feelings,
> The wise one can fully know them all.
> Having fully known all feelings,
> In the present life one exhausts all āsravas,
> Relying on wisdom at the end of life,
> One attains nirvāṇa beyond reckoning."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1029)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, as described above. At that time, a large number of bhikṣus had gathered at the Kārīrī Hall, and many were ill, as described above. The difference is as follows: "Up to when a noble disciple contemplates thus, he is liberated from form, liberated from feeling, perception, volitional formations, and consciousness. I declare that such ones are liberated from birth, aging, illness, and death."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "One with wisdom and wide learning
> Does not fail to be aware of feelings.
> Whether regarding painful or pleasant feelings,
> One discerns and knows them clearly and thoroughly.
> One should know this firm matter:
> Ordinary people have ups and downs.
> Regarding pleasure, one is not stained with attachment;
> Regarding pain, one is not shaken.
> Knowing that feelings do not give rise to rebirth,
> Dependent on desire and aversion being perceived,
> Having severed these and the like,
> One's mind is well liberated.
> Binding mindfulness to an excellent object,
> Rightly directed, awaiting the end of life,
> If a bhikṣu is diligent,
> With clear comprehension, unwavering,
> Regarding all these feelings,
> The wise one can know them all.
> Having fully known all feelings,
> In the present life one exhausts all āsravas,
> Relying on wisdom at the end of life,
> One attains nirvāṇa beyond reckoning."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus rejoiced, bowed, and departed.

---

### (1030)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the householder Anāthapiṇḍada fell ill, with extreme bodily pain. The World-Honored One, hearing of this, in the early morning donned his robe and took his bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food, and begging in sequence arrived at the house of the householder Anāthapiṇḍada.

The householder, seeing the World-Honored One from afar, leaned on his bed and tried to rise.

The World-Honored One, seeing this, immediately told him: "Householder, do not rise! It will increase your suffering." The World-Honored One then sat down and told the householder: "How is it, householder? Can you endure the illness? Is the bodily suffering increasing or decreasing?"

The householder said to the Buddha: "It is very painful, World-Honored One, difficult to bear." He continued speaking of the three feelings, as described in the Chama Sūtra in full, up to "the painful feeling only increases without abating."

The Buddha told the householder: "You should train thus: 'Unwavering faith in the Buddha, unwavering faith in the Dharma, unwavering faith in the Sangha, and fulfillment of the noble precepts.'"

The householder said to the Buddha: "As the World-Honored One speaks of the four kinds of unwavering faith, I possess this Dharma, and this Dharma is in me. World-Honored One, I now have unwavering faith in the Buddha, unwavering faith in the Dharma and the Sangha, and fulfillment of the noble precepts."

The Buddha told the householder: "Excellent! Excellent!" And he immediately declared for the householder the attainment of the fruit of non-returner (anāgāmin).

The householder said to the Buddha: "May the World-Honored One please accept a meal here today." At that time, the World-Honored One silently consented.

The householder then ordered the preparation of various pure and fine foods and drinks to offer to the World-Honored One. After the World-Honored One had eaten, he gave the householder various Dharma teachings, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him, and then rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (1031)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Venerable Ānanda heard that the householder Anāthapiṇḍada was afflicted with bodily suffering, and he went to his house. The householder, seeing Ānanda from afar, leaned on his bed and tried to rise, and so forth, speaking of the three feelings as described previously in the Chama Sūtra in full, up to "the painful feeling only increases without abating."

Venerable Ānanda then told the householder: "Do not be afraid! If a foolish, unlearned ordinary person has no faith in the Buddha, no faith in the Dharma and the Sangha, and is incomplete in the noble precepts, then there is cause for fear, and also fear of death and suffering in the afterlife. But your lack of faith has already been cut off and is already known. You have complete pure faith in the Buddha, complete pure faith in the Dharma and the Sangha, and fulfillment of the noble precepts."

The householder said to Venerable Ānanda: "What should I be afraid of now? When I first saw the World-Honored One at the cold grove charnel ground near Rājagṛha, I immediately attained unwavering faith in the Buddha, unwavering faith in the Dharma and the Sangha, and fulfillment of the noble precepts. Since then, whatever wealth my household has had, I have shared it all with the Buddha and his disciples: bhikṣus, bhikṣuṇīs, upāsakas, and upāsikās."

Venerable Ānanda said: "Excellent, householder! You have declared for yourself the fruit of stream-entry (srotāpanna)."

The householder said to Venerable Ānanda: "May you take a meal here." Venerable Ānanda silently accepted the invitation.

Various pure and fine foods and drinks were then prepared and offered to Venerable Ānanda. After eating, he further gave the householder various Dharma teachings, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him, and then rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (1032)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra heard that the householder Anāthapiṇḍada was afflicted with bodily suffering. Having heard this, he said to Venerable Ānanda: "Did you know? The householder Anāthapiṇḍada is afflicted with bodily suffering. We should go together to visit him." Venerable Ānanda silently consented.

Then Venerable Śāriputra and Venerable Ānanda together went to the house of the householder Anāthapiṇḍada. The householder, seeing Venerable Śāriputra from afar, supported himself on his bed and tried to rise, and so forth, speaking of the three kinds of feelings as described in the Chama Sūtra in full: "The bodily suffering only increases without abating."

Venerable Śāriputra told the householder: "You should train thus: 'Not attached to the eye, not producing desire-consciousness based on the eye-element; not attached to the ear, nose, tongue, or body; not attached to the mind either, not producing desire-consciousness based on the mind-element. Not attached to form, not producing desire-consciousness based on the form-element; not attached to sound, smell, taste, touch, or dharmas, not producing desire-consciousness based on the dharma-element. Not attached to the earth element, not producing desire-consciousness based on the earth element; not attached to the water, fire, wind, space, or consciousness elements, not producing desire-consciousness based on the consciousness element. Not attached to the form aggregate, not producing desire-consciousness based on the form aggregate; not attached to the feeling, perception, volitional formations, or consciousness aggregates, not producing desire-consciousness based on the consciousness aggregate.'"

At that time, the householder Anāthapiṇḍada wept with tears of sorrow. Venerable Ānanda told the householder: "Are you now faint-hearted?"

The householder said to Ānanda: "I am not faint-hearted. I reflect on the fact that, since I have attended upon the Buddha for over twenty years, I have never heard Venerable Śāriputra expound such profound and wonderful Dharma as what I have heard today."

Venerable Śāriputra told the householder: "I too, for a long time, have never before taught such Dharma to householders."

The householder said to Venerable Śāriputra: "There are lay people in white robes who have superior faith, superior mindfulness, and superior joy, yet do not hear the profound Dharma and thus regress and decline. Excellent, Venerable Śāriputra! You should teach the profound and wonderful Dharma to lay people in white robes, out of compassion! Venerable Śāriputra, please take a meal here today." Venerable Śāriputra and the others silently accepted the invitation.

Various pure and fine foods and drinks were then prepared and respectfully offered. After eating, he further gave the householder various Dharma teachings, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him. Having instructed, taught, illuminated, and delighted him, they then rose from their seats and departed.

---

### (1033)

The sūtra of the householder Dharmatinna is also as described in the first sūtra spoken by the World-Honored One for the householder Anāthapiṇḍada in full, and the second sūtra is also spoken in the same way. The difference is: "Furthermore, householder, relying on these four kinds of unwavering faith, you should further cultivate the six recollections: namely, recollection of the Tathāgata, up to recollection of the heavens."

The householder said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, relying on the four kinds of unwavering faith and further cultivating the six recollections, I have accomplished all of these. I always cultivate recollection of the Tathāgata, up to recollection of the heavens."

The Buddha told the householder: "Excellent! Excellent! You have now declared for yourself the fruit of non-returner (anāgāmin)."

The householder said to the Buddha: "May the World-Honored One please accept my invitation to eat." At that time, the World-Honored One silently accepted the invitation.

The householder, knowing the Buddha had accepted the invitation, then prepared various pure and fine foods and drinks, and respectfully made offerings. After the World-Honored One had eaten, he further gave the householder various Dharma teachings, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him, and then rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (1034)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, there was the boy Dīrghāyu, the grandson of the householder Jyotiṣka, who was gravely ill.

The World-Honored One, hearing that the boy Dīrghāyu was gravely ill, in the early morning donned his robe and took his bowl, entered Rājagṛha to beg for food, and in sequence arrived at the house of the boy Dīrghāyu. The boy Dīrghāyu, seeing the World-Honored One from afar, supported himself on his bed and tried to rise, and so forth, speaking of the three feelings, as described in the Chama Sūtra in full, up to: "The illness only increases without abating. Therefore, boy, you should train thus: 'Unwavering faith in the Buddha, unwavering faith in the Dharma and the Sangha, and fulfillment of the noble precepts.' You should train thus."

The boy said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, as the World-Honored One speaks of the four kinds of unwavering faith, I possess them all. I always have unwavering faith in the Buddha, unwavering faith in the Dharma and the Sangha, and fulfillment of the noble precepts."

The Buddha told the boy: "You should rely on the four kinds of unwavering faith and further cultivate the six clear-knowledge contemplations. What are the six? Namely, the contemplation of impermanence regarding all formations, the contemplation that what is impermanent is suffering, the contemplation that what is suffering is non-self, the contemplation of food, the contemplation that nothing in the entire world is delightful, and the contemplation of death."

The boy said to the Buddha: "As the World-Honored One speaks, relying on the four kinds of unwavering faith and cultivating the six clear-knowledge contemplations, I possess them all. However, I think thus: 'After I pass away, I do not know what will become of my grandfather, the householder Jyotiṣka.'"

At that time, the householder Jyotiṣka said to the boy Dīrghāyu: "On my account, set aside such thoughts for now. You should now listen to the World-Honored One teach the Dharma, and reflect upon and remember it, so that you may attain long-lasting benefit, well-being, and happiness."

Then the boy Dīrghāyu said: "I contemplate all formations as impermanent, what is impermanent as suffering, what is suffering as non-self, I contemplate food, I contemplate that nothing in the entire world is delightful, and I contemplate death, always keeping these present before me."

The Buddha told the boy: "You have now declared for yourself the fruit of once-returner (sakṛdāgāmin)."

The boy Dīrghāyu said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, may the World-Honored One please stay and eat at my house." At that time, the World-Honored One silently consented.

The boy Dīrghāyu then prepared various pure and fine foods and drinks, and respectfully made offerings. After the World-Honored One had eaten, he further gave the boy various Dharma teachings, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him, and then rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (1035)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, at the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park.

At that time, the householder Vāsu was afflicted with bodily suffering.

The World-Honored One, hearing that the householder Vāsu was afflicted with bodily suffering, as previously described in full in the sūtra of the householder Dharmatinna, received the prediction of the fruit of non-returner (anāgāmin), up to rising from his seat and departing.

---

### (1036)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Kapilavastu, in the Nyagrodha Park.

At that time, there was a Śākyan named Śāla who was gravely ill and in critical condition.

The World-Honored One, hearing that the Śākyan Śāla was gravely ill and in critical condition, in the early morning donned his robe and took his bowl, entered Kapilavastu to beg for food, and in sequence arrived at the house of the Śākyan Śāla. The Śākyan Śāla, seeing the World-Honored One from afar, supported himself on his bed and tried to rise, and so forth, speaking of the three feelings, as described in the Chama Sūtra in full, up to: "The suffering only increases without abating. Therefore, Śākyan Śāla, you should train thus: 'Unwavering faith in the Buddha, unwavering faith in the Dharma and the Sangha, and fulfillment of the noble precepts.'"

The Śākyan Śāla said to the Buddha: "As the World-Honored One says, 'Unwavering faith in the Buddha, unwavering faith in the Dharma and the Sangha, and fulfillment of the noble precepts,' I possess all of these. I always have unwavering faith in the Buddha, unwavering faith in the Dharma and the Sangha, and fulfillment of the noble precepts."

The Buddha told the Śākyan Śāla: "Therefore, you should rely on unwavering faith in the Buddha, unwavering faith in the Dharma and the Sangha, and fulfillment of the noble precepts, and further cultivate the five bases of joy. What are the five? Namely, recollection of the Tathāgata, up to recollection of one's own giving."

The Śākyan Śāla said to the Buddha: "As the World-Honored One says, relying on the four kinds of unwavering faith and cultivating the five bases of joy, I possess them as well. I always recollect the Tathāgata, up to recollection of my own giving."

The Buddha said: "Excellent! Excellent! You have now declared for yourself the fruit of once-returner (sakṛdāgāmin)."

Śāla said to the Buddha: "May the World-Honored One please eat at my house today." At that time, the World-Honored One silently consented.

The householder Śāla then prepared various pure and fine foods and drinks, and respectfully made offerings. After the World-Honored One had eaten, he further gave the householder Śāla various Dharma teachings, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him, and then rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (1037)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the Nāḷī village, at the Curved Valley Monastery.

At that time, the householder Yaśoda was gravely ill and in critical condition, and so forth, up to receiving the prediction of the fruit of non-returner (anāgāmin), as described in full in the sūtra of Dharmatinna.

---

### (1038)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Campā, beside the Gaggarā Lotus Pond. At that time, there was a householder named Mānadinna who had recently recovered from illness.

The householder Mānadinna then said to a man: "Good man, go to Venerable Anuruddha and on my behalf bow at Anuruddha's feet, inquire about his health: 'Are you well and at ease, dwelling in comfort?' Tomorrow, including himself making four persons, I wish him to accept my invitation. If he accepts the invitation, also tell him on my behalf: 'I am a layperson with many royal affairs. I cannot come in person to escort you. May Venerable Anuruddha, including himself making four persons, come at the proper time tomorrow to accept my invitation, out of compassion!'"

The man received the householder's instruction and went to Venerable Anuruddha. He bowed his head at his feet and said: "Venerable one, the householder Mānadinna respectfully bows and inquires: 'Are you in good health, free from troubles, moving about easily, and dwelling in comfort? May Venerable Anuruddha, including himself making four persons, accept my invitation for tomorrow's midday meal.'" Venerable Anuruddha then silently accepted the invitation.

The man then conveyed the householder Mānadinna's words to Venerable Anuruddha: "I am a layperson with many royal affairs. I cannot come in person to escort you. May Venerable Anuruddha, including himself making four persons, come at the proper time tomorrow for the midday meal to accept my invitation, out of compassion!"

Venerable Anuruddha said: "You may return at ease. I know the proper time. Tomorrow, including myself making four persons, I will go to his house."

The man received Venerable Anuruddha's instruction, returned, and told the householder: "Master, you should know that I have already gone to Venerable Anuruddha and conveyed your full message. Venerable Anuruddha said: 'You may return at ease; I know the proper time.'"

That night, the householder Mānadinna prepared pure and fine foods and drinks. In the early morning, he again told the man: "Go to Venerable Anuruddha and say: 'The time has come.'"

The man then received the instruction, went to Venerable Anuruddha, bowed his head at his feet, and said: "The offerings are prepared. May you know the proper time!"

Venerable Anuruddha then donned his robe and took his bowl, and including himself making four persons, went to the householder's house.

The householder Mānadinna, surrounded by his attendant women, stood to the left of the inner gate. Seeing Venerable Anuruddha, he prostrated his whole body, grasped his feet in respectful greeting, and led him inside to sit down. Each person individually bowed, inquired about his health, and then withdrew to sit to one side.

Venerable Anuruddha inquired of the householder: "Can you bear it? Are you dwelling in comfort?"

The householder replied: "Yes, Venerable One, I can bear it and dwell in comfort. Previously I suffered from illness and was in critical condition at the time, but now I have been fortunate to recover."

Venerable Anuruddha asked the householder: "What practice did you maintain that enabled your illness and suffering to be alleviated in time?"

The householder said: "Venerable Anuruddha, I dwelled in the four foundations of mindfulness, dedicatedly cultivating them with focused mindfulness, and therefore the bodily suffering was alleviated in time. What are the four? Namely, contemplation of the body internally as a foundation of mindfulness, with diligent effort, right mindfulness, and clear comprehension, subduing worldly desire and sorrow. The body externally, the body both internally and externally; internal feelings, external feelings, feelings both internal and external; internal mind, external mind, mind both internal and external; internal dharmas, external dharmas, dharmas both internal and external as foundations of mindfulness, with diligent effort, right mindfulness, and clear comprehension, subduing worldly desire and sorrow. Thus, Venerable Anuruddha, because I bound my mind and dwelled in the four foundations of mindfulness, the bodily suffering was alleviated in time. Venerable Anuruddha, by this dwelling, the bodily suffering was alleviated in time."

Venerable Anuruddha told the householder: "You have now declared for yourself the fruit of non-returner (anāgāmin)."

The householder Mānadinna then with his own hands offered various pure and fine foods and drinks, satisfying them to their fill. After eating, they rinsed their hands and mouths. The householder Mānadinna then sat on a low seat to listen to the Dharma. Venerable Anuruddha gave various Dharma teachings, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him, and then rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (1039)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Goldsmith's Monastery. At that time, the householder Cunda came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side.

The World-Honored One then asked the householder Cunda: "What kind of śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa purification practices do you now favor and delight in?"

Cunda said to the Buddha: "There are śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas who worship water, who worship the god Viśvadeva, who hold staffs and water-jugs, and who always keep their hands clean. Such righteous men can skillfully teach the Dharma, saying: 'Good man, on the fifteenth day, use sesame powder and āmalaka powder to wash your hair, practice the observance of the fast, put on new, clean, long-haired white cotton garments, smear the ground with cow dung, and lie upon it. Good man, rise early in the morning, touch the ground with your hand, and say thus: "This ground is pure; I am likewise pure." Take a ball of cow dung in your hand and hold fresh grass, and speak these words: "This is pure; I am likewise pure." If one does this, one is seen as pure; if one does not do this, one is never pure.' World-Honored One, such śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, if they practice purification, are those I revere and follow."

The Buddha told Cunda: "There are dark dharmas with dark results, impure with impure fruits, burdened and tending downward. One who is endowed with such evil dharmas, even if he rises early in the morning and touches the ground with his hand, proclaiming purity, is still impure. Even if he does not touch it, he is also not pure. Holding a ball of cow dung and grasping fresh grass, proclaiming purity, he is also not pure. Even if he does not touch them, he is also not pure.

"Cunda, what are these dark dharmas with dark results, impure with impure fruits, burdened and tending downward, which even with touching or not touching are all impure? Cunda, it is the evil action of killing living beings, with hands constantly bloody, the mind always thinking of beating, striking, and killing, without shame or remorse, miserly and stingy, not refraining from killing toward any living being, even insects. Regarding others' possessions, whether in villages or empty places, one does not refrain from stealing. One practices sexual misconduct: even those protected by father, mother, siblings, husband, relatives, and even those to whom a flower garland has been given, one forcefully violates, not refraining from sexual misconduct. One speaks false words: whether before kings, before assemblies of truthful speakers, among gatherings of many people, in places where testimony is sought, one speaks untruthfully, saying one has seen when one has not seen, saying one has not seen when one has seen, saying one has not heard when one has heard, saying one has heard when one has not heard, saying one knows when one does not know, saying one does not know when one knows. For one's own sake, for others' sake, or for the sake of gain, one knowingly speaks falsely without abandoning it; this is called false speech. One engages in divisive speech, conveying words from here to there and from there to here, mutually causing destruction, making those who are united become separated and those who are separated rejoice; this is called divisive speech. One does not refrain from harsh speech. When people speak gentle words, pleasing to the ear and heart, proper and easy to understand, delightful to hear, without dependence, and loved by many, agreeable and conducive to samādhi, one abandons such speech and instead speaks harshly, in ways many people find hateful, displeasing, disagreeable, and not conducive to samādhi. Such words, not free from coarseness, are called harsh speech. One engages in frivolous and corrupting speech: untimely speech, untruthful speech, meaningless speech, speech contrary to Dharma, speech without reflection; such speech is called corrupting speech.

"One does not abandon covetousness, desiring others' possessions and thinking: 'If only that thing were mine!' One does not abandon ill-will and malice, thinking in one's mind: 'Those beings should be bound, should be whipped, should be subdued, should be killed,' wishing to create difficulties for them. One does not abandon wrong views, holding distorted views, seeing thus and saying thus: 'There is no giving, no result, no merit, no fruit of good and evil actions, no this world, no other world, no father, no mother, no beings born spontaneously, no arahants in the world who correctly progress and correctly practice, who directly know and realize for themselves in this world and the next: "My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that there is no further existence."' Cunda, these are called dark dharmas with dark results, impure with impure fruits, which even with touching or not touching are all impure.

"Cunda, there are bright dharmas with bright results, pure with pure fruits, light and ascending upward. Having accomplished these, even if one touches the ground early in the morning and says 'This is pure, I am pure,' one is indeed pure; and even if one does not touch it, one is also pure. Holding a ball of cow dung and grasping fresh grass, speaking of purity as cause and pure fruit, whether holding or not holding, one is also pure.

"Cunda, what are these bright dharmas with bright results, which whether touching or not touching are all pure? It means there are people who do not kill, who have abandoned killing, who set aside knife and staff, who have shame and compassion, who are mindful of all living beings with kindness. One does not steal, is far removed from stealing; what is given one takes, what is not given one does not take, with a pure mind free from covetousness. One is far removed from sexual misconduct: those protected by father and mother, and even those to whom a flower garland has been given, one does not forcefully commit sexual misconduct. One is far removed from false speech, speaking with careful truthfulness. One is far removed from divisive speech, not conveying words from here to there or from there to here to cause mutual destruction; one causes those who are separated to be reconciled and those who are reconciled to rejoice. One is far removed from harsh speech, not being harsh, and many people delight in one's words. One is far removed from corrupting speech, speaking truthfully, timely, truthfully, meaningfully, according to Dharma, and with reflection. One is free from covetousness, not regarding others' possessions and belongings as one's own and generating attachment. One is free from ill-will, not thinking: 'Beat, bind, and kill them, and create difficulties for them.' One accomplishes right view, not holding distorted views: there is giving, there is spoken result, there is merit, there are fruits of good and evil actions, there is this world, there is a father, there is a mother, there are beings born spontaneously, there are arahants in the world who in this world and the next directly know and realize for themselves: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that there is no further existence.' Cunda, these are called bright dharmas with bright results, which whether touching or not touching are all pure."

At that time, the householder Cunda, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was delighted, bowed, and departed.

---

### (1040)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Goldsmith's Monastery.

At that time, a certain brāhmaṇa, on the fifteenth day, had washed his head, observed the fast, put on new long-haired white cotton garments, and holding fresh grass in his hand, came to visit the Buddha. He exchanged greetings and cordial words with the World-Honored One, then withdrew to sit to one side.

The Buddha then told the brāhmaṇa: "You have washed your head and put on new long-haired white cotton garments. Whose teaching is this?"

The brāhmaṇa said to the Buddha: "Gautama, it is the practice of renunciation."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "How do brāhmaṇas practice renunciation?"

The brāhmaṇa said to the Buddha: "Gautama, on the fifteenth day, one washes one's head, observes the Dharma fast, puts on new, clean, long-haired white cotton garments, holds fresh grass in one's hand, and according to one's ability, gives charity and performs meritorious acts. Gautama, this is called a brāhmaṇa's practice of renunciation."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "The practice of renunciation in the noble Dharma and Vinaya is different from this."

The brāhmaṇa said to the Buddha: "Gautama, what is the practice of renunciation in the noble Dharma and Vinaya?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "It means abandoning killing, not delighting in killing," as described previously in the section on purity in full. "Relying on non-killing, one renounces the act of killing," and so forth, as described previously in the section on purity in full. "Abandoning stealing, not delighting in stealing, relying on non-stealing, one renounces taking what is not given. Abandoning sexual misconduct, not delighting in sexual misconduct, relying on non-misconduct, one renounces unchaste behavior. Abandoning false speech, not delighting in false speech, relying on non-false speech, one renounces untruthful words. Abandoning divisive speech, not delighting in divisive speech, relying on non-divisive speech, one renounces the practice of causing separation. Abandoning harsh speech, not delighting in harsh speech, relying on non-harsh speech, one renounces coarse words. Abandoning frivolous speech, not delighting in frivolous speech, relying on non-frivolous speech, one renounces meaningless words. Severing covetousness, distancing oneself from the suffering of covetousness, relying on a mind free from covetousness, one renounces attachment. Severing ill-will, not generating anger, relying on freedom from ill-will, one renounces hatred. Cultivating right view, not giving rise to distortion, relying on right view, one renounces wrong views. Brāhmaṇa, this is called the practice of renunciation in the noble Dharma and Vinaya."

The brāhmaṇa said to the Buddha: "Excellent! Gautama, the practice of renunciation in the noble Dharma and Vinaya!"

The brāhmaṇa, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was delighted, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (1041)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a brāhmaṇa named Jānuśroṇi came to visit the Buddha. He exchanged greetings and cordial words with the World-Honored One, then withdrew to sit to one side and said to the Buddha: "Gautama, I have a relative whom I loved dearly, who suddenly passed away. For his sake, I make offerings with faith. How is it, World-Honored One? Can he receive them?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "It is not in every case that they are received. If your relative was reborn among the beings in hell, he receives the food of hell beings and sustains his life thereby, but does not receive the food and drink you offer with faith. If he was reborn as an animal, a hungry ghost, or among humans, he receives the food of humans and does not receive what you offered. Brāhmaṇa, within the realm of hungry ghosts there is a certain place called the 'admitting' hungry ghost realm. If your relative was reborn among the admitting hungry ghosts, then he can receive the food you offer."

The brāhmaṇa said to the Buddha: "If my relative was not reborn among the admitting hungry ghosts, who should receive my faithful offering?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "If the relative for whom you made the faithful offering was not reborn among the admitting hungry ghosts, there must be other relatives or acquaintances who were reborn among the admitting hungry ghosts, and they can receive it."

The brāhmaṇa said to the Buddha: "Gautama, if my relative for whom I made the faithful offering was not reborn among the admitting hungry ghosts, and there are no other relatives or acquaintances reborn among the admitting hungry ghosts, who then should receive this food of faithful offering?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "Even if the relatives and acquaintances for whom you made the offering were not reborn among the admitting hungry ghosts, and there are no other acquaintances reborn among the hungry ghosts, still, by giving with faith, you yourself obtain the merit. The giver who makes a faithful offering does not lose the dakṣiṇā."

The brāhmaṇa said to the Buddha: "How is it that a giver who gives receives that dakṣiṇā?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "There is a person who kills, acts with evil, whose hands are constantly bloody, and so forth, up to the ten unwholesome courses of action," as described in the Cunda Sūtra in full, "and yet he gives to śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, and even to the poor and destitute, giving them money, clothing, food and drink, lamps, and various adornments. Brāhmaṇa, if that generous patron who violates the precepts is reborn among elephants, because he previously gave to śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas money, clothing, food and drink, and various adornments, even though he is among elephants, he receives the reward of those gifts: clothing, food and drink, and various adornments.

"If furthermore he is reborn among cattle, horses, donkeys, mules, or various other animal realms, because of the merit of his previous generosity, he receives its reward in full. Whatever the place of rebirth and whatever the appropriate provisions, he obtains them all. Brāhmaṇa, if furthermore the patron upholds the precepts, does not kill, does not steal, and so forth up to right view, and gives to śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, and even to the destitute, money, clothing, food and drink, and even lamps, by the merit of this, being reborn in the human realm, he receives the reward seated therein: clothing, food and drink, and even various provisions.

"Furthermore, brāhmaṇa, if one who upholds the precepts is reborn in the heavens, those acts of generosity bring their reward in the heavens: wealth, clothing, food and drink, and even various adornments. Brāhmaṇa, this is called a giver who gives and the giver who receives the dakṣiṇā, for the fruits of the reward are not lost."

The brāhmaṇa Jānuśroṇi, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was delighted, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (1042)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling through the country of Kosala, and stayed in the Śiṃśapā Forest, north of the village of Velāma.

The brāhmaṇas and householders of Velāma village heard that the World-Honored One was staying in the Śiṃśapā Forest north of the village. Having heard this, they gathered together and went to visit the Buddha. They bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what cause and condition leads to beings, at the breaking up of the body after death, being reborn in hell?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇas and householders: "Because of practicing unlawful conduct and practicing dangerous conduct, at the breaking up of the body after death, one is reborn in hell." The brāhmaṇas and householders said to the Buddha: "What kind of unlawful conduct and dangerous conduct leads to being reborn in hell at the breaking up of the body after death?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇas and householders: "Killing, and so forth up to wrong views, being endowed with the ten unwholesome courses of action. Brāhmaṇas, this is unlawful conduct and dangerous conduct; at the breaking up of the body after death, one is reborn in hell."

The brāhmaṇas said to the Buddha: "What cause and condition leads to beings, at the breaking up of the body after death, being reborn in heaven?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇas and householders: "Practicing lawful conduct and practicing right conduct: because of this cause and condition, at the breaking up of the body after death, one is reborn in heaven."

They further asked: "World-Honored One, what kind of lawful conduct and right conduct leads to being reborn in heaven at the breaking up of the body after death?"

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇas and householders: "Namely, abandoning killing, and so forth up to right view, the ten wholesome courses of action; because of these causes and conditions, at the breaking up of the body after death, one is reborn in heaven. Brāhmaṇas and householders, if one practices this lawful conduct and this right conduct, and wishes to be reborn in a great kṣatriya family, a great brāhmaṇa family, or a great householder family,
