# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Thirty-Eight

---

### (1062)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a venerable named Sujāta, who had newly shaved his beard and hair, donned the kāṣāya robes, and with right faith left the household life to go forth and train in the Way. He came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side.

Then the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Bhikṣus! You should know that this good man Sujāta possesses two kinds of dignified beauty. First, shaving off his beard and hair, donning the kāṣāya robes, and with right faith leaving the household life to go forth and train in the Way. Second, having exhausted all the outflows, he has attained the outflow-free liberation of mind and liberation by wisdom, knowing and realizing for himself in the present life: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further becoming.'"

Then the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "Tranquil, all outflows ended,
> the bhikṣu is beautifully adorned.
> Free from desire, all fetters severed,
> in nirvāṇa, born no more.
> Bearing this final body,
> he has crushed and vanquished Māra, the enemy."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1063)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain bhikṣu whose appearance was ugly and unpleasant to look at, and he was looked down upon by the other bhikṣus. He came to where the Buddha was.

At that time, the World-Honored One was surrounded by the four assemblies. Seeing that bhikṣu coming, they all gave rise to contemptuous thoughts and said to one another: "What sort of bhikṣu is that coming along the road? His appearance is ugly and unpleasant to look at, and he is despised by people."

Then the World-Honored One, knowing what the bhikṣus were thinking, addressed them: "Do you see that bhikṣu coming, whose form is very ugly and unpleasant to look at, causing people to feel contempt?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "Indeed, we have seen him."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "You should not give rise to contemptuous thoughts toward that bhikṣu. Why? Because that bhikṣu has already exhausted all outflows, has done what was to be done, has cast off the heavy burden, has severed all fetters of existence, possesses right knowledge, and his mind is well liberated. Bhikṣus! You should not rashly measure others. Only the Tathāgata can truly measure a person."

That bhikṣu then approached the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side.

Then the World-Honored One again addressed the bhikṣus: "Do you see this bhikṣu bowing his head in salutation and then withdrawing to sit to one side?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "Indeed, we have seen him."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "You should not give rise to contemptuous thoughts toward this bhikṣu, and so forth: you should not measure others. Only the Tathāgata can know a person." Then the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "Birds that fly and beasts that run,
> none do not fear the lion.
> Only the lion, king of beasts,
> has no equal among them.
> So too a person of wisdom,
> though small in stature, is truly great.
> Do not judge by bodily appearance
> and give rise to a mind of contempt.
> What use is a huge body,
> much flesh but without wisdom?
> This worthy one of superior wisdom
> is truly an excellent person.
> Free from desire, all fetters severed,
> in nirvāṇa, never born again.
> Bearing this final body,
> he has crushed and vanquished the armies of Māra."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1064)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, gains had arisen for Devadatta. Ajātaśatru, the son of Vaidehī, king of Magadha, daily attended upon him with a retinue of five hundred carriages. Daily he brought five hundred cauldrons of food to offer to Devadatta, and Devadatta, leading a separate group of five hundred, received these offerings.

At that time, many bhikṣus put on their robes in the early morning, took their bowls, and entered Rājagṛha to beg for food. They heard that such gains had arisen for Devadatta, and so forth, that a separate group of five hundred received his offerings. Having finished begging for food, they returned to the monastery, put away their robes and bowls, washed their feet, and went to where the Buddha was. They bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! This morning we put on our robes, took our bowls, and entered Rājagṛha to beg for food. We heard that such gains have arisen for Devadatta, and so forth, that a separate group of five hundred receives his offerings."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "You should not speak approvingly of the gains that Devadatta has received. Why? That Devadatta, by separately receiving those gains, destroys himself in this life and will also be destroyed in the next life. It is like the plantain, the bamboo, and the reed: when they bear fruit, they die, and the following year they are also destroyed. Devadatta is likewise; receiving those gains, he is destroyed in this life and will also be destroyed in the next life. It is like the mule: upon becoming pregnant, it necessarily dies. Devadatta is likewise; receiving those gains, he is destroyed in this life and will also be destroyed in the next life. That foolish Devadatta, for however long he receives those gains, will suffer harm and misery through the long night. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train yourselves thus: 'Should gains arise for me, I will not become attached to them.'"

Then the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "The plantain bears fruit and dies,
> the bamboo and reed, bearing seeds, likewise.
> The mule dies from being with foal;
> a person by greed brings about their own ruin.
> One who constantly practices unrighteous conduct,
> though learned much, does not escape foolishness.
> Wholesome dharmas daily diminish and decline;
> the stalk withers and the root is also harmed."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1065)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, in the state of Śrāvastī, there was a bhikṣu named Hasta, a son of the Śākya clan, who had passed away in Śrāvastī.

At that time, many bhikṣus put on their robes in the early morning, took their bowls, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. They heard that the Śākya son, the bhikṣu Hasta, had passed away in the state of Śrāvastī. Having heard this, they entered the city of Śrāvastī, begged for food, returned, put away their robes and bowls, washed their feet, and went to where the Buddha was. They bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Today in the early morning, many bhikṣus put on their robes, took their bowls, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. We heard that the Śākya son, the bhikṣu Hasta, has passed away in the state of Śrāvastī. How is it, World-Honored One? Where has the bhikṣu Hasta been reborn after death? What form of rebirth has he taken? What is his future state?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This bhikṣu Hasta had accomplished three unwholesome dharmas. Having passed away, he should be reborn in an evil destiny, in hell. What are the three unwholesome dharmas? They are greed, hatred, and delusion. These three unwholesome dharmas bound and fettered his mind. The Śākya son, the bhikṣu Hasta, has been reborn in an evil destiny, in hell."

Then the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "Greed, hatred, and delusion
> bind and fetter a person's mind.
> Arising within, they harm oneself,
> like the bamboo or reed bearing fruit.
> Without greed, hatred, or delusion in mind,
> such a one is called truly wise.
> Arising within, they do not harm oneself;
> this is called being victorious and transcendent.
> Therefore one should abandon greed,
> hatred, and the darkness of a deluded mind.
> The bhikṣu with the light of wisdom
> puts an end to suffering and enters parinirvāṇa."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1066)

As with the bhikṣu Hasta, the Nanda sūtra is likewise spoken.

---

### (1067)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Nanda, the son of the Buddha's maternal aunt, liked to wear fine garments, dyed fabrics pounded and polished to a sheen. He carried a fine bowl and liked to go about jesting and joking.

At that time, many bhikṣus came to where the Buddha was, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The Venerable Nanda, the son of the Buddha's maternal aunt, likes to wear fine garments, pounded and polished to a sheen. He carries a fine bowl and likes to go about jesting and joking."

Then the World-Honored One told a bhikṣu: "Go to where the bhikṣu Nanda is and say to him: 'Nanda! The Great Teacher summons you.'"

That bhikṣu, having received the World-Honored One's instruction, went and said to Nanda: "The World-Honored One summons you."

Nanda, having heard this, immediately went to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to stand to one side.

The Buddha told Nanda: "Is it true that you like to wear fine garments, pounded and polished to a sheen, and like to go about jesting and joking?"

Nanda said to the Buddha: "It is true, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Nanda: "You are the son of the Buddha's maternal aunt, of noble birth, having gone forth. You should not wear fine garments, pounded to a sheen, carry a fine bowl, and go about jesting and joking. You should give rise to this thought: 'I am the son of the Buddha's maternal aunt, of noble birth, having gone forth. I should practice as a forest-dweller, beg for food, wear rag-robes, and always praise the wearing of rag-robes. I should always dwell in mountains and marshes, not caring for the five sensual desires.'"

At that time, Nanda, having received the Buddha's instruction, practiced the araṇya way, begged for food, wore rag-robes, and also constantly praised those who wore rag-robes. He delighted in dwelling in mountains and marshes, not caring for sensual desires. Then the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "Nanda, how joyful to see you
> cultivating the araṇya practice,
> going from house to house begging for food,
> wearing rag-robes upon your body,
> delighting in dwelling in mountains and marshes,
> not caring for the five sensual desires."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Nanda, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1068)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Tiṣya thought to himself: "Because I am the Buddha's cousin, I need not cultivate respect, nor need I have any regard or fear, and I cannot be admonished or restrained."

At that time, many bhikṣus went to where the Buddha was, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The Venerable Tiṣya, because he thinks he is the World-Honored One's cousin, does not cultivate respect, has no regard, no fear, and cannot be admonished or restrained."

Then the World-Honored One told a bhikṣu: "Go to where the bhikṣu Tiṣya is and say to him: 'Tiṣya! The Great Teacher summons you!'"

That bhikṣu, having received the World-Honored One's instruction, went and said to the bhikṣu Tiṣya: "The World-Honored One summons you!"

The bhikṣu Tiṣya immediately went to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to stand to one side.

The Buddha told Tiṣya: "Do you truly think: 'I am the World-Honored One's cousin, so I need not cultivate respect, need have no regard or fear, and cannot endure being admonished'?"

Tiṣya said to the Buddha: "It is true, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Tiṣya: "You should not be this way! You should think: 'Because I am the World-Honored One's cousin, I should cultivate respect, fear, and be able to endure admonishment and restraint.'" Then the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "It would be good, Tiṣya, for you
> to abandon anger; it would be wholesome.
> Do not give rise to a mind of anger;
> anger is unwholesome.
> If you can abandon anger and pride,
> and cultivate a gentle and humble mind,
> then in my presence
> you can practice the holy life."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Tiṣya, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was gladdened, bowed, and departed.

---

### (1069)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Viśākhā Pañcālīputra, gathered in the offering hall, was teaching the Dharma for many bhikṣus. His words were complete and full, his voice was wonderful and clear, his phrasing and meaning were well-articulated and correct. He spoke in accordance with wisdom. Those who listened delighted in hearing. He spoke without dependence, revealing profound meaning, causing the bhikṣus to listen with single-minded concentration.

At that time, the World-Honored One entered the midday meditative attainment. With his purified divine ear surpassing the human ear, he heard the sound of the Dharma being taught. Arising from samādhi, he went to the lecture hall, sat before the great assembly, and told Viśākhā Pañcālīputra: "Excellent! Excellent! Viśākhā! You are able to teach the Dharma for the bhikṣus in this offering hall, for many bhikṣus. Your words are complete and full, and so forth, revealing profound meaning, causing the bhikṣus to listen with devoted respect and single-minded delight. You should frequently teach the Dharma for the bhikṣus in this way, causing the bhikṣus to listen with devoted respect and single-minded delight. They will thereby gain benefit and ease through the long night, dwelling in comfort and happiness."

Then the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "If no one speaks the Dharma,
> the foolish and the wise are mixed and hard to distinguish.
> This one is foolish, that one is wise,
> but there is no way for this to become manifest.
> When the cooling Dharma is well spoken,
> through speaking, wisdom is revealed.
> Speaking the Dharma is a bright illumination,
> the banner that displays the Great Sage.
> Good speaking is the banner of the sage;
> the Dharma is the banner of the arhat."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Viśākhā Pañcālīputra, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was gladdened, bowed, and departed.

---

### (1070)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, many bhikṣus had gathered in the offering hall, all working together to make robes. There was a young bhikṣu who had gone forth not long ago and had only recently entered the Dharma and Vinaya. He did not wish to help the bhikṣus in making robes.

At that time, many bhikṣus went to where the World-Honored One was, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Many bhikṣus have gathered in the offering hall for the purpose of making robes. There is a young bhikṣu who has gone forth not long ago and has only recently entered the Dharma and Vinaya. He does not wish to help the bhikṣus in making robes."

Then the World-Honored One asked that bhikṣu: "Is it true that you do not wish to help the bhikṣus in making robes?"

That bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! To the extent of my ability, I shall do my best to help."

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing what was in that bhikṣu's mind, told the bhikṣus: "You should not speak to this young bhikṣu about this. Why? Because this bhikṣu has attained the four higher mental states and abides in the bliss of the present Dharma in right attainment, without exertion. That original aspiration for which he shaved his beard and hair, donned the kāṣāya robes, and went forth from the household life to train in the Way, advancing in further cultivation, he has, in the present Dharma, himself known and realized: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further becoming.'"

Then the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "Not through inferior means,
> nor through meager virtue or little wisdom,
> rightly directed toward nirvāṇa,
> freed from the shackles of afflictions.
> This worthy young one
> has reached the station of the supreme person.
> Free from desire, his mind liberated,
> in nirvāṇa, born no more.
> Bearing this final body,
> he has crushed and vanquished the armies of Māra."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1071)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Elder who dwelt alone in a solitary place, and also constantly praised those who dwelt alone. He would go alone to beg for food, and after eating, would return alone, and sit alone in meditation.

At that time, many bhikṣus went to where the Buddha was, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! There is a venerable named Elder who delights in dwelling alone in a solitary place, and also constantly praises those who dwell alone. He enters the village alone to beg for food, leaves the village alone, returns to his dwelling, and sits alone in meditation."

Then the World-Honored One told a bhikṣu: "Go to where the bhikṣu Elder is and say to the bhikṣu Elder: 'The Great Teacher summons you!'"

The bhikṣu, having received the instruction, went to where the bhikṣu Elder was and said: "Venerable one! The Great Teacher summons you!"

At that time, the bhikṣu Elder immediately complied with the summons and went to where the World-Honored One was, bowed his head at his feet, and withdrew to stand to one side.

Then the World-Honored One told the bhikṣu Elder: "Is it true that you dwell alone in a quiet place, praise those who dwell alone, go alone to beg for food, leave the village alone, and sit alone in meditation?"

The bhikṣu Elder said to the Buddha: "It is true, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu Elder: "How do you dwell alone in a solitary place, praise those who dwell alone, go alone to beg for food, return alone to your dwelling, and sit alone in meditation?"

The bhikṣu Elder said to the Buddha: "I simply dwell alone in a quiet place, praise those who dwell alone, go alone to beg for food, leave the village alone, and sit alone in meditation."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu Elder: "You are one who dwells alone; I do not say you are not one who dwells alone. However, there is a more supreme and wondrous dwelling alone. What is the more supreme and wondrous dwelling alone? It is when a bhikṣu has dried up the past, extinguished the future, and is without craving or delight in the present. This is a brāhmaṇa whose mind is free from hesitation, who has abandoned worry and remorse, who has departed from all craving for existence, and whose fetters of association are severed. This is called dwelling alone, and there is no dwelling superior to this."

Then the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "Illuminating everything entirely,
> fully knowing all the worlds,
> not attached to any dharma,
> completely freed from all craving:
> one who dwells happily thus,
> I declare to be the one who truly dwells alone."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Elder, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was gladdened, bowed, and departed.

---

### (1072)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Saṅgārava was wandering among the people of Kosala and arrived at Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. That bhikṣu Saṅgārava had a former wife living in the city of Śrāvastī. She heard that the bhikṣu Saṅgārava had been wandering among the people of Kosala and had arrived at Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. Having heard this, she put on fine clothes and ornaments, adorned herself with floral garlands, and carrying her child, came to the Jetavana and arrived before the bhikṣu Saṅgārava's dwelling.

At that time, the Venerable Saṅgārava had come out of his room and was walking in meditation on the open ground. The former wife approached him and said: "This child is still young. You have left to go forth, so who will support and raise him?"

At that time, the bhikṣu Saṅgārava did not speak with her. She spoke thus a second and third time, and still he did not speak with her.

Then the former wife said: "I have told you three times, yet you neither speak with me nor look at me. I shall now leave the child here." She placed the child at the end of the walking path and departed, saying: "Śramaṇa! This is your son. You yourself raise him. I am now leaving."

The Venerable Saṅgārava also did not look at the child.

The former wife then said: "This śramaṇa does not look at this child at all. He must have attained a state difficult for sages to attain. Excellent, śramaṇa! You must have attained liberation." Her wish unfulfilled, she picked up the child and departed.

At that time, the World-Honored One had entered the midday meditative attainment. With his divine ear surpassing the human ear, he heard what the Venerable Saṅgārava's former wife had said, and immediately spoke this verse:

> "At her coming, he felt no joy;
> at her going, he felt no sorrow.
> Freed from worldly union,
> liberated and unattached.
> I declare such a bhikṣu
> to be a true brāhmaṇa.
> At her coming, he felt no joy;
> at her going, he felt no sorrow.
> Unattached and free from grief,
> both minds equally stilled.
> I declare such a bhikṣu
> to be a true brāhmaṇa."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Saṅgārava, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was gladdened, bowed, and departed.

---

### (1073)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda, while alone in a quiet place, contemplated thus: "There are three kinds of fragrance that spread only with the wind and cannot spread against the wind. What are the three? They are the fragrance of roots, the fragrance of stems, and the fragrance of flowers. Or could there be a fragrance that spreads with the wind, and also against the wind, and also both with and against the wind?" Having contemplated thus, in the late afternoon he arose from meditation, went to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! While alone in a quiet place, I contemplated thus: 'There are three kinds of fragrance that spread only with the wind and cannot spread against the wind. What are the three? They are the fragrance of roots, the fragrance of stems, and the fragrance of flowers. Or could there be a fragrance that spreads with the wind, against the wind, and both with and against the wind?'"

The Buddha told Ānanda: "So it is! So it is! There are three kinds of fragrance that spread with the wind and cannot spread against the wind: the fragrance of roots, the fragrance of stems, and the fragrance of flowers. Ānanda! There is indeed a fragrance that spreads with the wind, against the wind, and both with and against the wind. Ānanda, the fragrance that spreads with the wind, against the wind, and both with and against the wind is this: Ānanda, there are good men and good women who, in whatever city or village they dwell, accomplish the true Dharma. For the duration of their lives they do not kill, do not steal, do not commit sexual misconduct, do not speak falsely, and do not drink intoxicants. Such good men and good women, in the eight directions and above and below, are esteemed by virtuous people, and there is none who does not praise them, saying: 'In such-and-such a direction, in such-and-such a village, there are good men and good women who uphold the precepts in purity, who accomplish the true Dharma. For the duration of their lives they do not kill, and so forth, do not drink intoxicants.' Ānanda! This is what is called the fragrance that spreads with the wind, against the wind, and both with and against the wind."

Then the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "Not the fragrance of roots, stems, or flowers
> can spread against the wind.
> Only the virtuous man or woman,
> whose fragrance is the purity of morality,
> pervades in all directions, with and against the wind;
> there is none who does not know of it everywhere.
> Tagara, candana,
> utpala, and mallikā:
> among all such fragrances,
> the fragrance of morality is supreme.
> Candana and other such fragrances
> spread only within limited bounds.
> Only the fragrance of moral virtue
> flows, spreads, and rises up to the heavens.
> Those of such pure morality and fragrance,
> who are heedful and in right concentration,
> with right knowledge and complete liberation:
> Māra's path cannot enter them.
> This is called the path of safety;
> this path is indeed pure.
> Rightly directed toward wondrous meditation,
> severing all of Māra's bonds."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was gladdened, bowed, and departed.

---

### (1074)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was wandering among the people in the state of Magadha, together with a thousand bhikṣus, all of whom were formerly matted-hair ascetics who had gone forth, and all had attained arhatship. Their outflows were already ended, what was to be done had been done, they had cast off the heavy burden, had attained their own benefit, had exhausted all fetters of existence, possessed right knowledge, and were well liberated. They came to stay in the Supatiṭṭha Cetiya Staff Grove.

King Bimbisāra of Magadha heard that the World-Honored One was wandering among the people of Magadha and had arrived at the Supatiṭṭha Cetiya Staff Grove. Together with his minor kings, ministers, and attendants, with twelve thousand carriages, eighteen thousand horses, and an innumerable following on foot, the brāhmaṇas and householders of Magadha all accompanied him.

The king departed from the city of Rājagṛha and went to where the World-Honored One was, to pay him respect and make offerings. Arriving at the junction of the road, he dismounted from his carriage and proceeded on foot. Reaching the inner gate, he removed his five ornaments, taking off his crown, putting aside his parasol, removing his fan, setting aside his sword, and taking off his leather shoes. Arriving before the Buddha, he straightened his garments, bared his right shoulder, paid homage to the Buddha, circumambulated him three times to the right, announced his own clan name, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I am Bimbisāra, King of Magadha."

The Buddha told Bimbisāra: "So it is, Great King! You are Bimbisāra. You may take this seat and sit as is comfortable for you."

Then King Bimbisāra again bowed at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. The kings, great ministers, brāhmaṇas, and householders all bowed at the Buddha's feet and sat down in order.

At that time, Uruvilvā Kāśyapa was also present in the assembly.

Then the brāhmaṇas and householders of Magadha had this thought: "Does the Great Śramaṇa practice the holy life under Uruvilvā Kāśyapa? Or does Uruvilvā Kāśyapa practice the holy life under the Great Śramaṇa?"

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing the thoughts of the Magadhan brāhmaṇas and householders, spoke a verse in inquiry:

> "Uruvilvā Kāśyapa,
> seeing what benefit here
> did you abandon your former rites,
> the fire worship and such practices?
> Now you may explain its meaning:
> the reason you abandoned fire worship."

Uruvilvā Kāśyapa spoke a verse in reply to the Buddha:

> "Wealth, tastes, and other delights,
> women, the five desires' fruits:
> contemplating their future consequences,
> these are all great defilements.
> Therefore I entirely abandoned
> the former fire-worshipping rites."

Then the World-Honored One again spoke a verse in inquiry:

> "You are not attached to the world's
> wealth and the five flavors of form.
> Then what further of heavenly and human things have you abandoned?
> Kāśyapa, explain according to the meaning."

Kāśyapa again answered the World-Honored One in verse:

> "Seeing the Way free from residue,
> the quiescent path without remainder's trace,
> having nothing, unattached,
> with no other goal or other path:
> therefore I entirely abandoned
> the former fire-worshipping rites.
> Great assemblies and their observances,
> worshipping water and fire:
> foolish ones sank within these,
> seeking the path of liberation.
> Blind, without the eye of wisdom,
> heading toward birth, old age, sickness, and death,
> not seeing the correct path
> that forever leaves the way of birth and death.
> Now, for the first time, through the World-Honored One,
> I have seen the unconditioned path.
> By the power of the Great Dragon's teaching,
> I have crossed to the other shore.
> The Muni broadly saves and ferries over,
> comforting immeasurable beings.
> Now I truly know that Gautama
> is the truthful one who has transcended."

The Buddha again spoke a verse praising Kāśyapa:

> "Excellent, Kāśyapa!
> Previously you deliberated not unwisely,
> investigating step by step,
> and finally reached the supreme place.

"Now, Kāśyapa, you should put at ease the minds of your followers."

Then Uruvilvā Kāśyapa entered right concentration. By the power of his supernormal abilities, he rose into the eastern sky, performing the four kinds of supernatural transformations: walking, standing, sitting, and lying down. He entered the fire samādhi, and his entire body blazed with radiance in blue, yellow, red, white, and crystal-red colors. Water issued from the upper part of his body, fire issued from the lower part of his body, and the fire burned his body. Water issued from the upper part of his body to drench his body. Or fire issued from the upper part of his body to burn his body, and water issued from the lower part of his body to drench his body. Having displayed these various supernatural transformations, he descended, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The Buddha is my teacher; I am his disciple."

The Buddha told Kāśyapa: "I am your teacher; you are my disciple. Take your seat as is comfortable for you and sit down again."

Then Uruvilvā Kāśyapa returned to his former seat.

At that time, the brāhmaṇas and householders of Magadha had this thought: "Uruvilvā Kāśyapa has certainly been practicing the holy life under the Great Śramaṇa."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Bimbisāra of Magadha and the brāhmaṇas and householders, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and were gladdened, bowed, and departed.

---

### (1075)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, there was Dharmapāla Mallāputra, who had long resided in Rājagṛha, serving as the one who managed the Sangha's food and seating assignments, arranging invitations in proper order, not allowing anyone to skip the sequence.

At that time, there was a bhikṣu named Mettiya, who three times in succession received turns that led him to places with coarse food, making his mealtimes difficult and unpleasant. He thought: "How terrible! How painful! The bhikṣu Dharmapāla Mallāputra, out of personal bias, vexes me with coarse food, making my mealtimes extremely unpleasant. How can I do something to harm him?"

At that time, the bhikṣu Mettiya had a sister who was a bhikṣuṇī named Mittā, who dwelt in the Royal Park bhikṣuṇī community in Rājagṛha. The bhikṣuṇī Mittā came to visit the bhikṣu Mettiya, bowed at his feet, and stood to one side. The bhikṣu Mettiya did not look at her or speak with her. The bhikṣuṇī Mittā said to the bhikṣu Mettiya: "Venerable brother! Why do you not look at me or speak with me?"

The bhikṣu Mettiya said: "The bhikṣu Dharmapāla Mallāputra repeatedly vexes me with coarse food, making my mealtimes extremely unpleasant. And you too have abandoned me."

The bhikṣuṇī said: "What should I do?"

The bhikṣu Mettiya said: "You should go to the World-Honored One and say: 'World-Honored One! The bhikṣu Dharmapāla Mallāputra has done what is improper and unbecoming. He has committed the pārājika offense of unchaste conduct with me.' I will then testify and say: 'It is so, World-Honored One! It is as my sister has said.'"

The bhikṣuṇī said: "Venerable brother! How can I falsely accuse a bhikṣu who practices the holy life with a pārājika offense?"

The bhikṣu Mettiya said: "If you do not do this, I will sever relations with you. We will no longer come and go, speak together, or look upon each other."

Then the bhikṣuṇī pondered silently for a moment and then said: "Venerable brother! If you wish me to do so, I shall follow your instructions."

The bhikṣu Mettiya said: "Wait, let me go to the World-Honored One first, and then you come after me."

Then the bhikṣu Mettiya went, bowed his head at the World-Honored One's feet, and withdrew to stand to one side. The bhikṣuṇī Mittā then followed after, bowed her head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! How improper and unbecoming is Dharmapāla Mallāputra! He committed the pārājika offense of unchaste conduct with me."

The bhikṣu Mettiya then also said to the Buddha: "It is as my sister has said. I also knew of this beforehand."

At that time, the bhikṣu Dharmapāla Mallāputra was right there in the great assembly.

Then the World-Honored One told the bhikṣu Dharmapāla Mallāputra: "Did you hear what was said?"

The bhikṣu Dharmapāla Mallāputra said: "I have heard, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu Dharmapāla Mallāputra: "What do you say now?"

Dharmapāla Mallāputra said to the Buddha: "As the World-Honored One knows, as the Well-Gone One knows."

The Buddha told Dharmapāla Mallāputra: "You say, 'As the World-Honored One knows.' Now is not the time for that. If you remember, you should say you remember. If you do not remember, you should say you do not remember."

Dharmapāla Mallāputra said: "I do not recall any such thing."

At that time, the Venerable Rāhula was standing behind the Buddha, holding a fan and fanning the Buddha. He said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! How improper and unbecoming it is for this bhikṣuṇī to say: 'The Venerable Dharmapāla Mallāputra committed unchaste conduct with me.' And the bhikṣu Mettiya says: 'It is so, World-Honored One! I already knew beforehand, as my sister has said.'"

The Buddha told Rāhula: "I will now ask you; answer as you wish. If the bhikṣuṇī Mittā were to come and tell me: 'World-Honored One! How improper and unbecoming is Rāhula! He committed the pārājika offense of unchaste conduct with me.' And the bhikṣu Mettiya were to further say to me: 'It is so, World-Honored One! As my sister has said, I also knew beforehand.' What would you say?"

Rāhula said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! If I remembered, I would say I remember. If I did not remember, I would say I do not remember."

The Buddha said: "Rāhula! Even a foolish person like you can give such an answer. Why should Dharmapāla Mallāputra, a pure bhikṣu, not be able to give such an answer?"

Then the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Regarding the bhikṣu Dharmapāla Mallāputra, you should perform the act of recollection. Regarding the bhikṣuṇī Mittā, she should be dealt with by the procedure of self-confession. Regarding the bhikṣu Mettiya, the Sangha should thoroughly reprove and admonish him: 'What did you see? Where did you see it? For what reason did you go and see it?'"

Having given these instructions, the World-Honored One arose from his seat and entered his room to sit in meditation.

At that time, the bhikṣus performed the act of recollection for the bhikṣu Dharmapāla Mallāputra, applied the procedure of self-confession for the bhikṣuṇī Mittā, and thoroughly reproved and admonished the bhikṣu Mettiya, saying: "What did you see? Where did you see it? For what reason did you go and see it?"

When thus admonished, he said: "Dharmapāla Mallāputra did not commit unchaste conduct and did not commit the pārājika offense. However, the bhikṣu Dharmapāla Mallāputra three times frightened me with coarse, unpleasant food, making my mealtimes unpleasant. Out of attachment, anger, delusion, and fear toward the bhikṣu Dharmapāla Mallāputra, I made that statement. However, Dharmapāla Mallāputra is pure and without offense."

At that time, the World-Honored One, in the late afternoon, arose from meditation, came to the great assembly, spread a seat, and sat before them. The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! We have performed the act of recollection for the bhikṣu Dharmapāla Mallāputra, applied the procedure of self-confession for the bhikṣuṇī Mittā, and thoroughly reproved and admonished the bhikṣu Mettiya, until he said: 'Dharmapāla Mallāputra is pure and without offense.'"

Then the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "How foolish! Because of food, he knowingly spoke a deliberate lie." Then the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "If one can abandon this one dharma,
> knowingly speaking a deliberate lie,
> without reckoning on future lives,
> there is no evil he would not do.
> Better to eat a ball of hot iron,
> like blazing burning coals,
> than to break the precepts
> and eat the faithful offerings of the Sangha."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1076)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Venerable Dharmapāla Mallāputra went to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I wish to take parinirvāṇa in the Buddha's presence."

The World-Honored One was silent. He made this request three times. The Buddha told Dharmapāla Mallāputra: "These are conditioned formations; the Dharma is naturally thus."

Then the Venerable Dharmapāla Mallāputra, right there before the Buddha, entered samādhi. In accordance with his right attainment, he rose into the eastern sky, performing the four deportments: walking, standing, sitting, and lying down. He entered the fire samādhi, and fire issued from the lower part of his body. His entire body blazed with radiance, spreading light in all directions: blue, yellow, red, white, and crystal-red colors. Fire issued from the lower part of his body and burned his body. Water issued from the upper part of his body and sprinkled his body. Or fire issued from the upper part of his body to burn his body, and water issued from the lower part of his body to sprinkle his body. Throughout the ten directions, having displayed various supernatural transformations, he then, in the midst of the sky, brought forth fire from within his body, which burned his body of its own accord. He took the nirvāṇa without remainder, completely consumed and extinguished in silence, leaving not a trace of dust. Just as a lamp lit in the sky, when its oil and wick are both exhausted, Dharmapāla Mallāputra's nirvāṇa in the sky, his body and mind both exhausted, was likewise.

Then the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "Just as a burning ball of iron,
> its flames blazing intensely,
> the heat gradually subsides and ceases:
> none can know where it has gone.
> So too are those thus liberated,
> who have crossed the mire of afflictions.
> All streams are forever severed:
> none can know where they have gone.
> Having reached the unshakable state,
> they enter nirvāṇa without remainder."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1077)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was wandering among the people of the Aṅguttarāpa country. Passing through the Dhavajālikā forest, he saw cowherds, goatherds, gatherers of wood and grass, and various other workers. Seeing the World-Honored One traveling on the road, they all said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Do not go by this road! Ahead there is the bandit Aṅgulimāla, who terrifies and frightens people."

The Buddha told the people: "I have no fear." Having said this, he continued along the road. They warned him a second and third time, yet the World-Honored One still went on. From afar he saw Aṅgulimāla running toward him with sword and shield in hand. The World-Honored One, by his supernatural power, appeared to walk slowly, so that Aṅgulimāla, running at full speed, could not catch up. Exhausted from running, he called out from a distance to the World-Honored One: "Stop! Stop! Do not go!"

The World-Honored One continued walking and replied: "I am always stopped. It is you who do not stop."

Then Aṅgulimāla spoke this verse:

> "The śramaṇa is still walking swiftly,
> yet says 'I am always stopped.'
> I am now tired and have stopped,
> yet you say I have not stopped.
> Śramaṇa, explain what you mean:
> how are you stopped and I not stopped?"

Then the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "Aṅgulimāla, when I say
> I am always stopped, I mean
> that toward all living beings
> I have laid down the sword and staff.
> You terrorize living beings;
> your evil deeds do not cease.
> I, toward all creatures,
> have laid down the sword and staff.
> You, toward all creatures,
> constantly oppress and terrorize them.
> Creating ferocious evil deeds,
> you never have a moment of rest.
> I, toward all spirits,
> have laid down the sword and staff.
> You, toward all spirits,
> through the long night cause suffering and oppression.
> Creating dark evil deeds,
> even now you do not cease.
> I abide in the dharma of cessation,
> fully heedful in all things.
> You do not see the Four Noble Truths,
> and therefore do not cease from heedlessness."

Aṅgulimāla spoke a verse to the Buddha:

> "Long has it been since I have seen a muni,
> so I followed along the road in pursuit.
> Now hearing this true and wondrous teaching,
> I shall abandon my long-held evil.
> Having spoken thus,
> he immediately cast aside his sword and shield,
> threw himself at the World-Honored One's feet,
> and begged to be allowed to go forth.
> The Buddha, with a compassionate mind,
> the Great Sage of much pity,
> said to the bhikṣu: 'Welcome, come!'
> And he went forth and received full ordination."

At that time, after Aṅgulimāla had gone forth, dwelling alone in a quiet place, practicing with focused diligence, he contemplated: "The reason a son of good family shaves his beard and hair, dons the kāṣāya robes, and with right faith leaves the household life to go forth and train in the Way, advancing in the holy life, is to know and realize for oneself in the present Dharma: 'My births are ended, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further becoming.'"

At that time, Aṅgulimāla attained arhatship, and experiencing the joy and happiness of liberation, he spoke this verse:

> "Originally given the name 'Harmless,'
> yet in the midst I committed much killing.
> Now I have obtained the name 'Seeing Truth,'
> forever freed from harming and killing.
> My bodily actions are free from killing;
> my speech and mind are likewise.
> Know that this is true non-killing:
> not oppressing any living being.
> My hands, once constantly stained with blood,
> bore the name Aṅgulimāla.
> Swept away by the rushing torrent,
> the Three Refuges brought me to a halt.
> Having taken refuge in the Three Jewels,
> I went forth and received full ordination,
> accomplished the threefold knowledge;
> what the Buddha taught, I have done.
> Oxen are tamed with whips and rods,
> elephants are subdued with iron hooks.
> Not by swords, whips, or rods,
> but through right means are gods and humans guided.
> Sharp swords are ground with water and stone,
> straight arrows are tempered with warm fire,
> timber is shaped with axe and adze,
> but the self is tamed with wise discernment.
> Those who formerly practiced heedlessly,
> if afterward they can restrain themselves:
> they illuminate the world
> like the moon emerging from the clouds.
> Those who formerly practiced heedlessly,
> if afterward they can restrain themselves:
> amid the world's stream of love and attachment,
> with right mindfulness they transcend it.
> Those who go forth in youth,
> diligently cultivating the Buddha's teaching:
> they illuminate the world
> like the moon emerging from the clouds.
> Those who go forth in youth,
> diligently cultivating the Buddha's teaching:
> amid the world's stream of love and attachment,
> with right mindfulness they transcend it.
> If one has committed evil deeds,
> yet right goodness can extinguish them:
> such a one illuminates the world
> like the moon emerging from the clouds.
> If one formerly committed evil deeds,
> yet right goodness can extinguish them:
> amid the world's stream of love and attachment,
> with right mindfulness one can transcend it.
> I had already committed evil deeds
> and was surely heading for evil destinies.
> Having already received the evil retribution,
> my former debts have been paid and consumed.
> If those who are my enemies
> hear this correct Dharma,
> obtain the pure Dharma-eye,
> and practice forbearance toward me,
> no longer raising disputes or quarrels,
> it is through the power of the Buddha's grace.
> My enemies practice forbearance,
> and I too always praise forbearance.
> Whenever I hear the correct Dharma,
> upon hearing it I practice accordingly."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Aṅgulimāla, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1078)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, there was a certain bhikṣu who, at the time when the dawn signs appeared at night, went out to the bank of the Tapodā River, removed his robes and placed them on the bank, entered the water to bathe. After bathing, he came up to the bank, put on a single robe, and waited for his body to dry.

At that time, a certain devatā, radiating light from his body, illuminated the area by the Tapodā River, and said to the bhikṣu: "You are young, having gone forth, with fresh white skin and dark hair. At the very beginning of the prime of youth, you should enjoy the five desires, adorn yourself with garlands, anoint yourself with fragrant perfumes and floral wreaths, and amuse yourself with the five pleasures. Yet at such a time, you have left your relatives behind, wept and parted in sorrow, shaved your beard and hair, donned the kāṣāya robes, and with right faith left the household life to go forth and train in the Way. How can you abandon present happiness and seek profit that is not yet timely?"

The bhikṣu replied: "I have not abandoned present happiness to seek untimely happiness. Rather, I have abandoned untimely happiness and obtained present happiness."

The devatā asked the bhikṣu: "How have you abandoned untimely happiness and obtained present happiness?"

The bhikṣu replied: "As the World-Honored One has taught, the desires of the untimely have little pleasure and much suffering, little benefit and much difficulty. Now, in the present Dharma, I have already left behind the burning. Without waiting for any season, I can penetrate it myself, directly observing in the present, and know it through my own awareness. Thus, devatā, this is what is called abandoning untimely happiness and obtaining present happiness."

The devatā again asked the bhikṣu: "How is it again that the Tathāgata has said that the desires of the untimely have little pleasure and much suffering? And how is it again that the Tathāgata has said there is benefit and happiness in the present Dharma, and so forth, down to knowing through one's own awareness?"

The bhikṣu replied: "I went forth at a young age and am unable to broadly proclaim the correct Dharma and Vinaya as taught by the Tathāgata. The World-Honored One is nearby in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. You may go to the Tathāgata and ask him about your doubts. As the World-Honored One explains, accordingly remember and retain it."

The devatā then said: "Bhikṣu! Around the Tathāgata, there are many powerful devatās surrounding him. Since I have no prior question arranged, it would not be easy for me to approach. Bhikṣu, if you could first announce me to the World-Honored One, I could follow along."

The bhikṣu replied: "I shall go on your behalf."

The devatā said to the bhikṣu: "Very well, venerable one. I shall follow behind."

Then that bhikṣu went to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and fully reported to the World-Honored One the questions and answers exchanged with the devatā: "Now, World-Honored One, if that devatā speaks truthfully, he should arrive shortly. If he is not truthful, then naturally he will not come."

At that time, the devatā called out from afar to the bhikṣu: "I am already here! I am already here!"

Then the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "Beings follow thoughts of love,
> and abide through thoughts of love.
> Because they do not understand love,
> this becomes the means of death."

The Buddha told the devatā: "If you understand this verse, you may ask your question."

The devatā said to the Buddha: "I do not understand, World-Honored One! I do not understand, Well-Gone One!"

The Buddha again spoke a verse and told the devatā:

> "If one understands what is loved,
> one does not give rise to love for it.
> When there is nothing of either this or that,
> no other person can speak of it."

The Buddha told the devatā: "If you understand this meaning, you may ask your question."

The devatā said to the Buddha: "I do not understand, World-Honored One! I do not understand, Well-Gone One!"

The Buddha again spoke this verse:

> "When one perceives others as equal, superior, or inferior,
> then disputes and arguments arise.
> When these three do not shake one,
> there is no soft, middling, or high."

The Buddha told the devatā: "If you understand this meaning, then you may ask your question."

The devatā said to the Buddha: "I do not understand, World-Honored One! I do not understand, Well-Gone One!"

The Buddha again spoke this verse:

> "Sever craving and name-and-form,
> remove conceit, without any binding.
> Tranquil, with anger extinguished,
> freed from fetters, abandoning all hope:
> one sees neither humans nor gods,
> neither in this world nor in the next."

The Buddha told the devatā: "If you understand this meaning, then you may ask your question."

The devatā said to the Buddha: "I understand, World-Honored One! I understand, Well-Gone One!"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the devatā, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was gladdened, and immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1079)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu, during the latter part of the night, went to the bank of the Tapodā River, removed his robes and placed them on the bank, entered the water to bathe. After bathing, he returned to the bank, put on a single robe, and waited for his body to dry.

At that time, a certain devatā, radiating light from his body, illuminated the area by the Tapodā River, and said to the bhikṣu: "Bhikṣu! Bhikṣu! This is a burial mound. At night it gives off smoke; by day it burns with fire. That brāhmaṇa, upon seeing this, said: 'Break open this burial mound!' The digger is wise; take up the sword. He then found a great turtle. The brāhmaṇa, upon seeing this, said: 'Remove this great turtle!' The digger is wise; take up the sword. He then found a carpet. The brāhmaṇa, upon seeing this, said: 'Remove this carpet!' The digger is wise; take up the sword. He then found a lump of flesh. The brāhmaṇa, upon seeing this, said: 'Remove this lump of flesh!' The digger is wise; take up the sword. He then found a slaughterhouse. The brāhmaṇa, upon seeing this, said: 'Destroy this slaughterhouse!' The digger is wise; take up the sword. He then found a laṅgī. The brāhmaṇa, upon seeing this, said: 'Remove this laṅgī!' The digger is wise; take up the sword. He then found a fork in the road. The brāhmaṇa, upon seeing this, said: 'Remove this fork in the road!' The digger is wise; take up the sword. He then found a door panel. The brāhmaṇa, upon seeing this, said: 'Remove this door panel!' The digger is wise; take up the sword. He then found a great dragon. The brāhmaṇa, upon seeing this, said: 'Stop! Do not remove the great dragon. You should show it respect.' Bhikṣu! Come, take this discourse and go ask the World-Honored One. As the Buddha explains it, you should accordingly retain it. Why? Except for the Tathāgata, I see no one among the devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, or brāhmaṇas in the world who would be delighted in mind by this discourse. If any disciple has heard it from me, then afterward they can explain it."

At that time, the bhikṣu, having heard this discourse from that devatā, went to where the World-Honored One was, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and broadly asked the World-Honored One about all the questions posed by the devatā: "What is the burial mound? What is meant by 'at night it gives off smoke'? What is meant by 'by day it burns with fire'? What is the brāhmaṇa? What is the digging? What is the wise one? What is the sword? What is the great turtle? What is the carpet? What is the lump of flesh? What is the slaughterhouse? What is the laṅgī? What is the fork in the road? What is the door panel? What is the great dragon?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "The burial mound refers to the body of a sentient being, the coarse material form of the four great elements, the body inherited from parents, nourished by food, clothed and covered, bathed and rubbed and adorned, sustained and maintained: all of which is subject to change, destruction, wearing away, and perishing. 'Smoke arising at night' means that a person at night gives rise to thoughts and mental investigations. 'By day carrying out those plans' means bodily deeds and verbal deeds. The brāhmaṇa refers to the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Enlightened One. The digging refers to energetic effort and diligent practice. The wise one refers to the learned noble disciple. The sword refers to the sword of wisdom. The great turtle refers to the five hindrances. The carpet refers to anger and resentment. The lump of flesh refers to stinginess and jealousy. The slaughterhouse refers to the five kinds of sensual pleasure. The laṅgī refers to ignorance. The fork in the road refers to doubt and uncertainty. The door panel refers to the conceit of 'I.' The great dragon refers to the arhat whose outflows are ended. Thus, bhikṣu, whatever the Great Teacher has done for his disciples, out of compassion and pity, comforting them with meaning and bringing them to peace, this has already been done for you. What you ought to do, you should do: in the open air, in the forest, in empty dwellings, in mountain valleys and caves, spreading grass and tree leaves, practicing contemplation and meditation, not giving rise to heedlessness, lest you later have regrets. This is my instruction in accordance with the teaching."

Then he spoke this verse:

> "The body is called the burial mound;
> thoughts and investigations are the smoke arising at night.
> Daytime deeds are the fire burning.
> The brāhmaṇa is the Perfectly Enlightened One.
> Diligent effort is the digging.
> The discerning and wise one is the intelligent person.

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.
