# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Thirty-Nine

---

### (1081)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī in the Deer Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One, in the early morning, put on his robes and took up his bowl, and entered the city of Vārāṇasī to beg for food. At that time, there was a certain bhikṣu who had also put on his robes and taken up his bowl, and entered the city to beg for food. On the roadside, he stood beneath a tree and gave rise to unwholesome thoughts, relying on evil craving.

At that time, the World-Honored One saw that bhikṣu standing beneath a tree, having given rise to unwholesome thoughts, relying on evil craving, and said to him: "Bhikṣu! Bhikṣu! Do not plant seeds of suffering that send forth a foul stench, with putrid fluid oozing out. If a bhikṣu plants seeds of suffering, sending forth a foul stench with putrid fluid oozing out, and hopes that maggots and flies will not swarm upon him, that is impossible!"

At that time, that bhikṣu thought: "The World-Honored One knows the evil thoughts of my mind." He immediately became frightened, and his hairs stood on end.

At that time, the World-Honored One, having entered the city and finished begging for food, returned to the monastery, put away his robes and bowl, washed his feet, and entered his room to sit in meditation. In the late afternoon, he emerged from meditation, went to the Sangha, spread his seat before the assembly, sat down, and told the bhikṣus: "This morning, I put on my robes and took up my bowl, and entered the city to beg for food. I saw a bhikṣu standing beneath a tree, having given rise to unwholesome thoughts, relying on evil craving. Seeing this, I immediately said to him: 'Bhikṣu! Bhikṣu! Do not plant seeds of suffering that send forth a foul stench, with putrid fluid oozing out. If a bhikṣu plants seeds of suffering, sending forth a foul stench with putrid fluid oozing out, and hopes that maggots and flies will not swarm upon him, that is impossible!' At that time, that bhikṣu thought: 'The Buddha already knows the thoughts of my mind.' Ashamed and frightened, his mind alarmed and hairs standing on end, he went on his way."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, bared his right shoulder, joined his palms and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! What are the seeds of suffering? What is the foul stench? What is the oozing fluid? What are the maggots and flies?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Anger and resentful vexation are called the seeds of suffering. The five objects of desire are called the foul stench. Not guarding the sense faculties at the six sense-contact bases is called the oozing fluid. When one does not restrain the sense-contact bases, covetousness, grief, and various evil unwholesome mental states arise and swarm; this is called the maggots and flies." At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke in verse:

> "When ears and eyes are left unguarded,
> craving arises from these;
> this is called the seed of suffering,
> with foul stench and fluid seeping forth.
> The flavors of various thoughts and reflections
> rely upon evil craving;
> whether in villages or in empty places,
> whether by day or by night,
> one should distance oneself and cultivate the holy life,
> to reach the final end of suffering.
> If the inner mind is tranquil,
> with decisive, clear understanding,
> lying down or awake, one is always at ease;
> the evil maggots and flies are extinguished.
> What the noble ones frequent,
> is the well-spoken path of the noble sages.
> Fully knowing the Noble Eightfold Path,
> one does not return to receive another body."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (1082)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One, in the early morning, put on his robes and took up his bowl, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. Having finished eating, he returned to the monastery, washed his feet, and entered the Andha Grove to sit in meditation.

At that time, there was a certain bhikṣu who likewise in the early morning put on his robes and took up his bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. Having finished eating, he returned to the monastery, washed his feet, entered the Andha Grove, sat beneath a tree, and entered into daytime meditative attainment. While this bhikṣu was in his daytime meditative attainment, evil, unwholesome thoughts arose, relying on a mind of craving.

At that time, a heavenly spirit who dwelled in the Andha Grove thought: "This bhikṣu is improper and unseemly. He sits in meditation in the Andha Grove yet gives rise to unwholesome thoughts, his mind relying on evil craving. I should go and reproach him." Having thought this, he went and said to the bhikṣu: "Bhikṣu! Bhikṣu! Are you making a sore?"

The bhikṣu answered: "I shall treat it and make it heal!"

The heavenly spirit said to the bhikṣu: "The sore is like an iron cauldron; how can it be healed?"

The bhikṣu answered: "Right mindfulness and right awareness are sufficient to make it heal."

The heavenly spirit said: "Excellent! Excellent! This is truly a wise treatment for the sore. Treating a sore in this way, it can ultimately be healed and will never recur."

At that time, the World-Honored One, in the late afternoon, emerged from meditation, returned to Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, entered the Sangha, spread his seat before the great assembly, sat down, and told the bhikṣus: "This morning I put on my robes and took up my bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After begging for food, I returned and went to the Andha Grove to sit in meditation, entering daytime meditative attainment. A certain bhikṣu also, after begging for food, returned to the Andha Grove, sat beneath a tree, and entered daytime meditative attainment. But that bhikṣu gave rise to unwholesome thoughts, his mind relying on evil craving. A heavenly spirit dwelling in the Andha Grove said to the bhikṣu: 'Bhikṣu! Bhikṣu! Are you making a sore?'" As was spoken in full above, up to: "'Thus, bhikṣu! Excellent! Excellent! This is a cure of the wise.'" At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke in verse:

> "A man creates a sore,
> giving rise to his own suffering;
> longing for worldly desires,
> his mind relies on evil craving.
> Because a sore has been made,
> maggots and flies swarm to gather;
> craving and desire are the sore,
> maggots and flies are the evil thoughts,
> along with all the craving minds;
> all of these arise from the mind,
> boring and drilling into a man's heart,
> seeking flowers of fame and profit.
> The fire of desire blazes ever more fiercely,
> with deluded, unwholesome thoughts;
> body and mind grow weaker day and night,
> far removed from the path of tranquility.
> If the inner mind is tranquil,
> with decisive wisdom, clear understanding,
> there will be no such sore;
> one sees the Buddha's path of peace and safety.
> The path that noble ones travel,
> the noble sages have well proclaimed;
> the path known by the wise,
> one does not again receive further existence."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (1083)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pool, in the Double-Storied Hall.

At that time, there were many bhikṣus who in the early morning put on their robes, took up their bowls, and entered Vaiśālī to beg for food.

At that time, there was a young bhikṣu who had gone forth not long before and was not well-versed in the Dharma and Vinaya. When it was time to beg for food, he did not know the proper sequence and order. Other bhikṣus, seeing this, said to him: "You are young and have gone forth not long ago. You do not yet know the Dharma and Vinaya. Do not skip ahead! Do not double back! Do not lose the proper sequence in going to beg for food, or you will long suffer with no benefit."

The young bhikṣu said: "The senior monks also skip the order and do not follow the proper sequence. It is not only I who do so." He said this again and again, and they could not make him stop.

When the many bhikṣus had finished begging for food, they returned to the monastery, put away their robes and bowls, washed their feet, went to the Buddha, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! This morning we put on our robes and took up our bowls and entered Vaiśālī to beg for food. There was a young bhikṣu who had gone forth in this Dharma and Vinaya not long ago. When going to beg for food, he did not follow the proper order, going back and forth and doubling over. The bhikṣus admonished him repeatedly, but he would not accept it, and said: 'The senior monks also do not follow the order; why do you reproach me?' We bhikṣus admonished him three times, but he would not accept it. Therefore we have come to report to the World-Honored One. We only wish that the World-Honored One, out of compassion, would remove this improper practice!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "It is like a great lake in an empty marsh, where a great dragon elephant dwells. It pulls up lotus roots, washes off the mud, and then eats them. Having eaten, its body is plump and joyful, with great strength and great ease. Because of this, it always dwells in happiness. There is an elephant of another species, of thin and small build, which imitates that dragon elephant and pulls up lotus roots but cannot wash them clean, and eats them together with the mud. Having eaten, the food is not digested, its body is not plump and joyful, and it grows ever weaker, until because of this it dies, or suffers as if dying.

"In the same way, bhikṣus of long-standing virtue who have practiced the path for a long time, who do not delight in frivolity, who have long cultivated the holy life, praised by the Great Teacher, and also praised by other wise practitioners of the holy life: such bhikṣus, dwelling in dependence upon towns and villages, in the early morning put on their robes, take up their bowls, and enter the city to beg for food. They well protect their body and speech, well restrain their sense faculties, focus their minds with mindful attention, and are able to make those who do not have faith gain faith, and those who have faith not waver. If they obtain material gains: robes, food and drink, bedding, or medicines, they are not defiled, not attached, not covetous, not craving, not confused, not pursuing. They see the danger in such things and see the way to liberation from them. Yet they still eat. Having eaten, their body and mind are refreshed, they gain health and strength, and because of this they always obtain ease and happiness.

"But that young bhikṣu, who went forth not long ago and is not well-versed in the Dharma and Vinaya, depending on the senior monks, dwelling in dependence upon villages, puts on his robes, takes up his bowl, and enters the village to beg for food. He does not well protect his body, does not guard the gates of his senses, does not focus his mind with attention. He is unable to make those who do not have faith gain faith, or those who have faith not waver. If he obtains material gains: robes, food and drink, bedding, or medicines, he is defiled, attached, covetous, and pursuing. He does not see the danger, does not see the way out. With a mind of craving desire he eats, and the food cannot make his body refreshed, at ease, and happy. Because of this food, he moves toward death, or suffering like death. What is meant by 'death' is abandoning the precepts and returning to lay life, losing the true Dharma and true Vinaya. What is meant by 'suffering like death' is transgressing the true Dharma and Vinaya, not recognizing the nature of one's offenses, and not knowing how to remove offenses." At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke in verse:

> "The dragon elephant pulls up lotus roots,
> washes them in water and eats them;
> an elephant of another species imitates it,
> eating them mixed with mud.
> Because of eating with mud,
> it grows sick and weak and finally dies."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (1084)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Cool Forest, among the burial mounds.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Life is extremely brief and quickly proceeds to the next world. One should diligently practice wholesome dharmas and cultivate the holy life. There is no one who is born and does not die, yet people in the world do not diligently apply themselves to exclusively cultivating wholesome dharmas, cultivating what is virtuous and what is beneficial."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Cool Forest, among the burial mounds, and is teaching the Dharma to his śrāvakas in this way: 'Human life is extremely brief,' and so forth up to 'not cultivating what is virtuous and what is beneficial.' I shall now go there and cause disturbance."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas transformed himself into a young man, went and stood before the Buddha, and spoke in verse:

> "Always pressing upon beings,
> they obtain long life in the human realm;
> with confused and intoxicated, heedless minds,
> they also do not head toward death."

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "This is the evil Māra who has come to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "Always pressing upon beings,
> who receive birth with extremely short lives;
> one should diligently cultivate vigor,
> as if extinguishing a fire upon one's head.
> Do not be idle for even a moment,
> lest the demon of death suddenly arrive.
> I know you are the evil Māra;
> quickly vanish from here."

The heavenly Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Ashamed, grieved and sorrowful, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1085)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Cool Forest, among the burial mounds.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "All formations are impermanent, all formations are unstable, not at ease, not restful, and are of the nature of change. One should therefore cease all conditioned formations, become disenchanted, take no delight, and be liberated."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is now dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Cool Forest, and is teaching the Dharma to his śrāvakas in this way: 'All formations are impermanent, unstable, not restful, and are of the nature of change; one should therefore cease all conditioned activities, become disenchanted, take no delight, and be liberated.' I shall now go there and cause disturbance." He immediately transformed into a young man, went to the Buddha, stood before the Buddha, and spoke in verse:

> "Life flows on day and night,
> never coming to an end;
> life shall come and go,
> just like a turning wheel."

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "This is the evil Māra who wishes to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "Day and night constantly flowing on,
> lifespan also diminishes accordingly;
> human life gradually wastes away,
> like the water of a small stream.
> I know you are the evil Māra;
> go and vanish on your own."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Ashamed, grieved and sorrowful, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1086)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One rose during the night and practiced walking meditation. In the last watch of the night, he washed his feet, entered his room, composed his body in proper sitting posture, and focused his mind with mindful attention.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is now at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. He rose during the night and practiced walking meditation. In the last watch of the night, he washed his feet, entered his room, sat upright with proper posture, and is engaged in mindful meditation. I shall now go there and cause disturbance." He immediately transformed into a young man, stood before the Buddha, and spoke in verse:

> "My mind in the empty sky,
> holds a long rope as a snare cast down;
> I intend to bind this śramaṇa,
> and not allow you to escape."

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "The evil Māra Pāpīyas wishes to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "I say that in this world,
> there are the five desires and the mind as the sixth;
> from these I have forever departed,
> and all suffering has been cut off.
> I have left behind those desires,
> and mental consciousness has also ceased.
> Pāpīyas, I know you;
> quickly vanish from here."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa already knows my mind." Ashamed, grieved and sorrowful, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1087)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One rose during the night and practiced walking meditation. In the last watch of the night, he washed his feet, entered his room, lay down on his right side to rest, mindfully fixed on the sign of light, with right mindfulness and right awareness, making the perception of rising.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is now dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove," and so forth up to "making the perception of rising. I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He immediately transformed into a young man, went and stood before the Buddha, and spoke in verse:

> "Why sleep? Why do you sleep?
> Having ceased, why sleep again?
> In an empty hut, why sleep?
> Having attained release, why sleep again?"

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "The evil Māra Pāpīyas wishes to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "Through the net of craving one is bound in attachment;
> without craving, who could carry one away?
> When all that remains has been exhausted,
> only the Buddha attains peaceful sleep.
> You, evil Māra Pāpīyas,
> what is there here for you to say?"

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Ashamed, grieved and sorrowful, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1088)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

At that time, the World-Honored One, during the dark of night, when there was a light drizzle and lightning flashed, came out of his room to practice walking meditation.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is now dwelling at Rājagṛha on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa. During the dark night with light rain and flashing lightning, he has come out of his room to practice walking meditation. I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He took a great boulder and, tossing it between his two hands, came before the Buddha and crushed it into fine dust.

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "The evil Māra Pāpīyas wishes to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "Even if Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa
> were smashed to pieces before me,
> for a Buddha who is fully liberated,
> it could not stir a single hair.
> Even if within the four seas,
> all the mountains and the earth,
> kinsmen of heedlessness,
> were ground to dust,
> they still could not shake
> even a single hair of the Tathāgata."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1089)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

At that time, the World-Honored One rose during the night and practiced walking meditation. In the last watch of the night, he washed his feet, entered his room, sat upright with proper posture, and fixed his mindful attention before him.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is now dwelling at Rājagṛha on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa. He rose during the night and practiced walking meditation. In the last watch of the night, he entered his room, sat upright with proper posture, and fixed his mindful attention before him. I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He immediately transformed into a great dragon, which coiled around the Buddha's body seven times, raised its head over the top of the Buddha's head. Its body was like a great ship, its head like a great sail, its eyes like copper furnaces, its tongue like trailing lightning, and the sound of its breathing in and out was like thunder and hail.

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "The evil Māra Pāpīyas wishes to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "Just as an empty house,
> the mind of the sage is void and still;
> one may revolve within it,
> and the Buddha's body is likewise.
> Countless fierce and malicious dragons,
> mosquitoes, gadflies, flies, and fleas,
> may all gather and feed upon his body,
> yet cannot stir a single hair.
> Even if the sky were rent apart,
> and the great earth overturned,
> and all classes of beings
> came to create terror,
> with swords, spears, lances, and sharp arrows,
> all coming to harm the Buddha's body,
> such violent attacks
> cannot injure even a single hair."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1090)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha on Mount Vaibhāra, in the Stone Cave of the Seven-Leaf Tree Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One rose during the night and sat or practiced walking meditation in the open air. In the last watch of the night, he washed his feet, entered his room, settled his body to rest, lying on his right side with one foot upon the other, mindfully fixed on the sign of light, with right mindfulness and right awareness, making the perception of rising.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling at Rājagṛha on Mount Vaibhāra, in the Stone Cave of the Seven-Leaf Tree Grove. He rose during the night and sat or walked in the open air. In the last watch of the night, he washed his feet, entered his room and sat, then lay on his right side to rest, with one foot upon the other, mindfully fixed on the sign of light, with right mindfulness and right awareness, making the perception of rising. I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He transformed into a young man, went and stood before the Buddha, and spoke in verse:

> "Do you sleep because of me?
> Or is it because this is the final rest?
> Having much wealth and treasure,
> why do you keep to an empty wilderness?
> Alone and without companions,
> you indulge in sleep."

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "The evil Māra Pāpīyas wishes to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "I do not sleep because of you,
> nor is this the final rest;
> nor do I have much wealth,
> I have only gathered the treasure of freedom from sorrow.
> Out of compassion for the world,
> I lie on my right side to rest.
> Awake, I have no doubt or confusion;
> asleep, I have no fear.
> Whether by day or by night,
> there is neither increase nor decrease.
> Out of compassion for beings, I sleep,
> therefore there is no loss.
> Even if a hundred spears
> were driven through my body, constantly piercing,
> I would still sleep in peace and ease,
> for I have already left behind the inner spear."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1091)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha on Mount Vaibhāra, in the Stone Cave of the Seven-Leaf Tree Grove.

At that time, there was a venerable one named Godhika, who was dwelling at Rājagṛha on the side of the Sages' Mountain, in the Black Rock Cave. Alone, in contemplation, practicing without heedlessness, diligently cultivating for his own benefit, he attained the temporary liberation of mind realized through the body, but repeatedly fell back from it. One, two, three, four, five, six times he fell back, and then again attained the temporary liberation of mind realized through the body, and again fell back.

The Venerable Godhika then thought: "Alone in a quiet place, contemplating, practicing without heedlessness, diligently cultivating for my own benefit, I attain the temporary liberation of mind realized through the body, but repeatedly fall back, up to six times, and still I fall back. I should now kill myself with a knife, lest I fall back a seventh time."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling at Rājagṛha on the side of Mount Vaibhāra, in the Stone Cave of the Seven-Leaf Tree Grove. He has a disciple named Godhika, dwelling at Rājagṛha on the side of the Sages' Mountain, in the Black Rock Cave. Alone in a quiet place, with focused contemplation, he attained the temporary liberation of mind realized through the body, but fell back six times and then again attained it. He has thought: 'I have already fallen back six times and then again attained it; lest I fall back a seventh time, I would rather kill myself with a knife than fall back a seventh time.' If that bhikṣu kills himself with a knife, do not let him kill himself and escape beyond my domain. I shall now go and inform his great teacher."

At that time, Pāpīyas, holding a lapis lazuli-handled lute, went to the World-Honored One and, strumming the strings, spoke in verse:

> "O one of great wisdom and great skillful means,
> with mastery and great spiritual power,
> you have obtained a blazing disciple,
> yet now he wishes to take his own life.
> O great sage, you should restrain him;
> do not let him kill himself!
> How is it that the World-Honored One,
> in the true Dharma and Vinaya, a śrāvaka
> should seek what he has not attained,
> and take his own life?"

At that time, after Māra spoke this verse, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Pāpīyas, sower of heedlessness,
> you have come on your own business.
> A man of firm resolve and perfect qualities
> always abides in wondrous meditation.
> Day and night he cultivates with vigor,
> not regarding his own life.
> Seeing the three realms of existence as fearful,
> he has cut off craving and desire.
> Having already vanquished Māra's army,
> Godhika has attained parinirvāṇa.
> Pāpīyas, your heart grieves,
> and the lute falls to the ground.
> Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow,
> he immediately vanished and was no longer seen."

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "All of you, come. Let us go together to the side of the Sages' Mountain, to the Black Rock Cave, and see the bhikṣu Godhika who has killed himself with a knife."

At that time, the World-Honored One, together with many bhikṣus, went to the side of the Sages' Mountain, to the Black Rock Cave, and saw the bhikṣu Godhika, having killed himself, lying on the ground. He told the bhikṣus: "Do you see this bhikṣu Godhika, having killed himself, lying on the ground?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "Indeed we see him, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Do you see the dark black smoke rising from all around the body of the bhikṣu Godhika, filling the four directions?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "We have seen it, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "This is the evil Māra Pāpīyas, who at the side of the good man Godhika's body is searching all around for his consciousness-spirit. However, the bhikṣu Godhika, with a mind that does not abide, took a knife and killed himself."

At that time, the World-Honored One gave the bhikṣu Godhika the foremost prediction.

At that time, Pāpīyas spoke in verse:

> "Above, below, and in all directions,
> I searched everywhere for his consciousness-spirit;
> I could not find its location anywhere.
> Where has Godhika gone?"

At that time, the World-Honored One again spoke in verse:

> "Such a man of firm resolve,
> who sought nothing at all,
> having uprooted the root of attachments and love,
> Godhika has attained parinirvāṇa."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (1092)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the village of Uruvilvā, beside the Nairañjanā River, beneath the Bodhi Tree, not long after attaining Buddhahood.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is now dwelling at the village of Uruvilvā, beside the Nairañjanā River, beneath the Bodhi Tree, not long after attaining Buddhahood. I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He immediately transformed into a young man, went and stood before the Buddha, and spoke in verse:

> "Alone you have entered an empty place,
> in meditation, quietly contemplating.
> Having already abandoned a kingdom and its treasures,
> what do you seek here?
> If you seek the gain of villages,
> why do you not associate with people?
> Since you do not associate with people,
> what will you finally obtain?"

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "The evil Māra Pāpīyas wishes to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "Having already obtained great gain,
> my aspiration is fulfilled in peaceful cessation.
> Having vanquished Māra's army,
> I do not cling to form and desire.
> Alone I meditate,
> partaking of the wondrous bliss of meditation.
> Therefore I do not associate
> and mingle with people."

Māra again spoke in verse:

> "Gautama, if you yourself know
> the path of peace, nirvāṇa,
> and alone delight in the bliss of non-action,
> why do you forcibly convert others?"

The Buddha again spoke a verse in reply:

> "From a place not under Māra's control,
> they come to ask about reaching the other shore.
> I then answer them correctly,
> enabling them to attain nirvāṇa.
> When they attain unceasing diligence,
> they are no longer under Māra's power."

Māra again spoke in verse:

> "There is a stone that resembles congealed fat;
> a flying bird comes wishing to eat it,
> but in the end gets no flavor,
> and with a damaged beak returns to empty sky.
> I am now just like that,
> returning in vain to the heavenly palace."

Having said this, Māra inwardly harbored grief and sorrow, his mind filled with remorse. He hung his head, prostrated on the ground, and drew lines on the earth with his finger.

Māra had three daughters. The first was named Desire, the second was named Delight, and the third was named Pleasure. They came to Pāpīyas and spoke in verse:

> "Father, why are you so distressed?
> What man is worth worrying about?
> With the rope of desire and love,
> we shall bind him like a tamed elephant,
> lead him before our father,
> and make him submit to father's power."

Māra answered his daughters:

> "He has already left behind attachments and love;
> he cannot be lured by desire.
> He has already gone beyond Māra's domain;
> this is why I grieve."

Then the three daughters of Māra, their bodies radiating blazing light like lightning in the clouds, went to the Buddha, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "We now take refuge at the World-Honored One's feet, to serve and attend upon you."

At that time, the World-Honored One paid them no regard whatsoever.

He knew that the Tathāgata had departed from all craving and desire, his mind being well liberated. Thus a second and a third time they spoke.

At that time, the three daughters of Māra said to one another: "Men have all kinds of desires corresponding to different forms. Let us now each transform ourselves into a hundred kinds of young maiden forms, a hundred kinds of newly-wed forms, a hundred kinds of forms of women who have not yet given birth, a hundred kinds of forms of women who have already given birth, a hundred kinds of middle-aged forms, and a hundred kinds of elderly forms. Making these various kinds of forms, let us go to the śramaṇa Gautama and say: 'We now entirely take refuge at your feet, to serve and attend upon you.'"

Having made this plan, they immediately made various transformations, as described above, went to the World-Honored One, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! We today take refuge at your feet, to serve and attend upon you."

At that time, the World-Honored One paid them no regard whatsoever.

"The Tathāgata's Dharma has departed from all craving and desire." Thus they spoke a second and a third time.

Then the three daughters of Māra said to one another: "If a man who has not departed from desire were to see our various wondrous bodies, his mind would become confused and bewildered, the force of desire would surge and strike him, his chest would burst open, and hot blood would flush his face. Yet now the śramaṇa Gautama pays us no regard whatsoever. It is as the Tathāgata has departed from desire and is liberated, having attained the perception of good liberation. Today we should each speak a verse and question him." They went again before the Buddha, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew and stood to one side.

The celestial maiden Desire then spoke in verse:

> "Alone, in meditation, silent and still,
> having abandoned worldly wealth and treasure.
> Having already abandoned worldly gain,
> what do you now seek?
> If you seek the gain of villages,
> why do you not associate with people?
> You utterly do not associate with people;
> what will you finally obtain?"

The Buddha spoke a verse in reply:

> "Having already obtained great gain,
> my aspiration is fulfilled in peaceful cessation.
> Having vanquished Māra's army,
> I do not cling to form and desire.
> Therefore I do not associate
> and mingle with people."

The celestial maiden Delight then spoke in verse:

> "What wondrous meditation do you cultivate
> to cross the flood of the five desires?
> And by what skillful means
> do you cross the sixth ocean?
> How do you cultivate wondrous meditation,
> that amid the deep and vast desires,
> you attain the crossing to the other shore,
> and are not held by craving?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "The body attains the bliss of calm abiding,
> the mind attains good liberation;
> without action, without doing,
> right mindfulness does not waver.
> Fully knowing all dharmas,
> no disordered thoughts arise.
> The coverings of craving, aversion, and drowsiness:
> all these have been left behind.
> Cultivating in this way extensively,
> one crosses the flood of the five desires,
> and also crosses the sixth ocean,
> entirely reaching the other shore.
> Cultivating meditation in this way,
> amid the deep and vast desires,
> one entirely reaches the other shore,
> and is not held by them."

Then the celestial maiden Pleasure spoke in verse:

> "Having already cut off attachments and love,
> the pure and deep accumulation of desire;
> one generates pure faith in many people,
> and crosses the flood of desire.
> Opening and revealing bright wisdom,
> one transcends the domain of the demon of death."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "By great skillful means, broadly delivering,
> one enters the Tathāgata's Dharma and Vinaya.
> All such people have already crossed;
> what then would the wise one worry about?"

At that time, the three celestial maidens, their aspirations unfulfilled, returned to their father Māra Pāpīyas.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas, seeing his daughters coming from afar, spoke a verse to taunt them:

> "You three maidens,
> boasting of your abilities,
> each radiating bodily light and flame,
> flowing like lightning in the clouds.
> You went before the great vigorous one,
> each displaying your charms;
> but instead you were defeated by him,
> like cotton blown by the wind.
> Wishing to scratch a mountain with your nails,
> to chew through iron pellets with your teeth,
> wishing with a hair or lotus fiber
> to turn a great mountain,
> while he is entirely liberated from all compounding:
> you hoped to disturb his mind!
> If one could bind the feet of the wind,
> or make the moon fall from empty sky,
> or scoop out the great ocean with one's hands,
> or blow and move a snowy mountain with one's breath;
> he is entirely liberated from all compounding:
> could you make him waver?
> In the depths of a vast ocean,
> one seeks solid ground to stand upon;
> the Tathāgata is entirely
> liberated from all compounding.
> To seek to make him waver in the ocean of perfect awakening
> is equally so."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas, having taunted his three daughters, immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1093)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Uruvilvā, beside the Nairañjanā River, beneath the great Bodhi Tree, having just attained the Way of Buddhahood.

The heavenly Māra Pāpīyas thought: "This śramaṇa Gautama is at the Uruvilvā dwelling place, beside the Nairañjanā River, beneath the Bodhi Tree, having just attained the Way of Buddhahood. I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He then transformed his own body, creating a hundred kinds of pure and impure forms, and went to the Buddha.

The Buddha saw from afar that Pāpīyas had a hundred kinds of pure and impure forms, and thought: "The evil Māra Pāpīyas has created a hundred kinds of pure and impure forms, wishing to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "Throughout the long night of birth and death,
> you have created pure and impure forms.
> Why do you make these?
> You have not crossed to the other shore of suffering.
> If, regarding body, speech, and mind,
> one creates no obstruction,
> Māra cannot instruct such a one,
> and one is not under Māra's power.
> Thus knowing the evil Māra,
> he will vanish from here on his own."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1094)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Uruvilvā, beside the Nairañjanā River, beneath the Bodhi Tree, having first attained true awakening.

At that time, the World-Honored One, alone in a quiet place, focused in meditative contemplation, had this thought: "I am now liberated from ascetic practices. It is excellent that I am now well liberated from ascetic practices. I previously cultivated right aspirations, and now I have indeed attained unsurpassed bodhi."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is now dwelling at Uruvilvā, beside the Nairañjanā River, beneath the Bodhi Tree, having first attained true awakening. I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He immediately transformed into a young man, stood before the Buddha, and spoke in verse:

> "The place of great ascetic practice
> is able to bring about purity.
> Yet now you have abandoned it;
> what do you seek here?
> If you wish to seek purity here,
> purity cannot be obtained by this means."

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "This is Māra Pāpīyas who wishes to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "Knowing that all ascetic practices
> are entirely without meaning,
> one ultimately gains no benefit from them,
> like the sound of a bow being plucked.
> The path of morality, concentration, learning, and wisdom
> I have already fully cultivated;
> I have attained the foremost purity,
> a purity unsurpassed."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1095)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the brāhmaṇa village of Sālā.

At that time, the World-Honored One, in the early morning, put on his robes and took up his bowl, and entered the Sālā village to beg for food. At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama, in the early morning, has put on his robes and taken up his bowl, and entered the Sālā village to beg for food. I shall now go there first, enter the houses, and tell the faithful brāhmaṇa elders so that the śramaṇa Gautama leaves with an empty bowl."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas followed behind the Buddha, calling out: "Śramaṇa! Śramaṇa! Have you received no food at all?"

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "The evil Māra Pāpīyas wishes to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "You have just now, against the Tathāgata,
> acquired immeasurable transgression.
> Do you think that calling to the Tathāgata
> will make him suffer distress?"

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas said: "Gautama, enter the village again, and I shall make sure you get food."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "Even if I have nothing at all,
> I live happily and at ease,
> like the Ābhāsvara gods,
> who always feed on the food of joy.
> Even if I have nothing at all,
> I live happily and at ease,
> always feeding on the food of joy,
> not depending on a material body."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1096)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Sages' Dwelling Place, the Deer Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "I have been liberated from the bonds of humans and devas. You too have been liberated from the bonds of humans and devas. You should go forth among the people, to bring benefit to many, to bring welfare to many, for the ease and happiness of humans and devas. You need not travel in pairs; go one by one. I too shall go to the Uruvilvā dwelling place, wandering among the people."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Sages' Dwelling Place, the Deer Park, and is teaching the Dharma to his śrāvakas in this way: 'I have been liberated from the bonds of humans and devas, and you too are able.' Each of you separately teach and transform among the people, and so forth up to 'I too shall go to the Uruvilvā dwelling place, wandering among the people.' I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He immediately transformed into a young man, stood before the Buddha, and spoke in verse:

> "Not freed, you imagine you are freed,
> claiming you are already liberated;
> you are bound by the great bonds.
> I shall never let you go."

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "The evil Māra Pāpīyas wishes to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "I have already been freed from all
> the bonds of humans and devas.
> I already know you, Pāpīyas;
> now go and vanish on your own."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1097)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling among the Śākyas, at the village of Śilāmaya Śākyas.

At that time, in the village of Śilāmaya Śākyas, many people were dying of pestilence. From all directions, people, whether men or women, came to take the Three Refuges. Among the sick, whether men or women, whether old or young, all, prompted by those who came, stated their own names: "I, so-and-so, take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, take refuge in the Bhikṣu Sangha." Entire villages and entire towns all did likewise.

At that time, the World-Honored One was diligently teaching the Dharma to his śrāvakas.

At that time, those who had faith and took the Three Refuges were all reborn in the realms of humans and devas.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is now dwelling among the Śākyas, at the village of Śilāmaya Śākyas, diligently teaching the Dharma to the four assemblies. I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He transformed into a young man, went and stood before the Buddha, and spoke in verse:

> "Why do you diligently teach the Dharma,
> instructing and converting the people?
> Whether they oppose or do not oppose,
> they are not freed from being driven about.
> It is because they are bound
> that you teach the Dharma to them?"

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "The evil Māra Pāpīyas wishes to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "You yakṣa, you should know,
> beings are born in groups.
> Among those who have wisdom,
> who could fail to feel compassion?
> Because of having compassion,
> one cannot not teach and convert.
> Having compassion for all beings,
> the Dharma itself is naturally so."

The evil Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1098)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling among the Śākyas, at the village of Śilāmaya Śākyas.

At that time, the World-Honored One, alone in a quiet place, was in meditative contemplation and had this thought: "Could there be a king who rules without killing, without ordering others to kill, who solely practices what is right and does not practice what is wrong?"

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is now dwelling at the village of Śilāmaya Śākyas, alone in meditation, thinking: 'Could there be a king who rules without killing, without ordering others to kill, who solely practices what is right and does not practice what is wrong?' I shall now go there and speak the Dharma to him." He transformed into a young man, went and stood before the Buddha, and said: "It is so, World-Honored One! It is so, Sugata! One can indeed become a king without killing, without ordering others to kill, solely practicing what is right and not practicing what is wrong. World-Honored One! You can now become a king. Sugata! You can now become a king. You can surely obtain whatever you wish."

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "The evil Māra Pāpīyas wishes to cause disturbance." He then said to Māra: "Māra Pāpīyas! Why do you say such words: 'Become a king! World-Honored One! Become a king! Sugata! You can obtain whatever you wish'?"

Māra addressed the Buddha: "I have personally heard from the Buddha that he said: 'If the four bases of spiritual power are cultivated and cultivated extensively, then if one wishes to make the king of snowy mountains transform into real gold, it can be done without fail.' The World-Honored One now possesses the four bases of spiritual power, cultivated and cultivated extensively. Making the king of snowy mountains transform into real gold would be no different from your wish. Therefore I address the World-Honored One: 'Become a king! World-Honored One! Become a king! Sugata! You can obtain whatever you wish.'"

The Buddha told Pāpīyas: "I have absolutely no desire to become a king; how then would I do so? I also have no desire to make the king of snowy mountains transform into real gold; how could such a transformation occur?"

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke in verse:

> "Even if there were real gold
> as vast as the king of snowy mountains,
> if one person obtained all this gold,
> he still would not be satisfied.
> Therefore the wise one
> views gold and stone as the same."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1099)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling among the Śākyas, at the village of Śilāmaya Śākyas. At that time, there were many bhikṣus gathered in the offering hall, engaged in the making of robes.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is now dwelling among the Śākyas, at the village of Śilāmaya Śākyas. Many bhikṣus are gathered in the offering hall for the purpose of making robes. I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He transformed himself into the appearance of a young, robust brāhmaṇa, with a great coil of matted hair, wearing an animal-skin garment, holding a crooked staff. He went to the offering hall and stood silently before the many bhikṣus. After a moment, he said to the bhikṣus: "You who are young have gone forth while your skin is fair and your hair black, in the prime of your lives. You should enjoy the five desires and adorn yourselves with pleasures. Why do you go against your parents and leave your clans, parting from them in tears and sorrow, believing in the homeless life, going forth to study the Way? Why do you abandon the pleasures of the present life to seek the untimely pleasures of a future life?"

The bhikṣus said to the brāhmaṇa: "We do not abandon present pleasures to seek untimely pleasures of a future life. Rather, we abandon untimely pleasures and take up present pleasures."

Pāpīyas asked again: "How is it that you abandon untimely pleasures and take up present pleasures?"

The bhikṣus answered: "As the World-Honored One has said, future life pleasures have little flavor, much suffering, little benefit, and many dangers. The World-Honored One speaks of present pleasures as being free from all burning afflictions, not depending on time or season, able to be penetrated by oneself. Within this, one observes and knows through one's own realization. Brāhmaṇa, this is called present pleasure."

At that time, the brāhmaṇa shook his head three times, fell mute, struck the ground with his staff, and immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

At that time, the bhikṣus immediately became frightened, and their hairs stood on end. "What sort of brāhmaṇa was that, who came and performed such a transformation?" They then went to the Buddha, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Many of us bhikṣus were gathered in the offering hall for the purpose of making robes. A robust brāhmaṇa with matted hair in a great coil came to us and said: 'You who are young have gone forth,'" as was spoken in full above, up to "shook his head three times, fell mute, struck the ground with his staff, and immediately vanished and was no longer seen. We immediately became frightened, and our hairs stood on end. What sort of brāhmaṇa was that, who came and performed such a transformation?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "That was not a brāhmaṇa. It was Māra Pāpīyas who came to your place wishing to cause disturbance." At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke in verse:

> "All sufferings and afflictions that arise
> are all due to craving and desire.
> Knowing the world is entirely made of sword-points,
> who would delight in desire?
> Realizing that everything remaining in the world
> is entirely made of sword-points,
> therefore the wise one
> should diligently subdue himself.
> A great accumulation of real gold,
> vast as the king of snowy mountains,
> if received by one person,
> his mind would still not be satisfied.
> Therefore the wise one
> should cultivate the view of equanimity."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (1100)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling among the Śākyas, at the village of Śilāmaya Śākyas.

At that time, there was a venerable one named Subhadra, who in the early morning put on his robes and took up his bowl, and entered the village of Śilāmaya Śākyas to beg for food. Having finished eating, he returned to the monastery, put away his robes and bowl, washed his feet, took his sitting cloth and placed it on his right shoulder, entered the forest, sat beneath a tree, and entered into daytime meditative attainment. He had this thought: "I have obtained a great blessing! I have gone forth in the true Dharma and Vinaya to study the Way. I have obtained a great blessing! I have encountered the Great Teacher, the Tathāgata, the Perfectly Enlightened One. I have obtained a great blessing! I dwell among a holy community of those who practice the holy life, who maintain the precepts, who are endowed with virtue, who are worthy and truthful. I shall now attain a worthy end to my life, and in the life to come, it shall also be worthy."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama has gone to the village of Śilāmaya Śākyas, where he has a śrāvaka disciple named Subhadra, who put on his robes and took up his bowl," as was spoken in full above, up to "attain a worthy end to my life, and in the life to come, it shall also be worthy. I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He transformed into a great bodied, robust, and powerful form, terrifying to those who saw it, as if its strength could overturn and shake the great earth. He went to the bhikṣu Subhadra.

The bhikṣu Subhadra, seeing from afar the great bodied, fierce and powerful warrior, immediately became frightened. He rose from his seat, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at his feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! This morning I put on my robes and took up my bowl," and spoke in full as above, up to "attain a worthy end to my life, and in the life to come, it shall also be worthy. I saw a great bodied warrior, fierce, robust and blazing, with the power to shake the earth. Seeing this, I became frightened; my mind was alarmed and my hairs stood on end."

The Buddha told Subhadra: "That was not a great bodied warrior. It was Māra Pāpīyas wishing to cause disturbance. You should now return, go back to that tree, and cultivate your former samādhi. Stir that Māra, and through this, escape from suffering."

The Venerable Subhadra then returned to his original place. The next morning, he put on his robes and took up his bowl, entered the village of Śilāmaya Śākyas to beg for food. Having finished eating, he returned to the monastery, and as was spoken in full above, up to "attain a worthy end to my life, and in the life to come, it shall also be worthy."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas again thought: "This śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling among the Śākyas and has a disciple named Subhadra," as was spoken in full above, up to "attain a worthy end to my life, and in the life to come, it shall also be worthy. I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He again transformed into a great body, fierce, robust and blazing, with the power to shake the earth, and went and stood before him.

The bhikṣu Subhadra again saw him from afar. He then spoke in verse:

> "With right faith, I left the household life
> and went forth to study the Way.
> In the priceless jewel of the Buddha,
> with right mindfulness I fix my mind.
> Whatever forms you may transform into,
> my mind is not shaken.
> I perceive you to be an illusion;
> you may vanish from here."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "This śramaṇa already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1101)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Sages' Dwelling Place, the Deer Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "The Tathāgata's śrāvakas make the lion's roar, saying: 'I have known! I have known!' But people do not know with regard to what dharmas the Tathāgata's śrāvakas have known and known, and thus make the lion's roar. It is with regard to the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the origin of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering."

At that time, the heavenly Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling at Vārāṇasī, in the Sages' Dwelling Place, the Deer Park, teaching the Dharma to his śrāvakas, and so forth up to having known the Four Noble Truths. I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He transformed into a young man, stood before the Buddha, and spoke in verse:

> "Why in the great assembly
> do you fearlessly make the lion's roar,
> claiming that there is no adversary,
> aspiring to tame and subdue all?"

At that time, the World-Honored One thought: "The evil Māra Pāpīyas wishes to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "The Tathāgata, regarding all
> the profound true Dharma and Vinaya,
> skillfully makes the lion's roar,
> fearless in the Dharma.
> If there are those with wisdom,
> why would they worry and be afraid?"

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he immediately vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1102)

Thus have I heard:

At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Wilderness Trodden by Many, together with five hundred bhikṣus, and was teaching the Dharma. The five hundred bowls were placed in the central courtyard.

At that time, the World-Honored One was teaching the five hundred bhikṣus the Dharma of the arising and ceasing of the five appropriated aggregates.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Wilderness Trodden by Many, together with five hundred bhikṣus," and so forth up to "teaching the Dharma of the arising and ceasing of the five appropriated aggregates. I shall now go there and create an obstruction." He transformed into a great ox and went to the Buddha, entering among the five hundred bowls. The bhikṣus immediately tried to drive it away, lest it destroy the bowls.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "This is not an ox. It is Māra Pāpīyas wishing to cause disturbance." He then spoke in verse:

> "Form, feeling, perception,

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099, Fascicle 40

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

---

## (1104)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If one can uphold seven kinds of observances, by this cause and condition one will be born in the place of the heavenly lord Śakra. That is, when the heavenly lord Śakra was formerly a human, he supported his parents and the elders of his household, spoke with a gentle countenance and soft words, did not use harsh speech, did not engage in divisive speech, and always spoke truthful words. In a world of miserliness, although living as a householder, he was not stingy, but practiced liberating generosity, diligent giving, always delighting in giving, making offerings at charitable assemblies, and giving equally to all."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "Supporting one's parents,
> and the elders of one's household,
> with gentle, respectful, and humble words,
> free from harsh speech and divisive talk.
> Taming the miserly heart,
> always cultivating truthful speech;
> the Trāyastriṃśa gods,
> seeing one who practices these seven principles,
> all declare together:
> 'This one shall be born in our heaven.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1105)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Vaiśālī, at the Markaṭa-hrada (Monkey Pond), in the double-storied hall.

At that time, a Licchavi named Mahāli came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, have you seen the heavenly lord Śakra?"

The Buddha answered: "I have seen him."

The Licchavi further asked: "World-Honored One, have you seen a spirit that resembles Śakra in form?"

The Buddha told the Licchavi: "I know the heavenly lord Śakra, and I also know that there is a spirit that resembles the heavenly lord Śakra. I also know the dharmas of that Śakra; because of upholding those dharmas, he was born in the place of Śakra. Licchavi, when Śakra was formerly a human, he supported his parents, and so forth up to practicing equal giving."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "Supporting one's parents,
> and the elders of one's household,
> with gentle, respectful, and humble words,
> free from harsh speech and divisive talk.
> Taming the miserly heart,
> always cultivating truthful speech;
> the Trāyastriṃśa gods,
> seeing one who practices these seven principles,
> all declare together:
> 'This one shall be born in our heaven.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Licchavi Mahāli, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid respects, and departed.

---

## (1106)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Vaiśālī, at the Markaṭa-hrada (Monkey Pond), in the double-storied hall.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is the cause and condition by which Śakra Devānām Indra is named Śakra Devānām Indra?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "When Śakra Devānām Indra was formerly a human, he practiced immediate and generous giving. To śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, the poor, the destitute, travelers, and beggars, he gave food and drink, money, grain, cloth, flowers, incense, adornments, bedding, and lamps. Because he was capable of this, he is named Śakra Devānām Indra."

The bhikṣu further addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is the cause and condition by which Śakra Devānām Indra is also named Purindada?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "When Śakra Devānām Indra was formerly a human, he repeatedly gave clothing, food and drink, and so forth up to lamps. For this cause and condition, he is named Purindada."

The bhikṣu further addressed the Buddha: "What is the cause and condition by which he is also named Maghavān?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "When Śakra Devānām Indra was formerly a human, his name was Maghavān. Therefore Śakra Devānām Indra is called by his original name, Maghavān."

The bhikṣu further addressed the Buddha: "What is the cause and condition by which he is also named Vāsava?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "When Śakra Devānām Indra was formerly a human, he repeatedly gave vāsasī cloth as offerings and donations. For this cause and condition, Śakra Devānām Indra is named Vāsava."

The bhikṣu further addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is the cause and condition by which Śakra Devānām Indra is also named Kauśika?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "When Śakra Devānām Indra was formerly a human, he was a person of the Kauśika clan. For this cause and condition, Śakra Devānām Indra is also named Kauśika."

The bhikṣu asked the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is the cause and condition by which Śakra Devānām Indra is named Śacīpati?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "There was an asura maiden named Śacī who became the foremost queen of the heavenly lord Śakra. Therefore the lord Śakra is named Śacīpati."

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, what is the cause and condition by which Śakra Devānām Indra is also named Thousand-Eyed?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "When Śakra Devānām Indra was formerly a human, he was intelligent and wise. In a single sitting, he could contemplate a thousand different meanings and examine and evaluate them. For this cause and condition, the heavenly lord Śakra is also named Thousand-Eyed."

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "What is the cause and condition by which the heavenly lord Śakra is also named Indra?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "The heavenly lord Śakra is king and lord over the Trāyastriṃśa gods. For this cause and condition, the heavenly lord Śakra is named Indra."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Furthermore, when Śakra Devānām Indra was formerly a human, he upheld seven kinds of observances. By this cause and condition, he became the heavenly lord Śakra. What are the seven? When Śakra Devānām Indra was formerly a human, he supported his parents, and so forth up to practicing equal and generous giving. These are the seven kinds of observances. By this cause and condition, he became the heavenly lord Śakra."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse as extensively explained above.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1107)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Vaiśālī, at the Markaṭa-hrada (Monkey Pond), in the double-storied hall.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In the past, there was a yakṣa spirit, ugly and of hideous appearance, who sat upon the empty throne of Lord Śakra. The Trāyastriṃśa gods saw this ugly and hideous spirit sitting upon Śakra's empty throne. Seeing this, they all became angry. As the gods became extremely angry, that spirit, corresponding to their anger, gradually became more and more handsome.

"At that time, the Trāyastriṃśa gods went to the heavenly lord Śakra and said: 'Kauśika, you should know that a strange spirit, ugly and of hideous appearance, is sitting upon the heavenly king's empty throne. We gods, seeing that ugly and hideous spirit sitting upon the heavenly king's throne, became extremely angry. Yet as we gods became angry, that spirit gradually became more handsome.' Śakra Devānām Indra told the Trāyastriṃśa gods: 'That is a spirit that feeds on anger.'

"At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra himself went to where that spirit was, adjusted his garments, bared his right shoulder, joined his palms, and three times stated his name, saying: 'Venerable one, I am Śakra Devānām Indra.' As Śakra Devānām Indra showed such respect and humility, that spirit, correspondingly, gradually became ugly again, and then vanished.

"At that time, Śakra Devānām Indra sat down in his own seat and spoke this verse:

> "'One should never become angry;
> seeing anger, do not repay with anger.
> Toward evil, do not give rise to evil;
> one should destroy pride.
> Not angry and not harmful,
> one is called one who dwells among the noble assembly.
> Evil and sin arise from anger,
> standing firm like a mountain of stone.
> One who can restrain intense anger,
> like controlling a runaway chariot,
> that one I call a good charioteer,
> not the one who merely holds the reins.'"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Śakra Devānām Indra, being the sovereign king of the Trāyastriṃśa gods, praises non-anger. You who have gone forth in right faith from the household life to study the Way should also praise non-anger. You should train in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1108)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, in the early morning, the World-Honored One put on his robes, took his bowl, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. Having finished begging for food, he returned to the monastery, put away his robes and bowl, washed his feet, took his sitting cloth, placed it over his right shoulder, went to the Andha Grove, spread out his sitting cloth, and sat beneath a tree, entering midday meditation.

At that time, in the Jeta Grove, two bhikṣus had a quarrel. One was abusing and reviling, and the other remained silent. The one who had been reviling then felt remorse and sought to apologize to the other, but that bhikṣu would not accept his apology. Because he would not accept the apology, many bhikṣus in the monastery tried to mediate and persuade them, and there was loud noise and disturbance.

At that time, the World-Honored One, with his purified divine ear surpassing the human ear, heard the loud noise and disturbance in the Jeta Grove. Having heard this, he arose from meditation, returned to the monastery, spread his seat before the great assembly and sat down, and told the bhikṣus: "This morning, after begging for food, I went to the Andha Grove, sat in meditation, and entered midday meditation. I heard loud voices and great noise, tumult and disturbance from the monastery. Who was it?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "In this monastery, two bhikṣus had a quarrel. One bhikṣu was reviling, and the other remained silent. The reviling bhikṣu then sought to apologize and express remorse, but the other would not accept it. Because he would not accept, many people tried to mediate and persuade, which caused the loud voices and noisy disturbance."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "How can this be, bhikṣus? What a foolish person! When someone comes to apologize, he does not accept the apology. If a person does not accept an apology when offered, that foolish person will, through the long night, experience harm and suffering. Bhikṣus, in the past, Śakra Devānām Indra had a quarrel with the Trāyastriṃśa gods, and spoke a verse of admonition:

> "'Toward others, harbor no harmful intent;
> when anger arises, do not let it bind you.
> Do not hold resentment for long;
> do not dwell in anger.
> Even when anger blazes fiercely,
> do not utter harsh words.
> Do not seek out others' weak points
> to expose their faults and shortcomings.
> Always guard and protect yourself;
> examine within by means of what is right.
> Not angry and not harmful,
> always dwelling with the noble and holy.
> If one associates with evil people,
> one becomes hard and rigid like a mountain of stone.
> One who can restrain fierce anger,
> like controlling a runaway chariot;
> that one I call a good charioteer,
> not the one who merely holds the reins.'"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Śakra Devānām Indra, being the sovereign king of the Trāyastriṃśa gods, always practiced patience and also praised those who practice patience. You bhikṣus, who have gone forth in right faith from the household life to study the Way, should practice patience and praise those who are patient. You should train in this way!"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1109)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In the past, when the gods and the asuras were arrayed in battle formation, about to fight, Śakra Devānām Indra said to Vemacitrin, the asura king: 'Let us not kill and harm each other. Instead, let us debate. The one whose reasoning is defeated shall submit.' Vemacitrin, the asura king, said: 'If we debate, who will be the witnesses to judge whose reasoning is sound or flawed?' The heavenly lord Śakra said: 'Among the host of gods there are those who are wise and of clear discernment, and among the asura host there are likewise those of clear discernment.' Vemacitrin the asura said: 'That is acceptable.' Śakra Devānām Indra said: 'You may first set forth your argument, and then I shall follow with mine; that will not be difficult.'

"At that time, Vemacitrin, the asura king, spoke a verse to set forth his argument:

> "'If I were to practice patience,
> there would be a deficiency in my affairs.
> Fools would say:
> He practices patience out of fear.'

"Śakra Devānām Indra spoke a verse in reply:

> "'Even if fools say
> one is patient out of fear,
> or if they say nothing at all,
> how does it harm what is right?'

"Vemacitrin the asura spoke another verse:

> "'If one does not restrain the foolish,
> the foolish will then harm people,
> just as a fierce and evil bull,
> if released, will run and gore people.
> Taking up the stick to forcefully restrain them,
> through fear they are then tamed.
> Therefore, firmly wield the stick
> to subdue the foolish.'

"Lord Śakra spoke another verse in reply:

> "'I constantly observe this:
> the one who restrains the foolish;
> when a fool's anger blazes fiercely,
> the wise subdue it with calm silence.
> Not angry and not harmful,
> always dwelling with the noble and holy;
> evil and sin arise from anger,
> standing firm like a mountain of stone.
> One who can restrain fierce anger,
> like controlling a runaway chariot;
> that one I call a good charioteer,
> not the one who merely holds the reins.'

"At that time, among the host of gods there were wise gods, and among the host of asuras there were wise asuras. They contemplated, evaluated, and examined these verses, and thought thus: 'The verses spoken by Vemacitrin, the asura king, will ultimately, through the long night, give rise to quarrels, disputes, and warfare. One should know that Vemacitrin, the asura king, teaches people to engage through the long night in quarrels, disputes, and warfare. The verses spoken by Śakra Devānām Indra will ultimately, through the long night, bring about the cessation of quarrels, disputes, and warfare. One should know that the heavenly lord Śakra teaches people through the long night to cease quarrels, disputes, and warfare. Therefore, one should know that Lord Śakra has won the debate through good argument.'"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Śakra Devānām Indra defeated the asuras through good argument. Bhikṣus, Śakra Devānām Indra, being the sovereign king of the Trāyastriṃśa gods, established good argument and praised good argument. You bhikṣus should also be like this: having gone forth in right faith from the household life to study the Way, you too should engage in good argument and praise good argument. You should train in this way!"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1110)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In the past, there was the heavenly lord Śakra, and the gods and asuras were arrayed in battle formation, about to fight. Śakra Devānām Indra said to the Trāyastriṃśa gods: 'Today, if the gods are victorious and the asuras are defeated in battle, we shall capture Vemacitrin, the asura king, alive, bind him with five bonds, and bring him back to the heavenly palace.' Vemacitrin, the asura king, told the asura host: 'Today, if the gods and asuras battle and the asuras are victorious and the gods are defeated, we shall capture Śakra Devānām Indra alive, bind him with five bonds, and bring him back to the asura palace.' When the battle took place, the gods were victorious, and the asuras were defeated.

"At that time, the gods seized Vemacitrin, the asura king, bound him with five bonds, and brought him back to the heavenly palace, binding him at the gateway before Lord Śakra's Hall of Justice. Whenever Lord Śakra entered and exited through this gate, Vemacitrin the asura, bound at the side of the gate, would rage and hurl insults at him.

"At that time, Lord Śakra's charioteer saw the asura king, bound with five bonds at the side of the gate, raging and hurling insults at Lord Śakra whenever he entered and exited. Seeing this, he spoke a verse, addressing Lord Śakra:

> "'Śakra, is it because you fear him,
> or because your strength is insufficient,
> that you can endure the asura
> reviling and insulting you to your face?'

"Śakra then replied:

> "'It is not out of fear that I endure,
> nor because my strength is insufficient.
> What wise person
> would contend with a fool?'

"The charioteer further said:

> "'If one only practices patience,
> there would be a deficiency in one's affairs.
> Fools would say:
> He practices patience out of fear.
> Therefore one should punish severely,
> using wisdom to restrain the foolish.'

"Lord Śakra replied:

> "'I constantly observe this:
> the one who restrains the foolish.
> When I see a fool's anger blazing fiercely,
> the wise subdue it with calm silence.
> What is not force yet serves as force;
> that is the power of the foolish.
> The foolish depart far from the Dharma;
> on the path, they have nothing.
> If one of great strength
> can be patient toward the lesser,
> this is called supreme patience.
> Without strength, how can there be patience?
> When others hurl extreme abuse and insult,
> one of great strength can endure it;
> this is called supreme patience.
> Without strength, what patience is there?
> For oneself and for others,
> protecting well against great fear,
> knowing that the other's anger blazes fiercely,
> one returns to guarding one's own calm silence.
> In this way, both purposes are fulfilled:
> benefiting oneself and benefiting others.
> Those who call such a one a fool
> do so because they do not see the Dharma.
> A fool thinks he surpasses the patient one
> and redoubles his abusive words,
> not knowing that patience under abuse
> always conquers over the other.
> Practicing patience toward one who is stronger:
> this is called patience born of fear.
> Practicing patience toward one who is equal:
> this is called patience in contention.
> Practicing patience toward one who is inferior:
> this is called supreme patience.'"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Śakra Devānām Indra, being the sovereign king of the Trāyastriṃśa gods, always practiced patience and praised patience. You bhikṣus, having gone forth in right faith from the household life to study the Way, should likewise practice patience and praise patience. You should train in this way!"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1111)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In the past, Śakra Devānām Indra wished to enter a pleasure garden. At that time, he commanded his charioteer to prepare the thousand-horse chariot and proceed to the garden. The charioteer received the command and prepared the thousand-horse chariot, then went and informed Lord Śakra: 'Kauśika, the chariot is now ready. May the king proceed at his convenience.' The heavenly lord Śakra then descended from the Sudarśana Palace, faced east, joined his palms, and paid respects to the Buddha. At that time, the charioteer saw this, and his heart was startled, his hair stood on end, and the horse-whip fell to the ground.

"At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra, seeing the charioteer's heart startled, his hair standing on end, and the horse-whip fallen to the ground, spoke this verse:

> "'What do you see that causes such fear,
> that the horse-whip falls to the ground?'

"The charioteer spoke a verse, addressing Lord Śakra:

> "'Seeing the king, the heavenly lord Śakra,
> the husband of Śacī,
> this is why I am struck with fear,
> and the horse-whip fell to the ground.
> I have always seen the heavenly lord Śakra
> to whom the entire great earth,
> the great and lesser kings of humans and gods,
> and the four world-protecting kings,
> and the host of Trāyastriṃśa gods,
> all pay homage with respect.
> Where could there be one more honored,
> one more venerable than Lord Śakra,
> to whom you now face east,
> joining palms in reverent worship?'

"At that time, Lord Śakra spoke a verse in reply:

> "'I am indeed, among all
