> the great and lesser kings of the world,
> and the four world-protecting kings,
> and the host of Trāyastriṃśa gods,
> the most honored and supreme king;
> therefore they all come to pay respect.
> Yet there exists in the world
> a Perfectly Enlightened One, the Samyak-saṃbuddha,
> whose title is the Complete Teacher of Gods and Humans.
> Therefore I bow my head in worship.'

"The charioteer further said:

> "'This must be the supreme one of the world,
> which causes the heavenly king Śakra
> to join his palms in reverence
> and bow his head in worship facing east.
> I too shall now pay homage
> to the one whom the heavenly king venerates.'

"At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra, the husband of Śacī, having spoken these verses and paid respects to the Buddha, mounted the thousand-horse chariot and proceeded to the pleasure garden."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "That heavenly lord Śakra, being the sovereign king of the Trāyastriṃśa gods, reveres the Buddha and also praises those who revere the Buddha. You bhikṣus, having gone forth in right faith from the household life to study the Way, should also revere the Buddha and should praise those who revere the Buddha. You should train in this way!"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1112)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, as extensively stated above. The difference is: "At that time, Lord Śakra descended from the Sudarśana Palace, joined his palms, faced east, and paid reverent respects to the noble Dharma." And so forth.

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, as extensively stated above. The difference is: "At that time, Lord Śakra spoke a verse in reply to the charioteer:

> "'I am indeed, over the great earth,
> the great and lesser kings of the world,
> and the four world-protecting kings,
> and the host of Trāyastriṃśa gods;
> all such as these
> revere and respect me.
> Yet there are those of pure precepts,
> who through the long night enter right concentration,
> who in right faith have gone forth from home,
> and who have completed the holy life;
> therefore, toward them,
> I revere, respect, and pay homage.
> Also, those who have tamed greed and anger,
> who have transcended the realm of delusion,
> who train in heedfulness,
> I also respectfully pay homage to them.
> Those from whom greed, anger, and delusion
> have been completely and permanently removed,
> the arahants whose defilements are exhausted,
> I should also respectfully pay homage to them.
> Also, those who remain as householders
> but maintain the pure precepts,
> who practice the uposatha according to the Dharma,
> I should also respectfully pay homage to them.'

"The charioteer addressed Lord Śakra:

> "'These must be the supreme ones of the world,
> whom the heavenly king pays respects to.
> I too shall be likewise,
> following the heavenly king in reverence.'

"Bhikṣus, the heavenly lord Śakra, the husband of Śacī, pays reverent respects to the Dharma and the Sangha, and also praises those who pay respects to the Dharma and the Sangha. You who have already gone forth in right faith from the household life to study the Way should also pay reverent respects to the Dharma and the Sangha, and should also praise those who pay respects to the Dharma and the Sangha."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1113)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In the past, the heavenly lord Śakra wished to enter a pleasure garden. The king commanded his charioteer to prepare the thousand-horse chariot. The charioteer received the command, prepared the chariot, and returned to inform Lord Śakra: 'The chariot is now ready. May the king proceed at his convenience.'

"At that time, Lord Śakra came down from the Sudarśana Palace, turned in all directions, and joined his palms in reverent respect.

"At that time, the charioteer saw the heavenly lord Śakra come down from the palace, stand in the central courtyard, turn in all directions, and join his palms in reverent respect. Seeing this, he was startled and afraid, the horse-whip fell to the ground, and he spoke this verse:

> "'In all directions there are only humans,
> foul-smelling, born from embryonic sacs,
> spirits dwelling in putrid corpses,
> constantly scorched by hunger and thirst.
> Why does Kauśika
> so greatly honor those who have gone forth?
> Explain this meaning to me;
> I am hungry and thirsty, wishing to hear.'

"At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra spoke a verse in reply:

> "'I respectfully revere those
> who are able to go forth from the household life,
> who freely wander in all directions,
> not concerned with where they go or stay.
> The attractions of cities, nations, and lands
> cannot burden their minds.
> They do not store up possessions for livelihood,
> going forth with single-minded determination free of desire.
> Where they go, they seek nothing;
> only the unconditioned is their joy.
> When they speak, their words are certainly good;
> when silent, they abide in stillness.
> The gods and asuras
> each oppose one another;
> among humans, they also quarrel among themselves,
> opposing each other just the same.
> Only those who have gone forth
> are without quarrel among all quarrels.
> Toward all living beings,
> they have set aside the sword and the stick.
> With regard to wealth, they are free from material attractions,
> neither intoxicated nor dissolute.
> Far removed from all evil;
> therefore I pay homage to them.'

"At that time, the charioteer spoke another verse:

> "'Those whom the heavenly king reveres
> must be supreme in the world.
> Therefore, from this day forth,
> I shall pay homage to those who have gone forth.'

"Having spoken thus, the heavenly lord Śakra, having paid reverent respects in all directions to the entire Sangha, ascended the horse chariot and toured the pleasure gardens."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "That heavenly lord Śakra, being the sovereign king of the Trāyastriṃśa gods, always reveres the Sangha of renunciants, and also always praises the merit of revering the Sangha. You bhikṣus, having gone forth in right faith from the household life to study the Way, should also revere the Sangha and should also praise the merit of revering the Sangha."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1114)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In the past, the asura king raised four kinds of troops: elephant troops, horse troops, chariot troops, and foot soldiers. At that time, the Trāyastriṃśa gods wished to fight against them. The heavenly lord Śakra heard that the asura king had raised four kinds of troops: elephant troops, horse troops, chariot troops, and foot soldiers, wishing to do battle. Having heard this, he told the god Suvīra: 'Grandfather, do you know? The asuras have raised four kinds of troops: elephant troops, horse troops, chariot troops, and foot soldiers, wishing to fight against the Trāyastriṃśa gods. Grandfather, you should command the Trāyastriṃśa gods to raise four kinds of troops: elephant troops, horse troops, chariot troops, and foot soldiers, to fight against the asuras.' At that time, the god Suvīra received Lord Śakra's command, but returned to his own heavenly palace, sluggish and lax, without making diligent effort.

"The asura host had already set out on the road. Lord Śakra, hearing this, again told the god Suvīra: 'Grandfather, the asura army is already on the road. Grandfather, you should quickly issue the command to raise the four kinds of troops to fight the asuras.' The god Suvīra received Lord Śakra's command, but again returned to his palace, negligent and lax.

"At that time, the asura king's army was nearly arrived. Śakra Devānām Indra, hearing that the asura army was already on the near road, again told the god Suvīra: 'Grandfather, do you know? The asura army is already on the near road. Grandfather, quickly command the gods to raise the four kinds of troops.'

"At that time, the god Suvīra spoke this verse:

> "'If there were a place of non-arising,
> with unconditioned peace and bliss,
> if one could obtain such a place,
> without action and without worry,
> give me such a place,
> and let me obtain peace and ease.'

"At that time, Lord Śakra spoke a verse in reply:

> "'If there were a place of non-arising,
> with unconditioned peace and bliss,
> if one could obtain such a place,
> without action and without worry,
> if you obtain such a place,
> you should also take me there.'

"The god Suvīra spoke another verse:

> "'If there were a place without exertion,
> with non-arising peace and bliss,
> if one could obtain that place,
> without action and without worry,
> give me such a place,
> and let me obtain peace and ease.'

"At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra spoke a verse in reply:

> "'If there were a place without exertion,
> with non-arising peace and bliss,
> if one could obtain that place,
> without action and without worry,
> if you obtain such a place,
> you should also take me there.'

"The god Suvīra spoke another verse:

> "'If there were a place of heedlessness,
> with non-arising peace and bliss,
> if one could obtain such a place,
> without action and without worry,
> give me such a place,
> and let me obtain peaceful bliss.'

"At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra spoke a verse in reply:

> "'If there were a place of heedlessness,
> with non-arising peace and bliss,
> if one could obtain such a place,
> without action and without worry,
> if you obtain such a place,
> you should also take me there.'

"The god Suvīra spoke another verse:

> "'Lazy with nothing to arise for,
> not knowing what has been done or should be done,
> with all desires fully met;
> give me such a place.'

"At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra spoke a verse in reply:

> "'Lazy with nothing to arise for,
> yet obtaining ultimate bliss;
> if you obtain that place,
> you should also take me there.'

"The god Suvīra spoke another verse:

> "'Even without activity, one obtains bliss,
> without action and without worry.
> If you give me such a place,
> let me obtain that bliss.'

"The heavenly lord Śakra spoke a verse in reply:

> "'If there were seen or heard
> a being who does nothing at all,
> if you obtain such a place,
> you should also take me there.
> If you fear all activity
> and have no thought for conditioned existence,
> then you should quickly purify and clear
> the direct path to nirvāṇa.'

"At that time, the god Suvīra prepared the four kinds of troops: elephant troops, horse troops, chariot troops, and foot soldiers, and fought against the asuras, crushing the asura host. The gods were victorious and returned to the heavenly palace."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Śakra Devānām Indra raised the four kinds of troops, fought against the asuras, and through diligent effort was victorious. Bhikṣus, Śakra Devānām Indra, being the sovereign king of the Trāyastriṃśa gods, always engaged in diligent effort and also always praised the virtue of diligence. You bhikṣus, having gone forth in right faith from the household life to study the Way, should be diligently energetic and should praise diligence."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1115)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In the past, there was a village, and there were sages who dwelt in a secluded place near the village.

"At that time, the gods and asuras, not far from the village, arrayed in battle formation and fought.

"At that time, Vemacitrin, the asura king, removed his five adornments: he took off his heavenly crown, put aside his parasol and canopy, removed his sword, set aside his jeweled fly-whisk, and took off his leather sandals. He went to the sages' dwelling place, entered through the gate, looked around in all directions, but did not acknowledge or greet the sages. Having looked around, he departed.

"At that time, one sage saw from afar Vemacitrin, the asura king, remove his five adornments, enter the garden, look around, and then depart. Having seen this, he said to the other sages: 'What sort of person was that? He had an undisciplined appearance, did not look like a proper person, lacked dignified deportment, and seemed like a rustic villager, not a nobleman's son. He removed his five adornments, entered through the garden gate, looked around loftily, but did not acknowledge or greet any of the sages.' One sage answered: 'That was Vemacitrin, the asura king, who removed his five adornments, looked around, and then left.' The first sage said: 'He is not a worthy man. He is not good, not virtuous, not wise, not proper. He removed his five adornments, entered through the garden gate, looked around, and then departed without acknowledging or greeting any of the sages. Because of this, one should know that the host of gods will increase and the asura host will diminish.'

"At that time, Śakra Devānām Indra removed his five adornments, entered the sages' dwelling place, greeted all the sages face to face with words of inquiry and comfort, and then departed.

"Again, a sage saw the heavenly lord Śakra remove his five adornments, enter through the garden gate, and greet everyone all around. Having seen this, he asked the other sages: 'Who was that person? He entered the garden with a disciplined appearance, a pleasing demeanor, and dignified deportment, not like a rustic villager but like a nobleman's son. He removed his five adornments, entered through the garden gate, greeted everyone all around, and then departed.' A sage answered: 'That was the heavenly lord Śakra, who removed his five adornments, entered through the garden gate, greeted everyone all around, and then departed.' The first sage said: 'He is a worthy man, of goodness, truth, and dignified deportment. He removed his five adornments, entered through the garden gate, greeted everyone all around, and then departed. Because of this, one should know that the host of gods will increase and the asura host will diminish.'

"At that time, Vemacitrin, the asura king, heard the sages praise the gods. Having heard this, his anger blazed fiercely.

"At that time, the sages of that secluded place, hearing that the asura king's anger was blazing fiercely, went to where Vemacitrin, the asura king, was, and spoke this verse:

> "'The sages have come here expressly
> to beg and seek the gift of fearlessness.
> If you can grant fearlessness,
> bestow upon the munis your gracious teaching.'

"Vemacitrin replied with a verse:

> "'For you sages,
> there is no gift of fearlessness,
> for you have turned against the asuras
> and associated closely with Lord Śakra.
> Instead of granting fearlessness,
> I shall send you terror.'

"The sage spoke another verse:

> "'Whatever seeds one plants according to one's deeds,
> the fruit will ripen according to its kind.
> Having come to beg for fearlessness,
> you send us terror instead.
> You shall reap inexhaustible fear,
> for having sown seeds of terror.'

"At that time, the sages pronounced an incantation before Vemacitrin, the asura king, and then soared into the sky and departed.

"That very night, Vemacitrin, the asura king, was startled awake three times, and in his sleep heard an evil voice saying: 'Śakra Devānām Indra is raising four kinds of troops to do battle with the asuras!' He awoke in terror and dread, fearing that battle would surely end in defeat, and retreated in flight back to the asura palace.

"At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra, having defeated the enemy, went to the secluded dwelling place of the sages, bowed at the feet of the sages, withdrew to the west side before the sages, and sat facing east.

"At that time, an east wind arose, and a certain sage spoke this verse:

> "'These munis here
> went forth from home long ago.
> The sweat from their armpits is foul;
> do not sit downwind of them.
> O Thousand-Eyed One, you may move your seat;
> this odor is hard to bear.'

"At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra spoke a verse in reply:

> "'Various kinds of fragrant flowers,
> strung together as garlands;
> the fragrance I now smell
> surpasses even those.
> I would rather smell this fragrance for a long time
> and never tire of it.'"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "That heavenly lord Śakra, being the sovereign king of the Trāyastriṃśa gods, reveres those who have gone forth and also always praises those who have gone forth, and also always praises the virtue of reverence. You bhikṣus, having gone forth in right faith from the household life to study the Way, should always revere fellow practitioners of the holy life and should also praise the virtue of reverence."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1116)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra came to visit the Buddha in the early morning, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. Through Lord Śakra's divine power, the light from his body illuminated the entire Jeta Grove monastery.

At that time, Śakra Devānām Indra spoke a verse, questioning the Buddha:

> "By killing what
> does one sleep in peace?
> By killing what
> does one become free from worry and fear?
> What thing, when killed,
> does Gautama praise?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "By slaying fierce and cruel anger,
> one sleeps in peace.
> By slaying fierce and cruel anger,
> the mind becomes free from worry and fear.
> Anger is the poisonous root;
> destroying that bitter seed,
> destroying that bitter seed,
> one becomes free from worry and fear.
> Because that bitter seed is destroyed,
> the noble and holy praise it."

At that time, Śakra Devānām Indra, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid respects, and departed.

---

## (1117)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "On the eighth day of the month, the Four Great Heavenly Kings dispatch their ministers to survey the world: 'How many people support their parents, śramaṇas, and brāhmaṇas, respect their elders and clan members, perform meritorious deeds, see the evils of this present world, fear the retribution for sins in future lives, practice giving and create merit, uphold the precepts and observe the fast, and on the eighth, fourteenth, and fifteenth days of the month, as well as during the month of supernatural transformations, take up the precepts and observe the uposatha?' On the fourteenth day, they dispatch the crown prince to descend and observe the world: 'How many people support their parents, and so forth up to taking up the precepts and observing the uposatha?' On the fifteenth day, the Four Great Heavenly Kings themselves descend to the world to observe sentient beings: 'How many people support their parents, and so forth up to taking up the precepts and observing the uposatha?' Bhikṣus, at that time, when there are not many people in the world who support their parents, and so forth up to taking up the precepts and observing the uposatha,

"the Four Heavenly Kings then go to the Trāyastriṃśa gods assembled in the Hall of the Dharma and address the heavenly lord Śakra: 'Heavenly King, you should know that now in the world there are not many people who support their parents, and so forth up to taking up the precepts and observing the uposatha.' At that time, the host of Trāyastriṃśa gods, hearing this, are displeased and tell one another: 'The people of the world are not virtuous, not good, not proper, not upright, lack truthful conduct, do not support their parents, and so forth up to not taking up the precepts and observing the uposatha. Because of these sins, the host of gods will diminish, and the host of asuras will gradually increase.'

"Bhikṣus, at that time, when there are many people in the world who support their parents, and so forth up to taking up the precepts and observing the uposatha, the Four Heavenly Kings go to the Trāyastriṃśa gods assembled in the Hall of the Dharma and address the heavenly lord Śakra: 'Heavenly King, you should know that now in the world there are many people who support their parents, and so forth up to taking up the precepts and observing the uposatha.' At that time, the Trāyastriṃśa gods are all joyful and tell one another: 'Now in the world, there are virtuous, noble, truthful, and righteous people; many people support their parents, and so forth up to taking up the precepts and observing the uposatha. Because of these merits, the host of asuras will diminish and the host of gods will increase.'

"At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra, knowing that all the gods were joyful, spoke this verse:

> "'If a person on the eighth of the month,
> the fourteenth and fifteenth days,
> and during the month of supernatural transformations,
> upholds the eight-limbed fast,
> just as I have practiced,
> that person also practices likewise.'"

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "The verse spoken by the heavenly lord Śakra:

> "'If a person on the eighth of the month,
> the fourteenth and fifteenth days,
> and during the month of supernatural transformations,
> upholds the eight-limbed fast,
> just as I have practiced,
> that person also practices likewise.'

"This is not well spoken. Why is that? Because that heavenly lord Śakra still has the afflictions of greed, anger, and delusion, and is not liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, and distress.

"But if an arahant bhikṣu whose defilements are exhausted, who has done what was to be done, who has laid down the heavy burden, who has severed the bonds of existence, whose mind is well-liberated, were to speak this verse:

> "'If a person on the eighth of the month,
> the fourteenth and fifteenth days,
> and during the month of supernatural transformations,
> upholds the eight-limbed fast,
> just as I have practiced,
> that person also practices likewise.'

"If spoken thus, this would be well spoken. Why is that? Because an arahant bhikṣu has departed from greed, anger, and delusion, and is already liberated from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, and distress. Therefore this verse would be well spoken."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1118)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In the past, Vemacitrin, the asura king, was severely afflicted with illness. He went to visit Śakra Devānām Indra and said to Śakra Devānām Indra: 'Kauśika, you should know that I am severely afflicted with illness. Please cure me and give me relief!' Śakra Devānām Indra said to Vemacitrin the asura: 'You should teach me your magical arts, and I shall cure your illness and give you relief.' Vemacitrin the asura said to Lord Śakra: 'I shall go back and ask the asura assembly. If they consent, I shall teach Lord Śakra the asura magical arts.'

"At that time, Vemacitrin, the asura king, went to the asura assembly and said to the asuras: 'You all should know that I am severely afflicted with illness. I went to visit Śakra Devānām Indra to seek a cure for my illness. He said to me: "If you can teach me the asura magical arts, I shall cure your illness and give you relief." I now wish to go and teach him the asura magical arts.'

"At that time, a cunning and deceitful asura said to Vemacitrin the asura: 'That heavenly lord Śakra is straightforward, trusting, and without deceit. Just tell him: "Heavenly King, these asura magical arts, if studied, will cause a person to fall into hell and suffer for immeasurable hundreds of thousands of years." That heavenly lord Śakra will certainly give up the thought and no longer seek to learn them, and will say: "Go then! May your illness be cured and may you have peace and ease!"'

"At that time, Vemacitrin, the asura king, went again to Lord Śakra and spoke a verse, addressing him:

> "'O Thousand-Eyed honored heavenly king,
> the asura magical arts
> are all false and deceptive methods
> that cause a person to fall into hell
> for immeasurable hundreds of thousands of years,
> suffering without rest.'

"At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra said to Vemacitrin the asura: 'Enough! Enough! Such magical arts are not what I need. You may now go; may your bodily illness be stilled and cease, may you gain strength and ease!'"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Śakra Devānām Indra, being the sovereign king of the Trāyastriṃśa gods, through the long night is truthful, without trickery or deceit, virtuous and straightforward. You bhikṣus, having gone forth in right faith from the household life to study the Way, should also be like this: without trickery or deceit, virtuous and straightforward. You should train in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1119)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Once, the heavenly lord Śakra and Vairocana's son, the young asura king Vepacitta, were both of supremely wonderful appearance. In the early morning, they both came to visit the Buddha, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra and Vairocana's son, the young asura king, emitted light from their bodies that universally illuminated the Jeta Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park."

At that time, Vairocana's asura king spoke a verse, addressing the Buddha:

> "A person should diligently strive
> to ensure that benefits are fully obtained.
> Once benefits are fully obtained,
> why would one need further striving?"

At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra spoke a verse:

> "If a person diligently strives
> to ensure that benefits are fully obtained,
> once benefits are fully obtained,
> cultivating patience is unsurpassed."

Having spoken these verses, they both addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, which one has spoken well?" The World-Honored One replied: "What both of you have said, both are well spoken."

"However, now listen further as I speak:

> "All classes of sentient beings
> all seek their own benefit.
> Each of those various beings
> seeks according to their own needs.
> As for the world's conventional harmony
> and ultimate truth,
> one should know that worldly harmony
> is a thing of impermanence.
> If a person diligently strives
> to ensure that benefits are fully obtained,
> once benefits are fully obtained,
> cultivating patience is unsurpassed."

At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra and Vairocana's son, the young asura king, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed their approval, paid respects, and departed.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Śakra Devānām Indra, being the sovereign king of the Trāyastriṃśa gods, cultivates patience and praises patience. You bhikṣus, having gone forth in right faith from the household life to study the Way, should also cultivate patience and praise patience!"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

## (1120)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In the past, the heavenly lord Śakra addressed the Buddha, saying: 'World-Honored One, I now take this vow: as long as the Buddha-Dharma abides in the world and for the remainder of my life, if anyone should trouble me, I shall never retaliate by troubling them.' At that time, Vemacitrin, the asura king, heard that the heavenly lord Śakra had taken this vow: 'As long as the Buddha-Dharma abides in the world and for the remainder of my life, if anyone should trouble me, I shall never retaliate by troubling them.' Having heard this, he took up a sharp sword and came along the road to confront him.

"At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra saw from afar Vemacitrin, the asura king, carrying a sharp sword and coming along the road toward him. He called out from afar: 'Asura, halt! You are bound and cannot move!'

"Vemacitrin, the asura king, was immediately unable to move. He said to Lord Śakra: 'Did you not take this vow: "As long as the Buddha-Dharma abides in the world and for the remainder of my life, if anyone should trouble me, I shall never retaliate"?' The heavenly lord Śakra answered: 'I did indeed take such a vow. But you must stop and remain bound.' The asura said: 'Release me now!' Lord Śakra answered: 'If you swear an oath not to cause trouble, then I shall release you.' The asura said: 'Release me, and I shall act according to the Dharma.' Lord Śakra answered: 'First act according to the Dharma, and then I shall release you.'

"At that time, Vemacitrin, the asura king, spoke this verse:

> "'Where greed leads,
> and where anger leads,
> where false speech leads,
> and where slandering the noble ones leads;
> if I cause trouble,
> may I go to those same destinations.'

"Śakra Devānām Indra then said: 'I release you. Go wherever you please.'

"At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra, having made the asura king swear his oath, went to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: 'World-Honored One, I took this vow before the Buddha: "As long as the Buddha-Dharma abides in the world and for the remainder of my life, if anyone should trouble me, I shall never retaliate." Vemacitrin, the asura king, heard of my vow and came carrying a sharp sword along the road. I saw him from afar and called out: "Asura, halt! Halt! You are bound and cannot move!" The asura said: "Did you not take a vow?" I answered: "I did indeed take a vow. But now you must halt; you are bound and cannot move." He then sought release. I told him: "If you swear an oath not to cause trouble, then I shall let you go." The asura said: "Release me first, and I shall speak the oath." I then said: "First speak the oath, and then I shall release you." He then spoke a verse, making his oath:

> "'Where greed leads,
> and where anger leads,
> where false speech leads,
> and where slandering the noble ones leads;
> if I cause trouble,
> may I go to those same destinations.'

"'Thus it was, World-Honored One. I compelled the asura king to speak this oath. Was this in accordance with the Dharma? Will that asura cause trouble again?'

"The Buddha told the heavenly lord Śakra: 'Excellent! Excellent! Your compelling him to swear the oath was in accordance with the Dharma and not in violation. He also will not dare to cause trouble again.' At that time, the heavenly lord Śakra, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid respects, and departed."

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "That heavenly lord Śakra, being the sovereign king of the Trāyastriṃśa gods, does not cause trouble and also always praises the principle of non-disturbance. You bhikṣus, having gone forth in right faith from the household life to study the Way, should also practice non-disturbance and should also praise the principle of non-disturbance."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

---

It appears that the text content for Fascicle 40 (卷第四十) was not included in the request. The passage between the divider lines is empty, containing no Chinese source text to translate.

If you could provide the actual Chinese text of this fascicle, I would be happy to translate it in full.

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Forty-One

---

### (1121)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Kapilavastu at the Nyagrodha Park. At that time, a number of Śākyas came to visit the Buddha. They bowed their heads to his feet and withdrew to sit to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the Śākyas: "You Gotamas, on the days of the Dharma observance and during the months of miraculous powers, do you undertake the observance precepts and cultivate meritorious deeds?"

The Śākyas addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, on the various observance days, sometimes we are able to undertake the observance precepts, and sometimes we are not. During the months of miraculous powers, sometimes we keep the observance precepts and cultivate various meritorious deeds, and sometimes we do not."

The Buddha addressed the Śākyas: "Gotamas, you have not obtained good benefit. You are arrogant ones, afflicted people, people of grief and sorrow, people of vexation and suffering. Why is it that on the various observance days you sometimes keep the observance precepts and sometimes do not? And during the months of miraculous powers you sometimes keep the observance precepts and perform various meritorious deeds and sometimes do not? Gotamas, suppose a man seeks profit, and it increases day by day: on the first day one coin, on the second day two coins, on the third day four coins, on the fourth day eight coins, on the fifth day sixteen coins, on the sixth day thirty-two coins. If such a man's wealth constantly increases in this way, after eight days, nine days, and up to one month, would his monetary wealth continue to increase and expand?"

The householders addressed the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha addressed the Śākyas: "What do you think, Gotamas? If such a man's monetary wealth continues to increase, could he then, desiring for himself to abide for ten years in one-pointed joyful happiness, mental pleasure, and much dwelling in meditative concentration, attain this or not?"

The Śākyas answered: "No, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha addressed the Śākyas: "Could he attain nine years, eight years, seven years, six years, five years, four years, three years, two years, or one year of joyful happiness, mental pleasure, and much dwelling in meditative concentration or not?"

The Śākyas answered: "No, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha addressed the Śākyas: "Setting aside years, could he attain ten months, nine months, eight months, and down to one month of joyful happiness, mental pleasure, and much dwelling in meditative concentration or not? Setting aside one month, could he attain ten days, nine days, eight days, and down to one day and one night of joyful happiness, mental pleasure, and much dwelling in meditative concentration or not?"

The Śākyas answered: "No, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha addressed the Śākyas: "I now tell you. Among my disciples there are those of upright mind, without deceit or pretense. If I teach and guide such a person for ten years, by this cause and condition, that person can then attain hundreds of thousands of myriads of years of one-pointed joyful happiness, mental pleasure, and much dwelling in meditative concentration; this is possible! Setting aside ten years, if for nine years, eight years, and down to one year, ten months, nine months, and down to one month, ten days, nine days, and down to one day and one night I teach and guide them, by dawn I can cause them to advance. If I teach in the morning, by evening I can cause them to advance. By this cause and condition, they can attain hundreds of thousands of myriads of years of one-pointed joyful happiness, mental pleasure, and much dwelling in meditative concentration, and accomplish one of two fruits: either the fruit of a sakṛdāgāmin or the fruit of an anāgāmin. This is because that person has already previously attained the fruit of a srotāpanna."

The Śākyas addressed the Buddha: "Excellent, World-Honored One! From this day forward, on the various observance days we shall cultivate the observance precepts, including all eight branches, and during the months of miraculous powers we shall undertake the observance precepts, give charity according to our ability, and cultivate various meritorious deeds."

The Buddha addressed the Śākyas: "Excellent, Gotamas! This is truly essential."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the Śākyas, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and were gladdened. They paid their respects and departed.

---

### (1122)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Kapilavastu at the Nyagrodha Park.

At that time, a number of Śākyas had gathered in the assembly hall and were engaged in the following discussion. At that time, some Śākyas said to the Śākya Nandiya: "Sometimes we are able to visit the Tathāgata and pay reverent offerings, and sometimes we are not. Sometimes we are able to draw near to and make offerings to friendly bhikṣus whom we know, and sometimes we are not. Furthermore, we do not know: if there are wise upāsakas, and other wise upāsakas or wise upāsikās are afflicted with illness and in distress, how should we teach, instruct, and expound the Dharma to them? Let us now go together to the World-Honored One and ask about this matter. As the World-Honored One teaches, we shall receive and practice accordingly."

At that time, Nandiya together with the Śākyas went to the Buddha. They bowed their heads to his feet, withdrew, and stood to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, we Śākyas gathered in the assembly hall and engaged in the following discussion. Some Śākyas said to me: 'Nandiya, sometimes we see the Tathāgata and pay reverent offerings, and sometimes we do not. Sometimes we go to see the bhikṣus we know and draw near to make offerings, and sometimes we cannot.'" He spoke extensively as above, up to: "'As the Buddha teaches and instructs, we shall receive and practice accordingly.' We today ask the World-Honored One: if a wise upāsaka wishes to teach, instruct, and expound the Dharma to other wise upāsakas or upāsikās who are afflicted with illness and in distress, how should he do so?"

The Buddha addressed Nandiya: "If there is a wise upāsaka, he should go to other wise upāsakas or upāsikās who are afflicted with illness and in distress. With three places of comfort he should instruct them, saying: 'Venerable one, you should establish unshakable faith in the Buddha, unshakable faith in the Dharma, and unshakable faith in the Sangha.' Having instructed them with these three places of comfort, he should further ask: 'Do you have attachment and longing for your parents?' If that person has attachment and longing for parents, he should teach them to let go, saying to them: 'If by your attachment and longing for your parents you could live, then it would be appropriate to cling to them. But since you cannot survive through such attachment, what use is clinging?' If the person says they have no attachment or longing for parents, one should praise and rejoice in their goodness. One should then further ask: 'Do you have attachment and concern for your wife and children, servants, money, and possessions?' If the person says they do, one should teach them to let go, just as with letting go of attachment to parents. If they say they have no such concern, one should praise and rejoice in their goodness. One should then further ask: 'Do you have attachment and longing for the five sensual desires of the human realm?' If they say they do, one should say to them: 'The five sensual desires of the human realm are impure, foul, and corrupt, a place of foulness. They are not as good as the superior and wondrous five desires of the heavens.' One should teach them to abandon the five sensual desires of the human realm and teach them to aspire to the five desires of the heavens. If that person says, 'My mind has already become distant from the five sensual desires of the human realm; I have already been thinking of the superior and wondrous desires of the heavens,' one should praise and rejoice in their goodness. One should further say to them: 'The wondrous desires of the heavens are impermanent, suffering, empty, and subject to change and decay. The bodily existence among the devas is superior to the five desires of the heavens.' If they say they have already abandoned attachment and longing for heavenly desires and are now thinking of the pleasures of bodily existence superior to those desires, one should praise and rejoice in their goodness. One should further teach them: 'The desires of bodily existence are also impermanent and subject to change and decay. There is the cessation of formations, nirvāṇa, the bliss of release. You should abandon attachment and longing for bodily existence and delight in the bliss of nirvāṇa, the bliss of quiescence and cessation, as the highest and most supreme.' If that noble disciple is already able to abandon attachment and longing for bodily existence and delights in nirvāṇa, one should praise and rejoice in their goodness.

"Thus, Nandiya, that noble disciple is taught and instructed in this step-by-step, sequential manner, enabling them to attain non-arising, nirvāṇa, just as a bhikṣu of a hundred years' lifespan attains liberation and nirvāṇa."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the Śākya Nandiya and the others, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and were gladdened. They paid their respects and departed.

---

### (1123)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Kapilavastu at the Nyagrodha Park.

At that time, there was a Śākya named Bodhi who came to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head to the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side. He addressed the Buddha: "Excellent, World-Honored One! We have obtained a fine benefit indeed, in being relatives of the World-Honored One."

The Buddha addressed Bodhi: "Do not speak thus: 'I have obtained good benefit because I am a relative of the World-Honored One.' Bodhi, what is called a good benefit is unshakable faith in the Buddha, unshakable faith in the Dharma, unshakable faith in the Sangha, and accomplishment in the noble precepts. Therefore, Bodhi, you should train thus: 'I shall establish unshakable faith in the Buddha, unshakable faith in the Dharma, unshakable faith in the Sangha, and accomplishment in the noble precepts.'"

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the Śākya Bodhi, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was gladdened. He paid his respects and departed.

---

### (1124)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Kapilavastu at the Nyagrodha Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "When a noble disciple accomplishes unshakable faith in the Buddha, at that time, those devas who were formerly reborn there due to the cause and condition of having accomplished unshakable faith in the Buddha and the precepts all greatly rejoice and proclaim: 'Because I accomplished unshakable faith in the Buddha, I was reborn here in this good destiny among the heavens. That noble disciple has now accomplished unshakable faith in the Buddha, and by this cause and condition, shall likewise come to be reborn in this good destiny among the heavens.' Concerning unshakable faith in the Dharma, unshakable faith in the Sangha, and accomplishment in the noble precepts, it is said in the same way."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1125)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four factors that constitute the path of a srotāpanna: associating with good people, hearing the true Dharma, proper internal reflection, and practicing the Dharma in conformity with the Dharma."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1126)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four factors of a srotāpanna. What are the four? Unshakable faith in the Buddha, unshakable faith in the Dharma, unshakable faith in the Sangha, and accomplishment in the noble precepts. These are called the factors of a srotāpanna."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1127)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If one is endowed with four dharmas, one should be known as a srotāpanna. What are the four? Unshakable faith in the Buddha, unshakable faith in the Dharma, unshakable faith in the Sangha, and accomplishment in the noble precepts. These are called the four dharmas; one who is endowed with them should be known as a srotāpanna."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As stated without distinction, so also with distinction: a bhikṣu, bhikṣuṇī, śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, śrāmaṇerī, upāsaka, or upāsikā who is endowed with four dharmas should be known as a srotāpanna. Each sūtra is as stated above.

---

### (1128)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four fruits of the śramaṇa. What are the four? The fruit of a srotāpanna, the fruit of a sakṛdāgāmin, the fruit of an anāgāmin, and the fruit of an arhat."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1129)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are four fruits of the śramaṇa. What are the four? The fruit of a srotāpanna, the fruit of a sakṛdāgāmin, the fruit of an anāgāmin, and the fruit of an arhat. What is the fruit of a srotāpanna? The destruction of the three fetters: this is called the fruit of a srotāpanna. What is the fruit of a sakṛdāgāmin? The destruction of the three fetters, with greed, aversion, and delusion weakened: this is called the fruit of a sakṛdāgāmin. What is the fruit of an anāgāmin? The destruction of the five lower fetters: this is called the fruit of an anāgāmin. What is the fruit of an arhat? When greed is completely exhausted, aversion is completely exhausted, delusion is completely exhausted, and all afflictions are completely exhausted: this is called the fruit of an arhat."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1130)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If at a certain place a bhikṣu was walking in meditation and at that place attained any one of the four fruits of the śramaṇa, that bhikṣu, for the rest of his life, should always remember that place."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

As with a place of walking meditation, so also with a place of standing, a place of sitting, and a place of lying down: it is said in the same way.

As with a bhikṣu, so also with a bhikṣuṇī, śikṣamāṇā, śrāmaṇera, śrāmaṇerī, upāsaka, or upāsikā: each of the four sūtras is as stated above.

---

### (1131)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Just as the four kinds of food sustain the four great elements, establishing, benefiting, and nurturing sentient beings. What are the four? Morsel food, contact food, mental volition food, and consciousness food. In the same way, there are four kinds of merit-moistening, wholesome-Dharma-moistening, blissful nourishment. What are the four? Accomplishing unshakable faith in the Buddha is merit-moistening, wholesome-Dharma-moistening, blissful nourishment. Unshakable faith in the Dharma, unshakable faith in the Sangha, and accomplishment in the noble precepts are merit-moistening, wholesome-Dharma-moistening, blissful nourishment."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1132)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "Accomplishing unshakable faith in the Buddha is merit-moistening, wholesome-Dharma-moistening, blissful nourishment. Unshakable faith in the Dharma, hearing the Dharma that is agreeable and dear, and accomplishment in the noble precepts are merit-moistening, wholesome-Dharma-moistening, blissful nourishment."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1133)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "One who accomplishes unshakable faith in the Buddha has merit-moistening, wholesome-Dharma-moistening, blissful nourishment. Regarding the Dharma, and regarding sentient beings who are bound by the stain of miserliness, if one's mind is free from the stain of miserliness, dwelling abundantly in it, practicing the liberation of giving, constantly giving, delighting in relinquishment, giving with an impartial mind, and accomplishing the noble precepts, this is merit-moistening, wholesome-Dharma-moistening, blissful nourishment."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1134)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus as stated above. The difference is: "These four kinds of merit-moistening, wholesome-Dharma-moistening, blissful nourishment: the meritorious fruit and reward of that noble disciple cannot be measured, saying 'one obtains such-and-such merit' or 'such-and-such fruit and reward.' Rather, being of much merit, they fall into the category of a great accumulation of merit, as explained previously in the Sūtra of the Parable of the Five Rivers, up to the speaking of the verse."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1135)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, forty devas of extremely wondrous form, after the night had passed at dawn, came to the Buddha. They bowed their heads to his feet, withdrew, and sat to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the devas: "Excellent! Excellent, devas! Have you accomplished unshakable faith in the Buddha, unshakable faith in the Dharma, unshakable faith in the Sangha, and accomplishment in the noble precepts?"

At that time, the devas rose from their seats, arranged their garments, bowed their heads to the Buddha's feet, joined their palms, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I have accomplished unshakable faith in the Buddha. By the merit of this, when my body perished and my life ended, I was reborn in heaven."

One deva addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I have accomplished unshakable faith in the Dharma. By the merit of this, when my body perished and my life ended, I was reborn in heaven."

One deva addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I have accomplished unshakable faith in the Sangha. By the merit of this, when my body perished and my life ended, I was reborn in heaven."

One deva addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I have accomplished the noble precepts. By the merit of this, when my body perished and my life ended, I was reborn in heaven."

At that time, the forty devas, each having declared before the Buddha their attainment of the fruit of a srotāpanna, then vanished from sight.

As with the forty devas, so also with four hundred devas, eight hundred devas, ten thousand devas, twenty thousand devas, thirty thousand devas, forty thousand devas, fifty thousand devas, sixty thousand devas, seventy thousand devas, and eighty thousand devas: each, before the Buddha, declared their attainment of the fruit of a srotāpanna, and then vanished from sight.

---

### (1136)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should dwell like the moon as a metaphor, like a new student, with shame, modesty, gentleness, and humility, with controlled mind and restrained body, when entering another's home. Just as a clear-sighted man approaching a deep precipice and ascending a peak controls his mind and restrains his body, carefully advancing forward. In this way, a bhikṣu should dwell like the moon as a metaphor, like a new student, with shame, modesty, gentleness, and humility, controlling his mind and restraining his body when entering another's home. The bhikṣu Kāśyapa dwells like the moon as a metaphor, like a new student, with shame, modesty, and humility that subdues all pride. He controls his mind and restrains his body when entering another's home, just as a clear-sighted man approaching a deep precipice and ascending a peak controls his mind and restrains his body, observing correctly as he advances."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? What kind of appearance and conduct should a bhikṣu have when entering another's home?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. We wish that you would explain this broadly! When the bhikṣus have heard, they will receive and practice accordingly."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it for you. If a bhikṣu's mind is not bound and attached to, or delighting in, another's household, and when others gain benefits and perform meritorious deeds, he rejoices as though they were his own, without giving rise to jealous thoughts, neither exalting himself nor disparaging others, such a type of bhikṣu is fit to enter another's home."

At that time, the World-Honored One, stroking the empty space with his hand, addressed the bhikṣus: "Does my hand now cling to space, bind space, or defile space?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "The Dharma of a bhikṣu should always be thus: with a mind that does not cling, does not bind, and does not defile, one enters another's home. Only the bhikṣu Kāśyapa enters another's home with a mind that does not cling, does not bind, and does not defile. When others gain benefits and perform meritorious deeds, he rejoices as though they were his own, without giving rise to jealous thoughts, neither exalting himself nor disparaging others. Only the bhikṣu Kāśyapa is fit to enter another's home."

At that time, the World-Honored One again, stroking the empty space with his hand, addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Does my hand now cling to space, bind space, or defile space?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "Only the bhikṣu Kāśyapa's mind is always thus. With a mind that does not cling, does not bind, and does not defile, he enters another's home."

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "What kind of appearance and conduct should a bhikṣu have when expounding the Dharma in a pure manner?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. We wish that you would explain this broadly! When the bhikṣus have heard, they will receive and practice accordingly."

The Buddha addressed the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it for you. If a bhikṣu, when expounding the Dharma to others, has this thought: 'May those people give rise to pure faith in me, so that because of this they will offer me robes, food and drink, bedding, and medicine,' such an exposition is called an impure expounding of the Dharma.

"If, however, a bhikṣu, when expounding the Dharma to others, has this thought: 'The World-Honored One has revealed the true Dharma and Vinaya, free from all burning afflictions, not dependent on time, to be realized in this very body, to be personally known, leading directly to nirvāṇa. Yet sentient beings are drowning in old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering. If such sentient beings hear the true Dharma, it will be of benefit to them in meaning and bring them long-lasting peace and happiness.' It is out of the causes and conditions of this true Dharma, with a mind of loving-kindness, a mind of compassion, a mind of sympathetic concern, and a mind wishing that the true Dharma may long endure, that one expounds the Dharma to others: this is called a pure expounding of the Dharma. Only the bhikṣu Kāśyapa has such a pure mind when expounding the Dharma to others, based on the Tathāgata's true Dharma and Vinaya, up to expounding with a mind wishing that the Dharma may long endure. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus, and when expounding the Dharma, based on the Tathāgata's true Dharma and Vinaya, up to expounding the Dharma to others with a mind wishing that the Dharma may long endure."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1137)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a bhikṣu, wishing to enter another's home, has this thought: 'May they give to me; may they not fail to give. May they give generously, not gradually. May they give much, not little. May they give fine things, not coarse things. May they give quickly, not slowly.' If he goes to another's home with such a mind, and the other does not give, or gives slowly, that bhikṣu's mind will feel humiliated. Because of this, his mind will become dejected and withdrawn, and he will create obstacles for himself.

"If, however, a bhikṣu wishing to enter another's home has this thought: 'As a renunciant who suddenly arrives at another's home, how could I expect to receive gifts rather than not receive them, to receive generous gifts rather than gradual ones, to receive much rather than little, to receive fine things rather than coarse things, to receive quickly rather than slowly?' Having such a thought, he goes to another's home. If the other does not give, or gives slowly, that bhikṣu's mind will not feel humiliated, nor will it become dejected and withdrawn, nor will he create obstacles. Only the bhikṣu Kāśyapa has such thoughts when entering another's home. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus, having such thoughts when entering another's home: 'As a renunciant who suddenly arrives at another's home, how could I expect to receive gifts rather than not receive them, and so on up to to receive quickly rather than slowly?'"

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1138)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa was dwelling at the Eastern Park, the Lecture Hall of Migāra's Mother, in Śrāvastī. In the afternoon, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa arose from meditation and went to visit the Buddha. He bowed his head to the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the Venerable Mahākāśyapa: "You should expound the Dharma to the bhikṣus, teaching and instructing them. Why? I constantly expound the Dharma to the bhikṣus, teaching and instructing them; you also should do likewise."

The Venerable Mahākāśyapa addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, bhikṣus nowadays are difficult to teach and instruct. Some bhikṣus cannot bear to hear what is spoken."

The Buddha addressed Mahākāśyapa: "What reason do you have for saying this?"

Mahākāśyapa addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I have seen two bhikṣus. One is named Bandhu, a disciple of Ānanda; the second is named Abhijika, a disciple of Mahāmaudgalyāyana. These two are disputing about learning, each saying: 'Come, let us debate. Who knows more? Who knows better?'"

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda was standing behind the Buddha, fanning the Buddha. He said to the Venerable Mahākāśyapa: "Please stop, Venerable Mahākāśyapa! Please be patient, Venerable Kāśyapa! These are young bhikṣus of little wisdom and poor judgment."

The Venerable Mahākāśyapa said to the Venerable Ānanda: "You be silent! Do not make me question you about this matter before the Sangha."

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda then remained silent.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed a certain bhikṣu: "Go to where the bhikṣus Bandhu and Abhijika are and tell them: 'The Great Teacher summons you.'" That bhikṣu then received the instruction and went to where the bhikṣus Bandhu and Abhijika were, and told them: "The Great Teacher summons you."

At that time, the bhikṣus Bandhu and Abhijika replied that they would obey, and both went to the Buddha. They bowed their heads to his feet, withdrew, and stood to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the two bhikṣus: "Did you two actually dispute with each other, each saying: 'Come, let us try debating. Who knows more? Who knows better?'"

The two bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "It is true, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha addressed the two bhikṣus: "Did you take what I have taught, the sūtras, geyas, vyākaraṇas, gāthās, udānas, nidānas, avadānas, itivṛttakas, jātakas, vaipulyas, adbhutadharmas, and upadeśas, and dispute over them, each saying: 'Come, let us try debating. Who knows more? Who knows better?'"

The two bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha addressed the two bhikṣus: "Should you not use what I have taught, the sūtras and so on up to the upadeśas, to tame yourselves, to calm yourselves, and to seek nirvāṇa for yourselves?"

The two bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha addressed the two bhikṣus: "Knowing what I have taught, the sūtras and so on up to the upadeśas, should you foolish men dispute over who knows more and who knows better?"

At that time, the two bhikṣus prostrated before the Buddha's feet and again addressed the Buddha: "We repent, World-Honored One! We repent, Sugata! We were foolish, we were ignorant, unskillful and unwise, and yet we disputed with each other."

The Buddha addressed the two bhikṣus: "Truly knowing your transgression, you repent. You were foolish, unskillful, and unwise, and you disputed with each other. Now that you have recognized your own transgression and seen your own transgression, and knowing and seeing, you repent, in the future the discipline of the precepts will arise in you. I now accept your repentance, out of compassion, so that your wholesome dharmas may increase and never diminish. Why? Because if one recognizes one's own transgression, sees one's own transgression, and knowing and seeing, repents, in the future the discipline of the precepts will arise, and one will never diminish."

At that time, the two bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and were gladdened. They paid their respects and departed.

---

### (1139)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa was dwelling at the Eastern Park, the Lecture Hall of Migāra's Mother, in Śrāvastī. In the afternoon, he arose from meditation and went to the World-Honored One. He bowed his head to the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side.

The Buddha addressed Kāśyapa: "You should teach, instruct, and admonish the bhikṣus! Expound the Dharma to the bhikṣus, teaching and instructing them. Why? I constantly expound the Dharma to the bhikṣus, teaching and instructing them; you also should do likewise."

The Venerable Mahākāśyapa addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, bhikṣus nowadays are difficult to expound the Dharma to. If one expounds the Dharma, there will be bhikṣus who cannot bear it and are displeased."

The Buddha addressed Kāśyapa: "What causes and conditions do you see that you speak thus?"

Mahākāśyapa addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if there are bhikṣus who have no faith or reverence for wholesome dharmas, and they hear the Dharma expounded, they will become dejected and withdrawn. If there are people of poor wisdom who have no diligence, no shame, no conscience, and no wisdom regarding wholesome dharmas, when they hear the Dharma expounded, they will become dejected and withdrawn. If there are people of greed, aversion, drowsiness, restlessness and worry, and doubt, whose bodies act with arrogance and violence, who have anger, resentment, loss of mindfulness, lack of concentration, and lack of wisdom, when they hear the Dharma expounded, they will become dejected and withdrawn. World-Honored One, for such evil people as these, they cannot even cause their minds to abide in wholesome dharmas, let alone advance further! One should know that such people's wholesome dharmas will decline day and night, unable to increase.

"World-Honored One, if there is a person whose faith in wholesome dharmas is pure, he will not decline. If one has diligence, shame, conscience, and wisdom regarding wholesome dharmas, one will not decline. If one is free from greed, aversion, drowsiness, restlessness and worry, and doubt, one will not decline. If one's body does not act with corruption, one's mind is not defiled, one is free from anger and resentment, and one has settled mind, right mindfulness, and wisdom, one will not decline. Such a person's wholesome dharmas increase day and night; how much more so when the mind abides! This person constantly seeks superior advancement day and night, and will never decline."

The Buddha addressed Kāśyapa: "So it is, so it is. One who has no faith in wholesome dharmas will indeed decline." He also spoke as Kāśyapa had elaborated in sequence.

At that time, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was gladdened. He rose from his seat, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (1140)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa was dwelling at the Eastern Park, the Lecture Hall of Migāra's Mother, in Śrāvastī. In the afternoon, he arose from meditation and came to the Buddha. He bowed his head to the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed Mahākāśyapa: "You should expound the Dharma to the bhikṣus, teaching and instructing them. Why? I constantly expound the Dharma to the bhikṣus, teaching and instructing them; you also should do likewise."

The Venerable Mahākāśyapa addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, bhikṣus nowadays are difficult to teach, instruct, and admonish in the Dharma. There are bhikṣus who, when they hear what is spoken, cannot bear it and are displeased."

The Buddha addressed Mahākāśyapa: "What reason do you have for saying this?"

Mahākāśyapa addressed the Buddha: "The World-Honored One is the root of the Dharma, the eye of the Dharma, the foundation of the Dharma. May the World-Honored One expound the Dharma to the bhikṣus. When the bhikṣus have heard, they will receive and practice accordingly."

The Buddha addressed Kāśyapa: "Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it for you."

The Buddha addressed Kāśyapa: "In former times, forest-dwelling bhikṣus, in the presence of other forest-dwelling bhikṣus, would praise and extol the practice of forest-dwelling. In the presence of alms-round bhikṣus, they would praise and extol the merit of alms-round practice. In the presence of rag-robe bhikṣus, they would praise and extol the merit of wearing rag-robes. In the presence of bhikṣus of few desires, contentment, cultivation of seclusion, vigorous effort, right mindfulness, right concentration, wisdom, and destruction of the outflows with bodily realization, they would praise and extol them according to their respective practices. Kāśyapa, when in the presence of forest-dwellers they would praise the forest-dwelling practice, up to in the presence of bhikṣus whose outflows were exhausted, they would praise the destruction of outflows and bodily realization. When they saw such a person, they would all speak with him, comfort him appropriately, and say 'Welcome!': 'What is your name? Whose disciple are you?' They would offer their seats, invite them to sit, and praise their virtue, saying that according to their Dharma and manner, they had the meaning of a śramaṇa and the aspiration of a śramaṇa. When they praised them thus, those who lived and practiced together with them would then certainly follow their conduct, and before long would come to share the same views and the same aspirations."

The Buddha addressed Kāśyapa: "If a young bhikṣu sees such a forest-dwelling bhikṣu come and praise the forest-dwelling practice, up to the destruction of outflows and bodily realization, that young bhikṣu should rise to go out and greet him, pay reverent respects, bow, and inquire after his well-being. Up to those who dwell together with him will before long attain benefit for themselves. Those who are reverent in this way will obtain peace and benefit for a long time."

The Buddha addressed Kāśyapa: "Bhikṣus today, when they see someone coming who is known as a great elder of extensive virtue, able to attract material gifts, robes, food and drink, bedding, and medicine, speak with him, reverently inquire after his well-being, and praise him saying 'Welcome!': 'What is your name? Whose disciple are you?' They praise his merit, saying he can attract great benefits of robes, food and drink, bedding, and medicine. 'If one associates closely with this venerable one, one will also be abundantly supplied with robes, food and drink, bedding, and medicine.' And if a young bhikṣu sees such a one coming, a person of great wisdom and great virtue, able to attract material gifts, robes, food and drink, bedding, and medicine, he quickly rises to go out and greet him, reverently inquires after his well-being, and praises him saying 'Welcome, one of great wisdom and great virtue, able to attract great benefits of robes, food and drink, bedding, and medicine.' Kāśyapa, such a young bhikṣu will for a long time obtain what is not beneficial, not advantageous, and suffering.

"Thus, Kāśyapa, such bhikṣus are a blight upon the śramaṇa way, a drowning of the holy life, a great obstruction, evil and unwholesome dharmas, the blight of afflictions, leading to the heavy taking up of further existences, the blazing of birth and death, and the future suffering of birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, suffering, and vexation. Therefore, Kāśyapa, you should train thus: 'Be a forest-dweller, and in the presence of forest-dwellers, praise and extol the practices of rag-robes, alms-rounds, few desires, contentment, cultivation of seclusion, vigorous effort, right mindfulness, right concentration, right knowledge, destruction of outflows, and bodily realization; praise and extol them.' You should train thus."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was gladdened. He paid his respects and departed.

---

### (1141)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa was dwelling at the Eastern Park, the Lecture Hall of Migāra's Mother, in Śrāvastī. In the afternoon, he arose from meditation and went to the World-Honored One. He bowed his head to the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed Mahākāśyapa: "You are now old, aged, with mature faculties. Your rag-robes are heavy. My robes are light and fine. You may now stay among the Sangha and wear the light robes of householder's subdued color."

Kāśyapa addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I have long practiced forest-dwelling, praising forest-dwelling, rag-robes, and alms-rounds."

The Buddha addressed Kāśyapa: "Considering how many kinds of benefit, do you practice forest-dwelling, praising forest-dwelling, rag-robes, and alms-rounds, and praising the practice of rag-robes and alms-rounds?"

Kāśyapa addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I consider two kinds of benefit. First, the benefit of attaining peaceful and happy dwelling in the present life. Second, for the sake of sentient beings of future generations, to serve as a great illumination. Sentient beings of future generations will think thus: 'The senior elders of the past, endowed with the six supernatural powers, who had gone forth long ago, whose pure conduct was fully matured, who were praised by the World-Honored One, and who were honored by those of wisdom and holy conduct, they throughout the long night practiced forest-dwelling, praising forest-dwelling, rag-robes, and alms-rounds, and praising the practice of rag-robes and alms-rounds. All who hear of this, with purified minds, rejoice. For a long time they will attain peace and benefit.'"

The Buddha addressed Kāśyapa: "Excellent! Excellent, Kāśyapa! You will for a long time bring benefit and well-being to many, peace and happiness to sentient beings, compassion for the world, peace and happiness for devas and humans."

The Buddha addressed Kāśyapa: "If anyone disparages the dhūta (ascetic) practices, they disparage me. If anyone praises the dhūta practices, they praise me. Why? Because the dhūta practices are what I have praised and extolled throughout the long night. Therefore, Kāśyapa, one who is a forest-dweller should praise forest-dwelling. One who practices rag-robes and alms-rounds should praise the practice of rag-robes and alms-rounds."

After the Buddha spoke this sūtra, Mahākāśyapa, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and was gladdened. He paid his respects and departed.

---

### (1142)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī at Jetavana, Anāthapiṇḍada's park.

At that time, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa had been dwelling for a long time in a forest hermitage near Śrāvastī. His beard and hair had grown long, and he wore tattered patched robes. He came to visit the Buddha. At that time, the World-Honored One was surrounded by an immense assembly, expounding the Dharma.

At that time, the bhikṣus saw Mahākāśyapa coming from afar. Seeing him, they gave rise to thoughts of contempt for the Venerable Mahākāśyapa, saying: "What sort of bhikṣu is this? His robes are coarse and wretched, he has no proper bearing, and he comes swaggering along in such garments."

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing the thoughts in the bhikṣus' minds, addressed Mahākāśyapa: "Welcome, Kāśyapa! Here, take this half of my seat. I wonder who went forth first, you or I?"

Those bhikṣus became terrified, with their hair standing on end, and they said to each other: "How extraordinary, venerable ones! That Venerable Mahākāśyapa is of great virtue and great power, a disciple of the Great Teacher, and the Buddha invites him to share half his seat."

At that time, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa, with palms joined, addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, the Buddha is my teacher; I am his disciple."

The Buddha addressed Kāśyapa: "So it is, so it is. I am the Great Teacher; you are my disciple. Now sit wherever you are comfortable."

The Venerable Mahākāśyapa bowed his head to the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and sat to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One, wishing further to awaken and alert the bhikṣus, and to manifest before the assembly the supreme and vast merits that the Venerable Mahākāśyapa shared equally with himself, addressed the bhikṣus: "I am secluded from sensual desires and unwholesome dharmas, with applied thought and sustained thought, and dwell having fully attained the first dhyāna, whether for a day, a night, or a day and night. Mahākāśyapa also, just like me, is secluded from sensual desires and unwholesome dharmas, up to fully attaining the first dhyāna, whether for a day, a night, or a day and night. I desire to fully attain and dwell in the second, third, and fourth dhyānas, whether for a day, a night, or a day and night. Mahākāśyapa likewise. Up to fully attaining and dwelling in the fourth dhyāna, whether for a day, a night, or a day and night. As I wish, I dwell having fully attained loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity; the sphere of boundless space, the sphere of boundless consciousness, the sphere of nothingness, and the sphere of neither-perception-nor-non-perception; the realms of supernatural powers, the divine ear, knowledge of others' minds, knowledge of past lives, knowledge of birth and death, and the knowledge of the exhaustion of outflows, whether for a day, a night, or a day and night. The bhikṣu Kāśyapa also, just like me, up to fully attaining and dwelling in the knowledge of the exhaustion of outflows, whether for a day, a night, or a day and night."

At that time, the World-Honored One, before the immense assembly, praised Mahākāśyapa as possessing the same vast and supreme merits as himself. The bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1143)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. The Venerable Mahākāśyapa and the Venerable Ānanda were staying at Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain.
