At that time, the Venerable Ānanda went to the Venerable Mahākāśyapa and said to him: "Today we might go out from Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain and enter Rājagṛha to beg for food."

The Venerable Mahākāśyapa silently assented.

At that time, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa and the Venerable Ānanda put on their robes and took their bowls and entered Rājagṛha to beg for food. The Venerable Ānanda said to the Venerable Mahākāśyapa: "The hour is still early. We could briefly stop by the bhikṣuṇī monastery." They then went there.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇīs saw the Venerable Mahākāśyapa and the Venerable Ānanda coming from afar. They quickly prepared seats and invited them to sit. At that time, the bhikṣuṇīs bowed at the feet of the Venerable Mahākāśyapa and Ānanda, then withdrew and sat to one side.

The Venerable Mahākāśyapa then expounded the Dharma to the bhikṣuṇīs in various ways, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening them. After instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening them, the bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā was displeased and spoke these harsh words: "How is it that the Venerable Mahākāśyapa presumes to expound the Dharma to bhikṣuṇīs in the presence of the Venerable Ānanda, the Vaidehī Sage? It is like a needle-peddler's boy trying to sell needles in a needle-master's shop. Just so, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa presumes to expound the Dharma to bhikṣuṇīs in the presence of the Venerable Ānanda, the Vaidehī Sage."

The Venerable Mahākāśyapa heard that the bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā was displeased in mind and had spoken harsh words. Having heard this, he said to the Venerable Ānanda: "Look at this! The bhikṣuṇī Thullanandā is displeased in mind and has spoken harsh words. What do you think, Ānanda? Am I a needle-peddler's boy, and you the needle-master, before whom I am selling?"

The Venerable Ānanda said to the Venerable Mahākāśyapa: "Please stop! Let it go, Venerable Mahākāśyapa! This is a foolish old woman of little wisdom who has never trained."

"Ānanda, have you heard the World-Honored One, the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Awakened One, in his knowledge and vision, expound the Moon Simile Sūtra before the great assembly, teaching and instructing: 'Bhikṣus, you should dwell like the moon as a metaphor, always like a new student,' and so forth in detail? Was this spoken about you, Ānanda, to dwell like the moon, always like a new student?"

Ānanda answered: "No, Venerable Mahākāśyapa."

"Ānanda, have you heard the World-Honored One, the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Awakened One, in his knowledge and vision, say: 'Bhikṣus, you should dwell like the moon as a metaphor, always like a new student; this refers only to the bhikṣu Mahākāśyapa'?"

Ānanda answered: "So it is, Venerable Mahākāśyapa."

"Ānanda, were you ever, in the knowledge and vision of the World-Honored One, the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Awakened One, before a measureless great assembly, invited to come and sit? And moreover, did the World-Honored One, with merits equal to his own vast virtues, praise you thus: 'Ānanda is secluded from sensual desires and unwholesome dharmas, up to the penetration of the exhaustion of outflows'?"

He answered: "No, Venerable Mahākāśyapa."

"Thus, Ānanda, the World-Honored One, the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Awakened One, before a measureless great assembly, personally spoke with his own mouth: 'Welcome, Mahākāśyapa! I invite you to share half my seat.' And moreover, before the great assembly, with merits equal to his own vast virtues, being secluded from sensual desires and unwholesome dharmas, up to the penetration of the exhaustion of outflows, he praised Mahākāśyapa?"

Ānanda answered: "So it is, Venerable Mahākāśyapa."

At that time, Mahākāśyapa, having given the lion's roar before the assembly of bhikṣuṇīs, departed.

---

### (1144)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa and the Venerable Ānanda were dwelling in Rājagṛha at Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain. The World-Honored One had passed into nirvāṇa not long before. At that time there was famine in the world, and alms food was difficult to obtain.

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda, together with many young bhikṣus, was unable to properly restrain the sense faculties. They ate without knowing moderation, were unable to diligently practice meditation in the first and last watches of the night, were attached to sleep, and constantly sought worldly gains. While wandering among people and traveling to the southern lands of India, thirty young disciples abandoned the precepts and returned to lay life, and the remaining ones were mostly youths. At that time, the Venerable Ānanda, after wandering in the southern mountain country, returned to Rājagṛha with a diminished following. At that time, the Venerable Ānanda put away his robes and bowl, washed his feet, and went to the Venerable Mahākāśyapa. He bowed his head to his feet, withdrew, and sat to one side.

At that time, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa asked the Venerable Ānanda: "Where have you come from? Your following is quite small."

Ānanda answered: "I have been wandering among people in the southern mountain country. Thirty young bhikṣus abandoned the precepts and returned to lay life. My following has diminished, and moreover, those who remain are mostly youths."

The Venerable Mahākāśyapa said to Ānanda: "For how many benefits did the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Awakened One, in his knowledge and vision, allow three or more people and then establish the rule against group meals?"

Ānanda answered: "For two reasons. What are the two? First, for the sake of poor and humble households. Second, because many evil people might form parties and bands, causing mutual disruption and destruction. It was so that evil people would not dwell in the Sangha, receiving the name of the assembly, obstructing and overshadowing the great assembly, separately forming two factions, and generating mutual suspicion and disputes."

The Venerable Kāśyapa said to Ānanda: "You know this principle, so why during the famine did you wander with many young disciples through the southern mountain country, causing thirty people to abandon the precepts and return to lay life, diminishing your following, with the rest being mostly youths? Ānanda, your following has been consumed. You are a youth who does not know how to measure things."

Ānanda answered: "How is it, Venerable Mahākāśyapa? My hair is already gray on both sides, yet you still call me a youth?"

The Venerable Mahākāśyapa said: "You, during the famine, wandered among people with young disciples, causing thirty disciples to abandon the precepts and return to lay life, with the rest being youths. Your following has been consumed. You do not know how to measure things, yet you speak of being a veteran. The assembly is ruined, Ānanda! The assembly is utterly ruined, Ānanda! You are a youth, because you do not know how to measure things."

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Tissa heard that the Venerable Mahākāśyapa had rebuked the Venerable Ānanda, the Vaidehī Sage, calling him a youth. Having heard this, she was displeased and spoke these harsh words: "How is it that the Venerable Mahākāśyapa, a former non-Buddhist practitioner, rebukes the Venerable Ānanda, the Vaidehī Sage, as a youth, and lets the name 'youth' circulate about him?"

The Venerable Mahākāśyapa, with the divine ear, heard that the bhikṣuṇī Tissa was displeased in mind and had spoken harsh words. Having heard, he said to the Venerable Ānanda: "Look at this! The bhikṣuṇī Tissa is displeased in mind and speaks harsh words, saying: 'Mahākāśyapa was formerly a non-Buddhist practitioner, yet he rebukes the Venerable Ānanda, the Vaidehī Sage, as a youth, and lets the name "youth" circulate about him.'"

The Venerable Ānanda answered: "Please stop, Venerable Mahākāśyapa! Be patient, Venerable Mahākāśyapa! This is a foolish old woman without innate wisdom."

The Venerable Mahākāśyapa said to Ānanda: "Since I went forth, I have never known any other teacher than the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Perfectly Awakened One. Before I went forth, I constantly reflected on birth, old age, sickness, death, grief, sorrow, vexation, and suffering. I knew that household life is full of worldly entanglements and many afflictions, while going forth to the open air of renunciation is difficult for a householder to practice in the non-household state, yet it is entirely pure. For the duration of one's life, fully complete in purity, the holy life is immaculate. I resolved to shave off my beard and hair, put on the kāṣāya robe, and with right faith leave the household to study the Way. I took my robe of hundreds of thousands of gold pieces' worth and cut it into strips to make a saṅghāṭī. I thought, 'If there is an arhat in the world, I shall go forth under him in obscurity.' After I went forth, on the road between Rājagṛha and the village of Nālandā, at the Bahuputtaka Shrine, I encountered the World-Honored One seated upright, with wondrous and extraordinary marks, his faculties tranquil, of supreme quiescence, like a golden mountain.

"Seeing him at that time, I thought: 'This is my teacher! This is the World-Honored One! This is an arhat! This is the Perfectly Awakened One!' At that time, with single mind and palms joined, I reverently bowed and addressed the Buddha: 'He is my great teacher! I am his disciple.'

"The Buddha told me: 'So it is, Kāśyapa. I am your teacher; you are my disciple. Kāśyapa, having accomplished such a truly pure mind of reverence, if one who does not know claims to know, does not see claims to see, is not an arhat yet claims to be an arhat, is not a perfectly awakened one yet claims to be a perfectly awakened one, such a person's body should naturally shatter into seven pieces. Kāśyapa, I say "I know" because I truly know. I say "I see" because I truly see. I am a true arhat, and I say "arhat." I am a true perfectly awakened one, and I say "perfectly awakened one." Kāśyapa, when I expound the Dharma to disciples, it is with cause and condition, not without cause and condition. It is with a foundation, not without a foundation. It is with spiritual power, not without spiritual power. Therefore, Kāśyapa, if you wish to hear the Dharma, you should train thus: "If I wish to hear the Dharma, to be benefited by its meaning, I should unify my mind, revere and honor it, listen attentively with focused attention, and think thus: 'I shall correctly contemplate the arising and ceasing of the five aggregates, the gathering and disappearing of the six sense-contact bases. I shall dwell with right mindfulness happily in the four foundations of mindfulness, cultivate the seven factors of awakening, and personally realize the eight liberations. I shall constantly be mindful of my body, never ceasing. Free from shamelessness and lack of conscience, in the presence of the Great Teacher and great elders of holy conduct, I shall always abide in shame and conscience.'" You should train thus!'

"At that time, the World-Honored One expounded the Dharma for me, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening me. After instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening me, he rose from his seat and departed. I followed him toward his dwelling place. I had made my saṅghāṭī of cloth worth hundreds of thousands of gold pieces, folding it fourfold as a seat. At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing my sincere intention, stopped along the way at various places. I then spread out my robe as a seat-covering and invited the Buddha to sit. The World-Honored One sat down, touched the robe with his hand, and praised it: 'Kāśyapa, this robe is light and fine. This robe is soft.' I then addressed him: 'So it is, World-Honored One. This robe is light and fine. This robe is soft. I humbly request that the World-Honored One accept this robe of mine.' The Buddha addressed Kāśyapa:

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Sanskrit into Chinese during the Liu Song dynasty by Guṇabhadra.

---

It appears that the text content for Fascicle 41 (卷第四十一) was not included in your message. The Chinese text field between the delimiter lines is empty. Could you please provide the actual Chinese text of this fascicle so that I can translate it in full?

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Forty-Two

---

### (1145)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, to whom should one give?"

The Buddha said: "Great King, give wherever your heart delights."

King Prasenajit again addressed the Buddha, saying: "Where should one give to obtain great fruit and reward?"

The Buddha said: "Great King, this is a different question. Your question 'Where should one give?' is one matter. Your further question 'Where should one give to obtain great fruit?' is yet another matter. I will now ask you; answer me as you wish. Great King, suppose this country were to go to battle, mustering all its warriors, and there came a brahmin youth from the east, young and tender, soft and fair, with white skin and black hair, unskilled in martial arts, unlearned in strategy and tactics, fearful and cowardly, unable to steady himself, unable to endure facing the enemy; whether stabbed or shot at, he has no skill and cannot wound the foe. What do you think, Great King? Would the king reward such a man?"

The king addressed the Buddha: "He would not be rewarded, World-Honored One."

"Likewise, Great King, if a kṣatriya youth came from the south, a vaiśya youth from the west, and a śūdra youth from the north, all without any skills, just like the brahmin youth from the east, would the king reward them?"

The king addressed the Buddha: "He would not reward them, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the great king: "Suppose when this country musters its army and goes to battle, a brahmin youth comes from the east, young and fair, with white skin and black hair, well-trained in martial arts, knowing the strategies and methods of combat, brave and fearless, fighting hard without retreating, standing firm and observing carefully, wielding weapons that can wound, and able to defeat great enemies. What do you think, Great King? Would you bestow a heavy reward on such a warrior?"

The king addressed the Buddha: "A heavy reward, World-Honored One!"

"Likewise, if a kṣatriya youth comes from the south, a vaiśya youth from the west, and a śūdra youth from the north, all young and fair, skilled in all arts and tactics, brave and capable, fighting hard and repelling the enemy, just like the brahmin youth from the east, would the king reward such warriors?"

The king addressed the Buddha: "A heavy reward, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha said: "Great King, in the same way, śramaṇas and brahmins who have abandoned the five factors and accomplished the five factors establish a field of merit. Those who give to this field obtain great benefit and great fruit and reward. What are the five factors that are abandoned? They are the hindrances of sensual desire, ill-will, sloth-and-torpor, restlessness-and-remorse, and doubt, which have been cut off and fully known. This is called the abandoning of the five factors. What are the five factors that are accomplished? They are the accomplishment of the body of training-free morality, the body of training-free concentration, the body of wisdom, the body of liberation, and the body of the knowledge and vision of liberation. This is called the accomplishment of the five factors. Great King, those who have thus abandoned the five factors and accomplished the five factors establish a field of merit. Those who give to this field obtain great fruit and reward." At that time, the World-Honored One further spoke in verse:

> "Wielding weapons in fierce battle,
> the capable and brave warrior,
> because of his valor in combat,
> is rewarded heavily according to his merit.
> One does not reward those of noble lineage
> who are timid, weak, and without courage.
> Those who practice patience, cultivate goodness,
> who see truth establish a field of merit.
> Complete in the discipline of noble ones,
> accomplished in deep and wondrous wisdom,
> though their lineage may be humble,
> they are worthy fields of merit for giving.
> Clothing, food, money and treasures,
> bedding and various provisions,
> all should be given with respect,
> because they uphold pure precepts.
> People set up along forest borders
> wells to provide for travelers,
> bridges over streams and valleys,
> and shelters along desolate roads.
> Those of virtue and much learning,
> travelers find rest among them,
> just as when heavy clouds arise,
> thunder and lightning shine and flash.
> Rain falls broadly upon the soil,
> and all the plants flourish lushly;
> birds and beasts all rejoice,
> and farmers are filled with delight.
> So too, with pure and trusting hearts,
> with hearing-wisdom, abandoning the stain of stinginess,
> with wealth and abundant food and drink,
> one constantly gives to the good field of merit.
> Singing out with increased joy and love,
> like thunder-rain upon good fields,
> merit flows like streams of moisture,
> soaking and permeating the donor's heart.
> Wealth, fame, and renown flow forth,
> along with the great fruit of nirvāṇa."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (1146)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "How is it, World-Honored One? Do brahmins, after death, only return to be reborn in brahmin families of their own caste, or in kṣatriya, vaiśya, or śūdra families?"

The Buddha said: "Great King, how can that be so? Great King, you should know that there are four kinds of people. What are the four? There is one kind of person who goes from darkness into darkness. There is one kind of person who goes from darkness into light. There is one kind of person who goes from light into darkness. There is one kind of person who goes from light into light.

"Great King, how does one kind of person go from darkness into darkness? There are people born in lowly families, whether in a caṇḍāla family, a fisher or hunter family, a bamboo-worker family, a chariot-maker family, or various other families of low and degraded crafts, being poor, short-lived, with haggard and worn bodies. Moreover, they serve in lowly households and also serve others in menial tasks. This is called darkness. Dwelling in this darkness, they further engage in evil bodily conduct, evil verbal conduct, and evil mental conduct. Because of these causes and conditions, when the body breaks up and life ends, they are reborn in an evil destiny, falling into hell. It is like someone going from darkness into darkness, from a latrine into a latrine, washing blood with blood, abandoning evil only to take on evil. One who goes from darkness into darkness is just like this. Therefore it is called going from darkness into darkness.

"How is one called going from darkness into light? There are people in the world born into lowly families, and so forth down to performing menial tasks for others. This is called darkness. Yet such a person, within this darkness, engages in good bodily conduct, good verbal conduct, and good mental conduct. Because of these causes and conditions, when the body breaks up and life ends, they are reborn in a good destiny, receiving heavenly rebirth by transformation. It is like someone mounting a bed and then a horse, and from a horse ascending an elephant. One who goes from darkness into light is just like this. This is called a person going from darkness into light.

"How does a person go from light into darkness? There are people in the world born into wealthy and prosperous families, whether in a great kṣatriya clan, a great brahmin clan, a great elder's clan, or various other wealthy and prosperous families, possessing much money and property, servants and attendants, widely acquainted and connected, receiving fine physical form, being clever and intelligent. This is called light. Within this light, they engage in evil bodily conduct, evil verbal conduct, and evil mental conduct. Because of these causes and conditions, when the body breaks up and life ends, they are reborn in an evil destiny, falling into hell. It is like someone descending from a high tower and mounting a great elephant, then descending from the elephant to mount a horse, descending from the horse to mount a carriage, descending from the carriage to sit on a bed, descending from the bed to fall on the ground, and from the ground falling into a pit. One who goes from light into darkness is just like this.

"How does a person go from light into light? There are people in the world born into wealthy and prosperous families, and so forth down to having fine and dignified appearance. This is called light. Within this light, they engage in good bodily conduct, good verbal conduct, and good mental conduct. Because of these causes and conditions, when the body breaks up and life ends, they are reborn in a good destiny, receiving heavenly rebirth by transformation. It is like someone going from one pavilion to another pavilion, and so forth, from one bed to another bed. One who goes from light into light is just like this. This is called a person going from light into light." At that time, the World-Honored One further spoke in verse:

> "Those who are poor and afflicted,
> lacking faith, increasing in anger and hatred,
> miserly, greedy, with evil and wrong thoughts,
> deluded and foolish, without reverence.
> Seeing śramaṇas and men of the way,
> who keep precepts and are learned,
> they revile and do not praise them,
> and obstruct others from giving and receiving.
> Such men as these,
> from this world to the next,
> shall fall into hell,
> going from darkness into darkness.
> If there are poor people
> with faith and little anger and hatred,
> who constantly give rise to a sense of shame and conscience,
> who practice generosity, free from the stain of miserliness,
> seeing śramaṇas and pure practitioners,
> who keep precepts and are learned,
> they humbly greet and inquire after them,
> and provide for them suitably and well.
> They encourage others to give,
> and praise both giving and receiving.
> Such people who cultivate goodness,
> from this world to the next,
> are reborn in a good destiny, ascending to heaven,
> going from darkness into light.
> There are wealthy and prosperous men
> lacking faith, with much anger and hatred,
> miserly, greedy, with hateful and evil thoughts,
> perverse, deluded, and without reverence.
> Seeing śramaṇas and pure practitioners,
> they revile and do not praise them,
> and obstruct others from being generous,
> and also cut off those who would receive gifts.
> Such evil men,
> from this world to the next,
> shall be reborn in the painful hells,
> going from light into darkness.
> If there are wealthy men
> with faith and without anger and hatred,
> who constantly give rise to a sense of shame and conscience,
> who practice generosity, free from anger and jealousy,
> seeing śramaṇas and pure practitioners,
> who keep precepts and are learned,
> they first come forward to greet and inquire,
> and provide whatever is needed as appropriate.
> They encourage others to make offerings,
> and praise both giving and receiving.
> Such men as these,
> from this world to the next,
> are reborn in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three,
> going from light into light."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (1147)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit, day after day, his body covered with dust, came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side.

The Buddha said: "Great King, where have you come from?"

King Prasenajit addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, such is the dharma of a consecrated king, sovereign among people; with diligence and effort, ruling over great lands, administering the affairs of the kingdom, traveling about and inspecting, and so I have come here."

The Buddha told the great king: "I will now ask the great king; answer me as you wish. Suppose a person came from the east, trustworthy and reliable, who has never spoken falsely, and addressed the king, saying: 'I come from the east. I saw a mountain of stone, extremely broad and vast, unbroken and undamaged, without any cracks, grinding along the ground as it comes, crushing all living beings, grasses, and trees.' From the south, west, and north, there also came people, trustworthy and reliable, who also never speak falsely, and addressed the king, saying: 'I saw a mountain of stone, broad, vast, and towering, unbroken and undamaged, without any cracks, grinding along the ground as it comes, crushing all living beings, grasses, and trees.' Great King, what do you think? Faced with such a terrifying spectacle, with fearful calamity and mutual destruction, with the fate of all beings running out, and human existence being so hard to obtain, what plan would you make?"

The king addressed the Buddha: "If it were so, there would be no other plan than to cultivate goodness, and in the Buddha's Dharma and Vinaya, to devote oneself single-mindedly with diligence."

The Buddha told the great king: "Why do you say, 'Faced with fearful, terrifying calamity suddenly arising in the world, the fate of all beings running out, human birth being hard to obtain, one should only practice the Dharma, practice righteousness, practice merit, and devote oneself diligently in the Buddha's teaching'? Why do you not say, 'Being a consecrated king, the chief among people, with sovereign authority over the great earth, with many affairs requiring management'?"

The king addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, to speak of it at leisure: 'Being a consecrated king, the chief among people, ruling over the great earth, with much to manage; when contending with words against words, with wealth against wealth, with elephants against elephants, with chariots against chariots, with infantry against infantry, at such times there is no sovereignty, for there is either victory or defeat.' Therefore I say: 'Faced with fearful, terrifying calamity suddenly arising, the fate of all beings running out, human birth being hard to obtain, there is no other plan than to practice righteousness, practice the Dharma, practice merit, and in the Buddha's teaching devote oneself wholeheartedly with reliance.'"

The Buddha told the great king: "So it is! So it is! There is a constant grinding pressure, namely the evil eons of old age, illness, and suffering, which grind and press upon all beings. What plan should be made? It is right to cultivate righteousness, cultivate the Dharma, cultivate merit, cultivate goodness, cultivate loving-kindness, and in the Buddha's Dharma devote oneself with diligence." At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "As if there were a great mountain of stone,
> towering, vast, without crack or flaw,
> coming from all four directions,
> grinding and pressing upon this great earth.
> Neither soldiers, horses, spells, nor magical arts
> have the power to ward it off.
> The evil eons of old age, illness, and death
> constantly grind and press upon all beings.
> The four great noble clans,
> caṇḍālas and hunters,
> those dwelling at home and those gone forth,
> those who keep precepts and those who break them,
> all are ground and pressed,
> and none can rescue or protect them.
> Therefore the wise person,
> observing what benefits oneself,
> establishes pure faith,
> trusting in the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha.
> With body, speech, and mind pure,
> following and conforming to the true Dharma,
> in the present world, fame and renown flow forth,
> and at the end, one is reborn in heaven above."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (1148)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. At that time, there were seven Nirgrantha ascetics, seven Jaṭila ascetics, and seven Ekaśāṭaka ascetics, all with large, rough bodies, wandering about and standing outside the gate of Jeta's Grove.

At that time, King Prasenajit saw them from afar, wandering outside the gate. He immediately rose from his seat, went before them, joined his palms in greeting, and three times announced his own name, saying: "I am King Prasenajit, the King of Kośala."

At that time, the World-Honored One said to King Prasenajit: "Why did you just now show such reverence to those people, three times announcing your name and greeting them with joined palms?"

The king addressed the Buddha: "I thought thus: 'If there are arhats in the world, these must be they.'"

The Buddha told King Prasenajit: "Stop for now, Great King! You do not know who is an arhat and who is not an arhat, for you have not attained the knowledge of others' minds. You should first associate closely with them and observe their moral conduct. Only over a long time can you come to know; do not decide hastily. Examine carefully and deliberately; do not be merely casual. You should use wisdom, not lack of wisdom. Through undergoing various trials and hardships, one can distinguish for oneself. Through dealings and interactions, one can discern what is genuine and what is false. Through seeing their speech, one comes to know their understanding. Only over a long time can one know; it cannot be known in haste. One should reflect and observe with wisdom."

The king addressed the Buddha: "How marvelous, World-Honored One! How well you have explained this principle, saying: 'Through long association, observe their moral conduct, and so forth, through hearing their speech, one knows their understanding.' I have household servants who also went forth, taking on the appearance of such ascetics, traveling through other countries, and then returned home, discarded their robes, and again indulged in the five sensual pleasures. Therefore I know that the World-Honored One has spoken well: one should dwell together with them and observe their moral conduct, and so forth, through their speech one knows whether they have wisdom."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "Do not judge a person's good or evil
> by outward appearance alone.
> One should not, upon a brief meeting,
> entrust one's heart and mind to them.
> Some present a guarded body and speech,
> while their worldly mind is unrestrained.
> They are like brass and copper
> coated with the color of real gold;
> inwardly harboring base and impure minds,
> outwardly displaying the dignified bearing of sages,
> they travel through various lands,
> deceiving the people of the world."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (1149)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, with King Prasenajit at the head, together with seven other kings and their various ministers, all assembled together and engaged in this discussion: "Among the five sensual pleasures, which is the foremost?" One person said: "Visible forms are the foremost." Others declared sound, smell, taste, or touch to be the foremost. Among them, one person said: "Each of us has stated our own preference as the foremost, but there is no definitive conclusion. Let us go to the World-Honored One and ask about this matter. Whatever the World-Honored One says, we shall remember and uphold together."

At that time, with King Prasenajit at the head, along with the seven kings, ministers, and their retinues, they came to visit the Buddha, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side. They addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, we seven kings and our ministers have been discussing: 'Among the five qualities of sensual pleasure, which is superior?' Among us, some said form is superior, some said sound is superior, some said smell is superior, some said taste is superior, and some said touch is superior. There was no definitive conclusion. We have come to ask the World-Honored One: ultimately, which is superior?"

The Buddha told the kings: "Each accords with what one finds pleasing. I have a further explanation for all of them, and for this reason I speak of the five qualities of sensual pleasure. Yet there are people who find a particular form pleasing, and they cling only to that one form, satisfied in their desire. Even if there were forms superior to it, if those are not what they love, they neither touch nor look at them, and they say what they love is the foremost, unsurpassed. Likewise for those who love form, so too for sound, smell, taste, and touch. Whatever one loves, one declares to be the most excellent, joyfully delighting and clinging to it. Though there may be things more excellent surpassing it, if it is not what one desires, one neither touches nor looks at it, declaring: 'What I love is the most excellent and most wondrous, without comparison, without superior.'"

At that time, there was an upāsaka in the assembly named Candana, who rose from his seat, arranged his robes, bared his right shoulder, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha: "Well spoken, World-Honored One! Well spoken, Sugata!"

The Buddha told the upāsaka: "Well spoken, Candana! Excellently spoken, Candana!"

At that time, the upāsaka Candana then spoke in verse:

> "The king of the Aṅga clan,
> wearing pearl necklaces and armor,
> the people of Magadha gathered in celebration,
> for the Tathāgata appeared in their land.
> His fame spreads everywhere,
> like the king of snowy mountains.
> Like a lotus in pure water,
> pure and without blemish,
> opening in the sunlight,
> its fragrance perfumes the land.
> The land of Aṅga shines forth
> like the sun in the sky.
> Beholding the Tathāgata's power of wisdom
> is like lighting a torch in the night.
> He is an eye, a great light;
> to those who come, he resolves their doubts."

At that time, the kings praised him: "Well spoken, upāsaka Candana!"

Then the seven kings removed their seven jeweled upper garments and presented them to the upāsaka.

At that time, the seven kings, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed their approval, rose from their seats, and departed.

At that time, the upāsaka Candana, knowing that the kings had departed, rose from his seat, arranged his robes, bared his right shoulder, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha: "The seven kings have now given me seven upper garments. I humbly request that the World-Honored One accept these seven garments, out of compassion."

At that time, the World-Honored One, out of compassion, accepted the seven garments. The upāsaka Candana rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (1150)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit, his body extremely corpulent, his entire body streaming with sweat, came to visit the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, panting and gasping for breath.

At that time, the World-Honored One said to King Prasenajit: "Great King, your body is extremely corpulent."

The great king addressed the Buddha: "Indeed so, World-Honored One. I suffer from this corpulent body. Because of this extreme corpulence, I constantly feel shame, disgust, and distress."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "A person should be self-mindful;
> at every meal, know moderation.
> Then one's sensations will be light,
> digesting well and preserving long life."

At that time, there was a youth named Uttara sitting in the assembly. King Prasenajit told Uttara: "Can you receive from the World-Honored One the verse just spoken, and at every mealtime recite it for me? If you can do so, I will bestow upon you one hundred thousand gold coins, and will always provide you with food."

Uttara addressed the king: "I accept the command and shall recite it."

At that time, King Prasenajit, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

Then Uttara, knowing the king had departed, came before the World-Honored One and received the verse that had been spoken. At every mealtime, at each meal he recited it, saying: "Great King, as the Buddha, the World-Honored One, the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, with his knowledge and vision, spoke this verse:

> "'A person should be self-mindful;
> at every meal, know moderation.
> Then one's sensations will be light,
> digesting well and preserving long life.'"

In this way, King Prasenajit gradually, over time, became slender and well-proportioned in body, with a dignified and handsome appearance. Standing atop his palace, facing toward the direction of the Buddha's dwelling, he joined his palms in reverence, placed his right knee on the ground, and three times uttered these words: "Homage and reverence to the World-Honored One, the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One! Homage and reverence to the World-Honored One, the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One! You have given me benefit in the present life, benefit in future lives, and benefit in both present and future lives, because of knowing moderation in food."

---

### (1151)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī.

At that time, a young Asura came to visit the Buddha, and in the Buddha's presence uttered coarse, evil, and unwholesome words, reviling and reproaching him with anger.

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "Not being angry conquers anger;
> the unwholesome is overcome by the wholesome.
> Generosity overcomes miserliness;
> truthful words destroy false speech.
> Not reviling, nor being cruel,
> always abiding in the mind of noble sages;
> the evil person abides in anger and hatred,
> unmoving like a mountain boulder.
> One who can restrain rising anger
> is like one who controls a team of wild horses.
> I call such a person a skilled charioteer,
> not the one who merely holds the reins."

At that time, the young Asura addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, I now repent! I was foolish and ignorant, undiscerning and unwise, and in Gautama's presence I reviled and insulted him." Having thus confessed, the Asura, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (1152)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a young man, Bhāradvāja the brahmin, came to visit the Buddha, and in the World-Honored One's presence uttered coarse, evil, and unwholesome words, reviling and reproaching him with anger.

At that time, the World-Honored One said to the young Bhāradvāja: "On some occasion, on an auspicious day marked by good stars, do you hold a feast for all your kinsmen and relatives?"

Bhāradvāja addressed the Buddha: "Indeed, Gautama."

The Buddha told Bhāradvāja: "If your kinsmen do not accept the food, to whom would it belong?"

Bhāradvāja addressed the Buddha: "If they do not accept the food, the food would still belong to me."

The Buddha told Bhāradvāja: "You are just like this. In the Tathāgata's presence you have uttered coarse, evil, and unwholesome words, reviling and reproaching. I have utterly not accepted them. Such reviling, to whom should it belong?"

Bhāradvāja addressed the Buddha: "Even though Gautama does not accept it, I have offered it as a gift, and so it has been given."

The Buddha told Bhāradvāja: "That is not called mutually presenting gifts. How can it be called a mutual exchange?"

Bhāradvāja addressed the Buddha: "What is called mutually presenting gifts and making an exchange? And what is called not accepting what is presented, and not making an exchange?"

The Buddha told Bhāradvāja: "If one returns reviling for reviling, anger for anger, striking for striking, fighting for fighting, that is called mutually presenting gifts, called making an exchange. But, Bhāradvāja, if one does not return reviling for reviling, does not return anger for anger, does not return striking for striking, does not return fighting for fighting, then that is not mutually presenting gifts, and is not called an exchange."

Bhāradvāja addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, I have heard that the ancient brahmin elders, the senior and venerable practitioners of the way, great teachers, have said: 'The Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, when reviled, insulted, and reproached with anger in his presence, does not become angry or wrathful.' And now, does Gautama have anger?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "One without anger, how could there be anger?
> With right livelihood, he tames and subdues.
> With right knowledge, the mind is liberated;
> the wise one has no anger.
> One who returns anger with anger
> is an evil person.
> One who does not return anger with anger
> conquers in battle what is hard to conquer.
> Not being angry is superior to anger;
> the remaining three verses are as stated previously."

At that time, the young Bhāradvāja addressed the Buddha: "I repent, Gautama! I was foolish and ignorant, undiscerning and unwise, and in the śramaṇa Gautama's presence uttered coarse, evil, and unwholesome words, reviling and reproaching." Having heard what the Buddha said, he rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

---

### (1153)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in the Eastern Park, at the Lecture Hall of Mṛgāramātā.

At that time, in the afternoon, the World-Honored One emerged from meditation and went to the eastern shade of the lecture hall, walking back and forth in the open air.

At that time, there was a brahmin named Akkosaka Bhāradvāja ("Abusive Bhāradvāja") who came to visit the Buddha. In the World-Honored One's presence, he uttered coarse, evil, and unwholesome words, reviling and reproaching him. The World-Honored One was walking back and forth, and the brahmin followed behind the World-Honored One as he walked. When the World-Honored One finished walking and stopped at one place, the brahmin said: "Gautama, are you defeated?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "The victor only increases enmity;
> the vanquished lies down in unease.
> Abandoning both victory and defeat,
> one attains peaceful and secure sleep."

The brahmin said: "Gautama, I now repent! I was foolish and ignorant, undiscerning and unwise. How could I have uttered coarse, evil, and unwholesome words, reviling and reproaching, in Gautama's presence?"

At that time, the brahmin, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, and went back the way he came.

---

### (1154)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in the Eastern Park, at the Lecture Hall of Mṛgāramātā. In the early morning, the World-Honored One put on his robe, took his bowl, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. At that time, the brahmin Akkosaka Bhāradvāja saw the World-Honored One from afar and uttered coarse, evil, and unwholesome words, reviling and reproaching him with anger, and grabbing dirt, he threw it at the Buddha. At that time, a contrary wind blew the dirt back, and it fell upon the brahmin's own body.

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "If a person is without anger or hatred,
> and another reviles and insults him,
> one who is pure, without defilements or stains,
> that evil returns to the one who sent it.
> It is like throwing dirt at another;
> a contrary wind returns the dust upon oneself."

At that time, the brahmin addressed the Buddha: "I repent, Gautama! I was foolish and ignorant, lacking in goodness and discernment. How could I have uttered coarse, evil, and unwholesome words, reviling and reproaching, in Gautama's presence?"

At that time, the brahmin, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, and went back the way he came.

---

### (1155)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling in the land of Kośala, and arrived at Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a brahmin named Viruddha ("Contrary to Meaning"), who heard that the śramaṇa Gautama was traveling through the land of Kośala and had arrived at Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. Having heard this, he thought: "I shall go to the śramaṇa Gautama. When I hear him teach the Dharma, I will contradict its meaning." Having thought this, he went to the monastery and arrived at the World-Honored One's place.

At that time, the World-Honored One was surrounded by an immeasurable retinue, teaching the Dharma. The World-Honored One saw the brahmin Viruddha coming from afar and immediately became silent.

The brahmin Viruddha addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, please teach the Dharma; I would be glad to hear it!"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "The brahmin Viruddha
> has not yet understood the deep meaning.
> Inwardly harboring a mind of jealousy and anger,
> he wishes to raise objections to the Dharma.
> Tame the contrary and opposing mind,
> and all faithless and unpleasant intentions.
> Quiet all obstructing defilements,
> and then you will understand the deep and wondrous teaching."

At that time, the brahmin Viruddha thought: "The śramaṇa Gautama already knows my mind." Having heard what the Buddha said, he rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (1156)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. In the early morning, the World-Honored One put on his robe, took his bowl, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food.

At that time, there was a brahmin named Ahiṃsaka ("Non-harming") who came to visit the Buddha and addressed him, saying: "World-Honored One, my name is Ahiṃsaka (Non-harming). Is this name truly fitting?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "If it is truly fitting, then the body does not harm, the speech does not harm, and the mind does not harm; then the name is truly fitting." At that time, the World-Honored One spoke in verse:

> "If the mind does not kill or harm,
> and speech and thought are likewise so,
> then one is truly free from harm,
> and does not cause fear in living beings."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the brahmin Ahiṃsaka, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, and went back the way he came.

---

### (1157)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. In the early morning, the World-Honored One put on his robe, took his bowl, and entered the city of Rājagṛha to beg for food. Going from house to house in order, he came to the house of the brahmin Agnidatta ("Fire-Giver").

The brahmin Agnidatta saw the Buddha coming from afar and immediately prepared various fine foods and drinks, filling the bowl and giving it to him.

On the second day and the third day, when the Buddha came again to beg at his house, the brahmin Agnidatta saw the Buddha coming from afar and thought: "Why does this bald-headed śramaṇa keep coming back? Is he greedy for fine food?"

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing the thoughts in the brahmin Agnidatta's mind, spoke in verse:

> "The king of heaven rains day after day;
> the farmer plows day and night.
> Again and again seeds are planted;
> this field again and again yields grain.
> Just as a woman is pregnant again and again,
> and a milch cow again and again bears calves,
> again and again there are those who seek,
> and so one can again and again give generously.
> Because of giving again and again,
> one constantly obtains great fame and renown.
> Again and again corpses are discarded;
> again and again there is weeping and grief.
> Again and again there is birth and death;
> again and again sorrow and suffering.
> Again and again burned by fire;
> again and again eaten by worms.
> If one attains the Noble Path,
> one does not again and again receive existences.
> One does not again and again undergo birth and death;
> not again and again sorrow and suffering.
> Not again and again burned by fire;
> not again and again eaten by worms."

At that time, the brahmin Agnidatta, hearing the Buddha's verses, regained his faith. He again prepared various fine foods and drinks, filled the bowl, and offered it. The World-Honored One did not accept it, because it was given as a result of having spoken verses. He then spoke further in verse:

> "Because of having spoken the Dharma in verse,
> one should not accept food in return.
> One should observe one's own principle;
> teaching the Dharma, one does not accept food.
> Brahmin, you should know,
> this is the way of pure livelihood.
> You should make other offerings
> to the pure Great Sage,
> who has exhausted all outflows,
> and in whom all defiling dharmas are cut off.
> Offer food and drink
> to this excellent field of merit.
> One who wishes to seek merit
> should regard me as that excellent field."

The brahmin Agnidatta addressed the Buddha: "Then to whom should I give this food?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "I do not see anyone among the devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brahmins, celestial spirits, or worldly people who could eat this faith-offering and have their body be at ease. Take this food and dispose of it in water where there are no insects, or on ground where there is little living grass."

At that time, the brahmin took the food and placed it in water where there were no insects. The water immediately let off smoke, boiled, and hissed. It was like a red-hot iron ball thrown into water: the water immediately sends up smoke, boils, and hisses. In the same way, when the brahmin placed the food in the water, the water immediately let off smoke, boiled, and hissed. At that time, the brahmin Agnidatta exclaimed: "How extraordinary! Gautama possesses great virtue and great power, to make this food produce such a miraculous transformation!"

At that time, the brahmin Agnidatta, because of this miraculous transformation of the food, gained faith and reverence, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One, may I be permitted to go forth and receive full ordination in the True Dharma, and cultivate the holy life?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "You may now go forth and receive full ordination in the True Dharma."

Having gone forth, he reflected thus: "It is for this reason that sons of good families shave off their hair and beard, don the kāṣāya robe, and with right faith leave the household life to go forth and study the Way." And so forth, until he attained arhatship and his mind was well liberated.

---

### (1158)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, in the country of Śrāvastī, there was a brahmin woman named Vāseṭṭhī who had faith in the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, who had taken refuge in the Buddha, refuge in the Dharma, and refuge in the Sangha of bhikṣus. Regarding the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, she had abandoned doubt. Regarding suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the path, she had also abandoned doubt. She had seen the truth, attained the fruit, attained uninterrupted wisdom. Her husband was a brahmin of the Bhāradvāja clan. Whenever she performed any task in her daily activities, if there was the slightest success or failure, she would exclaim "Homage to the Buddha!" and, facing toward the direction where the Tathāgata was dwelling, she would join her palms according to that direction and three times utter these words: "Homage to the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, whose body is of pure gold, with a circular halo of one fathom, whose well-proportioned body is like a nyagrodha tree, who teaches the wondrous Dharma well, the Honored One among munis, the foremost among sages. He is my great teacher!"

At that time, her husband the brahmin, hearing this, became angry and displeased, and said to his wife: "Are you possessed by a ghost? There is no meaning in this! You abandon the great brahmins of the Three Vedas and their virtues, and instead praise that bald-headed śramaṇa, who belongs to the portion of darkness and is not honored by the world! I shall now go and debate with your great teacher. That will be enough to show who is superior."

The wife said to her husband: "I do not see anyone among the devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brahmins, celestial spirits, or worldly people who could debate with the World-Honored One, the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, whose body is of golden hue, with a circular halo of one fathom, whose well-proportioned body is like a nyagrodha tree, whose speech is subtle and wondrous, the foremost among sages, my great teacher. However, brahmin, go ahead, and you will find out for yourself."

At that time, the brahmin went to visit the Buddha. They exchanged greetings and courtesies, and he withdrew and sat to one side. He then spoke in verse:

> "By killing what
> does one sleep in peace?
> By killing what
> does the mind become free of sorrow?
> By killing what
> does Gautama praise?"

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing the thoughts in the brahmin's mind, spoke in verse:

> "By killing anger and hatred,
> one sleeps in peace.
> By killing anger and enmity,
> the mind becomes free of sorrow.
> Anger is the root of poison;
> it can destroy the seed of sweetness.
> One who can destroy that,
> the noble sages praise.
> If one can destroy that,
> the mind becomes free of sorrow."

At that time, the Bhāradvāja brahmin, hearing what the Buddha said, was instructed, taught, illuminated, and delighted. The Buddha then progressively taught the Dharma, namely, the teaching on giving, the teaching on morality, the teaching on heavenly rebirth, the teaching on the gratification of sensual desires, their danger and defilement, and on purity, renunciation, and detachment, following in order the benefits of merit and purity, distinguishing and expounding broadly. Just as a clean white cloth readily takes dye, so the Bhāradvāja brahmin, right there on his seat, with respect to the Four Noble Truths, attained uninterrupted penetration, namely: suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the path.

This brahmin saw the Dharma, attained the Dharma, knew the Dharma, entered the Dharma, crossed beyond all doubts without depending on another, and in the True Dharma and Vinaya attained fearlessness. He immediately rose from his seat, bared his right shoulder, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha: "I have crossed over, World-Honored One! I have crossed over, Sugata! I now take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, take refuge in the Sangha of bhikṣus. For the rest of my life I shall be an upāsaka. Bear witness to me!"

At that time, the Bhāradvāja brahmin, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed, returning to his own home.

His wife, the upāsikā, saw her husband coming from afar. Seeing him, she said: "Have you already debated with the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, whose body is of pure gold, with a circular halo of one fathom, whose well-proportioned body is like a nyagrodha tree, the supreme in wondrous speech, the foremost among sages, the great Honored One among munis, my great teacher?"

Her husband answered: "I have never seen anyone among the devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brahmins, celestial spirits, or worldly people who could debate with the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, whose body is of true golden hue, with a circular halo of one fathom, whose well-proportioned body is like a nyagrodha tree, the supreme in wondrous speech, the foremost among sages, the Honored One among munis, your great teacher. Now prepare good Dharma robes for me. I shall take them to the World-Honored One's place to go forth and study the Way."

At that time, the wife took fresh and clean white cloth and had it made into Dharma robes.

At that time, the brahmin took the robes and went to the World-Honored One's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and said: "World-Honored One, may I be permitted to go forth and study the Way in the World-Honored One's Dharma, and cultivate the holy life?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "You may now go forth in this Dharma and Vinaya, study the Way, and cultivate the holy life."

Having gone forth, he reflected in solitude: "It is for this reason that sons of good families shave off their hair and beard, don the kāṣāya robe, and go forth from household life to study the Way." And so forth, until he attained arhatship and his mind was well liberated.

---

### (1159)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a brahmin named Māgha came to visit the Buddha. He exchanged greetings and courtesies with the World-Honored One, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama, I constantly practice giving at home. When one person comes, I give to that one person. When two, three, or even up to a hundred or a thousand come, I give to all of them. Do I obtain much merit by giving in this way?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "By giving in this way, you truly obtain great merit. Why? Because at home you constantly practice giving. When one person comes seeking, you give to that one. When two, three, or even up to a hundred or a thousand come, you give to all of them. Therefore you obtain great merit."

At that time, the brahmin Māgha spoke in verse:

> "At home, what I undertake and do,
> giving and also great assemblies,
> through this generous giving,
> I wish to seek great merit.
> I now ask the Muni
> what I ought to know.
> As Brahmā himself would see it,
> please explain and distinguish for me.
> What constitutes liberation
> and the excellent good destiny?
> How does one cultivate the means
> to be reborn in the Brahmā world?
> How does one give according to one's delight
> and be reborn in the radiant Brahmā heaven?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "The donor who holds a great assembly,
> giving according to what others delight in,
> with joyful and pure faith of mind,
> relies on the merit of wholesome deeds.
> Through what one has established,
> one seeks to leave behind all faults and evils.
> Far removed from sensual desire,
> one's mind is well liberated.
> Cultivating a mind of loving-kindness,
> the merit thereof is immeasurable,
> how much more when one adds sincerity
> and broadly sets up a great assembly.
> If among those gathered
> one attains wholesome states of mind,
> rightly directed toward good liberation,
> or toward other pure good destinies,
> through such excellent causes and conditions
> one is reborn in the Brahmā world.
> Through such generous giving,
> because one's mind is impartial,
> one is reborn in the Brahmā world,
> and one's lifespan is extended."

At that time, the brahmin Māgha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (1160)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a brahmin carrying a golden parasol and wearing fine garments, with an escort, came to visit the Buddha. He exchanged greetings and courtesies with the World-Honored One, withdrew and sat to one side, and spoke in verse:

> "None but the brahmin
> has conduct that is pure.
> The kṣatriya who practices austerities
> still deviates from tranquility.
> The brahmin of the Three Vedas,
> he alone is pure.
> Such purity as this
> does not exist among other beings."

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "Not knowing the path of purity,
> nor the supreme purity,
> those who seek stillness elsewhere
> will ultimately never attain purity."

The brahmin addressed the Buddha: "Does Gautama teach the path of purity and supreme purity? What is the path of purity? What is the supreme purity?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "Right view is the path of purity. When right view is cultivated and greatly cultivated, one cuts off sensual desire, cuts off ill-will, cuts off delusion. When, brahmin, sensual desire is permanently cut off, ill-will and delusion are permanently cut off, and all afflictions are permanently cut off, this is called supreme purity. Right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration: this is the path of purity. When right concentration is cultivated and greatly cultivated, one cuts off sensual desire, cuts off ill-will, cuts off delusion. When, brahmin, sensual desire is permanently cut off, ill-will and delusion are permanently cut off, and all afflictions are permanently cut off, this is called supreme purity."

The brahmin addressed the Buddha: "Does Gautama teach the path of purity and supreme purity? Gautama, I have many worldly affairs. I shall now take my leave and depart."

The Buddha told the brahmin: "You may go when you see fit."

The brahmin carrying the golden parasol, wearing fine garments, with his escort, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (1161)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain brahmin came to visit the Buddha. He exchanged greetings and courtesies with the World-Honored One, withdrew and sat to one side, and spoke in verse:

> "What constitutes moral conduct?
> What is proper deportment?
> What constitutes merit?
> What is called karma?
> By accomplishing what dharmas
> is one an arhat brahmin?"

At that time, the World-Honored One replied in verse:

> "The knowledge of recollecting past lives,
> seeing rebirth in heavens and evil destinies,
> attaining the end of all rebirths,
> the Muni's illumination is definitive.
> Knowing the mind is well liberated,
> liberated from all craving,
> complete in the three knowledges:
> such is a brahmin of the three knowledges."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the brahmin, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (1162)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

In the early morning, the World-Honored One put on his robe, took his bowl, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. The Venerable Ānanda followed behind the World-Honored One.

At that time, there were two old people, husband and wife, advanced in age with faculties decayed, their backs bent like hooks, who were squatting together before a fire at a rubbish heap at the head of a lane, warming themselves.

The World-Honored One saw the two old husband and wife, aged and foolish in their old age, backs bent like hooks, squatting together before the fire like old herons, gazing at each other with desire. Having seen them, he said to the Venerable Ānanda: "Do you see those two, husband and wife, aged and foolish in their old age, backs bent like hooks, squatting together before the fire like old herons, gazing at each other with desire?"

Ānanda addressed the Buddha: "Indeed so, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "If this old couple, during their youth, in the prime of their bodies, had diligently sought wealth, they could have become the foremost wealthy elder in the city of Śrāvastī. Or if they had shaved off their hair and beard, donned the kāṣāya robe, and with right faith left the household life to go forth and study the Way, diligently cultivating and practicing, they could have attained the foremost and highest fruit of arhatship. In the second stage, in the prime of their bodies, had they diligently sought wealth, they could have become the second wealthiest in the city of Śrāvastī. Or if they had shaved off their hair and beard, donned the kāṣāya robe, and with right faith left the household life to go forth and study the Way, they could have attained the fruit of non-returner. In the third stage, in their middle-aged bodies, had they diligently sought wealth, they could have become the third wealthiest in the city of Śrāvastī. Or if they had shaved off their hair and beard, donned the kāṣāya robe, and with right faith left the household life to go forth and study the Way, they could have attained the fruit of once-returner. Now, in their old age, with decayed faculties, they have no money, no means, no capability. They are no longer able to seek wealth or to attain the Dharma that surpasses ordinary human states."

At that time, the World-Honored One further spoke in verse:

> "Not having practiced the holy life,
> not having gained wealth in youth,
> they brood over things of the past,
> lying on the ground like bent bows.
> Not having cultivated the holy life,
> not having gained wealth in youth,
> they are like old heron birds,
> guarding their deaths by an empty pond."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Venerable Ānanda, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and put it into practice.

---

### (1163)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park, as described above. The difference is that only a different verse was spoken:

> "Destroyed by old age and death,
> the body and all its experiences perish.
> Only the merit of generous giving
> serves as provisions that follow oneself.
> Relying on good self-restraint,
> and cultivating the merit of meditation,
> according to one's ability, practice giving
> of money, property, food, and drink.
> Among the multitude, whether sleeping or waking,
> one does not live in vain."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the brahmin, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, paid his respects, and departed.

# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.
