# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Forty-Three

---

### (1164)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Bārāṇasī in the Deer Park at the Sages' Dwelling Place.

At that time, a great many bhikṣus gathered in the lecture hall and engaged in the following discussion: "Venerables! As the World-Honored One spoke in the Pārāyaṇa, the question of Tiṣya Maitreya:

> 'One who knows the two extremes,
> In the middle is forever unattached.
> Such a one is called a great person,
> Who does not cling to the five desires,
> Free from the fetters of afflictions,
> And transcends the worry of stitching.'

"Venerables! What is the meaning of this? What is an extreme? What are the two extremes? What is the middle? What is stitching? How does one contemplate? Knowing through wisdom, understanding through clear comprehension; what wisdom knows, what clear comprehension understands, making an end of suffering, liberation from suffering."

One answered, saying: "The six internal sense bases are one extreme. The six external sense bases are the second extreme. Feeling is the middle. Craving is the stitching. One who cultivates feeling obtains this and that cause; the body gradually grows, increases, and arises. Regarding this very dharma, knowing through wisdom, understanding through clear comprehension; what wisdom knows, what clear comprehension understands, making an end of suffering, liberation from suffering."

Another said: "Past lives are one extreme. Future lives are the second extreme. The present life is called the middle. Craving is the stitching. Cultivating and drawing near to this craving, through this and that cause, the body gradually contacts, increases, and arises, and so forth up to liberation from suffering."

Another said: "Pleasant feeling is one extreme. Painful feeling is the second extreme. Neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling is the middle. Craving is the stitching. Cultivating and drawing near to this craving, through this and that obtained, one's own body gradually contacts, increases, and arises, and so forth up to liberation from suffering."

Another said: "Existence is one extreme. Accumulation is the second extreme. Feeling is the middle. Craving is the stitching." Thus spoken in full, up to "liberation from suffering."

Another said: "The body is one extreme. The origination of the body is the second extreme. Craving is the stitching." Thus spoken in full, up to "liberation from suffering."

Another said: "All that we have said differs. That is, the various different explanations just given are not definitely known to be correct. What other teaching does the World-Honored One have regarding the Pārāyaṇa, the sūtra of the question of Tiṣya Maitreya? We should go and ask the World-Honored One fully. As the World-Honored One teaches, we shall uphold it."

At that time, the many bhikṣus went to the World-Honored One, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Earlier, the bhikṣus gathered in the lecture hall and said: 'Regarding the World-Honored One's teaching in the Pārāyaṇa, the sūtra of the question of Tiṣya Maitreya, concerning the two extremes, up to liberation from suffering.' Someone said: 'The six internal sense bases are said to be one extreme. The six external sense bases are said to be the second extreme. Feeling is the middle. Craving is the stitching.' As previously spoken in full, all were not definitive. Today we have come specifically to ask the World-Honored One, to fully inquire about this meaning. Of what we have said, who has grasped the meaning?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "What all of you have said is well said. I shall now teach you the further sūtra. What I taught in the Pārāyaṇa to Tiṣya Maitreya as the further sūtra is this: contact is one extreme, the origination of contact is the second extreme, feeling is the middle, craving is the stitching. Having cultivated and drawn near to craving, through this and that obtained, the body, conditioned by contact, increases and arises. Regarding this dharma, knowing through wisdom, understanding through clear comprehension; what wisdom knows, what clear comprehension understands, making an end of suffering, liberation from suffering."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1165)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Venerable Piṇḍola was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, there was a king of the Vatsa country named Udayana, who went to the Venerable Piṇḍola's place. They exchanged greetings, and after the greetings, the king withdrew and sat to one side. King Udayana of Vatsa said to the Venerable Piṇḍola: "I wish to ask something. Would you have the leisure to answer?"

The Venerable Piṇḍola replied: "Great King! Please ask, great King. What I know, I shall answer."

King Udayana of Vatsa asked the Venerable Piṇḍola: "What is the cause, what is the condition, that newly ordained young bhikṣus, who have gone forth not long ago in this Dharma and Vinaya, dwell in extreme ease and happiness, their faculties joyful and delighted, their complexion pure, their skin bright and fair, delighting in stillness, seldom agitated, living dependent on others, their minds like wild deer, capable of maintaining the holy life for their entire lifespan, purely and completely clean?"

The Venerable Piṇḍola answered: "As the Buddha has said, as the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, who knows and sees, has told the bhikṣus: 'You bhikṣus! When you see an elderly woman, you should regard her as a mother. When you see a middle-aged woman, regard her as a sister. When you see a young woman, regard her as a daughter. For this reason, young bhikṣus in this Dharma and Vinaya, who have gone forth not long ago, dwell in ease and happiness, their faculties joyful and delighted, their complexion pure, their skin bright and fair, delighting in stillness, seldom agitated, living dependent on others, their minds like wild deer, capable of maintaining the holy life for their entire lifespan, purely and completely clean.'"

King Udayana of Vatsa said to the Venerable Piṇḍola: "In the present world, the mind of covetous desire is such that when one sees an elderly woman and regards her as a mother, when one sees a middle-aged woman and regards her as a sister, when one sees a young woman and regards her as a daughter, at that very time, the mind can still arise with the burning of desire, the burning of anger, the burning of delusion. Is there not some superior cause and condition?"

The Venerable Piṇḍola said to King Udayana of Vatsa: "There is a further cause and condition. As the World-Honored One has said, as the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, who knows and sees, has told the bhikṣus: 'This body, from the soles of the feet to the crown of the head, bones plastered with flesh, covered by thin skin, is filled with all manner of impurities. Thoroughly observe: hair of the head, hair of the body, nails, teeth, dust and grime, drool and saliva, skin, flesh, white bones, sinews, veins, heart, liver, lungs, spleen, kidneys, intestines, stomach, raw entrails, cooked entrails, membrane, tears, sweat, mucus, foam, fat, grease, marrow, phlegm, pus, blood, brain, bodily fluids, feces, and urine.' Great King! This cause, this condition, is why young bhikṣus in this Dharma and Vinaya, who have gone forth not long ago, dwell in ease and happiness, and so forth up to purely and completely fulfilled in purity."

King Udayana of Vatsa said to the Venerable Piṇḍola: "The human mind is swift and fickle. When one contemplates impurity, perceptions of purity may still arise. Is there yet another cause and condition that enables young bhikṣus in this Dharma and Vinaya, who have gone forth not long ago, to dwell in ease and happiness, and so forth up to purely and completely fulfilled in purity?"

The Venerable Piṇḍola said: "Great King! There is a cause, there is a condition. As the World-Honored One has said, as the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One, who knows and sees, has told the bhikṣus: 'You should guard the doors of the sense faculties and well restrain your minds. When the eye sees forms, do not grasp at the sign of the form, do not grasp at its secondary features, and do not hold on with intensified attachment. If you do not restrain and settle the eye faculty, then worldly desire, craving, and unwholesome evil dharmas will leak into your mind. Therefore, you should undertake and maintain the discipline of the eye faculty. Ear and sound, nose and smell, tongue and taste, body and touch, mind and dharmas are likewise. Up to undertaking and maintaining the discipline of the mind faculty.'"

At that time, King Udayana of Vatsa said to the Venerable Piṇḍola: "Excellent! Well spoken in Dharma, up to undertaking and maintaining the discipline of the sense faculties. Venerable Piṇḍola! I too am like this. Sometimes when I do not guard my body, do not maintain the discipline of the sense faculties, and do not unify my mindfulness, and I enter the inner palace, my mind gives rise to intense burning of desire, burning of anger, and burning of delusion. Even in a secluded room all alone, the three poisons likewise burn my mind, how much more so in the palace! Yet at other times, when I well guard my body, well restrain the sense faculties, and unify my mindfulness, and I enter the inner palace, desire, anger, and delusion do not arise to burn my mind. Even in the inner palace they do not burn my body nor burn my mind, how much less so in seclusion! For this reason, this cause, this condition, enables young bhikṣus in this Dharma and Vinaya, who have gone forth not long ago, to dwell in ease and happiness, and so forth up to purely and completely fulfilled in purity."

At that time, King Udayana of Vatsa, hearing what the Venerable Piṇḍola had said, rejoiced and was gladdened, rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (1166)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Because there are hands, one knows grasping and releasing. Because there are feet, one knows coming and going. Because there are joints, one knows bending and stretching. Because there is a belly, one knows hunger and thirst. In the same way, bhikṣus, because there is the eye, there arises feeling born of eye-contact as a condition, internally experienced as either painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise.

"Bhikṣus! If there were no hands, one would not know grasping and releasing. If there were no feet, one would not know coming and going. If there were no joints, one would not know bending and stretching. If there were no belly, one would not know hunger and thirst. In the same way, bhikṣus, if there were no eye, there would be no feeling born of eye-contact as a condition, internally experienced as either painful, pleasant, or neither-painful-nor-pleasant. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1167)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In the past, there was a turtle living among river grasses. At that time, a jackal, hungry and searching for food, saw the turtle from afar and quickly came to seize it. The turtle, seeing it coming, immediately withdrew its six limbs. The jackal kept watch, hoping it would extend its head and feet so it could catch and eat it. After a long watch, the turtle never extended its head nor its feet. The jackal, starving and angry, departed.

"Bhikṣus! You too are like this today. Know that Māra Pāpīyas constantly watches for your opportunities, hoping that your eye will become attached to forms, your ear to sounds, your nose to smells, your tongue to tastes, your body to touches, and your mind to dharmas, wishing to make you produce attachment to the six sense objects. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should today constantly uphold the discipline of the eye faculty and dwell in it, upholding the discipline of the eye faculty. Then the evil Māra Pāpīyas cannot find an opportunity, regardless of what arises and what conditions occur. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. Regarding the six faculties, whether in what arises or what conditions occur, he cannot find an opportunity, just as with the turtle, the jackal could not find an opportunity."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke this verse:

> "The turtle, fearing the jackal,
> Hid its six limbs within its shell.
> The bhikṣu who well restrains the mind,
> Conceals and hides all thoughts and perceptions.
> Not depending, not fearing others,
> Covering the mind, let there be no speech."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1168)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Suppose there were a heap of sesame placed at a crossroads, and six strong men beat it together with clubs. In a short time it would be smashed to dust. Then a seventh man comes and beats it again with a club. Bhikṣus! What do you think? That heap of sesame, beaten by six men, beaten again by a seventh, would it be utterly pulverized?"

The bhikṣus said to the Buddha: "Indeed so, World-Honored One!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "In the same way, the foolish person is beaten by the six sense-contact bases. What are the six? The eye-contact base constantly beats one. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind contact bases constantly beat one. That foolish person, beaten by the six sense-contact bases, still further wishes and seeks future existence, like a seventh person beating it to pieces again.

"Bhikṣu! If one says 'This is self,' that is agitation. If one says 'This is mine,' that is agitation. 'There will be a future existence,' that is agitation. 'There will be no future existence,' that is agitation. 'There will be form,' that is agitation. 'There will be no form,' that is agitation. 'There will be perception,' that is agitation. 'There will be no perception,' that is agitation. 'There will be neither perception nor non-perception,' that is agitation. Because of agitation, there is illness. Because of agitation, there is abscess. Because of agitation, there is thorn. Because of agitation, there is attachment. One who rightly observes that agitation is suffering attains a non-agitated mind, cultivates it extensively and dwells in it, with mindfulness well established and clear comprehension.

"As with agitation, so with deliberation, deception, and conditioned formations caused by craving. Saying 'self,' that is craving. Saying 'mine,' that is craving. Saying 'there will be a future existence,' that is craving. Saying 'there will be no future existence,' that is craving. 'There will be form,' that is craving. 'There will be no form,' that is craving. 'There will be perception,' that is craving. 'There will be no perception,' that is craving. 'There will be neither perception nor non-perception,' that is craving. Craving is therefore illness. Craving is therefore abscess. Craving is therefore thorn. One who well contemplates and observes that craving produces suffering should dwell much in a mind free from craving, with right mindfulness and right wisdom.

"Bhikṣus! In the past, the asuras raised their army and fought with Śakra. At that time, Śakra, lord of the devas, told the Thirty-Three Gods: 'Today, if the devas are victorious and the asuras are defeated, you should seize the asura king alive, bind him with five bonds, and send him back to the heavenly palace.' The asura king addressed his forces: 'Today, if the asura army fights with the devas and the asuras are victorious and the devas are defeated, you should seize Śakra alive, bind him with five bonds, and bring him back to the asura palace.' In the course of the battle, the devas were victorious and the asuras were defeated. At that time, the Thirty-Three Gods seized Vemacitrin, king of the asuras, alive, bound him with five bonds, and brought him back to the heavenly palace.

"At that time, Vemacitrin, the asura king, his body bound with five bonds, was placed in the Hall of the True Dharma and entertained with various heavenly five-fold sense pleasures. Vemacitrin, the asura king, thought: 'Only the asuras are virtuous, wise, and intelligent. Though the devas are good, I should now return to the asura palace.' When he had this thought, he immediately saw his body bound with five bonds, and the heavenly five-fold sense pleasures naturally vanished.

"Vemacitrin, the asura king, then thought: 'The devas are virtuous, wise, and clearly intelligent. Though the asuras are good, I should stay in this heavenly palace.' When he had this thought, he immediately saw his body freed from the five bonds, and the heavenly five-fold sense pleasures naturally reappeared.

"Vemacitrin, the asura king's bonds were this subtle. The bonds of Māra Pāpīyas are even more subtle than this. When the mind is agitated, Māra binds accordingly. When the mind is not agitated, Māra releases accordingly. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should dwell much with a non-agitated mind, with right mindfulness and right wisdom. This you should learn!"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1169)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "If there is any bhikṣu or bhikṣuṇī in whom, due to the conditions of eye, form, and consciousness, there arises desire, lust, affection, longing, or determined attachment, one should well guard and protect the mind against those. Why? Because these are paths of fear, with obstruction and difficulty. These are what evil persons rely on, not what good persons rely on. Therefore one should guard against them. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. It is like a farmer who has fine field crops, but whose field guardian is lazy and negligent, and cattle break in and eat the crops. The foolish ordinary person is likewise. The six sense-contact bases, up to negligence, are likewise.

"If there are fine field crops and the field guardian is not negligent, cattle do not run wild. If they do enter the field, he drives them all out. That is to say, regarding mind, intention, and consciousness, the well-learned noble disciple well guards and protects himself with regard to the five desire-objects, exhausting the mind and causing it to cease. If there are fine field crops and the field guardian is not negligent, and cattle enter the boundary, with his left hand he seizes the nose and with his right hand takes a stick and beats the entire body, driving them out of the field. Bhikṣus! What do you think? After that cow has suffered such pain, going from village to stable, from stable to village, would it again eat the field crops as before?"

They answered: "No, World-Honored One! Why? Because it remembers the pain of being beaten with a stick when it previously entered the field."

"In the same way, bhikṣus, regarding mind, intention, and consciousness, the well-learned noble disciple generates extreme revulsion and fear toward the six sense-contact bases, settles the inner mind, and restrains it to single-pointedness. Bhikṣus! In the past, there was a king who heard an extraordinarily beautiful sound of lute playing. He became extremely fond of it, infatuated and attached to it. He asked his ministers: 'What is that sound? It is so delightful!' The ministers answered: 'That is the sound of a lute.' He said to the ministers: 'Bring me that sound.' The ministers received the order, went and fetched the lute, and said: 'Great King! This is the lute that makes the beautiful sound.' The king said to the ministers: 'I don't want the lute. Bring me the beautiful and delightful sound I heard before.' The ministers answered: 'A lute such as this has many components, namely a handle, a resonating body, ornamental attachments, strings, and a skin covering. When a skilled person plays it, obtaining all these components as conditions, then the sound is produced. Without obtaining all these components, there is no sound. The sound you previously heard has long since passed away, and it has also already ceased and vanished. It cannot be brought here.'

"At that time, the great king thought: 'Bah! What use is this deceptive thing? The lute of this world is a deceptive thing that makes worldly people become infatuated and attached. Take it away now, break it into pieces, and scatter them in all directions.' The ministers received the order, broke it into a hundred pieces, and scattered them everywhere. In the same way, bhikṣus, if regarding form, feeling, perception, volition, and desire, one knows that these dharmas are impermanent, conditioned, produced by mind as a condition, yet one says 'This is self, this is mine,' at another time all of these will be entirely gone. Bhikṣus! You should, with such balanced right wisdom, observe as it really is."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1170)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "It is like a leper whose body is covered with sores. When he enters among reeds and rushes, he is pricked and pierced by thorns and leaves, and his suffering increases manifold. In the same way, the foolish ordinary person suffers pain through the six sense-contact bases just like that. Like that leper pricked by grass blades and thorns, with pus and blood flowing out; in the same way, the foolish ordinary person, being of a wretched and violent nature, when touched through the six sense-contact bases, gives rise to anger, and evil speech flows out, like that leper. Why? Because the mind of the foolish, unlearned ordinary person is like a leprous sore.

"I shall now explain restraint and non-restraint. What is restraint? What is non-restraint? When the foolish, unlearned ordinary person sees forms with the eye, with regard to agreeable forms he gives rise to desire and attachment, and with regard to disagreeable forms he gives rise to anger. Thereupon, one after another, many thoughts and conceptions arise in succession. He does not see the danger; or even if he sees the danger, he cannot eliminate it. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. Bhikṣus! This is called non-restraint. What is restraint? The well-learned noble disciple, when the eye sees forms, with regard to agreeable forms does not give rise to thoughts of desire, and with regard to disagreeable forms does not give rise to thoughts of anger. One after another, the many thoughts and conceptions do not arise in succession and abide. One sees the danger of forms; having seen the danger, one is able to abandon and depart from them. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. This is called restraint."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1171)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Suppose a man, wandering in an empty dwelling, caught six different creatures. The first was a dog, which he seized and tied to one place. Next he caught a bird. Next a venomous snake. Next a jackal. Next a crocodile. Next a monkey. Having caught these creatures, he tied them all to one place. The dog wished to enter the village. The bird constantly wished to fly into the sky. The snake constantly wished to enter a hole. The jackal wished to go to the charnel ground. The crocodile wished to enter the sea. The monkey wished to enter the mountain forest. These six creatures, all tied to one place, each desired different things, each craving to go to its preferred place. Each did not delight in the others' places; yet being bound, each pulled with its own strength toward its preferred direction but could not break free.

"In the same way, the six sense faculties have various objects, each seeking its preferred objects, not delighting in other objects. The eye faculty constantly seeks agreeable forms; with disagreeable forms it generates aversion. The ear faculty constantly seeks agreeable sounds; with disagreeable sounds it generates aversion. The nose faculty constantly seeks agreeable smells; with disagreeable smells it generates aversion. The tongue faculty constantly seeks agreeable tastes; with disagreeable tastes it generates aversion. The body faculty constantly seeks agreeable touches; with disagreeable touches it generates aversion. The mind faculty constantly seeks agreeable dharmas; with disagreeable dharmas it generates aversion. These six faculties have various domains and various objects; each does not seek another faculty's objects. Among these six faculties, whichever has the most strength can freely follow its preferred objects. It is as if that man tied the six creatures to a sturdy pillar: they would go out and pull with force, going wherever they wished, going back and forth until exhausted, and because of the rope binding, they would finally rest at the pillar.

"Bhikṣus! I speak this simile to reveal the meaning to you. The six creatures are an analogy for the six sense faculties. The sturdy pillar is an analogy for the mindful contemplation of the body. If one well cultivates mindfulness of the body, then with regard to forms, whether attended to or not, seeing agreeable forms one does not generate attachment, and seeing disagreeable forms one does not generate aversion. Ear and sounds, nose and smells, tongue and tastes, body and touches, mind and dharmas: with regard to agreeable dharmas one does not seek desire, and with regard to disagreeable dharmas one does not generate aversion. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should diligently cultivate and dwell much in mindfulness of the body."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1172)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Suppose there were four venomous snakes, fierce, evil, and poisonous, placed inside a single box. At that time, there was a man, intelligent and not foolish, possessing wisdom, seeking happiness and disliking suffering, seeking life and disliking death. Then another man said to that man: 'You must now take this box of poisonous snakes, massage them, bathe them, feed them with affection and care, and bring them in and out at the proper times. If any of the four poisonous snakes should happen to harm you, they might kill you or bring you near death. You must be on guard.' At that time, the man, terrified, fled. 'Suddenly, there are five enemies with drawn swords pursuing you, seeking to kill you. You must be on guard.' At that time, the man, fearing the four poisonous snakes and the five enemies with drawn swords, fled and ran.

"Someone further said: 'Sir! Internally there are six bandits who follow and spy on you. Given the chance, they will kill you. You must be on guard.' At that time, the man, fearing the four poisonous snakes, the five enemies with drawn swords, and the six internal bandits, fled in terror. He came to an empty village and saw empty houses there, crumbling, rotten, and ruined, full of dangerous things. Everything he grasped was fragile and precarious, with nothing solid.

"Someone further said: 'Sir! This empty village will be attacked by bands of robbers who will surely harm you.' At that time, the man, fearing the four poisonous snakes, the five enemies with drawn swords, the six internal evil bandits, and the robber bands of the empty village, fled further. Suddenly, on the road, he came to a great river. Its waters were deep and swift. He saw that this bank had many terrors, while the far bank appeared safe, secure, happy, cool, and without fear. There was no bridge or boat by which to cross to the far bank. He thought: 'I shall gather grasses and wood, bind them into a raft, and with the effort of my hands and feet, cross to the far bank.' Having thought this, he gathered grasses and wood, and along the bank bound them into a raft, and with the effort of his hands and feet, cut across the current and crossed to the other side.

"In this way, the man was freed from the four poisonous snakes, the five enemies with drawn swords, the six internal evil bandits, and also escaped the robber bands of the empty village. He crossed the deep current, left behind the various terrors of this bank, and reached the far bank of safety, security, and happiness. I speak this simile, and you should understand its meaning. Bhikṣus! The box is an analogy for this body of coarse form composed of the four great elements, made from the four great elements, a body of blood and vital essence, sustained by impure food, nourished, bathed, and clothed, a dharma of impermanence, change, decay, fragility, and destruction. The poisonous snakes are an analogy for the four great elements: the earth element, water element, fire element, and wind element. When the earth element is in conflict, it can cause the body to die or bring it near death. When water, fire, or wind elements are in conflict, it is likewise. The five enemies with drawn swords are an analogy for the five clinging aggregates. The six internal bandits are an analogy for the six objects of desire and delight. The empty village is an analogy for the six internal sense bases. Good man! When one examines the eye sense base, it is impermanent and subject to change and decay. That which grasps the eye is also an impermanent, deceptive dharma. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind sense bases are likewise. The robber bands of the empty village are an analogy for the six external sense bases. The eye is harmed by agreeable and disagreeable forms. Ear and sounds, nose and smells, tongue and tastes, body and touches, mind is harmed by agreeable and disagreeable dharmas. The deep current is an analogy for the four floods: the flood of desire, the flood of existence, the flood of views, and the flood of ignorance. The river is an analogy for the three cravings: craving for sensuality, craving for form, and craving for the formless. This bank of many terrors is an analogy for the existing body. The far bank of coolness, safety, and happiness is an analogy for nirvāṇa without remainder. The raft is an analogy for the Noble Eightfold Path. The effort of hands and feet cutting across the current is an analogy for vigorous and courageous effort to reach the far bank. The brahmin's dwelling place is an analogy for the Tathāgata, the Worthy One, the Perfectly Enlightened One.

"In this way, bhikṣus, the Great Teacher, out of compassion, comforts his disciples and does what should be done for them. What I should do, I have already done. You too should now do what should be done: in empty places, under trees, in pure dwellings, spreading grass for a seat, in open ground, in charnel grounds, sitting far apart, diligently practicing meditation. Be careful not to be negligent, lest you later have regret! This is my teaching and instruction."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1173)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kauśāmbī, in Ghoṣira's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "The well-learned noble disciple, with regard to all suffering, its origination, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape, knows and sees as it really is. He sees the five desires as a fire pit. Having thus observed the five desires, with regard to desire for the five desires, craving for desire, longing for desire, and attachment to desire, they do not permanently cover his mind. He knows the pathways where the desiring mind walks and dwells, and he guards and shuts himself against them. Having preemptively guarded and shut himself against the pathways where it walks and dwells, worldly desire, sorrow, and unwholesome evil dharmas do not leak into his mind.

"What does it mean that the well-learned noble disciple, with regard to all suffering, its origination, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape, knows and sees as it really is? The well-learned noble disciple knows as it really is: 'This is the noble truth of suffering. This is the origination of suffering. This is the cessation of suffering. This is the path leading to the cessation of suffering as the noble truth.' This is called the well-learned noble disciple who, with regard to all suffering, its origination, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape, knows and sees as it really is.

"How does the well-learned noble disciple see the five desires as a fire pit, up to worldly desire, sorrow, and unwholesome evil dharmas not permanently covering his mind? Suppose near a village there is a deep pit filled with fire, without smoke or flame. At that time, a man, not foolish, not deluded, intelligent and wise, loving happiness and disliking suffering, loving life and disliking death, thinks: 'Here is a fire pit filled with fire. If I fall in, I will certainly die without doubt.' With regard to that, he generates distancing, thinks of distancing, and desires distancing. In the same way, the well-learned noble disciple sees the five desires as a fire pit, up to worldly desire, sorrow, and unwholesome evil dharmas not permanently covering his mind. In the pathways and dwelling places, he preemptively guards and preemptively knows, up to worldly desire, sorrow, and unwholesome evil dharmas not leaking into his mind.

"Suppose near a village there is a grove of neem trees with many thorns. At that time, a man enters the grove for some task. Having entered the grove, in front, behind, left, right, above, and below, there are thorns everywhere. At that time, the man walks with right mindfulness, comes and goes with right mindfulness, looks with right mindfulness and clear sight, observes with right mindfulness and proper focus, and bends his body with right mindfulness. Why? Lest the sharp thorns injure and damage his body. The well-learned noble disciple is likewise. If dwelling depending on a village or city, in the early morning he dons his robes and takes his bowl, enters the village to beg for food, well guarding his body, well maintaining his mind, established in right mindfulness, walking with right mindfulness, looking with right mindfulness and clear sight, observing with right mindfulness. Why? Lest the sharp thorns injure the noble Dharma and Vinaya.

"What are the sharp thorns that injure the noble Dharma and Vinaya? Agreeable and beloved forms. These are called the sharp thorns that injure the noble Dharma and Vinaya. What are these agreeable and beloved forms that injure the noble Dharma and Vinaya? The five desire-objects: eye-consciousness of forms producing fondness and longing, nourishing desire and pleasure; ear-consciousness of sounds, nose-consciousness of smells, tongue-consciousness of tastes, body-consciousness of touches producing fondness and longing, nourishing desire and pleasure. These are called the agreeable and beloved forms that injure the noble Dharma and Vinaya. This is called the well-learned noble disciple, in pathways and dwelling places, preemptively guarding and preemptively knowing, up to not allowing worldly desire, sorrow, and unwholesome dharmas to leak into his mind.

"Sometimes the well-learned noble disciple, having generated right mindfulness, still produces evil, unwholesome thoughts that nourish desire, nourish anger, and nourish delusion. This is being of dull faculties. The well-learned noble disciple, though arising and ceasing occur, has desire covering the mind. It is like an iron ball heated until extremely hot: when a few drops of water are sprinkled on it, they immediately dry up and evaporate. In the same way, for the well-learned noble disciple of dull faculties, when thoughts arise, they are quickly extinguished. When the well-learned noble disciple practices thus and dwells thus, if a king, great minister, or relative comes to visit and invites him with an official salary, saying: 'Man! What is the use of shaving your head, carrying a clay bowl, wearing a kāṣāya robe, and begging from house to house? Would it not be better to enjoy the five desires in comfort, to give and create merit?' What do you think, bhikṣus? Would the well-learned noble disciple, when invited with an official salary by the king, ministers, or relatives, then disrobe and regress?"

They answered: "No. Why? Because the well-learned noble disciple, with regard to all suffering, its origination, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape, knows and sees as it really is. Seeing the fire pit as an analogy for the five desires, up to worldly desire, sorrow, and unwholesome evil dharmas not permanently covering his mind; in pathways and dwelling places, preemptively guarding and preemptively knowing, up to worldly desire, sorrow, and unwholesome evil dharmas not leaking into his mind. If further invited by the king, ministers, or relatives with an official salary, to disrobe and regress, that is impossible!"

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Excellent! Excellent! That well-learned noble disciple, his mind through the long night inclines, flows, and pours toward seclusion, toward dispassion, toward nirvāṇa, in peaceful renunciation, delighting in nirvāṇa. In the realm of the taints, there is quiescence and coolness. If invited by a king, elder, or relative to disrobe and regress, that is impossible! It would only bring great suffering. It is like the Ganges River, which through the long night inclines, flows, and pours toward the east. If a great multitude were to cut it off and try to make it incline, flow, and pour toward the west, could they succeed?"

They answered: "They could not, World-Honored One! Why? The Ganges waters through the long night flow and pour toward the east. To try to make them flow west is not possible. Those people would only toil in vain! In the same way, the well-learned noble disciple through the long night inclines, flows, and pours toward seclusion, up to trying to make him regress, that is impossible; it would only be toiling in vain!"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1174)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Abhijā on the bank of the Ganges River. At that time, a certain bhikṣu came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and said to the Buddha: "Excellent, World-Honored One! Please teach me the Dharma. Having heard the Dharma, I shall dwell alone in a secluded place, with focused thought, diligent and not negligent: 'For this reason the clansman shaves his head and beard, with right faith leaves the household life, goes forth to study the Way, in the higher practice cultivates the holy life, sees the Dharma, and knows for himself and realizes: "Birth is exhausted, the holy life has been established, what was to be done has been done, I know for myself that there will be no further existence."'"

At that time, the World-Honored One looked at the river water and saw a great tree in the Ganges River flowing downstream. He said to that bhikṣu: "Do you see that great tree flowing in the Ganges River?"

He answered: "I have seen it, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "If this great tree does not attach to this bank, does not attach to that bank, does not sink to the river bottom, does not get caught on a sandbar, does not enter a whirlpool, is not seized by people, is not seized by non-humans, and does not rot, will it follow the water's flow, inclining, flowing, and pouring into the great ocean?"

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "Indeed so, World-Honored One."

The Buddha said: "A bhikṣu is likewise. He does not attach to this bank, does not attach to that bank, does not sink to the bottom, does not get caught on a sandbar, does not enter a whirlpool, is not seized by people, is not seized by non-humans, and does not rot. Then he will incline, flow, and pour toward nirvāṇa."

The bhikṣu said to the Buddha: "What is this bank? What is that bank? What is sinking? What is a sandbar? What is a whirlpool? What is seized by people? What is seized by non-humans? What is rotting? May the World-Honored One explain this fully for me. Having heard the Dharma, I shall dwell alone in a secluded place, with focused thought, diligent and not negligent, up to knowing for myself that there will be no further existence."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "This bank means the six internal sense bases. That bank means the six external sense bases. Seized by people means, for instance, someone who keeps company with householders and renunciants, sharing in their joys, sorrows, pains, and pleasures, participating in whatever they do, following them from beginning to end. This is called seized by people. Seized by non-humans means, for instance, someone who, aspiring to practice the holy life, thinks: 'By maintaining my precepts and austerities and cultivating the holy life, I shall be reborn in such and such a place among the heavens.' This is seized by non-humans. A whirlpool means, for instance, someone who abandons the precepts and reverts. Rotting means one who violates the precepts and practices evil, unwholesome dharmas, rotten and of little learning, like chaff among grain, like the sound blown from a conch shell, not a true renunciant but wearing the appearance of a renunciant, not truly celibate but wearing the appearance of celibacy. In this way, bhikṣu, this is called not attaching to this bank or that bank, up to pouring toward nirvāṇa."

At that time, the bhikṣu, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and was gladdened, paid respects, and departed.

Then that bhikṣu, dwelling alone in a secluded place, contemplated the sūtra teaching that the Buddha had spoken about the tree in the flowing water, and so forth up to knowing for himself that there would be no further existence. He became an arhat.

At that time, there was a cowherd named Nandika, not far from the Buddha, tending cattle with a staff. After the bhikṣu had left, he went to the World-Honored One, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, stood to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I am now able to not attach to this bank, not attach to that bank, not sink, not get caught on a sandbar, not be seized by people, not be seized by non-humans, not enter a whirlpool, and not rot. May I go forth from the World-Honored One's true Dharma and Vinaya and cultivate the holy life?"

The Buddha said to the cowherd: "Have you returned the cattle to their owner?"

The cowherd said: "Among the cattle there are calves, and they can return home on their own. They do not need to be escorted. Please just allow me to go forth and study the Way."

The Buddha told the cowherd: "Although the cattle can return home, you have been receiving the food and clothing provided by another person. You must first go back and report to the master of the household."

At that time, the cowherd, hearing the Buddha's instruction, rejoiced and was gladdened, paid respects, and departed.

Then the Venerable Śāriputra, who was in the assembly, shortly after the cowherd had left, said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The cowherd Nandika wished to go forth. Why did the World-Honored One send him back home?"

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "It is impossible that the cowherd Nandika will return home and indulge in the five desires. After he has delivered the cattle to their owner, he will certainly come back on his own. In this Dharma and Vinaya, he will go forth and study the Way, purely cultivate the holy life, up to knowing for himself that there will be no further existence. He will become an arhat."

At that time, the cowherd Nandika, having delivered the cattle to their owner, returned to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The cattle have been delivered to their owner. Please allow me to go forth and study the Way in the true Dharma and Vinaya."

The Buddha told the cowherd Nandika: "You may go forth in this Dharma and Vinaya, receive the full precepts, and obtain the status of bhikṣu."

Having gone forth, he contemplated: "For this reason the clansman shaves his head and beard, dons the kāṣāya robe, with right faith leaves the household life, goes forth to study the Way, and cultivates the holy life in a higher way, up to knowing for himself that there will be no further existence." He became an arhat.

---

### (1175)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu, sitting alone in meditation, thought: "How should a bhikṣu know, how should he see, to attain purity of vision?" Having thought this, he went to some bhikṣus and said to them: "Venerable bhikṣus! How should one know, how should one see, to attain purity of vision?"

The bhikṣus answered: "Venerable one! With regard to the six sense-contact bases, their origination, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape, one should know rightly as they really are. Bhikṣu! One who knows thus and sees thus attains purity of vision."

That bhikṣu, hearing the other bhikṣus' explanation, was not pleased in his mind. He went to other bhikṣus and asked them: "Venerable bhikṣus! How should one know, how should one see, to attain purity of vision?"

Those bhikṣus answered: "With regard to the six elements, their origination, cessation, gratification, danger, and escape, one should know rightly as they really are. In this way, bhikṣu, knowing thus and seeing thus, one attains purity of vision."

At that time, the bhikṣu, hearing their explanation, was also not pleased. He went to yet other bhikṣus and asked: "Bhikṣus! How should one know, how should one see, to attain purity of vision?"

Those bhikṣus answered: "With regard to the five clinging aggregates, one should observe them as illness, as abscess, as thorn, as killing, as impermanent, suffering, empty, and not-self. Knowing thus and seeing thus, one attains purity of vision."

That bhikṣu, hearing the various bhikṣus' explanations, was also not pleased. He went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I was sitting alone in quiet contemplation, thinking: 'How should a bhikṣu know, how should he see, to attain purity of vision?' Having thought this, I went to the bhikṣus. At three places, what they said, I have fully reported to the World-Honored One. Hearing what they said, I was not pleased. I have come to the World-Honored One. Therefore, with this meaning, I ask the World-Honored One: 'How should a bhikṣu know, how should he see, to attain purity of vision?'"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "In the past, there was a man who had never seen a kiṃśuka tree. He went to someone who had seen a kiṃśuka tree and asked that man: 'Do you know the kiṃśuka tree?' He answered: 'I know it.' He further asked: 'What does it look like?' He answered: 'Its color is black, like a fire-burned pillar.' At the time that person had seen the kiṃśuka tree, it was black in color, like a fire-burned pillar.

"At that time, the man, hearing that the kiṃśuka tree was black like a fire-burned pillar, was not very pleased. He went to another man who had seen a kiṃśuka tree and asked: 'Do you know the kiṃśuka tree?' He answered: 'I know it.' He further asked: 'What does it look like?' That man who had seen the kiṃśuka tree answered: 'Its color is red and blossoming, looking like a piece of flesh.' At the time that person had seen the kiṃśuka tree, it was blossoming and indeed looked like a piece of flesh.

"That man, hearing what was said, was still not pleased. He went to yet another man who had seen a kiṃśuka tree and asked: 'Do you know the kiṃśuka tree?' He answered: 'I know it.' He further asked: 'What does it look like?' He answered: 'Its hanging branches droop down like a śirīṣa fruit.'

"That man, hearing this, was still not pleased. He went further and asked another who knew the kiṃśuka tree: 'Do you know the kiṃśuka tree?' He answered: 'I know it.' He further asked: 'What does it look like?' He answered: 'Its leaves are green, its leaves are smooth, its leaves are long and broad, like a nyagrodha tree.' Just as that man, inquiring about the kiṃśuka tree, was not pleased with what he heard and kept searching elsewhere, those various people who had seen the kiṃśuka tree described it according to what they had seen at different times. Therefore their descriptions differed.

"In the same way, bhikṣus, if in solitary practice with focused thought, dwelling diligently and not negligently, the dharma that one contemplates leads to the non-arising of taints and the mind is liberated, one describes it according to what one has seen. Now listen further as I explain with another simile. The wise can understand through similes.

"Suppose there is a border kingdom whose king has well-fortified city walls, with gates below that are firm and strong, and crossroads that are level and straight. At the four city gates, he stations four gate-keepers, all of whom are intelligent and know who comes and goes. In the center of the city, at the crossroads, he places a bed, and the city lord sits upon it. If a messenger comes from the east, he asks the gate-keeper: 'Where is the city lord?' The gate-keeper answers: 'The lord is in the city, at the crossroads, sitting upon a bed.' The messenger, hearing this, goes to the city lord, receives his orders, and returns by the same road. Messengers coming from the south, west, or north likewise ask the gate-keepers: 'Where is the city lord?' They likewise answer: 'He is in the city, at the crossroads.' The messengers, hearing this, all go to the city lord, receive his orders, and each returns to his original place."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "I have spoken this simile, and now I shall explain its meaning. The city is an analogy for the human body of coarse form, as stated in the Sūtra of the Box of Poisonous Snakes. The well-fortified walls are right view. The level and straight crossroads are the six internal sense bases. The four gates are the four bases of consciousness. The four gate-keepers are the four foundations of mindfulness. The city lord is the consciousness aggregate. The messengers are calm-abiding and insight. Speaking truthfully is the Four Noble Truths. Returning by the same road is the Noble Eightfold Path."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "What the Great Teacher should do for his disciples, I have already done, out of compassion, as stated in the Sūtra of the Box of Poisonous Snakes."

At that time, the bhikṣu, hearing what the Buddha taught, with focused thought, diligent and not negligent, cultivated the holy life in a higher way, up to not receiving further existence. He became an arhat.

---

### (1176)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the World-Honored One was traveling among the Śākyan people and arrived at Kapilavastu, dwelling in the Nyagrodha Park.

At that time, the Śākyas of Kapilavastu had built a new lecture hall. No śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, young Śākyas, or common people had yet dwelt in it. Hearing that the World-Honored One had come, traveling among the Śākyan people at Kapilavastu, and was dwelling in the Nyagrodha Park, they discussed the meaning of suffering and happiness: "This hall is newly built and no one has yet dwelt in it. We can invite the World-Honored One and the great assembly to make offerings there, gaining merit and rewards, long night of peace and security, and afterwards we ourselves shall make use of it." Having thus decided, they all went out of the city to the World-Honored One, bowed their heads at his feet, withdrew and sat to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the Śākyas the essential Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening them, and then sat in silence.

Then the Śākyas rose from their seats, straightened their robes, paid respects to the Buddha, placed their right knees on the ground, joined their palms, and said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! We Śākyas have newly built a lecture hall, and no one has yet dwelt in it. We now invite the World-Honored One and the great assembly to accept offerings there, gaining merit and rewards, a long night of peace and security. Afterwards we ourselves shall make use of it." At that time, the World-Honored One silently accepted the invitation.

When the Śākyas knew the World-Honored One had accepted the invitation, they bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet and each returned to their places. That very day, with carts and provisions, they transported various necessities, adorned the new hall, laid out beds and seats, spread soft grass on the ground, and prepared fragrant oil lamps. When everything was ready, they went to the Buddha, bowed their heads, and said: "Everything is prepared. May the Holy One know the time."

At that time, the World-Honored One, with the great assembly surrounding him in front and behind, went to the outside of the new hall, washed his feet, and then ascended the hall. Beside the central pillar, he sat facing east. The bhikṣus also washed their feet, followed into the lecture hall, and behind the World-Honored One, facing east on the western side, sat in order. The Śākyas then sat on the eastern side, facing west.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the Śākyas the essential Dharma extensively, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening them, and then said to the Śākyas: "Gautamas! The first watch of the night has passed. It is now time for you to return to Kapilavastu." The Śākyas, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and were gladdened, paid respects, and departed.

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing the Śākyas had left, told Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "You should teach the Dharma to the bhikṣus. I now have back pain and should take rest." Mahāmaudgalyāyana silently accepted the instruction.

At that time, the World-Honored One folded his uttarāsaṅga four times, placed it beneath his side, folded his saṅghāṭī and placed it under his head, lay down on his right side with knees bent, feet together, mindfulness fixed on the sign of light, and with the thought of arising.

At that time, Mahāmaudgalyāyana addressed the bhikṣus: "The Dharma taught by the Buddha is good in the beginning, middle, and end, with good meaning and good phrasing, purely complete and clean, the pure white holy life. I shall now teach the dharma of taints and non-taints. Listen carefully! What is the dharma of taints? The foolish, unlearned ordinary person, having seen forms with the eye, generates delighted attachment toward agreeable forms and generates hatred toward disagreeable forms. He does not dwell in mindfulness of the body. In mind-liberation and wisdom-liberation, he lacks even a small portion of wisdom. Various evil, unwholesome dharmas arise, and he does not completely extinguish them, does not completely end them. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise. Bhikṣus! For such a one, the celestial Māra Pāpīyas goes to his place, spying for his weaknesses. Regarding his eye and forms, he finds an opening. Ear and sounds, nose and smells, tongue and tastes, body and touches, mind and dharmas are likewise; he finds an opening.

"It is like a heap of dry grass: fire arises from all four sides and immediately burns it. In the same way, bhikṣus, regarding his eye and forms, the celestial Māra Pāpīyas finds an opening. Such a bhikṣu does not overcome forms. Regarding ear and sounds, nose and smells, tongue and tastes, body and touches, and mind and dharmas, he is controlled by dharmas and cannot overcome dharmas. Not overcoming forms, not overcoming sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and dharmas, nor overcoming the mind's unwholesome dharmas, the blazing of afflictions, the suffering of karmic retribution, and future birth, aging, illness, and death. Venerables! I received these dharmas of taints directly from the World-Honored One. This is called the Sūtra of Dharmas with Taints.

"What is the Sūtra of Dharmas without Taints? The well-learned noble disciple, when the eye sees forms, does not generate delighted attachment toward agreeable forms and does not generate hatred toward disagreeable forms. He dwells with mindfulness established. In immeasurable mind-liberation and wisdom-liberation, he knows as it really is. Those evil, unwholesome dharmas that have arisen are completely extinguished without remainder. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind are likewise.

"For such a bhikṣu, the wretched Māra Pāpīyas goes to his place, spying for his weaknesses regarding his eye and forms, but finds no weakness. Regarding ear and sounds, nose and smells, tongue and tastes, body and touches, mind and dharmas, he spies for weaknesses but finds no weakness. It is like a building with solid walls, firmly closed windows and doors, thickly plastered with mud: fire arises from all four sides but cannot burn it. Such bhikṣus are likewise. The wretched Māra Pāpīyas goes to their place, spying for weaknesses, but finds no weakness. Such a bhikṣu can overcome those forms and is not overcome by those forms. He overcomes sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and dharmas, and is not overcome by those dharmas. Having overcome forms, having overcome sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and dharmas, he also overcomes evil unwholesome dharmas, the blazing of afflictions, the suffering of karmic retribution, and future birth, aging, illness, and death. I received this dharma directly face-to-face from the World-Honored One. This is called the Sūtra of Dharmas without Taints."

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing that Mahāmaudgalyāyana had finished teaching the Dharma, arose, sat upright, with mindfulness established before him, and told Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "Excellent! Excellent! Maudgalyāyana! Teaching this sūtra dharma to people greatly benefits them, greatly delivers them, and brings peace and happiness through the long night to devas and humans of the world."

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "You should receive and uphold the Sūtra of Dharmas with Taints and without Taints and teach it widely to others. Why? Because its meaning is complete, its Dharma is complete, its holy life is complete. It opens and develops spiritual penetration, rightly directed toward nirvāṇa. Even faithful good men and women, whether lay or ordained, should receive, uphold, recite, and widely teach it to others."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1177)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "Suppose there is a river of ashes, with both banks extremely hot, full of sharp thorns, in a dark place. Many sinners are in the river, drifting and sinking with the current. Among them, there is one person, not foolish, not deluded, intelligent and wise, loving happiness and disliking suffering, loving life and disliking death. He thinks: 'Why am I now in this river of ashes, with both banks extremely hot, full of sharp thorns, in a dark place, drifting with the current? I should use my hands and feet with effort to go against the current and upstream.' Gradually he sees a small light. That person silently thinks: 'Now, with vigor, I have seen this small light.' He further uses his hands and feet, diligently applying effort, and finally sees level ground. He then stands on that ground and surveys the four directions. He sees a great stone mountain, unbroken, undamaged, and without holes. He climbs up onto it. He then sees cool water with eight qualities, namely: cold, pure, light, soft, fragrant, clean, not choking when drunk, and not obstructing the throat. Having drunk it, his body is at ease. He enters into it, bathing and drinking, freed from all distress and heat.

"Then he further advances up the great mountain and sees seven kinds of flowers, namely: utpala flowers, padma flowers, kumuda flowers, puṇḍarīka flowers, sugandhi flowers, mirihikā sugandhi flowers, and atimukta flowers. Having smelled the fragrance of the flowers, he further ascends the stone mountain and sees a four-tiered hall. He sits upon it and sees a canopy with five pillars. He enters therein, composes his body, and sits properly, with various cushions and bedding, scattered flowers everywhere, magnificently adorned. Within it, he freely sits or reclines. Cool breezes blow from all four directions, making his body peaceful and comfortable. Sitting beneath a tall grove, he calls out in a loud voice: 'Beings of the ash river! Worthy ones! This ash river has both banks extremely hot, full of sharp thorns, and the place is dark. Seek to get out of there!'

"Among those in the river, one hears the voice and calls out: 'From which direction can one get out? From which way can one escape?' Among them, someone says: 'Why do you bother asking where to get out? The one who called out himself does not know or see from where he escaped. He too must still be in this ash river, with both banks extremely hot, full of sharp thorns, drifting downstream in the darkness. What use is it to ask him?'

"In this way, bhikṣus, I have spoken this simile, and now I shall explain its meaning. The ashes are the three evil, unwholesome thoughts. What three? Thoughts of desire, thoughts of ill will, and thoughts of harmfulness. The river is the three cravings: craving for sensuality, craving for form, and craving for the formless. Both banks being extremely hot are the internal and external six sense bases. The many sharp thorns are the five desire-objects. The dark place is ignorance, which obstructs the eye of wisdom. The many people are the foolish ordinary persons. The current is the river of birth and death. The one person among them who is not foolish and not deluded is the bodh
