# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Forty-Four

---

### (1178)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Mithilā, in an āmra garden.

At that time, there was a brahmin woman named Vāsiṣṭhā whose six sons had died one after another. Grieving for her sons, she went mad, running about naked with disheveled hair, wandering along the roads, until she came to the āmra garden in Mithilā.

At that time, the World-Honored One was surrounded by a great multitude and was teaching the Dharma. The brahmin woman Vāsiṣṭhā saw the World-Honored One from afar, and upon seeing him, she immediately recovered her original mind. Feeling shame and embarrassment, she crouched down and sat with her body drawn in.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the Venerable Ānanda: "Take your uttarāsaṅga and give it to that brahmin woman Vāsiṣṭhā, and have her put it on to listen to the Dharma." The Venerable Ānanda received the Buddha's instruction, took the robe, and had her put it on.

At that time, the brahmin woman, having received the robe and put it on, went before the Buddha, bowed her head in homage to the Buddha, and withdrew to sit to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught her the Dharma, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting her. As was the Buddha's customary method, he taught the Dharma in sequence, until her faith became purified, and she received the three refuges. Having heard what the Buddha taught, she rejoiced and expressed her approval, made obeisance, and departed.

Afterward, the upāsikā Vāsiṣṭhā's seventh son suddenly also died. The upāsikā did not weep, grieve, lament, or suffer. At that time, the husband of the upāsikā Vāsiṣṭhā spoke a verse to the upāsikā Vāsiṣṭhā:

> "When previous sons died,
> Grieving for them you gave birth to sorrow and suffering,
> Day and night you would not eat or drink,
> Even to the point of going mad.
> Now your seventh son has died,
> Yet you do not give rise to grief and suffering?"

The upāsikā Vāsiṣṭhā then spoke a verse in reply to her husband:

> "Sons and grandsons number in the thousands,
> Born through causes and conditions coming together,
> Through the long night they pass away and are gone;
> I and you, my lord, are likewise.
> Sons, grandsons, and kinfolk,
> Their number is without limit.
> In the various places where they are born,
> They mutually devour one another.
> If one knows the essential truth of birth,
> What reason is there to give rise to grief and suffering?
> I have already understood the way of deliverance,
> The nature of birth, death, existence, and passing.
> I no longer give rise to grief and suffering,
> Because I have entered the Buddha's right teaching."

At that time, the husband of the upāsikā Vāsiṣṭhā spoke a verse in praise:

> "Never before have I heard such a teaching,
> Yet now I hear you speak it.
> Where did you hear this Dharma taught,
> That you no longer grieve for your sons?"

The upāsikā Vāsiṣṭhā spoke a verse in reply:

> "Today the Perfectly Enlightened One
> Is in the country of Mithilā,
> In the āmra tree garden,
> Having forever left behind all suffering.
> He expounds all suffering, the origin of suffering, the cessation of suffering,
> And the Noble Eightfold Path,
> Peacefully leading to nirvāṇa!
> He is my great teacher;
> I deeply delight in his right teaching.
> I have already understood the true Dharma,
> Which can dispel the grief for sons."

Her husband, the brahmin, then spoke a verse:

> "I too should now go
> To the āmra garden in Mithilā.
> That World-Honored One will surely also
> Dispel my grief for my sons."

The upāsikā then spoke a verse:

> "You should behold the Perfectly Enlightened One,
> His soft, golden-colored body.
> He who tames the untamed
> And broadly ferries across those drifting in the ocean of existence."

At that time, the brahmin prepared his chariot and rode his horse-drawn carriage to the āmra garden in Mithilā. Seeing the World-Honored One from afar, his faith and delight increased further, and he went before the Great Teacher.

At that time, the Great Teacher spoke verses for him, opening his Dharma eye regarding suffering, its origin, its cessation, and the path, directing him toward nirvāṇa. He then saw the Dharma and attained the realization without interval. Having understood the Dharma, he requested to go forth into homelessness.

At that time, the brahmin then obtained ordination. Dwelling alone in quiet contemplation, he eventually attained arhatship. The World-Honored One declared: "Within the third night, he has attained the three knowledges." Having attained the three knowledges, the Buddha then told him: "Send your charioteer to drive the carriage home and inform the upāsikā Vāsiṣṭhā, so she may give rise to sympathetic joy. Tell her: 'The brahmin went to see the World-Honored One and gained pure faith. He attended the Great Teacher, who then taught him the Dharma and opened the Dharma eye for him. He saw the noble truth of suffering, the origin of suffering, the cessation of suffering, and the Noble Eightfold Path, peacefully leading to nirvāṇa, and attained the realization without interval. Having understood the Dharma, he requested to go forth. The World-Honored One declared: "Within the third night, he has fully attained the three knowledges."'"

At that time, the charioteer followed the instruction and swiftly returned. The upāsikā Vāsiṣṭhā saw the charioteer returning from afar with the carriage empty. She called out from a distance: "Did the brahmin see the Buddha? Did the Buddha teach him the Dharma, open the Dharma eye for him, and let him see the noble truths?"

The charioteer replied: "The brahmin has already seen the World-Honored One, gained pure faith, and attended the Great Teacher, who opened the Dharma eye for him, taught the Four Noble Truths, and he attained the realization without interval. Having understood the Dharma, he requested to go forth and devoted himself to focused contemplation. The World-Honored One declared: 'Within the third night, he has fully attained the three knowledges.'"

At that time, the upāsikā's heart was filled with sympathetic joy, and she said to the charioteer: "The carriage and horses belong to you. Additionally, I grant you one thousand gold coins, because you have conveyed the message that the brahmin Sujāti has attained the three knowledges, and this has made me joyful."

The charioteer replied: "What use do I have for the carriage, horses, and gold coins? Let the carriage, horses, and gold coins return to the upāsikā. I shall now go back to where the brahmin is and follow him in going forth."

The upāsikā said: "If that is your intention, then go quickly. Before long, you too will attain what he has attained, fully completing the three knowledges, and go forth in his wake."

The charioteer replied: "So it is, upāsikā! Just as he has gone forth, I too shall do the same."

The upāsikā said: "Your father has gone forth, and you follow him in going forth. Before long, I too shall follow. Like a great dragon in an empty wilderness that soars through the sky, and all the other dragons, dragon-sons, and dragon-daughters all follow after it, so shall I likewise, carrying my robes and bowl, easily sustained and easily satisfied." The charioteer replied: "Upāsikā! If this is so, your wish will surely be fulfilled. Before long, I shall see the upāsikā with few desires and content, carrying her robes and bowl, accepting what others discard and begging for food, with hair shaved and wearing dyed robes. Regarding the aggregates, elements, and sense bases, she will have cut off desire, become free from the bonds of craving, and exhausted all outflows."

That brahmin, his charioteer, the upāsikā Vāsiṣṭhā, and the upāsikā's granddaughter Sundara-Pālī all went forth and reached the end of suffering.

---

### (1179)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Vaiśālī, in the Great Forest Monastery.

At that time, there was a brahmin named Vīrya Bhāradvāja who had bought a cow in the early morning but had not yet paid its price. That very day he lost the cow and did not find it for six days.

While searching for the cow, the brahmin came to the Great Forest Monastery. From afar he saw the World-Honored One sitting beneath a tree, his appearance noble and distinguished, his faculties pure, his mind silent and serene, having perfected calm and insight. His body was golden in color, radiating brilliant light. Seeing this, he went before him and spoke this verse:

> "Why do you seek nothing,
> Empty and still, sitting here?
> Alone in this secluded place,
> How do you find delight in your heart?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Whether losing or gaining,
> My mind is not disturbed.
> Brahmin, you should know: do not think he is like me.
> A mind that calculates gain and loss,
> That mind has no freedom."

At that time, the brahmin then spoke a verse:

> "The most excellent place of a brahmin sage
> Is as the bhikṣu has described.
> Now I shall speak for myself;
> Listen to my truthful words.
> The śramaṇa surely is not one
> Who lost a cow this morning
> And for six days could not find it;
> Therefore he dwells in ease.
> The śramaṇa surely does not have
> A field of sesame planted,
> Fearing it will be overrun by weeds;
> Therefore he dwells in ease.
> The śramaṇa surely does not have
> A rice paddy lacking water,
> Fearing the leaves will wither and die;
> Therefore he dwells in ease.
> The śramaṇa surely does not have
> Seven widowed daughters,
> All raising orphaned children;
> Therefore he dwells in ease.
> The śramaṇa surely does not have
> Seven disobedient sons,
> Reckless and deeply in debt;
> Therefore he dwells in ease.
> The śramaṇa surely does not have
> Creditors guarding his door,
> Demanding repayment with compound interest;
> Therefore he dwells in ease.
> The śramaṇa surely does not have
> Seven layers of heavy bedding,
> Anxiously picking out all the vermin;
> Therefore he dwells in ease.
> The śramaṇa surely does not have
> A red-eyed, yellow-haired wife,
> Hearing her harsh voice day and night;
> Therefore he dwells in ease.
> The śramaṇa surely does not have
> An empty granary where rats play,
> Always worrying about scarcity and want;
> Therefore he dwells in ease."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Today I surely have not
> Lost a cow this morning
> And for six days could not find it;
> Therefore I dwell in ease.
> Today I surely do not have
> A field of sesame planted,
> Always fearing it will be overrun;
> Therefore I dwell in ease.
> Today I surely do not have
> A rice paddy lacking water,
> Fearing the leaves will wither and die;
> Therefore I dwell in ease.
> Today I surely do not have
> Seven widowed daughters,
> All raising orphaned children;
> Therefore I dwell in ease.
> Today I surely do not have
> Seven disobedient sons,
> Reckless and deeply in debt;
> Therefore I dwell in ease.
> Today I surely do not have
> Creditors guarding my door,
> Demanding repayment with compound interest;
> Therefore I dwell in ease.
> Today I surely do not have
> Seven layers of heavy bedding,
> Anxiously picking out all the vermin;
> Therefore I dwell in ease.
> Today I surely do not have
> A yellow-headed, red-eyed wife,
> Hearing her harsh voice day and night;
> Therefore I dwell in ease.
> Today I surely do not have
> An empty granary where rats play,
> Always worrying about scarcity and want;
> Therefore I dwell in ease.
> Not clinging to what is pleasant or unpleasant,
> Beings may dwell in ease.
> Having cut off desire and left behind affection,
> One attains dwelling in ease."

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the brahmin Vīrya Bhāradvāja the Dharma in various ways, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him. As was the Buddha's customary method, he taught the Dharma in sequence: generosity, moral conduct, and so forth, until within the true Dharma, his mind attained fearlessness. He then rose from his seat, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha: "May I now be permitted to go forth in the true Dharma and Vinaya, to train on the path, to become a bhikṣu, and to practice the holy life?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "You may now go forth in the true Dharma and Vinaya, receive full ordination, and cultivate the holy life." And so forth, until he attained arhatship and his mind was well liberated.

At that time, the brahmin Vīrya Bhāradvāja, having attained arhatship and knowing it for himself through direct realization, attaining the bliss of liberation, spoke this verse:

> "Today I greatly rejoice,
> In the supreme Dharma of the Great Sage.
> Having gained the bliss of freedom from desire,
> I shall not again be reborn to see the Buddha."

---

### (1180)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in a brahmin village in a sāla tree grove. At that time, the World-Honored One, in the early morning, put on his robes and took his bowl and entered the brahmin village to beg for food. Untimely clouds arose.

At that time, the World-Honored One had this thought: "I should now go to the great assembly hall of the brahmin elder in the brahmin village." Having had this thought, he went toward that great assembly hall.

At that time, the brahmin elders were all gathered in the hall. Seeing the World-Honored One from afar, they said to each other: "What could that shaven-headed śramaṇa possibly know of the Dharma?"

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the brahmin elders of the brahmin village: "Among brahmins, there are those who know the Dharma and those who do not. Among kṣatriya elders also, there are those who know the Dharma and those who do not."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke this verse:

> "Without companions, one cannot overcome those with companions;
> A king cannot subdue those who are hard to subdue.
> A wife should not seek to surpass her husband;
> Without sons, one cannot honor a father.
> Without an assembly, there are no wise ones;
> Without the wise, there is no speaking of Dharma.
> When craving, anger, and delusion are all cut off,
> That one is called a wise person."

At that time, the brahmin elders addressed the Buddha: "Excellent one, Gautama! Excellent one, you may enter this hall and take a seat." After the World-Honored One had sat down, they addressed him: "May Gautama teach the Dharma; we would be delighted to listen."

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the Dharma in various ways for the brahmins and elders of the great assembly, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting them. He then spoke another verse:

> "When the foolish and the wise gather in assembly,
> Without speaking, how would one know who is bright?
> One who can speak the path of tranquility,
> Through speaking, wisdom and eloquence are revealed.
> The speaker illuminates the true Dharma
> And raises the banner of the Great Sage.
> Skillful teaching is the banner of the sages;
> The Dharma is the banner of the arhats."

At that time, the World-Honored One, having established the brahmin elders of the brahmin village in the true Dharma, having instructed, taught, illuminated, and delighted them, rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (1181)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kosala and arrived at the village of Phulī, where he stayed in the āmra garden of the brahmin Devahita. The Venerable Upavāṇa was his attendant.

At that time, the World-Honored One was suffering from back pain, and he told the Venerable Upavāṇa: "After you have put away your robes and bowl, go to the house of the brahmin Devahita."

At that time, the brahmin Devahita was in his central hall having a barber trim and shave his beard and hair. He saw the Venerable Upavāṇa standing outside the gate, and upon seeing him, spoke this verse:

> "Who is this one with shaved head and beard,
> Wearing a saṅghāṭī robe,
> Standing outside that gate?
> What do you seek?"

The Venerable Upavāṇa spoke a verse in reply:

> "The Arhat, the Well-Gone One of the world,
> Is suffering from wind ailment in his back.
> Might there be any wholesome water
> To heal the Sage's illness?"

At that time, the brahmin Devahita filled a bowl with ghee, a jar of oil, and a jar of rock honey, and had a man carry them, along with warm water, following the Venerable Upavāṇa to the World-Honored One's location. They applied it to his body and washed him with warm water. The ghee and honey were made into a drink, and the World-Honored One's back ailment was immediately relieved.

Then the brahmin Devahita rose early the next morning and went to the Buddha's location. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and spoke this verse:

> "Who may be called a brahmin?
> Giving to whom yields great fruit?
> What constitutes a timely gift?
> What is a pure field of merit?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "One who has gained knowledge of past lives,
> Who sees the destinations of celestial rebirth,
> Who has exhausted all outflows,
> The Sage who has arisen in the three knowledges,
> Who knows well the liberation of mind,
> Liberated from all craving:
> That one is called a brahmin.
> Giving to such a one yields great fruit.
> Giving to such a one is a timely gift,
> A field of merit according to one's wish."

At that time, the brahmin Devahita, having heard what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and expressed his approval, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (1182)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kosala and one night stayed in a sāla grove.

At that time, there was a brahmin who lived not far from the sāla grove and was engaged in agricultural work. Rising early in the morning, he came to the sāla grove and from afar saw the World-Honored One seated beneath a tree, his appearance upright and dignified, his faculties pure, his mind settled in stillness, having fully accomplished the foremost calm and insight. His body was golden in color, radiating brilliant light. Seeing this, he went to the Buddha's location and said: "Gautama, I live nearby and attend to my agricultural affairs, and so I enjoy this grove. What affairs does Gautama have here that you enjoy this grove?" He then spoke this verse:

> "Bhikṣu, in this grove,
> For what affairs
> Do you alone keep to this seclusion,
> Delighting in this forest?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "I have no affairs in this grove.
> The root of the grove has long been cut.
> In the grove, freed from the grove, liberated,
> In meditative thought, I do not delight in cutting."

At that time, the brahmin, having heard what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and expressed his approval, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (1183)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kosala and for a night stayed in a sāla grove.

At that time, there was a brahmin who lived near the edge of that grove with five hundred young brahmins. That brahmin constantly praised and longingly thought: "When will I see the World-Honored One? If he should ever travel to this grove, I might have the chance to see him and ask about my doubts. Would he have the leisure to explain them for me?"

At that time, a young disciple of the brahmin entered the grove to gather firewood. From afar he saw the World-Honored One seated beneath a tree, his appearance upright and dignified, his faculties calm and still, his mind settled in stillness, his form like a golden mountain, radiating brilliant light. Seeing this, he thought: "My preceptor, the brahmin, always praises and longingly wishes to see Gautama and ask about his doubts. Now this śramaṇa Gautama has come to this grove. I should hurry back and inform my preceptor." He immediately took his bundle of firewood and rushed back to the school. Having put down his firewood, he went to his preceptor and said: "Preceptor, you should know: you have always praised and admiringly wished to see the śramaṇa Gautama, hoping that if he should come to this grove, you would ask about your doubts. Today, Gautama has arrived in this grove. May the preceptor go at an opportune time!"

At that time, the brahmin went to the World-Honored One's location. They exchanged greetings and courteous inquiries, then he withdrew and sat to one side. He spoke this verse:

> "Alone you have entered this fearful,
> Deep and dense forest,
> Standing firm and unshaken,
> Well cultivating the practice of right effort.
> Without songs or music,
> Silent, dwelling in seclusion.
> Never before have I seen
> One who delights alone in the deep forest.
> Do you seek to become
> A sovereign lord of the world?
> Or for the thirty-three heavens,
> The bliss of celestial sovereignty?
> Why in this deep forest
> Do you practice austerities, withering yourself?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Those who make various kinds of seeking,
> With attachments to the many realms:
> All of these are
> The root of foolishness.
> All such seeking
> I have long ago entirely expelled.
> Not seeking, not deceitful,
> Not touching anything at all.
> Regarding all dharmas,
> There is only the one pure contemplation.
> Having attained supreme bodhi,
> In meditative thought, I cultivate non-delight."

The brahmin then spoke a verse:

> "I now pay homage to you,
> Great Silent Sage, the Honored One,
> Supreme king of meditative thought,
> Awakened to the boundless great awakening.
> The Tathāgata, savior of gods and humans,
> Majestic like a golden mountain.
> Liberated from the thicket,
> In the thicket forever unattached;
> Having pulled out the deep and sharp thorns,
> Purified without remaining trace.
> Foremost among teachers,
> Supreme in eloquent speech.
> A lion among humans,
> Roaring in the deep forest.
> Revealing the noble truth of suffering,
> Its origin, its cessation, and the Eightfold Path,
> Able to end the mass of suffering,
> Riding forth, pure and stainless.
> Having freed yourself from all suffering,
> You rescue suffering beings.
> For the ease and welfare of beings,
> You expound the true Dharma.
> Having cut off affection,
> Far removed from the net of desire,
> Having severed all
> Bonds of craving for existence.
> Like a lotus born from water,
> Undefiled by dust or water;
> Like the sun resting in the sky,
> Clear and pure, without cloud or haze.
> How excellent it is today
> That I have come to this grove of Kosala
> And seen the Great Teacher,
> The most honored among two-footed beings.
> Great Forest, Great Vigor,
> Having attained the foremost vast liberation.
> Chief among tamers,
> Homage to the Fearless One."

At that time, the brahmin, having extensively spoken these verses in praise of the Buddha, having heard what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and expressed his approval, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (1184)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kosala and stayed beside the Sundarikā River.

At that time, the World-Honored One had recently shaved his head. During the last watch of the night, he sat cross-legged with body upright, contemplating, mindfully focused, with his robe covering his head.

At that time, beside the Sundarikā River there dwelt a brahmin. He rose in the night to perform a fire sacrifice. Having leftover food from the offering, he went to the riverbank, wishing to find a great and virtuous brahmin to present it to.

At that time, the World-Honored One heard the voice of the brahmin by the river. Having heard it, he cleared his throat and made a sound, then drew back his robe and revealed his head.

The brahmin beside the Sundarikā River, seeing the Buddha, thought: "This is a shaven-headed śramaṇa, not a brahmin." He wanted to take the food and go back. Then the brahmin had another thought: "It is not only śramaṇas who are shaven-headed; among brahmins too there are those who shave their heads. I should go and ask about his birth." At that time, the brahmin beside the Sundarikā River approached the World-Honored One and asked him: "What clan were you born into?"

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke this verse:

> "Do not ask about birth;
> Rather ask about conduct.
> From carving wood a fire-drill is made,
> And from it fire can be produced.
> From a lowly caste
> Can be born a steadfast sage.
> With wisdom, a sense of shame,
> Vigor, and good self-discipline,
> Reaching the ultimate shore of great illumination,
> Pure in the practice of the holy life.
> Now is the right time;
> You should offer your leftover food."

At that time, the brahmin beside the Sundarikā River then spoke this verse:

> "Today is my auspicious day.
> Seeking merit, I perform offerings.
> I have had the fortune to meet a great being,
> The most honored one of the three times.
> If I had not seen the Buddha,
> I would have given it to someone else."

At that time, the brahmin beside the Sundarikā River gained further faith and offered the leftover food to the World-Honored One. The World-Honored One did not accept it, because it was obtained through speaking verses, as has been extensively explained above regarding food obtained through speaking verses.

The brahmin beside the Sundarikā River addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, where shall this offered food be placed?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "I do not see among gods, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brahmins, celestial beings, or worldly people anyone who could eat this food and have their body remain at ease. You should take this food and place it in water free of insects, or on ground with sparse living grass."

At that time, the brahmin took the food and placed it in water free of insects. The water then emitted smoke, bubbled and boiled, and made hissing sounds, just like a heated iron ball plunged into cold water, producing smoke, bubbling and boiling, making hissing sounds. Likewise, when that food was placed in the insect-free water, smoke arose, it bubbled and boiled, and made hissing sounds. The brahmin beside the Sundarikā River became frightened, his hair standing on end, thinking it was a portent of disaster. He rushed up the bank, gathered dry wood, and made offerings to the fire sacrifice, hoping to quell the ominous signs.

The World-Honored One saw him gathering dry wood and making offerings to the fire sacrifice, hoping to quell the ominous signs. Seeing this, he spoke this verse:

> "Brahmin, in your fire sacrifice,
> Burning dry grass and wood,
> Do not call this the pure path,
> Able to ward off disasters.
> This is an unwholesome offering,
> Yet you consider it clever.
> Through such causes and conditions,
> Non-Buddhists seek purification.
> Now abandon your firewood and flames,
> And kindle the inner fire to blaze.
> Always cultivate diligence without negligence,
> Always enriching your offerings.
> Everywhere give rise to pure faith,
> Broadly establishing great assemblies.
> The mind's intentions are the bundled firewood,
> Anger is the black smoke that rises,
> False speech is the dust and flavor,
> The tongue is the wooden ladle,
> The chest is the place where fire burns,
> The fire of desire constantly blazes.
> You should well tame and subdue yourself,
> And extinguish the fire of the man.
> Right faith is the great river,
> Pure precepts are the ferry crossing,
> With clear, pure, flowing water,
> Praised by the wise.
> Among humans, those of heavenly purity and virtue,
> Should bathe therein.
> Crossing the water, it does not cling to the body;
> Peacefully and happily, one reaches the other shore.
> The true Dharma is the deep pool,
> Merit is the lower crossing,
> Filled with clear, pure water,
> Praised by the wise.
> Among humans, those of heavenly purity and virtue,
> Should bathe therein.
> Crossing the water, it does not cling to the body;
> Peacefully and happily, one reaches the other shore.
> True truth, good taming,
> Guarded cultivation of the holy life,
> Compassion as austerity,
> A truthful heart, purity:
> Bathing in the true Dharma
> Is what the wise praise."

At that time, the brahmin beside the Sundarikā River, having heard what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and expressed his approval, and went back the way he came.

---

### (1185)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kosala and was staying in a thicket beside the Sundarikā River.

At that time, a brahmin who dwelt beside the Sundarikā River came to the Buddha's location. They exchanged greetings and courteous inquiries, then he withdrew and sat to one side, and asked the Buddha: "Gautama, do you go to the Sundarikā River to bathe?"

The Buddha told the brahmin: "What use is bathing in the Sundarikā River?"

The brahmin addressed the Buddha: "Gautama, the Sundarikā River is counted as a means of salvation, counted as auspicious, counted as purifying. Whoever bathes in it can remove all evils."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke this verse:

> "Neither the Sundarikā River,
> Nor the Bāhukā,
> Nor the Gayā, nor the Sarala,
> Nor any such rivers
> Can purify one who has done
> Unwholesome evil deeds.
> The Ganges, the Bāhukā,
> The Sundarikā, and such rivers:
> Fools may constantly dwell in them,
> But they cannot remove their evils.
> For one who is already pure,
> What need is there for bathing?
> For one who is already pure,
> What need is there for uposatha observance?
> Pure deeds purify oneself;
> This is what arises from upholding them.
> Not killing, not stealing,
> Not engaging in sexual misconduct, not speaking falsely,
> Giving with faith, removing the stain of miserliness:
> Bathe in this.
> Toward all sentient beings,
> Always give rise to a heart of compassion.
> Bathing with well water suffices;
> What need is there for the Gayā and such?
> When the inner heart is itself pure,
> There is no need to wash the outside.
> A lowly farmhand child,
> Whose body is covered with filth,
> Uses water to wash off dust and grime,
> But cannot purify the inside."

At that time, the brahmin beside the Sundarikā River, having heard what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (1186)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kapilavastu, in the Nigrodha Garden.

At that time, there was a brahmin named Jaṭā Bhāradvāja who, when formerly a layperson, had been a good friend of the Buddha. He came to the Buddha's location. They exchanged greetings and courteous inquiries, then he withdrew and sat to one side. He spoke this verse:

> "Those who wear matted hair on the outside,
> They bear the name 'matted-hair' only in name.
> Those who have matted hair within the heart,
> Those are the ones truly entangled among beings.
> I now ask Gautama:
> How does one untangle the matted hair?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Upholding pure moral conduct,
> Inwardly cultivating right awakening,
> Devoted and diligent in skillful means:
> This is how to untangle the matted hair."

At that time, the brahmin Jaṭā, having heard what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (1187)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Kapilavastu, in the Nigrodha Garden.

At that time, there was a brahmin named Jaṭā Bhāradvāja who came to the Buddha's location. He came forward, exchanged greetings and courteous inquiries, then withdrew and sat to one side. He spoke this verse:

> "Those who wear matted hair on the outside,
> They bear the name 'matted-hair' only in name.
> Those who have matted hair within the heart,
> Those are the ones truly entangled among beings.
> I now ask Gautama:
> Regarding such matted hair,
> How does one make the effort,
> And where does one cut the matted hair?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "The eye, ear, and nose,
> Tongue, body, and mind as sense bases:
> Regarding name-and-form therein,
> Completely extinguish them without remainder.
> Where consciousness is forever extinguished,
> There the matted hair is cut."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the brahmin Jaṭā Bhāradvāja, having heard what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, and departed.

---

### (1188)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the village of Uruvilā, beside the Nairañjanā River, under the Bodhi tree, having recently attained Buddhahood.

At that time, the World-Honored One, dwelling alone in quiet contemplation, had this thought: "One who is without reverence suffers greatly, being without proper order. Having no other sovereign one to fear, one regresses from the great purpose. One who has something to revere, who has proper order, who has another sovereign one, dwells in happiness; having something to revere, having proper order, having another sovereign one, the great purpose is fulfilled. Is there among gods, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brahmins, celestial beings, or worldly people anyone who surpasses me in moral conduct, surpasses me in samādhi, surpasses me in wisdom, surpasses me in liberation, surpasses me in the knowledge and vision of liberation, whom I might revere, honor, serve, and make offerings to, and upon whom I might rely and dwell?"

He then had this thought: "There is no one among gods, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brahmins, celestial beings, or worldly people who surpasses me in moral conduct, surpasses me in samādhi, surpasses me in wisdom, surpasses me in liberation, surpasses me in the knowledge and vision of liberation, whom I might revere, honor, serve, and make offerings to, and upon whom I might rely and dwell. Only the true Dharma, through which I myself have awakened and become a samyak-saṃbuddha: I should revere, honor, serve, and make offerings to that Dharma, and rely upon it and dwell therein. Why is this? Past Tathāgatas, Arhats, Perfectly Enlightened Ones also revered, honored, served, and made offerings to the true Dharma, and relied upon it and dwelt therein. Future Tathāgatas, Arhats, Perfectly Enlightened Ones will also revere, honor, serve, and make offerings to the true Dharma, and rely upon it and dwell therein."

At that time, the Brahmā King, lord of the sahā world, knowing the World-Honored One's thought, just as a strong man might flex and extend his arm, vanished from the Brahmā heaven and appeared before the Buddha, praising: "Excellent! Just so, World-Honored One! Just so, Well-Gone One! One who is negligent and without reverence suffers greatly," and so forth extensively, up to "the great purpose is fulfilled. Truly, there is no one among gods, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brahmins, celestial beings, or worldly people who surpasses the World-Honored One in moral conduct, surpasses him in samādhi, surpasses him in wisdom, surpasses him in liberation, surpasses him in the knowledge and vision of liberation, whom the World-Honored One might revere, honor, serve, and make offerings to, and upon whom he might rely and dwell. Only the true Dharma, through which the Tathāgata has himself awakened and become perfectly enlightened: that is what the Tathāgata should revere, honor, serve, and make offerings to, and rely upon and dwell therein. Why is this? Past Tathāgatas, Arhats, Perfectly Enlightened Ones also revered, honored, served, and made offerings to the true Dharma, and relied upon it and dwelt therein. Future Tathāgatas, Arhats, Perfectly Enlightened Ones will also revere, honor, serve, and make offerings to the true Dharma, and rely upon it and dwell therein. The World-Honored One should also revere, honor, serve, and make offerings to that true Dharma, and rely upon it and dwell therein."

At that time, the Brahmā King then spoke this verse:

> "Past Perfectly Enlightened Ones,
> And future Buddhas,
> And the present Buddha, World-Honored One,
> Who can remove the sorrows of beings:
> All revere the Dharma,
> Relying upon the true Dharma and dwelling therein.
> Such reverence
> Is the way of all Buddhas."

At that time, the Brahmā King, having heard what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and then vanished.

---

### (1189)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the village of Uruvilā, beside the Nairañjanā River, under the Bodhi tree, having recently attained Buddhahood.

At that time, the World-Honored One, dwelling alone in quiet contemplation, had this thought: "There is a single path that can purify beings, enable them to cross over sorrows and grief, eliminate suffering and distress, and attain the true Dharma, namely the four foundations of mindfulness. What are the four? Contemplation of the body as body, contemplation of feelings, contemplation of mind, and contemplation of dharmas as dharmas. If there are people who do not delight in the four foundations of mindfulness, they do not delight in the noble Dharma. Those who do not delight in the noble Dharma do not delight in the noble path. Those who do not delight in the noble path do not delight in the nectar of Dharma. Those who do not delight in the nectar of Dharma do not attain liberation from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and anguish. If one delights in cultivating the four foundations of mindfulness, one delights in cultivating the noble Dharma. Delighting in cultivating the noble Dharma, one delights in the noble path. Delighting in the noble path, one delights in the nectar of Dharma. Delighting in the nectar of Dharma, one attains liberation from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and anguish."

At that time, the Brahmā King, lord of the sahā world, knowing the Buddha's thought, just as a strong man might flex and extend his arm, vanished from the Brahmā heaven and appeared before the Buddha, making this exclamation: "Just so, World-Honored One! Just so, Well-Gone One! There is a single path that can purify beings, namely the four foundations of mindfulness, and so forth, up to liberation from birth, old age, sickness, death, sorrow, grief, distress, and anguish."

At that time, the Brahmā King then spoke this verse:

> "There is said to be a single path,
> Which sees the edge of the various realms of existence,
> Expounding the true Dharma,
> Bringing comfort to suffering beings.
> Past World-Honored Ones
> Crossed over by riding this path.
> Future World-Honored Ones
> Will also cross over by riding this path.
> The present Honored Perfectly Enlightened One
> Rides this path to cross the ocean stream.
> Reaching the ultimate shore of birth and death,
> The mind tamed and purified,
> In the cycle of birth and death,
> All has been forever consumed.
> Knowing the various realms,
> The eye of wisdom reveals the true path.
> Just as the waters of the Ganges
> All flow toward the great ocean,
> The surging current carrying things far away,
> The true path is likewise.
> Broad wisdom skillfully reveals
> The attainment of the nectar of Dharma.
> The supreme wheel of the true Dharma,
> Never before heard,
> Out of compassion for beings,
> Is turned for the sake of beings.
> Sheltering and protecting the host of gods and humans,
> Enabling them to cross to the further shore.
> Therefore all beings
> Should all bow their heads in homage."

At that time, the Brahmā King, having heard what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and then vanished.

---

### (1190)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at the village of Uruvilā, beside the Nairañjanā River, under the Bodhi tree, having recently attained Buddhahood.

At that time, the Brahmā King, lord of the sahā world, with an exquisitely beautiful body, during the last watch of the night came to the Buddha's location, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and spoke this verse:

> "Among all the castes,
> The kṣatriya is the most honored among two-footed beings.
> One endowed with knowledge and conduct
> Is the most excellent among gods and humans."

The Buddha told the Brahmā King: "Just so, Brahmā! Just so, Brahmā!

> "Among all the castes,
> The kṣatriya is the most honored among two-footed beings.
> One endowed with knowledge and conduct
> Is the most excellent among gods and humans."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Brahmā King, lord of the sahā world, having heard what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and then vanished.

---

### (1191)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kosala and stayed in an uninhabited, secluded place. Together with an assembly of bhikṣus, they spent the night there. At that time, the World-Honored One was teaching the bhikṣus the Dharma in accordance with the forest-dwelling practice.

At that time, the Brahmā King, lord of the sahā world, had this thought: "Now the World-Honored One is traveling among the people of Kosala, staying in an uninhabited, secluded place, together with the great assembly spending the night in the wilderness. The World-Honored One is teaching the great assembly the Dharma in accordance with the practice of emptiness. I should now go and offer praise in accord with it."

Just as a strong man might flex and extend his arm, he vanished from the Brahmā heaven and appeared before the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and spoke this verse:

> "One who frequents forest seats by the edge,
> Cuts off all afflictions.
> If one does not delight in seclusion,
> Entering the assembly, one should guard oneself.
> Taming and subduing one's own mind,
> Going from house to house begging for food,
> Restraining the sense faculties,
> Devoted and attentive with mindful awareness.
> Then one should frequent secluded places,
> The forest-dweller's bed and seat,
> Far removed from all that is fearful,
> Dwelling fearless and at ease.
> Even amid fierce dangers,
> Poisonous snakes and venomous harms,
> Dark clouds of great blackness,
> Thundering and flashing lightning,
> Because one has left behind all afflictions,
> Day and night one dwells in ease.
> As I have heard the Dharma,
> Until one reaches the ultimate end,
> Alone cultivating the holy life,
> Not fearing a thousand deaths from Māra;
> If one cultivates the path of awakening,
> Not fearing even ten thousand.
> All the stream-enterers,
> Or those who have attained the once-returner state,
> And the non-returners:
> Their number is likewise immeasurable.
> One cannot fix their number,
> For fear of speaking falsely."

At that time, the Brahmā King, lord of the sahā world, having heard what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and expressed his approval, made obeisance to the Buddha, and then vanished.

---

### (1192)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Kapilavastu, in the Kapilavastu Forest, together with five hundred bhikṣus, all of whom were arhats. Their outflows were already exhausted, what was to be done had been done, they had laid down the heavy burden, attained their own benefit, exhausted the bonds of existence, and were well liberated through right knowledge. At that time, the World-Honored One was teaching the great assembly the Dharma related to nirvāṇa.

At that time, powerful devas from the ten directions of the world all came to the assembly to make offerings to the World-Honored One and the Sangha of bhikṣus. Furthermore, various Brahmā Kings, abiding in the Brahmā world, had this thought: "Today the Buddha is staying in the country of Kapilavastu," as extensively stated above, up to "making offerings to the World-Honored One and the great assembly. We should now go and each offer our praises."

Having had this thought, just as a strong man might flex and extend his arm, they vanished from the Brahmā heaven and appeared before the Buddha. The first Brahmā then spoke this verse:

> "In this great forest,
> The great assembly gathers like clouds.
> The hosts of devas from the ten directions
> Have all come to pay their respects.
> Therefore I have come from afar to pay homage
> To the most excellent, unconquerable Sangha."

The second Brahmā then spoke this verse:

> "These bhikṣus of the Sangha
> Are truly diligent with sincere minds.
> In this great forest,
> Restraining their faculties, they seek liberation."

The third Brahmā then spoke this verse:

> "With skillful means they melt away
> The deep and sharp thorns of affection.
> Firm and immovable,
> Like the banner of Indra.
> Crossing the deep moat and water stream,
> Pure and not seeking desire:
> Good guides for the crossing,
> These great tamed dragons."

The fourth Brahmā then spoke this verse:

> "Those who take refuge in the Buddha
> Will never fall into evil destinies.
> Able to cut off the human body,
> They attain celestial bodies and enjoy bliss."

Having each spoken their verses, the four Brahmā beings then vanished.

---

### (1193)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the city of Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, the Brahmā King, lord of the sahā world, day after day diligently went to the Buddha's location to honor and make offerings.

The lord of the sahā world had this thought: "Today I have come too early to see the Buddha, and it so happens the World-Honored One has entered a great samādhi. I should first enter the room of the bhikṣu Kokālika, a partisan of Devadatta." Having had this thought, he entered that room, stood in the doorway, knocked on the door with his finger, and spoke these words: "Kokālika! Kokālika! Give rise to pure faith toward Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana. Do not bring upon yourself suffering through the long night!"

Kokālika said: "Who are you?"

The Brahmā King replied: "I am the Brahmā King, lord of the sahā world."

Kokālika said: "Did the World-Honored One not declare you to have attained the state of an anāgāmin?"

The Brahmā King said: "Indeed so, bhikṣu."

Kokālika said: "Then why have you come?"

The Brahmā King, lord of the sahā world, replied: "This one is beyond help." He then spoke this verse:

> "Regarding immeasurable things,
> You give rise to a mind that wishes to estimate.
> What wise person
> Would give rise to such a thought?
> Wishing to measure the immeasurable:
> This is a deluded worldling covered by the aggregates."

At that time, the Brahmā King, lord of the sahā world, went to the Buddha's location, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I always diligently come daily to the Buddha's location to pay my respects and make offerings. I had this thought: 'Today I have come too early to see the World-Honored One, and it so happens the World-Honored One has entered a great samādhi. I should first enter the room of the bhikṣu Kokālika, a partisan of Devadatta.' I then stood in the doorway, gently knocked on the door, and spoke these words: 'Kokālika! Kokālika! You should give rise to pure faith toward Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana, who are virtuous and wise. Do not bring upon yourself suffering through the long night!' Kokālika said: 'Who are you?' I then replied: 'I am the Brahmā King, lord of the sahā world.' Kokālika said: 'Did the World-Honored One not declare you to have attained the state of an anāgāmin?' I then replied: 'Indeed so.' Kokālika again said: 'Then why have you come?' I then thought: 'This one is beyond help.' I then spoke this verse:

> "'Regarding things that cannot be measured,
> You give rise to a mind that wishes to estimate.
> What cannot be measured, you wish to measure:
> This is a deluded worldling covered by the aggregates.'"

The Buddha told the Brahmā King: "Just so, just so, Brahmā King.

> "Regarding things that cannot be measured,
> You give rise to a mind that wishes to measure.
> What wise person
> Would give rise to such a deluded thought?
> What cannot be measured, you wish to measure:
> This is a deluded worldling covered by the aggregates."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the Brahmā King, lord of the sahā world, having heard what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and expressed his approval, rose from his seat, made obeisance to the Buddha, and then vanished.

---

### (1194)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the city of Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Great Brahmā King, along with another separate Brahmā named Subāhu, the separate Brahmā, day after day diligently went to see and make offerings to the World-Honored One.

At that time, a Brahmā named Baka saw the separate Brahmā Subāhu diligently making efforts, and asked him: "Where do you wish to go?"

He replied: "I wish to see the World-Honored One and pay respects and make offerings."

At that time, the Brahmā Baka then spoke this verse:

> "There are four swan-colored birds,
> Three kinds of golden palaces,
> Five hundred and seventy-two
> Practitioners cultivating meditative contemplation,
> With blazing golden-colored bodies,
> Illuminating the Brahmā palace.
> Just look at my body;
> What need is there to go there?"

At that time, the good Brahmā, the separate Brahmā Subāhu, then spoke this verse in reply:

> "Although you have a golden-colored body,
> Illuminating the Brahmā palace,
> Those who are wise
> Know that form involves afflictions.
> The wise do not delight in form;
> In their minds, they are liberated."

At that time, the good Brahmā, the separate Brahmā Subāhu, went to the Buddha's location, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, today I was making efforts to come see the World-Honored One and pay respects and make offerings. The Brahmā Baka saw me making efforts and asked me: 'Where are you making efforts to go now?' I replied: 'I wish to go see the World-Honored One, to pay respects and make offerings.' The Brahmā Baka then spoke this verse:

> "'There are four kinds of swan-colored birds,
> Three kinds of golden palaces,
> Five hundred and seventy-two
> Practicing meditative contemplation within.
> Behold my golden-colored body,
> Illuminating the Brahmā palace.
> Just look at my body;
> What need is there to go there?'

"I then spoke a verse in reply to him:

> "'Although you have a golden-colored body,
> Illuminating the Brahmā palace,
> You should know that true golden color
> Is a matter of afflictions.
> The wise are liberated from form;
> In form, they no longer delight.'"

The Buddha told the Brahmā: "Just so, Brahmā! Just so, Brahmā!

> "Although there is a true golden color,
> Illuminating the Brahmā palace,
> You should know that true golden color
> Is a matter of afflictions.
> The wise are liberated from form;
> In form, they no longer delight."

At that time, that Brahmā, on account of the bhikṣu Kaṭamodakatissa, spoke this verse:

> "When a person is born in the world,
> A sharp axe is in their mouth,
> With which they cut their own body;
> This is due to evil speech.
> Praising those who should be condemned,
> Condemning those who should be praised:
> Evil speech increases one's faults,
> And wherever one is born, there is no happiness.
> Gambling and drinking squander wealth,
> But their fault is comparatively small.
> An evil mind directed toward the Well-Gone One,
> This is the truly great fault.
> Hells numbering in the hundreds of thousands,
> Named Niraurava,
> Thirty-six hundred,
> And five Abbuda:
> All these are hells for slandering the noble ones,
> Because of evil intentions and speech."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, those Brahmā beings, having heard what the Buddha taught, rejoiced and expressed their approval, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, and then vanished.

---

### (1195)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the city of Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Brahmā Baka, dwelling in the Brahmā heaven, gave rise to such wrong views, saying: "This place is permanent, eternal, unchanging, the sole place of deliverance. Never has anyone been seen to come to this place, let alone anyone who has surpassed it."

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing the Brahmā Baka's thoughts, entered samādhi. In accordance with his meditative attainment, he vanished from Rājagṛha and appeared in the Brahmā heaven.

The Brahmā Baka, seeing the World-Honored One from afar, spoke this verse:

> "Brahmā, through seventy-two eons,
> Has created meritorious bliss.
> Self-sovereign and permanently abiding,
> Birth, old age, and death already passed.
> I have in the various sacred texts
> Practiced and reached ultimate mastery.
> Those hosts of devas
> Only declare me to be eternal."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "This is extremely short-lived,
> Not the long-enduring one.
> Yet the Brahmā Baka
> Claims himself to be long-lived.
> The Niraurava hells,
> Their lifespans numbering hundreds of thousands:
> I recall and know them all.
> Yet you call yourself long-enduring!"

The Brahmā Baka then spoke another verse:

> "The Buddha, the World-Honored One, sees
> Limitless numbers of eons.
> Birth, old age, death, sorrow, and grief
> Have all already been passed beyond.
> I pray you to speak of what you know
> About what I have experienced in the past:
> What precepts and deeds did I uphold
> That I came to be born here?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "In the distant past, in a remote eon,
> In a great wilderness,
> There was a great assembly traveling,
> Many virtuous ones of holy conduct.
> They were famished and without provisions.
> You rescued them and enabled them to cross over.
> This compassionate, rescuing mind continued
> Through eons without being lost.
> This is then your past
> Merit that you upheld.
> I recall and know it all;
> Past or recent, as if waking from sleep.
> In the past, there was a village
> Plundered by bandits.
> At that time, you rescued them all,
> Enabling them to gain liberation.
> This is then your past
> Meritorious deeds that you upheld.
> I recall this causal connection;
> Past or recent, as if waking from sleep.
> In the past, there was a group of people
> In a boat on the Ganges River.
> An evil dragon seized that boat,
> Wishing to kill all their lives.
> At that time, with your spiritual power,
> You rescued them and enabled their liberation.
> This is then your past
> Meritorious deeds that you upheld.
> I recall this causal connection;
> Past or recent, as if waking from sleep."

The Brahmā Baka then spoke another verse:

> "You truly know with certainty
> My past and present life and lifespan,
> And also know everything else besides.
> This indeed is the Perfectly Enlightened One.
> Therefore your body that you have received
> Blazes with golden light universally.
> Your body abiding here
> Illuminates the entire world."

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the Brahmā Baka the Dharma in various ways, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and delighting him. Then, in accordance with his meditative attainment, he vanished from the Brahmā heaven and returned to Rājagṛha.

---

### (1196)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a certain Brahmā dwelling in the Brahmā heaven who gave rise to such wrong views, saying: "This place is permanent, eternal, unchanging, the sole place of deliverance. Never has anyone been seen to come to this place, let alone anyone who has surpassed it."

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing that Brahmā's thoughts, entered samādhi. In accordance with his meditative attainment, he vanished from the country of Śrāvastī and appeared in the Brahmā palace, directly above that Brahmā's head, in the empty space, sitting cross-legged with body upright, mindfully focused.

At that time, the Venerable Ājñāta-Kauṇḍinya had this thought: "Where is the World-Honored One today?" With the divine eye, pure and surpassing the human eye, he observed and saw the World-Honored One in the Brahmā heaven. Having seen this, he entered samādhi. In accordance with his meditative attainment, he vanished from the country of Śrāvastī and appeared in that Brahmā world, in the eastern direction, facing west toward the Buddha, sitting cross-legged with body upright, mindfully focused, below the Buddha's seat and above the Brahmā's seat.

At that time, the Venerable Mahākāśyapa had this thought: "Where is the World-Honored One today?" With the divine eye, pure and surpassing the human eye, he saw the World-Honored One in the Brahmā heaven. Having seen this, he entered samādhi. In accordance with his meditative attainment, he vanished from the country of Śrāvastī and appeared in the Brahmā heaven, in the southern direction, facing north toward the Buddha, sitting cross-legged with body upright, mindfully focused, below the Buddha's seat and above the Brahmā's seat.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra had this thought: "Where is the World-Honored One today?" With the divine eye, pure and surpassing the human eye, he saw the World-Honored One in the Brahmā heaven. Having seen this, he entered samādhi. In accordance with his meditative attainment, he vanished from the country of Śrāvastī and appeared in the Brahmā heaven, in the western direction, facing east toward the Buddha, sitting cross-legged with body upright, mindfully focused, below the Buddha's seat and above the Brahmā's seat.

At that time, the Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana had this thought: "Where is the World-Honored One today?" With the divine eye, pure and surpassing the human eye, he saw from afar the World-Honored One in the Brahmā heaven. Having seen this, he entered samādhi. In accordance with his meditative attainment, he vanished from the country of Śrāvastī and appeared in the Brahmā heaven, in the northern direction, facing south toward the Buddha, sitting cross-legged with body upright, mindfully focused, below the Buddha's seat and above the Brahmā's seat.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the Brahmā: "Do you still give rise to such a view that 'Since the beginning, I have never seen anyone who has surpassed me'?"

The Brahmā addressed the Buddha: "I no longer dare to say: 'I have never seen anyone who has surpassed me.' I see only that the Brahmā's light is obscured." At
