# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Forty-Five

---

### (1198)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was the bhikṣuṇī Āḷavikā, dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park Vihāra in Śrāvastī. The bhikṣuṇī Āḷavikā, early in the morning, put on her robes and took her bowl, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her right shoulder, and entered the Andhavana forest to sit in meditation.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "Now the śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. His disciple, the bhikṣuṇī Āḷavikā, is dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park Vihāra in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her right shoulder, and entered the Andhavana forest to sit in meditation. I shall now go there and create an obstruction for her." He then transformed himself into a young man of handsome appearance, went to that bhikṣuṇī's place, and said to the bhikṣuṇī: "Auntie, where do you wish to go?"

The bhikṣuṇī answered: "Good sir, I am going to a place of seclusion."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas then spoke a verse:

> "There is no escape from the world;
> What use is seeking seclusion for?
> Return and partake of the five sensual pleasures;
> Do not later feel regret."

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Āḷavikā had this thought: "Who is this? Trying to frighten me. Is it a human? Is it a non-human? Is it a deceitful person?"

Her mind then reflected: "This must be the evil Māra, wishing to disturb me." Having realized this, she spoke a verse:

> "There is an escape from the world;
> I myself know what I have attained.
> Base and lowly evil Māra,
> You do not know the path.
> Just as a sharp blade causes harm,
> The five sensual pleasures are likewise;
> Just as the cutting of flesh and form,
> So too is the painful aggregate of feeling.
> What you just spoke of,
> Partaking in the pleasures of the five desires,
> That indeed is no pleasure,
> But a place of great terror.
> Having abandoned all joy and pleasure,
> Having discarded all great darkness,
> With cessation realized as witness,
> I abide securely, free from all outflows.
> Knowing you, evil Māra,
> You shall now vanish and depart."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "The bhikṣuṇī Āḷavikā already knows my mind." Grieved and unhappy, he vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1199)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was the bhikṣuṇī Somā, dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park Vihāra in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her right shoulder, and went to the Andhavana forest to sit in meditation.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "Now the śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. The bhikṣuṇī Somā is dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park Vihāra in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her right shoulder, and entered the Andhavana forest to sit in meditation. I shall now go there and create an obstruction for her." He then transformed himself into a young man of handsome appearance, went to the bhikṣuṇī Somā, and asked: "Auntie, where do you wish to go?"

She answered: "Good sir, I wish to go to a place of seclusion."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas then spoke a verse:

> "The place where sages dwell,
> That place is extremely hard to reach.
> It is not with that two-fingered wisdom
> That one can arrive at that place."

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Somā had this thought: "What sort of person is this? Trying to frighten me. Is it a human? A non-human? A deceitful person?" Having considered this, decisive wisdom arose, and she knew it was the evil Māra who had come to disturb her. She then spoke a verse:

> "When the mind has entered right concentration,
> What does female form matter?
> When wisdom has already arisen,
> One attains the unsurpassed Dharma.
> If, regarding the perception of male and female,
> The mind cannot be freed from both,
> Then such a one follows Māra's words;
> You should go speak to such a person.
> Having departed from all suffering,
> Having discarded all darkness,
> Having attained the realization of cessation,
> I abide securely with all outflows exhausted.
> Knowing you, evil Māra,
> You shall now be worn away and depart."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "The bhikṣuṇī Somā already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and regret, he vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1200)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was the bhikṣuṇī Kisāgotamī, dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park Vihāra in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, went to the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, and entered the Andhavana forest. Beneath a certain tree she sat cross-legged and entered the daytime right concentration.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "Now the śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. The bhikṣuṇī Kisāgotamī is dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park Vihāra in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, and entered the Andhavana forest. Beneath a certain tree she sat cross-legged and entered the daytime right concentration. I shall now go there and create an obstruction for her." He then transformed himself into a young man of handsome appearance, went to the bhikṣuṇī Kisāgotamī, and spoke a verse:

> "Why have you lost your son,
> With tear-stained, grieving countenance?
> Sitting alone beneath a tree,
> Are you searching for a man?"

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Kisāgotamī had this thought: "Who is trying to frighten me? Is it a human? A non-human? A deceitful person?" Having considered this, decisive wisdom arose: "It is Māra Pāpīyas who has come to disturb me." She then spoke a verse:

> "Sons without end or limit,
> All of them lost and gone.
> This is the realm of men;
> I have already crossed beyond the realm of men.
> I neither grieve nor sorrow;
> What the Buddha taught, I have accomplished.
> Having departed from all the suffering of love,
> Having discarded all darkness,
> I have realized cessation as witness;
> Securely I abide with all outflows exhausted.
> I have known you, wretched Māra;
> From here, now vanish and depart."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "The bhikṣuṇī Kisāgotamī already knows my mind." Grieved, distressed, and afflicted, he vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1201)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was the bhikṣuṇī Uppalavaṇṇā, dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, entered the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "Now the śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. The bhikṣuṇī Uppalavaṇṇā is dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, entered the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration. I shall now go there and create an obstruction for her." He then transformed himself into a young man of handsome appearance, went to the bhikṣuṇī Uppalavaṇṇā, and spoke a verse:

> "Beneath a flowering, sturdy tree,
> You rest beneath that tree.
> Alone, without a companion,
> Do you not fear evil men?"

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Uppalavaṇṇā had this thought: "What sort of person is this? Trying to frighten me. Is it a human? A non-human? A deceitful person?" Having considered this, she came to realize: "This must be the evil Māra Pāpīyas, wishing to disturb me." She then spoke a verse:

> "Even if a hundred thousand
> Deceitful people,
> Evil Māras like you,
> Were to come to my place,
> They could not move a single hair.
> I do not fear you, evil Māra."

Māra further spoke a verse:

> "I shall now enter your belly
> And dwell within your internal organs,
> Or dwell between your eyebrows;
> You will not be able to see me."

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Uppalavaṇṇā further spoke a verse:

> "My mind possesses great power,
> Well cultivated in supernormal abilities.
> Freed from the great bonds,
> I do not fear you, evil Māra.
> I have expelled the three defilements,
> The root of all fear.
> I abide in the place of fearlessness,
> Unafraid of Māra's army.
> Having departed from all love and delight,
> Having left behind all darkness,
> Having realized quiescence,
> I securely abide with all outflows exhausted.
> Knowing you, evil Māra,
> You shall now be consumed and depart."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "The bhikṣuṇī Uppalavaṇṇā already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief, he vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1202)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Śīlā was dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, entered the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "Now the śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. The bhikṣuṇī Śīlā is dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park Vihāra in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, entered the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration. I shall now go there and create an obstruction for her." He then transformed himself into a young man of handsome appearance, went before the bhikṣuṇī Śīlā, and spoke a verse:

> "How are beings born? Who is their maker?
> Where do beings arise from?
> And where do they go when they depart?"

The bhikṣuṇī Śīlā had this thought: "Who is this person? Trying to frighten me. Is it a human? A non-human? A deceitful person?" Having considered this, awareness arose in her: "This is the evil Māra wishing to create an obstruction." She then spoke a verse:

> "You say there are 'beings';
> This is the evil Māra's view.
> There is only an empty aggregate of skandhas;
> There is no such thing as a 'being.'
> Just as with the combination of timbers,
> The world calls it a 'chariot,'
> So the skandhas combined through causes and conditions
> Are conventionally called 'beings.'
> Their arising is merely suffering arising;
> Their abiding is merely suffering abiding.
> No other dharma gives rise to suffering;
> Suffering arises, and suffering itself ceases.
> Having discarded all the suffering of attachment,
> Having departed from all darkness,
> Having realized quiescence,
> I securely abide with all outflows exhausted.
> Having known you, evil Māra,
> You shall now be consumed and depart."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "The bhikṣuṇī Śīlā already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1203)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Vīrā was dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, entered the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "Now the śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. The bhikṣuṇī Vīrā is dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, entered the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration. I shall go there and create an obstruction for her." He then transformed himself into a young man of handsome appearance, went to the bhikṣuṇī Vīrā, and spoke a verse:

> "How was this form made? Who is its maker?
> Where does this form arise from?
> And where does it go when it departs?"

The bhikṣuṇī Vīrā had this thought: "Who is this person who has come to frighten me? Is it a human? A non-human? A deceitful person?" Having considered this, she came to realize: "It is Māra Pāpīyas wishing to disturb me." She then spoke a verse:

> "This form was not self-made,
> Nor was it made by another.
> Through the meeting of causes and conditions it arises;
> When conditions disperse, it is worn away and ceases.
> Just as the various seeds in the world
> Grow because of the great earth,
> Depending on earth, water, fire, and wind,
> So too are the skandhas, elements, and sense-bases.
> Through the combination of causes and conditions they arise;
> When conditions separate, they are worn away and cease.
> Having discarded all the suffering of attachment,
> Having departed from all darkness,
> Having realized quiescence,
> I securely abide with all outflows exhausted.
> Evil Māra, now knowing you,
> You shall be worn away and depart."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "The bhikṣuṇī Vīrā already knows my mind." Great grief and sorrow arose, and he vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1204)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was the bhikṣuṇī Vijayā, dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, entered the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "This śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. His disciple, the bhikṣuṇī Vijayā, is dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, entered the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration. I shall now go there and create an obstruction for her." He then transformed himself into a young man of handsome appearance, went before her, and spoke a verse:

> "You are now young in years,
> And I too am young.
> Here together at this place,
> Let us make the five kinds of music
> And enjoy ourselves together.
> What use is this dhyāna-thought?"

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Vijayā had this thought: "What sort of person is this? Trying to frighten me. Is it a human? A non-human? A deceitful person?" Having considered this, she came to realize: "This is Māra Pāpīyas wishing to disturb me." She then spoke a verse:

> "Singing, dancing, and performing arts,
> Various kinds of entertainment,
> I now entirely bestow upon you;
> These are not what I need.
> Whether the quiescent right concentration,
> Or the five desires of gods and humans,
> Take all of them for yourself;
> They are not what I need.
> Having discarded all joy and pleasure,
> Having departed from all darkness,
> Having realized quiescence as witness,
> I securely abide with all outflows exhausted.
> Having known you, evil Māra,
> You shall now be consumed and depart."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "The bhikṣuṇī Vijayā already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1205)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Cālā was dwelling among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, went to the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "Now the śramaṇa Gautama is in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. The bhikṣuṇī Cālā also dwells among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, washed her feet, put away her robes and bowl, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, entered the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration. I shall now go there and create an obstruction for her." He transformed himself into a young man of handsome appearance, went before the bhikṣuṇī Cālā, and spoke a verse:

> "Awakened feeling: birth is joyful;
> Born, one partakes of the five desires.
> Who has instructed and received you,
> Causing you to be disgusted with birth?"

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Cālā had this thought: "Who is this person? Trying to frighten me. Is it a human? A non-human? A deceitful person? Coming here wishing to disturb me." She then spoke a verse:

> "What is born must surely die;
> Birth entails receiving all kinds of suffering,
> Whippings, beatings, and various afflictions;
> All these are conditioned by birth and existence.
> One should sever all suffering
> And transcend all birth.
> With the wisdom-eye, contemplate the noble truths,
> The Dharma taught by the Muni.
> The suffering of suffering, and the origin of suffering,
> The cessation that is free from all suffering,
> The cultivation of the eightfold noble path,
> Peacefully proceeding toward nirvāṇa.
> The great Teacher's Dharma of equality;
> I delight in that Dharma.
> Because I know that Dharma,
> I no longer take pleasure in birth.
> Having departed from all love and delight,
> Having discarded all darkness,
> Having realized quiescence as witness,
> I securely abide with all outflows exhausted.
> Knowing you, evil Māra,
> You shall now be consumed and depart."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "The bhikṣuṇī Cālā already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1206)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Upacālā also dwelt among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, entered the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "Now the śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. The bhikṣuṇī Upacālā also dwells among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, entered the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration. I shall now go there and create an obstruction for her." He then transformed himself into a young man of handsome appearance, went to the bhikṣuṇī Upacālā, and spoke a verse:

> "Among the Trāyastriṃśa gods above,
> The Yāma, Tuṣita,
> Nirmāṇarati, and Paranirmitavaśavartin heavens;
> Make a vow to be reborn there."

The bhikṣuṇī Upacālā had this thought: "What sort of person is this? Trying to frighten me. Is it a human? A non-human? Is it a deceitful person?" Reflecting and awakening, she realized: "This must be the evil Māra wishing to disturb me." She then spoke a verse:

> "Among the Trāyastriṃśa gods above,
> The Yāma, Tuṣita,
> Nirmāṇarati, and Paranirmitavaśavartin,
> All those heavens above,
> They do not depart from conditioned activity,
> And therefore follow Māra's dominion.
> All the worlds
> Are entirely aggregates of activities.
> All the worlds
> Are entirely subject to agitation.
> All the worlds
> Are constantly blazing with the fire of suffering.
> All the worlds
> Are entirely arising with smoke and dust.
> That which does not shake, does not sway,
> Does not associate with common people,
> Does not follow Māra's course;
> In that place I take delight.
> Having departed from all the suffering of attachment,
> Having discarded all darkness,
> Having realized quiescence as witness,
> I securely abide with all outflows exhausted.
> Having realized you, evil Māra,
> You shall be worn away and depart."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "The bhikṣuṇī Upacālā already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1207)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Śīsupacālā also dwelt among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, entered the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration.

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "Now the śramaṇa Gautama is dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. The bhikṣuṇī Śīsupacālā also dwells among the community of bhikṣuṇīs at the King's Park in Śrāvastī. Early in the morning, she put on her robes and took her bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. After eating, she returned to the vihāra, put away her robes and bowl, washed her feet, took her sitting cloth, placed it over her shoulder, entered the Andhavana forest, sat beneath a certain tree, and entered the daytime right concentration. I shall go there and create an obstruction for her." He transformed himself into a young man of handsome appearance, went to the bhikṣuṇī Śīsupacālā, and said: "Auntie, which of the various paths do you delight in?"

The bhikṣuṇī answered: "I delight in none of them at all."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas then spoke a verse:

> "Whose instructions did you receive,
> Shaving your head and becoming a śramaṇa?
> Your body wears the kāṣāya robe,
> Bearing the appearance of a renunciant.
> Yet you delight in none of the various paths,
> And abide in guarding foolish ignorance."

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Śīsupacālā had this thought: "What sort of person is this? Trying to frighten me. Is it a human? A non-human? A deceitful person?" Having considered this, she came to realize: "It is Māra Pāpīyas wishing to disturb me." She then spoke a verse:

> "The various paths outside this Dharma
> Are entangled by wrong views.
> Bound by those various views,
> One forever follows Māra's dominion.
> But being born into the Śākya clan,
> One receives the incomparable great Teacher,
> Who is able to subdue the enemy Māra,
> And is not subdued by him.
> Purely and completely liberated,
> With the Dharma-eye observing everywhere,
> The omniscient one who knows all,
> The most excellent, free from all outflows.
> He is my great Teacher;
> I delight solely in his Dharma.
> Having entered his Dharma,
> I have attained the seclusion of far-reaching quiescence.
> Having departed from all love and delight,
> Having discarded all darkness,
> Having realized quiescence as witness,
> I securely abide with all outflows exhausted.
> Having known you, evil Māra,
> Thus vanish and depart."

At that time, Māra Pāpīyas had this thought: "The bhikṣuṇī Śīsupacālā already knows my mind." Inwardly harboring grief and sorrow, he vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1208)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Campā, beside the Gaggarā Lotus Pond.

At that time, the World-Honored One, on the fifteenth day of the month, at the time of the poṣadha observance, sat before the great assembly. When the moon had just risen, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa, in the midst of the great assembly, had this thought: "I now wish to praise the moon-simile verse before the Buddha." Having had this thought, he rose from his seat, arranged his robes, paid respect to the Buddha, and with palms joined said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I wish to speak. Sugata, I wish to speak."

The Buddha told Vaṅgīsa: "If you wish to speak, then speak."

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa, in the presence of the Buddha, spoke a verse:

> "Like the moon suspended in empty space,
> Bright and pure, without cloud or obscuration,
> With radiant and brilliant splendor,
> Illuminating universally in all ten directions.
> The Tathāgata is likewise:
> The light of wisdom illuminates the world.
> Merit, virtue, and renowned fame
> Pervade and fill the ten directions."

When the Venerable Vaṅgīsa spoke this verse, the bhikṣus who heard what he said all greatly rejoiced.

---

### (1209)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Campā, beside the Gaggarā Lotus Pond.

At that time, the Venerable Ājñāta Kauṇḍinya had long dwelt in the secluded araṇya forest. He came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, pressed his face upon the Buddha's feet, and said these words: "It has been long since I have seen the World-Honored One! Long since I have seen the Sugata!"

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa was in the assembly and had this thought: "I should now, in the presence of the Venerable Ājñāta Kauṇḍinya, praise him with a simile of an elder." Having had this thought, he rose from his seat, arranged his robes, paid respect to the Buddha, and with palms joined said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I wish to speak. Sugata, I wish to speak."

The Buddha told Vaṅgīsa: "If you wish to speak, then speak."

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa spoke a verse:

> "Elder among elders,
> The Venerable Kauṇḍinya,
> Has already crossed over, already transcended,
> And attained peaceful right concentration.
> In the araṇya forest,
> He constantly delights in seclusion.
> All that a śrāvaka should do,
> The great Teacher's correct Dharma instructions,
> All of these he has fully practiced.
> In right concentration, without negligence,
> Of great virtue and power, with the three knowledges,
> He clearly knows the minds of others.
> The elder Kauṇḍinya
> Protects and upholds the Buddha's Dharma-treasury.
> With a mind of supreme reverence,
> He bows his head at the Buddha's feet."

When the Venerable Vaṅgīsa spoke these words, the bhikṣus who heard what he said all greatly rejoiced.

---

### (1210)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the land of Campā, beside the Gaggarā Lotus Pond.

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra was in the offering hall, where many bhikṣus had gathered and he was teaching the Dharma for them. His phrases and meanings were complete, his eloquence was pure and refined, easy to understand and pleasant to hear, unhindered and unbroken, with deep meaning clearly manifest. Those bhikṣus listened with focused attention and delight, with reverence and mindfulness, with one-pointed attentive listening.

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa was in the assembly and had this thought: "I should now, in the presence of the Venerable Śāriputra, speak a verse of praise." Having had this thought, he rose, with palms joined, and said to the Venerable Śāriputra: "I wish to speak."

Śāriputra said: "Speak as you please."

The Venerable Vaṅgīsa then spoke a verse:

> "Skillfully able to teach the Dharma in brief,
> Causing the assembly to broadly comprehend.
> The excellent Upatissa,
> Expounds to the great assembly.
> When he teaches the Dharma,
> From his throat comes a beautiful voice,
> A joyful, delightful, and cherished voice,
> A harmonious, gradually progressing voice.
> Hearing his voice, all are delighted,
> With focused mindfulness, unwavering."

When the Venerable Vaṅgīsa spoke these words, the bhikṣus who heard what he said all greatly rejoiced.

---

### (1211)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, on the side of Mount Nāga, together with five hundred bhikṣus, all of whom were arhats. Their outflows were exhausted, what was to be done had been done, they had cast off the heavy burden, attained their own benefit, severed the bonds of existence, and their minds were well liberated through right knowledge.

The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana surveyed the minds of the great assembly: all were completely liberated from desire. At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa, in the midst of the great assembly, had this thought: "I should now, in the presence of the World-Honored One and the Sangha of bhikṣus, speak a verse of praise." Having had this thought, he rose from his seat, arranged his robes, with palms joined, said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I wish to speak. Sugata, I wish to speak."

The Buddha told Vaṅgīsa: "Speak as you please."

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa spoke a verse:

> "The Guide, the Unsurpassed One,
> Dwells on the side of Mount Nāga.
> The assembly of five hundred bhikṣus
> Attends upon the great Teacher.
> The Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana,
> With supernormal powers, truly and clearly,
> Surveys the minds of the great assembly:
> All are freed from desire.
> Thus fully accomplished in the crossing,
> The Muni has crossed to the far shore.
> Bearing this final body,
> I now bow my head in reverence."

When the Venerable Vaṅgīsa spoke these words, the bhikṣus who heard what he said all greatly rejoiced.

---

### (1212)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove, for the rains retreat, together with a great assembly of five hundred bhikṣus, all of whom were arhats. Their outflows were exhausted, what was to be done had been done, they had cast off the heavy burden, severed the bonds of existence, and their minds were well liberated through right knowledge, except for one bhikṣu, namely the Venerable Ānanda, of whom the World-Honored One had declared that he would attain the realization of complete knowledge in this very life.

At that time, the World-Honored One, on the fifteenth day of the month, at the time of the poṣadha meal-offering, sat before the great assembly, and having sat down, addressed the bhikṣus: "I am a brahman who has attained parinirvāṇa, bearing this final body. I am a great physician, able to extract all arrow-thorns. I am a brahman who has attained parinirvāṇa, bearing this final body, the unsurpassed physician, able to extract arrow-thorns. You are my sons, born from my mouth, born from the Dharma-transformation, obtaining the surplus wealth of the Dharma. You should cherish and accept me. Do not let there be anything regarding my body, speech, or mind that could be reproached."

At that time, the Venerable Śāriputra was in the assembly. He rose from his seat, arranged his robes, paid respect to the Buddha, and with palms joined said to the Buddha: "The World-Honored One just now made this statement: 'I am a brahman who has attained parinirvāṇa, bearing the final body, the unsurpassed great physician, able to extract arrow-thorns. You are my sons, born from the Buddha's mouth, born from the Dharma-transformation, obtaining the surplus wealth of the Dharma. Bhikṣus, you should cherish and accept me. Do not let there be anything regarding my body, speech, or mind that could be reproached.' We do not see in the World-Honored One anything regarding body, speech, or mind that could be reproached. Why is this? The World-Honored One can bring the untamed to discipline, the unquiet to peace, the unrested to rest, and those who have not attained parinirvāṇa to parinirvāṇa. The Tathāgata knows the path, the Tathāgata teaches the path, the Tathāgata points toward the path. Then the śrāvakas accomplish it, following the path, devoted to the path, receiving the Teacher's instructions. According to his teachings, they rightly proceed with delight in the true and wholesome Dharma.

"I see nothing in the World-Honored One regarding body, speech, or mind that could be reproached. I now, before the World-Honored One, request that he accept my confession regarding any offenses of seeing, hearing, or doubting, and any reproachable conduct of body, speech, or mind."

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "I see nothing in you regarding offenses of seeing, hearing, or doubting, or body, speech, or mind that could be reproached. Why is this? You, Śāriputra, uphold the precepts, are learned, of few desires and contentment, cultivate seclusion, are energetic and diligent, with right mindfulness and right concentration. You possess swift wisdom, bright wisdom, wisdom of escape, wisdom of disenchantment, great wisdom, vast wisdom, deep wisdom, incomparable wisdom, and the accomplishment of the jewel of wisdom. You instruct, teach, illumine, and bring joy. You also constantly praise instructing, teaching, illumining, and bringing joy. You teach the Dharma for the assembly without ever growing weary.

"Just as a wheel-turning sage king's eldest son, who should receive the consecration but has not yet been consecrated, already abides in the conduct of consecration. As his father's Dharma that should be turned, he also turns in accordance. You now are likewise: you are my eldest son, close to receiving the consecration but not yet consecrated, abiding in the conduct. The Dharma-wheel that I should turn, you also turn in accordance. You have attained the state of non-arising, with all outflows exhausted, and your mind is well liberated. Thus, Śāriputra, I see nothing in you whatsoever regarding offenses of seeing, hearing, or doubting, or body, speech, or mind that could be reproached."

Śāriputra said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, if I have no offenses of seeing, hearing, or doubting, and no reproachable conduct of body, speech, or mind, do these five hundred bhikṣus also have no offenses of seeing, hearing, or doubting, and no reproachable conduct of body, speech, or mind?"

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "I also see nothing in these five hundred bhikṣus regarding offenses of seeing, hearing, or doubting, or body, speech, or mind that could be reproached. Why is this? These five hundred bhikṣus are all arhats. Their outflows are exhausted, what was to be done has been done, they have cast off the heavy burden, severed the bonds of existence, and their minds are well liberated through right knowledge, except for one bhikṣu, namely the Venerable Ānanda, of whom I have declared that he will attain the realization of complete knowledge in this very life. Therefore, among these five hundred bhikṣus, I see none having offenses of seeing, hearing, or doubting regarding body, speech, or mind that could be reproached."

Śāriputra said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, since these five hundred bhikṣus have no offenses of seeing, hearing, or doubting, and no reproachable conduct of body, speech, or mind, how many bhikṣus among them have attained the three knowledges? How many have attained both-ways liberation? How many have attained liberation by wisdom?"

The Buddha told Śāriputra: "Among these five hundred bhikṣus, ninety have attained the three knowledges, ninety have attained both-ways liberation, and the remainder have attained liberation by wisdom. Śāriputra, these bhikṣus have departed from all wavering; they are without skin or husk, truly solid and firm."

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa was in the assembly and had this thought: "I should now, in the presence of the World-Honored One and the great assembly, speak verses of praise regarding cherishing and acceptance." Having had this thought, he rose from his seat, arranged his robes, paid respect to the Buddha, knelt on his right knee, and with palms joined said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I wish to speak. Sugata, I wish to speak."

The Buddha told Vaṅgīsa: "Speak as you please."

At that time, Vaṅgīsa spoke a verse:

> "On this fifteenth day of purity,
> The assembly is five hundred persons.
> Having severed all bonds,
> Great sages with existence exhausted.
> In purity they associate together,
> In purity they are broadly liberated.
> They will not again undergo any form of existence;
> Birth and death are forever ended.
> What was to be done has been done;
> They have attained the exhaustion of all outflows.
> The five hindrances, like clouds, have cleared away;
> The thorn of craving, the root, is pulled out.
> Like lions, without fear,
> Departed from all residual remainder,
> Having destroyed the bonds of the enemy of existence,
> They have transcended the realm of residual remainder.
> The outflows, those hostile foes,
> Are all completely subdued.
> Just as a wheel-turning king
> Cherishes and accepts his retinue,
> With a compassionate mind broadly proclaiming and transforming,
> All within the seas entirely serve him.
> Able to subdue the enemies of Māra,
> He is the unsurpassed guide.
> Those who with faith and reverence attend him,
> With the three knowledges, have extinguished old age and death.
> They are the true sons of the Dharma,
> Without the affliction of wavering.
> Having pulled out the thorns of defilements,
> They pay homage to the scion of the Solar Lineage."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (1213)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Venerable Nigrodha-kappa was dwelling in the wilderness, a place where birds and beasts resided. The Venerable Vaṅgīsa had gone forth not long before and had this deportment: depending on villages and towns for his dwelling, early in the morning he would put on his robes and take his bowl, and in those villages and towns beg for food, well guarding his body, guarding the sense-doors, with a collected and mindful mind. After eating, he would return to his dwelling, put away his robes and bowl, wash his feet, enter his room, and sit in meditation. He would quickly arise from meditation, without attachment to begging for food. Yet he had no one to give him timely instruction or admonition, and his mind was not at ease, dwelling in concealment and covering. Thus he dwelt deeply within.

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa had this thought: "It is not beneficial for me; it is hard to obtain and not easily obtained. I do not timely receive instruction or admonition, and cannot attain the joy of dwelling fully covered. I should now praise verses of self-disenchantment." He then spoke a verse:

> "One should abandon both the pleasant and unpleasant,
> As well as all thoughts of desire.
> Doing nothing regarding what is near,
> Separated from defilement is called a bhikṣu.
> Regarding the six kinds of mental perceptions,
> The mind gallops throughout the world.
> Evil, unwholesome coverings,
> Cannot remove the skin and husk.
> Those who delight in defilement within the mind,
> They are not called bhikṣus.
> There are remaining bonds that bind,
> Together with seeing, hearing, cognizing, and knowing.
> But for one who is awakened regarding desire,
> That place is no longer defiled.
> Such a one who is undefiled,
> That is called a muni.
> The great earth and empty space,
> All images of the world,
> These are all subject to wearing away;
> In stillness, one makes one's own determination.
> The vessel of the Dharma, long cultivated,
> Attains samādhi.
> Without touching, without crookedness or deceit,
> The mind is extremely focused.
> That sage has long ago attained nirvāṇa;
> With mindfulness bound, awaiting the time of cessation."

At that time, after the Venerable Vaṅgīsa had spoken these verses of self-disenchantment, his mind awakened itself. Having awakened from displeasure and the like, he dwelt with a joyful mind.

---

### (1214)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Ānanda, early in the morning, put on his robes and took his bowl, entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food, with the Venerable Vaṅgīsa as his companion. At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa saw a woman of supreme beauty, and having seen her, a mind of desire arose.

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa had this thought: "I have gained what is not beneficial, I have gained suffering and not gained pleasure. I have seen a young woman of exquisite beauty, and a mind of desire has arisen. I should now, to generate disenchantment, speak a verse." He said:

> "Covered by desire,
> It blazes and burns my mind.
> Now, Venerable Ānanda,
> Extinguish for me the fire of desire.
> Out of compassion and sympathy,
> Please skillfully teach me."

The Venerable Ānanda spoke a verse in reply:

> "It is through inverted perception
> That your mind blazes and burns.
> Turn away from the perception of the attractive,
> Which nourishes desire.
> You should cultivate the contemplation of the impure,
> Constantly with one-pointed right concentration.
> Quickly extinguish the fire of desire;
> Do not let it burn your mind.
> Truly contemplate all formations:
> Suffering, empty, and not-self.
> With mindfulness bound, rightly contemplate the body;
> Greatly cultivate disenchantment.
> Cultivate the signless,
> And eliminate the tendency of conceit.
> Having attained the penetration of conceit without interval,
> You will reach the final end of suffering."

When the Venerable Ānanda spoke these words, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa, hearing what he said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (1215)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain householder invited the Buddha and the Sangha to eat at his house. After entering his house, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa, being the one on duty to guard the monastery, had his share of food brought to him.

At that time, many householders' wives came out from the village and went to the vihāra. The Venerable Vaṅgīsa, seeing young women of beautiful and fine appearance, had a mind of desire arise.

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa had this thought: "I am not benefited today, I have not obtained benefit; I have gained suffering, not gained pleasure. Seeing other women of beautiful and fine appearance, a mind of desire has arisen. I should now speak verses of disenchantment." Having reflected, he spoke a verse:

> "I have already achieved the going forth,
> Leaving the household to become a renunciant.
> Yet desire follows and pursues me,
> Like a bull longing for another's sprouts.
> I should be like a great general's son,
> With great strength wielding a mighty bow,
> Able to break through heavy battle formations,
> One person subduing a thousand.
> Now, from the scion of the Solar Lineage,
> In his presence I have heard what was taught:
> Rightly proceeding on the path to nirvāṇa,
> With a determined mind I delight in abiding.
> Thus, without negligence,
> I abide in quiescent right concentration.
> No one can, in my mind,
> Deceive or delude me with illusions.
> With determined, careful contemplation,
> Securely abiding in the correct Dharma,
> Even if innumerable
> Desires come to deceive and delude me,
> Such evil māras
> Cannot even see me."

At that time, after the Venerable Vaṅgīsa spoke this verse, his mind attained peaceful abiding.

---

### (1216)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa, considering himself capable of skillful speech through his own wisdom, among the wise and eloquent practitioners of the holy life, gave rise to a mind of conceit. His mind then reflected: "I am not benefited, I have not obtained benefit; I have gained suffering, not gained pleasure. I myself, through my wisdom, look down upon those wise practitioners of the holy life with conceit. I should now speak a verse that will generate disenchantment." He then spoke a verse:

> "Gautama, do not give rise to conceit;
> Sever conceit, leaving no remainder.
> Do not give rise to thoughts of conceit;
> Do not backslide and feel regret.
> Do not conceal or cover others;
> In the mud and mire, conceit is slain and falls.
> Right concentration can remove sorrow;
> Seeing the path, abide in the right path.
> When the mind attains joy and happiness,
> Seeing the path, one takes hold of oneself.
> Therefore, unhindered eloquence,
> Pure and free from all hindrances.
> Sever all conceit,
> And give rise to all forms of clear knowledge:
> Right mindfulness in the three knowledges,
> Supernormal powers, and knowledge of others' minds."

At that time, after the Venerable Vaṅgīsa spoke this verse of disenchantment, his mind attained purity.

---

### (1217)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa was dwelling at the Eastern Park, in the Hall of Migāra's Mother, in Śrāvastī, alone in contemplation, dwelling in heedfulness, devoted to his own practice, and he attained the three knowledges, realized through direct experience.

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa had this thought: "I, alone in a quiet place, contemplating, dwelling in heedfulness, devoted to my own practice, have given rise to the three knowledges, realized through direct experience. I should now speak a verse praising the three knowledges." He then spoke a verse:

> "Formerly, with a mind crazed and confused by desire,
> From village to village, from house to house,
> Wandering, I encountered the Buddha,
> Who bestowed upon me the supreme Dharma.
> Gautama, out of compassion,
> Taught me the correct Dharma.
> Hearing the Dharma, I gained pure faith,
> Renounced the household, and went forth.
> Having heard his teaching of the Dharma,
> I rightly abide in the Dharma instruction.
> Diligently striving with mindfulness bound,
> Firm, constant, and capable,
> I have attained the three knowledges;
> In the Buddha's teaching, the work has been done.
> The World-Honored One has well revealed it;
> The scion of the Solar Lineage has taught it.
> For beings born blind,
> He opens the gate of escape.
> The suffering of suffering, and the cause of suffering,
> The cessation of suffering realized as witness,
> The eightfold noble path that departs from suffering,
> Peacefully proceeding toward nirvāṇa.
> With wholesome meaning, wholesome phrasing and flavor,
> The holy life has no superior.
> The World-Honored One has well revealed
> Nirvāṇa, the salvation of beings."

---

### (1218)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now teach the four statements of Dharma. Listen attentively and reflect well; I shall teach you. What are the four?

> "The noble and virtuous one speaks the Dharma well;
> This is foremost.
> Speaking what is lovely, not what is unlovely;
> This is the second.
> Speaking truth, not falsehood;
> This is the third statement.
> Speaking the Dharma, not speaking contrary words;
> This is the fourth.

"Bhikṣus, these are called the four statements of Dharma."

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa, in the midst of the assembly, had this thought: "The World-Honored One has taught the four statements of Dharma among the fourfold assembly. I should praise, extol, and rejoice in them with four kinds of praise." He then rose from his seat, arranged his robes, paid respect to the Buddha, and with palms joined said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I wish to speak. Sugata, I wish to speak."

The Buddha told Vaṅgīsa: "Speak as you please."

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa spoke a verse:

> "If one speaks the Dharma well,
> Neither distressing oneself
> Nor causing fear to others,
> This is called speaking well.
> What is called speaking what is lovely:
> Speaking so as to cause the other to rejoice,
> Without causing the other to do evil;
> This is called speaking what is lovely.
> Speaking truth knows the ambrosia;
> Speaking truth knows the unsurpassed.
> Speaking truth, speaking the meaning of the Dharma,
> Is the ground upon which the upright ones stand.
> As the Buddha teaches the Dharma,
> The peaceful path of nirvāṇa,
> Extinguishing all suffering:
> This is called speaking the Dharma well."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, joyfully practiced in accordance.

---

### (1219)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, on the side of Mount Nāga, together with a thousand bhikṣus, all of whom were arhats. Their outflows were exhausted, what was to be done had been done, they had cast off the heavy burden, attained their own benefit, exhausted the bonds of existence, and their minds were well liberated through right knowledge.

At that time, the Venerable Vaṅgīsa was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Cool Forest, among the burial mounds, and had this thought: "Now the World-Honored One is dwelling at Rājagṛha, on the side of Mount Nāga, together with a thousand bhikṣus, all of whom are arhats. Their outflows are exhausted, what was to be done has been done, they have cast off the heavy burden, attained their own benefit, exhausted the bonds of existence, and their minds are well liberated through right knowledge. I should now go there and individually praise the World-Honored One and the Sangha of bhikṣus." Having had this thought, he went to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew, and stood to one side. He then spoke a verse:

> "The Guide, the Unsurpassed One,
> Dwells on the side of Mount Nāga.
> A thousand bhikṣus as his retinue
> Attend upon the Tathāgata.
> The great Teacher broadly teaches the Dharma,
> The cool path of nirvāṇa.
> They listen attentively to the pure white Dharma,
> Spoken by the Perfectly Awakened One.
> The Perfectly Awakened One, honored by all,
> Sits in the midst of the great assembly,
> A great nāga of the aggregate of virtue,
> The foremost among sages.
> He arises clouds of merit and virtue,
> Showering rain upon the śrāvaka assembly.
> Rising from the daytime right concentration,
> He comes to pay respects to the great Teacher.
> The disciple Vaṅgīsa
> Bows his head in reverence.

"World-Honored One, I wish to speak. May it be so, Sugata, I wish to speak."

The Buddha told Vaṅgīsa: "Speak as you wish. Do not first deliberate."

At that time, Vaṅgīsa spoke a verse:

> "When Pāpīyas raises subtle evil,
> Suppress and swiftly extinguish it.
> He can obstruct and block the māras,
> Causing them to realize their own faults.
> He observes, analyzes, and unties bonds,
> Distinguishing the pure white Dharma.
> Shining bright like the sun and moon,
> He is king among those of other paths.
> Transcending with wisdom, he realizes as witness,
> And teaches the foremost Dharma.
> Going beyond the floods of defilements,
> He teaches the path in countless ways.
> He establishes the path of ambrosia,
> Seeing the truth, the real Dharma.
> Such
