# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Forty-Six

---

### (1222)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In a past age, the devas and the asuras arrayed themselves for battle. The asuras were victorious and the devas were defeated. At that time, Śakra, Lord of the Devas, had his army broken and scattered. He was extremely terrified, and mounting his chariot, he fled northward, returning toward the heavenly palace.

"Below Mount Sumeru, there was a road passing through a dense forest, and beneath the forest there were nests of garuḍa birds with many garuḍa chicks. At that time, Śakra feared that his chariot and horses passing through would trample and kill the chicks. He told his charioteer: 'Turn the chariot back! Do not kill the chicks.' The charioteer addressed the king: 'The asura army is pursuing us from behind. If we turn back, we shall be overwhelmed by them.' Śakra replied: 'I would rather turn back and be killed by the asuras than trample and kill living beings with my army.' Thereupon, on the road, the charioteer turned the chariot to face south. The asura army, seeing from afar that Śakra had turned his chariot and was coming back, thought it was a battle stratagem. They immediately retreated and fled, overcome with great fear, their formations broken and scattered, and they returned to the asura palace."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "That Śakra, Lord of the Devas, being the sovereign king of the Trāyastriṃśa Heaven, through his compassionate mind, with his majestic power crushed and subdued the asura army, and he also constantly praised the merit of a compassionate mind. You bhikṣus, having left the household life with right faith to study the path, should cultivate a compassionate mind and should also praise the merit of a compassionate mind."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1223)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, in the city of Rājagṛha there was a man who was poor and endured hardship, yet he had faith in the Buddha, the Dharma, and the Sangha, upheld the moral precepts, was learned and broadly studied, diligently practiced giving, and had accomplished right view. When his body perished and his life ended, he was reborn in the heavens, born in the Trāyastriṃśa Heaven. He surpassed the other Trāyastriṃśa devas in three respects. What were the three? First, heavenly lifespan; second, heavenly appearance; third, heavenly renown. The devas of the Trāyastriṃśa Heaven saw this deva-son excelling in these three respects: heavenly lifespan, heavenly appearance, and heavenly renown.

The other devas, seeing this, went to Śakra, Lord of the Devas, and spoke thus: "Kauśika, you should know that a deva-son has just been born in this heaven who surpasses all the former devas in three respects: heavenly lifespan, heavenly appearance, and heavenly renown."

At that time, Śakra, Lord of the Devas, addressed that deva-son: "Venerable ones! I saw this person when he was a man in Rājagṛha, poor and enduring hardship. In the Tathāgata's Dharma and Vinaya he attained a mind of faith, and so forth, up to accomplishing right view. When his body perished and his life ended, he came to be born in this heaven, surpassing all the Trāyastriṃśa devas in three respects: heavenly lifespan, heavenly appearance, and heavenly renown." At that time, Śakra, Lord of the Devas, then spoke this verse:

> "With right faith in the Tathāgata,
> firm and unwavering,
> upholding true and genuine precepts,
> noble precepts without weariness;
> with a mind pure toward the Buddha,
> having accomplished right view:
> know that such a one is not truly poor,
> and does not live in vain.
> Therefore, toward the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha,
> one should generate pure faith.
> With wisdom's power growing bright,
> contemplate the Buddha's right teaching."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1224)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, on Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain. At that time, the people of Rājagṛha had arranged a great public festival and had invited all the various heterodox practitioners.

Those who served the Cārakā heterodox practitioners had this thought: "I shall now invite the devas of the Cārakā path to be the foremost field of merit." Some served heterodox renunciants; some served the Nirgrantha path; some served the Elder Disciples; some served the Fire Disciples; and some served the Sangha of the Buddha's disciples, all thinking: "We should now make the Sangha in the Buddha's presence the foremost field of merit."

At that time, Śakra, Lord of the Devas, had this thought: "I must not let the people of Rājagṛha abandon the Sangha in the Buddha's presence and serve other paths in seeking a field of merit. I should go quickly and establish the field of merit for the people of Rājagṛha." He then transformed himself into a great brāhmaṇa, dignified and impeccable in deportment, riding a white horse-drawn chariot, with a retinue of young brāhmaṇas before and behind him as escorts, carrying a golden bowl and a parasol canopy. He went to Rājagṛha and proceeded to the various great assembly gatherings throughout the city.

All the men and women of Rājagṛha had this thought: "We should simply watch and see where this great brāhmaṇa pays his respects; we shall follow him and make offerings there first, as a fine field of merit."

At that time, Śakra, Lord of the Devas, knowing the thoughts of all the men and women of Rājagṛha, drove his chariot with his retinue and went directly to Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain. Arriving at the gate, he removed his five ornaments, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and spoke this verse:

> "You who skillfully distinguish and reveal
> the far shore of all dharmas,
> and who ferry beings across all fears:
> therefore I bow my head to Gautama.
> The people have arranged a public festival,
> wishing to seek great merit.
> Each has set up a great offering,
> always wishing for abundant fruit.
> Please explain the field of merit,
> so that these offerings may bear fruit."

> "Śakra, the great sovereign,
> the king of devas, has asked this question
> on Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain;
> the Great Teacher now proclaims the answer.
> The people have arranged a public festival,
> wishing to seek great merit.
> Each has set up a great offering,
> always wishing for abundant fruit.
> Now I shall explain the field of merit,
> the place where giving yields great fruit.
> Those rightly directed are four,
> and four saints abide in the fruit.
> This is called the Sangha, the field of merit,
> replete in knowledge, conduct, and concentration.
> The Sangha as a field of merit is vast and expansive,
> immeasurable, surpassing the great ocean.
> The Tamer of Men's disciples,
> illuminating and revealing the true Dharma:
> those who make good offerings to them,
> giving to the Sangha, the fine field of merit,
> from the Sangha, the fine field of merit,
> the Buddha has said one obtains great fruit.
> Because the Sangha has abandoned the five hindrances,
> pure and worthy of praise,
> giving to this supreme field,
> a small gift reaps great benefit.
> Therefore, all people
> should give to the Sangha, the field of merit,
> and thereby gain the supreme, wondrous Dharma,
> in accord with knowledge, conduct, and concentration.
> Offering to this precious jewel Sangha,
> the donors' hearts are joyful.
> Giving rise to three kinds of mind,
> they give robes, food, and drink.
> Free from dust, defilements, swords, and thorns,
> they transcend the evil destinies.
> Personally going forth to invite,
> with one's own hands giving equally:
> benefiting oneself and benefiting others,
> such giving obtains great benefit.
> The wise, giving in this way,
> with pure faith and mind liberated,
> giving without guilt, in peace and happiness,
> ride upon wisdom and are reborn there."

At that time, Śakra, Lord of the Devas, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and approved. He paid respects to the Buddha and then vanished from sight.

At that time, the people of Rājagṛha rose from their seats, arranged their garments, paid respects to the Buddha, and with palms joined addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! We only wish that the World-Honored One, together with the great assembly, would accept our offerings." At that time, the World-Honored One silently accepted the invitation.

When the people of Rājagṛha knew that the World-Honored One had silently accepted their invitation, they paid respects and departed. They went to the assembly places, prepared food and drink, laid out seats and couches, and in the early morning sent a messenger to address the Buddha: "The time has come; may you please be aware of the time!"

At that time, the World-Honored One, together with the great assembly, donned their robes and took up their bowls and went to the great assembly place. Before the great assembly, a seat was spread and he sat down.

The people of Rājagṛha, knowing the Buddha was seated, personally served various abundant and delicious food and drink. When the meal was finished, the bowls were washed and mouths rinsed, and they returned to their original seats to listen to the Buddha teach the Dharma.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the Dharma in various ways for the people of Rājagṛha, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening them. Then he rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (1225)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Rājagṛha, on Gṛdhrakūṭa Mountain, as broadly stated above. The difference is that at that time, Śakra, Lord of the Devas, spoke a different verse, asking the Buddha:

> "Now I ask Gautama,
> of subtle, deep, and wondrous wisdom,
> which the World-Honored One embodies,
> with unobstructed knowledge and vision."

The verses about the people arranging a public festival are as broadly stated above, up to teaching the Dharma in various ways for the people of Rājagṛha who had arranged the festival, instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening them. Then he rose from his seat and departed.

---

### (1226)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kośala and arrived at Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit heard that the World-Honored One had been traveling among the people of Kośala and had arrived at Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. Having heard this, he went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I have heard that the World-Honored One has declared of himself that he has attained anuttarā samyaksaṃbodhi. Those who have transmitted this, have they perhaps spoken falsely or exaggerated? Do they speak as you spoke, speak in accordance with the Dharma, and speak in conformity with the Dharma? Is it not the case that others who oppose the Dharma, in questioning and answering, find grounds for contempt?"

The Buddha told the great king: "Those who speak thus speak truthfully, not falsely. They speak as I spoke, speak in accordance with the Dharma, and speak in conformity with the Dharma. It is not the case that others who oppose the Dharma, in questioning and answering, find grounds for contempt. Why is this? Great King, I have indeed truly attained anuttarā samyaksaṃbodhi."

King Prasenajit addressed the Buddha: "Although the World-Honored One says this, I still do not believe it. Why? Here there are senior and venerable śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, namely Pūraṇa Kāśyapa, Maskarin Gośālīputra, Sañjayin Vairaṭṭīputra, Ajita Keśakambala, Kakuda Kātyāyana, and Nirgrantha Jñātiputra. Even they do not claim to have attained anuttarā samyaksaṃbodhi. How can the World-Honored One, young in age and only recently gone forth, claim to have realized anuttarā samyaksaṃbodhi?"

The Buddha told the great king: "There are four things that, though small, should not be taken lightly. What are the four? A young prince of the kṣatriya class, though young and small, should not be taken lightly. A young dragon, though young and small, should not be taken lightly. A small fire, though tiny, should not be taken lightly. A young bhikṣu, though young, should not be taken lightly." At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke these verses:

> "A kṣatriya of perfect form,
> of noble clan, with rising fame:
> though he is still young in years,
> the wise do not take him lightly.
> He will surely come to the throne,
> and in resentment cause harm.
> Therefore he is hard to take lightly;
> one should show great reverence.
> Those who seek to protect themselves
> should protect themselves as they protect their lives.
> By equally protecting oneself,
> one equally protects one's life.
>
> In villages or in the wilderness,
> if one sees a young dragon,
> do not, because it is a small serpent,
> give rise to thoughts of contempt.
> A young dragon of variegated form
> should also be allowed its comfort.
> Those who slight serpents, whether men or women,
> are all harmed by their venom.
> Therefore, those who would protect themselves
> should do so as they protect their own lives.
> By thus well protecting themselves,
> they equally protect that life.
>
> What a raging fire consumes,
> though small, it consumes without limit.
> A small flame can also burn;
> given fuel, it spreads ever wider.
> From small, it gradually advances and burns,
> consuming villages and cities.
> Therefore, those who would protect themselves
> should do so as they protect their own lives.
> By thus well protecting themselves,
> they equally protect that life.
> What a blazing fire burns,
> all the grasses are swept away and consumed.
> After it is extinguished, though it seems gone,
> the fire of precepts arises again.
> If one slights and reviles a bhikṣu
> who upholds the pure fire of precepts,
> it burns oneself, one's children and grandchildren;
> calamities flow for a hundred generations,
> like burning a tāla palm tree:
> there is no period of regrowth.
> Therefore one should protect oneself
> as one protects one's own life.
> By thus well protecting oneself,
> one equally protects that life.
>
> A kṣatriya of perfect form,
> a young dragon, and a small fire,
> a bhikṣu endowed with pure precepts:
> one should not give rise to thoughts of contempt.
> Therefore one should protect oneself
> as one protects one's own life.
> By thus well protecting oneself,
> one equally protects that life."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and approved, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (1227)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit had a grandmother whom he deeply respected. She suddenly passed away. He went out of the city to cremate her, made offerings to her relics, and then, with disheveled clothes and disordered hair, came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One asked King Prasenajit: "Great King! Where have you come from, with disheveled clothes and disordered hair?"

King Prasenajit addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! My beloved grandmother, whom I deeply respected, has left me and passed away. I went outside the city to cremate her, and after the offerings were completed, I came to see the World-Honored One."

The Buddha asked the great king: "Did you deeply love, respect, and cherish your grandmother?"

King Prasenajit addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I deeply respected, loved, and was attached to her. World-Honored One! If all the elephants, horses, and seven treasures in my kingdom, even the throne itself, could be given to someone who could save my grandmother's life, I would give it all. But since I could not save her, and in the long separation of birth and death, I am overcome with grief, sorrow, and suffering, unable to bear it. I once heard the World-Honored One say: 'All beings, all creatures, all spirits: those who are born all die. None escape exhaustion. There is none who is born and does not die.' Today I truly know that the World-Honored One spoke well."

The Buddha said: "Great King! So it is! So it is! All beings, all creatures, all spirits: those who are born always die. In the end they are all exhausted. There is not a single one who is born and does not die."

The Buddha told the great king: "Even among the great brāhmaṇa families, great kṣatriya families, and great elder families, those who are born all die; there are none who do not die. Even a great kṣatriya king, consecrated and enthroned, ruling over the four quarters of the world, possessed of power and sovereignty, with no enemy unconquered, in the end reaches his limit; there is none who does not die. Furthermore, Great King, even those born among the long-lived devas, ruling in heavenly palaces in freedom and happiness, they too in the end reach exhaustion; there is none who does not die. Furthermore, Great King, even arhat bhikṣus whose outflows are exhausted, who have laid down the heavy burden, who have accomplished what was to be done, who have obtained their own benefit, who have ended all bonds of existence, whose minds are well liberated through right knowledge, they too in the end reach exhaustion, abandoning the body and entering nirvāṇa. Even pratyekabuddhas, well-tamed and well-stilled, at the end of this body and life, ultimately enter nirvāṇa. Even the Buddhas, the World-Honored Ones, endowed with the ten powers, the four fearlessnesses, roaring the supreme lion's roar, they too in the end abandon the body and attain parinirvāṇa. By such comparisons, Great King, you should know that all beings, all creatures, all spirits: those who are born always die. In the end they are worn away; there is none who does not die." At that time, the World-Honored One further spoke these verses:

> "All classes of living beings,
> having life, ultimately return to death.
> Each follows the course of their karma;
> the fruits of good and evil are self-received.
> Evil karma leads to hell;
> doing good, one ascends to heaven.
> Cultivating the supreme, wondrous path,
> with outflows exhausted, one enters parinirvāṇa.
> The Tathāgata, and the pratyekabuddhas,
> and the Buddha's śrāvaka disciples:
> all must abandon body and life.
> How much more so common worldlings!"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and approved, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (1228)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit, alone in a quiet place, meditated and reflected, having this thought: "How does one cherish oneself? How does one not cherish oneself?" He further thought: "If one practices evil conduct of body, evil conduct of speech, and evil conduct of mind, one should know that such a person does not cherish himself. If one practices good conduct of body, good conduct of speech, and good conduct of mind, one should know that such a person truly cherishes himself." Having risen from meditation, he went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to stand to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Alone in a quiet place, I was reflecting and had this thought: 'How does one cherish oneself? How does one not cherish oneself?' I further thought: 'If one practices evil conduct of body, evil conduct of speech, and evil conduct of mind, one should know that such a person does not cherish himself. If one practices good conduct of body, good conduct of speech, and good conduct of mind, one should know that such a person truly cherishes himself.'"

The Buddha told the great king: "So it is, Great King! So it is, Great King! If one practices evil conduct of body, evil conduct of speech, and evil conduct of mind, one should know that such a person does not cherish himself. Though he may claim, 'I cherish and care for myself,' in truth he does not cherish himself. Why? Because there is no one, even an evil friend, whose actions toward one he does not care for, toward one he does not love, whose actions could be like what one does to oneself. Therefore, such a person does not cherish himself.

"Furthermore, Great King, if one practices good conduct of body, good conduct of speech, and good conduct of mind, one should know that such a person truly cherishes himself. Such a person may claim, 'I do not love or care for myself,' yet in truth he does cherish himself. Why? Because there is no good friend who, toward a good friend, acts with care, acts with love, acts as though doing it for himself, whose actions could match what one does for oneself. Therefore, such a person truly cherishes himself." At that time, the World-Honored One further spoke these verses:

> "If one claims to cherish oneself,
> one should not commit evil deeds.
> Never through evil deeds
> does one obtain peace and happiness for oneself.
> If one claims to cherish oneself,
> one will never commit evil deeds.
> One who creates good karma
> brings peace and happiness to oneself.
> If one loves and cherishes oneself,
> one should guard and protect oneself well,
> as a king who guards his nation well
> defends the frontier cities from without.
> If one loves and cherishes oneself,
> one should treasure oneself most carefully,
> as a king who guards well
> defends the frontier cities from within.
> Thus treasuring oneself,
> without a single moment's gap:
> a moment's gap brings sorrow,
> and long suffering in the evil destinies."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and approved, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (1229)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit, alone in quiet reflection, had this thought: "How does one protect oneself? How does one not protect oneself?" He further thought: "If one practices evil conduct of body, evil conduct of speech, and evil conduct of mind, one should know that such a person does not protect himself. If one practices good conduct of body, good conduct of speech, and good conduct of mind, one should know that such a person truly protects himself." Having risen from meditation, he went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Alone in quiet reflection, I had this thought: 'How does one protect oneself? How does one not protect oneself?' I further thought: 'If one practices evil conduct of body, evil conduct of speech, and evil conduct of mind, one should know that such a person does not protect himself. If one practices good conduct of body, good conduct of speech, and good conduct of mind, one should know that such a person truly protects himself.'"

The Buddha told the great king: "So it is, Great King! So it is, Great King! If one practices evil conduct of body, evil conduct of speech, and evil conduct of mind, one should know that such a person does not protect himself. Though he may claim to be able to protect himself, with elephant armies, horse armies, chariot armies, and infantry to defend himself, though he claims self-protection, in truth he does not protect himself. Why? Because he guards only the exterior and does not guard the interior. Therefore, Great King, this is called not protecting oneself. Great King, if one practices good conduct of body, good conduct of speech, and good conduct of mind, one should know that such a person truly protects himself. Though he may not defend himself with the four armies of elephants, horses, chariots, and infantry, in truth he does protect himself. Why? Because guarding one's interior is called truly protecting oneself; it does not refer to guarding the exterior." At that time, the World-Honored One further spoke this verse:

> "Well guarding body and speech,
> and all deeds of the mind,
> with conscience and shame as one's defense:
> this is called being well guarded."

At that time, King Prasenajit, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and approved, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (1230)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit, alone in quiet reflection, had this thought: "Rare in the world are those who, having obtained excellent wealth and gain, can remain heedful, can refrain from greed and attachment, and can refrain from evil conduct toward beings. Most people in the world, having obtained excellent wealth and gain, become heedless, increase their greed and attachment, and engage in wrong conduct." Having thought this, he went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Alone in quiet reflection, I had this thought: 'Rare in the world are those who, having obtained excellent wealth, can remain free from heedlessness regarding wealth, free from greed and attachment, and free from wrong conduct. Most people in the world, having obtained excellent wealth, become heedless, develop greed and attachment, and engage in much wrong conduct.'"

The Buddha told King Prasenajit: "So it is, Great King! So it is, Great King! Rare in the world are those who, having obtained excellent wealth and gain, can remain free from greed and attachment, free from heedlessness, and free from wrong conduct. Most people in the world, having obtained excellent wealth and gain, become heedless regarding wealth, develop greed and attachment, and engage in wrong conduct. Great King, you should know that those worldly people who, having obtained excellent wealth and gain, become heedless regarding wealth, develop greed and attachment, and engage in wrong conduct are foolish people who, through the long night, will reap disadvantage and suffering. Great King! It is as if a hunter or a hunter's apprentice, in a wild and desolate forest, spreads nets and sets snares, killing many birds and beasts, causing suffering to living beings, with evil karma growing ever wider. Just so, those worldly people who, having obtained excellent wealth and gain, become heedless regarding wealth, develop greed and attachment, and engage in wrong conduct: it is just the same. They are foolish people who, through the long night, will reap disadvantage and suffering." At that time, the World-Honored One further spoke this verse:

> "Greedy for excellent wealth,
> intoxicated and deluded by greed,
> crazed and confused, unaware of themselves,
> like those who hunt and capture prey:
> because of such heedlessness,
> they shall receive great suffering as retribution."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and approved, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (1231)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit was in his main hall personally overseeing the adjudication of royal affairs. He saw eminent kṣatriya families, eminent brāhmaṇa families, and eminent elder families, who, because of greed and desire, committed deceitful and false speech. He then had this thought: "Let me cease this adjudication! Let me put an end to this adjudication! I shall no longer personally preside over the adjudication of affairs. I have a worthy son; I shall have him adjudicate. Why should I personally witness these eminent kṣatriya families, brāhmaṇa families, and elder families who, because of greed and desire, commit deceitful and false speech?"

At that time, King Prasenajit, having had this thought, went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! While in my hall personally adjudicating royal affairs, I saw eminent kṣatriya families, brāhmaṇa families, and elder families who, because of greed for gain, committed deceitful and false speech. World-Honored One! Seeing this, I had this thought: 'From today I shall cease this adjudication, put an end to this adjudication. I have a worthy son; I shall have him adjudicate. I shall not personally witness these eminent kṣatriya families, brāhmaṇa families, and elder families who, because of greed for gain, commit deceitful and false speech.'"

The Buddha told King Prasenajit: "So it is, Great King! So it is, Great King! Those eminent kṣatriya families, brāhmaṇa families, and elder families who, because of greed for gain, commit deceitful and false speech, those foolish people, through the long night, will reap disadvantage and suffering! Great King, you should know: it is as if a fisherman or a fisherman's apprentice, in rivers, streams, and valleys, blocks the flow and spreads nets, slaughtering living beings and causing them great suffering. Just so, Great King, those eminent kṣatriya families, brāhmaṇa families, and elder families who, because of greed for gain, commit deceitful and false speech, through the long night, will reap disadvantage and suffering!" At that time, the World-Honored One further spoke this verse:

> "Regarding wealth, giving rise to greed and desire,
> intoxicated and deluded by greed,
> crazed and confused, unaware of themselves,
> like those who fish and catch prey:
> because of such evil karma,
> they shall receive intense suffering as retribution."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and approved, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (1232)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! In this city of Śrāvastī there is an elder named Mahānāma. He possesses great wealth and enormous riches; his stored genuine gold amounts to hundreds of thousands of koṭis, not to mention his other wealth. World-Honored One! The elder Mahānāma, though so enormously wealthy, eats in this manner: he eats coarse, broken rice; he eats bean gruel; he eats rotten, spoiled ginger. He wears coarse cloth garments and single-layered leather sandals; he rides in a worn-out, broken-down chariot, and holds a canopy made of tree leaves. It has never been heard that he makes offerings or gives to śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas, that he provides relief to the poor and destitute, to travelers weary on the road, or to various beggars. He eats with his doors shut, lest śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, the poor, travelers, or beggars see him."

The Buddha told King Prasenajit: "This is not a true man. Having obtained excellent wealth and gain, he does not enjoy it himself, does not know how to support his parents, provide for his wife, children, relatives, and dependents, care for his servants, or give to friends and acquaintances. He does not know how to make timely offerings to śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, to plant excellent fields of merit, to direct himself toward the supreme destination, and to enjoy long-lasting peace and happiness and future rebirth in the heavens. Having obtained excellent wealth, he does not know how to use it broadly and reap great benefit. Great King! It is like a lake in the wilderness that collects water, but no one uses it for bathing or drinking; it simply evaporates and is consumed right there in the marsh. Just so, a person who is not a true man obtains excellent wealth but does not broadly enjoy it or reap great benefit, like that pool of water.

"Great King! There is a good man who obtains excellent wealth and gain. He enjoys it happily, supports his parents, provides for his wife, children, relatives, and dependents, cares for his servants, gives to friends and acquaintances, and from time to time makes offerings to śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, planting excellent fields of merit, directing himself toward the supreme destination, with future rebirth in the heavens. Having obtained excellent wealth, he is able to use it broadly and reap great benefit many times over. It is as if, Great King, near a village or city there were a pool of water, clear and pure, cool and refreshing, shaded by trees, a delight for people, enjoyed by many, even by birds and beasts. Just so, a good man who obtains excellent wealth enjoys it himself in comfort, supports his parents, and so forth, up to planting excellent fields of merit and broadly reaping great benefit." At that time, the World-Honored One further spoke these verses:

> "A lake in the wilderness,
> its water clear, cool, and pure,
> if none make use of it,
> it simply evaporates and is consumed.
> Just so, excellent wealth
> obtained by an unworthy man:
> he cannot enjoy it himself,
> nor does he provide relief to others.
> He only toils to accumulate,
> and having accumulated, he perishes.
> The wise one who obtains excellent wealth
> is able to enjoy it happily.
> Broadly giving and making merit,
> together with relatives and kin,
> he gives to each as is appropriate,
> like a bull king leading the herd.
> Giving, enjoying, and using,
> never failing in what is proper:
> following principle, when life comes to an end,
> one is born in heaven to receive blessed happiness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and approved, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (1233)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, in the country of Śrāvastī, the elder named Mahānāma had passed away without any sons or heirs. King Prasenajit, because the elder had no children and no relatives, took all his wealth into the royal treasury. King Prasenajit, day after day inspecting and tallying the wealth, his body covered with dust, came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One asked King Prasenajit: "Great King! Where have you come from? Your body is covered with dust, as if you are weary."

King Prasenajit addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The elder Mahānāma of this country has passed away. As he had no children, his wealth has all been taken into the royal treasury. I have been overseeing and managing it, which has caused me fatigue, and dust has covered my body. I have come from his residence."

The Buddha asked King Prasenajit: "Was the elder Mahānāma extremely wealthy with much property?"

King Prasenajit addressed the Buddha: "Extremely wealthy, World-Honored One! His money and possessions were immense: hundreds of thousands of koṭis of gold coins and treasures, not to mention his other wealth. World-Honored One! When the elder Mahānāma was alive, he ate coarse food and wore wretched clothes," as broadly stated above.

The Buddha told King Prasenajit: "In a past life, the elder Mahānāma once encountered the pratyekabuddha Tagaraśikhin and offered him one meal of food. But he did not give with a pure and faithful mind, he did not give respectfully, and he did not give with his own hand. After giving, he regretted it, saying: 'This food could have been used to feed my servants. For no reason I used it to give to a śramaṇa!' Because of the merit of that gift, for seven successive lives he was reborn among the Trāyastriṃśa gods, and for seven lives he was reborn in this city of Śrāvastī among the most eminent families with the greatest wealth. But because when he gave to the pratyekabuddha, he did not give with a pure and faithful mind, he did not give with his own hand, he did not give respectfully, and he regretted it afterward, therefore in every place of rebirth, though he obtained wealth, he still experienced only coarse clothes, coarse food, coarse and wretched bedding, dwellings, and conveyances. He never once enjoyed superior sights, sounds, scents, tastes, or tangible objects for his own comfort.

"Furthermore, Great King, at one time that elder Mahānāma killed his half-brother and took his wealth. Because of that sin, for hundreds of thousands of years he fell into hell. The remaining retribution of that sin caused him to be reborn in Śrāvastī for seven successive lives, always without children, and his wealth was confiscated by the royal treasury. Great King, the elder Mahānāma has now ended this life. His past merit from that offering is exhausted in this very existence. Because of his miserliness and greed, his heedlessness regarding wealth, and the transgressions he committed, now that this life has ended, he has fallen into hell to undergo extreme suffering."

King Prasenajit addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Has the elder Mahānāma, after his death, entered hell to undergo suffering?"

The Buddha said: "So it is, Great King! He has already entered hell."

At that time, King Prasenajit, thinking of him with sorrow and weeping, wiped his tears with his robe and spoke this verse:

> "Wealth, real gold, and treasures,
> elephants, horses, and adornments,
> servants and attendants,
> and all fields and estates:
> all are abandoned and left behind.
> Naked and alone, the spirit departs.
> The fortune of merit is exhausted,
> and one forever leaves the human body.
> What does he now possess?
> What does he carry with him as he goes?
> In what matter is he not abandoned,
> following him like a shadow follows a form?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke these verses in reply:

> "Only the karma of sin and merit,
> whatever a person has already done:
> this alone is truly one's own,
> and this one always carries along.
> In birth and death it is never abandoned,
> like a shadow following a form.
> Like a person with few provisions
> who travels far and encounters hardship,
> one who does not cultivate merit
> must surely pass through the suffering of evil destinies.
> Like a person with abundant provisions
> who travels far in peace and happiness,
> one who cultivates virtue purely and generously
> enjoys the good destinies and long happiness.
> Like a person who has traveled far,
> who after a long time returns safely home,
> and relatives and friends
> joyfully gather to welcome him:
> so one who has well cultivated merit,
> passing away from here and born in another world,
> is seen by relatives and kin,
> and their hearts rejoice.
> Therefore one should cultivate merit
> and accumulate it for the long term.
> Merit is able, for a person,
> to establish happiness in future worlds.
> Merit is praised by the devas,
> who equally cultivate right conduct.
> In the present life, one is not reviled by others,
> and at the end one is born in heaven."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and approved, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (1234)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit had arranged a great public festival. For the great festival, a thousand specially selected bulls were lined up and tied in rows, and all manner of offerings were assembled. From afar, all the various heterodox practitioners had come and gathered at the place of King Prasenajit's great festival.

At that time, a number of bhikṣus also, in the early morning, donned their robes, took up their bowls, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. They heard that King Prasenajit had arranged a great public festival, as broadly stated above, up to: and the various heterodox practitioners had all come and gathered. Having heard this, they completed their alms round and returned to the monastery, put away their robes and bowls, washed their feet, went to the Buddha, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Today we, a number of bhikṣus, in the early morning donned our robes, took up our bowls, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. We heard that King Prasenajit had arranged a great public festival," as broadly stated above, up to: and the various heterodox practitioners had gathered at the festival grounds.

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke these verses:

> "Month after month one may hold great festivals,
> even up to hundreds and thousands in number,
> but they are not worth one-sixteenth
> of right faith in the Buddha.
> Likewise, faith in the Dharma and Sangha,
> and compassion toward living beings:
> the merit of those great festivals
> is not worth one-sixteenth.
> If a person in this world
> for a hundred million years performs meritorious deeds,
> they are not worth one-fourth
> of a single prostration made with a straight mind."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1235)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit was angry with the people of his country and had many of them seized and imprisoned: whether kṣatriyas, brāhmaṇas, vaiśyas, śūdras, or caṇḍālas; whether they kept the precepts or violated the precepts; whether householders or renunciants, all were arrested. Some were shackled, some placed in stocks and cangues, some bound with ropes.

At that time, a number of bhikṣus, in the early morning, donned their robes, took up their bowls, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. They heard that King Prasenajit had made many arrests, even to the point of shackling and binding. Having completed their alms round, they returned to the monastery, put away their robes and bowls, washed their feet, went to the Buddha, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Today we, a number of bhikṣus, entered the city to beg for food and heard that King Prasenajit had made many arrests, even to the point of shackling and binding."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke these verses:

> "Not ropes, shackles, stocks, and cangues
> are called firm and strong bonds.
> A defiled mind brooding over
> money, treasures, and wife and children:
> these bonds are long and strong.
> Though they seem loose, they are hard to escape.
> The wise do not brood
> over worldly five-sense pleasures.
> This is to sever all bonds
> and peacefully, forever transcend the world."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1236)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit and King Ajātaśatru Vaidehīputra of Magadha were in conflict with each other. King Ajātaśatru Vaidehīputra of Magadha raised four kinds of armies: elephant army, horse army, chariot army, and infantry, and came to the country of Kośala. King Prasenajit, hearing that King Ajātaśatru Vaidehīputra had arrived with four kinds of armies, also assembled four kinds of armies: elephant army, horse army, chariot army, and infantry, and went out to battle. King Ajātaśatru's four armies were victorious, and King Prasenajit's four armies were defeated. Routed and scattered, he fled alone in a single chariot, racing back to the city of Śrāvastī.

At that time, a number of bhikṣus, in the early morning, donned their robes, took up their bowls, and entered the city of Śrāvastī to beg for food. They heard that King Ajātaśatru Vaidehīputra of Magadha had raised four kinds of armies and come to the country of Kośala; that King Prasenajit had raised four kinds of armies and gone out to battle; that King Prasenajit's four armies had been defeated; that the forces were routed and scattered; and that King Prasenajit, in fear and distress, had fled alone in a single chariot, racing back to the city of Śrāvastī. Having heard this, they completed their alms round and returned to the monastery, put away their robes and bowls, washed their feet, went to the Buddha, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Today we, a number of bhikṣus, entered the city to beg for food and heard that King Ajātaśatru Vaidehīputra of Magadha had raised four kinds of armies," and so forth as broadly stated, up to "fled alone in a single chariot, racing back to the city of Śrāvastī."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke these verses:

> "Victory in battle breeds enmity;
> defeat brings suffering, lying sleepless.
> Abandoning both victory and defeat,
> one lies down and wakes in tranquil happiness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1237)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit and King Ajātaśatru Vaidehīputra of Magadha were in conflict with each other. King Ajātaśatru Vaidehīputra of Magadha raised four kinds of armies and came to the country of Kośala. King Prasenajit raised an even larger force of four armies and went out to battle.

King Prasenajit's four kinds of armies were victorious, and King Ajātaśatru's four kinds of armies were defeated, crushed, and scattered. King Prasenajit captured all of King Ajātaśatru's elephants, horses, chariots, money, and treasures. He captured King Ajātaśatru alive and placed him in the same chariot. Together they went to the Buddha, and he bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit to one side. King Prasenajit addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! This is King Ajātaśatru Vaidehīputra. Through the long night, he has harbored enmity against me, one who bears him no hatred. He has acted badly toward one who has been good to him. Yet he is the son of my good friend, so I shall release him and let him return to his country."

The Buddha told King Prasenajit: "Excellent, Great King! Release him and let him go, and through the long night you shall enjoy peace and benefit." At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke this verse:

> "When one has the power and sovereignty
> to widely capture and plunder the other,
> yet assists one's former enemy when his strength grows again,
> one reaps many times the benefit for oneself and others."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit and King Ajātaśatru Vaidehīputra, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and approved, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (1238)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit, alone in quiet reflection, had this thought: "The World-Honored One's true Dharma is visible in this very life, free from all burning afflictions, not dependent on time, penetrating, directly visible, and self-realized. This Dharma is that of a good friend, a good companion, not an evil friend, not an evil companion." Having had this thought, he went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Alone in quiet reflection, I had this thought: 'The World-Honored One's true Dharma is visible in this very life, free from all burning afflictions, not dependent on time, penetrating, directly visible, and self-realized. It is that of a good friend, a good companion, not an evil friend, not an evil companion.'"

The Buddha told King Prasenajit: "So it is, Great King! So it is, Great King! The World-Honored One's true Dharma and Vinaya are visible in this very life, free from all burning afflictions, not dependent on time, penetrating, directly visible, and self-realized. They are of a good friend, a good companion, not an evil friend, not an evil companion. Why? I am a good friend to all beings. Beings who are subject to birth are liberated from birth. Beings who are subject to old age, illness, death, grief, sorrow, distress, and suffering are all liberated from these.

"Great King! Once I was dwelling at Rājagṛha, in the Mountain Valley Monastery. At that time, the bhikṣu Ānanda, alone in quiet reflection, had this thought: 'Half of the holy life consists of good friendship, good companionship, not evil friendship, not evil companionship.' Having had this thought, he came to me, bowed his head at my feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and addressed me: 'World-Honored One! Alone in quiet reflection, I had this thought: "Half of the holy life consists of good friendship, good companionship, not evil friendship, not evil companionship."' At that time I told him: 'Ānanda! Do not say this, that half of the holy life consists of good friendship, good companionship, not evil friendship, not evil companionship. Why? The complete fullness and pure perfection of the holy life is good friendship, good companionship, not evil friendship, not evil companionship. Why? I am always a good friend to all beings. All those beings who are subject to birth, you should know that the World-Honored One's true Dharma, visible in this very life, liberates them from birth. Those subject to old age, illness, death, grief, sorrow, distress, and suffering are freed from all burning afflictions, not dependent on time, presently liberated from distress and suffering, seen, penetrated, and self-realized. This is good friendship, good companionship, not evil friendship, not evil companionship.'" At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke this verse:

> "Praising heedfulness:
> this is the Buddha's right teaching.
> Cultivating meditation with heedfulness,
> one attains and realizes the end of all outflows."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and approved, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (1239)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit, alone in quiet reflection, had this thought: "Is there a single dharma which, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, fulfills wishes in the present life, fulfills wishes in future lives, and fulfills wishes in both the present life and future lives?" Having had this thought, he went to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Alone in quiet reflection, I had this thought: 'Is there a single dharma which, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, fulfills wishes in the present life, fulfills wishes in future lives, and fulfills wishes in both the present life and future lives?'"

The Buddha told King Prasenajit: "So it is, Great King! So it is, Great King! There is a single dharma which, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, fulfills wishes in the present life, fulfills wishes in future lives, and fulfills wishes in both the present life and future lives. It is heedfulness in wholesome dharmas. Heedfulness in wholesome dharmas, when cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, fulfills wishes in the present life, fulfills wishes in future lives, and fulfills wishes in both the present life and future lives.

"Great King! Just as all the coarse tasks performed in the world are all established upon the ground, so too heedfulness in wholesome dharmas is just like this. When cultivated and repeatedly cultivated, it fulfills wishes in the present life, fulfills wishes in future lives, and fulfills wishes in both the present life and future lives. As with strength, so too with seeds, roots, solidity, land, water, feet, travel, the lion, and dwelling: it should be explained in the same way. Therefore, Great King, you should abide in heedfulness and rely on heedfulness. Having abided in heedfulness and relied on heedfulness, the queen should then have this thought: 'The great king abides in heedfulness and relies on heedfulness; I too should now likewise abide in heedfulness and rely on heedfulness.' Like the queen, so too the great ministers, the crown prince, and the generals should do likewise. The people of the country should think: 'The great king abides in heedfulness and relies on heedfulness; the queen, crown prince, great ministers, and generals abide in heedfulness and rely on heedfulness; we too should likewise abide in heedfulness and rely on heedfulness.' Great King! If one abides in heedfulness and relies on heedfulness, then one can protect oneself. The queen and court ladies can also keep themselves safe, and the treasuries and storehouses will increase and overflow with abundance." At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke these verses:

> "Praised is heedfulness;
> heedlessness is censured.
> Śakra, through heedfulness,
> was able to become lord of the Trāyastriṃśa Heaven.
> Praised is heedfulness;
> heedlessness is censured.
> Heedfulness, when complete,
> encompasses both aims:
> first, benefit in the present life,
> and second, likewise in the future.
> This is called the penetration of truth;
> such is one of deep wisdom."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and approved, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (1240)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, King Prasenajit, alone in quiet reflection, had this thought: "There are three things that are unwelcome to everyone in the world. What are the three? They are old age, illness, and death. These three things are unwelcome to everyone in the world. If there were not these three things unwelcome to the world, Buddhas, World-Honored Ones, would not appear in the world, and the world would not know that there exist the dharmas that the Buddhas and Tathāgatas have realized, which they broadly teach to others. Because there are these three things unwelcome to the world, namely old age, illness, and death, the Buddhas and Tathāgatas appear in the world, and the world comes to know that there exist the dharmas that the Buddhas and Tathāgatas have realized, which are broadly proclaimed and taught." Having had this thought, King Prasenajit came to the Buddha, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and broadly related his reflections to the World-Honored One.

The Buddha told King Prasenajit: "So it is, Great King! So it is, Great King! There are these three things that the world finds unwelcome, namely old age, illness, and death, and so forth, up to: the world comes to know that there exist the dharmas that the Tathāgata has realized, which he broadly teaches to others." At that time, the World-Honored One further spoke these verses:

> "The jeweled chariot that a king rides
> will ultimately decay and fall apart.
> This body is likewise:
> it shifts and changes, inevitably growing old.
> Only the Tathāgata's true Dharma
> has no aspect of decline or aging.
> Those who partake of this true Dharma
> forever reach the place of peace and safety.
> But ordinary, lowly decline and aging,
> ugly, wretched, and disfigured,
> when decline and aging come trampling upon one,
> delude and bewitch the minds of fools.
> If a person lives a hundred years,
> always fearing death's approach,
> old age and illness compete to pursue,
> and seize any chance to do harm."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, King Prasenajit, hearing what the Buddha had said, rejoiced and approved, paid respects, and departed.

# 雜阿含經

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from the Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.
