# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Forty-Seven

---

### (1241)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the householder Anāthapiṇḍada came to the Buddha's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and sat to one side, and addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One! All those who stay in my household attain pure faith. And all those who die while staying in my household are reborn in heaven."

The Buddha said: "Excellent! Excellent, householder! This is a profound and wondrous statement. This is a one-sided claim. In the midst of a great assembly, you make a lion's roar, saying: 'Those who stay in my household all attain pure faith, and when they die, they are all reborn in heaven.' Was there some bhikṣu of great virtue and spiritual power who told you: 'All those who die in your household are reborn in heaven'?"

The householder addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One."

He further asked: "How about this? Was it a bhikṣuṇī, or devas, or did you hear it spoken directly from me?"

The householder addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One."

"How about this, householder? Do you, through your own direct knowledge and vision, know that 'those who die in my household are all reborn in heaven'?"

The householder addressed the Buddha: "No, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the householder: "Since you have neither heard it from a bhikṣu of great virtue and spiritual power, nor from a bhikṣuṇī, nor from devas, nor heard it spoken directly from me, and since you do not know through your own direct knowledge and vision that 'all those who die in my household are reborn in heaven,' how then can you make such a profound and wondrous statement, make such a one-sided claim, and make a lion's roar in the midst of a great assembly, saying: 'Those who die in my household are all reborn in heaven'?"

The householder addressed the Buddha: "No bhikṣu of great virtue and spiritual power came to tell me," and so forth as stated extensively above, up to "all are reborn in heaven. However, World-Honored One, whenever a servant woman in my household becomes pregnant, I immediately instruct her, and for the sake of her child, I teach taking refuge in the Buddha, taking refuge in the Dharma, and taking refuge in the Bhikṣu Sangha. When the child is born, I again instruct in the three refuges. When the child grows to the age of knowing and understanding, I further instruct in the observance of the precepts. If there are servant women, low-ranking people, or hired workers who become pregnant or give birth, I likewise instruct them. If someone wishes to sell a male or female servant, I always go to them and say: 'Good person, I wish to buy someone. You should take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, and take refuge in the Bhikṣu Sangha, and accept and uphold the precepts.' If they follow my instruction, I then confer the five precepts and afterward purchase them at the agreed price. If they do not follow my instruction, I do not take them. If there are guests who come to stay, or hired laborers, I also first require them to accept the three refuges and five precepts, and only then receive them. If someone comes seeking to be a disciple, or if someone comes seeking a loan with interest, I always require the three refuges and five precepts, and only then accept them. Furthermore, when in my household we make offerings to the Buddha and the Bhikṣu Sangha, I recite the names of my father and mother, brothers, wife and children, clansmen and acquaintances, kings and ministers, devas, nāgas, and spirits, whether living or deceased, śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas, inner and outer relatives, down to servants, and I recite all their names and make aspirational blessings for them. Also, I have heard from the World-Honored One that through the causes and conditions of reciting names and making aspirational blessings, all are reborn in heaven. Or through the giving of gardens and fields, or through the giving of buildings, or through the giving of beds and bedding, or through constant giving, or through giving to travelers, down to giving even a single morsel of food to sentient beings: through all these causes and conditions, they are reborn in heaven."

The Buddha said: "Excellent! Excellent, householder! It is through your faith that you are able to make such a statement. The Tathāgata, with regard to that, possesses unsurpassed knowledge and vision. He truly knows that those who die in your household are all reborn in heaven."

At that time, the householder Anāthapiṇḍada, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and approved, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (1242)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should dwell with reverence, always with a controlled mind, always with fearful caution, deferring to the authority of fellow practitioners of the holy life, whether of senior, middle, or junior rank. Why is this? If a bhikṣu does not dwell with reverence, does not control the mind, does not maintain fearful caution, and does not defer to the authority of fellow practitioners of the holy life, whether of senior, middle, or junior rank, yet wishes to have his deportment complete, there is no such possibility. Without complete deportment, to wish for fulfillment in the training rules, there is no such possibility. Without fulfillment in the training rules, to wish for the aggregate of morality, the aggregate of concentration, the aggregate of wisdom, the aggregate of liberation, and the aggregate of the knowledge and vision of liberation to be complete, there is no such possibility. Without the knowledge and vision of liberation being complete, to wish to attain nirvāṇa without remainder, there is no such possibility.

"Thus, bhikṣus, if you diligently maintain reverence, a controlled mind, and fearful caution, deferring to the virtue and power of fellow practitioners of the holy life, whether of senior, middle, or junior rank, then for deportment to be complete, this is possible. When deportment is complete, for the training rules to be complete, this is possible. When the training rules are complete, for the aggregate of morality, the aggregate of concentration, the aggregate of wisdom, the aggregate of liberation, and the aggregate of the knowledge and vision of liberation to be complete, this is possible. When the aggregate of the knowledge and vision of liberation is complete, to attain nirvāṇa without remainder, this is possible. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should diligently maintain reverence, a controlled mind, and fearful caution, deferring to the virtue and power of fellow practitioners of the holy life, whether of senior, middle, or junior rank. From the completeness of deportment up to nirvāṇa without remainder, you should train in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1243)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There are two pure qualities that protect the world. What are the two? They are called moral shame and moral dread. If the world lacked these two pure qualities, the world would not know that there are fathers and mothers, brothers, sisters, wives and children, clansmen, teachers, or the order of senior and junior. Things would be confused and disordered, like the realm of animals. Because there are these two pure qualities, namely moral shame and moral dread, the world knows that there are fathers and mothers, and so forth up to teachers and the order of senior and junior, and it is not confused and disordered like the realm of animals."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke in verse:

> "If the world were without
> the two qualities of shame and dread,
> it would transgress the pure path
> and head toward birth, old age, sickness, and death.
> If the world accomplishes
> the two qualities of shame and dread,
> it increases the pure path
> and forever closes the gate of birth and death."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1244)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "There is the burning dharma and the non-burning dharma. Listen carefully and reflect well; I shall explain it to you.

"What is the burning dharma? If a man or woman transgresses the precepts and practices evil, unwholesome dharmas, accomplishing evil bodily conduct, accomplishing evil verbal and mental conduct, then when at a later time they fall gravely ill, suffering intensely, sinking and prostrate on their sickbed, enduring bitter pains: at that time, all the evil they previously committed comes back to their memory. Just as a great mountain, when the sun is in the west, is covered by shadow, so too with these beings: all the evil they previously committed through body, speech, and mind, all the unwholesome dharmas, appear vividly at the time of death. Their mind then pursues regret: 'Alas! Alas! Previously I did not cultivate good but only practiced all manner of evil. I shall surely fall into evil destinies and suffer bitter pains.' Having recalled this, their mind is set ablaze with burning, their mind is filled with remorseful change. Having felt remorse, they cannot attain a wholesome state of mind, and at the end of their life, in the next world, an unwholesome state of mind continues to arise. This is called the burning dharma.

"What is the non-burning dharma? If a man or woman upholds the pure precepts and cultivates the true Dharma, accomplishing wholesome bodily actions, accomplishing wholesome verbal and mental actions, then when at the end of their lifespan they are afflicted by bodily suffering, sinking and prostrate on their sickbed, with various pains pressing upon the body: their mind recalls the wholesome dharmas they previously cultivated, their wholesome bodily conduct, and their accomplishment of wholesome verbal and mental conduct. At that time, they grasp onto the wholesome dharmas: 'I have performed such wholesome deeds of body, speech, and mind, and have not committed various evils. I shall be reborn in a good destiny and shall not fall into an evil destiny.' Their mind does not undergo remorseful change. Because it does not undergo remorseful change, with a wholesome mind they end their life, and in the next world, goodness continues. This is called the non-burning dharma."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke in verse:

> "Having planted burning karma,
> living dependent on what is unwholesome,
> riding upon these evil deeds,
> one will surely be born in a hell.
> Saṃjīva and Kālasūtra,
> Saṃghāta, and the two Raurava,
> Tapana and Pratāpana,
> Avīci, the great hells.
> These eight great hells
> are of extreme suffering, hard to pass through.
> Because of various kinds of evil karma,
> each has sixteen separate places.
> Four walls surround with four gates,
> the inner measurements all equal.
> Iron forms the four enclosing walls,
> the four gate-doors are also iron.
> The iron ground blazes with fire,
> its flames pervade everywhere.
> A hundred yojanas in length and breadth,
> flames blaze without cessation.
> These are for taming the unrighteous,
> punishing the strong and violent.
> Through the long night, torments are inflicted,
> and their suffering is hard to behold.
> Those who see it are struck with fear,
> trembling, their body hair standing on end.
> When falling into that hell,
> feet above and head downward.
> Those who oppose the holy, gentle-minded ones,
> who practice the holy life,
> who with contemptuous mind toward such noble ones
> commit unrighteous acts,
> and who kill and harm sentient beings,
> fall into these burning hells,
> writhing in the fire,
> like a fish roasted over flames.
> Crying out in anguished wailing,
> like the sound of a herd of warring elephants.
> The great fire arises of itself;
> this is due to one's own karma."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1245)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "The abandoning of evil bodily conduct is possible because one can attain the cessation of evil bodily conduct. If it were not possible to attain the cessation of evil bodily conduct, I would not speak of abandoning evil bodily conduct. Because one can attain the cessation of evil bodily conduct, therefore I speak of abandoning evil bodily conduct. Evil bodily conduct does not bring benefit, welfare, or happiness to beings. By separating from evil bodily conduct, one gains benefit, welfare, and happiness. Therefore I speak of abandoning evil bodily conduct. Evil verbal and mental conduct are also spoken of in the same way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1246)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the city of Rājagṛha, at the goldsmith's residence.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Just as a gold smelter gathers sand and soil and places it in a trough, then washes it with water: the coarse, gross defilements, hard stones, and solid clumps are carried away by the water, yet there still remains coarse sand entangled with it. He washes again with water, and the coarse sand flows out with the water. After that, the raw gold still has fine sand and dark earth entangled with it. He washes again with water, and the fine sand and dark earth flow out with the water. Then the true gold is pure and without impurities, yet there are still gold-like subtle impurities. The goldsmith then places it in a furnace, adds fire and works the bellows, melting it so that all impurities are removed. Yet the raw gold is still not light, not soft, does not emit brightness, and when bent it breaks. The goldsmith or goldsmith's apprentice places it in the furnace again, adds fire and works the bellows, turning and refining it. After that, the raw gold becomes light, soft, lustrous, and when bent it does not break; it can be fashioned at will into hairpins, ear ornaments, rings, bracelets, and various adornments.

"In the same way, a bhikṣu with a purified mind who advances diligently gradually cuts off and eliminates the coarse afflictive fetters, the evil unwholesome karmas, and the various evil wrong views, just as that raw gold is washed clean of hard stones and solid clumps.

"Furthermore, the bhikṣu with a purified mind who advances diligently removes the next coarser defilements: thoughts of sensual desire, thoughts of ill-will, and thoughts of harming, just as that raw gold has its coarse sand and gravel removed.

"Furthermore, the bhikṣu with a purified mind who advances diligently next removes the subtle defilements: thoughts of relatives, thoughts of social gatherings, and thoughts of heavenly rebirth, contemplating and eliminating them, just as that raw gold has its dust, fine sand, and dark earth removed.

"Furthermore, the bhikṣu with a purified mind who advances diligently has wholesome dharma thoughts, which he contemplates and eliminates, making the mind pure, just as the raw gold has removed the impurities that resemble gold in color, making it purely clean.

"Furthermore, a bhikṣu who, regarding the various samādhis, is held by conditioned activities, is like a pond of water held on all sides by its banks. Being held by dharmas, he cannot attain the quiescent and sublime, cannot attain the bliss of peace, and cannot exhaust all the outflows. This is like the goldsmith or goldsmith's apprentice who refines the raw gold and removes its impurities, yet it is still not light, not soft, does not emit brightness, and when bent it breaks, and cannot be fashioned at will into ornaments.

"Furthermore, when a bhikṣu attains the various samādhis, he is no longer held by conditioned activities. He attains the quiescent and sublime, attains the path of the bliss of peace, with one-pointed mind, and exhausts all the outflows. This is like the goldsmith or goldsmith's apprentice who refines the raw gold, making it light, soft, unbreakable, and lustrous, bending and stretching it at will.

"Furthermore, a bhikṣu separates from all thinking and deliberation, and so forth, up to attaining the second, third, and fourth dhyānas. With such right concentration, purely one, clean and pure, free from all afflictions, soft, genuine, and unmoving: for whatever sphere of mastery he wishes to realize and attain, he is able to realize and attain them all. This is like the goldsmith who refines the raw gold to the utmost lightness, softness, and luster, without breaking, and can fashion it into whatever vessel he desires. In the same way, a bhikṣu in right concentration of samādhi, up to being able to realize and attain all the spheres of mastery."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1247)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the city of Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "You should concentrate your mind with skillful means, and from time to time contemplate the three signs. What are the three? From time to time contemplate the sign of calming, from time to time contemplate the sign of arousing, and from time to time contemplate the sign of equanimity. If a bhikṣu one-sidedly contemplates only the sign of calming, then his mind becomes sluggish in that state. If he one-sidedly contemplates only the sign of arousing, then in that state restlessness and agitation of mind arise. If he one-sidedly contemplates only the sign of equanimity, then in that state he cannot attain right concentration and exhaust all the outflows.

"Because that bhikṣu from time to time contemplates the sign of calming, from time to time contemplates the sign of arousing, and from time to time contemplates the sign of equanimity, his mind then attains right concentration and exhausts all the outflows. Just as a skilled goldsmith or goldsmith's apprentice places raw gold in a furnace with added fire, and from time to time works the bellows, from time to time sprinkles water, and from time to time does both and rests. If he one-sidedly works the bellows, the raw gold would be burned to nothing in that state. If he one-sidedly sprinkles water, the raw gold would become hard and rigid in that state. If he one-sidedly does both and rests, the raw gold in that state would not be properly tempered and would be of no use. Therefore, a skilled goldsmith or goldsmith's apprentice, with regard to that raw gold, from time to time works the bellows, from time to time sprinkles water, and from time to time does both and rests. In this way the raw gold becomes properly and evenly tempered, suitable for whatever purpose. In the same way, a bhikṣu, with concentrated mind and skillful means, from time to time contemplates and recollects the three signs, up to the exhaustion of the outflows."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1248)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the city of Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In a past age, a cattle herder of the Magadha country was foolish and without wisdom. At the end of summer and the beginning of autumn, he did not skillfully examine this bank of the Ganges, nor did he skillfully examine the far bank of the Ganges. He drove the herd of cattle down a steep bank and up a steep bank, through whirlpools in between, and many difficulties arose. Bhikṣus, in a past age, there was a cattle herder of the Magadha country who was not foolish and not lacking in wisdom, who had skillful means. At the end of summer and the beginning of autumn, he was able to skillfully examine this bank of the Ganges and also skillfully examine the far bank of the Ganges. He skillfully led his cattle across to flat, broad mountain valleys with good water and grass. When first crossing, he first sent across the great bull that could lead the herd, cutting through the swift current. Next he drove across the second group of strong but younger cattle, who followed behind and crossed. Then the third group, the weak and small ones, were driven along the lower current, and all crossed safely in order. The newborn calves, attached to their mothers, also followed behind them and reached the far bank.

"In this way, bhikṣus, I speak this simile. You should understand its meaning. That Magadha cattle herder who was foolish and without wisdom represents the six teachers such as Pūraṇa and the rest, who likewise practice wrong views and head toward wrong paths. Like that cattle herder who was foolish and without wisdom, who at the end of summer and the beginning of autumn did not skillfully examine this bank or the far bank, going down steep cliffs and up steep cliffs, through whirlpools in between, causing many difficulties: so too the six teachers such as Pūraṇa and the rest, foolish and without wisdom, did not examine this bank, meaning this world, and did not examine the far bank, meaning the other world. Through whirlpools in between, meaning the domain of Māra, they encountered suffering and difficulty themselves. Those who follow their views and train in their teachings also encounter suffering and difficulty.

"That Magadha skillful cattle herder who was not foolish and not lacking in wisdom, who had skillful means, represents the Tathāgata, the Arhat, the Samyak-sambuddha. Just as the cattle herder skillfully examined this bank and the far bank and skillfully led his cattle across to flat, broad mountain valleys, first sending across the great bull that could lead the herd, cutting across the swift current, safely reaching the far bank: so too my śrāvakas who can exhaust all the outflows, and so forth, up to knowing for themselves 'there is no further rebirth,' cut across the current of Māra's worldly craving and safely cross to the far bank of birth and death.

"Just as the Magadha skillful cattle herder next sends across the second group of strong but younger cattle, cutting across the current and crossing: so too my śrāvakas who sever the five lower fetters and attain anāgāmin, being reborn there without returning to this world, also cut across the current of Māra's craving and safely cross to the far bank of birth and death.

"Just as the Magadha skillful cattle herder drives the third group of weak and small cattle along the lower current to safely cross: so too my śrāvakas who sever the three fetters, with greed, hatred, and delusion weakened, attain sakṛdāgāmin, returning once more to this world to reach the final end of suffering, cutting across the current of Māra's craving and safely crossing to the far bank of birth and death.

"Just as the Magadha skillful cattle herder's newborn calves, attached to their mothers, also follow them and cross: so too my śrāvakas who sever the three fetters and attain srotāpanna, not falling into evil destinies, are assured of proceeding toward correct enlightenment, and after at most seven lives among devas and humans, reach the final end of suffering, cutting across the current of Māra's craving and safely crossing to the far bank of birth and death."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke in verse:

> "This world and the other world
> are clearly and skillfully revealed by the wise one.
> All that Māra has obtained or not obtained,
> even including the realm of Death Māra,
> all is fully known
> by the wisdom of the Samyak-sambuddha.
> Cutting across the current of Māra,
> he destroys and causes it to perish.
> He opens the gate of ambrosia
> and reveals the true and right path.
> His mind is always filled with great joy,
> having attained a place of peace and security."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1249)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "If a cattle herder possesses eleven qualities, he is unable to make his cattle increase, nor is he able to protect and oversee a great herd of cattle and give them equal comfort and happiness. What are the eleven? He does not know their color, does not know their distinguishing marks, does not remove parasites, does not cover their wounds, does not make smoke, does not know how to choose a road, does not know how to choose a place, does not know the fording place, does not know the grazing place, milks them dry, and does not properly care for the lead bull. These are the eleven qualities that, when possessed, prevent him from guarding and overseeing a great herd of cattle.

"In the same way, a bhikṣu who possesses eleven qualities is unable to bring peace to himself, nor to bring peace to others. What are the eleven? He does not know form, does not know the distinguishing marks, is unable to remove harmful parasites, does not cover wounds, is unable to make smoke, does not know the right road, does not know the resting place, does not know the fording place, does not know the grazing place, milks dry, and does not, toward senior elders who are learned, long-practiced in the holy life, and praised by the great teacher, acclaim and praise their virtue before the wise practitioners of the holy life so as to cause all to revere, serve, and make offerings to them.

"What does it mean to 'not know form'? All forms whatsoever are the four great elements and that which is created by the four great elements. This is called form, and one does not know it as it really is.

"What does it mean to 'not know the distinguishing marks'? Action is the sign of the transgression; action is the sign of wisdom. Not knowing this as it really is is called not knowing the distinguishing marks.

"What does it mean to 'not know how to remove parasites'? When a thought of sensual desire arises, one tolerates it, does not abandon it, does not become aware of it, and does not eliminate it. When thoughts of ill-will and thoughts of harming arise, one tolerates them, does not abandon them, does not become aware of them, and does not eliminate them. This is called not removing parasites.

"What does it mean to 'not cover wounds'? When the eye sees form, one grasps at its features and does not guard the eye faculty. Worldly craving, sorrow, evil, and unwholesome dharmas follow and arise, causing outflows, and one is unable to prevent them. The ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind faculties are likewise. This is called not covering wounds.

"What does it mean to 'not make smoke'? When one has heard and received the Dharma, one is unable to distinguish and teach it clearly to others. This is called not making smoke.

"What does it mean to 'not know the right road'? The Noble Eightfold Path and the noble Dharma-Vinaya are called the road. Not knowing it as it really is is called not knowing the road.

"What does it mean to 'not know the resting place'? When in the Dharma known by the Tathāgata, one does not attain joy, delight, the sublime, liberation, or benefit, this is called not knowing the resting place.

"What does it mean to 'not know the fording place'? When one does not know the sūtras, the Vinaya, and the Abhidharma, and does not go at the proper time to those places to inquire and request instruction, asking: 'What is wholesome? What is unwholesome? What is with fault? What is without fault? What dharmas are practiced that are excellent and not evil?' When one is unable to disclose what is hidden and cannot extensively inquire into what is openly taught, and when regarding profound sentences and meanings that one has come to know oneself, one is unable to broadly proclaim and make them known: this is called not knowing the fording place.

"What does it mean to 'not know the grazing place'? The four foundations of mindfulness and the noble Dharma-Vinaya are called the grazing place. Not knowing this as it really is is called not knowing the grazing place.

"What does it mean to 'milk them dry'? When kṣatriyas, brāhmaṇas, and householders generously give robes, food and drink, bedding, medicines, and the necessities of life, and the bhikṣu who receives them does not know moderation: this is called milking dry.

"What does it mean to not acclaim the virtue of senior elders of great learning and long practice, and so forth, up to not enabling the wise practitioners of the holy life to revere, serve, and make offerings to them so that they may be pleased? It means a bhikṣu does not acclaim those senior elders, up to not enabling the wise practitioners of the holy life to go and visit them, to serve them with appropriate bodily, verbal, and mental conduct, attending and serving them. This is called not, toward senior elders of great learning and long practice, and so forth, enabling the wise practitioners of the holy life to go and visit them, attend and serve them, and bring them happiness.

"That cattle herder who possesses the eleven qualities is able to make the herd of cattle increase, to guard and oversee the herd, and to give them happiness. What are the eleven? He knows their color, knows their distinguishing marks, and so forth as explained in the pure division above, up to properly caring for the lead bull at the right time, giving it comfort and happiness. These are the eleven accomplishments of the cattle herder that enable the herd of cattle to increase, that guard and oversee them and give them happiness. In the same way, a bhikṣu who possesses the eleven qualities is able to bring peace and happiness to himself and also to bring peace to others. What are the eleven? He knows form, knows the distinguishing marks, up to the eleven," as extensively explained in the pure division, "These are the eleven accomplishments of the bhikṣu that bring peace to himself and peace to others."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1250)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kośala and arrived at the Icchānaṅgala village, staying in the Icchānaṅgala grove.

At that time, there was a venerable named Nādika who was a long-term resident of the Icchānaṅgala village. The śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas of the Icchānaṅgala village heard that the śramaṇa Gautama was traveling among the people of Kośala and had arrived at the Icchānaṅgala village, staying in the Icchānaṅgala grove. Having heard this, each prepared a pot of food, placed it at the entrance, and made this thought: "I shall offer to the World-Honored One first! I shall offer to the Sugata first!" Each called out loudly and in great voices, proclaiming this.

At that time, the World-Honored One heard the many voices of the great crowd calling out loudly in the grove, and he addressed Venerable Nādika: "What is the cause and condition for the many people calling out in loud, great voices in the grove?"

Venerable Nādika addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, the kṣatriyas, brāhmaṇas, and householders of this Icchānaṅgala village, having heard that the World-Honored One is staying in this grove, have each prepared a pot of food and placed it in the grove. Each proclaims: 'I shall offer to the World-Honored One first! I shall offer to the Sugata first!' For this reason, there are many people calling out in loud, great voices in this grove. May the World-Honored One please accept their food."

The Buddha told Nādika: "Do not seek gain for me; I do not seek gain. Do not seek fame for me; I do not seek fame. Nādika, if from the Tathāgata one can thus obtain the happiness of renunciation, seclusion, cessation, and perfect awakening, then why would one, in this or that instance, seek the pleasure that arises from gain? Nādika, it is only I who can obtain this kind of happiness of renunciation, seclusion, cessation, and perfect awakening, obtaining it without seeking, obtaining it without suffering. Why then, in this or that instance, would one seek the pleasure that arises from gain? Nādika, you and others, because you cannot obtain this kind of happiness of renunciation, seclusion, cessation, and perfect awakening, cannot obtain the happiness that comes without seeking, the happiness that comes without suffering. Nādika, even devas cannot obtain this kind of happiness of renunciation, seclusion, cessation, and perfect awakening, the happiness without seeking, the happiness without suffering. It is only I who obtains this kind of happiness of renunciation, seclusion, cessation, and perfect awakening, the happiness without seeking, the happiness without suffering. Why then, in this or that instance, would one seek the pleasure that arises from gain?"

Nādika addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, I now wish to speak by way of simile."

The Buddha told Nādika: "You may speak when you see fit."

Nādika addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, just as when the heavens rain, the water flows and runs downward. Wherever the World-Honored One stays, in that place the kṣatriyas, brāhmaṇas, and householders have faith, reverence, and serve him, because the World-Honored One's morality and virtue are pure, and his right view is true and straight. Therefore, I now speak in this way. May the World-Honored One compassionately accept their invitation."

The Buddha told Nādika: "Do not seek gain for me; I do not seek gain. And so forth, up to: why then, in this or that instance, would one seek the pleasure that arises from gain? Nādika, I have seen a bhikṣu who, after eating fine food, lies on his back, sighing and breathing heavily. Seeing this, I thought: 'This elder has not obtained the happiness of renunciation, seclusion, cessation, and perfect awakening, the happiness without seeking, the happiness without suffering.' Furthermore, Nādika, I have seen these two bhikṣus who, after eating fine food, with full bellies, gasping for breath, walk about languidly. I made this thought: 'Those elders are not able to obtain the happiness of renunciation, seclusion, cessation, and perfect awakening, the happiness without seeking, the happiness without suffering.'

"Nādika, I have seen many bhikṣus who, after eating fine food, go from garden to garden, from room to room, from person to person, from group to group. Seeing this, I made this thought: 'Those elders are not able, in this way, to obtain the happiness of renunciation, seclusion, cessation, and perfect awakening, the happiness without seeking, the happiness without suffering. I have obtained this kind of happiness of renunciation, seclusion, cessation, and perfect awakening, the happiness without seeking, the happiness without suffering.'

"Furthermore, Nādika, at one time I was walking along the road, and I saw a bhikṣu going far ahead, and there was also a bhikṣu coming from behind, also far away. At that time, I was at ease, peaceful and without deliberation, and had no need for bodily functions. Why is this? Depending on food and drink, delighting in and being attached to flavors, there are bodily functions. This, then, is a dependency. Observing the five aggregates of clinging, their arising and ceasing, dwelling in disenchantment: this, then, is a dependency. At the six sense-contact bases, observing the arising and ceasing of their origination and cessation, dwelling in disenchantment: this, then, is a dependency. Delighting in the pleasure of social gatherings and diligently pursuing social gatherings while being averse to seclusion: this, then, is a dependency. Delighting in and cultivating seclusion, diligently pursuing seclusion while being averse to social gatherings: this, then, is a dependency. Therefore, Nādika, you should train in this way: 'Regarding the five aggregates of clinging, observe their arising and ceasing. At the six sense-contact bases, observe the arising and ceasing of origination and cessation. Delight in seclusion and diligently pursue seclusion.' Train in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Venerable Nādika, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and approved, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (1251)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kośala and arrived at the Nāḷaṅgala village, as extensively stated above, up to: "in this or that instance, seeking gain."

The Buddha told Nādika: "I have seen, near a village, a monastery where a bhikṣu sat in meditation. Seeing this, I made the following thought: 'Now this venerable one, with villagers, or perhaps novices, coming and going with noise and disturbance, will be obstructed in his meditative contemplation and disturbed from his right concentration. What he has not yet reached, he desires to reach; what he has not yet attained, he desires to attain; what he has not yet realized, he desires to realize, yet obstacles and hindrances are created.' Nādika, I do not approve of that bhikṣu dwelling in a village monastery.

"Nādika, I have seen a bhikṣu dwelling in an empty, secluded place, lying on his back, yawning and sighing. Seeing this, I made the following thought: 'May that bhikṣu awaken from his drowsiness and contemplate the perception of an empty, secluded place.' Nādika, I also do not approve of such a bhikṣu dwelling in an empty, secluded place.

"Nādika, I have further seen a bhikṣu dwelling in an empty, secluded place, sitting and swaying with drowsiness. Seeing this, I made the following thought: 'May this bhikṣu awaken from his drowsiness. When he is not yet concentrated, may he attain concentration. When his mind is concentrated, may he attain liberation.' Therefore, Nādika, I do not approve of such a bhikṣu dwelling in an empty, secluded place.

"Nādika, I have further seen a bhikṣu dwelling in an empty, secluded place, sitting upright in right concentration. Seeing this, I made the following thought: 'May this bhikṣu, if not yet liberated, swiftly attain liberation; if already liberated, may he guard and protect himself, so that he does not regress and lose it.' Nādika, I approve of such a bhikṣu dwelling in an empty, secluded place.

"Nādika, I have further seen a bhikṣu dwelling in an empty, secluded place, who at a later time, departing from the empty place, gathers up and abandons his bedding and returns to a village to take up bedding there. Nādika, I also do not approve of such a bhikṣu returning to a village.

"Furthermore, Nādika, I have seen a bhikṣu dwelling in a village monastery, who is famous and of great virtue, able to attract material gains: robes, food and drink, medicines, and the necessities of life. He at a later time gathers up and abandons his gains and his village bedding, and goes to an empty, secluded place to settle his bedding and rest. Nādika, I approve of such a bhikṣu who gathers up and abandons his gains and village bedding to dwell in an empty, secluded place. Nādika, bhikṣus should train in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣu Nādika rejoiced and approved, made obeisance, and departed.

---

### (1252)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pool, in the Double-Storied Hall.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "The Licchavi people always use wooden pillows, their hands and feet are cracked and callused, vigilant and fearful lest King Ajātaśatru Vaidehi-putra of Magadha find an opportunity against them. Therefore they are always self-alerting, not dwelling in heedlessness. Because they dwell without heedlessness, King Ajātaśatru Vaidehi-putra of Magadha is unable to spy out and find an opportunity against them. In a future time, before long, the Licchavi people will indulge in pleasures and ease, their hands and feet soft, using silk-cotton for pillows, their four limbs at rest, sleeping past sunrise, dwelling in heedlessness. Because they dwell in heedlessness, King Ajātaśatru Vaidehi-putra of Magadha will find an opportunity against them.

"In the same way, when a bhikṣu, with diligent effort, firm and capable, does not abandon wholesome dharmas, even if his flesh wastes away and his sinews connect only with bone, with diligent effort he does not abandon wholesome dharmas: until he has attained what should be attained, he does not abandon his diligence. Always controlling his mind, he dwells without heedlessness. Because he dwells without heedlessness, Māra Pāpīyān cannot find an opportunity against him.

"In a future time, there will be bhikṣus who indulge in pleasures and ease, their hands and feet soft, using silk-cotton for pillows, their four limbs at rest, sleeping past sunrise, dwelling in heedlessness. Because they dwell in heedlessness, the evil Māra Pāpīyān will find an opportunity against them. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train in this way: 'With diligent effort, up to not having attained what is yet unattained, do not abandon skillful means.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1253)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose a man, in the morning, gives three hundred pots of food in charity to beings, and at midday and in the evening does likewise. A second man, for the duration of just a moment, cultivates a mind of loving-kindness toward all beings, even for the time it takes to milk a cow: compared to the first man's merit of charitable giving, it cannot be matched, not in a hundredth part, not in a thousandth part, not in a hundred-million-myriad part; no calculation or comparison can be made! Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train in this way: 'For the duration of just a moment, cultivate a mind of loving-kindness toward all beings, even for the time it takes to milk a cow.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1254)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose there is a household with many women and few men. You should know that such a household is easily robbed and plundered by thieves. In the same way, if a good man or good woman is unable to frequently, even for the time it takes to milk a cow, cultivate a mind of loving-kindness toward all beings, you should know that such a person is easily deceived by evil ghosts and spirits.

"Suppose there is a household with many men and few women. It will not be frequently robbed and plundered by thieves. In the same way, if a good man frequently, even for the time it takes to milk a cow, cultivates a mind of loving-kindness toward all beings, he will not be deceived by evil ghosts and spirits. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should always, at the appropriate time, frequently, even for the time it takes to milk a cow, cultivate a mind of loving-kindness."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1255)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose there is a person who has a dagger-like short sword with a wide and sharp blade. A strong man says: 'I can, with my hand and fist, pound and smash your sword to pieces.' Bhikṣus, could that strong man, with his hand and fist, pound and smash that sword to pieces?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "He could not, World-Honored One. That dagger-like short sword has a wide and sharp blade. That man could not, with his hand and fist, pound and smash it to pieces; he would only injure himself.

"In the same way, bhikṣus, if a śramaṇa or brāhmaṇa, even for the time it takes to milk a cow, cultivates a mind of loving-kindness toward all beings, and if evil ghosts and spirits wish to go and spy out his faults, they cannot find an opportunity against him; it would only rebound to injure themselves. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train in this way: 'Frequently, even for the time it takes to milk a cow, cultivate a mind of loving-kindness.'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1256)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One scooped up some dirt on his fingernail and addressed the bhikṣus: "What do you think? Is the soil on my fingernail more, or is the soil of the great earth more?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, the soil on the fingernail is extremely, extremely little. The soil of the great earth is immeasurable and countless; there is no comparison."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "In the same way, those beings who are able to frequently, even for the snap of a finger, cultivate a mind of loving-kindness toward all beings, are like the soil on the fingernail. Those beings who are unable to frequently, even for the snap of a finger, cultivate a mind of loving-kindness toward all beings, are like the soil of the great earth. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should always frequently cultivate a mind of loving-kindness toward all beings."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1257)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pool, in the Double-Storied Hall.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "All conditioned things are impermanent, not enduring, not secure; they are dharmas of change and transformation. Bhikṣus, you should always observe all conditioned things and cultivate disenchantment, non-delight, and liberation."

At that time, a certain bhikṣu rose from his seat, arranged his robes, made obeisance to the Buddha, placed his right knee on the ground, joined his palms, and addressed the Buddha: "How quickly does the lifespan change and perish?"

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "I am able to explain this, but it would be difficult for you to understand."

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "Can it be explained by means of a simile?"

The Buddha said: "It can be explained."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Suppose four men, each holding a strong bow, simultaneously release their arrows, shooting in the four directions. There is one man who, before any arrow has fallen, catches all four arrows. How about that, bhikṣu? Is such a man swift?"

The bhikṣu addressed the Buddha: "He is swift, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣu: "Although that arrow-catching man is swift, there is an earth spirit deva-child who is many times swifter than he. There is a space spirit deva many times swifter than the earth spirit. The deva-children of the Four Heavenly Kings come and go many times swifter than the space spirit deva. The deva-children of the sun and moon are many times swifter again than the deva-children of the Four Heavenly Kings. The spirits that guide the sun and moon are many times swifter again than the deva-children of the sun and moon. Bhikṣus, the passage and transformation of the life-force is many times swifter than the spirits that guide the sun and moon. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should diligently and skillfully observe that the passage of the life-force is as impermanent and rapid as this."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1258)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Vārāṇasī, at the Ṛṣipatana Deer Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In a past age, there was a man named Daśaratha. That Daśaratha had a drum called Ānaka. It had a fine sound, a beautiful sound, a deep sound that carried for forty li. After a long time, the drum cracked and split in many places. At that time, the drum-maker cut cowhide and wrapped it all around. Although it was wrapped, the drum no longer had its loud sound, its beautiful sound, its deep sound. In a subsequent time, it further decayed, and the leather greatly peeled away, leaving only a pile of wood.

"In the same way, bhikṣus, those who cultivate the body, cultivate morality, cultivate the mind, and cultivate wisdom: because of that cultivation of the body, morality, mind, and wisdom, regarding the sūtras spoken by the Tathāgata that are profound, illuminating, hard to see, hard to perceive, beyond calculation, subtly definitive, and known by the wise, they are able to receive them completely, to receive them thoroughly. Hearing what is spoken, they rejoice and honor and practice it, gaining renunciation and benefit. Bhikṣus of the future who do not cultivate the body, do not cultivate morality, do not cultivate the mind, and do not cultivate wisdom: when they hear the sūtras spoken by the Tathāgata that are profound, illuminating, corresponding to emptiness, and in accord with dependent origination, they will not receive them completely, will not receive them thoroughly. Those who hear such teachings will not rejoice and honor and practice them, and they will not gain renunciation and benefit. Rather, regarding the worldly miscellaneous and varied discourses, adorned with literary elegance, composed of worldly miscellaneous phrases, they will devote their minds and receive them with the highest respect. Hearing such teachings, they will rejoice and honor and practice them, but will not gain renunciation and benefit. Regarding the profound, illuminating, essential dharmas corresponding to emptiness spoken by the Tathāgata, in accord with dependent origination, these will then perish, just as that drum decayed and split, leaving only a pile of wood. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should diligently and skillfully cultivate the body, cultivate morality, cultivate the mind, and cultivate wisdom. Regarding the profound, illuminating, essential dharmas corresponding to emptiness spoken by the Tathāgata, in accord with dependent origination, receive them completely and receive them thoroughly. Hearing what is spoken, rejoice and honor and practice it, for renunciation and benefit."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1259)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Suppose an iron ball is placed in fire until it becomes the same color as the fire, then placed into a mass of kapok cotton. What do you think, bhikṣus? Would it quickly catch fire?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "Indeed, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "A foolish person who resides near a village, in the morning puts on his robes, takes his bowl, and enters the village to beg for food. He does not properly guard his body, does not guard the gates of his sense faculties, and does not maintain mindfulness. If he sees a young woman and engages in improper attention, grasping at her physical features, desire arises in his mind. Desire burns his mind, desire burns his body. When body and mind are burned, he abandons the precepts and falls away. Such a foolish person, through the long night, will encounter what is not beneficial. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train in this way: 'Properly guard the body, guard the gates of the sense faculties, maintain mindfulness, and enter the village to beg for food.' Train in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1260)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "In a past age, there was a cat, hungry and thirsty, lean and emaciated. It watched at a hole, seeking mouse pups. If a mouse pup came out, it would seize and eat it. At one time, a mouse pup came out of its hole to play. The cat quickly seized and swallowed it. Because the mouse pup was small, it entered the belly alive. Once inside the belly, it gnawed at the cat's innards. As it gnawed at the innards, the cat became confused and dazed, running madly east and west, through empty dwellings and grave mounds, not knowing where to stop, and finally died.

"In the same way, bhikṣus, there is a foolish person who resides near a village. In the morning he puts on his robes, takes his bowl, and enters the village to beg for food. He does not properly guard his body, does not guard the gates of his sense faculties, and does not maintain mindfulness. Seeing women, he engages in improper attention, grasping at their physical features, and desire arises in his mind. When desire arises, the fire of craving blazes fiercely, burning his body and mind. When body and mind are burned, his mind races and runs wild. He takes no delight in the monastery, no delight in solitude, no delight in sitting beneath a tree. Evil, unwholesome mental states eat away at his inner Dharma, and he abandons the precepts and falls away. This foolish person, through the long night, constantly encounters suffering without benefit. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train in this way: 'Properly guard the body, guard the gates of the sense faculties, maintain a mind of right mindfulness, and enter the village to beg for food.' Train in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1261)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the state of Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "Just as a wooden pestle that is constantly used without ceasing wears away day and night. In the same way, bhikṣus, if śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas, from the beginning, do not close the gates of their sense faculties, do not know moderation in eating, and in the first and last watches of the night do not diligently remain wakeful and cultivate wholesome dharmas, you should know that such people will decline and diminish day after day, never increasing in wholesome dharmas, like that wooden pestle.

"Bhikṣus, just as the utpala, padma, kumuda, and puṇḍarīka lotuses are born in water, grow in water, and increase as the water increases: in the same way, if śramaṇas or brāhmaṇas properly close the gates of their sense faculties, know moderation in eating, and in the first and last watches of the night diligently remain wakeful, you should know that for such people, wholesome roots and meritorious qualities increase day and night, never declining or diminishing. You should train in this way: 'Properly close the gates of the sense faculties, know moderation in eating, and in the first and last watches of the night diligently remain wakeful, so that meritorious and wholesome dharmas increase day and night.' Train in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1262)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the city of Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, the World-Honored One, during the latter part of the night, heard the cry of a jackal.

At that time, after the night had passed and dawn had come, the World-Honored One, in front of the great assembly, spread his seat and sat down. He addressed the bhikṣus: "Did you hear the cry of the jackal during the latter part of the night?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "Indeed, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "There is a foolish person who has this thought: 'Let me receive a body of such a form and make such sounds.' This foolish person, wishing to receive rebirth in such a form and place, why would he not be able to? Therefore, bhikṣus, you should only diligently and skillfully seek to cut off all forms of existence. Do not use skillful means to increase the various forms of existence. Train in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1263)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the city of Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the bhikṣus: "I do not praise even the receiving of a small amount of existence, let alone much! Why is this? Receiving existence is suffering. Just as with excrement and filth, even a small amount is foul-smelling, how much more a large amount? In the same way, even a small amount of existence I do not praise, even down to a single moment, how much more a large amount? Why is this? Because existence is suffering. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train in this way: 'Cut off and eliminate the various forms of existence. Do not increase existence.' Train in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1264)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the city of Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove. At that time, the World-Honored One, during the latter part of the night, heard the cry of a jackal.

When the night had passed, in front of the great assembly he spread his seat and sat down. He addressed the bhikṣus: "Did you hear the cry of the jackal during the latter part of the night?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "Indeed, World-Honored One."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "That jackal is afflicted by mange sores, and therefore it cries out. If someone could treat the jackal's mange sores, the jackal would surely know gratitude and repay the kindness. Yet now there are foolish people who have no sense of gratitude and do not repay kindness. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train in this way: 'Know gratitude and repay kindness.' Even small favors should be repaid and never forgotten, how much more great kindness?"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1265)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the city of Rājagṛha, in the Goldsmith's Monastery. At that time, there was a venerable named Vakkali staying in the city of Rājagṛha at the Goldsmith's Monastery, suffering from grave illness. The Venerable Puliṇa was attending to and caring for him.

At that time, Vakkali said to Puliṇa: "Please go to the World-Honored One's place on my behalf. Bow your head at the World-Honored One's feet and inquire of the World-Honored One whether he is free from illness, free from affliction, rising and going about with ease, and dwelling in peace and happiness. Then say: 'Vakkali, staying at the Goldsmith's Monastery, is suffering from grave illness, confined to his bed, and wishes to see the World-Honored One. He is afflicted by illness and his strength is feeble; he has no means to come before you. May the World-Honored One please come to this Goldsmith's Monastery, out of compassion.'"

Thereupon Puliṇa, having received Vakkali's words, went to the World-Honored One's place, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew and stood to one side, and addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, the Venerable Vakkali bows his head at the World-Honored One's feet and inquires of the World-Honored One whether he is free from illness, free from affliction, rising and going about with ease, and dwelling in peace and happiness."

The World-Honored One replied: "May he be at ease."

Puliṇa addressed the Buddha: "World-Honored One, the Venerable Vakkali is staying at the Goldsmith's Monastery, suffering from grave illness, confined to his bed. He wishes to see the World-Honored One but has no bodily strength to come before the World-Honored One. May the World-Honored One please go to the Goldsmith's Monastery, out of compassion."

At that time, the World-Honored One silently assented. When Puliṇa knew the World-Honored One had assented, he bowed at the Buddha's feet and departed.

Then the World-Honored One, in the afternoon, arising from meditation, went to the Goldsmith's Monastery and arrived at Vakkali's room. The bhikṣu Vakkali, seeing the World-Honored One from afar, tried to rise from his bed.

The Buddha told Vakkali: "Stop! Do not rise."

The World-Honored One then sat on another bed and said to Vakkali: "Can your mind endure the suffering of this illness? Is the affliction of your body increasing or decreasing?"

Vakkali addressed the Buddha as did the bhikṣu Khaḍḍa previously in that sūtra, as extensively stated. "World-Honored One, the pain of my body is extremely difficult to endure. I wish to take a knife and end my life; I do not delight in living in suffering."

The Buddha told Vakkali: "I shall now question you; answer as you see fit. How about this, Vakkali? Is form permanent, or is it impermanent?"

Vakkali answered: "Impermanent, World-Honored One."

He further asked: "If it is impermanent, is it suffering?"

He answered: "It is suffering, World-Honored One."

He further asked: "Vakkali, if it is impermanent and suffering, being a dharma of change and transformation, could there be anything in it worthy of craving or desiring?"

Vakkali addressed
