# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Forty-Eight

---

### (1267)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One! Can a bhikṣu cross over the swift current?"

The Buddha said: "Yes, devaputra."

The devaputra asked again: "Without grasping at anything, and without standing on anything, can one cross over the swift current?"

The Buddha said: "Yes, devaputra."

The devaputra asked again: "Without grasping at anything, and without standing on anything, to cross over the swift current: what is the meaning of this?"

The Buddha said: "Devaputra! When I struggled in such and such a way, and pressed forward in such and such a way, I was swept away by the water. When I did not struggle in such and such a way, and did not press forward in such and such a way, I was not swept away by the water. Thus, devaputra, this is called 'without grasping at anything, and without standing on anything, crossing over the swift current.'"

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Then that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1268)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park. At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra addressed the Buddha, saying: "Does a bhikṣu know the attachments and accumulations of all sentient beings, and attain definite liberation, broad liberation, and the most extensive liberation?"

The Buddha told the devaputra: "I fully know the attachments and accumulations of all sentient beings, and have attained definite liberation, broad liberation, and the most extensive liberation."

The devaputra addressed the Buddha: "How does a bhikṣu know the attachments and accumulations of all sentient beings, and attain definite liberation, broad liberation, and the most extensive liberation?"

The Buddha told the devaputra: "When delight and craving are completely extinguished, my mind is liberated. Since my mind is liberated, I therefore know the attachments and accumulations of all sentient beings, and attain definite liberation, broad liberation, and the most extensive liberation."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Then that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1269)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, asking the Buddha:

> "Who crosses over the currents? Diligently striving day and night,
> Without grasping, without standing,
> In what defilements is one not attached?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "One who is complete in all precepts,
> With wisdom and good right concentration,
> Inwardly contemplating with mindful awareness,
> Crosses the currents so difficult to cross.
> Not delighting in sensual perceptions,
> Transcending the fetters of form,
> Neither bound nor standing,
> In defilements one is not attached."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Then that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1270)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Mountain Valley Monastery.

At that time, there was Kokanada, a goddess of radiant light, of surpassingly fine appearance. In the last watch of the night, she came to the Buddha's dwelling place. She bowed her head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of her body illuminated the entire mountain valley.

Then Kokanada the goddess spoke a verse, saying:

> "Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous."

The Buddha told the goddess: "So it is, so it is.

> "Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous."

Then Kokanasā the goddess, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed her approval, bowed her head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

At that time, the World-Honored One, when the night had passed and morning had come, entered the Sangha assembly. He spread out his sitting cloth and sat before the great assembly. He told the bhikṣus: "Last night, in the last watch, the goddess Kokanada, of surpassingly fine appearance, came to my dwelling place. She bowed her head at my feet, withdrew to sit at one side, and spoke a verse, saying:

> "'Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous.'

"I then replied, saying: 'So it is, goddess! So it is, goddess!

> "'Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous.'

"When these words were spoken, the goddess Kokanada, having heard what I said, rejoiced and expressed her approval, bowed her head at my feet, and instantly vanished."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1271)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Mountain Valley Monastery.

At that time, Venerable Ānanda addressed the bhikṣus: "I shall now expound the Sūtra of the Four-Line Verse. Listen carefully and ponder it well; I shall explain it for you. What is the Sūtra of the Four-Line Verse?"

Then Venerable Ānanda spoke a verse, saying:

> "Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous.

"Bhikṣus, this is called the Sūtra of the Four-Line Verse."

At that time, there was a certain brāhmaṇa not far from Venerable Ānanda, who was teaching young brāhmaṇas to recite scriptures. That brāhmaṇa thought: "If the verse spoken by the śramaṇa Ānanda is the same as what is said in our scriptures, then it must be a non-human utterance."

Then that brāhmaṇa went to the Buddha's dwelling place. Having exchanged greetings and courtesies face to face with the World-Honored One, he withdrew to sit at one side and addressed the Buddha, saying: "Gautama! The verse spoken by the śramaṇa Ānanda:

> "'Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous.'

"Such words as these are non-human speech, not human speech."

The Buddha told the brāhmaṇa: "So it is, so it is, brāhmaṇa! This is non-human speech, not human speech. At that time, the goddess Kokanada came to my dwelling place. She bowed her head at my feet, withdrew to sit at one side, and spoke a verse, saying:

> "'Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous.'

"At that time I replied, saying: 'So it is, so it is, as the goddess has said:

> "'"Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous."'

"Therefore, brāhmaṇa, you should know that this verse is spoken by a non-human, not spoken by a human."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, that brāhmaṇa, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed at the Buddha's feet, and departed.

---

### (1272)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Mountain Valley Monastery.

At that time, there was Kokanasā the goddess, a goddess of radiant light, who produced great flashes of lightning, blazing brilliantly. She took refuge in the Buddha, took refuge in the Dharma, and took refuge in the Bhikṣu Sangha. She came to the Buddha's dwelling place, bowed her head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of her body illuminated the entire mountain valley. Then, before the Buddha, she spoke a verse, saying:

> "Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous."

At that time, the World-Honored One told the goddess: "So it is, so it is, goddess! As you have said:

> "'Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous.'"

Then Kokanasā the goddess, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed her approval, bowed her head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

At that time, the World-Honored One, when the night had passed and morning had come, entered the Sangha assembly. He spread out his sitting cloth and sat before the great assembly. He told the bhikṣus: "Last night, in the last watch, Kokanasā the goddess, the goddess of radiant light, came to my dwelling place. She bowed her head at my feet, withdrew to sit at one side, and spoke a verse, saying:

> "'Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous.'

"At that time I replied, saying: 'So it is, goddess! So it is, goddess! As you have said:

> "'"Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous."'

> "Kokanada the goddess,
> With lightning blazing brilliantly,
> Reverently paid homage to the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha,
> And spoke verses of beneficial meaning."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1273)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Mountain Valley Monastery.

At that time, there was Kokanasā the goddess, a goddess of radiant light, who emitted flashes of lightning, blazing brilliantly. In the last watch of the night, she came to the Buddha's dwelling place. She bowed her head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of her body illuminated the entire mountain valley. Then, before the Buddha, she spoke a verse, saying:

> "I could extensively analyze
> The Tathāgata's true Dharma and Vinaya,
> But now I shall speak only briefly,
> Sufficient to express my heart.
> Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous."

The Buddha told the goddess: "So it is, goddess! So it is, goddess! As you have said:

> "'Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous.'"

Then Kokanasā the goddess, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced, bowed her head, and instantly vanished.

At that time, the World-Honored One, when the night had passed and morning had come, entered the Sangha assembly. He spread out his seat before the great assembly and sat down. He told the bhikṣus: "In the last watch of last night, Kokanasā the goddess came to my dwelling place. She reverently paid respects, withdrew to sit at one side, and spoke a verse, saying:

> "'I could extensively analyze
> The Tathāgata's true Dharma and Vinaya,
> But now I shall speak only briefly,
> Sufficient to express my heart.
> Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all empty,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous.'

"At that time I replied, saying: 'So it is, goddess! As you have said:

> "'"Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all false,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous."'

"Then that goddess, having heard what I said, rejoiced and expressed her approval, bowed her head at my feet, and instantly vanished."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1274)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Vaiśālī, beside the Monkey Pond, in the Double-Storied Lecture Hall.

At that time, there were the goddesses Kokanasā and Cūḷaratā, of surpassingly fine appearance. In the last watch of the night, they came to the Buddha's dwelling place. They bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of their bodies illuminated all around the Monkey Pond.

Then the goddess Cūḷaratā spoke a verse, addressing the Buddha:

> "The Great Teacher, the Perfectly Awakened One,
> Dwells in the land of Vaiśālī.
> Kokanasā and Cūḷaratā
> Bow their heads in reverent homage.
> Never before have I heard
> The Muni's true Dharma and Vinaya.
> Now I am able to see in person
> The true Dharma expounded in my presence.
> If, toward the noble Dharma and Vinaya,
> One with evil wisdom generates aversion,
> One will surely fall into evil destinies
> And suffer long through the long night.
> If, toward the noble Dharma and Vinaya,
> One abides with right mindfulness and complete restraint,
> Then one will be born in the heavens above
> And enjoy peace and bliss through the long night."

Then the goddess Kokanasā spoke a verse, saying:

> "Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all false,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous."

The Buddha told the goddess: "So it is, so it is, as you have said:

> "'Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all false,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous.'"

Then those goddesses, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed their approval, and instantly vanished.

At that time, the World-Honored One, when the night had passed and morning had come, entered the Sangha assembly. He spread out his seat and sat down. He told the bhikṣus: "In the last watch of last night, two goddesses of surpassingly fine appearance came to my dwelling place. They paid homage to me, withdrew to sit at one side. The goddess Cūḷaratā spoke a verse, saying:

> "'The Great Teacher, the Perfectly Awakened One,
> Dwells in the land of Vaiśālī.
> I, Kokanasā,
> Together with Cūḷaratā,
> These two goddesses
> Bow their heads at the Buddha's feet in homage.
> Never before have I heard
> The Muni's true Dharma and Vinaya.
> Now I behold the Perfectly Awakened One,
> Expounding the subtle and wondrous Dharma.
> If, toward the true Dharma and Vinaya,
> One is averse and abides in evil wisdom,
> One will surely fall into evil destinies
> And suffer greatly through the long night.
> If, toward the true Dharma and Vinaya,
> One abides with right mindfulness and complete restraint,
> One will be born in good destinies and the heavens above
> And enjoy peace and bliss through the long night.'

"Then the goddess Kokanada spoke a verse, saying:

> "'Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all false,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous.'

"At that time I replied, saying: 'So it is, so it is, as you have said:

> "'"Whose mind does no evil,
> Nor body nor speech in the world,
> Who knows the five desires are all false,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Who does not approach and engage in suffering,
> Nor associates with what is not righteous."'"

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1275)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "One who is not struck does not strike back;
> But if struck, one strikes back in return.
> Because of striking back when struck,
> Not being angry, one does not invite anger."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "If someone toward a person who is without anger
> Directs anger toward them,
> A pure and upright person
> Who has left behind all fetters of affliction,
> If one harbors an evil mind toward such a one,
> That evil mind returns to oneself,
> Like throwing dust against the wind:
> It only covers one's own body."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Then that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1276)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "What the foolish person does
> Does not accord with what is wise.
> By one's own evil conduct,
> One becomes one's own evil companion.
> The many evil deeds one has done
> Will in the end bring painful retribution."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Having performed unwholesome deeds,
> In the end one suffers anguish.
> Though one delighted in performing the deeds,
> One weeps when receiving the retribution.
> One who performs wholesome deeds
> In the end does not suffer anguish.
> Joyfully one performs the deeds,
> And in peace and happiness one receives the retribution."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Then that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1277)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "One should not always speak,
> Nor only listen in one direction,
> And thereby attain the path's tracks.
> Firmly and rightly one should cross over.
> Contemplating the wholesome state of cessation,
> One is liberated from Māra's bonds.
> What one practices, one may speak of;
> What one does not practice, one should not speak of.
> One who speaks without practicing,
> The wise know to be wrong.
> Not practicing what one ought to practice,
> Not doing yet claiming to have done,
> This is the same as a thief's wrongdoing."

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the devaputra: "Do you now have some criticism to make?"

The devaputra addressed the Buddha: "I repent, World-Honored One! I repent, Well-Gone One!"

At that time, the World-Honored One smiled with a serene countenance.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "I now repent of my fault,
> Yet the World-Honored One does not accept it.
> Harboring an evil mind within,
> One clings to resentment and does not let go."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "If one speaks words of repentance,
> Yet within does not calm the mind,
> How can one put resentment to rest?
> How is that called cultivating goodness?"

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Who is there that has no faults?
> What person is without wrongdoing?
> Who is it that has no foolishness?
> Who can always be firm and steadfast?"

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Then that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1278)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandakanivāpa Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the bhikṣu Kokālika, a companion and partisan of Devadatta, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣu Kokālika: "Kokālika! Why do you harbor impure thoughts toward Śāriputra and Maudgalyāyana, who practice the pure holy life? Through the long night you will obtain what is unprofitable and suffering."

The bhikṣu Kokālika addressed the Buddha, saying: "World-Honored One! I now trust the World-Honored One's words, which speak nothing false. However, Śāriputra and Mahā-Maudgalyāyana have evil desires in their hearts." Thus he spoke a second and a third time. The bhikṣu Kokālika, companion and partisan of Devadatta, upon the World-Honored One's repeated admonitions, rebelled and refused to accept them. He rose from his seat and left. After he departed, boils and sores appeared all over his body, all the size of millet grains, which gradually increased in size until they were all the size of peaches and plums.

Then the bhikṣu Kokālika, afflicted with painful suffering, cried out, saying: "Extreme burning! Extreme burning!" Pus and blood flowed forth. His body was destroyed and his life ended, and he was reborn in the Great Padma Hell.

At that time, three devaputras of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. They bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to stand at one side. Then one devaputra addressed the Buddha, saying: "The bhikṣu Kokālika, companion and partisan of Devadatta, has now ended his life." Then a second devaputra said: "Venerable ones, you should know that the bhikṣu Kokālika has ended his life and fallen into hell." The third devaputra then spoke a verse, saying:

> "When a man is born into the world,
> An axe is born within his mouth.
> He cuts down his own body with it;
> This is due to his evil speech.
> One who praises what should be censured,
> And censures what should be praised,
> That sin arises from the mouth;
> At death one falls into evil destinies.
> Losing wealth through gambling
> Is not the greatest fault.
> Slandering the Buddha and his śrāvakas,
> That is the gravest transgression."

When those three devaputras had spoken these verses, they instantly vanished.

At that time, the World-Honored One, when the night had passed and morning had come, entered the Sangha assembly. He spread out his seat before the great assembly and sat down. He told the bhikṣus: "In the last watch of last night, three devaputras came to my dwelling place. They bowed their heads at my feet and withdrew to stand at one side. The first devaputra said to me: 'World-Honored One! The bhikṣu Kokālika, companion and partisan of Devadatta, has now ended his life.' The second devaputra told the other devaputras: 'The bhikṣu Kokālika has ended his life and fallen into hell.' The third devaputra then spoke a verse, saying:

> "'When a man is born into the world,
> An axe is born within his mouth.
> He cuts down his own body with it;
> This is due to his evil speech.
> One who praises what should be censured,
> And censures what should be praised,
> That sin arises from the mouth;
> At death one falls into evil destinies.'

"Having spoken these verses, they instantly vanished. Bhikṣus! Do you wish to hear the lifespan measure of beings born in the Arbuda Hell?"

The bhikṣus addressed the Buddha: "Now is the right time. May the World-Honored One explain to the great assembly the lifespan measure of beings in the Arbuda Hell. The bhikṣus, having heard, will receive and practice accordingly."

The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "Listen carefully and ponder it well; I shall explain it for you. Suppose in the land of Kosala, four pecks make one āḍhaka, four āḍhakas make one droṇa, sixteen droṇas make one jhamaṇa, sixteen jhamaṇas make one māṇī, twenty māṇīs make one khāri, and twenty khāris make one storehouse, filled with mustard seeds. If a person were to remove one mustard seed every hundred years, and continued until the entire storehouse of mustard seeds was completely exhausted, the lifespan of beings in the Arbuda Hell would still not be exhausted. Thus, twenty Arbuda Hell lifespans equal one Nirarbuda Hell lifespan. Twenty Nirarbuda Hell lifespans equal one Aṭaṭa Hell lifespan. Twenty Aṭaṭa Hell lifespans equal one Apapa Hell lifespan. Twenty Apapa Hell lifespans equal one Ahuhu Hell lifespan. Twenty Ahuhu Hell lifespans equal one Utpala Hell lifespan. Twenty Utpala Hell lifespans equal one Padma Hell lifespan. Twenty Padma Hell lifespans equal one Mahāpadma Hell lifespan.

"Bhikṣus! That bhikṣu Kokālika has ended his life and fallen into the Mahāpadma Hell, because he harbored an evil mind and slandered the venerable bhikṣus Śāriputra and Mahā-Maudgalyāyana. Therefore, bhikṣus, you should train thus: 'Even toward a burnt and charred stump, we should not wish to damage it; how much less should we damage a sentient being who possesses consciousness!'" The Buddha told the bhikṣus: "You should train in this way."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1279)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, asking the Buddha:

> "Falling back and entering the place of defeat,
> How can this be known?
> May the World-Honored One please explain,
> What are the doors to the place of defeat?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "The place of victory is easy to know,
> And the place of defeat is also easy to know.
> Delighting in the Dharma is the place of victory;
> Destroying the Dharma is the place of defeat.
> Delighting in evil companions,
> And not delighting in good companions,
> Generating enmity toward good friends:
> This is called the door to falling into defeat.
> Delighting in unwholesome persons,
> And harboring aversion toward good persons,
> Desiring evil and not desiring good:
> This is called the door to the place of defeat.
> Using scales and measures to deceive others:
> This is called the door to falling into defeat.
> Gambling and indulging in drink,
> Wandering frivolously and chasing after women,
> Squandering and wasting one's wealth:
> This is called the door to falling into defeat.
> A woman who does not guard herself,
> Abandoning her lord to follow another;
> A man whose mind is unrestrained,
> Abandoning his wife to chase after other women;
> Those who manage their households in this way
> All fall through the doors of defeat.
> An old woman who obtains a young husband,
> Her heart always filled with jealousy,
> Lying awake unable to rest due to jealousy:
> This is the door to falling into defeat.
> An old man who obtains a young wife
> Likewise falls through the door of defeat.
> Always delighting in and attached to sleep,
> Engaging in idle socializing with companions,
> Lazy and fond of anger and resentment:
> All these are doors to falling into defeat.
> Having much wealth and befriending companions,
> Extravagant and unrestrained in drink and food,
> Spending much and wasting one's wealth:
> All these are doors to falling into defeat.
> Having little wealth yet harboring much craving,
> Giving rise to the ambitions of a kṣatriya,
> Always seeking to become a king:
> This is the door to falling into defeat.
> Seeking pearl earrings and necklaces,
> Leather shoes, sandals, and parasols,
> Adorning oneself while being miserly:
> This is the door to falling into defeat.
> Accepting others' rich and fine food,
> Yet being stingy with one's own wealth,
> Eating from others without reciprocating:
> This is the door to falling into defeat.
> When śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas
> Are invited into one's home,
> Being stingy and not giving in a timely manner:
> This is the door to falling into defeat.
> When śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas
> Come begging for alms in succession,
> Rebuking them and not wishing to give:
> This is the door to falling into defeat.
> If one's father and mother are aged
> And one does not care for them in a timely manner,
> Having wealth yet not giving to them:
> This is the door to falling into defeat.
> Striking and verbally abusing
> One's father, mother, and siblings,
> Having no sense of proper order between elder and younger:
> This is the door to falling into defeat.
> The Buddha and his assembly of disciples,
> Whether householders or renunciants,
> Slandering them and showing no respect:
> This is the door to falling into defeat.
> One who truly is not an arhat,
> Yet falsely claims to have attained arhatship:
> Such a one is a thief in the world,
> And falls through the door of defeat.
> These are the places of defeat in the world;
> I know them and see them, hence I speak of them.
> They are like a perilous and fearful road;
> The wise should keep far away from them."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Then that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1280)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, asking the Buddha:

> "Who bends down and follows the bending?
> Who rises up and follows the rising?
> How is it like children at play,
> Like children throwing clods at each other?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Craving bends down, and one follows its bending;
> Craving rises up, and one follows its rising.
> Craving plays with the foolish,
> Like children throwing clods at each other."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Then that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1281)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "With determination one should restrain by restraint,
> Deluded thoughts come of their own.
> If a person restrains everything,
> One will not be oppressed by them."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "With determination one should restrain by restraint,
> Deluded thoughts come of their own.
> One need not restrain everything,
> But only restrain evil deeds.
> Having restrained those various evils,
> One will not be oppressed by them."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Then that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1282)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, asking the Buddha:

> "How does one obtain a good reputation?
> How does one obtain great wealth?
> How does one's virtue become widely known?
> How does one obtain good friends?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Keeping the precepts, one obtains a good reputation.
> Through generosity, one obtains great wealth.
> Through truthfulness, one's virtue becomes widely known.
> Through kindness and benevolence, one obtains good friends."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Then that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1283)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, asking the Buddha:

> "How should a person act,
> And with wisdom seek wealth,
> So as to equally manage one's wealth,
> Whether superior or inferior?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "First learn a skilled craft or trade,
> And by skillful means accumulate wealth.
> Having obtained that wealth,
> One should divide it into four portions.
> One portion for one's own food and use,
> Two portions for managing one's livelihood,
> The remaining one portion stored away in reserve,
> To provide for times of poverty and want.
> For managing one's livelihood:
> Farming, planting, trading and commerce,
> Raising cattle, sheep, and lending at interest,
> Renting lodging houses for profit,
> Building houses and furnishing beds,
> Six kinds of means of livelihood.
> By skillful means one arranges all these provisions,
> Living in peace and comfort in the world.
> One who thus skillfully manages one's livelihood,
> Wisely seeking wealth,
> Wealth and treasure will naturally accumulate,
> Like many streams flowing to the sea.
> With wealth thus increased,
> Like bees collecting many flavors,
> Day and night one's wealth increases,
> Like an anthill gradually piling up.
> Do not entrust wealth to the very old or the very young;
> Do not deposit it with border-dwelling people;
> Do not trust deceitful and crafty persons,
> Nor the stingy and miserly.
> Associate closely with those who accomplish things;
> Keep distance from those who do not accomplish things.
> A man who can accomplish things
> Is like a blazing fire.
> Good friends, those worthy of esteem,
> Who are alert, discreet, and of good character;
> Relatives of the same lineage and siblings
> Who are good at caring for one another;
> Dwelling among one's relatives and kin,
> One stands out prominently like a bull king.
> According to what is appropriate for each,
> One distributes wealth and offers food and drink.
> When life ends and one's life is over,
> One will be reborn in heaven and enjoy bliss."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Then that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1284)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣus: "In a past age, in the land of Kosala, there was a lute player named Gūthaka. He was wandering among the people of Kosala and stopped to rest in a wilderness. At that time, six goddesses of great and spacious heavenly palaces came to the Kosala lute player Gūthaka's location. They said to the lute player Gūthaka: 'Uncle! Uncle! Play the lute for us, and we shall sing and dance.' The lute player Gūthaka said: 'Very well, sisters! I shall play the lute for you, but you must tell me: who are you? By what cause were you born here?' The goddesses replied: 'Uncle! Just play the lute, and we shall sing and dance. In our songs and verses, we shall ourselves explain the causes and conditions by which we were born here.' Then the Kosala lute player Gūthaka played the lute, and the six goddesses sang and danced.

"The first goddess spoke a verse in song:

> "'If a man or a woman
> Gives in charity excellent and fine garments,
> By the cause and condition of giving garments,
> Wherever one is born, one attains excellence.
> Giving what one loves and cherishes,
> One is born in heaven with all one's wishes fulfilled.
> Behold me dwelling in my palace,
> Traveling through the sky.
> My heavenly body is like a mass of gold;
> Among goddesses, I am foremost among a hundred.
> Observe this merit and virtue:
> It is the supreme among dedications of merit.'

"The second goddess spoke a verse, saying:

> "'If a man or a woman
> Gives in charity excellent and fine fragrances,
> Giving what one loves and cherishes with a willing heart,
> One is born in heaven with all one's wishes fulfilled.
> Behold me dwelling in my palace,
> Traveling through the sky.
> My heavenly body is like a mass of gold;
> Among goddesses, I am foremost among a hundred.
> Observe this merit and virtue:
> It is the supreme among dedications of merit.'

"The third goddess spoke a verse, saying:

> "'If a man or a woman
> Gives food in charity,
> Giving what is agreeable and loved with a willing heart,
> One is born in heaven with all one's wishes fulfilled.
> Behold me dwelling in my palace,
> Traveling through the void.
> My heavenly body is like a mass of gold;
> Among goddesses, I am foremost among a hundred.
> Observe this merit and virtue:
> It is the supreme among dedications of merit.'

"The fourth goddess spoke a verse, saying:

> "'I remember in a former life,
> I was once a serving maid.
> I did not steal, nor was I greedy or gluttonous;
> Diligently I worked without laziness.
> Measuring my own belly, I moderated myself;
> I shared my food to aid the poor.
> Now behold me dwelling in my palace,
> Traveling through the void.
> My heavenly body is like a mass of gold;
> Among goddesses, I am foremost among a hundred.
> Observe this merit and virtue:
> It is the supreme among offerings.'

"The fifth goddess spoke a verse, saying:

> "'I remember in a former life,
> I was a daughter-in-law in someone's household.
> My parents-in-law were wild-tempered,
> Always addressing me with rough and harsh words.
> I maintained my virtue and practiced wifely propriety,
> Humble and obedient in serving them.
> Now behold me dwelling in my palace,
> Traveling through the void.
> My heavenly body is like a mass of gold;
> Among goddesses, I am foremost among a hundred.
> Observe this merit and virtue:
> It is the supreme among offerings.'

"The sixth goddess spoke a verse, saying:

> "'Once I saw those walking the path,
> Bhikṣus and bhikṣuṇīs.
> From them I heard the true Dharma,
> And for one night I observed the precepts of the upoṣadha fast.
> Now behold me dwelling in my heavenly palace,
> Traveling through the void.
> My heavenly body is like a mass of gold;
> Among goddesses, I am foremost among a hundred.
> Observe this merit and virtue:
> It is the supreme among dedications of merit.'

"At that time, the Kosala lute player Gūthaka spoke a verse, saying:

> "'How fortunate that I have come here,
> To this forest in Kosala,
> Where I have seen these goddesses,
> Complete with their wondrous heavenly forms.
> Having seen them and heard their words,
> I shall increase my cultivation of good deeds.
> By virtue of cultivating merit now,
> I too shall be reborn in heaven.'"

"When these words were spoken, the goddesses instantly vanished."

When the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1285)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, asking the Buddha:

> "What dharma, when it arises, should be extinguished?
> What, when it arises, should be guarded against?
> What dharma should be abandoned?
> By evenly contemplating what does one attain bliss?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "When anger arises, it should be extinguished.
> When craving arises, one should guard against it.
> Ignorance should be abandoned.
> By evenly contemplating the noble truths, one attains bliss.
> Desires give rise to afflictions;
> Desire is the root of suffering's arising.
> One who tames afflictions
> Thereby tames all suffering.
> One who tames all suffering
> Thereby tames afflictions as well."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Thereupon that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1286)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, asking the Buddha:

> "If a person acts with heedlessness,
> Foolish and apart from evil wisdom,
> Through meditation, being heedful,
> One quickly exhausts all the āsravas."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "It is not the various things of the world
> That constitute desire.
> The dharmas of the mind that race after conceptual thoughts:
> These are called the desires of a person.
> The various things of the world
> Always remain in the world.
> The wise one who cultivates meditation
> Causes craving and desire to be forever subdued.
> Faith is a person's companion;
> Without faith, one cannot cross over.
> Faith increases one's renown;
> At life's end, one is reborn in heaven.
> Regarding the body, one contemplates emptiness;
> Name-and-form are not solid.
> One who does not cling to name-and-form
> Distances oneself from accumulation.
> Contemplate this true and real meaning,
> As one liberated out of compassion.
> Through this wisdom,
> The world praises and makes offerings.
> One who can sever various mixed characteristics
> And transcend the stream of birth and death,
> Having crossed over the currents:
> Such a one is called a bhikṣu."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Thereupon that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1287)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, asking the Buddha:

> "With whom should one associate?
> With whom should one work together?
> By understanding the dharma of what kind of person
> Is one called excellent and not evil?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Associate with upright persons,
> Work together with upright persons.
> Understanding the dharma of upright persons:
> This is called excellent and not evil."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Then that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1288)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "When stinginess arises in the heart,
> One is unable to practice generosity.
> A wise person who seeks merit
> Is the one who can practice giving."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "One who fears and does not give
> Always obtains the fear of not giving.
> Fearing hunger and thirst,
> Stinginess arises from fear.
> In this world and the next,
> The foolish always fear hunger and thirst.
> At death, it does not follow one in death;
> One goes alone, without provisions.
> One who is able to give though possessing little,
> And one who gives though it is difficult when possessing much:
> Giving what is difficult to give,
> This is called the most difficult generosity.
> The unknowing do not realize this;
> The wise know what is difficult to know.
> With the Dharma one supports one's wife and children,
> And with little wealth one gives with a pure heart.
> A hundred thousand grand ceremonial feasts,
> Whatever merit they may yield,
> Compared to the former giving in accordance with the Dharma,
> Do not equal one-sixteenth of it.
> Beating, binding, and tormenting sentient beings,
> The wealth obtained from such,
> Even if used for charitable giving to pacify the land:
> This is called giving with transgression.
> Compared to equal and impartial giving,
> It cannot be weighed or measured against it.
> Wealth obtained in accordance with the Dharma, not through wrongdoing,
> Giving from such wealth,
> Giving what is difficult to give,
> This corresponds to the giving of noble sages.
> Wherever one goes, one always obtains merit,
> And at life's end, one is reborn in heaven."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Then that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1289)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, on Mount Kiṃbala, in a stone cave at the dwelling place of the spirit Kiṃbala.

At that time, the World-Honored One's foot was pierced by a golden spear. Not long after, bodily pain arose. He was able to maintain an equanimous mind, with right knowledge and right mindfulness, enduring and bearing it with composure, without any thought of decline.

There were eight mountain-spirit devaputras who thought: "Today the World-Honored One is dwelling in Rājagṛha, on Mount Kiṃbala, in the stone cave at the dwelling place of the spirit Kiṃbala. His foot was pierced by a golden spear, and bodily pain has arisen, yet he is able to maintain an equanimous mind, with right mindfulness and right knowledge, enduring and bearing it with composure, without any decline. We should go and praise him before his face." Having had this thought, they went to the Buddha's dwelling place, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to stand at one side.

The first devaputra spoke a verse of praise:

> "The śramaṇa Gautama,
> A lion among men, experiencing bodily pain,
> Endures and bears it with composure,
> With right knowledge and right mindfulness, without any decline."

The second devaputra spoke further in praise:

> "A great being, a great dragon,
> A great being, a bull king,
> A great being of great courage,
> A great being, a fine steed,
> A great being, foremost among men,
> A great being, supreme among men."

The third devaputra spoke further in praise:

> "This śramaṇa Gautama,
> A white lotus among men,
> Though bodily pains have arisen,
> He is able to practice equanimity of mind,
> Abiding in right knowledge and right mindfulness,
> Enduring and bearing it with composure,
> Without any decline."

The fourth devaputra spoke further in praise: "If anyone speaks in opposition to, or criticizes, the śramaṇa Gautama, the white lotus among men, such persons should know that they will, through the long night, obtain what is unprofitable and suffering, except for those who do not know the truth."

The fifth devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Observe his samādhi concentration,
> Well-established in right attainment,
> Liberated, free from all defilements,
> Neither elated nor depressed.
> His mind abides in peace and tranquility,
> Having attained liberation of mind."

The sixth devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "For five hundred years,
> Reciting brāhmaṇical scriptures,
> Diligently practicing austerities,
> Yet not liberated from defilements:
> Such ones are of a base class;
> They cannot reach the other shore."

The seventh devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Driven by desires,
> Bound by precepts and rules,
> Vigorously performing austerities
> For a hundred years,
> Yet their minds are not liberated,
> Not freed from defilements:
> Such ones are of a base class;
> They cannot reach the other shore."

The eighth devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "The mind dwelling in pride and desire,
> Unable to tame and subdue itself,
> Not attaining the samādhi concentration
> Of the Muni's right attainment,
> Alone dwelling in mountain forests,
> With a mind always heedless:
> Against the army of Death,
> One cannot reach the other shore."

At that time, those eight mountain-spirit devaputras, having each spoken their praises, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet and instantly vanished.

---

### (1290)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Nothing is broader than the earth;
> Nothing is deeper than the sea;
> Nothing is higher than Mount Sumeru;
> Among great beings, none surpasses Viṣṇu."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Nothing is broader than craving;
> Nothing is deeper than the belly;
> Nothing is higher than pride;
> Among great beings, none surpasses the Buddha."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Thereupon that devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1291)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of surpassingly fine appearance, in the last watch of the night, came to the Buddha's dwelling place. He bowed his head at the Buddha's feet and withdrew to sit at one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, asking the Buddha:

> "What is not burned by fire?
> What cannot be blown away by wind?
> When floods destroy the great earth,
> What is not scattered and swept away?
> Evil kings and thieves
> Forcefully rob people of their wealth;
> What man or woman
> Is not despoiled by them?
> What treasure of precious things
> Is never ultimately lost?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Merit is not burned by fire;
> Merit is not blown away by wind.
> When floods destroy the great earth,
> The waters of merit are not scattered and swept away.
> Evil kings and thieves
> Forcefully rob people of their treasures;
> Whether man or woman,
> Merit is not despoiled.
> The treasure that yields joyful retribution
> Is never ultimately lost."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse, saying:

> "Long have I seen the brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears he has passed beyond,
> Forever transcending worldly love and attachment."

Thereu
