# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Forty-Nine

---

### (1294)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of supremely wonderful appearance, during the last watch of the night, came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "One of great power has sovereign bliss,
> Whatever is sought, nothing is unattained.
> What could be superior to that,
> Where all desires are fully supplied?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "One of great power with sovereign bliss,
> That one has nothing to seek.
> If there is one who has seeking and desire,
> That is suffering, not bliss.
> When seeking has already been transcended,
> That indeed is bliss beyond the other."

Then that devaputra spoke another verse:

> "Long have I beheld this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears have been surpassed,
> Forever transcending worldly attachments."

Thereupon, the devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1295)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of supremely wonderful appearance, during the last watch of the night, came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "From where does the chariot arise? Who is able to turn the chariot?
> Where does the turning chariot arrive? Why does it break down and wear away?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "The chariot arises from various actions; the mind-consciousness is able to turn the chariot.
> Following causes, it turns and arrives; when causes are destroyed, the chariot perishes."

Then that devaputra spoke another verse:

> "Long have I beheld this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears have been surpassed,
> Forever transcending worldly attachments."

Thereupon, the devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1296)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of supremely wonderful appearance, during the last watch of the night, came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Supratiṣṭhā, the daughter of King Koliya, has today given birth to a child."

The Buddha told the devaputra: "This is unwholesome, not wholesome."

Then that devaputra spoke a verse:

> "People rejoice when a child is born; the world delights in having children.
> When father and mother grow old and feeble, a child can support and nourish them.
> Gautama, why do you say that the birth of a child is unwholesome?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Know that there is constant impermanence; the aggregates are purely empty, not a child.
> Bearing children always brings suffering; the foolish call it happiness.
> Therefore I say: the birth of a child is not wholesome.
> What is unwholesome has the appearance of the wholesome; what is attractive is in truth unattractive.
> What is truly suffering appears as happiness; it is trodden upon by heedlessness."

Then that devaputra spoke another verse:

> "Long have I beheld this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears have been surpassed,
> Forever transcending worldly attachments."

Thereupon, the devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1297)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of supremely wonderful appearance, during the last watch of the night, came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "What is the count that is counted? What count is not concealed?
> What is the count within the count? What is called the spoken explanation?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "The Buddha-Dharma is hard to fathom; the two streams do not appear manifest.
> When name-and-form are extinguished completely without remainder,
> This is called the count that is counted; that count is not concealed.
> This is the count within the count; this is called the explanation of the count."

Then that devaputra spoke another verse:

> "Long have I beheld this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears have been surpassed,
> Forever transcending worldly attachments."

Thereupon, the devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1298)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of supremely wonderful appearance, during the last watch of the night, came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "What is heavier than the earth? What is higher than the sky?
> What is swifter than the wind? What is more numerous than grass?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Moral virtue is heavier than the earth; pride is higher than empty space.
> Recollection is swifter than the wind; thoughts are more numerous than grass."

Then that devaputra spoke another verse:

> "Long have I beheld this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears have been surpassed,
> Forever transcending worldly attachments."

Thereupon, the devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1299)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain devaputra of supremely wonderful appearance, during the last watch of the night, came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "What precepts, what deportment, what attainment, what conduct as livelihood?
> How should a wise one dwell? How does one proceed to be born in heaven?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Keep far from killing living beings; uphold precepts, guard oneself, and find happiness.
> Do not inflict a harmful mind upon beings; this is the path to birth in heaven.
> Keep far from taking what is not given; give with a joyful heart.
> Sever the mind of theft and robbery; this is the path to birth in heaven.
> Do not engage with what belongs to others; keep far from sexual misconduct.
> With what one has received, know contentment; this is the path to birth in heaven.
> Whether for oneself, for others, for wealth, or in jest,
> Do not engage in false speech; this is the path to birth in heaven.
> Sever divisive speech; do not separate others from their friends.
> Always intend to harmonize this side and that; this is the path to birth in heaven.
> Keep far from unpleasant words; speak gently without harming others.
> Always speak words that are pure and beautiful; this is the path to birth in heaven.
> Do not engage in insincere speech, in meaningless and unbeneficial words.
> Always accord with speech that accords with the Dharma; this is the path to birth in heaven.
> In village or on empty ground, seeing gain, saying 'I possess it';
> Do not practice such covetous thoughts; this is the path to birth in heaven.
> With a compassionate mind free of harmful thoughts, not harming living beings,
> With a mind always free of enmity and resentment; this is the path to birth in heaven.
> In karmic suffering and its fruits, generate pure faith in both.
> Uphold right view; this is the path to birth in heaven.
> Such are the wholesome dharmas, the ten kinds of pure courses of action.
> Receive them equally and hold them firmly; this is the path to birth in heaven."

Then that devaputra spoke another verse:

> "Long have I beheld this brāhmaṇa,
> Who has attained parinirvāṇa.
> All fears have been surpassed,
> Forever transcending worldly attachments."

Thereupon, the devaputra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1300)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Śakra, lord of the devas, during the last watch of the night, came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Śakra spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "What does life not know? What does life not perceive?
> What fetters life? What binds life?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Form is what life does not know; formations are what life does not perceive.
> The body fetters life; feeling binds the one who lives."

Śakra then spoke another verse:

> "Form is not life itself;
> This the Buddhas have taught.
> How does it come to ripen?
> In that profoundly hidden place,
> How does a lump of flesh abide?
> How does one know the body of life?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "The kalala is the beginning; from the kalala arises the arbuda.
> From the arbuda arises the lump of flesh; from the lump of flesh arises the firm and thick.
> From the firm and thick arise the limbs and joints, as well as hair and such.
> Form and the sense faculties gradually form the body.
> Through the mother's food and drink and such, that embryonic body is nourished and sustained."

At that time, Śakra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1301)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Dīrghajayin, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Dīrghajayin spoke a verse:

> "Well learn the subtle and wondrous teachings; associate closely with śramaṇas.
> Alone, without a companion or equal, with right thought, be silent and still."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Well learn the subtle and wondrous teachings; associate closely with śramaṇas.
> Alone, without a companion or equal, be silent and still, with all faculties at peace."

At that time, the devaputra Dīrghajayin, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1302)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Śibi, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Śibi spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "With whom should one dwell together? With what kind of person should one work?
> What kind of dharma should one know, which is progressively superior and not evil?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Dwell together with upright persons; work together with upright persons.
> One should know the dharma of upright persons; this is progressively superior and not evil."

At that time, the devaputra Śibi, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1303)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Candana Prabhāsa (Moon-Sovereign), of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Moon-Sovereign spoke a verse:

> "That one shall reach the ultimate goal, like a mosquito clinging to grass.
> If one gains right mindfulness, with one-pointed mind in proper concentration."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "That one shall reach the other shore, like a fish breaking through its net.
> Dwelling complete in meditative absorption, the mind always attains joy and happiness."

At that time, the devaputra Moon-Sovereign, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1304)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Viṣṇu, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Viṣṇu spoke a verse:

> "Making offerings to the Tathāgata, joy constantly increases.
> Delighting in the true Dharma and Vinaya, one learns following along without heedlessness."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "If one thus speaks of the Dharma, guarding against heedlessness,
> Because of non-heedlessness, one does not fall under Māra's power."

Thereupon, the devaputra Viṣṇu, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1305)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Pañcālacaṇḍa, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Pañcālacaṇḍa spoke a verse:

> "In a place of confusion and disturbance, the wise one is able to awaken.
> Through meditative thought, awakening to what is to be awakened; this is the power of the muni's contemplation."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Thoroughly knowing the dharma of confusion, with right awakening one attains nirvāṇa.
> If one gains right mindfulness, with one-pointed mind in proper concentration."

At that time, the devaputra Pañcālacaṇḍa, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1306)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Susīma, together with five hundred attendants, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the World-Honored One addressed the Venerable Ānanda: "Do you find it difficult not to rejoice in your heart at the Venerable Śāriputra's skillful exposition of the Dharma?" Ānanda said to the Buddha: "So it is, World-Honored One! What kind of person who is not foolish, not deluded, and has wisdom would not rejoice in the Venerable Śāriputra's skillful exposition of the Dharma? Why is that? The Venerable Śāriputra upholds the precepts, is learned in much, has few desires and knows contentment, is vigorous and devoted to seclusion, has right mindfulness firmly established, has wisdom and right concentration. He possesses swift wisdom, sharp wisdom, wisdom of liberation, decisive wisdom, great wisdom, vast wisdom, deep wisdom, unequaled wisdom, and the accomplishment of the jewel of wisdom. He is skilled in teaching and transforming, in instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening. He also constantly praises instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening. He constantly teaches the Dharma to the fourfold assembly without weariness."

The Buddha told Ānanda: "So it is, so it is, just as you have said. Ānanda! What kind of person who is not foolish, not deluded, and has wisdom would not rejoice upon hearing the Venerable Śāriputra's skillful exposition of the Dharma? Why is that? The bhikṣu Śāriputra upholds the precepts, is learned in much, has few desires and knows contentment, is vigorous, has right mindfulness, wisdom and right concentration. He possesses surpassing wisdom, swift wisdom, sharp wisdom, wisdom of liberation, decisive wisdom, great wisdom, vast wisdom, deep wisdom, unequaled wisdom, and the accomplishment of the jewel of wisdom. He is skilled in teaching and transforming, in instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening. He also constantly praises instructing, teaching, illuminating, and gladdening. He constantly teaches the Dharma to the fourfold assembly without weariness."

As the World-Honored One thus praised to the Venerable Ānanda, thus praising what Śāriputra had taught, in the same measure the devaputra Susīma and his attendants were filled with inner joy, and the light of their bodies increased in brightness, pure and radiant.

At that time, the devaputra Susīma, filled with inner joy and emitting pure radiant light from his body, spoke a verse:

> "Śāriputra is learned in much, of bright wisdom and balanced insight.
> He upholds the precepts and is well disciplined; he attains nirvāṇa without residue.
> Bearing this final body, he has vanquished the army of Māra."

At that time, the devaputra Susīma and his five hundred attendants, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed their approval, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1307)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Rohitassa (Red Horse), of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Rohitassa said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Is anyone able to travel beyond the edge of the world and reach a place where there is no birth, no old age, and no death?"

The Buddha told Rohitassa: "There is no one who can travel beyond the edge of the world and reach a place where there is no birth, no old age, and no death."

The devaputra Rohitassa said to the Buddha: "How wonderful, World-Honored One! Well has this meaning been spoken. As the World-Honored One has said: 'There is no one who can travel beyond the edge of the world and reach a place where there is no birth, no old age, and no death.' Why is that? World-Honored One, I myself recall my past life. I was named Rohitassa and was a non-Buddhist sage who had attained supernormal powers and was free from all sensual desires. At that time I thought: 'I have such swift supernormal feet that, like a strong man shooting a sharp arrow across the shadow of a palmyra tree, I can ascend from one Mount Sumeru to another, and with my feet stepping over the eastern sea, leap across to the western sea.' At that time I thought: 'Having accomplished such swift supernormal power, today I should seek the edge of the world.' Having so thought, I then set out on my journey. Apart from eating, drinking, relieving myself, and reducing my sleep, I traveled constantly for a hundred years, and at the end of that life, I was still unable to travel beyond the edge of the world and reach a place where there is no birth, no old age, and no death."

The Buddha told Rohitassa: "I now, with just this one-fathom body, declare the world, the arising of the world, the cessation of the world, and the path leading to the cessation of the world. Rohitassa, devaputra! What is the world? It is the five clinging-aggregates. What are the five? The form clinging-aggregate, the feeling clinging-aggregate, the perception clinging-aggregate, the formations clinging-aggregate, and the consciousness clinging-aggregate. These are called the world. What is the arising of the world? It is craving for future becoming, accompanied by delight and lust, delighting in this and that. This is called the arising of the world. What is the cessation of the world? When that craving for future becoming, accompanied by delight and lust, delighting in this and that, is completely cut off without remainder, abandoned, relinquished, exhausted, freed from desire, extinguished, stilled, and disappeared, this is called the cessation of the world. What is the path leading to the cessation of the world? It is the Noble Eightfold Path: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration. This is called the path leading to the cessation of the world.

"Rohitassa! Fully knowing the world, one severs the world. Fully knowing the arising of the world, one severs the arising of the world. Fully knowing the cessation of the world, one realizes the cessation of the world. Fully knowing the path leading to the cessation of the world, one cultivates that path to cessation. Rohitassa! If a bhikṣu, regarding the suffering of the world, knows it and severs it; regarding the arising of the world, knows it and severs it; regarding the cessation of the world, knows it and realizes it; regarding the path leading to the cessation of the world, knows it and cultivates it; Rohitassa! This is called reaching the edge of the world, transcending the craving of the world."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse again:

> "Never by traveling far has one reached the edge of the world.
> Without reaching the edge of the world, one never reaches the end of suffering.
> Therefore the muni is able to know the edge of the world,
> Well understanding the edge of the world; the holy life has been established.
> Regarding that edge of the world, the one who knows it with balanced awakening,
> This is called the practice of the noble and wise, crossing to the far shore of the world."

At that time, the devaputra Rohitassa, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1308)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha on the side of Mount Vipula. There were six devaputras who had formerly been non-Buddhist renunciants. The first was named Abhijña, the second Adhika-Abhijña, the third Arthadharṣin, the fourth Vīlamba, the fifth Akuṭa, and the sixth Kālama. They came to where the Buddha was.

The devaputra Abhijña then spoke a verse:

> "A bhikṣu, devoted and single-minded, constantly cultivating disenchantment,
> In the first and last watches of the night, contemplating and well guarding himself;
> Having heard what is spoken through seeing and hearing, he does not fall into hell."

The devaputra Adhika-Abhijña then spoke a verse:

> "Disenchanted with darkness, the mind constantly guards itself.
> Forever departing from the worldly dharmas of verbal disputes and arguments,
> From the Tathāgata, the great teacher, receiving the dharma of the śramaṇa,
> Well guarding the world, not allowing the creation of various evils."

The devaputra Arthadharṣin then spoke a verse:

> "Cutting, crushing, beating, and killing; making offerings to Kāśyapa.
> I do not see these as evil, nor do I see them as merit."

The devaputra Vīlamba then spoke a verse:

> "I speak of those Nirgranthas, the non-Buddhist Jñātiputras,
> Who went forth to practice the path, enduring difficult practices through the long night.
> Among the great teacher's followers, they stay far from false speech.
> I say that such persons are not far from arhatship."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "A dead, emaciated wild fox constantly travels with the lion,
> Yet throughout the day, being small and feeble, it cannot become a lion.
> The Nirgrantha's great teacher and his followers falsely praise themselves.
> These are evil-minded false speakers, far indeed from arhatship."

At that time, the demon king Māra Pāpīyān possessed the devaputra Akuṭa and spoke a verse:

> "Striving diligently, abandoning darkness, constantly guarding and maintaining seclusion,
> Deeply attached to subtle and wondrous forms, craving and delighting in the Brahmā world;
> I shall teach and convert these beings, enabling them to be born in the Brahmā heaven."

At that time, the World-Honored One had this thought: "What the devaputra Akuṭa has spoken in verse, this is Māra Pāpīyān who has added his power to him. It is not what the devaputra Akuṭa said from his own mind." He then spoke these words:

> "'Striving diligently, abandoning darkness, guarding and maintaining seclusion,
> Deeply attached to subtle and wondrous forms, craving and delighting in the Brahmā world;'
> He would teach and convert these beings, enabling them to be born in the Brahmā heaven."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a further verse in reply:

> "Whatever forms there may be, here or there,
> Or in empty space, each shining with separate light;
> Know that all of these do not escape Māra and Māra's bonds,
> Like dangling bait on a hook, hooking and catching a swimming fish."

At that time, those devaputras each thought: "Today the devaputra Akuṭa spoke a verse, and the śramaṇa Gautama says it was spoken by Māra. Why does the śramaṇa Gautama say it was spoken by Māra?"

At that time, the World-Honored One, knowing what the devaputras were thinking in their minds, addressed them: "The verse that the devaputra Akuṭa spoke today was not spoken from that devaputra's own mind. It was because Māra Pāpīyān added his power to him." He thus spoke these words:

> "'Striving diligently, abandoning darkness, guarding and maintaining seclusion,
> Deeply attached to subtle and wondrous forms, craving and delighting in the Brahmā heaven;'
> He would teach and convert these beings, enabling them to be born in the Brahmā heaven.
> Therefore I spoke this verse:
>
> 'Whatever forms there may be, here or there,
> Or in empty space, each shining with separate light;
> Know that all of these do not escape Māra and Māra's bonds,
> Like dangling bait on a hook, hooking and catching a swimming fish.'"

At that time, those devaputras further thought: "How wonderful! The śramaṇa Gautama possesses great supernormal power and great virtue, able to see Māra Pāpīyān, while we cannot see him. We should each speak verses to praise the śramaṇa Gautama." They then spoke verses:

> "Sever all notions of craving and attachment to embodied existence;
> May this good protector remove all false speech.
> If one wishes to sever craving and desire, one should make offerings to the great teacher.
> Sever craving for the three realms of existence; destroy false speech.
> Having severed the attachment to views, one should make offerings to the great teacher.
> In Rājagṛha, the foremost is Mount Vipula.
> Among the snow mountains, it is the greatest of mountains; among the golden-winged birds, it bears the name.
> In the eight directions, above and below, in all the realms of beings,
> Among all devas and humans, the Perfectly Enlightened One is supreme."

At that time, those devaputras, having spoken verses praising the Buddha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed their approval, bowed their heads at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1309)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Māgha, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Māgha spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "Killing what does one sleep peacefully? Killing what does one obtain true happiness?
> What kind of person, when killed, does Gautama praise?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "If one kills anger, one sleeps peacefully.
> Killing anger brings people joy.
> Anger is the root of poison; killing it is what I praise.
> Having killed that anger, through the long night one is free from worry and affliction."

Thereupon, the devaputra Māgha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1310)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Mīgika, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Mīgika spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "How many kinds of luminous lights are there that illuminate the world?
> I beseech the World-Honored One to explain: which light is supreme?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "There are three kinds of radiant light that illuminate the world:
> By day, the sun gives light; by night, the moon gives light;
> Lamp-fire illuminates both day and night, illuminating various forms here and there.
> Above, below, and in all directions, all beings are bathed in their light.
> Among the lights of humans and devas, the Buddha's light is supreme."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the devaputra Mīgika, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1311)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Dāmani, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Dāmani spoke a verse:

> "For the sake of the brāhmaṇa's task, strive to sever, do not grow weary;
> Sever all sensual desires, and do not seek to receive a future body."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "The brāhmaṇa has no more tasks; what needed to be done has been done.
> Only until one has not yet reached the shore does one day and night constantly strive with diligence.
> Having already reached the other shore and standing there, what further need to strive upon the shore?
> This is the brāhmaṇa, devoted to the meditation of exhausting all outflows.
> All worries and afflictions, blazing, have been forever severed.
> This is called reaching the other shore; in nirvāṇa there is nothing more to seek."

At that time, the devaputra Dāmani, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1312)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Tārakagandha, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "How many should be cut off? How many should be relinquished?
> How many dharmas should one further cultivate?
> Transcending how many kinds of accumulations is one called a bhikṣu who has crossed the flood?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Cut off five, relinquish five, further cultivate five dharmas.
> Transcend five kinds of accumulations; such a one is called a bhikṣu who has crossed the flood."

At that time, the devaputra Tārakagandha, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1313)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Kāma, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Kāma said to the Buddha: "Very difficult, World-Honored One! Very difficult, Well-Gone One!"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "What is learned is very difficult: to be complete in precepts and samādhi,
> To leave behind the household life, to delight in the tranquility of secluded dwelling."

The devaputra Kāma said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! It is very difficult to attain silence and stillness!"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Attaining what is difficult to attain in one's training, complete in precepts and samādhi,
> Day and night constantly devoted, cultivating and practicing what the mind delights in."

The devaputra Kāma said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! It is difficult to attain right concentration of mind!"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Difficult to maintain is the abiding in right concentration, with faculties and mind decisively settled.
> Able to sever the bonds of Māra, the death-demon; the noble one advances as he wishes."

The devaputra Kāma again said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! The perilous road is very difficult to travel!"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "On the difficult and perilous road, one should travel advancing in peace and happiness.
> The ignoble fall upon it, feet up and head pointing down.
> The noble and wise ride the straight path; the perilous road naturally becomes level."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the devaputra Kāma, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1314)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Kāma, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Kāma spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "What is the cause of lust and anger? Of displeasure and hair standing on end?
> From where does fear arise? From where do thoughts of awareness arise?
> Just as a young child depends upon and clings to its nursing mother."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Love arises and grows from oneself, like a banyan tree.
> It attaches everywhere it reaches, like a tangled thicket of vines and creepers.
> If one knows the cause of these, one awakens and opens to realization.
> One crosses the ocean of birth and death, and never again receives further existence."

At that time, the devaputra Kāma, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1315)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Candana, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Candana spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "Having heard that Gautama possesses great wisdom, with unobstructed knowledge and vision,
> What should one abide in, what should one learn, so as not to encounter evil in the next world?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Restrain body, speech, and mind; do not commit the three evil deeds.
> While dwelling as a householder, broadly gather companions and guests.
> With faith, generosity, wealth, and Dharma-giving, establish all through the Dharma.
> Abiding in this and learning this Dharma, there is no fear of the next world."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, the devaputra Candana, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1316)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Candana, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, that devaputra spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "Who crosses the various floods, diligent day and night without slackening?
> Without grasping and with no foothold, how does one avoid sinking and drowning?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "One complete in all precepts, with wisdom and proper concentration,
> Inwardly contemplating with right mindfulness, one can cross the flood difficult to cross.
> Not stained by thoughts of sensual desire, transcending attachment to form,
> With delight and craving completely exhausted, one does not enter into the unfathomable."

At that time, the devaputra Candana, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1317)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Kāśyapa, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Kāśyapa said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I now wish to speak of the bhikṣu and the bhikṣu's virtues."

The Buddha told the devaputra: "Speak as you wish."

Then the devaputra Kāśyapa spoke a verse:

> "A bhikṣu cultivates right mindfulness; his mind is well liberated.
> Day and night he constantly and diligently seeks; he harbors all manner of virtues.
> He thoroughly knows the world and extinguishes all forms of existence.
> The bhikṣu attains freedom from worry; his mind is without any attachment.

"World-Honored One! This is called a bhikṣu; this is called the bhikṣu's virtues."

The Buddha told Kāśyapa: "Excellent! Excellent! It is as you have said."

The devaputra Kāśyapa, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1318)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Śrāvastī, in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a devaputra named Kāśyapa, of supremely wonderful appearance, who during the last watch of the night came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to sit to one side. The radiance of his body illuminated all of Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, the devaputra Kāśyapa said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I now wish to speak of the bhikṣu and what the bhikṣu has spoken."

The Buddha told the devaputra Kāśyapa: "Speak as you please!"

Then the devaputra Kāśyapa spoke a verse:

> "A bhikṣu guards right mindfulness; his mind is well liberated.
> Day and night he constantly and diligently seeks; he attains the state free from dust and defilement.
> Clearly understanding and knowing the world, from dust he is free of dust and defilement.
> The bhikṣu is without worry and affliction; his mind is without any attachment.

"World-Honored One! This is called a bhikṣu; this is called what the bhikṣu has spoken."

The Buddha told Kāśyapa: "So it is, so it is, just as you have said."

The devaputra Kāśyapa, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1319)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was in the land of Magadha, traveling among the people. At dusk, together with five hundred bhikṣus, he lodged at the dwelling place of the yakṣa Kumbhīra. At that time, the yakṣa Kumbhīra came to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and withdrew to stand to one side.

At that time, the yakṣa Kumbhīra said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! I now invite the World-Honored One and the great assembly to lodge here tonight."

At that time, the World-Honored One silently accepted the invitation.

Then, the yakṣa Kumbhīra, knowing the World-Honored One had silently accepted, magically created five hundred multi-storied chambers, with reclining beds, sitting beds, and squatting beds, along with five hundred sets each of folded blankets and pillows, all magically created. He magically created five hundred lamps, without any smoke or flame. Having created all these, he went to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and invited the World-Honored One to enter his dwelling. He had the bhikṣus in turn receive their chambers and bedding. When all had completely received theirs, he returned to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and spoke a verse:

> "The virtuous one possesses right mindfulness; the virtuous one is constantly mindful.
> With right mindfulness, one sleeps in peace and safety, in this world and the next.
> The virtuous one possesses right mindfulness; the virtuous one is constantly mindful.
> With right mindfulness, one sleeps in peace and safety; one's mind is always tranquil and still.
> The virtuous one possesses right mindfulness; the virtuous one is constantly mindful.
> With right mindfulness, one sleeps in peace and safety; one has abandoned and conquered the enemy's army.
> The virtuous one possesses right mindfulness; the virtuous one is constantly mindful.
> One does not kill nor instruct others to kill; one does not subjugate nor instruct others to subjugate.
> With a compassionate mind toward all, the mind harbors no enmity or resentment."

At that time, the World-Honored One told the yakṣa Kumbhīra: "So it is, so it is, just as you have said."

At that time, the yakṣa Kumbhīra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and returned to his own dwelling place.

---

### (1320)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling at Mount Makula, with the Venerable Nāgapāla as his personal attendant.

At that time, the World-Honored One, during the dark of night when a light rain was falling and lightning was flashing, went outside his dwelling and walked in meditation in the open air.

At that time, Śakra, lord of the devas, had this thought: "Today the World-Honored One is dwelling at Mount Makula, with the Venerable Nāgapāla personally attending and serving him. The night is dark, the sky rains lightly, and lightning flashes. The World-Honored One has left his dwelling and walks in meditation in the open air. I should magically create a pavilion of vaiḍūrya, hold it, and follow the Buddha as he walks in meditation." Having so thought, he immediately created a pavilion of vaiḍūrya, brought it to where the Buddha was, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and followed the Buddha as he walked in meditation.

At that time, when the men and women of Magadha had children who cried at night, they would frighten them by invoking the Makula ghost, and the children would then stop crying. According to the attendant-disciple's rule, one waits until the teacher finishes meditation before going to sleep. At that time, the World-Honored One walked in meditation during the night for a long time for the sake of Śakra.

At that time, the Venerable Nāgapāla had this thought: "The World-Honored One has been walking in meditation tonight for a very long time. I should now take on the form of the Makula ghost to frighten him!" Then the bhikṣu Nāgapāla reversed his robe, wearing it inside out with the long hair on the outside, went to the head of the World-Honored One's walking meditation path, and said to the Buddha: "The Makula ghost is coming! The Makula ghost is coming!"

At that time, the World-Honored One told the bhikṣu Nāgapāla: "You, Nāgapāla, foolish man! Do you try to frighten the Buddha with the appearance of the Makula ghost? You cannot disturb even a single hair of the Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One. The Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One has long since been free of all fear!"

At that time, Śakra said to the Buddha: "World-Honored One! Are there even such people in the World-Honored One's true Dharma and Vinaya?"

The Buddha said: "Kauśika! The Gautama family is extremely vast and broad. These beings, in the future, shall also be caused to attain the pure Dharma."

At that time, the World-Honored One then spoke a verse:

> "If a brāhmaṇa, regarding the Dharma he himself has attained,
> Has reached the other shore; whether a piśāca
> Or a makula, all are completely transcended.
> If a brāhmaṇa, regarding the Dharma he himself practices,
> Has observed that all various feelings and perceptions are extinguished;
> If a brāhmaṇa has crossed to the other shore by his own Dharma,
> All various causes and conditions are completely exhausted;
> If a brāhmaṇa has crossed to the other shore by his own Dharma,
> All notions of self and person are completely extinguished;
> If a brāhmaṇa has crossed to the other shore by his own Dharma,
> Birth, old age, sickness, and death are all completely transcended."

When the Buddha had finished speaking this sūtra, Śakra, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed his approval, bowed his head at the Buddha's feet, and instantly vanished.

---

### (1321)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha, in the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the Venerable Anuruddha was traveling among the people of Magadha and came to lodge at the dwelling place of the yakṣiṇī Piṅgala and her children. During the last part of the night, the Venerable Anuruddha sat upright with his body straight and recited the Udāna, the Pārāyaṇa, the Seeing of True Reality, the verses spoken by the senior monks, the verses spoken by the bhikṣuṇīs, the Śaila verses, the Arthavargīya, the Munigāthā, and the sūtras, reciting them all extensively.

At that time, the child of the yakṣiṇī Piṅgala was crying at night. The yakṣiṇī mother Piṅgala spoke a verse to her child to scold and silence it:

> "Child of Piṅgala, do not cry now!
> Listen to that bhikṣu reciting and studying the Dharma-verses.
> If one knows the Dharma-verses, one is able to guard and uphold the precepts oneself.
> Keeping far from killing living beings, speaking truthfully without false speech,
> Able to relinquish what is unwholesome, one is liberated from the path of ghosts and spirits."

When the yakṣiṇī mother Piṅgala spoke this verse, the crying of the child of Piṅgala immediately ceased.

---

### (1322)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Magadha, together with a great assembly. They came to lodge at the dwelling place of the yakṣiṇī Pūṇā and her children.

At that time, the World-Honored One taught the bhikṣus the Dharma connected with the Four Noble Truths, namely the noble truth of suffering, the noble truth of the arising of suffering, the noble truth of the cessation of suffering, and the noble truth of the path leading to the cessation of suffering.

At that time, the yakṣiṇī mother Pūṇā's son Pūṇābhadra and her yakṣa daughter Uttarā, the two small yakṣa children, were crying at night. Then the yakṣiṇī mother Pūṇā, in order to instruct her son and daughter, spoke a verse:

> "You, Pūṇābhadra, and Uttarā, do not cry!
> Let me hear the Dharma that the Tathāgata teaches.
> It is not father or mother who can cause their children to be liberated from suffering.
> By hearing the Dharma taught by the Tathāgata, one's suffering is liberated.
> Worldly people follow craving and desire, oppressed by manifold sufferings.
> The Tathāgata teaches the Dharma for them, to destroy birth and death.
> I now wish to hear the Dharma; you two should be silent."
>
> Then Pūṇābhadra, and the yakṣa daughter Uttarā,
> All accepted their mother's words, were silent and listened quietly.
> They said to their mother: "How excellent! We too delight in hearing the Dharma.
> This Perfectly Enlightened World-Honored One, upon the supreme mountain of Magadha,
> For the sake of all classes of beings, expounds the Dharma of liberation from suffering.
> He teaches suffering and the cause of suffering, the cessation of suffering and the path to end suffering.
> From these Four Noble Truths, one proceeds in safety to nirvāṇa.
> Mother, now simply listen well to the Dharma taught by the World-Honored One."

Then the yakṣiṇī mother Pūṇā spoke a verse:

> "How wonderful, my wise children, who so well follow my heart!
> You, Pūṇābhadra, well praise the Buddha, the guiding teacher.
> You, Pūṇābhadra, and you, Uttarā,
> You should give rise to hearts of sympathetic joy; I have already seen the Noble Truths."

When the yakṣiṇī mother Pūṇā spoke this verse, the yakṣa son and daughter rejoiced and fell silent.

---

### (1323)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Magadha, together with the great assembly, and came to lodge at the dwelling place of the yakṣa Maṇicara for the night.

At that time, the yakṣa Maṇicara had assembled various ghosts and spirits, gathered together in one place.

Then a certain woman, bearing incense, flowers, garlands, ornaments, food, and drink, came to the dwelling place of the yakṣa spirit Maṇicara. That woman saw from afar the World-Honored One sitting at the dwelling place of the yakṣa spirit Maṇicara. Seeing this, she thought: "I am now seeing the yakṣa spirit Maṇicara himself." She then spoke a verse:

> "How excellent, Maṇicara, dwelling in the land of Magadha!
> The people of Magadha obtain whatever they wish.
> How, in this world, can one always dwell in peace and happiness?
> And in the next world, how can one obtain the happiness of rebirth in heaven?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Do not be heedless and arrogant; of what use is the Maṇi spirit?
> If you yourself cultivate your own practice, you can attain the happiness of rebirth in heaven."

At that time, the woman thought: "This is not the yakṣa Maṇicara; this is the śramaṇa Gautama." Knowing this, she then offered incense, flowers, garlands, and ornaments to the World-Honored One, bowed her head at his feet, withdrew to sit to one side, and spoke a verse:

> "What path leads to peace and happiness? What practices should one cultivate?
> So that in this world one is always safe and secure, and in the next world one is born in heavenly bliss?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Generosity, well-taming the mind, joyfully guarding the sense faculties,
> Right view, cultivating virtuous conduct, associating closely with śramaṇas;
> With right livelihood sustaining oneself, in the next world one is born in heavenly bliss.
> What use are the thirty-three heavens, with the suffering-nets of the devas?
> One should only unify one's mind and sever craving and desire.
> I shall teach the stainless, the ambrosial Dharma; listen well."

At that time, the woman heard the World-Honored One's teaching, and he instructed, taught, illuminated, and gladdened her. As is the Buddha's constant way, he spoke of generosity, upholding precepts, the merit of rebirth in heaven, the taste of desire, the danger of desire, the afflictions, and the pure way of renunciation, liberation, and seclusion, with their merits and blessings, expounding in sequence the pure Buddha-Dharma. Just as a clean, fresh white cloth is easily dyed with color, so too was that woman. Right there on her seat, regarding the Four Noble Truths, she attained the balanced contemplation of suffering, its arising, its cessation, and the path.

At that time, the woman saw the Dharma, obtained the Dharma, knew the Dharma, entered the Dharma, crossed beyond all doubt and perplexity, not relying on others, and in the true Dharma and Vinaya attained fearlessness. She then rose from her seat, straightened her garments, joined her palms, and said to the Buddha: "I have crossed over, World-Honored One! I have crossed over, Well-Gone One! From this day forth, for the rest of my life, I take refuge in the Buddha, take refuge in the Dharma, take refuge in the Sangha of bhikṣus."

At that time, the woman, having heard what the Buddha said, rejoiced and expressed her approval, bowed to the Buddha, and departed.

---

### (1324)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Magadha and came to lodge at the dwelling place of the yakṣa Sūciloma (Needle-Hair) for the night. At that time, the yakṣa Sūciloma had assembled various ghosts and spirits, gathered together in one place.

Then a yakṣa named Khara saw the World-Honored One lodging at the dwelling place of the yakṣa Sūciloma for the night. Seeing this, he went to where the yakṣa Sūciloma was and said to the yakṣa Sūciloma: "Village chief! Today you have obtained great and good fortune. Now the Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One is lodging at your dwelling!"

The yakṣa Sūciloma said: "Let me test and see whether this is the Tathāgata or not."

At that time, the yakṣa Sūciloma, after the assembly of ghosts and spirits had concluded, returned to his own dwelling. He contracted his body and charged at the Buddha. At that time, the World-Honored One drew back his body to avoid him. In this way three times, he contracted his body and charged at the Buddha, and the Buddha likewise three times drew back his body to avoid him.

At that time, the yakṣa Sūciloma said: "Is the śramaṇa afraid?"

The Buddha said: "Village chief! I am not afraid, but your touch is foul."

The yakṣa Sūciloma said: "I now have something to ask. You should explain it for me. If you can make me happy, well and good. If you cannot make me happy, I will destroy your mind, split open your chest, make hot blood come out of your face, seize your two arms, and hurl you to the other bank of the Ganges."

The Buddha told the yakṣa Sūciloma: "Village chief! I do not see anyone among devas, Māras, Brahmās, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, celestial spirits, or worldly people who can destroy the mind of the Tathāgata, Arhat, Perfectly Enlightened One, or who can split
