# 雜阿含經
## Saṃyuktāgama (Connected Discourses)

Taishō Tripiṭaka No. 0099

Translated from Chinese. Liu Song dynasty, translated by Guṇabhadra.

## Fascicle Fifty

---

### (1325)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, there was an upāsikā whose son had undertaken the eight-limbed observance day (upoṣadha), but immediately broke the precepts, and was thereupon seized by a ghost-spirit.

At that time, the upāsikā spoke this verse:

> "On the fourteenth and fifteenth days,
> and the eighth day of each half-month,
> in the month of miraculous portents,
> the eight-limbed observance rightly undertaken,
> one who upholds the observance precepts
> is not seized by ghosts.
> In the past I repeatedly inquired,
> and the World-Honored One spoke thus."

At that time, that ghost spoke this verse:

> "On the fourteenth and fifteenth days,
> and the eighth day of each half-month,
> in the month of miraculous portents,
> the eight-limbed observance rightly cultivated,
> abiding in the observance with purity and reverence,
> with precept-virtue well guarded,
> one is not toyed with by ghosts.
> How excellent, what was heard from the Buddha!
> You should say the word 'release,'
> and I shall release your son.
> Those whose practice is slack and careless,
> who cultivate austerities with defilement,
> whose holy conduct is not pure,
> will never obtain great fruit.
> Just as when pulling kośa grass,
> if one grasps loosely, it cuts the hand;
> a śramaṇa who commits evil deeds
> shall fall into hell.
> Just as when pulling kośa grass,
> if one grasps firmly, it does not cut the hand;
> a śramaṇa who restrains well
> shall reach parinirvāṇa."

Then that ghost-spirit released the upāsikā's son. At that time, the upāsikā spoke a verse to tell her son:

> "Son, now listen to me,
> I shall tell what that ghost-spirit said.
> If one's practice is slack and careless,
> cultivating austerities with defilement,
> with impure holy conduct,
> such a one will not obtain great fruit.
> Just as when pulling kośa grass,
> if one grasps loosely, it cuts the hand;
> a śramaṇa who commits evil deeds
> shall fall into hell.
> As when firmly grasping kośa grass,
> it does not cut the hand;
> a śramaṇa who guards well
> attains parinirvāṇa."

At that time, the upāsikā's son, having been thus awakened, shaved off his hair and beard, donned the kāṣāya robe, and with right faith left the household life to go forth and study the Way. Yet his mind did not find joy, and he returned to his own home. His mother saw her son from afar and spoke this verse:

> "You left the world to go forth;
> why do you return to the village?
> When a house is burning, one rushes out with one's goods;
> how could one then throw them back into the fire?"

Her son, the bhikṣu, spoke a verse in reply:

> "I only thought of my mother's death;
> alive or dead, we would not see each other again.
> Therefore I came back to look upon you;
> why do you not rejoice at seeing your son?"

Then the mother, the upāsikā, spoke a verse in reply:

> "You renounced desires and went forth;
> now you wish to consume them again.
> Therefore I grieve and worry,
> fearing you follow Māra's control."

At that time, the upāsikā thus and thus awakened her son. Thus, her son returned to a secluded place, diligently and earnestly contemplating, cut off all afflictions and fetters, and attained the fruit of arhatship.

---

### (1326)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Magadha and came to the dwelling place of the yakṣa Āḷavaka, where he stayed for the night.

At that time, the yakṣa Āḷavaka had gathered together the various ghost-spirits for an assembly. At that time, a yakṣa named Gadrabha saw the World-Honored One staying the night at the dwelling place of the yakṣa Āḷavaka. Having seen this, he went to the yakṣa Āḷavaka and said to him: "Village chief! You have gained great benefit; the Tathāgata is staying at your dwelling place."

The yakṣa Āḷavaka said: "Is a living person really staying in my abode today? I shall now make him know whether he is a Tathāgata or not!"

At that time, after the yakṣa Āḷavaka's assembly of ghost-spirits had concluded, he returned to his own dwelling and said to the World-Honored One: "Get out, śramaṇa!"

At that time, the World-Honored One, out of consideration for another's home, went out of the dwelling.

The yakṣa Āḷavaka again said: "Śramaṇa, come in!" The Buddha then went back in. In order to subdue pride, this happened three times.

Then the yakṣa Āḷavaka a fourth time said to the World-Honored One: "Śramaṇa, get out!"

At that time, the World-Honored One said to the yakṣa Āḷavaka: "Village chief! You have already summoned me three times. Now I shall not go out again."

The yakṣa Āḷavaka said: "Now I shall ask the śramaṇa a question. If the śramaṇa answers and can make me glad, it will be well. If you cannot make me glad, I shall destroy your mind, split open your chest, cause hot blood to pour from your face, seize you by both hands, and hurl you to the far bank of the Ganges!"

The World-Honored One replied: "Village chief! I see no one among devas, Māra, Brahmā, śramaṇas, brāhmaṇas, celestial spirits, or worldly people who could destroy my mind, split open my chest, cause hot blood to pour from my face, seize me by both hands, and hurl me to the far bank of the Ganges. Nevertheless, village chief, you may ask your question, and I shall explain it to you so that your mind may be gladdened."

Then the yakṣa Āḷavaka spoke a verse to question the Buddha:

> "What is declared to be the supreme
> possession of a worthy person?
> By practicing what dharma
> does one obtain a happy reward?
> What is the finest of flavors?
> What is supreme among lives?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Pure faith is the most supreme
> possession of a worthy person.
> Practicing the Dharma, one obtains happy fruit.
> The taste of liberation is the highest of all.
> Wisdom, which removes aging and death,
> is supreme among lives."

Then the yakṣa Āḷavaka spoke another verse:

> "How does one gain renown?" And so forth as stated in the above verses.

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Upholding precepts, renown flows forth." And so forth as stated in the above verses.

Then the yakṣa Āḷavaka spoke another verse:

> "How many things give rise to the world?
> How many things bring harmony to the world?
> How many things does the world grasp and cling to?
> How many things diminish the world?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Six things give rise to the world.
> Six things bring harmony to the world.
> Six things the world grasps and clings to.
> Six things diminish the world."

The yakṣa Āḷavaka spoke another verse to question the Buddha:

> "Who can cross over the floods,
> striving diligently day and night?
> With nothing to cling to and no resting place,
> who is able to not be submerged?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "One endowed with all the precepts,
> with wisdom and right samādhi,
> with right mindfulness and inner contemplation,
> can cross the flood so hard to cross.
> Not delighting in the five sensual desires,
> and also transcending attachment to form,
> with nothing to cling to and no resting place,
> such a one is not submerged or drowned."

Then the yakṣa Āḷavaka spoke another verse to question the Buddha:

> "By what dharma does one cross the flood?
> By what does one cross the great ocean?
> By what does one abandon suffering?
> By what does one attain purity?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "By faith one crosses the river-flood.
> By heedfulness one crosses the ocean.
> By vigor one can remove suffering.
> By wisdom one attains purity.
> You may further question other
> śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas about the Dharma,
> but there is no dharma surpassing
> truth, generosity, and self-discipline."

Then the yakṣa Āḷavaka spoke another verse to question the Buddha:

> "Why should I bother asking other
> śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas about the Dharma?
> Today the most supreme being
> has revealed the great torch of Dharma.
> To that Gadrabha
> I should always repay his kindness,
> for he told me of the Perfectly Awakened One,
> the unsurpassed guiding teacher.
> From today I shall travel
> from village to village,
> attending upon the Perfectly Awakened One,
> listening to and receiving the Dharma he teaches."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the yakṣa Āḷavaka was delighted, rejoiced, paid respects, and departed.

---

### (1327)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, there was a bhikṣuṇī named Śukla dwelling among the bhikṣuṇī Sangha at the King's Park in Rājagṛha, who was revered and supplied with offerings by the people of Rājagṛha as if she were an arhat.

On another occasion, the people of Rājagṛha gathered together for a great festival on an auspicious star-day. On that very day, the offerings to her were neglected. A certain ghost-spirit, out of reverence for that bhikṣuṇī, went into the streets and lanes of Rājagṛha, and at every house spoke this verse:

> "People of Rājagṛha,
> drunk on wine, lying asleep,
> not diligently making offerings to
> the bhikṣuṇī Śukla!
> Because she has well cultivated her faculties,
> she is called Śukla.
> She skillfully teaches the stainless Dharma,
> the cool place of nirvāṇa.
> Following and listening to what she teaches,
> all day one delights without weariness.
> Riding on the wisdom gained from hearing the Dharma,
> one crosses the flood of birth and death,
> like ocean-going merchants
> who depend upon the powerful horse-king."

At that time, one upāsaka made an offering of cloth to the bhikṣuṇī Śukla. Another upāsaka made an offering of food.

Then that ghost-spirit spoke this verse:

> "The wise upāsaka
> gains abundant merit and benefit,
> giving cloth to Śukla,
> who is free from all afflictions.
> The wise upāsaka
> gains abundant merit and benefit,
> giving food to Śukla,
> who is free from all accumulations."

After that ghost-spirit had spoken these verses, it vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1328)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, the bhikṣuṇī Vīrā was dwelling among the bhikṣuṇī Sangha at the King's Park in Rājagṛha. The people of Rājagṛha gathered together for a great festival on an auspicious star-day. On that day, no one made offerings to the bhikṣuṇī Vīrā.

At that time, a ghost-spirit who revered the bhikṣuṇī Vīrā entered Rājagṛha and went to the various streets, lanes, and crossroads, and spoke this verse:

> "People of Rājagṛha,
> drunk on wine, stupefied and sleeping,
> the bhikṣuṇī Vīrā
> has no one to make offerings to her!
> The bhikṣuṇī Vīrā
> heroically cultivates her faculties,
> skillfully teaches the stainless Dharma,
> the cool Dharma of nirvāṇa.
> All who follow what she teaches,
> all day delight without weariness.
> Riding on the wisdom gained from hearing the Dharma,
> one crosses the flood of birth and death."

At that time, one upāsaka offered cloth to the bhikṣuṇī Vīrā. Another upāsaka offered food.

Then that ghost-spirit spoke this verse:

> "The wise upāsaka
> now gains great merit and benefit,
> giving cloth to the fetter-cutter,
> the bhikṣuṇī Vīrā.
> The wise upāsaka
> now gains great merit and benefit,
> giving food to the bhikṣuṇī Vīrā,
> who is free from all compounded things."

After that ghost-spirit had spoken these verses, it vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1329)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, there were two celestial spirits, Sātāgiri and Hemavata, who had made a mutual agreement: "If an extraordinary treasure appears in either of our palaces, we must inform each other. Whoever fails to inform the other shall incur the penalty for violating the agreement."

At that time, in the palace of the celestial spirit Hemavata, an unprecedented treasure appeared: a padma flower with a thousand petals, as large as a chariot wheel, with a golden, jeweled stem.

Then the celestial spirit Hemavata sent a messenger to tell Sātāgiri: "Village chief! In my palace there has suddenly appeared an unprecedented treasure: a padma flower with a thousand petals, as large as a chariot wheel, with a golden, jeweled stem. You should come and see it!"

The celestial spirit Sātāgiri sent a messenger to Hemavata's abode, saying: "Village chief! What use are hundreds of thousands of padma flowers? In my palace there has appeared an unprecedented great padma, namely the Tathāgata, Arhat, Samyaksaṃbuddha, Perfect in Knowledge and Conduct, Well-Gone One, Knower of the World, Unsurpassed One, Tamer of Men, Teacher of Devas and Humans, the Buddha, the World-Honored One. You should come and serve and make offerings to him."

At that time, the celestial spirit Hemavata, together with five hundred attendants, went to the abode of the celestial spirit Sātāgiri and spoke a verse to ask:

> "On the fifteenth, an auspicious time,
> the celestial night encounters a joyful assembly.
> What observance should one undertake,
> and from what arhat should one receive it?"

Then Sātāgiri spoke a verse in reply:

> "Today the Buddha, the World-Honored One,
> is in the supreme land of Magadha,
> dwelling in the city of Rājagṛha,
> at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove,
> expounding the subtle and wondrous Dharma,
> eliminating the suffering of sentient beings:
> suffering itself, and the arising of suffering,
> the cessation of suffering fully realized,
> and the Noble Eightfold Path leading out of suffering,
> peacefully tending toward nirvāṇa.
> We should go and make offerings
> to our arhat, the World-Honored One."

Hemavata spoke a verse to ask:

> "Does he have a mind of aspiration and joy,
> with compassion that saves all beings?
> Regarding what is received and not received,
> is his mind's perception equal?"

Sātāgiri spoke a verse in reply:

> "He has a wondrous, aspirational, compassionate mind,
> saving all sentient beings.
> Regarding what is received and not received,
> his mind's perception is always equal."

Then Hemavata spoke a verse to ask:

> "Is he fully endowed with knowledge and insight?
> Has his conduct been accomplished?
> Are all outflows permanently extinguished?
> Does he not receive any further existence?"

Sātāgiri spoke a verse in reply:

> "Knowledge and insight are well and fully accomplished.
> Right conduct has been completed.
> All outflows are permanently extinguished.
> He does not again receive any further existence."

Hemavata spoke a verse to ask:

> "Is the Muni's mind-action fulfilled,
> as well as his bodily and verbal deeds?
> Are knowledge and conduct completely accomplished,
> so that he is praised according to the Dharma?"

Sātāgiri spoke a verse in reply:

> "The Muni's mind is fully accomplished,
> and his bodily and verbal deeds are fulfilled.
> Knowledge and conduct are completely accomplished,
> and he is praised according to the Dharma."

Hemavata spoke a verse to ask:

> "Is he far removed from harming living beings?
> Does he not take what is not given?
> Is he far removed from dissipation?
> Does he not depart from meditative contemplation?"

Sātāgiri spoke another verse:

> "He always does not harm living beings.
> He does not wrongly take what is not given.
> He is far removed from dissipation.
> Day and night he constantly contemplates in meditation."

Hemavata spoke another verse to ask:

> "Does he not delight in the five sensual desires?
> Is his mind free from turbidity and confusion?
> Does he have the pure Dharma-eye?
> Has he destroyed all delusion?"

Sātāgiri spoke a verse in reply:

> "His mind constantly does not delight in desires.
> He also has no turbid or confused mind.
> The Buddha's Dharma-eye is pure.
> Delusion is exhausted without remainder."

Hemavata spoke another verse to ask:

> "Does he speak truthfully without falsehood?
> Is he free from coarse and harsh speech?
> Does he not engage in divisive speech?
> Is he free from insincere speech?"

Sātāgiri spoke a verse in reply:

> "He speaks truthfully without falsehood.
> He also has no coarse or harsh speech.
> He does not divide others' friendships.
> He always speaks words in accord with the Dharma."

Hemavata spoke another verse to ask:

> "Does he uphold the pure precepts?
> Is his right mindfulness tranquil?
> Is he endowed with equal liberation?
> Does he possess the Tathāgata's great wisdom?"

Sātāgiri spoke a verse in reply:

> "His pure precepts are fully accomplished.
> His right mindfulness is always tranquil.
> Equal liberation is accomplished.
> He has attained the Tathāgata's great wisdom."

Hemavata spoke another verse to ask:

> "Knowledge and insight are fully accomplished.
> Right conduct has been purified.
> All outflows whatsoever are exhausted.
> He does not again receive any further existence."

Sātāgiri spoke a verse in reply:

> "Knowledge and insight are fully accomplished.
> Right conduct has been purified.
> All outflows whatsoever are exhausted.
> There is no further becoming or existence."

Hemavata spoke another verse:

> "The Muni's wholesome mind is complete,
> as well as the traces of his bodily and verbal deeds.
> Knowledge and conduct are all accomplished,
> therefore one praises his Dharma."

Sātāgiri spoke a verse in reply:

> "The Muni's wholesome mind is complete,
> as well as the traces of his bodily and verbal deeds.
> Knowledge and conduct are all accomplished;
> one praises his Dharma."

Hemavata spoke another verse:

> "With deer-like Aiṇeya limbs,
> the supreme marks of a sage,
> eating little, free from bodily greed,
> the Muni dwells in forest meditation.
> You should now go together with me
> to pay respects to that Gautama."

At that time, surrounded by hundreds of thousands of ghost-spirit attendants, Sātāgiri and Hemavata swiftly arrived before the Buddha to bow and make offerings. They arranged their robes, bared the right shoulder, joined their palms in reverence, and spoke this verse:

> "With deer-like Aiṇeya limbs,
> the supreme marks of a sage,
> eating little, free from craving,
> the Muni delights in forest meditation.
> We have now come here
> to ask a question of Gautama.
> Lion who walks alone,
> great dragon free from fear,
> we have now come to ask;
> O Muni, please resolve our doubts.
> How does one escape from suffering?
> How does one gain liberation from suffering?
> We pray that you explain liberation:
> where is suffering extinguished?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "The five sensual desire-objects of the world,
> and the sixth, which is called mind:
> toward these, being free from craving,
> one is liberated from all suffering.
> Thus one escapes from suffering.
> Thus one is liberated from suffering.
> Now I answer what you ask:
> suffering is extinguished from these."

Sātāgiri and Hemavata spoke another verse to question the Buddha:

> "From where does the spring-source turn back?
> Where does the evil path cease to turn?
> Where are the world's sufferings and joys
> completely extinguished?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "The eye, ear, nose, tongue, body,
> and the sense-base of mind:
> when name-and-form are there
> permanently extinguished without remainder,
> it is there that the spring-source turns back.
> It is there that the path ceases to turn.
> It is there that suffering and joy
> attain complete extinction without remainder."

Sātāgiri and Hemavata spoke another verse to question the Buddha:

> "How many things give rise to the world?
> How many things bring the world together?
> How many things does the world grasp and receive?
> How many things cause the world to decline?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "Six things give rise to the world.
> Six things bring the world together.
> Six things the world grasps and receives.
> Six things cause the world to decline."

Sātāgiri and Hemavata spoke another verse to ask:

> "How does one cross the floods,
> striving diligently day and night?
> With nothing to cling to and no resting place,
> yet not drown in the deep abyss?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "One endowed with all the precepts,
> with wisdom and right samādhi,
> contemplating thus with mindful recollection,
> one is able to cross the deep abyss.
> Not delighting in perceptions of desire,
> and also transcending the bonds of form,
> with nothing to cling to and no resting place,
> one does not drown in the deep abyss."

Sātāgiri and Hemavata spoke another verse to question the Buddha:

> "By what dharma does one cross the floods?
> By what does one cross the great ocean?
> How does one abandon suffering?
> How does one attain purity?"

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke a verse in reply:

> "By right faith one crosses the river-flood.
> By heedfulness one crosses the ocean.
> By vigor one can sever suffering.
> By wisdom one attains purity."

At that time, the World-Honored One spoke another verse:

> "You may further question other
> śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas about the Dharma,
> but there is no dharma surpassing
> truth, generosity, and self-discipline."

Hemavata spoke another verse:

> "Why should I further question other
> śramaṇas and brāhmaṇas about the Dharma?
> The Greatly Vigorous One today
> has well provided thorough guidance.
> I should now repay
> the kindness of Sātāgiri,
> who was able to tell me and the others
> about the guiding teacher.
> I shall go from village to village,
> from house to house, following the Buddha,
> attending, making offerings with reverence,
> hearing the right Dharma from the Buddha.
> These hundreds of thousands of ghost-spirits
> all join their palms in reverence.
> All take refuge in the Buddha,
> the Muni, the great teacher.
> He has gained the unsurpassed name;
> he has certainly seen the true meaning.
> He has accomplished great wisdom;
> toward desires he is unattached.
> The wise should observe:
> the one who rescues and protects the world
> has attained the Noble path;
> this is the great sage."

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, Sātāgiri, Hemavata, and all their attendants, the five hundred ghost-spirits, having heard what the Buddha said, were all greatly delighted, rejoiced, paid respects to the Buddha, and departed.

---

### (1330)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra and Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana were staying on Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa.

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra had newly shaved his hair and beard. There were two yakṣas, named Ghaṭa and Upaghaṭa. The yakṣa Upaghaṭa saw Venerable Śāriputra with his newly shaved head and said to the yakṣa Ghaṭa: "I am now going to strike that śramaṇa on the head."

The yakṣa Ghaṭa said: "Upaghaṭa! Do not say such things. This śramaṇa is of great virtue and great power. Do not bring upon yourself great harm and suffering for a long time!" He said this three times.

But the yakṣa Upaghaṭa three times ignored the words of the yakṣa Ghaṭa and struck Venerable Śāriputra on the head with his hand. Having struck him, he immediately cried out: "I am burning, Ghaṭa! I am boiling, Ghaṭa!" After crying out three times, he sank into the earth and fell into the Avīci Hell.

Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, hearing the sound of Venerable Śāriputra being struck by the yakṣa, went to Venerable Śāriputra and asked him: "How is it, Venerable One? Can you bear the pain?"

Venerable Śāriputra replied: "Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana, although there is pain, my mind can endure it. It does not amount to great suffering."

Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana said to Venerable Śāriputra: "How remarkable, Venerable Śāriputra! You are truly of great virtue and great power. If this yakṣa had struck Mount Gṛdhrakūṭa with his hand, he could have crushed it to chaff and powder. How much more when striking a person, that there should be no pain!"

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra said to Venerable Mahāmaudgalyāyana: "I truly am not in great pain."

Then Venerable Śāriputra and Mahāmaudgalyāyana consoled one another.

At that time, the World-Honored One, hearing their conversation with his divine ear, thereupon spoke this verse:

> "One whose mind is like solid rock,
> firmly established, not swaying,
> having abandoned attachment,
> when angered, does not retaliate.
> If one thus cultivates the mind,
> how could there be pain or sorrow?"

After the Buddha had spoken this sūtra, the bhikṣus, having heard what the Buddha said, were delighted and practiced accordingly.

---

### (1331)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a number of bhikṣus traveling among the people in the country of Kośala were staying in a certain forest for the summer retreat. There was a celestial spirit dwelling in that forest who knew that on the fifteenth day the bhikṣus would perform the pavāraṇā ceremony (end-of-rains invitation), and became deeply sorrowful.

Another celestial spirit said to that celestial spirit: "Why have you suddenly become so sad and distressed? You should rejoice that the bhikṣus keep the precepts in purity and are today performing the pavāraṇā."

The forest-dwelling celestial spirit replied: "I know that the bhikṣus are performing the pavāraṇā today. It is not like the shameless non-Buddhist practitioners who perform their pavāraṇā. However, these diligent bhikṣus, after performing the pavāraṇā, will take their robes and bowls and tomorrow go elsewhere. This forest will then be empty."

After the bhikṣus had departed, the forest-dwelling celestial spirit spoke this verse:

> "Now my heart is joyless,
> seeing only empty forest trees.
> The bhikṣus of pure minds who teach the Dharma,
> learned in much hearing,
> disciples of Gautama,
> where have they all gone now?"

At that time, another celestial being spoke this verse:

> "Some have gone to Magadha,
> some to Kośala,
> and some to the Vajra Land.
> Everywhere they cultivate seclusion,
> like wild birds and beasts
> who roam wherever they please."

---

### (1332)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu was among the people in the country of Kośala, dwelling in a certain forest. He entered daytime meditation, but his body became extremely fatigued, and at night he slept.

At that time, the celestial spirit dwelling in that forest thought: "This is not the conduct of a bhikṣu. In an empty forest, he enters daytime meditation yet at night he sleeps. I should now go and awaken him."

Then the celestial spirit went before the bhikṣu and spoke this verse:

> "Bhikṣu, arise, arise!
> Why do you indulge in sleep?
> What benefit is there in sleep?
> When you are sick, why not sleep then?
> When a sharp thorn pierces your body,
> how can you sleep?
> You originally left the household life;
> what was your aspiration for going forth?
> You should act in accordance with your original aspiration,
> day and night seeking progress.
> Do not fall into sleep,
> letting your mind be uncontrolled.
> Impermanence, ever unstable in its desires,
> intoxicates and deludes the fool.
> Others are all still bound;
> you have already been liberated.
> With right faith you went forth;
> why do you indulge in sleep?
> Having already tamed craving and desire,
> your mind has gained liberation.
> Endowed with supreme, wondrous wisdom,
> having gone forth, why do you sleep?
> Diligently cultivate vigor and right samādhi,
> always practicing firm and steady strength.
> Single-mindedly seeking parinirvāṇa,
> how can you sleep?
> Arouse knowledge, sever ignorance,
> completely destroy all outflows.
> Tame this final body;
> how can you indulge in sleep?"

When that celestial spirit spoke these verses, that bhikṣu, hearing what was said, contemplated with focused diligence and attained arhatship.

---

### (1333)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu was in the country of Kośala, dwelling in a forest. He entered daytime meditation, but unwholesome thoughts arose in his mind, resting upon evil craving.

At that time, the celestial spirit dwelling in that forest thought: "This is not the conduct of a bhikṣu, dwelling in the forest, entering daytime meditation, yet producing unwholesome thoughts resting upon evil craving. I should now go and enlighten him."

Then that celestial spirit spoke this verse:

> "Your mind desired seclusion,
> rightly dwelling in an empty forest.
> Yet your distracted mind follows external conditions,
> with confused thoughts running wildly.
> Tame the mind that delights in the world;
> always delight in the mind's liberation.
> You should abandon the mind of discontent;
> grasp and dwell in ease and joy.
> Do not think contrary to right mindfulness;
> do not cling to 'I' and 'mine.'
> It is as if one dyes with dust upon one's head;
> such attachment is extremely hard to remove.
> Do not let attachment and clinging
> be stirred up by the turbid mind of desire.
> Like a warrior elephant shaking off dust,
> vigorous, casting away filth,
> the bhikṣu, upon his own body,
> with right mindfulness removes the dust.
> 'Dust' means craving and desire,
> not the dust and dirt of the world.
> The clever and wise one
> should awaken to those dusts.
> In the Tathāgata's Dharma and discipline,
> hold the mind and do not be heedless.
> 'Dust and defilement' means anger and hatred,
> not the dust and dirt of the world.
> The clever and wise one
> should awaken to those dusts.
> In the Tathāgata's Dharma and discipline,
> hold the mind and do not be heedless.
> 'Dust and defilement' means delusion,
> not the dust and dirt of the world.
> The bright, wise, and clever one
> should abandon those dusts.
> In the Tathāgata's Dharma and discipline,
> hold the mind and do not be heedless."

After that celestial spirit had spoken these verses, that bhikṣu, hearing what was said, contemplated with focused diligence, severed all afflictions of the mind, and attained arhatship.

---

### (1334)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu was among the people of Kośala, dwelling in a certain forest. He entered daytime meditation, but improper thoughts arose.

At that time, the celestial spirit dwelling in that forest thought: "This is not the conduct of a bhikṣu, dwelling in the forest, entering daytime meditation, yet producing improper thoughts. I should now go and skillfully awaken him."

Then that celestial spirit spoke this verse:

> "Why do improper thoughts
> consume your contemplation and rest?
> You should abandon improper mindfulness
> and devote yourself to right samādhi.
> Revere the Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha,
> and your own pure precepts.
> Always give rise to the mind of sympathetic joy;
> joyful delight will further advance.
> Because the mind is joyful,
> you will swiftly reach the end of suffering."

After that celestial spirit had spoken the verse to encourage him, that bhikṣu contemplated with focused diligence, exhausted all afflictions, and attained arhatship.

---

### (1335)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu was among the people of Kośala, dwelling in a certain forest, entering daytime meditation. At that time, at midday, a feeling of discontent arose in that bhikṣu, and he spoke this verse:

> "At this midday time,
> all the birds are silent.
> In the empty wilderness, a sudden sound
> causes fear in my heart."

At that time, the celestial spirit dwelling in that forest spoke this verse:

> "At this midday time,
> all the birds are quiet.
> The sudden sound in the empty wilderness
> responds to your discontented mind.
> You should abandon discontent
> and devote yourself to cultivating right samādhi."

After that celestial being had spoken the verse to awaken that bhikṣu, that bhikṣu contemplated with focused diligence, cast off afflictions, and attained arhatship.

---

### (1336)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Venerable Anuruddha was among the people of Kośala, dwelling in a certain forest.

At that time, there was a celestial spirit named Jālinī, who was a former close acquaintance of Venerable Anuruddha. She went to visit Venerable Anuruddha and, arriving at his place, spoke this verse:

> "You should now make a vow,
> wishing to be reborn in your former place,
> in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three,
> where all five sensual pleasures are complete.
> Hundreds of kinds of music
> are always there for your enjoyment.
> Whenever you fall asleep,
> music awakens you.
> Hosts of celestial maidens
> attend at your side day and night."

Venerable Anuruddha spoke a verse in reply:

> "Hosts of celestial maidens:
> these are all great heaps of suffering.
> Due to inverted perceptions,
> they are bound by the view of a self.
> Those who seek rebirth there:
> this too is great suffering.
> Jālinī, you should know
> I do not wish to be born there.
> Birth and death have been forever ended;
> I will not receive any further existence."

When Venerable Anuruddha spoke these words, the celestial spirit Jālinī, hearing what Venerable Anuruddha said, was delighted, rejoiced, and then vanished.

---

### (1337)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was a bhikṣu among the people of Kośala, dwelling in a forest. He diligently recited the sūtras and diligently taught and discussed them. Through earnest contemplation, he attained the fruit of arhatship. Having attained it, he no longer diligently recited or taught.

At that time, a celestial spirit dwelling in that forest spoke this verse:

> "Bhikṣu, in former times
> you diligently recited and studied day and night,
> always discussing with other bhikṣus
> the definitive meaning.
> But now regarding the Dharma-verses
> you are silent and say nothing,
> not discussing with other bhikṣus
> the definitive meaning."

At that time, that bhikṣu spoke a verse in reply:

> "Formerly, when I had not yet separated from desire,
> my mind always delighted in the Dharma-verses.
> Now that I have attained the correspondence with the separation from desire,
> the task of reciting and teaching is finished.
> One who already knows, the path is fully complete;
> what use is hearing or seeing the path?
> As for worldly hearing and seeing,
> the ignorant all abandon them heedlessly."

At that time, that celestial spirit, hearing what the bhikṣu said, was delighted, rejoiced, and then vanished.

---

### (1338)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu was among the people of Kośala, dwelling in a certain forest. That bhikṣu had an eye ailment, and his teacher instructed him to inhale the fragrance of padma flowers. At that time, that bhikṣu, having received his teacher's instruction, went to the side of a padma pond. At the bank of the pond, he sat facing the wind and inhaled the fragrance as it came with the breeze.

At that time, a celestial spirit, the guardian of that pond, said to the bhikṣu: "Why are you stealing the flowers? You are now a thief of fragrance!"

At that time, the bhikṣu spoke a verse in reply:

> "I do not damage or pluck them;
> from afar I merely follow and inhale the fragrance.
> Why do you now say
> that I am a thief of fragrance?"

At that time, the celestial spirit spoke another verse:

> "One who does not seek yet does not give up;
> the world calls such a one a thief.
> You now, without anyone's permission,
> just take it for yourself in one direction.
> This is what the world calls
> a true thief of fragrance."

At that time, a certain man was pulling out lotus roots, heavily loaded, and was about to leave.

At that time, the bhikṣu spoke a verse to that celestial spirit:

> "Look at that man now,
> cutting apart the puṇḍarīka lotuses,
> pulling up roots, heavily loaded and leaving;
> he is a villainous and deceitful person.
> Why do you not stop him,
> yet say I am stealing fragrance?"

At that time, that celestial spirit spoke a verse in reply:

> "A wild and deceitful, villainous person
> is like a wet-nurse's garment;
> what use is it to speak to such a one?
> But it is fitting to speak with you.
> On a kāṣāya robe, stains do not show;
> on black cloth, ink does not stain.
> To a villainous and wicked person,
> the world does not bother speaking.
> But a fly's footprint stains white silk;
> to the clear-sighted, a small fault is visible.
> Like a spot of ink on a conch shell,
> though small, it is entirely visible.
> One who always seeks purity from such things,
> free from bonds and afflictions:
> even a hair's worth of evil in such a one
> people see as large as Mount Sumeru."

At that time, that bhikṣu spoke another verse:

> "Excellent, excellent, well spoken!
> With this meaning you comfort me.
> Would you always, for my sake,
> repeatedly speak such verses?"

At that time, that celestial spirit spoke another verse:

> "I am not your purchased servant,
> nor was I given to you by anyone.
> Why should I always follow you
> and repeatedly admonish you?
> You yourself should know
> what is beneficial in each and every case."

After that celestial being had spoken these verses, that bhikṣu, hearing what was said, was delighted and rejoiced, rose from his seat and departed. In a secluded place, he contemplated with focused diligence, severed all afflictions, and attained arhatship.

---

### (1339)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, Venerable Daśabala Kāśyapa was dwelling in the Seer's Cave in Rājagṛha. At that time, there was a hunter named Chichi, who was not far from Daśabala Kāśyapa, spreading nets to catch deer. At that time, Daśabala Kāśyapa, out of compassion for that hunter, taught him the Dharma. But the hunter did not understand what was said. Then Daśabala Kāśyapa, through supernatural power, caused fire to blaze from the tip of his finger. Even so, the hunter still did not awaken.

At that time, a celestial spirit dwelling in the Seer's Cave spoke this verse:

> "A hunter deep in the mountains,
> of little wisdom, blind and without eyes:
> why teach the Dharma at an improper time?
> He is thin in merit and without discernment.
> What he hears he also does not understand;
> even in brightness he sees nothing.
> Regarding the wholesome and supreme Dharma,
> the fool is utterly unable to comprehend.
> Even if all ten fingers were set ablaze,
> he would never see the truth."

After that celestial spirit had spoken these verses, Venerable Daśabala Kāśyapa thereupon remained in silence.

---

### (1340)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, there was Venerable Vajraputra, dwelling in a forest in Pāṭaliputra. At that time, the people of Pāṭaliputra, after the four months of summer had passed, held a Kaumudī great festival.

At that time, Venerable Vajraputra, hearing the worldly festival, produced a feeling of discontent and spoke this verse:

> "Alone in an empty forest,
> like a discarded withered log.
> The four months of summer have ended;
> the world delights in adornment.
> Looking all around the world,
> no one's suffering surpasses mine."

At that time, a celestial spirit dwelling in that forest spoke this verse:

> "Alone in an empty forest,
> like a discarded withered log:
> this is what the Thirty-Three Gods
> always wish and aspire to in their hearts.
> It is like those in hell
> who look upward, yearning for birth in the human realm."

At that time, Vajraputra, having been encouraged by that celestial spirit, contemplated with focused diligence, severed all afflictions, and attained arhatship.

---

### (1341)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu was among the people of Kośala, dwelling in a certain forest. He only delighted in maintaining the precepts and was unable to increase and advance in higher virtues.

At that time, the celestial spirit dwelling in that forest thought: "This is not the conduct of a bhikṣu, dwelling in the forest, only delighting in maintaining the precepts and unable to cultivate higher virtues. I should now devise a means to awaken him." Then it spoke this verse:

> "Not by precept-keeping alone,
> nor by cultivating much learning,
> nor by solitary, quiet dhyāna-samādhi,
> nor by secluded, remote dwelling.
> A bhikṣu who rests one-sidedly
> will never reach the end of outflows.
> The joy of equal and right awakening
> is far beyond the reach of ordinary people."

At that time, that bhikṣu, having been encouraged by the celestial spirit, contemplated with focused diligence, severed all afflictions, and attained arhatship.

---

### (1342)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

There was Venerable Nāgadatta among the people of Kośala, dwelling in a certain forest. He had frequent close association with both laypersons and monastics.

At that time, the celestial spirit dwelling in that forest thought: "This is not the conduct of a bhikṣu, dwelling in the forest yet associating and socializing closely with laypersons and monastics. I should now go and skillfully awaken him." Then it spoke this verse:

> "The bhikṣu goes out early in the morning,
> returning to the forest at late dusk.
> When renunciants and laypersons are closely familiar,
> they must share in each other's joys and sorrows.
> I fear that heedlessness will arise from household ways,
> and he will follow Māra's control."

At that time, Venerable Nāgadatta, having been thus and thus enlightened by that celestial spirit, thus and thus contemplated with focused diligence, severed all afflictions, and attained arhatship.

---

### (1343)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a number of bhikṣus were among the people of Kośala, dwelling in a certain forest. They engaged in talk and play, spending the whole day scattered and confused, their minds unable to attain concentration, their sense-faculties unrestrained, running after the six sense-objects.

At that time, the celestial spirit dwelling in that forest, seeing these bhikṣus with their unrestrained deportment, was displeased in heart and spoke this verse:

> "Previously there were disciples of Gautama
> who lived rightly here.
> With impermanent mind they begged for food;
> with impermanent mind they accepted bedding.
> Because they contemplated the impermanence of the world,
> they reached the end of suffering.
> But now there are those difficult to maintain,
> śramaṇas who dwell in this place.
> Everywhere they seek food and drink,
> wandering to the houses of others.
> They have gone forth hoping for wealth,
> without genuine aspiration for the śramaṇa's way.
> Their saṃghāṭī robes hang down
> like an old ox dragging its tail."

At that time, the bhikṣus said to the celestial spirit: "Do you wish to disparage us?"

Then that celestial spirit spoke another verse:

> "I do not point out names or surnames,
> nor do I wrongly identify persons.
> Rather, I address the whole assembly
> about their unwholesome conduct.
> Those who show obvious flaws,
> I point out their faults as a skillful means.
> To those who diligently cultivate with vigor,
> I take refuge in and respectfully honor."

Those bhikṣus, having been encouraged by the celestial spirit, contemplated with focused diligence, severed all afflictions, and attained arhatship.

---

### (1344)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu was among the people of Kośala, dwelling in a certain forest. That bhikṣu had been playful with a householder's woman, and an evil reputation arose.

At that time, that bhikṣu thought: "I am now in a disgraceful situation. Together with another's woman, an evil reputation has arisen. I should now kill myself here in this forest."

At that time, the celestial spirit dwelling in that forest thought: "This is unwholesome and improper. This bhikṣu is not corrupted and is without fault, yet he wishes to kill himself in the forest. I should now devise a means to enlighten him."

Then that celestial spirit transformed into the form of a householder's daughter and said to the bhikṣu: "In all the streets, lanes, and crossroads, the people of the world have spread an evil reputation about me and you, saying that you and I have associated intimately and done improper things. Since there is already an evil reputation, you should now return to lay life, and we can enjoy ourselves together."

The bhikṣu replied: "Because in those streets, lanes, and crossroads, the people have spread an evil reputation about me and you, saying we have associated intimately and done improper things, I shall now kill myself!"

Then that celestial spirit resumed its celestial form and spoke this verse:

> "Though one hears many evil rumors,
> an ascetic endures them.
> One should not torment oneself with suffering,
> nor should one give rise to vexation.
> One who is frightened upon hearing sounds
> is but a beast of the forest,
> a frivolous creature;
> such is not the way of one who has gone forth.
> The benevolent one should endure patiently;
> one should not dwell in evil reputation.
> One who holds the mind firmly
> fulfills the way of one who has gone forth.
> It is not due to others' words
> that you become a bandit.
> Nor is it due to others' words
> that you become an arhat.
> As you yourself know yourself,
> so the devas also know you."

At that time, that bhikṣu, having been enlightened by that celestial spirit, contemplated with focused diligence, severed afflictions, and attained arhatship.

---

### (1345)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, there was Venerable Bhikṣu Dṛṣṭānta (Jiàn Duō, "Many-Seeing") among the people of Kośala, dwelling in a certain forest, wearing rag-robes. At that time, Brahmā, together with seven hundred Brahma-deities, riding upon their palaces, came to visit Venerable Bhikṣu Dṛṣṭānta, paying homage with reverence.

At that time, a celestial spirit dwelling in that forest spoke this verse:

> "Behold, his faculties are tranquil,
> able to inspire good offerings.
> Endowed with the three knowledges,
> having attained the unshakeable Dharma.
> Having crossed beyond all expedients,
> a humble wearer of rag-robes.
> Seven hundred Brahma-beings,
> riding their palaces, come to pay respects.
> They see the one who has reached the end of birth and death;
> now they bow to the one who has crossed to the other shore."

After that celestial spirit had spoken the verse praising Venerable Bhikṣu Dṛṣṭānta, it vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1346)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu was among the people of Kośala, dwelling in a certain forest. That bhikṣu's body was extremely fatigued, and at night he slept.

At that time, a celestial spirit dwelling in that forest came to awaken him and spoke this verse:

> "You may rise, rise, bhikṣu!
> Why do you indulge in sleep?
> What meaning is there in sleep?
> Cultivate meditation; do not sleep!"

At that time, that bhikṣu spoke a verse in reply:

> "I am unwilling; what should I do?
> I am lazy and have little means.
> My conditions are exhausted, my four limbs are weak;
> at night I then fall asleep."

At that time, that celestial spirit spoke another verse:

> "For now you should just hold on,
> and do not cry out loudly.
> You have already attained the leisure for practice;
> do not let it decline and be lost."

At that time, that bhikṣu spoke a verse in reply:

> "I shall heed your words,
> diligently cultivating with skillful means.
> I shall not let sleep
> repeatedly overwhelm my mind."

At that time, that celestial spirit thus and thus awakened that bhikṣu. That bhikṣu, with focused vigor, severed all afflictions and attained arhatship. Then that celestial spirit spoke another verse:

> "Were you not able to rise by yourself,
> with focused vigor and diligent means,
> not letting Māra's army
> weary you into sleep?"

At that time, that bhikṣu spoke a verse in reply:

> "From now, for seven nights,
> I shall constantly sit in right contemplation.
> My body shall give rise to joy and bliss,
> with not a single place unfilled.
> In the first watch I contemplate past lives.
> In the middle watch, the divine eye is purified.
> In the last watch, I destroy ignorance,
> seeing the suffering and joy of beings,
> upper, middle, and lower forms,
> good appearances and bad appearances,
> knowing what karmic causes and conditions
> bring about these fruitions.
> Whatever a man has done,
> what he has done, he himself sees.
> The good one sees his own goodness;
> the evil one himself sees his evil."

At that time, that celestial spirit spoke another verse:

> "I knew previously that all
> fourteen bhikṣus
> were all stream-enterers (sotāpanna),
> having all attained right samādhi in dhyāna.
> When they came to this forest,
> they were to attain arhatship.
> But seeing you alone being lazy,
> lying on your back asleep,
> lest you remain an ordinary person,
> I therefore skillfully awakened you."

At that time, the bhikṣu spoke another verse:

> "Excellent, celestial spirit!
> You have comforted me with meaning.
> With sincerity you enlightened me,
> causing me to exhaust all outflows."

At that time, that celestial spirit spoke another verse:

> "A bhikṣu should be like this,
> with faith leaving the household life.
> Even one who is foolish when going forth
> can attain the purity of vision.
> The Dharma that I now take up and protect,
> I shall contemplate for the rest of my life.
> If you should fall ill,
> I shall provide you with good medicine."

After that celestial spirit had spoken these verses, it vanished and was no longer seen.

---

### (1347)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra was among the people of Kośala, staying near a village, dwelling beside a field. At that time, Venerable Śāriputra, in the early morning, put on his robe and took his bowl, and entered the village to beg for food.

At that time, there was a Nirgrantha (Jain ascetic), drunk and intoxicated with wine, holding a jug of wine as he came out of the village. He saw Venerable Śāriputra and spoke this verse:

> "Rice-liquor perfumes my body;
> I carry a jug of rice-liquor.
> Mountains, land, grass, and trees,
> I see them all as golden in color."

At that time, Venerable Śāriputra thought: "This one making such ugly sounds is an evil, wicked creature, yet he speaks this verse. How could I not reply with a verse?" Then Venerable Śāriputra spoke this verse:

> "Suffused with the perception-free taste,
> I carry the jar of emptiness-samādhi.
> Mountains, land, grass, and trees,
> I see them all as spit and phlegm."

---

### (1348)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in the country of Śrāvastī, at Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍada's Park.

At that time, a certain bhikṣu was among the people of Kośala, dwelling in a certain forest. He had attained the knowledge of others' minds, but still had remaining afflictions. Not far from the forest there was a well, and a jackal had gotten its neck caught in a jar-hook used for drawing water.

At that time, that jackal tried various means to free itself and thought to itself: "Day is about to dawn, and the farmers may come out. They will surely frighten me. You water-jar, you have frightened me for so long; can you now let me go?"

At that time, that bhikṣu, knowing the thoughts in that jackal's mind, spoke this verse:

> "When the sun of the Tathāgata's wisdom rises,
> leaving the forest, he teaches the Dharma of emptiness.
> O mind, you have long frightened me;
> now you may release me and let me go."

At that time, that bhikṣu, having thus instructed himself, exhausted all fetters and attained arhatship.

---

### (1349)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was traveling among the people of Kośala and was staying in a certain forest.

At that time, a celestial spirit dependent on that forest saw the Buddha's footprints, bowed its head, and gazed intently, cultivating recollection of the Buddha. At that time, there was an owl standing in the path, about to step on the Buddha's footprint.

At that time, the celestial spirit spoke this verse:

> "You now, owl,
> round-eyed one perched among the trees,
> do not disturb the Tathāgata's footprint
> and ruin my contemplation of the Buddha."

After that celestial spirit had spoken this verse, it silently contemplated the Buddha.

---

### (1350)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was among the people of Kośala, staying in a certain forest, dwelling beneath a pāṭali tree.

At that time, a celestial spirit dependent on that forest spoke this verse:

> "Today the wind suddenly rises,
> blowing the pāṭali tree,
> and scattering pāṭali blossoms
> as an offering to the Tathāgata."

After that celestial spirit had spoken the verse, it silently remained.

---

### (1351)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a number of bhikṣus were staying on the slopes of Mount Caitya. They were all forest-dwelling (āraṇyaka) bhikṣus who wore rag-robes and always practiced alms-begging.

At that time, the mountain spirit dwelling on that mountain spoke this verse:

> "Peacocks with embroidered bodies,
> dwelling on Mount Vaidehī,
> at the proper time utter their wondrous calls,
> awakening the alms-begging bhikṣus.
> Peacocks with embroidered bodies,
> dwelling on Mount Vaidehī,
> at the proper time utter their wondrous calls,
> awakening the rag-robe wearers.
> Peacocks with embroidered bodies,
> dwelling on Mount Vaidehī,
> at the proper time utter their wondrous calls,
> awakening those who sit beneath trees."

After that celestial spirit had spoken these verses, it silently remained.

---

### (1352)

Thus have I heard. At one time, the Buddha was dwelling in Rājagṛha at the Kalandaka Bamboo Grove.

At that time, a number of bhikṣus were staying on Mount Caitya. All of them practiced the forest-dwelling (āraṇyaka) conduct, wore rag-robes, and always practiced alms-begging. At that time, the bank of the Nasākhata River collapsed and killed three bhikṣus who were engaged in construction work.

At that time, the celestial spirit dwelling on Mount Caitya spoke this verse:

> "Alms-begging forest-dwellers,
> be careful not to engage in construction work!
> Did you not see the Khata River,
>
